USER: In delivering my son from me, I bury a second husband.
BOT: And I in going, madam, weep o'er my father's death anew; but I must attend his majesty's command, to whom I am now in ward, evermore in subjection.
USER: What hope is there of his majesty's amendment?
BOT: He hath abandon'd his physicians, madam; under whose practices he hath persecuted time with hope, and finds no other advantage in the process but only the losing of hope by time.
USER: How called you the man you speak of, madam?
BOT: He was famous, sir, in his profession, and it was his great right to be so: Gerard de Narbon.
USER: He was excellent indeed, madam; the king very lately spoke of him admiringly, and mourningly; he was skilful enough to have liv'd still, if knowledge could be set up against mortality.
BOT: What is it, my good lord, the king languishes of?
USER: A fistula, my lord.
BOT: I heard not of it before.
USER: I would it were not notorious. Was this gentlewoman the daughter of Gerard de Narbon?
BOT: Your commendations, madam, get from her tears.
USER: I do affect a sorrow indeed, but I have it too.
BOT: Moderate lamentation is the right of the dead; excessive grief the enemy to the living.
USER: If the living be enemy to the grief, the excess makes it soon mortal.
BOT: Madam, I desire your holy wishes.
USER: How understand we that?
BOT: He cannot want the best That shall attend his love.
USER: Heaven bless him! Farewell, Bertram.
BOT: The best wishes that can be forg'd in your thoughts be servants to you! much of her.
USER: Farewell, pretty lady, you must hold the credit of your father.
BOT: Save you, fair queen!
USER: And you, monarch!
BOT: No.
USER: And no.
BOT: Are you meditating on virginity?
USER: Ay. You have some stain of soldier in you; let me ask you a question. Man is enemy to virginity; how may we barricado it against him?
BOT: Keep him out.
USER: But he assails; and our virginity, though valiant, in the defence, yet is weak. Unfold to us some warlike resistance.
BOT: There is none. Man setting down before you will undermine you and blow you up.
USER: Bless our poor virginity from underminers and blowers-up! Is there no military policy how virgins might blow up men?
BOT: I will stand for't a little, though therefore I die a virgin.
USER: How might one do, sir, to lose it to her own liking?
BOT: What one, i' faith?
USER: That I wish well. 'Tis pity -
BOT: What's pity?
USER: Monsieur Parolles, my lord calls for you.
BOT: Little Helen, farewell. If I can remember thee, I will think of thee at court.
USER: Monsieur Parolles, you were born under a charitable star.
BOT: Under Mars, I.
USER: I especially think, under Mars.
BOT: Why under Mars?
USER: The wars hath so kept you under, that you must needs be born under Mars.
BOT: When he was predominant.
USER: When he was retrograde, I think rather.
BOT: Why think you so?
USER: You go so much backward when you fight.
BOT: That's for advantage.
USER: So is running away, when fear proposes the safety: but the composition that your valour and fear makes in you is a virtue of a good wing, and I like the wear well.

USER: The Florentines and Senoys are by th' ears; Have fought with equal fortune, and continue A braving war.
BOT: So 'tis reported, sir.
USER: His love and wisdom, Approv'd so to your majesty, may plead For amplest credence.
BOT: He hath arm'd our answer, And Florence is denied before he comes: Yet, for our gentlemen that mean to see The Tuscan service, freely have they leave To stand on either part.
USER: It well may serve A nursery to our gentry, who are sick For breathing and exploit.
BOT: What's he comes here?
USER: It is the Count Rossillon, my good lord, Young Bertram.
BOT: Youth, thou bear'st thy father's face; Frank nature, rather curious than in haste, Hath well compos'd thee. Thy father's moral parts Mayst thou inherit too! Welcome to Paris.
USER: My thanks and duty are your majesty's.
BOT: His good remembrance, sir, Lies richer in your thoughts than on his tomb; So in approof lives not his epitaph As in your royal speech.
USER: You're lov'd, sir; They that least lend it you shall lack you first.
BOT: I fill a place, I know't. How long is't, Count, Since the physician at your father's died? He was much fam'd.
USER: Some six months since, my lord.
BOT: If he were living, I would try him yet; - Lend me an arm; - the rest have worn me out With several applications; nature and sickness Debate it at their leisure. Welcome, Count; My son's no dearer.
USER: Thank your majesty.

USER: I will now hear. What say you of this gentlewoman?
BOT: 'Tis not unknown to you, madam, I am a poor fellow.
USER: Well, sir.
BOT: No, madam, 'tis not so well that I am poor, though many of the rich are damned; but if I may have your ladyship's good will to go to the world, Isbel the woman and I will do as we may.
USER: Wilt thou needs be a beggar?
BOT: I do beg your good will in this case.
USER: In what case?
BOT: In Isbel's case and mine own. Service is no heritage, and I think I shall never have the blessing of God till I have issue of my body; for they say barnes are blessings.
USER: Tell me thy reason why thou wilt marry.
BOT: My poor body, madam, requires it; I am driven on by the flesh, and he must needs go that the devil drives.
USER: Is this all your worship's reason?
BOT: Faith, madam, I have other holy reasons, such as they are.
USER: May the world know them?
BOT: I have been, madam, a wicked creature, as you and all flesh and blood are; and indeed I do marry that I may repent.
USER: Thy marriage, sooner than thy wickedness.
BOT: I am out of friends, madam, and I hope to have friends for my wife's sake.
USER: Such friends are thine enemies, knave.
BOT: Wilt thou ever be a foul-mouth'd and calumnious knave?
USER: A prophet I, madam; and I speak the truth the next way: _For I the ballad will repeat, Which men full true shall find; Your marriage comes by destiny, Your cuckoo sings by kind._
BOT: Get you gone, sir; I'll talk with you more anon.
USER: May it please you, madam, that he bid Helen come to you; of her I am to speak.
BOT: Sirrah, tell my gentlewoman I would speak with her; Helen I mean.
USER: What, one good in ten? You corrupt the song, sirrah.
BOT: You'll be gone, sir knave, and do as I command you!
USER: Well, now.
BOT: I know, madam, you love your gentlewoman entirely.
USER: What is your pleasure, madam?
BOT: You know, Helen, I am a mother to you.
USER: Mine honourable mistress.
BOT: That I am not.
USER: I say, I am your mother.
BOT: Nor I your mother?
USER: Good madam, pardon me.
BOT: Do you love my son?
USER: Your pardon, noble mistress.
BOT: Love you my son?
USER: Do not you love him, madam?
BOT: Go not about; my love hath in't a bond Whereof the world takes note. Come, come, disclose The state of your affection, for your passions Have to the full appeach'd.
USER: Had you not lately an intent, - speak truly, - To go to Paris?
BOT: Madam, I had.
USER: Wherefore? tell true.
BOT: This was your motive For Paris, was it? Speak.
USER: My lord your son made me to think of this; Else Paris, and the medicine, and the king, Had from the conversation of my thoughts Haply been absent then.
BOT: Dost thou believe't?
USER: Ay, madam, knowingly.

USER: Farewell, young lords; these warlike principles Do not throw from you; and you, my lords, farewell; Share the advice betwixt you; if both gain all, The gift doth stretch itself as 'tis receiv'd, And is enough for both.
BOT: 'Tis our hope, sir, After well-ent'red soldiers, to return And find your grace in health.
USER: Health, at your bidding serve your majesty!
BOT: Those girls of Italy, take heed of them; They say our French lack language to deny If they demand; beware of being captives Before you serve.
USER: Our hearts receive your warnings.
BOT: Farewell. - Come hither to me.
USER: O my sweet lord, that you will stay behind us!
BOT: 'Tis not his fault; the spark.
USER: O, 'tis brave wars!
BOT: Most admirable! I have seen those wars.
USER: I am commanded here, and kept a coil with, "Too young", and "the next year" and "'tis too early".
BOT: An thy mind stand to't, boy, steal away bravely.
USER: I shall stay here the forehorse to a smock, Creaking my shoes on the plain masonry, Till honour be bought up, and no sword worn But one to dance with. By heaven, I'll steal away.
BOT: There's honour in the theft.
USER: Commit it, count.
BOT: I am your accessary; and so farewell.
USER: I grow to you, and our parting is a tortur'd body.
BOT: Farewell, captain.
USER: Sweet Monsieur Parolles!
BOT: We shall, noble captain.
USER: Mars dote on you for his novices! What will ye do?
BOT: Stay the king.
USER: And I will do so.
BOT: Worthy fellows, and like to prove most sinewy sword-men.
USER: Pardon, my lord [_kneeling_], for me and for my tidings.
BOT: I'll fee thee to stand up.
USER: Then here's a man stands that has brought his pardon. I would you had kneel'd, my lord, to ask me mercy, And that at my bidding you could so stand up.
BOT: I would I had; so I had broke thy pate, And ask'd thee mercy for't.
USER: Good faith, across; But, my good lord, 'tis thus: will you be cur'd Of your infirmity?
BOT: No.
USER: What 'her' is this?
BOT: Now, good Lafew, Bring in the admiration; that we with thee May spend our wonder too, or take off thine By wond'ring how thou took'st it.
USER: Nay, I'll fit you, And not be all day neither.
BOT: Thus he his special nothing ever prologues.
USER: Nay, come your ways.
BOT: This haste hath wings indeed.
USER: Nay, come your ways. This is his majesty, say your mind to him. A traitor you do look like, but such traitors His majesty seldom fears; I am Cressid's uncle, That dare leave two together. Fare you well.
BOT: Now, fair one, does your business follow us?
USER: Ay, my good lord. Gerard de Narbon was my father, In what he did profess, well found.
BOT: I knew him.
USER: My duty then shall pay me for my pains. I will no more enforce mine office on you, Humbly entreating from your royal thoughts A modest one to bear me back again.
BOT: I must not hear thee. Fare thee well, kind maid. Thy pains, not us'd, must by thyself be paid; Proffers, not took, reap thanks for their reward.
USER: Art thou so confident? Within what space Hop'st thou my cure?
BOT: Upon thy certainty and confidence What dar'st thou venture?
USER: Tax of impudence, A strumpet's boldness, a divulged shame, Traduc'd by odious ballads; my maiden's name Sear'd otherwise; nay worse of worst extended With vilest torture, let my life be ended.
BOT: If I break time, or flinch in property Of what I spoke, unpitied let me die, And well deserv'd. Not helping, death's my fee; But if I help, what do you promise me?
USER: Make thy demand.
BOT: But will you make it even?
USER: Ay, by my sceptre and my hopes of heaven.

USER: Come on, sir; I shall now put you to the height of your breeding.
BOT: I will show myself highly fed and lowly taught. I know my business is but to the court.
USER: To the court! Why, what place make you special, when you put off that with such contempt? But to the court!
BOT: Marry, that's a bountiful answer that fits all questions.
USER: It is like a barber's chair, that fits all buttocks - the pin-buttock, the quatch-buttock, the brawn-buttock, or any buttock.
BOT: Will your answer serve fit to all questions?
USER: Have you, I say, an answer of such fitness for all questions?
BOT: From below your duke to beneath your constable, it will fit any question.
USER: It must be an answer of most monstrous size that must fit all demands.
BOT: But a trifle neither, in good faith, if the learned should speak truth of it. Here it is, and all that belongs to't. Ask me if I am a courtier; it shall do you no harm to learn.
USER: To be young again, if we could: I will be a fool in question, hoping to be the wiser by your answer. I pray you, sir, are you a courtier?
BOT: O Lord, sir! There's a simple putting off. More, more, a hundred of them.
USER: Sir, I am a poor friend of yours, that loves you.
BOT: O Lord, sir! Thick, thick; spare not me.
USER: I think, sir, you can eat none of this homely meat.
BOT: O Lord, sir! Nay, put me to't, I warrant you.
USER: You were lately whipp'd, sir, as I think.
BOT: O Lord, sir! Spare not me.
USER: Do you cry 'O Lord, sir!' at your whipping, and 'spare not me'? Indeed your 'O Lord, sir!' is very sequent to your whipping. You would answer very well to a whipping, if you were but bound to't.
BOT: I ne'er had worse luck in my life in my 'O Lord, sir!' I see things may serve long, but not serve ever.
USER: I play the noble housewife with the time, to entertain it so merrily with a fool.
BOT: O Lord, sir! Why, there't serves well again.
USER: An end, sir! To your business. Give Helen this, And urge her to a present answer back. Commend me to my kinsmen and my son. This is not much.
BOT: Not much commendation to them?
USER: Not much employment for you. You understand me?
BOT: Most fruitfully. I am there before my legs.
USER: Haste you again.

USER: Why, 'tis the rarest argument of wonder that hath shot out in our latter times.
BOT: And so 'tis.
USER: To be relinquish'd of the artists, -
BOT: So I say; both of Galen and Paracelsus.
USER: Of all the learned and authentic fellows, -
BOT: Right; so I say.
USER: That gave him out incurable, -
BOT: Why, there 'tis; so say I too.
USER: Not to be helped.
BOT: Right; as 'twere a man assur'd of a -
USER: Uncertain life and sure death.
BOT: Just; you say well. So would I have said.
USER: I may truly say, it is a novelty to the world.
BOT: It is indeed; if you will have it in showing, you shall read it in what do you call there?
USER: A showing of a heavenly effect in an earthly actor.
BOT: That's it; I would have said the very same.
USER: Why, your dolphin is not lustier; fore me, I speak in respect -
BOT: Nay, 'tis strange, 'tis very strange; that is the brief and the tedious of it; and he's of a most facinerious spirit that will not acknowledge it to be the -
USER: Very hand of heaven.
BOT: Ay, so I say.
USER: In a most weak -
BOT: And debile minister, great power, great transcendence, which should indeed give us a further use to be made than alone the recov'ry of the king, as to be -
USER: Generally thankful.
BOT: I would have said it; you say well. Here comes the king.
USER: Lustique, as the Dutchman says. I'll like a maid the better, whilst I have a tooth in my head. Why, he's able to lead her a coranto.
BOT: _Mor du vinager!_ is not this Helen?
USER: Fore God, I think so.
BOT: To each of you one fair and virtuous mistress Fall, when love please! Marry, to each but one!
USER: I'd give bay curtal and his furniture My mouth no more were broken than these boys', And writ as little beard.
BOT: Peruse them well. Not one of those but had a noble father. She addresses her to a Lord.
USER: Gentlemen, Heaven hath through me restor'd the king to health.
BOT: We understand it, and thank heaven for you.
USER: Make choice; and, see, Who shuns thy love shuns all his love in me.
BOT: Now, Dian, from thy altar do I fly, And to imperial Love, that god most high, Do my sighs stream. [_To first Lord._] Sir, will you hear my suit?
USER: And grant it.
BOT: Thanks, sir; all the rest is mute.
USER: I had rather be in this choice than throw ames-ace for my life.
BOT: Before I speak, too threat'ningly replies. Love make your fortunes twenty times above Her that so wishes, and her humble love!
USER: No better, if you please.
BOT: My wish receive, Which great Love grant; and so I take my leave.
USER: Do all they deny her? An they were sons of mine I'd have them whipp'd; or I would send them to th' Turk to make eunuchs of.
BOT: I'll never do you wrong for your own sake. Blessing upon your vows, and in your bed Find fairer fortune, if you ever wed!
USER: These boys are boys of ice, they'll none have her. Sure, they are bastards to the English; the French ne'er got 'em.
BOT: To make yourself a son out of my blood.
USER: Fair one, I think not so.
BOT: There's one grape yet. I am sure thy father drank wine. But if thou beest not an ass, I am a youth of fourteen; I have known thee already.
USER: Me and my service, ever whilst I live, Into your guiding power. This is the man.
BOT: Why, then, young Bertram, take her; she's thy wife.
USER: My wife, my liege! I shall beseech your highness, In such a business give me leave to use The help of mine own eyes.
BOT: Know'st thou not, Bertram, What she has done for me?
USER: Yes, my good lord, But never hope to know why I should marry her.
BOT: Thou know'st she has rais'd me from my sickly bed.
USER: But follows it, my lord, to bring me down Must answer for your raising? I know her well; She had her breeding at my father's charge: A poor physician's daughter my wife! Disdain Rather corrupt me ever!
BOT: I cannot love her, nor will strive to do 't.
USER: Thou wrong'st thyself, if thou shouldst strive to choose.
BOT: That you are well restor'd, my lord, I am glad. Let the rest go.
USER: Take her by the hand, And tell her she is thine; to whom I promise A counterpoise; if not to thy estate, A balance more replete.
BOT: I take her hand.
USER: Do you hear, monsieur? A word with you.
BOT: Your pleasure, sir.
USER: Your lord and master did well to make his recantation.
BOT: Recantation! My lord! My master!
USER: Ay. Is it not a language I speak?
BOT: A most harsh one, and not to be understood without bloody succeeding. My master!
USER: Are you companion to the Count Rossillon?
BOT: To any count; to all counts; to what is man.
USER: To what is count's man: count's master is of another style.
BOT: You are too old, sir; let it satisfy you, you are too old.
USER: I must tell thee, sirrah, I write man; to which title age cannot bring thee.
BOT: What I dare too well do, I dare not do.
USER: Hadst thou not the privilege of antiquity upon thee -
BOT: My lord, you give me most egregious indignity.
USER: Ay, with all my heart; and thou art worthy of it.
BOT: I have not, my lord, deserv'd it.
USER: Yes, good faith, every dram of it; and I will not bate thee a scruple.
BOT: Well, I shall be wiser.
USER: My lord, you do me most insupportable vexation.
BOT: I would it were hell-pains for thy sake, and my poor doing eternal; for doing I am past, as I will by thee, in what motion age will give me leave.
USER: Sirrah, your lord and master's married; there's news for you; you have a new mistress.
BOT: I most unfeignedly beseech your lordship to make some reservation of your wrongs. He is my good lord; whom I serve above is my master.
USER: Who? God?
BOT: Ay, sir.
USER: This is hard and undeserved measure, my lord.
BOT: Good, very good, it is so then. Good, very good; let it be conceal'd awhile.
USER: Undone, and forfeited to cares for ever!
BOT: What's the matter, sweetheart?
USER: Although before the solemn priest I have sworn, I will not bed her.
BOT: What, what, sweetheart?
USER: O my Parolles, they have married me! I'll to the Tuscan wars, and never bed her.
BOT: France is a dog-hole, and it no more merits The tread of a man's foot: to the wars!
USER: There's letters from my mother; what th' import is I know not yet.
BOT: Will this caprichio hold in thee, art sure?
USER: Go with me to my chamber and advise me. I'll send her straight away. Tomorrow I'll to the wars, she to her single sorrow.
BOT: Why, these balls bound; there's noise in it. 'Tis hard: A young man married is a man that's marr'd. Therefore away, and leave her bravely; go. The king has done you wrong; but hush 'tis so.

USER: My mother greets me kindly: is she well?
BOT: She is not well, but yet she has her health; she's very merry, but yet she is not well. But thanks be given, she's very well, and wants nothing i' the world; but yet she is not well.
USER: If she be very well, what does she ail that she's not very well?
BOT: Truly, she's very well indeed, but for two things.
USER: What two things?
BOT: One, that she's not in heaven, whither God send her quickly! The other, that she's in earth, from whence God send her quickly!
USER: Bless you, my fortunate lady!
BOT: I hope, sir, I have your good will to have mine own good fortune.
USER: You had my prayers to lead them on; and to keep them on, have them still. O, my knave how does my old lady?
BOT: So that you had her wrinkles and I her money, I would she did as you say.
USER: Why, I say nothing.
BOT: Away! Thou art a knave.
USER: You should have said, sir, before a knave thou art a knave; that is before me thou art a knave. This had been truth, sir.
BOT: Go to, thou art a witty fool; I have found thee.
USER: Did you find me in yourself, sir? or were you taught to find me? The search, sir, was profitable; and much fool may you find in you, even to the world's pleasure and the increase of laughter.
BOT: What's his will else?
USER: That you will take your instant leave o' the king, And make this haste as your own good proceeding, Strengthen'd with what apology you think May make it probable need.
BOT: What more commands he?
USER: That, having this obtain'd, you presently Attend his further pleasure.
BOT: In everything I wait upon his will.
USER: I shall report it so.
BOT: I pray you. Come, sirrah.

USER: But I hope your lordship thinks not him a soldier.
BOT: Yes, my lord, and of very valiant approof.
USER: You have it from his own deliverance.
BOT: And by other warranted testimony.
USER: Then my dial goes not true; I took this lark for a bunting.
BOT: I do assure you, my lord, he is very great in knowledge, and accordingly valiant.
USER: Pray you, sir, who's his tailor?
BOT: Sir!
USER: O, I know him well, I, sir; he, sir, is a good workman, a very good tailor.
BOT: She is.
USER: Will she away tonight?
BOT: As you'll have her.
USER: I have writ my letters, casketed my treasure, Given order for our horses; and tonight, When I should take possession of the bride, End ere I do begin.
BOT: A good traveller is something at the latter end of a dinner; but one that lies three-thirds and uses a known truth to pass a thousand nothings with, should be once heard and thrice beaten. - God save you, Captain.
USER: Is there any unkindness between my lord and you, monsieur?
BOT: I know not how I have deserved to run into my lord's displeasure.
USER: You have made shift to run into 't, boots and spurs and all, like him that leapt into the custard; and out of it you'll run again, rather than suffer question for your residence.
BOT: It may be you have mistaken him, my lord.
USER: An idle lord, I swear.
BOT: I think so.
USER: Why, do you not know him?
BOT: Yes, I do know him well; and common speech Gives him a worthy pass. Here comes my clog.
USER: I have, sir, as I was commanded from you, Spoke with the king, and have procur'd his leave For present parting; only he desires Some private speech with you.
BOT: Sir, I can nothing say But that I am your most obedient servant.
USER: Come, come, no more of that.
BOT: And ever shall With true observance seek to eke out that Wherein toward me my homely stars have fail'd To equal my great fortune.
USER: Let that go. My haste is very great. Farewell; hie home.
BOT: Pray, sir, your pardon.
USER: Well, what would you say?
BOT: I am not worthy of the wealth I owe; Nor dare I say 'tis mine, and yet it is; But, like a timorous thief, most fain would steal What law does vouch mine own.
USER: What would you have?
BOT: Something; and scarce so much; nothing indeed. I would not tell you what I would, my lord. Faith, yes, Strangers and foes do sunder and not kiss.
USER: I pray you, stay not, but in haste to horse.
BOT: I shall not break your bidding, good my lord. Where are my other men, monsieur? Farewell,
USER: Go thou toward home, where I will never come Whilst I can shake my sword or hear the drum. Away, and for our flight.
BOT: Bravely, coragio!

USER: So that, from point to point, now have you heard The fundamental reasons of this war, Whose great decision hath much blood let forth, And more thirsts after.
BOT: Holy seems the quarrel Upon your Grace's part; black and fearful On the opposer.
USER: Therefore we marvel much our cousin France Would, in so just a business, shut his bosom Against our borrowing prayers.
BOT: Be it his pleasure.
USER: But I am sure the younger of our nature, That surfeit on their ease, will day by day Come here for physic.
BOT: Welcome shall they be; And all the honours that can fly from us Shall on them settle. You know your places well; When better fall, for your avails they fell. Tomorrow to the field.

USER: It hath happen'd all as I would have had it, save that he comes not along with her.
BOT: By my troth, I take my young lord to be a very melancholy man.
USER: By what observance, I pray you?
BOT: Why, he will look upon his boot and sing; mend the ruff and sing; ask questions and sing; pick his teeth and sing. I know a man that had this trick of melancholy sold a goodly manor for a song.
USER: Let me see what he writes, and when he means to come.
BOT: What have we here?
USER: E'en that you have there.
BOT: This is not well, rash and unbridled boy, To fly the favours of so good a king, To pluck his indignation on thy head By the misprizing of a maid too virtuous For the contempt of empire.
USER: O madam, yonder is heavy news within between two soldiers and my young lady.
BOT: What is the matter?
USER: Nay, there is some comfort in the news, some comfort; your son will not be kill'd so soon as I thought he would.
BOT: Why should he be kill'd?
USER: Save you, good madam.
BOT: Madam, my lord is gone, for ever gone.
USER: Do not say so.
BOT: Think upon patience. Pray you, gentlemen, - I have felt so many quirks of joy and grief That the first face of neither on the start Can woman me unto 't. Where is my son, I pray you?
USER: Madam, he's gone to serve the Duke of Florence; We met him thitherward, for thence we came, And, after some despatch in hand at court, Thither we bend again.
BOT: Look on this letter, madam; here's my passport. shall come off, and show me a child begotten of thy body that I am father to, then call me husband; but in such a "then" I write a "never"._ This is a dreadful sentence.
USER: Brought you this letter, gentlemen?
BOT: Ay, madam; And for the contents' sake, are sorry for our pains.
USER: I pr'ythee, lady, have a better cheer; If thou engrossest all the griefs are thine, Thou robb'st me of a moiety. He was my son, But I do wash his name out of my blood, And thou art all my child. Towards Florence is he?
BOT: Ay, madam.
USER: And to be a soldier?
BOT: Such is his noble purpose, and, believe't, The duke will lay upon him all the honour That good convenience claims.
USER: Return you thither?
BOT: Ay, madam, with the swiftest wing of speed.
USER: 'Tis bitter.
BOT: Find you that there?
USER: Ay, madam.
BOT: 'Tis but the boldness of his hand haply, which his heart was not consenting to.
USER: Nothing in France until he have no wife! There's nothing here that is too good for him But only she, and she deserves a lord That twenty such rude boys might tend upon, And call her hourly mistress. Who was with him?
BOT: A servant only, and a gentleman which I have sometime known.
USER: Parolles, was it not?
BOT: Ay, my good lady, he.
USER: A very tainted fellow, and full of wickedness. My son corrupts a well-derived nature With his inducement.
BOT: Indeed, good lady, The fellow has a deal of that too much, Which holds him much to have.
USER: Y'are welcome, gentlemen. I will entreat you, when you see my son, To tell him that his sword can never win The honour that he loses: more I'll entreat you Written to bear along.
BOT: We serve you, madam, In that and all your worthiest affairs.
USER: Not so, but as we change our courtesies. Will you draw near?

USER: The general of our horse thou art, and we, Great in our hope, lay our best love and credence Upon thy promising fortune.
BOT: Sir, it is A charge too heavy for my strength; but yet We'll strive to bear it for your worthy sake To th'extreme edge of hazard.
USER: Then go thou forth; And fortune play upon thy prosperous helm, As thy auspicious mistress!
BOT: This very day, Great Mars, I put myself into thy file; Make me but like my thoughts, and I shall prove A lover of thy drum, hater of love.

USER: Alas! and would you take the letter of her? Might you not know she would do as she has done, By sending me a letter? Read it again.
BOT: Ah, what sharp stings are in her mildest words! Rynaldo, you did never lack advice so much As letting her pass so; had I spoke with her, I could have well diverted her intents, Which thus she hath prevented.
USER: Pardon me, madam; If I had given you this at over-night, She might have been o'erta'en; and yet she writes Pursuit would be but vain.

USER: Nay, come; for if they do approach the city, we shall lose all the sight.
BOT: They say the French count has done most honourable service.
USER: It is reported that he has taken their great'st commander, and that with his own hand he slew the duke's brother. We have lost our labour; they are gone a contrary way. Hark! you may know by their trumpets.
BOT: Come, let's return again, and suffice ourselves with the report of it. Well, Diana, take heed of this French earl; the honour of a maid is her name; and no legacy is so rich as honesty.
USER: I have told my neighbour how you have been solicited by a gentleman his companion.
BOT: You shall not need to fear me.
USER: I hope so. Look, here comes a pilgrim. I know she will lie at my house; thither they send one another; I'll question her. God save you, pilgrim! Whither are bound?
BOT: To Saint Jaques le Grand. Where do the palmers lodge, I do beseech you?
USER: At the Saint Francis here, beside the port.
BOT: Is this the way?
USER: Ay, marry, is't. Hark you, they come this way. If you will tarry, holy pilgrim, But till the troops come by, I will conduct you where you shall be lodg'd; The rather for I think I know your hostess As ample as myself.
BOT: Is it yourself?
USER: If you shall please so, pilgrim.
BOT: I thank you, and will stay upon your leisure.
USER: You came, I think, from France?
BOT: I did so.
USER: Here you shall see a countryman of yours That has done worthy service.
BOT: His name, I pray you.
USER: The Count Rossillon. Know you such a one?
BOT: But by the ear, that hears most nobly of him; His face I know not.
USER: Whatsome'er he is, He's bravely taken here. He stole from France, As 'tis reported, for the king had married him Against his liking. Think you it is so?
BOT: Ay, surely, mere the truth; I know his lady.
USER: There is a gentleman that serves the count Reports but coarsely of her.
BOT: What's his name?
USER: Monsieur Parolles.
BOT: O, I believe with him, In argument of praise, or to the worth Of the great count himself, she is too mean To have her name repeated; all her deserving Is a reserved honesty, and that I have not heard examin'd.
USER: Alas, poor lady! 'Tis a hard bondage to become the wife Of a detesting lord.
BOT: Ay, right; good creature, wheresoe'er she is, Her heart weighs sadly. This young maid might do her A shrewd turn, if she pleas'd.
USER: How do you mean? Maybe the amorous count solicits her In the unlawful purpose.
BOT: The gods forbid else!
USER: So, now they come. That is Antonio, the Duke's eldest son; That Escalus.
BOT: Which is the Frenchman?
USER: He; That with the plume; 'tis a most gallant fellow. I would he lov'd his wife; if he were honester He were much goodlier. Is't not a handsome gentleman?
BOT: I like him well.
USER: 'Tis pity he is not honest. Yond's that same knave That leads him to these places. Were I his lady I would poison that vile rascal.
BOT: Which is he?
USER: That jack-an-apes with scarfs. Why is he melancholy?
BOT: Perchance he's hurt i' the battle.
USER: Lose our drum! Well.
BOT: He's shrewdly vex'd at something. Look, he has spied us.
USER: Marry, hang you!
BOT: And your courtesy, for a ring-carrier!
USER: The troop is past. Come, pilgrim, I will bring you Where you shall host; of enjoin'd penitents There's four or five, to great Saint Jaques bound, Already at my house.
BOT: I humbly thank you. Please it this matron and this gentle maid To eat with us tonight; the charge and thanking Shall be for me; and, to requite you further, I will bestow some precepts of this virgin, Worthy the note.
USER: We'll take your offer kindly.

USER: Nay, good my lord, put him to't; let him have his way.
BOT: If your lordship find him not a hilding, hold me no more in your respect.
USER: On my life, my lord, a bubble.
BOT: Do you think I am so far deceived in him?
USER: It were fit you knew him; lest, reposing too far in his virtue, which he hath not, he might at some great and trusty business, in a main danger fail you.
BOT: I would I knew in what particular action to try him.
USER: None better than to let him fetch off his drum, which you hear him so confidently undertake to do.
BOT: O, for the love of laughter, hinder not the honour of his design: let him fetch off his drum in any hand.
USER: How now, monsieur! This drum sticks sorely in your disposition.
BOT: A pox on 't; let it go; 'tis but a drum.
USER: But a drum! Is't but a drum? A drum so lost! There was excellent command, to charge in with our horse upon our own wings, and to rend our own soldiers.
BOT: That was not to be blam'd in the command of the service; it was a disaster of war that Caesar himself could not have prevented, if he had been there to command.
USER: Well, we cannot greatly condemn our success: some dishonour we had in the loss of that drum, but it is not to be recovered.
BOT: It might have been recovered.
USER: It might, but it is not now.
BOT: It is to be recovered. But that the merit of service is seldom attributed to the true and exact performer, I would have that drum or another, or _hic jacet_.
USER: By the hand of a soldier, I will undertake it.
BOT: But you must not now slumber in it.
USER: I'll about it this evening; and I will presently pen down my dilemmas, encourage myself in my certainty, put myself into my mortal preparation; and by midnight look to hear further from me.
BOT: May I be bold to acquaint his grace you are gone about it?
USER: I know not what the success will be, my lord, but the attempt I vow.
BOT: I know th'art valiant; and to the possibility of thy soldiership, will subscribe for thee. Farewell.
USER: I love not many words.
BOT: You do not know him, my lord, as we do; certain it is that he will steal himself into a man's favour, and for a week escape a great deal of discoveries, but when you find him out, you have him ever after.
USER: Why, do you think he will make no deed at all of this, that so seriously he does address himself unto?
BOT: None in the world; but return with an invention, and clap upon you two or three probable lies; but we have almost embossed him; you shall see his fall tonight; for indeed he is not for your lordship's respect.
USER: We'll make you some sport with the fox ere we case him. He was first smok'd by the old Lord Lafew; when his disguise and he is parted, tell me what a sprat you shall find him; which you shall see this very night.
BOT: I must go look my twigs. He shall be caught.
USER: Your brother, he shall go along with me.
BOT: As't please your lordship. I'll leave you.
USER: Now will I lead you to the house, and show you The lass I spoke of.
BOT: But you say she's honest.
USER: With all my heart, my lord.

USER: If you misdoubt me that I am not she, I know not how I shall assure you further, But I shall lose the grounds I work upon.
BOT: Though my estate be fall'n, I was well born, Nothing acquainted with these businesses, And would not put my reputation now In any staining act.
USER: I should believe you, For you have show'd me that which well approves Y'are great in fortune.
BOT: Now I see The bottom of your purpose.
USER: Why then tonight Let us assay our plot; which, if it speed, Is wicked meaning in a lawful deed, And lawful meaning in a lawful act, Where both not sin, and yet a sinful fact. But let's about it.

USER: Good captain, let me be th' interpreter.
BOT: Art not acquainted with him? Knows he not thy voice?
USER: No sir, I warrant you.
BOT: But what linsey-woolsey has thou to speak to us again?
USER: E'en such as you speak to me.
BOT: guilty of.
USER: I would the cutting of my garments would serve the turn, or the breaking of my Spanish sword.
BOT: Or the baring of my beard, and to say it was in stratagem.
USER: Or to drown my clothes, and say I was stripped.
BOT: Though I swore I leap'd from the window of the citadel, -
USER: Thirty fathom.
BOT: I would I had any drum of the enemy's; I would swear I recover'd it.
USER: A drum now of the enemy's!
BOT: _Throca movousus, cargo, cargo, cargo._
USER: _Cargo, cargo, cargo, villianda par corbo, cargo._
BOT: O, ransom, ransom! Do not hide mine eyes.
USER: _Boskos thromuldo boskos._
BOT: _Boskos vauvado._ I understand thee, and can speak thy tongue. _Kerelybonto._ Sir, Betake thee to thy faith, for seventeen poniards are at thy bosom.
USER: O!
BOT: O, pray, pray, pray! _Manka revania dulche._
USER: _Oscorbidulchos volivorco._
BOT: The General is content to spare thee yet; And, hoodwink'd as thou art, will lead thee on To gather from thee. Haply thou mayst inform Something to save thy life.
USER: O, let me live, And all the secrets of our camp I'll show, Their force, their purposes; nay, I'll speak that Which you will wonder at.
BOT: But wilt thou faithfully?
USER: If I do not, damn me.
BOT: _Acordo linta._ Come on; thou art granted space. A short alarum within.
USER: Go tell the Count Rossillon and my brother We have caught the woodcock, and will keep him muffled Till we do hear from them.
BOT: Captain, I will.
USER: 'A will betray us all unto ourselves; Inform on that.
BOT: So I will, sir.
USER: Till then I'll keep him dark, and safely lock'd.

USER: They told me that your name was Fontybell.
BOT: No, my good lord, Diana.
USER: She then was honest.
BOT: So should you be.
USER: No. My mother did but duty; such, my lord, As you owe to your wife.
BOT: No more a' that! I pr'ythee do not strive against my vows; I was compell'd to her; but I love thee By love's own sweet constraint, and will for ever Do thee all rights of service.
USER: Ay, so you serve us Till we serve you; but when you have our roses, You barely leave our thorns to prick ourselves, And mock us with our bareness.
BOT: How have I sworn?
USER: I see that men make hopes in such a case, That we'll forsake ourselves. Give me that ring.
BOT: I'll lend it thee, my dear, but have no power To give it from me.
USER: Will you not, my lord?
BOT: It is an honour 'longing to our house, Bequeathed down from many ancestors, Which were the greatest obloquy i' the world In me to lose.
USER: Here, take my ring; My house, mine honour, yea, my life be thine, And I'll be bid by thee.
BOT: A heaven on earth I have won by wooing thee.

USER: You have not given him his mother's letter?
BOT: I have deliv'red it an hour since; there is something in't that stings his nature; for on the reading it, he chang'd almost into another man.
USER: He has much worthy blame laid upon him for shaking off so good a wife and so sweet a lady.
BOT: Especially he hath incurred the everlasting displeasure of the king, who had even tun'd his bounty to sing happiness to him. I will tell you a thing, but you shall let it dwell darkly with you.
USER: When you have spoken it, 'tis dead, and I am the grave of it.
BOT: Now, God delay our rebellion! As we are ourselves, what things are we!
USER: Is it not meant damnable in us to be trumpeters of our unlawful intents? We shall not then have his company tonight?
BOT: Not till after midnight; for he is dieted to his hour.
USER: That approaches apace. I would gladly have him see his company anatomized, that he might take a measure of his own judgments, wherein so curiously he had set this counterfeit.
BOT: We will not meddle with him till he come; for his presence must be the whip of the other.
USER: In the meantime, what hear you of these wars?
BOT: I hear there is an overture of peace.
USER: Nay, I assure you, a peace concluded.
BOT: What will Count Rossillon do then? Will he travel higher, or return again into France?
USER: I perceive by this demand, you are not altogether of his council.
BOT: Let it be forbid, sir! So should I be a great deal of his act.
USER: How is this justified?
BOT: The stronger part of it by her own letters, which makes her story true, even to the point of her death. Her death itself, which could not be her office to say is come, was faithfully confirm'd by the rector of the place.
USER: Hath the count all this intelligence?
BOT: Ay, and the particular confirmations, point from point, to the full arming of the verity.
USER: I am heartily sorry that he'll be glad of this.
BOT: How mightily sometimes we make us comforts of our losses!
USER: And how mightily some other times we drown our gain in tears! The great dignity that his valour hath here acquir'd for him shall at home be encountered with a shame as ample.
BOT: He met the duke in the street, sir; of whom he hath taken a solemn leave: his lordship will next morning for France. The duke hath offered him letters of commendations to the king.
USER: They shall be no more than needful there, if they were more than they can commend.
BOT: They cannot be too sweet for the king's tartness. Here's his lordship now. How now, my lord, is't not after midnight?
USER: If the business be of any difficulty and this morning your departure hence, it requires haste of your lordship.
BOT: Bring him forth. Has sat i' the stocks all night, poor gallant knave.
USER: No matter; his heels have deserv'd it, in usurping his spurs so long. How does he carry himself?
BOT: Nothing of me, has he?
USER: His confession is taken, and it shall be read to his face; if your lordship be in't, as I believe you are, you must have the patience to hear it.
BOT: A plague upon him! muffled! he can say nothing of me; hush, hush!
USER: Hoodman comes! _Portotartarossa._
BOT: He calls for the tortures. What will you say without 'em?
USER: I will confess what I know without constraint. If ye pinch me like a pasty I can say no more.
BOT: _Bosko chimurcho._
USER: _Boblibindo chicurmurco._
BOT: You are a merciful general. Our general bids you answer to what I shall ask you out of a note.
USER: And truly, as I hope to live.
BOT: 'First demand of him how many horse the duke is strong.' What say you to that?
USER: Five or six thousand; but very weak and unserviceable: the troops are all scattered, and the commanders very poor rogues, upon my reputation and credit, and as I hope to live.
BOT: Shall I set down your answer so?
USER: Do. I'll take the sacrament on 't, how and which way you will.
BOT: All's one to him. What a past-saving slave is this!
USER: You are deceived, my lord; this is Monsieur Parolles, the gallant militarist (that was his own phrase), that had the whole theoric of war in the knot of his scarf, and the practice in the chape of his dagger.
BOT: I will never trust a man again for keeping his sword clean, nor believe he can have everything in him by wearing his apparel neatly.
USER: Well, that's set down.
BOT: 'Five or six thousand horse' I said - I will say true - or thereabouts, set down, - for I'll speak truth.
USER: He's very near the truth in this.
BOT: But I con him no thanks for't in the nature he delivers it.
USER: Poor rogues, I pray you say.
BOT: Well, that's set down.
USER: I humbly thank you, sir; a truth's a truth, the rogues are marvellous poor.
BOT: 'Demand of him of what strength they are a-foot.' What say you to that?
USER: What shall be done to him?
BOT: Nothing, but let him have thanks. Demand of him my condition, and what credit I have with the duke.
USER: I beseech you, let me answer to the particular of the inter'gatories. Demand them singly.
BOT: Do you know this Captain Dumaine?
USER: I know him: he was a botcher's 'prentice in Paris, from whence he was whipped for getting the shrieve's fool with child, a dumb innocent that could not say him nay.
BOT: Nay, by your leave, hold your hands; though I know his brains are forfeit to the next tile that falls.
USER: Well, is this captain in the Duke of Florence's camp?
BOT: Upon my knowledge, he is, and lousy.
USER: Nay, look not so upon me; we shall hear of your lordship anon.
BOT: What is his reputation with the duke?
USER: The duke knows him for no other but a poor officer of mine, and writ to me this other day to turn him out o' the band. I think I have his letter in my pocket.
BOT: Marry, we'll search.
USER: In good sadness, I do not know; either it is there or it is upon a file, with the duke's other letters, in my tent.
BOT: Here 'tis; here's a paper; shall I read it to you?
USER: I do not know if it be it or no.
BOT: Our interpreter does it well.
USER: Excellently.
BOT: Nay, I'll read it first by your favour.
USER: My meaning in't, I protest, was very honest in the behalf of the maid; for I knew the young count to be a dangerous and lascivious boy, who is a whale to virginity, and devours up all the fry it finds.
BOT: Damnable both sides rogue!
USER: He shall be whipped through the army with this rhyme in's forehead.
BOT: This is your devoted friend, sir, the manifold linguist, and the armipotent soldier.
USER: I could endure anything before but a cat, and now he's a cat to me.
BOT: I perceive, sir, by our general's looks we shall be fain to hang you.
USER: My life, sir, in any case. Not that I am afraid to die, but that, my offences being many, I would repent out the remainder of nature. Let me live, sir, in a dungeon, i' the stocks, or anywhere, so I may live.
BOT: We'll see what may be done, so you confess freely. Therefore, once more to this Captain Dumaine: you have answer'd to his reputation with the duke, and to his valour. What is his honesty?
USER: I begin to love him for this.
BOT: For this description of thine honesty? A pox upon him for me, he's more and more a cat.
USER: What say you to his expertness in war?
BOT: He hath out-villain'd villainy so far that the rarity redeems him.
USER: A pox on him! He's a cat still.
BOT: His qualities being at this poor price, I need not to ask you if gold will corrupt him to revolt.
USER: Sir, for a quart d'ecu he will sell the fee-simple of his salvation, the inheritance of it, and cut the entail from all remainders, and a perpetual succession for it perpetually.
BOT: What's his brother, the other Captain Dumaine?
USER: Why does he ask him of me?
BOT: What's he?
USER: If your life be saved, will you undertake to betray the Florentine?
BOT: Ay, and the captain of his horse, Count Rossillon.
USER: I'll whisper with the general, and know his pleasure.
BOT: to deserve well, and to beguile the supposition of that lascivious young boy the count, have I run into this danger: yet who would have suspected an ambush where I was taken?
USER: O Lord! sir, let me live, or let me see my death.
BOT: That shall you, and take your leave of all your friends. So, look about you; know you any here?
USER: Good morrow, noble captain.
BOT: God bless you, Captain Parolles.
USER: God save you, noble captain.
BOT: Captain, what greeting will you to my Lord Lafew? I am for France.
USER: Good Captain, will you give me a copy of the sonnet you writ to Diana in behalf of the Count Rossillon? And I were not a very coward I'd compel it of you; but fare you well.
BOT: You are undone, captain: all but your scarf; that has a knot on't yet.
USER: Who cannot be crushed with a plot?
BOT: If you could find out a country where but women were that had received so much shame, you might begin an impudent nation. Fare ye well, sir. I am for France too; we shall speak of you there.

USER: Gentle madam, You never had a servant to whose trust Your business was more welcome.
BOT: Let death and honesty Go with your impositions, I am yours Upon your will to suffer.

USER: 'Twas a good lady, 'twas a good lady. We may pick a thousand salads ere we light on such another herb.
BOT: Indeed, sir, she was the sweet marjoram of the salad, or, rather, the herb of grace.
USER: They are not herbs, you knave; they are nose-herbs.
BOT: I am no great Nebuchadnezzar, sir; I have not much skill in grass.
USER: Whether dost thou profess thyself, - a knave or a fool?
BOT: A fool, sir, at a woman's service, and a knave at a man's.
USER: Your distinction?
BOT: I would cozen the man of his wife, and do his service.
USER: So you were a knave at his service indeed.
BOT: And I would give his wife my bauble, sir, to do her service.
USER: I will subscribe for thee; thou art both knave and fool.
BOT: At your service.
USER: No, no, no.
BOT: Why, sir, if I cannot serve you, I can serve as great a prince as you are.
USER: Who's that? a Frenchman?
BOT: Faith, sir, 'a has an English name; but his phisnomy is more hotter in France than there.
USER: What prince is that?
BOT: The black prince, sir; alias the prince of darkness; alias the devil.
USER: Hold thee, there's my purse. I give thee not this to suggest thee from thy master thou talk'st of; serve him still.
BOT: Go thy ways, I begin to be a-weary of thee; and I tell thee so before, because I would not fall out with thee. Go thy ways; let my horses be well look'd to, without any tricks.
USER: If I put any tricks upon 'em, sir, they shall be jades' tricks, which are their own right by the law of nature.
BOT: A shrewd knave, and an unhappy.
USER: So he is. My lord that's gone made himself much sport out of him; by his authority he remains here, which he thinks is a patent for his sauciness; and indeed he has no pace, but runs where he will.
BOT: With very much content, my lord, and I wish it happily effected.
USER: His highness comes post from Marseilles, of as able body as when he number'd thirty; he will be here tomorrow, or I am deceived by him that in such intelligence hath seldom fail'd.
BOT: It rejoices me that I hope I shall see him ere I die. I have letters that my son will be here tonight. I shall beseech your lordship to remain with me till they meet together.
USER: Madam, I was thinking with what manners I might safely be admitted.
BOT: You need but plead your honourable privilege.
USER: Lady, of that I have made a bold charter; but, I thank my God, it holds yet.
BOT: A scar nobly got, or a noble scar, is a good livery of honour; so belike is that.
USER: But it is your carbonado'd face.
BOT: Let us go see your son, I pray you. I long to talk with the young noble soldier.
USER: Faith, there's a dozen of 'em, with delicate fine hats, and most courteous feathers, which bow the head and nod at every man.

USER: And you.
BOT: Sir, I have seen you in the court of France.
USER: I have been sometimes there.
BOT: What's your will?
USER: That it will please you To give this poor petition to the king, And aid me with that store of power you have To come into his presence.
BOT: The king's not here.
USER: Not here, sir?
BOT: Not indeed. He hence remov'd last night, and with more haste Than is his use.
USER: Lord, how we lose our pains!
BOT: All's well that ends well yet, Though time seem so adverse and means unfit. I do beseech you, whither is he gone?
USER: Marry, as I take it, to Rossillon; Whither I am going.
BOT: This I'll do for you.
USER: And you shall find yourself to be well thank'd, Whate'er falls more. We must to horse again. Go, go, provide.

USER: Truly, Fortune's displeasure is but sluttish, if it smell so strongly as thou speak'st of. I will henceforth eat no fish of Fortune's buttering. Pr'ythee, allow the wind.
BOT: Nay, you need not to stop your nose, sir. I spake but by a metaphor.
USER: Indeed, sir, if your metaphor stink, I will stop my nose, or against any man's metaphor. Pr'ythee, get thee further.
BOT: Pray you, sir, deliver me this paper.
USER: My lord, I am a man whom Fortune hath cruelly scratch'd.
BOT: I beseech your honour to hear me one single word.
USER: You beg a single penny more. Come, you shall ha't; save your word.
BOT: My name, my good lord, is Parolles.
USER: You beg more than word then. Cox my passion! Give me your hand. How does your drum?
BOT: O my good lord, you were the first that found me.
USER: Was I, in sooth? And I was the first that lost thee.
BOT: It lies in you, my lord, to bring me in some grace, for you did bring me out.
USER: I praise God for you.

USER: We lost a jewel of her, and our esteem Was made much poorer by it; but your son, As mad in folly, lack'd the sense to know Her estimation home.
BOT: 'Tis past, my liege, And I beseech your majesty to make it Natural rebellion, done i' the blaze of youth, When oil and fire, too strong for reason's force, O'erbears it and burns on.
USER: My honour'd lady, I have forgiven and forgotten all, Though my revenges were high bent upon him, And watch'd the time to shoot.
BOT: I shall, my liege.
USER: What says he to your daughter? Have you spoke?
BOT: All that he is hath reference to your highness.
USER: Then shall we have a match. I have letters sent me That sets him high in fame.
BOT: He looks well on 't.
USER: I am not a day of season, For thou mayst see a sunshine and a hail In me at once. But to the brightest beams Distracted clouds give way; so stand thou forth; The time is fair again.
BOT: My high-repented blames Dear sovereign, pardon to me.
USER: Which better than the first, O dear heaven, bless! Or, ere they meet, in me, O nature, cesse!
BOT: Hers it was not.
USER: My gracious sovereign, Howe'er it pleases you to take it so, The ring was never hers.
BOT: Son, on my life, I have seen her wear it; and she reckon'd it At her life's rate.
USER: I am sure I saw her wear it.
BOT: She never saw it.
USER: If you shall prove This ring was ever hers, you shall as easy Prove that I husbanded her bed in Florence, Where she yet never was.
BOT: I am wrapp'd in dismal thinkings.
USER: I will buy me a son-in-law in a fair, and toll for this. I'll none of him.
BOT: The heavens have thought well on thee, Lafew, To bring forth this discovery. Seek these suitors. Go speedily, and bring again the count. I am afeard the life of Helen, lady, Was foully snatch'd.
USER: Now, justice on the doers!
BOT: I wonder, sir, since wives are monsters to you, And that you fly them as you swear them lordship, Yet you desire to marry. What woman's that?
USER: I am, my lord, a wretched Florentine, Derived from the ancient Capilet; My suit, as I do understand, you know, And therefore know how far I may be pitied.
BOT: I am her mother, sir, whose age and honour Both suffer under this complaint we bring, And both shall cease, without your remedy.
USER: Come hither, count; do you know these women?
BOT: My lord, I neither can nor will deny But that I know them. Do they charge me further?
USER: Why do you look so strange upon your wife?
BOT: She's none of mine, my lord.
USER: no husband for her.
BOT: My lord, this is a fond and desperate creature Whom sometime I have laugh'd with. Let your highness Lay a more noble thought upon mine honour Than for to think that I would sink it here.
USER: Sir, for my thoughts, you have them ill to friend Till your deeds gain them; fairer prove your honour Than in my thought it lies!
BOT: Good my lord, Ask him upon his oath, if he does think He had not my virginity.
USER: What say'st thou to her?
BOT: She's impudent, my lord, And was a common gamester to the camp.
USER: He blushes, and 'tis it. Of six preceding ancestors, that gem Conferr'd by testament to th' sequent issue, Hath it been owed and worn. This is his wife; That ring's a thousand proofs.
BOT: Methought you said You saw one here in court could witness it.
USER: I did, my lord, but loath am to produce So bad an instrument; his name's Parolles.
BOT: I saw the man today, if man he be.
USER: Find him, and bring him hither.
BOT: What of him? He's quoted for a most perfidious slave, With all the spots o' the world tax'd and debauch'd: Whose nature sickens but to speak a truth. Am I or that or this for what he'll utter, That will speak anything?
USER: She hath that ring of yours.
BOT: I must be patient. You that have turn'd off a first so noble wife May justly diet me. I pray you yet, - Since you lack virtue, I will lose a husband - Send for your ring, I will return it home, And give me mine again.
USER: I have it not.
BOT: What ring was yours, I pray you?
USER: Sir, much like The same upon your finger.
BOT: Know you this ring? This ring was his of late.
USER: And this was it I gave him, being abed.
BOT: The story then goes false you threw it him Out of a casement.
USER: I have spoke the truth.
BOT: My lord, I do confess the ring was hers.
USER: You boggle shrewdly; every feather starts you. Is this the man you speak of?
BOT: Ay, my lord.
USER: Tell me, sirrah, but tell me true I charge you, Not fearing the displeasure of your master, Which on your just proceeding, I'll keep off, - By him and by this woman here what know you?
BOT: So please your majesty, my master hath been an honourable gentleman. Tricks he hath had in him, which gentlemen have.
USER: Come, come, to the purpose. Did he love this woman?
BOT: Faith, sir, he did love her; but how?
USER: How, I pray you?
BOT: He did love her, sir, as a gentleman loves a woman.
USER: How is that?
BOT: He lov'd her, sir, and lov'd her not.
USER: As thou art a knave and no knave. What an equivocal companion is this!
BOT: I am a poor man, and at your majesty's command.
USER: He's a good drum, my lord, but a naughty orator.
BOT: Do you know he promised me marriage?
USER: Faith, I know more than I'll speak.
BOT: But wilt thou not speak all thou know'st?
USER: Thou hast spoken all already, unless thou canst say they are married; but thou art too fine in thy evidence; therefore stand aside. This ring, you say, was yours?
BOT: Ay, my good lord.
USER: Where did you buy it? Or who gave it you?
BOT: It was not given me, nor I did not buy it.
USER: Who lent it you?
BOT: It was not lent me neither.
USER: Where did you find it then?
BOT: I found it not.
USER: If it were yours by none of all these ways, How could you give it him?
BOT: I never gave it him.
USER: This woman's an easy glove, my lord; she goes off and on at pleasure.
BOT: This ring was mine, I gave it his first wife.
USER: It might be yours or hers for ought I know.
BOT: Take her away, I do not like her now. To prison with her. And away with him. Unless thou tell'st me where thou hadst this ring, Thou diest within this hour.
USER: I'll never tell you.
BOT: Take her away.
USER: I'll put in bail, my liege.
BOT: I think thee now some common customer.
USER: By Jove, if ever I knew man, 'twas you.
BOT: Wherefore hast thou accus'd him all this while?
USER: Because he's guilty, and he is not guilty. He knows I am no maid, and he'll swear to't: I'll swear I am a maid, and he knows not. Great King, I am no strumpet, by my life; I am either maid, or else this old man's wife.
BOT: She does abuse our ears; to prison with her.
USER: Is there no exorcist Beguiles the truer office of mine eyes? Is't real that I see?
BOT: No, my good lord; 'Tis but the shadow of a wife you see, The name, and not the thing.
USER: Both, both. O, pardon!
BOT: If she, my liege, can make me know this clearly, I'll love her dearly, ever, ever dearly.
USER: If it appear not plain, and prove untrue, Deadly divorce step between me and you! O my dear mother, do I see you living?
BOT: Mine eyes smell onions; I shall weep anon. So, I thank thee. Wait on me home, I'll make sport with thee. Let thy courtesies alone, they are scurvy ones.

USER: If it be love indeed, tell me how much.
BOT: There's beggary in the love that can be reckoned.
USER: I'll set a bourn how far to be beloved.
BOT: Then must thou needs find out new heaven, new earth.
USER: News, my good lord, from Rome.
BOT: Grates me, the sum.
USER: How, my love?
BOT: Excellent falsehood! Why did he marry Fulvia, and not love her? I'll seem the fool I am not. Antony Will be himself.
USER: But stirred by Cleopatra. Now, for the love of Love and her soft hours, Let's not confound the time with conference harsh. There's not a minute of our lives should stretch Without some pleasure now. What sport tonight?
BOT: Hear the ambassadors.
USER: Is Caesar with Antonius prized so slight?
BOT: Sir, sometimes when he is not Antony, He comes too short of that great property Which still should go with Antony.
USER: I am full sorry That he approves the common liar who Thus speaks of him at Rome, but I will hope Of better deeds tomorrow. Rest you happy!

USER: Lord Alexas, sweet Alexas, most anything Alexas, almost most absolute Alexas, where's the soothsayer that you praised so to th' queen? O, that I knew this husband which you say must charge his horns with garlands!
BOT: Soothsayer!
USER: Your will?
BOT: Is this the man? Is't you, sir, that know things?
USER: In nature's infinite book of secrecy A little I can read.
BOT: Show him your hand.
USER: Bring in the banquet quickly; wine enough Cleopatra's health to drink.
BOT: Good, sir, give me good fortune.
USER: I make not, but foresee.
BOT: Pray, then, foresee me one.
USER: You shall be yet far fairer than you are.
BOT: He means in flesh.
USER: No, you shall paint when you are old.
BOT: Wrinkles forbid!
USER: Vex not his prescience. Be attentive.
BOT: Hush!
USER: You shall be more beloving than beloved.
BOT: I had rather heat my liver with drinking.
USER: Nay, hear him.
BOT: You shall outlive the lady whom you serve.
USER: O, excellent! I love long life better than figs.
BOT: You have seen and proved a fairer former fortune Than that which is to approach.
USER: Then belike my children shall have no names. Prithee, how many boys and wenches must I have?
BOT: If every of your wishes had a womb, And fertile every wish, a million.
USER: Out, fool! I forgive thee for a witch.
BOT: You think none but your sheets are privy to your wishes.
USER: Nay, come, tell Iras hers.
BOT: We'll know all our fortunes.
USER: Mine, and most of our fortunes tonight, shall be drunk to bed.
BOT: There's a palm presages chastity, if nothing else.
USER: E'en as the o'erflowing Nilus presageth famine.
BOT: Go, you wild bedfellow, you cannot soothsay.
USER: Nay, if an oily palm be not a fruitful prognostication, I cannot scratch mine ear. Prithee, tell her but workaday fortune.
BOT: Your fortunes are alike.
USER: But how, but how? give me particulars.
BOT: I have said.
USER: Am I not an inch of fortune better than she?
BOT: Well, if you were but an inch of fortune better than I, where would you choose it?
USER: Not in my husband's nose.
BOT: Amen.
USER: Lo now, if it lay in their hands to make me a cuckold, they would make themselves whores but they'd do't!
BOT: Hush, Here comes Antony.
USER: Not he, the queen.
BOT: Saw you my lord?
USER: No, lady.
BOT: Was he not here?
USER: No, madam.
BOT: He was disposed to mirth; but on the sudden A Roman thought hath struck him. Enobarbus!
USER: Madam?
BOT: Seek him and bring him hither. Where's Alexas?
USER: Here, at your service. My lord approaches.
BOT: We will not look upon him. Go with us. Soothsayer._]
USER: Fulvia thy wife first came into the field.
BOT: Against my brother Lucius.
USER: Ay. But soon that war had end, and the time's state Made friends of them, jointing their force 'gainst Caesar, Whose better issue in the war from Italy Upon the first encounter drave them.
BOT: Well, what worst?
USER: The nature of bad news infects the teller.
BOT: When it concerns the fool or coward. On. Things that are past are done with me. 'Tis thus: Who tells me true, though in his tale lie death, I hear him as he flattered.
USER: Labienus - This is stiff news - hath with his Parthian force Extended Asia from Euphrates His conquering banner shook from Syria To Lydia and to Ionia, Whilst -
BOT: "Antony", thou wouldst say -
USER: O, my lord!
BOT: At your noble pleasure.
USER: From Sicyon, ho, the news? Speak there!
BOT: The man from Sicyon -
USER: Is there such a one?
BOT: He stays upon your will.
USER: Let him appear. These strong Egyptian fetters I must break, Or lose myself in dotage. What are you?
BOT: Fulvia thy wife is dead.
USER: Where died she?
BOT: In Sicyon: Her length of sickness, with what else more serious Importeth thee to know, this bears.
USER: What's your pleasure, sir?
BOT: I must with haste from hence.
USER: Why then we kill all our women. We see how mortal an unkindness is to them. If they suffer our departure, death's the word.
BOT: I must be gone.
USER: She is cunning past man's thought.
BOT: Would I had never seen her!
USER: O, sir, you had then left unseen a wonderful piece of work, which not to have been blest withal would have discredited your travel.
BOT: Fulvia is dead.
USER: Sir?
BOT: Fulvia is dead.
USER: Fulvia?
BOT: Dead.
USER: The business she hath broached in the state Cannot endure my absence.
BOT: And the business you have broached here cannot be without you, especially that of Cleopatra's, which wholly depends on your abode.
USER: I shall do't.

USER: Where is he?
BOT: I did not see him since.
USER: See where he is, who's with him, what he does. I did not send you. If you find him sad, Say I am dancing; if in mirth, report That I am sudden sick. Quick, and return.
BOT: Madam, methinks, if you did love him dearly, You do not hold the method to enforce The like from him.
USER: What should I do I do not?
BOT: In each thing give him way; cross him in nothing.
USER: Thou teachest like a fool: the way to lose him.
BOT: Tempt him not so too far; I wish, forbear. In time we hate that which we often fear. But here comes Antony.
USER: I am sick and sullen.
BOT: I am sorry to give breathing to my purpose -
USER: Help me away, dear Charmian! I shall fall. It cannot be thus long; the sides of nature Will not sustain it.
BOT: Now, my dearest queen -
USER: Pray you, stand farther from me.
BOT: What's the matter?
USER: I know by that same eye there's some good news. What, says the married woman you may go? Would she had never given you leave to come! Let her not say 'tis I that keep you here. I have no power upon you; hers you are.
BOT: The gods best know -
USER: O, never was there queen So mightily betrayed! Yet at the first I saw the treasons planted.
BOT: Cleopatra -
USER: Why should I think you can be mine and true, Though you in swearing shake the throned gods, Who have been false to Fulvia? Riotous madness, To be entangled with those mouth-made vows Which break themselves in swearing!
BOT: Most sweet queen -
USER: How now, lady!
BOT: I would I had thy inches, thou shouldst know There were a heart in Egypt.
USER: Though age from folly could not give me freedom, It does from childishness. Can Fulvia die?
BOT: She's dead, my queen. Look here, and at thy sovereign leisure read The garboils she awaked; at the last, best, See when and where she died.
USER: O most false love! Where be the sacred vials thou shouldst fill With sorrowful water? Now I see, I see, In Fulvia's death how mine received shall be.
BOT: Cut my lace, Charmian, come! But let it be; I am quickly ill and well, So Antony loves.
USER: My precious queen, forbear, And give true evidence to his love, which stands An honourable trial.
BOT: So Fulvia told me. I prithee, turn aside and weep for her, Then bid adieu to me, and say the tears Belong to Egypt. Good now, play one scene Of excellent dissembling, and let it look Like perfect honour.
USER: You'll heat my blood. No more.
BOT: You can do better yet, but this is meetly.
USER: Now, by my sword -
BOT: And target. Still he mends. But this is not the best. Look, prithee, Charmian, How this Herculean Roman does become The carriage of his chafe.
USER: I'll leave you, lady.
BOT: Courteous lord, one word. Sir, you and I must part, but that's not it; Sir, you and I have loved, but there's not it; That you know well. Something it is I would - O, my oblivion is a very Antony, And I am all forgotten.
USER: But that your royalty Holds idleness your subject, I should take you For idleness itself.
BOT: Let us go. Come. Our separation so abides and flies That thou, residing here, goes yet with me, And I, hence fleeting, here remain with thee. Away!

USER: Here's more news.
BOT: 'Tis pity of him.
USER: Let his shames quickly Drive him to Rome. 'Tis time we twain Did show ourselves i' th' field, and to that end Assemble we immediate council. Pompey Thrives in our idleness.
BOT: Tomorrow, Caesar, I shall be furnished to inform you rightly Both what by sea and land I can be able To front this present time.
USER: Till which encounter It is my business too. Farewell.
BOT: Farewell, my lord. What you shall know meantime Of stirs abroad, I shall beseech you, sir, To let me be partaker.
USER: Doubt not, sir. I knew it for my bond.

USER: Charmian!
BOT: Madam?
USER: Ha, ha! Give me to drink mandragora.
BOT: Why, madam?
USER: That I might sleep out this great gap of time My Antony is away.
BOT: You think of him too much.
USER: O, 'tis treason!
BOT: Madam, I trust not so.
USER: Thou, eunuch Mardian!
BOT: What's your highness' pleasure?
USER: Not now to hear thee sing. I take no pleasure In aught an eunuch has. 'Tis well for thee That, being unseminared, thy freer thoughts May not fly forth of Egypt. Hast thou affections?
BOT: Yes, gracious madam.
USER: Indeed?
BOT: Not in deed, madam, for I can do nothing But what indeed is honest to be done. Yet have I fierce affections, and think What Venus did with Mars.
USER: Sovereign of Egypt, hail!
BOT: How much unlike art thou Mark Antony! Yet, coming from him, that great medicine hath With his tinct gilded thee. How goes it with my brave Mark Antony?
USER: Last thing he did, dear queen, He kissed - the last of many doubled kisses - This orient pearl. His speech sticks in my heart.
BOT: Mine ear must pluck it thence.
USER: What, was he sad or merry?
BOT: Like to the time o' th' year between the extremes Of hot and cold, he was nor sad nor merry.
USER: Ay, madam, twenty several messengers. Why do you send so thick?
BOT: Who's born that day When I forget to send to Antony Shall die a beggar. - Ink and paper, Charmian. - Welcome, my good Alexas. - Did I, Charmian, Ever love Caesar so?
USER: O that brave Caesar!
BOT: Be choked with such another emphasis! Say "the brave Antony."
USER: The valiant Caesar!
BOT: By Isis, I will give thee bloody teeth If thou with Caesar paragon again My man of men.
USER: By your most gracious pardon, I sing but after you.
BOT: My salad days, When I was green in judgment, cold in blood, To say as I said then. But come, away, Get me ink and paper. He shall have every day a several greeting, Or I'll unpeople Egypt.

USER: If the great gods be just, they shall assist The deeds of justest men.
BOT: Know, worthy Pompey, That what they do delay they not deny.
USER: Whiles we are suitors to their throne, decays The thing we sue for.
BOT: We, ignorant of ourselves, Beg often our own harms, which the wise powers Deny us for our good; so find we profit By losing of our prayers.
USER: Caesar and Lepidus Are in the field. A mighty strength they carry.
BOT: Where have you this? 'Tis false.
USER: From Silvius, sir.
BOT: This is most certain that I shall deliver: Mark Antony is every hour in Rome Expected. Since he went from Egypt 'tis A space for farther travel.
USER: I cannot hope Caesar and Antony shall well greet together. His wife that's dead did trespasses to Caesar; His brother warred upon him, although I think, Not moved by Antony.

USER: Good Enobarbus, 'tis a worthy deed, And shall become you well, to entreat your captain To soft and gentle speech.
BOT: I shall entreat him To answer like himself. If Caesar move him, Let Antony look over Caesar's head And speak as loud as Mars. By Jupiter, Were I the wearer of Antonius' beard, I would not shave't today.
USER: 'Tis not a time For private stomaching.
BOT: Every time Serves for the matter that is then born in't.
USER: But small to greater matters must give way.
BOT: Not if the small come first.
USER: Your speech is passion; But pray you stir no embers up. Here comes The noble Antony.
BOT: And yonder Caesar.
USER: If we compose well here, to Parthia. Hark, Ventidius.
BOT: I do not know, Maecenas. Ask Agrippa.
USER: 'Tis spoken well. Were we before our armies, and to fight, I should do thus.
BOT: Welcome to Rome.
USER: Thank you.
BOT: Sit.
USER: Sit, sir.
BOT: Nay, then.
USER: I learn you take things ill which are not so, Or being, concern you not.
BOT: I must be laughed at If, or for nothing or a little, I Should say myself offended, and with you Chiefly i' th' world; more laughed at that I should Once name you derogately when to sound your name It not concerned me.
USER: My being in Egypt, Caesar, What was't to you?
BOT: No more than my residing here at Rome Might be to you in Egypt. Yet if you there Did practise on my state, your being in Egypt Might be my question.
USER: How intend you, practised?
BOT: You may be pleased to catch at mine intent By what did here befall me. Your wife and brother Made wars upon me, and their contestation Was theme for you; you were the word of war.
USER: You praise yourself By laying defects of judgment to me; but You patched up your excuses.
BOT: Would we had all such wives, that the men Might go to wars with the women.
USER: So much uncurbable, her garboils, Caesar, Made out of her impatience - which not wanted Shrewdness of policy too - I grieving grant Did you too much disquiet. For that you must But say I could not help it.
BOT: I wrote to you When rioting in Alexandria; you Did pocket up my letters, and with taunts Did gibe my missive out of audience.
USER: You have broken The article of your oath, which you shall never Have tongue to charge me with.
BOT: Soft, Caesar!
USER: No, Lepidus, let him speak. The honour is sacred which he talks on now, Supposing that I lacked it. But on, Caesar: The article of my oath?
BOT: To lend me arms and aid when I required them, The which you both denied.
USER: 'Tis noble spoken.
BOT: If it might please you to enforce no further The griefs between ye; to forget them quite Were to remember that the present need Speaks to atone you.
USER: Worthily spoken, Maecenas.
BOT: Or, if you borrow one another's love for the instant, you may, when you hear no more words of Pompey, return it again. You shall have time to wrangle in when you have nothing else to do.
USER: Thou art a soldier only. Speak no more.
BOT: That truth should be silent I had almost forgot.
USER: You wrong this presence; therefore speak no more.
BOT: Go to, then. Your considerate stone!
USER: Give me leave, Caesar.
BOT: Speak, Agrippa.
USER: Thou hast a sister by the mother's side, Admired Octavia. Great Mark Antony Is now a widower.
BOT: Say not so, Agrippa. If Cleopatra heard you, your reproof Were well deserved of rashness.
USER: I am not married, Caesar. Let me hear Agrippa further speak.
BOT: Will Caesar speak?
USER: Not till he hears how Antony is touched With what is spoke already.
BOT: What power is in Agrippa, If I would say "Agrippa, be it so," To make this good?
USER: The power of Caesar, and His power unto Octavia.
BOT: May I never To this good purpose, that so fairly shows, Dream of impediment! Let me have thy hand. Further this act of grace; and from this hour The heart of brothers govern in our loves And sway our great designs!
USER: There's my hand. A sister I bequeath you, whom no brother Did ever love so dearly. Let her live To join our kingdoms and our hearts; and never Fly off our loves again!
BOT: Happily, amen!
USER: I did not think to draw my sword 'gainst Pompey, For he hath laid strange courtesies and great Of late upon me. I must thank him only, Lest my remembrance suffer ill report; At heel of that, defy him.
BOT: Time calls upon 's. Of us must Pompey presently be sought, Or else he seeks out us.
USER: Where lies he?
BOT: About the Mount Misena.
USER: What is his strength by land?
BOT: Great and increasing; but by sea He is an absolute master.
USER: So is the fame. Would we had spoke together! Haste we for it. Yet, ere we put ourselves in arms, dispatch we The business we have talked of.
BOT: With most gladness, And do invite you to my sister's view, Whither straight I'll lead you.
USER: Let us, Lepidus, not lack your company.
BOT: Noble Antony, not sickness should detain me.
USER: Welcome from Egypt, sir.
BOT: Half the heart of Caesar, worthy Maecenas! My honourable friend, Agrippa!
USER: Good Enobarbus!
BOT: We have cause to be glad that matters are so well digested. You stayed well by 't in Egypt.
USER: Ay, sir, we did sleep day out of countenance and made the night light with drinking.
BOT: Eight wild boars roasted whole at a breakfast, and but twelve persons there. Is this true?
USER: This was but as a fly by an eagle. We had much more monstrous matter of feast, which worthily deserved noting.
BOT: She's a most triumphant lady, if report be square to her.
USER: When she first met Mark Antony, she pursed up his heart upon the river of Cydnus.
BOT: There she appeared indeed, or my reporter devised well for her.
USER: O, rare for Antony!
BOT: Rare Egyptian!
USER: Royal wench! She made great Caesar lay his sword to bed. He ploughed her, and she cropped.
BOT: I saw her once Hop forty paces through the public street And, having lost her breath, she spoke and panted, That she did make defect perfection, And, breathless, pour breath forth.
USER: Now Antony must leave her utterly.
BOT: If beauty, wisdom, modesty can settle The heart of Antony, Octavia is A blessed lottery to him.
USER: Let us go. Good Enobarbus, make yourself my guest Whilst you abide here.
BOT: Humbly, sir, I thank you.

USER: The world and my great office will sometimes Divide me from your bosom.
BOT: All which time Before the gods my knee shall bow my prayers To them for you.
USER: Good night, sir. - My Octavia, Read not my blemishes in the world's report. I have not kept my square, but that to come Shall all be done by th' rule. Good night, dear lady.
BOT: Good night, sir.
USER: Good night.
BOT: Now, sirrah, you do wish yourself in Egypt?
USER: Would I had never come from thence, nor you thither!
BOT: If you can, your reason.
USER: I see it in my motion, have it not in my tongue. But yet hie you to Egypt again.
BOT: Say to me, Whose fortunes shall rise higher, Caesar's or mine?
USER: Speak this no more.

USER: Trouble yourselves no further. Pray you hasten Your generals after.
BOT: Sir, Mark Antony Will e'en but kiss Octavia, and we'll follow.
USER: Till I shall see you in your soldier's dress, Which will become you both, farewell.
BOT: We shall, As I conceive the journey, be at the Mount Before you, Lepidus.
USER: Your way is shorter; My purposes do draw me much about. You'll win two days upon me.
BOT: Sir, good success!
USER: Farewell.

USER: Give me some music - music, moody food Of us that trade in love.
BOT: The music, ho!
USER: Let it alone. Let's to billiards. Come, Charmian.
BOT: My arm is sore. Best play with Mardian.
USER: As well a woman with an eunuch played As with a woman. Come, you'll play with me, sir?
BOT: As well as I can, madam.
USER: 'Twas merry when You wagered on your angling; when your diver Did hang a salt fish on his hook, which he With fervency drew up.
BOT: Madam, madam -
USER: Antony's dead! If thou say so, villain, Thou kill'st thy mistress. But well and free, If thou so yield him, there is gold, and here My bluest veins to kiss, a hand that kings Have lipped, and trembled kissing.
BOT: First, madam, he's well.
USER: Why, there's more gold. But sirrah, mark, we use To say the dead are well. Bring it to that, The gold I give thee will I melt and pour Down thy ill-uttering throat.
BOT: Good madam, hear me.
USER: Will't please you hear me?
BOT: I have a mind to strike thee ere thou speak'st. Yet if thou say Antony lives, is well, Or friends with Caesar, or not captive to him, I'll set thee in a shower of gold and hail Rich pearls upon thee.
USER: Madam, he's well.
BOT: Well said.
USER: And friends with Caesar.
BOT: Th' art an honest man.
USER: Caesar and he are greater friends than ever.
