BOT: Make thee a fortune from me.
USER: But yet, madam -
BOT: Free, madam? No. I made no such report. He's bound unto Octavia.
USER: For what good turn?
BOT: For the best turn i' th' bed.
USER: I am pale, Charmian.
BOT: Madam, he's married to Octavia.
USER: The most infectious pestilence upon thee!
BOT: Good madam, patience.
USER: What say you? Hence, horrible villain, or I'll spurn thine eyes Like balls before me! I'll unhair thy head! Thou shalt be whipped with wire and stewed in brine, Smarting in ling'ring pickle.
BOT: Gracious madam, I that do bring the news made not the match.
USER: Say 'tis not so, a province I will give thee, And make thy fortunes proud. The blow thou hadst Shall make thy peace for moving me to rage, And I will boot thee with what gift beside Thy modesty can beg.
BOT: He's married, madam.
USER: Rogue, thou hast lived too long.
BOT: Nay then I'll run. What mean you, madam? I have made no fault.
USER: Good madam, keep yourself within yourself. The man is innocent.
BOT: Some innocents 'scape not the thunderbolt. Melt Egypt into Nile, and kindly creatures Turn all to serpents! Call the slave again. Though I am mad, I will not bite him. Call!
USER: He is afeard to come.
BOT: I have done my duty.
USER: Is he married? I cannot hate thee worser than I do If thou again say "Yes."
BOT: He's married, madam.
USER: The gods confound thee! Dost thou hold there still!
BOT: Should I lie, madam?
USER: O, I would thou didst, So half my Egypt were submerged and made A cistern for scaled snakes! Go, get thee hence. Hadst thou Narcissus in thy face, to me Thou wouldst appear most ugly. He is married?
BOT: I crave your highness' pardon.
USER: He is married?
BOT: Take no offence that I would not offend you. To punish me for what you make me do Seems much unequal. He's married to Octavia.
USER: O, that his fault should make a knave of thee That art not what thou'rt sure of! Get thee hence! The merchandise which thou hast brought from Rome Are all too dear for me. Lie they upon thy hand, And be undone by 'em!
BOT: Good your highness, patience.
USER: In praising Antony I have dispraised Caesar.
BOT: Many times, madam.

USER: Your hostages I have, so have you mine, And we shall talk before we fight.
BOT: Take your time.
USER: Thou canst not fear us, Pompey, with thy sails. We'll speak with thee at sea. At land thou know'st How much we do o'ercount thee.
BOT: At land indeed Thou dost o'ercount me of my father's house; But since the cuckoo builds not for himself, Remain in't as thou mayst.
USER: Be pleased to tell us - For this is from the present - how you take The offers we have sent you.
BOT: There's the point.
USER: Which do not be entreated to, but weigh What it is worth embraced.
BOT: And what may follow To try a larger fortune.
USER: I have heard it, Pompey, And am well studied for a liberal thanks Which I do owe you.
BOT: Let me have your hand. I did not think, sir, to have met you here.
USER: The beds i' th' East are soft; and thanks to you, That called me timelier than my purpose hither, For I have gained by 't.
BOT: Since I saw you last, There is a change upon you.
USER: Well, I know not What counts harsh Fortune casts upon my face, But in my bosom shall she never come To make my heart her vassal.
BOT: Well met here.
USER: I hope so, Lepidus. Thus we are agreed. I crave our composition may be written And sealed between us.
BOT: That's the next to do.
USER: We'll feast each other ere we part, and let's Draw lots who shall begin.
BOT: That will I, Pompey.
USER: No, Antony, take the lot. But, first or last, your fine Egyptian cookery Shall have the fame. I have heard that Julius Caesar Grew fat with feasting there.
BOT: You have heard much.
USER: I have fair meanings, sir.
BOT: And fair words to them.
USER: Then so much have I heard. And I have heard Apollodorus carried -
BOT: No more of that. He did so.
USER: What, I pray you?
BOT: A certain queen to Caesar in a mattress.
USER: I know thee now. How far'st thou, soldier?
BOT: Well; And well am like to do, for I perceive Four feasts are toward.
USER: Let me shake thy hand. I never hated thee. I have seen thee fight When I have envied thy behaviour.
BOT: Sir, I never loved you much, but I ha' praised ye When you have well deserved ten times as much As I have said you did.
USER: Enjoy thy plainness; It nothing ill becomes thee. Aboard my galley I invite you all. Will you lead, lords? CAESAR, ANTONY, and LEPIDUS. Show's the way, sir.
BOT: Come.
USER: You and I have known, sir.
BOT: At sea, I think.
USER: We have, sir.
BOT: You have done well by water.
USER: And you by land.
BOT: I will praise any man that will praise me, though it cannot be denied what I have done by land.
USER: Nor what I have done by water.
BOT: Yes, something you can deny for your own safety: you have been a great thief by sea.
USER: And you by land.
BOT: There I deny my land service. But give me your hand, Menas. If our eyes had authority, here they might take two thieves kissing.
USER: All men's faces are true, whatsome'er their hands are.
BOT: But there is never a fair woman has a true face.
USER: No slander. They steal hearts.
BOT: We came hither to fight with you.
USER: For my part, I am sorry it is turned to a drinking. Pompey doth this day laugh away his fortune.
BOT: If he do, sure he cannot weep 't back again.
USER: You have said, sir. We looked not for Mark Antony here. Pray you, is he married to Cleopatra?
BOT: Caesar's sister is called Octavia.
USER: True, sir. She was the wife of Caius Marcellus.
BOT: But she is now the wife of Marcus Antonius.
USER: Pray you, sir?
BOT: 'Tis true.
USER: Then is Caesar and he for ever knit together.
BOT: If I were bound to divine of this unity, I would not prophesy so.
USER: I think the policy of that purpose made more in the marriage than the love of the parties.
BOT: I think so too. But you shall find the band that seems to tie their friendship together will be the very strangler of their amity. Octavia is of a holy, cold, and still conversation.
USER: Who would not have his wife so?
BOT: And thus it may be. Come, sir, will you aboard? I have a health for you.
USER: I shall take it, sir. We have used our throats in Egypt.
BOT: Come, let's away.

USER: Here they'll be, man. Some o' their plants are ill-rooted already; the least wind i' th' world will blow them down.
BOT: Lepidus is high-coloured.
USER: They have made him drink alms-drink.
BOT: As they pinch one another by the disposition, he cries out "no more", reconciles them to his entreaty and himself to th' drink.
USER: But it raises the greater war between him and his discretion.
BOT: Why, this it is to have a name in great men's fellowship. I had as lief have a reed that will do me no service as a partisan I could not heave.
USER: You've strange serpents there?
BOT: Ay, Lepidus.
USER: Your serpent of Egypt is bred now of your mud by the operation of your sun; so is your crocodile.
BOT: They are so.
USER: Sit, and some wine! A health to Lepidus!
BOT: I am not so well as I should be, but I'll ne'er out.
USER: Not till you have slept. I fear me you'll be in till then.
BOT: Nay, certainly, I have heard the Ptolemies' pyramises are very goodly things. Without contradiction I have heard that.
USER: And hear me speak a word.
BOT: This wine for Lepidus!
USER: What manner o' thing is your crocodile?
BOT: It is shaped, sir, like itself, and it is as broad as it hath breadth. It is just so high as it is, and moves with it own organs. It lives by that which nourisheth it, and the elements once out of it, it transmigrates.
USER: What colour is it of?
BOT: Of its own colour too.
USER: 'Tis a strange serpent.
BOT: 'Tis so, and the tears of it are wet.
USER: Will this description satisfy him?
BOT: With the health that Pompey gives him, else he is a very epicure.
USER: Do as I bid you. - Where's this cup I called for?
BOT: Rise from thy stool.
USER: The matter?
BOT: I have ever held my cap off to thy fortunes.
USER: Thou hast served me with much faith. What's else to say? - Be jolly, lords.
BOT: These quicksands, Lepidus, Keep off them, for you sink.
USER: Wilt thou be lord of all the world?
BOT: What sayst thou?
USER: Wilt thou be lord of the whole world? That's twice.
BOT: How should that be?
USER: But entertain it, And though you think me poor, I am the man Will give thee all the world.
BOT: Hast thou drunk well?
USER: No, Pompey, I have kept me from the cup. Thou art, if thou dar'st be, the earthly Jove. Whate'er the ocean pales or sky inclips Is thine, if thou wilt have't.
BOT: Show me which way.
USER: These three world-sharers, these competitors, Are in thy vessel. Let me cut the cable, And when we are put off, fall to their throats. All then is thine.
BOT: I'll never follow thy palled fortunes more. Who seeks, and will not take when once 'tis offered, Shall never find it more.
USER: This health to Lepidus!
BOT: Bear him ashore. I'll pledge it for him, Pompey.
USER: Here's to thee, Menas!
BOT: Enobarbus, welcome!
USER: Fill till the cup be hid.
BOT: There's a strong fellow, Menas.
USER: Why?
BOT: 'A bears the third part of the world, man. Seest not?
USER: The third part, then, is drunk. Would it were all, That it might go on wheels!
BOT: Drink thou. Increase the reels.
USER: Come.
BOT: This is not yet an Alexandrian feast.
USER: It ripens towards it. Strike the vessels, ho! Here is to Caesar!
BOT: I could well forbear't. It's monstrous labour when I wash my brain And it grows fouler.
USER: Be a child o' the time.
BOT: Possess it, I'll make answer. But I had rather fast from all, four days, Than drink so much in one.
USER: Shall we dance now the Egyptian Bacchanals And celebrate our drink?
BOT: Let's ha't, good soldier.
USER: Come, let's all take hands Till that the conquering wine hath steeped our sense In soft and delicate Lethe.
BOT: Come, thou monarch of the vine, Plumpy Bacchus with pink eyne! In thy vats our cares be drowned, With thy grapes our hairs be crowned. Cup us till the world go round, Cup us till the world go round!
USER: I'll try you on the shore.
BOT: And shall, sir. Give's your hand.
USER: O Antony, You have my father's house. But, what? We are friends. Come, down into the boat.
BOT: Take heed you fall not. Menas, I'll not on shore.
USER: No, to my cabin. These drums, these trumpets, flutes! What! Let Neptune hear we bid a loud farewell To these great fellows. Sound and be hanged, sound out!
BOT: Hoo, says 'a! There's my cap!
USER: Hoo! Noble captain, come.

USER: Now, darting Parthia, art thou struck, and now Pleased Fortune does of Marcus Crassus' death Make me revenger. Bear the king's son's body Before our army. Thy Pacorus, Orodes, Pays this for Marcus Crassus.
BOT: Thou hast, Ventidius, that Without the which a soldier and his sword Grants scarce distinction. Thou wilt write to Antony?
USER: I'll humbly signify what in his name, That magical word of war, we have effected; How, with his banners, and his well-paid ranks, The ne'er-yet-beaten horse of Parthia We have jaded out o' th' field.
BOT: Where is he now?
USER: He purposeth to Athens, whither, with what haste The weight we must convey with 's will permit, We shall appear before him. - On there, pass along!

USER: What, are the brothers parted?
BOT: They have dispatched with Pompey; he is gone. The other three are sealing. Octavia weeps To part from Rome. Caesar is sad, and Lepidus, Since Pompey's feast, as Menas says, is troubled With the greensickness.
USER: 'Tis a noble Lepidus.
BOT: A very fine one. O, how he loves Caesar!
USER: Nay, but how dearly he adores Mark Antony!
BOT: Caesar? Why he's the Jupiter of men.
USER: What's Antony? The god of Jupiter.
BOT: Spake you of Caesar? How, the nonpareil!
USER: O, Antony! O thou Arabian bird!
BOT: Would you praise Caesar, say "Caesar". Go no further.
USER: Indeed, he plied them both with excellent praises.
BOT: Both he loves.
USER: They are his shards, and he their beetle. So, This is to horse. Adieu, noble Agrippa.
BOT: Good fortune, worthy soldier, and farewell.
USER: No further, sir.
BOT: Make me not offended In your distrust.
USER: I have said.
BOT: You shall not find, Though you be therein curious, the least cause For what you seem to fear. So the gods keep you, And make the hearts of Romans serve your ends. We will here part.
USER: Farewell, my dearest sister, fare thee well. The elements be kind to thee, and make Thy spirits all of comfort! Fare thee well.
BOT: My noble brother!
USER: The April's in her eyes. It is love's spring, And these the showers to bring it on. - Be cheerful.
BOT: Sir, look well to my husband's house, and -
USER: What, Octavia?
BOT: I'll tell you in your ear.
USER: Her tongue will not obey her heart, nor can Her heart inform her tongue - the swan's-down feather, That stands upon the swell at the full of tide, And neither way inclines.
BOT: So is he, being a man.
USER: When Antony found Julius Caesar dead, He cried almost to roaring, and he wept When at Philippi he found Brutus slain.
BOT: What willingly he did confound he wailed, Believe 't, till I weep too.
USER: No, sweet Octavia, You shall hear from me still. The time shall not Outgo my thinking on you.
BOT: Come, sir, come, I'll wrestle with you in my strength of love. Look, here I have you, thus I let you go, And give you to the gods.
USER: Adieu, be happy!
BOT: Let all the number of the stars give light To thy fair way!
USER: Farewell, farewell!
BOT: Farewell!

USER: Where is the fellow?
BOT: Half afeared to come.
USER: Go to, go to. Come hither, sir.
BOT: Good majesty, Herod of Jewry dare not look upon you But when you are well pleased.
USER: That Herod's head I'll have! But how, when Antony is gone, Through whom I might command it? - Come thou near.
BOT: Most gracious majesty!
USER: Didst thou behold Octavia?
BOT: Ay, dread queen.
USER: Where?
BOT: Madam, in Rome I looked her in the face, and saw her led Between her brother and Mark Antony.
USER: Is she as tall as me?
BOT: She is not, madam.
USER: Didst hear her speak? Is she shrill-tongued or low?
BOT: Madam, I heard her speak. She is low-voiced.
USER: That's not so good. He cannot like her long.
BOT: Like her? O Isis! 'Tis impossible.
USER: I think so, Charmian: dull of tongue and dwarfish! What majesty is in her gait? Remember, If e'er thou look'dst on majesty.
BOT: She creeps. Her motion and her station are as one. She shows a body rather than a life, A statue than a breather.
USER: Is this certain?
BOT: Or I have no observance.
USER: Three in Egypt Cannot make better note.
BOT: He's very knowing; I do perceive't. There's nothing in her yet. The fellow has good judgment.
USER: Excellent.
BOT: Guess at her years, I prithee.
USER: Madam, She was a widow.
BOT: Widow! Charmian, hark!
USER: And I do think she's thirty.
BOT: Bear'st thou her face in mind? Is't long or round?
USER: Round even to faultiness.
BOT: For the most part, too, they are foolish that are so. Her hair, what colour?
USER: Brown, madam, and her forehead As low as she would wish it.
BOT: There's gold for thee. Thou must not take my former sharpness ill. I will employ thee back again; I find thee Most fit for business. Go make thee ready; Our letters are prepared.
USER: A proper man.
BOT: Indeed, he is so. I repent me much That so I harried him. Why, methinks, by him, This creature's no such thing.
USER: Nothing, madam.
BOT: The man hath seen some majesty, and should know.
USER: Hath he seen majesty? Isis else defend, And serving you so long!
BOT: I have one thing more to ask him yet, good Charmian. But 'tis no matter; thou shalt bring him to me Where I will write. All may be well enough.
USER: I warrant you, madam.

USER: How now, friend Eros?
BOT: There's strange news come, sir.
USER: What, man?
BOT: Caesar and Lepidus have made wars upon Pompey.
USER: This is old. What is the success?
BOT: Then, world, thou hast a pair of chaps, no more, And throw between them all the food thou hast, They'll grind the one the other. Where's Antony?
USER: He's walking in the garden, thus, and spurns The rush that lies before him; cries "Fool Lepidus!" And threats the throat of that his officer That murdered Pompey.
BOT: Our great navy's rigged.
USER: For Italy and Caesar. More, Domitius: My lord desires you presently. My news I might have told hereafter.
BOT: 'Twill be naught, But let it be. Bring me to Antony.
USER: Come, sir.

USER: This in the public eye?
BOT: Let Rome be thus informed.
USER: Who, queasy with his insolence already, Will their good thoughts call from him.
BOT: The people knows it and have now received His accusations.
USER: Who does he accuse?
BOT: Sir, this should be answered.
USER: He'll never yield to that.
BOT: Nor must not then be yielded to in this.
USER: Hail, Caesar, and my lord! Hail, most dear Caesar!
BOT: That ever I should call thee castaway!
USER: You have not called me so, nor have you cause.
BOT: Good my lord, To come thus was I not constrained, but did it On my free will. My lord, Mark Antony, Hearing that you prepared for war, acquainted My grieved ear withal, whereon I begged His pardon for return.
USER: Which soon he granted, Being an abstract 'tween his lust and him.
BOT: Do not say so, my lord.
USER: I have eyes upon him, And his affairs come to me on the wind. Where is he now?
BOT: My lord, in Athens.
USER: Ay me, most wretched, That have my heart parted betwixt two friends That does afflict each other!
BOT: Welcome, lady.
USER: Welcome, dear madam. Each heart in Rome does love and pity you. Only th' adulterous Antony, most large In his abominations, turns you off And gives his potent regiment to a trull That noises it against us.
BOT: Is it so, sir?
USER: Most certain. Sister, welcome. Pray you Be ever known to patience. My dear'st sister!

USER: I will be even with thee, doubt it not.
BOT: But why, why, why?
USER: Thou hast forspoke my being in these wars And say'st it is not fit.
BOT: Well, is it, is it?
USER: Is 't not denounced against us? Why should not we Be there in person?
BOT: Well, I could reply: If we should serve with horse and mares together, The horse were merely lost. The mares would bear A soldier and his horse.
USER: What is't you say?
BOT: Sink Rome, and their tongues rot That speak against us! A charge we bear i' th' war, And, as the president of my kingdom, will Appear there for a man. Speak not against it. I will not stay behind.
USER: Nay, I have done. Here comes the Emperor.
BOT: Is it not strange, Canidius, That from Tarentum and Brundusium He could so quickly cut the Ionian sea And take in Toryne? - You have heard on 't, sweet?
USER: Celerity is never more admired Than by the negligent.
BOT: A good rebuke, Which might have well becomed the best of men To taunt at slackness. - Canidius, we Will fight with him by sea.
USER: By sea, what else?
BOT: Why will my lord do so?
USER: For that he dares us to 't.
BOT: So hath my lord dared him to single fight.
USER: Ay, and to wage this battle at Pharsalia, Where Caesar fought with Pompey. But these offers, Which serve not for his vantage, he shakes off, And so should you.
BOT: By sea, by sea.
USER: I'll fight at sea.
BOT: I have sixty sails, Caesar none better.
USER: Our overplus of shipping will we burn, And with the rest full-manned, from th' head of Actium Beat th' approaching Caesar. But if we fail, We then can do 't at land. Thy business?
BOT: The news is true, my lord; he is descried. Caesar has taken Toryne.
USER: Well, well, away.
BOT: By Hercules, I think I am i' th' right.
USER: Soldier, thou art. But his whole action grows Not in the power on 't. So our leader's led, And we are women's men.
BOT: You keep by land The legions and the horse whole, do you not?
USER: Marcus Octavius, Marcus Justeius, Publicola, and Caelius are for sea, But we keep whole by land. This speed of Caesar's Carries beyond belief.
BOT: While he was yet in Rome, His power went out in such distractions as Beguiled all spies.
USER: Who's his lieutenant, hear you?
BOT: They say one Taurus.
USER: Well I know the man.
BOT: The Emperor calls Canidius.
USER: With news the time's with labour, and throes forth Each minute some.

USER: Taurus!
BOT: My lord?
USER: Strike not by land; keep whole; provoke not battle Till we have done at sea. Do not exceed The prescript of this scroll. Our fortune lies Upon this jump.

USER: Naught, naught, all naught! I can behold no longer. Th' Antoniad, the Egyptian admiral, With all their sixty, fly and turn the rudder. To see 't mine eyes are blasted.
BOT: Gods and goddesses, All the whole synod of them!
USER: What's thy passion?
BOT: The greater cantle of the world is lost With very ignorance. We have kissed away Kingdoms and provinces.
USER: How appears the fight?
BOT: That I beheld. Mine eyes did sicken at the sight and could not Endure a further view.
USER: Alack, alack!
BOT: Our fortune on the sea is out of breath And sinks most lamentably. Had our general Been what he knew himself, it had gone well. O, he has given example for our flight Most grossly by his own!
USER: Ay, are you thereabouts? Why, then, good night indeed.
BOT: Toward Peloponnesus are they fled.
USER: 'Tis easy to't, and there I will attend What further comes.
BOT: To Caesar will I render My legions and my horse. Six kings already Show me the way of yielding.
USER: I'll yet follow The wounded chance of Antony, though my reason Sits in the wind against me.

USER: Fly? Not we.
BOT: Nay, gentle madam, to him! Comfort him.
USER: Do, most dear queen.
BOT: Do! Why, what else?
USER: Let me sit down. O Juno!
BOT: No, no, no, no, no.
USER: See you here, sir?
BOT: O, fie, fie, fie!
USER: Madam.
BOT: Madam, O good empress!
USER: Sir, sir!
BOT: Ah, stand by.
USER: The Queen, my lord, the Queen!
BOT: Go to him, madam; speak to him. He is unqualitied with very shame.
USER: Well then, sustain me. O!
BOT: Most noble sir, arise. The Queen approaches. Her head's declined, and death will seize her but Your comfort makes the rescue.
USER: I have offended reputation, A most unnoble swerving.
BOT: Sir, the Queen.
USER: O, whither hast thou led me, Egypt? See How I convey my shame out of thine eyes By looking back what I have left behind 'Stroyed in dishonour.
BOT: O my lord, my lord, Forgive my fearful sails! I little thought You would have followed.
USER: O, my pardon!
BOT: Pardon, pardon!

USER: Let him appear that's come from Antony. Know you him?
BOT: Caesar, 'tis his schoolmaster - An argument that he is plucked, when hither He sends so poor a pinion of his wing, Which had superfluous kings for messengers Not many moons gone by.
USER: Approach, and speak.
BOT: Such as I am, I come from Antony. I was of late as petty to his ends As is the morn-dew on the myrtle leaf To his grand sea.
USER: Be't so. Declare thine office.
BOT: Fortune pursue thee!
USER: Caesar, I go.
BOT: Observe how Antony becomes his flaw, And what thou think'st his very action speaks In every power that moves.
USER: Caesar, I shall.

USER: What shall we do, Enobarbus?
BOT: Think, and die.
USER: Is Antony or we in fault for this?
BOT: Prithee, peace.
USER: Is that his answer?
BOT: Ay, my lord.
USER: The Queen shall then have courtesy, so she Will yield us up.
BOT: He says so.
USER: Let her know't. - To the boy Caesar send this grizzled head, And he will fill thy wishes to the brim With principalities.
BOT: That head, my lord?
USER: A messenger from Caesar.
BOT: What, no more ceremony? See, my women, Against the blown rose may they stop their nose That kneeled unto the buds. Admit him, sir.
USER: The loyalty well held to fools does make Our faith mere folly. Yet he that can endure To follow with allegiance a fallen lord Does conquer him that did his master conquer, And earns a place i' th' story.
BOT: Caesar's will?
USER: Hear it apart.
BOT: None but friends. Say boldly.
USER: So haply are they friends to Antony.
BOT: He needs as many, sir, as Caesar has, Or needs not us. If Caesar please, our master Will leap to be his friend. For us, you know Whose he is we are, and that is Caesar's.
USER: So. - Thus then, thou most renowned: Caesar entreats Not to consider in what case thou stand'st Further than he is Caesar.
BOT: Go on; right royal.
USER: He knows that you embrace not Antony As you did love, but as you feared him.
BOT: O!
USER: The scars upon your honour, therefore, he Does pity as constrained blemishes, Not as deserved.
BOT: He is a god and knows What is most right. Mine honour was not yielded, But conquered merely.
USER: I will ask Antony. Sir, sir, thou art so leaky That we must leave thee to thy sinking, for Thy dearest quit thee.
BOT: What's your name?
USER: My name is Thidias.
BOT: Most kind messenger, Say to great Caesar this in deputation: I kiss his conqu'ring hand. Tell him I am prompt To lay my crown at's feet, and there to kneel. Tell him, from his all-obeying breath I hear The doom of Egypt.
USER: 'Tis your noblest course. Wisdom and fortune combating together, If that the former dare but what it can, No chance may shake it. Give me grace to lay My duty on your hand.
BOT: Your Caesar's father oft, When he hath mused of taking kingdoms in, Bestowed his lips on that unworthy place As it rained kisses.
USER: Favours, by Jove that thunders! What art thou, fellow?
BOT: One that but performs The bidding of the fullest man and worthiest To have command obeyed.
USER: 'Tis better playing with a lion's whelp Than with an old one dying.
BOT: Mark Antony -
USER: Good my lord -
BOT: O, is't come to this?
USER: Wherefore is this?
BOT: Soundly, my lord.
USER: Cried he? And begged he pardon?
BOT: He did ask favour.
USER: Have you done yet?
BOT: Alack, our terrene moon is now eclipsed, And it portends alone the fall of Antony.
USER: I must stay his time.
BOT: To flatter Caesar, would you mingle eyes With one that ties his points?
USER: Not know me yet?
BOT: Cold-hearted toward me?
USER: That's my brave lord!
BOT: It is my birthday. I had thought t'have held it poor, but since my lord Is Antony again, I will be Cleopatra.
USER: We will yet do well.
BOT: Call all his noble captains to my lord.

USER: He will not fight with me, Domitius?
BOT: No.
USER: Why should he not?
BOT: He thinks, being twenty times of better fortune, He is twenty men to one.
USER: Tomorrow, soldier, By sea and land I'll fight. Or I will live, Or bathe my dying honour in the blood Shall make it live again. Woo't thou fight well?
BOT: I'll strike, and cry "Take all."
USER: shoots Out of the mind.
BOT: And thou art honest too. I wish I could be made so many men, And all of you clapped up together in An Antony, that I might do you service So good as you have done.
USER: The gods forbid!
BOT: Well, my good fellows, wait on me tonight. Scant not my cups, and make as much of me As when mine empire was your fellow too And suffered my command.
USER: What mean you, sir, To give them this discomfort? Look, they weep, And I, an ass, am onion-eyed. For shame, Transform us not to women.

USER: Brother, good night. Tomorrow is the day.
BOT: It will determine one way. Fare you well. Heard you of nothing strange about the streets?
USER: Nothing. What news?
BOT: Belike 'tis but a rumour. Good night to you.
USER: Well, sir, good night.
BOT: Soldiers, have careful watch.
USER: And you. Good night, good night.
BOT: Here we. And if tomorrow Our navy thrive, I have an absolute hope Our landmen will stand up.
USER: 'Tis a brave army, and full of purpose.
BOT: Peace, what noise?
USER: List, list!
BOT: Hark!
USER: Music i' th' air.
BOT: Under the earth.
USER: It signs well, does it not?
BOT: No.
USER: Peace, I say! What should this mean?
BOT: 'Tis the god Hercules, whom Antony loved, Now leaves him.
USER: Walk. Let's see if other watchmen Do hear what we do.
BOT: How now, masters!
USER: How now! How now! Do you hear this?
BOT: Ay. Is't not strange?
USER: Do you hear, masters? Do you hear?
BOT: Follow the noise so far as we have quarter. Let's see how it will give off.
USER: Content. 'Tis strange.

USER: Eros! Mine armour, Eros!
BOT: Sleep a little.
USER: No, my chuck. - Eros! Come, mine armour, Eros! Come, good fellow, put thine iron on. If fortune be not ours today, it is Because we brave her. Come.
BOT: Nay, I'll help too. What's this for?
USER: Ah, let be, let be! Thou art The armourer of my heart. False, false. This, this!
BOT: Sooth, la, I'll help. Thus it must be.
USER: Well, well, We shall thrive now. Seest thou, my good fellow? Go put on thy defences.
BOT: Briefly, sir.
USER: Is not this buckled well?
BOT: A thousand, sir, Early though't be, have on their riveted trim And at the port expect you.
USER: The morn is fair. Good morrow, general.
BOT: Good morrow, general.
USER: Please you, retire to your chamber.
BOT: Lead me. He goes forth gallantly. That he and Caesar might Determine this great war in single fight! Then Antony - but now - . Well, on.

USER: The gods make this a happy day to Antony!
BOT: Would thou and those thy scars had once prevailed To make me fight at land!
USER: Hadst thou done so, The kings that have revolted and the soldier That has this morning left thee would have still Followed thy heels.
BOT: Who's gone this morning?
USER: Who? One ever near thee. Call for Enobarbus, He shall not hear thee, or from Caesar's camp Say "I am none of thine."
BOT: What sayest thou?
USER: Sir, He is with Caesar.
BOT: Sir, his chests and treasure He has not with him.
USER: Is he gone?
BOT: Most certain.

USER: Go forth, Agrippa, and begin the fight. Our will is Antony be took alive; Make it so known.
BOT: Caesar, I shall.
USER: The time of universal peace is near. Prove this a prosp'rous day, the three-nooked world Shall bear the olive freely.
BOT: Antony Is come into the field.
USER: Go charge Agrippa Plant those that have revolted in the van That Antony may seem to spend his fury Upon himself.
BOT: Enobarbus, Antony Hath after thee sent all thy treasure, with His bounty overplus. The messenger Came on my guard, and at thy tent is now Unloading of his mules.
USER: I give it you.
BOT: Mock not, Enobarbus. I tell you true. Best you safed the bringer Out of the host. I must attend mine office, Or would have done't myself. Your emperor Continues still a Jove.

USER: Retire! We have engaged ourselves too far. Caesar himself has work, and our oppression Exceeds what we expected. Alarums. Enter Antony and Scarus wounded.
BOT: O my brave emperor, this is fought indeed! Had we done so at first, we had droven them home With clouts about their heads.
USER: Thou bleed'st apace.
BOT: I had a wound here that was like a T, But now 'tis made an H. _Sounds retreat far off._
USER: They do retire.
BOT: We'll beat 'em into bench-holes. I have yet Room for six scotches more.
USER: They are beaten, sir, and our advantage serves For a fair victory.
BOT: Let us score their backs And snatch 'em up as we take hares, behind. 'Tis sport to maul a runner.
USER: I will reward thee Once for thy sprightly comfort, and tenfold For thy good valour. Come thee on.
BOT: I'll halt after.

USER: Lord of lords! O infinite virtue, com'st thou smiling from The world's great snare uncaught?
BOT: I'll give thee, friend, An armour all of gold. It was a king's.

USER: If we be not relieved within this hour, We must return to th' court of guard. The night Is shiny, and they say we shall embattle By th' second hour i' th' morn.
BOT: This last day was a shrewd one to's.
USER: O, bear me witness, night. -
BOT: What man is this?
USER: Stand close and list him.
BOT: Be witness to me, O thou blessed moon, When men revolted shall upon record Bear hateful memory, poor Enobarbus did Before thy face repent.
USER: Enobarbus?
BOT: Peace! Hark further.
USER: Let's speak to him.
BOT: Let's hear him, for the things he speaks may concern Caesar.
USER: Let's do so. But he sleeps.
BOT: Swoons rather, for so bad a prayer as his Was never yet for sleep.
USER: Go we to him.
BOT: Awake, sir, awake! Speak to us.
USER: Hear you, sir?
BOT: The hand of death hath raught him. Hark! The drums Demurely wake the sleepers. Let us bear him To th' court of guard; he is of note. Our hour Is fully out.
USER: Come on, then. He may recover yet.

USER: Their preparation is today by sea; We please them not by land.
BOT: For both, my lord.

USER: Yet they are not joined. Where yond pine does stand I shall discover all. I'll bring thee word Straight how 'tis like to go.
BOT: Why is my lord enraged against his love?

USER: Help me, my women! O, he is more mad Than Telamon for his shield; the boar of Thessaly Was never so embossed.
BOT: To th' monument! There lock yourself and send him word you are dead. The soul and body rive not more in parting Than greatness going off.
USER: To th' monument! Mardian, go tell him I have slain myself. Say that the last I spoke was "Antony", And word it, prithee, piteously. Hence, Mardian, And bring me how he takes my death. - To th' monument!

USER: Eros, thou yet behold'st me?
BOT: Ay, noble lord.
USER: Ay, my lord.
BOT: That which is now a horse, even with a thought The rack dislimns and makes it indistinct As water is in water.
USER: It does, my lord.
BOT: No, Antony, My mistress loved thee and her fortunes mingled With thine entirely.
USER: Hence, saucy eunuch! Peace! She hath betrayed me and shall die the death.
BOT: Dead, then?
USER: Dead.
BOT: What would my lord?
USER: The gods withhold me! Shall I do that which all the Parthian darts, Though enemy, lost aim and could not?
BOT: I would not see't.
USER: Come, then, for with a wound I must be cured. Draw that thy honest sword, which thou hast worn Most useful for thy country.
BOT: O, sir, pardon me!
USER: When I did make thee free, swor'st thou not then To do this when I bade thee? Do it at once, Or thy precedent services are all But accidents unpurposed. Draw, and come.
BOT: Turn from me then that noble countenance Wherein the worship of the whole world lies.
USER: Lo thee!
BOT: My sword is drawn.
USER: Then let it do at once The thing why thou hast drawn it.
BOT: My dear master, My captain and my emperor, let me say, Before I strike this bloody stroke, farewell.
USER: 'Tis said, man, and farewell.
BOT: Farewell, great chief. Shall I strike now?
USER: Now, Eros.
BOT: Why, there then! [_kills himself_] Thus do I escape the sorrow Of Antony's death.
USER: What's the noise?
BOT: I have done my work ill, friends. O, make an end Of what I have begun.
USER: The star is fallen.
BOT: And time is at his period.
USER: Alas, and woe!
BOT: Let him that loves me strike me dead.
USER: Not I.
BOT: Nor I.
USER: Nor anyone.
BOT: Thy death and fortunes bid thy followers fly. This sword but shown to Caesar with this tidings, Shall enter me with him.
USER: Where's Antony?
BOT: There, Diomed, there.
USER: Lives he? Wilt thou not answer, man?
BOT: Art thou there, Diomed? Draw thy sword and give me Sufficing strokes for death.
USER: Most absolute lord, My mistress Cleopatra sent me to thee.
BOT: When did she send thee?
USER: Now, my lord.
BOT: Where is she?
USER: Too late, good Diomed. Call my guard, I prithee.
BOT: What, ho! The emperor's guard! The guard, what ho! Come, your lord calls!
USER: Bear me, good friends, where Cleopatra bides. 'Tis the last service that I shall command you.
BOT: Woe, woe are we, sir, you may not live to wear All your true followers out.
USER: Most heavy day!

USER: O Charmian, I will never go from hence.
BOT: Be comforted, dear madam.
USER: No, I will not. All strange and terrible events are welcome, But comforts we despise. Our size of sorrow, Proportioned to our cause, must be as great As that which makes it. Enter, below Diomedes. How now! Is he dead?
BOT: His death's upon him, but not dead. Look out o' th' other side your monument; His guard have brought him thither. Enter, below Antony borne by the Guard.
USER: O sun, Burn the great sphere thou mov'st in! Darkling stand The varying shore o' th' world. O Antony, Antony, Antony! Help, Charmian! Help, Iras, help! Help, friends below! Let's draw him hither.
BOT: Peace! Not Caesar's valour hath o'erthrown Antony, But Antony's hath triumphed on itself.
USER: So it should be, that none but Antony Should conquer Antony, but woe 'tis so!
BOT: I am dying, Egypt, dying. Only I here importune death awhile until Of many thousand kisses the poor last I lay upon thy lips.
USER: O, quick, or I am gone.
BOT: A heavy sight!
USER: I am dying, Egypt, dying. Give me some wine, and let me speak a little.
BOT: No, let me speak, and let me rail so high That the false huswife Fortune break her wheel, Provoked by my offence.
USER: One word, sweet queen: Of Caesar seek your honour, with your safety. O!
BOT: They do not go together.
USER: Gentle, hear me. None about Caesar trust but Proculeius.
BOT: My resolution and my hands I'll trust; None about Caesar.
USER: O, quietness, lady!
BOT: She is dead too, our sovereign.
USER: Lady!
BOT: Madam!
USER: O madam, madam, madam!
BOT: Royal Egypt, Empress!
USER: Peace, peace, Iras!

USER: Go to him, Dolabella, bid him yield. Being so frustrate, tell him, he mocks The pauses that he makes.
BOT: Caesar, I shall.
USER: Wherefore is that? And what art thou that dar'st Appear thus to us?
BOT: What is't thou say'st?
USER: I say, O Caesar, Antony is dead.
BOT: Look you sad, friends? The gods rebuke me, but it is tidings To wash the eyes of kings.
USER: And strange it is That nature must compel us to lament Our most persisted deeds.
BOT: His taints and honours Waged equal with him.
USER: A rarer spirit never Did steer humanity, but you gods will give us Some faults to make us men. Caesar is touched.
BOT: When such a spacious mirror's set before him, He needs must see himself.
USER: A poor Egyptian yet. The queen, my mistress, Confined in all she has, her monument, Of thy intents desires instruction, That she preparedly may frame herself To the way she's forced to.
BOT: Bid her have good heart. She soon shall know of us, by some of ours, How honourable and how kindly we Determine for her. For Caesar cannot lean To be ungentle.
USER: So the gods preserve thee!
BOT: Caesar, I shall.
USER: Gallus, go you along. Where's Dolabella, to second Proculeius?
BOT: Dolabella!

USER: Caesar sends greetings to the queen of Egypt, And bids thee study on what fair demands Thou mean'st to have him grant thee.
BOT: What's thy name?
USER: My name is Proculeius.
BOT: Pray you tell him I am his fortune's vassal and I send him The greatness he has got. I hourly learn A doctrine of obedience, and would gladly Look him i' th' face.
USER: This I'll report, dear lady. Have comfort, for I know your plight is pitied Of him that caused it. You see how easily she may be surprised. Guard her till Caesar come.
BOT: Royal queen!
USER: O Cleopatra, thou art taken, queen!
BOT: Quick, quick, good hands.
USER: Hold, worthy lady, hold! Do not yourself such wrong, who are in this Relieved, but not betrayed.
BOT: What, of death too, That rids our dogs of languish?
USER: Cleopatra, Do not abuse my master's bounty by Th' undoing of yourself. Let the world see His nobleness well acted, which your death Will never let come forth.
BOT: Where art thou, Death? Come hither, come! Come, come, and take a queen Worth many babes and beggars!
USER: O, temperance, lady!
BOT: You do extend These thoughts of horror further than you shall Find cause in Caesar.
USER: Proculeius, What thou hast done thy master Caesar knows, And he hath sent for thee. For the queen, I'll take her to my guard.
BOT: So, Dolabella, It shall content me best. Be gentle to her. If you'll employ me to him.
USER: Say I would die.
BOT: Most noble empress, you have heard of me?
USER: I cannot tell.
BOT: Assuredly you know me.
USER: No matter, sir, what I have heard or known. You laugh when boys or women tell their dreams; Is't not your trick?
BOT: I understand not, madam.
USER: I dreamt there was an Emperor Antony. O, such another sleep, that I might see But such another man!
BOT: If it might please you -
USER: His face was as the heavens, and therein stuck A sun and moon, which kept their course, and lighted The little O, the earth.
BOT: Most sovereign creature -
USER: Cleopatra -
BOT: Think you there was or might be such a man As this I dreamt of?
USER: Gentle madam, no.
BOT: I thank you, sir. Know you what Caesar means to do with me?
USER: I am loath to tell you what I would you knew.
BOT: Nay, pray you, sir.
USER: Though he be honourable -
BOT: He'll lead me, then, in triumph.
USER: Madam, he will. I know it. Flourish. Enter Caesar, Proculeius, Gallus, Maecenas and others of his train.
BOT: Make way there! Caesar!
USER: Which is the Queen of Egypt?
BOT: It is the Emperor, madam.
USER: Arise, you shall not kneel. I pray you, rise. Rise, Egypt.
BOT: Sir, the gods Will have it thus. My master and my lord I must obey.
USER: Take to you no hard thoughts. The record of what injuries you did us, Though written in our flesh, we shall remember As things but done by chance.
BOT: Sole sir o' th' world, I cannot project mine own cause so well To make it clear, but do confess I have Been laden with like frailties which before Have often shamed our sex.
USER: And may, through all the world. 'Tis yours, and we, Your scutcheons and your signs of conquest, shall Hang in what place you please. Here, my good lord.
BOT: You shall advise me in all for Cleopatra.
USER: This is the brief of money, plate, and jewels I am possessed of. 'Tis exactly valued, Not petty things admitted. Where's Seleucus?
BOT: Here, madam.
USER: This is my treasurer. Let him speak, my lord, Upon his peril, that I have reserved To myself nothing. Speak the truth, Seleucus.
BOT: Madam, I had rather seal my lips Than to my peril speak that which is not.
USER: What have I kept back?
BOT: Enough to purchase what you have made known.
USER: Nay, blush not, Cleopatra. I approve Your wisdom in the deed.
BOT: Good queen, let us entreat you.
USER: Forbear, Seleucus.
BOT: Be it known that we, the greatest, are misthought For things that others do; and when we fall, We answer others' merits in our name, Are therefore to be pitied.
USER: My master and my lord!
BOT: Not so. Adieu.
USER: He words me, girls, he words me, that I should not Be noble to myself. But hark thee, Charmian!
BOT: Finish, good lady. The bright day is done, And we are for the dark.
USER: Hie thee again. I have spoke already, and it is provided. Go put it to the haste.
BOT: Madam, I will.
USER: Where's the Queen?
BOT: Behold, sir.
USER: Dolabella!
BOT: Dolabella, I shall remain your debtor.
USER: I your servant. Adieu, good queen. I must attend on Caesar.
BOT: The gods forbid!
USER: O the good gods!
BOT: Nay, that's certain.
USER: I'll never see't, for I am sure mine nails Are stronger than mine eyes.
BOT: Here is a rural fellow That will not be denied your highness' presence. He brings you figs.
USER: This is the man.
BOT: Avoid, and leave him. Hast thou the pretty worm of Nilus there That kills and pains not?
USER: Truly, I have him, but I would not be the party that should desire you to touch him, for his biting is immortal. Those that do die of it do seldom or never recover.
BOT: Remember'st thou any that have died on't?
USER: Get thee hence. Farewell.
BOT: I wish you all joy of the worm.
USER: Farewell.
BOT: You must think this, look you, that the worm will do his kind.
USER: Ay, ay, farewell.
BOT: Look you, the worm is not to be trusted but in the keeping of wise people; for indeed there is no goodness in the worm.
USER: Take thou no care; it shall be heeded.
BOT: Very good. Give it nothing, I pray you, for it is not worth the feeding.
USER: Will it eat me?
BOT: Well, get thee gone. Farewell.
USER: Yes, forsooth. I wish you joy o' th' worm.
BOT: Dissolve, thick cloud, and rain, that I may say The gods themselves do weep!
USER: O eastern star!
BOT: Peace, peace! Dost thou not see my baby at my breast That sucks the nurse asleep?
USER: O, break! O, break!
BOT: As sweet as balm, as soft as air, as gentle - O Antony! - Nay, I will take thee too. What should I stay -
USER: Where's the queen?
BOT: Speak softly. Wake her not.
USER: Caesar hath sent -
BOT: Too slow a messenger. O, come apace, dispatch! I partly feel thee.
USER: Approach, ho! All's not well. Caesar's beguiled.
BOT: There's Dolabella sent from Caesar. Call him.
USER: What work is here, Charmian? Is this well done?
BOT: It is well done, and fitting for a princess Descended of so many royal kings. Ah, soldier!
USER: How goes it here?
BOT: All dead.
USER: Caesar, thy thoughts Touch their effects in this. Thyself art coming To see performed the dreaded act which thou So sought'st to hinder.
BOT: A way there, a way for Caesar!
USER: O sir, you are too sure an augurer: That you did fear is done.
BOT: Bravest at the last, She levelled at our purposes and, being royal, Took her own way. The manner of their deaths? I do not see them bleed.
USER: Who was last with them?
BOT: A simple countryman that brought her figs. This was his basket.
USER: Poisoned then.
BOT: O Caesar, This Charmian lived but now; she stood and spake. I found her trimming up the diadem On her dead mistress; tremblingly she stood, And on the sudden dropped.
USER: O noble weakness! If they had swallowed poison 'twould appear By external swelling; but she looks like sleep, As she would catch another Antony In her strong toil of grace.
BOT: Here on her breast There is a vent of blood, and something blown. The like is on her arm.
USER: This is an aspic's trail, and these fig leaves Have slime upon them, such as th' aspic leaves Upon the caves of Nile.

USER: Yonder comes my master, your brother.
BOT: Go apart, Adam, and thou shalt hear how he will shake me up.
USER: Now, sir, what make you here?
BOT: Nothing. I am not taught to make anything.
USER: What mar you then, sir?
BOT: Marry, sir, I am helping you to mar that which God made, a poor unworthy brother of yours, with idleness.
USER: Marry, sir, be better employed, and be naught awhile.
BOT: Shall I keep your hogs, and eat husks with them? What prodigal portion have I spent that I should come to such penury?
USER: Know you where you are, sir?
BOT: O, sir, very well: here in your orchard.
USER: Know you before whom, sir?
BOT: What, boy!
USER: Come, come, elder brother, you are too young in this.
BOT: Wilt thou lay hands on me, villain?
USER: remembrance, be at accord.
BOT: Let me go, I say.
USER: And what wilt thou do? Beg when that is spent? Well, sir, get you in. I will not long be troubled with you. You shall have some part of your will. I pray you leave me.
BOT: I no further offend you than becomes me for my good.
USER: Get you with him, you old dog.
BOT: Is "old dog" my reward? Most true, I have lost my teeth in your service. God be with my old master. He would not have spoke such a word.
USER: Is it even so? Begin you to grow upon me? I will physic your rankness, and yet give no thousand crowns neither. Holla, Dennis! DENNIS Calls your worship?
BOT: Was not Charles, the Duke's wrestler, here to speak with me? DENNIS So please you, he is here at the door and importunes access to you.
USER: Call him in. 'Twill be a good way, and tomorrow the wrestling is.
BOT: Good morrow to your worship.
USER: Good Monsieur Charles. What's the new news at the new court?
BOT: Can you tell if Rosalind, the Duke's daughter, be banished with her father?
USER: Where will the old Duke live?
BOT: What, you wrestle tomorrow before the new Duke?
USER: I am heartily glad I came hither to you. If he come tomorrow I'll give him his payment. If ever he go alone again I'll never wrestle for prize more. And so, God keep your worship.

USER: I pray thee, Rosalind, sweet my coz, be merry.
BOT: Dear Celia, I show more mirth than I am mistress of, and would you yet I were merrier? Unless you could teach me to forget a banished father, you must not learn me how to remember any extraordinary pleasure.
USER: Well, I will forget the condition of my estate to rejoice in yours.
BOT: From henceforth I will, coz, and devise sports. Let me see - what think you of falling in love?
USER: Marry, I prithee do, to make sport withal; but love no man in good earnest, nor no further in sport neither than with safety of a pure blush thou mayst in honour come off again.
BOT: What shall be our sport, then?
USER: Let us sit and mock the good housewife Fortune from her wheel, that her gifts may henceforth be bestowed equally.
BOT: I would we could do so, for her benefits are mightily misplaced, and the bountiful blind woman doth most mistake in her gifts to women.
USER: 'Tis true, for those that she makes fair she scarce makes honest, and those that she makes honest she makes very ill-favouredly.
BOT: Nay, now thou goest from Fortune's office to Nature's. Fortune reigns in gifts of the world, not in the lineaments of Nature.
USER: No? When Nature hath made a fair creature, may she not by Fortune fall into the fire? Though Nature hath given us wit to flout at Fortune, hath not Fortune sent in this fool to cut off the argument?
BOT: Indeed, there is Fortune too hard for Nature, when Fortune makes Nature's natural the cutter-off of Nature's wit.
USER: Mistress, you must come away to your father.
BOT: Were you made the messenger?
USER: No, by mine honour, but I was bid to come for you.
BOT: Where learned you that oath, fool?
USER: How prove you that in the great heap of your knowledge?
BOT: Ay, marry, now unmuzzle your wisdom.
USER: Stand you both forth now: stroke your chins, and swear by your beards that I am a knave.
BOT: By our beards, if we had them, thou art.
USER: Prithee, who is't that thou mean'st?
BOT: One that old Frederick, your father, loves.
USER: My father's love is enough to honour him. Enough! Speak no more of him. You'll be whipped for taxation one of these days.
BOT: The more pity that fools may not speak wisely what wise men do foolishly.
USER: By my troth, thou sayest true. For since the little wit that fools have was silenced, the little foolery that wise men have makes a great show. Here comes Monsieur Le Beau.
BOT: With his mouth full of news.
USER: Which he will put on us as pigeons feed their young.
BOT: Then shall we be news-crammed.
USER: All the better; we shall be the more marketable. _Bonjour_, Monsieur Le Beau. What's the news?
BOT: Fair princess, you have lost much good sport.
USER: Sport! Of what colour?
BOT: What colour, madam? How shall I answer you?
USER: As wit and fortune will.
BOT: Or as the destinies decrees.
USER: Well said. That was laid on with a trowel.
BOT: Nay, if I keep not my rank -
USER: Thou losest thy old smell.
BOT: You amaze me, ladies. I would have told you of good wrestling, which you have lost the sight of.
USER: Yet tell us the manner of the wrestling.
BOT: I will tell you the beginning and, if it please your ladyships, you may see the end, for the best is yet to do; and here, where you are, they are coming to perform it.
USER: Well, the beginning that is dead and buried.
BOT: There comes an old man and his three sons -
USER: I could match this beginning with an old tale.
BOT: Three proper young men of excellent growth and presence.
USER: With bills on their necks: "Be it known unto all men by these presents."
BOT: Alas!
USER: But what is the sport, monsieur, that the ladies have lost?
BOT: Why, this that I speak of.
USER: Thus men may grow wiser every day. It is the first time that ever I heard breaking of ribs was sport for ladies.
BOT: Or I, I promise thee.
USER: But is there any else longs to see this broken music in his sides? Is there yet another dotes upon rib-breaking? Shall we see this wrestling, cousin?
BOT: You must if you stay here, for here is the place appointed for the wrestling, and they are ready to perform it.
USER: Yonder, sure, they are coming. Let us now stay and see it. Flourish. Enter Duke Frederick, Lords, Orlando, Charles and Attendants.
BOT: Come on. Since the youth will not be entreated, his own peril on his forwardness.
USER: Is yonder the man?
BOT: Even he, madam.
USER: Alas, he is too young. Yet he looks successfully.
BOT: How now, daughter and cousin? Are you crept hither to see the wrestling?
USER: Ay, my liege, so please you give us leave.
BOT: You will take little delight in it, I can tell you, there is such odds in the man. In pity of the challenger's youth I would fain dissuade him, but he will not be entreated. Speak to him, ladies; see if you can move him.
USER: Call him hither, good Monsieur Le Beau.
BOT: Do so; I'll not be by.
USER: Monsieur the challenger, the Princess calls for you.
BOT: I attend them with all respect and duty.
USER: Young man, have you challenged Charles the wrestler?
BOT: No, fair princess. He is the general challenger. I come but in as others do, to try with him the strength of my youth.
USER: Do, young sir. Your reputation shall not therefore be misprized. We will make it our suit to the Duke that the wrestling might not go forward.
BOT: The little strength that I have, I would it were with you.
USER: And mine to eke out hers.
BOT: Fare you well. Pray heaven I be deceived in you.
USER: Your heart's desires be with you.
BOT: Come, where is this young gallant that is so desirous to lie with his mother earth?
USER: Ready, sir; but his will hath in it a more modest working.
BOT: You shall try but one fall.
USER: No, I warrant your grace you shall not entreat him to a second, that have so mightily persuaded him from a first.
BOT: You mean to mock me after; you should not have mocked me before. But come your ways.
USER: Now, Hercules be thy speed, young man!
BOT: I would I were invisible, to catch the strong fellow by the leg.
USER: O excellent young man!
BOT: If I had a thunderbolt in mine eye, I can tell who should down.
USER: No more, no more.
BOT: Yes, I beseech your grace. I am not yet well breathed.
USER: How dost thou, Charles?
BOT: He cannot speak, my lord.
USER: Bear him away. What is thy name, young man?
BOT: Orlando, my liege, the youngest son of Sir Rowland de Boys.
USER: Were I my father, coz, would I do this?
BOT: I am more proud to be Sir Rowland's son, His youngest son, and would not change that calling To be adopted heir to Frederick.
USER: My father loved Sir Rowland as his soul, And all the world was of my father's mind. Had I before known this young man his son, I should have given him tears unto entreaties Ere he should thus have ventured.
BOT: Gentleman, Wear this for me - one out of suits with Fortune, That could give more but that her hand lacks means. - Shall we go, coz?
USER: Ay. - Fare you well, fair gentleman.
BOT: Can I not say, I thank you? My better parts Are all thrown down, and that which here stands up Is but a quintain, a mere lifeless block.
USER: He calls us back. My pride fell with my fortunes. I'll ask him what he would. - Did you call, sir? - Sir, you have wrestled well and overthrown More than your enemies.
BOT: Will you go, coz?
USER: Have with you. - Fare you well.
BOT: What passion hangs these weights upon my tongue? I cannot speak to her, yet she urged conference. O poor Orlando, thou art overthrown. Or Charles or something weaker masters thee.
USER: I thank you, sir; and pray you tell me this: Which of the two was daughter of the Duke That here was at the wrestling?
BOT: I rest much bounden to you; fare you well! Thus must I from the smoke into the smother, From tyrant Duke unto a tyrant brother. But heavenly Rosalind!

USER: Why, cousin, why, Rosalind! Cupid have mercy! Not a word?
BOT: Not one to throw at a dog.
USER: No, thy words are too precious to be cast away upon curs. Throw some of them at me. Come, lame me with reasons.
BOT: Then there were two cousins laid up, when the one should be lamed with reasons and the other mad without any.
USER: But is all this for your father?
BOT: No, some of it is for my child's father. O, how full of briers is this working-day world!
USER: They are but burs, cousin, thrown upon thee in holiday foolery. If we walk not in the trodden paths, our very petticoats will catch them.
BOT: I could shake them off my coat; these burs are in my heart.
USER: Hem them away.
BOT: I would try, if I could cry "hem" and have him.
USER: Come, come, wrestle with thy affections.
BOT: O, they take the part of a better wrestler than myself.
USER: The Duke my father loved his father dearly.
BOT: Doth it therefore ensue that you should love his son dearly? By this kind of chase I should hate him, for my father hated his father dearly; yet I hate not Orlando.
USER: No, faith, hate him not, for my sake.
BOT: Why should I not? Doth he not deserve well?
USER: Let me love him for that, and do you love him because I do. - Look, here comes the Duke.
BOT: With his eyes full of anger.
USER: Mistress, dispatch you with your safest haste, And get you from our court.
BOT: Me, uncle?
USER: You, cousin. Within these ten days if that thou be'st found So near our public court as twenty miles, Thou diest for it.
BOT: Thus do all traitors. If their purgation did consist in words, They are as innocent as grace itself. Let it suffice thee that I trust thee not.
USER: Yet your mistrust cannot make me a traitor. Tell me whereon the likelihood depends.
BOT: Thou art thy father's daughter, there's enough.
USER: Dear sovereign, hear me speak.
BOT: Ay, Celia, we stayed her for your sake, Else had she with her father ranged along.
USER: Pronounce that sentence then on me, my liege. I cannot live out of her company.
BOT: You are a fool. You, niece, provide yourself. If you outstay the time, upon mine honour And in the greatness of my word, you die.
USER: O my poor Rosalind, whither wilt thou go? Wilt thou change fathers? I will give thee mine. I charge thee, be not thou more grieved than I am.
BOT: I have more cause.
USER: Thou hast not, cousin. Prithee be cheerful. Know'st thou not the Duke Hath banished me, his daughter?
BOT: That he hath not.
USER: Why, whither shall we go?
BOT: To seek my uncle in the Forest of Arden.
USER: Alas, what danger will it be to us, Maids as we are, to travel forth so far? Beauty provoketh thieves sooner than gold.
BOT: I'll put myself in poor and mean attire, And with a kind of umber smirch my face. The like do you; so shall we pass along And never stir assailants.
USER: What shall I call thee when thou art a man?
BOT: I'll have no worse a name than Jove's own page, And therefore look you call me Ganymede. But what will you be called?
USER: Something that hath a reference to my state: No longer Celia, but Aliena.
BOT: But, cousin, what if we assayed to steal The clownish fool out of your father's court? Would he not be a comfort to our travel?

USER: I would not change it. Happy is your grace, That can translate the stubbornness of fortune Into so quiet and so sweet a style.
BOT: Come, shall we go and kill us venison? And yet it irks me the poor dappled fools, Being native burghers of this desert city, Should in their own confines with forked heads Have their round haunches gored.
USER: But what said Jaques? Did he not moralize this spectacle?
BOT: And did you leave him in this contemplation?
USER: We did, my lord, weeping and commenting Upon the sobbing deer.
BOT: Show me the place. I love to cope him in these sullen fits, For then he's full of matter.
USER: I'll bring you to him straight.

USER: Can it be possible that no man saw them? It cannot be! Some villains of my court Are of consent and sufferance in this.
BOT: I cannot hear of any that did see her. The ladies, her attendants of her chamber, Saw her abed, and in the morning early They found the bed untreasured of their mistress.
USER: Send to his brother; fetch that gallant hither. If he be absent, bring his brother to me. I'll make him find him. Do this suddenly! And let not search and inquisition quail To bring again these foolish runaways.

USER: Who's there?
BOT: Why, what's the matter?
USER: Why, whither, Adam, wouldst thou have me go?
BOT: No matter whither, so you come not here.

USER: O Jupiter, how weary are my spirits!
BOT: I care not for my spirits, if my legs were not weary.
USER: I could find in my heart to disgrace my man's apparel, and to cry like a woman, but I must comfort the weaker vessel, as doublet and hose ought to show itself courageous to petticoat. Therefore, courage, good Aliena.
BOT: I pray you bear with me, I cannot go no further.
USER: For my part, I had rather bear with you than bear you. Yet I should bear no cross if I did bear you, for I think you have no money in your purse.
BOT: Well, this is the forest of Arden.
USER: Ay, now am I in Arden, the more fool I! When I was at home I was in a better place, but travellers must be content.
BOT: Ay, be so, good Touchstone. Look you, who comes here? A young man and an old in solemn talk.
USER: That is the way to make her scorn you still.
BOT: O Corin, that thou knew'st how I do love her!
USER: I partly guess, for I have loved ere now.
BOT: Into a thousand that I have forgotten.
USER: Alas, poor shepherd, searching of thy wound, I have by hard adventure found mine own.
BOT: Thou speak'st wiser than thou art ware of.
USER: Nay, I shall ne'er be ware of mine own wit till I break my shins against it.
BOT: Jove, Jove, this shepherd's passion Is much upon my fashion.
USER: And mine, but it grows something stale with me.
BOT: I pray you, one of you question yond man If he for gold will give us any food. I faint almost to death.
USER: Holla, you clown!
BOT: Peace, fool, he's not thy kinsman.
USER: Who calls?
BOT: Your betters, sir.
USER: Else are they very wretched.
BOT: Peace, I say. - Good even to you, friend.
USER: And to you, gentle sir, and to you all.
BOT: I prithee, shepherd, if that love or gold Can in this desert place buy entertainment, Bring us where we may rest ourselves and feed. Here's a young maid with travel much oppressed, And faints for succour.
USER: What is he that shall buy his flock and pasture?
BOT: That young swain that you saw here but erewhile, That little cares for buying anything.
USER: I pray thee, if it stand with honesty, Buy thou the cottage, pasture, and the flock, And thou shalt have to pay for it of us.
BOT: And we will mend thy wages. I like this place, And willingly could waste my time in it.
USER: Assuredly the thing is to be sold. Go with me. If you like upon report The soil, the profit, and this kind of life, I will your very faithful feeder be, And buy it with your gold right suddenly.

USER: Under the greenwood tree, Who loves to lie with me And turn his merry note Unto the sweet bird's throat, Come hither, come hither, come hither! Here shall he see No enemy But winter and rough weather.
BOT: More, more, I prithee, more.
USER: It will make you melancholy, Monsieur Jaques.
BOT: I thank it. More, I prithee, more. I can suck melancholy out of a song as a weasel sucks eggs. More, I prithee, more.
USER: My voice is ragged. I know I cannot please you.
BOT: I do not desire you to please me; I do desire you to sing. Come, more, another _stanzo_. Call you 'em _stanzos?_
USER: What you will, Monsieur Jaques.
BOT: Nay, I care not for their names. They owe me nothing. Will you sing?
USER: More at your request than to please myself.
BOT: Well, I'll end the song. - Sirs, cover the while. The Duke will drink under this tree; he hath been all this day to look you.
USER: And I have been all this day to avoid him. He is too disputable for my company. I think of as many matters as he, but I give heaven thanks and make no boast of them. Come, warble, come.
BOT: Who doth ambition shun And loves to live i' th' sun, Seeking the food he eats And pleased with what he gets, Come hither, come hither, come hither. Here shall he see No enemy But winter and rough weather.
USER: I'll give you a verse to this note that I made yesterday in despite of my invention.
BOT: And I'll sing it.
USER: Thus it goes: If it do come to pass That any man turn ass, Leaving his wealth and ease A stubborn will to please, Ducdame, ducdame, ducdame; Here shall he see Gross fools as he, An if he will come to me.
BOT: What's that "ducdame?"
USER: 'Tis a Greek invocation to call fools into a circle. I'll go sleep if I can; if I cannot, I'll rail against all the first-born of Egypt.
BOT: And I'll go seek the Duke; his banquet is prepared.

USER: I think he be transformed into a beast, For I can nowhere find him like a man.
BOT: My lord, he is but even now gone hence; Here was he merry, hearing of a song.
USER: If he, compact of jars, grow musical, We shall have shortly discord in the spheres. Go seek him, tell him I would speak with him.
BOT: He saves my labour by his own approach.
USER: Why, how now, monsieur? What a life is this That your poor friends must woo your company? What, you look merrily.
BOT: What fool is this?
USER: Thou shalt have one.
BOT: Fie on thee! I can tell what thou wouldst do.
USER: What, for a counter, would I do but good?
BOT: Forbear, and eat no more.
USER: Why, I have eat none yet.
BOT: Nor shalt not till necessity be served.
USER: Of what kind should this cock come of?
BOT: Art thou thus boldened, man, by thy distress? Or else a rude despiser of good manners, That in civility thou seem'st so empty?
USER: An you will not be answered with reason, I must die.
BOT: What would you have? Your gentleness shall force More than your force move us to gentleness.
USER: I almost die for food, and let me have it.
BOT: Sit down and feed, and welcome to our table.
USER: Go find him out, And we will nothing waste till you return.
BOT: I thank ye, and be blest for your good comfort.
USER: Thou seest we are not all alone unhappy. This wide and universal theatre Presents more woeful pageants than the scene Wherein we play in.
BOT: Welcome. Set down your venerable burden, And let him feed.
USER: I thank you most for him.
BOT: So had you need; I scarce can speak to thank you for myself.
USER: Welcome, fall to. I will not trouble you As yet to question you about your fortunes. Give us some music, and good cousin, sing.

USER: O that your highness knew my heart in this: I never loved my brother in my life.
BOT: More villain thou. Well, push him out of doors, And let my officers of such a nature Make an extent upon his house and lands. Do this expediently, and turn him going.

USER: And how like you this shepherd's life, Master Touchstone?
BOT: Such a one is a natural philosopher. Wast ever in court, shepherd?
USER: No, truly.
BOT: Then thou art damned.
USER: Nay, I hope.
BOT: Truly, thou art damned, like an ill-roasted egg, all on one side.
USER: For not being at court? Your reason.
BOT: Why, if thou never wast at court, thou never saw'st good manners; if thou never saw'st good manners, then thy manners must be wicked, and wickedness is sin, and sin is damnation. Thou art in a parlous state, shepherd.
USER: Instance, briefly. Come, instance.
BOT: Why, we are still handling our ewes, and their fells, you know, are greasy.
USER: Why, do not your courtier's hands sweat? And is not the grease of a mutton as wholesome as the sweat of a man? Shallow, shallow. A better instance, I say. Come.
BOT: Besides, our hands are hard.
USER: Your lips will feel them the sooner. Shallow again. A more sounder instance, come.
BOT: And they are often tarred over with the surgery of our sheep; and would you have us kiss tar? The courtier's hands are perfumed with civet.
USER: Most shallow man! Thou worm's meat in respect of a good piece of flesh indeed! Learn of the wise and perpend. Civet is of a baser birth than tar, the very uncleanly flux of a cat. Mend the instance, shepherd.
BOT: You have too courtly a wit for me. I'll rest.
USER: Wilt thou rest damned? God help thee, shallow man! God make incision in thee, thou art raw.
BOT: Here comes young Master Ganymede, my new mistress's brother.
USER: I'll rhyme you so eight years together, dinners and suppers and sleeping hours excepted. It is the right butter-women's rank to market.
BOT: Out, fool!
USER: Peace, you dull fool, I found them on a tree.
BOT: Truly, the tree yields bad fruit.
USER: I'll graft it with you, and then I shall graft it with a medlar. Then it will be the earliest fruit i' th' country, for you'll be rotten ere you be half ripe, and that's the right virtue of the medlar.
BOT: You have said, but whether wisely or no, let the forest judge.
USER: Peace, here comes my sister, reading. Stand aside.
BOT: O most gentle Jupiter, what tedious homily of love have you wearied your parishioners withal, and never cried "Have patience, good people!"
USER: How now! Back, friends. Shepherd, go off a little. Go with him, sirrah.
BOT: Come, shepherd, let us make an honourable retreat, though not with bag and baggage, yet with scrip and scrippage.
USER: Didst thou hear these verses?
BOT: O yes, I heard them all, and more too, for some of them had in them more feet than the verses would bear.
USER: That's no matter. The feet might bear the verses.
BOT: Ay, but the feet were lame and could not bear themselves without the verse, and therefore stood lamely in the verse.
USER: But didst thou hear without wondering how thy name should be hanged and carved upon these trees?
BOT: I was seven of the nine days out of the wonder before you came; for look here what I found on a palm-tree. I was never so berhymed since Pythagoras' time that I was an Irish rat, which I can hardly remember.
USER: Trow you who hath done this?
BOT: Is it a man?
USER: And a chain, that you once wore, about his neck. Change you colour?
BOT: I prithee, who?
USER: O Lord, Lord, it is a hard matter for friends to meet; but mountains may be removed with earthquakes and so encounter.
BOT: Nay, but who is it?
USER: Is it possible?
BOT: Nay, I prithee now, with most petitionary vehemence, tell me who it is.
USER: O wonderful, wonderful, most wonderful wonderful, and yet again wonderful, and after that, out of all whooping!
BOT: So you may put a man in your belly.
USER: Is he of God's making? What manner of man? Is his head worth a hat, or his chin worth a beard?
BOT: Nay, he hath but a little beard.
USER: Why, God will send more if the man will be thankful. Let me stay the growth of his beard, if thou delay me not the knowledge of his chin.
BOT: It is young Orlando, that tripped up the wrestler's heels and your heart both in an instant.
USER: Nay, but the devil take mocking! Speak sad brow and true maid.
BOT: I' faith, coz, 'tis he.
USER: Orlando?
BOT: Orlando.
USER: You must borrow me Gargantua's mouth first. 'Tis a word too great for any mouth of this age's size. To say ay and no to these particulars is more than to answer in a catechism.
BOT: But doth he know that I am in this forest and in man's apparel? Looks he as freshly as he did the day he wrestled?
USER: It is as easy to count atomies as to resolve the propositions of a lover. But take a taste of my finding him, and relish it with good observance. I found him under a tree, like a dropped acorn.
BOT: It may well be called Jove's tree when it drops forth such fruit.
USER: Give me audience, good madam.
BOT: Proceed.
USER: There lay he, stretched along like a wounded knight.
BOT: Though it be pity to see such a sight, it well becomes the ground.
USER: Cry "holla!" to thy tongue, I prithee. It curvets unseasonably. He was furnished like a hunter.
BOT: O, ominous! He comes to kill my heart.
USER: I would sing my song without a burden. Thou bring'st me out of tune.
BOT: Do you not know I am a woman? When I think, I must speak. Sweet, say on.
USER: You bring me out. Soft, comes he not here?
BOT: 'Tis he! Slink by, and note him.
USER: I thank you for your company but, good faith, I had as lief have been myself alone.
BOT: And so had I, but yet, for fashion sake, I thank you too for your society.
USER: God be wi' you, let's meet as little as we can.
BOT: I do desire we may be better strangers.
USER: I pray you, mar no more trees with writing love songs in their barks.
BOT: I pray you, mar no more of my verses with reading them ill-favouredly.
USER: Rosalind is your love's name?
BOT: Yes, just.
USER: I do not like her name.
BOT: There was no thought of pleasing you when she was christened.
USER: What stature is she of?
BOT: Just as high as my heart.
USER: You are full of pretty answers. Have you not been acquainted with goldsmiths' wives, and conned them out of rings?
BOT: Not so; but I answer you right painted cloth, from whence you have studied your questions.
USER: You have a nimble wit. I think 'twas made of Atalanta's heels. Will you sit down with me? And we two will rail against our mistress the world and all our misery.
BOT: I will chide no breather in the world but myself, against whom I know most faults.
USER: The worst fault you have is to be in love.
BOT: 'Tis a fault I will not change for your best virtue. I am weary of you.
USER: By my troth, I was seeking for a fool when I found you.
BOT: He is drowned in the brook. Look but in, and you shall see him.
USER: There I shall see mine own figure.
BOT: Which I take to be either a fool or a cipher.
USER: I'll tarry no longer with you. Farewell, good Signior Love.
BOT: I am glad of your departure. Adieu, good Monsieur Melancholy.
USER: I will speak to him like a saucy lackey, and under that habit play the knave with him. Do you hear, forester?
BOT: Very well. What would you?
USER: I pray you, what is't o'clock?
BOT: You should ask me what time o' day. There's no clock in the forest.
USER: Then there is no true lover in the forest, else sighing every minute and groaning every hour would detect the lazy foot of time as well as a clock.
BOT: And why not the swift foot of time? Had not that been as proper?
USER: By no means, sir. Time travels in divers paces with divers persons. I'll tell you who time ambles withal, who time trots withal, who time gallops withal, and who he stands still withal.
BOT: I prithee, who doth he trot withal?
USER: Marry, he trots hard with a young maid between the contract of her marriage and the day it is solemnized. If the interim be but a se'nnight, time's pace is so hard that it seems the length of seven year.
BOT: Who ambles time withal?
USER: Who doth he gallop withal?
BOT: With a thief to the gallows; for though he go as softly as foot can fall, he thinks himself too soon there.
USER: Who stays it still withal?
BOT: With lawyers in the vacation; for they sleep between term and term, and then they perceive not how time moves.
USER: Where dwell you, pretty youth?
BOT: With this shepherdess, my sister, here in the skirts of the forest, like fringe upon a petticoat.
USER: Are you native of this place?
BOT: As the coney that you see dwell where she is kindled.
USER: Your accent is something finer than you could purchase in so removed a dwelling.
BOT: Can you remember any of the principal evils that he laid to the charge of women?
USER: There were none principal. They were all like one another as halfpence are, every one fault seeming monstrous till his fellow fault came to match it.
BOT: I prithee recount some of them.
USER: I am he that is so love-shaked. I pray you tell me your remedy.
BOT: There is none of my uncle's marks upon you. He taught me how to know a man in love, in which cage of rushes I am sure you are not prisoner.
USER: What were his marks?
BOT: Fair youth, I would I could make thee believe I love.
USER: I swear to thee, youth, by the white hand of Rosalind, I am that he, that unfortunate he.
BOT: But are you so much in love as your rhymes speak?
USER: Neither rhyme nor reason can express how much.
BOT: Did you ever cure any so?
USER: I would not be cured, youth.
BOT: I would cure you, if you would but call me Rosalind and come every day to my cote and woo me.
USER: Now, by the faith of my love, I will. Tell me where it is.
BOT: Go with me to it, and I'll show it you; and by the way you shall tell me where in the forest you live. Will you go?
USER: With all my heart, good youth.
BOT: Nay, you must call me Rosalind. Come, sister, will you go?

USER: Come apace, good Audrey. I will fetch up your goats, Audrey. And how, Audrey? Am I the man yet? Doth my simple feature content you?
BOT: Your features, Lord warrant us! What features?
USER: I am here with thee and thy goats, as the most capricious poet, honest Ovid, was among the Goths.
BOT: house!
USER: I do not know what "poetical" is. Is it honest in deed and word? Is it a true thing?
BOT: No, truly; for the truest poetry is the most feigning, and lovers are given to poetry, and what they swear in poetry may be said, as lovers, they do feign.
USER: Do you wish, then, that the gods had made me poetical?
BOT: I do, truly, for thou swear'st to me thou art honest. Now if thou wert a poet, I might have some hope thou didst feign.
USER: Would you not have me honest?
BOT: No, truly, unless thou wert hard-favoured; for honesty coupled to beauty is to have honey a sauce to sugar.
USER: Well, I am not fair, and therefore I pray the gods make me honest.
BOT: Truly, and to cast away honesty upon a foul slut were to put good meat into an unclean dish.
USER: I am not a slut, though I thank the gods I am foul.
BOT: Well, the gods give us joy!
USER: Is there none here to give the woman?
BOT: I will not take her on gift of any man.
USER: Truly, she must be given, or the marriage is not lawful.
BOT: Good even, good Master What-ye-call't, how do you, sir? You are very well met. God 'ild you for your last company. I am very glad to see you. Even a toy in hand here, sir. Nay, pray be covered.
USER: Will you be married, motley?
BOT: As the ox hath his bow, sir, the horse his curb, and the falcon her bells, so man hath his desires; and as pigeons bill, so wedlock would be nibbling.
USER: than of another, for he is not like to marry me well, and not being well married, it will be a good excuse for me hereafter to leave my wife.
BOT: Go thou with me, and let me counsel thee.
USER: 'Tis no matter. Ne'er a fantastical knave of them all shall flout me out of my calling.

USER: Never talk to me, I will weep.
BOT: Do, I prithee, but yet have the grace to consider that tears do not become a man.
USER: But have I not cause to weep?
BOT: As good cause as one would desire; therefore weep.
USER: His very hair is of the dissembling colour.
BOT: Something browner than Judas's. Marry, his kisses are Judas's own children.
USER: I' faith, his hair is of a good colour.
BOT: An excellent colour. Your chestnut was ever the only colour.
USER: And his kissing is as full of sanctity as the touch of holy bread.
BOT: He hath bought a pair of cast lips of Diana. A nun of winter's sisterhood kisses not more religiously; the very ice of chastity is in them.
USER: But why did he swear he would come this morning, and comes not?
BOT: Nay, certainly, there is no truth in him.
USER: Do you think so?
BOT: Yes. I think he is not a pick-purse nor a horse-stealer, but for his verity in love, I do think him as concave as a covered goblet or a worm-eaten nut.
USER: Not true in love?
BOT: Yes, when he is in, but I think he is not in.
USER: You have heard him swear downright he was.
BOT: "Was" is not "is". Besides, the oath of a lover is no stronger than the word of a tapster. They are both the confirmer of false reckonings. He attends here in the forest on the Duke your father.
USER: I met the Duke yesterday, and had much question with him. He asked me of what parentage I was. I told him, of as good as he, so he laughed and let me go. But what talk we of fathers when there is such a man as Orlando?
BOT: Mistress and master, you have oft enquired After the shepherd that complained of love, Who you saw sitting by me on the turf, Praising the proud disdainful shepherdess That was his mistress.
USER: Well, and what of him?
BOT: If you will see a pageant truly played Between the pale complexion of true love And the red glow of scorn and proud disdain, Go hence a little, and I shall conduct you, If you will mark it.
USER: O, come, let us remove. The sight of lovers feedeth those in love. Bring us to this sight, and you shall say I'll prove a busy actor in their play.
