USER: O dear Phoebe, If ever - as that ever may be near - You meet in some fresh cheek the power of fancy, Then shall you know the wounds invisible That love's keen arrows make.
BOT: But till that time Come not thou near me. And when that time comes, Afflict me with thy mocks, pity me not, As till that time I shall not pity thee.
USER: Sweet youth, I pray you chide a year together! I had rather hear you chide than this man woo.
BOT: He's fall'n in love with your foulness, and she'll fall in love with my anger. If it be so, as fast as she answers thee with frowning looks, I'll sauce her with bitter words. Why look you so upon me?
USER: For no ill will I bear you.
BOT: Dead shepherd, now I find thy saw of might: "Who ever loved that loved not at first sight?"
USER: Sweet Phoebe -
BOT: Ha, what sayst thou, Silvius?
USER: Sweet Phoebe, pity me.
BOT: Why, I am sorry for thee, gentle Silvius.
USER: Wherever sorrow is, relief would be. If you do sorrow at my grief in love, By giving love your sorrow and my grief Were both extermined.
BOT: Thou hast my love. Is not that neighbourly?
USER: I would have you.
BOT: Know'st thou the youth that spoke to me erewhile?
USER: Not very well, but I have met him oft, And he hath bought the cottage and the bounds That the old carlot once was master of.
BOT: Phoebe, with all my heart.
USER: I'll write it straight, The matter's in my head and in my heart. I will be bitter with him and passing short. Go with me, Silvius.

USER: I prithee, pretty youth, let me be better acquainted with thee.
BOT: They say you are a melancholy fellow.
USER: I am so; I do love it better than laughing.
BOT: Those that are in extremity of either are abominable fellows, and betray themselves to every modern censure worse than drunkards.
USER: Why, 'tis good to be sad and say nothing.
BOT: Why then, 'tis good to be a post.
USER: A traveller! By my faith, you have great reason to be sad. I fear you have sold your own lands to see other men's. Then to have seen much and to have nothing is to have rich eyes and poor hands.
BOT: Yes, I have gained my experience.
USER: And your experience makes you sad. I had rather have a fool to make me merry than experience to make me sad - and to travel for it too.
BOT: Good day and happiness, dear Rosalind!
USER: Nay, then, God be wi' you, an you talk in blank verse.
BOT: My fair Rosalind, I come within an hour of my promise.
USER: Pardon me, dear Rosalind.
BOT: Nay, an you be so tardy, come no more in my sight. I had as lief be wooed of a snail.
USER: Of a snail?
BOT: Ay, of a snail, for though he comes slowly, he carries his house on his head - a better jointure, I think, than you make a woman. Besides, he brings his destiny with him.
USER: What's that?
BOT: Why, horns, which such as you are fain to be beholding to your wives for. But he comes armed in his fortune and prevents the slander of his wife.
USER: Virtue is no horn-maker and my Rosalind is virtuous.
BOT: And I am your Rosalind.
USER: It pleases him to call you so, but he hath a Rosalind of a better leer than you.
BOT: Come, woo me, woo me, for now I am in a holiday humour, and like enough to consent. What would you say to me now, an I were your very, very Rosalind?
USER: I would kiss before I spoke.
BOT: How if the kiss be denied?
USER: Then she puts you to entreaty, and there begins new matter.
BOT: Who could be out, being before his beloved mistress?
USER: Marry, that should you, if I were your mistress, or I should think my honesty ranker than my wit.
BOT: What, of my suit?
USER: Not out of your apparel, and yet out of your suit. Am not I your Rosalind?
BOT: I take some joy to say you are because I would be talking of her.
USER: Well, in her person, I say I will not have you.
BOT: Then, in mine own person, I die.
USER: I would not have my right Rosalind of this mind, for I protest her frown might kill me.
BOT: By this hand, it will not kill a fly. But come, now I will be your Rosalind in a more coming-on disposition, and ask me what you will, I will grant it.
USER: Then love me, Rosalind.
BOT: Yes, faith, will I, Fridays and Saturdays and all.
USER: And wilt thou have me?
BOT: Ay, and twenty such.
USER: What sayest thou?
BOT: Are you not good?
USER: I hope so.
BOT: Why then, can one desire too much of a good thing? - Come, sister, you shall be the priest and marry us. - Give me your hand, Orlando. - What do you say, sister?
USER: Pray thee, marry us.
BOT: I cannot say the words.
USER: You must begin "Will you, Orlando - "
BOT: Go to. - Will you, Orlando, have to wife this Rosalind?
USER: I will.
BOT: Ay, but when?
USER: Why now, as fast as she can marry us.
BOT: Then you must say "I take thee, Rosalind, for wife."
USER: I take thee, Rosalind, for wife.
BOT: I might ask you for your commission. But I do take thee, Orlando, for my husband. There's a girl goes before the priest, and certainly a woman's thought runs before her actions.
USER: So do all thoughts. They are winged.
BOT: Now tell me how long you would have her after you have possessed her.
USER: For ever and a day.
BOT: But will my Rosalind do so?
USER: By my life, she will do as I do.
BOT: O, but she is wise.
USER: A man that had a wife with such a wit, he might say, "Wit, whither wilt?"
BOT: Nay, you might keep that check for it till you met your wife's wit going to your neighbour's bed.
USER: And what wit could wit have to excuse that?
BOT: For these two hours, Rosalind, I will leave thee.
USER: Alas, dear love, I cannot lack thee two hours.
BOT: I must attend the Duke at dinner. By two o'clock I will be with thee again.
USER: Ay, sweet Rosalind.
BOT: With no less religion than if thou wert indeed my Rosalind. So, adieu.
USER: Well, Time is the old justice that examines all such offenders, and let time try. Adieu.
BOT: You have simply misused our sex in your love-prate! We must have your doublet and hose plucked over your head and show the world what the bird hath done to her own nest.
USER: O coz, coz, coz, my pretty little coz, that thou didst know how many fathom deep I am in love! But it cannot be sounded; my affection hath an unknown bottom, like the Bay of Portugal.
BOT: Or rather, bottomless, that as fast as you pour affection in, it runs out.
USER: And I'll sleep.

USER: Which is he that killed the deer?
BOT: Sir, it was I.
USER: Let's present him to the Duke, like a Roman conqueror, and it would do well to set the deer's horns upon his head for a branch of victory. Have you no song, forester, for this purpose?
BOT: Yes, sir.
USER: Sing it. 'Tis no matter how it be in tune, so it make noise enough. SONG

USER: How say you now? Is it not past two o'clock? And here much Orlando.
BOT: I warrant you, with pure love and troubled brain he hath ta'en his bow and arrows and is gone forth to sleep. Look who comes here.
USER: No, I protest, I know not the contents. Phoebe did write it.
BOT: Sure, it is hers.
USER: So please you, for I never heard it yet, Yet heard too much of Phoebe's cruelty.
BOT: She Phoebes me. Mark how the tyrant writes. _Art thou god to shepherd turned, That a maiden's heart hath burned?_ Can a woman rail thus?
USER: Call you this railing?
BOT: Call you this chiding?
USER: Alas, poor shepherd.
BOT: Good morrow, fair ones. Pray you, if you know, Where in the purlieus of this forest stands A sheepcote fenced about with olive trees?
USER: West of this place, down in the neighbour bottom; The rank of osiers, by the murmuring stream, Left on your right hand, brings you to the place. But at this hour the house doth keep itself. There's none within.
BOT: It is no boast, being asked, to say we are.
USER: Orlando doth commend him to you both, And to that youth he calls his Rosalind He sends this bloody napkin. Are you he?
BOT: I am. What must we understand by this?
USER: Some of my shame, if you will know of me What man I am, and how, and why, and where This handkerchief was stained.
BOT: I pray you tell it.
USER: O, I have heard him speak of that same brother, And he did render him the most unnatural That lived amongst men.
BOT: And well he might so do, For well I know he was unnatural.
USER: But, to Orlando: did he leave him there, Food to the sucked and hungry lioness?
BOT: Are you his brother?
USER: Was it you he rescued?
BOT: Was't you that did so oft contrive to kill him?
USER: 'Twas I; but 'tis not I. I do not shame To tell you what I was, since my conversion So sweetly tastes, being the thing I am.
BOT: But, for the bloody napkin?
USER: Why, how now, Ganymede, sweet Ganymede!
BOT: Many will swoon when they do look on blood.
USER: There is more in it. Cousin - Ganymede!
BOT: Look, he recovers.
USER: I would I were at home.
BOT: We'll lead you thither. I pray you, will you take him by the arm?
USER: Be of good cheer, youth. You a man? You lack a man's heart.
BOT: I do so, I confess it. Ah, sirrah, a body would think this was well counterfeited. I pray you tell your brother how well I counterfeited. Heigh-ho.
USER: This was not counterfeit. There is too great testimony in your complexion that it was a passion of earnest.
BOT: Counterfeit, I assure you.
USER: Well then, take a good heart, and counterfeit to be a man.
BOT: So I do. But, i' faith, I should have been a woman by right.
USER: Come, you look paler and paler. Pray you draw homewards. Good sir, go with us.
BOT: That will I, for I must bear answer back How you excuse my brother, Rosalind.
USER: I shall devise something. But I pray you commend my counterfeiting to him. Will you go?

USER: We shall find a time, Audrey; patience, gentle Audrey.
BOT: Faith, the priest was good enough, for all the old gentleman's saying.
USER: A most wicked Sir Oliver, Audrey, a most vile Martext. But Audrey, there is a youth here in the forest lays claim to you.
BOT: Ay, I know who 'tis. He hath no interest in me in the world. Here comes the man you mean.
USER: It is meat and drink to me to see a clown. By my troth, we that have good wits have much to answer for. We shall be flouting; we cannot hold.
BOT: Good ev'n, Audrey.
USER: God ye good ev'n, William.
BOT: And good ev'n to you, sir.
USER: Good ev'n, gentle friend. Cover thy head, cover thy head. Nay, prithee, be covered. How old are you, friend?
BOT: Five-and-twenty, sir.
USER: A ripe age. Is thy name William?
BOT: William, sir.
USER: A fair name. Wast born i' th' forest here?
BOT: Ay, sir, I thank God.
USER: "Thank God." A good answer. Art rich?
BOT: Faith, sir, so-so.
USER: "So-so" is good, very good, very excellent good. And yet it is not, it is but so-so. Art thou wise?
BOT: Ay, sir, I have a pretty wit.
USER: I do, sir.
BOT: Give me your hand. Art thou learned?
USER: No, sir.
BOT: Which he, sir?
USER: Do, good William.
BOT: God rest you merry, sir.
USER: Our master and mistress seek you. Come away, away.
BOT: Trip, Audrey, trip, Audrey! I attend, I attend.

USER: Is't possible that on so little acquaintance you should like her? That but seeing, you should love her? And loving woo? And wooing, she should grant? And will you persever to enjoy her?
BOT: You have my consent. Let your wedding be tomorrow. Thither will I invite the Duke and all's contented followers. Go you and prepare Aliena; for, look you, here comes my Rosalind.
USER: God save you, brother.
BOT: And you, fair sister.
USER: O my dear Orlando, how it grieves me to see thee wear thy heart in a scarf!
BOT: It is my arm.
USER: I thought thy heart had been wounded with the claws of a lion.
BOT: Wounded it is, but with the eyes of a lady.
USER: Did your brother tell you how I counterfeited to swoon when he showed me your handkercher?
BOT: Ay, and greater wonders than that.
USER: Why, then, tomorrow I cannot serve your turn for Rosalind?
BOT: I can live no longer by thinking.
USER: Speak'st thou in sober meanings?
BOT: Youth, you have done me much ungentleness To show the letter that I writ to you.
USER: I care not if I have; it is my study To seem despiteful and ungentle to you. You are there followed by a faithful shepherd. Look upon him, love him; he worships you.
BOT: Good shepherd, tell this youth what 'tis to love.
USER: It is to be all made of sighs and tears, And so am I for Phoebe.
BOT: And I for Ganymede.
USER: And I for Rosalind.
BOT: And I for no woman.
USER: It is to be all made of faith and service, And so am I for Phoebe.
BOT: And I for Ganymede.
USER: And I for Rosalind.
BOT: And I for no woman.
USER: And so am I for Ganymede.
BOT: And so am I for Rosalind.
USER: And so am I for no woman.
BOT: If this be so, why blame you me to love you?
USER: Why do you speak too, "Why blame you me to love you?"
BOT: To her that is not here, nor doth not hear.
USER: I'll not fail, if I live.
BOT: Nor I.
USER: Nor I.

USER: Tomorrow is the joyful day, Audrey, tomorrow will we be married.
BOT: I do desire it with all my heart; and I hope it is no dishonest desire to desire to be a woman of the world. Here come two of the banished Duke's pages.
USER: Well met, honest gentleman.
BOT: By my troth, well met. Come sit, sit, and a song.
USER: We are for you, sit i' th' middle.
BOT: Shall we clap into't roundly, without hawking or spitting or saying we are hoarse, which are the only prologues to a bad voice?
USER: I'faith, i'faith, and both in a tune like two gipsies on a horse. SONG
BOT: You are deceived, sir, we kept time, we lost not our time.
USER: By my troth, yes. I count it but time lost to hear such a foolish song. God be wi' you, and God mend your voices. Come, Audrey.

USER: Dost thou believe, Orlando, that the boy Can do all this that he hath promised?
BOT: I sometimes do believe and sometimes do not, As those that fear they hope, and know they fear.
USER: Patience once more whiles our compact is urged. You will bestow her on Orlando here?
BOT: That would I, had I kingdoms to give with her.
USER: That would I, were I of all kingdoms king.
BOT: That will I, should I die the hour after.
USER: But if you do refuse to marry me, You'll give yourself to this most faithful shepherd?
BOT: So is the bargain.
USER: Though to have her and death were both one thing.
BOT: I do remember in this shepherd boy Some lively touches of my daughter's favour.
USER: There is sure another flood toward, and these couples are coming to the ark. Here comes a pair of very strange beasts, which in all tongues are called fools.
BOT: Salutation and greeting to you all.
USER: Good my lord, bid him welcome. This is the motley-minded gentleman that I have so often met in the forest. He hath been a courtier, he swears.
BOT: And how was that ta'en up?
USER: Faith, we met, and found the quarrel was upon the seventh cause.
BOT: How seventh cause? - Good my lord, like this fellow?
USER: I like him very well.
BOT: By my faith, he is very swift and sententious.
USER: According to the fool's bolt, sir, and such dulcet diseases.
BOT: But, for the seventh cause. How did you find the quarrel on the seventh cause?
USER: And how oft did you say his beard was not well cut?
BOT: I durst go no further than the lie circumstantial, nor he durst not give me the lie direct; and so we measured swords and parted.
USER: Can you nominate in order now the degrees of the lie?
BOT: Is not this a rare fellow, my lord? He's as good at anything, and yet a fool.
USER: He uses his folly like a stalking-horse, and under the presentation of that he shoots his wit.
BOT: If there be truth in sight, you are my daughter.
USER: If there be truth in sight, you are my Rosalind.
BOT: If sight and shape be true, Why then, my love adieu.
USER: O my dear niece, welcome thou art to me Even daughter, welcome in no less degree.
BOT: Thy faith my fancy to thee doth combine.
USER: Sir, by your patience. If I heard you rightly, The Duke hath put on a religious life And thrown into neglect the pompous court.
BOT: He hath.
USER: Stay, Jaques, stay.
BOT: To see no pastime, I. What you would have I'll stay to know at your abandoned cave.
USER: Proceed, proceed! We will begin these rites, As we do trust they'll end, in true delights.

USER: Proceed, Solinus, to procure my fall, And by the doom of death end woes and all.
BOT: Yet this my comfort; when your words are done, My woes end likewise with the evening sun.
USER: Well, Syracusian, say in brief the cause Why thou departedst from thy native home, And for what cause thou cam'st to Ephesus.
BOT: Nay, forward, old man, do not break off so, For we may pity, though not pardon thee.
USER: And for the sake of them thou sorrowest for, Do me the favour to dilate at full What have befall'n of them and thee till now.
BOT: I will, my lord.
USER: Hopeless and helpless doth Egeon wend, But to procrastinate his lifeless end.

USER: Many a man would take you at your word, And go indeed, having so good a mean.
BOT: A trusty villain, sir, that very oft, When I am dull with care and melancholy, Lightens my humour with his merry jests. What, will you walk with me about the town, And then go to my inn and dine with me?
USER: Farewell till then: I will go lose myself, And wander up and down to view the city.
BOT: Sir, I commend you to your own content.
USER: Stop in your wind, sir, tell me this, I pray: Where have you left the money that I gave you?
BOT: O, sixpence that I had o' Wednesday last To pay the saddler for my mistress' crupper: The saddler had it, sir, I kept it not.
USER: I am not in a sportive humour now. Tell me, and dally not, where is the money? We being strangers here, how dar'st thou trust So great a charge from thine own custody?
BOT: Come, Dromio, come, these jests are out of season, Reserve them till a merrier hour than this. Where is the gold I gave in charge to thee?
USER: To me, sir? why, you gave no gold to me!
BOT: Come on, sir knave, have done your foolishness, And tell me how thou hast dispos'd thy charge.
USER: My charge was but to fetch you from the mart Home to your house, the Phoenix, sir, to dinner. My mistress and her sister stay for you.
BOT: Now, as I am a Christian, answer me In what safe place you have bestow'd my money, Or I shall break that merry sconce of yours That stands on tricks when I am undispos'd; Where is the thousand marks thou hadst of me?
USER: I have some marks of yours upon my pate, Some of my mistress' marks upon my shoulders, But not a thousand marks between you both. If I should pay your worship those again, Perchance you will not bear them patiently.
BOT: Thy mistress' marks? what mistress, slave, hast thou?
USER: Your worship's wife, my mistress at the Phoenix; She that doth fast till you come home to dinner, And prays that you will hie you home to dinner.
BOT: What, wilt thou flout me thus unto my face, Being forbid? There, take you that, sir knave.
USER: What mean you, sir? for God's sake hold your hands. Nay, an you will not, sir, I'll take my heels.

USER: Neither my husband nor the slave return'd That in such haste I sent to seek his master? Sure, Luciana, it is two o'clock.
BOT: Why should their liberty than ours be more?
USER: Because their business still lies out o' door.
BOT: Look when I serve him so, he takes it ill.
USER: O, know he is the bridle of your will.
BOT: There's none but asses will be bridled so.
USER: This servitude makes you to keep unwed.
BOT: Not this, but troubles of the marriage-bed.
USER: But, were you wedded, you would bear some sway.
BOT: Ere I learn love, I'll practise to obey.
USER: How if your husband start some other where?
BOT: Till he come home again, I would forbear.
USER: Well, I will marry one day, but to try. Here comes your man, now is your husband nigh.
BOT: Say, is your tardy master now at hand?
USER: Nay, he's at two hands with me, and that my two ears can witness.
BOT: Say, didst thou speak with him? know'st thou his mind?
USER: Ay, ay, he told his mind upon mine ear. Beshrew his hand, I scarce could understand it.
BOT: Spake he so doubtfully thou couldst not feel his meaning?
USER: Nay, he struck so plainly I could too well feel his blows; and withal so doubtfully that I could scarce understand them.
BOT: But say, I prithee, is he coming home? It seems he hath great care to please his wife.
USER: Why, mistress, sure my master is horn-mad.
BOT: Horn-mad, thou villain?
USER: Quoth who?
BOT: Quoth my master. "I know," quoth he, "no house, no wife, no mistress." So that my errand, due unto my tongue, I thank him, I bare home upon my shoulders; For, in conclusion, he did beat me there.
USER: Go back again, thou slave, and fetch him home.
BOT: Go back again, and be new beaten home? For God's sake, send some other messenger.
USER: Back slave, or I will break thy pate across.
BOT: And he will bless that cross with other beating. Between you I shall have a holy head.
USER: Hence, prating peasant. Fetch thy master home.
BOT: Am I so round with you, as you with me, That like a football you do spurn me thus? You spurn me hence, and he will spurn me hither. If I last in this service, you must case me in leather.
USER: Fie, how impatience loureth in your face.
BOT: Self-harming jealousy! fie, beat it hence.
USER: How many fond fools serve mad jealousy!

USER: What answer, sir? when spake I such a word?
BOT: Even now, even here, not half an hour since.
USER: I did not see you since you sent me hence, Home to the Centaur with the gold you gave me.
BOT: Villain, thou didst deny the gold's receipt, And told'st me of a mistress and a dinner, For which I hope thou felt'st I was displeas'd.
USER: I am glad to see you in this merry vein. What means this jest, I pray you, master, tell me?
BOT: Yea, dost thou jeer and flout me in the teeth? Think'st thou I jest? Hold, take thou that, and that.
USER: Hold, sir, for God's sake, now your jest is earnest. Upon what bargain do you give it me?
BOT: Dost thou not know?
USER: Nothing, sir, but that I am beaten.
BOT: Shall I tell you why?
USER: Ay, sir, and wherefore; for they say, every why hath a wherefore.
BOT: Why, first, for flouting me; and then wherefore, For urging it the second time to me.
USER: Was there ever any man thus beaten out of season, When in the why and the wherefore is neither rhyme nor reason? Well, sir, I thank you.
BOT: Thank me, sir, for what?
USER: Marry, sir, for this something that you gave me for nothing.
BOT: I'll make you amends next, to give you nothing for something. But say, sir, is it dinner-time?
USER: No, sir; I think the meat wants that I have.
BOT: In good time, sir, what's that?
USER: Basting.
BOT: Well, sir, then 'twill be dry.
USER: If it be, sir, I pray you eat none of it.
BOT: Your reason?
USER: Lest it make you choleric, and purchase me another dry basting.
BOT: Well, sir, learn to jest in good time. There's a time for all things.
USER: I durst have denied that before you were so choleric.
BOT: By what rule, sir?
USER: Marry, sir, by a rule as plain as the plain bald pate of Father Time himself.
BOT: Let's hear it.
USER: There's no time for a man to recover his hair that grows bald by nature.
BOT: May he not do it by fine and recovery?
USER: Yes, to pay a fine for a periwig, and recover the lost hair of another man.
BOT: Why is Time such a niggard of hair, being, as it is, so plentiful an excrement?
USER: Because it is a blessing that he bestows on beasts, and what he hath scanted men in hair he hath given them in wit.
BOT: Why, but there's many a man hath more hair than wit.
USER: Not a man of those but he hath the wit to lose his hair.
BOT: Why, thou didst conclude hairy men plain dealers without wit.
USER: The plainer dealer, the sooner lost. Yet he loseth it in a kind of jollity.
BOT: For what reason?
USER: For two, and sound ones too.
BOT: Nay, not sound, I pray you.
USER: Sure ones, then.
BOT: Nay, not sure, in a thing falsing.
USER: Certain ones, then.
BOT: Name them.
USER: The one, to save the money that he spends in tiring; the other, that at dinner they should not drop in his porridge.
BOT: You would all this time have proved there is no time for all things.
USER: Marry, and did, sir; namely, e'en no time to recover hair lost by nature.
BOT: But your reason was not substantial why there is no time to recover.
USER: Thus I mend it: Time himself is bald, and therefore, to the world's end will have bald followers.
BOT: I knew 'twould be a bald conclusion. But soft! who wafts us yonder?
USER: Plead you to me, fair dame? I know you not. In Ephesus I am but two hours old, As strange unto your town as to your talk, Who, every word by all my wit being scann'd, Wants wit in all one word to understand.
BOT: Fie, brother, how the world is chang'd with you. When were you wont to use my sister thus? She sent for you by Dromio home to dinner.
USER: By Dromio?
BOT: By me?
USER: By thee; and this thou didst return from him, That he did buffet thee, and in his blows Denied my house for his, me for his wife.
BOT: Did you converse, sir, with this gentlewoman? What is the course and drift of your compact?
USER: I, sir? I never saw her till this time.
BOT: Villain, thou liest, for even her very words Didst thou deliver to me on the mart.
USER: I never spake with her in all my life.
BOT: How can she thus, then, call us by our names? Unless it be by inspiration.
USER: Dromio, go bid the servants spread for dinner.
BOT: O, for my beads! I cross me for a sinner. This is the fairy land; O spite of spites! We talk with goblins, owls, and sprites; If we obey them not, this will ensue: They'll suck our breath, or pinch us black and blue.
USER: Why prat'st thou to thyself, and answer'st not? Dromio, thou drone, thou snail, thou slug, thou sot.
BOT: I am transformed, master, am I not?
USER: I think thou art in mind, and so am I.
BOT: Nay, master, both in mind and in my shape.
USER: Thou hast thine own form.
BOT: No, I am an ape.
USER: If thou art chang'd to aught, 'tis to an ass.
BOT: 'Tis true; she rides me, and I long for grass. 'Tis so, I am an ass; else it could never be But I should know her as well as she knows me.
USER: Am I in earth, in heaven, or in hell? Sleeping or waking, mad, or well-advis'd? Known unto these, and to myself disguis'd! I'll say as they say, and persever so, And in this mist at all adventures go.
BOT: Master, shall I be porter at the gate?
USER: Ay; and let none enter, lest I break your pate.
BOT: Come, come, Antipholus, we dine too late.

USER: Say what you will, sir, but I know what I know. That you beat me at the mart I have your hand to show; If the skin were parchment, and the blows you gave were ink, Your own handwriting would tell you what I think.
BOT: I think thou art an ass.
USER: Marry, so it doth appear By the wrongs I suffer and the blows I bear. I should kick, being kick'd; and being at that pass, You would keep from my heels, and beware of an ass.
BOT: You're sad, Signior Balthasar; pray God our cheer May answer my good will and your good welcome here.
USER: I hold your dainties cheap, sir, and your welcome dear.
BOT: O, Signior Balthasar, either at flesh or fish A table full of welcome makes scarce one dainty dish.
USER: Good meat, sir, is common; that every churl affords.
BOT: And welcome more common, for that's nothing but words. BALTHASAR Small cheer and great welcome makes a merry feast.
USER: Ay, to a niggardly host and more sparing guest. But though my cates be mean, take them in good part; Better cheer may you have, but not with better heart. But soft; my door is lock'd. Go bid them let us in.
BOT: Maud, Bridget, Marian, Cicely, Gillian, Ginn!
USER: Either get thee from the door or sit down at the hatch: Dost thou conjure for wenches, that thou call'st for such store When one is one too many? Go, get thee from the door.
BOT: What patch is made our porter? My master stays in the street.
USER: Let him walk from whence he came, lest he catch cold on's feet.
BOT: Who talks within there? Ho, open the door.
USER: Right, sir, I'll tell you when an you'll tell me wherefore.
BOT: Wherefore? For my dinner. I have not dined today.
USER: Nor today here you must not; come again when you may.
BOT: What art thou that keep'st me out from the house I owe?
USER: The porter for this time, sir, and my name is Dromio.
BOT: Let my master in, Luce.
USER: Faith, no, he comes too late, And so tell your master.
BOT: O Lord, I must laugh; Have at you with a proverb: - Shall I set in my staff?
USER: Have at you with another: that's - When? can you tell?
BOT: If thy name be called Luce, - Luce, thou hast answer'd him well.
USER: Do you hear, you minion? you'll let us in, I hope?
BOT: I thought to have ask'd you.
USER: And you said no.
BOT: So, come, help. Well struck, there was blow for blow.
USER: Thou baggage, let me in.
BOT: Can you tell for whose sake?
USER: Master, knock the door hard.
BOT: Let him knock till it ache.
USER: You'll cry for this, minion, if I beat the door down.
BOT: What needs all that, and a pair of stocks in the town?
USER: By my troth, your town is troubled with unruly boys.
BOT: Are you there, wife? you might have come before.
USER: Your wife, sir knave? go, get you from the door.
BOT: If you went in pain, master, this knave would go sore.
USER: Here is neither cheer, sir, nor welcome. We would fain have either.
BOT: In debating which was best, we shall part with neither.
USER: They stand at the door, master; bid them welcome hither.
BOT: There is something in the wind, that we cannot get in.
USER: You would say so, master, if your garments were thin. Your cake here is warm within; you stand here in the cold. It would make a man mad as a buck to be so bought and sold.
BOT: Go, fetch me something, I'll break ope the gate.
USER: Break any breaking here, and I'll break your knave's pate.
BOT: A man may break a word with you, sir, and words are but wind; Ay, and break it in your face, so he break it not behind.
USER: It seems thou want'st breaking; out upon thee, hind!
BOT: Here's too much "out upon thee"; I pray thee, let me in.
USER: Ay, when fowls have no feathers and fish have no fin.
BOT: Well, I'll break in; go, borrow me a crow.
USER: A crow without feather; master, mean you so? For a fish without a fin, there's a fowl without a feather. If a crow help us in, sirrah, we'll pluck a crow together.
BOT: Go, get thee gone; fetch me an iron crow.
USER: I'll meet you at that place some hour hence.
BOT: Do so; this jest shall cost me some expense.

USER: What, are you mad, that you do reason so?
BOT: Not mad, but mated; how, I do not know.
USER: It is a fault that springeth from your eye.
BOT: For gazing on your beams, fair sun, being by.
USER: Gaze where you should, and that will clear your sight.
BOT: As good to wink, sweet love, as look on night.
USER: Why call you me love? Call my sister so.
BOT: Thy sister's sister.
USER: That's my sister.
BOT: No, It is thyself, mine own self's better part, Mine eye's clear eye, my dear heart's dearer heart, My food, my fortune, and my sweet hope's aim, My sole earth's heaven, and my heaven's claim.
USER: All this my sister is, or else should be.
BOT: Call thyself sister, sweet, for I aim thee; Thee will I love, and with thee lead my life; Thou hast no husband yet, nor I no wife. Give me thy hand.
USER: O, soft, sir, hold you still; I'll fetch my sister to get her goodwill.
BOT: Why, how now, Dromio? where runn'st thou so fast?
USER: Do you know me, sir? Am I Dromio? Am I your man? Am I myself?
BOT: Thou art Dromio, thou art my man, thou art thyself.
USER: I am an ass, I am a woman's man, and besides myself.
BOT: What woman's man? and how besides thyself?
USER: Marry, sir, besides myself, I am due to a woman, one that claims me, one that haunts me, one that will have me.
BOT: What claim lays she to thee?
USER: Marry, sir, such claim as you would lay to your horse, and she would have me as a beast; not that I being a beast she would have me, but that she being a very beastly creature lays claim to me.
BOT: What is she?
USER: A very reverent body; ay, such a one as a man may not speak of without he say "sir-reverence". I have but lean luck in the match, and yet is she a wondrous fat marriage.
BOT: How dost thou mean a "fat marriage"?
USER: What complexion is she of?
BOT: Swart like my shoe, but her face nothing like so clean kept. For why? she sweats, a man may go overshoes in the grime of it.
USER: That's a fault that water will mend.
BOT: No, sir, 'tis in grain; Noah's flood could not do it.
USER: What's her name?
BOT: Nell, sir; but her name and three quarters, that's an ell and three quarters, will not measure her from hip to hip.
USER: Then she bears some breadth?
BOT: No longer from head to foot than from hip to hip. She is spherical, like a globe. I could find out countries in her.
USER: In what part of her body stands Ireland?
BOT: Marry, sir, in her buttocks; I found it out by the bogs.
USER: Where Scotland?
BOT: I found it by the barrenness, hard in the palm of the hand.
USER: Where France?
BOT: In her forehead; armed and reverted, making war against her hair.
USER: Where England?
BOT: I looked for the chalky cliffs, but I could find no whiteness in them. But I guess it stood in her chin, by the salt rheum that ran between France and it.
USER: Where Spain?
BOT: Faith, I saw it not; but I felt it hot in her breath.
USER: Where America, the Indies?
BOT: O, sir, upon her nose, all o'er-embellished with rubies, carbuncles, sapphires, declining their rich aspect to the hot breath of Spain, who sent whole armadoes of carracks to be ballast at her nose.
USER: Where stood Belgia, the Netherlands?
BOT: As from a bear a man would run for life, So fly I from her that would be my wife.
USER: Master Antipholus.
BOT: Ay, that's my name.
USER: I know it well, sir. Lo, here is the chain; I thought to have ta'en you at the Porpentine, The chain unfinish'd made me stay thus long.
BOT: What is your will that I shall do with this?
USER: What please yourself, sir; I have made it for you.
BOT: Made it for me, sir! I bespoke it not.
USER: Not once, nor twice, but twenty times you have. Go home with it, and please your wife withal, And soon at supper-time I'll visit you, And then receive my money for the chain.
BOT: I pray you, sir, receive the money now, For fear you ne'er see chain nor money more.
USER: You are a merry man, sir; fare you well.

USER: That labour may you save. See where he comes.
BOT: I buy a thousand pound a year! I buy a rope!
USER: Then you will bring the chain to her yourself.
BOT: No, bear it with you, lest I come not time enough.
USER: Well, sir, I will. Have you the chain about you?
BOT: And if I have not, sir, I hope you have, Or else you may return without your money.
USER: Nay, come, I pray you, sir, give me the chain; Both wind and tide stays for this gentleman, And I, to blame, have held him here too long.
BOT: Good Lord, you use this dalliance to excuse Your breach of promise to the Porpentine. I should have chid you for not bringing it, But, like a shrew, you first begin to brawl.
USER: The hour steals on; I pray you, sir, dispatch.
BOT: You hear how he importunes me. The chain!
USER: Why, give it to my wife, and fetch your money.
BOT: Come, come, you know I gave it you even now. Either send the chain or send by me some token.
USER: Fie, now you run this humour out of breath. Come, where's the chain? I pray you, let me see it.
BOT: My business cannot brook this dalliance. Good sir, say whe'er you'll answer me or no; If not, I'll leave him to the officer.
USER: I answer you? What should I answer you?
BOT: The money that you owe me for the chain.
USER: I owe you none till I receive the chain.
BOT: You know I gave it you half an hour since.
USER: You gave me none. You wrong me much to say so.
BOT: You wrong me more, sir, in denying it. Consider how it stands upon my credit.
USER: Well, officer, arrest him at my suit.
BOT: I do, and charge you in the duke's name to obey me.
USER: This touches me in reputation. Either consent to pay this sum for me, Or I attach you by this officer.
BOT: Consent to pay thee that I never had? Arrest me, foolish fellow, if thou dar'st.
USER: Here is thy fee; arrest him, officer. I would not spare my brother in this case If he should scorn me so apparently.
BOT: I do arrest you, sir. You hear the suit.
USER: I do obey thee till I give thee bail. But, sirrah, you shall buy this sport as dear As all the metal in your shop will answer.
BOT: Sir, sir, I shall have law in Ephesus, To your notorious shame, I doubt it not.
USER: How now? a madman? Why, thou peevish sheep, What ship of Epidamnum stays for me?
BOT: A ship you sent me to, to hire waftage.
USER: Thou drunken slave, I sent thee for a rope, And told thee to what purpose and what end.
BOT: You sent me for a rope's end as soon. You sent me to the bay, sir, for a bark.
USER: To Adriana, that is where we din'd, Where Dowsabel did claim me for her husband. She is too big, I hope, for me to compass. Thither I must, although against my will, For servants must their masters' minds fulfil.

USER: First he denied you had in him no right.
BOT: He meant he did me none; the more my spite.
USER: Then swore he that he was a stranger here.
BOT: And true he swore, though yet forsworn he were.
USER: Then pleaded I for you.
BOT: And what said he?
USER: That love I begg'd for you he begg'd of me.
BOT: With what persuasion did he tempt thy love?
USER: With words that in an honest suit might move. First he did praise my beauty, then my speech.
BOT: Did'st speak him fair?
USER: Have patience, I beseech.
BOT: Who would be jealous then of such a one? No evil lost is wail'd when it is gone.
USER: Ah, but I think him better than I say, And yet would herein others' eyes were worse: Far from her nest the lapwing cries away; My heart prays for him, though my tongue do curse.
BOT: Here, go; the desk, the purse, sweet now, make haste.
USER: How hast thou lost thy breath?
BOT: By running fast.
USER: Where is thy master, Dromio? is he well?
BOT: Why, man, what is the matter?
USER: I do not know the matter. He is 'rested on the case.
BOT: What, is he arrested? Tell me at whose suit?
USER: I know not at whose suit he is arrested, well; But he's in a suit of buff which 'rested him, that can I tell. Will you send him, mistress, redemption, the money in his desk?
BOT: Go fetch it, sister. This I wonder at, Thus he unknown to me should be in debt. Tell me, was he arrested on a band?
USER: Not on a band, but on a stronger thing; A chain, a chain. Do you not hear it ring?
BOT: What, the chain?
USER: No, no, the bell, 'tis time that I were gone. It was two ere I left him, and now the clock strikes one.
BOT: The hours come back! That did I never hear.
USER: O yes, if any hour meet a sergeant, 'a turns back for very fear.
BOT: As if time were in debt. How fondly dost thou reason!
USER: Go, Dromio, there's the money, bear it straight, And bring thy master home immediately. Come, sister, I am press'd down with conceit; Conceit, my comfort and my injury.

USER: Master, here's the gold you sent me for. What, have you got the picture of old Adam new apparelled?
BOT: What gold is this? What Adam dost thou mean?
USER: I understand thee not.
BOT: What! thou mean'st an officer?
USER: Ay, sir, the sergeant of the band; he that brings any man to answer it that breaks his band; one that thinks a man always going to bed, and says, "God give you good rest."
BOT: Well, sir, there rest in your foolery. Is there any ship puts forth tonight? may we be gone?
USER: Why, sir, I brought you word an hour since that the bark _Expedition_ put forth tonight, and then were you hindered by the sergeant to tarry for the hoy _Delay_. Here are the angels that you sent for to deliver you.
BOT: The fellow is distract, and so am I, And here we wander in illusions. Some blessed power deliver us from hence!
USER: Well met, well met, Master Antipholus. I see, sir, you have found the goldsmith now. Is that the chain you promis'd me today?
BOT: Satan, avoid! I charge thee, tempt me not.
USER: Master, is this Mistress Satan?
BOT: It is the devil.
USER: Your man and you are marvellous merry, sir. Will you go with me? We'll mend our dinner here.
BOT: Master, if you do, expect spoon-meat, or bespeak a long spoon.
USER: Why, Dromio?
BOT: Marry, he must have a long spoon that must eat with the devil.
USER: Avoid then, fiend! What tell'st thou me of supping? Thou art, as you are all, a sorceress. I conjure thee to leave me and be gone.
BOT: Give me the ring of mine you had at dinner, Or, for my diamond, the chain you promis'd, And I'll be gone, sir, and not trouble you.
USER: I pray you, sir, my ring, or else the chain; I hope you do not mean to cheat me so.
BOT: Avaunt, thou witch! Come, Dromio, let us go.
USER: Fly pride, says the peacock. Mistress, that you know.

USER: Here's that, I warrant you, will pay them all.
BOT: But where's the money?
USER: Why, sir, I gave the money for the rope.
BOT: Five hundred ducats, villain, for a rope?
USER: I'll serve you, sir, five hundred at the rate.
BOT: To what end did I bid thee hie thee home?
USER: To a rope's end, sir; and to that end am I return'd.
BOT: And to that end, sir, I will welcome you.
USER: Good sir, be patient.
BOT: Nay, 'tis for me to be patient. I am in adversity.
USER: Good now, hold thy tongue.
BOT: Nay, rather persuade him to hold his hands.
USER: Thou whoreson, senseless villain.
BOT: I would I were senseless, sir, that I might not feel your blows.
USER: Thou art sensible in nothing but blows, and so is an ass.
BOT: Come, go along, my wife is coming yonder.
USER: Mistress, _respice finem_, respect your end, or rather, the prophesy like the parrot, "Beware the rope's end."
BOT: Wilt thou still talk?
USER: How say you now? Is not your husband mad?
BOT: His incivility confirms no less. Good Doctor Pinch, you are a conjurer; Establish him in his true sense again, And I will please you what you will demand.
USER: Alas, how fiery and how sharp he looks!
BOT: Mark how he trembles in his ecstasy.
USER: Give me your hand, and let me feel your pulse.
BOT: There is my hand, and let it feel your ear.
USER: I charge thee, Satan, hous'd within this man, To yield possession to my holy prayers, And to thy state of darkness hie thee straight. I conjure thee by all the saints in heaven.
BOT: Peace, doting wizard, peace; I am not mad.
USER: O, that thou wert not, poor distressed soul!
BOT: You minion, you, are these your customers? Did this companion with the saffron face Revel and feast it at my house today, Whilst upon me the guilty doors were shut, And I denied to enter in my house?
USER: O husband, God doth know you din'd at home, Where would you had remain'd until this time, Free from these slanders and this open shame.
BOT: Din'd at home? Thou villain, what sayest thou?
USER: Sir, sooth to say, you did not dine at home.
BOT: Were not my doors lock'd up and I shut out?
USER: Perdy, your doors were lock'd, and you shut out.
BOT: And did not she herself revile me there?
USER: Sans fable, she herself revil'd you there.
BOT: Did not her kitchen-maid rail, taunt, and scorn me?
USER: Certes, she did, the kitchen-vestal scorn'd you.
BOT: And did not I in rage depart from thence?
USER: In verity, you did; my bones bear witness, That since have felt the vigour of his rage.
BOT: Is't good to soothe him in these contraries?
USER: It is no shame; the fellow finds his vein, And yielding to him, humours well his frenzy.
BOT: Thou hast suborn'd the goldsmith to arrest me.
USER: Alas! I sent you money to redeem you By Dromio here, who came in haste for it.
BOT: Money by me? Heart and goodwill you might, But surely, master, not a rag of money.
USER: Went'st not thou to her for a purse of ducats?
BOT: He came to me, and I deliver'd it.
USER: And I am witness with her that she did.
BOT: God and the rope-maker bear me witness That I was sent for nothing but a rope.
USER: Mistress, both man and master is possess'd, I know it by their pale and deadly looks. They must be bound and laid in some dark room.
BOT: Say, wherefore didst thou lock me forth today, And why dost thou deny the bag of gold?
USER: I did not, gentle husband, lock thee forth.
BOT: And gentle master, I receiv'd no gold; But I confess, sir, that we were lock'd out.
USER: Dissembling villain, thou speak'st false in both.
BOT: Dissembling harlot, thou art false in all, And art confederate with a damned pack To make a loathsome abject scorn of me. But with these nails I'll pluck out these false eyes That would behold in me this shameful sport.
USER: O, bind him, bind him; let him not come near me.
BOT: More company; the fiend is strong within him.
USER: Ay me, poor man, how pale and wan he looks!
BOT: What, will you murder me? Thou jailer, thou, I am thy prisoner. Wilt thou suffer them To make a rescue?
USER: Masters, let him go. He is my prisoner, and you shall not have him.
BOT: Go, bind this man, for he is frantic too.
USER: What wilt thou do, thou peevish officer? Hast thou delight to see a wretched man Do outrage and displeasure to himself?
BOT: He is my prisoner. If I let him go, The debt he owes will be requir'd of me.
USER: I will discharge thee ere I go from thee; Bear me forthwith unto his creditor, And knowing how the debt grows, I will pay it. Good master doctor, see him safe convey'd Home to my house. O most unhappy day!
BOT: O most unhappy strumpet!
USER: Master, I am here enter'd in bond for you.
BOT: Out on thee, villain! wherefore dost thou mad me?
USER: Will you be bound for nothing? Be mad, good master; cry, "the devil".
BOT: God help, poor souls, how idly do they talk!
USER: Go bear him hence. Sister, go you with me. of Ephesus._] Say now, whose suit is he arrested at?
BOT: One Angelo, a goldsmith; do you know him?
USER: I know the man. What is the sum he owes?
BOT: Two hundred ducats.
USER: Say, how grows it due?
BOT: Due for a chain your husband had of him.
USER: He did bespeak a chain for me, but had it not.
BOT: When as your husband, all in rage, today Came to my house and took away my ring, The ring I saw upon his finger now, Straight after did I meet him with a chain.
USER: It may be so, but I did never see it. Come, jailer, bring me where the goldsmith is, I long to know the truth hereof at large. Syracuse.
BOT: God, for thy mercy, they are loose again!
USER: And come with naked swords. Let's call more help To have them bound again.
BOT: Away, they'll kill us.
USER: I see these witches are afraid of swords.
BOT: She that would be your wife now ran from you.
USER: Come to the Centaur, fetch our stuff from thence. I long that we were safe and sound aboard.
BOT: I will not stay tonight for all the town; Therefore away, to get our stuff aboard.

USER: I am sorry, sir, that I have hinder'd you, But I protest he had the chain of me, Though most dishonestly he doth deny it.
BOT: How is the man esteem'd here in the city?
USER: Of very reverend reputation, sir, Of credit infinite, highly belov'd, Second to none that lives here in the city. His word might bear my wealth at any time.
BOT: Speak softly. Yonder, as I think, he walks.
USER: I think I had: I never did deny it.
BOT: Yes, that you did, sir, and forswore it too.
USER: Who heard me to deny it or forswear it?
BOT: These ears of mine, thou know'st, did hear thee. Fie on thee, wretch. 'Tis pity that thou liv'st To walk where any honest men resort.
USER: Thou art a villain to impeach me thus; I'll prove mine honour and mine honesty Against thee presently, if thou dar'st stand.
BOT: I dare, and do defy thee for a villain.
USER: Hold, hurt him not, for God's sake, he is mad. Some get within him, take his sword away. Bind Dromio too, and bear them to my house.
BOT: Run, master, run, for God's sake, take a house. This is some priory; in, or we are spoil'd. priory._]
USER: Be quiet, people. Wherefore throng you hither?
BOT: To fetch my poor distracted husband hence. Let us come in, that we may bind him fast And bear him home for his recovery.
USER: I knew he was not in his perfect wits.
BOT: I am sorry now that I did draw on him.
USER: How long hath this possession held the man?
BOT: This week he hath been heavy, sour, sad, And much different from the man he was. But till this afternoon his passion Ne'er brake into extremity of rage.
USER: To none of these, except it be the last, Namely, some love that drew him oft from home.
BOT: You should for that have reprehended him.
USER: Why, so I did.
BOT: Ay, but not rough enough.
USER: As roughly as my modesty would let me.
BOT: Haply in private.
USER: And in assemblies too.
BOT: Ay, but not enough.
USER: She never reprehended him but mildly, When he demean'd himself rough, rude, and wildly. Why bear you these rebukes and answer not?
BOT: She did betray me to my own reproof. Good people, enter and lay hold on him.
USER: No, not a creature enters in my house.
BOT: Then let your servants bring my husband forth.
USER: Neither. He took this place for sanctuary, And it shall privilege him from your hands Till I have brought him to his wits again, Or lose my labour in assaying it.
BOT: I will attend my husband, be his nurse, Diet his sickness, for it is my office, And will have no attorney but myself; And therefore let me have him home with me.
USER: I will not hence and leave my husband here; And ill it doth beseem your holiness To separate the husband and the wife.
BOT: Be quiet and depart. Thou shalt not have him.
USER: Complain unto the duke of this indignity.
BOT: Come, go. I will fall prostrate at his feet, And never rise until my tears and prayers Have won his grace to come in person hither And take perforce my husband from the abbess.
USER: By this, I think, the dial points at five. Anon, I'm sure, the Duke himself in person Comes this way to the melancholy vale, The place of death and sorry execution Behind the ditches of the abbey here.
BOT: Upon what cause?
USER: To see a reverend Syracusian merchant, Who put unluckily into this bay Against the laws and statutes of this town, Beheaded publicly for his offence.
BOT: See where they come. We will behold his death.
USER: Kneel to the Duke before he pass the abbey. other Officers.
BOT: Yet once again proclaim it publicly, If any friend will pay the sum for him, He shall not die; so much we tender him.
USER: Justice, most sacred duke, against the abbess!
BOT: She is a virtuous and a reverend lady, It cannot be that she hath done thee wrong.
USER: Peace, fool, thy master and his man are here, And that is false thou dost report to us.
BOT: Come, stand by me, fear nothing. Guard with halberds.
USER: Ay me, it is my husband. Witness you That he is borne about invisible. Even now we hous'd him in the abbey here, And now he's there, past thought of human reason.
BOT: Unless the fear of death doth make me dote, I see my son Antipholus and Dromio.
USER: Discover how, and thou shalt find me just.
BOT: This day, great duke, she shut the doors upon me While she with harlots feasted in my house.
USER: A grievous fault. Say, woman, didst thou so?
BOT: No, my good lord. Myself, he, and my sister Today did dine together. So befall my soul As this is false he burdens me withal.
USER: Ne'er may I look on day nor sleep on night But she tells to your highness simple truth.
BOT: O perjur'd woman! They are both forsworn. In this the madman justly chargeth them.
USER: My lord, in truth, thus far I witness with him, That he din'd not at home, but was lock'd out.
BOT: But had he such a chain of thee, or no?
USER: He had, my lord, and when he ran in here These people saw the chain about his neck.
BOT: I never came within these abbey walls, Nor ever didst thou draw thy sword on me. I never saw the chain, so help me heaven; And this is false you burden me withal.
USER: Sir, he dined with her there, at the Porpentine.
BOT: He did, and from my finger snatch'd that ring.
USER: 'Tis true, my liege, this ring I had of her.
BOT: Saw'st thou him enter at the abbey here?
USER: As sure, my liege, as I do see your grace.
BOT: Why, this is strange. Go call the abbess hither. I think you are all mated, or stark mad.
USER: Most mighty Duke, vouchsafe me speak a word; Haply I see a friend will save my life And pay the sum that may deliver me.
BOT: Speak freely, Syracusian, what thou wilt.
USER: Is not your name, sir, call'd Antipholus? And is not that your bondman Dromio?
BOT: Within this hour I was his bondman, sir, But he, I thank him, gnaw'd in two my cords. Now am I Dromio, and his man, unbound.
USER: I am sure you both of you remember me.
BOT: Ourselves we do remember, sir, by you. For lately we were bound as you are now. You are not Pinch's patient, are you, sir?
USER: Why look you strange on me? you know me well.
BOT: I never saw you in my life till now.
USER: O! grief hath chang'd me since you saw me last, And careful hours with time's deformed hand, Have written strange defeatures in my face. But tell me yet, dost thou not know my voice?
BOT: Neither.
USER: Dromio, nor thou?
BOT: No, trust me, sir, nor I.
USER: I am sure thou dost.
BOT: Ay, sir, but I am sure I do not, and whatsoever a man denies, you are now bound to believe him.
USER: I never saw my father in my life.
BOT: But seven years since, in Syracusa, boy, Thou know'st we parted; but perhaps, my son, Thou sham'st to acknowledge me in misery.
USER: The duke and all that know me in the city, Can witness with me that it is not so. I ne'er saw Syracusa in my life.
BOT: I tell thee, Syracusian, twenty years Have I been patron to Antipholus, During which time he ne'er saw Syracusa. I see thy age and dangers make thee dote.
USER: Most mighty duke, behold a man much wrong'd.
BOT: I see two husbands, or mine eyes deceive me.
USER: One of these men is _genius_ to the other; And so of these, which is the natural man, And which the spirit? Who deciphers them?
BOT: I, sir, am Dromio, command him away.
USER: I, sir, am Dromio, pray let me stay.
BOT: Egeon, art thou not? or else his ghost?
USER: O, my old master, who hath bound him here?
BOT: If I dream not, thou art Emilia. If thou art she, tell me where is that son That floated with thee on the fatal raft?
USER: Antipholus, thou cam'st from Corinth first?
BOT: No, sir, not I, I came from Syracuse.
USER: Stay, stand apart, I know not which is which.
BOT: I came from Corinth, my most gracious lord.
USER: And I with him.
BOT: Brought to this town by that most famous warrior, Duke Menaphon, your most renowned uncle.
USER: Which of you two did dine with me today?
BOT: I, gentle mistress.
USER: And are not you my husband?
BOT: No, I say nay to that.
USER: And so do I, yet did she call me so; And this fair gentlewoman, her sister here, Did call me brother. What I told you then, I hope I shall have leisure to make good, If this be not a dream I see and hear.
BOT: That is the chain, sir, which you had of me.
USER: I think it be, sir. I deny it not.
BOT: And you, sir, for this chain arrested me.
USER: I think I did, sir. I deny it not.
BOT: I sent you money, sir, to be your bail By Dromio, but I think he brought it not.
USER: No, none by me.
BOT: This purse of ducats I receiv'd from you, And Dromio my man did bring them me. I see we still did meet each other's man, And I was ta'en for him, and he for me, And thereupon these errors are arose.
USER: These ducats pawn I for my father here.
BOT: It shall not need, thy father hath his life.
USER: Sir, I must have that diamond from you.
BOT: There, take it, and much thanks for my good cheer.
USER: With all my heart, I'll gossip at this feast.
BOT: Master, shall I fetch your stuff from shipboard?
USER: Dromio, what stuff of mine hast thou embark'd?
BOT: Your goods that lay at host, sir, in the Centaur.
USER: He speaks to me; I am your master, Dromio. Come, go with us. We'll look to that anon. Embrace thy brother there, rejoice with him.
BOT: There is a fat friend at your master's house, That kitchen'd me for you today at dinner. She now shall be my sister, not my wife.
USER: Methinks you are my glass, and not my brother. I see by you I am a sweet-faced youth. Will you walk in to see their gossiping?
BOT: Not I, sir, you are my elder.
USER: That's a question, how shall we try it?
BOT: We'll draw cuts for the senior. Till then, lead thou first.
USER: Nay, then, thus: We came into the world like brother and brother, And now let's go hand in hand, not one before another. THE TRAGEDY OF CORIOLANUS Contents

USER: Before we proceed any further, hear me speak.
BOT: Speak, speak!
USER: You are all resolved rather to die than to famish?
BOT: Resolved, resolved!
USER: First, you know Caius Martius is chief enemy to the people.
BOT: We know't, we know't!
USER: Let us kill him, and we'll have corn at our own price. Is't a verdict?
BOT: No more talking on't; let it be done. Away, away!
USER: One word, good citizens.
BOT: Would you proceed especially against Caius Martius?
USER: Against him first. He's a very dog to the commonalty.
BOT: Consider you what services he has done for his country?
USER: Very well, and could be content to give him good report for't, but that he pays himself with being proud.
BOT: Nay, but speak not maliciously.
USER: What he cannot help in his nature you account a vice in him. You must in no way say he is covetous.
BOT: Come, come!
USER: Soft, who comes here?
BOT: Worthy Menenius Agrippa, one that hath always loved the people.
USER: He's one honest enough. Would all the rest were so!
BOT: What work's, my countrymen, in hand? Where go you With bats and clubs? The matter? Speak, I pray you.
USER: Our business is not unknown to th' Senate. They have had inkling this fortnight what we intend to do, which now we'll show 'em in deeds. They say poor suitors have strong breaths; they shall know we have strong arms too.
BOT: Why, masters, my good friends, mine honest neighbours, Will you undo yourselves?
USER: We cannot, sir; we are undone already.
BOT: Either you must confess yourselves wondrous malicious Or be accused of folly. I shall tell you A pretty tale. It may be you have heard it, But since it serves my purpose, I will venture To stale't a little more.
USER: Well, I'll hear it, sir; yet you must not think to fob off our disgrace with a tale. But, an't please you, deliver.
BOT: Well, sir, what answer made the belly?
USER: What then? 'Fore me, this fellow speaks. What then? What then?
BOT: Should by the cormorant belly be restrained, Who is the sink o' th' body -
USER: Well, what then?
BOT: The former agents, if they did complain, What could the belly answer?
USER: I will tell you, If you'll bestow a small - of what you have little - Patience awhile, you'st hear the belly's answer.
BOT: You are long about it.
USER: Ay, sir, well, well.
BOT: "Though all at once cannot See what I do deliver out to each, Yet I can make my audit up, that all From me do back receive the flour of all, And leave me but the bran." What say you to't?
USER: It was an answer. How apply you this?
BOT: I the great toe? Why the great toe?
USER: Thanks. - What's the matter, you dissentious rogues, That, rubbing the poor itch of your opinion, Make yourselves scabs?
BOT: We have ever your good word.
USER: For corn at their own rates, whereof they say The city is well stored.
BOT: Nay, these are almost thoroughly persuaded; For though abundantly they lack discretion, Yet are they passing cowardly. But I beseech you, What says the other troop?
USER: What is granted them?
BOT: This is strange.
USER: Go get you home, you fragments.
BOT: Where's Caius Martius?
USER: Here. What's the matter?
BOT: The news is, sir, the Volsces are in arms.
USER: I am glad on't. Then we shall ha' means to vent Our musty superfluity. Lartius with other Senators. See, our best elders.
BOT: Martius, 'tis true that you have lately told us: The Volsces are in arms.
USER: They have a leader, Tullus Aufidius, that will put you to't. I sin in envying his nobility, And, were I anything but what I am, I would wish me only he.
BOT: You have fought together.
USER: Were half to half the world by th' ears and he Upon my party, I'd revolt, to make Only my wars with him. He is a lion That I am proud to hunt.
BOT: Then, worthy Martius, Attend upon Cominius to these wars.
USER: It is your former promise.
BOT: Sir, it is, And I am constant. - Titus Lartius, thou Shalt see me once more strike at Tullus' face. What, art thou stiff? Stand'st out?
USER: No, Caius Martius, I'll lean upon one crutch and fight with th' other Ere stay behind this business.
BOT: O, true bred!
USER: Your company to th' Capitol, where I know Our greatest friends attend us.
BOT: Lead you on. Follow Cominius. We must follow you; Right worthy your priority.
USER: Noble Martius.
BOT: Hence to your homes, begone.
USER: Nay, let them follow. The Volsces have much corn; take these rats thither To gnaw their garners. Worshipful mutineers, Your valour puts well forth. Pray follow.
BOT: Was ever man so proud as is this Martius?
USER: He has no equal.
BOT: When we were chosen tribunes for the people -
USER: Marked you his lip and eyes?
BOT: Nay, but his taunts.
USER: Being moved, he will not spare to gird the gods.
BOT: Bemock the modest moon.
USER: The present wars devour him! He is grown Too proud to be so valiant.
BOT: Such a nature, Tickled with good success, disdains the shadow Which he treads on at noon. But I do wonder His insolence can brook to be commanded Under Cominius.
USER: Besides, if things go well, Opinion that so sticks on Martius shall Of his demerits rob Cominius.
BOT: Come. Half all Cominius' honours are to Martius, Though Martius earned them not, and all his faults To Martius shall be honours, though indeed In aught he merit not.
USER: Let's hence and hear How the dispatch is made, and in what fashion, More than in singularity, he goes Upon this present action.
BOT: Let's along.

USER: So, your opinion is, Aufidius, That they of Rome are entered in our counsels And know how we proceed.
BOT: Our army's in the field. We never yet made doubt but Rome was ready To answer us.
USER: Noble Aufidius, Take your commission; hie you to your bands. Let us alone to guard Corioles. If they set down before's, for the remove Bring up your army. But I think you'll find They've not prepared for us.
BOT: The gods assist you!
USER: And keep your Honours safe!
BOT: Farewell.
USER: Farewell.
BOT: Farewell.

USER: But had he died in the business, madam, how then?
BOT: Madam, the Lady Valeria is come to visit you.
USER: Beseech you, give me leave to retire myself.
BOT: His bloody brow? O Jupiter, no blood!
USER: Heavens bless my lord from fell Aufidius!
BOT: He'll beat Aufidius' head below his knee And tread upon his neck.
USER: My ladies both, good day to you.
BOT: Sweet madam.
USER: I am glad to see your Ladyship.
BOT: How do you both? You are manifest housekeepers. What are you sewing here? A fine spot, in good faith. How does your little son?
USER: I thank your Ladyship; well, good madam.
BOT: He had rather see the swords and hear a drum than look upon his schoolmaster.
USER: One on's father's moods.
BOT: Indeed, la, 'tis a noble child.
USER: A crack, madam.
BOT: Come, lay aside your stitchery. I must have you play the idle huswife with me this afternoon.
USER: No, good madam, I will not out of doors.
BOT: Not out of doors?
USER: She shall, she shall.
BOT: Indeed, no, by your patience. I'll not over the threshold till my lord return from the wars.
USER: Fie, you confine yourself most unreasonably. Come, you must go visit the good lady that lies in.
BOT: I will wish her speedy strength and visit her with my prayers, but I cannot go thither.
USER: Why, I pray you?
BOT: 'Tis not to save labour, nor that I want love.
USER: No, good madam, pardon me; indeed, I will not forth.
BOT: In truth, la, go with me, and I'll tell you excellent news of your husband.
USER: O, good madam, there can be none yet.
BOT: Verily, I do not jest with you. There came news from him last night.
USER: Indeed, madam!
BOT: Give me excuse, good madam. I will obey you in everything hereafter.
USER: Let her alone, lady. As she is now, she will but disease our better mirth.
BOT: In troth, I think she would. - Fare you well, then. - Come, good sweet lady. - Prithee, Virgilia, turn thy solemness out o' door, and go along with us.
USER: No, at a word, madam. Indeed I must not. I wish you much mirth.
BOT: Well then, farewell.

USER: Yonder comes news. A wager they have met.
BOT: My horse to yours, no.
USER: 'Tis done.
BOT: Agreed.
USER: They lie in view but have not spoke as yet.
BOT: So the good horse is mine.
USER: I'll buy him of you.
BOT: No, I'll nor sell nor give him. Lend you him I will For half a hundred years. - Summon the town.
USER: How far off lie these armies?
BOT: Within this mile and half.
USER: O, they are at it!
BOT: Their noise be our instruction. - Ladders, ho!
USER: Foolhardiness, not I.
BOT: Nor I.
USER: See, they have shut him in.
BOT: To th' pot, I warrant him.
USER: What is become of Martius?
BOT: Slain, sir, doubtless.
USER: Following the fliers at the very heels, With them he enters, who upon the sudden Clapped to their gates. He is himself alone, To answer all the city.
BOT: Look, sir.
USER: O, 'tis Martius! Let's fetch him off or make remain alike.

USER: This will I carry to Rome.
BOT: And I this.
USER: A murrain on't! I took this for silver.
BOT: Worthy sir, thou bleed'st. Thy exercise hath been too violent For a second course of fight.
USER: Sir, praise me not. My work hath yet not warmed me. Fare you well. The blood I drop is rather physical Than dangerous to me. To Aufidius thus I will appear and fight.
BOT: Now the fair goddess Fortune Fall deep in love with thee, and her great charms Misguide thy opposers' swords! Bold gentleman, Prosperity be thy page!
USER: Thy friend no less Than those she placeth highest! So farewell.
BOT: Thou worthiest Martius! Go sound thy trumpet in the marketplace. Call thither all the officers o' th' town, Where they shall know our mind. Away!

USER: The citizens of Corioles have issued, And given to Lartius and to Martius battle. I saw our party to their trenches driven, And then I came away.
BOT: Though thou speakest truth, Methinks thou speak'st not well. How long is't since?
USER: Above an hour, my lord.
BOT: 'Tis not a mile; briefly we heard their drums. How couldst thou in a mile confound an hour And bring thy news so late?
USER: Spies of the Volsces Held me in chase, that I was forced to wheel Three or four miles about; else had I, sir, Half an hour since brought my report.
BOT: Who's yonder, That does appear as he were flayed? O gods, He has the stamp of Martius, and I have Before-time seen him thus.
USER: Come I too late?
BOT: The shepherd knows not thunder from a tabor More than I know the sound of Martius' tongue From every meaner man.
USER: Come I too late?
BOT: Ay, if you come not in the blood of others, But mantled in your own.
USER: O, let me clip you In arms as sound as when I wooed, in heart As merry as when our nuptial day was done And tapers burned to bedward!
BOT: Flower of warriors, how is't with Titus Lartius?
USER: Where is that slave Which told me they had beat you to your trenches? Where's he? Call him hither.
BOT: Let him alone. He did inform the truth. But for our gentlemen, The common file - a plague! Tribunes for them! - The mouse ne'er shunned the cat as they did budge From rascals worse than they.
USER: But how prevailed you?
BOT: Will the time serve to tell? I do not think. Where is the enemy? Are you lords o' th' field? If not, why cease you till you are so?
USER: Martius, we have at disadvantage fought, And did retire to win our purpose.
BOT: How lies their battle? Know you on which side They have placed their men of trust?
USER: As I guess, Martius, Their bands i' th' vaward are the Antiates, Of their best trust; o'er them Aufidius, Their very heart of hope.
BOT: Though I could wish You were conducted to a gentle bath And balms applied to you, yet dare I never Deny your asking. Take your choice of those That best can aid your action.
USER: March on, my fellows. Make good this ostentation, and you shall Divide in all with us.

USER: So, let the ports be guarded. Keep your duties As I have set them down. If I do send, dispatch Those centuries to our aid; the rest will serve For a short holding. If we lose the field, We cannot keep the town.
BOT: Fear not our care, sir.
USER: Hence, and shut your gates upon's. Our guider, come. To th' Roman camp conduct us.

USER: I'll fight with none but thee, for I do hate thee Worse than a promise-breaker.
BOT: We hate alike. Not Afric owns a serpent I abhor More than thy fame and envy. Fix thy foot.
USER: Let the first budger die the other's slave, And the gods doom him after!
BOT: If I fly, Martius, Hollo me like a hare.
USER: Within these three hours, Tullus, Alone I fought in your Corioles' walls, And made what work I pleased. 'Tis not my blood Wherein thou seest me masked. For thy revenge Wrench up thy power to th' highest.
BOT: Wert thou the Hector That was the whip of your bragged progeny, Thou shouldst not scape me here. Officious and not valiant, you have shamed me In your condemned seconds. Martius exit, separately._]

USER: O general, Here is the steed, we the caparison. Hadst thou beheld -
BOT: I have some wounds upon me, and they smart To hear themselves remembered.
USER: Caius Martius Coriolanus!
BOT: I will go wash; And when my face is fair, you shall perceive Whether I blush or no. Howbeit, I thank you. I mean to stride your steed and at all times To undercrest your good addition To th' fairness of my power.
USER: So, to our tent, Where, ere we do repose us, we will write To Rome of our success. - You, Titus Lartius, Must to Corioles back. Send us to Rome The best, with whom we may articulate For their own good and ours.
BOT: I shall, my lord.
USER: The gods begin to mock me. I, that now Refused most princely gifts, am bound to beg Of my lord general.
BOT: Take't, 'tis yours. What is't?
USER: O, well begged! Were he the butcher of my son, he should Be free as is the wind. - Deliver him, Titus.
BOT: Martius, his name?
USER: By Jupiter, forgot! I am weary; yea, my memory is tired. Have we no wine here?
BOT: Go we to our tent. The blood upon your visage dries; 'tis time It should be looked to. Come.

USER: The town is ta'en.
BOT: 'Twill be delivered back on good condition.
USER: He's the devil.
BOT: Will not you go?
USER: I am attended at the cypress grove. I pray you - 'Tis south the city mills, - bring me word thither How the world goes, that to the pace of it I may spur on my journey.
BOT: I shall, sir.

USER: The augurer tells me we shall have news tonight.
BOT: Good or bad?
USER: Not according to the prayer of the people, for they love not Martius.
BOT: Nature teaches beasts to know their friends.
USER: Pray you, who does the wolf love?
BOT: The lamb.
USER: Ay, to devour him, as the hungry plebeians would the noble Martius.
BOT: He's a lamb indeed, that baas like a bear.
USER: He's a bear indeed, that lives like a lamb. You two are old men; tell me one thing that I shall ask you.
BOT: Well, sir.
USER: In what enormity is Martius poor in, that you two have not in abundance?
BOT: He's poor in no one fault, but stored with all.
USER: Especially in pride.
BOT: And topping all others in boasting.
USER: This is strange now. Do you two know how you are censured here in the city, I mean of us o' th' right-hand file, do you?
BOT: Why, how are we censured?
USER: Because you talk of pride now, will you not be angry?
BOT: Well, well, sir, well?
USER: We do it not alone, sir.
BOT: What then, sir?
USER: Why, then you should discover a brace of unmeriting, proud, violent, testy magistrates, alias fools, as any in Rome.
BOT: Menenius, you are known well enough, too.
USER: Come, sir, come; we know you well enough.
BOT: Come, come. You are well understood to be a perfecter giber for the table than a necessary bencher in the Capitol.
USER: Honourable Menenius, my boy Martius approaches. For the love of Juno, let's go!
BOT: Ha? Martius coming home?
USER: Ay, worthy Menenius, and with most prosperous approbation.
BOT: Take my cap, Jupiter, and I thank thee! Hoo! Martius coming home? VALERIA, VIRGILIA. Nay, 'tis true.
USER: Look, here's a letter from him. The state hath another, his wife another, and I think there's one at home for you.
BOT: I will make my very house reel tonight. A letter for me?
USER: Yes, certain, there's a letter for you; I saw it.
BOT: O, no, no, no!
USER: O, he is wounded, I thank the gods for't.
BOT: So do I too, if it be not too much. Brings he victory in his pocket, the wounds become him.
USER: On's brows, Menenius. He comes the third time home with the oaken garland.
BOT: Has he disciplined Aufidius soundly?
USER: Titus Lartius writes they fought together, but Aufidius got off.
BOT: And 'twas time for him too, I'll warrant him that. An he had stayed by him, I would not have been so 'fidiused for all the chests in Corioles and the gold that's in them. Is the Senate possessed of this?
USER: Good ladies, let's go. - Yes, yes, yes. The Senate has letters from the General, wherein he gives my son the whole name of the war. He hath in this action outdone his former deeds doubly.
BOT: In troth, there's wondrous things spoke of him.
USER: Wondrous? Ay, I warrant you, and not without his true purchasing.
BOT: The gods grant them true.
USER: True? Pow, waw!
BOT: True? I'll be sworn they are true. Where is he wounded? [_To the Tribunes_.] God save your good Worships! Martius is coming home; he has more cause to be proud. - Where is he wounded?
USER: I' th' shoulder and i' th' left arm. There will be large cicatrices to show the people when he shall stand for his place. He received in the repulse of Tarquin seven hurts i' th' body.
BOT: One i' th' neck and two i' th' thigh - there's nine that I know.
USER: He had, before this last expedition, twenty-five wounds upon him.
BOT: Now it's twenty-seven. Every gash was an enemy's grave. Hark, the trumpets!
USER: Know, Rome, that all alone Martius did fight Within Corioles' gates, where he hath won, With fame, a name to Caius Martius; these In honour follows "Coriolanus." Welcome to Rome, renowned Coriolanus.
BOT: Welcome to Rome, renowned Coriolanus!
USER: No more of this, it does offend my heart. Pray now, no more.
BOT: Look, sir, your mother.
USER: O, You have, I know, petitioned all the gods For my prosperity.
BOT: Nay, my good soldier, up. My gentle Martius, worthy Caius, and By deed-achieving honour newly named - What is it? Coriolanus must I call thee? But, O, thy wife -
USER: My gracious silence, hail. Wouldst thou have laughed had I come coffined home, That weep'st to see me triumph? Ah, my dear, Such eyes the widows in Corioles wear And mothers that lack sons.
BOT: Now the gods crown thee!
USER: And live you yet? [_To Valeria_] O my sweet lady, pardon.
BOT: I know not where to turn. O, welcome home! And welcome, general. - And you're welcome all.
USER: Ever right.
BOT: Menenius ever, ever.
USER: Give way there, and go on!
BOT: Ere in our own house I do shade my head, The good patricians must be visited, From whom I have received not only greetings, But with them change of honours.
USER: I have lived To see inherited my very wishes And the buildings of my fancy. Only There's one thing wanting, which I doubt not but Our Rome will cast upon thee.
BOT: Know, good mother, I had rather be their servant in my way Than sway with them in theirs.
USER: On, to the Capitol. Brutus and Sicinius come forward.
BOT: On the sudden I warrant him consul.
USER: Then our office may, During his power, go sleep.
BOT: He cannot temp'rately transport his honours From where he should begin and end, but will Lose those he hath won.
USER: In that there's comfort.
BOT: Doubt not the commoners, for whom we stand, But they, upon their ancient malice will forget With the least cause these his new honours - which That he will give them make as little question As he is proud to do't.
USER: 'Tis right.
BOT: It was his word. O, he would miss it rather Than carry it but by the suit of the gentry to him And the desire of the nobles.
USER: I wish no better Than have him hold that purpose and to put it In execution.
BOT: 'Tis most like he will.
USER: It shall be to him then, as our good wills, A sure destruction.
BOT: What's the matter?
USER: Let's to the Capitol; And carry with us ears and eyes for th' time, But hearts for the event.
BOT: Have with you.

USER: Come, come. They are almost here. How many stand for consulships?
BOT: Three, they say; but 'tis thought of everyone Coriolanus will carry it.
USER: That's a brave fellow, but he's vengeance proud and loves not the common people.
BOT: We are convented Upon a pleasing treaty and have hearts Inclinable to honour and advance The theme of our assembly.
USER: Which the rather We shall be blest to do if he remember A kinder value of the people than He hath hereto prized them at.
BOT: That's off, that's off! I would you rather had been silent. Please you To hear Cominius speak?
USER: Most willingly. But yet my caution was more pertinent Than the rebuke you give it.
BOT: He loves your people, But tie him not to be their bedfellow. - Worthy Cominius, speak. Nay, keep your place.
USER: Sit, Coriolanus. Never shame to hear What you have nobly done.
BOT: Your Honours, pardon. I had rather have my wounds to heal again Than hear say how I got them.
USER: Sir, I hope My words disbenched you not?
BOT: No, sir. Yet oft, When blows have made me stay, I fled from words. You soothed not, therefore hurt not; but your people, I love them as they weigh.
USER: Pray now, sit down.
BOT: I had rather have one scratch my head i' th' sun When the alarum were struck than idly sit To hear my nothings monstered.
USER: Masters of the people, Your multiplying spawn how can he flatter - That's thousand to one good one - when you now see He had rather venture all his limbs for honour Than one on's ears to hear it? - Proceed, Cominius.
BOT: Worthy man!
USER: He cannot but with measure fit the honours Which we devise him.
BOT: He's right noble. Let him be called for.
USER: Call Coriolanus.
BOT: He doth appear.
USER: The Senate, Coriolanus, are well pleased To make thee consul.
BOT: I do owe them still My life and services.
USER: It then remains That you do speak to the people.
BOT: I do beseech you Let me o'erleap that custom, for I cannot Put on the gown, stand naked, and entreat them For my wounds' sake to give their suffrage. Please you That I may pass this doing.
USER: Sir, the people Must have their voices; neither will they bate One jot of ceremony.
BOT: Put them not to't. Pray you, go fit you to the custom, and Take to you, as your predecessors have, Your honour with your form.
USER: It is a part That I shall blush in acting, and might well Be taken from the people.
BOT: Mark you that?
USER: To brag unto them, "thus I did, and thus!" Show them th' unaching scars which I should hide, As if I had received them for the hire Of their breath only!
BOT: Do not stand upon't. - We recommend to you, tribunes of the people, Our purpose to them, and to our noble consul Wish we all joy and honour.
USER: To Coriolanus come all joy and honour!
BOT: You see how he intends to use the people.
USER: May they perceive's intent! He will require them As if he did contemn what he requested Should be in them to give.
BOT: Come, we'll inform them Of our proceedings here. On th' marketplace I know they do attend us.

USER: Once, if he do require our voices, we ought not to deny him.
BOT: We may, sir, if we will.
USER: And to make us no better thought of, a little help will serve; for once we stood up about the corn, he himself stuck not to call us the many-headed multitude.
BOT: Think you so? Which way do you judge my wit would fly?
USER: Nay, your wit will not so soon out as another man's will; 'tis strongly wedged up in a blockhead. But if it were at liberty, 'twould, sure, southward.
BOT: Why that way?
USER: To lose itself in a fog, where being three parts melted away with rotten dews, the fourth would return for conscience' sake, to help to get thee a wife.
BOT: You are never without your tricks. You may, you may.
USER: Content, content.
