BOT: O sir, you are not right. Have you not known The worthiest men have done't?
USER: O me, the gods! You must not speak of that. You must desire them To think upon you.
BOT: Think upon me! Hang 'em! I would they would forget me, like the virtues Which our divines lose by 'em.
USER: You'll mar all. I'll leave you. Pray you speak to 'em, I pray you, In wholesome manner.
BOT: Bid them wash their faces And keep their teeth clean. So, here comes a brace. You know the cause, sirs, of my standing here.
USER: We do, sir. Tell us what hath brought you to't.
BOT: Mine own desert.
USER: Your own desert?
BOT: Ay, but not mine own desire.
USER: How, not your own desire?
BOT: No, sir, 'twas never my desire yet to trouble the poor with begging.
USER: You must think if we give you anything, we hope to gain by you.
BOT: Well then, I pray, your price o' th' consulship?
USER: The price is to ask it kindly.
BOT: Kindly, sir, I pray, let me ha't. I have wounds to show you, which shall be yours in private. - Your good voice, sir. What say you?
USER: You shall ha' it, worthy sir.
BOT: A match, sir. There's in all two worthy voices begged. I have your alms. Adieu.
USER: But this is something odd.
BOT: An 'twere to give again - but 'tis no matter.
USER: Pray you now, if it may stand with the tune of your voices that I may be consul, I have here the customary gown.
BOT: You have deserved nobly of your country, and you have not deserved nobly.
USER: Your enigma?
BOT: You have been a scourge to her enemies; you have been a rod to her friends. You have not indeed loved the common people.
USER: We hope to find you our friend, and therefore give you our voices heartily.
BOT: You have received many wounds for your country.
USER: I will not seal your knowledge with showing them. I will make much of your voices and so trouble you no farther.
BOT: The gods give you joy, sir, heartily.
USER: He has done nobly, and cannot go without any honest man's voice.
BOT: Therefore let him be consul. The gods give him joy, and make him good friend to the people!
USER: Amen, amen. God save thee, noble consul.
BOT: Worthy voices!
USER: You have stood your limitation, and the Tribunes Endue you with the people's voice. Remains That in th' official marks invested, you Anon do meet the Senate.
BOT: Is this done?
USER: The custom of request you have discharged. The people do admit you, and are summoned To meet anon upon your approbation.
BOT: Where? At the Senate House?
USER: There, Coriolanus.
BOT: May I change these garments?
USER: You may, sir.
BOT: That I'll straight do and, knowing myself again, Repair to th' Senate House.
USER: I'll keep you company. - Will you along?
BOT: We stay here for the people.
USER: Fare you well. He has it now; and by his looks, methinks, 'Tis warm at his heart.
BOT: With a proud heart he wore His humble weeds. Will you dismiss the people?
USER: How now, my masters, have you chose this man?
BOT: He has our voices, sir.
USER: We pray the gods he may deserve your loves.
BOT: Amen, sir. To my poor unworthy notice, He mocked us when he begged our voices.
USER: Certainly, he flouted us downright.
BOT: No, 'tis his kind of speech. He did not mock us.
USER: Not one amongst us, save yourself, but says He used us scornfully. He should have showed us His marks of merit, wounds received for's country.
BOT: Why, so he did, I am sure.
USER: No, no. No man saw 'em.
BOT: Why either were you ignorant to see't Or, seeing it, of such childish friendliness To yield your voices?
USER: Have you ere now denied the asker, and now Again, of him that did not ask but mock, Bestow your sued-for tongues?
BOT: He's not confirmed. We may deny him yet.
USER: And will deny him. I'll have five hundred voices of that sound.
BOT: I twice five hundred, and their friends to piece 'em.
USER: Get you hence instantly, and tell those friends They have chose a consul that will from them take Their liberties, make them of no more voice Than dogs that are as often beat for barking As therefore kept to do so.
BOT: Lay A fault on us, your tribunes, that we laboured, No impediment between, but that you must Cast your election on him.
USER: Say you ne'er had done't - Harp on that still - but by our putting on. And presently when you have drawn your number, Repair to th' Capitol.
BOT: We will so. Almost all Repent in their election.
USER: Let them go on. This mutiny were better put in hazard Than stay, past doubt, for greater. If, as his nature is, he fall in rage With their refusal, both observe and answer The vantage of his anger.
BOT: To th' Capitol, come. We will be there before the stream o' th' people, And this shall seem, as partly 'tis, their own, Which we have goaded onward.

USER: Tullus Aufidius then had made new head?
BOT: He had, my lord, and that it was which caused Our swifter composition.
USER: So then the Volsces stand but as at first, Ready, when time shall prompt them, to make road Upon's again.
BOT: They are worn, lord consul, so That we shall hardly in our ages see Their banners wave again.
USER: Saw you Aufidius?
BOT: On safeguard he came to me, and did curse Against the Volsces, for they had so vilely Yielded the town. He is retired to Antium.
USER: Spoke he of me?
BOT: He did, my lord.
USER: How? What?
BOT: How often he had met you sword to sword; That of all things upon the earth he hated Your person most; that he would pawn his fortunes To hopeless restitution, so he might Be called your vanquisher.
USER: At Antium lives he?
BOT: At Antium.
USER: Pass no further.
BOT: Ha? What is that?
USER: It will be dangerous to go on. No further.
BOT: What makes this change?
USER: The matter?
BOT: Hath he not passed the noble and the common?
USER: Cominius, no.
BOT: Have I had children's voices?
USER: Tribunes, give way. He shall to the marketplace.
BOT: The people are incensed against him.
USER: Stop, Or all will fall in broil.
BOT: Are these your herd? Must these have voices, that can yield them now And straight disclaim their tongues? What are your offices? You being their mouths, why rule you not their teeth? Have you not set them on?
USER: Be calm, be calm.
BOT: It is a purposed thing, and grows by plot, To curb the will of the nobility. Suffer't, and live with such as cannot rule Nor ever will be ruled.
USER: Call't not a plot. The people cry you mocked them; and, of late, When corn was given them gratis, you repined, Scandaled the suppliants for the people, called them Timepleasers, flatterers, foes to nobleness.
BOT: Why, this was known before.
USER: Not to them all.
BOT: Have you informed them sithence?
USER: How? I inform them?
BOT: You are like to do such business.
USER: Not unlike, each way, to better yours.
BOT: Why then should I be consul? By yond clouds, Let me deserve so ill as you, and make me Your fellow tribune.
USER: Let's be calm.
BOT: The people are abused, set on. This palt'ring Becomes not Rome, nor has Coriolanus Deserved this so dishonoured rub, laid falsely I' th' plain way of his merit.
USER: Tell me of corn? This was my speech, and I will speak't again.
BOT: Not now, not now.
USER: Not in this heat, sir, now.
BOT: Well, no more.
USER: No more words, we beseech you.
BOT: You speak o' th' people As if you were a god to punish, not A man of their infirmity.
USER: 'Twere well We let the people know't.
BOT: What, what? His choler?
USER: Choler? Were I as patient as the midnight sleep, By Jove, 'twould be my mind.
BOT: It is a mind That shall remain a poison where it is, Not poison any further.
USER: "Shall remain"? Hear you this Triton of the minnows? Mark you His absolute "shall"?
BOT: 'Twas from the canon.
USER: Well, on to th' marketplace.
BOT: Whoever gave that counsel to give forth The corn o' th' storehouse gratis, as 'twas used Sometime in Greece -
USER: Well, well, no more of that.
BOT: Though there the people had more absolute power, I say they nourished disobedience, fed The ruin of the state.
USER: Why shall the people give One that speaks thus their voice?
BOT: Come, enough.
USER: Enough, with over-measure.
BOT: 'Has said enough.
USER: 'Has spoken like a traitor, and shall answer As traitors do.
BOT: Manifest treason.
USER: This a consul? No.
BOT: The aediles, ho! Let him be apprehended.
USER: Go call the people; in whose name myself Attach thee as a traitorous innovator, A foe to th' public weal. Obey, I charge thee, And follow to thine answer.
BOT: Hence, old goat.
USER: We'll surety him.
BOT: Out of thy garments.
USER: Help, ye citizens!
BOT: On both sides more respect!
USER: Here's he that would take from you all your power.
BOT: Seize him, aediles.
USER: Down with him, down with him!
BOT: Weapons, weapons, weapons! Tribunes, patricians, citizens, what, ho! Sicinius, Brutus, Coriolanus, citizens!
USER: Peace, peace, peace! Stay, hold, peace!
BOT: What is about to be? I am out of breath. Confusion's near. I cannot speak. You tribunes To th' people! - Coriolanus, patience! - Speak, good Sicinius.
USER: Hear me, people! Peace!
BOT: Let's hear our tribune. Peace! Speak, speak, speak.
USER: You are at point to lose your liberties. Martius would have all from you, Martius, Whom late you have named for consul.
BOT: Fie, fie, fie! This is the way to kindle, not to quench.
USER: To unbuild the city and to lay all flat.
BOT: What is the city but the people?
USER: True, The people are the city.
BOT: By the consent of all, we were established The people's magistrates.
USER: You so remain.
BOT: And so are like to do.
USER: That is the way to lay the city flat, To bring the roof to the foundation And bury all which yet distinctly ranges In heaps and piles of ruin.
BOT: This deserves death.
USER: Or let us stand to our authority Or let us lose it. We do here pronounce, Upon the part o' th' people, in whose power We were elected theirs, Martius is worthy Of present death.
BOT: Therefore lay hold of him, Bear him to th' rock Tarpeian, and from thence Into destruction cast him.
USER: Aediles, seize him!
BOT: Yield, Martius, yield!
USER: Hear me one word. Beseech you, tribunes, hear me but a word.
BOT: Peace, peace!
USER: Be that you seem, truly your country's friend, And temp'rately proceed to what you would Thus violently redress.
BOT: Sir, those cold ways, That seem like prudent helps, are very poisonous Where the disease is violent. - Lay hands upon him, And bear him to the rock.
USER: No; I'll die here. There's some among you have beheld me fighting. Come, try upon yourselves what you have seen me.
BOT: Down with that sword! - Tribunes, withdraw awhile.
USER: Lay hands upon him!
BOT: Help Martius, help! You that be noble, help him, young and old!
USER: Down with him, down with him! in._]
BOT: Go, get you to your house. Begone, away. All will be naught else.
USER: Get you gone.
BOT: Stand fast! We have as many friends as enemies.
USER: Shall it be put to that?
BOT: The gods forbid! I prithee, noble friend, home to thy house; Leave us to cure this cause.
USER: For 'tis a sore upon us You cannot tent yourself. Begone, beseech you.
BOT: Come, sir, along with us.
USER: I would they were barbarians, as they are, Though in Rome littered, not Romans, as they are not, Though calved i' th' porch o' th' Capitol.
BOT: Begone! Put not your worthy rage into your tongue. One time will owe another.
USER: On fair ground I could beat forty of them.
BOT: I could myself Take up a brace o' th' best of them, yea, the two tribunes.
USER: Pray you, begone. I'll try whether my old wit be in request With those that have but little. This must be patched With cloth of any colour.
BOT: Nay, come away.
USER: This man has marred his fortune.
BOT: I would they were abed!
USER: I would they were in Tiber! What the vengeance, Could he not speak 'em fair?
BOT: Where is this viper That would depopulate the city and Be every man himself?
USER: You worthy tribunes -
BOT: He shall be thrown down the Tarpeian rock With rigorous hands. He hath resisted law, And therefore law shall scorn him further trial Than the severity of the public power Which he so sets at naught.
USER: He shall well know The noble tribunes are the people's mouths, And we their hands.
BOT: He shall, sure on't.
USER: Sir, sir -
BOT: Peace!
USER: Do not cry havoc where you should but hunt With modest warrant.
BOT: Sir, how comes't that you Have holp to make this rescue?
USER: Hear me speak. As I do know the Consul's worthiness, So can I name his faults.
BOT: Consul? What consul?
USER: The consul Coriolanus.
BOT: He consul?
USER: No, no, no, no, no!
BOT: If, by the Tribunes' leave, and yours, good people, I may be heard, I would crave a word or two, The which shall turn you to no further harm Than so much loss of time.
USER: Speak briefly then, For we are peremptory to dispatch This viperous traitor. To eject him hence Were but one danger, and to keep him here Our certain death. Therefore it is decreed He dies tonight.
BOT: Now the good gods forbid That our renowned Rome, whose gratitude Towards her deserved children is enrolled In Jove's own book, like an unnatural dam Should now eat up her own.
USER: He's a disease that must be cut away.
BOT: This is clean cam.
USER: Merely awry. When he did love his country, It honoured him.
BOT: The service of the foot, Being once gangrened, is not then respected For what before it was.
USER: We'll hear no more. Pursue him to his house, and pluck him thence, Lest his infection, being of catching nature, Spread further.
BOT: If it were so -
USER: What do ye talk? Have we not had a taste of his obedience? Our aediles smote! Ourselves resisted? Come.
BOT: Noble tribunes, It is the humane way: the other course Will prove too bloody, and the end of it Unknown to the beginning.
USER: Noble Menenius, Be you then as the people's officer. - Masters, lay down your weapons.
BOT: Go not home.
USER: Meet on the marketplace. We'll attend you there, Where if you bring not Martius, we'll proceed In our first way.
BOT: I'll bring him to you. Or what is worst will follow.
USER: Pray you, let's to him.

USER: You do the nobler.
BOT: O, sir, sir, sir, I would have had you put your power well on Before you had worn it out.
USER: Let go.
BOT: You might have been enough the man you are With striving less to be so. Lesser had been The thwartings of your dispositions if You had not showed them how ye were disposed Ere they lacked power to cross you.
USER: Let them hang!
BOT: Ay, and burn too.
USER: Come, come, you have been too rough, something too rough. You must return and mend it.
BOT: There's no remedy, Unless, by not so doing, our good city Cleave in the midst and perish.
USER: Pray be counselled. I have a heart as little apt as yours, But yet a brain that leads my use of anger To better vantage.
BOT: Well said, noble woman. Before he should thus stoop to th' herd - but that The violent fit o' th' time craves it as physic For the whole state - I would put mine armour on, Which I can scarcely bear.
USER: What must I do?
BOT: Return to th' Tribunes.
USER: Well, what then? What then?
BOT: Repent what you have spoke.
USER: For them? I cannot do it to the gods. Must I then do't to them?
BOT: Tush, tush!
USER: A good demand.
BOT: Why force you this?
USER: Noble lady! - Come, go with us; speak fair. You may salve so, Not what is dangerous present, but the loss Of what is past.
BOT: This but done Even as she speaks, why, their hearts were yours; For they have pardons, being asked, as free As words to little purpose.
USER: Prithee now, Go, and be ruled; although I know thou hadst rather Follow thine enemy in a fiery gulf Than flatter him in a bower. Here is Cominius.
BOT: I have been i' th' marketplace; and, sir, 'tis fit You make strong party or defend yourself By calmness or by absence. All's in anger.
USER: Only fair speech.
BOT: I think 'twill serve, if he Can thereto frame his spirit.
USER: He must, and will. - Prithee, now, say you will, and go about it.
BOT: Come, come, we'll prompt you.
USER: I prithee now, sweet son, as thou hast said My praises made thee first a soldier, so, To have my praise for this, perform a part Thou hast not done before.
BOT: Do your will.
USER: Away! The Tribunes do attend you. Arm yourself To answer mildly, for they are prepared With accusations, as I hear, more strong Than are upon you yet.
BOT: The word is "mildly." Pray you, let us go. Let them accuse me by invention, I Will answer in mine honour.
USER: Ay, but mildly.
BOT: Well, mildly be it, then. Mildly.

USER: In this point charge him home, that he affects Tyrannical power. If he evade us there, Enforce him with his envy to the people, And that the spoil got on the Antiates Was ne'er distributed. What, will he come?
BOT: He's coming.
USER: How accompanied?
BOT: With old Menenius, and those senators That always favoured him.
USER: Have you a catalogue Of all the voices that we have procured, Set down by th' poll?
BOT: I have. 'Tis ready.
USER: Have you collected them by tribes?
BOT: I have.
USER: I shall inform them.
BOT: And when such time they have begun to cry, Let them not cease, but with a din confused Enforce the present execution Of what we chance to sentence.
USER: Very well.
BOT: Make them be strong and ready for this hint When we shall hap to give't them.
USER: Well, here he comes.
BOT: Calmly, I do beseech you.
USER: Amen, amen.
BOT: A noble wish.
USER: Draw near, ye people.
BOT: List to your tribunes. Audience! Peace, I say!
USER: First, hear me speak.
BOT: Well, say. - Peace, ho!
USER: Shall I be charged no further than this present? Must all determine here?
BOT: I do demand If you submit you to the people's voices, Allow their officers, and are content To suffer lawful censure for such faults As shall be proved upon you.
USER: I am content.
BOT: Lo, citizens, he says he is content. The warlike service he has done, consider. Think Upon the wounds his body bears, which show Like graves i' th' holy churchyard.
USER: Scratches with briars, Scars to move laughter only.
BOT: Consider further, That when he speaks not like a citizen, You find him like a soldier. Do not take His rougher accents for malicious sounds, But, as I say, such as become a soldier Rather than envy you.
USER: Well, well, no more.
BOT: What is the matter, That, being passed for consul with full voice, I am so dishonoured that the very hour You take it off again?
USER: Answer to us.
BOT: Say then. 'Tis true, I ought so.
USER: We charge you that you have contrived to take From Rome all seasoned office and to wind Yourself into a power tyrannical, For which you are a traitor to the people.
BOT: How? Traitor?
USER: Nay, temperately! Your promise.
BOT: Mark you this, people?
USER: To th' rock, to th' rock with him!
BOT: But since he hath Served well for Rome -
USER: What do you prate of service?
BOT: I talk of that that know it.
USER: You?
BOT: Is this the promise that you made your mother?
USER: Know, I pray you -
BOT: It shall be so, it shall be so! Let him away! He's banished, and it shall be so.
USER: Hear me, my masters and my common friends -
BOT: He's sentenced. No more hearing.
USER: We know your drift. Speak what?
BOT: There's no more to be said, but he is banished As enemy to the people and his country. It shall be so.
USER: It shall be so, it shall be so!
BOT: The people's enemy is gone, is gone.
USER: Our enemy is banished; he is gone. Hoo, hoo!
BOT: Go see him out at gates, and follow him, As he hath followed you, with all despite. Give him deserved vexation. Let a guard Attend us through the city.
USER: Come, come, let's see him out at gates! Come! The gods preserve our noble tribunes! Come.

USER: O heavens! O heavens!
BOT: Nay, I prithee, woman -
USER: Now the red pestilence strike all trades in Rome, And occupations perish!
BOT: My first son, Whither wilt thou go? Take good Cominius With thee awhile. Determine on some course More than a wild exposture to each chance That starts i' th' way before thee.
USER: O the gods!
BOT: That's worthily As any ear can hear. Come, let's not weep. If I could shake off but one seven years From these old arms and legs, by the good gods, I'd with thee every foot.
USER: Give me thy hand. Come.

USER: Bid them all home. He's gone, and we'll no further. The nobility are vexed, whom we see have sided In his behalf.
BOT: Now we have shown our power, Let us seem humbler after it is done Than when it was a-doing.
USER: Bid them home. Say their great enemy is gone, and they Stand in their ancient strength.
BOT: Dismiss them home. Here comes his mother.
USER: Let's not meet her.
BOT: Why?
USER: They say she's mad.
BOT: They have ta'en note of us. Keep on your way.
USER: O, you're well met. The hoarded plague o' th' gods Requite your love!
BOT: Peace, peace! Be not so loud.
USER: If that I could for weeping, you should hear - Nay, and you shall hear some. [_To Sicinius_.] Will you be gone?
BOT: To say so to my husband.
USER: Are you mankind?
BOT: Ay, fool, is that a shame? Note but this, fool. Was not a man my father? Hadst thou foxship To banish him that struck more blows for Rome Than thou hast spoken words?
USER: O blessed heavens!
BOT: More noble blows than ever thou wise words, And for Rome's good. I'll tell thee what - yet go. Nay, but thou shalt stay too. I would my son Were in Arabia and thy tribe before him, His good sword in his hand.
USER: What then?
BOT: What then? He'd make an end of thy posterity.
USER: Bastards and all. Good man, the wounds that he does bear for Rome!
BOT: Come, come, peace.
USER: I would he had continued to his country As he began, and not unknit himself The noble knot he made.
BOT: I would he had.
USER: "I would he had?" 'Twas you incensed the rabble. Cats, that can judge as fitly of his worth As I can of those mysteries which heaven Will not have Earth to know.
BOT: Pray, let's go.
USER: Well, well, we'll leave you.
BOT: Why stay we to be baited With one that wants her wits?
USER: Take my prayers with you. I would the gods had nothing else to do But to confirm my curses. Could I meet 'em But once a day, it would unclog my heart Of what lies heavy to't.
BOT: You have told them home, And, by my troth, you have cause. You'll sup with me?
USER: Anger's my meat. I sup upon myself And so shall starve with feeding. Come, let's go. Leave this faint puling, and lament as I do, In anger, Juno-like. Come, come, come.
BOT: Fie, fie, fie!

USER: I know you well, sir, and you know me. Your name I think is Adrian.
BOT: It is so, sir. Truly, I have forgot you.
USER: I am a Roman, and my services are, as you are, against 'em. Know you me yet?
BOT: Nicanor, no?
USER: The same, sir.
BOT: You had more beard when I last saw you, but your favour is well approved by your tongue. What's the news in Rome? I have a note from the Volscian state to find you out there. You have well saved me a day's journey.
USER: There hath been in Rome strange insurrections, the people against the senators, patricians, and nobles.
BOT: Hath been? Is it ended, then? Our state thinks not so. They are in a most warlike preparation and hope to come upon them in the heat of their division.
USER: Coriolanus banished?
BOT: Banished, sir.
USER: You will be welcome with this intelligence, Nicanor.
BOT: He cannot choose. I am most fortunate thus accidentally to encounter you. You have ended my business, and I will merrily accompany you home.
USER: I shall between this and supper tell you most strange things from Rome, all tending to the good of their adversaries. Have you an army ready, say you?
BOT: A most royal one. The centurions and their charges, distinctly billeted, already in th' entertainment, and to be on foot at an hour's warning.
USER: I am joyful to hear of their readiness and am the man, I think, that shall set them in present action. So, sir, heartily well met, and most glad of your company.
BOT: You take my part from me, sir. I have the most cause to be glad of yours.
USER: Well, let us go together.

USER: And you.
BOT: Direct me, if it be your will, Where great Aufidius lies. Is he in Antium?
USER: He is, and feasts the nobles of the state At his house this night.
BOT: Which is his house, beseech you?
USER: This here before you.

USER: Wine, wine, wine! What service is here? I think our fellows are asleep.
BOT: Where's Cotus? My master calls for him. Cotus!
USER: A goodly house. The feast smells well, but I Appear not like a guest.
BOT: What would you have, friend? Whence are you? Here's no place for you. Pray go to the door.
USER: I have deserved no better entertainment In being Coriolanus.
BOT: Whence are you, sir? - Has the porter his eyes in his head, that he gives entrance to such companions? - Pray, get you out.
USER: Away!
BOT: Away? Get you away.
USER: Now th' art troublesome.
BOT: Are you so brave? I'll have you talked with anon.
USER: What fellow's this?
BOT: A strange one as ever I looked on. I cannot get him out o' th' house. Prithee call my master to him.
USER: What have you to do here, fellow? Pray you, avoid the house.
BOT: Let me but stand. I will not hurt your hearth.
USER: What are you?
BOT: A gentleman.
USER: A marv'llous poor one.
BOT: True, so I am.
USER: Pray you, poor gentleman, take up some other station. Here's no place for you. Pray you, avoid. Come.
BOT: Follow your function, go, and batten on cold bits.
USER: What, you will not? - Prithee, tell my master what a strange guest he has here.
BOT: And I shall.
USER: Where dwell'st thou?
BOT: Under the canopy.
USER: Under the canopy?
BOT: Ay.
USER: Where's that?
BOT: I' th' city of kites and crows.
USER: I' th' city of kites and crows? What an ass it is! Then thou dwell'st with daws too?
BOT: No, I serve not thy master.
USER: How, sir? Do you meddle with my master?
BOT: Ay, 'tis an honester service than to meddle with thy mistress. Thou prat'st and prat'st. Serve with thy trencher, hence!
USER: Where is this fellow?
BOT: Here, sir. I'd have beaten him like a dog, but for disturbing the lords within.
USER: Whence com'st thou? What wouldst thou? Thy name? Why speak'st not? Speak, man. What's thy name?
BOT: Not yet thou know'st me, and, seeing me, dost not Think me for the man I am, necessity Commands me name myself.
USER: What is thy name?
BOT: A name unmusical to the Volscians' ears And harsh in sound to thine.
USER: Say, what's thy name? Thou has a grim appearance, and thy face Bears a command in't. Though thy tackle's torn, Thou show'st a noble vessel. What's thy name?
BOT: Prepare thy brow to frown. Know'st thou me yet?
USER: I know thee not. Thy name?
BOT: You bless me, gods!
USER: Here's a strange alteration!
BOT: By my hand, I had thought to have strucken him with a cudgel, and yet my mind gave me his clothes made a false report of him.
USER: What an arm he has! He turned me about with his finger and his thumb as one would set up a top.
BOT: Nay, I knew by his face that there was something in him. He had, sir, a kind of face, methought - I cannot tell how to term it.
USER: He had so, looking as it were - Would I were hanged, but I thought there was more in him than I could think.
BOT: So did I, I'll be sworn. He is simply the rarest man i' th' world.
USER: I think he is. But a greater soldier than he you wot one.
BOT: Who, my master?
USER: Nay, it's no matter for that.
BOT: Worth six on him.
USER: Nay, not so neither. But I take him to be the greater soldier.
BOT: Faith, look you, one cannot tell how to say that. For the defence of a town our general is excellent.
USER: Ay, and for an assault too.
BOT: O slaves, I can tell you news, news, you rascals! FIRST and SECOND SERVINGMAN. What, what, what? Let's partake.
USER: I would not be a Roman, of all nations; I had as lief be a condemned man. FIRST and SECOND SERVINGMAN. Wherefore? Wherefore?
BOT: Why, here's he that was wont to thwack our general, Caius Martius.
USER: Why do you say, "thwack our general"?
BOT: I do not say "thwack our general," but he was always good enough for him.
USER: Come, we are fellows and friends. He was ever too hard for him; I have heard him say so himself.
BOT: He was too hard for him directly, to say the troth on't, before Corioles; he scotched him and notched him like a carbonado.
USER: An he had been cannibally given, he might have boiled and eaten him too.
BOT: But, more of thy news?
USER: And he's as like to do't as any man I can imagine.
BOT: Do't? He will do't! For look you, sir, he has as many friends as enemies, which friends, sir, as it were, durst not, look you, sir, show themselves, as we term it, his friends whilest he's in directitude.
USER: Directitude? What's that?
BOT: But when they shall see, sir, his crest up again, and the man in blood, they will out of their burrows like coneys after rain, and revel all with him.
USER: But when goes this forward?
BOT: Tomorrow, today, presently. You shall have the drum struck up this afternoon. 'Tis as it were parcel of their feast, and to be executed ere they wipe their lips.
USER: Why then, we shall have a stirring world again. This peace is nothing but to rust iron, increase tailors, and breed ballad-makers.
BOT: 'Tis so, and as war in some sort, may be said to be a ravisher, so it cannot be denied but peace is a great maker of cuckolds.
USER: Ay, and it makes men hate one another.
BOT: Reason: because they then less need one another. The wars for my money! I hope to see Romans as cheap as Volscians. They are rising; they are rising.
USER: In, in, in, in!

USER: We stood to't in good time. Is this Menenius?
BOT: 'Tis he, 'tis he. O, he is grown most kind Of late. - Hail, sir!
USER: Hail to you both.
BOT: Your Coriolanus is not much missed But with his friends. The commonwealth doth stand, And so would do were he more angry at it.
USER: All's well, and might have been much better if He could have temporized.
BOT: Where is he, hear you?
USER: Nay, I hear nothing; His mother and his wife hear nothing from him.
BOT: The gods preserve you both!
USER: Good e'en, our neighbours.
BOT: Good e'en to you all, good e'en to you all.
USER: Ourselves, our wives, and children, on our knees Are bound to pray for you both.
BOT: Live and thrive!
USER: Farewell, kind neighbours. We wished Coriolanus Had loved you as we did.
BOT: Now the gods keep you!
USER: Farewell, farewell.
BOT: This is a happier and more comely time Than when these fellows ran about the streets Crying confusion.
USER: Caius Martius was A worthy officer i' th' war, but insolent, O'ercome with pride, ambitious, past all thinking Self-loving.
BOT: And affecting one sole throne, without assistance.
USER: I think not so.
BOT: We should by this, to all our lamentation, If he had gone forth consul, found it so.
USER: The gods have well prevented it, and Rome Sits safe and still without him.
BOT: Worthy tribunes, There is a slave, whom we have put in prison, Reports the Volsces with two several powers Are entered in the Roman territories, And with the deepest malice of the war Destroy what lies before 'em.
USER: 'Tis Aufidius, Who, hearing of our Martius' banishment, Thrusts forth his horns again into the world, Which were inshelled when Martius stood for Rome, And durst not once peep out.
BOT: Come, what talk you of Martius?
USER: Go see this rumourer whipped. It cannot be The Volsces dare break with us.
BOT: Tell not me. I know this cannot be.
USER: Not possible.
BOT: The nobles in great earnestness are going All to the Senate House. Some news is coming That turns their countenances.
USER: 'Tis this slave - Go whip him 'fore the people's eyes - his raising, Nothing but his report.
BOT: Yes, worthy sir, The slave's report is seconded, and more, More fearful, is delivered.
USER: What more fearful?
BOT: It is spoke freely out of many mouths - How probable I do not know - that Martius, Joined with Aufidius, leads a power 'gainst Rome And vows revenge as spacious as between The young'st and oldest thing.
USER: This is most likely!
BOT: Raised only that the weaker sort may wish Good Martius home again.
USER: The very trick on 't.
BOT: This is unlikely; He and Aufidius can no more atone Than violent'st contrariety.
USER: You are sent for to the Senate. A fearful army, led by Caius Martius Associated with Aufidius, rages Upon our territories, and have already O'erborne their way, consumed with fire and took What lay before them.
BOT: O, you have made good work!
USER: What news? What news?
BOT: You have holp to ravish your own daughters and To melt the city leads upon your pates, To see your wives dishonoured to your noses -
USER: What's the news? What's the news?
BOT: Your temples burned in their cement, and Your franchises, whereon you stood, confined Into an auger's bore.
USER: Pray now, your news? - You have made fair work, I fear me. - Pray, your news? If Martius should be joined with Volscians -
BOT: You have made good work, You and your apron-men, you that stood so much Upon the voice of occupation and The breath of garlic eaters!
USER: He'll shake your Rome about your ears.
BOT: As Hercules did shake down mellow fruit. You have made fair work.
USER: But is this true, sir?
BOT: We are all undone unless The noble man have mercy.
USER: 'Tis true. If he were putting to my house the brand That should consume it, I have not the face To say "Beseech you, cease." - You have made fair hands, You and your crafts! You have crafted fair!
BOT: You have brought A trembling upon Rome such as was never S' incapable of help.
USER: Say not we brought it.
BOT: How? Was it we? We loved him, but like beasts And cowardly nobles, gave way unto your clusters, Who did hoot him out o' th' city.
USER: But I fear They'll roar him in again. Tullus Aufidius, The second name of men, obeys his points As if he were his officer. Desperation Is all the policy, strength, and defence That Rome can make against them.
BOT: Faith, we hear fearful news.
USER: For mine own part, When I said banish him, I said 'twas pity.
BOT: And so did I.
USER: And so did I. And, to say the truth, so did very many of us. That we did we did for the best; and though we willingly consented to his banishment, yet it was against our will.
BOT: You are goodly things, you voices!
USER: You have made good work, you and your cry! - Shall's to the Capitol?
BOT: O, ay, what else?
USER: Go, masters, get you home. Be not dismayed. These are a side that would be glad to have This true which they so seem to fear. Go home, And show no sign of fear.
BOT: The gods be good to us! Come, masters, let's home. I ever said we were i' th' wrong when we banished him.
USER: So did we all. But, come, let's home.
BOT: I do not like this news.
USER: Nor I.
BOT: Let's to the Capitol. Would half my wealth Would buy this for a lie!
USER: Pray let's go.

USER: Do they still fly to th' Roman?
BOT: I do not know what witchcraft's in him, but Your soldiers use him as the grace 'fore meat, Their talk at table, and their thanks at end; And you are dark'ned in this action, sir, Even by your own.
USER: Yet I wish, sir - I mean for your particular - you had not Joined in commission with him, but either Had borne the action of yourself or else To him had left it solely.
BOT: Sir, I beseech you, think you he'll carry Rome?

USER: He would not seem to know me.
BOT: Do you hear?
USER: Why, so; you have made good work! A pair of tribunes that have wracked Rome To make coals cheap! A noble memory!
BOT: I minded him how royal 'twas to pardon When it was less expected. He replied It was a bare petition of a state To one whom they had punished.
USER: Very well. Could he say less?
BOT: For one poor grain or two! I am one of those! His mother, wife, his child, And this brave fellow too, we are the grains; You are the musty chaff, and you are smelt Above the moon. We must be burnt for you.
USER: No, I'll not meddle.
BOT: Pray you, go to him.
USER: What should I do?
BOT: Only make trial what your love can do For Rome, towards Martius.
USER: Well, and say that Martius Return me, as Cominius is returned, unheard, What then? But as a discontented friend, Grief-shot with his unkindness? Say't be so?
BOT: Yet your good will Must have that thanks from Rome after the measure As you intended well.
USER: You know the very road into his kindness And cannot lose your way.
BOT: Good faith, I'll prove him, Speed how it will. I shall ere long have knowledge Of my success.
USER: He'll never hear him.
BOT: Not?

USER: Stay! Whence are you?
BOT: Stand, and go back.
USER: You guard like men; 'tis well. But by your leave, I am an officer of state and come To speak with Coriolanus.
BOT: From whence?
USER: From Rome.
BOT: You may not pass; you must return. Our general Will no more hear from thence.
USER: You'll see your Rome embraced with fire before You'll speak with Coriolanus.
BOT: Good my friends, If you have heard your general talk of Rome And of his friends there, it is lots to blanks My name hath touched your ears. It is Menenius.
USER: Be it so; go back. The virtue of your name Is not here passable.
BOT: Faith, sir, if you had told as many lies in his behalf as you have uttered words in your own, you should not pass here, no, though it were as virtuous to lie as to live chastely. Therefore, go back.
USER: Prithee, fellow, remember my name is Menenius, always factionary on the party of your general.
BOT: Howsoever you have been his liar, as you say you have, I am one that, telling true under him, must say you cannot pass. Therefore go back.
USER: Has he dined, can'st thou tell? For I would not speak with him till after dinner.
BOT: You are a Roman, are you?
USER: I am, as thy general is.
BOT: Sirrah, if thy captain knew I were here, he would use me with estimation.
USER: Come, my captain knows you not.
BOT: I mean thy general.
USER: My general cares not for you. Back, I say, go, lest I let forth your half pint of blood. Back! That's the utmost of your having. Back!
BOT: Nay, but fellow, fellow -
USER: What's the matter?
BOT: Away!
USER: How? Away?
BOT: You keep a constant temper.
USER: Now, sir, is your name Menenius?
BOT: 'Tis a spell, you see, of much power. You know the way home again.
USER: Do you hear how we are shent for keeping your Greatness back?
BOT: What cause do you think I have to swoon?
USER: A noble fellow, I warrant him.
BOT: The worthy fellow is our general. He is the rock, the oak not to be wind-shaken.

USER: We will before the walls of Rome tomorrow Set down our host. My partner in this action, You must report to th' Volscian lords how plainly I have borne this business.
BOT: Only their ends You have respected, stopped your ears against The general suit of Rome; never admitted A private whisper, no, not with such friends That thought them sure of you.
USER: My lord and husband.
BOT: These eyes are not the same I wore in Rome.
USER: The sorrow that delivers us thus changed Makes you think so.
BOT: O, stand up blest, Whilst with no softer cushion than the flint I kneel before thee and unproperly Show duty, as mistaken all this while Between the child and parent.
USER: Thou art my warrior; I holp to frame thee. Do you know this lady?
BOT: The noble sister of Publicola, The moon of Rome, chaste as the icicle That's curdied by the frost from purest snow And hangs on Dian's temple! - Dear Valeria.
USER: This is a poor epitome of yours, Which by th' interpretation of full time May show like all yourself.
BOT: That's my brave boy!
USER: Even he, your wife, this lady, and myself Are suitors to you.
BOT: Aufidius, and you Volsces, mark, for we'll Hear naught from Rome in private. Your request?
USER: Ay, and mine, That brought you forth this boy to keep your name Living to time.
BOT: He shall not tread on me. I'll run away till I am bigger, but then I'll fight.
USER: Not of a woman's tenderness to be Requires nor child nor woman's face to see. - I have sat too long.

USER: I was moved withal.
BOT: At difference in thee. Out of that I'll work Myself a former fortune.

USER: See you yond coign o' the Capitol, yond cornerstone?
BOT: Why, what of that?
USER: Is't possible that so short a time can alter the condition of a man?
BOT: There is differency between a grub and a butterfly, yet your butterfly was a grub. This Martius is grown from man to dragon. He has wings; he's more than a creeping thing.
USER: He loved his mother dearly.
BOT: Yes, mercy, if you report him truly.
USER: I paint him in the character. Mark what mercy his mother shall bring from him. There is no more mercy in him than there is milk in a male tiger. That shall our poor city find, and all this is long of you.
BOT: The gods be good unto us.
USER: No, in such a case the gods will not be good unto us. When we banished him, we respected not them; and he returning to break our necks, they respect not us.
BOT: Sir, if you'd save your life, fly to your house. The plebeians have got your fellow tribune And hale him up and down, all swearing if The Roman ladies bring not comfort home, They'll give him death by inches.
USER: What's the news?
BOT: Good news, good news! The ladies have prevailed. The Volscians are dislodged and Martius gone. A merrier day did never yet greet Rome, No, not th' expulsion of the Tarquins.
USER: Friend, Art thou certain this is true? Is't most certain?
BOT: First, the gods bless you for your tidings; next, accept my thankfulness.
USER: Sir, we have all great cause to give great thanks.
BOT: They are near the city?
USER: Almost at point to enter.
BOT: We'll meet them, and help the joy.

USER: How is it with our general?
BOT: Even so As with a man by his own alms empoisoned And with his charity slain.
USER: Most noble sir, If you do hold the same intent wherein You wished us parties, we'll deliver you Of your great danger.
BOT: Sir, I cannot tell. We must proceed as we do find the people.
USER: The people will remain uncertain whilst 'Twixt you there's difference, but the fall of either Makes the survivor heir of all.
BOT: Sir, his stoutness When he did stand for consul, which he lost By lack of stooping -
USER: So he did, my lord. The army marvelled at it, and, in the last, When he had carried Rome and that we looked For no less spoil than glory -
BOT: Your native town you entered like a post And had no welcomes home, but he returns Splitting the air with noise.
USER: And patient fools, Whose children he hath slain, their base throats tear With giving him glory.
BOT: Say no more. Here come the lords.
USER: You are most welcome home.
BOT: I have not deserved it. But, worthy lords, have you with heed perused What I have written to you?
USER: We have.
BOT: He approaches. You shall hear him.
USER: Read it not, noble lords, But tell the traitor in the highest degree He hath abused your powers.
BOT: "Traitor?" How now?
USER: Ay, traitor, Martius.
BOT: Martius?
USER: Hear'st thou, Mars?
BOT: Name not the god, thou boy of tears.
USER: Ha?
BOT: No more.
USER: Peace, both, and hear me speak.
BOT: Why, noble lords, Will you be put in mind of his blind fortune, Which was your shame, by this unholy braggart, 'Fore your own eyes and ears?
USER: Let him die for't. ALL PEOPLE Tear him to pieces! Do it presently! He killed my son! My daughter! He killed my cousin Marcus! He killed my father!
BOT: Peace, ho! No outrage! Peace! The man is noble, and his fame folds in This orb o' th' Earth. His last offences to us Shall have judicious hearing. Stand, Aufidius, And trouble not the peace.
USER: O that I had him, With six Aufidiuses, or more, his tribe, To use my lawful sword.
BOT: Insolent villain!
USER: Kill, kill, kill, kill, kill him! on him._]
BOT: Hold, hold, hold, hold!
USER: My noble masters, hear me speak.
BOT: O Tullus!
USER: Thou hast done a deed whereat valour will weep.
BOT: Tread not upon him. - Masters, all be quiet. - Put up your swords.
USER: Bear from hence his body, And mourn you for him. Let him be regarded As the most noble corse that ever herald Did follow to his urn.
BOT: His own impatience Takes from Aufidius a great part of blame. Let's make the best of it.

USER: You do not meet a man but frowns; our bloods No more obey the heavens than our courtiers Still seem as does the King's.
BOT: But what's the matter?
USER: None but the King?
BOT: He that hath lost her too. So is the Queen, That most desir'd the match. But not a courtier, Although they wear their faces to the bent Of the King's looks, hath a heart that is not Glad at the thing they scowl at.
USER: And why so?
BOT: You speak him far.
USER: I do extend him, sir, within himself; Crush him together rather than unfold His measure duly.
BOT: What's his name and birth?
USER: I honour him Even out of your report. But pray you tell me, Is she sole child to th' King?
BOT: How long is this ago?
USER: Some twenty years.
BOT: That a king's children should be so convey'd, So slackly guarded, and the search so slow That could not trace them!
USER: Howsoe'er 'tis strange, Or that the negligence may well be laugh'd at, Yet is it true, sir.
BOT: I do well believe you.
USER: We must forbear; here comes the gentleman, The Queen, and Princess.

USER: Please your Highness, I will from hence today.
BOT: You know the peril. I'll fetch a turn about the garden, pitying The pangs of barr'd affections, though the King Hath charg'd you should not speak together.
USER: Should we be taking leave As long a term as yet we have to live, The loathness to depart would grow. Adieu!
BOT: Nay, stay a little. Were you but riding forth to air yourself, Such parting were too petty. Look here, love: This diamond was my mother's; take it, heart; But keep it till you woo another wife, When Imogen is dead.
USER: O the gods! When shall we see again?
BOT: Alack, the King!
USER: Thou basest thing, avoid; hence from my sight If after this command thou fraught the court With thy unworthiness, thou diest. Away! Thou'rt poison to my blood.
BOT: The gods protect you, And bless the good remainders of the court! I am gone.
USER: There cannot be a pinch in death More sharp than this is.
BOT: O disloyal thing, That shouldst repair my youth, thou heap'st A year's age on me!
USER: I beseech you, sir, Harm not yourself with your vexation. I am senseless of your wrath; a touch more rare Subdues all pangs, all fears.
BOT: Past grace? obedience?
USER: Past hope, and in despair; that way past grace.
BOT: That mightst have had the sole son of my queen!
USER: O blessed that I might not! I chose an eagle, And did avoid a puttock.
BOT: Thou took'st a beggar, wouldst have made my throne A seat for baseness.
USER: No; I rather added A lustre to it.
BOT: O thou vile one!
USER: Sir, It is your fault that I have lov'd Posthumus. You bred him as my playfellow, and he is A man worth any woman; overbuys me Almost the sum he pays.
BOT: What, art thou mad?
USER: Almost, sir. Heaven restore me! Would I were A neat-herd's daughter, and my Leonatus Our neighbour shepherd's son!
BOT: Thou foolish thing! Not after our command. Away with her, And pen her up.
USER: Beseech your patience. Peace, Dear lady daughter, peace! - Sweet sovereign, Leave us to ourselves, and make yourself some comfort Out of your best advice.
BOT: Nay, let her languish A drop of blood a day and, being aged, Die of this folly.
USER: Fie! you must give way. Here is your servant. How now, sir! What news?
BOT: My lord your son drew on my master.
USER: Ha! No harm, I trust, is done?
BOT: There might have been, But that my master rather play'd than fought, And had no help of anger; they were parted By gentlemen at hand.
USER: I am very glad on't.
BOT: Your son's my father's friend; he takes his part To draw upon an exile! O brave sir! I would they were in Afric both together; Myself by with a needle, that I might prick The goer-back. Why came you from your master?
USER: On his command. He would not suffer me To bring him to the haven; left these notes Of what commands I should be subject to, When't pleas'd you to employ me.
BOT: This hath been Your faithful servant. I dare lay mine honour He will remain so.
USER: I humbly thank your Highness.
BOT: Pray walk awhile.
USER: About some half-hour hence, Pray you speak with me. You shall at least go see my lord aboard. For this time leave me.

USER: Sir, I would advise you to shift a shirt; the violence of action hath made you reek as a sacrifice. Where air comes out, air comes in; there's none abroad so wholesome as that you vent.
BOT: If my shirt were bloody, then to shift it. Have I hurt him?
USER: Hurt him! His body's a passable carcass if he be not hurt. It is a throughfare for steel if it be not hurt.
BOT: The villain would not stand me.
USER: Stand you? You have land enough of your own; but he added to your having, gave you some ground.
BOT: Puppies!
USER: I would they had not come between us.
BOT: upon the ground.
USER: And that she should love this fellow, and refuse me!
BOT: Sir, as I told you always, her beauty and her brain go not together; she's a good sign, but I have seen small reflection of her wit.
USER: her.
BOT: Come, I'll to my chamber. Would there had been some hurt done!
USER: is no great hurt.
BOT: You'll go with us?
USER: I'll attend your lordship.
BOT: Nay, come, let's go together.
USER: Well, my lord.

USER: I would thou grew'st unto the shores o' th' haven, And questioned'st every sail; if he should write, And I not have it, 'twere a paper lost, As offer'd mercy is. What was the last That he spake to thee?
BOT: It was: his queen, his queen!
USER: Then wav'd his handkerchief?
BOT: And kiss'd it, madam.
USER: Senseless linen, happier therein than I! And that was all?
BOT: Thou shouldst have made him As little as a crow, or less, ere left To after-eye him.
USER: Madam, so I did.
BOT: Be assur'd, madam, With his next vantage.
USER: The Queen, madam, Desires your Highness' company.
BOT: Those things I bid you do, get them dispatch'd. I will attend the Queen.
USER: Madam, I shall.

USER: You speak of him when he was less furnish'd than now he is with that which makes him both without and within.
BOT: I have seen him in France; we had very many there could behold the sun with as firm eyes as he.
USER: This matter of marrying his king's daughter, wherein he must be weighed rather by her value than his own, words him, I doubt not, a great deal from the matter.
BOT: And then his banishment.
USER: Sir, we have known together in Orleans.
BOT: Since when I have been debtor to you for courtesies, which I will be ever to pay and yet pay still.
USER: Faith, yes, to be put to the arbitrement of swords, and by such two that would by all likelihood have confounded one the other or have fall'n both.
BOT: Can we, with manners, ask what was the difference?
USER: That lady is not now living, or this gentleman's opinion, by this, worn out.
BOT: She holds her virtue still, and I my mind.
USER: You must not so far prefer her 'fore ours of Italy.
BOT: Being so far provok'd as I was in France, I would abate her nothing, though I profess myself her adorer, not her friend.
USER: I prais'd her as I rated her. So do I my stone.
BOT: What do you esteem it at?
USER: More than the world enjoys.
BOT: Either your unparagon'd mistress is dead, or she's outpriz'd by a trifle.
USER: You are mistaken: the one may be sold or given, if there were wealth enough for the purchase or merit for the gift; the other is not a thing for sale, and only the gift of the gods.
BOT: Which the gods have given you?
USER: Which by their graces I will keep.
BOT: Let us leave here, gentlemen.
USER: Sir, with all my heart. This worthy signior, I thank him, makes no stranger of me; we are familiar at first.
BOT: With five times so much conversation I should get ground of your fair mistress; make her go back even to the yielding, had I admittance and opportunity to friend.
USER: No, no.
BOT: You are a great deal abus'd in too bold a persuasion, and I doubt not you sustain what y'are worthy of by your attempt.
USER: What's that?
BOT: A repulse; though your attempt, as you call it, deserve more; a punishment too.
USER: Gentlemen, enough of this. It came in too suddenly; let it die as it was born, and I pray you be better acquainted.
BOT: Would I had put my estate and my neighbour's on th' approbation of what I have spoke!
USER: What lady would you choose to assail?
BOT: I will wage against your gold, gold to it. My ring I hold dear as my finger; 'tis part of it.
USER: You are a friend, and therein the wiser. If you buy ladies' flesh at a million a dram, you cannot preserve it from tainting. But I see you have some religion in you, that you fear.
BOT: This is but a custom in your tongue; you bear a graver purpose, I hope.
USER: I am the master of my speeches, and would undergo what's spoken, I swear.
BOT: Will you? I shall but lend my diamond till your return. Let there be covenants drawn between's. My mistress exceeds in goodness the hugeness of your unworthy thinking. I dare you to this match: here's my ring.
USER: I will have it no lay.
BOT: Your hand, a covenant! We will have these things set down by lawful counsel, and straight away for Britain, lest the bargain should catch cold and starve. I will fetch my gold and have our two wagers recorded.
USER: Agreed.
BOT: Will this hold, think you?
USER: Signior Iachimo will not from it. Pray let us follow 'em.

USER: Whiles yet the dew's on ground, gather those flowers; Make haste; who has the note of them?
BOT: I, madam.
USER: Dispatch. Now, Master Doctor, have you brought those drugs?
BOT: Your Highness Shall from this practice but make hard your heart; Besides, the seeing these effects will be Both noisome and infectious.
USER: O, content thee. Will I first work. He's for his master, An enemy to my son. How now, Pisanio! Doctor, your service for this time is ended; Take your own way.
BOT: But you shall do no harm.
USER: No further service, Doctor, Until I send for thee.
BOT: I humbly take my leave.
USER: And shall do. But when to my good lord I prove untrue I'll choke myself: there's all I'll do for you.

USER: Madam, a noble gentleman of Rome Comes from my lord with letters.
BOT: Change you, madam? The worthy Leonatus is in safety, And greets your Highness dearly.
USER: Thanks, good sir. You're kindly welcome.
BOT: What makes your admiration?
USER: What is the matter, trow?
BOT: The cloyed will - That satiate yet unsatisfied desire, that tub Both fill'd and running - ravening first the lamb, Longs after for the garbage.
USER: What, dear sir, Thus raps you? Are you well?
BOT: Thanks, madam; well. Beseech you, sir, Desire my man's abode where I did leave him. He's strange and peevish.
USER: I was going, sir, To give him welcome.
BOT: Continues well my lord? His health beseech you?
USER: Well, madam.
BOT: Is he dispos'd to mirth? I hope he is.
USER: Exceeding pleasant; none a stranger there So merry and so gamesome. He is call'd The Briton reveller.
BOT: When he was here He did incline to sadness, and oft-times Not knowing why.
USER: Will my lord say so?
BOT: Ay, madam, with his eyes in flood with laughter. It is a recreation to be by And hear him mock the Frenchman. But heavens know Some men are much to blame.
USER: Not he, I hope.
BOT: Not he; but yet heaven's bounty towards him might Be us'd more thankfully. In himself, 'tis much; In you, which I account his, beyond all talents. Whilst I am bound to wonder, I am bound To pity too.
USER: What do you pity, sir?
BOT: Two creatures heartily.
USER: Am I one, sir? You look on me: what wreck discern you in me Deserves your pity?
BOT: Lamentable! What, To hide me from the radiant sun and solace I' th' dungeon by a snuff?
USER: I pray you, sir, Deliver with more openness your answers To my demands. Why do you pity me?
BOT: That others do, I was about to say, enjoy your - But It is an office of the gods to venge it, Not mine to speak on't.
USER: My lord, I fear, Has forgot Britain.
BOT: And himself. Not I Inclin'd to this intelligence pronounce The beggary of his change; but 'tis your graces That from my mutest conscience to my tongue Charms this report out.
USER: Let me hear no more.
BOT: Reveng'd? How should I be reveng'd? If this be true, (As I have such a heart that both mine ears Must not in haste abuse) if it be true, How should I be reveng'd?
USER: What ho, Pisanio!
BOT: Let me my service tender on your lips.
USER: You make amends.
BOT: All's well, sir; take my pow'r i' th' court for yours.
USER: My humble thanks. I had almost forgot T' entreat your Grace but in a small request, And yet of moment too, for it concerns Your lord; myself and other noble friends Are partners in the business.
BOT: Pray what is't?
USER: Willingly; And pawn mine honour for their safety. Since My lord hath interest in them, I will keep them In my bedchamber.
BOT: They are in a trunk, Attended by my men. I will make bold To send them to you only for this night; I must aboard tomorrow.
USER: O, no, no.
BOT: Yes, I beseech; or I shall short my word By length'ning my return. From Gallia I cross'd the seas on purpose and on promise To see your Grace.
USER: I thank you for your pains. But not away tomorrow!
BOT: O, I must, madam. Therefore I shall beseech you, if you please To greet your lord with writing, do't tonight. I have outstood my time, which is material To th' tender of our present.
USER: I will write. Send your trunk to me; it shall safe be kept And truly yielded you. You're very welcome.

USER: What got he by that? You have broke his pate with your bowl.
BOT: run all out.
USER: When a gentleman is dispos'd to swear, it is not for any standers-by to curtail his oaths. Ha?
BOT: No, my lord; [_Aside._] nor crop the ears of them.
USER: Whoreson dog! I gave him satisfaction. Would he had been one of my rank!
BOT: comb on.
USER: Sayest thou?
BOT: It is not fit your lordship should undertake every companion that you give offence to.
USER: No, I know that; but it is fit I should commit offence to my inferiors.
BOT: Ay, it is fit for your lordship only.
USER: Why, so I say.
BOT: Did you hear of a stranger that's come to court tonight?
USER: A stranger, and I not known on't?
BOT: There's an Italian come, and, 'tis thought, one of Leonatus' friends.
USER: Leonatus? A banish'd rascal; and he's another, whatsoever he be. Who told you of this stranger?
BOT: One of your lordship's pages.
USER: Is it fit I went to look upon him? Is there no derogation in't?
BOT: You cannot derogate, my lord.
USER: Not easily, I think.
BOT: foolish, do not derogate.
USER: Come, I'll go see this Italian. What I have lost today at bowls I'll win tonight of him. Come, go.

USER: Who's there? My woman Helen?
BOT: Please you, madam.
USER: What hour is it?
BOT: Almost midnight, madam.

USER: Your lordship is the most patient man in loss, the most coldest that ever turn'd up ace.
BOT: It would make any man cold to lose.
USER: But not every man patient after the noble temper of your lordship. You are most hot and furious when you win.
BOT: Winning will put any man into courage. If I could get this foolish Imogen, I should have gold enough. It's almost morning, is't not?
USER: Day, my lord.
BOT: So, get you gone. If this penetrate, I will consider your music the better; if it do not, it is a vice in her ears which horsehairs and calves' guts, nor the voice of unpaved eunuch to boot, can never amend.
USER: Here comes the King.
BOT: I am glad I was up so late, for that's the reason I was up so early. He cannot choose but take this service I have done fatherly. - Good morrow to your Majesty and to my gracious mother.
USER: Attend you here the door of our stern daughter? Will she not forth?
BOT: I have assail'd her with musics, but she vouchsafes no notice.
USER: The exile of her minion is too new; She hath not yet forgot him; some more time Must wear the print of his remembrance on't, And then she's yours.
BOT: Senseless? Not so.
USER: So like you, sir, ambassadors from Rome; The one is Caius Lucius.
BOT: Who's there that knocks?
USER: A gentleman.
BOT: No more?
USER: Yes, and a gentlewoman's son.
BOT: That's more Than some whose tailors are as dear as yours Can justly boast of. What's your lordship's pleasure?
USER: Your lady's person; is she ready?
BOT: Ay, To keep her chamber.
USER: There is gold for you; sell me your good report.
BOT: How? My good name? or to report of you What I shall think is good? The Princess!
USER: Good morrow, fairest sister. Your sweet hand.
BOT: Good morrow, sir. You lay out too much pains For purchasing but trouble. The thanks I give Is telling you that I am poor of thanks, And scarce can spare them.
USER: Still I swear I love you.
BOT: If you but said so, 'twere as deep with me. If you swear still, your recompense is still That I regard it not.
USER: This is no answer.
BOT: But that you shall not say I yield, being silent, I would not speak. I pray you spare me. Faith, I shall unfold equal discourtesy To your best kindness; one of your great knowing Should learn, being taught, forbearance.
USER: To leave you in your madness 'twere my sin; I will not.
BOT: Fools are not mad folks.
USER: Do you call me fool?
BOT: The south fog rot him!
USER: 'His garment'! Now the devil -
BOT: To Dorothy my woman hie thee presently.
USER: 'His garment'!
BOT: 'Twill not be lost.
USER: I hope so. Go and search.
BOT: You have abus'd me. 'His meanest garment'!
USER: Ay, I said so, sir. If you will make 't an action, call witness to 't.
BOT: I will inform your father.
USER: Your mother too. She's my good lady and will conceive, I hope, But the worst of me. So I leave you, sir, To th' worst of discontent.
BOT: I'll be reveng'd. 'His mean'st garment'! Well.

USER: Fear it not, sir; I would I were so sure To win the King as I am bold her honour Will remain hers.
BOT: What means do you make to him?
USER: Not any; but abide the change of time, Quake in the present winter's state, and wish That warmer days would come. In these fear'd hopes I barely gratify your love; they failing, I must die much your debtor.
BOT: See! Iachimo!
USER: The swiftest harts have posted you by land, And winds of all the corners kiss'd your sails, To make your vessel nimble.
BOT: Welcome, sir.
USER: I hope the briefness of your answer made The speediness of your return.
BOT: Your lady Is one of the fairest that I have look'd upon.
USER: And therewithal the best; or let her beauty Look through a casement to allure false hearts, And be false with them.
BOT: Here are letters for you.
USER: Their tenour good, I trust.
BOT: 'Tis very like.
USER: Was Caius Lucius in the Britain court When you were there?
BOT: He was expected then, But not approach'd.
USER: All is well yet. Sparkles this stone as it was wont, or is't not Too dull for your good wearing?
BOT: If I have lost it, I should have lost the worth of it in gold. I'll make a journey twice as far t' enjoy A second night of such sweet shortness which Was mine in Britain; for the ring is won.
USER: The stone's too hard to come by.
BOT: Not a whit, Your lady being so easy.
USER: Make not, sir, Your loss your sport. I hope you know that we Must not continue friends.
BOT: Proceed.
USER: This is true; And this you might have heard of here, by me Or by some other.
BOT: More particulars Must justify my knowledge.
USER: So they must, Or do your honour injury.
BOT: The chimney Is south the chamber, and the chimneypiece Chaste Dian bathing. Never saw I figures So likely to report themselves. The cutter Was as another nature, dumb; outwent her, Motion and breath left out.
USER: This is a thing Which you might from relation likewise reap, Being, as it is, much spoke of.
BOT: The roof o' th' chamber With golden cherubins is fretted; her andirons (I had forgot them) were two winking Cupids Of silver, each on one foot standing, nicely Depending on their brands.
USER: This is her honour! Let it be granted you have seen all this, and praise Be given to your remembrance; the description Of what is in her chamber nothing saves The wager you have laid.
BOT: Then, if you can, [_Shows the bracelet_] Be pale. I beg but leave to air this jewel. See! And now 'tis up again. It must be married To that your diamond; I'll keep them.
USER: Jove! Once more let me behold it. Is it that Which I left with her?
BOT: Sir (I thank her) that. She stripp'd it from her arm; I see her yet; Her pretty action did outsell her gift, And yet enrich'd it too. She gave it me, and said She priz'd it once.
USER: May be she pluck'd it off To send it me.
BOT: She writes so to you, doth she?
USER: Have patience, sir, And take your ring again; 'tis not yet won. It may be probable she lost it, or Who knows if one her women, being corrupted Hath stol'n it from her?
BOT: Very true; And so I hope he came by't. Back my ring. Render to me some corporal sign about her, More evident than this; for this was stol'n.
USER: By Jupiter, I had it from her arm!
BOT: Sir, be patient; This is not strong enough to be believ'd Of one persuaded well of.
USER: Never talk on't; She hath been colted by him.
BOT: Ay, and it doth confirm Another stain, as big as hell can hold, Were there no more but it.
USER: Will you hear more?
BOT: Spare your arithmetic; never count the turns. Once, and a million!
USER: I'll be sworn -
BOT: No swearing. If you will swear you have not done't, you lie; And I will kill thee if thou dost deny Thou'st made me cuckold.
USER: I'll deny nothing.
BOT: O that I had her here to tear her limb-meal! I will go there and do't, i' th' court, before Her father. I'll do something -
USER: Quite besides The government of patience! You have won. Let's follow him and pervert the present wrath He hath against himself.
BOT: With all my heart.

USER: Now say, what would Augustus Cæsar with us?
BOT: And, to kill the marvel, Shall be so ever.
USER: There be many Cæsars ere such another Julius. Britain is a world by itself, and we will nothing pay for wearing our own noses.
BOT: Come, there's no more tribute to be paid. Our kingdom is stronger than it was at that time; and, as I said, there is no moe such Cæsars. Other of them may have crook'd noses; but to owe such straight arms, none.
USER: Son, let your mother end.
BOT: We do.
USER: Let proof speak.
BOT: So, sir.
USER: I know your master's pleasure, and he mine; All the remain is, welcome.

USER: How now, Pisanio?
BOT: Madam, here is a letter from my lord.
USER: One score 'twixt sun and sun, Madam, 's enough for you, and too much too.
BOT: Madam, you're best consider.
USER: I see before me, man. Nor here, nor here, Nor what ensues, but have a fog in them That I cannot look through. Away, I prithee; Do as I bid thee. There's no more to say. Accessible is none but Milford way.

USER: Hail, heaven!
BOT: Hail, heaven!
USER: Uncertain favour!

USER: Please you read, And you shall find me, wretched man, a thing The most disdain'd of fortune.
BOT: Alas, good lady!
USER: Good madam, hear me.
BOT: Hence, vile instrument! Thou shalt not damn my hand.
USER: O gracious lady, Since I receiv'd command to do this busines I have not slept one wink.
BOT: Do't, and to bed then.
USER: I'll wake mine eyeballs first.
BOT: But to win time To lose so bad employment, in the which I have consider'd of a course. Good lady, Hear me with patience.
USER: Talk thy tongue weary, speak. I have heard I am a strumpet, and mine ear, Therein false struck, can take no greater wound, Nor tent to bottom that. But speak.
BOT: Then, madam, I thought you would not back again.
USER: Most like, Bringing me here to kill me.
BOT: Not so, neither; But if I were as wise as honest, then My purpose would prove well. It cannot be But that my master is abus'd. Some villain, Ay, and singular in his art, hath done you both This cursed injury.
USER: Some Roman courtezan!
BOT: No, on my life! I'll give but notice you are dead, and send him Some bloody sign of it, for 'tis commanded I should do so. You shall be miss'd at court, And that will well confirm it.
USER: Why, good fellow, What shall I do the while? Where bide? How live? Or in my life what comfort, when I am Dead to my husband?
BOT: If you'll back to th' court -
USER: No court, no father, nor no more ado With that harsh, noble, simple nothing, That Cloten, whose love-suit hath been to me As fearful as a siege.
BOT: If not at court, Then not in Britain must you bide.
USER: O! for such means, Though peril to my modesty, not death on't, I would adventure.
BOT: Nay, be brief; I see into thy end, and am almost A man already.
USER: Amen. I thank thee.

USER: Thus far, and so farewell.
BOT: Thanks, royal sir. My emperor hath wrote; I must from hence, And am right sorry that I must report ye My master's enemy.
USER: Our subjects, sir, Will not endure his yoke; and for ourself To show less sovereignty than they, must needs Appear unkinglike.
BOT: So, sir. I desire of you A conduct overland to Milford Haven. Madam, all joy befall your Grace, and you!
USER: My lords, you are appointed for that office; The due of honour in no point omit. So farewell, noble Lucius.
BOT: Your hand, my lord.
USER: Receive it friendly; but from this time forth I wear it as your enemy.
BOT: Sir, the event Is yet to name the winner. Fare you well.
USER: Leave not the worthy Lucius, good my lords, Till he have cross'd the Severn. Happiness!
BOT: He goes hence frowning; but it honours us That we have given him cause.
USER: 'Tis all the better; Your valiant Britons have their wishes in it.
BOT: 'Tis not sleepy business, But must be look'd to speedily and strongly.
USER: Where is she, sir? How Can her contempt be answer'd?
BOT: Please you, sir, Her chambers are all lock'd, and there's no answer That will be given to th' loud of noise we make.
USER: Her doors lock'd? Not seen of late? Grant, heavens, that which I fear Prove false!
BOT: Son, I say, follow the King.
USER: That man of hers, Pisanio, her old servant, I have not seen these two days.
BOT: 'Tis certain she is fled. Go in and cheer the King. He rages; none Dare come about him.
USER: All the better. May This night forestall him of the coming day!
BOT: O good my lord!
USER: Alas, my lord, How can she be with him? When was she miss'd? He is in Rome.
BOT: Where is she, sir? Come nearer. No farther halting! Satisfy me home What is become of her.
USER: O my all-worthy lord!
BOT: All-worthy villain! Discover where thy mistress is at once, At the next word. No more of 'worthy lord'! Speak, or thy silence on the instant is Thy condemnation and thy death.
USER: Then, sir, This paper is the history of my knowledge Touching her flight.
BOT: Let's see't. I will pursue her Even to Augustus' throne.
USER: She's far enough; and what he learns by this May prove his travel, not her danger.
BOT: Humh!
USER: Safe mayst thou wander, safe return again!
BOT: Sirrah, is this letter true?
USER: Sir, as I think.
BOT: Well, my good lord.
USER: Wilt thou serve me? For since patiently and constantly thou hast stuck to the bare fortune of that beggar Posthumus, thou canst not, in the course of gratitude, but be a diligent follower of mine. Wilt thou serve me?
BOT: Sir, I will.
USER: Give me thy hand; here's my purse. Hast any of thy late master's garments in thy possession?
BOT: I have, my lord, at my lodging, the same suit he wore when he took leave of my lady and mistress.
USER: The first service thou dost me, fetch that suit hither. Let it be thy first service; go.
BOT: I shall, my lord.
USER: Ay, my noble lord.
BOT: How long is't since she went to Milford Haven?
USER: She can scarce be there yet.

USER: I am thoroughly weary.
BOT: I am weak with toil, yet strong in appetite.
USER: There is cold meat i' th' cave; we'll browse on that Whilst what we have kill'd be cook'd.
BOT: But that it eats our victuals, I should think Here were a fairy.
USER: What's the matter, sir?
BOT: By Jupiter, an angel! or, if not, An earthly paragon! Behold divineness No elder than a boy!
USER: Money, youth?
BOT: All gold and silver rather turn to dirt, As 'tis no better reckon'd but of those Who worship dirty gods.
USER: I see you're angry. Know, if you kill me for my fault, I should Have died had I not made it.
BOT: Whither bound?
USER: To Milford Haven.
BOT: What's your name?
USER: Fidele, sir. I have a kinsman who Is bound for Italy; he embark'd at Milford; To whom being going, almost spent with hunger, I am fall'n in this offence.
BOT: Were you a woman, youth, I should woo hard but be your groom. In honesty I bid for you as I'd buy.
USER: I'll make't my comfort He is a man. I'll love him as my brother; And such a welcome as I'd give to him After long absence, such is yours. Most welcome! Be sprightly, for you fall 'mongst friends.
BOT: 'Mongst friends, If brothers. [_Aside._] Would it had been so that they Had been my father's sons! Then had my prize Been less, and so more equal ballasting To thee, Posthumus.
USER: He wrings at some distress.
BOT: Would I could free't!
USER: Or I, whate'er it be, What pain it cost, what danger! Gods!
BOT: It shall be so. Boys, we'll go dress our hunt. Fair youth, come in. Discourse is heavy, fasting; when we have supp'd, We'll mannerly demand thee of thy story, So far as thou wilt speak it.
USER: Pray draw near.
BOT: The night to th' owl and morn to th' lark less welcome.
USER: Thanks, sir.
BOT: I pray draw near.

USER: Is Lucius general of the forces?
BOT: Ay.
USER: Remaining now in Gallia?
BOT: With those legions Which I have spoke of, whereunto your levy Must be supplyant. The words of your commission Will tie you to the numbers and the time Of their dispatch.
USER: We will discharge our duty.

USER: We'll come to you after hunting.
BOT: Are we not brothers?
USER: So man and man should be; But clay and clay differs in dignity, Whose dust is both alike. I am very sick.
BOT: Go you to hunting; I'll abide with him.
USER: I love thee; I have spoke it. How much the quantity, the weight as much As I do love my father.
BOT: What? how? how?
USER: Brother, farewell.
BOT: I wish ye sport.
USER: Your health. [_To Belarius._] So please you, sir.
BOT: I could not stir him. He said he was gentle, but unfortunate; Dishonestly afflicted, but yet honest.
USER: Thus did he answer me; yet said hereafter I might know more.
BOT: To th' field, to th' field! We'll leave you for this time. Go in and rest.
USER: We'll not be long away.
BOT: Pray be not sick, For you must be our huswife.
USER: Well, or ill, I am bound to you.
BOT: And shalt be ever. This youth, howe'er distress'd, appears he hath had Good ancestors.
USER: How angel-like he sings!
BOT: But his neat cookery! He cut our roots in characters, And sauc'd our broths as Juno had been sick, And he her dieter.
USER: Nobly he yokes A smiling with a sigh, as if the sigh Was that it was for not being such a smile; The smile mocking the sigh that it would fly From so divine a temple to commix With winds that sailors rail at.
BOT: I do note That grief and patience, rooted in him both, Mingle their spurs together.
USER: Grow patience! And let the stinking elder, grief, untwine His perishing root with the increasing vine!
BOT: It is great morning. Come, away! Who's there?
USER: I cannot find those runagates; that villain Hath mock'd me. I am faint.
BOT: Those runagates? Means he not us? I partly know him; 'tis Cloten, the son o' th' Queen. I fear some ambush. I saw him not these many years, and yet I know 'tis he. We are held as outlaws. Hence!
USER: He is but one; you and my brother search What companies are near. Pray you away; Let me alone with him.
BOT: Soft! What are you That fly me thus? Some villain mountaineers? I have heard of such. What slave art thou?
USER: A thing More slavish did I ne'er than answering A slave without a knock.
BOT: Thou art a robber, A law-breaker, a villain. Yield thee, thief.
USER: To who? To thee? What art thou? Have not I An arm as big as thine, a heart as big? Thy words, I grant, are bigger, for I wear not My dagger in my mouth. Say what thou art; Why I should yield to thee.
BOT: Thou villain base, Know'st me not by my clothes?
USER: No, nor thy tailor, rascal, Who is thy grandfather; he made those clothes, Which, as it seems, make thee.
BOT: Thou precious varlet, My tailor made them not.
USER: Hence, then, and thank The man that gave them thee. Thou art some fool; I am loath to beat thee.
BOT: Thou injurious thief, Hear but my name, and tremble.
USER: What's thy name?
BOT: Cloten, thou villain.
USER: Cloten, thou double villain, be thy name, I cannot tremble at it. Were it Toad, or Adder, Spider, 'Twould move me sooner.
BOT: To thy further fear, Nay, to thy mere confusion, thou shalt know I am son to th' Queen.
USER: I'm sorry for't; not seeming So worthy as thy birth.
BOT: Art not afeard?
USER: Those that I reverence, those I fear - the wise; At fools I laugh, not fear them.
BOT: Die the death. When I have slain thee with my proper hand, I'll follow those that even now fled hence, And on the gates of Lud's Town set your heads. Yield, rustic mountaineer.
USER: No company's abroad?
BOT: None in the world; you did mistake him, sure.
USER: I cannot tell; long is it since I saw him, But time hath nothing blurr'd those lines of favour Which then he wore; the snatches in his voice, And burst of speaking, were as his. I am absolute 'Twas very Cloten.
BOT: In this place we left them. I wish my brother make good time with him, You say he is so fell.
USER: Being scarce made up, I mean to man, he had not apprehension Or roaring terrors; for defect of judgement Is oft the cease of fear. But, see, thy brother.
BOT: This Cloten was a fool, an empty purse; There was no money in't. Not Hercules Could have knock'd out his brains, for he had none; Yet I not doing this, the fool had borne My head as I do his.
USER: What hast thou done?
BOT: We are all undone.
USER: Let ordinance Come as the gods foresay it. Howsoe'er, My brother hath done well.
BOT: I had no mind To hunt this day; the boy Fidele's sickness Did make my way long forth.
USER: I fear 'twill be reveng'd. Would, Polydore, thou hadst not done't! though valour Becomes thee well enough.
BOT: Well, 'tis done. We'll hunt no more today, nor seek for danger Where there's no profit. I prithee to our rock. You and Fidele play the cooks; I'll stay Till hasty Polydore return, and bring him To dinner presently.
USER: Poor sick Fidele! I'll willingly to him; to gain his colour I'd let a parish of such Cloten's blood, And praise myself for charity.
BOT: Where's my brother? I have sent Cloten's clotpoll down the stream, In embassy to his mother; his body's hostage For his return.
USER: My ingenious instrument! Hark, Polydore, it sounds. But what occasion Hath Cadwal now to give it motion? Hark!
BOT: Is he at home?
USER: He went hence even now.
BOT: Look, here he comes, And brings the dire occasion in his arms Of what we blame him for!
USER: The bird is dead That we have made so much on. I had rather Have skipp'd from sixteen years of age to sixty, To have turn'd my leaping time into a crutch, Than have seen this.
BOT: O sweetest, fairest lily! My brother wears thee not the one half so well As when thou grew'st thyself.
USER: Stark, as you see; Thus smiling, as some fly had tickled slumber, Not as death's dart, being laugh'd at; his right cheek Reposing on a cushion.
BOT: Where?
USER: O' th' floor; His arms thus leagu'd. I thought he slept, and put My clouted brogues from off my feet, whose rudeness Answer'd my steps too loud.
BOT: Why, he but sleeps. If he be gone he'll make his grave a bed; With female fairies will his tomb be haunted, And worms will not come to thee.
USER: Prithee have done, And do not play in wench-like words with that Which is so serious. Let us bury him, And not protract with admiration what Is now due debt. To th' grave.
BOT: Say, where shall's lay him?
USER: By good Euriphile, our mother.
BOT: Be't so; And let us, Polydore, though now our voices Have got the mannish crack, sing him to th' ground, As once to our mother; use like note and words, Save that Euriphile must be Fidele.
USER: Cadwal, I cannot sing. I'll weep, and word it with thee; For notes of sorrow out of tune are worse Than priests and fanes that lie.
BOT: We'll speak it, then.
USER: Pray you fetch him hither. Thersites' body is as good as Ajax', When neither are alive.
