BOT: If you'll go fetch him, We'll say our song the whilst. Brother, begin.
USER: Nay, Cadwal, we must lay his head to th' East; My father hath a reason for't.
BOT: 'Tis true.
USER: Come on, then, and remove him.
BOT: So. Begin. SONG
USER: _ Fear no more the heat o' th' sun, Nor the furious winter's rages; Thou thy worldly task hast done, Home art gone, and ta'en thy wages. Golden lads and girls all must, As chimney-sweepers, come to dust._
BOT: _ Fear no more the frown o' th' great; Thou art past the tyrant's stroke. Care no more to clothe and eat; To thee the reed is as the oak. The sceptre, learning, physic, must All follow this and come to dust._
USER: _ Fear no more the lightning flash._
BOT: _ Nor th' all-dreaded thunder-stone._
USER: _ Fear not slander, censure rash;_
BOT: _ Thou hast finish'd joy and moan._
USER: _ All lovers young, all lovers must Consign to thee and come to dust._
BOT: _ No exorciser harm thee!_
USER: _ Nor no witchcraft charm thee!_
BOT: _ Ghost unlaid forbear thee!_
USER: _ Nothing ill come near thee!_
BOT: _ Quiet consummation have, And renowned be thy grave!_
USER: We have done our obsequies. Come, lay him down.
BOT: To them the legions garrison'd in Gallia, After your will, have cross'd the sea, attending You here at Milford Haven; with your ships, They are in readiness.
USER: But what from Rome?
BOT: The Senate hath stirr'd up the confiners And gentlemen of Italy, most willing spirits, That promise noble service; and they come Under the conduct of bold Iachimo, Sienna's brother.
USER: When expect you them?
BOT: With the next benefit o' th' wind.
USER: This forwardness Makes our hopes fair. Command our present numbers Be muster'd; bid the captains look to't. Now, sir, What have you dream'd of late of this war's purpose?
BOT: He's alive, my lord.
USER: 'Lack, good youth! Thou mov'st no less with thy complaining than Thy master in bleeding. Say his name, good friend.
BOT: Richard du Champ. [_Aside._] If I do lie, and do No harm by it, though the gods hear, I hope They'll pardon it. - Say you, sir?
USER: Thy name?
BOT: Fidele, sir.

USER: Sir, my life is yours; I humbly set it at your will; but for my mistress, I nothing know where she remains, why gone, Nor when she purposes return. Beseech your Highness, Hold me your loyal servant.
BOT: Good my liege, The day that she was missing he was here. I dare be bound he's true and shall perform All parts of his subjection loyally. For Cloten, There wants no diligence in seeking him, And will no doubt be found.
USER: The time is troublesome. Does yet depend.
BOT: So please your Majesty, The Roman legions, all from Gallia drawn, Are landed on your coast, with a supply Of Roman gentlemen by the Senate sent.
USER: Now for the counsel of my son and queen! I am amaz'd with matter.
BOT: Good my liege, Your preparation can affront no less Than what you hear of. Come more, for more you're ready. The want is but to put those pow'rs in motion That long to move.
USER: I thank you. Let's withdraw, And meet the time as it seeks us. We fear not What can from Italy annoy us; but We grieve at chances here. Away!

USER: The noise is round about us.
BOT: Let us from it.
USER: What pleasure, sir, find we in life, to lock it From action and adventure?
BOT: Nay, what hope Have we in hiding us? This way the Romans Must or for Britons slay us, or receive us For barbarous and unnatural revolts During their use, and slay us after.
USER: This is, sir, a doubt In such a time nothing becoming you Nor satisfying us.
BOT: Than be so, Better to cease to be. Pray, sir, to th' army. I and my brother are not known; yourself So out of thought, and thereto so o'ergrown, Cannot be questioned.
USER: By heavens, I'll go! If you will bless me, sir, and give me leave, I'll take the better care; but if you will not, The hazard therefore due fall on me by The hands of Romans!
BOT: So say I. Amen.

USER: Away, boy, from the troops, and save thyself; For friends kill friends, and the disorder's such As war were hoodwink'd.
BOT: 'Tis their fresh supplies.
USER: It is a day turn'd strangely. Or betimes Let's reinforce or fly.

USER: Cam'st thou from where they made the stand?
BOT: I did: Though you, it seems, come from the fliers.
USER: I did.
BOT: Where was this lane?
USER: This was strange chance: A narrow lane, an old man, and two boys.
BOT: Nay, be not angry, sir.
USER: 'Lack, to what end? Who dares not stand his foe I'll be his friend; For if he'll do as he is made to do, I know he'll quickly fly my friendship too. You have put me into rhyme.
BOT: Farewell; you're angry.
USER: Great Jupiter be prais'd! Lucius is taken. 'Tis thought the old man and his sons were angels.
BOT: There was a fourth man, in a silly habit, That gave th' affront with them.
USER: So 'tis reported; But none of 'em can be found. Stand! who's there?
BOT: A Roman, Who had not now been drooping here if seconds Had answer'd him.

USER: Ay, or a stomach.
BOT: Lucina lent not me her aid, But took me in my throes, That from me was Posthumus ripp'd, Came crying 'mongst his foes, A thing of pity.
USER: Great Nature like his ancestry Moulded the stuff so fair That he deserv'd the praise o' th' world As great Sicilius' heir.
BOT: When once he was mature for man, In Britain where was he That could stand up his parallel, Or fruitful object be In eye of Imogen, that best Could deem his dignity?
USER: With marriage wherefore was he mock'd, To be exil'd and thrown From Leonati seat and cast From her his dearest one, Sweet Imogen?
BOT: Why did you suffer Iachimo, Slight thing of Italy, To taint his nobler heart and brain With needless jealousy, And to become the geck and scorn O' th' other's villainy?
USER: For this from stiller seats we came, Our parents and us twain, That, striking in our country's cause, Fell bravely and were slain, Our fealty and Tenantius' right With honour to maintain.
BOT: Like hardiment Posthumus hath To Cymbeline perform'd. Then, Jupiter, thou king of gods, Why hast thou thus adjourn'd The graces for his merits due, Being all to dolours turn'd?
USER: Thy crystal window ope; look out; No longer exercise Upon a valiant race thy harsh And potent injuries.
BOT: Since, Jupiter, our son is good, Take off his miseries.
USER: Peep through thy marble mansion. Help! Or we poor ghosts will cry To th' shining synod of the rest Against thy deity.
BOT: Help, Jupiter! or we appeal, And from thy justice fly. Jupiter descends in thunder and lightning, sitting upon an eagle. He throws a thunderbolt. The Ghosts fall on their knees.
USER: Thanks, Jupiter!
BOT: The marble pavement closes, he is enter'd His radiant roof. Away! and, to be blest, Let us with care perform his great behest.
USER: Come, sir, are you ready for death?
BOT: Over-roasted rather; ready long ago.
USER: Hanging is the word, sir; if you be ready for that, you are well cook'd.
BOT: So, if I prove a good repast to the spectators, the dish pays the shot.
USER: I am merrier to die than thou art to live.
BOT: Yes indeed do I, fellow.
USER: I tell thee, fellow, there are none want eyes to direct them the way I am going, but such as wink and will not use them.
BOT: What an infinite mock is this, that a man should have the best use of eyes to see the way of blindness! I am sure hanging's the way of winking.
USER: Knock off his manacles; bring your prisoner to the King.
BOT: Thou bring'st good news: I am call'd to be made free.
USER: I'll be hang'd then.
BOT: Thou shalt be then freer than a gaoler; no bolts for the dead.

USER: I never saw Such noble fury in so poor a thing; Such precious deeds in one that promis'd nought But beggary and poor looks.
BOT: No tidings of him?
USER: He hath been search'd among the dead and living, But no trace of him.
BOT: Sir, In Cambria are we born, and gentlemen; Further to boast were neither true nor modest, Unless I add we are honest.
USER: Hail, great King! To sour your happiness I must report The Queen is dead.
BOT: Who worse than a physician Would this report become? But I consider By med'cine life may be prolong'd, yet death Will seize the doctor too. How ended she?
USER: Prithee say.
BOT: First, she confess'd she never lov'd you; only Affected greatness got by you, not you; Married your royalty, was wife to your place; Abhorr'd your person.
USER: She alone knew this; And but she spoke it dying, I would not Believe her lips in opening it. Proceed.
BOT: Your daughter, whom she bore in hand to love With such integrity, she did confess Was as a scorpion to her sight; whose life, But that her flight prevented it, she had Ta'en off by poison.
USER: O most delicate fiend! Who is't can read a woman? Is there more?
BOT: Heard you all this, her women?
USER: We did, so please your Highness.
BOT: I humbly thank your Highness.
USER: I do not bid thee beg my life, good lad, And yet I know thou wilt.
BOT: No, no! Alack, There's other work in hand. I see a thing Bitter to me as death; your life, good master, Must shuffle for itself.
USER: The boy disdains me, He leaves me, scorns me. Briefly die their joys That place them on the truth of girls and boys. Why stands he so perplex'd?
BOT: What wouldst thou, boy? I love thee more and more; think more and more What's best to ask. Know'st him thou look'st on? Speak, Wilt have him live? Is he thy kin? thy friend?
USER: He is a Roman, no more kin to me Than I to your Highness; who, being born your vassal, Am something nearer.
BOT: Wherefore ey'st him so?
USER: I'll tell you, sir, in private, if you please To give me hearing.
BOT: Ay, with all my heart, And lend my best attention. What's thy name?
USER: Fidele, sir.
BOT: Thou'rt my good youth, my page; I'll be thy master. Walk with me; speak freely.
USER: Is not this boy reviv'd from death?
BOT: One sand another Not more resembles that sweet rosy lad Who died and was Fidele. What think you?
USER: The same dead thing alive.
BOT: Peace, peace! see further. He eyes us not; forbear. Creatures may be alike; were't he, I am sure He would have spoke to us.
USER: But we see him dead.
BOT: Be silent; let's see further.
USER: Since she is living, let the time run on To good or bad.
BOT: My boon is that this gentleman may render Of whom he had this ring.
USER: That diamond upon your finger, say How came it yours?
BOT: Thou'lt torture me to leave unspoken that Which to be spoke would torture thee.
USER: How? me?
BOT: All that belongs to this.
USER: That paragon, thy daughter, For whom my heart drops blood and my false spirits Quail to remember - Give me leave, I faint.
BOT: My daughter? What of her? Renew thy strength; I had rather thou shouldst live while nature will Than die ere I hear more. Strive, man, and speak.
USER: I stand on fire. Come to the matter.
BOT: Nay, nay, to th' purpose.
USER: Peace, my lord. Hear, hear!
BOT: Shall's have a play of this? Thou scornful page, There lies thy part.
USER: O gentlemen, help! Mine and your mistress! O, my lord Posthumus! You ne'er kill'd Imogen till now. Help, help! Mine honour'd lady!
BOT: Does the world go round?
USER: How comes these staggers on me?
BOT: Wake, my mistress!
USER: If this be so, the gods do mean to strike me To death with mortal joy.
BOT: How fares my mistress?
USER: O, get thee from my sight; Thou gav'st me poison. Dangerous fellow, hence! Breathe not where princes are.
BOT: The tune of Imogen!
USER: Lady, The gods throw stones of sulphur on me, if That box I gave you was not thought by me A precious thing! I had it from the Queen.
BOT: New matter still?
USER: It poison'd me.
BOT: O gods! I left out one thing which the Queen confess'd, Which must approve thee honest. 'If Pisanio Have' said she 'given his mistress that confection Which I gave him for cordial, she is serv'd As I would serve a rat.'
USER: What's this, Cornelius?
BOT: Most like I did, for I was dead.
USER: My boys, There was our error.
BOT: This is sure Fidele.
USER: Why did you throw your wedded lady from you? Think that you are upon a rock, and now Throw me again.
BOT: Hang there like fruit, my soul, Till the tree die!
USER: How now, my flesh? my child? What, mak'st thou me a dullard in this act? Wilt thou not speak to me?
BOT: ye not; You had a motive for't.
USER: My tears that fall Prove holy water on thee! Imogen, Thy mother's dead.
BOT: I am sorry for't, my lord.
USER: O, she was naught, and long of her it was That we meet here so strangely; but her son Is gone, we know not how nor where.
BOT: Let me end the story: I slew him there.
USER: Marry, the gods forfend! I would not thy good deeds should from my lips Pluck a hard sentence. Prithee, valiant youth, Deny't again.
BOT: I have spoke it, and I did it.
USER: He was a prince.
BOT: I am sorry for thee. By thine own tongue thou art condemn'd, and must Endure our law. Thou'rt dead.
USER: That headless man I thought had been my lord.
BOT: Bind the offender, And take him from our presence.
USER: Why, old soldier, Wilt thou undo the worth thou art unpaid for By tasting of our wrath? How of descent As good as we?
BOT: In that he spake too far.
USER: And thou shalt die for't.
BOT: We will die all three; But I will prove that two on's are as good As I have given out him. My sons, I must For mine own part unfold a dangerous speech, Though haply well for you.
USER: Your danger's ours.
BOT: And our good his.
USER: Have at it then by leave! Thou hadst, great King, a subject who Was call'd Belarius.
BOT: What of him? He is A banish'd traitor.
USER: He it is that hath Assum'd this age; indeed a banish'd man; I know not how a traitor.
BOT: Take him hence, The whole world shall not save him.
USER: Not too hot. First pay me for the nursing of thy sons, And let it be confiscate all, so soon As I have receiv'd it.
BOT: Nursing of my sons?
USER: How? my issue?
BOT: Thou weep'st and speak'st. The service that you three have done is more Unlike than this thou tell'st. I lost my children. If these be they, I know not how to wish A pair of worthier sons.
USER: Guiderius had Upon his neck a mole, a sanguine star; It was a mark of wonder.
BOT: This is he, Who hath upon him still that natural stamp. It was wise nature's end in the donation, To be his evidence now.
USER: No, my lord; I have got two worlds by't. O my gentle brothers, Have we thus met? O, never say hereafter But I am truest speaker! You call'd me brother, When I was but your sister: I you brothers, When we were so indeed.
BOT: Did you e'er meet?
USER: Ay, my good lord.
BOT: And at first meeting lov'd, Continu'd so until we thought he died.
USER: By the Queen's dram she swallow'd.
BOT: You are my father too, and did relieve me To see this gracious season.
USER: All o'erjoy'd Save these in bonds. Let them be joyful too, For they shall taste our comfort.
BOT: My good master, I will yet do you service.
USER: Happy be you!
BOT: The forlorn soldier, that so nobly fought, He would have well becom'd this place and grac'd The thankings of a king.
USER: I am, sir, The soldier that did company these three In poor beseeming; 'twas a fitment for The purpose I then follow'd. That I was he, Speak, Iachimo. I had you down, and might Have made you finish.
BOT: But now my heavy conscience sinks my knee, As then your force did. Take that life, beseech you, Which I so often owe; but your ring first, And here the bracelet of the truest princess That ever swore her faith.
USER: Kneel not to me. The pow'r that I have on you is to spare you; The malice towards you to forgive you. Live, And deal with others better.
BOT: Nobly doom'd! We'll learn our freeness of a son-in-law; Pardon's the word to all.
USER: You holp us, sir, As you did mean indeed to be our brother; Joy'd are we that you are.
BOT: Philarmonus!
USER: Here, my good lord.
BOT: Read, and declare the meaning.
USER: This hath some seeming.

USER: Who's there?
BOT: Nay, answer me. Stand and unfold yourself.
USER: Long live the King!
BOT: Barnardo?
USER: He.
BOT: You come most carefully upon your hour.
USER: 'Tis now struck twelve. Get thee to bed, Francisco.
BOT: For this relief much thanks. 'Tis bitter cold, And I am sick at heart.
USER: Have you had quiet guard?
BOT: Not a mouse stirring.
USER: Well, good night. If you do meet Horatio and Marcellus, The rivals of my watch, bid them make haste.
BOT: I think I hear them. Stand, ho! Who is there?
USER: Friends to this ground.
BOT: And liegemen to the Dane.
USER: Give you good night.
BOT: O, farewell, honest soldier, who hath reliev'd you?
USER: Barnardo has my place. Give you good-night.
BOT: Holla, Barnardo!
USER: Say, what, is Horatio there?
BOT: A piece of him.
USER: Welcome, Horatio. Welcome, good Marcellus.
BOT: What, has this thing appear'd again tonight?
USER: I have seen nothing.
BOT: Tush, tush, 'twill not appear.
USER: Sit down awhile, And let us once again assail your ears, That are so fortified against our story, What we two nights have seen.
BOT: Well, sit we down, And let us hear Barnardo speak of this.
USER: Last night of all, When yond same star that's westward from the pole, Had made his course t'illume that part of heaven Where now it burns, Marcellus and myself, The bell then beating one -
BOT: Peace, break thee off. Look where it comes again.
USER: In the same figure, like the King that's dead.
BOT: Thou art a scholar; speak to it, Horatio.
USER: Looks it not like the King? Mark it, Horatio.
BOT: Most like. It harrows me with fear and wonder. BARNARDO It would be spoke to.
USER: Question it, Horatio.
BOT: What art thou that usurp'st this time of night, Together with that fair and warlike form In which the majesty of buried Denmark Did sometimes march? By heaven I charge thee speak.
USER: It is offended.
BOT: See, it stalks away.
USER: Stay! speak, speak! I charge thee speak!
BOT: 'Tis gone, and will not answer.
USER: How now, Horatio! You tremble and look pale. Is not this something more than fantasy? What think you on't?
BOT: Before my God, I might not this believe Without the sensible and true avouch Of mine own eyes.
USER: Is it not like the King?
BOT: As thou art to thyself: Such was the very armour he had on When he th'ambitious Norway combated; So frown'd he once, when in an angry parle He smote the sledded Polacks on the ice. 'Tis strange.
USER: Thus twice before, and jump at this dead hour, With martial stalk hath he gone by our watch.
BOT: In what particular thought to work I know not; But in the gross and scope of my opinion, This bodes some strange eruption to our state.
USER: I think it be no other but e'en so: Well may it sort that this portentous figure Comes armed through our watch so like the King That was and is the question of these wars.
BOT: Shall I strike at it with my partisan?
USER: Do, if it will not stand.
BOT: 'Tis here!
USER: 'Tis here!
BOT: 'Tis gone! We do it wrong, being so majestical, To offer it the show of violence, For it is as the air, invulnerable, And our vain blows malicious mockery.
USER: It was about to speak, when the cock crew.
BOT: Let's do't, I pray, and I this morning know Where we shall find him most conveniently.

USER: Have you your father's leave? What says Polonius?
BOT: He hath, my lord, wrung from me my slow leave By laboursome petition; and at last Upon his will I seal'd my hard consent. I do beseech you give him leave to go.
USER: Take thy fair hour, Laertes; time be thine, And thy best graces spend it at thy will! But now, my cousin Hamlet, and my son -
BOT: How is it that the clouds still hang on you?
USER: Not so, my lord, I am too much i' the sun.
BOT: Ay, madam, it is common.
USER: If it be, Why seems it so particular with thee?
BOT: Let not thy mother lose her prayers, Hamlet. I pray thee stay with us; go not to Wittenberg.
USER: I shall in all my best obey you, madam.
BOT: Hail to your lordship!
USER: I am glad to see you well: Horatio, or I do forget myself.
BOT: The same, my lord, And your poor servant ever.
USER: Sir, my good friend; I'll change that name with you: And what make you from Wittenberg, Horatio? - Marcellus?
BOT: My good lord.
USER: I am very glad to see you. - Good even, sir. - But what, in faith, make you from Wittenberg?
BOT: A truant disposition, good my lord.
USER: My lord, I came to see your father's funeral.
BOT: I prithee do not mock me, fellow-student. I think it was to see my mother's wedding.
USER: Indeed, my lord, it follow'd hard upon.
BOT: Thrift, thrift, Horatio! The funeral bak'd meats Did coldly furnish forth the marriage tables. Would I had met my dearest foe in heaven Or ever I had seen that day, Horatio. My father, - methinks I see my father.
USER: Where, my lord?
BOT: In my mind's eye, Horatio.
USER: I saw him once; he was a goodly king.
BOT: He was a man, take him for all in all, I shall not look upon his like again.
USER: My lord, I think I saw him yesternight.
BOT: Saw? Who?
USER: My lord, the King your father.
BOT: The King my father!
USER: Season your admiration for a while With an attent ear, till I may deliver Upon the witness of these gentlemen This marvel to you.
BOT: For God's love let me hear.
USER: But where was this?
BOT: My lord, upon the platform where we watch.
USER: Did you not speak to it?
BOT: 'Tis very strange.
USER: As I do live, my honour'd lord, 'tis true; And we did think it writ down in our duty To let you know of it.
BOT: Indeed, indeed, sirs, but this troubles me. Hold you the watch tonight? MARCELLUS and BARNARDO. We do, my lord.
USER: Arm'd, say you? Both. Arm'd, my lord.
BOT: From top to toe?
USER: My lord, from head to foot.
BOT: Then saw you not his face?
USER: O yes, my lord, he wore his beaver up.
BOT: What, look'd he frowningly?
USER: A countenance more in sorrow than in anger.
BOT: Pale, or red?
USER: Nay, very pale.
BOT: And fix'd his eyes upon you?
USER: Most constantly.
BOT: I would I had been there.
USER: It would have much amaz'd you.
BOT: Very like, very like. Stay'd it long?
USER: While one with moderate haste might tell a hundred. MARCELLUS and BARNARDO. Longer, longer.
BOT: Not when I saw't.
USER: His beard was grizzled, no?
BOT: It was, as I have seen it in his life, A sable silver'd.
USER: I will watch tonight; Perchance 'twill walk again.
BOT: I warrant you it will.
USER: Our duty to your honour.

USER: My necessaries are embark'd. Farewell. And, sister, as the winds give benefit And convoy is assistant, do not sleep, But let me hear from you.
BOT: Do you doubt that?
USER: For Hamlet, and the trifling of his favour, Hold it a fashion and a toy in blood; A violet in the youth of primy nature, Forward, not permanent, sweet, not lasting; The perfume and suppliance of a minute; No more.
BOT: No more but so?
USER: O, fear me not. I stay too long. But here my father comes. A double blessing is a double grace; Occasion smiles upon a second leave.
BOT: Most humbly do I take my leave, my lord.
USER: The time invites you; go, your servants tend.
BOT: Farewell, Ophelia, and remember well What I have said to you.
USER: 'Tis in my memory lock'd, And you yourself shall keep the key of it.
BOT: Farewell.
USER: What is't, Ophelia, he hath said to you?
BOT: So please you, something touching the Lord Hamlet.
USER: He hath, my lord, of late made many tenders Of his affection to me.
BOT: Affection! Pooh! You speak like a green girl, Unsifted in such perilous circumstance. Do you believe his tenders, as you call them?
USER: I do not know, my lord, what I should think.
BOT: My lord, he hath importun'd me with love In honourable fashion.
USER: Ay, fashion you may call it; go to, go to.
BOT: And hath given countenance to his speech, my lord, With almost all the holy vows of heaven.
USER: I shall obey, my lord.

USER: The air bites shrewdly; it is very cold.
BOT: It is a nipping and an eager air.
USER: What hour now?
BOT: I think it lacks of twelve.
USER: No, it is struck.
BOT: Indeed? I heard it not. It then draws near the season Wherein the spirit held his wont to walk. What does this mean, my lord?
USER: The King doth wake tonight and takes his rouse, Keeps wassail, and the swaggering upspring reels; And as he drains his draughts of Rhenish down, The kettle-drum and trumpet thus bray out The triumph of his pledge.
BOT: Is it a custom?
USER: Look, my lord, it comes!
BOT: It beckons you to go away with it, As if it some impartment did desire To you alone.
USER: Look with what courteous action It waves you to a more removed ground. But do not go with it.
BOT: No, by no means.
USER: It will not speak; then will I follow it.
BOT: Do not, my lord.
USER: Why, what should be the fear? I do not set my life at a pin's fee; And for my soul, what can it do to that, Being a thing immortal as itself? It waves me forth again. I'll follow it.
BOT: It waves me still. Go on, I'll follow thee.
USER: You shall not go, my lord.
BOT: Hold off your hands.
USER: Be rul'd; you shall not go.
BOT: He waxes desperate with imagination.
USER: Let's follow; 'tis not fit thus to obey him.
BOT: Have after. To what issue will this come?
USER: Something is rotten in the state of Denmark.
BOT: Heaven will direct it.
USER: Nay, let's follow him.

USER: Whither wilt thou lead me? Speak, I'll go no further.
BOT: Mark me.
USER: I will.
BOT: My hour is almost come, When I to sulph'rous and tormenting flames Must render up myself.
USER: Alas, poor ghost!
BOT: Pity me not, but lend thy serious hearing To what I shall unfold.
USER: Speak, I am bound to hear.
BOT: So art thou to revenge, when thou shalt hear.
USER: What?
BOT: O God!
USER: Revenge his foul and most unnatural murder.
BOT: Murder!
USER: Murder most foul, as in the best it is; But this most foul, strange, and unnatural.
BOT: Haste me to know't, that I, with wings as swift As meditation or the thoughts of love May sweep to my revenge.
USER: O my prophetic soul! Mine uncle!
BOT: So be it!
USER: Hillo, ho, ho, boy! Come, bird, come.
BOT: How is't, my noble lord?
USER: What news, my lord?
BOT: O, wonderful!
USER: Good my lord, tell it.
BOT: No, you'll reveal it.
USER: Not I, my lord, by heaven.
BOT: Nor I, my lord.
USER: How say you then, would heart of man once think it? - But you'll be secret? HORATIO and MARCELLUS. Ay, by heaven, my lord.
BOT: There's ne'er a villain dwelling in all Denmark But he's an arrant knave.
USER: There needs no ghost, my lord, come from the grave To tell us this.
BOT: These are but wild and whirling words, my lord.
USER: I'm sorry they offend you, heartily; Yes faith, heartily.
BOT: There's no offence, my lord.
USER: What is't, my lord? We will.
BOT: Never make known what you have seen tonight. HORATIO and MARCELLUS. My lord, we will not.
USER: Nay, but swear't.
BOT: In faith, my lord, not I.
USER: Nor I, my lord, in faith.
BOT: Upon my sword.
USER: We have sworn, my lord, already.
BOT: Indeed, upon my sword, indeed.
USER: Ha, ha boy, sayst thou so? Art thou there, truepenny? Come on, you hear this fellow in the cellarage. Consent to swear.
BOT: Propose the oath, my lord.
USER: Never to speak of this that you have seen. Swear by my sword.
BOT: _Hic et ubique?_ Then we'll shift our ground. Come hither, gentlemen, And lay your hands again upon my sword. Never to speak of this that you have heard. Swear by my sword.
USER: Well said, old mole! Canst work i' th'earth so fast? A worthy pioner! Once more remove, good friends.
BOT: O day and night, but this is wondrous strange.

USER: Give him this money and these notes, Reynaldo.
BOT: I will, my lord.
USER: You shall do marvellous wisely, good Reynaldo, Before you visit him, to make inquiry Of his behaviour.
BOT: My lord, I did intend it.
USER: Ay, very well, my lord.
BOT: As gaming, my lord?
USER: Ay, or drinking, fencing, swearing, Quarrelling, drabbing. You may go so far.
BOT: My lord, that would dishonour him.
USER: But my good lord -
BOT: Wherefore should you do this?
USER: Ay, my lord, I would know that.
BOT: Very good, my lord.
USER: And then, sir, does he this, - He does - What was I about to say? By the mass, I was about to say something. Where did I leave?
BOT: At 'closes in the consequence.' At 'friend or so,' and 'gentleman.'
USER: My lord, I have.
BOT: God b' wi' you, fare you well.
USER: Good my lord.
BOT: Observe his inclination in yourself.
USER: I shall, my lord.
BOT: And let him ply his music.
USER: Well, my lord.
BOT: Farewell. How now, Ophelia, what's the matter?
USER: Alas, my lord, I have been so affrighted.
BOT: With what, in the name of God?
USER: Mad for thy love?
BOT: My lord, I do not know, but truly I do fear it.
USER: What said he?
BOT: No, my good lord; but as you did command, I did repel his letters and denied His access to me.

USER: Both your majesties Might, by the sovereign power you have of us, Put your dread pleasures more into command Than to entreaty.
BOT: We both obey, And here give up ourselves, in the full bent, To lay our service freely at your feet To be commanded.
USER: Thanks, Rosencrantz and gentle Guildenstern.
BOT: Thanks, Guildenstern and gentle Rosencrantz. And I beseech you instantly to visit My too much changed son. Go, some of you, And bring these gentlemen where Hamlet is.
USER: Heavens make our presence and our practices Pleasant and helpful to him.
BOT: Ay, amen.
USER: Th'ambassadors from Norway, my good lord, Are joyfully return'd.
BOT: Thou still hast been the father of good news.
USER: O speak of that, that do I long to hear.
BOT: Give first admittance to th'ambassadors; My news shall be the fruit to that great feast.
USER: Thyself do grace to them, and bring them in. He tells me, my sweet queen, that he hath found The head and source of all your son's distemper.
BOT: I doubt it is no other but the main, His father's death and our o'erhasty marriage.
USER: Well, we shall sift him. Welcome, my good friends! Say, Voltemand, what from our brother Norway?
BOT: It likes us well; And at our more consider'd time we'll read, Answer, and think upon this business. Meantime we thank you for your well-took labour. Go to your rest, at night we'll feast together:. Most welcome home.
USER: More matter, with less art.
BOT: Came this from Hamlet to her?
USER: But how hath she receiv'd his love?
BOT: What do you think of me?
USER: As of a man faithful and honourable.
BOT: Do you think 'tis this?
USER: It may be, very likely.
BOT: Hath there been such a time, I'd fain know that, That I have positively said ''Tis so,' When it prov'd otherwise?
USER: Not that I know.
BOT: Take this from this, if this be otherwise. If circumstances lead me, I will find Where truth is hid, though it were hid indeed Within the centre.
USER: How may we try it further?
BOT: You know sometimes he walks four hours together Here in the lobby.
USER: So he does indeed.
BOT: We will try it.
USER: But look where sadly the poor wretch comes reading.
BOT: Away, I do beseech you, both away I'll board him presently. O, give me leave. How does my good Lord Hamlet?
USER: Well, God-a-mercy.
BOT: Do you know me, my lord?
USER: Excellent well. You are a fishmonger.
BOT: Not I, my lord.
USER: Then I would you were so honest a man.
BOT: Honest, my lord?
USER: Ay sir, to be honest, as this world goes, is to be one man picked out of ten thousand.
BOT: That's very true, my lord.
USER: For if the sun breed maggots in a dead dog, being a good kissing carrion, - Have you a daughter?
BOT: I have, my lord.
USER: Let her not walk i' th' sun. Conception is a blessing, but not as your daughter may conceive. Friend, look to't.
BOT: Words, words, words.
USER: What is the matter, my lord?
BOT: Between who?
USER: I mean the matter that you read, my lord.
BOT: Will you walk out of the air, my lord?
USER: Into my grave?
BOT: You cannot, sir, take from me anything that I will more willingly part withal, except my life, except my life, except my life.
USER: Fare you well, my lord.
BOT: These tedious old fools.
USER: You go to seek the Lord Hamlet; there he is.
BOT: My honoured lord!
USER: My most dear lord!
BOT: My excellent good friends! How dost thou, Guildenstern? Ah, Rosencrantz. Good lads, how do ye both?
USER: As the indifferent children of the earth.
BOT: Happy in that we are not over-happy; On Fortune's cap we are not the very button.
USER: Nor the soles of her shoe?
BOT: Neither, my lord.
USER: Then you live about her waist, or in the middle of her favours?
BOT: Faith, her privates we.
USER: In the secret parts of Fortune? O, most true; she is a strumpet. What's the news?
BOT: None, my lord, but that the world's grown honest.
USER: Then is doomsday near. But your news is not true. Let me question more in particular. What have you, my good friends, deserved at the hands of Fortune, that she sends you to prison hither?
BOT: Prison, my lord?
USER: Denmark's a prison.
BOT: Then is the world one.
USER: A goodly one; in which there are many confines, wards, and dungeons, Denmark being one o' th' worst.
BOT: We think not so, my lord.
USER: Why, then 'tis none to you; for there is nothing either good or bad but thinking makes it so. To me it is a prison.
BOT: Why, then your ambition makes it one; 'tis too narrow for your mind.
USER: O God, I could be bounded in a nutshell, and count myself a king of infinite space, were it not that I have bad dreams.
BOT: Which dreams, indeed, are ambition; for the very substance of the ambitious is merely the shadow of a dream.
USER: A dream itself is but a shadow.
BOT: Truly, and I hold ambition of so airy and light a quality that it is but a shadow's shadow.
USER: Then are our beggars bodies, and our monarchs and outstretch'd heroes the beggars' shadows. Shall we to th' court? For, by my fay, I cannot reason. ROSENCRANTZ and GUILDENSTERN. We'll wait upon you.
BOT: No such matter. I will not sort you with the rest of my servants; for, to speak to you like an honest man, I am most dreadfully attended. But, in the beaten way of friendship, what make you at Elsinore?
USER: To visit you, my lord, no other occasion.
BOT: What should we say, my lord?
USER: Why, anything. But to the purpose. You were sent for; and there is a kind of confession in your looks, which your modesties have not craft enough to colour. I know the good King and Queen have sent for you.
BOT: To what end, my lord?
USER: off.
BOT: My lord, we were sent for.
USER: My lord, there was no such stuff in my thoughts.
BOT: Why did you laugh then, when I said 'Man delights not me'?
USER: To think, my lord, if you delight not in man, what Lenten entertainment the players shall receive from you. We coted them on the way, and hither are they coming to offer you service.
BOT: Even those you were wont to take such delight in - the tragedians of the city.
USER: How chances it they travel? Their residence, both in reputation and profit, was better both ways.
BOT: I think their inhibition comes by the means of the late innovation.
USER: Do they hold the same estimation they did when I was in the city? Are they so followed?
BOT: No, indeed, they are not.
USER: How comes it? Do they grow rusty?
BOT: Faith, there has been much to do on both sides; and the nation holds it no sin to tarre them to controversy. There was for a while, no money bid for argument unless the poet and the player went to cuffs in the question.
USER: Is't possible?
BOT: O, there has been much throwing about of brains.
USER: Do the boys carry it away?
BOT: Ay, that they do, my lord. Hercules and his load too.
USER: There are the players.
BOT: In what, my dear lord?
USER: I am but mad north-north-west. When the wind is southerly, I know a hawk from a handsaw.
BOT: Well be with you, gentlemen.
USER: Hark you, Guildenstern, and you too, at each ear a hearer. That great baby you see there is not yet out of his swaddling clouts.
BOT: Happily he's the second time come to them; for they say an old man is twice a child.
USER: I will prophesy he comes to tell me of the players. Mark it. - You say right, sir: for a Monday morning 'twas so indeed.
BOT: My lord, I have news to tell you.
USER: My lord, I have news to tell you. When Roscius was an actor in Rome -
BOT: The actors are come hither, my lord.
USER: Buzz, buzz.
BOT: Upon my honour.
USER: Then came each actor on his ass -
BOT: O Jephthah, judge of Israel, what a treasure hadst thou!
USER: What treasure had he, my lord?
BOT: Why - 'One fair daughter, and no more, The which he loved passing well.'
USER: Am I not i' th' right, old Jephthah?
BOT: If you call me Jephthah, my lord, I have a daughter that I love passing well.
USER: Nay, that follows not.
BOT: What follows then, my lord?
USER: What speech, my lord?
BOT: 'Fore God, my lord, well spoken, with good accent and good discretion.
USER: This is too long.
BOT: It shall to the barber's, with your beard. - Prithee say on. He's for a jig or a tale of bawdry, or he sleeps. Say on; come to Hecuba.
USER: _But who, O who, had seen the mobled queen, - _
BOT: 'The mobled queen'?
USER: That's good! 'Mobled queen' is good.
BOT: Look, where he has not turn'd his colour, and has tears in's eyes. Pray you, no more.
USER: My lord, I will use them according to their desert.
BOT: God's bodikin, man, much better. Use every man after his desert, and who should 'scape whipping? Use them after your own honour and dignity. The less they deserve, the more merit is in your bounty. Take them in.
USER: Come, sirs.
BOT: Follow him, friends. We'll hear a play tomorrow. Dost thou hear me, old friend? Can you play _The Murder of Gonzago_?
USER: Ay, my lord.
BOT: We'll ha't tomorrow night. You could for a need study a speech of some dozen or sixteen lines, which I would set down and insert in't, could you not?
USER: Ay, my lord.
BOT: Very well. Follow that lord, and look you mock him not. till night. You are welcome to Elsinore.
USER: Good my lord.

USER: And can you by no drift of circumstance Get from him why he puts on this confusion, Grating so harshly all his days of quiet With turbulent and dangerous lunacy?
BOT: He does confess he feels himself distracted, But from what cause he will by no means speak.
USER: Nor do we find him forward to be sounded, But with a crafty madness keeps aloof When we would bring him on to some confession Of his true state.
BOT: Did he receive you well?
USER: Most like a gentleman.
BOT: But with much forcing of his disposition.
USER: Niggard of question, but of our demands, Most free in his reply.
BOT: Did you assay him to any pastime?
USER: 'Tis most true; And he beseech'd me to entreat your Majesties To hear and see the matter.
BOT: With all my heart; and it doth much content me To hear him so inclin'd. Good gentlemen, give him a further edge, And drive his purpose on to these delights.
USER: We shall, my lord.
BOT: I shall obey you. And for your part, Ophelia, I do wish That your good beauties be the happy cause Of Hamlet's wildness: so shall I hope your virtues Will bring him to his wonted way again, To both your honours.
USER: Madam, I wish it may.
BOT: How smart a lash that speech doth give my conscience! The harlot's cheek, beautied with plastering art, Is not more ugly to the thing that helps it Than is my deed to my most painted word. O heavy burden!
USER: I hear him coming. Let's withdraw, my lord.
BOT: Good my lord, How does your honour for this many a day?
USER: I humbly thank you; well, well, well.
BOT: My lord, I have remembrances of yours That I have longed long to re-deliver. I pray you, now receive them.
USER: No, not I. I never gave you aught.
BOT: Ha, ha! Are you honest?
USER: My lord?
BOT: Are you fair?
USER: What means your lordship?
BOT: That if you be honest and fair, your honesty should admit no discourse to your beauty.
USER: Could beauty, my lord, have better commerce than with honesty?
BOT: Indeed, my lord, you made me believe so.
USER: You should not have believed me; for virtue cannot so inoculate our old stock but we shall relish of it. I loved you not.
BOT: I was the more deceived.
USER: At home, my lord.
BOT: Let the doors be shut upon him, that he may play the fool nowhere but in's own house. Farewell.
USER: O help him, you sweet heavens!
BOT: O heavenly powers, restore him!
USER: It shall be so. Madness in great ones must not unwatch'd go.

USER: I warrant your honour.
BOT: I hope we have reform'd that indifferently with us, sir.
USER: And the Queen too, and that presently.
BOT: Bid the players make haste. Will you two help to hasten them? ROSENCRANTZ and GUILDENSTERN. We will, my lord.
USER: What ho, Horatio!
BOT: Here, sweet lord, at your service.
USER: Horatio, thou art e'en as just a man As e'er my conversation cop'd withal.
BOT: O my dear lord.
USER: Well, my lord. If he steal aught the whilst this play is playing, And 'scape detecting, I will pay the theft.
BOT: They are coming to the play. I must be idle. Get you a place. Danish march. A flourish. Enter King, Queen, Polonius, Ophelia, Rosencrantz, Guildenstern and others.
USER: How fares our cousin Hamlet?
BOT: Excellent, i' faith; of the chameleon's dish: I eat the air, promise-crammed: you cannot feed capons so.
USER: I have nothing with this answer, Hamlet; these words are not mine.
BOT: No, nor mine now. [_To Polonius._] My lord, you play'd once i' th'university, you say?
USER: That did I, my lord, and was accounted a good actor.
BOT: What did you enact?
USER: I did enact Julius Caesar. I was kill'd i' th' Capitol. Brutus killed me.
BOT: It was a brute part of him to kill so capital a calf there. Be the players ready?
USER: Ay, my lord; they stay upon your patience.
BOT: Come hither, my dear Hamlet, sit by me.
USER: No, good mother, here's metal more attractive.
BOT: Lady, shall I lie in your lap?
USER: No, my lord.
BOT: I mean, my head upon your lap?
USER: Ay, my lord.
BOT: Do you think I meant country matters?
USER: I think nothing, my lord.
BOT: That's a fair thought to lie between maids' legs.
USER: What is, my lord?
BOT: Nothing.
USER: You are merry, my lord.
BOT: Who, I?
USER: Ay, my lord.
BOT: O God, your only jig-maker! What should a man do but be merry? For look you how cheerfully my mother looks, and my father died within's two hours.
USER: Nay, 'tis twice two months, my lord.

USER: What means this, my lord?
BOT: Marry, this is miching mallecho; it means mischief.
USER: Belike this show imports the argument of the play.
BOT: We shall know by this fellow: the players cannot keep counsel; they'll tell all.
USER: Will they tell us what this show meant?
BOT: Ay, or any show that you'll show him. Be not you ashamed to show, he'll not shame to tell you what it means.
USER: You are naught, you are naught: I'll mark the play.

USER: Is this a prologue, or the posy of a ring?
BOT: 'Tis brief, my lord.
USER: As woman's love.
BOT: Faith, I must leave thee, love, and shortly too: My operant powers their functions leave to do: And thou shalt live in this fair world behind, Honour'd, belov'd, and haply one as kind For husband shalt thou -
USER: O confound the rest. Such love must needs be treason in my breast. In second husband let me be accurst! None wed the second but who kill'd the first.
BOT: The instances that second marriage move Are base respects of thrift, but none of love. A second time I kill my husband dead, When second husband kisses me in bed.
USER: 'Tis deeply sworn. Sweet, leave me here awhile. My spirits grow dull, and fain I would beguile The tedious day with sleep.
BOT: Sleep rock thy brain, And never come mischance between us twain.
USER: Madam, how like you this play?
BOT: The lady protests too much, methinks.
USER: O, but she'll keep her word.
BOT: Have you heard the argument? Is there no offence in't?
USER: No, no, they do but jest, poison in jest; no offence i' th' world.
BOT: What do you call the play?
USER: You are a good chorus, my lord.
BOT: I could interpret between you and your love, if I could see the puppets dallying.
USER: You are keen, my lord, you are keen.
BOT: It would cost you a groaning to take off my edge.
USER: Still better, and worse.
BOT: So you mistake your husbands. - Begin, murderer. Pox, leave thy damnable faces, and begin. Come, the croaking raven doth bellow for revenge.
USER: He poisons him i' th'garden for's estate. His name's Gonzago. The story is extant, and written in very choice Italian. You shall see anon how the murderer gets the love of Gonzago's wife.
BOT: The King rises.
USER: What, frighted with false fire?
BOT: How fares my lord?
USER: Give o'er the play.
BOT: Give me some light. Away. All. Lights, lights, lights.
USER: Half a share.
BOT: A whole one, I. For thou dost know, O Damon dear, This realm dismantled was Of Jove himself, and now reigns here A very, very - pajock.
USER: You might have rhymed.
BOT: O good Horatio, I'll take the ghost's word for a thousand pound. Didst perceive?
USER: Very well, my lord.
BOT: Upon the talk of the poisoning?
USER: I did very well note him.
BOT: Ah, ha! Come, some music. Come, the recorders. For if the king like not the comedy, Why then, belike he likes it not, perdie. Come, some music.
USER: Good my lord, vouchsafe me a word with you.
BOT: Sir, a whole history.
USER: The King, sir -
BOT: Ay, sir, what of him?
USER: Is in his retirement, marvellous distempered.
BOT: With drink, sir?
USER: No, my lord; rather with choler.
BOT: Your wisdom should show itself more richer to signify this to the doctor, for me to put him to his purgation would perhaps plunge him into far more choler.
USER: Good my lord, put your discourse into some frame, and start not so wildly from my affair.
BOT: I am tame, sir, pronounce.
USER: The Queen your mother, in most great affliction of spirit, hath sent me to you.
BOT: You are welcome.
USER: Nay, good my lord, this courtesy is not of the right breed. If it shall please you to make me a wholesome answer, I will do your mother's commandment; if not, your pardon and my return shall be the end of my business.
BOT: Sir, I cannot.
USER: What, my lord?
BOT: Make you a wholesome answer. My wit's diseased. But, sir, such answer as I can make, you shall command; or rather, as you say, my mother. Therefore no more, but to the matter. My mother, you say, -
USER: Then thus she says: your behaviour hath struck her into amazement and admiration.
BOT: O wonderful son, that can so stonish a mother! But is there no sequel at the heels of this mother's admiration?
USER: She desires to speak with you in her closet ere you go to bed.
BOT: We shall obey, were she ten times our mother. Have you any further trade with us?
USER: My lord, you once did love me.
BOT: And so I do still, by these pickers and stealers.
USER: Good my lord, what is your cause of distemper? You do surely bar the door upon your own liberty if you deny your griefs to your friend.
BOT: Sir, I lack advancement.
USER: How can that be, when you have the voice of the King himself for your succession in Denmark?
BOT: Ay, sir, but while the grass grows - the proverb is something musty. O, the recorders. Let me see one. - To withdraw with you, why do you go about to recover the wind of me, as if you would drive me into a toil?
USER: O my lord, if my duty be too bold, my love is too unmannerly.
BOT: I do not well understand that. Will you play upon this pipe?
USER: My lord, I cannot.
BOT: I pray you.
USER: Believe me, I cannot.
BOT: I do beseech you.
USER: I know no touch of it, my lord.
BOT: 'Tis as easy as lying: govern these ventages with your finger and thumb, give it breath with your mouth, and it will discourse most eloquent music. Look you, these are the stops.
USER: But these cannot I command to any utterance of harmony. I have not the skill.
BOT: My lord, the Queen would speak with you, and presently.
USER: Do you see yonder cloud that's almost in shape of a camel?
BOT: By the mass, and 'tis like a camel indeed.
USER: Methinks it is like a weasel.
BOT: It is backed like a weasel.
USER: Or like a whale.
BOT: Very like a whale.
USER: Then will I come to my mother by and by. - They fool me to the top of my bent. - I will come by and by.
BOT: I will say so.

USER: We will ourselves provide. Most holy and religious fear it is To keep those many many bodies safe That live and feed upon your Majesty.
BOT: Arm you, I pray you, to this speedy voyage; For we will fetters put upon this fear, Which now goes too free-footed. ROSENCRANTZ and GUILDENSTERN. We will haste us.
USER: My words fly up, my thoughts remain below. Words without thoughts never to heaven go.

USER: I'll warrant you, Fear me not. Withdraw, I hear him coming.
BOT: Now, mother, what's the matter?
USER: Hamlet, thou hast thy father much offended.
BOT: Mother, you have my father much offended.
USER: Come, come, you answer with an idle tongue.
BOT: Go, go, you question with a wicked tongue.
USER: Why, how now, Hamlet?
BOT: What's the matter now?
USER: Have you forgot me?
BOT: No, by the rood, not so. You are the Queen, your husband's brother's wife, And, would it were not so. You are my mother.
USER: Nay, then I'll set those to you that can speak.
BOT: Come, come, and sit you down, you shall not budge. You go not till I set you up a glass Where you may see the inmost part of you.
USER: What wilt thou do? Thou wilt not murder me? Help, help, ho!
BOT: How now? A rat? [_Draws._] Dead for a ducat, dead!
USER: O me, what hast thou done?
BOT: Nay, I know not. Is it the King?
USER: O what a rash and bloody deed is this!
BOT: A bloody deed. Almost as bad, good mother, As kill a king and marry with his brother.
USER: As kill a king?
BOT: What have I done, that thou dar'st wag thy tongue In noise so rude against me?
USER: Ay me, what act, That roars so loud, and thunders in the index?
BOT: O Hamlet, speak no more. Thou turn'st mine eyes into my very soul, And there I see such black and grained spots As will not leave their tinct.
USER: Nay, but to live In the rank sweat of an enseamed bed, Stew'd in corruption, honeying and making love Over the nasty sty.
BOT: O speak to me no more; These words like daggers enter in mine ears; No more, sweet Hamlet.
USER: A murderer and a villain; A slave that is not twentieth part the tithe Of your precedent lord. A vice of kings, A cutpurse of the empire and the rule, That from a shelf the precious diadem stole And put it in his pocket!
BOT: No more.
USER: A king of shreds and patches! - Save me and hover o'er me with your wings, You heavenly guards! What would your gracious figure?
BOT: Alas, he's mad.
USER: Do you not come your tardy son to chide, That, laps'd in time and passion, lets go by The important acting of your dread command? O say!
BOT: Do not forget. This visitation Is but to whet thy almost blunted purpose. But look, amazement on thy mother sits. O step between her and her fighting soul. Conceit in weakest bodies strongest works. Speak to her, Hamlet.
USER: How is it with you, lady?
BOT: To whom do you speak this?
USER: Do you see nothing there?
BOT: Nothing at all; yet all that is I see.
USER: Nor did you nothing hear?
BOT: No, nothing but ourselves.
USER: Why, look you there! look how it steals away! My father, in his habit as he liv'd! Look where he goes even now out at the portal.
BOT: This is the very coinage of your brain. This bodiless creation ecstasy Is very cunning in.
USER: O Hamlet, thou hast cleft my heart in twain.
BOT: What shall I do?
USER: Be thou assur'd, if words be made of breath, And breath of life, I have no life to breathe What thou hast said to me.
BOT: I must to England, you know that?
USER: Alack, I had forgot. 'Tis so concluded on.

USER: There's matter in these sighs. These profound heaves You must translate; 'tis fit we understand them. Where is your son?
BOT: Bestow this place on us a little while. Ah, my good lord, what have I seen tonight!
USER: What, Gertrude? How does Hamlet?
BOT: To draw apart the body he hath kill'd, O'er whom his very madness, like some ore Among a mineral of metals base, Shows itself pure. He weeps for what is done.

USER: Safely stowed. ROSENCRANTZ and GUILDENSTERN.
BOT: What noise? Who calls on Hamlet? O, here they come.
USER: What have you done, my lord, with the dead body?
BOT: Compounded it with dust, whereto 'tis kin.
USER: Tell us where 'tis, that we may take it thence, And bear it to the chapel.
BOT: Do not believe it.
USER: Believe what?
BOT: That I can keep your counsel, and not mine own. Besides, to be demanded of a sponge - what replication should be made by the son of a king?
USER: Take you me for a sponge, my lord?
BOT: I understand you not, my lord.
USER: I am glad of it. A knavish speech sleeps in a foolish ear.
BOT: My lord, you must tell us where the body is and go with us to the King.
USER: The body is with the King, but the King is not with the body. The King is a thing -
BOT: A thing, my lord!
USER: Of nothing. Bring me to him. Hide fox, and all after.

USER: Where the dead body is bestow'd, my lord, We cannot get from him.
BOT: But where is he?
USER: Without, my lord, guarded, to know your pleasure.
BOT: Bring him before us.
USER: Ho, Guildenstern! Bring in my lord.
BOT: Now, Hamlet, where's Polonius?
USER: At supper.
BOT: At supper? Where?
USER: Alas, alas!
BOT: A man may fish with the worm that hath eat of a king, and eat of the fish that hath fed of that worm.
USER: What dost thou mean by this?
BOT: Nothing but to show you how a king may go a progress through the guts of a beggar.
USER: Where is Polonius?
BOT: In heaven. Send thither to see. If your messenger find him not there, seek him i' th'other place yourself. But indeed, if you find him not within this month, you shall nose him as you go up the stairs into the lobby.
USER: He will stay till you come.
BOT: For England?
USER: Ay, Hamlet.
BOT: Good.
USER: So is it, if thou knew'st our purposes.
BOT: I see a cherub that sees them. But, come; for England! Farewell, dear mother.
USER: Thy loving father, Hamlet.
BOT: My mother. Father and mother is man and wife; man and wife is one flesh; and so, my mother. Come, for England.

USER: I will do't, my lord.
BOT: Go softly on.
USER: Good sir, whose powers are these?
BOT: They are of Norway, sir.
USER: How purpos'd, sir, I pray you?
BOT: Against some part of Poland.
USER: Who commands them, sir?
BOT: The nephew to old Norway, Fortinbras.
USER: Goes it against the main of Poland, sir, Or for some frontier?
BOT: Why, then the Polack never will defend it.
USER: Yes, it is already garrison'd.
BOT: God b' wi' you, sir.
USER: Will't please you go, my lord?

USER: I will not speak with her.
BOT: She is importunate, indeed distract. Her mood will needs be pitied.
USER: What would she have?
BOT: Let her come in. To my sick soul, as sin's true nature is, Each toy seems prologue to some great amiss. So full of artless jealousy is guilt, It spills itself in fearing to be spilt.
USER: Where is the beauteous Majesty of Denmark?
BOT: How now, Ophelia?
USER: How should I your true love know From another one? By his cockle hat and staff And his sandal shoon.
BOT: Alas, sweet lady, what imports this song?
USER: Say you? Nay, pray you mark. He is dead and gone, lady, He is dead and gone, At his head a grass green turf, At his heels a stone.
BOT: Nay, but Ophelia -
USER: Pray you mark. White his shroud as the mountain snow.
BOT: Alas, look here, my lord!
USER: Larded all with sweet flowers; Which bewept to the grave did not go With true-love showers.
BOT: How do you, pretty lady?
USER: Well, God dild you! They say the owl was a baker's daughter. Lord, we know what we are, but know not what we may be. God be at your table!
BOT: Conceit upon her father.
USER: Pretty Ophelia!
BOT: How long hath she been thus?
USER: Alack, what noise is this?
BOT: Where are my Switzers? Let them guard the door. What is the matter?
USER: How cheerfully on the false trail they cry. O, this is counter, you false Danish dogs.
BOT: The doors are broke.
USER: Where is this king? - Sirs, stand you all without. Danes. No, let's come in.
BOT: I pray you, give me leave.
USER: We will, we will.
BOT: I thank you. Keep the door. O thou vile king, Give me my father.
USER: Calmly, good Laertes.
BOT: That drop of blood that's calm proclaims me bastard; Cries cuckold to my father, brands the harlot Even here between the chaste unsmirched brow Of my true mother.
USER: Where is my father?
BOT: Dead.
USER: But not by him.
BOT: Let him demand his fill.
USER: Who shall stay you?
BOT: My will, not all the world. And for my means, I'll husband them so well, They shall go far with little.
USER: Good Laertes, If you desire to know the certainty Of your dear father's death, is't writ in your revenge That, sweepstake, you will draw both friend and foe, Winner and loser?
BOT: None but his enemies.
USER: Will you know them then?
BOT: To his good friends thus wide I'll ope my arms; And, like the kind life-rendering pelican, Repast them with my blood.
USER: Why, now you speak Like a good child and a true gentleman. That I am guiltless of your father's death, And am most sensibly in grief for it, It shall as level to your judgement 'pear As day does to your eye.
BOT: They bore him barefac'd on the bier, Hey non nonny, nonny, hey nonny And on his grave rain'd many a tear. - Fare you well, my dove!
USER: Hadst thou thy wits, and didst persuade revenge, It could not move thus.
BOT: You must sing 'Down a-down, and you call him a-down-a.' O, how the wheel becomes it! It is the false steward that stole his master's daughter.
USER: This nothing's more than matter.
BOT: There's rosemary, that's for remembrance; pray love, remember. And there is pansies, that's for thoughts.
USER: A document in madness, thoughts and remembrance fitted.
BOT: Thought and affliction, passion, hell itself She turns to favour and to prettiness.
USER: Do you see this, O God?
BOT: So you shall. And where th'offence is let the great axe fall. I pray you go with me.

USER: What are they that would speak with me?
BOT: Sailors, sir. They say they have letters for you.
USER: Let them come in. I do not know from what part of the world I should be greeted, if not from Lord Hamlet.
BOT: God bless you, sir.
USER: Let him bless thee too.
BOT: He shall, sir, and't please him. There's a letter for you, sir. It comes from th'ambassador that was bound for England; if your name be Horatio, as I am let to know it is.

USER: Now must your conscience my acquittance seal, And you must put me in your heart for friend, Sith you have heard, and with a knowing ear, That he which hath your noble father slain Pursu'd my life.
BOT: It well appears. But tell me Why you proceeded not against these feats, So crimeful and so capital in nature, As by your safety, wisdom, all things else, You mainly were stirr'd up.
USER: And so have I a noble father lost, A sister driven into desperate terms, Whose worth, if praises may go back again, Stood challenger on mount of all the age For her perfections. But my revenge will come.
BOT: Letters, my lord, from Hamlet. This to your Majesty; this to the Queen.
USER: From Hamlet! Who brought them?
BOT: Sailors, my lord, they say; I saw them not. They were given me by Claudio. He receiv'd them Of him that brought them.
USER: Know you the hand?
BOT: 'Tis Hamlet's character. 'Naked!' And in a postscript here he says 'alone.' Can you advise me?
USER: I am lost in it, my lord. But let him come, It warms the very sickness in my heart That I shall live and tell him to his teeth, 'Thus diest thou.'
BOT: If it be so, Laertes, - As how should it be so? How otherwise? - Will you be rul'd by me?
USER: Ay, my lord; So you will not o'errule me to a peace.
BOT: My lord, I will be rul'd; The rather if you could devise it so That I might be the organ.
USER: What part is that, my lord?
BOT: A Norman was't?
USER: A Norman.
BOT: Upon my life, Lamord.
USER: The very same.
BOT: I know him well. He is the brooch indeed And gem of all the nation.
USER: What out of this, my lord?
BOT: Laertes, was your father dear to you? Or are you like the painting of a sorrow, A face without a heart?
USER: Why ask you this?
BOT: To cut his throat i' th' church.
USER: One woe doth tread upon another's heel, So fast they follow. Your sister's drown'd, Laertes.
BOT: Drown'd! O, where?
USER: Alas, then she is drown'd?
BOT: Drown'd, drown'd.
USER: Let's follow, Gertrude; How much I had to do to calm his rage! Now fear I this will give it start again; Therefore let's follow.

USER: Is she to be buried in Christian burial, when she wilfully seeks her own salvation?
BOT: I tell thee she is, and therefore make her grave straight. The crowner hath sat on her, and finds it Christian burial.
USER: How can that be, unless she drowned herself in her own defence?
BOT: Why, 'tis found so.
USER: Nay, but hear you, goodman delver, -
BOT: But is this law?
USER: Ay, marry, is't, crowner's quest law.
BOT: Will you ha' the truth on't? If this had not been a gentlewoman, she should have been buried out o' Christian burial.
USER: Was he a gentleman?
BOT: He was the first that ever bore arms.
USER: Why, he had none.
BOT: What, art a heathen? How dost thou understand the Scripture? The Scripture says Adam digg'd. Could he dig without arms? I'll put another question to thee. If thou answerest me not to the purpose, confess thyself -
USER: Go to.
BOT: What is he that builds stronger than either the mason, the shipwright, or the carpenter?
USER: The gallows-maker; for that frame outlives a thousand tenants.
BOT: Who builds stronger than a mason, a shipwright, or a carpenter?
USER: Ay, tell me that, and unyoke.
BOT: Marry, now I can tell.
USER: To't.
BOT: Mass, I cannot tell.
USER: Has this fellow no feeling of his business, that he sings at grave-making?
BOT: Custom hath made it in him a property of easiness.
USER: 'Tis e'en so; the hand of little employment hath the daintier sense.
BOT: But age with his stealing steps Hath claw'd me in his clutch, And hath shipp'd me into the land, As if I had never been such.
USER: It might, my lord.
BOT: Or of a courtier, which could say 'Good morrow, sweet lord! How dost thou, good lord?' This might be my lord such-a-one, that praised my lord such-a-one's horse when he meant to beg it, might it not?
USER: Ay, my lord.
BOT: A pickaxe and a spade, a spade, For and a shrouding-sheet; O, a pit of clay for to be made For such a guest is meet.
USER: Not a jot more, my lord.
BOT: Is not parchment made of sheep-skins?
USER: Ay, my lord, and of calf-skins too.
BOT: They are sheep and calves which seek out assurance in that. I will speak to this fellow. - Whose grave's this, sir?
USER: Mine, sir. O, a pit of clay for to be made For such a guest is meet.
BOT: I think it be thine indeed, for thou liest in't.
USER: You lie out on't, sir, and therefore 'tis not yours. For my part, I do not lie in't, yet it is mine.
BOT: Thou dost lie in't, to be in't and say it is thine. 'Tis for the dead, not for the quick; therefore thou liest.
USER: 'Tis a quick lie, sir; 't will away again from me to you.
BOT: What man dost thou dig it for?
USER: For no man, sir.
BOT: What woman then?
USER: For none neither.
BOT: Who is to be buried in't?
USER: One that was a woman, sir; but, rest her soul, she's dead.
BOT: Of all the days i' th' year, I came to't that day that our last King Hamlet o'ercame Fortinbras.
USER: How long is that since?
BOT: Cannot you tell that? Every fool can tell that. It was the very day that young Hamlet was born, - he that is mad, and sent into England.
USER: Ay, marry, why was he sent into England?
BOT: Why, because he was mad; he shall recover his wits there; or if he do not, it's no great matter there.
USER: Why?
BOT: 'Twill not be seen in him there; there the men are as mad as he.
USER: How came he mad?
BOT: Very strangely, they say.
USER: How strangely?
BOT: Faith, e'en with losing his wits.
USER: Upon what ground?
BOT: Why, here in Denmark. I have been sexton here, man and boy, thirty years.
USER: How long will a man lie i' th'earth ere he rot?
BOT: Faith, if he be not rotten before he die, - as we have many pocky corses nowadays that will scarce hold the laying in, - he will last you some eight year or nine year. A tanner will last you nine year.
USER: Why he more than another?
BOT: Whose was it?
USER: A whoreson, mad fellow's it was. Whose do you think it was?
BOT: Nay, I know not.
USER: A pestilence on him for a mad rogue! A pour'd a flagon of Rhenish on my head once. This same skull, sir, was Yorick's skull, the King's jester.
BOT: This?
USER: E'en that.
BOT: What's that, my lord?
USER: Dost thou think Alexander looked o' this fashion i' th'earth?
BOT: E'en so.
USER: And smelt so? Pah!
BOT: E'en so, my lord.
USER: To what base uses we may return, Horatio! Why may not imagination trace the noble dust of Alexander till he find it stopping a bung-hole?
BOT: 'Twere to consider too curiously to consider so.
USER: What ceremony else?
BOT: That is Laertes, a very noble youth. Mark.
USER: What ceremony else?
BOT: Must there no more be done?
USER: No more be done. We should profane the service of the dead To sing sage requiem and such rest to her As to peace-parted souls.
BOT: Lay her i' th'earth, And from her fair and unpolluted flesh May violets spring. I tell thee, churlish priest, A minist'ring angel shall my sister be When thou liest howling.
USER: What, the fair Ophelia?
BOT: I hop'd thou shouldst have been my Hamlet's wife; I thought thy bride-bed to have deck'd, sweet maid, And not have strew'd thy grave.
USER: What is he whose grief Bears such an emphasis? whose phrase of sorrow Conjures the wand'ring stars, and makes them stand Like wonder-wounded hearers? This is I, Hamlet the Dane.
BOT: Thou pray'st not well. I prithee take thy fingers from my throat; For though I am not splenative and rash, Yet have I in me something dangerous, Which let thy wiseness fear. Away thy hand!
USER: Pluck them asunder.
BOT: Hamlet! Hamlet! All. Gentlemen!
USER: Good my lord, be quiet.
BOT: Why, I will fight with him upon this theme Until my eyelids will no longer wag.
USER: O my son, what theme?
BOT: I lov'd Ophelia; forty thousand brothers Could not, with all their quantity of love, Make up my sum. What wilt thou do for her?
USER: O, he is mad, Laertes.
BOT: For love of God forbear him!
USER: This is mere madness: And thus awhile the fit will work on him; Anon, as patient as the female dove, When that her golden couplets are disclos'd, His silence will sit drooping.
BOT: Hear you, sir; What is the reason that you use me thus? I lov'd you ever. But it is no matter. Let Hercules himself do what he may, The cat will mew, and dog will have his day.

USER: So much for this, sir. Now let me see the other; You do remember all the circumstance?
BOT: Remember it, my lord!
USER: That is most certain.
BOT: Is't possible?
USER: Here's the commission, read it at more leisure. But wilt thou hear me how I did proceed?
BOT: I beseech you.
USER: Ay, good my lord.
BOT: How was this seal'd?
USER: So Guildenstern and Rosencrantz go to't.
BOT: Why, what a king is this!
USER: It must be shortly known to him from England What is the issue of the business there.
BOT: Peace, who comes here?
USER: Your lordship is right welcome back to Denmark.
BOT: I humbly thank you, sir. Dost know this waterfly?
USER: No, my good lord.
BOT: Sweet lord, if your lordship were at leisure, I should impart a thing to you from his Majesty.
USER: I will receive it with all diligence of spirit. Put your bonnet to his right use; 'tis for the head.
BOT: I thank your lordship, 'tis very hot.
USER: No, believe me, 'tis very cold, the wind is northerly.
BOT: It is indifferent cold, my lord, indeed.
USER: Methinks it is very sultry and hot for my complexion.
BOT: Exceedingly, my lord; it is very sultry, - as 'twere - I cannot tell how. But, my lord, his Majesty bade me signify to you that he has laid a great wager on your head. Sir, this is the matter, -
USER: I beseech you, remember, -
BOT: Your lordship speaks most infallibly of him.
USER: The concernancy, sir? Why do we wrap the gentleman in our more rawer breath?
BOT: Sir?
USER: Is't not possible to understand in another tongue? You will do't, sir, really.
BOT: What imports the nomination of this gentleman?
USER: Of Laertes?
BOT: His purse is empty already, all's golden words are spent.
USER: Of him, sir.
BOT: I know you are not ignorant, -
USER: I would you did, sir; yet in faith if you did, it would not much approve me. Well, sir?
BOT: You are not ignorant of what excellence Laertes is, -
USER: I dare not confess that, lest I should compare with him in excellence; but to know a man well were to know himself.
BOT: I mean, sir, for his weapon; but in the imputation laid on him, by them in his meed he's unfellowed.
USER: What's his weapon?
BOT: Rapier and dagger.
USER: That's two of his weapons. But well.
BOT: What call you the carriages?
USER: I knew you must be edified by the margin ere you had done.
BOT: The carriages, sir, are the hangers.
USER: The King, sir, hath laid that in a dozen passes between you and him, he shall not exceed you three hits. He hath laid on twelve for nine. And it would come to immediate trial if your lordship would vouchsafe the answer.
BOT: How if I answer no?
USER: I mean, my lord, the opposition of your person in trial.
BOT: Shall I re-deliver you e'en so?
USER: To this effect, sir; after what flourish your nature will.
BOT: I commend my duty to your lordship.
USER: Yours, yours. He does well to commend it himself, there are no tongues else for's turn.
BOT: This lapwing runs away with the shell on his head.
USER: My lord, his Majesty commended him to you by young Osric, who brings back to him that you attend him in the hall. He sends to know if your pleasure hold to play with Laertes or that you will take longer time.
BOT: I am constant to my purposes, they follow the King's pleasure. If his fitness speaks, mine is ready. Now or whensoever, provided I be so able as now.
USER: The King and Queen and all are coming down.
BOT: In happy time.
USER: The Queen desires you to use some gentle entertainment to Laertes before you fall to play.
BOT: She well instructs me.
USER: You will lose this wager, my lord.
BOT: I do not think so. Since he went into France, I have been in continual practice. I shall win at the odds. But thou wouldst not think how ill all's here about my heart: but it is no matter.
USER: Nay, good my lord.
BOT: It is but foolery; but it is such a kind of gain-giving as would perhaps trouble a woman.
USER: If your mind dislike anything, obey it. I will forestall their repair hither, and say you are not fit.
BOT: Come, Hamlet, come, and take this hand from me.
USER: I embrace it freely, And will this brother's wager frankly play. - Give us the foils; come on.
BOT: Come, one for me.
USER: I'll be your foil, Laertes; in mine ignorance Your skill shall like a star i' th' darkest night, Stick fiery off indeed.
BOT: You mock me, sir.
USER: No, by this hand.
BOT: Give them the foils, young Osric. Cousin Hamlet, You know the wager?
USER: Very well, my lord. Your Grace has laid the odds o' the weaker side.
BOT: I do not fear it. I have seen you both; But since he is better'd, we have therefore odds.
USER: This is too heavy. Let me see another.
BOT: This likes me well. These foils have all a length?
USER: Ay, my good lord.
BOT: Come on, sir.
USER: Come, my lord.
BOT: One.
USER: No.
BOT: Judgement.
USER: A hit, a very palpable hit.
BOT: Well; again.
USER: Stay, give me drink. Hamlet, this pearl is thine; Here's to thy health. Give him the cup.
BOT: I'll play this bout first; set it by awhile. Come. Another hit; what say you?
USER: A touch, a touch, I do confess.
BOT: Our son shall win.
USER: He's fat, and scant of breath. Here, Hamlet, take my napkin, rub thy brows. The Queen carouses to thy fortune, Hamlet.
BOT: Good madam.
USER: Gertrude, do not drink.
BOT: I will, my lord; I pray you pardon me.
USER: I dare not drink yet, madam. By and by.
BOT: Come, let me wipe thy face.
USER: My lord, I'll hit him now.
BOT: I do not think't.
USER: Come for the third, Laertes. You do but dally. I pray you pass with your best violence. I am afeard you make a wanton of me.
BOT: Say you so? Come on.
USER: Nothing neither way.
BOT: Have at you now. Hamlet wounds Laertes._]
USER: Part them; they are incens'd.
BOT: Nay, come again!
USER: Look to the Queen there, ho!
BOT: They bleed on both sides. How is it, my lord?
USER: How is't, Laertes?
BOT: Why, as a woodcock to my own springe, Osric. I am justly kill'd with mine own treachery.
USER: How does the Queen?
BOT: She swoons to see them bleed.
USER: No, no, the drink, the drink! O my dear Hamlet! The drink, the drink! I am poison'd.
BOT: O villany! Ho! Let the door be lock'd: Treachery! Seek it out.
USER: The point envenom'd too! Then, venom, to thy work. OSRIC and LORDS. Treason! treason!
BOT: O yet defend me, friends. I am but hurt.
USER: Here, thou incestuous, murderous, damned Dane, Drink off this potion. Is thy union here? Follow my mother.
BOT: He is justly serv'd. It is a poison temper'd by himself. Exchange forgiveness with me, noble Hamlet. Mine and my father's death come not upon thee, Nor thine on me.
USER: Never believe it. I am more an antique Roman than a Dane. Here's yet some liquor left.
BOT: Young Fortinbras, with conquest come from Poland, To the ambassadors of England gives This warlike volley.
USER: Now cracks a noble heart. Good night, sweet prince, And flights of angels sing thee to thy rest. Why does the drum come hither?
BOT: Where is this sight?
USER: What is it you would see? If aught of woe or wonder, cease your search.
BOT: This quarry cries on havoc. O proud death, What feast is toward in thine eternal cell, That thou so many princes at a shot So bloodily hast struck?
USER: Let us haste to hear it, And call the noblest to the audience. For me, with sorrow I embrace my fortune. I have some rights of memory in this kingdom, Which now to claim my vantage doth invite me.
BOT: Of that I shall have also cause to speak, And from his mouth whose voice will draw on more. But let this same be presently perform'd, Even while men's minds are wild, lest more mischance On plots and errors happen.

USER: It seems then that the tidings of this broil Brake off our business for the Holy Land.
BOT: In faith, it is a conquest for a prince to boast of.
USER: This is his uncle's teaching, this is Worcester, Malevolent to you in all aspects, Which makes him prune himself, and bristle up The crest of youth against your dignity.
BOT: I will, my liege.

USER: Now, Hal, what time of day is it, lad?
BOT: What, none?
USER: No, by my troth, not so much as will serve to be prologue to an egg and butter.
BOT: Well, how then? Come, roundly, roundly.
USER: By the Lord, thou say'st true, lad. And is not my hostess of the tavern a most sweet wench?
BOT: As the honey of Hybla, my old lad of the castle. And is not a buff jerkin a most sweet robe of durance?
USER: How now, how now, mad wag? What, in thy quips and thy quiddities? What a plague have I to do with a buff jerkin?
BOT: Why, what a pox have I to do with my hostess of the tavern?
USER: Well, thou hast called her to a reckoning many a time and oft.
BOT: Did I ever call for thee to pay thy part?
USER: No, I'll give thee thy due, thou hast paid all there.
BOT: Yea, and elsewhere, so far as my coin would stretch, and where it would not, I have used my credit.
USER: No, thou shalt.
BOT: Shall I? O rare! By the Lord, I'll be a brave judge.
USER: Thou judgest false already, I mean thou shalt have the hanging of the thieves, and so become a rare hangman.
BOT: Well, Hal, well; and in some sort it jumps with my humour, as well as waiting in the court, I can tell you.
USER: For obtaining of suits?
BOT: Yea, for obtaining of suits, whereof the hangman hath no lean wardrobe. 'Sblood, I am as melancholy as a gib cat or a lugged bear.
USER: Or an old lion, or a lover's lute.
BOT: Yea, or the drone of a Lincolnshire bagpipe.
USER: What sayest thou to a hare, or the melancholy of Moor-ditch?
BOT: Thou didst well, for wisdom cries out in the streets and no man regards it.
USER: Where shall we take a purse tomorrow, Jack?
BOT: Zounds, where thou wilt, lad, I'll make one. An I do not, call me villain and baffle me.
USER: I see a good amendment of life in thee, from praying to purse-taking.
BOT: Good morrow, Ned.
USER: Sir John stands to his word, the devil shall have his bargain, for he was never yet a breaker of proverbs. He will give the devil his due.
BOT: Then art thou damned for keeping thy word with the devil.
USER: Else he had been damned for cozening the devil.
BOT: Hear ye, Yedward, if I tarry at home and go not, I'll hang you for going.
USER: You will, chops?
BOT: Hal, wilt thou make one?
USER: Who, I rob? I a thief? Not I, by my faith.
BOT: There's neither honesty, manhood, nor good fellowship in thee, nor thou cam'st not of the blood royal, if thou darest not stand for ten shillings.
USER: Well then, once in my days I'll be a madcap.
BOT: Why, that's well said.
USER: Well, come what will, I'll tarry at home.
BOT: By the Lord, I'll be a traitor then, when thou art king.
USER: I care not.
BOT: Sir John, I prithee, leave the Prince and me alone. I will lay him down such reasons for this adventure, that he shall go.
USER: Farewell, thou latter spring! Farewell, All-hallown summer!
BOT: But how shall we part with them in setting forth?
USER: Yea, but 'tis like that they will know us by our horses, by our habits, and by every other appointment, to be ourselves.
BOT: Tut, our horses they shall not see, I'll tie them in the wood; our visards we will change after we leave them; and, sirrah, I have cases of buckram for the nonce, to immask our noted outward garments.
USER: Yea, but I doubt they will be too hard for us.
BOT: Well, I'll go with thee. Provide us all things necessary and meet me tomorrow night in Eastcheap; there I'll sup. Farewell.
USER: Farewell, my lord.

USER: Our house, my sovereign liege, little deserves The scourge of greatness to be used on it, And that same greatness too which our own hands Have holp to make so portly.
BOT: My lord, -
USER: An if the devil come and roar for them, I will not send them. I will after straight And tell him so, for I will ease my heart, Albeit I make a hazard of my head.
BOT: What, drunk with choler? Stay, and pause awhile. Here comes your uncle.
USER: Brother, the King hath made your nephew mad.
BOT: Who struck this heat up after I was gone?
USER: He will forsooth have all my prisoners, And when I urged the ransom once again Of my wife's brother, then his cheek look'd pale, And on my face he turn'd an eye of death, Trembling even at the name of Mortimer.
BOT: I cannot blame him. Was not he proclaim'd By Richard that dead is, the next of blood?
USER: And for whose death we in the world's wide mouth Live scandalized and foully spoken of.
BOT: But soft, I pray you, did King Richard then Proclaim my brother Edmund Mortimer Heir to the crown?
USER: He did; myself did hear it.
BOT: If we fall in, good night, or sink or swim! Send danger from the east unto the west, So honour cross it from the north to south, And let them grapple. O, the blood more stirs To rouse a lion than to start a hare!
USER: Imagination of some great exploit Drives him beyond the bounds of patience.
BOT: He apprehends a world of figures here, But not the form of what he should attend. - Good cousin, give me audience for a while.
USER: I cry you mercy.
BOT: Those same noble Scots That are your prisoners -
USER: I'll keep them all; By God, he shall not have a Scot of them, No, if a Scot would save his soul, he shall not. I'll keep them, by this hand!
BOT: You start away, And lend no ear unto my purposes: Those prisoners you shall keep -
USER: Hear you, cousin, a word.
BOT: Farewell, kinsman. I will talk to you When you are better temper'd to attend.
USER: Why, what a wasp-stung and impatient fool Art thou to break into this woman's mood, Tying thine ear to no tongue but thine own!
BOT: At Berkeley castle.
USER: Nay, if you have not, to it again, We will stay your leisure.
BOT: I have done, i'faith.
USER: Of York, is it not?
BOT: I smell it. Upon my life it will do well.
USER: Before the game is afoot thou still let'st slip.
