BOT: Why, it cannot choose but be a noble plot; And then the power of Scotland and of York To join with Mortimer, ha?
USER: And so they shall.
BOT: In faith, it is exceedingly well aim'd.
USER: He does, he does, we'll be revenged on him.
BOT: Farewell, good brother; we shall thrive, I trust.
USER: Uncle, adieu. O, let the hours be short, Till fields and blows and groans applaud our sport!

USER: Heigh-ho! an it be not four by the day, I'll be hang'd. Charles' wain is over the new chimney, and yet our horse not pack'd. - What, ostler!
BOT: I prithee, Tom, beat Cut's saddle, put a few flocks in the point; poor jade is wrung in the withers out of all cess.
USER: Peas and beans are as dank here as a dog, and that is the next way to give poor jades the bots. This house is turned upside down since Robin ostler died.
BOT: Poor fellow never joyed since the price of oats rose, it was the death of him.
USER: I think this be the most villainous house in all London road for fleas. I am stung like a tench.
BOT: Like a tench! By the Mass, there is ne'er a king christen could be better bit than I have been since the first cock.
USER: Why, they will allow us ne'er a jordan, and then we leak in your chimney, and your chamber-lye breeds fleas like a loach.
BOT: What, ostler! Come away and be hanged, come away.
USER: I have a gammon of bacon and two razes of ginger, to be delivered as far as Charing Cross.
BOT: Good morrow, carriers. What's o'clock?
USER: I think it be two o'clock.
BOT: I prithee, lend me thy lantern, to see my gelding in the stable.
USER: Nay, by God, soft! I know a trick worth two of that, i'faith.
BOT: I pray thee, lend me thine.
USER: Ay, when? Canst tell? "Lend me thy lantern," quoth he! Marry, I'll see thee hanged first.
BOT: Sirrah carrier, what time do you mean to come to London?
USER: Time enough to go to bed with a candle, I warrant thee. Come, neighbour Mugs, we'll call up the gentlemen. They will along with company, for they have great charge.
BOT: What, ho! Chamberlain!
USER: At hand, quoth pick-purse.
BOT: That's even as fair as "at hand, quoth the chamberlain," for thou variest no more from picking of purses than giving direction doth from labouring; thou layest the plot how.
USER: Sirrah, if they meet not with Saint Nicholas' clerks, I'll give thee this neck.
BOT: No, I'll none of it. I pray thee, keep that for the hangman, for I know thou worshippest Saint Nicholas as truly as a man of falsehood may.
USER: What, the commonwealth their boots? Will she hold out water in foul way?
BOT: She will, she will; justice hath liquored her. We steal as in a castle, cock-sure; we have the receipt of fern-seed, we walk invisible.
USER: Nay, by my faith, I think you are more beholding to the night than to fern-seed for your walking invisible.
BOT: Give me thy hand. Thou shalt have a share in our purchase, as I am a true man.
USER: Nay, rather let me have it, as you are a false thief.
BOT: Go to; _homo_ is a common name to all men. Bid the ostler bring my gelding out of the stable. Farewell, you muddy knave.

USER: Come, shelter, shelter! I have removed Falstaff's horse, and he frets like a gummed velvet.
BOT: Stand close.
USER: Poins! Poins, and be hanged! Poins!
BOT: Peace, ye fat-kidneyed rascal! What a brawling dost thou keep!
USER: Where's Poins, Hal?
BOT: He is walked up to the top of the hill. I'll go seek him.
USER: to the ground, and list if thou canst hear the tread of travellers.
BOT: Have you any levers to lift me up again, being down? 'Sblood, I'll not bear my own flesh so far afoot again for all the coin in thy father's exchequer. What a plague mean ye to colt me thus?
USER: Thou liest, thou art not colted, thou art uncolted.
BOT: I prithee, good Prince Hal, help me to my horse, good king's son.
USER: Out, ye rogue! Shall I be your ostler?
BOT: Stand!
USER: So I do, against my will.
BOT: O, 'tis our setter. I know his voice. Comes forward with Bardolph and Peto.
USER: What news?
BOT: Case ye, case ye, on with your visards. There's money of the King's coming down the hill, 'tis going to the King's exchequer.
USER: You lie, ye rogue, 'tis going to the King's tavern.
BOT: There's enough to make us all.
USER: To be hanged.
BOT: Sirs, you four shall front them in the narrow lane. Ned Poins and I will walk lower; if they 'scape from your encounter, then they light on us.
USER: How many be there of them?
BOT: Some eight or ten.
USER: Zounds, will they not rob us?
BOT: What, a coward, Sir John Paunch?
USER: Indeed, I am not John of Gaunt, your grandfather, but yet no coward, Hal.
BOT: Well, we leave that to the proof.
USER: Sirrah Jack, thy horse stands behind the hedge. When thou need'st him, there thou shalt find him. Farewell, and stand fast.
BOT: Now cannot I strike him, if I should be hanged.
USER: Now, my masters, happy man be his dole, say I. Every man to his business.
BOT: Come, neighbour, the boy shall lead our horses down the hill; we'll walk afoot awhile and ease our legs.
USER: Stand!
BOT: Jesu bless us!
USER: Strike, down with them, cut the villains' throats! Ah, whoreson caterpillars, bacon-fed knaves, they hate us youth. Down with them, fleece them!
BOT: O, we are undone, both we and ours for ever!
USER: Hang ye, gorbellied knaves, are ye undone? No, ye fat chuffs, I would your store were here! On, bacons, on! What, ye knaves! young men must live. You are grandjurors, are ye? We'll jure ye, faith.
BOT: The thieves have bound the true men. Now could thou and I rob the thieves, and go merrily to London, it would be argument for a week, laughter for a month, and a good jest for ever.
USER: Stand close, I hear them coming.
BOT: Come, my masters, let us share, and then to horse before day. An the Prince and Poins be not two arrant cowards, there's no equity stirring. There's no more valour in that Poins than in a wild duck.
USER: Your money!
BOT: Villains! booty behind them._]
USER: How the fat rogue roared!

USER: What, ho! Is Gilliams with the packet gone?
BOT: He is, my lord, an hour ago.
USER: Hath Butler brought those horses from the sheriff?
BOT: One horse, my lord, he brought even now.
USER: What horse? A roan, a crop-ear, is it not?
BOT: It is, my lord.
USER: That roan shall be my throne. Well, I will back him straight. O Esperance! Bid Butler lead him forth into the park.
BOT: But hear you, my lord.
USER: What say'st thou, my lady?
BOT: What is it carries you away?
USER: Why, my horse, my love, my horse.
BOT: So far afoot, I shall be weary, love.
USER: Come, come, you paraquito, answer me Directly unto this question that I ask. In faith, I'll break thy little finger, Harry, If thou wilt not tell me all things true.
BOT: Do you not love me? Do you not indeed? Well, do not, then, for since you love me not, I will not love myself. Do you not love me? Nay, tell me if you speak in jest or no.
USER: How? So far?
BOT: Not an inch further. But hark you, Kate, Whither I go, thither shall you go too. Today will I set forth, tomorrow you. Will this content you, Kate?
USER: It must, of force.

USER: Ned, prithee, come out of that fat room, and lend me thy hand to laugh a little.
BOT: Where hast been, Hal?
USER: Thou art perfect.
BOT: Anon, anon, sir. - Look down into the Pomegarnet, Ralph.
USER: Come hither, Francis.
BOT: My lord?
USER: How long hast thou to serve, Francis?
BOT: Forsooth, five years, and as much as to -
USER: Anon, anon, sir.
BOT: Five year! By'r Lady, a long lease for the clinking of pewter! But, Francis, darest thou be so valiant as to play the coward with thy indenture, and show it a fair pair of heels, and run from it?
USER: O Lord, sir, I'll be sworn upon all the books in England, I could find in my heart -
BOT: Anon, sir.
USER: How old art thou, Francis?
BOT: Let me see, about Michaelmas next I shall be -
USER: Anon, sir. - Pray, stay a little, my lord.
BOT: Nay, but hark you, Francis, for the sugar thou gavest me, 'twas a pennyworth, was't not?
USER: O Lord, I would it had been two!
BOT: I will give thee for it a thousand pound. Ask me when thou wilt, and thou shalt have it.
USER: Anon, anon.
BOT: Anon, Francis? No, Francis, but tomorrow, Francis; or, Francis, a Thursday; or indeed, Francis, when thou wilt. But, Francis, -
USER: My lord?
BOT: Wilt thou rob this leathern-jerkin, crystal-button, not-pated, agate-ring, puke-stocking, caddis-garter, smooth-tongue, Spanish-pouch -
USER: O Lord, sir, who do you mean?
BOT: Why, then, your brown bastard is your only drink, for look you, Francis, your white canvas doublet will sully. In Barbary, sir, it cannot come to so much.
USER: What, sir?
BOT: Away, you rogue! Dost thou not hear them call? way to go._]
USER: What, stand'st thou still, and hear'st such a calling? Look to the guests within. My lord, old Sir John with half-a-dozen more are at the door. Shall I let them in?
BOT: Let them alone awhile, and then open the door. Poins!
USER: Anon, anon, sir.
BOT: Sirrah, Falstaff and the rest of the thieves are at the door; shall we be merry?
USER: As merry as crickets, my lad. But hark ye, what cunning match have you made with this jest of the drawer? Come, what's the issue?
BOT: I am now of all humours that have showed themselves humours since the old days of goodman Adam to the pupil age of this present twelve o'clock at midnight. What's o'clock, Francis?
USER: Anon, anon, sir.
BOT: Welcome, Jack. Where hast thou been?
USER: Didst thou never see Titan kiss a dish of butter (pitiful-hearted Titan!), that melted at the sweet tale of the sun's? If thou didst, then behold that compound.
BOT: How now, wool-sack, what mutter you?
USER: A king's son! If I do not beat thee out of thy kingdom with a dagger of lath, and drive all thy subjects afore thee like a flock of wild-geese, I'll never wear hair on my face more. You, Prince of Wales!
BOT: Why, you whoreson round man, what's the matter?
USER: Are not you a coward? Answer me to that - and Poins there?
BOT: Zounds, ye fat paunch, an ye call me coward, by the Lord, I'll stab thee.
USER: O villain! Thy lips are scarce wiped since thou drunk'st last.
BOT: All is one for that. A plague of all cowards, still say I.
USER: What's the matter?
BOT: What's the matter? There be four of us here have ta'en a thousand pound this day morning.
USER: Where is it, Jack, where is it?
BOT: Where is it? Taken from us it is: a hundred upon poor four of us.
USER: What, a hundred, man?
BOT: Speak, sirs, how was it?
USER: We four set upon some dozen.
BOT: Sixteen at least, my lord.
USER: And bound them.
BOT: No, no, they were not bound.
USER: You rogue, they were bound, every man of them, or I am a Jew else, an Ebrew Jew.
BOT: As we were sharing, some six or seven fresh men set upon us.
USER: And unbound the rest, and then come in the other.
BOT: What, fought you with them all?
USER: All? I know not what you call all, but if I fought not with fifty of them I am a bunch of radish. If there were not two or three and fifty upon poor old Jack, then am I no two-legged creature.
BOT: Pray God you have not murdered some of them.
USER: What, four? Thou saidst but two even now.
BOT: Four, Hal, I told thee four.
USER: Ay, ay, he said four.
BOT: These four came all afront, and mainly thrust at me. I made me no more ado, but took all their seven points in my target, thus.
USER: Seven? Why, there were but four even now.
BOT: In buckram?
USER: Ay, four, in buckram suits.
BOT: Seven, by these hilts, or I am a villain else.
USER: Dost thou hear me, Hal?
BOT: Ay, and mark thee too, Jack.
USER: Do so, for it is worth the listening to. These nine in buckram that I told thee of -
BOT: So, two more already.
USER: Their points being broken -
BOT: Down fell their hose.
USER: Began to give me ground; but I followed me close, came in foot and hand, and with a thought seven of the eleven I paid.
BOT: O monstrous! Eleven buckram men grown out of two!
USER: But as the devil would have it, three misbegotten knaves in Kendal green came at my back and let drive at me, for it was so dark, Hal, that thou couldst not see thy hand.
BOT: These lies are like the father that begets them, gross as a mountain, open, palpable. Why, thou clay-brained guts, thou knotty-pated fool, thou whoreson, obscene greasy tallow-catch -
USER: What, art thou mad? Art thou mad? Is not the truth the truth?
BOT: Why, how couldst thou know these men in Kendal green, when it was so dark thou couldst not see thy hand? Come, tell us your reason. What sayest thou to this?
USER: Come, your reason, Jack, your reason.
BOT: I'll be no longer guilty of this sin. This sanguine coward, this bed-presser, this horse-back-breaker, this huge hill of flesh -
USER: 'Sblood, you starveling, you eel-skin, you dried neat's-tongue, you bull's pizzle, you stock-fish - O, for breath to utter what is like thee! You tailor's-yard, you sheath, you bow-case, you vile standing tuck -
BOT: Well, breathe awhile, and then to it again, and when thou hast tired thyself in base comparisons, hear me speak but this.
USER: Mark, Jack.
BOT: Come, let's hear, Jack, what trick hast thou now?
USER: Content; and the argument shall be thy running away.
BOT: Ah, no more of that, Hal, an thou lovest me!
USER: O Jesu, my lord the Prince -
BOT: How now, my lady the hostess! What say'st thou to me?
USER: Marry, my lord, there is a nobleman of the court at door would speak with you: he says he comes from your father.
BOT: Give him as much as will make him a royal man, and send him back again to my mother.
USER: What manner of man is he?
BOT: An old man.
USER: What doth gravity out of his bed at midnight? Shall I give him his answer?
BOT: Prithee do, Jack.
USER: Faith, and I'll send him packing.
BOT: Now, sirs: by'r Lady, you fought fair, so did you, Peto. So did you, Bardolph. You are lions, too, you ran away upon instinct, you will not touch the true prince, no, fie!
USER: Faith, I ran when I saw others run.
BOT: Faith, tell me now in earnest, how came Falstaff's sword so hacked?
USER: Why, he hacked it with his dagger, and said he would swear truth out of England but he would make you believe it was done in fight, and persuaded us to do the like.
BOT: My lord, do you see these meteors? Do you behold these exhalations?
USER: I do.
BOT: What think you they portend?
USER: Hot livers and cold purses.
BOT: Choler, my lord, if rightly taken.
USER: No, if rightly taken, halter. Here comes lean Jack, here comes bare-bone. How now, my sweet creature of bombast? How long is't ago, Jack, since thou sawest thine own knee?
BOT: O, Glendower.
USER: Owen, Owen, the same; and his son-in-law Mortimer, and old Northumberland, and that sprightly Scot of Scots, Douglas, that runs a-horseback up a hill perpendicular -
BOT: He that rides at high speed, and with his pistol kills a sparrow flying.
USER: You have hit it.
BOT: So did he never the sparrow.
USER: Well, that rascal hath good metal in him, he will not run.
BOT: Why, what a rascal art thou then, to praise him so for running!
USER: A-horseback, ye cuckoo, but afoot he will not budge a foot.
BOT: Yes, Jack, upon instinct.
USER: Why then, it is like if there come a hot June, and this civil buffeting hold, we shall buy maidenheads as they buy hobnails, by the hundreds.
BOT: Not a whit, i'faith. I lack some of thy instinct.
USER: Well, thou wilt be horribly chid tomorrow when thou comest to thy father. If thou love me practise an answer.
BOT: Do thou stand for my father and examine me upon the particulars of my life.
USER: Shall I? Content! This chair shall be my state, this dagger my sceptre, and this cushion my crown.
BOT: Thy state is taken for a joint-stool, thy golden sceptre for a leaden dagger, and thy precious rich crown for a pitiful bald crown.
USER: Well, here is my leg.
BOT: And here is my speech. Stand aside, nobility.
USER: O Jesu, this is excellent sport, i faith!
BOT: Weep not, sweet Queen, for trickling tears are vain.
USER: O, the Father, how he holds his countenance!
BOT: For God's sake, lords, convey my tristful Queen, For tears do stop the floodgates of her eyes.
USER: O Jesu, he doth it as like one of these harlotry players as ever I see!
BOT: What manner of man, an it like your Majesty?
USER: Dost thou speak like a king? Do thou stand for me, and I'll play my father.
BOT: Depose me? If thou dost it half so gravely, so majestically, both in word and matter, hang me up by the heels for a rabbit-sucker or a poulter's hare.
USER: Well, here I am set.
BOT: And here I stand. Judge, my masters.
USER: Now, Harry, whence come you?
BOT: My noble lord, from Eastcheap.
USER: The complaints I hear of thee are grievous.
BOT: 'Sblood, my lord, they are false. - Nay, I'll tickle ye for a young prince, i'faith.
USER: I would your Grace would take me with you. Whom means your Grace?
BOT: That villainous abominable misleader of youth, Falstaff, that old white-bearded Satan.
USER: My lord, the man I know.
BOT: I know thou dost.
USER: I do, I will.
BOT: O, my lord, my lord, the sheriff with a most monstrous watch is at the door.
USER: Out, ye rogue! Play out the play. I have much to say in the behalf of that Falstaff.
BOT: O Jesu, my lord, my lord -
USER: Heigh, heigh, the devil rides upon a fiddlestick. What's the matter?
BOT: The sheriff and all the watch are at the door. They are come to search the house. Shall I let them in?
USER: Dost thou hear, Hal? Never call a true piece of gold a counterfeit: thou art essentially made without seeming so.
BOT: And thou a natural coward without instinct.
USER: I deny your major. If you will deny the sheriff, so; if not, let him enter. If I become not a cart as well as another man, a plague on my bringing up! I hope I shall as soon be strangled with a halter as another.
BOT: Go hide thee behind the arras. The rest walk up above. Now, my masters, for a true face and good conscience.
USER: Both which I have had, but their date is out, and therefore I'll hide me.
BOT: Call in the sheriff. Now, master sheriff, what is your will with me?
USER: First, pardon me, my lord. A hue and cry Hath followed certain men unto this house.
BOT: What men?
USER: One of them is well known, my gracious lord, A gross fat man.
BOT: As fat as butter.
USER: I will, my lord. There are two gentlemen Have in this robbery lost three hundred marks.
BOT: It may be so. If he have robb'd these men, He shall be answerable; and so, farewell.
USER: Good night, my noble lord.
BOT: I think it is good morrow, is it not?
USER: Indeed, my lord, I think it be two o'clock.
BOT: This oily rascal is known as well as Paul's. Go, call him forth.
USER: Falstaff! - Fast asleep behind the arras, and snorting like a horse.
BOT: Hark, how hard he fetches breath. Search his pockets. What hast thou found?
USER: Nothing but papers, my lord.
BOT: Let's see what they be. Read them.
USER: Good morrow, good my lord.

USER: These promises are fair, the parties sure, And our induction full of prosperous hope.
BOT: Lord Mortimer and cousin Glendower, Will you sit down? And uncle Worcester, A plague upon it! I have forgot the map.
USER: No, here it is. Sit, cousin Percy, sit, good cousin Hotspur; For by that name as oft as Lancaster doth speak of you His cheek looks pale, and with a rising sigh He wisheth you in heaven.
BOT: And you in hell, As oft as he hears Owen Glendower spoke of.
USER: I cannot blame him. At my nativity The front of heaven was full of fiery shapes, Of burning cressets, and at my birth The frame and huge foundation of the Earth Shaked like a coward.
BOT: Why, so it would have done At the same season, if your mother's cat Had but kitten'd, though yourself had never been born.
USER: I say the Earth did shake when I was born.
BOT: And I say the Earth was not of my mind, If you suppose as fearing you it shook.
USER: The heavens were all on fire, the Earth did tremble.
BOT: I think there is no man speaks better Welsh. I'll to dinner.
USER: Peace, cousin Percy, you will make him mad.
BOT: I can call spirits from the vasty deep.
USER: Why, so can I, or so can any man, But will they come when you do call for them?
BOT: Why, I can teach you, cousin, to command the devil.
USER: Come, come, no more of this unprofitable chat.
BOT: Three times hath Henry Bolingbroke made head Against my power; thrice from the banks of Wye And sandy-bottom'd Severn have I sent him Bootless home and weather-beaten back.
USER: Home without boots, and in foul weather too! How 'scapes he agues, in the devil's name!
BOT: Come, here's the map, shall we divide our right According to our threefold order ta'en?
USER: A shorter time shall send me to you, lords, And in my conduct shall your ladies come, From whom you now must steal, and take no leave, For there will be a world of water shed Upon the parting of your wives and you.
BOT: Not wind? It shall, it must. You see it doth.
USER: Yea, but mark how he bears his course, and runs me up With like advantage on the other side, Gelding the opposed continent as much As on the other side it takes from you.
BOT: Yea, but a little charge will trench him here, And on this north side win this cape of land, And then he runs straight and even.
USER: I'll have it so, a little charge will do it.
BOT: I'll not have it altered.
USER: Will not you?
BOT: No, nor you shall not.
USER: Who shall say me nay?
BOT: Why, that will I.
USER: Let me not understand you, then; speak it in Welsh.
BOT: Come, you shall have Trent turn'd.
USER: I do not care. I'll give thrice so much land To any well-deserving friend; But in the way of bargain, mark ye me, I'll cavil on the ninth part of a hair. Are the indentures drawn? Shall we be gone?
BOT: The moon shines fair, you may away by night. I'll haste the writer, and withal Break with your wives of your departure hence. I am afraid my daughter will run mad, So much she doteth on her Mortimer.
USER: Fie, cousin Percy, how you cross my father!
BOT: Well, I am school'd. Good manners be your speed! Here come our wives, and let us take our leave.
USER: This is the deadly spite that angers me, My wife can speak no English, I no Welsh.
BOT: My daughter weeps, she'll not part with you, She'll be a soldier too, she'll to the wars.
USER: Good father, tell her that she and my aunt Percy Shall follow in your conduct speedily. the same._]
BOT: She is desperate here, a peevish self-willed harlotry, One that no persuasion can do good upon.
USER: Nay, if you melt, then will she run mad.
BOT: O, I am ignorance itself in this!
USER: With all my heart I'll sit and hear her sing, By that time will our book, I think, be drawn.
BOT: Do so, and those musicians that shall play to you Hang in the air a thousand leagues from hence, And straight they shall be here: sit, and attend.
USER: Come, Kate, thou art perfect in lying down. Come, quick, quick, that I may lay my head in thy lap.
BOT: Go, ye giddy goose.
USER: Now I perceive the devil understands Welsh, And 'tis no marvel he's so humorous. By'r Lady, he's a good musician.
BOT: Then should you be nothing but musical, For you are altogether governed by humours. Lie still, ye thief, and hear the lady sing in Welsh.
USER: I had rather hear Lady, my brach, howl in Irish.
BOT: Wouldst thou have thy head broken?
USER: No.
BOT: Then be still.
USER: Neither; 'tis a woman's fault.
BOT: Now God help thee!
USER: To the Welsh lady's bed.
BOT: What's that?
USER: Peace, she sings. Come, Kate, I'll have your song too.
BOT: Not mine, in good sooth.
USER: I will not sing.
BOT: 'Tis the next way to turn tailor, or be redbreast-teacher. An the indentures be drawn, I'll away within these two hours; and so come in when ye will.
USER: Come, come, Lord Mortimer, you are as slow As hot Lord Percy is on fire to go. By this our book is drawn. We'll but seal, And then to horse immediately.
BOT: With all my heart.

USER: I shall hereafter, my thrice gracious lord, Be more myself.
BOT: A hundred thousand rebels die in this. Thou shalt have charge and sovereign trust herein. How now, good Blunt? Thy looks are full of speed.

USER: Sir John, you are so fretful, you cannot live long.
BOT: Why, you are so fat, Sir John, that you must needs be out of all compass, out of all reasonable compass, Sir John.
USER: Do thou amend thy face, and I'll amend my life. Thou art our admiral, thou bearest the lantern in the poop, but 'tis in the nose of thee. Thou art the Knight of the Burning Lamp.
BOT: Why, Sir John, my face does you no harm.
USER: 'Sblood, I would my face were in your belly!
BOT: God-a-mercy! so should I be sure to be heartburnt. How now, Dame Partlet the hen, have you enquired yet who picked my pocket?
USER: Ye lie, hostess. Bardolph was shaved and lost many a hair, and I'll be sworn my pocket was picked. Go to, you are a woman, go.
BOT: Who, I? No; I defy thee: God's light, I was never called so in mine own house before.
USER: Go to, I know you well enough.
BOT: No, Sir John, you do not know me, Sir John. I know you, Sir John, you owe me money, Sir John, and now you pick a quarrel to beguile me of it. I bought you a dozen of shirts to your back.
USER: Dowlas, filthy dowlas. I have given them away to bakers' wives; and they have made bolters of them.
BOT: Now, as I am a true woman, holland of eight shillings an ell. You owe money here besides, Sir John, for your diet and by-drinkings, and money lent you, four-and-twenty pound.
USER: He had his part of it, let him pay.
BOT: He? Alas, he is poor, he hath nothing.
USER: O Jesu, I have heard the Prince tell him, I know not how oft, that that ring was copper.
BOT: How? The Prince is a Jack, a sneak-up. 'Sblood, an he were here, I would cudgel him like a dog if he would say so. his truncheon like a fife. How now, lad? Is the wind in that door, i'faith? Must we all march?
USER: Yea, two and two, Newgate fashion.
BOT: My lord, I pray you, hear me.
USER: What say'st thou, Mistress Quickly? How doth thy husband? I love him well; he is an honest man.
BOT: Good my lord, hear me.
USER: Prithee, let her alone, and list to me.
BOT: What say'st thou, Jack?
USER: The other night I fell asleep here, behind the arras, and had my pocket picked. This house is turned bawdy-house; they pick pockets.
BOT: What didst thou lose, Jack?
USER: Wilt thou believe me, Hal, three or four bonds of forty pound apiece and a seal-ring of my grandfather's.
BOT: A trifle, some eightpenny matter.
USER: So I told him, my lord, and I said I heard your Grace say so. And, my lord, he speaks most vilely of you, like a foul-mouthed man as he is, and said he would cudgel you.
BOT: What! he did not?
USER: There's neither faith, truth, nor womanhood in me else.
BOT: There's no more faith in thee than in a stewed prune, nor no more truth in thee than in a drawn fox; and, for woman-hood, Maid Marian may be the deputy's wife of the ward to thee. Go, you thing, go.
USER: Say, what thing, what thing?
BOT: What thing? Why, a thing to thank God on.
USER: I am no thing to thank God on, I would thou shouldst know it! I am an honest man's wife, and, setting thy knighthood aside, thou art a knave to call me so.
BOT: Setting thy womanhood aside, thou art a beast to say otherwise.
USER: Say, what beast, thou knave, thou?
BOT: What beast? Why, an otter.
USER: An otter, Sir John? Why an otter?
BOT: Why, she's neither fish nor flesh; a man knows not where to have her.
USER: Thou art an unjust man in saying so, thou or any man knows where to have me, thou knave, thou.
BOT: Thou say'st true, hostess, and he slanders thee most grossly.
USER: So he doth you, my lord, and said this other day you ought him a thousand pound.
BOT: Sirrah, do I owe you a thousand pound?
USER: A thousand pound, Hal? A million. Thy love is worth a million; thou owest me thy love.
BOT: Nay, my lord, he call'd you Jack, and said he would cudgel you.
USER: Did I, Bardolph?
BOT: Indeed, Sir John, you said so.
USER: Yea, if he said my ring was copper.
BOT: I say 'tis copper. Darest thou be as good as thy word now?
USER: Why, Hal, thou knowest, as thou art but man, I dare, but as thou art prince, I fear thee as I fear the roaring of the lion's whelp.
BOT: And why not as the lion?
USER: The King himself is to be feared as the lion. Dost thou think I'll fear thee as I fear thy father? Nay, an I do, I pray God my girdle break.
BOT: It appears so by the story.
USER: O, my sweet beef, I must still be good angel to thee. The money is paid back again.
BOT: O, I do not like that paying back, 'tis a double labour.
USER: I am good friends with my father, and may do anything.
BOT: Rob me the exchequer the first thing thou dost, and do it with unwashed hands too.
USER: Do, my lord.
BOT: I have procured thee, Jack, a charge of foot.
USER: Bardolph!
BOT: My lord?
USER: Rare words! Brave world! - Hostess, my breakfast, come. - O, I could wish this tavern were my drum.

USER: Thou art the king of honour. No man so potent breathes upon the ground But I will beard him.
BOT: Do so, and 'tis well. What letters hast thou there? I can but thank you.
USER: These letters come from your father.
BOT: Letters from him! Why comes he not himself?
USER: He cannot come, my lord, he is grievous sick.
BOT: Zounds, how has he the leisure to be sick In such a justling time? Who leads his power? Under whose government come they along?
USER: His letters bear his mind, not I, my lord.
BOT: I prithee, tell me, doth he keep his bed?
USER: He did, my lord, four days ere I set forth, And at the time of my departure thence He was much fear'd by his physicians.
BOT: I would the state of time had first been whole Ere he by sickness had been visited. His health was never better worth than now.
USER: Your father's sickness is a maim to us.
BOT: Faith, and so we should, where now remains A sweet reversion. We may boldly spend Upon the hope of what is to come in. A comfort of retirement lives in this.
USER: A rendezvous, a home to fly unto, If that the devil and mischance look big Upon the maidenhead of our affairs.
BOT: As heart can think. There is not such a word Spoke in Scotland as this term of fear.
USER: My cousin Vernon! Welcome, by my soul.
BOT: Pray God my news be worth a welcome, lord. The Earl of Westmoreland, seven thousand strong, Is marching hitherwards, with him Prince John.
USER: No harm, what more?
BOT: And further, I have learn'd The King himself in person is set forth, Or hitherwards intended speedily, With strong and mighty preparation.
USER: He shall be welcome too. Where is his son, The nimble-footed madcap Prince of Wales, And his comrades, that daffed the world aside And bid it pass?
BOT: There is more news. I learn'd in Worcester, as I rode along, He cannot draw his power this fourteen days.
USER: That's the worst tidings that I hear of yet.
BOT: Ay, by my faith, that bears a frosty sound.
USER: What may the King's whole battle reach unto?
BOT: To thirty thousand.
USER: Forty let it be. My father and Glendower being both away, The powers of us may serve so great a day. Come, let us take a muster speedily. Doomsday is near; die all, die merrily.
BOT: Talk not of dying. I am out of fear Of death or death's hand for this one half year.

USER: Bardolph, get thee before to Coventry; fill me a bottle of sack. Our soldiers shall march through; we'll to Sutton Co'fil' tonight.
BOT: Will you give me money, captain?
USER: Lay out, lay out.
BOT: This bottle makes an angel.
USER: An if it do, take it for thy labour. An if it make twenty, take them all, I'll answer the coinage. Bid my lieutenant Peto meet me at town's end.
BOT: I will, captain: farewell.
USER: How now, blown Jack? How now, quilt?
BOT: What, Hal! How now, mad wag? What a devil dost thou in Warwickshire? My good Lord of Westmoreland, I cry you mercy. I thought your honour had already been at Shrewsbury.
USER: Faith, Sir John, 'tis more than time that I were there, and you too, but my powers are there already. The King, I can tell you, looks for us all. We must away all night.
BOT: Tut, never fear me. I am as vigilant as a cat to steal cream.
USER: I think, to steal cream indeed, for thy theft hath already made thee butter. But tell me, Jack, whose fellows are these that come after?
BOT: Mine, Hal, mine.
USER: I did never see such pitiful rascals.
BOT: Tut, tut, good enough to toss; food for powder, food for powder, they'll fill a pit as well as better. Tush, man, mortal men, mortal men.
USER: Ay, but, Sir John, methinks they are exceeding poor and bare, too beggarly.
BOT: Faith, for their poverty, I know not where they had that; and for their bareness, I am sure they never learned that of me.
USER: No, I'll be sworn, unless you call three fingers on the ribs bare. But, sirrah, make haste. Percy is already in the field.
BOT: What, is the King encamped?
USER: He is, Sir John. I fear we shall stay too long.
BOT: Well, To the latter end of a fray and the beginning of a feast Fits a dull fighter and a keen guest.

USER: We'll fight with him tonight.
BOT: It may not be.
USER: You give him then advantage.
BOT: Not a whit.
USER: Why say you so? Looks he not for supply?
BOT: So do we.
USER: His is certain, ours is doubtful.
BOT: Good cousin, be advised, stir not tonight.
USER: Do not, my lord.
BOT: You do not counsel well. You speak it out of fear and cold heart.
USER: Yea, or tonight.
BOT: Content.
USER: Tonight, say I.
BOT: So are the horses of the enemy In general, journey-bated and brought low. The better part of ours are full of rest.
USER: The number of the King exceedeth ours. For God's sake, cousin, stay till all come in.
BOT: I come with gracious offers from the King, If you vouchsafe me hearing and respect.
USER: Tut, I came not to hear this.
BOT: Shall I return this answer to the King?
USER: Not so, Sir Walter. We'll withdraw awhile. Go to the King, and let there be impawn'd Some surety for a safe return again, And in the morning early shall my uncle Bring him our purposes. And so, farewell.
BOT: I would you would accept of grace and love.
USER: And maybe so we shall.
BOT: Pray God you do.

USER: Hie, good Sir Michael; bear this sealed brief With winged haste to the Lord Marshal, This to my cousin Scroop, and all the rest To whom they are directed. If you knew How much they do import, you would make haste.
BOT: My good lord, I guess their tenour.
USER: Why, my good lord, you need not fear, There is Douglas and Lord Mortimer.
BOT: No, Mortimer is not there.
USER: But there is Mordake, Vernon, Lord Harry Percy, And there is my Lord of Worcester, and a head Of gallant warriors, noble gentlemen.
BOT: Doubt not, my lord, they shall be well opposed.

USER: How bloodily the sun begins to peer Above yon bulky hill! The day looks pale At his distemp'rature.
BOT: The southern wind Doth play the trumpet to his purposes, And by his hollow whistling in the leaves Foretells a tempest and a blust'ring day.
USER: Hear me, my liege: For mine own part, I could be well content To entertain the lag end of my life With quiet hours. For I do protest I have not sought the day of this dislike.
BOT: You have not sought it? How comes it, then?
USER: Rebellion lay in his way, and he found it.
BOT: Peace, chewet, peace!
USER: It will not be accepted, on my life. The Douglas and the Hotspur both together Are confident against the world in arms.
BOT: Hence, therefore, every leader to his charge; For on their answer, will we set on them, And God befriend us as our cause is just!
USER: Hal, if thou see me down in the battle and bestride me, so; 'tis a point of friendship.
BOT: Nothing but a colossus can do thee that friendship. Say thy prayers, and farewell.
USER: I would 'twere bedtime, Hal, and all well.
BOT: Why, thou owest God a death.

USER: O no, my nephew must not know, Sir Richard, The liberal and kind offer of the King.
BOT: 'Twere best he did.
USER: Deliver what you will, I'll say 'tis so. Here comes your cousin.
BOT: My uncle is return'd. Deliver up my Lord of Westmoreland. Uncle, what news?
USER: The King will bid you battle presently.
BOT: Defy him by the Lord Of Westmoreland.
USER: Lord Douglas, go you and tell him so.
BOT: Marry, I shall, and very willingly.
USER: There is no seeming mercy in the King.
BOT: Did you beg any? God forbid!
USER: I told him gently of our grievances, Of his oath-breaking; which he mended thus, By now forswearing that he is forsworn. He calls us rebels, traitors, and will scourge With haughty arms this hateful name in us.
BOT: Arm, gentlemen; to arms! For I have thrown A brave defiance in King Henry's teeth, And Westmoreland, that was engaged, did bear it, Which cannot choose but bring him quickly on.
USER: The Prince of Wales stepp'd forth before the King, And, nephew, challenged you to single fight.
BOT: O, would the quarrel lay upon our heads, And that no man might draw short breath today But I and Harry Monmouth! Tell me, tell me, How show'd his tasking? Seem'd it in contempt?
USER: My lord, here are letters for you.
BOT: My lord, prepare. The King comes on apace.

USER: What is thy name that in the battle thus Thou crossest me? What honour dost thou seek Upon my head?
BOT: Know then my name is Douglas, And I do haunt thee in the battle thus Because some tell me that thou art a king.
USER: They tell thee true.
BOT: The Lord of Stafford dear today hath bought Thy likeness, for instead of thee, King Harry, This sword hath ended him. So shall it thee, Unless thou yield thee as my prisoner.
USER: I was not born a yielder, thou proud Scot, And thou shalt find a king that will revenge Lord Stafford's death.
BOT: O Douglas, hadst thou fought at Holmedon thus, I never had triumphed upon a Scot.
USER: All's done, all's won; here breathless lies the King.
BOT: Where?
USER: Here.
BOT: This, Douglas? No, I know this face full well. A gallant knight he was, his name was Blunt, Semblably furnish'd like the King himself.
USER: A fool go with thy soul, whither it goes! A borrow'd title hast thou bought too dear. Why didst thou tell me that thou wert a king?
BOT: The King hath many marching in his coats.
USER: Now, by my sword, I will kill all his coats; I'll murder all his wardrobe, piece by piece, Until I meet the King.
BOT: Up, and away! Our soldiers stand full fairly for the day. Alarums. Enter Falstaff solus.
USER: What, stand'st thou idle here? Lend me thy sword. Many a nobleman lies stark and stiff Under the hoofs of vaunting enemies, Whose deaths are yet unrevenged. I prithee Lend me thy sword.
BOT: O Hal, I prithee give me leave to breathe awhile. Turk Gregory never did such deeds in arms as I have done this day. I have paid Percy, I have made him sure.
USER: He is indeed, and living to kill thee. I prithee, lend me thy sword.
BOT: Nay, before God, Hal, if Percy be alive, thou gett'st not my sword; but take my pistol, if thou wilt.
USER: Give it me. What, is it in the case?
BOT: Ay, Hal, 'tis hot, 'tis hot. There's that will sack a city.
USER: What, is it a time to jest and dally now?

USER: I prithee, Harry, withdraw thyself, thou bleedest too much. Lord John of Lancaster, go you with him.
BOT: Not I, my lord, unless I did bleed too.
USER: I do beseech your Majesty, make up, Lest your retirement do amaze your friends.
BOT: I will do so. My Lord of Westmoreland, Lead him to his tent.
USER: Come, my lord, I'll lead you to your tent.
BOT: Lead me, my lord? I do not need your help, And God forbid a shallow scratch should drive The Prince of Wales from such a field as this, Where stain'd nobility lies trodden on, And rebels' arms triumph in massacres!
USER: We breathe too long. Come, cousin Westmoreland, Our duty this way lies. For God's sake, come.
BOT: By Heaven, thou hast deceived me, Lancaster, I did not think thee lord of such a spirit. Before, I loved thee as a brother, John, But now I do respect thee as my soul.
USER: I saw him hold Lord Percy at the point With lustier maintenance than I did look for Of such an ungrown warrior.
BOT: O, this boy Lends mettle to us all!
USER: Another king! They grow like Hydra's heads. I am the Douglas, fatal to all those That wear those colours on them. What art thou That counterfeit'st the person of a king?
BOT: I fear thou art another counterfeit, And yet, in faith, thou bearest thee like a king. But mine I am sure thou art, whoe'er thou be, And thus I win thee. They fight; the King being in danger, enter Prince Henry.
USER: Stay and breathe awhile. Thou hast redeem'd thy lost opinion, And show'd thou mak'st some tender of my life, In this fair rescue thou hast brought to me.
BOT: Make up to Clifton. I'll to Sir Nicholas Gawsey.
USER: If I mistake not, thou art Harry Monmouth.
BOT: Thou speak'st as if I would deny my name.
USER: My name is Harry Percy.
BOT: Nor shall it, Harry, for the hour is come To end the one of us, and would to God Thy name in arms were now as great as mine!
USER: I'll make it greater ere I part from thee, And all the budding honours on thy crest I'll crop to make a garland for my head.
BOT: I can no longer brook thy vanities.
USER: Well said, Hal! To it, Hal! Nay, you shall find no boy's play here, I can tell you. dead, and exit Douglas. The Prince kills Hotspur.
BOT: Come, brother John, full bravely hast thou flesh'd Thy maiden sword.
USER: But soft, whom have we here? Did you not tell me this fat man was dead?
BOT: Why, Percy I kill'd myself, and saw thee dead.
USER: This is the strangest tale that ever I heard.
BOT: I'll follow, as they say, for reward. He that rewards me, God reward him! If I do grow great, I'll grow less, for I'll purge, and leave sack, and live cleanly as a nobleman should do.

USER: What I have done my safety urged me to; And I embrace this fortune patiently, Since not to be avoided it falls on me.
BOT: Bear Worcester to the death, and Vernon too. Other offenders we will pause upon. How goes the field?
USER: With all my heart.
BOT: I thank your Grace for this high courtesy, Which I shall give away immediately.

USER: Lord CHIEF JUSTICE of the King's Bench. A SERVANT of the Chief Justice. Henry Percy, Earl of NORTHUMBERLAND. Scroop, ARCHBISHOP of York. Lord MOWBRAY. Lord HASTINGS.
BOT: TRAVERS and MORTON, retainers of Northumberland.
USER: His Page.
BOT: SHALLOW and SILENCE, country justices. DAVY, Servant to Shallow. MOULDY, SHADOW, WART, FEEBLE, and BULLCALF, recruits. FANG and SNARE, sheriff's officers.
USER: MISTRESS QUICKLY, hostess of a tavern in Eastcheap.
BOT: Lords and Attendants; Porter, Drawers, Musicians, Beadles, Grooms, etc. A Dancer, speaker of the epilogue.

USER: Who keeps the gate here, ho? The Porter opens the gate. Where is the Earl?
BOT: What shall I say you are?
USER: Tell thou the Earl That the Lord Bardolph doth attend him here.
BOT: His lordship is walk'd forth into the orchard. Please it your honour knock but at the gate, And he himself will answer.
USER: Here comes the Earl.
BOT: What news, Lord Bardolph? Every minute now Should be the father of some stratagem. The times are wild; contention, like a horse Full of high feeding, madly hath broke loose And bears down all before him.
USER: Noble earl, I bring you certain news from Shrewsbury.
BOT: Good, an God will!
USER: How is this derived? Saw you the field? Came you from Shrewsbury?
BOT: I spake with one, my lord, that came from thence, A gentleman well bred and of good name, That freely render'd me these news for true.
USER: Here comes my servant Travers, whom I sent On Tuesday last to listen after news.
BOT: My lord, I over-rode him on the way, And he is furnish'd with no certainties More than he haply may retail from me.
USER: Now, Travers, what good tidings comes with you?
BOT: Ha? Again: Said he young Harry Percy's spur was cold? Of Hotspur, Coldspur? That rebellion Had met ill luck?
USER: My lord, I'll tell you what: If my young lord your son have not the day, Upon mine honour, for a silken point I'll give my barony, never talk of it.
BOT: Why should that gentleman that rode by Travers Give then such instances of loss?
USER: Who, he? He was some hilding fellow that had stolen The horse he rode on, and, upon my life, Spoke at a venture. Look, here comes more news.
BOT: Yea, this man's brow, like to a title-leaf, Foretells the nature of a tragic volume. So looks the strand whereon the imperious flood Hath left a witness'd usurpation. Say, Morton, didst thou come from Shrewsbury?
USER: I ran from Shrewsbury, my noble lord, Where hateful death put on his ugliest mask To fright our party.
BOT: Douglas is living, and your brother, yet; But, for my lord your son -
USER: You are too great to be by me gainsaid, Your spirit is too true, your fears too certain.
BOT: I cannot think, my lord, your son is dead.
USER: This strained passion doth you wrong, my lord.

USER: Sirrah, you giant, what says the doctor to my water?
BOT: He said, sir, the water itself was a good healthy water; but, for the party that owed it, he might have moe diseases than he knew for.
USER: He said, sir, you should procure him better assurance than Bardolph. He would not take his band and yours, he liked not the security.
BOT: He's gone into Smithfield to buy your worship a horse.
USER: I bought him in Paul's, and he'll buy me a horse in Smithfield. An I could get me but a wife in the stews, I were manned, horsed, and wived.
BOT: Sir, here comes the nobleman that committed the Prince for striking him about Bardolph.
USER: Wait close, I will not see him.
BOT: What's he that goes there?
USER: Falstaff, an 't please your lordship.
BOT: He that was in question for the robbery?
USER: He, my lord; but he hath since done good service at Shrewsbury, and, as I hear, is now going with some charge to the Lord John of Lancaster.
BOT: What, to York? Call him back again.
USER: Sir John Falstaff!
BOT: Boy, tell him I am deaf.
USER: You must speak louder, my master is deaf.
BOT: I am sure he is, to the hearing of anything good. Go pluck him by the elbow, I must speak with him.
USER: Sir John!
BOT: You mistake me, sir.
USER: Why, sir, did I say you were an honest man? Setting my knighthood and my soldiership aside, I had lied in my throat if I had said so.
BOT: I pray you, sir, then set your knighthood and your soldiership aside, and give me leave to tell you, you lie in your throat, if you say I am any other than an honest man.
USER: I give thee leave to tell me so? I lay aside that which grows to me? If thou get'st any leave of me, hang me; if thou tak'st leave, thou wert better be hanged. You hunt counter. Hence! Avaunt!
BOT: Sir, my lord would speak with you.
USER: Sir John Falstaff, a word with you.
BOT: Sir John, I sent for you before your expedition to Shrewsbury.
USER: An 't please your lordship, I hear his Majesty is returned with some discomfort from Wales.
BOT: I talk not of his Majesty. You would not come when I sent for you.
USER: And I hear, moreover, his Highness is fallen into this same whoreson apoplexy.
BOT: Well, God mend him! I pray you let me speak with you.
USER: This apoplexy, as I take it, is a kind of lethargy, an 't please your lordship, a kind of sleeping in the blood, a whoreson tingling.
BOT: What tell you me of it? Be it as it is.
USER: It hath it original from much grief, from study and perturbation of the brain. I have read the cause of his effects in Galen. It is a kind of deafness.
BOT: I think you are fallen into the disease, for you hear not what I say to you.
USER: Very well, my lord, very well. Rather, an 't please you, it is the disease of not listening, the malady of not marking, that I am troubled withal.
BOT: To punish you by the heels would amend the attention of your ears, and I care not if I do become your physician.
USER: I sent for you, when there were matters against you for your life, to come speak with me.
BOT: As I was then advised by my learned counsel in the laws of this land-service, I did not come.
USER: Well, the truth is, Sir John, you live in great infamy.
BOT: He that buckles himself in my belt cannot live in less.
USER: Your means are very slender, and your waste is great.
BOT: I would it were otherwise, I would my means were greater and my waist slenderer.
USER: You have misled the youthful prince.
BOT: The young prince hath misled me. I am the fellow with the great belly, and he my dog.
USER: Well, I am loath to gall a new-healed wound. Your day's service at Shrewsbury hath a little gilded over your night's exploit on Gad's Hill. You may thank th' unquiet time for your quiet o'er-posting that action.
BOT: My lord!
USER: But since all is well, keep it so: wake not a sleeping wolf.
BOT: To wake a wolf is as bad as smell a fox.
USER: What! You are as a candle, the better part burnt out.
BOT: A wassail candle, my lord, all tallow. If I did say of wax, my growth would approve the truth.
USER: There is not a white hair in your face but should have his effect of gravity.
BOT: His effect of gravy, gravy, gravy.
USER: You follow the young prince up and down, like his ill angel.
BOT: Well, God send the Prince a better companion!
USER: God send the companion a better prince! I cannot rid my hands of him.
BOT: Well, the King hath severed you and Prince Harry. I hear you are going with Lord John of Lancaster against the Archbishop and the Earl of Northumberland.
USER: Well, be honest, be honest, and God bless your expedition!
BOT: Will your lordship lend me a thousand pound to furnish me forth?
USER: Not a penny, not a penny; you are too impatient to bear crosses. Fare you well: commend me to my cousin Westmoreland.
BOT: Sir?
USER: What money is in my purse?
BOT: Seven groats and two pence.

USER: Thus have you heard our cause and known our means, And, my most noble friends, I pray you all Speak plainly your opinions of our hopes. And first, Lord Marshal, what say you to it?
BOT: I well allow the occasion of our arms, But gladly would be better satisfied How in our means we should advance ourselves To look with forehead bold and big enough Upon the power and puissance of the King.
USER: Our present musters grow upon the file To five and twenty thousand men of choice; And our supplies live largely in the hope Of great Northumberland, whose bosom burns With an incensed fire of injuries.
BOT: The question then, Lord Hastings, standeth thus: Whether our present five and twenty thousand May hold up head without Northumberland.
USER: With him we may.
BOT: 'Tis very true, Lord Bardolph, for indeed It was young Hotspur's case at Shrewsbury.
USER: But, by your leave, it never yet did hurt To lay down likelihoods and forms of hope.
BOT: Grant that our hopes, yet likely of fair birth, Should be still-born, and that we now possess'd The utmost man of expectation, I think we are a body strong enough, Even as we are, to equal with the King.
USER: What, is the King but five and twenty thousand?
BOT: That he should draw his several strengths together And come against us in full puissance Need not be dreaded.
USER: If he should do so, He leaves his back unarm'd, the French and Welsh Baying him at the heels: never fear that.
BOT: Who is it like should lead his forces hither?
USER: The Duke of Lancaster and Westmoreland; Against the Welsh, himself and Harry Monmouth; But who is substituted 'gainst the French I have no certain notice.
BOT: Shall we go draw our numbers, and set on?
USER: We are time's subjects, and time bids be gone.

USER: Master Fang, have you entered the action?
BOT: It is entered.
USER: Where's your yeoman? Is 't a lusty yeoman? Will he stand to 't?
BOT: Sirrah, where's Snare?
USER: O Lord, ay! Good Master Snare.
BOT: Here, here.
USER: Snare, we must arrest Sir John Falstaff.
BOT: Yea, good Master Snare, I have entered him and all.
USER: It may chance cost some of our lives, for he will stab.
BOT: If I can close with him, I care not for his thrust.
USER: No, nor I neither. I'll be at your elbow.
BOT: An I but fist him once, an he come but within my vice, -
USER: How now, whose mare's dead? What's the matter?
BOT: Sir John, I arrest you at the suit of Mistress Quickly.
USER: Away, varlets! Draw, Bardolph! Cut me off the villain's head. Throw the quean in the channel.
BOT: Keep them off, Bardolph.
USER: A rescue! A rescue!
BOT: Good people, bring a rescue or two. Thou wo't, wo't thou? Thou wo't, wo't ta? Do, do, thou rogue! Do, thou hempseed!
USER: Away, you scullion! you rampallian! you fustilarian! I'll tickle your catastrophe.
BOT: What is the matter? Keep the peace here, ho!
USER: Good my lord, be good to me. I beseech you stand to me.
BOT: How now, Sir John? What are you brawling here? Doth this become your place, your time and business? You should have been well on your way to York. Stand from him, fellow. Wherefore hang'st thou upon him?
USER: O my most worshipful lord, an't please your Grace, I am a poor widow of Eastcheap, and he is arrested at my suit.
BOT: For what sum?
USER: I think I am as like to ride the mare if I have any vantage of ground to get up.
BOT: How comes this, Sir John? Fie! what man of good temper would endure this tempest of exclamation? Are you not ashamed to enforce a poor widow to so rough a course to come by her own?
USER: What is the gross sum that I owe thee?
BOT: Yea, in truth, my lord.
USER: Pray thee, peace. Pay her the debt you owe her, and unpay the villany you have done with her. The one you may do with sterling money, and the other with current repentance.
BOT: You speak as having power to do wrong; but answer in th' effect of your reputation, and satisfy the poor woman.
USER: Come hither, hostess.
BOT: Now, Master Gower, what news?
USER: The King, my lord, and Harry Prince of Wales Are near at hand: the rest the paper tells.
BOT: As I am a gentleman.
USER: Faith, you said so before.
BOT: As I am a gentleman. Come, no more words of it.
USER: By this heavenly ground I tread on, I must be fain to pawn both my plate and the tapestry of my dining-chambers.
BOT: Pray thee, Sir John, let it be but twenty nobles. I' faith, I am loath to pawn my plate, so God save me, la!
USER: Let it alone, I'll make other shift: you'll be a fool still.
BOT: Well, you shall have it, though I pawn my gown. I hope you'll come to supper. You'll pay me all together?
USER: Will I live? [_To Bardolph_.] Go, with her, with her. Hook on, hook on.
BOT: Will you have Doll Tearsheet meet you at supper?
USER: No more words, let's have her.
BOT: I have heard better news.
USER: What's the news, my lord?
BOT: Where lay the King tonight?
USER: At Basingstoke, my lord.
BOT: I hope, my lord, all's well. What is the news, my lord?
USER: Come all his forces back?
BOT: No, fifteen hundred foot, five hundred horse Are march'd up to my Lord of Lancaster, Against Northumberland and the Archbishop.
USER: Comes the King back from Wales, my noble lord?
BOT: You shall have letters of me presently. Come, go along with me, good Master Gower.
USER: My lord!
BOT: What's the matter?
USER: Master Gower, shall I entreat you with me to dinner?
BOT: I must wait upon my good lord here, I thank you, good Sir John.
USER: Sir John, you loiter here too long, being you are to take soldiers up in counties as you go.
BOT: Will you sup with me, Master Gower?
USER: What foolish master taught you these manners, Sir John?
BOT: Master Gower, if they become me not, he was a fool that taught them me. This is the right fencing grace, my lord; tap for tap, and so part fair.
USER: Now the Lord lighten thee, thou art a great fool.

USER: Before God, I am exceeding weary.
BOT: Is 't come to that? I had thought weariness durst not have attached one of so high blood.
USER: Faith, it does me, though it discolours the complexion of my greatness to acknowledge it. Doth it not show vilely in me to desire small beer?
BOT: Why, a prince should not be so loosely studied as to remember so weak a composition.
USER: How ill it follows, after you have laboured so hard, you should talk so idly! Tell me, how many good young princes would do so, their fathers being so sick as yours at this time is?
BOT: Shall I tell thee one thing, Poins?
USER: Yes, faith, and let it be an excellent good thing.
BOT: It shall serve among wits of no higher breeding than thine.
USER: Go to, I stand the push of your one thing that you will tell.
BOT: Marry, I tell thee it is not meet that I should be sad, now my father is sick; albeit I could tell to thee, as to one it pleases me, for fault of a better, to call my friend, I could be sad, and sad indeed too.
USER: Very hardly upon such a subject.
BOT: The reason?
USER: What wouldst thou think of me if I should weep?
BOT: I would think thee a most princely hypocrite.
USER: Why, because you have been so lewd and so much engraffed to Falstaff.
BOT: And to thee.
USER: And the boy that I gave Falstaff. He had him from me Christian, and look if the fat villain have not transformed him ape.
BOT: God save your Grace!
USER: And yours, most noble Bardolph!
BOT: Come, you virtuous ass, you bashful fool, must you be blushing? Wherefore blush you now? What a maidenly man-at-arms are you become! Is 't such a matter to get a pottle-pot's maidenhead?
USER: Has not the boy profited?
BOT: Away, you whoreson upright rabbit, away!
USER: Away, you rascally Althaea's dream, away!
BOT: Instruct us, boy; what dream, boy?
USER: Marry, my lord, Althaea dreamt she was delivered of a firebrand; and therefore I call him her dream.
BOT: A crown's worth of good interpretation. There 'tis, boy.
USER: O, that this blossom could be kept from cankers! Well, there is sixpence to preserve thee.
BOT: An you do not make him be hanged among you, the gallows shall have wrong.
USER: And how doth thy master, Bardolph?
BOT: Well, my lord. He heard of your Grace's coming to town. There's a letter for you.
USER: Delivered with good respect. And how doth the martlemas, your master?
BOT: In bodily health, sir.
USER: Marry, the immortal part needs a physician, but that moves not him. Though that be sick, it dies not.
BOT: I do allow this wen to be as familiar with me as my dog, and he holds his place, for look you how he writes.
USER: Nay, they will be kin to us, or they will fetch it from Japhet. But to the letter: "Sir John Falstaff, knight, to the son of the King, nearest his father, Harry Prince of Wales, greeting."
BOT: Why, this is a certificate.
USER: Peace! "I will imitate the honourable Romans in brevity."
BOT: He sure means brevity in breath, short-winded.
USER: My lord, I'll steep this letter in sack and make him eat it.
BOT: That's to make him eat twenty of his words. But do you use me thus, Ned? Must I marry your sister?
USER: God send the wench no worse fortune! But I never said so.
BOT: Well, thus we play the fools with the time, and the spirits of the wise sit in the clouds and mock us. Is your master here in London?
USER: Yea, my lord.
BOT: Where sups he? Doth the old boar feed in the old frank?
USER: At the old place, my lord, in Eastcheap.
BOT: What company?
USER: Ephesians, my lord, of the old church.
BOT: Sup any women with him?
USER: None, my lord, but old Mistress Quickly and Mistress Doll Tearsheet.
BOT: What pagan may that be?
USER: A proper gentlewoman, sir, and a kinswoman of my master's.
BOT: Even such kin as the parish heifers are to the town bull. Shall we steal upon them, Ned, at supper?
USER: I am your shadow, my lord, I'll follow you.
BOT: Sirrah, you boy, and Bardolph, no word to your master that I am yet come to town. There's for your silence.
USER: I have no tongue, sir.
BOT: And for mine, sir, I will govern it.
USER: Fare you well; go. This Doll Tearsheet should be some road.
BOT: I warrant you, as common as the way between Saint Albans and London.
USER: How might we see Falstaff bestow himself tonight in his true colours, and not ourselves be seen?
BOT: Put on two leathern jerkins and aprons, and wait upon him at his table as drawers.
USER: From a god to a bull? A heavy descension! It was Jove's case. From a prince to a 'prentice? A low transformation that shall be mine, for in everything the purpose must weigh with the folly. Follow me, Ned.

USER: I pray thee, loving wife, and gentle daughter, Give even way unto my rough affairs; Put not you on the visage of the times And be like them to Percy troublesome.
BOT: I have given over, I will speak no more. Do what you will; your wisdom be your guide.
USER: Alas, sweet wife, my honour is at pawn, And, but my going, nothing can redeem it.
BOT: Beshrew your heart, Fair daughter, you do draw my spirits from me With new lamenting ancient oversights. But I must go and meet with danger there, Or it will seek me in another place, And find me worse provided.
USER: O, fly to Scotland, Till that the nobles and the armed commons Have of their puissance made a little taste.

USER: What the devil hast thou brought there - applejohns? Thou knowest Sir John cannot endure an applejohn.
BOT: Why then, cover, and set them down, and see if thou canst find out Sneak's noise. Mistress Tearsheet would fain hear some music. Dispatch. The room where they supped is too hot, they'll come in straight.
USER: Sirrah, here will be the Prince and Master Poins anon, and they will put on two of our jerkins and aprons, and Sir John must not know of it. Bardolph hath brought word.
BOT: By the mass, here will be old utis. It will be an excellent stratagem.
USER: I'll see if I can find out Sneak.
BOT: Better than I was. Hem!
USER: Why, that's well said. A good heart's worth gold. Lo, here comes Sir John.
BOT: How now, Mistress Doll!
USER: Sick of a calm, yea, good faith.
BOT: So is all her sect; an they be once in a calm, they are sick.
USER: A pox damn you, you muddy rascal, is that all the comfort you give me?
BOT: You make fat rascals, Mistress Doll.
USER: I make them? Gluttony and diseases make them; I make them not.
BOT: If the cook help to make the gluttony, you help to make the diseases, Doll: we catch of you, Doll. We catch of you; grant that, my poor virtue, grant that.
USER: Yea, joy, our chains and our jewels.
BOT: "Your brooches, pearls, and ouches:" - for to serve bravely is to come halting off, you know; to come off the breach with his pike bent bravely, and to surgery bravely; to venture upon the charged chambers bravely -
USER: Hang yourself, you muddy conger, hang yourself!
BOT: Sir, Ancient Pistol's below, and would speak with you.
USER: Hang him, swaggering rascal! Let him not come hither: it is the foul-mouthed'st rogue in England.
BOT: Dost thou hear, hostess?
USER: Pray ye pacify yourself, Sir John. There comes no swaggerers here.
BOT: Dost thou hear? It is mine ancient.
USER: He's no swaggerer, hostess; a tame cheater, i' faith, you may stroke him as gently as a puppy greyhound. He'll not swagger with a Barbary hen, if her feathers turn back in any show of resistance. Call him up, drawer.
BOT: Cheater, call you him? I will bar no honest man my house, nor no cheater, but I do not love swaggering, by my troth, I am the worse when one says "swagger." Feel, masters, how I shake; look you, I warrant you.
USER: So you do, hostess.
BOT: Do I? Yea, in very truth, do I, an 'twere an aspen leaf. I cannot abide swaggerers.
USER: God save you, Sir John!
BOT: Welcome, Ancient Pistol. Here, Pistol, I charge you with a cup of sack. Do you discharge upon mine hostess.
USER: I will discharge upon her, Sir John, with two bullets.
BOT: She is pistol-proof, sir; you shall not hardly offend her.
USER: Come, I'll drink no proofs nor no bullets. I'll drink no more than will do me good, for no man's pleasure, I.
BOT: Then to you, Mistress Dorothy! I will charge you.
USER: Charge me! I scorn you, scurvy companion. What, you poor, base, rascally, cheating, lack-linen mate! Away, you mouldy rogue, away! I am meat for your master.
BOT: I know you, Mistress Dorothy.
USER: God let me not live, but I will murder your ruff for this.
BOT: No more, Pistol! I would not have you go off here. Discharge yourself of our company, Pistol.
USER: No, good Captain Pistol, not here, sweet captain.
BOT: Pray thee go down, good ancient.
USER: Hark thee hither, Mistress Doll.
BOT: Not I. I tell thee what, Corporal Bardolph, I could tear her. I'll be revenged of her.
USER: Pray thee go down.
BOT: I'll see her damned first to Pluto's damned lake, by this hand, to th' infernal deep, with Erebus and tortures vile also. Hold hook and line, say I. Down, down, dogs! Down, faitors! Have we not Hiren here?
USER: Good Captain Peesel, be quiet, 'tis very late, i' faith. I beseek you now, aggravate your choler.
BOT: By my troth, captain, these are very bitter words.
USER: Be gone, good ancient. This will grow to a brawl anon.
BOT: Die men like dogs! Give crowns like pins! Have we not Hiren here?
USER: O' my word, captain, there's none such here. What the good-year, do you think I would deny her? For God's sake, be quiet.
BOT: Pistol, I would be quiet.
USER: Sweet knight, I kiss thy neaf. What! we have seen the seven stars.
BOT: For God's sake, thrust him downstairs. I cannot endure such a fustian rascal.
USER: Thrust him downstairs? Know we not Galloway nags?
BOT: Quoit him down, Bardolph, like a shove-groat shilling. Nay, an he do nothing but speak nothing, he shall be nothing here.
USER: Come, get you downstairs.
BOT: What! shall we have incision? Shall we imbrue? Then death rock me asleep, abridge my doleful days! Why then, let grievous, ghastly, gaping wounds Untwind the Sisters Three! Come, Atropos, I say!
USER: Here's goodly stuff toward!
BOT: Give me my rapier, boy.
USER: I pray thee, Jack, I pray thee, do not draw.
BOT: Get you downstairs.
USER: Here's a goodly tumult! I'll forswear keeping house, afore I'll be in these tirrits and frights. So, murder, I warrant now. Alas, alas, put up your naked weapons, put up your naked weapons.
BOT: I pray thee, Jack, be quiet. The rascal's gone. Ah, you whoreson little valiant villain, you!
USER: Are you not hurt i' th' groin? Methought he made a shrewd thrust at your belly.
BOT: Have you turned him out o' doors?
USER: Yea, sir. The rascal's drunk. You have hurt him, sir, i' th' shoulder.
BOT: A rascal, to brave me!
USER: A rascally slave! I will toss the rogue in a blanket.
BOT: Do, an thou darest for thy heart. An thou dost, I'll canvass thee between a pair of sheets.
USER: The music is come, sir.
BOT: Let them play. Play, sirs. Sit on my knee, Doll. A rascal bragging slave! The rogue fled from me like quicksilver.
USER: Peace, good Doll, do not speak like a death's-head; do not bid me remember mine end.
BOT: Sirrah, what humour 's the Prince of?
USER: A good shallow young fellow; he would have made a good pantler; he would ha' chipped bread well.
BOT: They say Poins has a good wit.
USER: He a good wit? Hang him, baboon! His wit's as thick as Tewksbury mustard; there's no more conceit in him than is in a mallet.
BOT: Why does the Prince love him so, then?
USER: Would not this nave of a wheel have his ears cut off?
BOT: Let's beat him before his whore.
USER: Look whe'er the withered elder hath not his poll clawed like a parrot.
BOT: Is it not strange that desire should so many years outlive performance?
USER: Kiss me, Doll.
BOT: Saturn and Venus this year in conjunction! What says th' almanac to that?
USER: And look whether the fiery Trigon, his man, be not lisping to his master's old tables, his note-book, his counsel-keeper.
BOT: Thou dost give me flattering busses.
USER: By my troth, I kiss thee with a most constant heart.
BOT: I am old, I am old.
USER: I love thee better than I love e'er a scurvy young boy of them all.
BOT: What stuff wilt have a kirtle of? I shall receive money o' Thursday; shalt have a cap tomorrow. A merry song! Come, it grows late, we'll to bed. Thou'lt forget me when I am gone.
USER: By my troth, thou'lt set me a-weeping an thou sayest so. Prove that ever I dress myself handsome till thy return. Well, hearken a' th' end.
BOT: Some sack, Francis. PRINCE & POINS. Anon, anon, sir.
USER: Ha! A bastard son of the King's? And art thou not Poins his brother?
BOT: Why, thou globe of sinful continents, what a life dost thou lead!
USER: A better than thou. I am a gentleman, thou art a drawer.
BOT: Very true, sir, and I come to draw you out by the ears.
USER: O, the Lord preserve thy Grace! By my troth, welcome to London. Now, the Lord bless that sweet face of thine! O Jesu, are you come from Wales?
BOT: Thou whoreson mad compound of majesty, by this light flesh and corrupt blood, thou art welcome.
USER: How? You fat fool, I scorn you.
BOT: My lord, he will drive you out of your revenge and turn all to a merriment, if you take not the heat.
USER: You whoreson candle-mine, you, how vilely did you speak of me even now before this honest, virtuous, civil gentlewoman!
BOT: God's blessing of your good heart! and so she is, by my troth.
USER: Didst thou hear me?
BOT: Yea, and you knew me, as you did when you ran away by Gad's Hill. You knew I was at your back, and spoke it on purpose to try my patience.
USER: No, no, no, not so; I did not think thou wast within hearing.
BOT: I shall drive you then to confess the wilful abuse, and then I know how to handle you.
USER: No abuse, Hal, o' mine honour, no abuse.
BOT: Not to dispraise me, and call me pantler and bread-chipper and I know not what?
USER: No abuse, Hal.
BOT: No abuse?
USER: Answer, thou dead elm, answer.
BOT: The fiend hath pricked down Bardolph irrecoverable, and his face is Lucifer's privy-kitchen, where he doth nothing but roast malt-worms. For the boy, there is a good angel about him, but the devil outbids him too.
USER: For the women?
BOT: For one of them, she's in hell already, and burns poor souls. For th' other, I owe her money, and whether she be damned for that I know not.
USER: No, I warrant you.
BOT: No, I think thou art not, I think thou art quit for that. Marry, there is another indictment upon thee, for suffering flesh to be eaten in thy house, contrary to the law, for the which I think thou wilt howl.
USER: All victuallers do so. What's a joint of mutton or two in a whole Lent?
BOT: You, gentlewoman.
USER: What says your Grace?
BOT: His grace says that which his flesh rebels against.
USER: Who knocks so loud at door? Look to th' door there, Francis.
BOT: Peto, how now, what news?
USER: Now comes in the sweetest morsel of the night, and we must hence and leave it unpicked. How now, what's the matter?
BOT: You must away to court, sir, presently. A dozen captains stay at door for you.
USER: I cannot speak; if my heart be not ready to burst - well, sweet Jack, have a care of thyself.
BOT: Farewell, farewell.
USER: Well, fare thee well. I have known thee these twenty-nine years, come peascod-time; but an honester and truer-hearted man - well, fare thee well.
BOT: What's the matter?
USER: O, run, Doll, run; run, good Doll; come. She comes blubbered. Yea, will you come, Doll?

USER: Many good morrows to your Majesty!
BOT: Is it good morrow, lords?
USER: 'Tis one o'clock, and past.
BOT: Why then, good morrow to you all, my lords. Have you read o'er the letters that I sent you?
USER: We have, my liege.
BOT: Then you perceive the body of our kingdom How foul it is, what rank diseases grow, And with what danger, near the heart of it.
USER: It is but as a body yet distemper'd, Which to his former strength may be restored With good advice and little medicine. My Lord Northumberland will soon be cool'd.
BOT: Are these things then necessities? Then let us meet them like necessities; And that same word even now cries out on us. They say the bishop and Northumberland Are fifty thousand strong.
USER: I will take your counsel. And were these inward wars once out of hand, We would, dear lords, unto the Holy Land.

USER: Come on, come on, come on. Give me your hand, sir, give me your hand, sir. An early stirrer, by the rood! And how doth my good cousin Silence?
BOT: Good morrow, good cousin Shallow.
USER: And how doth my cousin, your bedfellow? And your fairest daughter and mine, my god-daughter Ellen?
BOT: Alas, a black ousel, cousin Shallow!
USER: By yea and no, sir, I dare say my cousin William is become a good scholar. He is at Oxford still, is he not?
BOT: Indeed, sir, to my cost.
USER: He must, then, to the Inns o' Court shortly. I was once of Clement's Inn, where I think they will talk of mad Shallow yet.
BOT: You were called "lusty Shallow" then, cousin.
USER: This Sir John, cousin, that comes hither anon about soldiers?
BOT: We shall all follow, cousin.
USER: Certain, 'tis certain, very sure, very sure. Death, as the Psalmist saith, is certain to all, all shall die. How a good yoke of bullocks at Stamford fair?
BOT: By my troth, I was not there.
USER: Death is certain. Is old Double of your town living yet?
BOT: Dead, sir.
USER: Thereafter as they be; a score of good ewes may be worth ten pounds.
BOT: And is old Double dead?
USER: Here come two of Sir John Falstaff's men, as I think.
BOT: Good morrow, honest gentlemen.
USER: I beseech you, which is Justice Shallow?
BOT: I am Robert Shallow, sir, a poor esquire of this county, and one of the King's justices of the peace. What is your good pleasure with me?
USER: My captain, sir, commends him to you, my captain, Sir John Falstaff, a tall gentleman, by heaven, and a most gallant leader.
BOT: He greets me well, sir. I knew him a good backsword man. How doth the good knight? May I ask how my lady his wife doth?
USER: Sir, pardon. A soldier is better accommodated than with a wife.
BOT: It is very just. Look, here comes good Sir John. Give me your good hand, give me your worship's good hand. By my troth, you like well and bear your years very well. Welcome, good Sir John.
USER: I am glad to see you well, good Master Robert Shallow. Master Surecard, as I think?
BOT: No, Sir John, it is my cousin Silence, in commission with me.
USER: Good Master Silence, it well befits you should be of the peace.
BOT: Your good worship is welcome.
USER: Fie, this is hot weather, gentlemen. Have you provided me here half a dozen sufficient men?
BOT: Marry, have we, sir. Will you sit?
USER: Let me see them, I beseech you.
BOT: Here, an it please you.
USER: What think you, Sir John? A good-limbed fellow, young, strong, and of good friends.
BOT: Is thy name Mouldy?
USER: Yea, an't please you.
BOT: 'Tis the more time thou wert used.
USER: Ha, ha, ha! most excellent, i' faith! Things that are mouldy lack use. Very singular good, in faith, well said, Sir John, very well said.
BOT: Prick him.
USER: I was pricked well enough before, an you could have let me alone. My old dame will be undone now for one to do her husbandry and her drudgery. You need not to have pricked me, there are other men fitter to go out than I.
BOT: Go to. Peace, Mouldy; you shall go. Mouldy, it is time you were spent.
USER: Spent?
BOT: Peace, fellow, peace. Stand aside. Know you where you are? For th'other, Sir John. Let me see: Simon Shadow!
USER: Yea, marry, let me have him to sit under. He's like to be a cold soldier.
BOT: Where's Shadow?
USER: Here, sir.
BOT: Shadow, whose son art thou?
USER: My mother's son, sir.
BOT: Thy mother's son! Like enough, and thy father's shadow. So the son of the female is the shadow of the male. It is often so indeed, but much of the father's substance!
USER: Do you like him, Sir John?
BOT: Shadow will serve for summer. Prick him, for we have a number of shadows to fill up the muster-book.
USER: Thomas Wart!
BOT: Where's he?
USER: Here, sir.
BOT: Is thy name Wart?
USER: Yea, sir.
BOT: Thou art a very ragged wart.
USER: Shall I prick him, Sir John?
BOT: It were superfluous, for his apparel is built upon his back, and the whole frame stands upon pins. Prick him no more.
USER: Ha, ha, ha! You can do it, sir, you can do it. I commend you well. Francis Feeble!
BOT: Here, sir.
USER: What trade art thou, Feeble?
BOT: A woman's tailor, sir.
USER: Shall I prick him, sir?
BOT: You may; but if he had been a man's tailor, he'd ha' pricked you. Wilt thou make as many holes in an enemy's battle as thou hast done in a woman's petticoat?
USER: I will do my good will, sir, you can have no more.
BOT: Well said, good woman's tailor! Well said, courageous Feeble! Thou wilt be as valiant as the wrathful dove or most magnanimous mouse. Prick the woman's tailor: well, Master Shallow, deep, Master Shallow.
USER: I would Wart might have gone, sir.
BOT: I would thou wert a man's tailor, that thou mightst mend him and make him fit to go. I cannot put him to a private soldier that is the leader of so many thousands. Let that suffice, most forcible Feeble.
USER: It shall suffice, sir.
BOT: I am bound to thee, reverend Feeble. Who is next?
USER: Peter Bullcalf o' th' green!
BOT: Yea, marry, let's see Bullcalf.
USER: Here, sir.
BOT: Fore God, a likely fellow! Come, prick me Bullcalf till he roar again.
USER: O Lord! good my lord captain -
BOT: What, dost thou roar before thou art pricked?
USER: O Lord, sir, I am a diseased man.
BOT: What disease hast thou?
USER: A whoreson cold, sir, a cough, sir, which I caught with ringing in the King's affairs upon his coronation day, sir.
BOT: Come, thou shalt go to the wars in a gown; we will have away thy cold, and I will take such order that thy friends shall ring for thee. Is here all?
USER: Here is two more called than your number; you must have but four here, sir; and so, I pray you, go in with me to dinner.
BOT: Come, I will go drink with you, but I cannot tarry dinner. I am glad to see you, by my troth, Master Shallow.
USER: O, Sir John, do you remember since we lay all night in the windmill in Saint George's Field?
BOT: No more of that, good Master Shallow, no more of that.
USER: Ha, 'twas a merry night. And is Jane Nightwork alive?
BOT: She lives, Master Shallow.
USER: She never could away with me.
BOT: Never, never; she would always say she could not abide Master Shallow.
USER: By the mass, I could anger her to th' heart. She was then a bona-roba. Doth she hold her own well?
BOT: Old, old, Master Shallow.
USER: Nay, she must be old, she cannot choose but be old, certain she's old, and had Robin Nightwork by old Nightwork before I came to Clement's Inn.
BOT: That's fifty-five year ago.
USER: Ha, cousin Silence, that thou hadst seen that that this knight and I have seen! Ha, Sir John, said I well?
BOT: We have heard the chimes at midnight, Master Shallow.
USER: That we have, that we have, that we have; in faith, Sir John, we have. Our watchword was "Hem boys!" Come, let's to dinner; come, let's to dinner. Jesus, the days that we have seen! Come, come.
BOT: Go to, stand aside.
USER: And, good Master Corporal Captain, for my old dame's sake, stand my friend. She has nobody to do anything about her when I am gone, and she is old, and cannot help herself. You shall have forty, sir.
BOT: Go to, stand aside.
USER: Well said, th'art a good fellow.
BOT: Faith, I'll bear no base mind.
USER: Come, sir, which men shall I have?
BOT: Four of which you please.
USER: Sir, a word with you. I have three pound to free Mouldy and Bullcalf.
BOT: Go to, well.
USER: Come, Sir John, which four will you have?
BOT: Do you choose for me.
USER: Marry, then, Mouldy, Bullcalf, Feeble, and Shadow.
BOT: Mouldy and Bullcalf: for you, Mouldy, stay at home till you are past service; and for your part, Bullcalf, grow till you come unto it. I will none of you.
USER: Sir John, Sir John, do not yourself wrong. They are your likeliest men, and I would have you served with the best.
BOT: Hold, Wart. Traverse. Thas, thas, thas.
USER: Come, manage me your caliver. So, very well, go to, very good, exceeding good. O, give me always a little, lean, old, chopt, bald shot. Well said, i' faith, Wart. Th'art a good scab. Hold, there's a tester for thee.
BOT: These fellows will do well. Master Shallow. God keep you, Master Silence: I will not use many words with you. Fare you well, gentlemen both. I thank you. I must a dozen mile tonight. Bardolph, give the soldiers coats.
USER: Sir John, the Lord bless you! God prosper your affairs! God send us peace! At your return, visit our house, let our old acquaintance be renewed. Peradventure I will with ye to the court.
BOT: Fore God, I would you would, Master Shallow.
USER: Go to, I have spoke at a word. God keep you.

USER: What is this forest call'd?
BOT: 'Tis Gaultree Forest, an 't shall please your Grace.
