USER: Here stand, my lords, and send discoverers forth To know the numbers of our enemies.
BOT: We have sent forth already.
USER: Thus do the hopes we have in him touch ground And dash themselves to pieces.
BOT: Now, what news?
USER: West of this forest, scarcely off a mile, In goodly form comes on the enemy, And, by the ground they hide, I judge their number Upon or near the rate of thirty thousand.
BOT: The just proportion that we gave them out. Let us sway on and face them in the field.
USER: What well-appointed leader fronts us here?
BOT: I think it is my Lord of Westmoreland.
USER: Health and fair greeting from our general, The prince, Lord John and Duke of Lancaster.
BOT: Say on, my Lord of Westmoreland, in peace, What doth concern your coming.
USER: My brother general, the commonwealth, To brother born an household cruelty, I make my quarrel in particular.
BOT: There is no need of any such redress, Or if there were, it not belongs to you.
USER: Why not to him in part, and to us all That feel the bruises of the days before, And suffer the condition of these times To lay a heavy and unequal hand Upon our honours?
BOT: But he hath forc'd us to compel this offer, And it proceeds from policy, not love.
USER: Well, by my will we shall admit no parley.
BOT: That argues but the shame of your offence: A rotten case abides no handling.
USER: Hath the Prince John a full commission, In very ample virtue of his father, To hear and absolutely to determine Of what conditions we shall stand upon?
BOT: That is intended in the general's name: I muse you make so slight a question.
USER: This will I show the general. Please you, lords, In sight of both our battles we may meet, And either end in peace, which God so frame! Or to the place of difference call the swords Which must decide it.
BOT: My lord, we will do so.
USER: There is a thing within my bosom tells me That no conditions of our peace can stand.
BOT: Fear you not that: if we can make our peace Upon such large terms and so absolute As our conditions shall consist upon, Our peace shall stand as firm as rocky mountains.
USER: Besides, the King hath wasted all his rods On late offenders, that he now doth lack The very instruments of chastisement; So that his power, like to a fangless lion, May offer, but not hold.
BOT: 'Tis very true, And therefore be assured, my good Lord Marshal, If we do now make our atonement well, Our peace will, like a broken limb united, Grow stronger for the breaking.
USER: Be it so. Here is return'd my Lord of Westmoreland.
BOT: The prince is here at hand. Pleaseth your lordship To meet his Grace just distance 'tween our armies.
USER: Your Grace of York, in God's name then set forward.
BOT: Before, and greet his Grace. My lord, we come.

USER: If not, we ready are to try our fortunes To the last man.
BOT: You are too shallow, Hastings, much too shallow, To sound the bottom of the after-times.
USER: Pleaseth your Grace to answer them directly How far forth you do like their articles.
BOT: I take your princely word for these redresses.
USER: I give it you, and will maintain my word; And thereupon I drink unto your Grace.
BOT: Go, captain, and deliver to the army This news of peace. Let them have pay, and part. I know it will please them. Hie thee, captain.
USER: To you, my noble Lord of Westmoreland.
BOT: I pledge your Grace; and if you knew what pains I have bestow'd to breed this present peace, You would drink freely; but my love to ye Shall show itself more openly hereafter.
USER: I do not doubt you.
BOT: I am glad of it. Health to my lord and gentle cousin, Mowbray.
USER: You wish me health in very happy season, For I am on the sudden something ill.
BOT: Against ill chances men are ever merry, But heaviness foreruns the good event.
USER: Therefore be merry, coz, since sudden sorrow Serves to say thus, "Some good thing comes tomorrow."
BOT: Believe me, I am passing light in spirit.
USER: So much the worse, if your own rule be true.
BOT: The word of peace is render'd. Hark how they shout!
USER: This had been cheerful after victory.
BOT: A peace is of the nature of a conquest; For then both parties nobly are subdued, And neither party loser.
USER: Go, my lord. And let our army be discharged too. And, good my lord, so please you, let our trains March by us, that we may peruse the men We should have coped withal.
BOT: Go, good Lord Hastings, And, ere they be dismiss'd, let them march by.
USER: I trust, lords, we shall lie tonight together. Now, cousin, wherefore stands our army still?
BOT: The leaders, having charge from you to stand, Will not go off until they hear you speak.
USER: They know their duties.
BOT: My lord, our army is dispersed already. Like youthful steers unyoked, they take their courses East, west, north, south; or, like a school broke up, Each hurries toward his home and sporting-place.
USER: Good tidings, my Lord Hastings; for the which I do arrest thee, traitor, of high treason; And you, Lord Archbishop, and you, Lord Mowbray, Of capital treason I attach you both.
BOT: Is this proceeding just and honourable?
USER: Is your assembly so?
BOT: Will you thus break your faith?

USER: What's your name, sir? Of what condition are you, and of what place, I pray?
BOT: I am a knight, sir, and my name is Colevile of the Dale.
USER: Are not you Sir John Falstaff?
BOT: I think you are Sir John Falstaff, and in that thought yield me.
USER: It was more of his courtesy than your deserving.
BOT: Thine's too heavy to mount.
USER: Let it shine, then.
BOT: Thine's too thick to shine.
USER: Let it do something, my good lord, that may do me good, and call it what you will.
BOT: Is thy name Colevile?
USER: It is, my lord.
BOT: A famous rebel art thou, Colevile.
USER: And a famous true subject took him.
BOT: I am, my lord, but as my betters are That led me hither. Had they been ruled by me, You should have won them dearer than you have.
USER: I know not how they sold themselves, but thou, like a kind fellow, gavest thyself away gratis, and I thank thee for thee.
BOT: Now, have you left pursuit?
USER: Retreat is made and execution stay'd.
BOT: My lord, I beseech you give me leave to go through Gloucestershire, and, when you come to court, stand my good lord, pray, in your good report.
USER: Fare you well, Falstaff. I, in my condition, Shall better speak of you than you deserve.
BOT: The army is discharged all and gone.
USER: Let them go. I'll through Gloucestershire, and there will I visit Master Robert Shallow, Esquire. I have him already tempering between my finger and my thumb, and shortly will I seal with him. Come away.

USER: Both which we doubt not but your Majesty Shall soon enjoy.
BOT: Humphrey, my son of Gloucester, Where is the Prince your brother?
USER: I think he's gone to hunt, my lord, at Windsor.
BOT: And how accompanied?
USER: I do not know, my lord.
BOT: Is not his brother Thomas of Clarence with him?
USER: No, my good lord, he is in presence here.
BOT: What would my lord and father?
USER: I shall observe him with all care and love.
BOT: Why art thou not at Windsor with him, Thomas?
USER: He is not there today; he dines in London.
BOT: And how accompanied? Canst thou tell that?
USER: With Poins, and other his continual followers.
BOT: 'Tis seldom when the bee doth leave her comb In the dead carrion. Who's here? Westmoreland?
USER: O Westmoreland, thou art a summer bird, Which ever in the haunch of winter sings The lifting up of day. Look, here's more news.
BOT: Comfort, your Majesty!
USER: O my royal father!
BOT: My sovereign lord, cheer up yourself, look up.
USER: Be patient, princes; you do know these fits Are with his Highness very ordinary. Stand from him, give him air; he'll straight be well.
BOT: No, no, he cannot long hold out these pangs. Th' incessant care and labour of his mind Hath wrought the mure that should confine it in So thin that life looks through and will break out.
USER: The people fear me, for they do observe Unfather'd heirs and loathly births of nature. The seasons change their manners, as the year Had found some months asleep and leap'd them over.
BOT: The river hath thrice flow'd, no ebb between, And the old folk, time's doting chronicles, Say it did so a little time before That our great-grandsire, Edward, sick'd and died.
USER: Speak lower, princes, for the King recovers.
BOT: This apoplexy will certain be his end.
USER: I pray you take me up, and bear me hence Into some other chamber: softly, pray.

USER: Let there be no noise made, my gentle friends, Unless some dull and favourable hand Will whisper music to my weary spirit.
BOT: Call for the music in the other room.
USER: Set me the crown upon my pillow here.
BOT: His eye is hollow, and he changes much.
USER: Less noise, less noise!
BOT: Who saw the Duke of Clarence?
USER: I am here, brother, full of heaviness.
BOT: How now, rain within doors, and none abroad? How doth the King?
USER: Exceeding ill.
BOT: Heard he the good news yet? Tell it him.
USER: He alt'red much upon the hearing it.
BOT: If he be sick with joy, he'll recover without physic.
USER: Not so much noise, my lords. Sweet prince, speak low; The King your father is disposed to sleep.
BOT: Let us withdraw into the other room.
USER: Will't please your Grace to go along with us?
BOT: Warwick! Gloucester! Clarence!
USER: Doth the King call?
BOT: What would your Majesty? How fares your Grace?
USER: Why did you leave me here alone, my lords?
BOT: We left the Prince my brother here, my liege, Who undertook to sit and watch by you.
USER: The Prince of Wales! Where is he? Let me see him. He is not here.
BOT: This door is open, he is gone this way.
USER: He came not through the chamber where we stay'd.
BOT: Where is the crown? Who took it from my pillow?
USER: When we withdrew, my liege, we left it here.
BOT: But wherefore did he take away the crown? Lo where he comes. Come hither to me, Harry. Depart the chamber, leave us here alone.
USER: I never thought to hear you speak again.
BOT: My gracious liege, You won it, wore it, kept it, gave it me; Then plain and right must my possession be, Which I with more than with a common pain 'Gainst all the world will rightfully maintain.
USER: Look, look, here comes my John of Lancaster.
BOT: Health, peace, and happiness to my royal father!
USER: Thou bring'st me happiness and peace, son John, But health, alack, with youthful wings is flown From this bare wither'd trunk. Upon thy sight My worldly business makes a period. Where is my Lord of Warwick?
BOT: My Lord of Warwick!
USER: Doth any name particular belong Unto the lodging where I first did swoon?
BOT: 'Tis call'd Jerusalem, my noble lord.

USER: By cock and pie, sir, you shall not away tonight. What, Davy, I say!
BOT: You must excuse me, Master Robert Shallow.
USER: I will not excuse you, you shall not be excused. Excuses shall not be admitted, there is no excuse shall serve, you shall not be excused. Why, Davy!
BOT: Here, sir.
USER: Davy, Davy, Davy, Davy, let me see, Davy, let me see, Davy, let me see. Yea, marry, William cook, bid him come hither. Sir John, you shall not be excused.
BOT: Marry, sir, thus: those precepts cannot be served; and again, sir - shall we sow the hade land with wheat?
USER: With red wheat, Davy. But for William cook, are there no young pigeons?
BOT: Yes, sir. Here is now the smith's note for shoeing and plough-irons.
USER: Let it be cast and paid. Sir John, you shall not be excused.
BOT: Now, sir, a new link to the bucket must needs be had. And, sir, do you mean to stop any of William's wages, about the sack he lost the other day at Hinckley fair?
USER: He shall answer it. Some pigeons, Davy, a couple of short-legged hens, a joint of mutton, and any pretty little tiny kickshaws, tell William cook.
BOT: Doth the man of war stay all night, sir?
USER: Yea, Davy, I will use him well: a friend i' th' court is better than a penny in purse. Use his men well, Davy, for they are arrant knaves, and will backbite.
BOT: No worse than they are backbitten, sir, for they have marvellous foul linen.
USER: Well conceited, Davy. About thy business, Davy.
BOT: I beseech you, sir, to countenance William Visor of Woncot against Clement Perkes o' th' hill.
USER: There is many complaints, Davy, against that Visor. That Visor is an arrant knave, on my knowledge.
BOT: Go to; I say he shall have no wrong. Look about, Davy. Where are you, Sir John? Come, come, come, off with your boots. Give me your hand, Master Bardolph.
USER: I am glad to see your worship.
BOT: I thank thee with all my heart, kind Master Bardolph; and welcome, my tall fellow [_to the Page_]. Come, Sir John.
USER: I come, Master Shallow, I come, Master Shallow.

USER: How now, my Lord Chief Justice, whither away?
BOT: How doth the King?
USER: Exceeding well. His cares are now all ended.
BOT: I hope, not dead.
USER: He's walk'd the way of nature, And to our purposes he lives no more.
BOT: I would his Majesty had call'd me with him. The service that I truly did his life Hath left me open to all injuries.
USER: Indeed I think the young King loves you not.
BOT: I know he doth not, and do arm myself To welcome the condition of the time, Which cannot look more hideously upon me Than I have drawn it in my fantasy.
USER: Here comes the heavy issue of dead Harry. O that the living Harry had the temper Of he the worst of these three gentlemen! How many nobles then should hold their places, That must strike sail to spirits of vile sort!
BOT: O God, I fear all will be overturn'd.
USER: Good morrow, cousin Warwick, good morrow. GLOUCESTER & CLARENCE. Good morrow, cousin.
BOT: We meet like men that had forgot to speak.
USER: We do remember, but our argument Is all too heavy to admit much talk.
BOT: Well, peace be with him that hath made us heavy!
USER: Peace be with us, lest we be heavier!
BOT: O, good my lord, you have lost a friend indeed; And I dare swear you borrow not that face Of seeming sorrow; it is sure your own.
USER: Though no man be assured what grace to find, You stand in coldest expectation. I am the sorrier; would 'twere otherwise.
BOT: Well, you must now speak Sir John Falstaff fair, Which swims against your stream of quality.
USER: Here comes the Prince.
BOT: Good morrow, and God save your Majesty!
USER: We hope no otherwise from your Majesty.
BOT: You all look strangely on me. And you most; You are, I think, assured I love you not.
USER: I am assured, if I be measured rightly, Your Majesty hath no just cause to hate me.

USER: Nay, you shall see my orchard, where, in an arbour, we will eat a last year's pippin of mine own graffing, with a dish of caraways, and so forth. Come, cousin Silence. And then to bed.
BOT: Fore God, you have here a goodly dwelling, and a rich.
USER: Barren, barren, barren; beggars all, beggars all, Sir John. Marry, good air. Spread, Davy, spread, Davy. Well said, Davy.
BOT: This Davy serves you for good uses; he is your serving-man and your husband.
USER: A good varlet, a good varlet, a very good varlet, Sir John. By the mass, I have drunk too much sack at supper. A good varlet. Now sit down, now sit down. Come, cousin.
BOT: There's a merry heart! Good Master Silence, I'll give you a health for that anon.
USER: Give Master Bardolph some wine, Davy.
BOT: Sweet sir, sit. I'll be with you anon. Most sweet sir, sit. Master page, good master page, sit. Proface! What you want in meat, we'll have in drink, but you must bear; the heart's all.
USER: Be merry, Master Bardolph, and, my little soldier there, be merry.
BOT: _Be merry, be merry, my wife has all, For women are shrews, both short and tall. 'Tis merry in hall when beards wag all, And welcome merry Shrove-tide. Be merry, be merry._
USER: I did not think Master Silence had been a man of this mettle.
BOT: Who, I? I have been merry twice and once ere now.
USER: Davy!
BOT: Your worship? I'll be with you straight.
USER: _A cup of wine that's brisk and fine, And drink unto thee, leman mine, And a merry heart lives long-a._
BOT: Well said, Master Silence.
USER: An we shall be merry, now comes in the sweet o' th' night.
BOT: Health and long life to you, Master Silence.
USER: _Fill the cup, and let it come, I'll pledge you a mile to th' bottom._
BOT: I hope to see London once ere I die.
USER: An I might see you there, Davy, -
BOT: By the mass, you'll crack a quart together, ha! will you not, Master Bardolph?
USER: Yea, sir, in a pottle-pot.
BOT: By God's liggens, I thank thee. The knave will stick by thee, I can assure thee that. He will not out, he. 'Tis true bred.
USER: And I'll stick by him, sir.
BOT: Why, there spoke a king. Lack nothing! Be merry. Look who's at door there, ho! Who knocks?
USER: right.
BOT: _Do me right, And dub me knight: Samingo._ Is't not so?
USER: 'Tis so.
BOT: Is't so? Why then, say an old man can do somewhat.
USER: An't please your worship, there's one Pistol come from the court with news.
BOT: From the court? Let him come in. How now, Pistol?
USER: Sir John, God save you!
BOT: What wind blew you hither, Pistol?
USER: Not the ill wind which blows no man to good. Sweet knight, thou art now one of the greatest men in this realm.
BOT: By'r lady, I think he be, but goodman Puff of Barson.
USER: Puff! Puff in thy teeth, most recreant coward base! Sir John, I am thy Pistol and thy friend, And helter-skelter have I rode to thee, And tidings do I bring and lucky joys, And golden times, and happy news of price.
BOT: I pray thee now, deliver them like a man of this world.
USER: A foutre for the world and worldlings base! I speak of Africa and golden joys.
BOT: O base Assyrian knight, what is thy news? Let King Cophetua know the truth thereof.
USER: Shall dunghill curs confront the Helicons? And shall good news be baffled? Then, Pistol, lay thy head in Furies' lap.
BOT: Honest gentleman, I know not your breeding.
USER: Why then, lament therefor.
BOT: Give me pardon, sir. If, sir, you come with news from the court, I take it there's but two ways, either to utter them, or conceal them. I am, sir, under the King, in some authority.
USER: Under which king, Besonian? Speak, or die.
BOT: Under King Harry.
USER: Harry the Fourth, or Fifth?
BOT: Harry the Fourth.
USER: A foutre for thine office! Sir John, thy tender lambkin now is King; Harry the Fifth's the man. I speak the truth. When Pistol lies, do this, and fig me, like The bragging Spaniard.
BOT: What, is the old King dead?
USER: As nail in door. The things I speak are just.
BOT: Away, Bardolph, saddle my horse. Master Robert Shallow, choose what office thou wilt in the land, 'tis thine. Pistol, I will double-charge thee with dignities.
USER: O joyful day! I would not take a knighthood for my fortune.
BOT: What! I do bring good news.
USER: Let vultures vile seize on his lungs also! "Where is the life that late I led?" say they: Why, here it is; welcome these pleasant days!

USER: No, thou arrant knave. I would to God that I might die, that I might have thee hanged. Thou hast drawn my shoulder out of joint.
BOT: The constables have delivered her over to me, and she shall have whipping-cheer enough, I warrant her. There hath been a man or two lately killed about her.
USER: Nut-hook, nut-hook, you lie! Come on, I'll tell thee what, thou damned tripe-visaged rascal, an the child I now go with do miscarry, thou wert better thou hadst struck thy mother, thou paper-faced villain.
BOT: O the Lord, that Sir John were come! He would make this a bloody day to somebody. But I pray God the fruit of her womb miscarry!
USER: If it do, you shall have a dozen of cushions again; you have but eleven now. Come, I charge you both go with me, for the man is dead that you and Pistol beat amongst you.
BOT: I'll tell you what, you thin man in a censer, I will have you as soundly swinged for this, you bluebottle rogue, you filthy famished correctioner, if you be not swinged, I'll forswear half-kirtles.
USER: Come, come, you she knight-errant, come.
BOT: O God, that right should thus overcome might! Well, of sufferance comes ease.
USER: Come, you rogue, come, bring me to a justice.
BOT: Ay, come, you starved bloodhound.
USER: Goodman death, goodman bones!
BOT: Thou atomy, thou!
USER: Come, you thin thing, come, you rascal!
BOT: Very well.

USER: More rushes, more rushes.
BOT: The trumpets have sounded twice.
USER: 'Twill be two o'clock ere they come from the coronation. Dispatch, dispatch. Trumpets sound, and the King and his train pass over the stage. Enter Falstaff, Shallow, Pistol, Bardolph and Page.
BOT: Stand here by me, Master Robert Shallow. I will make the King do you grace. I will leer upon him as he comes by, and do but mark the countenance that he will give me.
USER: God bless thy lungs, good knight!
BOT: It doth so.
USER: It shows my earnestness of affection -
BOT: It doth so.
USER: My devotion -
BOT: It doth, it doth, it doth.
USER: As it were, to ride day and night, and not to deliberate, not to remember, not to have patience to shift me -
BOT: It is best, certain.
USER: But to stand stained with travel, and sweating with desire to see him, thinking of nothing else, putting all affairs else in oblivion, as if there were nothing else to be done but to see him.
BOT: 'Tis _semper idem_, for _obsque hoc nihil est;_ 'tis all in every part.
USER: 'Tis so, indeed.
BOT: I will deliver her.
USER: There roar'd the sea, and trumpet-clangor sounds.
BOT: God save thy Grace, King Hal, my royal Hal!
USER: The heavens thee guard and keep, most royal imp of fame!
BOT: God save thee, my sweet boy!
USER: My Lord Chief Justice, speak to that vain man.
BOT: Have you your wits? Know you what 'tis you speak?
USER: My King! My Jove! I speak to thee, my heart!
BOT: Master Shallow, I owe you a thousand pounds.
USER: Yea, marry, Sir John, which I beseech you to let me have home with me.
BOT: That can hardly be, Master Shallow. Do not you grieve at this; I shall be sent for in private to him. Look you, he must seem thus to the world. Fear not your advancements; I will be the man yet that shall make you great.
USER: I cannot perceive how, unless you give me your doublet and stuff me out with straw. I beseech you, good Sir John, let me have five hundred of my thousand.
BOT: Sir, I will be as good as my word. This that you heard was but a colour.
USER: A colour that I fear you will die in, Sir John.
BOT: Fear no colours. Go with me to dinner. Come, Lieutenant Pistol; come, Bardolph. I shall be sent for soon at night.
USER: Go, carry Sir John Falstaff to the Fleet. Take all his company along with him.
BOT: My lord, my lord, -
USER: I cannot now speak. I will hear you soon. Take them away.
BOT: _Si fortuna me tormenta, spero me contenta._
USER: I like this fair proceeding of the King's. He hath intent his wonted followers Shall all be very well provided for, But all are banish'd till their conversations Appear more wise and modest to the world.
BOT: And so they are.
USER: The King hath call'd his parliament, my lord.
BOT: He hath.
USER: I will lay odds that, ere this year expire, We bear our civil swords and native fire As far as France. I heard a bird so sing, Whose music, to my thinking, pleased the King. Come, will you hence?

USER: DUKE OF CLARENCE, brother to the King. DUKE OF BEDFORD, brother to the King. DUKE OF GLOUCESTER, brother to the King. DUKE OF EXETER, uncle to the King. DUKE OF YORK, cousin to the King.
BOT: A Herald. CHARLES VI, king of France. LEWIS, the Dauphin.
USER: Lords, Ladies, Officers, Soldiers, Citizens, Messengers, and Attendants.

USER: My lord, I'll tell you, that self bill is urg'd Which in the eleventh year of the last king's reign Was like, and had indeed against us passed But that the scambling and unquiet time Did push it out of farther question.
BOT: But how, my lord, shall we resist it now?
USER: This would drink deep.
BOT: 'Twould drink the cup and all.
USER: But what prevention?
BOT: The King is full of grace and fair regard.
USER: And a true lover of the holy Church.
BOT: We are blessed in the change.
USER: It must be so, for miracles are ceased, And therefore we must needs admit the means How things are perfected.
BOT: But, my good lord, How now for mitigation of this bill Urged by the Commons? Doth his Majesty Incline to it, or no?
USER: How did this offer seem received, my lord?
BOT: What was th' impediment that broke this off?
USER: The French ambassador upon that instant Craved audience; and the hour, I think, is come To give him hearing. Is it four o'clock?
BOT: It is.
USER: Then go we in, to know his embassy, Which I could with a ready guess declare Before the Frenchman speak a word of it.
BOT: I'll wait upon you, and I long to hear it.

USER: Where is my gracious Lord of Canterbury?
BOT: Not here in presence.
USER: Send for him, good uncle.
BOT: Shall we call in th' ambassador, my liege?
USER: Not yet, my cousin. We would be resolved, Before we hear him, of some things of weight That task our thoughts concerning us and France.
BOT: God and his angels guard your sacred throne And make you long become it!
USER: May I with right and conscience make this claim?
BOT: Your brother kings and monarchs of the earth Do all expect that you should rouse yourself, As did the former lions of your blood.
USER: We must not only arm to invade the French, But lay down our proportions to defend Against the Scot, who will make road upon us With all advantages.
BOT: They of those marches, gracious sovereign, Shall be a wall sufficient to defend Our inland from the pilfering borderers.
USER: May't please your Majesty to give us leave Freely to render what we have in charge, Or shall we sparingly show you far off The Dauphin's meaning and our embassy?
BOT: We are no tyrant, but a Christian king, Unto whose grace our passion is as subject As is our wretches fett'red in our prisons; Therefore with frank and with uncurbed plainness Tell us the Dauphin's mind.
USER: What treasure, uncle?
BOT: Tennis-balls, my liege.
USER: This was a merry message.

USER: Well met, Corporal Nym.
BOT: Good morrow, Lieutenant Bardolph.
USER: What, are Ancient Pistol and you friends yet?
BOT: I will bestow a breakfast to make you friends; and we'll be all three sworn brothers to France. Let it be so, good Corporal Nym.
USER: Faith, I will live so long as I may, that's the certain of it; and when I cannot live any longer, I will do as I may. That is my rest, that is the rendezvous of it.
BOT: It is certain, corporal, that he is married to Nell Quickly; and certainly she did you wrong, for you were troth-plight to her.
USER: Here comes Ancient Pistol and his wife. Good Corporal, be patient here. How now, mine host Pistol!
BOT: Base tike, call'st thou me host? Now, by this hand, I swear I scorn the term; Nor shall my Nell keep lodgers.
USER: Good Lieutenant! good corporal! offer nothing here.
BOT: Pish!
USER: Pish for thee, Iceland dog! thou prick-ear'd cur of Iceland!
BOT: Good Corporal Nym, show thy valour, and put up your sword.
USER: Will you shog off? I would have you _solus_.
BOT: O braggart vile and damned furious wight! The grave doth gape, and doting death is near, Therefore exhale.
USER: Hear me, hear me what I say. He that strikes the first stroke I'll run him up to the hilts, as I am a soldier.
BOT: An oath of mickle might; and fury shall abate. Give me thy fist, thy fore-foot to me give. Thy spirits are most tall.
USER: I will cut thy throat, one time or other, in fair terms: that is the humour of it.
BOT: Mine host Pistol, you must come to my master, and you, hostess. He is very sick, and would to bed. Good Bardolph, put thy face between his sheets, and do the office of a warming-pan. Faith, he's very ill.
USER: Away, you rogue!
BOT: By my troth, he'll yield the crow a pudding one of these days. The King has kill'd his heart. Good husband, come home presently.
USER: Come, shall I make you two friends? We must to France together; why the devil should we keep knives to cut one another's throats?
BOT: Let floods o'erswell, and fiends for food howl on!
USER: You'll pay me the eight shillings I won of you at betting?
BOT: Base is the slave that pays.
USER: That now I will have: that's the humour of it.
BOT: As manhood shall compound. Push home.
USER: By this sword, he that makes the first thrust, I'll kill him; by this sword, I will.
BOT: Sword is an oath, and oaths must have their course.
USER: Corporal Nym, and thou wilt be friends, be friends; an thou wilt not, why, then, be enemies with me too. Prithee, put up.
BOT: I shall have my eight shillings I won from you at betting?
USER: I shall have my noble?
BOT: In cash most justly paid.
USER: Well, then, that's the humour of't.
BOT: As ever you come of women, come in quickly to Sir John. Ah, poor heart! he is so shak'd of a burning quotidian tertian, that it is most lamentable to behold. Sweet men, come to him.
USER: The King hath run bad humours on the knight; that's the even of it.
BOT: Nym, thou hast spoke the right. His heart is fracted and corroborate.
USER: The King is a good king; but it must be as it may; he passes some humours and careers.
BOT: Let us condole the knight; for, lambkins, we will live.

USER: 'Fore God, his Grace is bold, to trust these traitors.
BOT: They shall be apprehended by and by.
USER: How smooth and even they do bear themselves! As if allegiance in their bosoms sat Crowned with faith and constant loyalty.
BOT: The King hath note of all that they intend, By interception which they dream not of.
USER: No doubt, my liege, if each man do his best.
BOT: I doubt not that, since we are well persuaded We carry not a heart with us from hence That grows not in a fair consent with ours, Nor leave not one behind that doth not wish Success and conquest to attend on us.
USER: Never was monarch better fear'd and lov'd Than is your Majesty. There's not, I think, a subject That sits in heart-grief and uneasiness Under the sweet shade of your government.
BOT: True; those that were your father's enemies Have steep'd their galls in honey, and do serve you With hearts create of duty and of zeal.
USER: We therefore have great cause of thankfulness, And shall forget the office of our hand Sooner than quittance of desert and merit According to the weight and worthiness.
BOT: So service shall with steeled sinews toil, And labour shall refresh itself with hope, To do your Grace incessant services.
USER: We judge no less. Uncle of Exeter, Enlarge the man committed yesterday, That rail'd against our person. We consider It was excess of wine that set him on, And on his more advice we pardon him.
BOT: That's mercy, but too much security. Let him be punish'd, sovereign, lest example Breed, by his sufferance, more of such a kind.
USER: O, let us yet be merciful.
BOT: So may your Highness, and yet punish too.
USER: Sir, You show great mercy if you give him life After the taste of much correction.
BOT: I one, my lord. Your Highness bade me ask for it today.
USER: So did you me, my liege.
BOT: And I, my royal sovereign.
USER: I do confess my fault, And do submit me to your Highness' mercy. GREY, SCROOP. To which we all appeal.
BOT: Our purposes God justly hath discover'd, And I repent my fault more than my death, Which I beseech your Highness to forgive, Although my body pay the price of it.
USER: Never did faithful subject more rejoice At the discovery of most dangerous treason Than I do at this hour joy o'er myself, Prevented from a damned enterprise. My fault, but not my body, pardon, sovereign.

USER: Prithee, honey, sweet husband, let me bring thee to Staines.
BOT: No; for my manly heart doth yearn. Bardolph, be blithe; Nym, rouse thy vaunting veins; Boy, bristle thy courage up; for Falstaff he is dead, And we must yearn therefore.
USER: Would I were with him, wheresome'er he is, either in heaven or in hell!
BOT: They say he cried out of sack.
USER: Ay, that 'a did.
BOT: And of women.
USER: Nay, that 'a did not.
BOT: Yes, that 'a did; and said they were devils incarnate.
USER: 'A could never abide carnation; 'twas a colour he never liked.
BOT: 'A said once, the devil would have him about women.
USER: 'A did in some sort, indeed, handle women; but then he was rheumatic, and talk'd of the whore of Babylon.
BOT: Do you not remember, 'a saw a flea stick upon Bardolph's nose, and 'a said it was a black soul burning in hell-fire?
USER: Well, the fuel is gone that maintain'd that fire. That's all the riches I got in his service.
BOT: Shall we shog? The King will be gone from Southampton.
USER: And that's but unwholesome food, they say.
BOT: Touch her soft mouth, and march.
USER: Farewell, hostess.
BOT: I cannot kiss; that is the humour of it; but, adieu.
USER: Let housewifery appear. Keep close, I thee command.
BOT: Farewell; adieu.

USER: Ambassadors from Harry King of England Do crave admittance to your Majesty.
BOT: We'll give them present audience. Go, and bring them. You see this chase is hotly follow'd, friends.
USER: From our brother of England?
BOT: Or else what follows?
USER: For us, we will consider of this further. Tomorrow shall you bear our full intent Back to our brother of England.
BOT: For the Dauphin, I stand here for him. What to him from England?
USER: Say, if my father render fair return, It is against my will; for I desire Nothing but odds with England. To that end, As matching to his youth and vanity, I did present him with the Paris balls.
BOT: Tomorrow shall you know our mind at full.
USER: Dispatch us with all speed, lest that our king Come here himself to question our delay; For he is footed in this land already.
BOT: You shall be soon dispatch'd with fair conditions. A night is but small breath and little pause To answer matters of this consequence.

USER: On, on, on, on, on! To the breach, to the breach!
BOT: Pray thee, corporal, stay. The knocks are too hot; and, for mine own part, I have not a case of lives. The humour of it is too hot; that is the very plain-song of it.
USER: The plain-song is most just, for humours do abound. Knocks go and come; God's vassals drop and die; And sword and shield, In bloody field, Doth win immortal fame.
BOT: Would I were in an alehouse in London! I would give all my fame for a pot of ale and safety.
USER: And I. If wishes would prevail with me, My purpose should not fail with me, But thither would I hie.
BOT: As duly, But not as truly, As bird doth sing on bough.
USER: Up to the breach, you dogs! Avaunt, you cullions!
BOT: Be merciful, great Duke, to men of mould. Abate thy rage, abate thy manly rage, Abate thy rage, great Duke! Good bawcock, bate thy rage; use lenity, sweet chuck!
USER: These be good humours! Your honour wins bad humours.
BOT: Captain Fluellen, you must come presently to the mines. The Duke of Gloucester would speak with you.
USER: The Duke of Gloucester, to whom the order of the siege is given, is altogether directed by an Irishman, a very valiant gentleman, i' faith.
BOT: It is Captain Macmorris, is it not?
USER: I think it be.
BOT: By Cheshu, he is an ass, as in the world. I will verify as much in his beard. He has no more directions in the true disciplines of the wars, look you, of the Roman disciplines, than is a puppy-dog.
USER: Here 'a comes; and the Scots captain, Captain Jamy, with him.
BOT: I say gud-day, Captain Fluellen.
USER: God-den to your worship, good Captain James.
BOT: How now, Captain Macmorris! have you quit the mines? Have the pioneers given o'er?
USER: It sall be vary gud, gud feith, gud captains bath: and I sall quit you with gud leve, as I may pick occasion; that sall I, marry.
BOT: Captain Macmorris, I think, look you, under your correction, there is not many of your nation -
USER: Of my nation! What ish my nation? Ish a villain, and a bastard, and a knave, and a rascal? What ish my nation? Who talks of my nation?
BOT: I do not know you so good a man as myself. So Chrish save me, I will cut off your head.
USER: Gentlemen both, you will mistake each other.
BOT: Ah! that's a foul fault.
USER: The town sounds a parley.
BOT: Captain Macmorris, when there is more better opportunity to be required, look you, I will be so bold as to tell you I know the disciplines of war; and there is an end.

USER: _Alice, tu as été en Angleterre, et tu parles bien le langage._
BOT: _Un peu, madame._
USER: _Je te prie, m'enseignez; il faut que j'apprenne à parler. Comment appelez-vous la main en anglais?_
BOT: _La main? Elle est appelée_ de hand.
USER: De hand. _Et les doigts?_
BOT: _Les doigts? Ma foi, j'oublie les doigts; mais je me souviendrai. Les doigts? Je pense qu'ils sont appelés_ de fingres; _oui_, de fingres.
USER: _La main_, de hand; _les doigts_, de fingres. _Je pense que je suis le bon écolier; j'ai gagné deux mots d'anglais vitement. Comment appelez-vous les ongles?_
BOT: _Les ongles? Nous les appelons_ de nails.
USER: De nails. _Écoutez; dites-moi, si je parle bien:_ de hand, de fingres, _et_ de nails.
BOT: _C'est bien dit, madame; il est fort bon anglais._
USER: _Dites-moi l'anglais pour le bras._
BOT: De arm, _madame._
USER: _Et le coude?_
BOT: D'elbow.
USER: D'elbow. _Je m'en fais la répétition de tous les mots que vous m'avez appris dès à présent._
BOT: _Il est trop difficile, madame, comme je pense._
USER: _Excusez-moi, Alice. Écoutez:_ d'hand, de fingres, de nails, d'arm, de bilbow.
BOT: D'elbow, _madame._
USER: _O Seigneur Dieu, je m'en oublie!_ D'elbow. _Comment appelez-vous le col?_
BOT: De nick, _madame._
USER: De nick. _Et le menton?_
BOT: De chin.
USER: De sin. _Le col_, de nick; _le menton_, de sin.
BOT: _Oui. Sauf votre honneur, en vérité, vous prononcez les mots aussi droit que les natifs d'Angleterre._
USER: _Je ne doute point d'apprendre, par la grâce de Dieu, et en peu de temps._
BOT: _N'avez-vous pas déjà oublié ce que je vous ai enseigné?_
USER: _Non, je réciterai à vous promptement:_ d'hand, de fingres, de mails, -
BOT: De nails, _madame._
USER: De nails, de arm, de ilbow.
BOT: _Sauf votre honneur_, de elbow.
USER: _Ainsi dis-je_, d'elbow, de nick, _et_ de sin. _Comment appelez-vous le pied et la robe?_
BOT: De foot, _madame; et_ de coun.
USER: _Excellent, madame!_
BOT: _C'est assez pour une fois. Allons-nous à dîner._

USER: 'Tis certain he hath pass'd the river Somme.
BOT: And if he be not fought withal, my lord, Let us not live in France; let us quit all And give our vineyards to a barbarous people.
USER: _O Dieu vivant_! shall a few sprays of us, The emptying of our fathers' luxury, Our scions put in wild and savage stock, Spirt up so suddenly into the clouds, And overlook their grafters?
BOT: Normans, but bastard Normans, Norman bastards! _Mort de ma vie_, if they march along Unfought withal, but I will sell my dukedom, To buy a slobbery and a dirty farm In that nook-shotten isle of Albion.
USER: By faith and honour, Our madams mock at us, and plainly say Our mettle is bred out, and they will give Their bodies to the lust of English youth To new-store France with bastard warriors.
BOT: They bid us to the English dancing-schools, And teach lavoltas high, and swift corantos; Saying our grace is only in our heels, And that we are most lofty runaways.
USER: Therefore, Lord Constable, haste on Montjoy, And let him say to England that we send To know what willing ransom he will give. Prince Dauphin, you shall stay with us in Rouen.
BOT: Not so, I do beseech your Majesty.
USER: Be patient, for you shall remain with us. Now forth, Lord Constable and princes all, And quickly bring us word of England's fall.

USER: How now, Captain Fluellen! come you from the bridge?
BOT: I assure you, there is very excellent services committed at the bridge.
USER: Is the Duke of Exeter safe?
BOT: What do you call him?
USER: He is call'd Anchient Pistol.
BOT: I know him not.
USER: Here is the man.
BOT: Captain, I thee beseech to do me favours. The Duke of Exeter doth love thee well.
USER: Ay, I praise God; and I have merited some love at his hands.
BOT: Bardolph, a soldier, firm and sound of heart, And of buxom valour, hath by cruel fate And giddy Fortune's furious fickle wheel, That goddess blind, That stands upon the rolling restless stone -
USER: Anchient Pistol, I do partly understand your meaning.
BOT: Why then, rejoice therefore.
USER: Certainly, anchient, it is not a thing to rejoice at; for if, look you, he were my brother, I would desire the Duke to use his good pleasure, and put him to execution; for discipline ought to be used.
BOT: Die and be damn'd! and _fico_ for thy friendship!
USER: It is well.
BOT: The fig of Spain.
USER: Very good.
BOT: Why, this is an arrant counterfeit rascal. I remember him now; a bawd, a cutpurse.
USER: I'll assure you, 'a uttered as prave words at the pridge as you shall see in a summer's day. But it is very well; what he has spoke to me, that is well, I warrant you, when time is serve.
BOT: How now, Fluellen! cam'st thou from the bridge?
USER: What men have you lost, Fluellen?
BOT: You know me by my habit.
USER: Well then I know thee. What shall I know of thee?
BOT: My master's mind.
USER: Unfold it.
BOT: What is thy name? I know thy quality.
USER: Montjoy.
BOT: I shall deliver so. Thanks to your Highness.
USER: I hope they will not come upon us now.
BOT: We are in God's hands, brother, not in theirs. March to the bridge; it now draws toward night. Beyond the river we'll encamp ourselves, And on tomorrow bid them march away.

USER: Tut! I have the best armour of the world. Would it were day!
BOT: You have an excellent armour; but let my horse have his due.
USER: It is the best horse of Europe.
BOT: Will it never be morning?
USER: My Lord of Orleans, and my Lord High Constable, you talk of horse and armour?
BOT: You are as well provided of both as any prince in the world.
USER: He's of the colour of the nutmeg.
BOT: Indeed, my lord, it is a most absolute and excellent horse.
USER: It is the prince of palfreys; his neigh is like the bidding of a monarch, and his countenance enforces homage.
BOT: No more, cousin.
USER: I have heard a sonnet begin so to one's mistress.
BOT: Then did they imitate that which I compos'd to my courser, for my horse is my mistress.
USER: Your mistress bears well.
BOT: Me well; which is the prescript praise and perfection of a good and particular mistress.
USER: Nay, for methought yesterday your mistress shrewdly shook your back.
BOT: So perhaps did yours.
USER: Mine was not bridled.
BOT: O then belike she was old and gentle; and you rode, like a kern of Ireland, your French hose off, and in your strait strossers.
USER: You have good judgment in horsemanship.
BOT: Be warn'd by me, then; they that ride so and ride not warily, fall into foul bogs. I had rather have my horse to my mistress.
USER: I had as lief have my mistress a jade.
BOT: I tell thee, Constable, my mistress wears his own hair.
USER: I could make as true a boast as that, if I had a sow to my mistress.
BOT: "_Le chien est retourné à son propre vomissement, et la truie lavée au bourbier_." Thou mak'st use of anything.
USER: Yet do I not use my horse for my mistress, or any such proverb so little kin to the purpose.
BOT: My Lord Constable, the armour that I saw in your tent tonight, are those stars or suns upon it?
USER: Stars, my lord.
BOT: Some of them will fall tomorrow, I hope.
USER: And yet my sky shall not want.
BOT: That may be, for you bear a many superfluously, and 'twere more honour some were away.
USER: Even as your horse bears your praises; who would trot as well, were some of your brags dismounted.
BOT: Would I were able to load him with his desert! Will it never be day? I will trot tomorrow a mile, and my way shall be paved with English faces.
USER: I will not say so, for fear I should be fac'd out of my way. But I would it were morning; for I would fain be about the ears of the English.
BOT: Who will go to hazard with me for twenty prisoners?
USER: You must first go yourself to hazard, ere you have them.
BOT: 'Tis midnight; I'll go arm myself.
USER: The Dauphin longs for morning.
BOT: He longs to eat the English.
USER: I think he will eat all he kills.
BOT: By the white hand of my lady, he's a gallant prince.
USER: Swear by her foot that she may tread out the oath.
BOT: He is simply the most active gentleman of France.
USER: Doing is activity; and he will still be doing.
BOT: He never did harm, that I heard of.
USER: Nor will do none tomorrow. He will keep that good name still.
BOT: I know him to be valiant.
USER: I was told that by one that knows him better than you.
BOT: What's he?
USER: Marry, he told me so himself; and he said he car'd not who knew it.
BOT: He needs not; it is no hidden virtue in him.
USER: By my faith, sir, but it is; never anybody saw it but his lackey. 'Tis a hooded valour; and when it appears, it will bate.
BOT: "Ill will never said well."
USER: I will cap that proverb with "There is flattery in friendship."
BOT: And I will take up that with "Give the devil his due."
USER: Well plac'd. There stands your friend for the devil; have at the very eye of that proverb with "A pox of the devil."
BOT: You are the better at proverbs, by how much "A fool's bolt is soon shot."
USER: You have shot over.
BOT: 'Tis not the first time you were overshot.
USER: My Lord High Constable, the English lie within fifteen hundred paces of your tents.
BOT: Who hath measur'd the ground?
USER: The Lord Grandpré.
BOT: A valiant and most expert gentleman. Would it were day! Alas, poor Harry of England, he longs not for the dawning as we do.
USER: What a wretched and peevish fellow is this King of England, to mope with his fat-brain'd followers so far out of his knowledge!
BOT: If the English had any apprehension, they would run away.
USER: That they lack; for if their heads had any intellectual armour, they could never wear such heavy head-pieces.
BOT: That island of England breeds very valiant creatures. Their mastiffs are of unmatchable courage.
USER: Foolish curs, that run winking into the mouth of a Russian bear and have their heads crush'd like rotten apples! You may as well say, that's a valiant flea that dare eat his breakfast on the lip of a lion.
BOT: Ay, but these English are shrewdly out of beef.
USER: Then shall we find tomorrow they have only stomachs to eat and none to fight. Now is it time to arm. Come, shall we about it?
BOT: It is now two o'clock; but, let me see, by ten We shall have each a hundred Englishmen.

USER: Not so, my liege; this lodging likes me better, Since I may say, "Now lie I like a king."
BOT: We shall, my liege.
USER: Shall I attend your Grace?
BOT: No, my good knight; Go with my brothers to my lords of England. I and my bosom must debate a while, And then I would no other company.
USER: The Lord in heaven bless thee, noble Harry!
BOT: God-a-mercy, old heart! thou speak'st cheerfully.
USER: _Qui vous là?_
BOT: A friend.
USER: Discuss unto me; art thou officer? Or art thou base, common, and popular?
BOT: I am a gentleman of a company.
USER: Trail'st thou the puissant pike?
BOT: Even so. What are you?
USER: As good a gentleman as the Emperor.
BOT: Then you are a better than the King.
USER: The King's a bawcock, and a heart of gold, A lad of life, an imp of fame; Of parents good, of fist most valiant. I kiss his dirty shoe, and from heart-string I love the lovely bully. What is thy name?
BOT: Harry le Roy.
USER: Le Roy! a Cornish name. Art thou of Cornish crew?
BOT: No, I am a Welshman.
USER: Know'st thou Fluellen?
BOT: Yes.
USER: Tell him I'll knock his leek about his pate Upon Saint Davy's day.
BOT: Do not you wear your dagger in your cap that day, lest he knock that about yours.
USER: Art thou his friend?
BOT: And his kinsman too.
USER: The _fico_ for thee, then!
BOT: I thank you. God be with you!
USER: My name is Pistol call'd.
BOT: It sorts well with your fierceness.
USER: Captain Fluellen!
BOT: Why, the enemy is loud; you hear him all night.
USER: If the enemy is an ass and a fool and a prating coxcomb, is it meet, think you, that we should also, look you, be an ass and a fool and a prating coxcomb? In your own conscience, now?
BOT: I will speak lower.
USER: I pray you and beseech you that you will.
BOT: Though it appear a little out of fashion, There is much care and valour in this Welshman. Williams.
USER: Brother John Bates, is not that the morning which breaks yonder?
BOT: I think it be; but we have no great cause to desire the approach of day.
USER: We see yonder the beginning of the day, but I think we shall never see

USER: A friend.
BOT: Under what captain serve you?
USER: Under Sir Thomas Erpingham.
BOT: A good old commander and a most kind gentleman. I pray you, what thinks he of our estate?
USER: Even as men wreck'd upon a sand, that look to be wash'd off the next tide.
BOT: He hath not told his thought to the King?
USER: He may show what outward courage he will; but I believe, as cold a night as 'tis, he could wish himself in Thames up to the neck; and so I would he were, and I by him, at all adventures, so we were quit here.
BOT: By my troth, I will speak my conscience of the King: I think he would not wish himself anywhere but where he is.
USER: Then I would he were here alone; so should he be sure to be ransomed, and a many poor men's lives saved.
BOT: That's more than we know.
USER: Ay, or more than we should seek after; for we know enough, if we know we are the King's subjects. If his cause be wrong, our obedience to the King wipes the crime of it out of us.
BOT: 'Tis certain, every man that dies ill, the ill upon his own head, the King is not to answer for it.
USER: I do not desire he should answer for me; and yet I determine to fight lustily for him.
BOT: I myself heard the King say he would not be ransom'd.
USER: Ay, he said so, to make us fight cheerfully; but when our throats are cut, he may be ransom'd, and we ne'er the wiser.
BOT: If I live to see it, I will never trust his word after.
USER: Your reproof is something too round. I should be angry with you, if the time were convenient.
BOT: Let it be a quarrel between us if you live.
USER: I embrace it.
BOT: How shall I know thee again?
USER: Give me any gage of thine, and I will wear it in my bonnet; then, if ever thou dar'st acknowledge it, I will make it my quarrel.
BOT: Here's my glove; give me another of thine.
USER: There.
BOT: This will I also wear in my cap. If ever thou come to me and say, after tomorrow, "This is my glove," by this hand I will take thee a box on the ear.
USER: If ever I live to see it, I will challenge it.
BOT: Thou dar'st as well be hang'd.
USER: Well, I will do it, though I take thee in the King's company.
BOT: Keep thy word; fare thee well.
USER: Be friends, you English fools, be friends. We have French quarrels enough, if you could tell how to reckon.
BOT: My lord, your nobles, jealous of your absence, Seek through your camp to find you.
USER: Good old knight, Collect them all together at my tent. I'll be before thee.
BOT: I shall do't, my lord.
USER: My liege!
BOT: My brother Gloucester's voice? Ay; I know thy errand, I will go with thee. The day, my friends, and all things stay for me.

USER: The sun doth gild our armour; up, my lords!
BOT: _Monte à cheval!_ My horse, _varlet! laquais_, ha!
USER: O brave spirit!
BOT: _Via, les eaux et terre!_
USER: _Rien puis? L'air et feu?_
BOT: _Cieux_, cousin Orleans. Now, my Lord Constable!
USER: Hark, how our steeds for present service neigh!
BOT: Mount them, and make incision in their hides, That their hot blood may spin in English eyes, And dout them with superfluous courage, ha!
USER: What, will you have them weep our horses' blood? How shall we, then, behold their natural tears?
BOT: The English are embattl'd, you French peers.
USER: They have said their prayers, and they stay for death.
BOT: Shall we go send them dinners and fresh suits And give their fasting horses provender, And after fight with them?
USER: I stay but for my guard; on to the field! I will the banner from a trumpet take, And use it for my haste. Come, come, away! The sun is high, and we outwear the day.

USER: Where is the King?
BOT: The King himself is rode to view their battle.
USER: Of fighting men they have full three-score thousand.
BOT: There's five to one; besides, they all are fresh.
USER: Farewell, good Salisbury, and good luck go with thee!
BOT: Farewell, kind lord; fight valiantly today! And yet I do thee wrong to mind thee of it, For thou art fram'd of the firm truth of valour.
USER: He is as full of valour as of kindness, Princely in both.
BOT: O that we now had here But one ten thousand of those men in England That do no work today!
USER: My sovereign lord, bestow yourself with speed. The French are bravely in their battles set, And will with all expedience charge on us.
BOT: All things are ready, if our minds be so.
USER: Perish the man whose mind is backward now!
BOT: Thou dost not wish more help from England, coz?
USER: God's will! my liege, would you and I alone, Without more help, could fight this royal battle!
BOT: Why, now thou hast unwish'd five thousand men, Which likes me better than to wish us one. You know your places. God be with you all! Tucket. Enter Montjoy.
USER: Who hath sent thee now?
BOT: The Constable of France.
USER: I shall, King Harry. And so fare thee well; Thou never shalt hear herald any more.
BOT: I fear thou'lt once more come again for ransom.
USER: My lord, most humbly on my knee I beg The leading of the vaward.
BOT: Take it, brave York. Now, soldiers, march away; And how thou pleasest, God, dispose the day!

USER: Yield, cur!
BOT: _Je pense que vous êtes le gentilhomme de bonne qualité._
USER: _Qualité? Caleno custore me!_ Art thou a gentleman? What is thy name? Discuss.
BOT: _O Seigneur Dieu!_
USER: O, Signieur Dew should be a gentleman. Perpend my words, O Signieur Dew, and mark: O Signieur Dew, thou diest on point of fox, Except, O signieur, thou do give to me Egregious ransom.
BOT: _O, prenez miséricorde! Ayez pitié de moi!_
USER: Moy shall not serve; I will have forty moys, Or I will fetch thy rim out at thy throat In drops of crimson blood.
BOT: _Est-il impossible d'échapper la force de ton bras?_
USER: Brass, cur! Thou damned and luxurious mountain goat, Offer'st me brass?
BOT: _O pardonnez-moi!_
USER: Say'st thou me so? Is that a ton of moys? Come hither, boy; ask me this slave in French What is his name.
BOT: _Écoutez. Comment êtes-vous appelé?_
USER: _Monsieur le Fer._
BOT: He says his name is Master Fer.
USER: Master Fer! I'll fer him, and firk him, and ferret him. Discuss the same in French unto him.
BOT: I do not know the French for fer, and ferret, and firk.
USER: Bid him prepare; for I will cut his throat.
BOT: _Que dit-il, monsieur?_
USER: _Il me commande à vous dire que vous faites vous prêt, car ce soldat ici est disposé tout à cette heure de couper votre gorge._
BOT: Owy, cuppele gorge, permafoy, Peasant, unless thou give me crowns, brave crowns; Or mangled shalt thou be by this my sword.
USER: _O, je vous supplie, pour l'amour de Dieu, me pardonner! Je suis le gentilhomme de bonne maison; gardez ma vie, et je vous donnerai deux cents écus._
BOT: What are his words?
USER: He prays you to save his life. He is a gentleman of a good house; and for his ransom he will give you two hundred crowns.
BOT: Tell him my fury shall abate, and I The crowns will take.
USER: _Petit monsieur, que dit-il?_
BOT: _Encore qu'il est contre son jurement de pardonner aucun prisonnier; néanmoins, pour les écus que vous lui avez promis, il est content à vous donner la liberté, le franchisement._
USER: _Sur mes genoux je vous donne mille remerciements; et je m'estime heureux que je suis tombé entre les mains d'un chevalier, je pense, le plus brave, vaillant, et très distingué seigneur d'Angleterre._
BOT: Expound unto me, boy.
USER: He gives you upon his knees, a thousand thanks; and he esteems himself happy that he hath fallen into the hands of one, as he thinks, the most brave, valorous, and thrice-worthy _seigneur_ of England.
BOT: As I suck blood, I will some mercy show. Follow me!

USER: _O diable!_
BOT: _O Seigneur! le jour est perdu, tout est perdu!_
USER: _Mort de ma vie!_ all is confounded, all! Reproach and everlasting shame Sits mocking in our plumes. _O méchante Fortune!_ Do not run away.
BOT: Why, all our ranks are broke.
USER: O perdurable shame! Let's stab ourselves, Be these the wretches that we play'd at dice for?
BOT: Is this the king we sent to for his ransom?
USER: Disorder, that hath spoil'd us, friend us now! Let us on heaps go offer up our lives.
BOT: We are enough yet living in the field To smother up the English in our throngs, If any order might be thought upon.
USER: The devil take order now! I'll to the throng. Let life be short, else shame will be too long.

USER: Well have we done, thrice valiant countrymen. But all's not done; yet keep the French the field.
BOT: The Duke of York commends him to your Majesty.
USER: Lives he, good uncle? Thrice within this hour I saw him down; thrice up again, and fighting. From helmet to the spur all blood he was.

USER: Kill the poys and the luggage! 'Tis expressly against the law of arms. 'Tis as arrant a piece of knavery, mark you now, as can be offer't; in your conscience, now, is it not?
BOT: Ay, he was porn at Monmouth, Captain Gower. What call you the town's name where Alexander the Pig was born?
USER: Alexander the Great.
BOT: Why, I pray you, is not pig great? The pig, or the great, or the mighty, or the huge, or the magnanimous, are all one reckonings, save the phrase is a little variations.
USER: I think Alexander the Great was born in Macedon. His father was called Philip of Macedon, as I take it.
BOT: Our King is not like him in that. He never kill'd any of his friends.
USER: Sir John Falstaff.
BOT: That is he. I'll tell you there is good men porn at Monmouth.
USER: Here comes his Majesty. Alarum. Enter King Henry and forces; Warwick, Gloucester, Exeter with prisoners. Flourish.
BOT: Here comes the herald of the French, my liege.
USER: His eyes are humbler than they us'd to be.
BOT: How now! what means this, herald? Know'st thou not That I have fin'd these bones of mine for ransom? Com'st thou again for ransom?
USER: I tell thee truly, herald, I know not if the day be ours or no; For yet a many of your horsemen peer And gallop o'er the field.
BOT: The day is yours.
USER: Praised be God, and not our strength, for it! What is this castle call'd that stands hard by?
BOT: They call it Agincourt.
USER: Then call we this the field of Agincourt, Fought on the day of Crispin Crispianus.
BOT: Your grandfather of famous memory, an't please your Majesty, and your great-uncle Edward the Plack Prince of Wales, as I have read in the chronicles, fought a most prave pattle here in France.
USER: They did, Fluellen.
BOT: I wear it for a memorable honour; For I am Welsh, you know, good countryman.
USER: All the water in Wye cannot wash your Majesty's Welsh plood out of your pody, I can tell you that. Got pless it and preserve it, as long as it pleases His grace, and His majesty too!
BOT: Thanks, good my countryman.
USER: By Jeshu, I am your Majesty's countryman, I care not who know it. I will confess it to all the 'orld. I need not be asham'd of your Majesty, praised be God, so long as your Majesty is an honest man.
BOT: God keep me so! Our heralds go with him; Bring me just notice of the numbers dead On both our parts. Call yonder fellow hither.
USER: Soldier, you must come to the King.
BOT: Soldier, why wear'st thou that glove in thy cap?
USER: An't please your Majesty, 'tis the gage of one that I should fight withal, if he be alive.
BOT: An Englishman?
USER: What think you, Captain Fluellen, is it fit this soldier keep his oath?
BOT: He is a craven and a villain else, an't please your Majesty, in my conscience.
USER: It may be his enemy is a gentlemen of great sort, quite from the answer of his degree.
BOT: Then keep thy vow, sirrah, when thou meet'st the fellow.
USER: So I will, my liege, as I live.
BOT: Who serv'st thou under?
USER: Under Captain Gower, my liege.
BOT: Gower is a good captain, and is good knowledge and literatured in the wars.
USER: Call him hither to me, soldier.
BOT: I will, my liege.
USER: Know'st thou Gower?
BOT: He is my dear friend, an please you.
USER: Pray thee, go seek him, and bring him to my tent.
BOT: I will fetch him.

USER: I warrant it is to knight you, Captain.
BOT: God's will and his pleasure, captain, I beseech you now, come apace to the King. There is more good toward you peradventure than is in your knowledge to dream of.
USER: Sir, know you this glove?
BOT: Know the glove! I know the glove is a glove.
USER: I know this; and thus I challenge it.
BOT: 'Sblood! an arrant traitor as any is in the universal world, or in France, or in England!
USER: How now, sir! you villain!
BOT: Do you think I'll be forsworn?
USER: Stand away, Captain Gower. I will give treason his payment into plows, I warrant you.
BOT: I am no traitor.
USER: That's a lie in thy throat. I charge you in his Majesty's name, apprehend him; he's a friend of the Duke Alençon's.
BOT: How now, how now! what's the matter?
USER: My lord of Warwick, here is - praised be God for it! - a most contagious treason come to light, look you, as you shall desire in a summer's day. Here is his Majesty.
BOT: How now! what's the matter?
USER: My liege, here is a villain and a traitor, that, look your Grace, has struck the glove which your Majesty is take out of the helmet of Alençon.
BOT: Give me thy glove, soldier. Look, here is the fellow of it. 'Twas I, indeed, thou promisedst to strike; And thou hast given me most bitter terms.
USER: An it please your Majesty, let his neck answer for it, if there is any martial law in the world.
BOT: How canst thou make me satisfaction?
USER: All offences, my lord, come from the heart. Never came any from mine that might offend your Majesty.
BOT: It was ourself thou didst abuse.
USER: I will none of your money.
BOT: It is with a good will; I can tell you, it will serve you to mend your shoes. Come, wherefore should you be so pashful? Your shoes is not so good. 'Tis a good silling, I warrant you, or I will change it.
USER: Now, herald, are the dead numb'red?
BOT: Here is the number of the slaught'red French.
USER: What prisoners of good sort are taken, uncle?
BOT: Charles Duke of Orleans, nephew to the King; John Duke of Bourbon, and Lord Boucicault: Of other lords and barons, knights and squires, Full fifteen hundred, besides common men.
USER: 'Tis wonderful!
BOT: Come, go we in procession to the village; And be it death proclaimed through our host To boast of this or take that praise from God Which is His only.
USER: Is it not lawful, an please your Majesty, to tell how many is kill'd?
BOT: Yes, Captain; but with this acknowledgment, That God fought for us.
USER: Yes, my conscience, He did us great good.
BOT: Do we all holy rites. Let there be sung _Non nobis_ and _Te Deum_, The dead with charity enclos'd in clay, And then to Calais; and to England then, Where ne'er from France arriv'd more happy men.

USER: Nay, that's right; but why wear you your leek today? Saint Davy's day is past.
BOT: Why, here he comes, swelling like a turkey-cock.
USER: 'Tis no matter for his swellings nor his turkey-cocks. God pless you, Anchient Pistol! you scurvy, lousy knave, God pless you!
BOT: Ha! art thou bedlam? Dost thou thirst, base Trojan, To have me fold up Parca's fatal web? Hence! I am qualmish at the smell of leek.
USER: Not for Cadwallader and all his goats.
BOT: There is one goat for you. [_Strikes him._] Will you be so good, scald knave, as eat it?
USER: Base Trojan, thou shalt die.
BOT: Enough, captain; you have astonish'd him.
USER: I say, I will make him eat some part of my leek, or I will peat his pate four days. Bite, I pray you; it is good for your green wound and your ploody coxcomb.
BOT: Must I bite?
USER: Yes, certainly, and out of doubt and out of question too, and ambiguities.
BOT: By this leek, I will most horribly revenge. I eat and eat, I swear -
USER: Eat, I pray you. Will you have some more sauce to your leek? There is not enough leek to swear by.
BOT: Quiet thy cudgel; thou dost see I eat.
USER: Much good do you, scald knave, heartily. Nay, pray you, throw none away; the skin is good for your broken coxcomb. When you take occasions to see leeks hereafter, I pray you, mock at 'em; that is all.
BOT: Good.
USER: Ay, leeks is good. Hold you, there is a groat to heal your pate.
BOT: Me a groat!
USER: Yes, verily and in truth you shall take it; or I have another leek in my pocket, which you shall eat.
BOT: I take thy groat in earnest of revenge.
USER: If I owe you anything I will pay you in cudgels. You shall be a woodmonger, and buy nothing of me but cudgels. God be wi' you, and keep you, and heal your pate.
BOT: All hell shall stir for this.

USER: Right joyous are we to behold your face, Most worthy brother England; fairly met! So are you, princes English, every one.
BOT: To cry amen to that, thus we appear.
USER: You English princes all, I do salute you.
BOT: The King hath heard them; to the which as yet There is no answer made.
USER: Well, then, the peace, Which you before so urg'd, lies in his answer.
BOT: Our gracious brother, I will go with them. Haply a woman's voice may do some good, When articles too nicely urg'd be stood on.
USER: Yet leave our cousin Katharine here with us: She is our capital demand, compris'd Within the fore-rank of our articles.
BOT: She hath good leave.
USER: Fair Katharine, and most fair, Will you vouchsafe to teach a soldier terms Such as will enter at a lady's ear And plead his love-suit to her gentle heart?
BOT: Your Majesty shall mock me; I cannot speak your England.
USER: O fair Katharine, if you will love me soundly with your French heart, I will be glad to hear you confess it brokenly with your English tongue. Do you like me, Kate?
BOT: _Pardonnez-moi_, I cannot tell wat is "like me."
USER: An angel is like you, Kate, and you are like an angel.
BOT: _Que dit-il? Que je suis semblable à les anges?_
USER: _Oui, vraiment, sauf votre Grâce, ainsi dit-il._
BOT: I said so, dear Katharine; and I must not blush to affirm it.
USER: _O bon Dieu! les langues des hommes sont pleines de tromperies._
BOT: What says she, fair one? That the tongues of men are full of deceits?
USER: _Oui_, dat de tongues of de mans is be full of deceits: dat is de Princess.
BOT: _Sauf votre honneur_, me understand well.
USER: Is it possible dat I should love de enemy of France?
BOT: I cannot tell wat is dat.
USER: _Sauf votre honneur, le français que vous parlez, il est meilleur que l'anglais lequel je parle._
BOT: No, faith, is't not, Kate; but thy speaking of my tongue, and I thine, most truly-falsely, must needs be granted to be much at one. But, Kate, dost thou understand thus much English: canst thou love me?
USER: I cannot tell.
BOT: I do not know dat.
USER: Your Majestee 'ave _fausse_ French enough to deceive de most _sage demoiselle_ dat is _en France_.
BOT: Dat is as it shall please _le roi mon père_.
USER: Nay, it will please him well, Kate; it shall please him, Kate.
BOT: Den it sall also content me.
USER: Upon that I kiss your hand, and call you my queen.
BOT: Then I will kiss your lips, Kate.
USER: _Les dames et demoiselles pour être baisées devant leurs noces, il n'est pas la coutume de France._
BOT: Madame my interpreter, what says she?
USER: Dat it is not be de fashion _pour les_ ladies of France, - I cannot tell wat is _baiser en_ Anglish.
BOT: To kiss.
USER: Your Majestee _entend_ bettre _que moi_.
BOT: It is not a fashion for the maids in France to kiss before they are married, would she say?
USER: _Oui, vraiment._
BOT: God save your Majesty! My royal cousin, teach you our princess English?
USER: I would have her learn, my fair cousin, how perfectly I love her; and that is good English.
BOT: Is she not apt?
USER: Our tongue is rough, coz, and my condition is not smooth; so that, having neither the voice nor the heart of flattery about me, I cannot so conjure up the spirit of love in her, that he will appear in his true likeness.
BOT: Yet they do wink and yield, as love is blind and enforces.
USER: They are then excus'd, my lord, when they see not what they do.
BOT: Then, good my lord, teach your cousin to consent winking.
USER: This moral ties me over to time and a hot summer; and so I shall catch the fly, your cousin, in the latter end, and she must be blind too.
BOT: As love is, my lord, before it loves.
USER: It is so; and you may, some of you, thank love for my blindness, who cannot see many a fair French city for one fair French maid that stands in my way.
BOT: Yes, my lord, you see them perspectively, the cities turn'd into a maid; for they are all girdled with maiden walls that no war hath entered.
USER: Shall Kate be my wife?
BOT: So please you.
USER: I am content, so the maiden cities you talk of may wait on her; so the maid that stood in the way for my wish shall show me the way to my will.
BOT: We have consented to all terms of reason.
USER: Is't so, my lords of England?
BOT: The king hath granted every article; His daughter first, and then in sequel all, According to their firm proposed natures.
USER: Nor this I have not, brother, so denied But our request shall make me let it pass.
BOT: I pray you then, in love and dear alliance, Let that one article rank with the rest; And thereupon give me your daughter.
USER: Amen!
BOT: Now, welcome, Kate; and bear me witness all, That here I kiss her as my sovereign queen.
USER: Amen!

USER: He was a king bless'd of the King of kings; Unto the French the dreadful judgement-day So dreadful will not be as was his sight. The battles of the Lord of Hosts he fought: The Church's prayers made him so prosperous.
BOT: The Church! Where is it? Had not churchmen pray'd, His thread of life had not so soon decay'd. None do you like but an effeminate prince, Whom like a school-boy you may overawe.
USER: Gloucester, whate'er we like, thou art Protector, And lookest to command the Prince and realm. Thy wife is proud; she holdeth thee in awe More than God or religious churchmen may.
BOT: Name not religion, for thou lov'st the flesh, And ne'er throughout the year to church thou go'st, Except it be to pray against thy foes.
USER: My honourable lords, health to you all! Sad tidings bring I to you out of France, Of loss, of slaughter, and discomfiture: Guienne, Champaigne, Rheims, Rouen, Orleans, Paris, Guysors, Poictiers, are all quite lost.
BOT: What say'st thou, man, before dead Henry's corse? Speak softly, or the loss of those great towns Will make him burst his lead and rise from death.
USER: Is Paris lost? Is Rouen yielded up? If Henry were recall'd to life again, These news would cause him once more yield the ghost.
BOT: How were they lost? What treachery was us'd?
USER: Were our tears wanting to this funeral, These tidings would call forth their flowing tides.
BOT: Me they concern; Regent I am of France. Give me my steeled coat. I'll fight for France. Away with these disgraceful wailing robes! Wounds will I lend the French instead of eyes, To weep their intermissive miseries.
USER: The Dauphin crowned king! All fly to him! O, whither shall we fly from this reproach?
BOT: We will not fly but to our enemies' throats. Bedford, if thou be slack, I'll fight it out.
USER: Gloucester, why doubt'st thou of my forwardness? An army have I muster'd in my thoughts, Wherewith already France is overrun.
BOT: My gracious lords, to add to your laments, Wherewith you now bedew King Henry's hearse, I must inform you of a dismal fight Betwixt the stout Lord Talbot and the French.
USER: What! Wherein Talbot overcame, is't so?
BOT: Is Talbot slain? Then I will slay myself, For living idly here, in pomp and ease, Whilst such a worthy leader, wanting aid, Unto his dastard foemen is betray'd.
USER: O no, he lives, but is took prisoner, And Lord Scales with him, and Lord Hungerford; Most of the rest slaughter'd or took likewise.
BOT: So you had need; for Orleans is besieg'd The English army is grown weak and faint; The Earl of Salisbury craveth supply And hardly keeps his men from mutiny, Since they, so few, watch such a multitude.
USER: Remember, lords, your oaths to Henry sworn, Either to quell the Dauphin utterly, Or bring him in obedience to your yoke.
BOT: I do remember it, and here take my leave To go about my preparation.
USER: I'll to the Tower with all the haste I can To view th' artillery and munition; And then I will proclaim young Henry king.
BOT: To Eltham will I, where the young King is, Being ordain'd his special governor; And for his safety there I'll best devise.
USER: Each hath his place and function to attend. I am left out; for me nothing remains. But long I will not be Jack out of office. The King from Eltham I intend to steal, And sit at chiefest stern of public weal.

USER: Let's raise the siege. Why live we idly here? Talbot is taken, whom we wont to fear. Remaineth none but mad-brain'd Salisbury, And he may well in fretting spend his gall; Nor men nor money hath he to make war.
BOT: Who ever saw the like? What men have I! Dogs, cowards, dastards! I would ne'er have fled But that they left me 'midst my enemies.
USER: Let's leave this town; for they are hare-brain'd slaves, And hunger will enforce them to be more eager. Of old I know them; rather with their teeth The walls they'll tear down than forsake the siege.
BOT: I think by some odd gimmers or device Their arms are set like clocks, still to strike on; Else ne'er could they hold out so as they do. By my consent, we'll even let them alone. ALENÇON. Be it so.
USER: Where's the Prince Dauphin? I have news for him.
BOT: Bastard of Orleans, thrice welcome to us.
USER: Go, call her in. But first, to try her skill, Reignier, stand thou as Dauphin in my place; Question her proudly; let thy looks be stern. By this means shall we sound what skill she hath.
BOT: Fair maid, is 't thou wilt do these wondrous feats?
USER: She takes upon her bravely at first dash.
BOT: Thou hast astonish'd me with thy high terms. Only this proof I'll of thy valour make: In single combat thou shalt buckle with me, And if thou vanquishest, thy words are true; Otherwise I renounce all confidence.
USER: I am prepared. Here is my keen-edg'd sword, Deck'd with five flower-de-luces on each side, The which at Touraine, in Saint Katharine's churchyard, Out of a great deal of old iron I chose forth.
BOT: Then come, o' God's name; I fear no woman.
USER: And while I live, I'll ne'er fly from a man.
BOT: Stay, stay thy hands; thou art an Amazon, And fightest with the sword of Deborah.
USER: Christ's Mother helps me, else I were too weak.
BOT: I must not yield to any rites of love, For my profession's sacred from above. When I have chased all thy foes from hence, Then will I think upon a recompense.
USER: Meantime look gracious on thy prostrate thrall.
BOT: My lord, methinks, is very long in talk. ALENÇON. Doubtless he shrives this woman to her smock; Else ne'er could he so long protract his speech.
USER: Shall we disturb him, since he keeps no mean? ALENÇON. He may mean more than we poor men do know. These women are shrewd tempters with their tongues.
BOT: My lord, where are you? What devise you on? Shall we give over Orleans, or no?
USER: Why, no, I say. Distrustful recreants! Fight till the last gasp; I will be your guard.
BOT: What she says I'll confirm. We'll fight it out.
USER: Woman, do what thou canst to save our honours; Drive them from Orleans and be immortalized.
BOT: Presently we'll try. Come, let's away about it. No prophet will I trust if she prove false.

USER: I am come to survey the Tower this day. Since Henry's death, I fear, there is conveyance. Where be these warders that they wait not here? Open the gates; 'tis Gloucester that calls.
BOT: It is the noble Duke of Gloucester.
USER: Villains, answer you so the Lord Protector?
BOT: We do no otherwise than we are will'd.
USER: Who willed you? Or whose will stands but mine? There's none Protector of the realm but I. Break up the gates, I'll be your warrantize. Shall I be flouted thus by dunghill grooms? Lieutenant, speaks within._]
BOT: What noise is this? What traitors have we here?
USER: Lieutenant, is it you whose voice I hear? Open the gates; here's Gloucester that would enter.
BOT: Have patience, noble Duke; I may not open; The Cardinal of Winchester forbids. From him I have express commandment That thou nor none of thine shall be let in.
USER: Open the gates unto the Lord Protector, Or we'll burst them open, if that you come not quickly. tawny coats.
BOT: How now, ambitious Humphrey! What means this?
USER: Peel'd priest, dost thou command me to be shut out?
BOT: I do, thou most usurping proditor, And not Protector, of the King or realm.
USER: Stand back, thou manifest conspirator, Thou that contrived'st to murder our dead lord; Thou that giv'st whores indulgences to sin: I'll canvass thee in thy broad cardinal's hat, If thou proceed in this thy insolence.
BOT: Nay, stand thou back; I will not budge a foot. This be Damascus, be thou cursed Cain, To slay thy brother Abel, if thou wilt.
USER: I will not slay thee, but I'll drive thee back. Thy scarlet robes, as a child's bearing-cloth, I'll use to carry thee out of this place.
BOT: Do what thou dar'st, I beard thee to thy face.
USER: Gloucester, thou wilt answer this before the Pope.
BOT: Fie, lords, that you, being supreme magistrates, Thus contumeliously should break the peace!
USER: Peace, Mayor! Thou know'st little of my wrongs. Here's Beaufort, that regards nor God nor king, Hath here distrain'd the Tower to his use.
BOT: I will not answer thee with words, but blows.
USER: Nought rests for me in this tumultuous strife But to make open proclamation. Come, officer, as loud as e'er thou canst, cry.
BOT: Cardinal, I'll be no breaker of the law; But we shall meet and break our minds at large.
USER: Gloucester, we will meet, to thy cost, be sure; Thy heart-blood I will have for this day's work.
BOT: I'll call for clubs, if you will not away. This Cardinal's more haughty than the devil.
USER: Mayor, farewell. Thou dost but what thou mayst.
BOT: Abominable Gloucester, guard thy head, For I intend to have it ere long.
USER: See the coast clear'd, and then we will depart. Good God, these nobles should such stomachs bear! I myself fight not once in forty year.

USER: Sirrah, thou know'st how Orleans is besieged, And how the English have the suburbs won.
BOT: Father, I know; and oft have shot at them, Howe'er unfortunate I miss'd my aim.
USER: Father, I warrant you; take you no care; I'll never trouble you if I may spy them. Enter, on the turrets, Salisbury and Talbot, Sir William Glansdale, Sir Thomas Gargrave and others.
BOT: Talbot, my life, my joy, again return'd! How wert thou handled, being prisoner? Or by what means got'st thou to be releas'd? Discourse, I prithee, on this turret's top.
USER: Yet tell'st thou not how thou wert entertain'd.
BOT: I think, at the north gate, for there stand lords.
USER: And I, here, at the bulwark of the bridge.
BOT: For aught I see, this city must be famish'd, Or with light skirmishes enfeebled. Here they shoot, and Salisbury and Gargrave fall down.
USER: O Lord, have mercy on us, wretched sinners!
BOT: O Lord, have mercy on me, woeful man!
USER: My lord, my lord, the French have gather'd head. The Dauphin, with one Joan la Pucelle join'd, A holy prophetess new risen up, Is come with a great power to raise the siege.

USER: Come, come, 'tis only I that must disgrace thee.
BOT: Heavens, can you suffer hell so to prevail? My breast I'll burst with straining of my courage, And from my shoulders crack my arms asunder, But I will chastise this high-minded strumpet.

USER: Sirs, take your places and be vigilant. If any noise or soldier you perceive Near to the walls, by some apparent sign Let us have knowledge at the court of guard.
BOT: Sergeant, you shall. Thus are poor servitors, When others sleep upon their quiet beds, Constrain'd to watch in darkness, rain, and cold.
