USER: Coward of France, how much he wrongs his fame, Despairing of his own arm's fortitude, To join with witches and the help of hell!
BOT: Traitors have never other company. But what's that Pucelle whom they term so pure?
USER: A maid, they say.
BOT: A maid! And be so martial!
USER: Pray God she prove not masculine ere long, If underneath the standard of the French She carry armour as she hath begun.
BOT: Well, let them practice and converse with spirits. God is our fortress, in whose conquering name Let us resolve to scale their flinty bulwarks.
USER: Ascend, brave Talbot; we will follow thee.
BOT: Not all together. Better far, I guess, That we do make our entrance several ways, That if it chance the one of us do fail, The other yet may rise against their force.
USER: Agreed. I'll to yond corner.
BOT: And I to this.
USER: And here will Talbot mount, or make his grave. Now, Salisbury, for thee, and for the right Of English Henry, shall this night appear How much in duty I am bound to both.
BOT: Unready! Ay, and glad we 'scap'd so well.
USER: 'Twas time, I trow, to wake and leave our beds, Hearing alarums at our chamber-doors. ALENÇON. Of all exploits since first I follow'd arms Ne'er heard I of a warlike enterprise More venturous or desperate than this.
BOT: I think this Talbot be a fiend of hell.
USER: If not of hell, the heavens, sure, favour him. ALENÇON. Here cometh Charles. I marvel how he sped.
BOT: Tut, holy Joan was his defensive guard.
USER: Is this thy cunning, thou deceitful dame? Didst thou at first, to flatter us withal, Make us partakers of a little gain, That now our loss might be ten times so much?
BOT: Mine was secure.
USER: And so was mine, my lord.
BOT: And for myself, most part of all this night, Within her quarter and mine own precinct I was employ'd in passing to and fro About relieving of the sentinels. Then how or which way should they first break in?
USER: I'll be so bold to take what they have left. The cry of "Talbot" serves me for a sword; For I have loaden me with many spoils, Using no other weapon but his name.

USER: The day begins to break, and night is fled, Whose pitchy mantle over-veil'd the earth. Here sound retreat and cease our hot pursuit.
BOT: 'Tis thought, Lord Talbot, when the fight began, Rous'd on the sudden from their drowsy beds, They did amongst the troops of armed men Leap o'er the walls for refuge in the field.
USER: All hail, my lords! Which of this princely train Call ye the warlike Talbot, for his acts So much applauded through the realm of France?
BOT: Here is the Talbot. Who would speak with him?
USER: Is it even so? Nay, then I see our wars Will turn unto a peaceful comic sport, When ladies crave to be encounter'd with. You may not, my lord, despise her gentle suit.
BOT: No, truly, it is more than manners will; And I have heard it said, unbidden guests Are often welcomest when they are gone.
USER: Well then, alone, since there's no remedy, I mean to prove this lady's courtesy. Come hither, Captain. [_Whispers_.] You perceive my mind?
BOT: I do, my lord, and mean accordingly.

USER: Porter, remember what I gave in charge; And when you have done so, bring the keys to me.
BOT: Madam, I will.
USER: Madam, according as your ladyship desired, By message craved, so is Lord Talbot come.
BOT: And he is welcome. What, is this the man?
USER: Madam, it is.
BOT: Madam, I have been bold to trouble you; But since your ladyship is not at leisure, I'll sort some other time to visit you.
USER: What means he now? Go ask him whither he goes.
BOT: Stay, my Lord Talbot, for my lady craves To know the cause of your abrupt departure.
USER: Marry, for that she's in a wrong belief, I go to certify her Talbot's here.
BOT: If thou be he, then art thou prisoner.
USER: Prisoner! To whom?
BOT: Ha, ha, ha!
USER: Laughest thou, wretch? Thy mirth shall turn to moan.
BOT: I laugh to see your ladyship so fond To think that you have aught but Talbot's shadow Whereon to practice your severity.
USER: Why, art not thou the man?
BOT: I am indeed.
USER: Then have I substance too.
BOT: This is a riddling merchant for the nonce; He will be here, and yet he is not here. How can these contrarieties agree?
USER: With all my heart, and think me honoured To feast so great a warrior in my house.

USER: Great lords and gentlemen, what means this silence? Dare no man answer in a case of truth?
BOT: Within the Temple Hall we were too loud; The garden here is more convenient.
USER: Then say at once if I maintain'd the truth; Or else was wrangling Somerset in th' error?
BOT: Faith, I have been a truant in the law And never yet could frame my will to it; And therefore frame the law unto my will.
USER: Judge you, my Lord of Warwick, then, between us.
BOT: Tut, tut, here is a mannerly forbearance! The truth appears so naked on my side That any purblind eye may find it out.
USER: And on my side it is so well apparell'd, So clear, so shining and so evident, That it will glimmer through a blind man's eye.
BOT: Let him that is no coward nor no flatterer, But dare maintain the party of the truth, Pluck a red rose from off this thorn with me.
USER: I love no colours, and without all colour Of base insinuating flattery I pluck this white rose with Plantagenet.
BOT: I pluck this red rose with young Somerset, And say withal I think he held the right.
USER: Stay, lords and gentlemen, and pluck no more Till you conclude that he upon whose side The fewest roses are cropp'd from the tree Shall yield the other in the right opinion.
BOT: Good Master Vernon, it is well objected: If I have fewest, I subscribe in silence.
USER: And I.
BOT: Then for the truth and plainness of the case, I pluck this pale and maiden blossom here, Giving my verdict on the white rose side.
USER: Prick not your finger as you pluck it off, Lest, bleeding, you do paint the white rose red, And fall on my side so against your will.
BOT: If I, my lord, for my opinion bleed, Opinion shall be surgeon to my hurt And keep me on the side where still I am.
USER: Well, well, come on, who else?
BOT: Unless my study and my books be false, The argument you held was wrong in law; In sign whereof I pluck a white rose too.
USER: Now, Somerset, where is your argument?
BOT: Here in my scabbard, meditating that Shall dye your white rose in a bloody red.
USER: Meantime your cheeks do counterfeit our roses; For pale they look with fear, as witnessing The truth on our side.
BOT: No, Plantagenet, 'Tis not for fear but anger that thy cheeks Blush for pure shame to counterfeit our roses, And yet thy tongue will not confess thy error.
USER: Hath not thy rose a canker, Somerset?
BOT: Hath not thy rose a thorn, Plantagenet?
USER: Ay, sharp and piercing, to maintain his truth; Whiles thy consuming canker eats his falsehood.
BOT: Well, I'll find friends to wear my bleeding roses, That shall maintain what I have said is true, Where false Plantagenet dare not be seen.
USER: Now, by this maiden blossom in my hand, I scorn thee and thy fashion, peevish boy.
BOT: Turn not thy scorns this way, Plantagenet.
USER: Proud Pole, I will, and scorn both him and thee.
BOT: I'll turn my part thereof into thy throat.
USER: Away, away, good William de la Pole! We grace the yeoman by conversing with him.
BOT: Now, by God's will, thou wrong'st him, Somerset; His grandfather was Lionel Duke of Clarence, Third son to the third Edward King of England. Spring crestless yeomen from so deep a root?
USER: He bears him on the place's privilege, Or durst not for his craven heart, say thus.
BOT: Ah, thou shalt find us ready for thee still; And know us by these colours for thy foes, For these my friends in spite of thee shall wear.
USER: And, by my soul, this pale and angry rose, As cognizance of my blood-drinking hate, Will I for ever and my faction wear, Until it wither with me to my grave, Or flourish to the height of my degree.
BOT: Go forward, and be chok'd with thy ambition! And so farewell until I meet thee next.
USER: Have with thee, Pole. Farewell, ambitious Richard.
BOT: How I am braved and must perforce endure it!
USER: Good Master Vernon, I am bound to you, That you on my behalf would pluck a flower.
BOT: In your behalf still will I wear the same.
USER: And so will I.
BOT: Thanks, gentlemen. Come, let us four to dinner. I dare say This quarrel will drink blood another day.

USER: Richard Plantagenet, my lord, will come. We sent unto the Temple, unto his chamber, And answer was return'd that he will come.
BOT: My lord, your loving nephew now is come.
USER: Richard Plantagenet, my friend, is he come?
BOT: Ay, noble uncle, thus ignobly used, Your nephew, late despised Richard, comes.
USER: That cause, fair nephew, that imprison'd me And hath detain'd me all my flowering youth Within a loathsome dungeon, there to pine, Was cursed instrument of his decease.
BOT: Discover more at large what cause that was, For I am ignorant and cannot guess.
USER: Of which, my lord, your honour is the last.
BOT: True; and thou seest that I no issue have, And that my fainting words do warrant death. Thou art my heir; the rest I wish thee gather. But yet be wary in thy studious care.
USER: Thy grave admonishments prevail with me. But yet methinks, my father's execution Was nothing less than bloody tyranny.
BOT: O uncle, would some part of my young years Might but redeem the passage of your age!

USER: As good! Thou bastard of my grandfather!
BOT: Ay, lordly sir; for what are you, I pray, But one imperious in another's throne?
USER: Am I not Protector, saucy priest?
BOT: And am not I a prelate of the church?
USER: Yes, as an outlaw in a castle keeps, And useth it to patronage his theft.
BOT: Unreverent Gloucester!
USER: Thou art reverend Touching thy spiritual function, not thy life.
BOT: Rome shall remedy this.
USER: Roam thither, then.
BOT: My lord, it were your duty to forbear.
USER: Ay, so the bishop be not overborne. Methinks my lord should be religious, And know the office that belongs to such.
BOT: Methinks his lordship should be humbler; It fitteth not a prelate so to plead.
USER: Yes, when his holy state is touch'd so near.
BOT: State holy or unhallow'd, what of that? Is not his Grace Protector to the King?
USER: Lest it be said, "Speak, sirrah, when you should; Must your bold verdict enter talk with lords?" Else would I have a fling at Winchester.
BOT: An uproar, I dare warrant, Begun through malice of the Bishop's men.
USER: We charge you, on allegiance to ourself, To hold your slaughtering hands and keep the peace. Pray, uncle Gloucester, mitigate this strife.
BOT: Nay, if we be forbidden stones, we'll fall to it with our teeth.
USER: Do what ye dare, we are as resolute.
BOT: You of my household, leave this peevish broil, And set this unaccustom'd fight aside.
USER: Ay, and the very parings of our nails Shall pitch a field when we are dead.
BOT: Stay, stay, I say! And if you love me, as you say you do, Let me persuade you to forbear awhile.
USER: He shall submit, or I will never yield.
BOT: Compassion on the King commands me stoop, Or I would see his heart out, ere the priest Should ever get that privilege of me.
USER: Behold, my Lord of Winchester, the Duke Hath banish'd moody discontented fury, As by his smoothed brows it doth appear. Why look you still so stern and tragical?
BOT: Here, Winchester, I offer thee my hand.
USER: Fie, uncle Beaufort! I have heard you preach That malice was a great and grievous sin; And will not you maintain the thing you teach, But prove a chief offender in the same?
BOT: Sweet King! The bishop hath a kindly gird. For shame, my Lord of Winchester, relent! What, shall a child instruct you what to do?
USER: Well, Duke of Gloucester, I will yield to thee; Love for thy love and hand for hand I give.
BOT: See here, my friends and loving countrymen, This token serveth for a flag of truce Betwixt ourselves and all our followers, So help me God, as I dissemble not!
USER: O loving uncle, kind Duke of Gloucester, How joyful am I made by this contract! Away, my masters, trouble us no more, But join in friendship, as your lords have done.
BOT: Content. I'll to the surgeon's.
USER: And so will I.
BOT: And I will see what physic the tavern affords.
USER: Accept this scroll, most gracious sovereign, Which in the right of Richard Plantagenet We do exhibit to your Majesty.
BOT: Well urged, my Lord of Warwick. For, sweet prince, An if your Grace mark every circumstance, You have great reason to do Richard right, Especially for those occasions At Eltham Place I told your Majesty.
USER: And those occasions, uncle, were of force; Therefore, my loving lords, our pleasure is That Richard be restored to his blood.
BOT: Let Richard be restored to his blood; So shall his father's wrongs be recompensed.
USER: As will the rest, so willeth Winchester.
BOT: If Richard will be true, not that alone But all the whole inheritance I give That doth belong unto the house of York, From whence you spring by lineal descent.
USER: Thy humble servant vows obedience And humble service till the point of death.
BOT: Stoop then and set your knee against my foot; And in reguerdon of that duty done I girt thee with the valiant sword of York. Rise, Richard, like a true Plantagenet, And rise created princely Duke of York.
USER: And so thrive Richard as thy foes may fall! And as my duty springs, so perish they That grudge one thought against your Majesty!
BOT: Welcome, high prince, the mighty Duke of York!
USER: Now will it best avail your Majesty To cross the seas and to be crown'd in France. The presence of a king engenders love Amongst his subjects and his loyal friends, As it disanimates his enemies.
BOT: When Gloucester says the word, King Henry goes; For friendly counsel cuts off many foes.
USER: Your ships already are in readiness.

USER: Our sacks shall be a mean to sack the city, And we be lords and rulers over Rouen; Therefore we'll knock. [_Knocks._]
BOT: _Paysans, la pauvres gens de France:_ Poor market folks that come to sell their corn.
USER: Enter, go in; the market bell is rung.
BOT: Now, Rouen, I'll shake thy bulwarks to the ground.
USER: Saint Denis bless this happy stratagem, And once again we'll sleep secure in Rouen.
BOT: Here enter'd Pucelle and her practisants; Now she is there, how will she specify Here is the best and safest passage in?
USER: By thrusting out a torch from yonder tower, Which, once discern'd, shows that her meaning is: No way to that, for weakness, which she enter'd.
BOT: Behold, this is the happy wedding torch That joineth Rouen unto her countrymen, But burning fatal to the Talbonites.
USER: See, noble Charles, the beacon of our friend; The burning torch, in yonder turret stands.
BOT: Now shine it like a comet of revenge, A prophet to the fall of all our foes!
USER: Defer no time, delays have dangerous ends; And then do execution on the watch. An alarum. Enter Talbot in an excursion.
BOT: Good morrow, gallants! Want ye corn for bread? I think the Duke of Burgundy will fast Before he'll buy again at such a rate. 'Twas full of darnel. Do you like the taste?
USER: Scoff on, vile fiend and shameless courtezan! I trust ere long to choke thee with thine own, And make thee curse the harvest of that corn.
BOT: Your Grace may starve, perhaps, before that time.
USER: O, let no words, but deeds, revenge this treason!
BOT: What will you do, good graybeard? Break a lance And run a tilt at Death within a chair?
USER: Are ye so hot? Yet, Pucelle, hold thy peace; If Talbot do but thunder, rain will follow. God speed the Parliament! Who shall be the Speaker?
BOT: Dare ye come forth and meet us in the field?
USER: Belike your lordship takes us then for fools, To try if that our own be ours or no.
BOT: I speak not to that railing Hecate, But unto thee, Alençon, and the rest; Will ye, like soldiers, come and fight it out? ALENÇON. Seignieur, no.
USER: Seignieur, hang! Base muleteers of France! Like peasant foot-boys do they keep the walls, And dare not take up arms like gentlemen.
BOT: Away, captains! Let's get us from the walls, For Talbot means no goodness by his looks. Goodbye, my lord; we came but to tell you That we are here.
USER: My vows are equal partners with thy vows.
BOT: But, ere we go, regard this dying prince, The valiant Duke of Bedford. Come, my lord, We will bestow you in some better place, Fitter for sickness and for crazy age.
USER: Lord Talbot, do not so dishonour me. Here will I sit before the walls of Rouen, And will be partner of your weal or woe.
BOT: Courageous Bedford, let us now persuade you.
USER: Not to be gone from hence; for once I read That stout Pendragon in his litter sick Came to the field and vanquished his foes. Methinks I should revive the soldiers' hearts, Because I ever found them as myself.
BOT: Whither away, Sir John Fastolf, in such haste?
USER: Whither away? To save myself by flight. We are like to have the overthrow again.
BOT: What! Will you fly, and leave Lord Talbot?
USER: Ay, All the Talbots in the world, to save my life.
BOT: Cowardly knight, ill fortune follow thee! Retreat. Excursions. La Pucelle, Alençon and Charles fly.
USER: Lost, and recover'd in a day again! This is a double honour, Burgundy. Yet heavens have glory for this victory!
BOT: Warlike and martial Talbot, Burgundy Enshrines thee in his heart, and there erects Thy noble deeds as valour's monuments.
USER: What wills Lord Talbot pleaseth Burgundy.

USER: We have been guided by thee hitherto, And of thy cunning had no diffidence. One sudden foil shall never breed distrust
BOT: Then thus it must be; this doth Joan devise: By fair persuasions mix'd with sugar'd words We will entice the Duke of Burgundy To leave the Talbot and to follow us.
USER: A parley with the Duke of Burgundy!
BOT: Who craves a parley with the Burgundy?
USER: The princely Charles of France, thy countryman.
BOT: What say'st thou, Charles? for I am marching hence.
USER: Speak, Pucelle, and enchant him with thy words.
BOT: Brave Burgundy, undoubted hope of France, Stay, let thy humble handmaid speak to thee.
USER: Speak on, but be not over-tedious.
BOT: Either she hath bewitch'd me with her words, Or nature makes me suddenly relent.
USER: Welcome, brave Duke! Thy friendship makes us fresh.
BOT: And doth beget new courage in our breasts. ALENÇON. Pucelle hath bravely play'd her part in this, And doth deserve a coronet of gold.
USER: Now let us on, my lords, and join our powers, And seek how we may prejudice the foe.

USER: Is this the Lord Talbot, uncle Gloucester, That hath so long been resident in France?
BOT: Yes, if it please your Majesty, my liege.
USER: Now, sir, to you that were so hot at sea, Disgracing of these colours that I wear In honour of my noble Lord of York, Dar'st thou maintain the former words thou spak'st?
BOT: Yes, sir, as well as you dare patronage The envious barking of your saucy tongue Against my lord the Duke of Somerset.
USER: Sirrah, thy lord I honour as he is.
BOT: Why, what is he? As good a man as York.
USER: Hark ye; not so: in witness, take ye that.
BOT: Well, miscreant, I'll be there as soon as you; And, after, meet you sooner than you would.

USER: Lord bishop, set the crown upon his head.
BOT: God save King Henry, of that name the Sixth!
USER: My gracious sovereign, as I rode from Calais, To haste unto your coronation, A letter was deliver'd to my hands, Writ to your Grace from th' Duke of Burgundy.
BOT: To say the truth, this fact was infamous And ill beseeming any common man, Much more a knight, a captain, and a leader.
USER: What! Doth my uncle Burgundy revolt?
BOT: He doth, my lord, and is become your foe.
USER: Is that the worst this letter doth contain?
BOT: It is the worst, and all, my lord, he writes.
USER: Why, then, Lord Talbot there shall talk with him And give him chastisement for this abuse. How say you, my lord, are you not content?
BOT: Content, my liege! Yes, but that I am prevented, I should have begg'd I might have been employ'd.
USER: Then gather strength and march unto him straight; Let him perceive how ill we brook his treason, And what offence it is to flout his friends.
BOT: I go, my lord, in heart desiring still You may behold confusion of your foes.
USER: Grant me the combat, gracious sovereign.
BOT: And me, my lord, grant me the combat too.
USER: This is my servant; hear him, noble prince.
BOT: And this is mine, sweet Henry, favour him.
USER: Be patient, lords, and give them leave to speak. Say, gentlemen, what makes you thus exclaim, And wherefore crave you combat, or with whom?
BOT: With him, my lord, for he hath done me wrong.
USER: And I with him, for he hath done me wrong.
BOT: What is that wrong whereof you both complain? First let me know, and then I'll answer you.
USER: Will not this malice, Somerset, be left?
BOT: Your private grudge, my Lord of York, will out, Though ne'er so cunningly you smother it.
USER: Good Lord, what madness rules in brainsick men, When for so slight and frivolous a cause Such factious emulations shall arise! Good cousins both, of York and Somerset, Quiet yourselves, I pray, and be at peace.
BOT: Let this dissension first be tried by fight, And then your Highness shall command a peace.
USER: The quarrel toucheth none but us alone; Betwixt ourselves let us decide it then.
BOT: There is my pledge; accept it, Somerset.
USER: Nay, let it rest where it began at first.
BOT: Confirm it so, mine honourable lord.
USER: It grieves his Highness. Good my lords, be friends.
BOT: My Lord of York, I promise you the King Prettily, methought, did play the orator.
USER: And so he did; but yet I like it not, In that he wears the badge of Somerset.
BOT: Tush, that was but his fancy; blame him not; I dare presume, sweet prince, he thought no harm.
USER: An if I wist he did - but let it rest; Other affairs must now be managed.

USER: Are not the speedy scouts return'd again That dogg'd the mighty army of the Dauphin?
BOT: O, send some succour to the distress'd lord!
USER: He dies, we lose; I break my warlike word; We mourn, France smiles; we lose, they daily get, All long of this vile traitor Somerset.

USER: Here is Sir William Lucy, who with me Set from our o'er-match'd forces forth for aid.
BOT: How now, Sir William, whither were you sent?
USER: York set him on; York should have sent him aid.
BOT: And York as fast upon your Grace exclaims, Swearing that you withhold his levied host Collected for this expedition.
USER: York lies; he might have sent and had the horse. I owe him little duty, and less love, And take foul scorn to fawn on him by sending.
BOT: The fraud of England, not the force of France, Hath now entrapp'd the noble-minded Talbot. Never to England shall he bear his life, But dies betray'd to fortune by your strife.
USER: Come, go; I will dispatch the horsemen straight. Within six hours they will be at his aid.
BOT: Too late comes rescue; he is ta'en or slain, For fly he could not if he would have fled; And fly would Talbot never, though he might.
USER: If he be dead, brave Talbot, then adieu!
BOT: His fame lives in the world, his shame in you.

USER: Fly, to revenge my death if I be slain.
BOT: He that flies so will ne'er return again.
USER: If we both stay, we both are sure to die.
BOT: Shall all thy mother's hopes lie in one tomb?
USER: Ay, rather than I'll shame my mother's womb.
BOT: Upon my blessing, I command thee go.
USER: To fight I will, but not to fly the foe.
BOT: Part of thy father may be saved in thee.
USER: No part of him but will be shame in me.
BOT: Thou never hadst renown, nor canst not lose it.
USER: Yes, your renowned name; shall flight abuse it?
BOT: Thy father's charge shall clear thee from that stain.
USER: You cannot witness for me, being slain. If death be so apparent, then both fly.
BOT: And leave my followers here to fight and die? My age was never tainted with such shame.
USER: And shall my youth be guilty of such blame? No more can I be sever'd from your side Than can yourself yourself in twain divide. Stay, go, do what you will, the like do I; For live I will not, if my father die.
BOT: Then here I take my leave of thee, fair son, Born to eclipse thy life this afternoon. Come, side by side together live and die, And soul with soul from France to heaven fly.

USER: O, twice my father, twice am I thy son! The life thou gav'st me first was lost and done, Till with thy warlike sword, despite of fate, To my determined time thou gav'st new date.
BOT: Then follow thou thy desperate sire of Crete, Thou Icarus; thy life to me is sweet. If thou wilt fight, fight by thy father's side, And, commendable proved, let's die in pride.

USER: O my dear lord, lo where your son is borne!
BOT: Had York and Somerset brought rescue in, We should have found a bloody day of this.
USER: How the young whelp of Talbot's, raging-wood, Did flesh his puny sword in Frenchmen's blood!
BOT: Doubtless he would have made a noble knight. See, where he lies inhearsed in the arms Of the most bloody nurser of his harms.
USER: Hew them to pieces, hack their bones asunder, Whose life was England's glory, Gallia's wonder.
BOT: O, no, forbear! For that which we have fled During the life, let us not wrong it dead.
USER: Herald, conduct me to the Dauphin's tent, To know who hath obtain'd the glory of the day.
BOT: On what submissive message art thou sent?
USER: Submission, Dauphin! 'Tis a mere French word. We English warriors wot not what it means. I come to know what prisoners thou hast ta'en, And to survey the bodies of the dead.
BOT: For prisoners ask'st thou? Hell our prison is. But tell me whom thou seek'st.
USER: Here's a silly stately style indeed! The Turk, that two and fifty kingdoms hath, Writes not so tedious a style as this. Him that thou magnifiest with all these titles Stinking and fly-blown lies here at our feet.
BOT: I think this upstart is old Talbot's ghost, He speaks with such a proud commanding spirit. For God's sake, let him have them; to keep them here, They would but stink and putrify the air.
USER: Go, take their bodies hence.
BOT: I'll bear them hence; But from their ashes shall be rear'd A phoenix that shall make all France afeard.
USER: So we be rid of them, do with them what thou wilt. And now to Paris in this conquering vein. All will be ours, now bloody Talbot's slain.

USER: Have you perused the letters from the Pope, The Emperor, and the Earl of Armagnac?
BOT: I have, my lord; and their intent is this: They humbly sue unto your Excellence To have a godly peace concluded of Between the realms of England and of France.
USER: How doth your Grace affect their motion?
BOT: Well, my good lord, and as the only means To stop effusion of our Christian blood And stablish quietness on every side.
USER: Ay, marry, uncle, for I always thought It was both impious and unnatural That such immanity and bloody strife Should reign among professors of one faith.
BOT: And for the proffer of my lord your master, I have inform'd his Highness so at large, As liking of the lady's virtuous gifts, Her beauty and the value of her dower, He doth intend she shall be England's Queen.
USER: In argument and proof of which contract, Bear her this jewel, pledge of my affection. And so, my Lord Protector, see them guarded And safely brought to Dover; where inshipp'd, Commit them to the fortune of the sea.
BOT: Stay my lord legate; you shall first receive The sum of money which I promised Should be deliver'd to his Holiness For clothing me in these grave ornaments.
USER: I will attend upon your lordship's leisure.

USER: Peace be amongst them, if they turn to us; Else ruin combat with their palaces!
BOT: Success unto our valiant general, And happiness to his accomplices!
USER: What tidings send our scouts? I prithee, speak.
BOT: The English army, that divided was Into two parties, is now conjoin'd in one, And means to give you battle presently.
USER: Somewhat too sudden, sirs, the warning is; But we will presently provide for them.
BOT: I trust the ghost of Talbot is not there. Now he is gone, my lord, you need not fear.
USER: Of all base passions, fear is most accursed. Command the conquest, Charles, it shall be thine; Let Henry fret and all the world repine.
BOT: Then on, my lords; and France be fortunate!

USER: Chang'd to a worser shape thou canst not be.
BOT: O, Charles the Dauphin is a proper man; No shape but his can please your dainty eye.
USER: A plaguing mischief light on Charles and thee! And may ye both be suddenly surprised By bloody hands, in sleeping on your beds!
BOT: Fell banning hag, enchantress, hold thy tongue!
USER: I prithee, give me leave to curse awhile.
BOT: Curse, miscreant, when thou com'st to the stake. Alarum. Enter Suffolk with Margaret in his hand.
USER: Margaret my name, and daughter to a king, The King of Naples, whosoe'er thou art.
BOT: Say, Earl of Suffolk, if thy name be so, What ransom must I pay before I pass? For I perceive I am thy prisoner.
USER: How canst thou tell she will deny thy suit, Before thou make a trial of her love?
BOT: Why speak'st thou not? What ransom must I pay?
USER: She's beautiful, and therefore to be woo'd; She is a woman, therefore to be won.
BOT: Wilt thou accept of ransom, yea, or no?
USER: Fond man, remember that thou hast a wife; Then how can Margaret be thy paramour?
BOT: I were best leave him, for he will not hear.
USER: There all is marr'd; there lies a cooling card.
BOT: He talks at random; sure, the man is mad.
USER: And yet a dispensation may be had.
BOT: And yet I would that you would answer me.
USER: I'll win this Lady Margaret. For whom? Why, for my king. Tush, that's a wooden thing!
BOT: He talks of wood. It is some carpenter.
USER: Hear ye, captain, are you not at leisure?
BOT: It shall be so, disdain they ne'er so much. Henry is youthful and will quickly yield. Madam, I have a secret to reveal.
USER: What though I be enthrall'd? He seems a knight, And will not any way dishonour me.
BOT: Lady, vouchsafe to listen what I say.
USER: Perhaps I shall be rescued by the French; And then I need not crave his courtesy.
BOT: Sweet madam, give me hearing in a cause.
USER: Tush, women have been captivate ere now.
BOT: Lady, wherefore talk you so?
USER: I cry you mercy, 'tis but _quid_ for _quo_.
BOT: Say, gentle princess, would you not suppose Your bondage happy, to be made a queen?
USER: To be a queen in bondage is more vile Than is a slave in base servility; For princes should be free.
BOT: And so shall you, If happy England's royal king be free.
USER: Why, what concerns his freedom unto me?
BOT: I'll undertake to make thee Henry's queen, To put a golden scepter in thy hand And set a precious crown upon thy head, If thou wilt condescend to be my -
USER: What?
BOT: His love.
USER: I am unworthy to be Henry's wife.
BOT: No, gentle madam, I unworthy am To woo so fair a dame to be his wife, And have no portion in the choice myself. How say you, madam, are ye so content?
USER: An if my father please, I am content.
BOT: Then call our captains and our colours forth. And, madam, at your father's castle walls We'll crave a parley, to confer with him. A parley sounded. Enter Reignier on the walls. See, Reignier, see, thy daughter prisoner!
USER: To whom?
BOT: To me.
USER: Suffolk, what remedy? I am a soldier, and unapt to weep Or to exclaim on fortune's fickleness.
BOT: Speaks Suffolk as he thinks?
USER: Fair Margaret knows That Suffolk doth not flatter, face, or feign.
BOT: Upon thy princely warrant, I descend To give thee answer of thy just demand.
USER: And here I will expect thy coming. Trumpets sound. Enter Reignier, below.
BOT: Welcome, brave earl, into our territories. Command in Anjou what your honour pleases.
USER: Thanks, Reignier, happy for so sweet a child, Fit to be made companion with a king. What answer makes your Grace unto my suit?
BOT: That is her ransom; I deliver her; And those two counties I will undertake Your Grace shall well and quietly enjoy.
USER: And I again, in Henry's royal name, As deputy unto that gracious king, Give thee her hand for sign of plighted faith.
BOT: I do embrace thee as I would embrace The Christian prince, King Henry, were he here.
USER: Farewell, my lord; good wishes, praise, and prayers Shall Suffolk ever have of Margaret. [_Going_].
BOT: Farewell, sweet madam; but hark you, Margaret, No princely commendations to my king?
USER: Such commendations as becomes a maid, A virgin and his servant, say to him.
BOT: Words sweetly placed and modestly directed. But, madam, I must trouble you again: No loving token to his Majesty?
USER: Yes, my good lord; a pure unspotted heart, Never yet taint with love, I send the King.
BOT: And this withal. [_Kisses her_.]
USER: That for thyself. I will not so presume To send such peevish tokens to a king.

USER: Bring forth that sorceress condemn'd to burn.
BOT: Decrepit miser, base ignoble wretch! I am descended of a gentler blood. Thou art no father nor no friend of mine.
USER: Out, out! My lords, as please you, 'tis not so; I did beget her, all the parish knows. Her mother liveth yet, can testify She was the first fruit of my bachelorship.
BOT: Graceless, wilt thou deny thy parentage?
USER: This argues what her kind of life hath been, Wicked and vile; and so her death concludes.
BOT: Fie, Joan, that thou wilt be so obstacle! God knows thou art a collop of my flesh; And for thy sake have I shed many a tear. Deny me not, I prithee, gentle Joan.
USER: Peasant, avaunt! You have suborn'd this man Of purpose to obscure my noble birth.
BOT: Take her away, for she hath lived too long, To fill the world with vicious qualities.
USER: Ay, ay; away with her to execution!
BOT: And hark ye, sirs; because she is a maid, Spare for no faggots, let there be enow. Place barrels of pitch upon the fatal stake, That so her torture may be shortened.
USER: Now heaven forfend! The holy maid with child?
BOT: The greatest miracle that e'er ye wrought! Is all your strict preciseness come to this?
USER: She and the Dauphin have been juggling. I did imagine what would be her refuge.
BOT: Well, go to; we'll have no bastards live, Especially since Charles must father it.
USER: You are deceived; my child is none of his. It was Alençon that enjoy'd my love.
BOT: Alençon, that notorious Machiavel! It dies and if it had a thousand lives.
USER: O, give me leave, I have deluded you. 'Twas neither Charles nor yet the Duke I named, But Reignier, King of Naples, that prevail'd.
BOT: A married man! That's most intolerable.
USER: Why, here's a girl! I think she knows not well - There were so many - whom she may accuse.
BOT: It's sign she hath been liberal and free.
USER: And yet, forsooth, she is a virgin pure! Strumpet, thy words condemn thy brat and thee. Use no entreaty, for it is in vain.
BOT: Break thou in pieces and consume to ashes, Thou foul accursed minister of hell!
USER: Be patient, York; if we conclude a peace, It shall be with such strict and severe covenants As little shall the Frenchmen gain thereby.
BOT: Since, lords of England, it is thus agreed That peaceful truce shall be proclaim'd in France, We come to be informed by yourselves What the conditions of that league must be.
USER: Speak, Winchester, for boiling choler chokes The hollow passage of my poison'd voice By sight of these our baleful enemies.
BOT: How say'st thou, Charles? Shall our condition stand?
USER: It shall; only reserv'd you claim no interest In any of our towns of garrison.

USER: And otherwise will Henry ne'er presume. Therefore, my Lord Protector, give consent That Margaret may be England's royal queen.
BOT: So should I give consent to flatter sin. You know, my lord, your Highness is betroth'd Unto another lady of esteem. How shall we then dispense with that contract, And not deface your honour with reproach?
USER: Why, what, I pray, is Margaret more than that? Her father is no better than an earl, Although in glorious titles he excel.
BOT: Yes, my lord, her father is a king, The King of Naples and Jerusalem; And of such great authority in France As his alliance will confirm our peace, And keep the Frenchmen in allegiance.
USER: And so the Earl of Armagnac may do, Because he is near kinsman unto Charles.
BOT: Beside, his wealth doth warrant a liberal dower, Where Reignier sooner will receive than give.
USER: Ay, grief, I fear me, both at first and last.

USER: We thank you all.
BOT: My Lord Protector, so it please your grace, Here are the articles of contracted peace Between our sovereign and the French king Charles, For eighteen months concluded by consent.
USER: Uncle, how now?
BOT: Pardon me, gracious lord. Some sudden qualm hath struck me at the heart And dimmed mine eyes, that I can read no further.
USER: Uncle of Winchester, I pray read on.
BOT: of Anjou and Maine shall be released and delivered to the King her father, and she sent over of the King of England's own proper cost and charges, without having any dowry._
USER: Nephew, what means this passionate discourse, This peroration with such circumstance? For France, 'tis ours; and we will keep it still.
BOT: Now, by the death of Him that died for all, These counties were the keys of Normandy! But wherefore weeps Warwick, my valiant son?
USER: A proper jest, and never heard before, That Suffolk should demand a whole fifteenth For costs and charges in transporting her! She should have staid in France, and starved in France, Before -
BOT: My Lord of Gloucester, now ye grow too hot. It was the pleasure of my lord the King.
USER: Why should he, then, protect our sovereign, He being of age to govern of himself? Cousin of Somerset, join you with me, And all together, with the Duke of Suffolk, We'll quickly hoist Duke Humphrey from his seat.
BOT: This weighty business will not brook delay; I'll to the Duke of Suffolk presently.
USER: Or thou or I, Somerset, will be Protector, Despite Duke Humphrey or the Cardinal.
BOT: So God help Warwick, as he loves the land And common profit of his country!
USER: And so says York, [_Aside_.] for he hath greatest cause.
BOT: Then let's make haste away and look unto the main.

USER: What dreamed my lord? Tell me, and I'll requite it With sweet rehearsal of my morning's dream.
BOT: What, what, my lord! Are you so choleric With Eleanor for telling but her dream? Next time I'll keep my dreams unto myself, And not be checked.
USER: Nay, be not angry, I am pleased again.
BOT: My Lord Protector, 'tis his highness' pleasure You do prepare to ride unto Saint Albans, Whereas the King and Queen do mean to hawk.
USER: I go. Come, Nell, thou wilt ride with us?
BOT: Jesus preserve your royal majesty!
USER: What sayst thou? Majesty! I am but grace.
BOT: But, by the grace of God, and Hume's advice, Your grace's title shall be multiplied.
USER: What sayst thou, man? Hast thou as yet conferred With Margery Jourdain, the cunning witch, With Roger Bolingbroke, the conjurer? And will they undertake to do me good?
BOT: This they have promised, to show your highness A spirit raised from depth of underground, That shall make answer to such questions As by your Grace shall be propounded him.

USER: How now, fellow; wouldst anything with me? 1 PETITIONER. I pray, my lord, pardon me, I took ye for my Lord Protector.
BOT: lordship? Let me see them. What is thine? 1 PETITIONER. Mine is, an 't please your grace, against John Goodman, my Lord Cardinal's man, for keeping my house and lands, and wife and all, from me.
USER: Thy wife too! That's some wrong, indeed. - What's yours? - What's here! Melford._ How now, sir knave! 2 PETITIONER. Alas, sir, I am but a poor petitioner of our whole township.
BOT: that the Duke of York was rightful heir to the crown.
USER: What sayst thou? Did the Duke of York say he was rightful heir to the crown?
BOT: That my master was? No, forsooth, my master said that he was, and that the King was an usurper.
USER: Who is there? Take this fellow in, and send for his master with a pursuivant presently. - We'll hear more of your matter before the King.
BOT: And as for you, that love to be protected Under the wings of our Protector's grace, Begin your suits anew, and sue to him. Away, base cullions! - Suffolk, let them go.
USER: Come, let's be gone.
BOT: Madam, be patient. As I was cause Your highness came to England, so will I In England work your grace's full content.
USER: Beside the haughty Protector, have we Beaufort The imperious churchman, Somerset, Buckingham, And grumbling York; and not the least of these But can do more in England than the King.
BOT: And he of these that can do most of all Cannot do more in England than the Nevilles; Salisbury and Warwick are no simple peers.
USER: For my part, noble lords, I care not which; Or Somerset or York, all's one to me.
BOT: If York have ill demeaned himself in France, Then let him be denied the regentship.
USER: If Somerset be unworthy of the place, Let York be regent; I will yield to him.
BOT: Whether your Grace be worthy, yea or no, Dispute not that; York is the worthier.
USER: Ambitious Warwick, let thy betters speak.
BOT: The Cardinal's not my better in the field.
USER: All in this presence are thy betters, Warwick.
BOT: Warwick may live to be the best of all.
USER: Peace, son! - And show some reason, Buckingham, Why Somerset should be preferred in this.
BOT: Because the King, forsooth, will have it so.
USER: Madam, the King is old enough himself To give his censure. These are no women's matters.
BOT: If he be old enough, what needs your grace To be Protector of his excellence?
USER: Madam, I am Protector of the realm, And at his pleasure will resign my place.
BOT: The commons hast thou racked; the clergy's bags Are lank and lean with thy extortions.
USER: Thy sumptuous buildings and thy wife's attire Have cost a mass of public treasury.
BOT: Thy cruelty in execution Upon offenders hath exceeded law, And left thee to the mercy of the law.
USER: Thy sale of offices and towns in France, If they were known, as the suspect is great, Would make thee quickly hop without thy head. Give me my fan. What minion! Can ye not? I cry your mercy, madam; was it you?
BOT: Was't I! Yea, I it was, proud Frenchwoman. Could I come near your beauty with my nails, I'd set my ten commandments in your face.
USER: Sweet aunt, be quiet; 'twas against her will.
BOT: Against her will! Good King, look to 't in time; She'll hamper thee and dandle thee like a baby. Though in this place most master wear no breeches, She shall not strike Dame Eleanor unrevenged.
USER: Lord Cardinal, I will follow Eleanor, And listen after Humphrey, how he proceeds. She's tickled now; her fume needs no spurs, She'll gallop far enough to her destruction.
BOT: Before we make election, give me leave To show some reason, of no little force, That York is most unmeet of any man.
USER: That can I witness, and a fouler fact Did never traitor in the land commit.
BOT: Peace, headstrong Warwick!
USER: Image of pride, why should I hold my peace?
BOT: Because here is a man accused of treason. Pray God the Duke of York excuse himself!
USER: Doth anyone accuse York for a traitor?
BOT: What mean'st thou, Suffolk? Tell me, what are these?
USER: Please it your majesty, this is the man That doth accuse his master of high treason. His words were these: that Richard, Duke of York Was rightful heir unto the English crown, And that your majesty was an usurper.
BOT: Say, man, were these thy words?
USER: An 't shall please your majesty, I never said nor thought any such matter. God is my witness, I am falsely accused by the villain.
BOT: By these ten bones, my lords, he did speak them to me in the garret one night as we were scouring my Lord of York's armour.
USER: Base dunghill villain and mechanical, I'll have thy head for this thy traitor's speech! - I do beseech your royal majesty, Let him have all the rigour of the law.
BOT: Uncle, what shall we say to this in law?
USER: I humbly thank your royal Majesty.
BOT: And I accept the combat willingly.
USER: Alas, my lord, I cannot fight; for God's sake, pity my case! The spite of man prevaileth against me. O Lord, have mercy upon me! I shall never be able to fight a blow. O Lord, my heart!
BOT: Sirrah, or you must fight or else be hanged.
USER: Away with them to prison; and the day Of combat shall be the last of the next month. Come, Somerset, we'll see thee sent away.

USER: Come, my masters. The duchess, I tell you, expects performance of your promises.
BOT: Master Hume, we are therefore provided. Will her ladyship behold and hear our exorcisms?
USER: Ay, what else? Fear you not her courage.
BOT: Well said, my masters; and welcome all. To this gear, the sooner the better.
USER: _Adsum_.
BOT: Asnath, By the eternal God, whose name and power Thou tremblest at, answer that I shall ask; For till thou speak thou shalt not pass from hence.
USER: Ask what thou wilt. That I had said and done!
BOT: The duke yet lives that Henry shall depose, But him outlive and die a violent death.
USER: By water shall he die and take his end.
BOT: Let him shun castles. Safer shall he be upon the sandy plains Than where castles mounted stand. Have done, for more I hardly can endure.
USER: Descend to darkness and the burning lake! False fiend, avoid! Sir Humphrey Stafford, and break in.
BOT: Not half so bad as thine to England's king, Injurious duke, that threatest where's no cause.
USER: Your Grace shall give me leave, my Lord of York, To be the post, in hope of his reward.
BOT: At your pleasure, my good lord. Who's within there, ho! Invite my Lords of Salisbury and Warwick To sup with me tomorrow night. Away!

USER: Believe me, lords, for flying at the brook I saw not better sport these seven years' day; Yet, by your leave, the wind was very high, And, ten to one, old Joan had not gone out.
BOT: But what a point, my lord, your falcon made, And what a pitch she flew above the rest! To see how God in all His creatures works! Yea, man and birds are fain of climbing high.
USER: No marvel, an it like your majesty, My Lord Protector's hawks do tower so well; They know their master loves to be aloft, And bears his thoughts above his falcon's pitch.
BOT: My lord, 'tis but a base ignoble mind That mounts no higher than a bird can soar.
USER: I thought as much. He would be above the clouds.
BOT: Ay, my Lord Cardinal, how think you by that? Were it not good your grace could fly to heaven?
USER: The treasury of everlasting joy.
BOT: Thy heaven is on earth; thine eyes and thoughts Beat on a crown, the treasure of thy heart, Pernicious Protector, dangerous peer, That smooth'st it so with king and commonweal!
USER: What, cardinal, is your priesthood grown peremptory? _Tantaene animis coelestibus irae?_ Churchmen so hot? Good uncle, hide such malice. With such holiness can you do it?
BOT: No malice, sir; no more than well becomes So good a quarrel and so bad a peer.
USER: As who, my lord?
BOT: Why, as you, my lord, An 't like your lordly Lord Protectorship.
USER: Why, Suffolk, England knows thine insolence.
BOT: And thy ambition, Gloucester.
USER: I prithee, peace, good queen, And whet not on these furious peers; For blessed are the peacemakers on earth.
BOT: Let me be blessed for the peace I make Against this proud Protector, with my sword!
USER: In thine own person answer thy abuse.
BOT: dar'st, This evening, on the east side of the grove.
USER: How now, my lords?
BOT: Believe me, cousin Gloucester, Had not your man put up the fowl so suddenly, We had had more sport. - [_Aside to Gloucester_.] Come with thy two-hand sword.
USER: True, uncle.
BOT: Why, how now, uncle Gloucester?
USER: Talking of hawking; nothing else, my lord. I'll shave your crown for this, Or all my fence shall fail.
BOT: Protector, see to 't well, protect yourself.
USER: The winds grow high; so do your stomachs, lords. How irksome is this music to my heart! When such strings jar, what hope of harmony? I pray, my lords, let me compound this strife.
BOT: What means this noise? Fellow, what miracle dost thou proclaim?
USER: A miracle! A miracle!
BOT: Come to the King, and tell him what miracle.
USER: Forsooth, a blind man at Saint Alban's shrine, Within this half hour, hath received his sight, A man that ne'er saw in his life before.
BOT: Now, God be praised, that to believing souls Gives light in darkness, comfort in despair! between two in a chair, Simpcox's Wife following.
USER: Here comes the townsmen on procession, To present your highness with the man.
BOT: Great is his comfort in this earthly vale, Although by his sight his sin be multiplied.
USER: Stand by, my masters. Bring him near the King. His highness' pleasure is to talk with him.
BOT: Good fellow, tell us here the circumstance, That we for thee may glorify the Lord. What, hast thou been long blind and now restored?
USER: Born blind, an 't please your grace.
BOT: Ay, indeed, was he.
USER: What woman is this?
BOT: His wife, an 't like your worship.
USER: Hadst thou been his mother, thou couldst have better told.
BOT: Where wert thou born?
USER: At Berwick in the north, an 't like your grace.
BOT: Poor soul, God's goodness hath been great to thee. Let never day nor night unhallowed pass, But still remember what the Lord hath done.
USER: Tell me, good fellow, cam'st thou here by chance, Or of devotion, to this holy shrine?
BOT: God knows, of pure devotion; being called A hundred times and oftener, in my sleep, By good Saint Alban, who said "Simpcox, come, Come, offer at my shrine, and I will help thee."
USER: Most true, forsooth; and many time and oft Myself have heard a voice to call him so.
BOT: What, art thou lame?
USER: Ay, God Almighty help me!
BOT: How cam'st thou so?
USER: A fall off of a tree.
BOT: A plum-tree, master.
USER: How long hast thou been blind?
BOT: O, born so, master.
USER: What, and wouldst climb a tree?
BOT: But that in all my life, when I was a youth.
USER: Too true; and bought his climbing very dear.
BOT: Mass, thou lov'dst plums well, that wouldst venture so.
USER: Alas, good master, my wife desired some damsons, And made me climb, with danger of my life.
BOT: A subtle knave! But yet it shall not serve. - Let me see thine eyes. Wink now. Now open them. In my opinion yet thou seest not well.
USER: Yes, master, clear as day, I thank God and Saint Alban.
BOT: Sayst thou me so? What colour is this cloak of?
USER: Red, master, red as blood.
BOT: Why, that's well said. What colour is my gown of?
USER: Black, forsooth, coal-black as jet.
BOT: Why, then, thou know'st what colour jet is of?
USER: And yet, I think, jet did he never see.
BOT: But cloaks and gowns before this day, a many.
USER: Never before this day in all his life.
BOT: Tell me, sirrah, what's my name?
USER: Alas, master, I know not.
BOT: What's his name?
USER: I know not.
BOT: Nor his?
USER: No, indeed, master.
BOT: What's thine own name?
USER: Sander Simpcox, an if it please you, master.
BOT: O master, that you could!
USER: My masters of Saint Albans, have you not beadles in your town, and things called whips?
BOT: Yes, my lord, if it please your grace.
USER: Then send for one presently.
BOT: Sirrah, go fetch the beadle hither straight.
USER: Now fetch me a stool hither by and by. - Now, sirrah, if you mean to save yourself from whipping, leap me over this stool and run away.
BOT: Alas, master, I am not able to stand alone. You go about to torture me in vain.
USER: Well, sir, we must have you find your legs. Sirrah beadle, whip him till he leap over that same stool.
BOT: I will, my lord. - Come on, sirrah; off with your doublet quickly.
USER: Alas, master, what shall I do? I am not able to stand. away; and they follow and cry, "A miracle!"_]
BOT: O God, seest Thou this, and bearest so long?
USER: It made me laugh to see the villain run.
BOT: Follow the knave, and take this drab away.
USER: Alas, sir, we did it for pure need.
BOT: Let them be whipped through every market town Till they come to Berwick, from whence they came.
USER: Duke Humphrey has done a miracle today.
BOT: True, made the lame to leap and fly away.
USER: But you have done more miracles than I. You made in a day, my lord, whole towns to fly.
BOT: What tidings with our cousin Buckingham?
USER: Your lady is forthcoming yet at London. This news, I think, hath turned your weapon's edge; 'Tis like, my lord, you will not keep your hour.
BOT: Ambitious churchman, leave to afflict my heart. Sorrow and grief have vanquished all my powers, And, vanquished as I am, I yield to thee, Or to the meanest groom.
USER: O God, what mischiefs work the wicked ones, Heaping confusion on their own heads thereby!
BOT: Gloucester, see here the tainture of thy nest, And look thyself be faultless, thou wert best.

USER: Now, my good Lords of Salisbury and Warwick, Our simple supper ended, give me leave In this close walk to satisfy myself In craving your opinion of my title, Which is infallible, to England's crown.
BOT: My lord, I long to hear it at full.
USER: Sweet York, begin; and if thy claim be good, The Nevilles are thy subjects to command.
BOT: Father, the Duke hath told the truth; Thus got the house of Lancaster the crown.
USER: Which now they hold by force and not by right; For Richard, the first son's heir, being dead, The issue of the next son should have reigned.
BOT: But William of Hatfield died without an heir.
USER: This Edmund, in the reign of Bolingbroke, As I have read, laid claim unto the crown And, but for Owen Glendower, had been king, Who kept him in captivity till he died. But to the rest.
BOT: Long live our sovereign Richard, England's king!
USER: My lord, break we off; we know your mind at full.
BOT: My heart assures me that the Earl of Warwick Shall one day make the Duke of York a king.
USER: And, Neville, this I do assure myself: Richard shall live to make the Earl of Warwick The greatest man in England but the king.

USER: Welcome is banishment; welcome were my death.
BOT: I see no reason why a king of years Should be to be protected like a child. God and King Henry govern England's realm! Give up your staff, sir, and the King his realm.
USER: Thus droops this lofty pine and hangs his sprays; Thus Eleanor's pride dies in her youngest days.
BOT: Lords, let him go. - Please it your majesty, This is the day appointed for the combat, And ready are the appellant and defendant, The armourer and his man, to enter the lists, So please your highness to behold the fight.
USER: Ay, good my lord; for purposely therefore Left I the court to see this quarrel tried.
BOT: I' God's name, see the lists and all things fit. Here let them end it, and God defend the right!
USER: Let it come, i' faith, and I'll pledge you all; and a fig for Peter! 1 PRENTICE. Here, Peter, I drink to thee, and be not afraid. 2 PRENTICE. Be merry, Peter, and fear not thy master. Fight for credit of the prentices.
BOT: Come, leave your drinking and fall to blows. Sirrah, what's thy name?
USER: Peter, forsooth.
BOT: Peter? What more?
USER: Thump.
BOT: Thump! Then see thou thump thy master well.
USER: Dispatch! This knave's tongue begins to double. Sound, trumpets. Alarum to the combatants!
BOT: Hold, Peter, hold! I confess, I confess treason.
USER: Take away his weapon. - Fellow, thank God and the good wine in thy master's way.
BOT: O God, have I overcome mine enemies in this presence? O Peter, thou hast prevailed in right!

USER: Thus sometimes hath the brightest day a cloud, And after summer evermore succeeds Barren winter, with his wrathful nipping cold; So cares and joys abound, as seasons fleet. Sirs, what's o'clock?
BOT: Ten, my lord.
USER: So please your Grace, we'll take her from the sheriff.
BOT: No, stir not for your lives; let her pass by.
USER: Be patient, gentle Nell, forget this grief.
BOT: I summon your grace to his majesty's parliament, Holden at Bury the first of this next month.
USER: And my consent ne'er asked herein before? This is close dealing. Well, I will be there. My Nell, I take my leave; and, master sheriff, Let not her penance exceed the King's commission.
BOT: An 't please your grace, here my commission stays, And Sir John Stanley is appointed now To take her with him to the Isle of Man.
USER: Must you, Sir John, protect my lady here?
BOT: So am I given in charge, may 't please your grace.
USER: Entreat her not the worse in that I pray You use her well. The world may laugh again, And I may live to do you kindness if You do it her. And so, Sir John, farewell.
BOT: What, gone, my lord, and bid me not farewell?
USER: Witness my tears, I cannot stay to speak.
BOT: Why, madam, that is to the Isle of Man, There to be used according to your state.
USER: That's bad enough, for I am but reproach; And shall I then be used reproachfully?
BOT: Like to a duchess, and Duke Humphrey's lady; According to that state you shall be used.
USER: Sheriff, farewell, and better than I fare, Although thou hast been conduct of my shame.
BOT: It is my office; and, madam, pardon me.
USER: Ay, ay, farewell; thy office is discharged. Come, Stanley, shall we go?
BOT: Madam, your penance done, throw off this sheet, And go we to attire you for our journey.
USER: My shame will not be shifted with my sheet, No, it will hang upon my richest robes And show itself, attire me how I can. Go, lead the way, I long to see my prison.

USER: I muse my Lord of Gloucester is not come. 'Tis not his wont to be the hindmost man, Whate'er occasion keeps him from us now.
BOT: Did he not, contrary to form of law, Devise strange deaths for small offences done?
USER: And did he not, in his protectorship, Levy great sums of money through the realm For soldiers' pay in France, and never sent it? By means whereof the towns each day revolted.
BOT: Tut, these are petty faults to faults unknown, Which time will bring to light in smooth Duke Humphrey.
USER: All health unto my gracious sovereign!
BOT: Welcome, Lord Somerset. What news from France?
USER: That all your interest in those territories Is utterly bereft you; all is lost.
BOT: Cold news, Lord Somerset; but God's will be done.
USER: As firmly as I hope for fertile England. Thus are my blossoms blasted in the bud, And caterpillars eat my leaves away; But I will remedy this gear ere long, Or sell my title for a glorious grave.
BOT: All happiness unto my lord the King! Pardon, my liege, that I have staid so long.
USER: Nay, Gloucester, know that thou art come too soon, Unless thou wert more loyal than thou art. I do arrest thee of high treason here.
BOT: 'Tis thought, my lord, that you took bribes of France, And, being Protector, stayed the soldiers' pay, By means whereof his highness hath lost France.
USER: It serves you well, my lord, to say so much.
BOT: I say no more than truth, so help me God!
USER: In your protectorship you did devise Strange tortures for offenders never heard of, That England was defamed by tyranny.
BOT: My Lord of Gloucester, 'tis my special hope That you will clear yourself from all suspense. My conscience tells me you are innocent.
USER: Hath he not twit our sovereign lady here With ignominious words, though clerkly couched, As if she had suborned some to swear False allegations to o'erthrow his state?
BOT: But I can give the loser leave to chide.
USER: Far truer spoke than meant. I lose, indeed. Beshrew the winners, for they played me false! And well such losers may have leave to speak.
BOT: He'll wrest the sense and hold us here all day. Lord Cardinal, he is your prisoner.
USER: Sirs, take away the Duke, and guard him sure.
BOT: My lords, what to your wisdoms seemeth best Do, or undo, as if ourself were here.
USER: What, will your highness leave the parliament?
BOT: That he should die is worthy policy, But yet we want a colour for his death. 'Tis meet he be condemned by course of law.
USER: But, in my mind, that were no policy. The King will labour still to save his life, The commons haply rise to save his life, And yet we have but trivial argument, More than mistrust, that shows him worthy death.
BOT: So that, by this, you would not have him die.
USER: Ah, York, no man alive so fain as I!
BOT: So the poor chicken should be sure of death.
USER: Thrice-noble Suffolk, 'tis resolutely spoke.
BOT: But I would have him dead, my Lord of Suffolk, Ere you can take due orders for a priest. Say you consent and censure well the deed, And I'll provide his executioner. I tender so the safety of my liege.
USER: Here is my hand, the deed is worthy doing.
BOT: And so say I.
USER: And I. And now we three have spoke it, It skills not greatly who impugns our doom.
BOT: A breach that craves a quick expedient stop! What counsel give you in this weighty cause?
USER: That Somerset be sent as regent thither. 'Tis meet that lucky ruler be employed; Witness the fortune he hath had in France.
BOT: If York, with all his far-fet policy, Had been the regent there instead of me, He never would have stayed in France so long.
USER: What, worse than naught? Nay, then a shame take all!
BOT: And, in the number, thee that wishest shame!
USER: I will, my lord, so please his majesty.
BOT: Why, our authority is his consent, And what we do establish he confirms. Then, noble York, take thou this task in hand.
USER: I am content. Provide me soldiers, lords, Whiles I take order for mine own affairs.
BOT: A charge, Lord York, that I will see performed. But now return we to the false Duke Humphrey.
USER: No more of him; for I will deal with him That henceforth he shall trouble us no more. And so break off; the day is almost spent. Lord Suffolk, you and I must talk of that event.
BOT: My Lord of Suffolk, within fourteen days At Bristol I expect my soldiers; For there I'll ship them all for Ireland.
USER: I'll see it truly done, my Lord of York.

USER: Now, sirs, have you dispatched this thing? 1 MURDERER. Ay, my good lord, he's dead.
BOT: Away, be gone! Sound trumpets. Enter the King, the Queen, Cardinal Beaufort, Somerset with attendants.
USER: Go, call our uncle to our presence straight; Say we intend to try his grace today If he be guilty, as 'tis published.
BOT: I'll call him presently, my noble lord.
USER: Lords, take your places; and, I pray you all, Proceed no straiter 'gainst our uncle Gloucester Than from true evidence of good esteem He be approved in practice culpable.
BOT: God forbid any malice should prevail That faultless may condemn a nobleman! Pray God he may acquit him of suspicion!
USER: I thank thee, Meg; these words content me much. How now? Why look'st thou pale? Why tremblest thou? Where is our uncle? What's the matter, Suffolk?
BOT: Dead in his bed, my lord; Gloucester is dead.
USER: Marry, God forfend!
BOT: God's secret judgment! I did dream tonight The Duke was dumb and could not speak a word.
USER: How fares my lord? Help, lords! the King is dead.
BOT: Rear up his body; wring him by the nose.
USER: Run, go, help, help! O Henry, ope thine eyes!
BOT: He doth revive again. Madam, be patient.
USER: O heavenly God!
BOT: How fares my gracious lord?
USER: Comfort, my sovereign! Gracious Henry, comfort!
BOT: Ah, woe is me for Gloucester, wretched man!
USER: That he is dead, good Warwick, 'tis too true; But how he died God knows, not Henry. And comment then upon his sudden death.
BOT: That shall I do, my liege. - Stay, Salisbury, With the rude multitude till I return. another._]
USER: Come hither, gracious sovereign, view this body.
BOT: That is to see how deep my grave is made, For with his soul fled all my worldly solace; For seeing him, I see my life in death.
USER: As surely as my soul intends to live With that dread King that took our state upon Him To free us from His Father's wrathful curse, I do believe that violent hands were laid Upon the life of this thrice-famed duke.
BOT: A dreadful oath, sworn with a solemn tongue! What instance gives Lord Warwick for his vow?
USER: Why, Warwick, who should do the Duke to death? Myself and Beaufort had him in protection, And we, I hope, sir, are no murderers.
BOT: But both of you were vowed Duke Humphrey's foes, And you, forsooth, had the good Duke to keep. 'Tis like you would not feast him like a friend, And 'tis well seen he found an enemy.
USER: Then you, belike, suspect these noblemen As guilty of Duke Humphrey's timeless death.
BOT: Are you the butcher, Suffolk? Where's your knife? Is Beaufort termed a kite? Where are his talons?
USER: What dares not Warwick, if false Suffolk dare him?
BOT: He dares not calm his contumelious spirit, Nor cease to be an arrogant controller, Though Suffolk dare him twenty thousand times.
USER: Madam, be still, with reverence may I say; For every word you speak in his behalf Is slander to your royal dignity.
BOT: Thou shalt be waking while I shed thy blood, If from this presence thou dar'st go with me.
USER: Away even now, or I will drag thee hence. Unworthy though thou art, I'll cope with thee And do some service to Duke Humphrey's ghost.
BOT: What stronger breastplate than a heart untainted? Thrice is he armed that hath his quarrel just, And he but naked, though locked up in steel, Whose conscience with injustice is corrupted.
USER: What noise is this?
BOT: Why, how now, lords? Your wrathful weapons drawn Here in our presence? Dare you be so bold? Why, what tumultuous clamour have we here?
USER: The traitorous Warwick with the men of Bury Set all upon me, mighty sovereign.
BOT: O Henry, let me plead for gentle Suffolk!
USER: Mischance and sorrow go along with you! Heart's discontent and sour affliction Be playfellows to keep you company! There's two of you; the devil make a third! And threefold vengeance tend upon your steps!
BOT: Cease, gentle Queen, these execrations, And let thy Suffolk take his heavy leave.
USER: Fie, coward woman and soft-hearted wretch! Has thou not spirit to curse thine enemies?
BOT: Enough, sweet Suffolk; thou torment'st thyself, And these dread curses, like the sun 'gainst glass, Or like an overcharged gun, recoil And turns the force of them upon thyself.
USER: Whither goes Vaux so fast? What news, I prithee?
BOT: Away! Though parting be a fretful corrosive, It is applied to a deathful wound. To France, sweet Suffolk! Let me hear from thee, For whereso'er thou art in this world's globe I'll have an Iris that shall find thee out.
USER: I go.
BOT: And take my heart with thee.
USER: A jewel, locked into the woefull'st cask That ever did contain a thing of worth. Even as a splitted bark, so sunder we. This way fall I to death.
BOT: This way for me.

USER: How fares my lord? Speak, Beaufort, to thy sovereign.
BOT: If thou be'st Death, I'll give thee England's treasure, Enough to purchase such another island, So thou wilt let me live and feel no pain.
USER: Ah, what a sign it is of evil life Where death's approach is seen so terrible!
BOT: Beaufort, it is thy sovereign speaks to thee.
USER: O Thou eternal mover of the heavens, Look with a gentle eye upon this wretch! O, beat away the busy meddling fiend That lays strong siege unto this wretch's soul, And from his bosom purge this black despair!
BOT: See how the pangs of death do make him grin!
USER: Disturb him not; let him pass peaceably.
BOT: Peace to his soul, if God's good pleasure be! Lord Cardinal, if thou think'st on heaven's bliss, Hold up thy hand, make signal of thy hope. He dies and makes no sign. O God, forgive him!
USER: So bad a death argues a monstrous life.
BOT: Forbear to judge, for we are sinners all. Close up his eyes, and draw the curtain close, And let us all to meditation.

USER: A thousand crowns, or else lay down your head.
BOT: And so much shall you give, or off goes yours.
USER: And therefore to revenge it shalt thou die; And so should these, if I might have my will.
BOT: Be not so rash; take ransom, let him live.
USER: Look on my George; I am a gentleman. Rate me at what thou wilt, thou shalt be paid.
BOT: And so am I; my name is Walter Whitmore. How now! Why starts thou? What, doth death affright?
USER: Thy name affrights me, in whose sound is death. A cunning man did calculate my birth And told me that by water I should die. Yet let not this make thee be bloody-minded; Thy name is Gaultier, being rightly sounded.
BOT: Stay, Whitmore, for thy prisoner is a prince, The Duke of Suffolk, William de la Pole.
USER: The Duke of Suffolk, muffled up in rags?
BOT: Ay, but these rags are no part of the Duke. Jove sometime went disguised, and why not I?
USER: But Jove was never slain, as thou shalt be.
BOT: Speak, captain, shall I stab the forlorn swain?
USER: First let my words stab him, as he hath me.
BOT: Base slave, thy words are blunt, and so art thou.
USER: Convey him hence, and on our longboat's side Strike off his head.
BOT: Thou dar'st not, for thy own.
USER: Yes, poll!
BOT: Pole!
USER: Walter.
BOT: Come, Suffolk, I must waft thee to thy death.
USER: _Pene gelidus timor occupat artus_. It is thee I fear.
BOT: Thou shalt have cause to fear before I leave thee. What, are ye daunted now? Now will ye stoop? 1 GENTLEMAN. My gracious lord, entreat him, speak him fair.
USER: Hale him away, and let him talk no more.
BOT: And as for these whose ransom we have set, It is our pleasure one of them depart. Therefore come you with us, and let him go.

USER: Come, and get thee a sword, though made of a lath; they have been up these two days.
BOT: They have the more need to sleep now, then.
USER: I tell thee, Jack Cade the clothier means to dress the commonwealth, and turn it, and set a new nap upon it.
BOT: So he had need, for 'tis threadbare. Well, I say it was never merry world in England since gentlemen came up.
USER: O miserable age! Virtue is not regarded in handicraftsmen.
BOT: The nobility think scorn to go in leather aprons.
USER: Nay, more, the King's Council are no good workmen.
BOT: True; and yet it is said, "Labour in thy vocation," which is as much to say as, "Let the magistrates be labouring men;" and therefore should we be magistrates.
USER: Thou hast hit it; for there's no better sign of a brave mind than a hard hand.
BOT: I see them! I see them! There's Best's son, the tanner of Wingham.
USER: He shall have the skin of our enemies, to make dog's leather of.
BOT: And Dick the butcher.
USER: Then is sin struck down like an ox, and iniquity's throat cut like a calf.
BOT: And Smith the weaver.
USER: Argo, their thread of life is spun.
BOT: Come, come, let's fall in with them. Drum. Enter Cade, Dick the Butcher, Smith the Weaver and a Sawyer with infinite numbers carrying long staves.
USER: We, John Cade, so termed of our supposed father -
BOT: For our enemies shall fall before us, inspired with the spirit of putting down kings and princes. Command silence.
USER: Silence!
BOT: My father was a Mortimer -
USER: My mother a Plantagenet -
BOT: My wife descended of the Lacies -
USER: she washes bucks here at home.
BOT: Therefore am I of an honourable house.
USER: born, under a hedge, for his father had never a house but the cage.
BOT: Valiant I am.
USER: I am able to endure much.
BOT: market-days together.
USER: I fear neither sword nor fire.
BOT: th' hand for stealing of sheep.
USER: God save your majesty!
BOT: I thank you, good people. - There shall be no money; all shall eat and drink on my score, and I will apparel them all in one livery, that they may agree like brothers and worship me their lord.
USER: The first thing we do, let's kill all the lawyers.
BOT: The clerk of Chartham. He can write and read and cast account.
USER: O, monstrous!
BOT: We took him setting of boys' copies.
USER: Here's a villain!
BOT: H'as a book in his pocket with red letters in 't.
USER: Nay, then, he is a conjurer.
BOT: Nay, he can make obligations and write court-hand.
USER: I am sorry for 't. The man is a proper man, of mine honour; unless I find him guilty, he shall not die. - Come hither, sirrah, I must examine thee. What is thy name?
BOT: Emmanuel.
USER: They use to write it on the top of letters. 'Twill go hard with you.
BOT: Let me alone. Dost thou use to write thy name? Or hast thou a mark to thyself, like a honest, plain-dealing man?
USER: Sir, I thank God, I have been so well brought up that I can write my name.
BOT: He hath confessed. Away with him! He's a villain and a traitor.
USER: Away with him, I say! Hang him with his pen and inkhorn about his neck.
BOT: Where's our general?
USER: Here I am, thou particular fellow.
BOT: Fly, fly, fly! Sir Humphrey Stafford and his brother are hard by, with the King's forces.
USER: Stand, villain, stand, or I'll fell thee down. He shall be encountered with a man as good as himself. He is but a knight, is he?
BOT: No.
USER: To equal him, I will make myself a knight presently.
BOT: Rebellious hinds, the filth and scum of Kent, Marked for the gallows, lay your weapons down; Home to your cottages, forsake this groom. The King is merciful, if you revolt.
USER: But angry, wrathful, and inclined to blood, If you go forward. Therefore yield, or die.
BOT: As for these silken-coated slaves, I pass not. It is to you, good people, that I speak, Over whom, in time to come, I hope to reign, For I am rightful heir unto the crown.
USER: Villain, thy father was a plasterer, And thou thyself a shearman, art thou not?
BOT: And Adam was a gardener.
USER: And what of that?
BOT: Marry, this: Edmund Mortimer, Earl of March, Married the Duke of Clarence' daughter, did he not?
USER: Ay, sir.
BOT: By her he had two children at one birth.
USER: That's false.
BOT: Nay, 'tis too true; therefore he shall be King.
USER: Sir, he made a chimney in my father's house, and the bricks are alive at this day to testify it; therefore deny it not.
BOT: And will you credit this base drudge's words, That speaks he knows not what?
USER: Ay, marry, will we; therefore get ye gone.
BOT: Jack Cade, the Duke of York hath taught you this.
USER: King from me that, for his father's sake, Henry the Fifth, in whose time boys went to span-counter for French crowns, I am content he shall reign, but I'll be Protector over him.
BOT: And furthermore, we'll have the Lord Saye's head for selling the dukedom of Maine.
USER: O gross and miserable ignorance!
BOT: Nay, answer if you can. The Frenchmen are our enemies; go to, then, I ask but this: can he that speaks with the tongue of an enemy be a good counsellor, or no?
USER: No, no, and therefore we'll have his head.
BOT: Well, seeing gentle words will not prevail, Assail them with the army of the King.
USER: They are all in order and march toward us.
BOT: But then are we in order when we are most out of order. Come, march forward.

USER: Where's Dick, the butcher of Ashford?
BOT: Here, sir.
USER: I desire no more.
BOT: If we mean to thrive and do good, break open the gaols and let out the prisoners.
USER: Fear not that, I warrant thee. Come, let's march towards London.

USER: And makes it fearful and degenerate; Think therefore on revenge and cease to weep. But who can cease to weep and look on this? Here may his head lie on my throbbing breast; But where's the body that I should embrace?
BOT: What answer makes your grace to the rebels' supplication?
USER: Ruled, like a wandering planet, over me, And could it not enforce them to relent That were unworthy to behold the same?
BOT: Lord Saye, Jack Cade hath sworn to have thy head.
USER: Ay, but I hope your highness shall have his.
BOT: How now, madam? Still lamenting and mourning for Suffolk's death? I fear me, love, if that I had been dead, Thou wouldst not have mourned so much for me.
USER: No, my love, I should not mourn, but die for thee.
BOT: How now, what news? Why com'st thou in such haste?
USER: O graceless men! They know not what they do.
BOT: My gracious lord, retire to Killingworth Until a power be raised to put them down.
USER: Ah, were the Duke of Suffolk now alive, These Kentish rebels would be soon appeased!
BOT: Lord Saye, the traitors hate thee; Therefore away with us to Killingworth.
USER: So might your grace's person be in danger. The sight of me is odious in their eyes; And therefore in this city will I stay And live alone as secret as I may.
BOT: Jack Cade hath gotten London Bridge; The citizens fly and forsake their houses. The rascal people, thirsting after prey, Join with the traitor, and they jointly swear To spoil the city and your royal court.
USER: Then linger not, my lord; away, take horse!
BOT: Come, Margaret. God, our hope, will succour us.
USER: Farewell, my lord. Trust not the Kentish rebels.
BOT: Trust nobody, for fear you be betrayed.
USER: The trust I have is in mine innocence, And therefore am I bold and resolute.

USER: Jack Cade! Jack Cade!
BOT: Knock him down there.
USER: If this fellow be wise, he'll never call ye Jack Cade more. I think he hath a very fair warning. My lord, there's an army gathered together in Smithfield.
BOT: Come then, let's go fight with them. But first, go and set London Bridge on fire; and, if you can, burn down the Tower too. Come, let's away.

USER: So, sirs. Now go some and pull down the Savoy; others to th' Inns of Court; down with them all.
BOT: I have a suit unto your lordship.
USER: Be it a lordship, thou shalt have it for that word.
BOT: Only that the laws of England may come out of your mouth.
USER: mouth with a spear, and 'tis not whole yet.
BOT: with eating toasted cheese.
USER: I have thought upon it, it shall be so. Away, burn all the records of the realm. My mouth shall be the parliament of England.
BOT: be pulled out.
USER: And henceforward all things shall be in common.
BOT: My lord, a prize, a prize! Here's the Lord Saye, which sold the towns in France; he that made us pay one-and-twenty fifteens, and one shilling to the pound, the last subsidy.
USER: What of that?
BOT: Marry, thou ought'st not to let thy horse wear a cloak when honester men than thou go in their hose and doublets.
USER: And work in their shirt too; as myself, for example, that am a butcher.
BOT: You men of Kent -
USER: What say you of Kent?
BOT: Nothing but this; 'tis _bona terra, mala gens_.
USER: Away with him, away with him! He speaks Latin.
BOT: Tut, when struck'st thou one blow in the field?
USER: Great men have reaching hands; oft have I struck Those that I never saw, and struck them dead.
BOT: O monstrous coward! What, to come behind folks?
USER: These cheeks are pale for watching for your good.
BOT: Give him a box o' th' ear, and that will make 'em red again.
USER: Long sitting to determine poor men's causes Hath made me full of sickness and diseases.
BOT: Ye shall have a hempen caudle then, and the help of hatchet.
USER: Why dost thou quiver, man?
BOT: The palsy, and not fear, provokes me.
USER: Nay, he nods at us, as who should say, "I'll be even with you." I'll see if his head will stand steadier on a pole or no. Take him away, and behead him.
BOT: It shall be done.
USER: Ah, countrymen, if when you make your prayers, God should be so obdurate as yourselves, How would it fare with your departed souls? And therefore yet relent, and save my life.
BOT: My lord, when shall we go to Cheapside and take up commodities upon our bills?
USER: Marry, presently.
BOT: O, brave!

USER: Up Fish Street! Down Saint Magnus' Corner! Kill and knock down! Throw them into Thames! [_Sound a parley_.] What noise is this I hear? Dare any be so bold to sound retreat or parley when I command them kill?
BOT: God save the King! God save the King!
USER: We'll follow Cade! We'll follow Cade!
BOT: A Clifford! A Clifford! We'll follow the King and Clifford.
USER: What, is he fled? Go some, and follow him; And he that brings his head unto the King Shall have a thousand crowns for his reward. Follow me, soldiers; we'll devise a mean To reconcile you all unto the King.

USER: Health and glad tidings to your majesty!
BOT: Why, Buckingham, is the traitor Cade surprised? Or is he but retired to make him strong?
USER: He is fled, my lord, and all his powers do yield, And humbly thus, with halters on their necks, Expect your highness' doom of life or death.
BOT: God save the King! God save the King!
USER: My lord, I'll yield myself to prison willingly, Or unto death, to do my country good.
BOT: In any case, be not too rough in terms, For he is fierce and cannot brook hard language.
