USER: I will, my lord, and doubt not so to deal As all things shall redound unto your good.
BOT: Come, wife, let's in, and learn to govern better; For yet may England curse my wretched reign.

USER: York, if thou meanest well, I greet thee well.
BOT: Humphrey of Buckingham, I accept thy greeting. Art thou a messenger, or come of pleasure?
USER: That is too much presumption on thy part; But if thy arms be to no other end, The King hath yielded unto thy demand: The Duke of Somerset is in the Tower.
BOT: Upon thine honour, is he prisoner?
USER: Upon mine honour, he is prisoner.
BOT: York, I commend this kind submission. We twain will go into his highness' tent.
USER: Buckingham, doth York intend no harm to us That thus he marcheth with thee arm in arm?
BOT: In all submission and humility York doth present himself unto your highness.
USER: Then what intends these forces thou dost bring?
BOT: To heave the traitor Somerset from hence And fight against that monstrous rebel Cade, Who since I heard to be discomfited.
USER: If one so rude and of so mean condition May pass into the presence of a king, Lo, I present your grace a traitor's head, The head of Cade, whom I in combat slew.
BOT: The head of Cade! Great God, how just art Thou! O, let me view his visage, being dead, That living wrought me such exceeding trouble. Tell me, my friend, art thou the man that slew him?
USER: I was, an 't like your majesty.
BOT: How art thou called? And what is thy degree?
USER: Alexander Iden, that's my name; A poor esquire of Kent, that loves his King.
BOT: So please it you, my lord, 'twere not amiss He were created knight for his good service.
USER: Iden, kneel down. [_He kneels_.] Rise up a knight. We give thee for reward a thousand marks, And will that thou henceforth attend on us.
BOT: May Iden live to merit such a bounty, And never live but true unto his liege!
USER: See, Buckingham, Somerset comes with the Queen. Go, bid her hide him quickly from the Duke.
BOT: For thousand Yorks he shall not hide his head, But boldly stand and front him to his face.
USER: O monstrous traitor! I arrest thee, York, Of capital treason 'gainst the King and crown. Obey, audacious traitor, kneel for grace.
BOT: Wouldst have me kneel? First let me ask of these If they can brook I bow a knee to man. Sirrah, call in my sons to be my bail. I know, ere they will have me go to ward, They'll pawn their swords for my enfranchisement.
USER: Call hither Clifford; bid him come amain, To say if that the bastard boys of York Shall be the surety for their traitor father.
BOT: And here comes Clifford to deny their bail.
USER: Health and all happiness to my lord the King.
BOT: I thank thee, Clifford. Say, what news with thee? Nay, do not fright us with an angry look. We are thy sovereign, Clifford, kneel again. For thy mistaking so, we pardon thee.
USER: This is my king, York, I do not mistake; But thou mistakes me much to think I do. To Bedlam with him! Is the man grown mad?
BOT: Ay, Clifford; a bedlam and ambitious humour Makes him oppose himself against his king.
USER: He is a traitor; let him to the Tower, And chop away that factious pate of his.
BOT: He is arrested, but will not obey; His sons, he says, shall give their words for him.
USER: Will you not, sons?
BOT: Ay, noble father, if our words will serve.
USER: And if words will not, then our weapons shall.
BOT: Why, what a brood of traitors have we here!
USER: Are these thy bears? We'll bait thy bears to death And manacle the bearherd in their chains, If thou dar'st bring them to the baiting-place.
BOT: Hence, heap of wrath, foul indigested lump, As crooked in thy manners as thy shape!
USER: Nay, we shall heat you thoroughly anon.
BOT: Take heed, lest by your heat you burn yourselves.
USER: My lord, I have considered with myself The title of this most renowned duke, And in my conscience do repute his grace The rightful heir to England's royal seat.
BOT: Hast thou not sworn allegiance unto me?
USER: I have.
BOT: Canst thou dispense with heaven for such an oath?
USER: A subtle traitor needs no sophister.
BOT: Call Buckingham, and bid him arm himself.
USER: Call Buckingham, and all the friends thou hast, I am resolved for death or dignity.
BOT: The first I warrant thee, if dreams prove true.
USER: You were best to go to bed and dream again, To keep thee from the tempest of the field.
BOT: I am resolved to bear a greater storm Than any thou canst conjure up today; And that I'll write upon thy burgonet, Might I but know thee by thy household badge.
USER: And from thy burgonet I'll rend thy bear And tread it under foot with all contempt, Despite the bearherd that protects the bear.
BOT: And so to arms, victorious father, To quell the rebels and their complices.
USER: Fie, charity, for shame! Speak not in spite, For you shall sup with Jesu Christ tonight.
BOT: Foul stigmatic, that's more than thou canst tell.
USER: If not in heaven, you'll surely sup in hell.

USER: The deadly-handed Clifford slew my steed, But match to match I have encountered him And made a prey for carrion kites and crows Even of the bonny beast he loved so well.
BOT: Of one or both of us the time is come.
USER: Hold, Warwick, seek thee out some other chase, For I myself must hunt this deer to death.
BOT: Then, nobly, York; 'tis for a crown thou fight'st. As I intend, Clifford, to thrive today, It grieves my soul to leave thee unassailed.
USER: What seest thou in me, York? Why dost thou pause?
BOT: With thy brave bearing should I be in love, But that thou art so fast mine enemy.
USER: Nor should thy prowess want praise and esteem, But that 'tis shown ignobly and in treason.
BOT: So let it help me now against thy sword As I in justice and true right express it!
USER: My soul and body on the action both!
BOT: A dreadful lay! Address thee instantly.
USER: _La fin couronne les oeuvres._
BOT: Thus war hath given thee peace, for thou art still. Peace with his soul, heaven, if it be thy will!
USER: Away, my lord! You are slow, for shame, away!
BOT: Can we outrun the heavens? Good Margaret, stay.

USER: I wonder how the King escaped our hands.
BOT: Lord Stafford's father, Duke of Buckingham, Is either slain or wounded dangerous; I cleft his beaver with a downright blow. That this is true, father, behold his blood.
USER: And, brother, here's the Earl of Wiltshire's blood, Whom I encountered as the battles joined.
BOT: Speak thou for me, and tell them what I did.
USER: Richard hath best deserved of all my sons. But is your Grace dead, my Lord of Somerset?
BOT: Such hope have all the line of John of Gaunt!
USER: Thus do I hope to shake King Henry's head.
BOT: Assist me, then, sweet Warwick, and I will; For hither we have broken in by force.
USER: We'll all assist you; he that flies shall die.
BOT: Thanks, gentle Norfolk. Stay by me, my lords; And, soldiers, stay and lodge by me this night.
USER: And when the King comes, offer him no violence, Unless he seek to thrust you out perforce.
BOT: The Queen this day here holds her parliament, But little thinks we shall be of her council. By words or blows here let us win our right.
USER: Armed as we are, let's stay within this house.
BOT: The bloody parliament shall this be called, Unless Plantagenet, Duke of York, be king, And bashful Henry deposed, whose cowardice Hath made us bywords to our enemies.
USER: Then leave me not, my lords; be resolute. I mean to take possession of my right.
BOT: If I be not, heavens be revenged on me!
USER: The hope thereof makes Clifford mourn in steel.
BOT: What, shall we suffer this? Let's pluck him down. My heart for anger burns. I cannot brook it.
USER: Be patient, gentle Earl of Westmoreland.
BOT: Patience is for poltroons, such as he. He durst not sit there had your father lived. My gracious lord, here in the parliament Let us assail the family of York.
USER: Well hast thou spoken, cousin. Be it so.
BOT: Ah, know you not the city favours them, And they have troops of soldiers at their beck?
USER: But when the Duke is slain, they'll quickly fly.
BOT: I am thine.
USER: For shame, come down. He made thee Duke of York.
BOT: 'Twas my inheritance, as the earldom was.
USER: Thy father was a traitor to the crown.
BOT: Exeter, thou art a traitor to the crown In following this usurping Henry.
USER: Whom should he follow but his natural king?
BOT: True, Clifford, that's Richard, Duke of York.
USER: And shall I stand, and thou sit in my throne?
BOT: It must and shall be so. Content thyself.
USER: Be Duke of Lancaster. Let him be king.
BOT: He is both King and Duke of Lancaster; And that the Lord of Westmoreland shall maintain.
USER: And Warwick shall disprove it. You forget That we are those which chased you from the field And slew your fathers, and with colours spread Marched through the city to the palace gates.
BOT: Yes, Warwick, I remember it to my grief; And, by his soul, thou and thy house shall rue it.
USER: Plantagenet, of thee and these thy sons, Thy kinsmen, and thy friends, I'll have more lives Than drops of blood were in my father's veins.
BOT: Urge it no more; lest that, instead of words, I send thee, Warwick, such a messenger As shall revenge his death before I stir.
USER: Poor Clifford, how I scorn his worthless threats!
BOT: Will you we show our title to the crown? If not, our swords shall plead it in the field.
USER: Talk not of France, sith thou hast lost it all.
BOT: The Lord Protector lost it, and not I. When I was crowned I was but nine months old.
USER: You are old enough now, and yet, methinks, you lose. Father, tear the crown from the usurper's head.
BOT: Sweet father, do so; set it on your head.
USER: Good brother, as thou lov'st and honourest arms, Let's fight it out and not stand cavilling thus.
BOT: Sound drums and trumpets, and the King will fly.
USER: Sons, peace!
BOT: Peace thou, and give King Henry leave to speak.
USER: Plantagenet shall speak first. Hear him, lords, And be you silent and attentive too, For he that interrupts him shall not live.
BOT: Prove it, Henry, and thou shalt be king.
USER: Henry the Fourth by conquest got the crown.
BOT: 'Twas by rebellion against his king.
USER: Tell me, may not a king adopt an heir?
BOT: What then?
USER: An if he may, then am I lawful king; For Richard, in the view of many lords, Resigned the crown to Henry the Fourth, Whose heir my father was, and I am his.
BOT: He rose against him, being his sovereign, And made him to resign his crown perforce.
USER: Suppose, my lords, he did it unconstrained, Think you 'twere prejudicial to his crown?
BOT: No, for he could not so resign his crown But that the next heir should succeed and reign.
USER: Art thou against us, Duke of Exeter?
BOT: His is the right, and therefore pardon me.
USER: Why whisper you, my lords, and answer not?
BOT: My conscience tells me he is lawful king.
USER: Plantagenet, for all the claim thou lay'st, Think not that Henry shall be so deposed.
BOT: Deposed he shall be, in despite of all.
USER: Thou art deceived. 'Tis not thy southern power, Of Essex, Norfolk, Suffolk, nor of Kent, Which makes thee thus presumptuous and proud, Can set the Duke up in despite of me.
BOT: King Henry, be thy title right or wrong, Lord Clifford vows to fight in thy defence. May that ground gape and swallow me alive, Where I shall kneel to him that slew my father!
USER: O Clifford, how thy words revive my heart!
BOT: Henry of Lancaster, resign thy crown. What mutter you, or what conspire you, lords?
USER: Do right unto this princely Duke of York, Or I will fill the house with armed men, And over the chair of state where now he sits, Write up his title with usurping blood.
BOT: My Lord of Warwick, hear but one word: Let me for this my lifetime reign as king.
USER: Confirm the crown to me, and to mine heirs, And thou shalt reign in quiet while thou liv'st.
BOT: I am content. Richard Plantagenet, Enjoy the kingdom after my decease.
USER: What wrong is this unto the Prince your son!
BOT: What good is this to England and himself!
USER: Base, fearful, and despairing Henry!
BOT: How hast thou injured both thyself and us!
USER: I cannot stay to hear these articles.
BOT: Nor I.
USER: Come, cousin, let us tell the Queen these news.
BOT: Farewell, faint-hearted and degenerate king, In whose cold blood no spark of honour bides.
USER: Be thou a prey unto the house of York, And die in bands for this unmanly deed!
BOT: In dreadful war mayst thou be overcome, Or live in peace abandoned and despised!
USER: Turn this way, Henry, and regard them not.
BOT: They seek revenge, and therefore will not yield.
USER: Ah, Exeter!
BOT: Why should you sigh, my lord?
USER: This oath I willingly take and will perform.
BOT: Long live King Henry! Plantagenet, embrace him.
USER: And long live thou, and these thy forward sons!
BOT: Now York and Lancaster are reconciled.
USER: Accursed be he that seeks to make them foes! Sennet. Here they come down.
BOT: Farewell, my gracious lord. I'll to my castle.
USER: And I'll keep London with my soldiers.
BOT: And I to Norfolk with my followers.
USER: And I unto the sea from whence I came. Soldiers._]
BOT: And I with grief and sorrow to the court.
USER: Here comes the Queen, whose looks bewray her anger. I'll steal away.
BOT: Exeter, so will I.
USER: Nay, go not from me; I will follow thee.
BOT: Be patient, gentle Queen, and I will stay.
USER: Father, you cannot disinherit me. If you be king, why should not I succeed?
BOT: Pardon me, Margaret; pardon me, sweet son. The Earl of Warwick and the Duke enforced me.
USER: Stay, gentle Margaret, and hear me speak.
BOT: Thou hast spoke too much already. Get thee gone.
USER: Gentle son Edward, thou wilt stay with me?
BOT: Ay, to be murdered by his enemies.
USER: When I return with victory from the field I'll see your Grace. Till then I'll follow her.
BOT: Come, son, away; we may not linger thus.
USER: And I, I hope, shall reconcile them all.

USER: Brother, though I be youngest, give me leave.
BOT: No, I can better play the orator.
USER: But I have reasons strong and forcible.
BOT: Why, how now, sons and brother, at a strife? What is your quarrel? How began it first?
USER: No quarrel, but a slight contention.
BOT: About what?
USER: About that which concerns your Grace and us: The crown of England, father, which is yours.
BOT: Mine, boy? Not till King Henry be dead.
USER: Your right depends not on his life or death.
BOT: Now you are heir, therefore enjoy it now. By giving the house of Lancaster leave to breathe, It will outrun you, father, in the end.
USER: I took an oath that he should quietly reign.
BOT: But for a kingdom any oath may be broken. I would break a thousand oaths to reign one year.
USER: No; God forbid your Grace should be forsworn.
BOT: I shall be, if I claim by open war.
USER: I'll prove the contrary if you'll hear me speak.
BOT: Thou canst not, son; it is impossible.
USER: The Queen, with all the northern earls and lords Intend here to besiege you in your castle. She is hard by with twenty thousand men; And therefore fortify your hold, my lord.
BOT: Brother, I go; I'll win them, fear it not. And thus most humbly I do take my leave.
USER: Sir John and Sir Hugh Mortimer, mine uncles, You are come to Sandal in a happy hour; The army of the Queen mean to besiege us.
BOT: She shall not need; we'll meet her in the field.
USER: What, with five thousand men?
BOT: Ay, with five hundred, father, for a need. A woman's general; what should we fear?
USER: I hear their drums. Let's set our men in order, And issue forth and bid them battle straight.
BOT: Five men to twenty! Though the odds be great, I doubt not, uncle, of our victory. Many a battle have I won in France Whenas the enemy hath been ten to one. Why should I not now have the like success?

USER: Ah, whither shall I fly to scape their hands? Ah, tutor, look where bloody Clifford comes.
BOT: Chaplain, away! Thy priesthood saves thy life. As for the brat of this accursed duke Whose father slew my father, he shall die.
USER: And I, my lord, will bear him company.
BOT: Soldiers, away with him!
USER: Ah, Clifford, murder not this innocent child, Lest thou be hated both of God and man.
BOT: How now? Is he dead already? Or is it fear That makes him close his eyes? I'll open them.
USER: In vain thou speak'st, poor boy; my father's blood Hath stopped the passage where thy words should enter.
BOT: Then let my father's blood open it again; He is a man, and, Clifford, cope with him.
USER: O, let me pray before I take my death! To thee I pray; sweet Clifford, pity me!
BOT: Such pity as my rapier's point affords.
USER: I never did thee harm; why wilt thou slay me?
BOT: Thy father hath.
USER: No cause? Thy father slew my father; therefore die.
BOT: _Di faciant laudis summa sit ista tuae!_
USER: Plantagenet! I come, Plantagenet! And this thy son's blood cleaving to my blade Shall rust upon my weapon till thy blood, Congealed with this, do make me wipe off both.

USER: Yield to our mercy, proud Plantagenet.
BOT: Ay, to such mercy as his ruthless arm With downright payment showed unto my father. Now Phaëthon hath tumbled from his car, And made an evening at the noontide prick.
USER: My ashes, as the phoenix, may bring forth A bird that will revenge upon you all; And in that hope I throw mine eyes to heaven, Scorning whate'er you can afflict me with. Why come you not? What, multitudes, and fear?
BOT: So cowards fight when they can fly no further; So doves do peck the falcon's piercing talons; So desperate thieves, all hopeless of their lives, Breathe out invectives 'gainst the officers.
USER: I will not bandy with thee word for word, But buckle with thee blows twice two for one.
BOT: Hold, valiant Clifford; for a thousand causes I would prolong awhile the traitor's life. Wrath makes him deaf; speak thou, Northumberland.
USER: Ay, ay, so strives the woodcock with the gin.
BOT: So doth the cony struggle in the net.
USER: So triumph thieves upon their conquered booty; So true men yield, with robbers so o'ermatched.
BOT: What would your Grace have done unto him now?
USER: That is my office, for my father's sake.
BOT: Nay, stay; let's hear the orisons he makes.
USER: Beshrew me, but his passion moves me so That hardly can I check my eyes from tears.
BOT: Had he been slaughter-man to all my kin, I should not for my life but weep with him, To see how inly sorrow gripes his soul.
USER: What, weeping-ripe, my Lord Northumberland? Think but upon the wrong he did us all, And that will quickly dry thy melting tears.
BOT: Here's for my oath, here's for my father's death.
USER: And here's to right our gentle-hearted king.
BOT: Open thy gate of mercy, gracious God! My soul flies through these wounds to seek out Thee.
USER: Off with his head, and set it on York gates; So York may overlook the town of York.

USER: Dazzle mine eyes, or do I see three suns?
BOT: Nay, bear three daughters: by your leave I speak it, You love the breeder better than the male. But what art thou, whose heavy looks foretell Some dreadful story hanging on thy tongue?
USER: Ah, one that was a woeful looker-on When as the noble Duke of York was slain, Your princely father and my loving lord.
BOT: O, speak no more, for I have heard too much!
USER: Say how he died, for I will hear it all.
BOT: His name that valiant duke hath left with thee; His dukedom and his chair with me is left.
USER: How now, fair lords! What fare? What news abroad?
BOT: O, Warwick, Warwick, that Plantagenet Which held thee dearly as his soul's redemption Is by the stern Lord Clifford done to death.
USER: Where is the Duke of Norfolk, gentle Warwick? And when came George from Burgundy to England?
BOT: Some six miles off the Duke is with the soldiers; And for your brother, he was lately sent From your kind aunt, Duchess of Burgundy, With aid of soldiers to this needful war.
USER: 'Twas odds, belike, when valiant Warwick fled. Oft have I heard his praises in pursuit, But ne'er till now his scandal of retire.
BOT: Ay, now methinks I hear great Warwick speak. Ne'er may he live to see a sunshine day That cries "Retire," if Warwick bid him stay.
USER: Lord Warwick, on thy shoulder will I lean; And when thou fail'st - as God forbid the hour! - Must Edward fall, which peril heaven forfend!
BOT: Then, Clifford, were thy heart as hard as steel, As thou hast shown it flinty by thy deeds, I come to pierce it, or to give thee mine.
USER: Then strike up, drums! God and Saint George for us!
BOT: How now, what news?
USER: The Duke of Norfolk sends you word by me, The Queen is coming with a puissant host, And craves your company for speedy counsel.
BOT: Why then it sorts; brave warriors, let's away.

USER: Welcome, my lord, to this brave town of York. Yonder's the head of that arch-enemy That sought to be encompassed with your crown. Doth not the object cheer your heart, my lord?
BOT: Ay, as the rocks cheer them that fear their wrack! To see this sight, it irks my very soul. Withhold revenge, dear God! 'Tis not my fault, Nor wittingly have I infringed my vow.
USER: My lord, cheer up your spirits; our foes are nigh, And this soft courage makes your followers faint. You promised knighthood to our forward son. Unsheathe your sword and dub him presently. - Edward, kneel down.
BOT: Edward Plantagenet, arise a knight; And learn this lesson: draw thy sword in right.
USER: My gracious father, by your kingly leave, I'll draw it as apparent to the crown, And in that quarrel use it to the death.
BOT: Why, that is spoken like a toward prince.
USER: I would your highness would depart the field. The Queen hath best success when you are absent.
BOT: Ay, good my lord, and leave us to our fortune.
USER: Why, that's my fortune too; therefore I'll stay.
BOT: Be it with resolution then to fight.
USER: Now, perjured Henry, wilt thou kneel for grace And set thy diadem upon my head, Or bide the mortal fortune of the field?
BOT: Go rate thy minions, proud insulting boy! Becomes it thee to be thus bold in terms Before thy sovereign and thy lawful king?
USER: And reason too: Who should succeed the father but the son?
BOT: Are you there, butcher? O, I cannot speak!
USER: Ay, crook-back; here I stand, to answer thee, Or any he, the proudest of thy sort.
BOT: 'Twas you that killed young Rutland, was it not?
USER: Ay, and old York, and yet not satisfied.
BOT: For God's sake, lords, give signal to the fight.
USER: What sayst thou, Henry, wilt thou yield the crown?
BOT: Why, how now, long-tongued Warwick, dare you speak? When you and I met at Saint Albans last, Your legs did better service than your hands.
USER: Then 'twas my turn to fly, and now 'tis thine.
BOT: You said so much before, and yet you fled.
USER: 'Twas not your valour, Clifford, drove me thence.
BOT: No, nor your manhood that durst make you stay.
USER: Northumberland, I hold thee reverently. Break off the parley; for scarce I can refrain The execution of my big-swoln heart Upon that Clifford, that cruel child-killer.
BOT: I slew thy father; call'st thou him a child?
USER: Ay, like a dastard and a treacherous coward, As thou didst kill our tender brother Rutland, But ere sunset I'll make thee curse the deed.
BOT: Have done with words, my lords, and hear me speak.
USER: Defy them then, or else hold close thy lips.
BOT: I prithee, give no limits to my tongue. I am a king, and privileged to speak.
USER: My liege, the wound that bred this meeting here Cannot be cured by words; therefore be still.
BOT: Then, executioner, unsheathe thy sword. By Him that made us all, I am resolved That Clifford's manhood lies upon his tongue.
USER: Say, Henry, shall I have my right, or no? A thousand men have broke their fasts today That ne'er shall dine unless thou yield the crown.
BOT: If thou deny, their blood upon thy head; For York in justice puts his armour on.
USER: If that be right which Warwick says is right, There is no wrong, but everything is right.
BOT: Whoever got thee, there thy mother stands; For well I wot thou hast thy mother's tongue.
USER: But thou art neither like thy sire nor dam, But like a foul misshapen stigmatic, Marked by the Destinies to be avoided, As venom toads or lizards' dreadful stings.
BOT: Iron of Naples, hid with English gilt, Whose father bears the title of a king, As if a channel should be called the sea, Sham'st thou not, knowing whence thou art extraught, To let thy tongue detect thy base-born heart?
USER: And in this resolution I defy thee; Not willing any longer conference, Since thou deniest the gentle King to speak. Sound trumpets! Let our bloody colours wave; And either victory or else a grave!
BOT: Stay, Edward.
USER: No, wrangling woman, we'll no longer stay. These words will cost ten thousand lives this day.

USER: Smile, gentle heaven, or strike, ungentle death; For this world frowns and Edward's sun is clouded.
BOT: How now, my lord, what hap? What hope of good?
USER: Our hap is loss, our hope but sad despair; Our ranks are broke and ruin follows us. What counsel give you? Whither shall we fly?
BOT: Bootless is flight, they follow us with wings; And weak we are and cannot shun pursuit.
USER: Brother, give me thy hand; and, gentle Warwick, Let me embrace thee in my weary arms. I, that did never weep, now melt with woe That winter should cut off our spring-time so.
BOT: Away, away! Once more, sweet lords, farewell.

USER: Now, Clifford, I have singled thee alone. Suppose this arm is for the Duke of York, And this for Rutland; both bound to revenge, Wert thou environed with a brazen wall.
BOT: Nay, Warwick, single out some other chase; For I myself will hunt this wolf to death.

USER: How will my mother for a father's death Take on with me and ne'er be satisfied!
BOT: How will my wife for slaughter of my son Shed seas of tears and ne'er be satisfied!
USER: How will the country for these woeful chances Misthink the King and not be satisfied!
BOT: Was ever son so rued a father's death?
USER: Was ever father so bemoaned his son?
BOT: Was ever king so grieved for subjects' woe? Much is your sorrow, mine ten times so much.
USER: I'll bear thee hence, where I may weep my fill.
BOT: Sad-hearted men, much overgone with care, Here sits a king more woeful than you are. Alarums. Excursions. Enter Queen Margaret, Prince of Wales and Exeter.
USER: Fly, father, fly, for all your friends are fled, And Warwick rages like a chafed bull. Away, for death doth hold us in pursuit.
BOT: Away, for vengeance comes along with them. Nay, stay not to expostulate; make speed, Or else come after; I'll away before.
USER: Nay, take me with thee, good sweet Exeter; Not that I fear to stay, but love to go Whither the Queen intends. Forward; away!

USER: No, 'tis impossible he should escape; For, though before his face I speak the words, Your brother Richard marked him for the grave, And whereso'er he is, he's surely dead.
BOT: Whose soul is that which takes her heavy leave? A deadly groan, like life and death's departing.
USER: See who it is; and, now the battle's ended, If friend or foe, let him be gently used.
BOT: From off the gates of York fetch down the head, Your father's head, which Clifford placed there; Instead whereof let this supply the room. Measure for measure must be answered.
USER: Bring forth that fatal screech-owl to our house, That nothing sung but death to us and ours; Now death shall stop his dismal threatening sound, And his ill-boding tongue no more shall speak.
BOT: I think his understanding is bereft. Speak, Clifford, dost thou know who speaks to thee? Dark cloudy death o'ershades his beams of life, And he nor sees nor hears us, what we say.
USER: O, would he did, and so, perhaps, he doth! 'Tis but his policy to counterfeit, Because he would avoid such bitter taunts Which in the time of death he gave our father.
BOT: If so thou think'st, vex him with eager words.
USER: Clifford, ask mercy, and obtain no grace.
BOT: Clifford, repent in bootless penitence.
USER: Clifford, devise excuses for thy faults.
BOT: While we devise fell tortures for thy faults.
USER: Thou didst love York, and I am son to York.
BOT: Thou pitied'st Rutland, I will pity thee.
USER: Where's Captain Margaret to fence you now?
BOT: They mock thee, Clifford; swear as thou wast wont.
USER: Let me be Duke of Clarence, George of Gloucester, For Gloucester's dukedom is too ominous.
BOT: Tut, that's a foolish observation. Richard, be Duke of Gloucester. Now to London, To see these honours in possession.

USER: Let me embrace thee, sour adversity, For wise men say it is the wisest course. 2 KEEPER. Why linger we? Let us lay hands upon him. 1 KEEPER. Forbear awhile; we'll hear a little more.
BOT: More than I seem, and less than I was born to: A man at least, for less I should not be; And men may talk of kings, and why not I? 2 KEEPER. Ay, but thou talk'st as if thou wert a king.
USER: Why, so I am, in mind; and that's enough. 2 KEEPER. But, if thou be a king, where is thy crown?
BOT: But did you never swear, and break an oath? 2 KEEPER. No, never such an oath; nor will not now.
USER: Where did you dwell when I was King of England? 2 KEEPER. Here in this country, where we now remain.
BOT: So would you be again to Henry If he were seated as King Edward is. 1 KEEPER. We charge you, in God's name and the King's To go with us unto the officers.
USER: In God's name, lead; your king's name be obeyed, And what God will, that let your king perform; And what he will, I humbly yield unto.

USER: Your Highness shall do well to grant her suit; It were dishonour to deny it her.
BOT: It were no less; but yet I'll make a pause.
USER: I see the lady hath a thing to grant Before the King will grant her humble suit.
BOT: Widow, we will consider of your suit, And come some other time to know our mind.
USER: Right gracious lord, I cannot brook delay. May it please your Highness to resolve me now, And what your pleasure is shall satisfy me.
BOT: An if what pleases him shall pleasure you. Fight closer, or, good faith, you'll catch a blow.
USER: How many children hast thou, widow? Tell me.
BOT: Three, my most gracious lord.
USER: 'Twere pity they should lose their father's lands.
BOT: Be pitiful, dread lord, and grant it then.
USER: Lords, give us leave; I'll try this widow's wit.
BOT: Till youth take leave and leave you to the crutch.
USER: Now tell me, madam, do you love your children?
BOT: Ay, full as dearly as I love myself.
USER: And would you not do much to do them good?
BOT: To do them good I would sustain some harm.
USER: Then get your husband's lands to do them good.
BOT: Therefore I came unto your majesty.
USER: I'll tell you how these lands are to be got.
BOT: So shall you bind me to your Highness' service.
USER: What service wilt thou do me if I give them?
BOT: What you command that rests in me to do.
USER: But you will take exceptions to my boon.
BOT: No, gracious lord, except I cannot do it.
USER: Ay, but thou canst do what I mean to ask.
BOT: Why, then, I will do what your Grace commands.
USER: Why stops my lord? Shall I not hear my task?
BOT: An easy task; 'tis but to love a king.
USER: That's soon performed, because I am a subject.
BOT: Why, then, thy husband's lands I freely give thee.
USER: I take my leave with many thousand thanks.
BOT: But stay thee; 'tis the fruits of love I mean.
USER: The fruits of love I mean, my loving liege.
BOT: Ay, but, I fear me, in another sense. What love, thinkst thou, I sue so much to get?
USER: My love till death, my humble thanks, my prayers; That love which virtue begs, and virtue grants.
BOT: No, by my troth, I did not mean such love.
USER: Why, then, you mean not as I thought you did.
BOT: But now you partly may perceive my mind.
USER: My mind will never grant what I perceive Your Highness aims at, if I aim aright.
BOT: To tell thee plain, I aim to lie with thee.
USER: To tell you plain, I had rather lie in prison.
BOT: Why, then thou shalt not have thy husband's lands.
USER: Why, then mine honesty shall be my dower, For by that loss I will not purchase them.
BOT: Therein thou wrong'st thy children mightily.
USER: Herein your Highness wrongs both them and me. But, mighty lord, this merry inclination Accords not with the sadness of my suit. Please you dismiss me either with ay or no.
BOT: Ay, if thou wilt say ay to my request; No, if thou dost say no to my demand.
USER: Then no, my lord. My suit is at an end.
BOT: Her words doth show her wit incomparable; All her perfections challenge sovereignty. One way or other, she is for a king, And she shall be my love, or else my queen. - Say that King Edward take thee for his queen?
USER: 'Tis better said than done, my gracious lord. I am a subject fit to jest withal, But far unfit to be a sovereign.
BOT: Sweet widow, by my state I swear to thee, I speak no more than what my soul intends; And that is to enjoy thee for my love.
USER: And that is more than I will yield unto. I know I am too mean to be your queen, And yet too good to be your concubine.
BOT: You cavil, widow; I did mean my queen.
USER: 'Twill grieve your Grace my sons should call you father.
BOT: Brothers, you muse what chat we two have had. Richard and George come forward.
USER: The widow likes it not, for she looks very sad.
BOT: You'd think it strange if I should marry her.
USER: To whom, my lord?
BOT: Why, Clarence, to myself.
USER: That would be ten days' wonder at the least.
BOT: That's a day longer than a wonder lasts.
USER: By so much is the wonder in extremes.
BOT: Well, jest on, brothers. I can tell you both Her suit is granted for her husband's lands.
USER: My gracious lord, Henry your foe is taken, And brought your prisoner to your palace gate.
BOT: See that he be conveyed unto the Tower. And go we, brothers, to the man that took him, To question of his apprehension. Widow, go you along. Lords, use her honourably.

USER: Fair Queen of England, worthy Margaret, Sit down with us. It ill befits thy state And birth that thou shouldst stand while Lewis doth sit.
BOT: Why, say, fair queen, whence springs this deep despair?
USER: From such a cause as fills mine eyes with tears And stops my tongue, while heart is drowned in cares.
BOT: Renowned queen, with patience calm the storm While we bethink a means to break it off.
USER: The more we stay, the stronger grows our foe.
BOT: The more I stay, the more I'll succour thee.
USER: O, but impatience waiteth on true sorrow. And see where comes the breeder of my sorrow.
BOT: What's he approacheth boldly to our presence?
USER: Our Earl of Warwick, Edward's greatest friend.
BOT: Welcome, brave Warwick. What brings thee to France?
USER: Ay, now begins a second storm to rise, For this is he that moves both wind and tide.
BOT: Injurious Margaret!
USER: And why not Queen?
BOT: Because thy father Henry did usurp, And thou no more art prince than she is queen.
USER: Why, Warwick, canst thou speak against thy liege, Whom thou obeyed'st thirty and six years, And not bewray thy treason with a blush?
BOT: Can Oxford, that did ever fence the right, Now buckler falsehood with a pedigree? For shame! Leave Henry, and call Edward king.
USER: And I the house of York.
BOT: Queen Margaret, Prince Edward, and Oxford, Vouchsafe at our request to stand aside While I use further conference with Warwick.
USER: Heavens grant that Warwick's words bewitch him not!
BOT: Now, Warwick, tell me, even upon thy conscience, Is Edward your true king? For I were loath To link with him that were not lawful chosen.
USER: Thereon I pawn my credit and mine honour.
BOT: But is he gracious in the people's eye?
USER: The more that Henry was unfortunate.
BOT: Then further, all dissembling set aside, Tell me for truth the measure of his love Unto our sister Bona.
USER: Now, sister, let us hear your firm resolve.
BOT: Your grant or your denial shall be mine. When I have heard your king's desert recounted, Mine ear hath tempted judgment to desire.
USER: To Edward, but not to the English king.
BOT: Deceitful Warwick, it was thy device By this alliance to make void my suit. Before thy coming Lewis was Henry's friend.
USER: Henry now lives in Scotland, at his ease, Where, having nothing, nothing can he lose. And as for you yourself, our quondam queen, You have a father able to maintain you, And better 'twere you troubled him than France.
BOT: Warwick, this is some post to us or thee.
USER: My lord ambassador, these letters are for you. Sent from your brother, Marquess Montague. These from our king unto your Majesty. And, madam, these for you, from whom I know not.
BOT: I like it well that our fair Queen and mistress Smiles at her news while Warwick frowns at his.
USER: Nay, mark how Lewis stamps as he were nettled. I hope all's for the best.
BOT: Warwick, what are thy news? And yours, fair Queen?
USER: Mine, such as fill my heart with unhoped joys.
BOT: Mine, full of sorrow and heart's discontent.
USER: What, has your king married the Lady Grey, And now, to soothe your forgery and his, Sends me a paper to persuade me patience? Is this th' alliance that he seeks with France? Dare he presume to scorn us in this manner?
BOT: I told your majesty as much before; This proveth Edward's love and Warwick's honesty.
USER: Warwick, these words have turned my hate to love; And I forgive and quite forget old faults, And joy that thou becom'st King Henry's friend.
BOT: Dear brother, how shall Bona be revenged But by thy help to this distressed queen?
USER: Renowned prince, how shall poor Henry live Unless thou rescue him from foul despair?
BOT: My quarrel and this English queen's are one.
USER: And mine, fair Lady Bona, joins with yours.
BOT: And mine with hers, and thine, and Margaret's. Therefore, at last I firmly am resolved You shall have aid.
USER: Let me give humble thanks for all at once.
BOT: Then, England's messenger, return in post And tell false Edward, thy supposed king, That Lewis of France is sending over maskers To revel it with him and his new bride. Thou seest what's past; go fear thy king withal.
USER: Tell him, in hope he'll prove a widower shortly, I'll wear the willow garland for his sake.
BOT: Tell him my mourning weeds are laid aside, And I am ready to put armour on.
USER: Tell him from me that he hath done me wrong, And therefore I'll uncrown him ere 't be long. There's thy reward; be gone.
BOT: This shall assure my constant loyalty: That if our Queen and this young prince agree, I'll join mine eldest daughter and my joy To him forthwith in holy wedlock bands.
USER: Yes, I agree, and thank you for your motion. Son Edward, she is fair and virtuous, Therefore delay not, give thy hand to Warwick, And with thy hand thy faith irrevocable That only Warwick's daughter shall be thine.
BOT: Yes, I accept her, for she well deserves it; And here, to pledge my vow, I give my hand.
USER: Why stay we now? These soldiers shall be levied, And thou, Lord Bourbon, our High Admiral, Shall waft them over with our royal fleet. I long till Edward fall by war's mischance For mocking marriage with a dame of France.

USER: Now tell me, brother Clarence, what think you Of this new marriage with the Lady Grey? Hath not our brother made a worthy choice?
BOT: Alas, you know 'tis far from hence to France! How could he stay till Warwick made return?
USER: My lords, forbear this talk; here comes the King. Flourish. Enter King Edward, attended; Lady Grey as Queen Elizabeth; Pembroke, Stafford, Hastings and others. Four stand on one side, and four on the other.
BOT: And his well-chosen bride.
USER: I mind to tell him plainly what I think.
BOT: Now, brother of Clarence, how like you our choice, That you stand pensive as half malcontent?
USER: As well as Lewis of France or the Earl of Warwick, Which are so weak of courage and in judgment That they'll take no offence at our abuse.
BOT: Suppose they take offence without a cause, They are but Lewis and Warwick; I am Edward, Your King and Warwick's, and must have my will.
USER: And shall have your will, because our King. Yet hasty marriage seldom proveth well.
BOT: Yea, brother Richard, are you offended too?
USER: Not I. No, God forbid that I should wish them severed Whom God hath joined together. Ay, and 'twere pity To sunder them that yoke so well together.
BOT: Setting your scorns and your mislike aside, Tell me some reason why the Lady Grey Should not become my wife and England's queen. And you too, Somerset and Montague, Speak freely what you think.
USER: Then this is mine opinion: that King Lewis Becomes your enemy for mocking him About the marriage of the Lady Bona.
BOT: And Warwick, doing what you gave in charge, Is now dishonoured by this new marriage.
USER: What if both Lewis and Warwick be appeased By such invention as I can devise?
BOT: Yet to have joined with France in such alliance Would more have strengthened this our commonwealth 'Gainst foreign storms than any home-bred marriage.
USER: Why, knows not Montague that of itself England is safe, if true within itself?
BOT: But the safer when 'tis backed with France.
USER: 'Tis better using France than trusting France. Let us be backed with God and with the seas Which He hath giv'n for fence impregnable, And with their helps only defend ourselves. In them and in ourselves our safety lies.
BOT: For this one speech Lord Hastings well deserves To have the heir of the Lord Hungerford.
USER: Ay, what of that? It was my will and grant; And for this once my will shall stand for law.
BOT: And yet, methinks, your Grace hath not done well To give the heir and daughter of Lord Scales Unto the brother of your loving bride. She better would have fitted me or Clarence; But in your bride you bury brotherhood.
USER: Or else you would not have bestowed the heir Of the Lord Bonville on your new wife's son, And leave your brothers to go speed elsewhere.
BOT: Alas, poor Clarence, is it for a wife That thou art malcontent? I will provide thee.
USER: In choosing for yourself you showed your judgment, Which being shallow, you shall give me leave To play the broker in mine own behalf; And to that end I shortly mind to leave you.
BOT: Leave me or tarry, Edward will be king, And not be tied unto his brother's will.
USER: Now, messenger, what letters or what news From France?
BOT: My sovereign liege, no letters, and few words, But such as I, without your special pardon, Dare not relate.
USER: Go to, we pardon thee. Therefore, in brief, Tell me their words as near as thou canst guess them. What answer makes King Lewis unto our letters?
BOT: At my depart these were his very words: "Go tell false Edward, thy supposed king, That Lewis of France is sending over maskers To revel it with him and his new bride."
USER: Is Lewis so brave? Belike he thinks me Henry. But what said Lady Bona to my marriage?
BOT: These were her words, uttered with mild disdain: "Tell him, in hope he'll prove a widower shortly, I'll wear the willow garland for his sake."
USER: I blame not her; she could say little less; She had the wrong. But what said Henry's queen? For I have heard that she was there in place.
BOT: "Tell him," quoth she "my mourning weeds are done, And I am ready to put armour on."
USER: Belike she minds to play the Amazon. But what said Warwick to these injuries?
BOT: He, more incensed against your Majesty Than all the rest, discharged me with these words: "Tell him from me that he hath done me wrong, And therefore I'll uncrown him ere 't be long."
USER: Ha! Durst the traitor breathe out so proud words? Well, I will arm me, being thus forewarned. They shall have wars and pay for their presumption. But say, is Warwick friends with Margaret?
BOT: Ay, gracious sovereign, they are so linked in friendship That young Prince Edward marries Warwick's daughter.
USER: I stay not for the love of Edward, but the crown.
BOT: So God help Montague as he proves true!
USER: And Hastings as he favours Edward's cause!
BOT: Now, brother Richard, will you stand by us?
USER: Ay, in despite of all that shall withstand you.
BOT: Why, so! Then am I sure of victory. Now, therefore, let us hence, and lose no hour Till we meet Warwick with his foreign power.

USER: Trust me, my lord, all hitherto goes well; The common people by numbers swarm to us. But see where Somerset and Clarence comes. Speak suddenly, my lords: are we all friends?
BOT: Fear not that, my lord.

USER: What are they that fly there?
BOT: Richard and Hastings. Let them go. Here is the Duke.
USER: The Duke? Why, Warwick, when we parted, Thou call'dst me king?
BOT: What fates impose, that men must needs abide; It boots not to resist both wind and tide.
USER: What now remains, my lords, for us to do, But march to London with our soldiers?
BOT: Ay, that's the first thing that we have to do, To free King Henry from imprisonment And see him seated in the regal throne.

USER: Madam, what makes you in this sudden change?
BOT: Why, brother Rivers, are you yet to learn What late misfortune is befall'n King Edward?
USER: What, loss of some pitched battle against Warwick?
BOT: No, but the loss of his own royal person.
USER: Then is my sovereign slain?
BOT: These news, I must confess, are full of grief; Yet, gracious madam, bear it as you may. Warwick may lose that now hath won the day.
USER: But, madam, where is Warwick then become?

USER: This way, my lord, for this way lies the game.
BOT: Nay, this way, man. See where the huntsmen stand. Now, brother of Gloucester, Lord Hastings, and the rest, Stand you thus close to steal the Bishop's deer?
USER: Brother, the time and case requireth haste; Your horse stands ready at the park corner.
BOT: But whither shall we then?
USER: To Lynn, my lord, and shipped from thence to Flanders.
BOT: Well guessed, believe me, for that was my meaning.
USER: Stanley, I will requite thy forwardness.
BOT: But wherefore stay we? 'Tis no time to talk.
USER: Huntsman, what sayst thou? Wilt thou go along?
BOT: Better do so than tarry and be hanged.
USER: Come then, away! Let's ha' no more ado.
BOT: Bishop, farewell; shield thee from Warwick's frown, And pray that I may repossess the crown.

USER: Master Lieutenant, now that God and friends Have shaken Edward from the regal seat And turned my captive state to liberty, My fear to hope, my sorrows unto joys, At our enlargement what are thy due fees?
BOT: Subjects may challenge nothing of their sovereigns; But if an humble prayer may prevail, I then crave pardon of your Majesty.
USER: No, Warwick, thou art worthy of the sway, To whom the heavens in thy nativity Adjudged an olive branch and laurel crown, As likely to be blest in peace and war; And therefore I yield thee my free consent.
BOT: And I choose Clarence only for Protector.
USER: What answers Clarence to his sovereign's will?
BOT: That he consents, if Warwick yield consent, For on thy fortune I repose myself.
USER: What else? And that succession be determined.
BOT: Ay, therein Clarence shall not want his part.
USER: It shall be done, my sovereign, with all speed.
BOT: My Lord of Somerset, what youth is that Of whom you seem to have so tender care?
USER: My liege, it is young Henry, Earl of Richmond.
BOT: What news, my friend?
USER: That Edward is escaped from your brother And fled, as he hears since, to Burgundy.
BOT: Unsavoury news! But how made he escape?
USER: He was conveyed by Richard, Duke of Gloucester And the Lord Hastings, who attended him In secret ambush on the forest side And from the Bishop's huntsmen rescued him, For hunting was his daily exercise.
BOT: My brother was too careless of his charge. But let us hence, my sovereign, to provide A salve for any sore that may betide.
USER: Ay, for if Edward repossess the crown, 'Tis like that Richmond with the rest shall down.
BOT: It shall be so. He shall to Brittany. Come therefore, let's about it speedily.

USER: The gates made fast! Brother, I like not this; For many men that stumble at the threshold Are well foretold that danger lurks within.
BOT: Tush, man, abodements must not now affright us. By fair or foul means we must enter in, For hither will our friends repair to us.
USER: My liege, I'll knock once more to summon them.
BOT: My lords, we were forewarned of your coming And shut the gates for safety of ourselves, For now we owe allegiance unto Henry.
USER: But, master Mayor, if Henry be your king, Yet Edward, at the least, is Duke of York.
BOT: True, my good lord, I know you for no less.
USER: Why, and I challenge nothing but my dukedom, As being well content with that alone.
BOT: He'll soon find means to make the body follow.
USER: Why, master Mayor, why stand you in a doubt? Open the gates; we are King Henry's friends.
BOT: Ay, say you so? The gates shall then be opened.
USER: A wise, stout captain, and soon persuaded.
BOT: The good old man would fain that all were well, So 'twere not long of him; but, being entered, I doubt not, I, but we shall soon persuade Both him and all his brothers unto reason.
USER: Brother, this is Sir John Montgomery, Our trusty friend unless I be deceived.
BOT: Welcome, Sir John! But why come you in arms?
USER: To help King Edward in his time of storm, As every loyal subject ought to do.
BOT: Thanks, good Montgomery; but we now forget Our title to the crown, and only claim Our dukedom till God please to send the rest.
USER: Then fare you well, for I will hence again. I came to serve a king, and not a duke. Drummer, strike up, and let us march away.
BOT: Nay, stay, Sir John, a while, and we'll debate By what safe means the crown may be recovered.
USER: What talk you of debating? In few words, If you'll not here proclaim yourself our king, I'll leave you to your fortune and be gone To keep them back that come to succour you. Why shall we fight if you pretend no title?
BOT: Why, brother, wherefore stand you on nice points?
USER: When we grow stronger, then we'll make our claim. Till then 'tis wisdom to conceal our meaning.
BOT: Away with scrupulous wit! Now arms must rule.
USER: And fearless minds climb soonest unto crowns. Brother, we will proclaim you out of hand; The bruit thereof will bring you many friends.
BOT: Then be it as you will; for 'tis my right, And Henry but usurps the diadem.
USER: Ay, now my sovereign speaketh like himself, And now will I be Edward's champion.
BOT: Sound, trumpet; Edward shall be here proclaimed. Come, fellow soldier, make thou proclamation.
USER: France, and Lord of Ireland, etc._
BOT: And whoso'er gainsays King Edward's right, By this I challenge him to single fight.
USER: Long live Edward the Fourth!

USER: What counsel, lords? Edward from Belgia, With hasty Germans and blunt Hollanders, Hath passed in safety through the Narrow Seas, And with his troops doth march amain to London; And many giddy people flock to him.
BOT: Let's levy men and beat him back again.
USER: A little fire is quickly trodden out, Which, being suffered, rivers cannot quench.
BOT: Farewell, my Hector, and my Troy's true hope.
USER: In sign of truth, I kiss your Highness' hand.
BOT: Well-minded Clarence, be thou fortunate.
USER: Comfort, my lord; and so I take my leave.
BOT: And thus [_kissing Henry's hand_] I seal my truth, and bid adieu.
USER: Sweet Oxford, and my loving Montague, And all at once, once more a happy farewell.
BOT: Farewell, sweet lords; let's meet at Coventry.
USER: Here at the palace will I rest a while. Cousin of Exeter, what thinks your lordship? Methinks the power that Edward hath in field Should not be able to encounter mine.
BOT: The doubt is that he will seduce the rest.
USER: Hark, hark, my lord, what shouts are these?
BOT: Away betimes, before his forces join, And take the great-grown traitor unawares. Brave warriors, march amain towards Coventry.

USER: Where is the post that came from valiant Oxford? How far hence is thy lord, mine honest fellow? 1 MESSENGER. By this at Dunsmore, marching hitherward.
BOT: How far off is our brother Montague? Where is the post that came from Montague? 2 MESSENGER. By this at Daintry, with a puissant troop.
USER: Say, Somerville, what says my loving son? And, by thy guess, how nigh is Clarence now?
BOT: At Southam I did leave him with his forces And do expect him here some two hours hence.
USER: Then Clarence is at hand; I hear his drum.
BOT: It is not his, my lord; here Southam lies. The drum your honour hears marcheth from Warwick.
USER: Who should that be? Belike, unlooked-for friends.
BOT: They are at hand, and you shall quickly know. March. Flourish. Enter King Edward, Richard (Duke of Gloucester) and Soldiers.
USER: Go, trumpet, to the walls and sound a parle.
BOT: See how the surly Warwick mans the wall.
USER: O, unbid spite! Is sportful Edward come? Where slept our scouts, or how are they seduced, That we could hear no news of his repair?
BOT: Now, Warwick, wilt thou ope the city gates, Speak gentle words and humbly bend thy knee? Call Edward King and at his hands beg mercy, And he shall pardon thee these outrages.
USER: Nay, rather, wilt thou draw thy forces hence, Confess who set thee up and plucked thee down, Call Warwick patron and be penitent, And thou shalt still remain the Duke of York.
BOT: I thought, at least, he would have said the King; Or did he make the jest against his will?
USER: Is not a dukedom, sir, a goodly gift?
BOT: Ay, by my faith, for a poor earl to give; I'll do thee service for so good a gift.
USER: 'Twas I that gave the kingdom to thy brother.
BOT: Why, then, 'tis mine, if but by Warwick's gift.
USER: Thou art no Atlas for so great a weight; And, weakling, Warwick takes his gift again; And Henry is my King, Warwick his subject.
BOT: But Warwick's king is Edward's prisoner; And, gallant Warwick, do but answer this: What is the body when the head is off?
USER: Alas, that Warwick had no more forecast, But, whiles he thought to steal the single ten, The king was slily fingered from the deck! You left poor Henry at the Bishop's palace, And ten to one you'll meet him in the Tower.
BOT: 'Tis even so; yet you are Warwick still.
USER: Come, Warwick, take the time; kneel down, kneel down. Nay, when? Strike now, or else the iron cools.
BOT: I had rather chop this hand off at a blow And with the other fling it at thy face, Than bear so low a sail to strike to thee.
USER: O cheerful colours! See where Oxford comes!
BOT: Oxford, Oxford, for Lancaster!
USER: The gates are open; let us enter too.
BOT: So other foes may set upon our backs. Stand we in good array, for they no doubt Will issue out again and bid us battle; If not, the city being but of small defence, We'll quietly rouse the traitors in the same.
USER: O, welcome, Oxford, for we want thy help.
BOT: Montague, Montague, for Lancaster!
USER: Thou and thy brother both shall buy this treason Even with the dearest blood your bodies bear.
BOT: The harder matched, the greater victory. My mind presageth happy gain and conquest.
USER: Somerset, Somerset, for Lancaster!
BOT: Two of thy name, both Dukes of Somerset, Have sold their lives unto the House of York; And thou shalt be the third if this sword hold.
USER: Now, welcome more, and ten times more beloved, Than if thou never hadst deserved our hate.
BOT: Welcome, good Clarence; this is brother-like.
USER: O passing traitor, perjured and unjust!
BOT: What, Warwick, wilt thou leave the town and fight? Or shall we beat the stones about thine ears?
USER: Alas! I am not cooped here for defence! I will away towards Barnet presently And bid thee battle, Edward, if thou dar'st.
BOT: Yes, Warwick, Edward dares, and leads the way. Lords, to the field! Saint George and victory!

USER: So, lie thou there. Die thou, and die our fear, For Warwick was a bug that feared us all. Now, Montague, sit fast; I seek for thee, That Warwick's bones may keep thine company.
BOT: Ah, Warwick, Warwick, wert thou as we are, We might recover all our loss again. The Queen from France hath brought a puissant power; Even now we heard the news. Ah, couldst thou fly!
USER: Sweet rest his soul! Fly, lords, and save yourselves, For Warwick bids you all farewell, to meet in heaven.
BOT: Away, away, to meet the Queen's great power!

USER: A little gale will soon disperse that cloud And blow it to the source from whence it came; Thy very beams will dry those vapours up, For every cloud engenders not a storm.
BOT: The Queen is valued thirty thousand strong, And Somerset, with Oxford, fled to her. If she have time to breathe, be well assured Her faction will be full as strong as ours.

USER: And he that will not fight for such a hope, Go home to bed and, like the owl by day, If he arise, be mocked and wondered at.
BOT: Thanks, gentle Somerset. Sweet Oxford, thanks.
USER: And take his thanks that yet hath nothing else.
BOT: Prepare you, lords, for Edward is at hand Ready to fight; therefore be resolute.
USER: I thought no less. It is his policy To haste thus fast, to find us unprovided.
BOT: But he's deceived; we are in readiness.
USER: This cheers my heart, to see your forwardness.
BOT: Here pitch our battle; hence we will not budge. Flourish and march. Enter King Edward, Richard, George and Soldiers.

USER: Now here a period of tumultuous broils. Away with Oxford to Hames Castle straight. For Somerset, off with his guilty head. Go, bear them hence; I will not hear them speak.
BOT: For my part, I'll not trouble thee with words.
USER: Nor I, but stoop with patience to my fortune.
BOT: So part we sadly in this troublous world, To meet with joy in sweet Jerusalem.
USER: Is proclamation made that who finds Edward Shall have a high reward, and he his life?
BOT: It is, and lo where youthful Edward comes.
USER: Ah, thy father had been so resolved!
BOT: That you might still have worn the petticoat And ne'er have stol'n the breech from Lancaster.
USER: Let Aesop fable in a winter's night; His currish riddle sorts not with this place.
BOT: By heaven, brat, I'll plague you for that word.
USER: Ay, thou wast born to be a plague to men.
BOT: For God's sake, take away this captive scold.
USER: Nay, take away this scolding crookback rather.
BOT: Peace, wilful boy, or I will charm your tongue.
USER: Untutored lad, thou art too malapert.
BOT: I know my duty; you are all undutiful. Lascivious Edward, and thou perjured George, And thou misshapen Dick, I tell ye all I am your better, traitors as ye are, And thou usurp'st my father's right and mine.
USER: Take that, the likeness of this railer here.
BOT: Sprawl'st thou? Take that to end thy agony.
USER: And there's for twitting me with perjury.
BOT: O, kill me too!
USER: Marry, and shall.
BOT: Hold, Richard, hold; for we have done too much.
USER: Why should she live to fill the world with words?
BOT: What, doth she swoon? Use means for her recovery.
USER: Clarence, excuse me to the King my brother. I'll hence to London on a serious matter. Ere ye come there, be sure to hear some news.
BOT: What? What?
USER: The Tower, the Tower!
BOT: Away with her; go bear her hence perforce.
USER: Nay, never bear me hence, dispatch me here. Here sheathe thy sword; I'll pardon thee my death. What, wilt thou not? Then, Clarence, do it thou.
BOT: By heaven, I will not do thee so much ease.
USER: Good Clarence, do; sweet Clarence, do thou do it.
BOT: Didst thou not hear me swear I would not do it?
USER: Away, I say! I charge ye, bear her hence.
BOT: So come to you and yours as to this prince!
USER: Where's Richard gone?
BOT: To London all in post, and, as I guess, To make a bloody supper in the Tower.
USER: He's sudden if a thing comes in his head. Now march we hence. Discharge the common sort With pay and thanks, and let's away to London And see our gentle Queen how well she fares. By this, I hope, she hath a son for me.

USER: Good day, my lord. What, at your book so hard?
BOT: Ay, my good lord - my lord, I should say rather. 'Tis sin to flatter; "good" was little better: "Good Gloucester" and "good devil" were alike, And both preposterous; therefore, not "good lord".
USER: Sirrah, leave us to ourselves; we must confer.
BOT: So flies the reckless shepherd from the wolf; So first the harmless sheep doth yield his fleece, And next his throat unto the butcher's knife. What scene of death hath Roscius now to act?
USER: Suspicion always haunts the guilty mind; The thief doth fear each bush an officer.
BOT: The bird that hath been limed in a bush With trembling wings misdoubteth every bush; And I, the hapless male to one sweet bird, Have now the fatal object in my eye Where my poor young was limed, was caught, and killed.
USER: Why, what a peevish fool was that of Crete That taught his son the office of a fowl! And yet, for all his wings, the fool was drowned.
BOT: Think'st thou I am an executioner?
USER: A persecutor I am sure thou art. If murdering innocents be executing, Why, then thou art an executioner.
BOT: Thy son I killed for his presumption.
USER: I'll hear no more. Die, prophet, in thy speech. For this, amongst the rest, was I ordained.
BOT: Ay, and for much more slaughter after this. O God, forgive my sins, and pardon thee!

USER: For yet I am not looked on in the world. This shoulder was ordained so thick to heave, And heave it shall some weight or break my back. Work thou the way, and that shall execute.
BOT: Clarence and Gloucester, love my lovely Queen; And kiss your princely nephew, brothers both.
USER: The duty that I owe unto your Majesty I seal upon the lips of this sweet babe.
BOT: Thanks, noble Clarence; worthy brother, thanks.
USER: And, that I love the tree from whence thou sprang'st, Witness the loving kiss I give the fruit. And cried "All hail!" when as he meant all harm.
BOT: Now am I seated as my soul delights, Having my country's peace and brothers' loves.
USER: What will your Grace have done with Margaret? Reignier, her father, to the King of France Hath pawned the Sicils and Jerusalem, And hither have they sent it for her ransom.

USER: Good morrow, and well met. How have ye done Since last we saw in France?
BOT: I thank your Grace, Healthful, and ever since a fresh admirer Of what I saw there.
USER: An untimely ague Stayed me a prisoner in my chamber when Those suns of glory, those two lights of men, Met in the vale of Andren.
BOT: All the whole time I was my chamber's prisoner.
USER: O, you go far.
BOT: Who did guide, I mean, who set the body and the limbs Of this great sport together, as you guess?
USER: One, certes, that promises no element In such a business.
BOT: I pray you who, my lord?
USER: All this was ordered by the good discretion Of the right reverend Cardinal of York.
BOT: I do know Kinsmen of mine, three at the least, that have By this so sickened their estates that never They shall abound as formerly.
USER: O, many Have broke their backs with laying manors on 'em For this great journey. What did this vanity But minister communication of A most poor issue?
BOT: Grievingly I think The peace between the French and us not values The cost that did conclude it.
USER: Every man, After the hideous storm that followed, was A thing inspired and, not consulting, broke Into a general prophecy, that this tempest, Dashing the garment of this peace, aboded The sudden breach on't.
BOT: Which is budded out, For France hath flawed the league, and hath attached Our merchants' goods at Bordeaux.
USER: Is it therefore Th' ambassador is silenced?
BOT: Marry, is't.
USER: A proper title of a peace, and purchased At a superfluous rate!
BOT: Why, all this business Our reverend Cardinal carried.
USER: The Duke of Buckingham's surveyor, ha? Where's his examination?
BOT: Here, so please you.
USER: Is he in person ready?
BOT: Ay, please your Grace.
USER: Well, we shall then know more, and Buckingham Shall lessen this big look.
BOT: This butcher's cur is venom-mouthed, and I Have not the power to muzzle him; therefore best Not wake him in his slumber. A beggar's book Outworths a noble's blood.
USER: What, are you chafed? Ask God for temp'rance. That's the appliance only Which your disease requires.
BOT: I read in 's looks Matter against me, and his eye reviled Me as his abject object. At this instant He bores me with some trick. He's gone to th' King. I'll follow, and outstare him.
USER: I'll to the King, And from a mouth of honour quite cry down This Ipswich fellow's insolence, or proclaim There's difference in no persons.
BOT: Say not "treasonous."
USER: Faith, and so it did.
BOT: I am sorry To hear this of him, and could wish he were Something mistaken in't.
USER: No, not a syllable. I do pronounce him in that very shape He shall appear in proof. Guard.
BOT: Your office, sergeant: execute it.
USER: Sir, My lord the Duke of Buckingham, and Earl Of Hereford, Stafford, and Northampton, I Arrest thee of high treason, in the name Of our most sovereign King.
BOT: Lo you, my lord, The net has fall'n upon me. I shall perish Under device and practice.
USER: I am sorry To see you ta'en from liberty, to look on The business present. 'Tis his Highness' pleasure You shall to th' Tower.
BOT: It will help nothing To plead mine innocence, for that dye is on me Which makes my whit'st part black. The will of heaven Be done in this and all things. I obey. O my Lord Abergavenny, fare you well.
USER: Nay, he must bear you company. Is pleased you shall to th' Tower, till you know How he determines further.
BOT: As the Duke said, The will of heaven be done, and the King's pleasure By me obeyed.
USER: Here is warrant from The King t' attach Lord Montague, and the bodies Of the Duke's confessor, John de la Car, One Gilbert Peck, his chancellor -
BOT: So, so; These are the limbs o' th' plot. No more, I hope?
USER: A monk o' th' Chartreux.
BOT: O, Nicholas Hopkins?
USER: He.

USER: Nay, we must longer kneel; I am a suitor.
BOT: Arise, and take place by us. Half your suit Never name to us; you have half our power; The other moiety ere you ask is given. Repeat your will and take it.
USER: Thank your Majesty. That you would love yourself, and in that love Not unconsidered leave your honour nor The dignity of your office, is the point Of my petition.
BOT: Lady mine, proceed.
USER: Taxation? Wherein? And what taxation? My Lord Cardinal, You that are blamed for it alike with us, Know you of this taxation?
BOT: Please you, sir, I know but of a single part in aught Pertains to th' state, and front but in that file Where others tell steps with me.
USER: Still exaction! The nature of it? In what kind, let's know, Is this exaction?
BOT: By my life, This is against our pleasure.
USER: I am sorry that the Duke of Buckingham Is run in your displeasure.
BOT: Stand forth, and with bold spirit relate what you, Most like a careful subject, have collected Out of the Duke of Buckingham.
USER: Speak freely.
BOT: Please your Highness, note This dangerous conception in this point, Not friended by his wish to your high person His will is most malignant, and it stretches Beyond you to your friends.
USER: My learned Lord Cardinal, Deliver all with charity.
BOT: Speak on. How grounded he his title to the crown? Upon our fail? To this point hast thou heard him At any time speak aught?
USER: He was brought to this By a vain prophecy of Nicholas Henton.
BOT: What was that Henton?
USER: Sir, a Chartreux friar, His confessor, who fed him every minute With words of sovereignty.
BOT: How know'st thou this?
USER: Let him on. Go forward.
BOT: Ha! What, so rank? Ah ha! There's mischief in this man. Canst thou say further?
USER: I can, my liege.
BOT: Proceed.
USER: Being at Greenwich, After your Highness had reproved the Duke About Sir William Bulmer -
BOT: I remember Of such a time, being my sworn servant, The Duke retained him his. But on. What hence?
USER: A giant traitor!
BOT: Now, madam, may his Highness live in freedom, And this man out of prison?
USER: God mend all.
BOT: There's something more would out of thee. What sayst?
USER: There's his period, To sheathe his knife in us. He is attached. Call him to present trial. If he may Find mercy in the law, 'tis his; if none, Let him not seek 't of us. By day and night, He's traitor to th' height!

USER: Is't possible the spells of France should juggle Men into such strange mysteries?
BOT: New customs, Though they be never so ridiculous - Nay, let 'em be unmanly - yet are followed.
USER: They have all new legs, and lame ones. One would take it, That never saw 'em pace before, the spavin Or springhalt reigned among 'em.
BOT: Death! My lord, Their clothes are after such a pagan cut to't, That, sure, they've worn out Christendom. How now? What news, Sir Thomas Lovell?
USER: Faith, my lord, I hear of none but the new proclamation That's clapped upon the court gate.
BOT: What is't for?
USER: The reformation of our travelled gallants That fill the court with quarrels, talk, and tailors.
BOT: I'm glad 'tis there. Now I would pray our monsieurs To think an English courtier may be wise And never see the Louvre.
USER: 'Tis time to give 'em physic, their diseases Are grown so catching.
BOT: What a loss our ladies Will have of these trim vanities!
USER: Ay, marry, There will be woe indeed, lords. The sly whoresons Have got a speeding trick to lay down ladies. A French song and a fiddle has no fellow.
BOT: Well said, Lord Sandys. Your colt's tooth is not cast yet.
USER: No, my lord, Nor shall not while I have a stump.
BOT: Sir Thomas, Whither were you a-going?
USER: To the Cardinal's. Your lordship is a guest too.
BOT: O, 'tis true. This night he makes a supper, and a great one, To many lords and ladies. There will be The beauty of this kingdom, I'll assure you.
USER: That churchman bears a bounteous mind indeed, A hand as fruitful as the land that feeds us. His dews fall everywhere.
BOT: No doubt he's noble; He had a black mouth that said other of him.
USER: He may, my lord; has wherewithal. In him Sparing would show a worse sin than ill doctrine. Men of his way should be most liberal; They are set here for examples.
BOT: I am your lordship's.

USER: You are young, Sir Harry Guildford.
BOT: Sir Thomas Lovell, had the Cardinal But half my lay thoughts in him, some of these Should find a running banquet ere they rested, I think would better please 'em. By my life, They are a sweet society of fair ones.
USER: O, that your lordship were but now confessor To one or two of these!
BOT: I would I were. They should find easy penance.
USER: Faith, how easy?
BOT: As easy as a down bed would afford it.
USER: By my faith, And thank your lordship. By your leave, sweet ladies. If I chance to talk a little wild, forgive me; I had it from my father.
BOT: Was he mad, sir?
USER: O, very mad, exceeding mad in love too; But he would bite none. Just as I do now, He would kiss you twenty with a breath.
BOT: Well said, my lord. So, now you're fairly seated. gentlemen, The penance lies on you if these fair ladies Pass away frowning.
USER: For my little cure, Let me alone. Hautboys. Enter Cardinal Wolsey and takes his state.
BOT: You're welcome, my fair guests. That noble lady Or gentleman that is not freely merry Is not my friend. This, to confirm my welcome; And to you all, good health.
USER: Your Grace is noble. Let me have such a bowl may hold my thanks And save me so much talking.
BOT: My Lord Sandys, I am beholding to you. Cheer your neighbours. Ladies, you are not merry. Gentlemen, Whose fault is this?
USER: The red wine first must rise In their fair cheeks, my lord; then we shall have 'em Talk us to silence.
BOT: You are a merry gamester, My Lord Sandys.
USER: Yes, if I make my play. Here's to your ladyship; and pledge it, madam, For 'tis to such a thing -
BOT: You cannot show me.
USER: I told your Grace they would talk anon.
BOT: What's that?
USER: Look out there, some of ye.
BOT: What warlike voice, And to what end, is this? Nay, ladies, fear not. By all the laws of war you're privileged.
USER: How now, what is't?
BOT: A noble troop of strangers, For so they seem. They've left their barge and landed, And hither make, as great ambassadors From foreign princes.
USER: Say, Lord Chamberlain, They have done my poor house grace; for which I pay 'em A thousand thanks and pray 'em take their pleasures.
BOT: The fairest hand I ever touched! O beauty, Till now I never knew thee.
USER: My lord!
BOT: Your Grace?
USER: Pray tell 'em thus much from me: There should be one amongst 'em, by his person More worthy this place than myself, to whom, If I but knew him, with my love and duty I would surrender it.
BOT: I will, my lord.
USER: What say they?
BOT: Such a one they all confess There is indeed, which they would have your Grace Find out, and he will take it.
USER: Let me see, then. By all your good leaves, gentlemen; here I'll make My royal choice.
BOT: You hold a fair assembly; you do well, lord. You are a churchman, or I'll tell you, Cardinal, I should judge now unhappily.
USER: I am glad Your Grace is grown so pleasant.
BOT: My Lord Chamberlain, Prithee come hither. What fair lady's that?
USER: An't please your Grace, Sir Thomas Bullen's daughter, The Viscount Rochford, one of her Highness' women.
BOT: By heaven, she is a dainty one. Sweetheart, I were unmannerly to take you out And not to kiss you. A health, gentlemen! Let it go round.
USER: Sir Thomas Lovell, is the banquet ready I' th' privy chamber?
BOT: Yes, my lord.
USER: Your Grace, I fear, with dancing is a little heated.
BOT: I fear, too much.
USER: There's fresher air, my lord, In the next chamber.

USER: Whither away so fast?
BOT: O, God save ye. Even to the Hall, to hear what shall become Of the great Duke of Buckingham.
USER: I'll save you That labour, sir. All's now done but the ceremony Of bringing back the prisoner.
BOT: Were you there?
USER: Yes, indeed, was I.
BOT: Pray speak what has happened.
USER: You may guess quickly what.
BOT: Is he found guilty?
USER: Yes, truly is he, and condemned upon't.
BOT: I am sorry for't.
USER: So are a number more.
BOT: But pray, how passed it?
USER: That was he That fed him with his prophecies?
BOT: After all this, how did he bear himself?
USER: I do not think he fears death.
BOT: Sure he does not; He never was so womanish. The cause He may a little grieve at.
USER: Certainly The Cardinal is the end of this.
BOT: 'Tis likely, By all conjectures: first, Kildare's attainder, Then deputy of Ireland, who removed, Earl Surrey was sent thither, and in haste too, Lest he should help his father.
USER: That trick of state Was a deep envious one.
BOT: At his return No doubt he will requite it. This is noted, And generally, whoever the King favours, The Cardinal instantly will find employment, And far enough from court too.
USER: Stay there, sir, And see the noble ruined man you speak of.
BOT: Let's stand close and behold him.
USER: I do beseech your Grace, for charity, If ever any malice in your heart Were hid against me, now to forgive me frankly.
BOT: To th' waterside I must conduct your Grace, Then give my charge up to Sir Nicholas Vaux, Who undertakes you to your end.
USER: Prepare there! The Duke is coming. See the barge be ready, And fit it with such furniture as suits The greatness of his person.
BOT: O, this is full of pity. Sir, it calls, I fear, too many curses on their heads That were the authors.
USER: If the Duke be guiltless, 'Tis full of woe. Yet I can give you inkling Of an ensuing evil, if it fall, Greater than this.
BOT: Good angels keep it from us! What may it be? You do not doubt my faith, sir?
USER: This secret is so weighty, 'twill require A strong faith to conceal it.
BOT: Let me have it. I do not talk much.
USER: I am confident; You shall, sir. Did you not of late days hear A buzzing of a separation Between the King and Katherine?
BOT: Yes, but it held not; For when the King once heard it, out of anger He sent command to the Lord Mayor straight To stop the rumour and allay those tongues That durst disperse it.
USER: 'Tis the Cardinal; And merely to revenge him on the Emperor For not bestowing on him at his asking, The archbishopric of Toledo this is purposed.
BOT: I think you have hit the mark. But is't not cruel That she should feel the smart of this? The Cardinal Will have his will, and she must fall.
USER: 'Tis woeful. We are too open here to argue this. Let's think in private more.

USER: Well met, my Lord Chamberlain.
BOT: Good day to both your Graces.
USER: How is the King employed?
BOT: I left him private, Full of sad thoughts and troubles.
USER: What's the cause?
BOT: It seems the marriage with his brother's wife Has crept too near his conscience.
USER: No, his conscience Has crept too near another lady.
BOT: 'Tis so. This is the Cardinal's doing, the king-cardinal. That blind priest, like the eldest son of Fortune, Turns what he list. The King will know him one day.
USER: Pray God he do! He'll never know himself else.
BOT: And free us from his slavery.
USER: We had need pray, And heartily, for our deliverance, Or this imperious man will work us all From princes into pages. All men's honours Lie like one lump before him, to be fashioned Into what pitch he please.
BOT: Let's in, And with some other business put the King From these sad thoughts that work too much upon him. My lord, you'll bear us company?
USER: Excuse me; The King has sent me otherwhere. Besides, You'll find a most unfit time to disturb him. Health to your lordships.
BOT: Thanks, my good Lord Chamberlain. reading pensively._]
USER: How sad he looks! Sure, he is much afflicted.
BOT: Who's there? Ha?
USER: Pray God he be not angry.
BOT: Who's there, I say? How dare you thrust yourselves Into my private meditations? Who am I? Ha?
USER: A gracious king that pardons all offences Malice ne'er meant. Our breach of duty this way Is business of estate, in which we come To know your royal pleasure.
BOT: Sir, you cannot. I would your Grace would give us but an hour Of private conference.
USER: I would not be so sick, though, for his place. But this cannot continue.
BOT: I'll venture one have-at-him.
USER: And once more in mine arms I bid him welcome, And thank the holy conclave for their loves. They have sent me such a man I would have wished for.
BOT: Two equal men. The Queen shall be acquainted Forthwith for what you come. Where's Gardiner?
USER: I know your Majesty has always loved her So dear in heart not to deny her that A woman of less place might ask by law: Scholars allowed freely to argue for her.
BOT: Ay, and the best she shall have, and my favour To him that does best. God forbid else. Cardinal, Prithee call Gardiner to me, my new secretary. I find him a fit fellow.
USER: Give me your hand. Much joy and favour to you; You are the King's now.
BOT: For ever by your Grace, whose hand has raised me.
USER: Come hither, Gardiner.
BOT: My lord of York, was not one Doctor Pace In this man's place before him?
USER: Yes, he was.
BOT: Was he not held a learned man?
USER: Yes, surely.
BOT: Believe me, there's an ill opinion spread, then Even of yourself, Lord Cardinal.
USER: How? Of me?
BOT: They will not stick to say you envied him And fearing he would rise - he was so virtuous - Kept him a foreign man still, which so grieved him That he ran mad and died.

USER: Hearts of most hard temper Melt and lament for her.
BOT: O, God's will! Much better She ne'er had known pomp; though't be temporal, Yet if that quarrel, Fortune, do divorce It from the bearer, 'tis a sufferance panging As soul and body's severing.
USER: Alas, poor lady, She's a stranger now again.
BOT: So much the more Must pity drop upon her. Verily, I swear, 'tis better to be lowly born And range with humble livers in content Than to be perked up in a glist'ring grief, And wear a golden sorrow.
USER: Our content Is our best having.
BOT: By my troth and maidenhead, I would not be a queen.
USER: Nay, good troth.
BOT: Yes, troth and troth. You would not be a queen?
USER: No, not for all the riches under heaven.
BOT: 'Tis strange. A threepence bowed would hire me, Old as I am, to queen it. But I pray you, What think you of a duchess? Have you limbs To bear that load of title?
USER: No, in truth.
BOT: Then you are weakly made. Pluck off a little. I would not be a young count in your way For more than blushing comes to. If your back Cannot vouchsafe this burden, 'tis too weak Ever to get a boy.
