USER: How you do talk! I swear again I would not be a queen For all the world.
BOT: In faith, for little England You'd venture an emballing. I myself Would for Caernarfonshire, although there longed No more to th' crown but that. Lo, who comes here?
USER: Good morrow, ladies. What were't worth to know The secret of your conference?
BOT: My good lord, Not your demand; it values not your asking. Our mistress' sorrows we were pitying.
USER: It was a gentle business, and becoming The action of good women. There is hope All will be well.
BOT: Now, I pray God, amen!
USER: My honoured lord.
BOT: This is strange to me.
USER: How tastes it? Is it bitter? Forty pence, no. There was a lady once - 'tis an old story - That would not be a queen, that would she not, For all the mud in Egypt. Have you heard it?
BOT: Come, you are pleasant.
USER: What do you think me?

USER: Whilst our commission from Rome is read, Let silence be commanded.
BOT: What's the need? It hath already publicly been read, And on all sides th' authority allowed; You may then spare that time.
USER: Be't so. Proceed.
BOT: Say, "Henry King of England, come into the court."
USER: Henry King of England, come into the court.
BOT: Here.
USER: Say, "Katherine Queen of England, come into the court."
BOT: Katherine Queen of England, come into the court. court, comes to the King, and kneels at his feet; then speaks._]
USER: His Grace Hath spoken well and justly. Therefore, madam, It's fit this royal session do proceed, And that without delay their arguments Be now produced and heard.
BOT: Lord Cardinal, To you I speak.
USER: Your pleasure, madam.
BOT: Sir, I am about to weep; but, thinking that We are a queen, or long have dreamed so, certain The daughter of a king, my drops of tears I'll turn to sparks of fire.
USER: Be patient yet.
BOT: The Queen is obstinate, Stubborn to justice, apt to accuse it, and Disdainful to be tried by't. 'Tis not well. She's going away.
USER: Call her again.
BOT: Katherine, Queen of England, come into the court.
USER: Madam, you are called back.
BOT: Very well, my liege.
USER: I have spoke long. Be pleased yourself to say How far you satisfied me.

USER: How now?
BOT: An't please your Grace, the two great Cardinals Wait in the presence.
USER: Would they speak with me?
BOT: They willed me say so, madam.
USER: Peace to your Highness.
BOT: Your Graces find me here part of housewife; I would be all, against the worst may happen. What are your pleasures with me, reverend lords?
USER: May it please you, noble madam, to withdraw Into your private chamber, we shall give you The full cause of our coming.
BOT: _Tanta est erga te mentis integritas, regina serenissima_ -
USER: Madam, you wrong the King's love with these fears; Your hopes and friends are infinite.
BOT: I would your Grace Would leave your griefs and take my counsel.
USER: How, sir?
BOT: Put your main cause into the King's protection. He's loving and most gracious. 'Twill be much Both for your honour better and your cause, For if the trial of the law o'ertake ye, You'll part away disgraced.
USER: He tells you rightly.
BOT: Ye tell me what ye wish for both: my ruin. Is this your Christian counsel? Out upon ye! Heaven is above all yet; there sits a judge That no king can corrupt.
USER: Your rage mistakes us.
BOT: Madam, this is a mere distraction. You turn the good we offer into envy.
USER: Your fears are worse.
BOT: Madam, you wander from the good we aim at.
USER: My lord, I dare not make myself so guilty To give up willingly that noble title Your master wed me to. Nothing but death Shall e'er divorce my dignities.
BOT: Pray hear me.

USER: I am joyful To meet the least occasion that may give me Remembrance of my father-in-law the Duke, To be revenged on him.
BOT: Which of the peers Have uncontemned gone by him, or at least Strangely neglected? When did he regard The stamp of nobleness in any person Out of himself?
USER: O, fear him not. His spell in that is out. The King hath found Matter against him that for ever mars The honey of his language. No, he's settled, Not to come off, in his displeasure.
BOT: Sir, I should be glad to hear such news as this Once every hour.
USER: Believe it, this is true. In the divorce his contrary proceedings Are all unfolded, wherein he appears As I would wish mine enemy.
BOT: How came His practices to light?
USER: Most strangely.
BOT: O, how, how?
USER: Has the King this?
BOT: Believe it.
USER: Will this work?
BOT: The King in this perceives him how he coasts And hedges his own way. But in this point All his tricks founder, and he brings his physic After his patient's death. The King already Hath married the fair lady.
USER: Would he had!
BOT: May you be happy in your wish, my lord, For I profess you have it.
USER: Now, all my joy Trace the conjunction!
BOT: My amen to't!
USER: All men's.
BOT: But will the King Digest this letter of the Cardinal's? The Lord forbid!
USER: Marry, amen!
BOT: Now, God incense him, And let him cry "Ha!" louder.
USER: But, my lord, When returns Cranmer?
BOT: This same Cranmer's A worthy fellow, and hath ta'en much pain In the King's business.
USER: He has, and we shall see him For it an archbishop.
BOT: So I hear.
USER: 'Tis so. The Cardinal!
BOT: Observe, observe; he's moody.
USER: The packet, Cromwell, Gave't you the King?
BOT: To his own hand, in 's bedchamber.
USER: Looked he o' th' inside of the paper?
BOT: Presently He did unseal them, and the first he viewed, He did it with a serious mind; a heed Was in his countenance. You he bade Attend him here this morning.
USER: Is he ready To come abroad?
BOT: I think by this he is.
USER: He's discontented.
BOT: Maybe he hears the King Does whet his anger to him.
USER: Sharp enough, Lord, for thy justice!
BOT: He is vexed at something.
USER: I would 'twere something that would fret the string, The master-cord on 's heart.
BOT: The King, the King!
USER: What piles of wealth hath he accumulated To his own portion! And what expense by th' hour Seems to flow from him! How, i' th' name of thrift Does he rake this together? Now, my lords, Saw you the Cardinal?
BOT: It's heaven's will! Some spirit put this paper in the packet To bless your eye withal.
USER: If we did think His contemplation were above the earth And fixed on spiritual object, he should still Dwell in his musings, but I am afraid His thinkings are below the moon, not worth His serious considering.
BOT: Heaven forgive me! Ever God bless your Highness.
USER: You have said well.
BOT: And ever may your Highness yoke together, As I will lend you cause, my doing well With my well saying.
USER: Have I not made you The prime man of the state? I pray you tell me, If what I now pronounce you have found true, And, if you may confess it, say withal If you are bound to us or no. What say you?
BOT: 'Tis nobly spoken. Take notice, lords: he has a loyal breast, For you have seen him open't. Read o'er this, And after, this; and then to breakfast with What appetite you have. smiling and whispering._]
USER: Hear the King's pleasure, Cardinal, who commands you To render up the great seal presently Into our hands, and to confine yourself To Asher House, my Lord of Winchester's, Till you hear further from his Highness.
BOT: Stay. Where's your commission, lords? Words cannot carry Authority so weighty.
USER: Who dares cross 'em, Bearing the King's will from his mouth expressly?
BOT: The King that gave it.
USER: It must be himself, then.
BOT: Thou art a proud traitor, priest.
USER: Proud lord, thou liest. Within these forty hours Surrey durst better Have burnt that tongue than said so.
BOT: All goodness Is poison to thy stomach.
USER: How much, methinks, I could despise this man, But that I am bound in charity against it!
BOT: Those articles, my lord, are in the King's hand; But thus much, they are foul ones.
USER: So much fairer And spotless shall mine innocence arise When the King knows my truth.
BOT: This cannot save you. I thank my memory I yet remember Some of these articles, and out they shall. Now, if you can blush and cry "Guilty," Cardinal, You'll show a little honesty.
USER: Speak on, sir; I dare your worst objections. If I blush, It is to see a nobleman want manners.
BOT: I had rather want those than my head. Have at you! First, that without the King's assent or knowledge, You wrought to be a legate, by which power You maimed the jurisdiction of all bishops.
USER: Then, that in all you writ to Rome, or else To foreign princes, "_ego et rex meus_" Was still inscribed, in which you brought the King To be your servant.
BOT: Then, that without the knowledge Either of King or Council, when you went Ambassador to the Emperor, you made bold To carry into Flanders the great seal.
USER: Item, you sent a large commission To Gregory de Cassado, to conclude, Without the King's will or the state's allowance, A league between his Highness and Ferrara.
BOT: That out of mere ambition you have caused Your holy hat to be stamped on the King's coin.
USER: O my lord, Press not a falling man too far! 'Tis virtue. His faults lie open to the laws; let them, Not you, correct him. My heart weeps to see him So little of his great self.
BOT: I forgive him.
USER: I have no power to speak, sir.
BOT: What, amazed At my misfortunes? Can thy spirit wonder A great man should decline? Nay, an you weep, I am fallen indeed.
USER: How does your Grace?
BOT: I am glad your Grace has made that right use of it.
USER: I hope I have. I am able now, methinks, Out of a fortitude of soul I feel, To endure more miseries and greater far Than my weak-hearted enemies dare offer. What news abroad?
BOT: The heaviest and the worst Is your displeasure with the King.
USER: God bless him.
BOT: The next is that Sir Thomas More is chosen Lord Chancellor in your place.
USER: That Cranmer is returned with welcome, Installed Lord Archbishop of Canterbury.
BOT: That's news indeed.
USER: Last, that the Lady Anne, Whom the King hath in secrecy long married, This day was viewed in open as his Queen, Going to chapel, and the voice is now Only about her coronation.
BOT: Good sir, have patience.
USER: So I have. Farewell, The hopes of court! My hopes in heaven do dwell.

USER: You're well met once again.
BOT: So are you.
USER: You come to take your stand here and behold The Lady Anne pass from her coronation?
BOT: 'Tis all my business. At our last encounter, The Duke of Buckingham came from his trial.
USER: 'Tis very true. But that time offered sorrow, This, general joy.
BOT: 'Tis well. The citizens, I am sure, have shown at full their royal minds, As, let 'em have their rights, they are ever forward In celebration of this day with shows, Pageants, and sights of honour.
USER: Never greater, Nor, I'll assure you, better taken, sir.
BOT: May I be bold to ask what that contains, That paper in your hand?
USER: I thank you, sir. Had I not known those customs, I should have been beholding to your paper. But I beseech you, what's become of Katherine, The Princess Dowager? How goes her business?
BOT: A royal train, believe me. These I know. Who's that that bears the sceptre?
USER: Marquess Dorset, And that the Earl of Surrey with the rod.
BOT: A bold brave gentleman. That should be The Duke of Suffolk.
USER: 'Tis the same: High Steward.
BOT: And that my Lord of Norfolk?
USER: Yes.
BOT: Thou hast the sweetest face I ever looked on. Sir, as I have a soul, she is an angel. Our King has all the Indies in his arms, And more, and richer, when he strains that lady. I cannot blame his conscience.
USER: They that bear The cloth of honour over her are four barons Of the Cinque Ports.
BOT: Those men are happy, and so are all are near her. I take it she that carries up the train Is that old noble lady, Duchess of Norfolk.
USER: It is, and all the rest are countesses.
BOT: Their coronets say so. These are stars indeed.
USER: And sometimes falling ones.
BOT: No more of that. God save you, sir. Where have you been broiling?
USER: Among the crowds i' th' Abbey, where a finger Could not be wedged in more. I am stifled With the mere rankness of their joy.
BOT: You saw The ceremony?
USER: That I did.
BOT: How was it?
USER: Well worth the seeing.
BOT: Good sir, speak it to us.
USER: But what followed?
BOT: Sir, You must no more call it "York Place", that's past; For since the Cardinal fell, that title's lost. 'Tis now the King's, and called "Whitehall".
USER: I know it, But 'tis so lately altered that the old name Is fresh about me.
BOT: What two reverend bishops Were those that went on each side of the Queen?
USER: Stokesley and Gardiner, the one of Winchester, Newly preferred from the King's secretary; The other, London.
BOT: He of Winchester Is held no great good lover of the Archbishop's, The virtuous Cranmer.
USER: All the land knows that. However, yet there is no great breach. When it comes, Cranmer will find a friend will not shrink from him.
BOT: Who may that be, I pray you?
USER: Thomas Cromwell, A man in much esteem with th' King, and truly A worthy friend. The King has made him Master o' th' Jewel House, And one already of the Privy Council.
BOT: He will deserve more.
USER: Yes, without all doubt. Come, gentlemen, ye shall go my way, Which is to th' court, and there ye shall be my guests, Something I can command. As I walk thither, I'll tell ye more.
BOT: You may command us, sir.

USER: How does your Grace?
BOT: Yes, madam, but I think your Grace, Out of the pain you suffered, gave no ear to't.
USER: Prithee, good Griffith, tell me how he died. If well, he stepped before me happily For my example.
BOT: Well, the voice goes, madam. For after the stout Earl Northumberland Arrested him at York and brought him forward, As a man sorely tainted, to his answer, He fell sick suddenly and grew so ill He could not sit his mule.
USER: Alas, poor man!
BOT: Noble madam, Men's evil manners live in brass; their virtues We write in water. May it please your Highness To hear me speak his good now?
USER: Yes, good Griffith; I were malicious else.
BOT: Spirits of peace, where are ye? Are ye all gone, And leave me here in wretchedness behind ye?
USER: Madam, we are here.
BOT: It is not you I call for. Saw ye none enter since I slept?
USER: None, madam.
BOT: I am most joyful, madam, such good dreams Possess your fancy.
USER: Bid the music leave, They are harsh and heavy to me.
BOT: Do you note How much her Grace is altered on the sudden? How long her face is drawn? How pale she looks, And of an earthly cold? Mark her eyes.
USER: She is going, wench. Pray, pray.
BOT: Heaven comfort her!
USER: An't like your Grace -
BOT: You are a saucy fellow. Deserve we no more reverence?
USER: You are to blame, Knowing she will not lose her wonted greatness, To use so rude behaviour. Go to, kneel.
BOT: I humbly do entreat your Highness' pardon. My haste made me unmannerly. There is staying A gentleman sent from the King to see you.
USER: Admit him entrance, Griffith. But this fellow Let me ne'er see again. If my sight fail not, You should be lord ambassador from the Emperor, My royal nephew, and your name Caputius.
BOT: Madam, the same. Your servant.
USER: O my lord, The times and titles now are altered strangely With me since first you knew me. But I pray you, What is your pleasure with me?
BOT: O my good lord, that comfort comes too late; 'Tis like a pardon after execution. That gentle physic given in time had cured me, But now I am past all comforts here but prayers. How does his Highness?
USER: Madam, in good health.
BOT: So may he ever do, and ever flourish, When I shall dwell with worms, and my poor name Banished the kingdom. Patience, is that letter I caused you write yet sent away?
USER: No, madam.
BOT: Sir, I most humbly pray you to deliver This to my lord the King.
USER: Most willing, madam.
BOT: By heaven, I will, Or let me lose the fashion of a man!

USER: It's one o'clock, boy, is't not?
BOT: It hath struck.
USER: These should be hours for necessities, Not for delights; times to repair our nature With comforting repose, and not for us To waste these times. Good hour of night, Sir Thomas! Whither so late?
BOT: Came you from the King, my lord?
USER: I did, Sir Thomas, and left him at primero With the Duke of Suffolk.
BOT: I must to him too, Before he go to bed. I'll take my leave.
USER: My lord, I love you, And durst commend a secret to your ear Much weightier than this work. The Queen's in labour - They say in great extremity, and feared She'll with the labour end.
BOT: The fruit she goes with I pray for heartily, that it may find Good time, and live; but for the stock, Sir Thomas, I wish it grubbed up now.
USER: Methinks I could Cry the amen, and yet my conscience says She's a good creature and, sweet lady, does Deserve our better wishes.
BOT: Many good nights, my lord. I rest your servant.
USER: Charles, I will play no more tonight. My mind's not on't; you are too hard for me.
BOT: Sir, I did never win of you before.
USER: But little, Charles, Nor shall not, when my fancy's on my play. Now, Lovell, from the Queen what is the news?
BOT: I could not personally deliver to her What you commanded me, but by her woman I sent your message, who returned her thanks In the great'st humbleness, and desired your Highness Most heartily to pray for her.
USER: What sayst thou, ha? To pray for her? What, is she crying out?
BOT: So said her woman, and that her suff'rance made Almost each pang a death.
USER: Alas, good lady!
BOT: God safely quit her of her burden, and With gentle travail, to the gladding of Your Highness with an heir!
USER: 'Tis midnight, Charles. Prithee, to bed, and in thy prayers remember Th' estate of my poor Queen. Leave me alone, For I must think of that which company Will not be friendly to.
BOT: I wish your Highness A quiet night, and my good mistress will Remember in my prayers.
USER: Charles, good night. Well, sir, what follows?
BOT: Sir, I have brought my lord the Archbishop, As you commanded me.
USER: Ha! Canterbury?
BOT: Ay, my good lord.
USER: 'Tis true. Where is he, Denny?
BOT: He attends your Highness' pleasure.
USER: Bring him to us.
BOT: I am happily come hither.
USER: Avoid the gallery. [_Lovell seems to stay_.] Ha! I have said. Be gone. What!
BOT: 'Tis his aspect of terror. All's not well.
USER: How now, my lord? You do desire to know Wherefore I sent for you.
BOT: T' attend your Highness' pleasure.
USER: And am right glad to catch this good occasion Most throughly to be winnowed, where my chaff And corn shall fly asunder. For I know There's none stands under more calumnious tongues Than I myself, poor man.
BOT: God and your Majesty Protect mine innocence, or I fall into The trap is laid for me.
USER: I'll not come back. The tidings that I bring Will make my boldness manners. Now, good angels Fly o'er thy royal head and shade thy person Under their blessed wings!
BOT: Now by thy looks I guess thy message. Is the Queen delivered? Say "Ay, and of a boy".
USER: Lovell.
BOT: Sir?
USER: Give her an hundred marks. I'll to the Queen.

USER: I hope I am not too late, and yet the gentleman That was sent to me from the Council prayed me To make great haste. All fast? What means this? Ho! Who waits there? Sure you know me?
BOT: Yes, my lord, But yet I cannot help you.
USER: Why?
BOT: Your Grace must wait till you be called for. Enter Doctor Butts.
USER: So.
BOT: I came this way so happily. The King Shall understand it presently.
USER: I'll show your Grace the strangest sight.
BOT: What's that, Butts?
USER: I think your Highness saw this many a day.
BOT: Body o' me, where is it?
USER: There, my lord: The high promotion of his Grace of Canterbury, Who holds his state at door, 'mongst pursuivants, Pages, and footboys.
BOT: Speak to the business, master secretary. Why are we met in council?
USER: Please your honours, The chief cause concerns his Grace of Canterbury.
BOT: Has he had knowledge of it?
USER: Yes.
BOT: Who waits there?
USER: Without, my noble lords?
BOT: Yes.
USER: My lord Archbishop, And has done half an hour, to know your pleasures.
BOT: Let him come in.
USER: Your Grace may enter now. Cranmer approaches the council table.
BOT: Nay, my lord, That cannot be. You are a councillor, And by that virtue no man dare accuse you.
USER: My lord, my lord, you are a sectary, That's the plain truth. Your painted gloss discovers, To men that understand you, words and weakness.
BOT: My Lord of Winchester, you are a little, By your good favour, too sharp. Men so noble, However faulty, yet should find respect For what they have been. 'Tis a cruelty To load a falling man.
USER: Good master secretary, I cry your honour mercy: you may worst Of all this table say so.
BOT: Why, my lord?
USER: Do not I know you for a favourer Of this new sect? Ye are not sound.
BOT: Not sound?
USER: Not sound, I say.
BOT: Would you were half so honest! Men's prayers then would seek you, not their fears.
USER: I shall remember this bold language.
BOT: Do. Remember your bold life too.
USER: This is too much. Forbear, for shame, my lords.
BOT: I have done.
USER: And I.
BOT: We are.
USER: Is there no other way of mercy But I must needs to th' Tower, my lords?
BOT: What other Would you expect? You are strangely troublesome. Let some o' th' guard be ready there.
USER: For me? Must I go like a traitor thither?
BOT: Receive him, And see him safe i' th' Tower.
USER: Stay, good my lords, I have a little yet to say. Look there, my lords. By virtue of that ring, I take my cause Out of the gripes of cruel men and give it To a most noble judge, the King my master.
BOT: This is the King's ring.
USER: 'Tis no counterfeit.
BOT: 'Tis the right ring, by heaven! I told ye all, When we first put this dangerous stone a-rolling, 'Twould fall upon ourselves.
USER: Do you think, my lords, The King will suffer but the little finger Of this man to be vexed?
BOT: 'Tis now too certain. How much more is his life in value with him? Would I were fairly out on't!
USER: My mind gave me, In seeking tales and informations Against this man, whose honesty the devil And his disciples only envy at, Ye blew the fire that burns ye. Now have at ye!
BOT: May it please your Grace -
USER: The greatest monarch now alive may glory In such an honour. How may I deserve it, That am a poor and humble subject to you?
BOT: With a true heart And brother-love I do it.
USER: And let heaven Witness how dear I hold this confirmation.

USER: You'll leave your noise anon, ye rascals. Do you take the court for Parish Garden? Ye rude slaves, leave your gaping.
BOT: How got they in, and be hanged?
USER: Alas, I know not. How gets the tide in? As much as one sound cudgel of four foot - You see the poor remainder - could distribute, I made no spare, sir.
BOT: You did nothing, sir.
USER: I shall be with you presently, good master puppy. - Keep the door close, sirrah.
BOT: What would you have me do?
USER: An't please your honour, We are but men; and what so many may do, Not being torn a-pieces, we have done. An army cannot rule 'em.
BOT: Make way there for the Princess!
USER: You great fellow, Stand close up, or I'll make your head ache.
BOT: You i' th' camlet, get up o' th' rail! I'll peck you o'er the pales else.

USER: Heaven, from thy endless goodness, send prosperous life, long and ever happy, to the high and mighty Princess of England, Elizabeth. Flourish. Enter King and Guard.
BOT: My noble partners and myself thus pray All comfort, joy, in this most gracious lady Heaven ever laid up to make parents happy May hourly fall upon ye!
USER: Thank you, good lord Archbishop. What is her name?
BOT: Elizabeth.
USER: Stand up, lord. With this kiss take my blessing: God protect thee, Into whose hand I give thy life.
BOT: Amen.
USER: My noble gossips, you've have been too prodigal. I thank ye heartily; so shall this lady, When she has so much English.
BOT: Thou speakest wonders.

USER: Now, say, Chatillion, what would France with us?
BOT: Thus, after greeting, speaks the King of France In my behaviour to the majesty, The borrow'd majesty, of England here.
USER: A strange beginning: "borrow'd majesty"!
BOT: Silence, good mother; hear the embassy.
USER: What follows if we disallow of this?
BOT: The proud control of fierce and bloody war, To enforce these rights so forcibly withheld.
USER: Here have we war for war and blood for blood, Controlment for controlment: so answer France.
BOT: Then take my king's defiance from my mouth, The farthest limit of my embassy.
USER: Our strong possession and our right for us.
BOT: Your strong possession much more than your right, Or else it must go wrong with you and me: So much my conscience whispers in your ear, Which none but heaven and you and I shall hear.
USER: My liege, here is the strangest controversy, Come from the country to be judg'd by you, That e'er I heard. Shall I produce the men?
BOT: Let them approach. Our abbeys and our priories shall pay This expedition's charge. What men are you?
USER: Your faithful subject I, a gentleman Born in Northamptonshire, and eldest son, As I suppose, to Robert Faulconbridge, A soldier by the honour-giving hand Of Cœur-de-lion knighted in the field.
BOT: What art thou?
USER: The son and heir to that same Faulconbridge.
BOT: Is that the elder, and art thou the heir? You came not of one mother then, it seems.
USER: Most certain of one mother, mighty king; That is well known; and, as I think, one father. But for the certain knowledge of that truth I put you o'er to heaven and to my mother. Of that I doubt, as all men's children may.
BOT: Out on thee, rude man! Thou dost shame thy mother And wound her honour with this diffidence.
USER: I, madam? No, I have no reason for it; That is my brother's plea, and none of mine; The which if he can prove, he pops me out At least from fair five hundred pound a year. Heaven guard my mother's honour and my land!
BOT: A good blunt fellow. Why, being younger born, Doth he lay claim to thine inheritance?
USER: Why, what a madcap hath heaven lent us here!
BOT: He hath a trick of Cœur-de-lion's face; The accent of his tongue affecteth him. Do you not read some tokens of my son In the large composition of this man?
USER: Mine eye hath well examined his parts And finds them perfect Richard. Sirrah, speak, What doth move you to claim your brother's land?
BOT: Because he hath a half-face, like my father. With half that face would he have all my land: A half-fac'd groat five hundred pound a year!
USER: My gracious liege, when that my father liv'd, Your brother did employ my father much -
BOT: Well, sir, by this you cannot get my land. Your tale must be how he employ'd my mother.
USER: Shall then my father's will be of no force To dispossess that child which is not his?
BOT: Of no more force to dispossess me, sir, Than was his will to get me, as I think.
USER: Whether hadst thou rather be: a Faulconbridge And like thy brother, to enjoy thy land, Or the reputed son of Cœur-de-lion, Lord of thy presence and no land besides?
BOT: I like thee well. Wilt thou forsake thy fortune, Bequeath thy land to him, and follow me? I am a soldier and now bound to France.
USER: Brother, take you my land, I'll take my chance. Your face hath got five hundred pound a year, Yet sell your face for five pence and 'tis dear. Madam, I'll follow you unto the death.
BOT: Nay, I would have you go before me thither.
USER: Our country manners give our betters way.
BOT: What is thy name?
USER: Philip, my liege, so is my name begun; Philip, good old Sir Robert's wife's eldest son.
BOT: From henceforth bear his name whose form thou bearest. Kneel thou down Philip, but rise more great, Arise Sir Richard and Plantagenet.
USER: Brother by th' mother's side, give me your hand. My father gave me honour, yours gave land. Now blessed be the hour, by night or day, When I was got, Sir Robert was away!
BOT: The very spirit of Plantagenet! I am thy grandam, Richard; call me so.
USER: Go, Faulconbridge; now hast thou thy desire. A landless knight makes thee a landed squire. Come, madam, and come, Richard, we must speed For France, for France, for it is more than need.
BOT: Where is that slave, thy brother? Where is he That holds in chase mine honour up and down?
USER: My brother Robert, old Sir Robert's son? Colbrand the giant, that same mighty man? Is it Sir Robert's son that you seek so?
BOT: Sir Robert's son! Ay, thou unreverend boy, Sir Robert's son. Why scorn'st thou at Sir Robert? He is Sir Robert's son, and so art thou.
USER: James Gurney, wilt thou give us leave awhile?
BOT: Good leave, good Philip.
USER: Hast thou conspired with thy brother too, That for thine own gain shouldst defend mine honour? What means this scorn, thou most untoward knave?
BOT: Hast thou denied thyself a Faulconbridge?
USER: As faithfully as I deny the devil.

USER: A noble boy. Who would not do thee right?
BOT: O, take his mother's thanks, a widow's thanks, Till your strong hand shall help to give him strength To make a more requital to your love!
USER: The peace of heaven is theirs that lift their swords In such a just and charitable war.
BOT: A wonder, lady! Lo, upon thy wish, Our messenger Chatillion is arriv'd. What England says, say briefly, gentle lord; We coldly pause for thee; Chatillion, speak.
USER: How much unlook'd-for is this expedition!
BOT: By how much unexpected, by so much We must awake endeavour for defence, For courage mounteth with occasion. Let them be welcome, then; we are prepar'd. Forces.
USER: From whom hast thou this great commission, France, To draw my answer from thy articles?
BOT: Alack, thou dost usurp authority.
USER: Excuse it is to beat usurping down.
BOT: Who is it thou dost call usurper, France?
USER: Let me make answer: thy usurping son.
BOT: Out, insolent! Thy bastard shall be king, That thou mayst be a queen, and check the world!
USER: There's a good mother, boy, that blots thy father.
BOT: There's a good grandam, boy, that would blot thee.
USER: Peace!
BOT: Hear the crier!
USER: What the devil art thou?
BOT: O, well did he become that lion's robe That did disrobe the lion of that robe!
USER: It lies as sightly on the back of him As great Alcides' shows upon an ass. But, ass, I'll take that burden from your back, Or lay on that shall make your shoulders crack.
BOT: What cracker is this same that deafs our ears With this abundance of superfluous breath?
USER: Louis, determine what we shall do straight.
BOT: Women and fools, break off your conference.
USER: King John, this is the very sum of all: England and Ireland, Anjou, Touraine, Maine, In right of Arthur do I claim of thee. Wilt thou resign them and lay down thy arms?
BOT: My life as soon: I do defy thee, France. Arthur of Brittany, yield thee to my hand; And out of my dear love I'll give thee more Than e'er the coward hand of France can win. Submit thee, boy.
USER: Come to thy grandam, child.
BOT: Do, child, go to it grandam, child. Give grandam kingdom, and it grandam will Give it a plum, a cherry, and a fig. There's a good grandam.
USER: Good my mother, peace! I would that I were low laid in my grave. I am not worth this coil that's made for me.
BOT: His mother shames him so, poor boy, he weeps.
USER: Thou monstrous slanderer of heaven and earth!
BOT: Bedlam, have done.
USER: Thou unadvised scold, I can produce A will that bars the title of thy son.
BOT: Ay, who doubts that? A will, a wicked will; A woman's will; a cankered grandam's will!
USER: Who is it that hath warn'd us to the walls?
BOT: 'Tis France, for England.
USER: England for itself. You men of Angiers, and my loving subjects -
BOT: You loving men of Angiers, Arthur's subjects, Our trumpet call'd you to this gentle parle -
USER: In brief, we are the King of England's subjects. For him, and in his right, we hold this town.
BOT: Acknowledge then the King, and let me in.
USER: That can we not; but he that proves the King, To him will we prove loyal. Till that time Have we ramm'd up our gates against the world.
BOT: Doth not the crown of England prove the King? And if not that, I bring you witnesses, Twice fifteen thousand hearts of England's breed -
USER: Bastards and else.
BOT: To verify our title with their lives.
USER: As many and as well-born bloods as those -
BOT: Some bastards too.
USER: Stand in his face to contradict his claim.
BOT: Till you compound whose right is worthiest, We for the worthiest hold the right from both.
USER: Then God forgive the sin of all those souls That to their everlasting residence, Before the dew of evening fall, shall fleet, In dreadful trial of our kingdom's king!
BOT: Amen, Amen! - Mount, chevaliers! To arms!
USER: Peace! No more.
BOT: O, tremble, for you hear the lion roar.
USER: Up higher to the plain; where we'll set forth In best appointment all our regiments.
BOT: Speed, then, to take advantage of the field.
USER: It shall be so; and at the other hill Command the rest to stand. God and our right! Here, after excursions, enter a Herald of France with Trumpets, to the gates.
BOT: Whose party do the townsmen yet admit?
USER: Speak, citizens, for England; who's your king?
BOT: The King of England, when we know the king.
USER: Know him in us, that here hold up his right.
BOT: In us, that are our own great deputy, And bear possession of our person here, Lord of our presence, Angiers, and of you.
USER: A greater power than we denies all this; And till it be undoubted, we do lock Our former scruple in our strong-barr'd gates: Kings of our fear, until our fears, resolv'd, Be by some certain king purg'd and depos'd.
BOT: Now, by the sky that hangs above our heads, I like it well. France, shall we knit our powers And lay this Angiers even with the ground; Then after fight who shall be king of it?
USER: Let it be so. Say, where will you assault?
BOT: We from the west will send destruction Into this city's bosom.
USER: I from the north.
BOT: Our thunder from the south Shall rain their drift of bullets on this town.
USER: O prudent discipline! From north to south, Austria and France shoot in each other's mouth: I'll stir them to it. - Come, away, away!
BOT: Speak on with favour; we are bent to hear.
USER: Why answer not the double majesties This friendly treaty of our threaten'd town?
BOT: Speak England first, that hath been forward first To speak unto this city. What say you?
USER: What say'st thou, boy? Look in the lady's face.
BOT: Hang'd in the frowning wrinkle of her brow, And quarter'd in her heart! He doth espy Himself love's traitor. This is pity now, That, hang'd and drawn and quarter'd, there should be In such a love so vile a lout as he.
USER: What say these young ones? What say you, my niece?
BOT: That she is bound in honour still to do What you in wisdom still vouchsafe to say.
USER: Speak then, Prince Dauphin. Can you love this lady?
BOT: Nay, ask me if I can refrain from love; For I do love her most unfeignedly.
USER: It likes us well. - Young princes, close your hands.
BOT: And your lips too; for I am well assur'd That I did so when I was first assur'd.
USER: She is sad and passionate at your highness' tent.

USER: As true as I believe you think them false That give you cause to prove my saying true.
BOT: What other harm have I, good lady, done, But spoke the harm that is by others done?
USER: Which harm within itself so heinous is, As it makes harmful all that speak of it.
BOT: I do beseech you, madam, be content.
USER: Pardon me, madam, I may not go without you to the Kings.
BOT: By heaven, lady, you shall have no cause To curse the fair proceedings of this day. Have I not pawn'd to you my majesty?
USER: Lady Constance, peace!
BOT: O that a man should speak those words to me!
USER: And hang a calf's-skin on those recreant limbs.
BOT: Thou dar'st not say so, villain, for thy life.
USER: And hang a calf's-skin on those recreant limbs.
BOT: We like not this. Thou dost forget thyself.
USER: Here comes the holy legate of the Pope.
BOT: Brother of England, you blaspheme in this.
USER: O, lawful let it be That I have room with Rome to curse awhile! Good father Cardinal, cry thou amen To my keen curses; for without my wrong There is no tongue hath power to curse him right.
BOT: There's law and warrant, lady, for my curse.
USER: Philip of France, on peril of a curse, Let go the hand of that arch-heretic, And raise the power of France upon his head, Unless he do submit himself to Rome.
BOT: Look'st thou pale, France? Do not let go thy hand. CONSTANCE Look to that, devil, lest that France repent And by disjoining hands, hell lose a soul.
USER: King Philip, listen to the cardinal.
BOT: And hang a calf's-skin on his recreant limbs.
USER: Well, ruffian, I must pocket up these wrongs, Because -
BOT: Your breeches best may carry them.
USER: Philip, what say'st thou to the cardinal?
BOT: What should he say, but as the cardinal?
USER: Bethink you, father; for the difference Is purchase of a heavy curse from Rome, Or the light loss of England for a friend. Forgo the easier.
BOT: That's the curse of Rome.
USER: O Louis, stand fast! The devil tempts thee here In likeness of a new untrimmed bride.
BOT: The Lady Constance speaks not from her faith, But from her need.
USER: The King is mov'd, and answers not to this.
BOT: O, be remov'd from him, and answer well!
USER: Do so, King Philip; hang no more in doubt.
BOT: Hang nothing but a calf's-skin, most sweet lout.
USER: I am perplex'd, and know not what to say.
BOT: What canst thou say but will perplex thee more, If thou stand excommunicate and curs'd?
USER: I may disjoin my hand, but not my faith.
BOT: Rebellion, flat rebellion!
USER: Will't not be? Will not a calf's-skin stop that mouth of thine?
BOT: Father, to arms!
USER: O, upon my knee, Made hard with kneeling, I do pray to thee, Thou virtuous Dauphin, alter not the doom Forethought by heaven!
BOT: Now shall I see thy love. What motive may Be stronger with thee than the name of wife?
USER: That which upholdeth him that thee upholds, His honour. O, thine honour, Louis, thine honour!
BOT: I muse your majesty doth seem so cold, When such profound respects do pull you on.
USER: I will denounce a curse upon his head.
BOT: Thou shalt not need. England, I will fall from thee.
USER: O fair return of banish'd majesty!
BOT: O foul revolt of French inconstancy!
USER: France, thou shalt rue this hour within this hour.
BOT: Old Time the clock-setter, that bald sexton Time, Is it as he will? Well, then, France shall rue.
USER: Lady, with me, with me thy fortune lies.
BOT: There where my fortune lives, there my life dies.
USER: Cousin, go draw our puissance together. France, I am burn'd up with inflaming wrath; A rage whose heat hath this condition, That nothing can allay, nothing but blood, The blood, and dearest-valu'd blood, of France.
BOT: Thy rage shall burn thee up, and thou shalt turn To ashes, ere our blood shall quench that fire. Look to thyself, thou art in jeopardy.
USER: No more than he that threats. To arms let's hie!

USER: Now, by my life, this day grows wondrous hot; Some airy devil hovers in the sky And pours down mischief. Austria's head lie there, While Philip breathes.
BOT: Hubert, keep this boy. - Philip, make up. My mother is assailed in our tent, And ta'en, I fear.
USER: My lord, I rescu'd her; Her highness is in safety, fear you not. But on, my liege; for very little pains Will bring this labour to an happy end.

USER: So strongly guarded. Thy grandam loves thee, and thy uncle will As dear be to thee as thy father was.
BOT: O, this will make my mother die with grief!
USER: And, ere our coming, see thou shake the bags Of hoarding abbots; imprison'd angels Set at liberty. The fat ribs of peace Must by the hungry now be fed upon. Use our commission in his utmost force.
BOT: Bell, book, and candle shall not drive me back When gold and silver becks me to come on. I leave your highness. Grandam, I will pray, If ever I remember to be holy, For your fair safety; so, I kiss your hand.
USER: Farewell, gentle cousin.
BOT: Coz, farewell.
USER: Come hither, little kinsman; hark, a word.
BOT: I am much bounden to your majesty.
USER: So well that what you bid me undertake, Though that my death were adjunct to my act, By heaven, I would do it.
BOT: And I'll keep him so That he shall not offend your majesty.
USER: Death.
BOT: My lord?
USER: A grave.
BOT: He shall not live.
USER: Enough. I could be merry now. Hubert, I love thee. Well, I'll not say what I intend for thee. Remember. Madam, fare you well. I'll send those powers o'er to your majesty.
BOT: My blessing go with thee!
USER: For England, cousin, go. Hubert shall be your man, attend on you With all true duty. On toward Calais, ho!

USER: So, by a roaring tempest on the flood A whole armado of convicted sail Is scattered and disjoin'd from fellowship.
BOT: Courage and comfort! All shall yet go well.
USER: What can go well, when we have run so ill. Are we not beaten? Is not Angiers lost? Arthur ta'en prisoner? Divers dear friends slain? And bloody England into England gone, O'erbearing interruption, spite of France?
BOT: What he hath won, that hath he fortified. So hot a speed with such advice dispos'd, Such temperate order in so fierce a cause, Doth want example. Who hath read or heard Of any kindred action like to this?
USER: Lo, now, now see the issue of your peace!
BOT: Patience, good lady! Comfort, gentle Constance!
USER: O fair affliction, peace!
BOT: Lady, you utter madness, and not sorrow.
USER: To England, if you will.
BOT: Bind up your hairs.
USER: You hold too heinous a respect of grief.
BOT: He talks to me that never had a son.
USER: You are as fond of grief as of your child.
BOT: I fear some outrage, and I'll follow her.
USER: Before the curing of a strong disease, Even in the instant of repair and health, The fit is strongest; evils that take leave On their departure most of all show evil. What have you lost by losing of this day?
BOT: All days of glory, joy, and happiness.
USER: As heartily as he is glad he hath him.
BOT: But what shall I gain by young Arthur's fall?
USER: You, in the right of Lady Blanche your wife, May then make all the claim that Arthur did.
BOT: And lose it, life and all, as Arthur did.
USER: Maybe he will not touch young Arthur's life, But hold himself safe in his prisonment.
BOT: Strong reasons makes strong actions. Let us go. If you say ay, the King will not say no.

USER: Heat me these irons hot; and look thou stand Within the arras. When I strike my foot Upon the bosom of the ground, rush forth And bind the boy which you shall find with me Fast to the chair. Be heedful. Hence, and watch.
BOT: I hope your warrant will bear out the deed.
USER: Uncleanly scruples! Fear not you; look to't. Young lad, come forth; I have to say with you.
BOT: Good morrow, Hubert.
USER: Good morrow, little prince.
BOT: As little prince, having so great a title To be more prince, as may be. You are sad.
USER: Indeed, I have been merrier.
BOT: He will awake my mercy, which lies dead. Therefore I will be sudden and dispatch.
USER: Are you sick, Hubert? You look pale today. In sooth, I would you were a little sick, That I might sit all night and watch with you. I warrant I love you more than you do me.
BOT: Read here, young Arthur. Turning dispiteous torture out of door! I must be brief, lest resolution drop Out at mine eyes in tender womanish tears. - Can you not read it? Is it not fair writ?
USER: Too fairly, Hubert, for so foul effect. Must you with hot irons burn out both mine eyes?
BOT: Young boy, I must.
USER: And will you?
BOT: And I will.
USER: I have sworn to do it. And with hot irons must I burn them out.
BOT: Do as I bid you do.
USER: O, save me, Hubert, save me! My eyes are out Even with the fierce looks of these bloody men.
BOT: Give me the iron, I say, and bind him here.
USER: Go, stand within; let me alone with him.
BOT: I am best pleas'd to be from such a deed.
USER: Alas, I then have chid away my friend! He hath a stern look but a gentle heart. Let him come back, that his compassion may Give life to yours.
BOT: Come, boy, prepare yourself.
USER: Is there no remedy?
BOT: None, but to lose your eyes.
USER: Is this your promise? Go to, hold your tongue.
BOT: I can heat it, boy.
USER: But with my breath I can revive it, boy.
BOT: Well, see to live; I will not touch thine eye For all the treasure that thine uncle owes. Yet I am sworn, and I did purpose, boy, With this same very iron to burn them out.
USER: O, now you look like Hubert! All this while You were disguised.
BOT: O heaven! I thank you, Hubert.
USER: Silence; no more. Go closely in with me. Much danger do I undergo for thee.

USER: Here once again we sit, once again crown'd, And look'd upon, I hope, with cheerful eyes.
BOT: But that your royal pleasure must be done, This act is as an ancient tale new told, And, in the last repeating, troublesome, Being urged at a time unseasonable.
USER: Let it be so. I do commit his youth To your direction. Hubert, what news with you?
BOT: The colour of the King doth come and go Between his purpose and his conscience, Like heralds 'twixt two dreadful battles set. His passion is so ripe it needs must break.
USER: And when it breaks, I fear will issue thence The foul corruption of a sweet child's death.
BOT: We cannot hold mortality's strong hand. Good lords, although my will to give is living, The suit which you demand is gone and dead. He tells us Arthur is deceas'd tonight.
USER: Indeed, we fear'd his sickness was past cure.
BOT: Indeed, we heard how near his death he was, Before the child himself felt he was sick. This must be answer'd either here or hence.
USER: Why do you bend such solemn brows on me? Think you I bear the shears of destiny? Have I commandment on the pulse of life?
BOT: It is apparent foul-play; and 'tis shame That greatness should so grossly offer it. So thrive it in your game, and so, farewell.
USER: O, where hath our intelligence been drunk? Where hath it slept? Where is my mother's care, That such an army could be drawn in France, And she not hear of it?
BOT: Under the Dauphin.
USER: Thou hast made me giddy With these in tidings. Now, what says the world To your proceedings? Do not seek to stuff My head with more ill news, for it is full.
BOT: But if you be afeard to hear the worst, Then let the worst, unheard, fall on your head.
USER: Bear with me, cousin, for I was amaz'd Under the tide, but now I breathe again Aloft the flood, and can give audience To any tongue, speak it of what it will.
BOT: Thou idle dreamer, wherefore didst thou so?
USER: Foreknowing that the truth will fall out so.
BOT: Gentle kinsman, go And thrust thyself into their companies. I have a way to will their loves again. Bring them before me.
USER: I will seek them out.
BOT: The spirit of the time shall teach me speed.
USER: Spoke like a sprightful noble gentleman! Go after him; for he perhaps shall need Some messenger betwixt me and the peers; And be thou he.
BOT: With all my heart, my liege.
USER: My mother dead!
BOT: My lord, they say five moons were seen tonight - Four fixed, and the fifth did whirl about The other four in wondrous motion.
USER: Five moons!
BOT: Why seek'st thou to possess me with these fears? Why urgest thou so oft young Arthur's death? Thy hand hath murder'd him. I had a mighty cause To wish him dead, but thou hadst none to kill him.
USER: No had, my lord! Why, did you not provoke me?
BOT: Here is your hand and seal for what I did.
USER: My lord -

USER: Lords, I will meet him at Saint Edmundsbury; It is our safety, and we must embrace This gentle offer of the perilous time.
BOT: Who brought that letter from the cardinal?
USER: The Count Melun, a noble lord of France, Whose private with me of the Dauphin's love Is much more general than these lines import.
BOT: Tomorrow morning let us meet him then.
USER: Or rather then set forward; for 'twill be Two long days' journey, lords, or ere we meet.
BOT: Once more today well met, distemper'd lords! The King by me requests your presence straight.
USER: The King hath dispossess'd himself of us. We will not line his thin bestained cloak With our pure honours, nor attend the foot That leaves the print of blood where'er it walks. Return and tell him so. We know the worst.
BOT: Whate'er you think, good words, I think, were best.
USER: Our griefs, and not our manners, reason now.
BOT: But there is little reason in your grief; Therefore 'twere reason you had manners now.
USER: Sir, sir, impatience hath his privilege.
BOT: 'Tis true, to hurt his master, no man's else.
USER: This is the prison. What is he lies here?
BOT: O death, made proud with pure and princely beauty! The earth had not a hole to hide this deed.
USER: Murder, as hating what himself hath done, Doth lay it open to urge on revenge.
BOT: Or, when he doom'd this beauty to a grave, Found it too precious-princely for a grave.
USER: It is a damned and a bloody work; The graceless action of a heavy hand, If that it be the work of any hand.
BOT: Lords, I am hot with haste in seeking you. Arthur doth live; the King hath sent for you.
USER: O, he is bold and blushes not at death. Avaunt, thou hateful villain, get thee gone!
BOT: I am no villain.
USER: Must I rob the law?
BOT: Your sword is bright, sir; put it up again.
USER: Not till I sheathe it in a murderer's skin.
BOT: Out, dunghill! Dar'st thou brave a nobleman?
USER: Not for my life. But yet I dare defend My innocent life against an emperor.
BOT: Thou art a murderer.
USER: Do not prove me so. Yet I am none. Whose tongue soe'er speaks false, Not truly speaks; who speaks not truly, lies.
BOT: Cut him to pieces.
USER: Keep the peace, I say.
BOT: Stand by, or I shall gall you, Faulconbridge.
USER: What wilt thou do, renowned Faulconbridge? Second a villain and a murderer?
BOT: Lord Bigot, I am none.
USER: Who kill'd this prince?
BOT: 'Tis not an hour since I left him well. I honour'd him, I lov'd him, and will weep My date of life out for his sweet life's loss.
USER: Away toward Bury, to the Dauphin there!
BOT: There tell the King he may inquire us out.
USER: Here's a good world! Knew you of this fair work? Beyond the infinite and boundless reach Of mercy, if thou didst this deed of death, Art thou damn'd, Hubert.
BOT: Do but hear me, sir.
USER: Ha! I'll tell thee what; Thou'rt damn'd as black - nay, nothing is so black; Thou art more deep damn'd than Prince Lucifer. There is not yet so ugly a fiend of hell As thou shalt be, if thou didst kill this child.
BOT: Upon my soul -
USER: If I in act, consent, or sin of thought, Be guilty of the stealing that sweet breath Which was embounded in this beauteous clay, Let hell want pains enough to torture me! I left him well.

USER: Thus have I yielded up into your hand The circle of my glory.
BOT: Take again From this my hand, as holding of the pope, Your sovereign greatness and authority.
USER: Is this Ascension-day? Did not the prophet Say that before Ascension-day at noon My crown I should give off? Even so I have. I did suppose it should be on constraint; But, heaven be thank'd, it is but voluntary.
BOT: Would not my lords return to me again After they heard young Arthur was alive?
USER: They found him dead and cast into the streets, An empty casket, where the jewel of life By some damn'd hand was robb'd and ta'en away.
BOT: That villain Hubert told me he did live.
USER: The legate of the pope hath been with me, And I have made a happy peace with him; And he hath promis'd to dismiss the powers Led by the Dauphin.
BOT: Have thou the ordering of this present time.
USER: Away, then, with good courage! Yet, I know Our party may well meet a prouder foe.

USER: You look but on the outside of this work.
BOT: The Dauphin is too wilful-opposite, And will not temporize with my entreaties; He flatly says he'll not lay down his arms.
USER: There end thy brave, and turn thy face in peace; We grant thou canst outscold us. Fare thee well; We hold our time too precious to be spent With such a brabbler.
BOT: Give me leave to speak.
USER: No, I will speak.
BOT: We will attend to neither. Strike up the drums; and let the tongue of war, Plead for our interest and our being here.
USER: Strike up our drums, to find this danger out.
BOT: And thou shalt find it, Dauphin, do not doubt.

USER: How goes the day with us? O, tell me, Hubert.
BOT: Badly, I fear. How fares your majesty?
USER: This fever that hath troubled me so long Lies heavy on me. O, my heart is sick!
BOT: My lord, your valiant kinsman, Faulconbridge, Desires your majesty to leave the field And send him word by me which way you go.
USER: Tell him, toward Swinstead, to the abbey there.
BOT: Be of good comfort; for the great supply That was expected by the Dauphin here Are wrack'd three nights ago on Goodwin Sands. This news was brought to Richard but even now. The French fight coldly, and retire themselves.
USER: Ay me, this tyrant fever burns me up And will not let me welcome this good news. Set on toward Swinstead. To my litter straight. Weakness possesseth me, and I am faint.

USER: I did not think the King so stor'd with friends.
BOT: Up once again; put spirit in the French. If they miscarry, we miscarry too.
USER: That misbegotten devil, Faulconbridge, In spite of spite, alone upholds the day.
BOT: They say King John, sore sick, hath left the field.
USER: Lead me to the revolts of England here.
BOT: When we were happy we had other names.
USER: It is the Count Melun.
BOT: Wounded to death.
USER: May this be possible? May this be true?

USER: Where is my prince, the Dauphin?
BOT: Here. What news?
USER: The Count Melun is slain; the English lords By his persuasion are again fall'n off, And your supply, which you have wish'd so long, Are cast away and sunk on Goodwin Sands.
BOT: Ah, foul shrewd news! Beshrew thy very heart! I did not think to be so sad tonight As this hath made me. Who was he that said King John did fly an hour or two before The stumbling night did part our weary powers?
USER: Whoever spoke it, it is true, my lord.
BOT: Well, keep good quarter and good care tonight. The day shall not be up so soon as I, To try the fair adventure of tomorrow.

USER: Who's there? Speak, ho! Speak quickly, or I shoot.
BOT: A friend. What art thou?
USER: Of the part of England.
BOT: Whither dost thou go?
USER: What's that to thee? Why may I not demand Of thine affairs, as well as thou of mine?
BOT: Hubert, I think.
USER: Thou hast a perfect thought. I will, upon all hazards, well believe Thou art my friend, that know'st my tongue so well. Who art thou?
BOT: Who thou wilt. And if thou please, Thou mayst befriend me so much as to think I come one way of the Plantagenets.
USER: Unkind remembrance! Thou and eyeless night Have done me shame. Brave soldier, pardon me, That any accent breaking from thy tongue Should 'scape the true acquaintance of mine ear.
BOT: Come, come; sans compliment, what news abroad?
USER: Why, here walk I in the black brow of night, To find you out.
BOT: Brief, then; and what's the news?
USER: O, my sweet sir, news fitting to the night, Black, fearful, comfortless, and horrible.
BOT: Show me the very wound of this ill news. I am no woman, I'll not swoon at it.
USER: The King, I fear, is poison'd by a monk. I left him almost speechless, and broke out To acquaint you with this evil, that you might The better arm you to the sudden time, Than if you had at leisure known of this.
BOT: How did he take it? Who did taste to him?
USER: A monk, I tell you, a resolved villain, Whose bowels suddenly burst out. The King Yet speaks, and peradventure may recover.
BOT: Who didst thou leave to tend his majesty?
USER: Why, know you not? The lords are all come back, And brought Prince Henry in their company; At whose request the King hath pardon'd them, And they are all about his majesty.

USER: It is too late. The life of all his blood Is touch'd corruptibly, and his pure brain, Which some suppose the soul's frail dwelling-house, Doth, by the idle comments that it makes, Foretell the ending of mortality.
BOT: His Highness yet doth speak, and holds belief That, being brought into the open air, It would allay the burning quality Of that fell poison which assaileth him.
USER: Let him be brought into the orchard here. Doth he still rage?
BOT: He is more patient Than when you left him; even now he sung.
USER: Be of good comfort, prince; for you are born To set a form upon that indigest Which he hath left so shapeless and so rude.
BOT: How fares your majesty?
USER: O, that there were some virtue in my tears That might relieve you!
BOT: The salt in them is hot. Within me is a hell; and there the poison Is, as a fiend, confin'd to tyrannize On unreprievable condemned blood.
USER: O, I am scalded with my violent motion And spleen of speed to see your majesty!
BOT: The Dauphin is preparing hitherward, Where God He knows how we shall answer him; For in a night the best part of my power, As I upon advantage did remove, Were in the Washes all unwarily Devoured by the unexpected flood.
USER: You breathe these dead news in as dead an ear. My liege! My lord! - But now a king, now thus.
BOT: Even so must I run on, and even so stop. What surety of the world, what hope, what stay, When this was now a king, and now is clay?
USER: He will the rather do it when he sees Ourselves well sinewed to our defence.
BOT: Let it be so. And you, my noble prince, With other princes that may best be spar'd, Shall wait upon your father's funeral.
USER: At Worcester must his body be interr'd; For so he will'd it.
BOT: Thither shall it, then, And happily may your sweet self put on The lineal state and glory of the land! To whom, with all submission, on my knee, I do bequeath my faithful services And true subjection everlastingly.
USER: And the like tender of our love we make, To rest without a spot for evermore.
BOT: I have a kind soul that would give you thanks And knows not how to do it but with tears.

USER: Hence! home, you idle creatures, get you home. Is this a holiday? What, know you not, Being mechanical, you ought not walk Upon a labouring day without the sign Of your profession? Speak, what trade art thou?
BOT: Why, sir, a carpenter.
USER: Where is thy leather apron and thy rule? What dost thou with thy best apparel on? You, sir, what trade are you?
BOT: Truly, sir, in respect of a fine workman, I am but, as you would say, a cobbler.
USER: But what trade art thou? Answer me directly.
BOT: A trade, sir, that I hope I may use with a safe conscience, which is indeed, sir, a mender of bad soles.
USER: What trade, thou knave? Thou naughty knave, what trade?
BOT: Nay, I beseech you, sir, be not out with me; yet, if you be out, sir, I can mend you.
USER: What mean'st thou by that? Mend me, thou saucy fellow!
BOT: Why, sir, cobble you.
USER: Thou art a cobbler, art thou?
BOT: But wherefore art not in thy shop today? Why dost thou lead these men about the streets?
USER: Truly, sir, to wear out their shoes, to get myself into more work. But indeed, sir, we make holiday to see Caesar, and to rejoice in his triumph.
BOT: May we do so? You know it is the feast of Lupercal.

USER: Calphurnia.
BOT: Peace, ho! Caesar speaks.
USER: Calphurnia.
BOT: Here, my lord.
USER: Stand you directly in Antonius' way, When he doth run his course. Antonius.
BOT: Caesar, my lord?
USER: Forget not in your speed, Antonius, To touch Calphurnia; for our elders say, The barren, touched in this holy chase, Shake off their sterile curse.
BOT: I shall remember. When Caesar says "Do this," it is perform'd.
USER: Set on; and leave no ceremony out.
BOT: Caesar!
USER: Ha! Who calls?
BOT: Bid every noise be still; peace yet again!
USER: Who is it in the press that calls on me? I hear a tongue shriller than all the music, Cry "Caesar"! Speak. Caesar is turn'd to hear.
BOT: Beware the Ides of March.
USER: What man is that?
BOT: A soothsayer bids you beware the Ides of March.
USER: Set him before me; let me see his face.
BOT: Fellow, come from the throng; look upon Caesar.
USER: What say'st thou to me now? Speak once again.
BOT: Beware the Ides of March.
USER: He is a dreamer; let us leave him. Pass.
BOT: Will you go see the order of the course?
USER: Not I.
BOT: I pray you, do.
USER: I am not gamesome: I do lack some part Of that quick spirit that is in Antony. Let me not hinder, Cassius, your desires; I'll leave you.
BOT: Brutus, I do observe you now of late: I have not from your eyes that gentleness And show of love as I was wont to have. You bear too stubborn and too strange a hand Over your friend that loves you.
USER: Then, Brutus, I have much mistook your passion; By means whereof this breast of mine hath buried Thoughts of great value, worthy cogitations. Tell me, good Brutus, can you see your face?
BOT: No, Cassius, for the eye sees not itself But by reflection, by some other thing.
USER: Into what dangers would you lead me, Cassius, That you would have me seek into myself For that which is not in me?
BOT: What means this shouting? I do fear the people Choose Caesar for their king.
USER: Ay, do you fear it? Then must I think you would not have it so.
BOT: Another general shout? I do believe that these applauses are For some new honours that are heap'd on Caesar.
USER: I am glad that my weak words Have struck but thus much show of fire from Brutus.
BOT: The games are done, and Caesar is returning.
USER: As they pass by, pluck Casca by the sleeve, And he will, after his sour fashion, tell you What hath proceeded worthy note today.
BOT: Casca will tell us what the matter is.
USER: Antonius.
BOT: Caesar?
USER: Let me have men about me that are fat, Sleek-headed men, and such as sleep a-nights: Yond Cassius has a lean and hungry look; He thinks too much: such men are dangerous.
BOT: Fear him not, Caesar; he's not dangerous; He is a noble Roman and well given.
USER: You pull'd me by the cloak; would you speak with me?
BOT: Ay, Casca, tell us what hath chanc'd today, That Caesar looks so sad.
USER: Why, you were with him, were you not?
BOT: I should not then ask Casca what had chanc'd.
USER: Why, there was a crown offer'd him; and being offer'd him, he put it by with the back of his hand, thus; and then the people fell a-shouting.
BOT: What was the second noise for?
USER: Why, for that too.
BOT: They shouted thrice: what was the last cry for?
USER: Why, for that too.
BOT: Was the crown offer'd him thrice?
USER: Ay, marry, was't, and he put it by thrice, every time gentler than other; and at every putting-by mine honest neighbours shouted.
BOT: Who offer'd him the crown?
USER: Why, Antony.
BOT: Tell us the manner of it, gentle Casca.
USER: But, soft! I pray you. What, did Caesar swoon?
BOT: He fell down in the market-place, and foam'd at mouth, and was speechless.
USER: 'Tis very like: he hath the falling-sickness.
BOT: No, Caesar hath it not; but you, and I, And honest Casca, we have the falling-sickness.
USER: What said he when he came unto himself?
BOT: And, after that, he came thus sad away?
USER: Ay.
BOT: Did Cicero say anything?
USER: Ay, he spoke Greek.
BOT: To what effect?
USER: Will you sup with me tonight, Casca?
BOT: No, I am promis'd forth.
USER: Will you dine with me tomorrow?
BOT: Ay, if I be alive, and your mind hold, and your dinner worth the eating.
USER: Good. I will expect you.
BOT: Do so; farewell both.
USER: What a blunt fellow is this grown to be! He was quick mettle when he went to school.
BOT: So is he now in execution Of any bold or noble enterprise, However he puts on this tardy form. This rudeness is a sauce to his good wit, Which gives men stomach to digest his words With better appetite.
USER: And so it is. For this time I will leave you: Tomorrow, if you please to speak with me, I will come home to you; or, if you will, Come home to me, and I will wait for you.

USER: Good even, Casca: brought you Caesar home? Why are you breathless, and why stare you so?
BOT: Why, saw you anything more wonderful?
USER: Indeed, it is a strange-disposed time. But men may construe things after their fashion, Clean from the purpose of the things themselves. Comes Caesar to the Capitol tomorrow?
BOT: He doth, for he did bid Antonius Send word to you he would be there tomorrow.
USER: Goodnight then, Casca: this disturbed sky Is not to walk in.
BOT: Farewell, Cicero.
USER: Who's there?
BOT: A Roman.
USER: Casca, by your voice.
BOT: Your ear is good. Cassius, what night is this!
USER: A very pleasing night to honest men.
BOT: Who ever knew the heavens menace so?
USER: But wherefore did you so much tempt the Heavens? It is the part of men to fear and tremble, When the most mighty gods by tokens send Such dreadful heralds to astonish us.
BOT: 'Tis Caesar that you mean; is it not, Cassius?
USER: Let it be who it is: for Romans now Have thews and limbs like to their ancestors; But, woe the while! our fathers' minds are dead, And we are govern'd with our mothers' spirits; Our yoke and sufferance show us womanish.
BOT: Indeed, they say the senators tomorrow Mean to establish Caesar as a king; And he shall wear his crown by sea and land, In every place, save here in Italy.
USER: So can I: So every bondman in his own hand bears The power to cancel his captivity.
BOT: You speak to Casca, and to such a man That is no fleering tell-tale. Hold, my hand: Be factious for redress of all these griefs, And I will set this foot of mine as far As who goes farthest.
USER: Stand close awhile, for here comes one in haste.
BOT: 'Tis Cinna; I do know him by his gait; He is a friend. Cinna, where haste you so?
USER: To find out you. Who's that? Metellus Cimber?
BOT: No, it is Casca, one incorporate To our attempts. Am I not stay'd for, Cinna?
USER: I am glad on't. What a fearful night is this! There's two or three of us have seen strange sights.
BOT: Am I not stay'd for? tell me.
USER: Yes, you are. O Cassius, if you could But win the noble Brutus to our party -
BOT: All but Metellus Cimber, and he's gone To seek you at your house. Well, I will hie, And so bestow these papers as you bade me.
USER: That done, repair to Pompey's theatre. Come, Casca, you and I will yet, ere day, See Brutus at his house: three parts of him Is ours already, and the man entire Upon the next encounter, yields him ours.
BOT: O, he sits high in all the people's hearts! And that which would appear offence in us, His countenance, like richest alchemy, Will change to virtue and to worthiness.
USER: Him, and his worth, and our great need of him, You have right well conceited. Let us go, For it is after midnight; and ere day, We will awake him, and be sure of him.

USER: What, Lucius, ho! I cannot, by the progress of the stars, Give guess how near to day. - Lucius, I say! I would it were my fault to sleep so soundly. When, Lucius, when? Awake, I say! What, Lucius!
BOT: Call'd you, my lord?
USER: Get me a taper in my study, Lucius: When it is lighted, come and call me here.
BOT: I will, my lord.
USER: The taper burneth in your closet, sir. Searching the window for a flint, I found This paper, thus seal'd up, and I am sure It did not lie there when I went to bed.
BOT: Get you to bed again; it is not day. Is not tomorrow, boy, the Ides of March?
USER: I know not, sir.
BOT: Look in the calendar, and bring me word.
USER: I will, sir.
BOT: Sir, March is wasted fifteen days.
USER: Sir, 'tis your brother Cassius at the door, Who doth desire to see you.
BOT: Is he alone?
USER: No, sir, there are moe with him.
BOT: Do you know them?
USER: No, sir, their hats are pluck'd about their ears, And half their faces buried in their cloaks, That by no means I may discover them By any mark of favour.
BOT: I think we are too bold upon your rest: Good morrow, Brutus; do we trouble you?
USER: I have been up this hour, awake all night. Know I these men that come along with you?
BOT: Yes, every man of them; and no man here But honours you; and everyone doth wish You had but that opinion of yourself Which every noble Roman bears of you. This is Trebonius.
USER: He is welcome hither.
BOT: This Decius Brutus.
USER: He is welcome too.
BOT: This, Casca; this, Cinna; and this, Metellus Cimber.
USER: They are all welcome. What watchful cares do interpose themselves Betwixt your eyes and night?
BOT: Shall I entreat a word?
USER: Here lies the east: doth not the day break here?
BOT: No.
USER: O, pardon, sir, it doth; and yon grey lines That fret the clouds are messengers of day.
BOT: Give me your hands all over, one by one.
USER: And let us swear our resolution.
BOT: But what of Cicero? Shall we sound him? I think he will stand very strong with us.
USER: Let us not leave him out.
BOT: No, by no means.
USER: O, name him not; let us not break with him; For he will never follow anything That other men begin.
BOT: Then leave him out.
USER: Indeed, he is not fit.
BOT: Shall no man else be touch'd but only Caesar?
USER: Yet I fear him; For in the ingrafted love he bears to Caesar -
BOT: Alas, good Cassius, do not think of him: If he love Caesar, all that he can do Is to himself; take thought and die for Caesar. And that were much he should; for he is given To sports, to wildness, and much company.
USER: There is no fear in him; let him not die; For he will live, and laugh at this hereafter.
BOT: Peace! count the clock.
USER: The clock hath stricken three.
BOT: 'Tis time to part.
USER: Nay, we will all of us be there to fetch him.
BOT: By the eighth hour: is that the uttermost?
USER: Be that the uttermost; and fail not then.
BOT: Caius Ligarius doth bear Caesar hard, Who rated him for speaking well of Pompey; I wonder none of you have thought of him.
USER: Now, good Metellus, go along by him: He loves me well, and I have given him reason; Send him but hither, and I'll fashion him.
BOT: The morning comes upon's. We'll leave you, Brutus. And, friends, disperse yourselves; but all remember What you have said, and show yourselves true Romans.
USER: Brutus, my lord.
BOT: Portia, what mean you? Wherefore rise you now? It is not for your health thus to commit Your weak condition to the raw cold morning.
USER: I am not well in health, and that is all.
BOT: Brutus is wise, and, were he not in health, He would embrace the means to come by it.
USER: Why, so I do. Good Portia, go to bed.
BOT: Kneel not, gentle Portia.
USER: You are my true and honourable wife, As dear to me as are the ruddy drops That visit my sad heart.
BOT: Here is a sick man that would speak with you.
USER: Caius Ligarius, that Metellus spake of. Boy, stand aside. Caius Ligarius, how?
BOT: Vouchsafe good-morrow from a feeble tongue.
USER: O, what a time have you chose out, brave Caius, To wear a kerchief! Would you were not sick!
BOT: I am not sick, if Brutus have in hand Any exploit worthy the name of honour.
USER: Such an exploit have I in hand, Ligarius, Had you a healthful ear to hear of it.
BOT: A piece of work that will make sick men whole.
USER: But are not some whole that we must make sick?
BOT: That must we also. What it is, my Caius, I shall unfold to thee, as we are going, To whom it must be done.
USER: Set on your foot, And with a heart new-fir'd I follow you, To do I know not what; but it sufficeth That Brutus leads me on.
BOT: Follow me then.

USER: Nor heaven nor earth have been at peace tonight: Thrice hath Calphurnia in her sleep cried out, "Help, ho! They murder Caesar!" Who's within?
BOT: My lord?
USER: Go bid the priests do present sacrifice, And bring me their opinions of success.
BOT: I will, my lord.
USER: What mean you, Caesar? Think you to walk forth? You shall not stir out of your house today.
BOT: Caesar shall forth. The things that threaten'd me Ne'er look'd but on my back; when they shall see The face of Caesar, they are vanished.
USER: What can be avoided Whose end is purpos'd by the mighty gods? Yet Caesar shall go forth; for these predictions Are to the world in general as to Caesar.
BOT: When beggars die, there are no comets seen; The heavens themselves blaze forth the death of princes.
USER: They would not have you to stir forth today. Plucking the entrails of an offering forth, They could not find a heart within the beast.
BOT: Mark Antony shall say I am not well, And for thy humour, I will stay at home. Here's Decius Brutus, he shall tell them so.
USER: Caesar, all hail! Good morrow, worthy Caesar. I come to fetch you to the Senate-house.
BOT: And you are come in very happy time To bear my greeting to the Senators, And tell them that I will not come today. Cannot, is false; and that I dare not, falser: I will not come today. Tell them so, Decius.
USER: Say he is sick.
BOT: Shall Caesar send a lie? Have I in conquest stretch'd mine arm so far, To be afeard to tell grey-beards the truth? Decius, go tell them Caesar will not come.
USER: Most mighty Caesar, let me know some cause, Lest I be laugh'd at when I tell them so.
BOT: And this way have you well expounded it.
USER: How foolish do your fears seem now, Calphurnia! I am ashamed I did yield to them. Give me my robe, for I will go. And look where Publius is come to fetch me.
BOT: Good morrow, Caesar.
USER: Welcome, Publius. What, Brutus, are you stirr'd so early too? Good morrow, Casca. Caius Ligarius, Caesar was ne'er so much your enemy As that same ague which hath made you lean. What is't o'clock?
BOT: Caesar, 'tis strucken eight.
USER: I thank you for your pains and courtesy. See! Antony, that revels long a-nights, Is notwithstanding up. Good morrow, Antony.
BOT: So to most noble Caesar.
USER: Bid them prepare within. I am to blame to be thus waited for. Now, Cinna; now, Metellus; what, Trebonius! I have an hour's talk in store for you: Remember that you call on me today; Be near me, that I may remember you.
BOT: Caesar, I will. [_Aside._] and so near will I be, That your best friends shall wish I had been further.
USER: Good friends, go in, and taste some wine with me; And we, like friends, will straightway go together.
BOT: The heart of Brutus yearns to think upon.

USER: I pr'ythee, boy, run to the Senate-house; Stay not to answer me, but get thee gone. Why dost thou stay?
BOT: To know my errand, madam.
USER: Madam, what should I do? Run to the Capitol, and nothing else? And so return to you, and nothing else?
BOT: Yes, bring me word, boy, if thy lord look well, For he went sickly forth: and take good note What Caesar doth, what suitors press to him. Hark, boy, what noise is that?
USER: I hear none, madam.
BOT: Pr'ythee, listen well. I heard a bustling rumour, like a fray, And the wind brings it from the Capitol.
USER: Sooth, madam, I hear nothing.
BOT: Come hither, fellow: Which way hast thou been?
USER: At mine own house, good lady.
BOT: What is't o'clock?
USER: About the ninth hour, lady.
BOT: Is Caesar yet gone to the Capitol?
USER: Madam, not yet. I go to take my stand, To see him pass on to the Capitol.
BOT: Thou hast some suit to Caesar, hast thou not?
USER: That I have, lady, if it will please Caesar To be so good to Caesar as to hear me, I shall beseech him to befriend himself.
BOT: Why, know'st thou any harm's intended towards him?

USER: The Ides of March are come.
BOT: Ay, Caesar; but not gone.
USER: Hail, Caesar! Read this schedule.
BOT: Trebonius doth desire you to o'er-read, At your best leisure, this his humble suit.
USER: O Caesar, read mine first; for mine's a suit That touches Caesar nearer. Read it, great Caesar.
BOT: What touches us ourself shall be last serv'd.
USER: Delay not, Caesar. Read it instantly.
BOT: What, is the fellow mad?
USER: Sirrah, give place.
BOT: What, urge you your petitions in the street? Come to the Capitol. Caesar enters the Capitol, the rest following. All the Senators rise.
USER: I wish your enterprise today may thrive.
BOT: What enterprise, Popilius?
USER: Fare you well.
BOT: What said Popilius Lena?
USER: He wish'd today our enterprise might thrive. I fear our purpose is discovered.
BOT: Look how he makes to Caesar: mark him.
USER: Casca, be sudden, for we fear prevention. Brutus, what shall be done? If this be known, Cassius or Caesar never shall turn back, For I will slay myself.
BOT: Cassius, be constant: Popilius Lena speaks not of our purposes; For look, he smiles, and Caesar doth not change.
USER: Trebonius knows his time, for look you, Brutus, He draws Mark Antony out of the way. seats._]
BOT: Where is Metellus Cimber? Let him go, And presently prefer his suit to Caesar.
USER: He is address'd; press near and second him.
BOT: Casca, you are the first that rears your hand.
USER: Are we all ready? What is now amiss That Caesar and his Senate must redress?
BOT: Most high, most mighty, and most puissant Caesar, Metellus Cimber throws before thy seat An humble heart.
USER: Is there no voice more worthy than my own, To sound more sweetly in great Caesar's ear For the repealing of my banish'd brother?
BOT: I kiss thy hand, but not in flattery, Caesar; Desiring thee that Publius Cimber may Have an immediate freedom of repeal.
USER: What, Brutus?
BOT: Pardon, Caesar; Caesar, pardon: As low as to thy foot doth Cassius fall, To beg enfranchisement for Publius Cimber.
USER: O Caesar, -
BOT: Hence! wilt thou lift up Olympus?
USER: Great Caesar, -
BOT: Doth not Brutus bootless kneel?
USER: Speak, hands, for me! then stabbed by several other Conspirators, and at last by Marcus Brutus._]
BOT: _Et tu, Brute?_ - Then fall, Caesar!
USER: Liberty! Freedom! Tyranny is dead! Run hence, proclaim, cry it about the streets.
BOT: Some to the common pulpits and cry out, "Liberty, freedom, and enfranchisement!"
USER: People and Senators, be not affrighted. Fly not; stand still; ambition's debt is paid.
BOT: Go to the pulpit, Brutus.
USER: And Cassius too.
BOT: Where's Publius?
USER: Here, quite confounded with this mutiny.
BOT: Stand fast together, lest some friend of Caesar's Should chance -
USER: Talk not of standing. Publius, good cheer! There is no harm intended to your person, Nor to no Roman else. So tell them, Publius.
BOT: And leave us, Publius; lest that the people Rushing on us, should do your age some mischief.
USER: Do so; and let no man abide this deed But we the doers.
BOT: Where's Antony?
USER: Fled to his house amaz'd. Men, wives, and children stare, cry out, and run, As it were doomsday.
BOT: Fates, we will know your pleasures. That we shall die, we know; 'tis but the time And drawing days out, that men stand upon.
USER: Why, he that cuts off twenty years of life Cuts off so many years of fearing death.
BOT: Stoop then, and wash. How many ages hence Shall this our lofty scene be acted over In States unborn, and accents yet unknown!
USER: How many times shall Caesar bleed in sport, That now on Pompey's basis lies along, No worthier than the dust!
BOT: So oft as that shall be, So often shall the knot of us be call'd The men that gave their country liberty.
USER: What, shall we forth?
BOT: Ay, every man away. Brutus shall lead; and we will grace his heels With the most boldest and best hearts of Rome.
USER: Soft, who comes here? A friend of Antony's.
BOT: Thy master is a wise and valiant Roman; I never thought him worse. Tell him, so please him come unto this place, He shall be satisfied and, by my honour, Depart untouch'd.
USER: I'll fetch him presently.
BOT: I know that we shall have him well to friend.
USER: I wish we may: but yet have I a mind That fears him much; and my misgiving still Falls shrewdly to the purpose.
BOT: But here comes Antony. Welcome, Mark Antony.
USER: Your voice shall be as strong as any man's In the disposing of new dignities.
BOT: Only be patient till we have appeas'd The multitude, beside themselves with fear, And then we will deliver you the cause Why I, that did love Caesar when I struck him, Have thus proceeded.
USER: Mark Antony, -
BOT: Pardon me, Caius Cassius: The enemies of Caesar shall say this; Then, in a friend, it is cold modesty.
USER: I blame you not for praising Caesar so; But what compact mean you to have with us? Will you be prick'd in number of our friends, Or shall we on, and not depend on you?
BOT: Or else were this a savage spectacle. Our reasons are so full of good regard That were you, Antony, the son of Caesar, You should be satisfied.
