USER: That's all I seek, And am moreover suitor that I may Produce his body to the market-place; And in the pulpit, as becomes a friend, Speak in the order of his funeral.
BOT: You shall, Mark Antony.
USER: Brutus, a word with you. That Antony speak in his funeral. Know you how much the people may be mov'd By that which he will utter?
BOT: Be it so; I do desire no more.
USER: Prepare the body, then, and follow us.
BOT: I do, Mark Antony.
USER: Caesar did write for him to come to Rome.
BOT: He did receive his letters, and is coming, And bid me say to you by word of mouth, -
USER: Thy heart is big, get thee apart and weep. Passion, I see, is catching; for mine eyes, Seeing those beads of sorrow stand in thine, Began to water. Is thy master coming?
BOT: He lies tonight within seven leagues of Rome.

USER: We will be satisfied; let us be satisfied.
BOT: I will hear Brutus speak.
USER: I will hear Cassius; and compare their reasons, When severally we hear them rendered. rostrum._]
BOT: The noble Brutus is ascended: silence!
USER: None, Brutus, none.
BOT: Live, Brutus! live, live!
USER: Bring him with triumph home unto his house.
BOT: Give him a statue with his ancestors.
USER: Let him be Caesar.
BOT: Caesar's better parts Shall be crown'd in Brutus.
USER: We'll bring him to his house with shouts and clamours.
BOT: My countrymen, -
USER: Peace! Silence! Brutus speaks.
BOT: Peace, ho!
USER: Stay, ho! and let us hear Mark Antony.
BOT: Let him go up into the public chair. We'll hear him. Noble Antony, go up.
USER: For Brutus' sake, I am beholding to you.
BOT: What does he say of Brutus?
USER: He says, for Brutus' sake He finds himself beholding to us all.
BOT: 'Twere best he speak no harm of Brutus here!
USER: This Caesar was a tyrant.
BOT: Nay, that's certain. We are blest that Rome is rid of him.
USER: Peace! let us hear what Antony can say.
BOT: You gentle Romans, -
USER: Peace, ho! let us hear him.
BOT: Methinks there is much reason in his sayings.
USER: If thou consider rightly of the matter, Caesar has had great wrong.
BOT: Has he, masters? I fear there will a worse come in his place.
USER: Mark'd ye his words? He would not take the crown; Therefore 'tis certain he was not ambitious.
BOT: If it be found so, some will dear abide it.
USER: Poor soul, his eyes are red as fire with weeping.
BOT: There's not a nobler man in Rome than Antony.
USER: Now mark him; he begins again to speak.
BOT: We'll hear the will. Read it, Mark Antony.
USER: The will, the will! We will hear Caesar's will.
BOT: Read the will! We'll hear it, Antony; You shall read us the will, Caesar's will!
USER: Will you be patient? Will you stay awhile? I have o'ershot myself to tell you of it. I fear I wrong the honourable men Whose daggers have stabb'd Caesar; I do fear it.
BOT: They were traitors. Honourable men!
USER: The will! The testament!
BOT: They were villains, murderers. The will! Read the will!
USER: You will compel me then to read the will? Then make a ring about the corpse of Caesar, And let me show you him that made the will. Shall I descend? and will you give me leave?
BOT: Come down.
USER: Descend.
BOT: You shall have leave.
USER: A ring! Stand round.
BOT: Stand from the hearse, stand from the body.
USER: Room for Antony, most noble Antony!
BOT: Nay, press not so upon me; stand far off.
USER: Stand back; room! bear back.
BOT: O piteous spectacle!
USER: O noble Caesar!
BOT: O woeful day!
USER: O traitors, villains!
BOT: O most bloody sight!
USER: We will be revenged.
BOT: Revenge, - about, - seek, - burn, - fire, - kill, - slay, - let not a traitor live!
USER: Stay, countrymen.
BOT: Peace there! Hear the noble Antony.
USER: We'll hear him, we'll follow him, we'll die with him.
BOT: We'll mutiny.
USER: We'll burn the house of Brutus.
BOT: Away, then! come, seek the conspirators.
USER: Yet hear me, countrymen; yet hear me speak.
BOT: Peace, ho! Hear Antony; most noble Antony.
USER: Why, friends, you go to do you know not what. Wherein hath Caesar thus deserved your loves? Alas, you know not; I must tell you then. You have forgot the will I told you of.
BOT: Most true; the will! - let's stay, and hear the will.
USER: Here is the will, and under Caesar's seal. To every Roman citizen he gives, To every several man, seventy-five drachmas.
BOT: Most noble Caesar! We'll revenge his death.
USER: O, royal Caesar!
BOT: Hear me with patience.
USER: Peace, ho!
BOT: Never, never. Come, away, away! We'll burn his body in the holy place, And with the brands fire the traitors' houses. Take up the body.
USER: Go, fetch fire.
BOT: Pluck down benches.
USER: Pluck down forms, windows, anything.
BOT: Now let it work. Mischief, thou art afoot, Take thou what course thou wilt! How now, fellow?
USER: Sir, Octavius is already come to Rome.
BOT: Where is he?
USER: He and Lepidus are at Caesar's house.
BOT: And thither will I straight to visit him. He comes upon a wish. Fortune is merry, And in this mood will give us anything.
USER: I heard him say Brutus and Cassius Are rid like madmen through the gates of Rome.
BOT: Belike they had some notice of the people, How I had moved them. Bring me to Octavius.

USER: I dreamt tonight that I did feast with Caesar, And things unluckily charge my fantasy. I have no will to wander forth of doors, Yet something leads me forth.
BOT: What is your name?
USER: Whither are you going?
BOT: Where do you dwell?
USER: Are you a married man or a bachelor?
BOT: Answer every man directly.
USER: Ay, and briefly.
BOT: Ay, and wisely.
USER: Ay, and truly, you were best.
BOT: What is my name? Whither am I going? Where do I dwell? Am I a married man or a bachelor? Then, to answer every man directly and briefly, wisely and truly. Wisely I say I am a bachelor.
USER: That's as much as to say they are fools that marry; you'll bear me a bang for that, I fear. Proceed, directly.
BOT: Directly, I am going to Caesar's funeral.
USER: As a friend, or an enemy?
BOT: As a friend.
USER: That matter is answered directly.
BOT: For your dwelling, briefly.
USER: Briefly, I dwell by the Capitol.
BOT: Your name, sir, truly.
USER: Truly, my name is Cinna.
BOT: Tear him to pieces! He's a conspirator.
USER: I am Cinna the poet, I am Cinna the poet.
BOT: Tear him for his bad verses, tear him for his bad verses.
USER: I am not Cinna the conspirator.
BOT: It is no matter, his name's Cinna; pluck but his name out of his heart, and turn him going.
USER: Tear him, tear him! Come; brands, ho! firebrands. To Brutus', to Cassius'; burn all. Some to Decius' house, and some to Casca's, some to Ligarius'. Away, go!

USER: These many then shall die; their names are prick'd.
BOT: Your brother too must die; consent you, Lepidus?
USER: I do consent, -
BOT: Prick him down, Antony.
USER: Upon condition Publius shall not live, Who is your sister's son, Mark Antony.
BOT: He shall not live; look, with a spot I damn him. But, Lepidus, go you to Caesar's house; Fetch the will hither, and we shall determine How to cut off some charge in legacies.
USER: What, shall I find you here?
BOT: Or here, or at the Capitol.
USER: This is a slight unmeritable man, Meet to be sent on errands. Is it fit, The three-fold world divided, he should stand One of the three to share it?
BOT: So you thought him, And took his voice who should be prick'd to die In our black sentence and proscription.
USER: You may do your will; But he's a tried and valiant soldier.
BOT: Let us do so: for we are at the stake, And bay'd about with many enemies; And some that smile have in their hearts, I fear, Millions of mischiefs.

USER: Stand, ho!
BOT: Give the word, ho! and stand.
USER: What now, Lucilius! is Cassius near?
BOT: He is at hand, and Pindarus is come To do you salutation from his master.
USER: He greets me well. Your master, Pindarus, In his own change, or by ill officers, Hath given me some worthy cause to wish Things done, undone: but, if he be at hand, I shall be satisfied.
BOT: I do not doubt But that my noble master will appear Such as he is, full of regard and honour.
USER: He is not doubted. A word, Lucilius; How he received you, let me be resolv'd.
BOT: With courtesy and with respect enough, But not with such familiar instances, Nor with such free and friendly conference, As he hath us'd of old.
USER: They meant this night in Sardis to be quarter'd; The greater part, the horse in general, Are come with Cassius.
BOT: Hark! he is arriv'd. March gently on to meet him.
USER: Stand, ho!
BOT: Stand, ho! Speak the word along.
USER: Stand!
BOT: Stand!
USER: Stand!
BOT: Most noble brother, you have done me wrong.
USER: Judge me, you gods; wrong I mine enemies? And if not so, how should I wrong a brother?
BOT: Brutus, this sober form of yours hides wrongs; And when you do them -
USER: Pindarus, Bid our commanders lead their charges off A little from this ground.
BOT: Lucilius, do you the like; and let no man Come to our tent till we have done our conference. Lucius and Titinius, guard our door.

USER: That you have wrong'd me doth appear in this: You have condemn'd and noted Lucius Pella For taking bribes here of the Sardians; Wherein my letters, praying on his side Because I knew the man, were slighted off.
BOT: You wrong'd yourself to write in such a case.
USER: In such a time as this it is not meet That every nice offence should bear his comment.
BOT: Let me tell you, Cassius, you yourself Are much condemn'd to have an itching palm, To sell and mart your offices for gold To undeservers.
USER: I an itching palm! You know that you are Brutus that speak this, Or, by the gods, this speech were else your last.
BOT: The name of Cassius honours this corruption, And chastisement doth therefore hide his head.
USER: Chastisement!
BOT: Brutus, bait not me, I'll not endure it. You forget yourself, To hedge me in. I am a soldier, I, Older in practice, abler than yourself To make conditions.
USER: Go to; you are not, Cassius.
BOT: I am.
USER: I say you are not.
BOT: Urge me no more, I shall forget myself; Have mind upon your health, tempt me no farther.
USER: Away, slight man!
BOT: Is't possible?
USER: Hear me, for I will speak. Must I give way and room to your rash choler? Shall I be frighted when a madman stares?
BOT: O ye gods, ye gods! Must I endure all this?
USER: Is it come to this?
BOT: You say you are a better soldier: Let it appear so; make your vaunting true, And it shall please me well. For mine own part, I shall be glad to learn of noble men.
USER: You wrong me every way, you wrong me, Brutus. I said, an elder soldier, not a better: Did I say better?
BOT: If you did, I care not.
USER: When Caesar liv'd, he durst not thus have mov'd me.
BOT: Peace, peace! you durst not so have tempted him.
USER: I durst not?
BOT: No.
USER: What? durst not tempt him?
BOT: For your life you durst not.
USER: Do not presume too much upon my love. I may do that I shall be sorry for.
BOT: I denied you not.
USER: You did.
BOT: I did not. He was but a fool That brought my answer back. Brutus hath riv'd my heart. A friend should bear his friend's infirmities; But Brutus makes mine greater than they are.
USER: I do not, till you practise them on me.
BOT: You love me not.
USER: I do not like your faults.
BOT: A friendly eye could never see such faults.
USER: A flatterer's would not, though they do appear As huge as high Olympus.
BOT: Hath Cassius liv'd To be but mirth and laughter to his Brutus, When grief and blood ill-temper'd vexeth him?
USER: When I spoke that, I was ill-temper'd too.
BOT: Do you confess so much? Give me your hand.
USER: And my heart too.
BOT: O Brutus!
USER: What's the matter?
BOT: Have not you love enough to bear with me, When that rash humour which my mother gave me Makes me forgetful?
USER: Yes, Cassius; and from henceforth, When you are over-earnest with your Brutus, He'll think your mother chides, and leave you so.
BOT: There is some grudge between 'em; 'tis not meet They be alone.
USER: How now! What's the matter?
BOT: For shame, you generals! What do you mean? Love, and be friends, as two such men should be; For I have seen more years, I'm sure, than ye.
USER: Ha, ha! How vilely doth this cynic rhyme!
BOT: Get you hence, sirrah. Saucy fellow, hence!
USER: Bear with him, Brutus; 'tis his fashion.
BOT: I'll know his humour when he knows his time. What should the wars do with these jigging fools? Companion, hence!
USER: Away, away, be gone!
BOT: Lucilius and Titinius, bid the commanders Prepare to lodge their companies tonight.
USER: And come yourselves and bring Messala with you Immediately to us.
BOT: Lucius, a bowl of wine.
USER: I did not think you could have been so angry.
BOT: O Cassius, I am sick of many griefs.
USER: Of your philosophy you make no use, If you give place to accidental evils.
BOT: No man bears sorrow better. Portia is dead.
USER: Ha? Portia?
BOT: She is dead.
USER: How 'scap'd I killing, when I cross'd you so? O insupportable and touching loss! Upon what sickness?
BOT: Impatient of my absence, And grief that young Octavius with Mark Antony Have made themselves so strong; for with her death That tidings came. With this she fell distract, And, her attendants absent, swallow'd fire.
USER: And died so?
BOT: Even so.
USER: O ye immortal gods!
BOT: Speak no more of her. Give me a bowl of wine. In this I bury all unkindness, Cassius.
USER: My heart is thirsty for that noble pledge. Fill, Lucius, till the wine o'erswell the cup. I cannot drink too much of Brutus' love.
BOT: Come in, Titinius! Welcome, good Messala. Now sit we close about this taper here, And call in question our necessities.
USER: Portia, art thou gone?
BOT: No more, I pray you. Messala, I have here received letters, That young Octavius and Mark Antony Come down upon us with a mighty power, Bending their expedition toward Philippi.
USER: Myself have letters of the selfsame tenor.
BOT: With what addition?
USER: That by proscription and bills of outlawry Octavius, Antony, and Lepidus Have put to death an hundred Senators.
BOT: Therein our letters do not well agree. Mine speak of seventy Senators that died By their proscriptions, Cicero being one.
USER: Cicero one!
BOT: Cicero is dead, And by that order of proscription. Had you your letters from your wife, my lord?
USER: No, Messala.
BOT: Nor nothing in your letters writ of her?
USER: Nothing, Messala.
BOT: That, methinks, is strange.
USER: Why ask you? Hear you aught of her in yours?
BOT: No, my lord.
USER: Now as you are a Roman, tell me true.
BOT: Then like a Roman bear the truth I tell, For certain she is dead, and by strange manner.
USER: Why, farewell, Portia. We must die, Messala. With meditating that she must die once, I have the patience to endure it now.
BOT: Even so great men great losses should endure.
USER: I have as much of this in art as you, But yet my nature could not bear it so.
BOT: Well, to our work alive. What do you think Of marching to Philippi presently?
USER: I do not think it good.
BOT: Your reason?
USER: This it is: 'Tis better that the enemy seek us; So shall he waste his means, weary his soldiers, Doing himself offence, whilst we, lying still, Are full of rest, defence, and nimbleness.
BOT: Hear me, good brother.
USER: Then, with your will, go on: We'll along ourselves, and meet them at Philippi.
BOT: The deep of night is crept upon our talk, And nature must obey necessity, Which we will niggard with a little rest. There is no more to say?
USER: No more. Good night: Early tomorrow will we rise, and hence.
BOT: Lucius! My gown. Farewell now, good Messala. Good night, Titinius. Noble, noble Cassius, Good night, and good repose.
USER: O my dear brother! This was an ill beginning of the night. Never come such division 'tween our souls! Let it not, Brutus.
BOT: Everything is well.
USER: Good night, my lord.
BOT: Good night, good brother. TITINIUS and MESSALA. Good night, Lord Brutus.
USER: Farewell, everyone. Give me the gown. Where is thy instrument?
BOT: Here in the tent.
USER: What, thou speak'st drowsily? Poor knave, I blame thee not, thou art o'er-watch'd. Call Claudius and some other of my men; I'll have them sleep on cushions in my tent.
BOT: Varro and Claudius!
USER: Calls my lord?
BOT: I pray you, sirs, lie in my tent and sleep; It may be I shall raise you by-and-by On business to my brother Cassius.
USER: So please you, we will stand and watch your pleasure.
BOT: I will not have it so; lie down, good sirs, It may be I shall otherwise bethink me. Look, Lucius, here's the book I sought for so; I put it in the pocket of my gown.
USER: I was sure your lordship did not give it me.
BOT: Bear with me, good boy, I am much forgetful. Canst thou hold up thy heavy eyes awhile, And touch thy instrument a strain or two?
USER: Ay, my lord, an't please you.
BOT: It does, my boy. I trouble thee too much, but thou art willing.
USER: It is my duty, sir.
BOT: I should not urge thy duty past thy might; I know young bloods look for a time of rest.
USER: I have slept, my lord, already.
BOT: Thy evil spirit, Brutus.
USER: Why com'st thou?
BOT: To tell thee thou shalt see me at Philippi.
USER: Well; then I shall see thee again?
BOT: Ay, at Philippi.
USER: Why, I will see thee at Philippi then. Now I have taken heart, thou vanishest. Ill spirit, I would hold more talk with thee. Boy! Lucius! Varro! Claudius! Sirs, awake! Claudius!
BOT: The strings, my lord, are false.
USER: He thinks he still is at his instrument. Lucius, awake!
BOT: My lord?
USER: Didst thou dream, Lucius, that thou so criedst out?
BOT: My lord, I do not know that I did cry.
USER: Yes, that thou didst. Didst thou see anything?
BOT: Nothing, my lord.
USER: Sleep again, Lucius. Sirrah Claudius! Fellow thou, awake!
BOT: My lord?
USER: My lord?
BOT: Why did you so cry out, sirs, in your sleep?
USER: Did we, my lord?
BOT: Ay. Saw you anything?
USER: No, my lord, I saw nothing.
BOT: Nor I, my lord.
USER: Go and commend me to my brother Cassius; Bid him set on his powers betimes before, And we will follow.
BOT: It shall be done, my lord.

USER: Prepare you, generals. The enemy comes on in gallant show; Their bloody sign of battle is hung out, And something to be done immediately.
BOT: Octavius, lead your battle softly on Upon the left hand of the even field.
USER: Upon the right hand I. Keep thou the left.
BOT: Why do you cross me in this exigent?
USER: I do not cross you; but I will do so. Drum. Enter Brutus, Cassius and their Army; Lucilius, Titinius, Messala and others.
BOT: They stand, and would have parley.
USER: Stand fast, Titinius; we must out and talk.
BOT: Mark Antony, shall we give sign of battle?
USER: No, Caesar, we will answer on their charge. Make forth; the generals would have some words.
BOT: Stir not until the signal.
USER: Words before blows: is it so, countrymen?
BOT: Not that we love words better, as you do.
USER: Good words are better than bad strokes, Octavius.
BOT: In your bad strokes, Brutus, you give good words; Witness the hole you made in Caesar's heart, Crying, "Long live! Hail, Caesar!"
USER: Antony, The posture of your blows are yet unknown; But for your words, they rob the Hybla bees, And leave them honeyless.
BOT: Not stingless too.
USER: O yes, and soundless too, For you have stol'n their buzzing, Antony, And very wisely threat before you sting.
BOT: Flatterers! Now, Brutus, thank yourself. This tongue had not offended so today, If Cassius might have rul'd.
USER: Caesar, thou canst not die by traitors' hands, Unless thou bring'st them with thee.
BOT: So I hope. I was not born to die on Brutus' sword.
USER: O, if thou wert the noblest of thy strain, Young man, thou couldst not die more honourable.
BOT: A peevish school-boy, worthless of such honour, Join'd with a masker and a reveller.
USER: Old Cassius still!
BOT: Come, Antony; away! Defiance, traitors, hurl we in your teeth. If you dare fight today, come to the field; If not, when you have stomachs.
USER: Why now, blow wind, swell billow, and swim bark! The storm is up, and all is on the hazard.
BOT: Ho, Lucilius! Hark, a word with you.
USER: My lord?
BOT: Messala.
USER: What says my General?
BOT: Believe not so.
USER: I but believe it partly, For I am fresh of spirit, and resolv'd To meet all perils very constantly.
BOT: Even so, Lucilius.
USER: Then, if we lose this battle, You are contented to be led in triumph Thorough the streets of Rome?
BOT: For ever and for ever farewell, Brutus. If we do meet again, we'll smile indeed; If not, 'tis true this parting was well made.
USER: Why then, lead on. O, that a man might know

USER: O, look, Titinius, look, the villains fly! Myself have to mine own turn'd enemy: This ensign here of mine was turning back; I slew the coward, and did take it from him.
BOT: O Cassius, Brutus gave the word too early, Who, having some advantage on Octavius, Took it too eagerly: his soldiers fell to spoil, Whilst we by Antony are all enclos'd.
USER: Fly further off, my lord, fly further off; Mark Antony is in your tents, my lord. Fly, therefore, noble Cassius, fly far off.
BOT: This hill is far enough. Look, look, Titinius; Are those my tents where I perceive the fire?
USER: They are, my lord.
BOT: Titinius, if thou lovest me, Mount thou my horse and hide thy spurs in him, Till he have brought thee up to yonder troops And here again, that I may rest assur'd Whether yond troops are friend or enemy.
USER: I will be here again, even with a thought.
BOT: What news?
USER: With horsemen, that make to him on the spur, Yet he spurs on. Now they are almost on him. Now, Titinius! Now some light. O, he lights too. He's ta'en! And, hark! they shout for joy.
BOT: So, I am free, yet would not so have been, Durst I have done my will. O Cassius! Far from this country Pindarus shall run, Where never Roman shall take note of him.
USER: It is but change, Titinius; for Octavius Is overthrown by noble Brutus' power, As Cassius' legions are by Antony.
BOT: These tidings would well comfort Cassius.
USER: Where did you leave him?
BOT: All disconsolate, With Pindarus his bondman, on this hill.
USER: Is not that he that lies upon the ground?
BOT: He lies not like the living. O my heart!
USER: Is not that he?
BOT: What, Pindarus! where art thou, Pindarus?
USER: Where, where, Messala, doth his body lie?
BOT: Lo, yonder, and Titinius mourning it.
USER: Titinius' face is upward.
BOT: He is slain.
USER: O Julius Caesar, thou art mighty yet! Thy spirit walks abroad, and turns our swords In our own proper entrails.
BOT: Brave Titinius! Look whether he have not crown'd dead Cassius!

USER: Yet, countrymen, O, yet hold up your heads!
BOT: What bastard doth not? Who will go with me? I will proclaim my name about the field. I am the son of Marcus Cato, ho! A foe to tyrants, and my country's friend. I am the son of Marcus Cato, ho!
USER: And I am Brutus, Marcus Brutus, I; Brutus, my country's friend; know me for Brutus!
BOT: O young and noble Cato, art thou down? Why, now thou diest as bravely as Titinius, And mayst be honour'd, being Cato's son.
USER: Yield, or thou diest.
BOT: Only I yield to die: There is so much that thou wilt kill me straight; Kill Brutus, and be honour'd in his death.
USER: We must not. A noble prisoner!
BOT: Room, ho! Tell Antony, Brutus is ta'en.
USER: I'll tell the news. Here comes the General. Brutus is ta'en, Brutus is ta'en, my lord.
BOT: Where is he?

USER: Come, poor remains of friends, rest on this rock.
BOT: Statilius show'd the torch-light; but, my lord, He came not back: he is or ta'en or slain.
USER: Sit thee down, Clitus. Slaying is the word; It is a deed in fashion. Hark thee, Clitus.
BOT: What, I, my lord? No, not for all the world.
USER: Peace then, no words.
BOT: I'll rather kill myself.
USER: Hark thee, Dardanius.
BOT: Shall I do such a deed?
USER: O Dardanius!
BOT: O Clitus!
USER: What ill request did Brutus make to thee?
BOT: To kill him, Clitus. Look, he meditates.
USER: Now is that noble vessel full of grief, That it runs over even at his eyes.
BOT: Come hither, good Volumnius; list a word.
USER: What says my lord?
BOT: Why, this, Volumnius: The ghost of Caesar hath appear'd to me Two several times by night; at Sardis once, And this last night here in Philippi fields. I know my hour is come.
USER: Not so, my lord.
BOT: That's not an office for a friend, my lord.
USER: Fly, fly, my lord! there is no tarrying here.
BOT: Fly, my lord, fly!
USER: Give me your hand first. Fare you well, my lord.
BOT: Farewell, good Strato. - Caesar, now be still: I kill'd not thee with half so good a will. Alarum. Retreat. Enter Antony, Octavius, Messala, Lucilius and the Army.
USER: What man is that?
BOT: My master's man. Strato, where is thy master?
USER: Free from the bondage you are in, Messala. The conquerors can but make a fire of him; For Brutus only overcame himself, And no man else hath honour by his death.
BOT: So Brutus should be found. I thank thee, Brutus, That thou hast prov'd Lucilius' saying true.
USER: All that serv'd Brutus, I will entertain them. Fellow, wilt thou bestow thy time with me?
BOT: Ay, if Messala will prefer me to you.
USER: Do so, good Messala.
BOT: How died my master, Strato?
USER: I held the sword, and he did run on it.
BOT: Octavius, then take him to follow thee, That did the latest service to my master.

USER: I thought the King had more affected the Duke of Albany than Cornwall.
BOT: It did always seem so to us; but now, in the division of the kingdom, it appears not which of the Dukes he values most, for qualities are so weighed that curiosity in neither can make choice of either's moiety.
USER: Is not this your son, my lord?
BOT: His breeding, sir, hath been at my charge: I have so often blush'd to acknowledge him that now I am braz'd to't.
USER: I cannot conceive you.
BOT: Sir, this young fellow's mother could; whereupon she grew round-wombed, and had indeed, sir, a son for her cradle ere she had a husband for her bed. Do you smell a fault?
USER: I cannot wish the fault undone, the issue of it being so proper.
BOT: No, my lord.
USER: My Lord of Kent: remember him hereafter as my honourable friend.
BOT: My services to your lordship.
USER: I must love you, and sue to know you better.
BOT: Sir, I shall study deserving.
USER: He hath been out nine years, and away he shall again. The King is coming.
BOT: Attend the lords of France and Burgundy, Gloucester.
USER: I shall, my lord.
BOT: And yet not so; since, I am sure, my love's More ponderous than my tongue.
USER: Nothing, my lord.
BOT: Nothing?
USER: Nothing.
BOT: Nothing will come of nothing: speak again.
USER: Unhappy that I am, I cannot heave My heart into my mouth: I love your majesty According to my bond; no more nor less.
BOT: How, how, Cordelia? Mend your speech a little, Lest you may mar your fortunes.
USER: But goes thy heart with this?
BOT: Ay, my good lord.
USER: So young, and so untender?
BOT: So young, my lord, and true.
USER: Good my liege, -
BOT: Royal Lear, Whom I have ever honour'd as my king, Lov'd as my father, as my master follow'd, As my great patron thought on in my prayers. -
USER: The bow is bent and drawn; make from the shaft.
BOT: Kent, on thy life, no more.
USER: My life I never held but as a pawn To wage against thine enemies; ne'er fear to lose it, Thy safety being the motive.
BOT: Out of my sight!
USER: See better, Lear; and let me still remain The true blank of thine eye.
BOT: Now, by Apollo, -
USER: Now by Apollo, King, Thou swear'st thy gods in vain.
BOT: O vassal! Miscreant! ALBANY and CORNWALL. Dear sir, forbear!
USER: Kill thy physician, and the fee bestow Upon the foul disease. Revoke thy gift, Or, whilst I can vent clamour from my throat, I'll tell thee thou dost evil.
BOT: Here's France and Burgundy, my noble lord.
USER: My Lord of Burgundy, We first address toward you, who with this king Hath rivall'd for our daughter: what in the least Will you require in present dower with her, Or cease your quest of love?
BOT: Most royal majesty, I crave no more than hath your highness offer'd, Nor will you tender less.
USER: I know no answer.
BOT: Will you, with those infirmities she owes, Unfriended, new adopted to our hate, Dower'd with our curse, and stranger'd with our oath, Take her or leave her?
USER: Pardon me, royal sir; Election makes not up in such conditions.
BOT: Better thou hadst Not been born than not to have pleas'd me better.
USER: Royal King, Give but that portion which yourself propos'd, And here I take Cordelia by the hand, Duchess of Burgundy.
BOT: Nothing: I have sworn; I am firm.
USER: I am sorry, then, you have so lost a father That you must lose a husband.
BOT: Peace be with Burgundy! Since that respects of fortunes are his love, I shall not be his wife.
USER: Thou hast her, France: let her be thine; for we Have no such daughter, nor shall ever see That face of hers again. Therefore be gone Without our grace, our love, our benison. Come, noble Burgundy. Attendants._]
BOT: Bid farewell to your sisters.
USER: Prescribe not us our duties.
BOT: Let your study Be to content your lord, who hath receiv'd you At fortune's alms. You have obedience scanted, And well are worth the want that you have wanted.
USER: Time shall unfold what plighted cunning hides: Who covers faults, at last shame derides. Well may you prosper.
BOT: Come, my fair Cordelia.
USER: Sister, it is not little I have to say of what most nearly appertains to us both. I think our father will hence tonight.
BOT: That's most certain, and with you; next month with us.
USER: You see how full of changes his age is; the observation we have made of it hath not been little: he always loved our sister most; and with what poor judgement he hath now cast her off appears too grossly.
BOT: 'Tis the infirmity of his age: yet he hath ever but slenderly known himself.
USER: Such unconstant starts are we like to have from him as this of Kent's banishment.
BOT: There is further compliment of leave-taking between France and him. Pray you let us hit together: if our father carry authority with such disposition as he bears, this last surrender of his will but offend us.
USER: We shall further think of it.
BOT: We must do something, and i' th' heat.

USER: Kent banish'd thus! and France in choler parted! And the King gone tonight! Prescrib'd his pow'r! Confin'd to exhibition! All this done Upon the gad! - Edmund, how now! What news?
BOT: So please your lordship, none.
USER: Why so earnestly seek you to put up that letter?
BOT: I know no news, my lord.
USER: What paper were you reading?
BOT: Nothing, my lord.
USER: No? What needed then that terrible dispatch of it into your pocket? The quality of nothing hath not such need to hide itself. Let's see. Come, if it be nothing, I shall not need spectacles.
BOT: I beseech you, sir, pardon me. It is a letter from my brother that I have not all o'er-read; and for so much as I have perus'd, I find it not fit for your o'er-looking.
USER: Give me the letter, sir.
BOT: I shall offend, either to detain or give it. The contents, as in part I understand them, are to blame.
USER: Let's see, let's see!
BOT: I hope, for my brother's justification, he wrote this but as an essay, or taste of my virtue.
USER: It was not brought me, my lord, there's the cunning of it. I found it thrown in at the casement of my closet.
BOT: You know the character to be your brother's?
USER: If the matter were good, my lord, I durst swear it were his; but in respect of that, I would fain think it were not.
BOT: It is his.
USER: It is his hand, my lord; but I hope his heart is not in the contents.
BOT: Has he never before sounded you in this business?
USER: Never, my lord. But I have heard him oft maintain it to be fit that, sons at perfect age, and fathers declined, the father should be as ward to the son, and the son manage his revenue.
BOT: O villain, villain! His very opinion in the letter! Abhorred villain! Unnatural, detested, brutish villain! worse than brutish! Go, sirrah, seek him; I'll apprehend him. Abominable villain, Where is he?
USER: Think you so?
BOT: If your honour judge it meet, I will place you where you shall hear us confer of this, and by an auricular assurance have your satisfaction, and that without any further delay than this very evening.
USER: He cannot be such a monster.
BOT: Nor is not, sure.
USER: To his father, that so tenderly and entirely loves him. Heaven and earth! Edmund, seek him out; wind me into him, I pray you: frame the business after your own wisdom. I would unstate myself to be in a due resolution.
BOT: I will seek him, sir, presently; convey the business as I shall find means, and acquaint you withal.
USER: How now, brother Edmund, what serious contemplation are you in?
BOT: I am thinking, brother, of a prediction I read this other day, what should follow these eclipses.
USER: Do you busy yourself with that?
BOT: How long have you been a sectary astronomical?
USER: Come, come! when saw you my father last?
BOT: The night gone by.
USER: Spake you with him?
BOT: Ay, two hours together.
USER: Parted you in good terms? Found you no displeasure in him, by word nor countenance?
BOT: None at all.
USER: Some villain hath done me wrong.
BOT: Armed, brother?
USER: Brother, I advise you to the best; I am no honest man if there be any good meaning toward you: I have told you what I have seen and heard. But faintly; nothing like the image and horror of it: pray you, away!
BOT: Shall I hear from you anon?

USER: Did my father strike my gentleman for chiding of his fool?
BOT: Ay, madam.
USER: He's coming, madam; I hear him.
BOT: Very well, madam.

USER: Let me not stay a jot for dinner; go get it ready. How now! what art thou?
BOT: A man, sir.
USER: What dost thou profess? What wouldst thou with us?
BOT: What art thou?
USER: A very honest-hearted fellow, and as poor as the King.
BOT: If thou be'st as poor for a subject as he's for a king, thou art poor enough. What wouldst thou?
USER: Service.
BOT: Who wouldst thou serve?
USER: You.
BOT: Dost thou know me, fellow?
USER: No, sir; but you have that in your countenance which I would fain call master.
BOT: What's that?
USER: Authority.
BOT: What services canst thou do?
USER: I can keep honest counsel, ride, run, mar a curious tale in telling it and deliver a plain message bluntly. That which ordinary men are fit for, I am qualified in, and the best of me is diligence.
BOT: How old art thou?
USER: Not so young, sir, to love a woman for singing; nor so old to dote on her for anything: I have years on my back forty-eight.
BOT: Follow me; thou shalt serve me. If I like thee no worse after dinner, I will not part from thee yet. Dinner, ho, dinner! Where's my knave? my fool? Go you and call my fool hither. You, you, sirrah, where's my daughter?
USER: So please you, -
BOT: What says the fellow there? Call the clotpoll back. Where's my fool? Ho, I think the world's asleep. How now! where's that mongrel?
USER: He says, my lord, your daughter is not well.
BOT: Why came not the slave back to me when I called him?
USER: Sir, he answered me in the roundest manner, he would not.
BOT: He would not?
USER: Ha! say'st thou so?
BOT: I beseech you pardon me, my lord, if I be mistaken; for my duty cannot be silent when I think your highness wronged.
USER: Since my young lady's going into France, sir, the fool hath much pined away.
BOT: No more of that; I have noted it well. Go you and tell my daughter I would speak with her. Go you, call hither my fool. O, you, sir, you, come you hither, sir: who am I, sir?
USER: My lady's father.
BOT: My lady's father! my lord's knave: you whoreson dog! you slave! you cur!
USER: I am none of these, my lord; I beseech your pardon.
BOT: Do you bandy looks with me, you rascal?
USER: I'll not be struck, my lord.
BOT: Nor tripp'd neither, you base football player.
USER: I thank thee, fellow. Thou serv'st me, and I'll love thee.
BOT: Come, sir, arise, away! I'll teach you differences: away, away! If you will measure your lubber's length again, tarry; but away! go to; have you wisdom? So.
USER: Now, my friendly knave, I thank thee: there's earnest of thy service.
BOT: Let me hire him too; here's my coxcomb.
USER: How now, my pretty knave, how dost thou?
BOT: Sirrah, you were best take my coxcomb.
USER: Why, fool?
BOT: Why, my boy?
USER: If I gave them all my living, I'd keep my coxcombs myself. There's mine; beg another of thy daughters.
BOT: Take heed, sirrah, the whip.
USER: Truth's a dog must to kennel; he must be whipped out, when the Lady Brach may stand by the fire and stink.
BOT: A pestilent gall to me!
USER: Sirrah, I'll teach thee a speech.
BOT: Do.
USER: This is nothing, fool.
BOT: Then 'tis like the breath of an unfee'd lawyer, you gave me nothing for't. Can you make no use of nothing, nuncle?
USER: Why, no, boy; nothing can be made out of nothing.
BOT: comes to: he will not believe a fool.
USER: A bitter fool.
BOT: Dost thou know the difference, my boy, between a bitter fool and a sweet one?
USER: No, lad; teach me.
BOT: That lord that counsell'd thee To give away thy land, Come place him here by me, Do thou for him stand. The sweet and bitter fool Will presently appear; The one in motley here, The other found out there.
USER: Dost thou call me fool, boy?
BOT: All thy other titles thou hast given away; that thou wast born with.
USER: This is not altogether fool, my lord.
BOT: What two crowns shall they be?
USER: When were you wont to be so full of songs, sirrah?
BOT: An you lie, sirrah, we'll have you whipped.
USER: How now, daughter? What makes that frontlet on? Methinks you are too much of late i' the frown.
BOT: For you know, nuncle, The hedge-sparrow fed the cuckoo so long That it's had it head bit off by it young. So out went the candle, and we were left darkling.
USER: Are you our daughter?
BOT: Come, sir, I would you would make use of that good wisdom, Whereof I know you are fraught; and put away These dispositions, which of late transform you From what you rightly are.
USER: May not an ass know when the cart draws the horse? Whoop, Jug! I love thee!
BOT: Doth any here know me? This is not Lear; Doth Lear walk thus? speak thus? Where are his eyes? Either his notion weakens, his discernings Are lethargied. Ha! waking? 'Tis not so! Who is it that can tell me who I am?
USER: Lear's shadow.
BOT: I would learn that; for by the marks of sovereignty, knowledge and reason, I should be false persuaded I had daughters.
USER: Which they will make an obedient father.
BOT: Your name, fair gentlewoman?
USER: Darkness and devils! Saddle my horses; call my train together. Degenerate bastard! I'll not trouble thee: Yet have I left a daughter.
BOT: You strike my people; and your disorder'd rabble Make servants of their betters.
USER: Woe that too late repents! - Is it your will? Speak, sir. - Prepare my horses. Ingratitude, thou marble-hearted fiend, More hideous when thou show'st thee in a child Than the sea-monster!
BOT: Pray, sir, be patient.
USER: My lord, I am guiltless, as I am ignorant Of what hath moved you.
BOT: Now, gods that we adore, whereof comes this?
USER: Never afflict yourself to know more of it; But let his disposition have that scope That dotage gives it.
BOT: What, fifty of my followers at a clap? Within a fortnight?
USER: What's the matter, sir?
BOT: Do you mark that?
USER: I cannot be so partial, Goneril, To the great love I bear you, -
BOT: Pray you, content. What, Oswald, ho!
USER: Nuncle Lear, nuncle Lear, tarry and take the fool with thee. A fox when one has caught her, And such a daughter, Should sure to the slaughter, If my cap would buy a halter; So the fool follows after.
BOT: Well, you may fear too far.
USER: Ay, madam.
BOT: How far your eyes may pierce I cannot tell: Striving to better, oft we mar what's well.
USER: Nay then, -
BOT: Well, well; the event.

USER: Go you before to Gloucester with these letters: acquaint my daughter no further with anything you know than comes from her demand out of the letter. If your diligence be not speedy, I shall be there afore you.
BOT: I will not sleep, my lord, till I have delivered your letter.
USER: If a man's brains were in's heels, were't not in danger of kibes?
BOT: Ay, boy.
USER: Then I prithee be merry; thy wit shall not go slipshod.
BOT: Ha, ha, ha!
USER: Shalt see thy other daughter will use thee kindly, for though she's as like this as a crab's like an apple, yet I can tell what I can tell.
BOT: What canst tell, boy?
USER: She'll taste as like this as a crab does to a crab. Thou canst tell why one's nose stands i'the middle on's face?
BOT: No.
USER: Why, to keep one's eyes of either side's nose, that what a man cannot smell out, he may spy into.
BOT: I did her wrong.
USER: Canst tell how an oyster makes his shell?
BOT: No.
USER: Nor I neither; but I can tell why a snail has a house.
BOT: Why?
USER: Why, to put's head in; not to give it away to his daughters, and leave his horns without a case.
BOT: I will forget my nature. So kind a father! Be my horses ready?
USER: Thy asses are gone about 'em. The reason why the seven stars are no more than seven is a pretty reason.
BOT: Because they are not eight?
USER: Yes indeed: thou wouldst make a good fool.
BOT: To tak't again perforce! - Monster ingratitude!
USER: If thou wert my fool, nuncle, I'd have thee beaten for being old before thy time.
BOT: How's that?
USER: Thou shouldst not have been old till thou hadst been wise.
BOT: O, let me not be mad, not mad, sweet heaven! Keep me in temper; I would not be mad! How now? are the horses ready?
USER: Ready, my lord.
BOT: Come, boy.
USER: She that's a maid now, and laughs at my departure, Shall not be a maid long, unless things be cut shorter.

USER: Save thee, Curan.
BOT: And you, sir. I have been with your father, and given him notice that the Duke of Cornwall and Regan his Duchess will be here with him this night.
USER: How comes that?
BOT: Nay, I know not. You have heard of the news abroad; I mean the whispered ones, for they are yet but ear-kissing arguments?
USER: Not I: pray you, what are they?
BOT: Have you heard of no likely wars toward, 'twixt the two dukes of Cornwall and Albany?
USER: Not a word.
BOT: You may do, then, in time. Fare you well, sir.
USER: I am sure on't, not a word.
BOT: Now, Edmund, where's the villain?
USER: Here stood he in the dark, his sharp sword out, Mumbling of wicked charms, conjuring the moon To stand auspicious mistress.
BOT: But where is he?
USER: Look, sir, I bleed.
BOT: Where is the villain, Edmund?
USER: Fled this way, sir. When by no means he could, -
BOT: Pursue him, ho! Go after. - By no means what?
USER: How now, my noble friend! since I came hither, Which I can call but now, I have heard strange news.
BOT: If it be true, all vengeance comes too short Which can pursue th'offender. How dost, my lord?
USER: O madam, my old heart is crack'd, it's crack'd!
BOT: What, did my father's godson seek your life? He whom my father nam'd? your Edgar?
USER: O lady, lady, shame would have it hid!
BOT: Was he not companion with the riotous knights That tend upon my father?
USER: I know not, madam; 'tis too bad, too bad.
BOT: Yes, madam, he was of that consort.
USER: Nor I, assure thee, Regan. Edmund, I hear that you have shown your father A childlike office.
BOT: It was my duty, sir.
USER: He did bewray his practice; and receiv'd This hurt you see, striving to apprehend him.
BOT: Is he pursued?
USER: Ay, my good lord.
BOT: I shall serve you, sir, truly, however else.
USER: For him I thank your grace.
BOT: You know not why we came to visit you?
USER: I serve you, madam: Your graces are right welcome.

USER: Good dawning to thee, friend: art of this house?
BOT: Ay.
USER: Where may we set our horses?
BOT: I' the mire.
USER: Prithee, if thou lov'st me, tell me.
BOT: I love thee not.
USER: Why then, I care not for thee.
BOT: If I had thee in Lipsbury pinfold, I would make thee care for me.
USER: Why dost thou use me thus? I know thee not.
BOT: Fellow, I know thee.
USER: What dost thou know me for?
BOT: Why, what a monstrous fellow art thou, thus to rail on one that's neither known of thee nor knows thee?
USER: Away! I have nothing to do with thee.
BOT: Draw, you rascal: you come with letters against the King; and take vanity the puppet's part against the royalty of her father: draw, you rogue, or I'll so carbonado your shanks: - draw, you rascal; come your ways!
USER: Help, ho! murder! help!
BOT: Strike, you slave; stand, rogue, stand; you neat slave, strike!
USER: Help, ho! murder! murder! Gloucester and Servants.
BOT: How now! What's the matter? Part!
USER: With you, goodman boy, if you please: come, I'll flesh ye; come on, young master.
BOT: Weapons! arms! What's the matter here?
USER: Keep peace, upon your lives, he dies that strikes again. What is the matter?
BOT: The messengers from our sister and the King.
USER: What is your difference? Speak.
BOT: I am scarce in breath, my lord.
USER: No marvel, you have so bestirr'd your valour. You cowardly rascal, nature disclaims in thee; a tailor made thee.
BOT: Thou art a strange fellow: a tailor make a man?
USER: Ay, a tailor, sir: a stonecutter or a painter could not have made him so ill, though he had been but two years at the trade.
BOT: Speak yet, how grew your quarrel?
USER: This ancient ruffian, sir, whose life I have spared at suit of his grey beard, -
BOT: Thou whoreson zed! thou unnecessary letter! My lord, if you'll give me leave, I will tread this unbolted villain into mortar and daub the walls of a jakes with him. Spare my grey beard, you wagtail?
USER: Peace, sirrah! You beastly knave, know you no reverence?
BOT: Yes, sir; but anger hath a privilege.
USER: Why art thou angry?
BOT: What, art thou mad, old fellow?
USER: How fell you out? Say that.
BOT: No contraries hold more antipathy Than I and such a knave.
USER: Why dost thou call him knave? What is his fault?
BOT: His countenance likes me not.
USER: No more perchance does mine, or his, or hers.
BOT: Sir, 'tis my occupation to be plain: I have seen better faces in my time Than stands on any shoulder that I see Before me at this instant.
USER: Sir, in good faith, in sincere verity, Under th'allowance of your great aspect, Whose influence, like the wreath of radiant fire On flickering Phoebus' front, -
BOT: What mean'st by this?
USER: What was the offence you gave him?
BOT: None of these rogues and cowards But Ajax is their fool.
USER: Fetch forth the stocks! You stubborn ancient knave, you reverent braggart, We'll teach you.
BOT: Fetch forth the stocks! As I have life and honour, there shall he sit till noon.
USER: Till noon! Till night, my lord; and all night too!
BOT: Why, madam, if I were your father's dog, You should not use me so.
USER: Sir, being his knave, I will.
BOT: This is a fellow of the selfsame colour Our sister speaks of. Come, bring away the stocks!
USER: I'll answer that.
BOT: My sister may receive it much more worse, To have her gentleman abus'd, assaulted, For following her affairs. Put in his legs. stocks._]
USER: Come, my good lord, away.
BOT: I am sorry for thee, friend; 'tis the Duke's pleasure, Whose disposition, all the world well knows, Will not be rubb'd nor stopp'd; I'll entreat for thee.
USER: Pray do not, sir: I have watch'd, and travell'd hard; Some time I shall sleep out, the rest I'll whistle. A good man's fortune may grow out at heels: Give you good morrow!
BOT: The Duke's to blame in this: 'twill be ill taken.

USER: 'Tis strange that they should so depart from home, And not send back my messenger.
BOT: As I learn'd, The night before there was no purpose in them Of this remove.
USER: Hail to thee, noble master!
BOT: Ha! Mak'st thou this shame thy pastime?
USER: No, my lord.
BOT: Ha, ha! he wears cruel garters. Horses are tied by the heads; dogs and bears by the neck, monkeys by the loins, and men by the legs: when a man is overlusty at legs, then he wears wooden nether-stocks.
USER: What's he that hath so much thy place mistook To set thee here?
BOT: It is both he and she, Your son and daughter.
USER: No.
BOT: Yes.
USER: No, I say.
BOT: I say, yea.
USER: No, no; they would not.
BOT: Yes, they have.
USER: By Jupiter, I swear no.
BOT: By Juno, I swear ay.
USER: O, how this mother swells up toward my heart! _Hysterica passio_, down, thou climbing sorrow, Thy element's below! Where is this daughter?
BOT: With the earl, sir, here within.
USER: Follow me not; stay here.
BOT: Made you no more offence but what you speak of?
USER: None. How chance the King comes with so small a number?
BOT: An thou hadst been set i' the stocks for that question, thou hadst well deserved it.
USER: Why, fool?
BOT: Where learn'd you this, fool?
USER: Not i' the stocks, fool.
BOT: Deny to speak with me? They are sick? they are weary? They have travell'd all the night? Mere fetches; The images of revolt and flying off. Fetch me a better answer.
USER: My dear lord, You know the fiery quality of the Duke; How unremovable and fix'd he is In his own course.
BOT: Vengeance! plague! death! confusion! Fiery? What quality? Why, Gloucester, Gloucester, I'd speak with the Duke of Cornwall and his wife.
USER: Well, my good lord, I have inform'd them so.
BOT: Inform'd them! Dost thou understand me, man?
USER: Ay, my good lord.
BOT: I would have all well betwixt you.
USER: O me, my heart, my rising heart! But down!
BOT: Good morrow to you both.
USER: Hail to your grace!
BOT: I am glad to see your highness.
USER: I pray you, sir, take patience. I have hope You less know how to value her desert Than she to scant her duty.
BOT: Say, how is that?
USER: I cannot think my sister in the least Would fail her obligation. If, sir, perchance She have restrain'd the riots of your followers, 'Tis on such ground, and to such wholesome end, As clears her from all blame.
BOT: My curses on her.
USER: Ask her forgiveness? Do you but mark how this becomes the house? 'Dear daughter, I confess that I am old; Age is unnecessary: on my knees I beg That you'll vouchsafe me raiment, bed, and food.'
BOT: Good sir, no more! These are unsightly tricks: Return you to my sister.
USER: Fie, sir, fie!
BOT: You nimble lightnings, dart your blinding flames Into her scornful eyes! Infect her beauty, You fen-suck'd fogs, drawn by the powerful sun, To fall and blast her pride!
USER: O the blest gods! So will you wish on me when the rash mood is on.
BOT: Good sir, to the purpose.
USER: Who put my man i' the stocks?
BOT: What trumpet's that?
USER: I know't, my sister's: this approves her letter, That she would soon be here. Is your lady come?
BOT: This is a slave, whose easy borrowed pride Dwells in the fickle grace of her he follows. Out, varlet, from my sight!
USER: What means your grace?
BOT: Why not by the hand, sir? How have I offended? All's not offence that indiscretion finds And dotage terms so.
USER: O sides, you are too tough! Will you yet hold? How came my man i' the stocks?
BOT: I set him there, sir: but his own disorders Deserv'd much less advancement.
USER: You? Did you?
BOT: At your choice, sir.
USER: Is this well spoken?
BOT: Why might not you, my lord, receive attendance From those that she calls servants, or from mine?
USER: Why not, my lord? If then they chanc'd to slack ye, We could control them. If you will come to me, - For now I spy a danger, - I entreat you To bring but five-and-twenty: to no more Will I give place or notice.
BOT: I gave you all, -
USER: And in good time you gave it.
BOT: Made you my guardians, my depositaries; But kept a reservation to be followed With such a number. What, must I come to you With five-and-twenty, Regan, said you so?
USER: And speak't again my lord; no more with me.
BOT: Those wicked creatures yet do look well-favour'd When others are more wicked; not being the worst Stands in some rank of praise. Thy fifty yet doth double five-and-twenty, And thou art twice her love.
USER: Hear me, my lord: What need you five-and-twenty? Ten? Or five? To follow in a house where twice so many Have a command to tend you?
BOT: What need one?
USER: Let us withdraw; 'twill be a storm.
BOT: This house is little: the old man and his people Cannot be well bestow'd.
USER: 'Tis his own blame; hath put himself from rest And must needs taste his folly.
BOT: For his particular, I'll receive him gladly, But not one follower.
USER: So am I purpos'd. Where is my lord of Gloucester?
BOT: Followed the old man forth, he is return'd.
USER: The King is in high rage.
BOT: Whither is he going?
USER: He calls to horse; but will I know not whither.
BOT: 'Tis best to give him way; he leads himself.
USER: My lord, entreat him by no means to stay.
BOT: Alack, the night comes on, and the high winds Do sorely ruffle; for many miles about There's scarce a bush.
USER: Shut up your doors, my lord; 'tis a wild night. My Regan counsels well: come out o' the storm.

USER: Who's there, besides foul weather?
BOT: One minded like the weather, most unquietly.
USER: I know you. Where's the King?
BOT: But who is with him?
USER: None but the fool, who labours to out-jest His heart-struck injuries.
BOT: I will talk further with you.
USER: Give me your hand: have you no more to say?
BOT: Few words, but, to effect, more than all yet: That, when we have found the King, in which your pain That way, I'll this; he that first lights on him Holla the other.

USER: O nuncle, court holy-water in a dry house is better than this rain-water out o' door. Good nuncle, in; and ask thy daughters blessing: here's a night pities neither wise men nor fools.
BOT: No, I will be the pattern of all patience; I will say nothing.
USER: Who's there?
BOT: Marry, here's grace and a codpiece; that's a wise man and a fool.
USER: He that has and a little tiny wit, With heigh-ho, the wind and the rain, Must make content with his fortunes fit, Though the rain it raineth every day.
BOT: True, boy. Come, bring us to this hovel.

USER: Most savage and unnatural!
BOT: This courtesy, forbid thee, shall the Duke Instantly know; and of that letter too. This seems a fair deserving, and must draw me That which my father loses, no less than all: The younger rises when the old doth fall.

USER: Here is the place, my lord; good my lord, enter: The tyranny of the open night's too rough For nature to endure.
BOT: Let me alone.
USER: Good my lord, enter here.
BOT: Wilt break my heart?
USER: I had rather break mine own. Good my lord, enter.
BOT: Good my lord, enter here.
USER: Come not in here, nuncle, here's a spirit. Help me, help me!
BOT: Give me thy hand. Who's there?
USER: A spirit, a spirit: he says his name's poor Tom.
BOT: What art thou that dost grumble there i' the straw? Come forth. madman.
USER: Away! the foul fiend follows me! Through the sharp hawthorn blows the cold wind. Humh! go to thy cold bed, and warm thee.
BOT: Didst thou give all to thy two daughters? And art thou come to this?
USER: What, have his daughters brought him to this pass? Couldst thou save nothing? Didst thou give 'em all?
BOT: Nay, he reserv'd a blanket, else we had been all shamed.
USER: Now all the plagues that in the pendulous air Hang fated o'er men's faults light on thy daughters!
BOT: He hath no daughters, sir.
USER: Pillicock sat on Pillicock hill, Alow, alow, loo loo!
BOT: This cold night will turn us all to fools and madmen.
USER: Take heed o' th' foul fiend: obey thy parents; keep thy word justly; swear not; commit not with man's sworn spouse; set not thy sweet-heart on proud array. Tom's a-cold.
BOT: What hast thou been?
USER: Prithee, nuncle, be contented; 'tis a naughty night to swim in. Now a little fire in a wild field were like an old lecher's heart, a small spark, all the rest on's body cold. Look, here comes a walking fire.
BOT: How fares your grace? torch.
USER: What's he?
BOT: Who's there? What is't you seek?
USER: What are you there? Your names?
BOT: What, hath your grace no better company?
USER: The prince of darkness is a gentleman: Modo he's call'd, and Mahu.
BOT: Our flesh and blood, my lord, is grown so vile That it doth hate what gets it.
USER: Poor Tom's a-cold.
BOT: First let me talk with this philosopher. What is the cause of thunder?
USER: Good my lord, take his offer; go into the house.
BOT: I'll talk a word with this same learned Theban. What is your study?
USER: How to prevent the fiend and to kill vermin.
BOT: Let me ask you one word in private.
USER: Importune him once more to go, my lord; His wits begin t'unsettle.
BOT: O, cry you mercy, sir. Noble philosopher, your company.
USER: Tom's a-cold.
BOT: In, fellow, there, into the hovel; keep thee warm.
USER: Come, let's in all.
BOT: This way, my lord.
USER: With him; I will keep still with my philosopher.
BOT: Good my lord, soothe him; let him take the fellow.
USER: Take him you on.
BOT: Sirrah, come on; go along with us.
USER: Come, good Athenian.
BOT: No words, no words, hush.
USER: Child Rowland to the dark tower came, His word was still - Fie, foh, and fum, I smell the blood of a British man.

USER: I will have my revenge ere I depart his house.
BOT: How, my lord, I may be censured, that nature thus gives way to loyalty, something fears me to think of.
USER: I now perceive it was not altogether your brother's evil disposition made him seek his death; but a provoking merit, set a-work by a reproveable badness in himself.
BOT: Go with me to the Duchess.
USER: If the matter of this paper be certain, you have mighty business in hand.
BOT: True or false, it hath made thee Earl of Gloucester. Seek out where thy father is, that he may be ready for our apprehension.
USER: suspicion more fully. I will persever in my course of loyalty, though the conflict be sore between that and my blood.
BOT: I will lay trust upon thee; and thou shalt find a dearer father in my love.

USER: Here is better than the open air; take it thankfully. I will piece out the comfort with what addition I can: I will not be long from you.
BOT: All the power of his wits have given way to his impatience: - the gods reward your kindness!
USER: Frateretto calls me; and tells me Nero is an angler in the lake of darkness. Pray, innocent, and beware the foul fiend.
BOT: Prithee, nuncle, tell me whether a madman be a gentleman or a yeoman.
USER: A king, a king!
BOT: No, he's a yeoman that has a gentleman to his son; for he's a mad yeoman that sees his son a gentleman before him.
USER: To have a thousand with red burning spits Come hissing in upon 'em.
BOT: The foul fiend bites my back.
USER: He's mad that trusts in the tameness of a wolf, a horse's health, a boy's love, or a whore's oath.
BOT: It shall be done; I will arraign them straight.
USER: Look, where he stands and glares! Want'st thou eyes at trial, madam? Come o'er the bourn, Bessy, to me.
BOT: Her boat hath a leak, And she must not speak Why she dares not come over to thee.
USER: The foul fiend haunts poor Tom in the voice of a nightingale. Hoppedance cries in Tom's belly for two white herring. Croak not, black angel; I have no food for thee.
BOT: How do you, sir? Stand you not so amaz'd; Will you lie down and rest upon the cushions?
USER: I'll see their trial first. Bring in their evidence. Bench by his side. [_To Kent._] You are o' the commission, Sit you too.
BOT: Let us deal justly. Sleepest or wakest thou, jolly shepherd? Thy sheep be in the corn; And for one blast of thy minikin mouth Thy sheep shall take no harm. Purr! the cat is grey.
USER: Arraign her first; 'tis Goneril. I here take my oath before this honourable assembly, she kicked the poor King her father.
BOT: Come hither, mistress. Is your name Goneril?
USER: She cannot deny it.
BOT: Cry you mercy, I took you for a joint-stool.
USER: And here's another, whose warp'd looks proclaim What store her heart is made on. Stop her there! Arms, arms! sword! fire! Corruption in the place! False justicer, why hast thou let her 'scape?
BOT: Bless thy five wits!
USER: O pity! Sir, where is the patience now That you so oft have boasted to retain?
BOT: They mar my counterfeiting.
USER: The little dogs and all, Trey, Blanch, and Sweetheart, see, they bark at me.
BOT: Now, good my lord, lie here and rest awhile.
USER: Make no noise, make no noise; draw the curtains. So, so. We'll go to supper i' the morning.
BOT: And I'll go to bed at noon.
USER: Come hither, friend; Where is the King my master?
BOT: Here, sir; but trouble him not, his wits are gone.
USER: Oppressed nature sleeps. This rest might yet have balm'd thy broken sinews, Which, if convenience will not allow, Stand in hard cure. Come, help to bear thy master;
BOT: Come, come, away!

USER: Post speedily to my lord your husband, show him this letter: the army of France is landed. Seek out the traitor Gloucester.
BOT: Hang him instantly.
USER: Pluck out his eyes.
BOT: Get horses for your mistress.
USER: Farewell, sweet lord, and sister.
BOT: Ingrateful fox! 'tis he.
USER: Bind fast his corky arms.
BOT: What mean your graces? Good my friends, consider you are my guests. Do me no foul play, friends.
USER: Bind him, I say.
BOT: Hard, hard. O filthy traitor!
USER: Unmerciful lady as you are, I'm none.
BOT: To this chair bind him. Villain, thou shalt find -
USER: By the kind gods, 'tis most ignobly done To pluck me by the beard.
BOT: So white, and such a traitor!
USER: Naughty lady, These hairs which thou dost ravish from my chin Will quicken, and accuse thee. I am your host: With robber's hands my hospitable favours You should not ruffle thus. What will you do?
BOT: Come, sir, what letters had you late from France?
USER: Be simple answer'd, for we know the truth.
BOT: And what confederacy have you with the traitors, Late footed in the kingdom?
USER: To whose hands have you sent the lunatic King? Speak.
BOT: I have a letter guessingly set down, Which came from one that's of a neutral heart, And not from one oppos'd.
USER: Cunning.
BOT: And false.
USER: Where hast thou sent the King?
BOT: To Dover.
USER: Wherefore to Dover? Wast thou not charg'd at peril, -
BOT: Wherefore to Dover? Let him first answer that.
USER: I am tied to the stake, and I must stand the course.
BOT: Wherefore to Dover, sir?
USER: See't shalt thou never. Fellows, hold the chair. Upon these eyes of thine I'll set my foot. of his eyes and sets his foot on it._]
BOT: He that will think to live till he be old, Give me some help! - O cruel! O you gods!
USER: One side will mock another; the other too!
BOT: If you see vengeance -
USER: Hold your hand, my lord: I have serv'd you ever since I was a child; But better service have I never done you Than now to bid you hold.
BOT: How now, you dog!
USER: If you did wear a beard upon your chin, I'd shake it on this quarrel. What do you mean?
BOT: My villain?
USER: Nay, then, come on, and take the chance of anger.
BOT: O, I am slain! My lord, you have one eye left To see some mischief on him. O!
USER: Lest it see more, prevent it. Out, vile jelly! Where is thy lustre now?
BOT: All dark and comfortless. Where's my son Edmund? Edmund, enkindle all the sparks of nature To quit this horrid act.
USER: Out, treacherous villain! Thou call'st on him that hates thee: it was he That made the overture of thy treasons to us; Who is too good to pity thee.
BOT: O my follies! Then Edgar was abus'd. Kind gods, forgive me that, and prosper him!
USER: Go thrust him out at gates, and let him smell His way to Dover. How is't, my lord? How look you?
BOT: I have receiv'd a hurt: follow me, lady. Turn out that eyeless villain. Throw this slave Upon the dunghill. Regan, I bleed apace: Untimely comes this hurt: give me your arm. him out._]
USER: I'll never care what wickedness I do, If this man come to good.
BOT: If she live long, And in the end meet the old course of death, Women will all turn monsters.
USER: Let's follow the old Earl, and get the bedlam To lead him where he would: his roguish madness Allows itself to anything.
BOT: Go thou: I'll fetch some flax and whites of eggs To apply to his bleeding face. Now heaven help him!

USER: O my good lord, I have been your tenant, and your father's tenant these fourscore years.
BOT: Away, get thee away; good friend, be gone. Thy comforts can do me no good at all; Thee they may hurt.
USER: You cannot see your way.
BOT: How now! Who's there?
USER: worst'? I am worse than e'er I was.
BOT: 'Tis poor mad Tom.
USER: So long as we can say 'This is the worst.'
BOT: Fellow, where goest?
USER: Is it a beggar-man?
BOT: Madman, and beggar too.
USER: Bad is the trade that must play fool to sorrow, Angering itself and others. Bless thee, master!
BOT: Is that the naked fellow?
USER: Ay, my lord.
BOT: Then prithee get thee away. If for my sake Thou wilt o'ertake us hence a mile or twain, I' the way toward Dover, do it for ancient love, And bring some covering for this naked soul, Which I'll entreat to lead me.
USER: Alack, sir, he is mad.
BOT: 'Tis the time's plague when madmen lead the blind. Do as I bid thee, or rather do thy pleasure; Above the rest, be gone.
USER: I'll bring him the best 'parel that I have, Come on't what will.
BOT: Sirrah naked fellow.
USER: Poor Tom's a-cold.
BOT: Come hither, fellow.
USER: Know'st thou the way to Dover?
BOT: Ay, master.
USER: Give me thy arm: Poor Tom shall lead thee.

USER: Welcome, my lord. I marvel our mild husband Not met us on the way. Now, where's your master?
BOT: Yours in the ranks of death.
USER: My most dear Gloucester. O, the difference of man and man! To thee a woman's services are due; My fool usurps my body.
BOT: Madam, here comes my lord.
USER: I have been worth the whistle.
BOT: No more; the text is foolish.
USER: See thyself, devil! Proper deformity seems not in the fiend So horrid as in woman.
BOT: O vain fool!
USER: Marry, your manhood, mew!
BOT: What news?
USER: O, my good lord, the Duke of Cornwall's dead; Slain by his servant, going to put out The other eye of Gloucester.
BOT: Gloucester's eyes!
USER: This shows you are above, You justicers, that these our nether crimes So speedily can venge! But, O poor Gloucester! Lost he his other eye?
BOT: Both, both, my lord. This letter, madam, craves a speedy answer; 'Tis from your sister.
USER: But being widow, and my Gloucester with her, May all the building in my fancy pluck Upon my hateful life. Another way The news is not so tart. I'll read, and answer.
BOT: Where was his son when they did take his eyes?
USER: Come with my lady hither.
BOT: He is not here.
USER: No, my good lord; I met him back again.
BOT: Knows he the wickedness?
USER: Ay, my good lord. 'Twas he inform'd against him; And quit the house on purpose, that their punishment Might have the freer course.
BOT: Gloucester, I live To thank thee for the love thou show'dst the King, And to revenge thine eyes. Come hither, friend, Tell me what more thou know'st.

USER: Why the King of France is so suddenly gone back, know you no reason?
BOT: Something he left imperfect in the state, which since his coming forth is thought of, which imports to the kingdom so much fear and danger that his personal return was most required and necessary.
USER: Who hath he left behind him general?
BOT: The Mareschal of France, Monsieur La Far.
USER: Did your letters pierce the queen to any demonstration of grief?
BOT: Ay, sir; she took them, read them in my presence; And now and then an ample tear trill'd down Her delicate cheek. It seem'd she was a queen Over her passion; who, most rebel-like, Sought to be king o'er her.
USER: O, then it mov'd her.
BOT: Made she no verbal question?
USER: It is the stars, The stars above us govern our conditions; Else one self mate and make could not beget Such different issues. You spoke not with her since?
BOT: No.
USER: Was this before the King return'd?
BOT: No, since.
USER: Well, sir, the poor distressed Lear's i' the town; Who sometime, in his better tune, remembers What we are come about, and by no means Will yield to see his daughter.
BOT: Why, good sir?
USER: Alack, poor gentleman!
BOT: Of Albany's and Cornwall's powers you heard not?
USER: 'Tis so; they are afoot.

USER: There is means, madam: Our foster nurse of nature is repose, The which he lacks; that to provoke in him Are many simples operative, whose power Will close the eye of anguish.
BOT: News, madam; The British powers are marching hitherward.

USER: But are my brother's powers set forth?
BOT: Ay, madam.
USER: Himself in person there?
BOT: Madam, with much ado. Your sister is the better soldier.
USER: Lord Edmund spake not with your lord at home?
BOT: No, madam.
USER: What might import my sister's letter to him?
BOT: I know not, lady.
USER: I must needs after him, madam, with my letter.
BOT: Our troops set forth tomorrow; stay with us; The ways are dangerous.
USER: I may not, madam: My lady charg'd my duty in this business.
BOT: Why should she write to Edmund? Might not you Transport her purposes by word? Belike, Somethings, I know not what, I'll love thee much. Let me unseal the letter.
USER: Madam, I had rather -
BOT: I know your lady does not love her husband; I am sure of that; and at her late being here She gave strange oeillades and most speaking looks To noble Edmund. I know you are of her bosom.
USER: I, madam?
BOT: Would I could meet him, madam! I should show What party I do follow.
USER: Fare thee well.

USER: When shall I come to the top of that same hill?
BOT: You do climb up it now. Look how we labour.
USER: Methinks the ground is even.
BOT: Horrible steep. Hark, do you hear the sea?
USER: No, truly.
BOT: Why, then, your other senses grow imperfect By your eyes' anguish.
USER: So may it be indeed. Methinks thy voice is alter'd; and thou speak'st In better phrase and matter than thou didst.
BOT: Y'are much deceiv'd: in nothing am I chang'd But in my garments.
USER: Methinks you're better spoken.
BOT: Set me where you stand.
USER: Give me your hand. You are now within a foot of th'extreme verge. For all beneath the moon would I not leap upright.
BOT: Let go my hand. Here, friend, 's another purse; in it a jewel Well worth a poor man's taking. Fairies and gods Prosper it with thee! Go thou further off; Bid me farewell, and let me hear thee going.
USER: Now fare ye well, good sir.
BOT: With all my heart.
USER: Is done to cure it.
BOT: Away, and let me die.
USER: But have I fall'n, or no?
BOT: From the dread summit of this chalky bourn. Look up a-height, the shrill-gorg'd lark so far Cannot be seen or heard. Do but look up.
USER: Alack, I have no eyes. Is wretchedness depriv'd that benefit To end itself by death? 'Twas yet some comfort When misery could beguile the tyrant's rage And frustrate his proud will.
BOT: Give me your arm. Up, so. How is't? Feel you your legs? You stand.
USER: Too well, too well.
BOT: This is above all strangeness. Upon the crown o' the cliff what thing was that Which parted from you?
USER: A poor unfortunate beggar.
BOT: I do remember now: henceforth I'll bear Affliction till it do cry out itself 'Enough, enough,' and die. That thing you speak of, I took it for a man; often 'twould say, 'The fiend, the fiend'; he led me to that place.
USER: Bear free and patient thoughts. But who comes here? dressed up with flowers. The safer sense will ne'er accommodate His master thus.
BOT: No, they cannot touch me for coining. I am the King himself.
USER: O thou side-piercing sight!
BOT: Sweet marjoram.
USER: Pass.
BOT: I know that voice.
USER: The trick of that voice I do well remember: Is't not the King?
BOT: O, let me kiss that hand!
USER: Let me wipe it first; it smells of mortality.
BOT: O ruin'd piece of nature, this great world Shall so wear out to naught. Dost thou know me?
USER: I remember thine eyes well enough. Dost thou squiny at me? No, do thy worst, blind Cupid; I'll not love. Read thou this challenge; mark but the penning of it.
BOT: Were all the letters suns, I could not see one.
USER: I would not take this from report, It is, and my heart breaks at it.
BOT: Read.
USER: What, with the case of eyes?
BOT: O, ho, are you there with me? No eyes in your head, nor no money in your purse? Your eyes are in a heavy case, your purse in a light, yet you see how this world goes.
USER: I see it feelingly.
BOT: Ay, sir.
USER: O, matter and impertinency mix'd! Reason in madness!
BOT: Alack, alack the day!
USER: O, here he is: lay hand upon him. Sir, Your most dear daughter -
BOT: No rescue? What, a prisoner? I am even The natural fool of fortune. Use me well; You shall have ransom. Let me have surgeons; I am cut to the brains.
USER: You shall have anything.
BOT: No seconds? All myself? Why, this would make a man a man of salt, To use his eyes for garden water-pots, Ay, and for laying autumn's dust.
USER: Good sir.
BOT: I will die bravely, like a smug bridegroom. What! I will be jovial. Come, come, I am a king, my masters, know you that.
USER: You are a royal one, and we obey you.
BOT: Then there's life in't. Come, and you get it, You shall get it by running. Sa, sa, sa, sa!
USER: A sight most pitiful in the meanest wretch, Past speaking of in a king! Thou hast one daughter Who redeems nature from the general curse Which twain have brought her to.
BOT: Hail, gentle sir.
USER: Sir, speed you. What's your will?
BOT: Do you hear aught, sir, of a battle toward?
USER: Most sure and vulgar. Everyone hears that, which can distinguish sound.
BOT: But, by your favour, How near's the other army?
USER: Near and on speedy foot; the main descry Stands on the hourly thought.
BOT: I thank you sir, that's all.
USER: Though that the queen on special cause is here, Her army is mov'd on.
BOT: I thank you, sir.
USER: You ever-gentle gods, take my breath from me; Let not my worser spirit tempt me again To die before you please.
BOT: Well pray you, father.
USER: Now, good sir, what are you?
BOT: A most poor man, made tame to fortune's blows; Who, by the art of known and feeling sorrows, Am pregnant to good pity. Give me your hand, I'll lead you to some biding.
USER: Hearty thanks: The bounty and the benison of heaven To boot, and boot.
BOT: A proclaim'd prize! Most happy! That eyeless head of thine was first fram'd flesh To raise my fortunes. Thou old unhappy traitor, Briefly thyself remember. The sword is out That must destroy thee.
USER: Now let thy friendly hand Put strength enough to't.
BOT: Wherefore, bold peasant, Dar'st thou support a publish'd traitor? Hence; Lest that th'infection of his fortune take Like hold on thee. Let go his arm.
USER: Chill not let go, zir, without vurther 'casion.
BOT: Let go, slave, or thou diest!
USER: Out, dunghill!
BOT: Chill pick your teeth, zir. Come! No matter vor your foins.
USER: I know thee well. A serviceable villain, As duteous to the vices of thy mistress As badness would desire.
BOT: What, is he dead?
USER: Give me your hand. Far off methinks I hear the beaten drum. Come, father, I'll bestow you with a friend.

USER: O thou good Kent, how shall I live and work To match thy goodness? My life will be too short, And every measure fail me.
BOT: To be acknowledg'd, madam, is o'erpaid. All my reports go with the modest truth; Nor more, nor clipp'd, but so.
USER: Be better suited, These weeds are memories of those worser hours: I prithee put them off.
BOT: Pardon, dear madam; Yet to be known shortens my made intent. My boon I make it that you know me not Till time and I think meet.
USER: Then be't so, my good lord. [_To the Physician._] How does the King?
BOT: Madam, sleeps still.
USER: O you kind gods, Cure this great breach in his abused nature! The untun'd and jarring senses, O, wind up Of this child-changed father.
BOT: So please your majesty That we may wake the King: he hath slept long.
USER: Be govern'd by your knowledge, and proceed I' the sway of your own will. Is he array'd?
BOT: Ay, madam. In the heaviness of sleep We put fresh garments on him. Be by, good madam, when we do awake him; I doubt not of his temperance.
USER: Very well.
BOT: Please you draw near. Louder the music there!
USER: O my dear father! Restoration hang Thy medicine on my lips; and let this kiss Repair those violent harms that my two sisters Have in thy reverence made!
BOT: Kind and dear princess!
USER: Madam, do you; 'tis fittest.
BOT: How does my royal lord? How fares your majesty?
USER: You do me wrong to take me out o' the grave. Thou art a soul in bliss; but I am bound Upon a wheel of fire, that mine own tears Do scald like molten lead.
BOT: Sir, do you know me?
USER: You are a spirit, I know: when did you die?
BOT: Still, still, far wide!
USER: He's scarce awake: let him alone awhile.
BOT: O, look upon me, sir, And hold your hands in benediction o'er me. No, sir, you must not kneel.
USER: And so I am. I am.
BOT: Be your tears wet? Yes, faith. I pray weep not: If you have poison for me, I will drink it. I know you do not love me; for your sisters Have, as I do remember, done me wrong. You have some cause, they have not.
USER: No cause, no cause.
BOT: Am I in France?
USER: In your own kingdom, sir.
BOT: Do not abuse me.
USER: Be comforted, good madam, the great rage, You see, is kill'd in him: and yet it is danger To make him even o'er the time he has lost. Desire him to go in; trouble him no more Till further settling.
BOT: Will't please your highness walk?
USER: You must bear with me: Pray you now, forget and forgive: I am old and foolish.
BOT: Holds it true, sir, that the Duke of Cornwall was so slain?
USER: Most certain, sir.
BOT: Who is conductor of his people?
USER: As 'tis said, the bastard son of Gloucester.
BOT: They say Edgar, his banished son, is with the Earl of Kent in Germany.
USER: Report is changeable. 'Tis time to look about; the powers of the kingdom approach apace.
BOT: The arbitrement is like to be bloody. Fare you well, sir.
USER: My point and period will be throughly wrought, Or well or ill, as this day's battle's fought.

USER: Know of the Duke if his last purpose hold, Or whether since he is advis'd by aught To change the course, he's full of alteration And self-reproving, bring his constant pleasure.
BOT: Our sister's man is certainly miscarried.
USER: 'Tis to be doubted, madam.
BOT: Now, sweet lord, You know the goodness I intend upon you: Tell me but truly, but then speak the truth, Do you not love my sister?
USER: In honour'd love.
BOT: But have you never found my brother's way To the forfended place?
USER: That thought abuses you.
BOT: I am doubtful that you have been conjunct And bosom'd with her, as far as we call hers.
USER: No, by mine honour, madam.
BOT: I never shall endure her, dear my lord, Be not familiar with her.
USER: Fear not, She and the Duke her husband!
BOT: Should loosen him and me.
USER: Sir, you speak nobly.
BOT: Why is this reason'd?
USER: Combine together 'gainst the enemy; For these domestic and particular broils Are not the question here.
BOT: Let's, then, determine with the ancient of war On our proceeding.
USER: I shall attend you presently at your tent.
BOT: Sister, you'll go with us?
USER: No.
BOT: 'Tis most convenient; pray you, go with us.
USER: As they are going out, enter Edgar disguised.
BOT: If e'er your grace had speech with man so poor, Hear me one word.
USER: I'll overtake you. Speak.
BOT: Stay till I have read the letter.
USER: I was forbid it. When time shall serve, let but the herald cry, And I'll appear again.
BOT: Why, fare thee well. I will o'erlook thy paper.
USER: The enemy's in view; draw up your powers. Here is the guess of their true strength and forces By diligent discovery; but your haste Is now urg'd on you.
BOT: We will greet the time.

USER: Here, father, take the shadow of this tree For your good host; pray that the right may thrive: If ever I return to you again, I'll bring you comfort.
BOT: Grace go with you, sir! Alarum and retreat within. Enter Edgar.
USER: Away, old man, give me thy hand, away! King Lear hath lost, he and his daughter ta'en: Give me thy hand; come on!
BOT: No further, sir; a man may rot even here.
USER: What, in ill thoughts again? Men must endure Their going hence, even as their coming hither; Ripeness is all. Come on.
BOT: And that's true too.

USER: Some officers take them away: good guard Until their greater pleasures first be known That are to censure them.
