BOT: We are not the first Who with best meaning have incurr'd the worst. For thee, oppressed King, I am cast down; Myself could else out-frown false fortune's frown. Shall we not see these daughters and these sisters?
USER: Take them away.
BOT: I'll do't, my lord.
USER: About it; and write happy when thou hast done. Mark, I say, instantly; and carry it so As I have set it down.
BOT: I cannot draw a cart, nor eat dried oats; If it be man's work, I'll do't. Flourish. Enter Albany, Goneril, Regan, Officers and Attendants.
USER: Sir, by your patience, I hold you but a subject of this war, Not as a brother.
BOT: Not so hot: In his own grace he doth exalt himself, More than in your addition.
USER: In my rights, By me invested, he compeers the best.
BOT: That were the most, if he should husband you.
USER: Jesters do oft prove prophets.
BOT: Holla, holla! That eye that told you so look'd but asquint.
USER: Mean you to enjoy him?
BOT: The let-alone lies not in your good will.
USER: Nor in thine, lord.
BOT: Half-blooded fellow, yes.
USER: An interlude!
BOT: Sick, O, sick!
USER: A herald, ho! Trust to thy single virtue; for thy soldiers, All levied in my name, have in my name Took their discharge.
BOT: My sickness grows upon me.
USER: She is not well. Convey her to my tent. Come hither, herald. Let the trumpet sound And read out this.
BOT: Sound, trumpet!
USER: the army will maintain upon Edmund, supposed Earl of Gloucester, that he is a manifold traitor, let him appear by the third sound of the trumpet. He is bold in his defence.'
BOT: Sound!
USER: Again!
BOT: Again! Third trumpet. Trumpet answers within. Enter Edgar, armed, preceded by a trumpet.
USER: Ask him his purposes, why he appears Upon this call o' the trumpet.
BOT: What are you? Your name, your quality? and why you answer This present summons?
USER: Know my name is lost; By treason's tooth bare-gnawn and canker-bit. Yet am I noble as the adversary I come to cope.
BOT: Which is that adversary?
USER: What's he that speaks for Edmund, Earl of Gloucester?
BOT: Himself, what say'st thou to him?
USER: Save him, save him!
BOT: This is mere practice, Gloucester: By the law of arms thou wast not bound to answer An unknown opposite; thou art not vanquish'd, But cozen'd and beguil'd.
USER: Shut your mouth, dame, Or with this paper shall I stop it. Hold, sir; Thou worse than any name, read thine own evil. No tearing, lady; I perceive you know it.
BOT: Say if I do, the laws are mine, not thine: Who can arraign me for't?
USER: Most monstrous! O! Know'st thou this paper?
BOT: Ask me not what I know.
USER: govern her.
BOT: What you have charg'd me with, that have I done; And more, much more; the time will bring it out. 'Tis past, and so am I. But what art thou That hast this fortune on me? If thou'rt noble, I do forgive thee.
USER: Thou hast spoken right, 'tis true; The wheel is come full circle; I am here.
BOT: Methought thy very gait did prophesy A royal nobleness. I must embrace thee. Let sorrow split my heart if ever I Did hate thee or thy father.
USER: Worthy prince, I know't.
BOT: Where have you hid yourself? How have you known the miseries of your father?
USER: This speech of yours hath mov'd me, And shall perchance do good, but speak you on; You look as you had something more to say.
BOT: If there be more, more woeful, hold it in; For I am almost ready to dissolve, Hearing of this.
USER: But who was this?
BOT: Kent, sir, the banish'd Kent; who in disguise Follow'd his enemy king and did him service Improper for a slave. with a bloody knife.
USER: Help, help! O, help!
BOT: What kind of help?
USER: Speak, man.
BOT: What means this bloody knife?
USER: 'Tis hot, it smokes; It came even from the heart of - O! she's dead!
BOT: Who dead? Speak, man.
USER: Your lady, sir, your lady; and her sister By her is poisoned; she hath confesses it.
BOT: I was contracted to them both, all three Now marry in an instant.
USER: Here comes Kent.
BOT: Produce their bodies, be they alive or dead. This judgement of the heavens that makes us tremble Touches us not with pity. O, is this he? The time will not allow the compliment Which very manners urges.
USER: I am come To bid my King and master aye good night: Is he not here?
BOT: Great thing of us forgot! Speak, Edmund, where's the King? and where's Cordelia? The bodies of Goneril and Regan are brought in. Seest thou this object, Kent?
USER: Alack, why thus?
BOT: Yet Edmund was belov'd. The one the other poisoned for my sake, And after slew herself.
USER: Even so. Cover their faces.
BOT: I pant for life. Some good I mean to do, Despite of mine own nature. Quickly send, Be brief in it, to the castle; for my writ Is on the life of Lear and on Cordelia; Nay, send in time.
USER: Run, run, O, run!
BOT: To who, my lord? Who has the office? Send Thy token of reprieve.
USER: Well thought on: take my sword, Give it the captain.
BOT: Haste thee for thy life.
USER: He hath commission from thy wife and me To hang Cordelia in the prison, and To lay the blame upon her own despair, That she fordid herself.
BOT: The gods defend her! Bear him hence awhile. Officer and others following.
USER: Is this the promis'd end?
BOT: Or image of that horror?
USER: Fall, and cease!
BOT: This feather stirs; she lives! If it be so, It is a chance which does redeem all sorrows That ever I have felt.
USER: O, my good master! [_Kneeling._]
BOT: Prithee, away!
USER: 'Tis noble Kent, your friend.
BOT: 'Tis true, my lords, he did.
USER: Did I not, fellow? I have seen the day, with my good biting falchion I would have made them skip. I am old now, And these same crosses spoil me. Who are you? Mine eyes are not o' the best, I'll tell you straight.
BOT: If Fortune brag of two she lov'd and hated, One of them we behold.
USER: This is a dull sight. Are you not Kent?
BOT: The same, Your servant Kent. Where is your servant Caius?
USER: He's a good fellow, I can tell you that; He'll strike, and quickly too:. He's dead and rotten.
BOT: No, my good lord; I am the very man.
USER: I'll see that straight.
BOT: That from your first of difference and decay Have follow'd your sad steps.
USER: You are welcome hither.
BOT: Nor no man else. All's cheerless, dark and deadly. Your eldest daughters have fordone themselves, And desperately are dead.
USER: Ay, so I think.
BOT: He knows not what he says; and vain is it That we present us to him.
USER: Very bootless.
BOT: Edmund is dead, my lord.
USER: He faints! My lord, my lord!
BOT: Break, heart; I prithee break!
USER: Look up, my lord.
BOT: Vex not his ghost: O, let him pass! He hates him That would upon the rack of this rough world Stretch him out longer.
USER: He is gone indeed.
BOT: The wonder is, he hath endur'd so long: He but usurp'd his life.
USER: Bear them from hence. Our present business Is general woe. [_To Edgar and Kent._] Friends of my soul, you twain, Rule in this realm and the gor'd state sustain.
BOT: I have a journey, sir, shortly to go; My master calls me, I must not say no.
USER: The weight of this sad time we must obey; Speak what we feel, not what we ought to say. The oldest hath borne most; we that are young Shall never see so much, nor live so long. LOVE'S LABOUR'S LOST Contents

USER: I am resolved. 'Tis but a three years' fast. The mind shall banquet, though the body pine. Fat paunches have lean pates, and dainty bits Make rich the ribs, but bankrupt quite the wits.
BOT: My loving lord, Dumaine is mortified. The grosser manner of these world's delights He throws upon the gross world's baser slaves. To love, to wealth, to pomp, I pine and die, With all these living in philosophy.
USER: Your oath is passed to pass away from these.
BOT: Let me say no, my liege, an if you please. I only swore to study with your Grace And stay here in your court for three years' space.
USER: You swore to that, Berowne, and to the rest.
BOT: By yea and nay, sir, then I swore in jest. What is the end of study, let me know?
USER: Why, that to know which else we should not know.
BOT: Things hid and barred, you mean, from common sense?
USER: Ay, that is study's god-like recompense.
BOT: These be the stops that hinder study quite, And train our intellects to vain delight.
USER: How well he's read, to reason against reading.
BOT: Proceeded well, to stop all good proceeding.
USER: He weeds the corn, and still lets grow the weeding.
BOT: The spring is near when green geese are a-breeding.
USER: How follows that?
BOT: Fit in his place and time.
USER: In reason nothing.
BOT: Something then in rhyme.
USER: Berowne is like an envious sneaping frost That bites the first-born infants of the spring.
BOT: Well, sit you out. Go home, Berowne. Adieu.
USER: How well this yielding rescues thee from shame.
BOT: Hath this been proclaimed?
USER: Four days ago.
BOT: Let's see the penalty. [_Reads_.] _On pain of losing her tongue._ Who devised this penalty?
USER: Marry, that did I.
BOT: Sweet lord, and why?
USER: To fright them hence with that dread penalty.
BOT: What say you, lords? Why, this was quite forgot.
USER: So study evermore is overshot. While it doth study to have what it would, It doth forget to do the thing it should; And when it hath the thing it hunteth most, 'Tis won as towns with fire: so won, so lost.
BOT: We must of force dispense with this decree. She must lie here on mere necessity.
USER: Armado is a most illustrious wight, A man of fire-new words, fashion's own knight.
BOT: Costard the swain and he shall be our sport, And so to study three years is but short.
USER: Which is the Duke's own person?
BOT: This, fellow. What wouldst?
USER: I myself reprehend his own person, for I am his Grace's farborough. But I would see his own person in flesh and blood.
BOT: This is he.
USER: Signior Arm… Arm… commends you. There's villainy abroad. This letter will tell you more.
BOT: Sir, the contempts thereof are as touching me.
USER: A letter from the magnificent Armado.
BOT: How long soever the matter, I hope in God for high words.
USER: A high hope for a low heaven. God grant us patience!
BOT: To hear, or forbear laughing?
USER: To hear meekly, sir, and to laugh moderately, or to forbear both.
BOT: Well, sir, be it as the style shall give us cause to climb in the merriness.
USER: The matter is to me, sir, as concerning Jaquenetta. The manner of it is, I was taken with the manner.
BOT: In what manner?
USER: For the "following", sir?
BOT: As it shall follow in my correction, and God defend the right!
USER: Will you hear this letter with attention?
BOT: As we would hear an oracle.
USER: Such is the simplicity of man to hearken after the flesh.
BOT: Navarre, my soul's earth's god and body's fostering patron - _
USER: Not a word of Costard yet.
BOT: It may be so; but if he say it is so, he is, in telling true, but so.
USER: Peace!
BOT: Be to me, and every man that dares not fight.
USER: No words!
BOT: Of other men's secrets, I beseech you.
USER: Me?
BOT: Me?
USER: Still me?
BOT: O me!
USER: proclaimed edict and continent canon, which with, O, with - but with this I passion to say wherewith - _
BOT: With a wench.
USER: Me, an't shall please you; I am Antony Dull.
BOT: This is not so well as I looked for, but the best that ever I heard.
USER: Ay, the best for the worst. But, sirrah, what say you to this?
BOT: Sir, I confess the wench.
USER: Did you hear the proclamation?
BOT: I do confess much of the hearing it, but little of the marking of it.
USER: It was proclaimed a year's imprisonment to be taken with a wench.
BOT: I was taken with none, sir. I was taken with a damsel.
USER: Well, it was proclaimed "damsel".
BOT: This was no damsel neither, sir; she was a virgin.
USER: It is so varied too, for it was proclaimed "virgin".
BOT: If it were, I deny her virginity. I was taken with a maid.
USER: This maid will not serve your turn, sir.
BOT: This maid will serve my turn, sir.
USER: Sir, I will pronounce your sentence: you shall fast a week with bran and water.
BOT: I had rather pray a month with mutton and porridge.
USER: And Don Armado shall be your keeper. My Lord Berowne, see him delivered o'er; And go we, lords, to put in practice that Which each to other hath so strongly sworn.
BOT: I'll lay my head to any good man's hat These oaths and laws will prove an idle scorn. Sirrah, come on.

USER: Boy, what sign is it when a man of great spirit grows melancholy?
BOT: A great sign, sir, that he will look sad.
USER: Why, sadness is one and the selfsame thing, dear imp.
BOT: No, no, O Lord, sir, no.
USER: How canst thou part sadness and melancholy, my tender juvenal?
BOT: By a familiar demonstration of the working, my tough signior.
USER: Why tough signior? Why tough signior?
BOT: Why tender juvenal? Why tender juvenal?
USER: I spoke it, tender juvenal, as a congruent epitheton appertaining to thy young days, which we may nominate tender.
BOT: And I, tough signior, as an appertinent title to your old time, which we may name tough.
USER: Pretty and apt.
BOT: How mean you, sir? I pretty and my saying apt, or I apt, and my saying pretty?
USER: Thou pretty, because little.
BOT: Little pretty, because little. Wherefore apt?
USER: And therefore apt, because quick.
BOT: Speak you this in my praise, master?
USER: In thy condign praise.
BOT: I will praise an eel with the same praise.
USER: What, that an eel is ingenious?
BOT: That an eel is quick.
USER: I do say thou art quick in answers. Thou heat'st my blood.
BOT: I am answered, sir.
USER: I love not to be crossed.
BOT: I have promised to study three years with the Duke.
USER: You may do it in an hour, sir.
BOT: Impossible.
USER: How many is one thrice told?
BOT: I am ill at reckoning. It fitteth the spirit of a tapster.
USER: You are a gentleman and a gamester, sir.
BOT: I confess both. They are both the varnish of a complete man.
USER: Then I am sure you know how much the gross sum of deuce-ace amounts to.
BOT: It doth amount to one more than two.
USER: Which the base vulgar do call three.
BOT: True.
USER: Why, sir, is this such a piece of study? Now here's three studied ere ye'll thrice wink. And how easy it is to put "years" to the word "three", and study three years in two words, the dancing horse will tell you.
BOT: A most fine figure!
USER: Hercules, master.
BOT: Most sweet Hercules! More authority, dear boy, name more; and, sweet my child, let them be men of good repute and carriage.
USER: Samson, master. He was a man of good carriage, great carriage, for he carried the town gates on his back like a porter, and he was in love.
BOT: O well-knit Samson, strong-jointed Samson! I do excel thee in my rapier as much as thou didst me in carrying gates. I am in love too. Who was Samson's love, my dear Moth?
USER: A woman, master.
BOT: Of what complexion?
USER: Of all the four, or the three, or the two, or one of the four.
BOT: Tell me precisely of what complexion.
USER: Of the sea-water green, sir.
BOT: Is that one of the four complexions?
USER: As I have read, sir; and the best of them too.
BOT: Green indeed is the colour of lovers. But to have a love of that colour, methinks Samson had small reason for it. He surely affected her for her wit.
USER: It was so, sir, for she had a green wit.
BOT: My love is most immaculate white and red.
USER: Most maculate thoughts, master, are masked under such colours.
BOT: Define, define, well-educated infant.
USER: My father's wit and my mother's tongue assist me!
BOT: Sweet invocation of a child, most pretty, and pathetical!
USER: Is there not a ballad, boy, of the King and the Beggar?
BOT: The world was very guilty of such a ballad some three ages since, but I think now 'tis not to be found; or if it were, it would neither serve for the writing nor the tune.
USER: I will have that subject newly writ o'er, that I may example my digression by some mighty precedent. Boy, I do love that country girl that I took in the park with the rational hind Costard. She deserves well.
BOT: Sing, boy. My spirit grows heavy in love.
USER: And that's great marvel, loving a light wench.
BOT: I say, sing.
USER: Forbear till this company be past.
BOT: I do betray myself with blushing. - Maid.
USER: Man.
BOT: I will visit thee at the lodge.
USER: That's hereby.
BOT: I know where it is situate.
USER: Lord, how wise you are!
BOT: I will tell thee wonders.
USER: With that face?
BOT: I love thee.
USER: So I heard you say.
BOT: And so, farewell.
USER: Fair weather after you!
BOT: Come, Jaquenetta, away.
USER: Villain, thou shalt fast for thy offences ere thou be pardoned.
BOT: Well, sir, I hope when I do it I shall do it on a full stomach.
USER: Thou shalt be heavily punished.
BOT: I am more bound to you than your fellows, for they are but lightly rewarded.
USER: Take away this villain. Shut him up.
BOT: Come, you transgressing slave, away!
USER: Let me not be pent up, sir. I will fast being loose.
BOT: No, sir, that were fast and loose. Thou shalt to prison.
USER: Well, if ever I do see the merry days of desolation that I have seen, some shall see.
BOT: What shall some see?

USER: Proud of employment, willingly I go.
BOT: All pride is willing pride, and yours is so. Who are the votaries, my loving lords, That are vow-fellows with this virtuous Duke?
USER: Lord Longaville is one.
BOT: Know you the man?
USER: Some merry mocking lord, belike. Is't so?
BOT: They say so most that most his humours know.
USER: Such short-lived wits do wither as they grow. Who are the rest?
BOT: God bless my ladies! Are they all in love, That every one her own hath garnished With such bedecking ornaments of praise?
USER: Here comes Boyet.
BOT: Now, what admittance, lord?
USER: Fair Princess, welcome to the court of Navarre.
BOT: "Fair" I give you back again, and "welcome" I have not yet. The roof of this court is too high to be yours, and welcome to the wide fields too base to be mine.
USER: You shall be welcome, madam, to my court.
BOT: I will be welcome then. Conduct me thither.
USER: Hear me, dear lady. I have sworn an oath.
BOT: Our Lady help my lord! He'll be forsworn.
USER: Not for the world, fair madam, by my will.
BOT: Why, will shall break it; will, and nothing else.
USER: Your ladyship is ignorant what it is.
BOT: Madam, I will, if suddenly I may.
USER: You will the sooner that I were away, For you'll prove perjured if you make me stay.
BOT: Did not I dance with you in Brabant once?
USER: I know you did.
BOT: How needless was it then To ask the question!
USER: You must not be so quick.
BOT: 'Tis long of you that spur me with such questions.
USER: Your wit's too hot, it speeds too fast, 'twill tire.
BOT: Not till it leave the rider in the mire.
USER: What time o' day?
BOT: The hour that fools should ask.
USER: Now fair befall your mask.
BOT: Fair fall the face it covers.
USER: And send you many lovers!
BOT: Amen, so you be none.
USER: Nay, then will I be gone.
BOT: You do the King my father too much wrong, And wrong the reputation of your name, In so unseeming to confess receipt Of that which hath so faithfully been paid.
USER: I do protest I never heard of it; And, if you prove it, I'll repay it back Or yield up Aquitaine.
BOT: We arrest your word. Boyet, you can produce acquittances For such a sum from special officers Of Charles his father.
USER: Satisfy me so.
BOT: So please your Grace, the packet is not come Where that and other specialties are bound. Tomorrow you shall have a sight of them.
USER: Sweet health and fair desires consort your Grace.
BOT: Thy own wish wish I thee in every place.
USER: Lady, I will commend you to mine own heart.
BOT: Pray you, do my commendations; I would be glad to see it.
USER: I would you heard it groan.
BOT: Is the fool sick?
USER: Sick at the heart.
BOT: Alack, let it blood.
USER: Would that do it good?
BOT: My physic says "ay".
USER: Will you prick't with your eye?
BOT: _Non point_, with my knife.
USER: Now, God save thy life.
BOT: And yours from long living.
USER: I cannot stay thanksgiving.
BOT: Sir, I pray you, a word. What lady is that same?
USER: The heir of Alençon, Katharine her name.
BOT: A gallant lady. Monsieur, fare you well.
USER: I beseech you a word. What is she in the white?
BOT: A woman sometimes, an you saw her in the light.
USER: Perchance light in the light. I desire her name.
BOT: She hath but one for herself; to desire that were a shame.
USER: Pray you, sir, whose daughter?
BOT: Her mother's, I have heard.
USER: God's blessing on your beard!
BOT: Good sir, be not offended. She is an heir of Falconbridge.
USER: Nay, my choler is ended. She is a most sweet lady.
BOT: Not unlike, sir; that may be.
USER: What's her name in the cap?
BOT: Rosaline, by good hap.
USER: Is she wedded or no?
BOT: To her will, sir, or so.
USER: You are welcome, sir. Adieu.
BOT: Farewell to me, sir, and welcome to you.
USER: That last is Berowne, the merry madcap lord. Not a word with him but a jest.
BOT: And every jest but a word.
USER: It was well done of you to take him at his word.
BOT: I was as willing to grapple as he was to board.
USER: Two hot sheeps, marry!
BOT: And wherefore not ships? No sheep, sweet lamb, unless we feed on your lips.
USER: You sheep and I pasture. Shall that finish the jest?
BOT: So you grant pasture for me.
USER: Not so, gentle beast. My lips are no common, though several they be.
BOT: Belonging to whom?
USER: To my fortunes and me.
BOT: Good wits will be jangling; but, gentles, agree. This civil war of wits were much better used On Navarre and his bookmen, for here 'tis abused.
USER: If my observation, which very seldom lies, By the heart's still rhetoric disclosed with eyes, Deceive me not now, Navarre is infected.
BOT: With what?
USER: With that which we lovers entitle "affected".
BOT: Your reason.
USER: Come, to our pavilion. Boyet is disposed.
BOT: But to speak that in words which his eye hath disclosed. I only have made a mouth of his eye By adding a tongue which I know will not lie.
USER: Thou art an old love-monger, and speakest skilfully.
BOT: He is Cupid's grandfather, and learns news of him.
USER: Then was Venus like her mother; for her father is but grim.
BOT: Do you hear, my mad wenches?
USER: No.
BOT: What, then, do you see?
USER: Ay, our way to be gone.
BOT: You are too hard for me.

USER: Warble, child, make passionate my sense of hearing.
BOT: Concolinel.
USER: Sweet air! Go, tenderness of years, take this key, give enlargement to the swain, bring him festinately hither. I must employ him in a letter to my love.
BOT: Master, will you win your love with a French brawl?
USER: How meanest thou? Brawling in French?
BOT: How hast thou purchased this experience?
USER: By my penny of observation.
BOT: But O - but O -
USER: "The hobby-horse is forgot."
BOT: Call'st thou my love "hobby-horse"?
USER: No, master. The hobby-horse is but a colt, and your love perhaps a hackney. But have you forgot your love?
BOT: Almost I had.
USER: Negligent student! Learn her by heart.
BOT: By heart and in heart, boy.
USER: And out of heart, master. All those three I will prove.
BOT: What wilt thou prove?
USER: I am all these three.
BOT: And three times as much more, and yet nothing at all.
USER: Fetch hither the swain. He must carry me a letter.
BOT: A message well sympathized: a horse to be ambassador for an ass.
USER: Ha, ha, what sayest thou?
BOT: Marry, sir, you must send the ass upon the horse, for he is very slow-gaited. But I go.
USER: The way is but short. Away!
BOT: As swift as lead, sir.
USER: The meaning, pretty ingenious? Is not lead a metal heavy, dull, and slow?
BOT: _Minime_, honest master; or rather, master, no.
USER: I say lead is slow.
BOT: You are too swift, sir, to say so. Is that lead slow which is fired from a gun?
USER: Sweet smoke of rhetoric! He reputes me a cannon; and the bullet, that's he. I shoot thee at the swain.
BOT: Thump then, and I flee.
USER: A most acute juvenal, voluble and free of grace! By thy favour, sweet welkin, I must sigh in thy face. Most rude melancholy, valour gives thee place. My herald is returned.
BOT: A wonder, master! Here's a costard broken in a shin.
USER: Some enigma, some riddle. Come, thy _l'envoi_ begin.
BOT: No egma, no riddle, no _l'envoi_, no salve in the mail, sir. O, sir, plantain, a plain plantain! No _l'envoi_, no _l'envoi_, no salve, sir, but a plantain.
USER: Do the wise think them other? Is not _l'envoi_ a _salve?_
BOT: I will add the _l'envoi_. Say the moral again.
USER: The fox, the ape, and the humble-bee Were still at odds, being but three.
BOT: Until the goose came out of door, And stayed the odds by adding four. Now will I begin your moral, and do you follow with my _l'envoi_. The fox, the ape, and the humble-bee Were still at odds, being but three.
USER: Until the goose came out of door, Staying the odds by adding four.
BOT: A good _l'envoi_, ending in the goose. Would you desire more?
USER: The boy hath sold him a bargain, a goose, that's flat. Sir, your pennyworth is good, an your goose be fat. To sell a bargain well is as cunning as fast and loose. Let me see: a fat _l'envoi_ - ay, that's a fat goose.
BOT: Come hither, come hither. How did this argument begin?
USER: By saying that a costard was broken in a shin. Then called you for the _l'envoi_.
BOT: True, and I for a plantain. Thus came your argument in. Then the boy's fat _l'envoi_, the goose that you bought; and he ended the market.
USER: But tell me, how was there a costard broken in a shin?
BOT: I will tell you sensibly.
USER: Thou hast no feeling of it, Moth. I will speak that _l'envoi_. I, Costard, running out, that was safely within, Fell over the threshold and broke my shin.
BOT: We will talk no more of this matter.
USER: Till there be more matter in the shin.
BOT: Sirrah Costard, I will enfranchise thee.
USER: O, marry me to one Frances! I smell some _l'envoi_, some goose, in this.
BOT: By my sweet soul, I mean setting thee at liberty, enfreedoming thy person. Thou wert immured, restrained, captivated, bound.
USER: True, true; and now you will be my purgation, and let me loose.
BOT: Like the sequel, I. Signior Costard, adieu.
USER: My good knave Costard, exceedingly well met.
BOT: Pray you, sir, how much carnation ribbon may a man buy for a remuneration?
USER: What is a remuneration?
BOT: Marry, sir, halfpenny farthing.
USER: Why, then, three-farthing worth of silk.
BOT: I thank your worship. God be wi' you.
USER: Stay, slave. I must employ thee. As thou wilt win my favour, good my knave, Do one thing for me that I shall entreat.
BOT: When would you have it done, sir?
USER: This afternoon.
BOT: Well, I will do it, sir. Fare you well.
USER: Thou knowest not what it is.
BOT: I shall know, sir, when I have done it.
USER: Why, villain, thou must know first.
BOT: I will come to your worship tomorrow morning.
USER: Gardon, O sweet gardon! Better than remuneration, a 'levenpence farthing better. Most sweet gardon! I will do it, sir, in print. Gardon! Remuneration!

USER: Was that the King that spurred his horse so hard Against the steep uprising of the hill?
BOT: I know not, but I think it was not he.
USER: Whoe'er he was, he showed a mounting mind. Well, lords, today we shall have our dispatch; On Saturday we will return to France. Then, forester, my friend, where is the bush That we must stand and play the murderer in?
BOT: Hereby, upon the edge of yonder coppice, A stand where you may make "the fairest shoot".
USER: I thank my beauty, I am fair that shoot, And thereupon thou speak'st the fairest shoot.
BOT: Pardon me, madam, for I meant not so.
USER: What, what? First praise me, and again say no? O short-lived pride! Not fair? Alack for woe!
BOT: Yes, madam, fair.
USER: Nay, never paint me now. Where fair is not, praise cannot mend the brow. Here, good my glass, take this for telling true: Fair payment for foul words is more than due.
BOT: Nothing but fair is that which you inherit.
USER: Do not curst wives hold that self-sovereignty Only for praise' sake, when they strive to be Lords o'er their lords?
BOT: Only for praise; and praise we may afford To any lady that subdues a lord.
USER: Here comes a member of the commonwealth.
BOT: God dig-you-den all! Pray you, which is the head lady?
USER: Thou shalt know her, fellow, by the rest that have no heads.
BOT: Which is the greatest lady, the highest?
USER: The thickest and the tallest.
BOT: What's your will, sir? What's your will?
USER: I have a letter from Monsieur Berowne to one Lady Rosaline.
BOT: O, thy letter, thy letter! He's a good friend of mine. Stand aside, good bearer. Boyet, you can carve. Break up this capon.
USER: I am bound to serve. This letter is mistook; it importeth none here. It is writ to Jaquenetta.
BOT: We will read it, I swear. Break the neck of the wax, and everyone give ear.
USER: What plume of feathers is he that indited this letter? What vane? What weathercock? Did you ever hear better?
BOT: I am much deceived but I remember the style.
USER: Else your memory is bad, going o'er it erewhile.
BOT: This Armado is a Spaniard that keeps here in court, A phantasime, a Monarcho, and one that makes sport To the Prince and his book-mates.
USER: Thou, fellow, a word. Who gave thee this letter?
BOT: I told you: my lord.
USER: To whom shouldst thou give it?
BOT: From my lord to my lady.
USER: From which lord to which lady?
BOT: From my Lord Berowne, a good master of mine, To a lady of France that he called Rosaline.
USER: Thou hast mistaken his letter. Come, lords, away. Here, sweet, put up this: 'twill be thine another day.
BOT: Who is the shooter? Who is the shooter?
USER: Shall I teach you to know?
BOT: Ay, my continent of beauty.
USER: Why, she that bears the bow. Finely put off!
BOT: My lady goes to kill horns, but if thou marry, Hang me by the neck if horns that year miscarry. Finely put on!
USER: Well, then, I am the shooter.
BOT: And who is your deer?
USER: If we choose by the horns, yourself come not near. Finely put on indeed!
BOT: You still wrangle with her, Boyet, and she strikes at the brow.
USER: But she herself is hit lower. Have I hit her now?
BOT: Shall I come upon thee with an old saying, that was a man when King Pepin of France was a little boy, as touching the hit it?
USER: So I may answer thee with one as old, that was a woman when Queen Guinevere of Britain was a little wench, as touching the hit it.
BOT: Thou canst not hit it, hit it, hit it, Thou canst not hit it, my good man.
USER: An I cannot, cannot, cannot, An I cannot, another can.
BOT: By my troth, most pleasant. How both did fit it!
USER: A mark marvellous well shot, for they both did hit it.
BOT: A mark! O, mark but that mark! A mark, says my lady! Let the mark have a prick in't, to mete at, if it may be.
USER: Wide o' the bow hand! I' faith, your hand is out.
BOT: Indeed, a' must shoot nearer, or he'll ne'er hit the clout.
USER: An if my hand be out, then belike your hand is in.
BOT: Then will she get the upshoot by cleaving the pin.
USER: Come, come, you talk greasily, your lips grow foul.
BOT: She's too hard for you at pricks, sir. Challenge her to bowl.
USER: I fear too much rubbing. Good night, my good owl.

USER: Very reverend sport, truly, and done in the testimony of a good conscience.
BOT: Truly, Master Holofernes, the epithets are sweetly varied, like a scholar at the least. But, sir, I assure ye it was a buck of the first head.
USER: Sir Nathaniel, _haud credo_.
BOT: 'Twas not a "auld grey doe", 'twas a pricket.
USER: I said the deer was not a "auld grey doe", 'twas a pricket.
BOT: Twice-sod simplicity, _bis coctus!_ O, thou monster Ignorance, how deformed dost thou look!
USER: You two are bookmen. Can you tell me by your wit What was a month old at Cain's birth, that's not five weeks old as yet?
BOT: Dictynna, goodman Dull. Dictynna, goodman Dull.
USER: What is Dictynna?
BOT: A title to Phoebe, to Luna, to the moon.
USER: The moon was a month old when Adam was no more, And raught not to five weeks when he came to five-score. Th' allusion holds in the exchange.
BOT: 'Tis true, indeed. The collusion holds in the exchange.
USER: God comfort thy capacity! I say, th' allusion holds in the exchange.
BOT: And I say the pollution holds in the exchange, for the moon is never but a month old; and I say beside that 'twas a pricket that the Princess killed.
USER: Sir Nathaniel, will you hear an extemporal epitaph on the death of the deer? And, to humour the ignorant, call I the deer the Princess killed a pricket.
BOT: _Perge_, good Master Holofernes, _perge_, so it shall please you to abrogate scurrility.
USER: A rare talent!
BOT: Sir, I praise the Lord for you, and so may my parishioners, for their sons are well tutored by you, and their daughters profit very greatly under you. You are a good member of the commonwealth.
USER: _Mehercle!_ If their sons be ingenious, they shall want no instruction; if their daughters be capable, I will put it to them. But, _vir sapit qui pauca loquitur_. A soul feminine saluteth us.
BOT: God give you good morrow, Master Person.
USER: Master Person, _quasi_ pierce one. And if one should be pierced, which is the one?
BOT: Marry, Master schoolmaster, he that is likest to a hogshead.
USER: Of piercing a hogshead! A good lustre or conceit in a turf of earth; fire enough for a flint, pearl enough for a swine. 'Tis pretty; it is well.
BOT: Good Master Parson, be so good as read me this letter. It was given me by Costard, and sent me from Don Armado. I beseech you read it.
USER: Ay, sir, and very learned.
BOT: Let me hear a staff, a stanze, a verse, _Lege, domine_.
USER: Ay, sir, from one Monsieur Berowne, one of the strange queen's lords.
BOT: Good Costard, go with me. Sir, God save your life.
USER: Have with thee, my girl.
BOT: Sir, you have done this in the fear of God, very religiously; and, as a certain Father saith -
USER: Sir, tell not me of the Father, I do fear colourable colours. But to return to the verses: did they please you, Sir Nathaniel?
BOT: Marvellous well for the pen.
USER: And thank you too; for society, saith the text, is the happiness of life.
BOT: And certes, the text most infallibly concludes it. [_To Dull_.] Sir, I do invite you too. You shall not say me nay. _Pauca verba_. Away! The gentles are at their game, and we will to our recreation.

USER: Ay me!
BOT: with thy birdbolt under the left pap. In faith, secrets!
USER: Ay me! I am forsworn.
BOT: Why, he comes in like a perjure, wearing papers.
USER: In love, I hope. Sweet fellowship in shame.
BOT: One drunkard loves another of the name.
USER: Am I the first that have been perjured so?
BOT: I could put thee in comfort: not by two that I know. Thou makest the triumviry, the corner-cap of society, The shape of love's Tyburn, that hangs up simplicity.
USER: I fear these stubborn lines lack power to move. O sweet Maria, empress of my love, These numbers will I tear, and write in prose.
BOT: O, rhymes are guards on wanton Cupid's hose. Disfigure not his shop.
USER: This is the liver vein, which makes flesh a deity, A green goose a goddess. Pure, pure idolatry. God amend us, God amend! We are much out o' th' way.
BOT: By whom shall I send this? - Company! Stay.
USER: O most divine Kate!
BOT: O most profane coxcomb!
USER: By heaven, the wonder in a mortal eye!
BOT: By earth, she is but corporal. There you lie.
USER: Her amber hairs for foul hath amber quoted.
BOT: An amber-coloured raven was well noted.
USER: As upright as the cedar.
BOT: Stoop, I say. Her shoulder is with child.
USER: As fair as day.
BOT: Ay, as some days, but then no sun must shine.
USER: O, that I had my wish!
BOT: And I had mine!
USER: And I mine too, good Lord!
BOT: Amen, so I had mine. Is not that a good word?
USER: I would forget her; but a fever she Reigns in my blood, and will remembered be.
BOT: A fever in your blood? Why, then incision Would let her out in saucers. Sweet misprision!
USER: Once more I'll read the ode that I have writ.
BOT: Once more I'll mark how love can vary wit.
USER: That in love's grief desir'st society. You may look pale, but I should blush, I know, To be o'erheard and taken napping so.
BOT: Too bitter is thy jest. Are we betrayed thus to thy over-view?
USER: Soft! Whither away so fast? A true man, or a thief, that gallops so?
BOT: I post from love. Good lover, let me go.
USER: God bless the King!
BOT: What present hast thou there?
USER: Some certain treason.
BOT: What makes treason here?
USER: Nay, it makes nothing, sir.
BOT: If it mar nothing neither, The treason and you go in peace away together.
USER: I beseech your Grace, let this letter be read. Our person misdoubts it; 'twas treason, he said.
BOT: Berowne, read it over. Where hadst thou it?
USER: Of Costard.
BOT: Where hadst thou it?
USER: Of Dun Adramadio, Dun Adramadio.
BOT: How now, what is in you? Why dost thou tear it?
USER: A toy, my liege, a toy. Your Grace needs not fear it.
BOT: It did move him to passion, and therefore let's hear it.
USER: It is Berowne's writing, and here is his name.
BOT: shame. Guilty, my lord, guilty. I confess, I confess.
USER: What?
BOT: That you three fools lacked me fool to make up the mess. He, he, and you - and you, my liege - and I Are pick-purses in love, and we deserve to die. O, dismiss this audience, and I shall tell you more.
USER: Now the number is even.
BOT: True, true, we are four. Will these turtles be gone?
USER: Hence, sirs, away!
BOT: Walk aside the true folk, and let the traitors stay.
USER: What, did these rent lines show some love of thine?
BOT: What zeal, what fury hath inspired thee now? My love, her mistress, is a gracious moon; She, an attending star, scarce seen a light.
USER: By heaven, thy love is black as ebony.
BOT: O paradox! Black is the badge of hell, The hue of dungeons and the school of night; And beauty's crest becomes the heavens well.
USER: To look like her are chimney-sweepers black.
BOT: And since her time are colliers counted bright.
USER: And Ethiopes of their sweet complexion crack.
BOT: Dark needs no candles now, for dark is light.
USER: Your mistresses dare never come in rain, For fear their colours should be washed away.
BOT: 'Twere good yours did; for, sir, to tell you plain, I'll find a fairer face not washed today.
USER: I'll prove her fair, or talk till doomsday here.
BOT: No devil will fright thee then so much as she.
USER: I never knew man hold vile stuff so dear.
BOT: Look, here's thy love, my foot and her face see.
USER: O, if the streets were paved with thine eyes, Her feet were much too dainty for such tread.
BOT: O vile! Then, as she goes, what upward lies The street should see as she walked over head.
USER: But what of this? Are we not all in love?
BOT: Nothing so sure, and thereby all forsworn.
USER: Then leave this chat, and, good Berowne, now prove Our loving lawful, and our faith not torn.
BOT: Ay, marry, there; some flattery for this evil.
USER: O, some authority how to proceed. Some tricks, some quillets, how to cheat the devil.
BOT: Some salve for perjury.
USER: Saint Cupid, then, and, soldiers, to the field!
BOT: Advance your standards, and upon them, lords! Pell-mell, down with them! But be first advised In conflict that you get the sun of them.
USER: Now to plain dealing. Lay these glozes by. Shall we resolve to woo these girls of France?
BOT: And win them too. Therefore let us devise Some entertainment for them in their tents.
USER: Away, away! No time shall be omitted That will betime and may by us be fitted.
BOT: _Allons! allons!_ Sowed cockle reaped no corn, And justice always whirls in equal measure. Light wenches may prove plagues to men forsworn; If so, our copper buys no better treasure.

USER: _Satis quod sufficit._
BOT: A most singular and choice epithet.
USER: _Laus Deo, bone intelligo._
BOT: _Bone? Bone_ for _bene?_ Priscian a little scratched; 'twill serve.
USER: _Videsne quis venit?_
BOT: _Video, et gaudeo._
USER: _Chirrah!_
BOT: _Quare_ "chirrah", not "sirrah"?
USER: Men of peace, well encountered.
BOT: Most military sir, salutation.
USER: stolen the scraps.
BOT: O, they have lived long on the almsbasket of words. I marvel thy master hath not eaten thee for a word, for thou art not so long by the head as _honorificabilitudinitatibus_. Thou art easier swallowed than a flap-dragon.
USER: Peace! The peal begins.
BOT: Yes, yes, he teaches boys the hornbook. What is _a, b_, spelt backward with the horn on his head?
USER: _Ba, pueritia_, with a horn added.
BOT: _Ba_, most silly sheep with a horn. You hear his learning.
USER: _Quis, quis_, thou consonant?
BOT: The third of the five vowels, if you repeat them; or the fifth, if I.
USER: I will repeat them: _a, e, i_ -
BOT: The sheep. The other two concludes it: _o, u_.
USER: Now, by the salt wave of the Mediterraneum, a sweet touch, a quick venue of wit! Snip, snap, quick and home! It rejoiceth my intellect. True wit!
BOT: Offered by a child to an old man - which is wit-old.
USER: What is the figure? What is the figure?
BOT: Horns.
USER: Thou disputes like an infant. Go whip thy gig.
BOT: Lend me your horn to make one, and I will whip about your infamy _unum cita_. A gig of a cuckold's horn.
USER: O, I smell false Latin! _Dunghill_ for _unguem_.
BOT: Arts-man, preambulate. We will be singuled from the barbarous. Do you not educate youth at the charge-house on the top of the mountain?
USER: Or _mons_, the hill.
BOT: At your sweet pleasure, for the mountain.
USER: I do, _sans question_.
BOT: Sir, it is the King's most sweet pleasure and affection to congratulate the Princess at her pavilion in the posteriors of this day, which the rude multitude call the afternoon.
USER: The posterior of the day, most generous sir, is liable, congruent, and measurable for the afternoon. The word is well culled, chose, sweet, and apt, I do assure you, sir, I do assure.
BOT: Where will you find men worthy enough to present them?
USER: Joshua, yourself; myself; and this gallant gentleman, Judas Maccabaeus. This swain, because of his great limb or joint, shall pass Pompey the Great; the page, Hercules.
BOT: Pardon, sir; error. He is not quantity enough for that Worthy's thumb; he is not so big as the end of his club.
USER: Shall I have audience? He shall present Hercules in minority. His enter and exit shall be strangling a snake; and I will have an apology for that purpose.
BOT: An excellent device! So, if any of the audience hiss, you may cry "Well done, Hercules, now thou crushest the snake!" That is the way to make an offence gracious, though few have the grace to do it.
USER: For the rest of the Worthies?
BOT: I will play three myself.
USER: Thrice-worthy gentleman!
BOT: Shall I tell you a thing?
USER: We attend.
BOT: We will have, if this fadge not, an antic. I beseech you, follow.
USER: _Via_, goodman Dull! Thou has spoken no word all this while.
BOT: Nor understood none neither, sir.
USER: _Allons!_ we will employ thee.
BOT: I'll make one in a dance, or so; or I will play on the tabor to the Worthies, and let them dance the hay.
USER: Most dull, honest Dull! To our sport, away.

USER: Sweet hearts, we shall be rich ere we depart, If fairings come thus plentifully in. A lady walled about with diamonds! Look you what I have from the loving King.
BOT: Madam, came nothing else along with that?
USER: Nothing but this? Yes, as much love in rhyme As would be crammed up in a sheet of paper Writ o' both sides the leaf, margent and all, That he was fain to seal on Cupid's name.
BOT: That was the way to make his godhead wax, For he hath been five thousand years a boy.
USER: Ay, and a shrewd unhappy gallows too.
BOT: You'll ne'er be friends with him. He killed your sister.
USER: He made her melancholy, sad, and heavy; And so she died. Had she been light, like you, Of such a merry, nimble, stirring spirit, She might ha' been a grandam ere she died. And so may you, for a light heart lives long.
BOT: What's your dark meaning, mouse, of this light word?
USER: A light condition in a beauty dark.
BOT: We need more light to find your meaning out.
USER: You'll mar the light by taking it in snuff; Therefore I'll darkly end the argument.
BOT: Look what you do, you do it still i' th' dark.
USER: So do not you, for you are a light wench.
BOT: Indeed, I weigh not you, and therefore light.
USER: You weigh me not? O, that's you care not for me.
BOT: Great reason, for past cure is still past care.
USER: Well bandied both; a set of wit well played. But, Rosaline, you have a favour too. Who sent it? And what is it?
BOT: Anything like?
USER: Much in the letters, nothing in the praise.
BOT: Beauteous as ink: a good conclusion.
USER: Fair as a text B in a copy-book.
BOT: 'Ware pencils, how! Let me not die your debtor, My red dominical, my golden letter. O, that your face were not so full of O's!
USER: A pox of that jest! And beshrew all shrews. But, Katharine, what was sent to you from fair Dumaine?
BOT: Madam, this glove.
USER: Did he not send you twain?
BOT: Yes, madam, and moreover, Some thousand verses of a faithful lover. A huge translation of hypocrisy, Vilely compiled, profound simplicity.
USER: This, and these pearls, to me sent Longaville. The letter is too long by half a mile.
BOT: I think no less. Dost thou not wish in heart The chain were longer and the letter short?
USER: Ay, or I would these hands might never part.
BOT: We are wise girls to mock our lovers so.
USER: None are so surely caught, when they are catched, As wit turned fool. Folly, in wisdom hatched, Hath wisdom's warrant and the help of school And wit's own grace to grace a learned fool.
BOT: The blood of youth burns not with such excess As gravity's revolt to wantonness.
USER: Folly in fools bears not so strong a note As fool'ry in the wise when wit doth dote, Since all the power thereof it doth apply To prove, by wit, worth in simplicity.
BOT: Here comes Boyet, and mirth is in his face.
USER: O, I am stabbed with laughter! Where's her Grace?
BOT: Thy news, Boyet?
USER: Saint Denis to Saint Cupid! What are they That charge their breath against us? Say, scout, say.
BOT: But what, but what, come they to visit us?
USER: Come on, then, wear the favours most in sight.
BOT: But in this changing, what is your intent?
USER: But shall we dance, if they desire us to't?
BOT: No, to the death we will not move a foot, Nor to their penned speech render we no grace, But while 'tis spoke each turn away her face.
USER: Why, that contempt will kill the speaker's heart, And quite divorce his memory from his part.
BOT: The trumpet sounds. Be masked; the maskers come. Longaville and Dumaine disguised.
USER: _All hail, the richest beauties on the earth!_
BOT: Beauties no richer than rich taffeta.
USER: _A holy parcel of the fairest dames_ _That ever turned their_ - backs - _to mortal views!_
BOT: _Their eyes_, villain, _their eyes._
USER: _That ever turned their eyes to mortal views. Out_ -
BOT: True; out indeed.
USER: _Out of your favours, heavenly spirits, vouchsafe Not to behold_ -
BOT: _Once to behold_, rogue!
USER: _Once to behold with your sun-beamed eyes - With your sun-beamed eyes_ -
BOT: They will not answer to that epithet. You were best call it "daughter-beamed eyes".
USER: They do not mark me, and that brings me out.
BOT: Is this your perfectness? Be gone, you rogue!
USER: What would these strangers? Know their minds, Boyet. If they do speak our language, 'tis our will That some plain man recount their purposes. Know what they would.
BOT: What would you with the Princess?
USER: Nothing but peace and gentle visitation.
BOT: What would they, say they?
USER: Nothing but peace and gentle visitation.
BOT: Why, that they have, and bid them so be gone.
USER: She says you have it, and you may be gone.
BOT: Say to her we have measured many miles To tread a measure with her on this grass.
USER: They say that they have measured many a mile To tread a measure with you on this grass.
BOT: It is not so. Ask them how many inches Is in one mile? If they have measured many, The measure then of one is easily told.
USER: If to come hither you have measured miles, And many miles, the Princess bids you tell How many inches doth fill up one mile.
BOT: Tell her we measure them by weary steps.
USER: She hears herself.
BOT: How many weary steps Of many weary miles you have o'ergone Are numbered in the travel of one mile?
USER: We number nothing that we spend for you. Our duty is so rich, so infinite, That we may do it still without account. Vouchsafe to show the sunshine of your face, That we, like savages, may worship it.
BOT: My face is but a moon, and clouded too.
USER: Blessed are clouds, to do as such clouds do! Vouchsafe, bright moon, and these thy stars, to shine, Those clouds removed, upon our watery eyne.
BOT: O vain petitioner! Beg a greater matter! Thou now requests but moonshine in the water.
USER: Then in our measure do but vouchsafe one change. Thou bidd'st me beg; this begging is not strange.
BOT: Play, music, then! Nay, you must do it soon. Not yet? No dance! Thus change I like the moon.
USER: Will you not dance? How come you thus estranged?
BOT: You took the moon at full, but now she's changed.
USER: Yet still she is the moon, and I the man. The music plays, vouchsafe some motion to it.
BOT: Our ears vouchsafe it.
USER: But your legs should do it.
BOT: Since you are strangers and come here by chance, We'll not be nice. Take hands. We will not dance.
USER: Why take we hands then?
BOT: Only to part friends. Curtsy, sweet hearts, and so the measure ends.
USER: More measure of this measure! Be not nice.
BOT: We can afford no more at such a price.
USER: Price you yourselves? What buys your company?
BOT: Your absence only.
USER: That can never be.
BOT: Then cannot we be bought. And so adieu - Twice to your visor, and half once to you!
USER: If you deny to dance, let's hold more chat.
BOT: In private then.
USER: I am best pleased with that.
BOT: White-handed mistress, one sweet word with thee.
USER: Honey, and milk, and sugar: there is three.
BOT: Nay, then, two treys, an if you grow so nice, Metheglin, wort, and malmsey. Well run, dice! There's half a dozen sweets.
USER: Seventh sweet, adieu. Since you can cog, I'll play no more with you.
BOT: One word in secret.
USER: Let it not be sweet.
BOT: Thou griev'st my gall.
USER: Gall! Bitter.
BOT: Therefore meet.
USER: Will you vouchsafe with me to change a word?
BOT: Name it.
USER: Fair lady -
BOT: Say you so? Fair lord! Take that for your "fair lady".
USER: Please it you, As much in private, and I'll bid adieu.
BOT: What, was your visor made without a tongue?
USER: I know the reason, lady, why you ask.
BOT: O, for your reason! Quickly, sir, I long.
USER: You have a double tongue within your mask, And would afford my speechless visor half.
BOT: "Veal", quoth the Dutchman. Is not veal a calf?
USER: A calf, fair lady?
BOT: No, a fair lord calf.
USER: Let's part the word.
BOT: No, I'll not be your half. Take all and wean it; it may prove an ox.
USER: Look how you butt yourself in these sharp mocks. Will you give horns, chaste lady? Do not so.
BOT: Then die a calf before your horns do grow.
USER: One word in private with you ere I die.
BOT: Bleat softly, then; the butcher hears you cry.
USER: Not one word more, my maids; break off, break off.
BOT: By heaven, all dry-beaten with pure scoff!
USER: Farewell, mad wenches. You have simple wits.
BOT: Twenty adieus, my frozen Muscovites. Are these the breed of wits so wondered at?
USER: Tapers they are, with your sweet breaths puffed out.
BOT: Well-liking wits they have; gross, gross; fat, fat.
USER: O poverty in wit, kingly-poor flout! Will they not, think you, hang themselves tonight? Or ever but in vizors show their faces? This pert Berowne was out of countenance quite.
BOT: They were all in lamentable cases. The King was weeping-ripe for a good word.
USER: Berowne did swear himself out of all suit.
BOT: Dumaine was at my service, and his sword. "_Non point_," quoth I; my servant straight was mute.
USER: Lord Longaville said I came o'er his heart; And trow you what he called me?
BOT: Qualm, perhaps.
USER: Yes, in good faith.
BOT: Go, sickness as thou art!
USER: Well, better wits have worn plain statute-caps. But will you hear? The King is my love sworn.
BOT: And quick Berowne hath plighted faith to me.
USER: And Longaville was for my service born.
BOT: Dumaine is mine as sure as bark on tree.
USER: Madam, and pretty mistresses, give ear. Immediately they will again be here In their own shapes, for it can never be They will digest this harsh indignity.
BOT: Will they return?
USER: They will, they will, God knows, And leap for joy, though they are lame with blows. Therefore, change favours and, when they repair, Blow like sweet roses in this summer air.
BOT: How "blow"? How "blow"? Speak to be understood.
USER: Fair ladies masked are roses in their bud. Dismasked, their damask sweet commixture shown, Are angels vailing clouds, or roses blown.
BOT: Avaunt, perplexity! What shall we do If they return in their own shapes to woo?
USER: Ladies, withdraw. The gallants are at hand.
BOT: Whip to our tents, as roes run o'er the land.
USER: Fair sir, God save you. Where's the Princess?
BOT: Gone to her tent. Please it your Majesty Command me any service to her thither?
USER: That she vouchsafe me audience for one word.
BOT: I will; and so will she, I know, my lord.
USER: A blister on his sweet tongue, with my heart, That put Armado's page out of his part!
BOT: See where it comes! Behaviour, what wert thou Till this man showed thee, and what art thou now?
USER: All hail, sweet madam, and fair time of day.
BOT: "Fair" in "all hail" is foul, as I conceive.
USER: Construe my speeches better, if you may.
BOT: Then wish me better. I will give you leave.
USER: We came to visit you, and purpose now To lead you to our court. Vouchsafe it then.
BOT: This field shall hold me, and so hold your vow. Nor God nor I delights in perjured men.
USER: Rebuke me not for that which you provoke. The virtue of your eye must break my oath.
BOT: O, you have lived in desolation here, Unseen, unvisited, much to our shame.
USER: Not so, my lord. It is not so, I swear. We have had pastimes here and pleasant game. A mess of Russians left us but of late.
BOT: How, madam? Russians?
USER: Ay, in truth, my lord. Trim gallants, full of courtship and of state.
BOT: This proves you wise and rich, for in my eye -
USER: I am a fool, and full of poverty.
BOT: But that you take what doth to you belong, It were a fault to snatch words from my tongue.
USER: O, I am yours, and all that I possess.
BOT: All the fool mine?
USER: I cannot give you less.
BOT: Which of the visors was it that you wore?
USER: Where, when, what visor? Why demand you this?
BOT: There, then, that visor; that superfluous case That hid the worse and showed the better face.
USER: We are descried. They'll mock us now downright.
BOT: Let us confess and turn it to a jest.
USER: Amazed, my lord? Why looks your Highness sad?
BOT: Help! Hold his brows! He'll swoon. Why look you pale? Seasick, I think, coming from Muscovy.
USER: _Sans_ "_sans_," I pray you.
BOT: No, they are free that gave these tokens to us.
USER: Our states are forfeit. Seek not to undo us.
BOT: It is not so. For how can this be true, That you stand forfeit, being those that sue?
USER: Peace! for I will not have to do with you.
BOT: Nor shall not, if I do as I intend.
USER: Speak for yourselves. My wit is at an end.
BOT: Teach us, sweet madam, for our rude transgression Some fair excuse.
USER: The fairest is confession. Were not you here but even now, disguised?
BOT: Madam, I was.
USER: And were you well advised?
BOT: I was, fair madam.
USER: When you then were here, What did you whisper in your lady's ear?
BOT: That more than all the world I did respect her.
USER: When she shall challenge this, you will reject her.
BOT: Upon mine honour, no.
USER: Peace, peace, forbear! Your oath once broke, you force not to forswear.
BOT: Despise me when I break this oath of mine.
USER: I will; and therefore keep it. Rosaline, What did the Russian whisper in your ear?
BOT: Madam, he swore that he did hold me dear As precious eyesight, and did value me Above this world; adding thereto, moreover, That he would wed me, or else die my lover.
USER: God give thee joy of him! The noble lord Most honourably doth uphold his word.
BOT: What mean you, madam? By my life, my troth, I never swore this lady such an oath.
USER: By heaven, you did! And to confirm it plain, You gave me this. But take it, sir, again.
BOT: My faith and this the Princess I did give. I knew her by this jewel on her sleeve.
USER: Pardon me, sir, this jewel did she wear, And Lord Berowne, I thank him, is my dear. What, will you have me, or your pearl again?
BOT: Full merrily Hath this brave manage, this career, been run.
USER: Lo, he is tilting straight! Peace! I have done. Welcome, pure wit! Thou part'st a fair fray.
BOT: O Lord, sir, they would know Whether the three Worthies shall come in or no.
USER: What, are there but three?
BOT: No, sir; but it is vara fine, For every one pursents three.
USER: And three times thrice is nine.
BOT: Not so, sir, under correction, sir, I hope it is not so. You cannot beg us, sir, I can assure you, sir; we know what we know. I hope, sir, three times thrice, sir -
USER: Is not nine?
BOT: Under correction, sir, we know whereuntil it doth amount.
USER: By Jove, I always took three threes for nine.
BOT: O Lord, sir, it were pity you should get your living by reckoning, sir.
USER: How much is it?
BOT: O Lord, sir, the parties themselves, the actors, sir, will show whereuntil it doth amount. For mine own part, I am, as they say, but to parfect one man in one poor man - Pompion the Great, sir.
USER: Art thou one of the Worthies?
BOT: It pleased them to think me worthy of Pompey the Great. For mine own part, I know not the degree of the Worthy, but I am to stand for him.
USER: Go bid them prepare.
BOT: We will turn it finely off, sir; we will take some care.
USER: Berowne, they will shame us. Let them not approach.
BOT: We are shame-proof, my lord, and 'tis some policy To have one show worse than the King's and his company.
USER: I say they shall not come.
BOT: A right description of our sport, my lord.
USER: Anointed, I implore so much expense of thy royal sweet breath as will utter a brace of words.
BOT: Doth this man serve God?
USER: Why ask you?
BOT: He speaks not like a man of God his making.
USER: There is five in the first show.
BOT: You are deceived. 'Tis not so.
USER: The pedant, the braggart, the hedge-priest, the fool, and the boy. Abate throw at novum, and the whole world again Cannot pick out five such, take each one in his vein.
BOT: The ship is under sail, and here she comes amain.
USER: _I Pompey am_ -
BOT: You lie, you are not he.
USER: _I Pompey am_ -
BOT: With leopard's head on knee.
USER: Well said, old mocker. I must needs be friends with thee.
BOT: _I Pompey am, Pompey surnamed the Big._
USER: The "Great".
BOT: Great thanks, great Pompey.
USER: 'Tis not so much worth; but I hope I was perfect. I made a little fault in "Great".
BOT: My hat to a halfpenny, Pompey proves the best Worthy.
USER: _When in the world I lived, I was the world's commander; By east, west, north, and south, I spread my conquering might. My scutcheon plain declares that I am Alisander._
BOT: Your nose says, no, you are not; for it stands to right.
USER: Your nose smells "no" in this, most tender-smelling knight.
BOT: The conqueror is dismayed. Proceed, good Alexander.
USER: _When in the world I lived, I was the world's commander_ -
BOT: Most true; 'tis right. You were so, Alisander.
USER: Pompey the Great -
BOT: Your servant, and Costard.
USER: Take away the conqueror, take away Alisander.
BOT: Stand aside, good Pompey.
USER: A Judas!
BOT: Not Iscariot, sir. _Judas I am, ycleped Maccabaeus._
USER: Judas Maccabaeus clipped is plain Judas.
BOT: A kissing traitor. How art thou proved Judas?
USER: _Judas I am_ -
BOT: The more shame for you, Judas.
USER: What mean you, sir?
BOT: To make Judas hang himself.
USER: Begin, sir; you are my elder.
BOT: Well followed. Judas was hanged on an elder.
USER: I will not be put out of countenance.
BOT: Because thou hast no face.
USER: What is this?
BOT: A cittern-head.
USER: The head of a bodkin.
BOT: A death's face in a ring.
USER: The face of an old Roman coin, scarce seen.
BOT: The pommel of Caesar's falchion.
USER: The carved-bone face on a flask.
BOT: Saint George's half-cheek in a brooch.
USER: Ay, and in a brooch of lead.
BOT: Ay, and worn in the cap of a tooth-drawer. And now forward, for we have put thee in countenance.
USER: You have put me out of countenance.
BOT: False. We have given thee faces.
USER: But you have outfaced them all.
BOT: An thou wert a lion, we would do so.
USER: Therefore, as he is an ass, let him go. And so adieu, sweet Jude. Nay, why dost thou stay?
BOT: For the latter end of his name.
USER: For the ass to the Jude? Give it him. Jud-as, away!
BOT: This is not generous, not gentle, not humble.
USER: A light for Monsieur Judas! It grows dark; he may stumble.
BOT: Alas, poor Maccabaeus, how hath he been baited!
USER: Hide thy head, Achilles. Here comes Hector in arms.
BOT: Though my mocks come home by me, I will now be merry.
USER: Hector was but a Trojan in respect of this.
BOT: But is this Hector?
USER: I think Hector was not so clean-timbered.
BOT: His leg is too big for Hector's.
USER: More calf, certain.
BOT: No, he is best endued in the small.
USER: This cannot be Hector.
BOT: He's a god or a painter, for he makes faces.
USER: _The armipotent Mars, of lances the almighty, Gave Hector a gift_ -
BOT: A gilt nutmeg.
USER: A lemon.
BOT: Stuck with cloves.
USER: No, cloven.
BOT: Peace! _The armipotent Mars, of lances the almighty, Gave Hector a gift, the heir of Ilion; A man so breathed that certain he would fight, yea, From morn till night, out of his pavilion. I am that flower_ -
USER: That mint.
BOT: That columbine.
USER: Sweet Lord Longaville, rein thy tongue.
BOT: I must rather give it the rein, for it runs against Hector.
USER: Ay, and Hector's a greyhound.
BOT: Speak, brave Hector; we are much delighted.
USER: I do adore thy sweet Grace's slipper.
BOT: Loves her by the foot.
USER: He may not by the yard.
BOT: _This Hector far surmounted Hannibal. The party is gone_ -
USER: Fellow Hector, she is gone; she is two months on her way.
BOT: What meanest thou?
USER: Faith, unless you play the honest Trojan, the poor wench is cast away. She's quick; the child brags in her belly already. 'Tis yours.
BOT: Dost thou infamonize me among potentates? Thou shalt die.
USER: Then shall Hector be whipped for Jaquenetta that is quick by him, and hanged for Pompey that is dead by him.
BOT: Most rare Pompey!
USER: Renowned Pompey!
BOT: Greater than "Great"! Great, great, great Pompey! Pompey the Huge!
USER: Hector trembles.
BOT: Pompey is moved. More Ates, more Ates! Stir them on, stir them on!
USER: Hector will challenge him.
BOT: Ay, if he have no more man's blood in his belly than will sup a flea.
USER: By the north pole, I do challenge thee.
BOT: I will not fight with a pole, like a northern man. I'll slash, I'll do it by the sword. I bepray you, let me borrow my arms again.
USER: Room for the incensed Worthies!
BOT: I'll do it in my shirt.
USER: Most resolute Pompey!
BOT: Master, let me take you a buttonhole lower. Do you not see Pompey is uncasing for the combat? What mean you? You will lose your reputation.
USER: Gentlemen and soldiers, pardon me. I will not combat in my shirt.
BOT: You may not deny it. Pompey hath made the challenge.
USER: Sweet bloods, I both may and will.
BOT: What reason have you for 't?
USER: The naked truth of it is, I have no shirt. I go woolward for penance.
BOT: True, and it was enjoined him in Rome for want of linen; since when, I'll be sworn, he wore none but a dishclout of Jaquenetta's, and that he wears next his heart for a favour. MARCADÉ. God save you, madam.
USER: Welcome, Marcadé, But that thou interruptest our merriment. MARCADÉ. I am sorry, madam, for the news I bring Is heavy in my tongue. The King your father -
BOT: Dead, for my life! MARCADÉ. Even so. My tale is told.
USER: Worthies away! The scene begins to cloud.
BOT: For mine own part, I breathe free breath. I have seen the day of wrong through the little hole of discretion, and I will right myself like a soldier.
USER: How fares your Majesty?
BOT: Boyet, prepare. I will away tonight.
USER: Madam, not so. I do beseech you stay.
BOT: I understand you not. My griefs are double.
USER: Our letters, madam, showed much more than jest.
BOT: So did our looks.
USER: We did not quote them so.
BOT: Now, at the latest minute of the hour, Grant us your loves.
USER: If this, or more than this, I would deny, To flatter up these powers of mine with rest, The sudden hand of death close up mine eye! Hence hermit, then. My heart is in thy breast.
BOT: And what to me, my love? But what to me? A wife?
USER: A beard, fair health, and honesty; With threefold love I wish you all these three.
BOT: O, shall I say, "I thank you, gentle wife"?
USER: No so, my lord. A twelvemonth and a day I'll mark no words that smooth-faced wooers say. Come when the King doth to my lady come; Then, if I have much love, I'll give you some.
BOT: I'll serve thee true and faithfully till then.
USER: Yet swear not, lest ye be forsworn again.
BOT: What says Maria?
USER: At the twelvemonth's end I'll change my black gown for a faithful friend.
BOT: I'll stay with patience, but the time is long.
USER: The liker you; few taller are so young.
BOT: Studies my lady? Mistress, look on me. Behold the window of my heart, mine eye, What humble suit attends thy answer there. Impose some service on me for thy love.
USER: To move wild laughter in the throat of death? It cannot be, it is impossible. Mirth cannot move a soul in agony.
BOT: A twelvemonth? Well, befall what will befall, I'll jest a twelvemonth in an hospital.
USER: No, madam, we will bring you on your way.
BOT: Our wooing doth not end like an old play. Jack hath not Jill. These ladies' courtesy Might well have made our sport a comedy.
USER: Come, sir, it wants a twelvemonth and a day, And then 'twill end.
BOT: That's too long for a play.
USER: Sweet Majesty, vouchsafe me -
BOT: Was not that Hector?
USER: The worthy knight of Troy.
BOT: Call them forth quickly; we will do so.
USER: Holla! Approach. This side is _Hiems_, Winter; this _Ver_, the Spring; the one maintained by the owl, th' other by the cuckoo. _Ver_, begin. The Song
BOT: The words of Mercury are harsh after the songs of Apollo. You that way, we this way. THE TRAGEDY OF MACBETH Contents

USER: When shall we three meet again? In thunder, lightning, or in rain?
BOT: When the hurlyburly's done, When the battle's lost and won.
USER: That will be ere the set of sun.
BOT: Where the place?
USER: Upon the heath.
BOT: There to meet with Macbeth.
USER: I come, Graymalkin!
BOT: Paddock calls.
USER: Anon.
BOT: Fair is foul, and foul is fair: Hover through the fog and filthy air.

USER: What bloody man is that? He can report, As seemeth by his plight, of the revolt The newest state.
BOT: This is the sergeant Who, like a good and hardy soldier, fought 'Gainst my captivity. - Hail, brave friend! Say to the King the knowledge of the broil As thou didst leave it.
USER: O valiant cousin! worthy gentleman!
BOT: Dismay'd not this Our captains, Macbeth and Banquo?
USER: So well thy words become thee as thy wounds: They smack of honour both. - Go, get him surgeons. Who comes here?
BOT: The worthy Thane of Ross.
USER: What a haste looks through his eyes! So should he look That seems to speak things strange.
BOT: God save the King!
USER: Whence cam'st thou, worthy thane?
BOT: Great happiness!
USER: That now Sweno, the Norways' king, craves composition; Nor would we deign him burial of his men Till he disbursed at Saint Colme's Inch Ten thousand dollars to our general use.
BOT: No more that Thane of Cawdor shall deceive Our bosom interest. Go pronounce his present death, And with his former title greet Macbeth.
USER: I'll see it done.
BOT: What he hath lost, noble Macbeth hath won.

USER: Where hast thou been, sister?
BOT: Killing swine.
USER: Sister, where thou?
BOT: I'll give thee a wind.
USER: Th'art kind.
BOT: And I another.
USER: Show me, show me.
BOT: Here I have a pilot's thumb, Wrack'd as homeward he did come.
USER: A drum, a drum! Macbeth doth come.
BOT: The Weird Sisters, hand in hand, Posters of the sea and land, Thus do go about, about: Thrice to thine, and thrice to mine, And thrice again, to make up nine. Peace! - the charm's wound up.
USER: So foul and fair a day I have not seen.
BOT: Speak, if you can; - what are you?
USER: All hail, Macbeth! hail to thee, Thane of Glamis!
BOT: All hail, Macbeth! hail to thee, Thane of Cawdor!
USER: All hail, Macbeth! that shalt be king hereafter!
BOT: Hail!
USER: Hail!
BOT: Hail!
USER: Lesser than Macbeth, and greater.
BOT: Not so happy, yet much happier.
USER: Thou shalt get kings, though thou be none: So all hail, Macbeth and Banquo!
BOT: Banquo and Macbeth, all hail!
USER: The earth hath bubbles, as the water has, And these are of them. Whither are they vanish'd?
BOT: Into the air; and what seem'd corporal, Melted as breath into the wind. Would they had stay'd!
USER: Were such things here as we do speak about? Or have we eaten on the insane root That takes the reason prisoner?
BOT: Your children shall be kings.
USER: You shall be king.
BOT: And Thane of Cawdor too; went it not so?
USER: To the selfsame tune and words. Who's here?
BOT: We are sent To give thee from our royal master thanks; Only to herald thee into his sight, Not pay thee.
USER: And, for an earnest of a greater honour, He bade me, from him, call thee Thane of Cawdor: In which addition, hail, most worthy thane, For it is thine.
BOT: What, can the devil speak true?
USER: The Thane of Cawdor lives: why do you dress me In borrow'd robes?
BOT: The greatest is behind. [_To Ross and Angus._] Thanks for your pains. When those that gave the Thane of Cawdor to me Promis'd no less to them?
USER: Look, how our partner's rapt.
BOT: Without my stir.
USER: New honours come upon him, Like our strange garments, cleave not to their mould But with the aid of use.
BOT: Time and the hour runs through the roughest day.
USER: Worthy Macbeth, we stay upon your leisure.
BOT: Very gladly.
USER: Till then, enough. - Come, friends.

USER: Is execution done on Cawdor? Are not Those in commission yet return'd?
BOT: There if I grow, The harvest is your own.
USER: The rest is labour, which is not us'd for you: I'll be myself the harbinger, and make joyful The hearing of my wife with your approach; So, humbly take my leave.
BOT: My worthy Cawdor!
USER: True, worthy Banquo! He is full so valiant; And in his commendations I am fed. It is a banquet to me. Let's after him, Whose care is gone before to bid us welcome: It is a peerless kinsman.

USER: The King comes here tonight.
BOT: Thou'rt mad to say it. Is not thy master with him? who, were't so, Would have inform'd for preparation.
USER: So please you, it is true. Our thane is coming. One of my fellows had the speed of him, Who, almost dead for breath, had scarcely more Than would make up his message.
BOT: My dearest love, Duncan comes here tonight.
USER: And when goes hence?
BOT: Tomorrow, as he purposes.
USER: We will speak further.
BOT: Only look up clear; To alter favour ever is to fear. Leave all the rest to me.

USER: This castle hath a pleasant seat. The air Nimbly and sweetly recommends itself Unto our gentle senses.
BOT: See, see, our honour'd hostess! - The love that follows us sometime is our trouble, Which still we thank as love. Herein I teach you How you shall bid God 'ild us for your pains, And thank us for your trouble.
USER: Your servants ever Have theirs, themselves, and what is theirs, in compt, To make their audit at your Highness' pleasure, Still to return your own.
BOT: Give me your hand; Conduct me to mine host: we love him highly, And shall continue our graces towards him. By your leave, hostess.

USER: He has almost supp'd. Why have you left the chamber?
BOT: Hath he ask'd for me?
USER: Know you not he has?
BOT: We will proceed no further in this business: He hath honour'd me of late; and I have bought Golden opinions from all sorts of people, Which would be worn now in their newest gloss, Not cast aside so soon.
USER: Pr'ythee, peace! I dare do all that may become a man; Who dares do more is none.
BOT: If we should fail?
USER: Who dares receive it other, As we shall make our griefs and clamour roar Upon his death?
BOT: I am settled, and bend up Each corporal agent to this terrible feat. Away, and mock the time with fairest show: False face must hide what the false heart doth know.

USER: How goes the night, boy?
BOT: The moon is down; I have not heard the clock.
USER: And she goes down at twelve.
BOT: I take't, 'tis later, sir.
USER: A friend.
BOT: Being unprepar'd, Our will became the servant to defect, Which else should free have wrought.
USER: All's well. I dreamt last night of the three Weird Sisters: To you they have show'd some truth.
BOT: I think not of them: Yet, when we can entreat an hour to serve, We would spend it in some words upon that business, If you would grant the time.
USER: At your kind'st leisure.
BOT: If you shall cleave to my consent, when 'tis, It shall make honour for you.
USER: So I lose none In seeking to augment it, but still keep My bosom franchis'd, and allegiance clear, I shall be counsell'd.
BOT: Good repose the while!
USER: Thanks, sir: the like to you.

USER: I have done the deed. - Didst thou not hear a noise?
BOT: I heard the owl scream and the crickets cry. Did not you speak?
USER: When?
BOT: Now.
USER: As I descended?
BOT: Ay.
USER: Hark! - Who lies i' th' second chamber?
BOT: Donalbain.
USER: This is a sorry sight.
BOT: A foolish thought, to say a sorry sight.
USER: There's one did laugh in's sleep, and one cried, "Murder!" That they did wake each other: I stood and heard them. But they did say their prayers, and address'd them Again to sleep.
BOT: There are two lodg'd together.
USER: One cried, "God bless us!" and, "Amen," the other, As they had seen me with these hangman's hands. List'ning their fear, I could not say "Amen," When they did say, "God bless us."
BOT: Consider it not so deeply.
USER: But wherefore could not I pronounce "Amen"? I had most need of blessing, and "Amen" Stuck in my throat.
BOT: These deeds must not be thought After these ways; so, it will make us mad.
USER: What do you mean?
BOT: Still it cried, "Sleep no more!" to all the house: "Glamis hath murder'd sleep, and therefore Cawdor Shall sleep no more. Macbeth shall sleep no more!"
USER: I'll go no more: I am afraid to think what I have done; Look on't again I dare not.
BOT: To know my deed, 'twere best not know myself. [_Knocking within._] Wake Duncan with thy knocking! I would thou couldst!

USER: Was it so late, friend, ere you went to bed, That you do lie so late?
BOT: Faith, sir, we were carousing till the second cock; and drink, sir, is a great provoker of three things.
USER: What three things does drink especially provoke?
BOT: I believe drink gave thee the lie last night.
USER: That it did, sir, i' the very throat on me; but I requited him for his lie; and (I think) being too strong for him, though he took up my legs sometime, yet I made a shift to cast him.
BOT: Is thy master stirring? Our knocking has awak'd him; here he comes.
USER: Good morrow, noble sir!
BOT: Good morrow, both!
USER: Is the King stirring, worthy thane?
BOT: Not yet.
USER: He did command me to call timely on him. I have almost slipp'd the hour.
BOT: I'll bring you to him.
USER: I know this is a joyful trouble to you; But yet 'tis one.
BOT: The labour we delight in physics pain. This is the door.
USER: I'll make so bold to call. For 'tis my limited service.
BOT: Goes the King hence today?
USER: He does. He did appoint so.
BOT: 'Twas a rough night.
USER: My young remembrance cannot parallel A fellow to it.
BOT: O horror, horror, horror! Tongue nor heart cannot conceive nor name thee! MACBETH, LENNOX. What's the matter?
USER: Confusion now hath made his masterpiece! Most sacrilegious murder hath broke ope The Lord's anointed temple, and stole thence The life o' th' building.
BOT: What is't you say? the life?
USER: Mean you his majesty?
BOT: What's the business, That such a hideous trumpet calls to parley The sleepers of the house? Speak, speak!
USER: O gentle lady, 'Tis not for you to hear what I can speak: The repetition, in a woman's ear, Would murder as it fell. O Banquo, Banquo! Our royal master's murder'd!
BOT: Woe, alas! What, in our house?
USER: Too cruel anywhere. - Dear Duff, I pr'ythee, contradict thyself, And say it is not so.
BOT: What is amiss?
USER: You are, and do not know't: The spring, the head, the fountain of your blood Is stopp'd; the very source of it is stopp'd.
BOT: Your royal father's murder'd.
USER: O, by whom?
BOT: O, yet I do repent me of my fury, That I did kill them.
USER: Wherefore did you so?
BOT: Help me hence, ho!
USER: Look to the lady.
BOT: Why do we hold our tongues, That most may claim this argument for ours?
USER: What should be spoken here, where our fate, Hid in an auger hole, may rush, and seize us? Let's away. Our tears are not yet brew'd.
BOT: Nor our strong sorrow Upon the foot of motion.
USER: And so do I.
BOT: So all.
USER: Let's briefly put on manly readiness, And meet i' th' hall together.
BOT: Well contented.
USER: What will you do? Let's not consort with them: To show an unfelt sorrow is an office Which the false man does easy. I'll to England.
BOT: To Ireland, I. Our separated fortune Shall keep us both the safer. Where we are, There's daggers in men's smiles: the near in blood, The nearer bloody.

USER: Threescore and ten I can remember well, Within the volume of which time I have seen Hours dreadful and things strange, but this sore night Hath trifled former knowings.
BOT: 'Tis unnatural, Even like the deed that's done. On Tuesday last, A falcon, towering in her pride of place, Was by a mousing owl hawk'd at and kill'd.
USER: 'Tis said they eat each other.
BOT: They did so; to the amazement of mine eyes, That look'd upon't. Here comes the good Macduff. How goes the world, sir, now?
USER: Why, see you not?
BOT: Is't known who did this more than bloody deed?
USER: Those that Macbeth hath slain.
BOT: Alas, the day! What good could they pretend?
USER: They were suborn'd. Malcolm and Donalbain, the King's two sons, Are stol'n away and fled; which puts upon them Suspicion of the deed.
BOT: 'Gainst nature still: Thriftless ambition, that will ravin up Thine own life's means! - Then 'tis most like The sovereignty will fall upon Macbeth.
USER: He is already nam'd; and gone to Scone To be invested.
BOT: Where is Duncan's body?
USER: Carried to Colmekill, The sacred storehouse of his predecessors, And guardian of their bones.
BOT: Will you to Scone?
USER: No, cousin, I'll to Fife.
BOT: Well, I will thither.
USER: Well, may you see things well done there. Adieu! Lest our old robes sit easier than our new!
BOT: Farewell, father.
USER: God's benison go with you; and with those That would make good of bad, and friends of foes!

USER: Here's our chief guest.
BOT: If he had been forgotten, It had been as a gap in our great feast, And all-thing unbecoming.
USER: Tonight we hold a solemn supper, sir, And I'll request your presence.
BOT: Let your Highness Command upon me, to the which my duties Are with a most indissoluble tie For ever knit.
USER: Ride you this afternoon?
BOT: Ay, my good lord.
USER: We should have else desir'd your good advice (Which still hath been both grave and prosperous) In this day's council; but we'll take tomorrow. Is't far you ride?
BOT: As far, my lord, as will fill up the time 'Twixt this and supper: go not my horse the better, I must become a borrower of the night, For a dark hour or twain.
USER: Fail not our feast.
BOT: My lord, I will not.
USER: Ay, my good lord: our time does call upon's.
