BOT: They are, my lord, without the palace gate.
USER: It was, so please your Highness.
BOT: You made it known to us.
USER: We are men, my liege.
BOT: I am one, my liege, Whom the vile blows and buffets of the world Hath so incens'd that I am reckless what I do to spite the world.
USER: And I another, So weary with disasters, tugg'd with fortune, That I would set my life on any chance, To mend it or be rid on't.
BOT: Both of you Know Banquo was your enemy.
USER: True, my lord.
BOT: We shall, my lord, Perform what you command us.
USER: Though our lives -
BOT: We are resolv'd, my lord.
USER: I'll call upon you straight: abide within. It is concluded. Banquo, thy soul's flight, If it find heaven, must find it out tonight.

USER: Is Banquo gone from court?
BOT: Ay, madam, but returns again tonight.
USER: Say to the King, I would attend his leisure For a few words.
BOT: Madam, I will.
USER: Come on, Gently my lord, sleek o'er your rugged looks; Be bright and jovial among your guests tonight.
BOT: You must leave this.
USER: O, full of scorpions is my mind, dear wife! Thou know'st that Banquo, and his Fleance, lives.
BOT: But in them nature's copy's not eterne.
USER: What's to be done?

USER: But who did bid thee join with us?
BOT: Macbeth.
USER: He needs not our mistrust; since he delivers Our offices and what we have to do To the direction just.
BOT: Then stand with us. The west yet glimmers with some streaks of day. Now spurs the lated traveller apace, To gain the timely inn; and near approaches The subject of our watch.
USER: Hark! I hear horses.
BOT: Then 'tis he; the rest That are within the note of expectation Already are i' th' court.
USER: His horses go about.
BOT: Almost a mile; but he does usually, So all men do, from hence to the palace gate Make it their walk.
USER: A light, a light!
BOT: 'Tis he.
USER: Stand to't.
BOT: It will be rain tonight.
USER: Let it come down.
BOT: O, treachery! Fly, good Fleance, fly, fly, fly! Thou mayst revenge - O slave!
USER: Who did strike out the light?
BOT: Was't not the way?
USER: There's but one down: the son is fled.
BOT: We have lost best half of our affair.
USER: Well, let's away, and say how much is done.

USER: You know your own degrees, sit down. At first And last the hearty welcome.
BOT: Thanks to your Majesty.
USER: Ourself will mingle with society, And play the humble host. Our hostess keeps her state; but, in best time, We will require her welcome.
BOT: Pronounce it for me, sir, to all our friends; For my heart speaks they are welcome.
USER: See, they encounter thee with their hearts' thanks. Both sides are even: here I'll sit i' th' midst. Be large in mirth; anon we'll drink a measure The table round. There's blood upon thy face.
BOT: 'Tis Banquo's then.
USER: 'Tis better thee without than he within. Is he dispatch'd?
BOT: My lord, his throat is cut. That I did for him.
USER: Thou art the best o' th' cut-throats; Yet he's good that did the like for Fleance: If thou didst it, thou art the nonpareil.
BOT: Most royal sir, Fleance is 'scap'd.
USER: Ay, my good lord. Safe in a ditch he bides, With twenty trenched gashes on his head; The least a death to nature.
BOT: Thanks for that. There the grown serpent lies; the worm that's fled Hath nature that in time will venom breed, No teeth for th' present. - Get thee gone; tomorrow We'll hear, ourselves, again.
USER: Sweet remembrancer! - Now, good digestion wait on appetite, And health on both!
BOT: May't please your Highness sit.
USER: Here had we now our country's honour roof'd, Were the grac'd person of our Banquo present; Who may I rather challenge for unkindness Than pity for mischance!
BOT: His absence, sir, Lays blame upon his promise. Please't your Highness To grace us with your royal company?
USER: The table's full.
BOT: Here is a place reserv'd, sir.
USER: Where?
BOT: Here, my good lord. What is't that moves your Highness?
USER: Which of you have done this?
BOT: What, my good lord?
USER: Thou canst not say I did it. Never shake Thy gory locks at me.
BOT: Gentlemen, rise; his Highness is not well.
USER: Ay, and a bold one, that dare look on that Which might appal the devil.
BOT: Pr'ythee, see there! Behold! look! lo! how say you? Why, what care I? If thou canst nod, speak too. - If charnel houses and our graves must send Those that we bury back, our monuments Shall be the maws of kites.
USER: What, quite unmann'd in folly?
BOT: If I stand here, I saw him.
USER: Fie, for shame!
BOT: My worthy lord, Your noble friends do lack you.
USER: Our duties, and the pledge.
BOT: Avaunt! and quit my sight! let the earth hide thee! Thy bones are marrowless, thy blood is cold; Thou hast no speculation in those eyes Which thou dost glare with!
USER: Think of this, good peers, But as a thing of custom: 'tis no other, Only it spoils the pleasure of the time.
BOT: You have displaced the mirth, broke the good meeting With most admir'd disorder.
USER: What sights, my lord?
BOT: I pray you, speak not; he grows worse and worse; Question enrages him. At once, good night: - Stand not upon the order of your going, But go at once.
USER: Good night; and better health Attend his Majesty!
BOT: A kind good night to all!
USER: Almost at odds with morning, which is which.
BOT: How say'st thou, that Macduff denies his person At our great bidding?
USER: Did you send to him, sir?
BOT: You lack the season of all natures, sleep.
USER: Come, we'll to sleep. My strange and self-abuse Is the initiate fear that wants hard use. We are yet but young in deed.

USER: Why, how now, Hecate? you look angerly.
BOT: Come, let's make haste; she'll soon be back again.

USER: Sent he to Macduff?
BOT: He did: and with an absolute "Sir, not I," The cloudy messenger turns me his back, And hums, as who should say, "You'll rue the time That clogs me with this answer."
USER: I'll send my prayers with him.

USER: Thrice the brinded cat hath mew'd.
BOT: Thrice, and once the hedge-pig whin'd.
USER: Harpier cries: - 'Tis time, 'tis time.
BOT: Round about the cauldron go; In the poison'd entrails throw. - Toad, that under cold stone Days and nights has thirty-one Swelter'd venom sleeping got, Boil thou first i' th' charmed pot!
USER: Double, double, toil and trouble; Fire, burn; and cauldron, bubble.
BOT: Double, double, toil and trouble; Fire, burn; and cauldron, bubble.
USER: Double, double, toil and trouble; Fire, burn; and cauldron, bubble.
BOT: Cool it with a baboon's blood. Then the charm is firm and good.
USER: O, well done! I commend your pains, And everyone shall share i' th' gains. And now about the cauldron sing, Like elves and fairies in a ring, Enchanting all that you put in.
BOT: By the pricking of my thumbs, Something wicked this way comes. Open, locks, Whoever knocks!
USER: How now, you secret, black, and midnight hags! What is't you do?
BOT: A deed without a name.
USER: Speak.
BOT: Demand.
USER: We'll answer.
BOT: Say, if thou'dst rather hear it from our mouths, Or from our masters?
USER: Call 'em, let me see 'em.
BOT: Pour in sow's blood, that hath eaten Her nine farrow; grease that's sweaten From the murderer's gibbet throw Into the flame.
USER: Come, high or low; Thyself and office deftly show!
BOT: Tell me, thou unknown power, -
USER: He knows thy thought: Hear his speech, but say thou naught.
BOT: Macbeth! Macbeth! Macbeth! Beware Macduff; Beware the Thane of Fife. - Dismiss me. - Enough.
USER: Whate'er thou art, for thy good caution, thanks; Thou hast harp'd my fear aright. - But one word more.
BOT: He will not be commanded. Here's another, More potent than the first.
USER: Macbeth! Macbeth! Macbeth!
BOT: Had I three ears, I'd hear thee.
USER: Be bloody, bold, and resolute. Laugh to scorn The power of man, for none of woman born Shall harm Macbeth.
BOT: Listen, but speak not to't.
USER: Be lion-mettled, proud, and take no care Who chafes, who frets, or where conspirers are: Macbeth shall never vanquish'd be, until Great Birnam wood to high Dunsinane hill Shall come against him.
BOT: Seek to know no more.
USER: I will be satisfied: deny me this, And an eternal curse fall on you! Let me know. Why sinks that cauldron? and what noise is this?
BOT: Show!
USER: Show!
BOT: Show!
USER: Show his eyes, and grieve his heart; Come like shadows, so depart! a glass in his hand; Banquo following._]
BOT: Where are they? Gone? - Let this pernicious hour Stand aye accursed in the calendar! - Come in, without there!
USER: What's your Grace's will?
BOT: Saw you the Weird Sisters?
USER: No, my lord.
BOT: Came they not by you?
USER: No, indeed, my lord.
BOT: Infected be the air whereon they ride; And damn'd all those that trust them! - I did hear The galloping of horse: who was't came by?
USER: 'Tis two or three, my lord, that bring you word Macduff is fled to England.
BOT: Fled to England!
USER: Ay, my good lord.

USER: What had he done, to make him fly the land?
BOT: You must have patience, madam.
USER: He had none: His flight was madness: when our actions do not, Our fears do make us traitors.
BOT: You know not Whether it was his wisdom or his fear.
USER: Father'd he is, and yet he's fatherless.
BOT: I am so much a fool, should I stay longer, It would be my disgrace and your discomfort: I take my leave at once.
USER: Sirrah, your father's dead. And what will you do now? How will you live?
BOT: As birds do, mother.
USER: What, with worms and flies?
BOT: With what I get, I mean; and so do they.
USER: Poor bird! thou'dst never fear the net nor lime, The pit-fall nor the gin.
BOT: Why should I, mother? Poor birds they are not set for. My father is not dead, for all your saying.
USER: Yes, he is dead: how wilt thou do for a father?
BOT: Nay, how will you do for a husband?
USER: Why, I can buy me twenty at any market.
BOT: Then you'll buy 'em to sell again.
USER: Thou speak'st with all thy wit; And yet, i' faith, with wit enough for thee.
BOT: Was my father a traitor, mother?
USER: Ay, that he was.
BOT: What is a traitor?
USER: Why, one that swears and lies.
BOT: And be all traitors that do so?
USER: Every one that does so is a traitor, and must be hanged.
BOT: And must they all be hanged that swear and lie?
USER: Every one.
BOT: Who must hang them?
USER: Why, the honest men.
BOT: Then the liars and swearers are fools: for there are liars and swearers enow to beat the honest men and hang up them.
USER: Now, God help thee, poor monkey! But how wilt thou do for a father?
BOT: If he were dead, you'ld weep for him: if you would not, it were a good sign that I should quickly have a new father.
USER: Poor prattler, how thou talk'st!
BOT: Where is your husband?
USER: I hope, in no place so unsanctified Where such as thou mayst find him.
BOT: He's a traitor.
USER: Thou liest, thou shag-ear'd villain!
BOT: What, you egg! Young fry of treachery!
USER: He has kill'd me, mother: Run away, I pray you! Murderers._]

USER: Let us seek out some desolate shade and there Weep our sad bosoms empty.
BOT: I am not treacherous.
USER: I have lost my hopes.
BOT: What should he be?
USER: Not in the legions Of horrid hell can come a devil more damn'd In evils to top Macbeth.
BOT: O Scotland, Scotland!
USER: If such a one be fit to govern, speak: I am as I have spoken.
BOT: Such welcome and unwelcome things at once 'Tis hard to reconcile.
USER: Well; more anon. - Comes the King forth, I pray you?
BOT: Ay, sir. There are a crew of wretched souls That stay his cure: their malady convinces The great assay of art; but at his touch, Such sanctity hath heaven given his hand, They presently amend.
USER: I thank you, doctor.
BOT: What's the disease he means?
USER: See, who comes here?
BOT: My countryman; but yet I know him not.
USER: My ever-gentle cousin, welcome hither.
BOT: I know him now. Good God, betimes remove The means that makes us strangers!
USER: Sir, amen.
BOT: Stands Scotland where it did?
USER: O, relation Too nice, and yet too true!
BOT: What's the newest grief?
USER: That of an hour's age doth hiss the speaker; Each minute teems a new one.
BOT: How does my wife?
USER: Why, well.
BOT: And all my children?
USER: Well too.
BOT: The tyrant has not batter'd at their peace?
USER: No; they were well at peace when I did leave 'em.
BOT: Be not a niggard of your speech: how goes't?
USER: Be't their comfort We are coming thither. Gracious England hath Lent us good Siward and ten thousand men; An older and a better soldier none That Christendom gives out.
BOT: Would I could answer This comfort with the like! But I have words That would be howl'd out in the desert air, Where hearing should not latch them.
USER: What concern they? The general cause? or is it a fee-grief Due to some single breast?
BOT: No mind that's honest But in it shares some woe, though the main part Pertains to you alone.
USER: If it be mine, Keep it not from me, quickly let me have it.
BOT: Let not your ears despise my tongue for ever, Which shall possess them with the heaviest sound That ever yet they heard.
USER: Humh! I guess at it.
BOT: Your castle is surpris'd; your wife and babes Savagely slaughter'd. To relate the manner Were, on the quarry of these murder'd deer, To add the death of you.
USER: Merciful heaven! - What, man! ne'er pull your hat upon your brows. Give sorrow words. The grief that does not speak Whispers the o'er-fraught heart, and bids it break.
BOT: My children too?
USER: Wife, children, servants, all That could be found.
BOT: And I must be from thence! My wife kill'd too?
USER: I have said.
BOT: Be comforted: Let's make us med'cines of our great revenge, To cure this deadly grief.
USER: He has no children. - All my pretty ones? Did you say all? - O hell-kite! - All? What, all my pretty chickens and their dam At one fell swoop?
BOT: Dispute it like a man.
USER: Be this the whetstone of your sword. Let grief Convert to anger; blunt not the heart, enrage it.

USER: I have two nights watched with you, but can perceive no truth in your report. When was it she last walked?
BOT: That, sir, which I will not report after her.
USER: You may to me; and 'tis most meet you should.
BOT: Neither to you nor anyone; having no witness to confirm my speech. Lo you, here she comes! This is her very guise; and, upon my life, fast asleep. Observe her; stand close.
USER: How came she by that light?
BOT: Why, it stood by her: she has light by her continually; 'tis her command.
USER: You see, her eyes are open.
BOT: Ay, but their sense are shut.
USER: What is it she does now? Look how she rubs her hands.
BOT: It is an accustomed action with her, to seem thus washing her hands. I have known her continue in this a quarter of an hour.
USER: Yet here's a spot.
BOT: Hark, she speaks. I will set down what comes from her, to satisfy my remembrance the more strongly.
USER: Do you mark that?
BOT: The Thane of Fife had a wife. Where is she now? - What, will these hands ne'er be clean? No more o' that, my lord, no more o' that: you mar all with this starting.
USER: Go to, go to. You have known what you should not.
BOT: She has spoke what she should not, I am sure of that: heaven knows what she has known.
USER: Here's the smell of the blood still: all the perfumes of Arabia will not sweeten this little hand. Oh, oh, oh!
BOT: What a sigh is there! The heart is sorely charged.
USER: I would not have such a heart in my bosom for the dignity of the whole body.
BOT: Well, well, well.
USER: Pray God it be, sir.
BOT: This disease is beyond my practice: yet I have known those which have walked in their sleep, who have died holily in their beds.
USER: Wash your hands, put on your nightgown; look not so pale. I tell you yet again, Banquo's buried; he cannot come out on's grave.
BOT: Even so?
USER: To bed, to bed. There's knocking at the gate. Come, come, come, come, give me your hand. What's done cannot be undone. To bed, to bed, to bed.
BOT: Will she go now to bed?
USER: Directly.
BOT: Good night, good doctor.

USER: The English power is near, led on by Malcolm, His uncle Siward, and the good Macduff. Revenges burn in them; for their dear causes Would to the bleeding and the grim alarm Excite the mortified man.
BOT: Near Birnam wood Shall we well meet them. That way are they coming.
USER: Who knows if Donalbain be with his brother?
BOT: For certain, sir, he is not. I have a file Of all the gentry: there is Siward's son And many unrough youths, that even now Protest their first of manhood.
USER: What does the tyrant?
BOT: Great Dunsinane he strongly fortifies. Some say he's mad; others, that lesser hate him, Do call it valiant fury: but, for certain, He cannot buckle his distemper'd cause Within the belt of rule.
USER: Who, then, shall blame His pester'd senses to recoil and start, When all that is within him does condemn Itself for being there?
BOT: Well, march we on, To give obedience where 'tis truly ow'd: Meet we the med'cine of the sickly weal; And with him pour we, in our country's purge, Each drop of us.
USER: Or so much as it needs To dew the sovereign flower, and drown the weeds. Make we our march towards Birnam.

USER: There is ten thousand -
BOT: Geese, villain?
USER: Soldiers, sir.
BOT: Go prick thy face and over-red thy fear, Thou lily-liver'd boy. What soldiers, patch? Death of thy soul! those linen cheeks of thine Are counsellors to fear. What soldiers, whey-face?
USER: The English force, so please you.
BOT: What's your gracious pleasure?
USER: What news more?
BOT: All is confirm'd, my lord, which was reported.
USER: I'll fight till from my bones my flesh be hack'd. Give me my armour.
BOT: 'Tis not needed yet.
USER: I'll put it on. Send out more horses, skirr the country round; Hang those that talk of fear. Give me mine armour. - How does your patient, doctor?
BOT: Not so sick, my lord, As she is troubled with thick-coming fancies, That keep her from her rest.
USER: Therein the patient Must minister to himself.
BOT: Ay, my good lord. Your royal preparation Makes us hear something.
USER: Bring it after me. - I will not be afraid of death and bane, Till Birnam forest come to Dunsinane.
BOT: Were I from Dunsinane away and clear, Profit again should hardly draw me here.

USER: Cousins, I hope the days are near at hand That chambers will be safe.
BOT: We doubt it nothing.
USER: What wood is this before us?
BOT: The wood of Birnam.
USER: Let every soldier hew him down a bough, And bear't before him. Thereby shall we shadow The numbers of our host, and make discovery Err in report of us.
BOT: It shall be done.
USER: We learn no other but the confident tyrant Keeps still in Dunsinane, and will endure Our setting down before't.
BOT: 'Tis his main hope; For where there is advantage to be given, Both more and less have given him the revolt, And none serve with him but constrained things, Whose hearts are absent too.
USER: Let our just censures Attend the true event, and put we on Industrious soldiership.

USER: It is the cry of women, my good lord.
BOT: The Queen, my lord, is dead.
USER: Gracious my lord, I should report that which I say I saw, But know not how to do't.
BOT: Well, say, sir.
USER: As I did stand my watch upon the hill, I look'd toward Birnam, and anon, methought, The wood began to move.
BOT: Liar, and slave!
USER: Let me endure your wrath, if't be not so. Within this three mile may you see it coming; I say, a moving grove.

USER: Fare you well. - Do we but find the tyrant's power tonight, Let us be beaten, if we cannot fight.
BOT: Make all our trumpets speak; give them all breath, Those clamorous harbingers of blood and death.

USER: They have tied me to a stake. I cannot fly, But, bear-like I must fight the course. - What's he That was not born of woman? Such a one Am I to fear, or none.
BOT: What is thy name?
USER: Thou'lt be afraid to hear it.
BOT: No; though thou call'st thyself a hotter name Than any is in hell.
USER: My name's Macbeth.
BOT: The devil himself could not pronounce a title More hateful to mine ear.
USER: No, nor more fearful.
BOT: Thou liest, abhorred tyrant. With my sword I'll prove the lie thou speak'st.
USER: Thou wast born of woman. But swords I smile at, weapons laugh to scorn, Brandish'd by man that's of a woman born. Alarums. Enter Macduff.
BOT: This way, my lord; - the castle's gently render'd: The tyrant's people on both sides do fight; The noble thanes do bravely in the war, The day almost itself professes yours, And little is to do.
USER: We have met with foes That strike beside us.
BOT: Enter, sir, the castle.

USER: Why should I play the Roman fool, and die On mine own sword? whiles I see lives, the gashes Do better upon them.
BOT: Turn, hell-hound, turn!
USER: Of all men else I have avoided thee: But get thee back; my soul is too much charg'd With blood of thine already.
BOT: I have no words; My voice is in my sword: thou bloodier villain Than terms can give thee out!
USER: Thou losest labour: As easy mayst thou the intrenchant air With thy keen sword impress, as make me bleed: Let fall thy blade on vulnerable crests; I bear a charmed life, which must not yield To one of woman born.
BOT: Despair thy charm; And let the angel whom thou still hast serv'd Tell thee, Macduff was from his mother's womb Untimely ripp'd.
USER: Then yield thee, coward, And live to be the show and gaze o' th' time. We'll have thee, as our rarer monsters are, Painted upon a pole, and underwrit, "Here may you see the tyrant."
BOT: I would the friends we miss were safe arriv'd.
USER: Some must go off; and yet, by these I see, So great a day as this is cheaply bought.
BOT: Macduff is missing, and your noble son.
USER: Your son, my lord, has paid a soldier's debt: He only liv'd but till he was a man; The which no sooner had his prowess confirm'd In the unshrinking station where he fought, But like a man he died.
BOT: Then he is dead?
USER: Ay, and brought off the field. Your cause of sorrow Must not be measur'd by his worth, for then It hath no end.
BOT: Had he his hurts before?
USER: Ay, on the front.
BOT: Why then, God's soldier be he! Had I as many sons as I have hairs, I would not wish them to a fairer death: And so his knell is knoll'd.
USER: He's worth more sorrow, And that I'll spend for him.
BOT: He's worth no more. They say he parted well and paid his score: And so, God be with him! - Here comes newer comfort.
USER: Hail, King of Scotland!

USER: Escalus.
BOT: My lord.
USER: If any in Vienna be of worth To undergo such ample grace and honour, It is Lord Angelo.
BOT: Look where he comes.
USER: Always obedient to your Grace's will, I come to know your pleasure.
BOT: Now, good my lord, Let there be some more test made of my metal, Before so noble and so great a figure Be stamped upon it.
USER: Yet give leave, my lord, That we may bring you something on the way.
BOT: The heavens give safety to your purposes!
USER: Lead forth and bring you back in happiness.
BOT: I thank you. Fare you well.
USER: I shall desire you, sir, to give me leave To have free speech with you; and it concerns me To look into the bottom of my place. A power I have, but of what strength and nature I am not yet instructed.
BOT: 'Tis so with me. Let us withdraw together, And we may soon our satisfaction have Touching that point.
USER: I'll wait upon your honour.

USER: If the Duke, with the other dukes, come not to composition with the King of Hungary, why then all the dukes fall upon the King.
BOT: Heaven grant us its peace, but not the King of Hungary's!
USER: Amen.
BOT: Thou conclud'st like the sanctimonious pirate that went to sea with the ten commandments, but scraped one out of the table.
USER: "Thou shalt not steal"?
BOT: Ay, that he razed.
USER: I never heard any soldier dislike it.
BOT: I believe thee; for I think thou never wast where grace was said.
USER: No? A dozen times at least.
BOT: What? In metre?
USER: In any proportion or in any language.
BOT: I think, or in any religion.
USER: Ay, why not? Grace is grace, despite of all controversy; as, for example, thou thyself art a wicked villain, despite of all grace.
BOT: Well, there went but a pair of shears between us.
USER: I grant, as there may between the lists and the velvet. Thou art the list.
BOT: And thou the velvet. Thou art good velvet; thou'rt a three-piled piece, I warrant thee. I had as lief be a list of an English kersey as be piled, as thou art piled, for a French velvet. Do I speak feelingly now?
USER: I think thou dost, and indeed, with most painful feeling of thy speech. I will, out of thine own confession, learn to begin thy health; but, whilst I live, forget to drink after thee.
BOT: I think I have done myself wrong, have I not?
USER: Yes, that thou hast, whether thou art tainted or free.
BOT: Behold, behold, where Madam Mitigation comes! I have purchased as many diseases under her roof as come to -
USER: To what, I pray?
BOT: Judge.
USER: To three thousand dolours a year.
BOT: Ay, and more.
USER: A French crown more.
BOT: Thou art always figuring diseases in me, but thou art full of error; I am sound.
USER: Nay, not, as one would say, healthy, but so sound as things that are hollow. Thy bones are hollow. Impiety has made a feast of thee.
BOT: How now, which of your hips has the most profound sciatica?
USER: Well, well! There's one yonder arrested and carried to prison was worth five thousand of you all.
BOT: Who's that, I pray thee?
USER: Marry, sir, that's Claudio, Signior Claudio.
BOT: Claudio to prison? 'Tis not so.
USER: Nay, but I know 'tis so. I saw him arrested, saw him carried away; and, which is more, within these three days his head to be chopped off.
BOT: But, after all this fooling, I would not have it so. Art thou sure of this?
USER: I am too sure of it. And it is for getting Madam Julietta with child.
BOT: Believe me, this may be. He promised to meet me two hours since, and he was ever precise in promise-keeping.
USER: Besides, you know, it draws something near to the speech we had to such a purpose.
BOT: But most of all agreeing with the proclamation.
USER: Away! Let's go learn the truth of it.
BOT: Thus, what with the war, what with the sweat, what with the gallows, and what with poverty, I am custom-shrunk. How now? What's the news with you?
USER: Yonder man is carried to prison.
BOT: Well, what has he done?
USER: A woman.
BOT: But what's his offence?
USER: Groping for trouts in a peculiar river.
BOT: What? Is there a maid with child by him?
USER: No, but there's a woman with maid by him. You have not heard of the proclamation, have you?
BOT: What proclamation, man?
USER: All houses in the suburbs of Vienna must be plucked down.
BOT: And what shall become of those in the city?
USER: They shall stand for seed. They had gone down too, but that a wise burgher put in for them.
BOT: But shall all our houses of resort in the suburbs be pulled down?
USER: To the ground, mistress.
BOT: Why, here's a change indeed in the commonwealth! What shall become of me?
USER: What's to do here, Thomas Tapster? Let's withdraw.
BOT: Here comes Signior Claudio, led by the Provost to prison. And there's Madam Juliet.
USER: Fellow, why dost thou show me thus to the world? Bear me to prison, where I am committed.
BOT: I do it not in evil disposition, But from Lord Angelo by special charge.
USER: Thus can the demi-god Authority Make us pay down for our offence by weight. The words of heaven; on whom it will, it will; On whom it will not, so; yet still 'tis just.
BOT: Why, how now, Claudio? Whence comes this restraint?
USER: If I could speak so wisely under an arrest, I would send for certain of my creditors; and yet, to say the truth, I had as lief have the foppery of freedom as the morality of imprisonment. What's thy offence, Claudio?
BOT: What but to speak of would offend again.
USER: What, is't murder?
BOT: No.
USER: Lechery?
BOT: Call it so.
USER: Away, sir; you must go.
BOT: One word, good friend. - Lucio, a word with you.
USER: A hundred, if they'll do you any good. Is lechery so looked after?
BOT: With child, perhaps?
USER: I warrant it is. And thy head stands so tickle on thy shoulders that a milkmaid, if she be in love, may sigh it off. Send after the Duke, and appeal to him.
BOT: I thank you, good friend Lucio.
USER: Within two hours.
BOT: Come, officer, away.

USER: May your Grace speak of it?
BOT: Gladly, my lord.
USER: It rested in your Grace To unloose this tied-up justice when you pleased; And it in you more dreadful would have seemed Than in Lord Angelo.

USER: And have you nuns no farther privileges?
BOT: Are not these large enough?
USER: Yes, truly; I speak not as desiring more, But rather wishing a more strict restraint Upon the sisterhood, the votarists of Saint Clare.
BOT: Who's that which calls?
USER: Peace and prosperity! Who is't that calls?
BOT: Hail, virgin, if you be, as those cheek-roses Proclaim you are no less. Can you so stead me As bring me to the sight of Isabella, A novice of this place, and the fair sister To her unhappy brother Claudio?
USER: Why "her unhappy brother"? let me ask, The rather for I now must make you know I am that Isabella, and his sister.
BOT: Gentle and fair, your brother kindly greets you. Not to be weary with you, he's in prison.
USER: Woe me! For what?
BOT: For that which, if myself might be his judge, He should receive his punishment in thanks: He hath got his friend with child.
USER: Sir, make me not your story.
BOT: You do blaspheme the good in mocking me.
USER: Someone with child by him? My cousin Juliet?
BOT: Is she your cousin?
USER: Adoptedly, as school-maids change their names By vain though apt affection.
BOT: She it is.
USER: O, let him marry her!
BOT: Doth he so Seek his life?
USER: Has censured him already; And, as I hear, the Provost hath a warrant For's execution.
BOT: Alas, what poor ability's in me To do him good?
USER: Assay the power you have.
BOT: My power? Alas, I doubt.
USER: I'll see what I can do.
BOT: But speedily.
USER: I will about it straight; No longer staying but to give the Mother Notice of my affair. I humbly thank you. Commend me to my brother. Soon at night I'll send him certain word of my success.
BOT: I take my leave of you.
USER: Good sir, adieu.

USER: We must not make a scarecrow of the law, Setting it up to fear the birds of prey, And let it keep one shape till custom make it Their perch, and not their terror.
BOT: Be it as your wisdom will.
USER: Where is the Provost?
BOT: Here, if it like your honour.
USER: See that Claudio Be executed by nine tomorrow morning. Bring him his confessor, let him be prepared, For that's the utmost of his pilgrimage.
BOT: Well, heaven forgive him; and forgive us all. Some rise by sin, and some by virtue fall. Some run from brakes of vice, and answer none, And some condemned for a fault alone.
USER: Come, bring them away. If these be good people in a commonweal that do nothing but use their abuses in common houses, I know no law. Bring them away.
BOT: How now, sir, what's your name? And what's the matter?
USER: If it please your honour, I am the poor Duke's constable, and my name is Elbow. I do lean upon justice, sir, and do bring in here before your good honour two notorious benefactors.
BOT: Benefactors? Well, what benefactors are they? Are they not malefactors?
USER: If it please your honour, I know not well what they are, but precise villains they are, that I am sure of, and void of all profanation in the world that good Christians ought to have.
BOT: This comes off well. Here's a wise officer.
USER: Go to. What quality are they of? Elbow is your name? Why dost thou not speak, Elbow?
BOT: He cannot, sir. He's out at elbow.
USER: What are you, sir?
BOT: He, sir? A tapster, sir; parcel bawd; one that serves a bad woman; whose house, sir, was, as they say, plucked down in the suburbs; and now she professes a hot-house, which, I think is a very ill house too.
USER: How know you that?
BOT: My wife, sir, whom I detest before heaven and your honour -
USER: How? Thy wife?
BOT: Ay, sir, whom I thank heaven is an honest woman -
USER: Dost thou detest her therefore?
BOT: I say, sir, I will detest myself also, as well as she, that this house, if it be not a bawd's house, it is pity of her life, for it is a naughty house.
USER: How dost thou know that, constable?
BOT: Marry, sir, by my wife, who, if she had been a woman cardinally given, might have been accused in fornication, adultery, and all uncleanliness there.
USER: By the woman's means?
BOT: Ay, sir, by Mistress Overdone's means; but as she spit in his face, so she defied him.
USER: Sir, if it please your honour, this is not so.
BOT: Prove it before these varlets here, thou honourable man, prove it.
USER: Go to, go to. No matter for the dish, sir.
BOT: No, indeed.
USER: Very well. You being then, if you be remembered, cracking the stones of the foresaid prunes -
BOT: Ay, so I did indeed.
USER: Why, very well. I telling you then, if you be remembered, that such a one and such a one were past cure of the thing you wot of, unless they kept very good diet, as I told you -
BOT: All this is true.
USER: Why, very well then -
BOT: Come, you are a tedious fool. To the purpose. What was done to Elbow's wife that he hath cause to complain of? Come me to what was done to her.
USER: Sir, your honour cannot come to that yet.
BOT: No, sir, nor I mean it not.
USER: Sir, but you shall come to it, by your honour's leave. And I beseech you, look into Master Froth here, sir, a man of fourscore pound a year; whose father died at Hallowmas - was't not at Hallowmas, Master Froth?
BOT: All-hallond Eve.
USER: Why, very well. I hope here be truths. He, sir, sitting, as I say, in a lower chair, sir - 'twas in the Bunch of Grapes, where, indeed, you have a delight to sit, have you not?
BOT: I have so, because it is an open room, and good for winter.
USER: Why, very well then. I hope here be truths.
BOT: This will last out a night in Russia When nights are longest there. I'll take my leave, And leave you to the hearing of the cause; Hoping you'll find good cause to whip them all.
USER: I think no less. Good morrow to your lordship. Now, sir, come on. What was done to Elbow's wife, once more?
BOT: Once, sir? There was nothing done to her once.
USER: I beseech you, sir, ask him what this man did to my wife.
BOT: I beseech your honour, ask me.
USER: Well, sir, what did this gentleman to her?
BOT: I beseech you, sir, look in this gentleman's face. Good Master Froth, look upon his honour; 'tis for a good purpose. - Doth your honour mark his face?
USER: Ay, sir, very well.
BOT: Nay, I beseech you, mark it well.
USER: Well, I do so.
BOT: Doth your honour see any harm in his face?
USER: Why, no.
BOT: I'll be supposed upon a book, his face is the worst thing about him. Good, then, if his face be the worst thing about him, how could Master Froth do the constable's wife any harm? I would know that of your honour.
USER: He's in the right. Constable. What say you to it?
BOT: First, an it like you, the house is a respected house; next, this is a respected fellow; and his mistress is a respected woman.
USER: By this hand, sir, his wife is a more respected person than any of us all.
BOT: Varlet, thou liest; thou liest, wicked varlet! The time is yet to come that she was ever respected with man, woman, or child.
USER: Sir, she was respected with him before he married with her.
BOT: Which is the wiser here, Justice or Iniquity? Is this true?
USER: If he took you a box o' th' ear, you might have your action of slander too.
BOT: Marry, I thank your good worship for it. What is't your worship's pleasure I shall do with this wicked caitiff?
USER: Truly, officer, because he hath some offences in him that thou wouldst discover if thou couldst, let him continue in his courses till thou know'st what they are.
BOT: Marry, I thank your worship for it. - Thou seest, thou wicked varlet, now, what's come upon thee. Thou art to continue now, thou varlet, thou art to continue.
USER: Here in Vienna, sir.
BOT: Are you of fourscore pounds a year?
USER: Yes, an't please you, sir.
BOT: So. [_To Pompey_.] What trade are you of, sir?
USER: A tapster, a poor widow's tapster.
BOT: Your mistress' name?
USER: Mistress Overdone.
BOT: Hath she had any more than one husband?
USER: Nine, sir; Overdone by the last.
BOT: Nine? - Come hither to me, Master Froth. Master Froth, I would not have you acquainted with tapsters; they will draw you, Master Froth, and you will hang them. Get you gone, and let me hear no more of you.
USER: I thank your worship. For mine own part, I never come into any room in a taphouse but I am drawn in.
BOT: Well, no more of it, Master Froth. Farewell. Come you hither to me, Master tapster. What's your name, Master tapster?
USER: Pompey.
BOT: What else?
USER: Bum, sir.
BOT: Truly, sir, I am a poor fellow that would live.
USER: How would you live, Pompey? By being a bawd? What do you think of the trade, Pompey? Is it a lawful trade?
BOT: If the law would allow it, sir.
USER: But the law will not allow it, Pompey; nor it shall not be allowed in Vienna.
BOT: Does your worship mean to geld and splay all the youth of the city?
USER: No, Pompey.
BOT: Truly, sir, in my poor opinion, they will to't then. If your worship will take order for the drabs and the knaves, you need not to fear the bawds.
USER: There is pretty orders beginning, I can tell you. It is but heading and hanging.
BOT: I thank your worship for your good counsel. [_Aside_.] But I shall follow it as the flesh and fortune shall better determine. Whip me? No, no; let carman whip his jade; The valiant heart's not whipped out of his trade.
USER: Come hither to me, Master Elbow. Come hither, Master Constable. How long have you been in this place of constable?
BOT: Seven year and a half, sir.
USER: I thought, by the readiness in the office, you had continued in it sometime. You say seven years together?
BOT: And a half, sir.
USER: Alas, it hath been great pains to you. They do you wrong to put you so oft upon't. Are there not men in your ward sufficient to serve it?
BOT: Faith, sir, few of any wit in such matters. As they are chosen, they are glad to choose me for them; I do it for some piece of money, and go through with all.
USER: Look you bring me in the names of some six or seven, the most sufficient of your parish.
BOT: To your worship's house, sir?
USER: To my house. Fare you well. What's o'clock, think you?
BOT: Eleven, sir.
USER: I pray you home to dinner with me.
BOT: I humbly thank you.
USER: It grieves me for the death of Claudio, But there's no remedy.
BOT: Lord Angelo is severe.
USER: It is but needful. Mercy is not itself that oft looks so; Pardon is still the nurse of second woe. But yet, Poor Claudio! There's no remedy. Come, sir.

USER: He's hearing of a cause. He will come straight. I'll tell him of you.
BOT: Pray you do. I'll know His pleasure, may be he will relent. Alas, He hath but as offended in a dream; All sects, all ages, smack of this vice, and he To die for 't!
USER: Now, what's the matter, Provost?
BOT: Is it your will Claudio shall die tomorrow?
USER: Did not I tell thee yea? Hadst thou not order? Why dost thou ask again?
BOT: Lest I might be too rash. Under your good correction, I have seen When, after execution, judgement hath Repented o'er his doom.
USER: Go to; let that be mine. Do you your office, or give up your place, And you shall well be spared.
BOT: I crave your honour's pardon. What shall be done, sir, with the groaning Juliet? She's very near her hour.
USER: Dispose of her To some more fitter place; and that with speed.
BOT: Here is the sister of the man condemned Desires access to you.
USER: Hath he a sister?
BOT: Ay, my good lord, a very virtuous maid, And to be shortly of a sisterhood, If not already.
USER: Well, let her be admitted. See you the fornicatress be removed; Let her have needful but not lavish means; There shall be order for it.
BOT: Stay a little while. [_To Isabella_.] You are welcome. What's your will?
USER: I am a woeful suitor to your honour, Please but your honour hear me.
BOT: Well, what's your suit?
USER: There is a vice that most I do abhor, And most desire should meet the blow of justice; For which I would not plead, but that I must; For which I must not plead, but that I am At war 'twixt will and will not.
BOT: Well, the matter?
USER: I have a brother is condemned to die; I do beseech you, let it be his fault, And not my brother.
BOT: Heaven give thee moving graces.
USER: Condemn the fault, and not the actor of it? Why, every fault's condemned ere it be done. Mine were the very cipher of a function To find the faults whose fine stands in record, And let go by the actor.
BOT: O just but severe law! I had a brother, then. Heaven keep your honour!
USER: Kneel down before him, hang upon his gown; You are too cold. If you should need a pin, You could not with more tame a tongue desire it. To him, I say.
BOT: Must he needs die?
USER: Maiden, no remedy.
BOT: Yes, I do think that you might pardon him, And neither heaven nor man grieve at the mercy.
USER: I will not do't.
BOT: But can you if you would?
USER: Look, what I will not, that I cannot do.
BOT: But might you do't, and do the world no wrong, If so your heart were touched with that remorse As mine is to him?
USER: He's sentenced, 'tis too late.
BOT: Pray you be gone.
USER: I would to heaven I had your potency, And you were Isabel! Should it then be thus? No; I would tell what 'twere to be a judge And what a prisoner.
BOT: Your brother is a forfeit of the law, And you but waste your words.
USER: Be you content, fair maid. It is the law, not I, condemns your brother. Were he my kinsman, brother, or my son, It should be thus with him. He must die tomorrow.
BOT: Ay, well said.
USER: Yet show some pity.
BOT: So you must be the first that gives this sentence, And he that suffers. O, it is excellent To have a giant's strength; but it is tyrannous To use it like a giant.
USER: That's well said.
BOT: O, to him, to him, wench! He will relent; He's coming. I perceive 't.
USER: Pray heaven she win him.
BOT: We cannot weigh our brother with ourself. Great men may jest with saints; 'tis wit in them, But in the less, foul profanation.
USER: Thou'rt i' th' right, girl; more o' that.
BOT: That in the captain's but a choleric word Which in the soldier is flat blasphemy.
USER: Art advised o' that? More on't.
BOT: Why do you put these sayings upon me?
USER: She speaks, and 'tis such sense That my sense breeds with it. [_Going_.] Fare you well.
BOT: Gentle my lord, turn back.
USER: I will bethink me. Come again tomorrow.
BOT: Hark how I'll bribe you. Good my lord, turn back.
USER: How? Bribe me?
BOT: Ay, with such gifts that heaven shall share with you.
USER: You had marred all else.
BOT: Well; come to me tomorrow.
USER: Heaven keep your honour safe.
BOT: For I am that way going to temptation, Where prayers cross.
USER: At what hour tomorrow Shall I attend your lordship?
BOT: At any time 'fore noon.
USER: Save your honour.

USER: Hail to you, Provost, so I think you are.
BOT: I am the Provost. What's your will, good friar?
USER: When must he die?
BOT: As I do think, tomorrow. And you shall be conducted.
USER: Repent you, fair one, of the sin you carry?
BOT: I do; and bear the shame most patiently.
USER: I'll teach you how you shall arraign your conscience, And try your penitence, if it be sound Or hollowly put on.
BOT: I'll gladly learn.
USER: Love you the man that wronged you?
BOT: Yes, as I love the woman that wronged him.
USER: So then it seems your most offenceful act Was mutually committed?
BOT: Mutually.
USER: Then was your sin of heavier kind than his.
BOT: I do confess it, and repent it, father.
USER: I do repent me as it is an evil, And take the shame with joy.
BOT: There rest. Your partner, as I hear, must die tomorrow, And I am going with instruction to him. Grace go with you! _Benedicite!_
USER: Must die tomorrow? O, injurious love That respites me a life, whose very comfort Is still a dying horror!
BOT: 'Tis pity of him.

USER: One Isabel, a sister, desires access to you.
BOT: I am come to know your pleasure.
USER: That you might know it, would much better please me Than to demand what 'tis. Your brother cannot live.
BOT: Even so. Heaven keep your honour.
USER: Yet may he live a while. And, it may be, As long as you or I. Yet he must die.
BOT: Under your sentence?
USER: Yea.
BOT: When, I beseech you? That in his reprieve, Longer or shorter, he may be so fitted That his soul sicken not.
USER: 'Tis set down so in heaven, but not in earth.
BOT: Say you so? Then I shall pose you quickly. Which had you rather, that the most just law Now took your brother's life; or, to redeem him, Give up your body to such sweet uncleanness As she that he hath stained?
USER: Sir, believe this: I had rather give my body than my soul.
BOT: I talk not of your soul. Our compelled sins Stand more for number than for accompt.
USER: How say you?
BOT: Please you to do't, I'll take it as a peril to my soul; It is no sin at all, but charity.
USER: Pleased you to do't at peril of your soul, Were equal poise of sin and charity.
BOT: That I do beg his life, if it be sin, Heaven let me bear it. You granting of my suit, If that be sin, I'll make it my morn prayer To have it added to the faults of mine, And nothing of your answer.
USER: Nay, but hear me. Your sense pursues not mine. Either you are ignorant, Or seem so, crafty; and that's not good.
BOT: Let me be ignorant, and in nothing good, But graciously to know I am no better.
USER: So.
BOT: And his offence is so, as it appears, Accountant to the law upon that pain.
USER: True.
BOT: Then must your brother die.
USER: And 'twere the cheaper way. Better it were a brother died at once Than that a sister, by redeeming him, Should die for ever.
BOT: Were not you then as cruel as the sentence That you have slandered so?
USER: Ignominy in ransom and free pardon Are of two houses. Lawful mercy Is nothing kin to foul redemption.
BOT: You seemed of late to make the law a tyrant, And rather proved the sliding of your brother A merriment than a vice.
USER: O, pardon me, my lord. It oft falls out, To have what we would have, we speak not what we mean. I something do excuse the thing I hate For his advantage that I dearly love.
BOT: We are all frail.
USER: Else let my brother die, If not a feodary but only he Owe and succeed by weakness.
BOT: Nay, women are frail too.
USER: I have no tongue but one. Gentle my lord, Let me intreat you speak the former language.
BOT: Plainly conceive, I love you.
USER: My brother did love Juliet, And you tell me that he shall die for 't.
BOT: He shall not, Isabel, if you give me love.
USER: I know your virtue hath a license in't, Which seems a little fouler than it is, To pluck on others.
BOT: Believe me, on mine honour, My words express my purpose.

USER: So then you hope of pardon from Lord Angelo?
BOT: The miserable have no other medicine But only hope. I have hope to live, and am prepared to die.
USER: I humbly thank you. To sue to live, I find I seek to die, And seeking death, find life. Let it come on.
BOT: Who's there? Come in. The wish deserves a welcome.
USER: Dear sir, ere long I'll visit you again.
BOT: Most holy sir, I thank you.
USER: My business is a word or two with Claudio.
BOT: And very welcome. Look, signior, here's your sister.
USER: Provost, a word with you.
BOT: As many as you please.
USER: Bring me to hear them speak, where I may be concealed.
BOT: Now, sister, what's the comfort?
USER: Is there no remedy?
BOT: None, but such remedy as, to save a head, To cleave a heart in twain.
USER: But is there any?
BOT: Yes, brother, you may live. There is a devilish mercy in the judge, If you'll implore it, that will free your life, But fetter you till death.
USER: Perpetual durance?
BOT: Ay, just; perpetual durance; a restraint, Though all the world's vastidity you had, To a determined scope.
USER: But in what nature?
BOT: In such a one as, you consenting to't, Would bark your honour from that trunk you bear, And leave you naked.
USER: Let me know the point.
BOT: Why give you me this shame? Think you I can a resolution fetch From flowery tenderness? If I must die, I will encounter darkness as a bride And hug it in mine arms.
USER: The precise Angelo?
BOT: O, 'tis the cunning livery of hell The damned'st body to invest and cover In precise guards! Dost thou think, Claudio, If I would yield him my virginity Thou mightst be freed?
USER: O heavens, it cannot be.
BOT: Yes, he would give it thee, from this rank offence, So to offend him still. This night's the time That I should do what I abhor to name, Or else thou diest tomorrow.
USER: Thou shalt not do't.
BOT: O, were it but my life, I'd throw it down for your deliverance As frankly as a pin.
USER: Thanks, dear Isabel.
BOT: Be ready, Claudio, for your death tomorrow.
USER: Yes. Has he affections in him That thus can make him bite the law by th' nose When he would force it? Sure it is no sin; Or of the deadly seven it is the least.
BOT: Which is the least?
USER: If it were damnable, he being so wise, Why would he for the momentary trick Be perdurably fined? O Isabel!
BOT: What says my brother?
USER: Death is a fearful thing.
BOT: And shamed life a hateful.
USER: Alas, alas!
BOT: Sweet sister, let me live. What sin you do to save a brother's life, Nature dispenses with the deed so far That it becomes a virtue.
USER: Nay, hear me, Isabel.
BOT: O fie, fie, fie! Thy sin's not accidental, but a trade. Mercy to thee would prove itself a bawd. 'Tis best that thou diest quickly.
USER: O, hear me, Isabella.
BOT: Vouchsafe a word, young sister, but one word.
USER: What is your will?
BOT: Might you dispense with your leisure, I would by and by have some speech with you. The satisfaction I would require is likewise your own benefit.
USER: I have no superfluous leisure, my stay must be stolen out of other affairs, but I will attend you a while.
BOT: Let me ask my sister pardon. I am so out of love with life that I will sue to be rid of it.
USER: Hold you there. Farewell. Provost, a word with you.
BOT: What's your will, father?
USER: That, now you are come, you will be gone. Leave me a while with the maid; my mind promises with my habit no loss shall touch her by my company.
BOT: In good time.
USER: Let me hear you speak farther. I have spirit to do anything that appears not foul in the truth of my spirit.
BOT: Virtue is bold, and goodness never fearful. Have you not heard speak of Mariana, the sister of Frederick, the great soldier who miscarried at sea?
USER: I have heard of the lady, and good words went with her name.
BOT: Can this be so? Did Angelo so leave her?
USER: What a merit were it in death to take this poor maid from the world! What corruption in this life, that it will let this man live! But how out of this can she avail?
BOT: It is a rupture that you may easily heal, and the cure of it not only saves your brother, but keeps you from dishonour in doing it.
USER: Show me how, good father.
BOT: The image of it gives me content already, and I trust it will grow to a most prosperous perfection.
USER: I thank you for this comfort. Fare you well, good father.

USER: Nay, if there be no remedy for it, but that you will needs buy and sell men and women like beasts, we shall have all the world drink brown and white bastard.
BOT: O heavens, what stuff is here?
USER: Come your way, sir. - Bless you, good father friar.
BOT: And you, good brother father. What offence hath this man made you, sir?
USER: Marry, sir, he hath offended the law; and, sir, we take him to be a thief too, sir; for we have found upon him, sir, a strange picklock, which we have sent to the deputy.
BOT: Indeed, it does stink in some sort, sir. But yet, sir, I would prove -
USER: Nay, if the devil have given thee proofs for sin, Thou wilt prove his. Take him to prison, officer. Correction and instruction must both work Ere this rude beast will profit.
BOT: He must before the deputy, sir; he has given him warning. The deputy cannot abide a whoremaster. If he be a whoremonger and comes before him, he were as good go a mile on his errand.
USER: That we were all, as some would seem to be, Free from our faults, as faults from seeming, free!
BOT: His neck will come to your waist - a cord, sir.
USER: I spy comfort, I cry bail! Here's a gentleman, and a friend of mine.
BOT: Still thus, and thus; still worse!
USER: How doth my dear morsel, thy mistress? Procures she still, ha?
BOT: Troth, sir, she hath eaten up all her beef, and she is herself in the tub.
USER: Why, 'tis good. It is the right of it. It must be so. Ever your fresh whore and your powdered bawd; an unshunned consequence; it must be so. Art going to prison, Pompey?
BOT: Yes, faith, sir.
USER: Why, 'tis not amiss, Pompey. Farewell. Go, say I sent thee thither. For debt, Pompey? Or how?
BOT: For being a bawd, for being a bawd.
USER: I hope, sir, your good worship will be my bail.
BOT: No, indeed, will I not, Pompey; it is not the wear. I will pray, Pompey, to increase your bondage. If you take it not patiently, why, your mettle is the more. Adieu, trusty Pompey. - Bless you, friar.
USER: And you.
BOT: Does Bridget paint still, Pompey, ha?
USER: Come your ways, sir, come.
BOT: You will not bail me then, sir?
USER: Then, Pompey, nor now. - What news abroad, friar? What news?
BOT: Come your ways, sir, come.
USER: Go to kennel, Pompey, go. What news, friar, of the Duke?
BOT: I know none. Can you tell me of any?
USER: Some say he is with the Emperor of Russia; other some, he is in Rome. But where is he, think you?
BOT: I know not where, but wheresoever, I wish him well.
USER: It was a mad fantastical trick of him to steal from the state and usurp the beggary he was never born to. Lord Angelo dukes it well in his absence. He puts transgression to't.
BOT: He does well in't.
USER: A little more lenity to lechery would do no harm in him. Something too crabbed that way, friar.
BOT: It is too general a vice, and severity must cure it.
USER: How should he be made, then?
BOT: You are pleasant, sir, and speak apace.
USER: I never heard the absent Duke much detected for women; he was not inclined that way.
BOT: O, sir, you are deceived.
USER: 'Tis not possible.
BOT: Who, not the Duke? Yes, your beggar of fifty; and his use was to put a ducat in her clack-dish. The Duke had crotchets in him. He would be drunk too, that let me inform you.
USER: You do him wrong, surely.
BOT: Sir, I was an inward of his. A shy fellow was the Duke; and I believe I know the cause of his withdrawing.
USER: What, I prithee, might be the cause?
BOT: No, pardon. 'Tis a secret must be locked within the teeth and the lips. But this I can let you understand: the greater file of the subject held the Duke to be wise.
USER: Wise? Why, no question but he was.
BOT: A very superficial, ignorant, unweighing fellow.
USER: Sir, I know him, and I love him.
BOT: Love talks with better knowledge, and knowledge with dearer love.
USER: Come, sir, I know what I know.
BOT: Sir, my name is Lucio, well known to the Duke.
USER: He shall know you better, sir, if I may live to report you.
BOT: I fear you not.
USER: O, you hope the Duke will return no more; or you imagine me too unhurtful an opposite. But indeed, I can do you little harm. You'll forswear this again.
BOT: I'll be hanged first! Thou art deceived in me, friar. But no more of this. Canst thou tell if Claudio die tomorrow or no?
USER: Why should he die, sir?
BOT: No might nor greatness in mortality Can censure 'scape. Back-wounding calumny The whitest virtue strikes. What king so strong Can tie the gall up in the slanderous tongue? But who comes here?
USER: Go, away with her to prison.
BOT: Good my lord, be good to me. Your honour is accounted a merciful man, good my lord.
USER: Double and treble admonition, and still forfeit in the same kind? This would make mercy swear and play the tyrant.
BOT: A bawd of eleven years' continuance, may it please your honour.
USER: So please you, this friar hath been with him, and advised him for th' entertainment of death.
BOT: Good even, good father.
USER: Bliss and goodness on you!
BOT: Of whence are you?
USER: Not of this country, though my chance is now To use it for my time. I am a brother Of gracious order, late come from the See In special business from his Holiness.
BOT: What news abroad i' th' world?
USER: One that, above all other strifes, contended especially to know himself.
BOT: What pleasure was he given to?
USER: If his own life answer the straitness of his proceeding, it shall become him well; wherein if he chance to fail, he hath sentenced himself.
BOT: I am going to visit the prisoner. Fare you well.

USER: 'Tis good; though music oft hath such a charm To make bad good and good provoke to harm. I pray you tell me, hath anybody inquired for me here today? Much upon this time have I promised here to meet.
BOT: You have not been inquired after. I have sat here all day.
USER: I do constantly believe you. The time is come even now. I shall crave your forbearance a little. Maybe I will call upon you anon for some advantage to yourself.
BOT: I am always bound to you.
USER: Very well met, and welcome. What is the news from this good deputy?
BOT: But shall you on your knowledge find this way?
USER: I have ta'en a due and wary note upon't; With whispering and most guilty diligence, In action all of precept, he did show me The way twice o'er.
BOT: Are there no other tokens Between you 'greed concerning her observance?
USER: 'Tis well borne up. I have not yet made known to Mariana A word of this. - What ho, within! Come forth. I pray you be acquainted with this maid; She comes to do you good.
BOT: I do desire the like.
USER: Do you persuade yourself that I respect you?
BOT: Good friar, I know you do, and have found it.
USER: Take, then, this your companion by the hand, Who hath a story ready for your ear. I shall attend your leisure; but make haste. The vaporous night approaches.
BOT: Will't please you walk aside?
USER: She'll take the enterprise upon her, father, If you advise it.
BOT: It is not my consent, But my entreaty too.
USER: Little have you to say When you depart from him, but, soft and low, "Remember now my brother."
BOT: Fear me not.

USER: Come hither, sirrah. Can you cut off a man's head?
BOT: If the man be a bachelor, sir, I can; but if he be a married man, he's his wife's head, and I can never cut off a woman's head.
USER: Sir, I have been an unlawful bawd time out of mind, but yet I will be content to be a lawful hangman. I would be glad to receive some instruction from my fellow-partner.
BOT: What ho, Abhorson! Where's Abhorson, there?
USER: Do you call, sir?
BOT: A bawd, sir? Fie upon him, he will discredit our mystery.
USER: Go to, sir; you weigh equally. A feather will turn the scale.
BOT: Pray, sir, by your good favour - for surely, sir, a good favour you have, but that you have a hanging look - do you call, sir, your occupation a mystery?
USER: Ay, sir, a mystery.
BOT: Sir, it is a mystery.
USER: Proof.
BOT: Are you agreed?
USER: Sir, I will serve him; for I do find your hangman is a more penitent trade than your bawd. He doth oftener ask forgiveness.
BOT: You, sirrah, provide your block and your axe tomorrow four o'clock.
USER: Come on, bawd. I will instruct thee in my trade. Follow.
BOT: I do desire to learn, sir; and I hope, if you have occasion to use me for your own turn, you shall find me yare. For truly, sir, for your kindness I owe you a good turn.
USER: As fast locked up in sleep as guiltless labour When it lies starkly in the traveller's bones. He will not wake.
BOT: The best and wholesom'st spirits of the night Envelop you, good Provost! Who called here of late?
USER: None, since the curfew rung.
BOT: Not Isabel?
USER: No.
BOT: They will then, ere't be long.
USER: What comfort is for Claudio?
BOT: There's some in hope.
USER: It is a bitter deputy.
BOT: There he must stay until the officer Arise to let him in. He is called up.
USER: Have you no countermand for Claudio yet, But he must die tomorrow?
BOT: None, sir, none.
USER: As near the dawning, Provost, as it is, You shall hear more ere morning.
BOT: And here comes Claudio's pardon.
USER: My lord hath sent you this note, and by me this further charge: that you swerve not from the smallest article of it, neither in time, matter, or other circumstance. Good morrow; for, as I take it, it is almost day.
BOT: I shall obey him.
USER: I told you: Lord Angelo, belike thinking me remiss in mine office, awakens me with this unwonted putting-on; methinks strangely, for he hath not used it before.
BOT: Pray you, let's hear.
USER: What is that Barnardine who is to be executed in th' afternoon?
BOT: A Bohemian born, but here nursed up and bred; one that is a prisoner nine years old.
USER: How came it that the absent Duke had not either delivered him to his liberty, or executed him? I have heard it was ever his manner to do so.
BOT: His friends still wrought reprieves for him; and indeed, his fact till now in the government of Lord Angelo, came not to an undoubtful proof.
USER: It is now apparent?
BOT: Most manifest, and not denied by himself.
USER: Hath he borne himself penitently in prison? How seems he to be touched?
BOT: A man that apprehends death no more dreadfully but as a drunken sleep; careless, reckless, and fearless of what's past, present, or to come; insensible of mortality and desperately mortal.
USER: He wants advice.
BOT: Pray, sir, in what?
USER: In the delaying death.
BOT: Alack, how may I do it? Having the hour limited, and an express command, under penalty, to deliver his head in the view of Angelo? I may make my case as Claudio's, to cross this in the smallest.
USER: By the vow of mine order, I warrant you, if my instructions may be your guide. Let this Barnardine be this morning executed, and his head borne to Angelo.
BOT: Angelo hath seen them both, and will discover the favour.
USER: Pardon me, good father; it is against my oath.
BOT: Were you sworn to the Duke, or to the Deputy?
USER: To him and to his substitutes.
BOT: You will think you have made no offence if the Duke avouch the justice of your dealing?
USER: But what likelihood is in that?
BOT: I know them both.

USER: Sirrah, bring Barnardine hither.
BOT: Master Barnardine! You must rise and be hanged, Master Barnardine.
USER: What ho, Barnardine!
BOT: you?
USER: Your friends, sir; the hangman. You must be so good, sir, to rise and be put to death.
BOT: Tell him he must awake, and that quickly too.
USER: Pray, Master Barnardine, awake till you are executed, and sleep afterwards.
BOT: Go in to him, and fetch him out.
USER: He is coming, sir, he is coming. I hear his straw rustle.
BOT: Is the axe upon the block, sirrah?
USER: Very ready, sir.
BOT: How now, Abhorson? What's the news with you?
USER: Truly, sir, I would desire you to clap into your prayers; for, look you, the warrant's come.
BOT: You rogue, I have been drinking all night; I am not fitted for't.
USER: O, the better, sir; for he that drinks all night and is hanged betimes in the morning may sleep the sounder all the next day.
BOT: Look you, sir, here comes your ghostly father. Do we jest now, think you?
USER: Sir, induced by my charity, and hearing how hastily you are to depart, I am come to advise you, comfort you, and pray with you.
BOT: Friar, not I. I have been drinking hard all night, and I will have more time to prepare me, or they shall beat out my brains with billets. I will not consent to die this day, that's certain.
USER: O, sir, you must; and therefore I beseech you Look forward on the journey you shall go.
BOT: I swear I will not die today for any man's persuasion.
USER: But hear you -
BOT: Not a word. If you have anything to say to me, come to my ward, for thence will not I today.
USER: Unfit to live or die. O gravel heart! After him, fellows; bring him to the block.
BOT: Now, sir, how do you find the prisoner?
USER: A creature unprepared, unmeet for death; And to transport him in the mind he is Were damnable.
BOT: O, 'tis an accident that heaven provides! Dispatch it presently; the hour draws on Prefixed by Angelo. See this be done, And sent according to command, whiles I Persuade this rude wretch willingly to die.
USER: This shall be done, good father, presently. But Barnardine must die this afternoon; And how shall we continue Claudio, To save me from the danger that might come If he were known alive?
BOT: Let this be done: Put them in secret holds, both Barnardine and Claudio. Ere twice the sun hath made his journal greeting To yonder generation, you shall find Your safety manifested.
USER: I am your free dependant.
BOT: Here is the head; I'll carry it myself.
USER: Convenient is it. Make a swift return; For I would commune with you of such things That want no ear but yours.
BOT: I'll make all speed.
USER: The tongue of Isabel. She's come to know If yet her brother's pardon be come hither. But I will keep her ignorant of her good, To make her heavenly comforts of despair When it is least expected.
BOT: Ho, by your leave!
USER: Good morning to you, fair and gracious daughter.
BOT: The better, given me by so holy a man. Hath yet the Deputy sent my brother's pardon?
USER: He hath released him, Isabel, from the world. His head is off, and sent to Angelo.
BOT: Nay, but it is not so.
USER: It is no other. Show your wisdom, daughter, in your close patience.
BOT: O, I will to him and pluck out his eyes!
USER: You shall not be admitted to his sight.
BOT: Unhappy Claudio! Wretched Isabel! Injurious world! Most damned Angelo!
USER: I am directed by you.
BOT: Good even. Friar, where is the Provost?
USER: Not within, sir.
BOT: Sir, the Duke is marvellous little beholding to your reports; but the best is, he lives not in them.
USER: Friar, thou knowest not the Duke so well as I do. He's a better woodman than thou tak'st him for.
BOT: Well, you'll answer this one day. Fare ye well.
USER: Nay, tarry, I'll go along with thee. I can tell thee pretty tales of the Duke.
BOT: You have told me too many of him already, sir, if they be true; if not true, none were enough.
USER: I was once before him for getting a wench with child.
BOT: Did you such a thing?
USER: Yes, marry, did I; but I was fain to forswear it. They would else have married me to the rotten medlar.
BOT: Sir, your company is fairer than honest. Rest you well.
USER: By my troth, I'll go with thee to the lane's end. If bawdy talk offend you, we'll have very little of it. Nay, friar, I am a kind of burr; I shall stick.

USER: Every letter he hath writ hath disvouched other.
BOT: In most uneven and distracted manner. His actions show much like to madness; pray heaven his wisdom be not tainted. And why meet him at the gates and redeliver our authorities there?
USER: I guess not.
BOT: And why should we proclaim it in an hour before his entering, that if any crave redress of injustice, they should exhibit their petitions in the street?
USER: He shows his reason for that: to have a dispatch of complaints, and to deliver us from devices hereafter, which shall then have no power to stand against us.
BOT: Well, I beseech you, let it be proclaimed. Betimes i' th' morn I'll call you at your house. Give notice to such men of sort and suit As are to meet him.
USER: I shall, sir. Fare you well.

USER: It shall be speeded well.
BOT: I thank thee, Varrius, thou hast made good haste. Come, we will walk. There's other of our friends Will greet us here anon. My gentle Varrius.

USER: To speak so indirectly I am loath; I would say the truth, but to accuse him so That is your part; yet I am advised to do it, He says, to veil full purpose.
BOT: Be ruled by him.
USER: Besides, he tells me that, if peradventure He speak against me on the adverse side, I should not think it strange, for 'tis a physic That's bitter to sweet end.
BOT: I would Friar Peter -
USER: O, peace, the friar is come.

USER: My very worthy cousin, fairly met. Our old and faithful friend, we are glad to see you. ANGELO and ESCALUS. Happy return be to your royal grace!
BOT: Many and hearty thankings to you both. We have made inquiry of you, and we hear Such goodness of your justice that our soul Cannot but yield you forth to public thanks, Forerunning more requital.
USER: You make my bonds still greater.
BOT: Now is your time. Speak loud, and kneel before him.
USER: Relate your wrongs. In what? By whom? Be brief. Here is Lord Angelo shall give you justice. Reveal yourself to him.
BOT: O worthy Duke, You bid me seek redemption of the devil. Hear me yourself, for that which I must speak Must either punish me, not being believed, Or wring redress from you. Hear me, O hear me, here!
USER: My lord, her wits, I fear me, are not firm. She hath been a suitor to me for her brother, Cut off by course of justice.
BOT: By course of justice!
USER: And she will speak most bitterly and strange.
BOT: Nay, it is ten times strange.
USER: It is not truer he is Angelo Than this is all as true as it is strange. Nay, it is ten times true, for truth is truth To th' end of reckoning.
BOT: Away with her. Poor soul, She speaks this in th' infirmity of sense.
USER: By mine honesty, If she be mad, as I believe no other, Her madness hath the oddest frame of sense, Such a dependency of thing on thing, As e'er I heard in madness.
BOT: O gracious Duke, Harp not on that; nor do not banish reason For inequality; but let your reason serve To make the truth appear where it seems hid, And hide the false seems true.
USER: Many that are not mad Have, sure, more lack of reason. What would you say?
BOT: I am the sister of one Claudio, Condemned upon the act of fornication To lose his head; condemned by Angelo. I, in probation of a sisterhood, Was sent to by my brother; one Lucio As then the messenger.
USER: That's I, an't like your Grace. I came to her from Claudio and desired her To try her gracious fortune with Lord Angelo For her poor brother's pardon.
BOT: That's he, indeed.
USER: You were not bid to speak.
BOT: No, my good lord, Nor wished to hold my peace.
USER: I wish you now, then; Pray you take note of it; and when you have A business for yourself, pray heaven you then Be perfect.
BOT: I warrant your honour.
USER: The warrant's for yourself. Take heed to it.
BOT: This gentleman told somewhat of my tale.
USER: Right.
BOT: It may be right, but you are i' the wrong To speak before your time. - Proceed.
USER: I went To this pernicious caitiff deputy.
BOT: That's somewhat madly spoken.
USER: Pardon it; The phrase is to the matter.
BOT: Mended again. The matter; proceed.
USER: This is most likely!
BOT: O, that it were as like as it is true!
USER: I know you'd fain be gone. An officer! To prison with her! Shall we thus permit A blasting and a scandalous breath to fall On him so near us? This needs must be a practice. Who knew of your intent and coming hither?
BOT: One that I would were here, Friar Lodowick.
USER: A ghostly father, belike. Who knows that Lodowick?
BOT: My lord, I know him. 'Tis a meddling friar. I do not like the man. Had he been lay, my lord, For certain words he spake against your Grace In your retirement, I had swinged him soundly.
USER: Words against me? This's a good friar, belike. And to set on this wretched woman here Against our substitute! Let this friar be found.
BOT: But yesternight, my lord, she and that friar, I saw them at the prison. A saucy friar, A very scurvy fellow.
USER: We did believe no less. Know you that Friar Lodowick that she speaks of?
BOT: I know him for a man divine and holy, Not scurvy, nor a temporary meddler, As he's reported by this gentleman; And, on my trust, a man that never yet Did, as he vouches, misreport your Grace.
USER: My lord, most villainously; believe it.
BOT: Pardon, my lord; I will not show my face Until my husband bid me.
USER: What, are you married?
BOT: No, my lord.
USER: Are you a maid?
BOT: No, my lord.
USER: A widow, then?
BOT: Neither, my lord.
USER: Why, you are nothing then, neither maid, widow, nor wife?
BOT: My lord, she may be a punk; for many of them are neither maid, widow, nor wife.
USER: Silence that fellow. I would he had some cause to prattle for himself.
BOT: Well, my lord.
USER: My lord, I do confess I ne'er was married, And I confess besides, I am no maid. I have known my husband; yet my husband Knows not that ever he knew me.
BOT: He was drunk, then, my lord; it can be no better.
USER: For the benefit of silence, would thou wert so too.
BOT: Well, my lord.
USER: This is no witness for Lord Angelo.
BOT: Now I come to't, my lord. She that accuses him of fornication In self-same manner doth accuse my husband, And charges him, my lord, with such a time When I'll depose I had him in mine arms With all th' effect of love.
USER: Charges she more than me?
BOT: Not that I know.
USER: No? You say your husband.
BOT: Why, just, my lord, and that is Angelo, Who thinks he knows that he ne'er knew my body, But knows, he thinks, that he knows Isabel's.
USER: This is a strange abuse. Let's see thy face.
BOT: Know you this woman?
USER: Carnally, she says.
BOT: Sirrah, no more.
USER: Enough, my lord.
BOT: Would he were here, my lord; for he indeed Hath set the women on to this complaint. Your Provost knows the place where he abides, And he may fetch him.
USER: My lord, we'll do it throughly. Signior Lucio, did not you say you knew that Friar Lodowick to be a dishonest person?
BOT: _Cucullus non facit monachum_, honest in nothing but in his clothes, and one that hath spoke most villainous speeches of the Duke.
USER: We shall entreat you to abide here till he come, and enforce them against him. We shall find this friar a notable fellow.
BOT: As any in Vienna, on my word.
USER: Call that same Isabel here once again. I would speak with her. Pray you, my lord, give me leave to question; you shall see how I'll handle her.
BOT: Not better than he, by her own report.
USER: Say you?
BOT: Marry, sir, I think, if you handled her privately, she would sooner confess; perchance, publicly, she'll be ashamed. Officers.
USER: I will go darkly to work with her.
BOT: That's the way; for women are light at midnight.
USER: that you have said.
BOT: My lord, here comes the rascal I spoke of, here with the Provost.
USER: In very good time. Speak not you to him till we call upon you.
BOT: Mum.
USER: Come, sir, did you set these women on to slander Lord Angelo? They have confessed you did.
BOT: 'Tis false.
USER: How! Know you where you are?
BOT: Respect to your great place; and let the devil Be sometime honoured for his burning throne. Where is the Duke? 'Tis he should hear me speak.
USER: The Duke's in us; and we will hear you speak. Look you speak justly.
BOT: This is the rascal; this is he I spoke of.
USER: Slander to the state! Away with him to prison!
BOT: What can you vouch against him, Signior Lucio? Is this the man that you did tell us of?
USER: 'Tis he, my lord. Come hither, goodman Baldpate. Do you know me?
BOT: I remember you, sir, by the sound of your voice. I met you at the prison, in the absence of the Duke.
USER: O did you so? And do you remember what you said of the Duke?
BOT: Most notedly, sir.
USER: Do you so, sir? And was the Duke a fleshmonger, a fool, and a coward, as you then reported him to be?
BOT: You must, sir, change persons with me ere you make that my report. You indeed spoke so of him, and much more, much worse.
USER: O thou damnable fellow! Did not I pluck thee by the nose for thy speeches?
BOT: I protest I love the Duke as I love myself.
USER: Hark how the villain would close now, after his treasonable abuses!
BOT: Stay, sir, stay a while.
USER: What, resists he? Help him, Lucio.
BOT: Come, sir, come, sir, come, sir. Foh, sir! Why, you bald-pated lying rascal! You must be hooded, must you? Show your knave's visage, with a pox to you! Show your sheep-biting face, and be hanged an hour! Will't not off?
USER: Thou art the first knave that e'er mad'st a duke. First, Provost, let me bail these gentle three. Must have a word anon. - Lay hold on him.
BOT: This may prove worse than hanging.
USER: We'll borrow place of him. [_To Angelo_.] Sir, by your leave. Hast thou or word, or wit, or impudence, That yet can do thee office? If thou hast, Rely upon it till my tale be heard, And hold no longer out.
BOT: Come hither, Mariana. Say, wast thou e'er contracted to this woman?
USER: I was, my lord.
BOT: Go, take her hence and marry her instantly. Do you the office, friar; which consummate, Return him here again. - Go with him, Provost.
USER: My lord, I am more amazed at his dishonour Than at the strangeness of it.
BOT: Come hither, Isabel. Your friar is now your prince. As I was then Advertising and holy to your business, Not changing heart with habit, I am still Attorneyed at your service.
USER: O, give me pardon, That I, your vassal, have employed and pained Your unknown sovereignty.
BOT: I do, my lord.
USER: O my most gracious lord, I hope you will not mock me with a husband.
BOT: O my dear lord, I crave no other, nor no better man.
USER: Never crave him; we are definitive.
BOT: You do but lose your labour. Away with him to death. [_To Lucio_.] Now, sir, to you.
USER: O my good lord. - Sweet Isabel, take my part; Lend me your knees, and all my life to come I'll lend you all my life to do you service.
BOT: Against all sense you do importune her. Should she kneel down in mercy of this fact, Her brother's ghost his paved bed would break, And take her hence in horror.
USER: He dies for Claudio's death.
BOT: Merely, my lord.
USER: Your suit's unprofitable. Stand up, I say. I have bethought me of another fault. Provost, how came it Claudio was beheaded At an unusual hour?
BOT: It was commanded so.
USER: Had you a special warrant for the deed?
BOT: No, my good lord, it was by private message.
USER: For which I do discharge you of your office. Give up your keys.
BOT: Pardon me, noble lord. I thought it was a fault, but knew it not; Yet did repent me after more advice. For testimony whereof, one in the prison That should by private order else have died, I have reserved alive.
USER: What's he?
BOT: His name is Barnardine.
USER: I would thou hadst done so by Claudio. Go fetch him hither, let me look upon him.
BOT: I am sorry one so learned and so wise As you, Lord Angelo, have still appeared, Should slip so grossly, both in the heat of blood And lack of tempered judgement afterward.
USER: I am sorry that such sorrow I procure, And so deep sticks it in my penitent heart That I crave death more willingly than mercy; 'Tis my deserving, and I do entreat it.
BOT: Which is that Barnardine?
USER: This, my lord.
BOT: This is another prisoner that I saved, Who should have died when Claudio lost his head; As like almost to Claudio as himself.
USER: Faith, my lord, I spoke it but according to the trick. If you will hang me for it, you may, but I had rather it would please you I might be whipped.
BOT: I beseech your Highness, do not marry me to a whore. Your highness said even now I made you a duke; good my lord, do not recompense me in making me a cuckold.
USER: Upon mine honour, thou shalt marry her. Thy slanders I forgive, and therewithal Remit thy other forfeits. - Take him to prison, And see our pleasure herein executed.
BOT: Marrying a punk, my lord, is pressing to death, whipping, and hanging.

USER: Believe me, no. I thank my fortune for it, My ventures are not in one bottom trusted, Nor to one place; nor is my whole estate Upon the fortune of this present year. Therefore my merchandise makes me not sad.
BOT: Why then you are in love.
USER: Fie, fie!
BOT: Here comes Bassanio, your most noble kinsman, Gratiano, and Lorenzo. Fare ye well. We leave you now with better company.
USER: I would have stay'd till I had made you merry, If worthier friends had not prevented me.
BOT: Your worth is very dear in my regard. I take it your own business calls on you, And you embrace th' occasion to depart.
USER: Good morrow, my good lords.
BOT: Good signiors both, when shall we laugh? Say, when? You grow exceeding strange. Must it be so?
USER: We'll make our leisures to attend on yours.
