BOT: My Lord Bassanio, since you have found Antonio, We two will leave you, but at dinner-time I pray you have in mind where we must meet.
USER: I will not fail you.
BOT: You look not well, Signior Antonio, You have too much respect upon the world. They lose it that do buy it with much care. Believe me, you are marvellously chang'd.
USER: I hold the world but as the world, Gratiano, A stage, where every man must play a part, And mine a sad one.
BOT: Well, we will leave you then till dinner-time. I must be one of these same dumb wise men, For Gratiano never lets me speak.
USER: Well, keep me company but two years moe, Thou shalt not know the sound of thine own tongue.
BOT: Fare you well. I'll grow a talker for this gear.
USER: Thanks, i' faith, for silence is only commendable In a neat's tongue dried, and a maid not vendible.
BOT: Is that anything now?
USER: Well, tell me now what lady is the same To whom you swore a secret pilgrimage, That you today promis'd to tell me of?
BOT: I pray you, good Bassanio, let me know it; And if it stand, as you yourself still do, Within the eye of honour, be assur'd My purse, my person, my extremest means Lie all unlock'd to your occasions.

USER: By my troth, Nerissa, my little body is aweary of this great world.
BOT: Good sentences, and well pronounc'd.
USER: They would be better if well followed.
BOT: I pray thee over-name them, and as thou namest them, I will describe them, and according to my description level at my affection.
USER: First, there is the Neapolitan prince.
BOT: Then is there the County Palatine.
USER: How say you by the French lord, Monsieur Le Bon?
BOT: What say you then to Falconbridge, the young baron of England?
USER: What think you of the Scottish lord, his neighbour?
BOT: That he hath a neighbourly charity in him, for he borrowed a box of the ear of the Englishman, and swore he would pay him again when he was able. I think the Frenchman became his surety, and seal'd under for another.
USER: How like you the young German, the Duke of Saxony's nephew?
BOT: If he should offer to choose, and choose the right casket, you should refuse to perform your father's will, if you should refuse to accept him.
USER: Do you not remember, lady, in your father's time, a Venetian, a scholar and a soldier, that came hither in company of the Marquis of Montferrat?
BOT: Yes, yes, it was Bassanio, as I think, so was he call'd.
USER: True, madam. He, of all the men that ever my foolish eyes look'd upon, was the best deserving a fair lady.
BOT: I remember him well, and I remember him worthy of thy praise. How now! what news?
USER: The four strangers seek for you, madam, to take their leave. And there is a forerunner come from a fifth, the Prince of Morocco, who brings word the Prince his master will be here tonight.

USER: Three thousand ducats, well.
BOT: Ay, sir, for three months.
USER: For three months, well.
BOT: For the which, as I told you, Antonio shall be bound.
USER: Antonio shall become bound, well.
BOT: May you stead me? Will you pleasure me? Shall I know your answer?
USER: Three thousand ducats for three months, and Antonio bound.
BOT: Your answer to that.
USER: Antonio is a good man.
BOT: Have you heard any imputation to the contrary?
USER: Be assured you may.
BOT: I will be assured I may. And that I may be assured, I will bethink me. May I speak with Antonio?
USER: If it please you to dine with us.
BOT: This is Signior Antonio.
USER: Shylock, do you hear?
BOT: Shylock, albeit I neither lend nor borrow By taking nor by giving of excess, Yet to supply the ripe wants of my friend, I'll break a custom. [_To Bassanio._] Is he yet possess'd How much ye would?
USER: Ay, ay, three thousand ducats.
BOT: And for three months.
USER: I had forgot, three months, you told me so. Well then, your bond. And let me see, but hear you, Methought you said you neither lend nor borrow Upon advantage.
BOT: I do never use it.
USER: When Jacob graz'd his uncle Laban's sheep, - This Jacob from our holy Abram was As his wise mother wrought in his behalf, The third possessor; ay, he was the third.
BOT: And what of him? Did he take interest?
USER: I cannot tell; I make it breed as fast. But note me, signior.
BOT: Three thousand ducats, 'tis a good round sum. Three months from twelve, then let me see the rate.
USER: Well, Shylock, shall we be beholding to you?
BOT: This were kindness.
USER: Content, in faith, I'll seal to such a bond, And say there is much kindness in the Jew.
BOT: You shall not seal to such a bond for me, I'll rather dwell in my necessity.
USER: Why, fear not, man, I will not forfeit it, Within these two months, that's a month before This bond expires, I do expect return Of thrice three times the value of this bond.
BOT: Yes, Shylock, I will seal unto this bond.
USER: Hie thee, gentle Jew. This Hebrew will turn Christian; he grows kind.
BOT: I like not fair terms and a villain's mind.
USER: Come on; in this there can be no dismay; My ships come home a month before the day.

USER: You must take your chance, And either not attempt to choose at all, Or swear before you choose, if you choose wrong Never to speak to lady afterward In way of marriage. Therefore be advis'd.
BOT: Nor will not. Come, bring me unto my chance.
USER: First, forward to the temple. After dinner Your hazard shall be made.
BOT: Good fortune then, To make me blest or cursed'st among men!

USER: Master young man, you, I pray you; which is the way to Master Jew's?
BOT: than sand-blind, high-gravel blind, knows me not. I will try confusions with him.
USER: Master young gentleman, I pray you, which is the way to Master Jew's?
BOT: Turn up on your right hand at the next turning, but at the next turning of all on your left; marry, at the very next turning, turn of no hand, but turn down indirectly to the Jew's house.
USER: Be God's sonties, 'twill be a hard way to hit. Can you tell me whether one Launcelet, that dwells with him, dwell with him or no?
BOT: Talk you of young Master Launcelet? [_Aside._] Mark me now, now will I raise the waters. Talk you of young Master Launcelet?
USER: No master, sir, but a poor man's son, his father, though I say't, is an honest exceeding poor man, and, God be thanked, well to live.
BOT: Well, let his father be what he will, we talk of young Master Launcelet.
USER: Your worship's friend, and Launcelet, sir.
BOT: But I pray you, _ergo_, old man, _ergo_, I beseech you, talk you of young Master Launcelet?
USER: Of Launcelet, an't please your mastership.
BOT: Marry, God forbid! The boy was the very staff of my age, my very prop.
USER: Do you know me, father?
BOT: Alack the day! I know you not, young gentleman, but I pray you tell me, is my boy, God rest his soul, alive or dead?
USER: Do you not know me, father?
BOT: Alack, sir, I am sand-blind, I know you not.
USER: Pray you, sir, stand up, I am sure you are not Launcelet my boy.
BOT: Pray you, let's have no more fooling about it, but give me your blessing. I am Launcelet, your boy that was, your son that is, your child that shall be.
USER: I cannot think you are my son.
BOT: I know not what I shall think of that; but I am Launcelet, the Jew's man, and I am sure Margery your wife is my mother.
USER: It should seem, then, that Dobbin's tail grows backward. I am sure he had more hair on his tail than I have on my face when I last saw him.
BOT: Lord, how art thou changed! How dost thou and thy master agree? I have brought him a present. How 'gree you now?
USER: You may do so, but let it be so hasted that supper be ready at the farthest by five of the clock. See these letters delivered, put the liveries to making, and desire Gratiano to come anon to my lodging.
BOT: To him, father.
USER: God bless your worship!
BOT: Gramercy, wouldst thou aught with me?
USER: Here's my son, sir, a poor boy.
BOT: Not a poor boy, sir, but the rich Jew's man, that would, sir, as my father shall specify.
USER: He hath a great infection, sir, as one would say, to serve.
BOT: Indeed the short and the long is, I serve the Jew, and have a desire, as my father shall specify.
USER: His master and he (saving your worship's reverence) are scarce cater-cousins.
BOT: To be brief, the very truth is that the Jew, having done me wrong, doth cause me, as my father, being I hope an old man, shall frutify unto you.
USER: I have here a dish of doves that I would bestow upon your worship, and my suit is -
BOT: In very brief, the suit is impertinent to myself, as your worship shall know by this honest old man, and though I say it, though old man, yet poor man, my father.
USER: One speak for both. What would you?
BOT: Serve you, sir.
USER: That is the very defect of the matter, sir.
BOT: I know thee well; thou hast obtain'd thy suit. Shylock thy master spoke with me this day, And hath preferr'd thee, if it be preferment To leave a rich Jew's service to become The follower of so poor a gentleman.
USER: The old proverb is very well parted between my master Shylock and you, sir: you have "the grace of God", sir, and he hath "enough".
BOT: Thou speak'st it well. Go, father, with thy son. Take leave of thy old master, and inquire My lodging out. [_To a Servant._] Give him a livery More guarded than his fellows'; see it done.
USER: I pray thee, good Leonardo, think on this. These things being bought and orderly bestow'd, Return in haste, for I do feast tonight My best esteem'd acquaintance; hie thee, go.
BOT: My best endeavours shall be done herein.
USER: Where's your master?
BOT: Yonder, sir, he walks.
USER: Signior Bassanio!
BOT: Gratiano!
USER: I have suit to you.
BOT: You have obtain'd it.
USER: You must not deny me, I must go with you to Belmont.
BOT: Well, we shall see your bearing.
USER: Nay, but I bar tonight, you shall not gauge me By what we do tonight.
BOT: No, that were pity. I would entreat you rather to put on Your boldest suit of mirth, for we have friends That purpose merriment. But fare you well, I have some business.
USER: And I must to Lorenzo and the rest, But we will visit you at supper-time.

USER: Nay, we will slink away in supper-time, Disguise us at my lodging, and return All in an hour.
BOT: We have not made good preparation.
USER: We have not spoke us yet of torch-bearers.
BOT: 'Tis vile, unless it may be quaintly order'd, And better in my mind not undertook.
USER: 'Tis now but four o'clock, we have two hours To furnish us. Friend Launcelet, what's the news?
BOT: And it shall please you to break up this, it shall seem to signify.
USER: I know the hand, in faith 'tis a fair hand, And whiter than the paper it writ on Is the fair hand that writ.
BOT: Love news, in faith.
USER: By your leave, sir.
BOT: Whither goest thou?
USER: Marry, sir, to bid my old master the Jew to sup tonight with my new master the Christian.
BOT: Hold here, take this. Tell gentle Jessica I will not fail her, speak it privately. Go, gentlemen, Will you prepare you for this masque tonight? I am provided of a torch-bearer.
USER: Ay, marry, I'll be gone about it straight.
BOT: And so will I.
USER: Meet me and Gratiano At Gratiano's lodging some hour hence.
BOT: 'Tis good we do so.
USER: Was not that letter from fair Jessica?

USER: Why, Jessica!
BOT: Who bids thee call? I do not bid thee call.
USER: Your worship was wont to tell me I could do nothing without bidding.
BOT: Call you? What is your will?
USER: I beseech you, sir, go. My young master doth expect your reproach.
BOT: So do I his.
USER: I will go before, sir. Mistress, look out at window for all this. There will come a Christian by Will be worth a Jewess' eye.
BOT: What says that fool of Hagar's offspring, ha?
USER: His words were "Farewell, mistress," nothing else.
BOT: Farewell, and if my fortune be not crost, I have a father, you a daughter, lost.

USER: This is the penthouse under which Lorenzo Desired us to make stand.
BOT: His hour is almost past.
USER: And it is marvel he out-dwells his hour, For lovers ever run before the clock.
BOT: O ten times faster Venus' pigeons fly To seal love's bonds new-made than they are wont To keep obliged faith unforfeited!
USER: Here comes Lorenzo, more of this hereafter.
BOT: Who are you? Tell me, for more certainty, Albeit I'll swear that I do know your tongue.
USER: Lorenzo, and thy love.
BOT: Lorenzo certain, and my love indeed, For who love I so much? And now who knows But you, Lorenzo, whether I am yours?
USER: Heaven and thy thoughts are witness that thou art.
BOT: Descend, for you must be my torch-bearer.
USER: What! must I hold a candle to my shames? They in themselves, good sooth, are too too light. Why, 'tis an office of discovery, love, And I should be obscur'd.
BOT: So are you, sweet, Even in the lovely garnish of a boy. But come at once, For the close night doth play the runaway, And we are stay'd for at Bassanio's feast.
USER: I will make fast the doors, and gild myself With some moe ducats, and be with you straight.
BOT: Now, by my hood, a gentle, and no Jew.
USER: Who's there?
BOT: Signior Antonio!
USER: Fie, fie, Gratiano! where are all the rest? 'Tis nine o'clock, our friends all stay for you. No masque tonight, the wind is come about; Bassanio presently will go aboard. I have sent twenty out to seek for you.
BOT: I am glad on't. I desire no more delight Than to be under sail and gone tonight.

USER: Go, draw aside the curtains and discover The several caskets to this noble prince. Now make your choice.
BOT: The one of them contains my picture, prince. If you choose that, then I am yours withal.
USER: There, take it, prince, and if my form lie there, Then I am yours.
BOT: A gentle riddance. Draw the curtains, go. Let all of his complexion choose me so.

USER: Why, man, I saw Bassanio under sail; With him is Gratiano gone along; And in their ship I am sure Lorenzo is not.
BOT: The villain Jew with outcries rais'd the Duke, Who went with him to search Bassanio's ship.
USER: Why, all the boys in Venice follow him, Crying, his stones, his daughter, and his ducats.
BOT: Let good Antonio look he keep his day Or he shall pay for this.
USER: You were best to tell Antonio what you hear, Yet do not suddenly, for it may grieve him.
BOT: I think he only loves the world for him. I pray thee, let us go and find him out And quicken his embraced heaviness With some delight or other.
USER: Do we so.

USER: Quick, quick, I pray thee, draw the curtain straight. The Prince of Arragon hath ta'en his oath, And comes to his election presently. Flourish of cornets. Enter the Prince of Arragon, his train, and Portia.
BOT: To these injunctions everyone doth swear That comes to hazard for my worthless self.
USER: Too long a pause for that which you find there.
BOT: To offend and judge are distinct offices, And of opposed natures.
USER: Thus hath the candle sing'd the moth. O, these deliberate fools! When they do choose, They have the wisdom by their wit to lose.
BOT: The ancient saying is no heresy: Hanging and wiving goes by destiny.
USER: Come, draw the curtain, Nerissa.
BOT: Where is my lady?
USER: Here. What would my lord?
BOT: No more, I pray thee. I am half afeard Thou wilt say anon he is some kin to thee, Thou spend'st such high-day wit in praising him. Come, come, Nerissa, for I long to see Quick Cupid's post that comes so mannerly.
USER: Bassanio, Lord Love, if thy will it be!

USER: Now, what news on the Rialto?
BOT: Come, the full stop.
USER: Ha, what sayest thou? Why, the end is, he hath lost a ship.
BOT: I would it might prove the end of his losses.
USER: Let me say "amen" betimes, lest the devil cross my prayer, for here he comes in the likeness of a Jew. How now, Shylock, what news among the merchants?
BOT: You knew, none so well, none so well as you, of my daughter's flight.
USER: That's certain, I, for my part, knew the tailor that made the wings she flew withal.
BOT: And Shylock, for his own part, knew the bird was fledged; and then it is the complexion of them all to leave the dam.
USER: She is damn'd for it.
BOT: That's certain, if the devil may be her judge.
USER: My own flesh and blood to rebel!
BOT: Out upon it, old carrion! Rebels it at these years?
USER: I say my daughter is my flesh and my blood.
BOT: Why, I am sure if he forfeit, thou wilt not take his flesh! What's that good for?
USER: Gentlemen, my master Antonio is at his house, and desires to speak with you both.
BOT: We have been up and down to seek him.
USER: Here comes another of the tribe; a third cannot be match'd, unless the devil himself turn Jew.
BOT: How now, Tubal, what news from Genoa? Hast thou found my daughter?
USER: I often came where I did hear of her, but cannot find her.
BOT: Yes, other men have ill luck too. Antonio, as I heard in Genoa -
USER: What, what, what? Ill luck, ill luck?
BOT: - hath an argosy cast away coming from Tripolis.
USER: I thank God! I thank God! Is it true, is it true?
BOT: I spoke with some of the sailors that escaped the wrack.
USER: I thank thee, good Tubal. Good news, good news! Ha, ha, heard in Genoa?
BOT: Your daughter spent in Genoa, as I heard, one night, fourscore ducats.
USER: Thou stick'st a dagger in me. I shall never see my gold again. Fourscore ducats at a sitting! Fourscore ducats!
BOT: There came divers of Antonio's creditors in my company to Venice that swear he cannot choose but break.
USER: I am very glad of it. I'll plague him, I'll torture him. I am glad of it.
BOT: One of them showed me a ring that he had of your daughter for a monkey.
USER: Out upon her! Thou torturest me, Tubal. It was my turquoise, I had it of Leah when I was a bachelor. I would not have given it for a wilderness of monkeys.
BOT: But Antonio is certainly undone.

USER: Let me choose, For as I am, I live upon the rack.
BOT: Upon the rack, Bassanio! Then confess What treason there is mingled with your love.
USER: None but that ugly treason of mistrust, Which makes me fear th' enjoying of my love. There may as well be amity and life 'Tween snow and fire as treason and my love.
BOT: Ay, but I fear you speak upon the rack Where men enforced do speak anything.
USER: Promise me life, and I'll confess the truth.
BOT: Well then, confess and live.
USER: "Confess and love" Had been the very sum of my confession: O happy torment, when my torturer Doth teach me answers for deliverance! But let me to my fortune and the caskets.
BOT: _Ding, dong, bell._
USER: My lord and lady, it is now our time, That have stood by and seen our wishes prosper, To cry, good joy. Good joy, my lord and lady!
BOT: With all my heart, so thou canst get a wife.
USER: Is this true, Nerissa?
BOT: Madam, it is, so you stand pleas'd withal.
USER: And do you, Gratiano, mean good faith?
BOT: Yes, faith, my lord.
USER: Our feast shall be much honoured in your marriage.
BOT: We'll play with them the first boy for a thousand ducats.
USER: What! and stake down?
BOT: No, we shall ne'er win at that sport and stake down. But who comes here? Lorenzo and his infidel? What, and my old Venetian friend, Salerio!
USER: Lorenzo and Salerio, welcome hither, If that the youth of my new int'rest here Have power to bid you welcome. By your leave, I bid my very friends and countrymen, Sweet Portia, welcome.
BOT: So do I, my lord, They are entirely welcome.
USER: I thank your honour. For my part, my lord, My purpose was not to have seen you here, But meeting with Salerio by the way, He did entreat me, past all saying nay, To come with him along.
BOT: I did, my lord, And I have reason for it. Signior Antonio Commends him to you.
USER: Ere I ope his letter, I pray you tell me how my good friend doth.
BOT: Not sick, my lord, unless it be in mind, Nor well, unless in mind. His letter there Will show you his estate.
USER: I would you had won the fleece that he hath lost.
BOT: Is it your dear friend that is thus in trouble?
USER: The dearest friend to me, the kindest man, The best condition'd and unwearied spirit In doing courtesies, and one in whom The ancient Roman honour more appears Than any that draws breath in Italy.
BOT: What sum owes he the Jew?
USER: For me three thousand ducats.
BOT: O love, dispatch all business and be gone!
USER: Since I have your good leave to go away, I will make haste; but, till I come again, No bed shall e'er be guilty of my stay, Nor rest be interposer 'twixt us twain.

USER: Gaoler, look to him. Tell not me of mercy. This is the fool that lent out money gratis. Gaoler, look to him.
BOT: Hear me yet, good Shylock.
USER: I pray thee hear me speak.
BOT: It is the most impenetrable cur That ever kept with men.
USER: Let him alone. I'll follow him no more with bootless prayers. He seeks my life, his reason well I know: I oft deliver'd from his forfeitures Many that have at times made moan to me. Therefore he hates me.
BOT: I am sure the Duke Will never grant this forfeiture to hold.

USER: Madam, with all my heart I shall obey you in all fair commands.
BOT: My people do already know my mind, And will acknowledge you and Jessica In place of Lord Bassanio and myself. So fare you well till we shall meet again.
USER: Fair thoughts and happy hours attend on you!
BOT: I wish your ladyship all heart's content.
USER: Madam, I go with all convenient speed.
BOT: Come on, Nerissa, I have work in hand That you yet know not of; we'll see our husbands Before they think of us.
USER: Shall they see us?
BOT: Why, shall we turn to men?

USER: And what hope is that, I pray thee?
BOT: Marry, you may partly hope that your father got you not, that you are not the Jew's daughter.
USER: That were a kind of bastard hope indeed; so the sins of my mother should be visited upon me.
BOT: Truly then I fear you are damn'd both by father and mother; thus when I shun Scylla your father, I fall into Charybdis your mother. Well, you are gone both ways.
USER: I shall be saved by my husband. He hath made me a Christian.
BOT: I'll tell my husband, Launcelet, what you say. Here he comes.
USER: I shall grow jealous of you shortly, Launcelet, if you thus get my wife into corners!
BOT: I shall answer that better to the commonwealth than you can the getting up of the negro's belly! The Moor is with child by you, Launcelet.
USER: It is much that the Moor should be more than reason; but if she be less than an honest woman, she is indeed more than I took her for.
BOT: How every fool can play upon the word! I think the best grace of wit will shortly turn into silence, and discourse grow commendable in none only but parrots. Go in, sirrah; bid them prepare for dinner.
USER: That is done, sir, they have all stomachs.
BOT: Goodly Lord, what a wit-snapper are you! Then bid them prepare dinner.
USER: That is done too, sir, only "cover" is the word.
BOT: Will you cover, then, sir?
USER: Not so, sir, neither. I know my duty.
BOT: For the table, sir, it shall be served in; for the meat, sir, it shall be covered; for your coming in to dinner, sir, why, let it be as humours and conceits shall govern.
USER: Even such a husband Hast thou of me as she is for a wife.
BOT: Nay, but ask my opinion too of that.
USER: I will anon. First let us go to dinner.
BOT: Nay, let me praise you while I have a stomach.
USER: No pray thee, let it serve for table-talk. Then howsome'er thou speak'st, 'mong other things I shall digest it.
BOT: Well, I'll set you forth.

USER: What, is Antonio here?
BOT: Ready, so please your Grace.
USER: I am sorry for thee, thou art come to answer A stony adversary, an inhuman wretch, Uncapable of pity, void and empty From any dram of mercy.
BOT: Go one and call the Jew into the court.
USER: He is ready at the door. He comes, my lord.
BOT: This is no answer, thou unfeeling man, To excuse the current of thy cruelty.
USER: I am not bound to please thee with my answer.
BOT: Do all men kill the things they do not love?
USER: Hates any man the thing he would not kill?
BOT: Every offence is not a hate at first.
USER: What, wouldst thou have a serpent sting thee twice?
BOT: For thy three thousand ducats here is six.
USER: If every ducat in six thousand ducats Were in six parts, and every part a ducat, I would not draw them, I would have my bond.
BOT: How shalt thou hope for mercy, rend'ring none?
USER: Upon my power I may dismiss this court, Unless Bellario, a learned doctor, Whom I have sent for to determine this, Come here today.
BOT: My lord, here stays without A messenger with letters from the doctor, New come from Padua.
USER: Bring us the letters. Call the messenger.
BOT: Good cheer, Antonio! What, man, courage yet! The Jew shall have my flesh, blood, bones, and all, Ere thou shalt lose for me one drop of blood.
USER: I am a tainted wether of the flock, Meetest for death, the weakest kind of fruit Drops earliest to the ground, and so let me. You cannot better be employ'd, Bassanio, Than to live still, and write mine epitaph.
BOT: Came you from Padua, from Bellario?
USER: From both, my lord. Bellario greets your Grace.
BOT: Why dost thou whet thy knife so earnestly?
USER: To cut the forfeiture from that bankrupt there.
BOT: Not on thy sole but on thy soul, harsh Jew, Thou mak'st thy knife keen. But no metal can, No, not the hangman's axe, bear half the keenness Of thy sharp envy. Can no prayers pierce thee?
USER: No, none that thou hast wit enough to make.
BOT: Till thou canst rail the seal from off my bond, Thou but offend'st thy lungs to speak so loud. Repair thy wit, good youth, or it will fall To cureless ruin. I stand here for law.
USER: This letter from Bellario doth commend A young and learned doctor to our court. Where is he?
BOT: He attendeth here hard by, To know your answer, whether you'll admit him.
USER: I did, my lord.
BOT: You are welcome. Take your place. Are you acquainted with the difference That holds this present question in the court?
USER: I am informed throughly of the cause. Which is the merchant here? And which the Jew?
BOT: Antonio and old Shylock, both stand forth.
USER: Is your name Shylock?
BOT: Shylock is my name.
USER: Of a strange nature is the suit you follow, Yet in such rule that the Venetian law Cannot impugn you as you do proceed.
BOT: Ay, so he says.
USER: Do you confess the bond?
BOT: I do.
USER: Then must the Jew be merciful.
BOT: On what compulsion must I? Tell me that.
USER: My deeds upon my head! I crave the law, The penalty and forfeit of my bond.
BOT: Is he not able to discharge the money?
USER: It must not be, there is no power in Venice Can alter a decree established; 'Twill be recorded for a precedent, And many an error by the same example Will rush into the state. It cannot be.
BOT: A Daniel come to judgment! Yea, a Daniel! O wise young judge, how I do honour thee!
USER: I pray you let me look upon the bond.
BOT: Here 'tis, most reverend doctor, here it is.
USER: Shylock, there's thrice thy money offered thee.
BOT: An oath, an oath! I have an oath in heaven. Shall I lay perjury upon my soul? No, not for Venice.
USER: Why, this bond is forfeit, And lawfully by this the Jew may claim A pound of flesh, to be by him cut off Nearest the merchant's heart. Be merciful, Take thrice thy money; bid me tear the bond.
BOT: Most heartily I do beseech the court To give the judgment.
USER: Why then, thus it is: You must prepare your bosom for his knife.
BOT: O noble judge! O excellent young man!
USER: For the intent and purpose of the law Hath full relation to the penalty, Which here appeareth due upon the bond.
BOT: 'Tis very true. O wise and upright judge, How much more elder art thou than thy looks!
USER: Therefore lay bare your bosom.
BOT: Ay, his breast So says the bond, doth it not, noble judge? "Nearest his heart": those are the very words.
USER: It is so. Are there balance here to weigh The flesh?
BOT: I have them ready.
USER: Have by some surgeon, Shylock, on your charge, To stop his wounds, lest he do bleed to death.
BOT: Is it so nominated in the bond?
USER: It is not so express'd, but what of that? 'Twere good you do so much for charity.
BOT: I cannot find it; 'tis not in the bond.
USER: You, merchant, have you anything to say?
BOT: Your wife would give you little thanks for that If she were by to hear you make the offer.
USER: I have a wife who I protest I love. I would she were in heaven, so she could Entreat some power to change this currish Jew.
BOT: 'Tis well you offer it behind her back, The wish would make else an unquiet house.
USER: These be the Christian husbands! I have a daughter - Would any of the stock of Barabbas Had been her husband, rather than a Christian! We trifle time, I pray thee, pursue sentence.
BOT: A pound of that same merchant's flesh is thine, The court awards it and the law doth give it.
USER: Most rightful judge!
BOT: And you must cut this flesh from off his breast. The law allows it and the court awards it.
USER: Most learned judge! A sentence! Come, prepare.
BOT: O upright judge! Mark, Jew. O learned judge!
USER: Is that the law?
BOT: Thyself shalt see the act. For, as thou urgest justice, be assur'd Thou shalt have justice more than thou desir'st.
USER: O learned judge! Mark, Jew, a learned judge!
BOT: I take this offer then. Pay the bond thrice And let the Christian go.
USER: Here is the money.
BOT: Soft! The Jew shall have all justice. Soft! no haste! He shall have nothing but the penalty.
USER: O Jew, an upright judge, a learned judge!
BOT: A second Daniel, a Daniel, Jew! Now, infidel, I have you on the hip.
USER: Why doth the Jew pause? Take thy forfeiture.
BOT: Give me my principal, and let me go.
USER: I have it ready for thee. Here it is.
BOT: He hath refus'd it in the open court, He shall have merely justice and his bond.
USER: A Daniel still say I, a second Daniel! I thank thee, Jew, for teaching me that word.
BOT: Shall I not have barely my principal?
USER: Thou shalt have nothing but the forfeiture To be so taken at thy peril, Jew.
BOT: Why, then the devil give him good of it! I'll stay no longer question.
USER: Beg that thou mayst have leave to hang thyself, And yet, thy wealth being forfeit to the state, Thou hast not left the value of a cord; Therefore thou must be hang'd at the state's charge.
BOT: That thou shalt see the difference of our spirit, I pardon thee thy life before thou ask it. For half thy wealth, it is Antonio's; The other half comes to the general state, Which humbleness may drive unto a fine.
USER: Ay, for the state, not for Antonio.
BOT: Nay, take my life and all, pardon not that. You take my house when you do take the prop That doth sustain my house; you take my life When you do take the means whereby I live.
USER: What mercy can you render him, Antonio?
BOT: A halter gratis, nothing else, for God's sake!
USER: He shall do this, or else I do recant The pardon that I late pronounced here.
BOT: Art thou contented, Jew? What dost thou say?
USER: I am content.
BOT: Clerk, draw a deed of gift.
USER: I pray you give me leave to go from hence; I am not well; send the deed after me And I will sign it.
BOT: Get thee gone, but do it.
USER: In christ'ning shalt thou have two god-fathers. Had I been judge, thou shouldst have had ten more, To bring thee to the gallows, not to the font.
BOT: Sir, I entreat you home with me to dinner.
USER: I humbly do desire your Grace of pardon, I must away this night toward Padua, And it is meet I presently set forth.
BOT: I am sorry that your leisure serves you not. Antonio, gratify this gentleman, For in my mind you are much bound to him.
USER: Most worthy gentleman, I and my friend Have by your wisdom been this day acquitted Of grievous penalties, in lieu whereof, Three thousand ducats due unto the Jew We freely cope your courteous pains withal.
BOT: And stand indebted, over and above In love and service to you evermore.
USER: Dear sir, of force I must attempt you further. Take some remembrance of us as a tribute, Not as fee. Grant me two things, I pray you, Not to deny me, and to pardon me.
BOT: You press me far, and therefore I will yield. Do not draw back your hand; I'll take no more, And you in love shall not deny me this.
USER: This ring, good sir? Alas, it is a trifle, I will not shame myself to give you this.
BOT: I will have nothing else but only this, And now methinks I have a mind to it.
USER: There's more depends on this than on the value. The dearest ring in Venice will I give you, And find it out by proclamation, Only for this I pray you pardon me.
BOT: I see, sir, you are liberal in offers. You taught me first to beg, and now methinks You teach me how a beggar should be answer'd.
USER: Good sir, this ring was given me by my wife, And when she put it on, she made me vow That I should neither sell, nor give, nor lose it.
BOT: That 'scuse serves many men to save their gifts. And if your wife be not a mad-woman, And know how well I have deserv'd this ring, She would not hold out enemy for ever For giving it to me. Well, peace be with you!
USER: My Lord Bassanio, let him have the ring. Let his deservings and my love withal Be valued 'gainst your wife's commandment.

USER: Inquire the Jew's house out, give him this deed, And let him sign it, we'll away tonight, And be a day before our husbands home. This deed will be well welcome to Lorenzo.
BOT: Fair sir, you are well o'erta'en. My Lord Bassanio upon more advice, Hath sent you here this ring, and doth entreat Your company at dinner.
USER: That cannot be; His ring I do accept most thankfully, And so I pray you tell him. Furthermore, I pray you show my youth old Shylock's house.
BOT: That will I do.
USER: Sir, I would speak with you. I'll see if I can get my husband's ring, Which I did make him swear to keep for ever.
BOT: That they did give the rings away to men; But we'll outface them, and outswear them too. Away! make haste! Thou know'st where I will tarry.
USER: Come, good sir, will you show me to this house?

USER: In such a night Did Thisbe fearfully o'ertrip the dew, And saw the lion's shadow ere himself, And ran dismay'd away.
BOT: In such a night Stood Dido with a willow in her hand Upon the wild sea-banks, and waft her love To come again to Carthage.
USER: In such a night Medea gathered the enchanted herbs That did renew old Æson.
BOT: In such a night Did Jessica steal from the wealthy Jew, And with an unthrift love did run from Venice As far as Belmont.
USER: In such a night Did young Lorenzo swear he loved her well, Stealing her soul with many vows of faith, And ne'er a true one.
BOT: In such a night Did pretty Jessica, like a little shrew, Slander her love, and he forgave it her.
USER: I would out-night you did no body come; But hark, I hear the footing of a man.
BOT: Who comes so fast in silence of the night?
USER: A friend.
BOT: A friend! What friend? Your name, I pray you, friend?
USER: Stephano is my name, and I bring word My mistress will before the break of day Be here at Belmont. She doth stray about By holy crosses where she kneels and prays For happy wedlock hours.
BOT: Who comes with her?
USER: None but a holy hermit and her maid. I pray you is my master yet return'd?
BOT: He is not, nor we have not heard from him. But go we in, I pray thee, Jessica, And ceremoniously let us prepare Some welcome for the mistress of the house. LAUNCELET. Sola, sola! wo ha, ho! sola, sola!
USER: Who calls?
BOT: Sola! Did you see Master Lorenzo? Master Lorenzo! Sola, sola!
USER: Leave holloaing, man. Here!
BOT: Sola! Where, where?
USER: Here!
BOT: Tell him there's a post come from my master with his horn full of good news. My master will be here ere morning.
USER: I am never merry when I hear sweet music.
BOT: That light we see is burning in my hall. How far that little candle throws his beams! So shines a good deed in a naughty world.
USER: When the moon shone we did not see the candle.
BOT: So doth the greater glory dim the less. A substitute shines brightly as a king Until a king be by, and then his state Empties itself, as doth an inland brook Into the main of waters. Music! hark!
USER: It is your music, madam, of the house.
BOT: Nothing is good, I see, without respect. Methinks it sounds much sweeter than by day.
USER: Silence bestows that virtue on it, madam.
BOT: That is the voice, Or I am much deceiv'd, of Portia.
USER: He knows me as the blind man knows the cuckoo, By the bad voice.
BOT: Dear lady, welcome home.
USER: We have been praying for our husbands' welfare, Which speed, we hope, the better for our words. Are they return'd?
BOT: Madam, they are not yet; But there is come a messenger before To signify their coming.
USER: Go in, Nerissa. Give order to my servants, that they take No note at all of our being absent hence, Nor you, Lorenzo; Jessica, nor you.
BOT: Your husband is at hand, I hear his trumpet. We are no tell-tales, madam, fear you not.
USER: This night methinks is but the daylight sick, It looks a little paler. 'Tis a day Such as the day is when the sun is hid.
BOT: We should hold day with the Antipodes, If you would walk in absence of the sun.
USER: Let me give light, but let me not be light, For a light wife doth make a heavy husband, And never be Bassanio so for me. But God sort all! You are welcome home, my lord.
BOT: I thank you, madam. Give welcome to my friend. This is the man, this is Antonio, To whom I am so infinitely bound.
USER: You should in all sense be much bound to him, For, as I hear, he was much bound for you.
BOT: No more than I am well acquitted of.
USER: Sir, you are very welcome to our house. It must appear in other ways than words, Therefore I scant this breathing courtesy.
BOT: In faith, I gave it to the judge's clerk. Would he were gelt that had it, for my part, Since you do take it, love, so much at heart.
USER: A quarrel, ho, already! What's the matter?
BOT: About a hoop of gold, a paltry ring That she did give me, whose posy was For all the world like cutlers' poetry Upon a knife, "Love me, and leave me not."
USER: He will, and if he live to be a man.
BOT: Ay, if a woman live to be a man.
USER: Now, by this hand, I gave it to a youth, A kind of boy, a little scrubbed boy, No higher than thyself, the judge's clerk, A prating boy that begg'd it as a fee, I could not for my heart deny it him.
BOT: And swear I lost the ring defending it.
USER: What ring gave you, my lord? Not that, I hope, which you receiv'd of me.
BOT: If I could add a lie unto a fault, I would deny it, but you see my finger Hath not the ring upon it, it is gone.
USER: Even so void is your false heart of truth. By heaven, I will ne'er come in your bed Until I see the ring.
BOT: Nor I in yours Till I again see mine!
USER: And I his clerk. Therefore be well advis'd How you do leave me to mine own protection.
BOT: Well, do you so. Let not me take him then, For if I do, I'll mar the young clerk's pen.
USER: I am th' unhappy subject of these quarrels.
BOT: Sir, grieve not you. You are welcome notwithstanding.
USER: Portia, forgive me this enforced wrong, And in the hearing of these many friends I swear to thee, even by thine own fair eyes, Wherein I see myself -
BOT: Mark you but that! In both my eyes he doubly sees himself, In each eye one. Swear by your double self, And there's an oath of credit.
USER: Nay, but hear me. Pardon this fault, and by my soul I swear I never more will break an oath with thee.
BOT: I once did lend my body for his wealth, Which but for him that had your husband's ring Had quite miscarried. I dare be bound again, My soul upon the forfeit, that your lord Will never more break faith advisedly.
USER: Then you shall be his surety. Give him this, And bid him keep it better than the other.
BOT: Here, Lord Bassanio, swear to keep this ring.
USER: By heaven, it is the same I gave the doctor!
BOT: I had it of him: pardon me, Bassanio, For by this ring, the doctor lay with me.
USER: And pardon me, my gentle Gratiano, For that same scrubbed boy, the doctor's clerk, In lieu of this, last night did lie with me.
BOT: Why, this is like the mending of highways In summer, where the ways are fair enough. What, are we cuckolds ere we have deserv'd it?
USER: I am dumb.
BOT: Were you the doctor, and I knew you not?
USER: Were you the clerk that is to make me cuckold?
BOT: Ay, but the clerk that never means to do it, Unless he live until he be a man.
USER: Sweet doctor, you shall be my bedfellow. When I am absent, then lie with my wife.
BOT: Sweet lady, you have given me life and living; For here I read for certain that my ships Are safely come to road.
USER: How now, Lorenzo! My clerk hath some good comforts too for you.
BOT: Ay, and I'll give them him without a fee. There do I give to you and Jessica, From the rich Jew, a special deed of gift, After his death, of all he dies possess'd of.
USER: Fair ladies, you drop manna in the way Of starved people.
BOT: It is almost morning, And yet I am sure you are not satisfied Of these events at full. Let us go in, And charge us there upon inter'gatories, And we will answer all things faithfully.

USER: Sir Hugh, persuade me not. I will make a Star Chamber matter of it. If he were twenty Sir John Falstaffs, he shall not abuse Robert Shallow, esquire.
BOT: In the county of Gloucester, Justice of Peace and Coram.
USER: Ay, cousin Slender, and Custalorum.
BOT: Ay, and Ratolorum too; and a gentleman born, Master Parson, who writes himself "Armigero" in any bill, warrant, quittance, or obligation - "Armigero."
USER: Ay, that I do, and have done any time these three hundred years.
BOT: All his successors, gone before him hath done't; and all his ancestors that come after him may. They may give the dozen white luces in their coat.
USER: It is an old coat.
BOT: The dozen white louses do become an old coat well. It agrees well, passant. It is a familiar beast to man, and signifies love.
USER: The luce is the fresh fish. The salt fish is an old coat.
BOT: I may quarter, coz.
USER: You may, by marrying.
BOT: It is marring indeed, if he quarter it.
USER: Not a whit.
BOT: The Council shall hear it; it is a riot.
USER: It is not meet the Council hear a riot. There is no fear of Got in a riot. The Council, look you, shall desire to hear the fear of Got, and not to hear a riot. Take your vizaments in that.
BOT: Ha! O' my life, if I were young again, the sword should end it.
USER: Mistress Anne Page? She has brown hair, and speaks small like a woman?
BOT: Did her grandsire leave her seven hundred pound?
USER: Ay, and her father is make her a petter penny.
BOT: I know the young gentlewoman; she has good gifts.
USER: Seven hundred pounds, and possibilities, is goot gifts.
BOT: Well, let us see honest Master Page. Is Falstaff there?
USER: Here is Got's plessing, and your friend, and Justice Shallow, and here young Master Slender, that peradventures shall tell you another tale, if matters grow to your likings.
BOT: I am glad to see your worships well. I thank you for my venison, Master Shallow.
USER: Master Page, I am glad to see you, much good do it your good heart! I wished your venison better; it was ill killed. How doth good Mistress Page? And I thank you always with my heart, la, with my heart.
BOT: Sir, I thank you.
USER: Sir, I thank you; by yea and no, I do.
BOT: I am glad to see you, good Master Slender.
USER: How does your fallow greyhound, sir? I heard say he was outrun on Cotsall.
BOT: It could not be judged, sir.
USER: You'll not confess, you'll not confess.
BOT: That he will not. 'Tis your fault; 'tis your fault. 'Tis a good dog.
USER: A cur, sir.
BOT: Sir, he's a good dog, and a fair dog, can there be more said? He is good, and fair. Is Sir John Falstaff here?
USER: Sir, he is within; and I would I could do a good office between you.
BOT: It is spoke as a Christians ought to speak.
USER: He hath wronged me, Master Page.
BOT: Sir, he doth in some sort confess it.
USER: If it be confessed, it is not redressed. Is not that so, Master Page? He hath wronged me, indeed he hath, at a word, he hath. Believe me. Robert Shallow, esquire, saith he is wronged.
BOT: Here comes Sir John.
USER: Now, Master Shallow, you'll complain of me to the King?
BOT: Knight, you have beaten my men, killed my deer, and broke open my lodge.
USER: But not kissed your keeper's daughter!
BOT: Tut, a pin! This shall be answered.
USER: I will answer it straight: I have done all this. That is now answered.
BOT: The Council shall know this.
USER: 'Twere better for you if it were known in counsel: you'll be laughed at.
BOT: _Pauca verba_, Sir John; goot worts.
USER: Good worts? Good cabbage! - Slender, I broke your head. What matter have you against me?
BOT: Marry, sir, I have matter in my head against you, and against your cony-catching rascals, Bardolph, Nym, and Pistol. They carried me to the tavern and made me drunk, and afterwards picked my pocket.
USER: You Banbury cheese!
BOT: Ay, it is no matter.
USER: How now, Mephostophilus?
BOT: Ay, it is no matter.
USER: Slice, I say! _Pauca, pauca_, slice, that's my humour.
BOT: Where's Simple, my man? Can you tell, cousin?
USER: We three to hear it and end it between them.
BOT: Fery goot. I will make a prief of it in my notebook, and we will afterwards 'ork upon the cause with as great discreetly as we can.
USER: Pistol!
BOT: He hears with ears.
USER: The tevil and his tam! What phrase is this, "He hears with ear"? Why, it is affectations.
BOT: Pistol, did you pick Master Slender's purse?
USER: Is this true, Pistol?
BOT: No, it is false, if it is a pick-purse.
USER: Ha, thou mountain-foreigner! - Sir John and master mine, I combat challenge of this latten bilbo. - Word of denial in thy _labras_ here! Word of denial! Froth and scum, thou liest.
BOT: Be avised, sir, and pass good humours. I will say "marry trap with you", if you run the nuthook's humour on me. That is the very note of it.
USER: By this hat, then, he in the red face had it. For though I cannot remember what I did when you made me drunk, yet I am not altogether an ass.
BOT: What say you, Scarlet and John?
USER: Why, sir, for my part, I say the gentleman had drunk himself out of his five sentences.
BOT: It is his "five senses". Fie, what the ignorance is!
USER: And being fap, sir, was, as they say, cashiered; and so conclusions passed the careers.
BOT: So Got 'udge me, that is a virtuous mind.
USER: You hear all these matters denied, gentlemen; you hear it. wine. PAGE Nay, daughter, carry the wine in, we'll drink within. SLENDER O heaven, this is Mistress Anne Page.
BOT: How now, Mistress Ford?
USER: Mistress Ford, by my troth, you are very well met. By your leave, good mistress.
BOT: Wife, bid these gentlemen welcome. Come, we have a hot venison pasty to dinner. Come, gentlemen, I hope we shall drink down all unkindness.
USER: I had rather than forty shillings I had my book of _Songs and Sonnets_ here. How now, Simple, where have you been? I must wait on myself, must I? You have not the _Book of Riddles_ about you, have you?
BOT: _Book of Riddles?_ Why, did you not lend it to Alice Shortcake upon Allhallowmas last, a fortnight afore Michaelmas?
USER: Come, coz; come, coz, we stay for you. A word with you, coz. Marry, this, coz: there is, as 'twere, a tender, a kind of tender, made afar off by Sir Hugh here. Do you understand me?
BOT: Ay, sir, you shall find me reasonable. If it be so, I shall do that that is reason.
USER: Nay, but understand me.
BOT: So I do, sir.
USER: Give ear to his motions, Master Slender. I will description the matter to you, if you be capacity of it.
BOT: Nay, I will do as my cousin Shallow says. I pray you pardon me, he's a Justice of Peace in his country, simple though I stand here.
USER: But that is not the question. The question is concerning your marriage.
BOT: Ay, there's the point, sir.
USER: Marry, is it; the very point of it - to Mistress Anne Page.
BOT: Why, if it be so, I will marry her upon any reasonable demands.
USER: Cousin Abraham Slender, can you love her?
BOT: I hope, sir, I will do as it shall become one that would do reason.
USER: Nay, Got's lords and his ladies! You must speak possitable, if you can carry her your desires towards her.
BOT: That you must. Will you, upon good dowry, marry her?
USER: I will do a greater thing than that, upon your request, cousin, in any reason.
BOT: Nay, conceive me, conceive me, sweet coz. What I do is to pleasure you, coz. Can you love the maid?
USER: It is a fery discretion answer, save the fall is in the 'ord "dissolutely." The 'ort is, according to our meaning, "resolutely." His meaning is good.
BOT: Ay, I think my cousin meant well.
USER: Ay, or else I would I might be hanged, la!
BOT: Here comes fair Mistress Anne.
USER: Here comes fair Mistress Anne. - Would I were young for your sake, Mistress Anne.
BOT: The dinner is on the table, my father desires your worships' company.
USER: I will wait on him, fair Mistress Anne.
BOT: 'Od's plessed will! I will not be absence at the grace. ANNE Will't please your worship to come in, sir?
USER: No, I thank you, forsooth, heartily; I am very well.
BOT: The dinner attends you, sir.
USER: I may not go in without your worship. They will not sit till you come.
BOT: I' faith, I'll eat nothing. I thank you as much as though I did.
USER: I pray you, sir, walk in.
BOT: I think there are, sir; I heard them talked of.
USER: I love the sport well, but I shall as soon quarrel at it as any man in England. You are afraid, if you see the bear loose, are you not?
BOT: Ay, indeed, sir.
USER: I'll eat nothing, I thank you, sir.
BOT: By cock and pie, you shall not choose, sir! Come, come.
USER: Nay, pray you lead the way.
BOT: Come on, sir.
USER: Mistress Anne, yourself shall go first.
BOT: Not I, sir; pray you keep on.
USER: Truly, I will not go first; truly, la! I will not do you that wrong.
BOT: I pray you, sir.
USER: I'll rather be unmannerly than troublesome. You do yourself wrong, indeed, la!

USER: Go your ways, and ask of Doctor Caius' house which is the way. And there dwells one Mistress Quickly, which is in the manner of his nurse, or his dry nurse, or his cook, or his laundry, his washer and his wringer.
BOT: Well, sir.

USER: Mine host of the Garter!
BOT: What says my bully rook? Speak scholarly and wisely.
USER: Truly, mine host, I must turn away some of my followers.
BOT: Discard, bully Hercules; cashier. Let them wag; trot, trot.
USER: I sit at ten pounds a week.
BOT: Thou'rt an emperor - Caesar, Keiser, and Pheazar. I will entertain Bardolph. He shall draw, he shall tap. Said I well, bully Hector?
USER: Do so, good mine host.
BOT: I have spoke, let him follow. - Let me see thee froth and lime. I am at a word, follow.
USER: Bardolph, follow him. A tapster is a good trade. An old cloak makes a new jerkin; a withered servingman a fresh tapster. Go, adieu.
BOT: It is a life that I have desired. I will thrive.
USER: O base Hungarian wight, wilt thou the spigot wield? NYM He was gotten in drink. Is not the humour conceited?
BOT: I am glad I am so acquit of this tinderbox. His thefts were too open. His filching was like an unskilful singer, he kept not time.
USER: The good humour is to steal at a minute's rest.
BOT: "Convey," the wise it call. "Steal?" Foh! A _fico_ for the phrase!
USER: Well, sirs, I am almost out at heels.
BOT: Why, then, let kibes ensue.
USER: There is no remedy, I must cony-catch, I must shift.
BOT: Young ravens must have food.
USER: Which of you know Ford of this town?
BOT: I ken the wight, he is of substance good.
USER: My honest lads, I will tell you what I am about.
BOT: Two yards, and more.
USER: He hath studied her will and translated her will - out of honesty into English.
BOT: The anchor is deep. Will that humour pass?
USER: Now, the report goes she has all the rule of her husband's purse. He hath a legion of angels.
BOT: As many devils entertain, and "To her, boy," say I.
USER: The humour rises; it is good. Humour me the angels.
BOT: Then did the sun on dunghill shine.
USER: I thank thee for that humour.
BOT: Shall I Sir Pandarus of Troy become, And by my side wear steel? Then Lucifer take all!
USER: I will run no base humour. Here, take the humour-letter. I will keep the 'haviour of reputation.
BOT: I have operations in my head which be humours of revenge.
USER: Wilt thou revenge?
BOT: By welkin and her star!
USER: With wit or steel?
BOT: With both the humours, I. I will discuss the humour of this love to Ford.
USER: And I to Page shall eke unfold How Falstaff, varlet vile, His dove will prove, his gold will hold, And his soft couch defile.
BOT: My humour shall not cool. I will incense Ford to deal with poison, I will possess him with yellowness, for the revolt of mine is dangerous. That is my true humour.
USER: Thou art the Mars of malcontents. I second thee. Troop on.

USER: I'll go watch.
BOT: Ay, for fault of a better.
USER: And Master Slender's your master?
BOT: Ay, forsooth.
USER: Does he not wear a great round beard, like a glover's paring-knife?
BOT: No, forsooth, he hath but a little wee face, with a little yellow beard, a Cain-coloured beard.
USER: A softly-sprighted man, is he not?
BOT: Ay, forsooth. But he is as tall a man of his hands as any is between this and his head. He hath fought with a warrener.
USER: How say you? O, I should remember him. Does he not hold up his head, as it were, and strut in his gait?
BOT: Yes, indeed, does he.
USER: Well, heaven send Anne Page no worse fortune! Tell Master Parson Evans I will do what I can for your master. Anne is a good girl, and I wish - RUGBY Out, alas! Here comes my master.
BOT: Ay, forsooth, I'll fetch it you. man, he would have been horn-mad.
USER: _Fe, fe, fe fe! Ma foi, il fait fort chaud. Je m'en vais à la cour - la grande affaire._
BOT: Is it this, sir?
USER: _Oui, mette-le au mon_ pocket. _Dépêche_, quickly - Vere is dat knave Rugby?
BOT: What, John Rugby, John! RUGBY Here, sir.
USER: You are John Rugby, and you are Jack Rugby. Come, take-a your rapier, and come after my heel to the court.
BOT: 'Tis ready, sir, here in the porch.
USER: By my trot, I tarry too long. 'Od's me! _Qu'ay j'oublié?_ Dere is some simples in my closet dat I vill not for the varld I shall leave behind.
BOT: Ay me, he'll find the young man there, and be mad!
USER: _O diable, diable!_ Vat is in my closet? Villainy! _Larron!_ [_Pulling Simple out_.] Rugby, my rapier!
BOT: Good master, be content.
USER: Wherefore shall I be content-a?
BOT: The young man is an honest man.
USER: What shall de honest man do in my closet? Dere is no honest man dat shall come in my closet.
BOT: I beseech you, be not so phlegmatic. Hear the truth of it. He came of an errand to me from Parson Hugh.
USER: Vell?
BOT: Ay, forsooth, to desire her to -
USER: Peace, I pray you.
BOT: Peace-a your tongue! - Speak-a your tale.
USER: To desire this honest gentlewoman, your maid, to speak a good word to Mistress Anne Page for my master in the way of marriage.
BOT: This is all, indeed, la! But I'll ne'er put my finger in the fire, and need not.
USER: Sir Hugh send-a you? - Rugby, _baille_ me some paper. - Tarry you a little-a while.
BOT: body's hand.
USER: Alas, he speaks but for his friend.
BOT: Sir, the maid loves you, and all shall be well. We must give folks leave to prate. What, the good-year!
USER: Rugby, come to the court with me. [_To Mistress Quickly_.] By gar, if I have not Anne Page, I shall turn your head out of my door. - Follow my heels, Rugby.
BOT: You shall have An - fool's head of your own. No, I know Anne's mind for that. Never a woman in Windsor knows more of Anne's mind than I do, nor can do more than I do with her, I thank heaven.
USER: Who's there, I trow? Come near the house, I pray you. FENTON How now, good woman? How dost thou?
BOT: The better, that it pleases your good worship to ask.
USER: What news? How does pretty Mistress Anne?
BOT: In truth, sir, and she is pretty, and honest, and gentle; and one that is your friend, I can tell you that by the way, I praise heaven for it.
USER: Shall I do any good, think'st thou? Shall I not lose my suit?
BOT: Troth, sir, all is in His hands above. But notwithstanding, Master Fenton, I'll be sworn on a book she loves you. Have not your worship a wart above your eye?
USER: Yes, marry, have I; what of that?
BOT: Well, I shall see her today. Hold, there's money for thee. Let me have thy voice in my behalf. If thou seest her before me, commend me.
USER: Will I? I' faith, that we will! And I will tell your worship more of the wart the next time we have confidence, and of other wooers.
BOT: Well, farewell, I am in great haste now.
USER: Farewell to your worship. Truly, an honest gentleman - but Anne loves him not, for I know Anne's mind as well as another does. Out upon 't, what have I forgot?

USER: Mistress Page! Trust me, I was going to your house.
BOT: And, trust me, I was coming to you. You look very ill.
USER: Nay, I'll ne'er believe that. I have to show to the contrary.
BOT: Faith, but you do, in my mind.
USER: Well, I do, then. Yet I say I could show you to the contrary. O, Mistress Page, give me some counsel.
BOT: What's the matter, woman?
USER: O woman, if it were not for one trifling respect, I could come to such honour!
BOT: Hang the trifle, woman; take the honour. What is it? Dispense with trifles. What is it?
USER: If I would but go to hell for an eternal moment or so, I could be knighted.
BOT: What? Thou liest! Sir Alice Ford! These knights will hack, and so thou shouldst not alter the article of thy gentry.
USER: Why, this is the very same - the very hand, the very words. What doth he think of us?
BOT: "Boarding" call you it? I'll be sure to keep him above deck.
USER: Nay, I will consent to act any villainy against him that may not sully the chariness of our honesty. O, that my husband saw this letter! It would give eternal food to his jealousy.
BOT: Why, look where he comes; and my good man too. He's as far from jealousy as I am from giving him cause, and that, I hope, is an unmeasurable distance.
USER: You are the happier woman.
BOT: Let's consult together against this greasy knight. Come hither. FORD Well, I hope it be not so.
USER: Hope is a curtal dog in some affairs. Sir John affects thy wife.
BOT: Why, sir, my wife is not young.
USER: He woos both high and low, both rich and poor, Both young and old, one with another, Ford. He loves the gallimaufry. Ford, perpend.
BOT: Love my wife?
USER: With liver burning hot. Prevent, or go thou like Sir Actaeon he, With Ringwood at thy heels. O, odious is the name!
BOT: What name, sir?
USER: The horn, I say. Farewell. Take heed, have open eye, for thieves do foot by night. Take heed, ere summer comes, or cuckoo birds do sing. - Away, Sir Corporal Nym. - Believe it, Page, he speaks sense. FORD
BOT: town commended him for a true man.
USER: Mistress Page and Mistress Ford come forward.
BOT: How now, Meg?
USER: Whither go you, George? Hark you.
BOT: How now, sweet Frank, why art thou melancholy?
USER: I melancholy? I am not melancholy. Get you home, go.
BOT: Faith, thou hast some crotchets in thy head now. - Will you go, Mistress Page?
USER: Have with you. You'll come to dinner, George? messenger to this paltry knight.
BOT: You are come to see my daughter Anne?
USER: Ay, forsooth. And, I pray, how does good Mistress Anne?
BOT: Go in with us and see. We'd have an hour's talk with you. PAGE How now, Master Ford?
USER: You heard what this knave told me, did you not?
BOT: Yes, and you heard what the other told me?
USER: Do you think there is truth in them?
BOT: Hang 'em, slaves! I do not think the knight would offer it, but these that accuse him in his intent towards our wives are a yoke of his discarded men, very rogues, now they be out of service.
USER: Were they his men?
BOT: Marry, were they.
USER: I like it never the better for that. Does he lie at the Garter?
BOT: Ay, marry, does he. If he should intend this voyage toward my wife, I would turn her loose to him; and what he gets more of her than sharp words, let it lie on my head.
USER: I do not misdoubt my wife, but I would be loath to turn them together. A man may be too confident. I would have nothing lie on my head. I cannot be thus satisfied.
BOT: Look where my ranting host of the Garter comes. There is either liquor in his pate or money in his purse when he looks so merrily. - How now, mine host?
USER: How now, bully rook? Thou'rt a gentleman. - Cavaliero Justice, I say!
BOT: I follow, mine host, I follow. - Good even and twenty, good Master Page. Master Page, will you go with us? We have sport in hand.
USER: Tell him, Cavaliero Justice; tell him, bully rook.
BOT: Sir, there is a fray to be fought between Sir Hugh the Welsh priest and Caius the French doctor.
USER: Good mine host o' the Garter, a word with you.
BOT: None, I protest. But I'll give you a pottle of burnt sack to give me recourse to him, and tell him my name is Brook, only for a jest.
USER: My hand, bully. Thou shalt have egress and regress - said I well? - and thy name shall be Brook. It is a merry knight. Will you go, myn-heers?
BOT: Have with you, mine host.
USER: I have heard the Frenchman hath good skill in his rapier.
BOT: Here, boys, here, here! Shall we wag?

USER: I will not lend thee a penny.
BOT: Why then, the world's mine oyster, Which I with sword will open.
USER: Didst not thou share? Hadst thou not fifteen pence?
BOT: I do relent. What wouldst thou more of man? ROBIN Sir, here's a woman would speak with you.
USER: Let her approach.
BOT: Give your worship good morrow.
USER: Good morrow, goodwife.
BOT: Not so, an't please your worship.
USER: Good maid, then.
BOT: I'll be sworn, as my mother was, the first hour I was born.
USER: I do believe the swearer. What with me?
BOT: Shall I vouchsafe your worship a word or two?
USER: Two thousand, fair woman; and I'll vouchsafe thee the hearing.
BOT: There is one Mistress Ford, sir - I pray, come a little nearer this ways. I myself dwell with Master Doctor Caius.
USER: Well, on; Mistress Ford, you say -
BOT: Your worship says very true. I pray your worship come a little nearer this ways.
USER: I warrant thee, nobody hears. Mine own people, mine own people.
BOT: Are they so? God bless them, and make them His servants!
USER: Well, Mistress Ford, what of her?
BOT: Why, sir, she's a good creature. Lord, Lord, your worship's a wanton! Well, heaven forgive you, and all of us, I pray!
USER: Mistress Ford, come, Mistress Ford.
BOT: But what says she to me? Be brief, my good she-Mercury.
USER: Marry, she hath received your letter, for the which she thanks you a thousand times; and she gives you to notify that her husband will be absence from his house between ten and eleven.
BOT: Ten and eleven?
USER: Ten and eleven. Woman, commend me to her; I will not fail her.
BOT: Not I, I assure thee. Setting the attraction of my good parts aside, I have no other charms.
USER: Blessing on your heart for 't!
BOT: But, I pray thee, tell me this: has Ford's wife and Page's wife acquainted each other how they love me?
USER: Why, I will.
BOT: Fare thee well, commend me to them both. There's my purse; I am yet thy debtor. Boy, go along with this woman. - This news distracts me.
USER: This punk is one of Cupid's carriers; Clap on more sails, pursue; up with your fights; Give fire! She is my prize, or ocean whelm them all!
BOT: Brook is his name?
USER: Ay, sir.
BOT: Call him in. Such Brooks are welcome to me, that o'erflow such liquor. Ah, ha, Mistress Ford and Mistress Page, have I encompassed you? Go to, _via!_ FORD God bless you, sir.
USER: And you, sir. Would you speak with me?
BOT: I make bold to press with so little preparation upon you.
USER: You're welcome. What's your will? - Give us leave, drawer. FORD Sir, I am a gentleman that have spent much. My name is Brook.
BOT: Good Master Brook, I desire more acquaintance of you.
USER: Money is a good soldier, sir, and will on.
BOT: Troth, and I have a bag of money here troubles me. If you will help to bear it, Sir John, take all, or half, for easing me of the carriage.
USER: Sir, I know not how I may deserve to be your porter.
BOT: I will tell you, sir, if you will give me the hearing.
USER: Speak, good Master Brook. I shall be glad to be your servant.
BOT: Very well, sir, proceed.
USER: There is a gentlewoman in this town, her husband's name is Ford.
BOT: Well, sir.
USER: Have you received no promise of satisfaction at her hands?
BOT: Never.
USER: Have you importuned her to such a purpose?
BOT: Never.
USER: Of what quality was your love, then?
BOT: Like a fair house built on another man's ground, so that I have lost my edifice by mistaking the place where I erected it.
USER: To what purpose have you unfolded this to me?
BOT: O, sir!
USER: Would it apply well to the vehemency of your affection that I should win what you would enjoy? Methinks you prescribe to yourself very preposterously.
BOT: Master Brook, I will first make bold with your money; next, give me your hand; and last, as I am a gentleman, you shall, if you will, enjoy Ford's wife.
USER: O good sir!
BOT: I say you shall.
USER: Want no money, Sir John; you shall want none.
BOT: I am blessed in your acquaintance. Do you know Ford, sir?
USER: I would you knew Ford, sir, that you might avoid him if you saw him.

USER: Jack Rugby!
BOT: Sir?
USER: Vat is de clock, Jack?
BOT: 'Tis past the hour, sir, that Sir Hugh promised to meet.
USER: By gar, he has save his soul, dat he is no come. He has pray his Pible well dat he is no come. By gar, Jack Rugby, he is dead already, if he be come.
BOT: He is wise, sir; he knew your worship would kill him if he came.
USER: By gar, de herring is no dead so as I vill kill him. Take your rapier, Jack; I vill tell you how I vill kill him.
BOT: Alas, sir, I cannot fence.
USER: Villainy, take your rapier.
BOT: Forbear; here's company. HOST God bless thee, bully doctor!
USER: God save you, Master Doctor Caius!
BOT: Now, good Master Doctor!
USER: Give you good morrow, sir.
BOT: Vat be all you, one, two, tree, four, come for?
USER: By gar, he is de coward Jack-priest of de vorld. He is not show his face.
BOT: Thou art a Castalion King Urinal Hector of Greece, my boy!
USER: I pray you, bear witness that me have stay six or seven, two, tree hours for him, and he is no come.
BOT: He is the wiser man, Master doctor. He is a curer of souls, and you a curer of bodies. If you should fight, you go against the hair of your professions. Is it not true, Master Page?
USER: Master Shallow, you have yourself been a great fighter, though now a man of peace.
BOT: 'Tis true, Master Shallow.
USER: Pardon, guest justice. - A word, Monsieur Mockwater.
BOT: Mockvater? Vat is dat?
USER: Mockwater, in our English tongue, is valour, bully.
BOT: By gar, then I have as much mockvater as de Englishman. Scurvy jack-dog priest! By gar, me vill cut his ears.
USER: He will clapper-claw thee tightly, bully.
BOT: Clapper-de-claw? Vat is dat?
USER: That is, he will make thee amends.
BOT: By gar, me do look he shall clapper-de-claw me, for, by gar, me vill have it.
USER: And I will provoke him to't, or let him wag.
BOT: Me tank you for dat.
USER: And, moreover, bully - but first, Master guest, and Master Page, and eke Cavaliero Slender, go you through the town to Frogmore. PAGE
BOT: bring the doctor about by the fields. Will it do well?
USER: PAGE, SHALLOW and SLENDER Adieu, good Master Doctor. CAIUS By gar, me vill kill de priest, for he speak for a jackanape to Anne Page.
BOT: By gar, me tank you for dat. By gar, I love you; and I shall procure-a you de good guest: de earl, de knight, de lords, de gentlemen, my patients.
USER: For the which I will be thy adversary toward Anne Page. Said I well?
BOT: By gar, 'tis good; vell said.
USER: Let us wag, then.
BOT: Come at my heels, Jack Rugby.

USER: I pray you now, good Master Slender's servingman, and friend Simple by your name, which way have you looked for Master Caius, that calls himself doctor of physic?
BOT: Marry, sir, the Petty-ward, the Park-ward, every way; old Windsor way, and every way but the town way.
USER: I most fehemently desire you, you will also look that way.
BOT: He's welcome. Heaven prosper the right! What weapons is he?
USER: No weapons, sir. There comes my master, Master Shallow, and another gentleman, from Frogmore, over the stile, this way.
BOT: Pray you, give me my gown - or else keep it in your arms. SHALLOW How now, Master Parson? Good morrow, good Sir Hugh. Keep a gamester from the dice, and a good student from his book, and it is wonderful.
USER: God save you, good Sir Hugh!
BOT: God pless you from his mercy sake, all of you!
USER: What, the sword and the word? Do you study them both, Master Parson?
BOT: And youthful still - in your doublet and hose, this raw rheumatic day?
USER: There is reasons and causes for it.
BOT: We are come to you to do a good office, Master Parson.
USER: Fery well; what is it?
BOT: Yonder is a most reverend gentleman who, belike having received wrong by some person, is at most odds with his own gravity and patience that ever you saw.
USER: I have lived fourscore years and upward; I never heard a man of his place, gravity, and learning, so wide of his own respect.
BOT: What is he?
USER: I think you know him: Master Doctor Caius, the renowned French physician.
BOT: Got's will and His passion of my heart! I had as lief you would tell me of a mess of porridge.
USER: Why?
BOT: He has no more knowledge in Hibbocrates and Galen, and he is a knave besides, a cowardly knave as you would desires to be acquainted withal.
USER: I warrant you, he's the man should fight with him.
BOT: It appears so by his weapons. Keep them asunder. Here comes Doctor Caius. PAGE Nay, good Master Parson, keep in your weapon.
USER: So do you, good Master Doctor.
BOT: Disarm them, and let them question. Let them keep their limbs whole and hack our English.
USER: I pray you, let-a me speak a word with your ear. Verefore will you not meet-a me?
BOT: By gar, you are de coward, de Jack dog, John ape.
USER: men's humours. I desire you in friendship, and I will one way or other make you amends. cogscomb.
BOT: _Diable!_ Jack Rugby, mine Host de Jarteer, have I not stay for him to kill him? Have I not, at de place I did appoint?
USER: As I am a Christians soul, now look you, this is the place appointed. I'll be judgment by mine host of the Garter.
BOT: Peace, I say, Gallia and Gaul, French and Welsh, soul-curer and body-curer!
USER: Ay, dat is very good; excellent.
BOT: Afore God, a mad host! Follow, gentlemen, follow.
USER: CAIUS Ha, do I perceive dat? Have you make-a de sot of us, ha, ha?
BOT: This is well, he has made us his vlouting-stog. I desire you that we may be friends, and let us knog our prains together to be revenge on this same scall, scurvy, cogging companion, the host of the Garter.
USER: By gar, with all my heart. He promise to bring me where is Anne Page; by gar, he deceive me too.
BOT: Well, I will smite his noddles. Pray you follow.

USER: Nay, keep your way, little gallant. You were wont to be a follower, but now you are a leader. Whether had you rather, lead mine eyes, or eye your master's heels?
BOT: I had rather, forsooth, go before you like a man than follow him like a dwarf.
USER: O, you are a flattering boy! Now I see you'll be a courtier. FORD Well met, Mistress Page. Whither go you?
BOT: Truly, sir, to see your wife. Is she at home?
USER: Ay, and as idle as she may hang together, for want of company. I think if your husbands were dead you two would marry.
BOT: Be sure of that - two other husbands.
USER: Where had you this pretty weathercock?
BOT: I cannot tell what the dickens his name is my husband had him of. What do you call your knight's name, sirrah?
USER: Sir John Falstaff.
BOT: Sir John Falstaff!
USER: He, he; I can never hit on's name. There is such a league between my good man and he! Is your wife at home indeed?
BOT: Indeed she is.
USER: Trust me, a good knot. I have good cheer at home, and I pray you all go with me.
BOT: I must excuse myself, Master Ford.
USER: And so must I, sir; we have appointed to dine with Mistress Anne, and I would not break with her for more money than I'll speak of.
BOT: We have lingered about a match between Anne Page and my cousin Slender, and this day we shall have our answer.
USER: I hope I have your good will, father Page.
BOT: You have, Master Slender, I stand wholly for you. - But my wife, Master doctor, is for you altogether.
USER: Ay, be-gar; and de maid is love-a me! My nursh-a Quickly tell me so mush.
BOT: What say you to young Master Fenton? He capers, he dances, he has eyes of youth, he writes verses, he speaks holiday, he smells April and May. He will carry 't, he will carry 't. 'Tis in his buttons he will carry 't.
USER: I beseech you, heartily, some of you go home with me to dinner. Besides your cheer, you shall have sport: I will show you a monster. Master Doctor, you shall go; so shall you, Master Page, and you, Sir Hugh.
BOT: Have with you to see this monster.

USER: What, John! What, Robert!
BOT: Quickly, quickly! Is the buck-basket -
USER: I warrant. - What, Robin, I say!
BOT: Come, come, come.
USER: Here, set it down.
BOT: Give your men the charge; we must be brief.
USER: You will do it?
BOT: I ha' told them over and over, they lack no direction. - Be gone, and come when you are called.
USER: Here comes little Robin.
BOT: How now, my eyas-musket, what news with you?
USER: My Master, Sir John, is come in at your back door, Mistress Ford, and requests your company.
BOT: You little Jack-a-Lent, have you been true to us?
USER: Ay, I'll be sworn. My master knows not of your being here, and hath threatened to put me into everlasting liberty if I tell you of it; for he swears he'll turn me away.
BOT: Thou'rt a good boy, this secrecy of thine shall be a tailor to thee, and shall make thee a new doublet and hose. I'll go hide me.
USER: Do so. - Go tell thy master I am alone. Mistress Page, remember you your cue.
BOT: I warrant thee. If I do not act it, hiss me.
USER: Go to, then. We'll use this unwholesome humidity, this gross watery pumpion; we'll teach him to know turtles from jays.
BOT: "Have I caught thee, my heavenly jewel?" Why, now let me die, for I have lived long enough. This is the period of my ambition. O this blessed hour!
USER: O, sweet Sir John!
BOT: Mistress Ford, I cannot cog, I cannot prate, Mistress Ford. Now shall I sin in my wish: I would thy husband were dead. I'll speak it before the best lord: I would make thee my lady.
USER: I your lady, Sir John? Alas, I should be a pitiful lady.
BOT: Let the court of France show me such another. I see how thine eye would emulate the diamond. Thou hast the right arched beauty of the brow that becomes the ship-tire, the tire-valiant, or any tire of Venetian admittance.
USER: A plain kerchief, Sir John. My brows become nothing else, nor that well neither.
BOT: Believe me, there's no such thing in me.
USER: Do not betray me, sir; I fear you love Mistress Page.
BOT: Thou mightst as well say I love to walk by the Counter gate, which is as hateful to me as the reek of a lime-kiln.
USER: Well, heaven knows how I love you, and you shall one day find it.
BOT: Keep in that mind, I'll deserve it.
USER: Nay, I must tell you, so you do; or else I could not be in that mind.
BOT: Mistress Ford, Mistress Ford, here's Mistress Page at the door, sweating and blowing and looking wildly, and would needs speak with you presently.
USER: She shall not see me; I will ensconce me behind the arras.
BOT: Pray you, do so; she's a very tattling woman. What's the matter? How now?
USER: O Mistress Ford, what have you done? You're shamed, you're overthrown, you're undone for ever!
BOT: What's the matter, good Mistress Page?
USER: O well-a-day, Mistress Ford, having an honest man to your husband, to give him such cause of suspicion!
BOT: What cause of suspicion?
USER: What cause of suspicion? Out upon you! How am I mistook in you!
BOT: Why, alas, what's the matter?
USER: Your husband's coming hither, woman, with all the officers in Windsor, to search for a gentleman that he says is here now in the house, by your consent, to take an ill advantage of his absence. You are undone.
BOT: 'Tis not so, I hope.
USER: What shall I do? There is a gentleman, my dear friend; and I fear not mine own shame as much as his peril. I had rather than a thousand pound he were out of the house.
BOT: He's too big to go in there. What shall I do?
USER: 't! I'll in, I'll in. Follow your friend's counsel. I'll in.
BOT: What, Sir John Falstaff? Are these your letters, knight?
USER: I love thee, and none but thee. Help me away. Let me creep in here. I'll never -
BOT: Help to cover your master, boy. - Call your men, Mistress Ford. - You dissembling knight!
USER: What, John! Robert! John! Go, take up these clothes here, quickly. Where's the cowl-staff? Look how you drumble! Carry them to the laundress in Datchet Mead; quickly, come.
BOT: Pray you come near. If I suspect without cause, why then make sport at me, then let me be your jest; I deserve it. - How now? Whither bear you this? JOHN and ROBERT. To the laundress, forsooth.
USER: Why, what have you to do whither they bear it? You were best meddle with buck-washing!
BOT: Good Master Ford, be contented: you wrong yourself too much.
USER: True, Master Page. - Up, gentlemen, you shall see sport anon. Follow me, gentlemen. EVANS This is fery fantastical humours and jealousies.
BOT: By gar, 'tis no the fashion of France; it is not jealous in France.
USER: Nay, follow him, gentlemen; see the issue of his search.
BOT: Is there not a double excellency in this?
USER: I know not which pleases me better, that my husband is deceived, or Sir John.
BOT: What a taking was he in when your husband asked who was in the basket!
USER: I am half afraid he will have need of washing, so throwing him into the water will do him a benefit.
BOT: Hang him, dishonest rascal! I would all of the same strain were in the same distress.
USER: I think my husband hath some special suspicion of Falstaff's being here, for I never saw him so gross in his jealousy till now.
BOT: I will lay a plot to try that, and we will yet have more tricks with Falstaff. His dissolute disease will scarce obey this medicine.
USER: Shall we send that foolish carrion Mistress Quickly to him, and excuse his throwing into the water, and give him another hope, to betray him to another punishment?
BOT: We will do it. Let him be sent for tomorrow eight o'clock to have amends. FORD I cannot find him. Maybe the knave bragged of that he could not compass.
USER: You use me well, Master Ford, do you?
BOT: Ay, I do so.
USER: Heaven make you better than your thoughts!
BOT: Amen!
USER: You do yourself mighty wrong, Master Ford.
BOT: Ay, ay; I must bear it.
USER: If there be anypody in the house, and in the chambers, and in the coffers, and in the presses, heaven forgive my sins at the day of judgment!
BOT: Be gar, nor I too; there is nobodies.
USER: Fie, fie, Master Ford, are you not ashamed? What spirit, what devil suggests this imagination? I would not ha' your distemper in this kind for the wealth of Windsor Castle.
BOT: 'Tis my fault, Master Page. I suffer for it.
USER: You suffer for a pad conscience. Your wife is as honest a 'omans as I will desires among five thousand, and five hundred too.
BOT: By gar, I see 'tis an honest woman.
USER: Well, I promised you a dinner. Come, come, walk in the park. I pray you pardon me; I will hereafter make known to you why I have done this. Come, wife, come, Mistress Page, I pray you pardon me. Pray heartily, pardon me.
BOT: Let's go in, gentlemen; but, trust me, we'll mock him. I do invite you tomorrow morning to my house to breakfast; after, we'll a-birding together; I have a fine hawk for the bush. Shall it be so?
USER: Anything.
BOT: If there is one, I shall make two in the company.
USER: If there be one or two, I shall make-a the turd.
BOT: Pray you go, Master Page.
USER: I pray you now, remembrance tomorrow on the lousy knave, mine host.
BOT: Dat is good, by gar, with all my heart.
USER: A lousy knave, to have his gibes and his mockeries!

USER: I see I cannot get thy father's love; Therefore no more turn me to him, sweet Nan.
BOT: Alas, how then?
USER: Maybe he tells you true.
