BOT: Gentle Master Fenton, Yet seek my father's love, still seek it, sir. If opportunity and humblest suit Cannot attain it, why then - hark you hither.
USER: Break their talk, Mistress Quickly. My kinsman shall speak for himself.
BOT: I'll make a shaft or a bolt on 't. 'Slid, 'tis but venturing.
USER: Be not dismayed.
BOT: No, she shall not dismay me. I care not for that, but that I am afeard.
USER: Hark ye, Master Slender would speak a word with you.
BOT: I come to him. O, what a world of vile ill-favoured faults Looks handsome in three hundred pounds a year!
USER: And how does good Master Fenton? Pray you, a word with you.
BOT: I had a father, Mistress Anne; my uncle can tell you good jests of him. - Pray you, uncle, tell Mistress Anne the jest how my father stole two geese out of a pen, good uncle.
USER: Mistress Anne, my cousin loves you.
BOT: Ay, that I do, as well as I love any woman in Gloucestershire.
USER: He will maintain you like a gentlewoman.
BOT: Ay, that I will, come cut and long-tail, under the degree of a squire.
USER: He will make you a hundred and fifty pounds jointure.
BOT: Good Master Shallow, let him woo for himself.
USER: Marry, I thank you for it, I thank you for that good comfort. - She calls you, coz; I'll leave you.
BOT: Now, Master Slender.
USER: Now, good Mistress Anne.
BOT: What is your will?
USER: My will? 'Od's heartlings, that's a pretty jest indeed! I ne'er made my will yet, I thank heaven. I am not such a sickly creature, I give heaven praise.
BOT: I mean, Master Slender, what would you with me?
USER: Nay, Master Page, be not impatient.
BOT: Good Master Fenton, come not to my child.
USER: She is no match for you.
BOT: Sir, will you hear me?
USER: No, good Master Fenton. - Come, Master Shallow; come, son Slender, in. - Knowing my mind, you wrong me, Master Fenton.
BOT: Speak to Mistress Page.
USER: Good Mistress Page, for that I love your daughter In such a righteous fashion as I do, Perforce, against all checks, rebukes, and manners, I must advance the colours of my love And not retire. Let me have your good will.
BOT: Good mother, do not marry me to yond fool.
USER: I mean it not; I seek you a better husband.
BOT: That's my master, Master Doctor.
USER: Alas, I had rather be set quick i' th' earth, And bowled to death with turnips.
BOT: Farewell, gentle mistress. Farewell, Nan.
USER: This is my doing now. "Nay," said I, "will you cast away your child on a fool, and a physician? Look on Master Fenton." This is my doing.
BOT: I thank thee; and I pray thee, once tonight Give my sweet Nan this ring. There's for thy pains.

USER: Bardolph, I say!
BOT: Here, sir.
USER: Come, let me pour in some sack to the Thames water, for my belly's as cold as if I had swallowed snowballs for pills to cool the reins. Call her in.
BOT: Come in, woman.
USER: By your leave, I cry you mercy. Give your worship good morrow.
BOT: Take away these chalices. Go, brew me a pottle of sack finely.
USER: With eggs, sir?
BOT: Simple of itself. I'll no pullet sperm in my brewage. How now?
USER: Marry, sir, I come to your worship from Mistress Ford.
BOT: Mistress Ford? I have had ford enough. I was thrown into the ford, I have my belly full of ford.
USER: Alas the day, good heart, that was not her fault. She does so take on with her men; they mistook their erection.
BOT: So did I mine, to build upon a foolish woman's promise.
USER: Well, I will visit her. Tell her so, and bid her think what a man is. Let her consider his frailty, and then judge of my merit.
BOT: I will tell her.
USER: Do so. Between nine and ten, sayst thou?
BOT: Eight and nine, sir.
USER: Well, be gone. I will not miss her.
BOT: Peace be with you, sir.
USER: I marvel I hear not of Master Brook; he sent me word to stay within. I like his money well. O, here he comes. FORD God bless you, sir.
BOT: Now, Master Brook, you come to know what hath passed between me and Ford's wife?
USER: That indeed, Sir John, is my business.
BOT: Master Brook, I will not lie to you. I was at her house the hour she appointed me.
USER: And how sped you, sir?
BOT: Very ill-favouredly, Master Brook.
USER: How so, sir? Did she change her determination?
BOT: What, while you were there?
USER: While I was there.
BOT: And did he search for you, and could not find you?
USER: You shall hear. As good luck would have it, comes in one Mistress Page, gives intelligence of Ford's approach; and, in her invention and Ford's wife's distraction, they conveyed me into a buck-basket.
BOT: A buck-basket!
USER: By the Lord, a buck-basket! Rammed me in with foul shirts and smocks, socks, foul stockings, greasy napkins, that, Master Brook, there was the rankest compound of villainous smell that ever offended nostril.
BOT: And how long lay you there?
USER: In good sadness, sir, I am sorry that for my sake you have suffered all this. My suit, then, is desperate. You'll undertake her no more?
BOT: 'Tis past eight already, sir.

USER: Is he at Master Ford's already, think'st thou?
BOT: Sure he is by this; or will be presently. But truly he is very courageous mad about his throwing into the water. Mistress Ford desires you to come suddenly.
USER: I'll be with her by and by. I'll but bring my young man here to school. Look where his master comes; 'tis a playing day, I see. How now, Sir Hugh, no school today?
BOT: No, Master Slender is let the boys leave to play.
USER: Blessing of his heart!
BOT: Sir Hugh, my husband says my son profits nothing in the world at his book. I pray you ask him some questions in his accidence.
USER: Come hither, William. Hold up your head, come.
BOT: Come on, sirrah. Hold up your head. Answer your master, be not afraid.
USER: William, how many numbers is in nouns?
BOT: Two.
USER: Truly, I thought there had been one number more, because they say "'Od's nouns."
BOT: Peace your tattlings! What is "fair," William?
USER: _Pulcher_.
BOT: Polecats? There are fairer things than polecats, sure.
USER: You are a very simplicity 'oman; I pray you, peace. - What is _lapis_, William?
BOT: A stone.
USER: And what is "a stone," William?
BOT: A pebble.
USER: No, it is _lapis_. I pray you remember in your prain.
BOT: _Lapis_.
USER: That is a good William. What is he, William, that does lend articles?
BOT: Articles are borrowed of the pronoun, and be thus declined: _singulariter, nominativo, hic, haec, hoc_.
USER: _Nominativo, hig, haeg, hog_, pray you, mark: _genitivo, huius_. Well, what is your accusative case?
BOT: _Accusativo, hinc_.
USER: I pray you, have your remembrance, child. _Accusativo, hung, hang, hog_.
BOT: "Hang-hog" is Latin for bacon, I warrant you.
USER: Leave your prabbles, 'oman. - What is the focative case, William?
BOT: O - _vocativo_ - O -
USER: Remember, William; focative is _caret_.
BOT: And that's a good root.
USER: 'Oman, forbear.
BOT: Peace.
USER: What is your genitive case plural, William?
BOT: Genitive case?
USER: Ay.
BOT: Genitive: _horum, harum, horum_.
USER: Vengeance of Jenny's case, fie on her! Never name her, child, if she be a whore.
BOT: For shame, 'oman.
USER: You do ill to teach the child such words. - He teaches him to hick and to hack, which they'll do fast enough of themselves; and to call "whore 'm"! - Fie upon you!
BOT: 'Oman, art thou lunatics? Hast thou no understandings for thy cases, and the numbers of the genders? Thou art as foolish Christian creatures as I would desires.
USER: Show me now, William, some declensions of your pronouns.
BOT: Forsooth, I have forgot.
USER: It is _qui, quae, quod_. If you forget your _quis_, your _quaes_, and your _quods_, you must be preeches. Go your ways and play, go.
BOT: He is a better scholar than I thought he was.
USER: He is a good sprag memory. Farewell, Mistress Page.
BOT: Adieu, good Sir Hugh. Get you home, boy. Come, we stay too long.

USER: He's a-birding, sweet Sir John.
BOT: Step into the chamber, Sir John.
USER: How now, sweetheart, who's at home besides yourself?
BOT: Why, none but mine own people.
USER: Indeed?
BOT: No, certainly.
USER: Truly, I am so glad you have nobody here.
BOT: Why?
USER: Why, does he talk of him?
BOT: How near is he, Mistress Page?
USER: Hard by, at street end. He will be here anon.
BOT: I am undone! The knight is here.
USER: Why, then, you are utterly shamed, and he's but a dead man. What a woman are you! Away with him, away with him! Better shame than murder.
BOT: Which way should he go? How should I bestow him? Shall I put him into the basket again?
USER: No, I'll come no more i' the basket. May I not go out ere he come?
BOT: Alas, three of Master Ford's brothers watch the door with pistols, that none shall issue out, otherwise you might slip away ere he came. But what make you here?
USER: What shall I do? I'll creep up into the chimney.
BOT: There they always use to discharge their birding-pieces.
USER: Creep into the kiln-hole.
BOT: Where is it?
USER: He will seek there, on my word. Neither press, coffer, chest, trunk, well, vault, but he hath an abstract for the remembrance of such places, and goes to them by his note. There is no hiding you in the house.
BOT: I'll go out then.
USER: If you go out in your own semblance, you die, Sir John - unless you go out disguised.
BOT: How might we disguise him?
USER: Alas the day, I know not. There is no woman's gown big enough for him; otherwise he might put on a hat, a muffler, and a kerchief, and so escape.
BOT: Good hearts, devise something. Any extremity rather than a mischief.
USER: My maid's aunt, the fat woman of Brentford, has a gown above.
BOT: On my word, it will serve him. She's as big as he is. And there's her thrummed hat, and her muffler too. - Run up, Sir John.
USER: Go, go, sweet Sir John. Mistress Page and I will look some linen for your head.
BOT: Quick, quick! We'll come dress you straight; put on the gown the while.
USER: I would my husband would meet him in this shape. He cannot abide the old woman of Brentford; he swears she's a witch, forbade her my house, and hath threatened to beat her.
BOT: Heaven guide him to thy husband's cudgel and the devil guide his cudgel afterwards!
USER: But is my husband coming?
BOT: Ay, in good sadness is he, and talks of the basket too, howsoever he hath had intelligence.
USER: We'll try that; for I'll appoint my men to carry the basket again, to meet him at the door with it as they did last time.
BOT: Nay, but he'll be here presently. Let's go dress him like the witch of Brentford.
USER: I'll first direct my men what they shall do with the basket. Go up, I'll bring linen for him straight.
BOT: Go, sirs, take the basket again on your shoulders. Your master is hard at door; if he bid you set it down, obey him. Quickly, dispatch.
USER: Come, come, take it up.
BOT: Pray heaven it be not full of knight again.
USER: Why, this passes, Master Ford! You are not to go loose any longer; you must be pinioned.
BOT: Why, this is lunatics, this is mad as a mad dog.
USER: Indeed, Master Ford, this is not well, indeed.
BOT: So say I too, sir. Come hither, Mistress Ford - Mistress Ford, the honest woman, the modest wife, the virtuous creature, that hath the jealous fool to her husband! I suspect without cause, mistress, do I?
USER: Heaven be my witness you do, if you suspect me in any dishonesty.
BOT: Well said, brazen-face, hold it out. - Come forth, sirrah.
USER: This passes.
BOT: Are you not ashamed? Let the clothes alone.
USER: I shall find you anon.
BOT: 'Tis unreasonable. Will you take up your wife's clothes? Come, away.
USER: Empty the basket, I say.
BOT: Why, man, why?
USER: If you find a man there, he shall die a flea's death.
BOT: Here's no man.
USER: By my fidelity, this is not well, Master Ford, this wrongs you.
BOT: Master Ford, you must pray, and not follow the imaginations of your own heart. This is jealousies.
USER: Well, he's not here I seek for.
BOT: What, ho, Mistress Page! Come you and the old woman down; my husband will come into the chamber.
USER: Old woman? What old woman's that?
BOT: Why, it is my maid's aunt of Brentford.
USER: Nay, good sweet husband! - Good gentlemen, let him not strike the old woman.
BOT: Come, Mother Prat; come, give me your hand.
USER: I'll prat her. [_Beats him_.] Out of my door, you witch, you rag, you baggage, you polecat, you runnion! Out, out! I'll conjure you, I'll fortune-tell you.
BOT: Are you not ashamed? I think you have killed the poor woman.
USER: Nay, he will do it. 'Tis a goodly credit for you.
BOT: Hang her, witch!
USER: By yea and no, I think the 'oman is a witch indeed. I like not when a 'oman has a great peard. I spy a great peard under her muffler.
BOT: Will you follow, gentlemen? I beseech you follow, see but the issue of my jealousy. If I cry out thus upon no trail, never trust me when I open again.
USER: Let's obey his humour a little further. Come, gentlemen.
BOT: Trust me, he beat him most pitifully.
USER: Nay, by th' mass, that he did not; he beat him most unpitifully, methought.
BOT: I'll have the cudgel hallowed and hung o'er the altar. It hath done meritorious service.
USER: What think you? May we, with the warrant of womanhood and the witness of a good conscience, pursue him with any further revenge?
BOT: The spirit of wantonness is sure scared out of him. If the devil have him not in fee-simple, with fine and recovery, he will never, I think, in the way of waste, attempt us again.
USER: Shall we tell our husbands how we have served him?
BOT: Yes, by all means, if it be but to scrape the figures out of your husband's brains. If they can find in their hearts the poor unvirtuous fat knight shall be any further afflicted, we two will still be the ministers.
USER: I'll warrant they'll have him publicly shamed, and methinks there would be no period to the jest should he not be publicly shamed.
BOT: Come, to the forge with it, then shape it. I would not have things cool.

USER: Sir, the Germans desire to have three of your horses. The Duke himself will be tomorrow at court, and they are going to meet him.
BOT: What duke should that be comes so secretly? I hear not of him in the court. Let me speak with the gentlemen. They speak English?
USER: Ay, sir. I'll call them to you.
BOT: They shall have my horses, but I'll make them pay, I'll sauce them. They have had my house a week at command; I have turned away my other guests. They must come off, I'll sauce them. Come.

USER: 'Tis one of the best discretions of a 'oman as ever I did look upon.
BOT: And did he send you both these letters at an instant?
USER: Within a quarter of an hour.
BOT: Pardon me, wife. Henceforth, do what thou wilt. I rather will suspect the sun with cold Than thee with wantonness. Now doth thy honour stand, In him that was of late an heretic, As firm as faith.
USER: There is no better way than that they spoke of.
BOT: How? To send him word they'll meet him in the park at midnight? Fie, fie, he'll never come.
USER: You say he has been thrown in the rivers, and has been grievously peaten as an old 'oman. Methinks there should be terrors in him, that he should not come. Methinks his flesh is punished; he shall have no desires.
BOT: So think I too.
USER: Devise but how you'll use him when he comes, And let us two devise to bring him thither.
BOT: Why, yet there want not many that do fear In deep of night to walk by this Herne's oak. But what of this?
USER: Marry, this is our device, That Falstaff at that oak shall meet with us, Disguised like Herne, with huge horns on his head.
BOT: Well, let it not be doubted but he'll come, And in this shape; when you have brought him thither, What shall be done with him? What is your plot?
USER: And till he tell the truth, Let the supposed fairies pinch him sound And burn him with their tapers.
BOT: The truth being known, We'll all present ourselves, dis-horn the spirit, And mock him home to Windsor.
USER: The children must Be practised well to this, or they'll ne'er do 't.
BOT: I will teach the children their behaviours, and I will be like a jackanapes also, to burn the knight with my taber.
USER: That will be excellent. I'll go buy them vizards.
BOT: My Nan shall be the queen of all the fairies, Finely attired in a robe of white.
USER: That silk will I go buy. Shall Master Slender steal my Nan away, And marry her at Eton. - Go, send to Falstaff straight.
BOT: Nay, I'll to him again in name of Brook. He'll tell me all his purpose. Sure, he'll come.
USER: Fear not you that. Go, get us properties And tricking for our fairies.
BOT: Let us about it. It is admirable pleasures and fery honest knaveries.

USER: What wouldst thou have, boor? What, thick-skin? Speak, breathe, discuss; brief, short, quick, snap.
BOT: Marry, sir, I come to speak with Sir John Falstaff from Master Slender.
USER: There's an old woman, a fat woman, gone up into his chamber. I'll be so bold as stay, sir, till she come down. I come to speak with her, indeed.
BOT: Ha? A fat woman? The knight may be robbed. I'll call. - Bully knight! Bully Sir John! Speak from thy lungs military. Art thou there? It is thine host, thine Ephesian, calls.
USER: Here's a Bohemian-Tartar tarries the coming down of thy fat woman. Let her descend, bully, let her descend. My chambers are honourable. Fie! Privacy? Fie!
BOT: There was, mine host, an old fat woman even now with me, but she's gone.
USER: Pray you, sir, was't not the wise woman of Brentford?
BOT: Ay, marry was it, mussel-shell. What would you with her?
USER: My master, sir, my Master Slender, sent to her, seeing her go through the streets, to know, sir, whether one Nym, sir, that beguiled him of a chain, had the chain or no.
BOT: I spake with the old woman about it.
USER: And what says she, I pray, sir?
BOT: Marry, she says that the very same man that beguiled Master Slender of his chain cozened him of it.
USER: I would I could have spoken with the woman herself. I had other things to have spoken with her too, from him.
BOT: What are they? Let us know.
USER: Ay, come. Quick.
BOT: I may not conceal them, sir.
USER: Conceal them, or thou diest.
BOT: Why, sir, they were nothing but about Mistress Anne Page, to know if it were my master's fortune to have her or no.
USER: 'Tis, 'tis his fortune.
BOT: What sir?
USER: To have her, or no. Go, say the woman told me so.
BOT: May I be bold to say so, sir?
USER: Ay, sir; like who more bold?
BOT: I thank your worship; I shall make my master glad with these tidings. HOST Thou art clerkly, thou art clerkly, Sir John. Was there a wise woman with thee?
USER: Ay, that there was, mine host; one that hath taught me more wit than ever I learned before in my life; and I paid nothing for it neither, but was paid for my learning. BARDOLPH Out, alas, sir, cozenage, mere cozenage!
BOT: Where be my horses? Speak well of them, varletto.
USER: Run away, with the cozeners. For so soon as I came beyond Eton, they threw me off from behind one of them, in a slough of mire, and set spurs and away, like three German devils, three Doctor Faustuses.
BOT: They are gone but to meet the Duke, villain, do not say they be fled. Germans are honest men. EVANS Where is mine host?
USER: What is the matter, sir?
BOT: Vere is mine host de Jarteer?
USER: Here, Master Doctor, in perplexity and doubtful dilemma.
BOT: From the two parties, forsooth.
USER: The devil take one party and his dam the other, and so they shall be both bestowed. I have suffered more for their sakes, more than the villainous inconstancy of man's disposition is able to bear.
BOT: And have not they suffered? Yes, I warrant, speciously one of them. Mistress Ford, good heart, is beaten black and blue, that you cannot see a white spot about her.
USER: Come up into my chamber.

USER: Master Fenton, talk not to me. My mind is heavy. I will give over all.
BOT: Yet hear me speak. Assist me in my purpose, And, as I am a gentleman, I'll give thee A hundred pound in gold more than your loss.
USER: I will hear you, Master Fenton, and I will, at the least, keep your counsel.
BOT: Which means she to deceive, father or mother?
USER: Both, my good host, to go along with me. And here it rests, that you'll procure the vicar To stay for me at church, 'twixt twelve and one, And, in the lawful name of marrying, To give our hearts united ceremony.
BOT: Well, husband your device; I'll to the vicar. Bring you the maid, you shall not lack a priest.
USER: So shall I evermore be bound to thee; Besides, I'll make a present recompense.

USER: Prithee, no more prattling. Go. I'll hold. This is the third time; I hope good luck lies in odd numbers. Away, go! They say there is divinity in odd numbers, either in nativity, chance, or death. Away!
BOT: I'll provide you a chain, and I'll do what I can to get you a pair of horns.
USER: Away, I say; time wears. Hold up your head, and mince. How now, Master Brook! Master Brook, the matter will be known tonight or never. Be you in the park about midnight, at Herne's oak, and you shall see wonders.
BOT: Went you not to her yesterday, sir, as you told me you had appointed?

USER: Come, come. We'll couch i' the castle ditch till we see the light of our fairies. Remember, son Slender, my daughter -
BOT: Ay, forsooth. I have spoke with her, and we have a nay-word how to know one another. I come to her in white and cry "mum"; she cries "budget"; and by that we know one another.
USER: That's good too. But what needs either your "mum" or her "budget"? The white will decipher her well enough. It hath struck ten o'clock.
BOT: The night is dark. Light and spirits will become it well. Heaven prosper our sport! No man means evil but the devil, and we shall know him by his horns. Let's away; follow me.

USER: Master Doctor, my daughter is in green. When you see your time, take her by the hand, away with her to the deanery, and dispatch it quickly. Go before into the park. We two must go together.
BOT: I know vat I have to do. Adieu.
USER: Fare you well, sir. My husband will not rejoice so much at the abuse of Falstaff as he will chafe at the doctor's marrying my daughter. But 'tis no matter. Better a little chiding than a great deal of heartbreak.
BOT: Where is Nan now, and her troop of fairies, and the Welsh devil Hugh?
USER: They are all couched in a pit hard by Herne's oak, with obscured lights, which, at the very instant of Falstaff's and our meeting, they will at once display to the night.
BOT: That cannot choose but amaze him.
USER: If he be not amazed, he will be mocked; if he be amazed, he will every way be mocked.
BOT: We'll betray him finely.
USER: Against such lewdsters and their lechery, Those that betray them do no treachery.
BOT: The hour draws on. To the oak, to the oak!

USER: Sir John? Art thou there, my deer, my male deer?
BOT: My doe with the black scut! Let the sky rain potatoes, let it thunder to the tune of "Greensleeves", hail kissing-comfits and snow eringoes; let there come a tempest of provocation, I will shelter me here.
USER: Mistress Page is come with me, sweetheart.
BOT: Alas, what noise?
USER: Heaven forgive our sins!
BOT: What should this be? MISTRESS FORD and MISTRESS PAGE. Away, away!
USER: I think the devil will not have me damned, lest the oil that's in me should set hell on fire; he would never else cross me thus. Satyr, Pistol as Hobgoblin, Anne Page and children as Fairies, carrying tapers.
BOT: Fairies, black, grey, green, and white, You moonshine revellers and shades of night, You orphan heirs of fixed destiny, Attend your office and your quality. Crier Hobgoblin, make the fairy oyes.
USER: Pray you, lock hand in hand, yourselves in order set; And twenty glow-worms shall our lanterns be, To guide our measure round about the tree. But stay, I smell a man of middle earth.
BOT: Heavens defend me from that Welsh fairy, lest he transform me to a piece of cheese!
USER: Vile worm, thou wast o'erlooked even in thy birth.
BOT: With trial-fire touch me his finger-end. If he be chaste, the flame will back descend And turn him to no pain; but if he start, It is the flesh of a corrupted heart.
USER: A trial, come.
BOT: Come, will this wood take fire?
USER: O, o, o!
BOT: Corrupt, corrupt, and tainted in desire! About him, fairies, sing a scornful rhyme, And, as you trip, still pinch him to your time.
USER: Nay, do not fly. I think we have watched you now. Will none but Herne the hunter serve your turn?
BOT: I pray you, come, hold up the jest no higher. - Now, good Sir John, how like you Windsor wives? See you these, husband? Do not these fair yokes Become the forest better than the town?
USER: Sir John, we have had ill luck, we could never meet. I will never take you for my love again, but I will always count you my deer.
BOT: I do begin to perceive that I am made an ass.
USER: Ay, and an ox too. Both the proofs are extant.
BOT: Sir John Falstaff, serve Got, and leave your desires, and fairies will not pinse you.
USER: Well said, fairy Hugh.
BOT: And leave you your jealousies too, I pray you.
USER: I will never mistrust my wife again, till thou art able to woo her in good English.
BOT: Seese is not good to give putter. Your belly is all putter.
USER: "Seese" and "putter"? Have I lived to stand at the taunt of one that makes fritters of English? This is enough to be the decay of lust and late-walking through the realm.
BOT: Why, Sir John, do you think, though we would have thrust virtue out of our hearts by the head and shoulders, and have given ourselves without scruple to hell, that ever the devil could have made you our delight?
USER: What, a hodge-pudding? A bag of flax?
BOT: A puffed man?
USER: Old, cold, withered, and of intolerable entrails?
BOT: And one that is as slanderous as Satan?
USER: And as poor as Job?
BOT: And as wicked as his wife?
USER: And given to fornications, and to taverns, and sack, and wine, and metheglins, and to drinkings and swearings and starings, pribbles and prabbles?
BOT: Well, I am your theme. You have the start of me. I am dejected, I am not able to answer the Welsh flannel. Ignorance itself is a plummet o'er me. Use me as you will.
USER: Yet be cheerful, knight. Thou shalt eat a posset tonight at my house, where I will desire thee to laugh at my wife, that now laughs at thee. Tell her Master Slender hath married her daughter.
BOT: this, Doctor Caius' wife. SLENDER Whoa, ho, ho, father Page!
USER: Son, how now! How now, son, have you dispatched?
BOT: Dispatched? I'll make the best in Gloucestershire know on't. Would I were hanged, la, else!
USER: Of what, son?
BOT: Upon my life, then, you took the wrong.
USER: What need you tell me that? I think so, when I took a boy for a girl. If I had been married to him, for all he was in woman's apparel, I would not have had him.
BOT: Why, this is your own folly. Did not I tell you how you should know my daughter by her garments?
USER: I went to her in white and cried "mum", and she cried "budget", as Anne and I had appointed, and yet it was not Anne, but a postmaster's boy.
BOT: Why, did you take her in green?
USER: Ay, by gar, and 'tis a boy. By gar, I'll raise all Windsor. FORD This is strange. Who hath got the right Anne?
BOT: My heart misgives me. Here comes Master Fenton. - How now, Master Fenton!
USER: Pardon, good father. Good my mother, pardon.
BOT: Now, mistress, how chance you went not with Master Slender?
USER: Why went you not with Master Doctor, maid?
BOT: Stand not amazed, here is no remedy. In love, the heavens themselves do guide the state. Money buys lands, and wives are sold by fate.
USER: I am glad, though you have ta'en a special stand to strike at me, that your arrow hath glanced.
BOT: Well, what remedy? Fenton, heaven give thee joy! What cannot be eschewed must be embraced.
USER: When night-dogs run, all sorts of deer are chased.
BOT: Well, I will muse no further. - Master Fenton, Heaven give you many, many merry days! Good husband, let us every one go home, And laugh this sport o'er by a country fire, Sir John and all.
USER: Let it be so, Sir John, To Master Brook you yet shall hold your word, For he tonight shall lie with Mistress Ford. A MIDSUMMER NIGHT'S DREAM Contents

USER: Four days will quickly steep themselves in night; Four nights will quickly dream away the time; And then the moon, like to a silver bow New bent in heaven, shall behold the night Of our solemnities.
BOT: Happy be Theseus, our renownèd Duke!
USER: Thanks, good Egeus. What's the news with thee?
BOT: So is Lysander.
USER: In himself he is. But in this kind, wanting your father's voice, The other must be held the worthier.
BOT: I would my father look'd but with my eyes.
USER: Rather your eyes must with his judgment look.
BOT: So will I grow, so live, so die, my lord, Ere I will yield my virgin patent up Unto his lordship, whose unwishèd yoke My soul consents not to give sovereignty.
USER: Relent, sweet Hermia; and, Lysander, yield Thy crazèd title to my certain right.
BOT: You have her father's love, Demetrius. Let me have Hermia's. Do you marry him.
USER: Scornful Lysander, true, he hath my love; And what is mine my love shall render him; And she is mine, and all my right of her I do estate unto Demetrius.
BOT: With duty and desire we follow you.
USER: How now, my love? Why is your cheek so pale? How chance the roses there do fade so fast?
BOT: Belike for want of rain, which I could well Beteem them from the tempest of my eyes.
USER: Ay me! For aught that I could ever read, Could ever hear by tale or history, The course of true love never did run smooth. But either it was different in blood -
BOT: O cross! Too high to be enthrall'd to low.
USER: Or else misgraffèd in respect of years -
BOT: O spite! Too old to be engag'd to young.
USER: Or else it stood upon the choice of friends -
BOT: O hell! to choose love by another's eyes!
USER: Keep promise, love. Look, here comes Helena.
BOT: God speed fair Helena! Whither away?
USER: I frown upon him, yet he loves me still.
BOT: O that your frowns would teach my smiles such skill!
USER: I give him curses, yet he gives me love.
BOT: O that my prayers could such affection move!
USER: The more I hate, the more he follows me.
BOT: The more I love, the more he hateth me.
USER: His folly, Helena, is no fault of mine.
BOT: None but your beauty; would that fault were mine!
USER: I will, my Hermia. Helena, adieu. As you on him, Demetrius dote on you!

USER: Is all our company here?
BOT: You were best to call them generally, man by man, according to the scrip.
USER: Here is the scroll of every man's name, which is thought fit through all Athens, to play in our interlude before the Duke and Duchess, on his wedding-day at night.
BOT: First, good Peter Quince, say what the play treats on; then read the names of the actors; and so grow to a point.
USER: Marry, our play is _The most lamentable comedy and most cruel death of Pyramus and Thisbe_.
BOT: A very good piece of work, I assure you, and a merry. Now, good Peter Quince, call forth your actors by the scroll. Masters, spread yourselves.
USER: Answer, as I call you. Nick Bottom, the weaver.
BOT: Ready. Name what part I am for, and proceed.
USER: You, Nick Bottom, are set down for Pyramus.
BOT: What is Pyramus - a lover, or a tyrant?
USER: A lover, that kills himself most gallantly for love.
BOT: Francis Flute, the bellows-mender.
USER: Here, Peter Quince.
BOT: Flute, you must take Thisbe on you.
USER: What is Thisbe? A wandering knight?
BOT: It is the lady that Pyramus must love.
USER: Nay, faith, let not me play a woman. I have a beard coming.
BOT: That's all one. You shall play it in a mask, and you may speak as small as you will.
USER: And I may hide my face, let me play Thisbe too. I'll speak in a monstrous little voice; 'Thisne, Thisne!' - 'Ah, Pyramus, my lover dear! thy Thisbe dear! and lady dear!'
BOT: No, no, you must play Pyramus; and, Flute, you Thisbe.
USER: Well, proceed.
BOT: Robin Starveling, the tailor.
USER: Here, Peter Quince.
BOT: Robin Starveling, you must play Thisbe's mother. Tom Snout, the tinker. SNOUT Here, Peter Quince.
USER: You, Pyramus' father; myself, Thisbe's father; Snug, the joiner, you, the lion's part. And, I hope here is a play fitted. SNUG Have you the lion's part written? Pray you, if it be, give it me, for I am slow of study.
BOT: You may do it extempore, for it is nothing but roaring.
USER: Let me play the lion too. I will roar that I will do any man's heart good to hear me. I will roar that I will make the Duke say 'Let him roar again, let him roar again.'
BOT: If you should do it too terribly, you would fright the Duchess and the ladies, that they would shriek; and that were enough to hang us all. ALL That would hang us every mother's son.
USER: You can play no part but Pyramus, for Pyramus is a sweet-faced man; a proper man as one shall see in a summer's day; a most lovely gentleman-like man. Therefore you must needs play Pyramus.
BOT: Well, I will undertake it. What beard were I best to play it in?
USER: Why, what you will.
BOT: I will discharge it in either your straw-colour beard, your orange-tawny beard, your purple-in-grain beard, or your French-crown-colour beard, your perfect yellow.
USER: We will meet, and there we may rehearse most obscenely and courageously. Take pains, be perfect; adieu.
BOT: At the Duke's oak we meet.
USER: Enough. Hold, or cut bow-strings.

USER: Ill met by moonlight, proud Titania.
BOT: What, jealous Oberon! Fairies, skip hence; I have forsworn his bed and company.
USER: Tarry, rash wanton; am not I thy lord?
BOT: Do you amend it, then. It lies in you. Why should Titania cross her Oberon? I do but beg a little changeling boy To be my henchman.
USER: How long within this wood intend you stay?
BOT: Perchance till after Theseus' wedding-day. If you will patiently dance in our round, And see our moonlight revels, go with us; If not, shun me, and I will spare your haunts.
USER: Give me that boy and I will go with thee.
BOT: Not for thy fairy kingdom. Fairies, away. We shall chide downright if I longer stay.
USER: I remember.
BOT: I'll put a girdle round about the earth In forty minutes.
USER: You draw me, you hard-hearted adamant, But yet you draw not iron, for my heart Is true as steel. Leave you your power to draw, And I shall have no power to follow you.
BOT: Do I entice you? Do I speak you fair? Or rather do I not in plainest truth Tell you I do not, nor I cannot love you?
USER: Tempt not too much the hatred of my spirit; For I am sick when I do look on thee.
BOT: And I am sick when I look not on you.
USER: I'll run from thee and hide me in the brakes, And leave thee to the mercy of wild beasts.
BOT: I will not stay thy questions. Let me go, Or if thou follow me, do not believe But I shall do thee mischief in the wood.
USER: Fare thee well, nymph. Ere he do leave this grove, Thou shalt fly him, and he shall seek thy love. Hast thou the flower there? Welcome, wanderer.
BOT: Ay, there it is.
USER: Fear not, my lord, your servant shall do so.

USER: You spotted snakes with double tongue, Thorny hedgehogs, be not seen; Newts and blind-worms do no wrong, Come not near our Fairy Queen:
BOT: Philomel, with melody, Sing in our sweet lullaby: Lulla, lulla, lullaby; lulla, lulla, lullaby. Never harm, nor spell, nor charm, Come our lovely lady nigh; So good night, with lullaby.
USER: Weaving spiders, come not here; Hence, you long-legg'd spinners, hence. Beetles black, approach not near; Worm nor snail do no offence.
BOT: Philomel with melody, &c.
USER: Hence away! Now all is well. One aloof stand sentinel.
BOT: Fair love, you faint with wand'ring in the wood. And, to speak troth, I have forgot our way. We'll rest us, Hermia, if you think it good, And tarry for the comfort of the day.
USER: Be it so, Lysander: find you out a bed, For I upon this bank will rest my head.
BOT: One turf shall serve as pillow for us both; One heart, one bed, two bosoms, and one troth.
USER: Nay, good Lysander; for my sake, my dear, Lie further off yet, do not lie so near.
BOT: Amen, amen, to that fair prayer say I; And then end life when I end loyalty! Here is my bed. Sleep give thee all his rest!
USER: With half that wish the wisher's eyes be pressed!
BOT: Stay, though thou kill me, sweet Demetrius.
USER: I charge thee, hence, and do not haunt me thus.
BOT: O, wilt thou darkling leave me? Do not so.
USER: Stay, on thy peril; I alone will go.
BOT: Transparent Helena! Nature shows art, That through thy bosom makes me see thy heart. Where is Demetrius? O, how fit a word Is that vile name to perish on my sword!
USER: Do not say so, Lysander, say not so. What though he love your Hermia? Lord, what though? Yet Hermia still loves you. Then be content.

USER: Are we all met?
BOT: Pat, pat; and here's a marvellous convenient place for our rehearsal. This green plot shall be our stage, this hawthorn brake our tiring-house; and we will do it in action, as we will do it before the Duke.
USER: Peter Quince?
BOT: What sayest thou, bully Bottom?
USER: There are things in this comedy of Pyramus and Thisbe that will never please. First, Pyramus must draw a sword to kill himself; which the ladies cannot abide. How answer you that? SNOUT By'r lakin, a parlous fear.
BOT: I believe we must leave the killing out, when all is done.
USER: Well, we will have such a prologue; and it shall be written in eight and six.
BOT: No, make it two more; let it be written in eight and eight. SNOUT Will not the ladies be afeard of the lion?
USER: I fear it, I promise you.
BOT: Well, it shall be so. But there is two hard things: that is, to bring the moonlight into a chamber, for you know, Pyramus and Thisbe meet by moonlight. SNOUT Doth the moon shine that night we play our play?
USER: A calendar, a calendar! Look in the almanack; find out moonshine, find out moonshine.
BOT: Yes, it doth shine that night.
USER: Why, then may you leave a casement of the great chamber window, where we play, open; and the moon may shine in at the casement.
BOT: If that may be, then all is well. Come, sit down, every mother's son, and rehearse your parts. Pyramus, you begin: when you have spoken your speech, enter into that brake; and so everyone according to his cue.
USER: What hempen homespuns have we swaggering here, So near the cradle of the Fairy Queen? What, a play toward? I'll be an auditor; An actor too perhaps, if I see cause.
BOT: Speak, Pyramus. - Thisbe, stand forth.
USER: _Thisbe, the flowers of odious savours sweet_
BOT: Odours, odours.
USER: _. . . odours savours sweet. So hath thy breath, my dearest Thisbe dear. But hark, a voice! Stay thou but here awhile, And by and by I will to thee appear._
BOT: A stranger Pyramus than e'er played here!
USER: Must I speak now?
BOT: Ay, marry, must you, For you must understand he goes but to see a noise that he heard, and is to come again.
USER: Ninus' tomb, man! Why, you must not speak that yet. That you answer to Pyramus. You speak all your part at once, cues, and all. - Pyramus enter! Your cue is past; it is 'never tire.'
BOT: O, _As true as truest horse, that yet would never tire._
USER: _If I were fair, Thisbe, I were only thine._
BOT: O monstrous! O strange! We are haunted. Pray, masters, fly, masters! Help!
USER: Why do they run away? This is a knavery of them to make me afeard. SNOUT O Bottom, thou art changed! What do I see on thee?
BOT: What do you see? You see an ass-head of your own, do you?
USER: Bless thee, Bottom! bless thee! Thou art translated.
BOT: I pray thee, gentle mortal, sing again. Mine ear is much enamour'd of thy note. So is mine eye enthrallèd to thy shape; And thy fair virtue's force perforce doth move me, On the first view, to say, to swear, I love thee.
USER: Thou art as wise as thou art beautiful.
BOT: Not so, neither; but if I had wit enough to get out of this wood, I have enough to serve mine own turn.
USER: Ready.
BOT: And I.
USER: And I.
BOT: And I.
USER: Where shall we go?
BOT: Hail, mortal!
USER: Hail!
BOT: Hail!
USER: Hail!
BOT: I cry your worships mercy, heartily. - I beseech your worship's name.
USER: Cobweb.
BOT: I shall desire you of more acquaintance, good Master Cobweb. If I cut my finger, I shall make bold with you. - Your name, honest gentleman?
USER: Peaseblossom.
BOT: I pray you, commend me to Mistress Squash, your mother, and to Master Peascod, your father. Good Master Peaseblossom, I shall desire you of more acquaintance too. - Your name, I beseech you, sir?
USER: Mustardseed.
BOT: Come, wait upon him; lead him to my bower. The moon, methinks, looks with a watery eye, And when she weeps, weeps every little flower, Lamenting some enforced chastity. Tie up my love's tongue, bring him silently.

USER: I wonder if Titania be awak'd; Then, what it was that next came in her eye, Which she must dote on in extremity. Here comes my messenger. How now, mad spirit? What night-rule now about this haunted grove?
BOT: This falls out better than I could devise. But hast thou yet latch'd the Athenian's eyes With the love-juice, as I did bid thee do?
USER: I took him sleeping - that is finish'd too - And the Athenian woman by his side, That, when he wak'd, of force she must be ey'd.
BOT: Stand close. This is the same Athenian.
USER: This is the woman, but not this the man.
BOT: O why rebuke you him that loves you so? Lay breath so bitter on your bitter foe.
USER: So should the murder'd look, and so should I, Pierc'd through the heart with your stern cruelty. Yet you, the murderer, look as bright, as clear, As yonder Venus in her glimmering sphere.
BOT: What's this to my Lysander? Where is he? Ah, good Demetrius, wilt thou give him me?
USER: I had rather give his carcass to my hounds.
BOT: You spend your passion on a mispris'd mood: I am not guilty of Lysander's blood; Nor is he dead, for aught that I can tell.
USER: I pray thee, tell me then that he is well.
BOT: And if I could, what should I get therefore?
USER: A privilege never to see me more. And from thy hated presence part I so: See me no more, whether he be dead or no.
BOT: What hast thou done? Thou hast mistaken quite, And laid the love-juice on some true-love's sight. Of thy misprision must perforce ensue Some true love turn'd, and not a false turn'd true.
USER: Then fate o'er-rules, that, one man holding troth, A million fail, confounding oath on oath.
BOT: I go, I go; look how I go, Swifter than arrow from the Tartar's bow.
USER: Flower of this purple dye, Hit with Cupid's archery, Sink in apple of his eye. When his love he doth espy, Let her shine as gloriously As the Venus of the sky. - When thou wak'st, if she be by, Beg of her for remedy.
BOT: Captain of our fairy band, Helena is here at hand, And the youth mistook by me, Pleading for a lover's fee. Shall we their fond pageant see? Lord, what fools these mortals be!
USER: Stand aside. The noise they make Will cause Demetrius to awake.
BOT: Then will two at once woo one. That must needs be sport alone; And those things do best please me That befall prepost'rously.
USER: I had no judgment when to her I swore.
BOT: Nor none, in my mind, now you give her o'er.
USER: Demetrius loves her, and he loves not you.
BOT: Never did mockers waste more idle breath.
USER: Lysander, keep thy Hermia; I will none. If e'er I lov'd her, all that love is gone. My heart to her but as guest-wise sojourn'd; And now to Helen is it home return'd, There to remain.
BOT: Helen, it is not so.
USER: Disparage not the faith thou dost not know, Lest to thy peril thou aby it dear. Look where thy love comes; yonder is thy dear.
BOT: Why should he stay whom love doth press to go?
USER: What love could press Lysander from my side?
BOT: You speak not as you think; it cannot be.
USER: I am amazèd at your passionate words: I scorn you not; it seems that you scorn me.
BOT: I understand not what you mean by this.
USER: Stay, gentle Helena; hear my excuse; My love, my life, my soul, fair Helena!
BOT: O excellent!
USER: Sweet, do not scorn her so.
BOT: If she cannot entreat, I can compel.
USER: I say I love thee more than he can do.
BOT: If thou say so, withdraw, and prove it too.
USER: Quick, come.
BOT: Lysander, whereto tends all this?
USER: Away, you Ethiope!
BOT: No, no. He will Seem to break loose. Take on as you would follow, But yet come not. You are a tame man, go!
USER: Hang off, thou cat, thou burr! Vile thing, let loose, Or I will shake thee from me like a serpent.
BOT: Why are you grown so rude? What change is this, Sweet love?
USER: Thy love? Out, tawny Tartar, out! Out, loathèd medicine! O hated potion, hence!
BOT: Do you not jest?
USER: Yes, sooth, and so do you.
BOT: Demetrius, I will keep my word with thee.
USER: I would I had your bond; for I perceive A weak bond holds you; I'll not trust your word.
BOT: What, should I hurt her, strike her, kill her dead? Although I hate her, I'll not harm her so.
USER: Ay, by my life; And never did desire to see thee more. Therefore be out of hope, of question, of doubt; Be certain, nothing truer; 'tis no jest That I do hate thee and love Helena.
BOT: O me! You juggler! You cankerblossom! You thief of love! What! have you come by night And stol'n my love's heart from him?
USER: Fine, i' faith! Have you no modesty, no maiden shame, No touch of bashfulness? What, will you tear Impatient answers from my gentle tongue? Fie, fie, you counterfeit, you puppet, you!
BOT: Lower! Hark, again.
USER: Why, get you gone. Who is't that hinders you?
BOT: A foolish heart that I leave here behind.
USER: What! with Lysander?
BOT: With Demetrius.
USER: Be not afraid; she shall not harm thee, Helena.
BOT: No, sir, she shall not, though you take her part.
USER: O, when she's angry, she is keen and shrewd. She was a vixen when she went to school, And though she be but little, she is fierce.
BOT: Little again! Nothing but low and little? Why will you suffer her to flout me thus? Let me come to her.
USER: Get you gone, you dwarf; You minimus, of hind'ring knot-grass made; You bead, you acorn.
BOT: You are too officious In her behalf that scorns your services. Let her alone. Speak not of Helena; Take not her part; for if thou dost intend Never so little show of love to her, Thou shalt aby it.
USER: Now she holds me not. Now follow, if thou dar'st, to try whose right, Of thine or mine, is most in Helena.
BOT: Follow! Nay, I'll go with thee, cheek by jole.
USER: You, mistress, all this coil is long of you. Nay, go not back.
BOT: I will not trust you, I, Nor longer stay in your curst company. Your hands than mine are quicker for a fray. My legs are longer though, to run away.
USER: I am amaz'd, and know not what to say.
BOT: This is thy negligence: still thou mistak'st, Or else commit'st thy knaveries willfully.
USER: Up and down, up and down, I will lead them up and down. I am fear'd in field and town. Goblin, lead them up and down. Here comes one.
BOT: Where art thou, proud Demetrius? Speak thou now.
USER: Here, villain, drawn and ready. Where art thou?
BOT: I will be with thee straight.
USER: Follow me then to plainer ground.
BOT: Lysander, speak again. Thou runaway, thou coward, art thou fled? Speak. In some bush? Where dost thou hide thy head?
USER: Thou coward, art thou bragging to the stars, Telling the bushes that thou look'st for wars, And wilt not come? Come, recreant, come, thou child! I'll whip thee with a rod. He is defil'd That draws a sword on thee.
BOT: Yea, art thou there?
USER: Follow my voice; we'll try no manhood here.
BOT: Ho, ho, ho! Coward, why com'st thou not?
USER: Abide me, if thou dar'st; for well I wot Thou runn'st before me, shifting every place, And dar'st not stand, nor look me in the face. Where art thou?
BOT: Come hither; I am here.
USER: Nay, then, thou mock'st me. Thou shalt buy this dear If ever I thy face by daylight see: Now go thy way. Faintness constraineth me To measure out my length on this cold bed. By day's approach look to be visited.
BOT: Yet but three? Come one more. Two of both kinds makes up four. Here she comes, curst and sad. Cupid is a knavish lad Thus to make poor females mad.

USER: Come, sit thee down upon this flowery bed, While I thy amiable cheeks do coy, And stick musk-roses in thy sleek smooth head, And kiss thy fair large ears, my gentle joy.
BOT: Where's Peaseblossom?
USER: Ready.
BOT: Scratch my head, Peaseblossom. Where's Monsieur Cobweb?
USER: Ready.
BOT: Ready.
USER: Give me your neaf, Monsieur Mustardseed. Pray you, leave your courtesy, good monsieur.
BOT: What's your will?
USER: What, wilt thou hear some music, my sweet love?
BOT: I have a reasonable good ear in music. Let us have the tongs and the bones.
USER: Or say, sweet love, what thou desirest to eat.
BOT: Truly, a peck of provender; I could munch your good dry oats. Methinks I have a great desire to a bottle of hay: good hay, sweet hay, hath no fellow.
USER: I have a venturous fairy that shall seek The squirrel's hoard, and fetch thee new nuts.
BOT: I had rather have a handful or two of dried peas. But, I pray you, let none of your people stir me; I have an exposition of sleep come upon me.
USER: My Oberon, what visions have I seen! Methought I was enamour'd of an ass.
BOT: There lies your love.
USER: How came these things to pass? O, how mine eyes do loathe his visage now!
BOT: Silence awhile. - Robin, take off this head. Titania, music call; and strike more dead Than common sleep, of all these five the sense.
USER: Music, ho, music, such as charmeth sleep.
BOT: Now when thou wak'st, with thine own fool's eyes peep.
USER: Fairy king, attend and mark. I do hear the morning lark.
BOT: Then, my queen, in silence sad, Trip we after night's shade. We the globe can compass soon, Swifter than the wand'ring moon.
USER: Come, my lord, and in our flight, Tell me how it came this night That I sleeping here was found With these mortals on the ground.
BOT: My lord, this is my daughter here asleep, And this Lysander; this Demetrius is; This Helena, old Nedar's Helena: I wonder of their being here together.
USER: No doubt they rose up early to observe The rite of May; and, hearing our intent, Came here in grace of our solemnity. But speak, Egeus; is not this the day That Hermia should give answer of her choice?
BOT: It is, my lord.
USER: Go, bid the huntsmen wake them with their horns. Horns, and shout within. Demetrius, Lysander, Hermia and Helena wake and start up. Good morrow, friends. Saint Valentine is past. Begin these wood-birds but to couple now?
BOT: Pardon, my lord. He and the rest kneel to Theseus.
USER: I pray you all, stand up. I know you two are rival enemies. How comes this gentle concord in the world, That hatred is so far from jealousy To sleep by hate, and fear no enmity?
BOT: These things seem small and undistinguishable, Like far-off mountains turnèd into clouds.
USER: Methinks I see these things with parted eye, When everything seems double.
BOT: So methinks. And I have found Demetrius like a jewel, Mine own, and not mine own.
USER: Are you sure That we are awake? It seems to me That yet we sleep, we dream. Do not you think The Duke was here, and bid us follow him?
BOT: Yea, and my father.
USER: And Hippolyta.
BOT: And he did bid us follow to the temple.
USER: Why, then, we are awake: let's follow him, And by the way let us recount our dreams.

USER: Have you sent to Bottom's house? Is he come home yet?
BOT: He cannot be heard of. Out of doubt he is transported.
USER: If he come not, then the play is marred. It goes not forward, doth it?
BOT: It is not possible. You have not a man in all Athens able to discharge Pyramus but he.
USER: No, he hath simply the best wit of any handicraft man in Athens.
BOT: Yea, and the best person too, and he is a very paramour for a sweet voice.
USER: Where are these lads? Where are these hearts?
BOT: Bottom! O most courageous day! O most happy hour!
USER: Masters, I am to discourse wonders: but ask me not what; for if I tell you, I am not true Athenian. I will tell you everything, right as it fell out.
BOT: Let us hear, sweet Bottom.

USER: 'Tis strange, my Theseus, that these lovers speak of.
BOT: But all the story of the night told over, And all their minds transfigur'd so together, More witnesseth than fancy's images, And grows to something of great constancy; But, howsoever, strange and admirable.
USER: Here come the lovers, full of joy and mirth. Joy, gentle friends, joy and fresh days of love Accompany your hearts!
BOT: More than to us Wait in your royal walks, your board, your bed!
USER: Here, mighty Theseus.
BOT: Say, what abridgment have you for this evening? What masque? What music? How shall we beguile The lazy time, if not with some delight?
USER: There is a brief how many sports are ripe. Make choice of which your Highness will see first.
BOT: What are they that do play it?
USER: Hard-handed men that work in Athens here, Which never labour'd in their minds till now; And now have toil'd their unbreath'd memories With this same play against your nuptial.
BOT: And we will hear it.
USER: No, my noble lord, It is not for you: I have heard it over, And it is nothing, nothing in the world; Unless you can find sport in their intents, Extremely stretch'd and conn'd with cruel pain To do you service.
BOT: I will hear that play; For never anything can be amiss When simpleness and duty tender it. Go, bring them in: and take your places, ladies.
USER: I love not to see wretchedness o'ercharged, And duty in his service perishing.
BOT: Why, gentle sweet, you shall see no such thing.
USER: He says they can do nothing in this kind.
BOT: So please your grace, the Prologue is address'd.
USER: Let him approach. Flourish of trumpets. Enter the Prologue.

USER: This fellow doth not stand upon points.
BOT: He hath rid his prologue like a rough colt; he knows not the stop. A good moral, my lord: it is not enough to speak, but to speak true.
USER: Indeed he hath played on this prologue like a child on a recorder; a sound, but not in government.
BOT: His speech was like a tangled chain; nothing impaired, but all disordered. Who is next?

USER: I wonder if the lion be to speak.
BOT: No wonder, my lord. One lion may, when many asses do.
USER: Would you desire lime and hair to speak better?
BOT: It is the wittiest partition that ever I heard discourse, my lord.
USER: Pyramus draws near the wall; silence.
BOT: The wall, methinks, being sensible, should curse again.
USER: No, in truth, sir, he should not. 'Deceiving me' is Thisbe's cue: she is to enter now, and I am to spy her through the wall. You shall see it will fall pat as I told you. Yonder she comes.
BOT: O wall, full often hast thou heard my moans, For parting my fair Pyramus and me. My cherry lips have often kiss'd thy stones, Thy stones with lime and hair knit up in thee.
USER: I see a voice; now will I to the chink, To spy an I can hear my Thisbe's face. Thisbe?
BOT: My love thou art, my love I think.
USER: Think what thou wilt, I am thy lover's grace; And like Limander am I trusty still.
BOT: And I like Helen, till the fates me kill.
USER: Not Shafalus to Procrus was so true.
BOT: As Shafalus to Procrus, I to you.
USER: O kiss me through the hole of this vile wall.
BOT: I kiss the wall's hole, not your lips at all.
USER: Wilt thou at Ninny's tomb meet me straightway?
BOT: 'Tide life, 'tide death, I come without delay.
USER: Thus have I, Wall, my part discharged so; And, being done, thus Wall away doth go.
BOT: Now is the mural down between the two neighbours.
USER: No remedy, my lord, when walls are so wilful to hear without warning.
BOT: This is the silliest stuff that ever I heard.
USER: The best in this kind are but shadows; and the worst are no worse, if imagination amend them.
BOT: It must be your imagination then, and not theirs.
USER: If we imagine no worse of them than they of themselves, they may pass for excellent men. Here come two noble beasts in, a man and a lion.
BOT: A very gentle beast, and of a good conscience.
USER: The very best at a beast, my lord, that e'er I saw.
BOT: This lion is a very fox for his valour.
USER: True; and a goose for his discretion.
BOT: Not so, my lord, for his valour cannot carry his discretion, and the fox carries the goose.
USER: His discretion, I am sure, cannot carry his valour; for the goose carries not the fox. It is well; leave it to his discretion, and let us listen to the moon.
BOT: This lanthorn doth the hornèd moon present.
USER: He should have worn the horns on his head.
BOT: He is no crescent, and his horns are invisible within the circumference.
USER: This lanthorn doth the hornèd moon present; Myself the man i' the moon do seem to be.
BOT: This is the greatest error of all the rest; the man should be put into the lantern. How is it else the man i' the moon?
USER: He dares not come there for the candle, for you see, it is already in snuff.
BOT: I am aweary of this moon. Would he would change!
USER: It appears by his small light of discretion that he is in the wane; but yet, in courtesy, in all reason, we must stay the time.
BOT: Proceed, Moon.
USER: All that I have to say, is to tell you that the lantern is the moon; I the man i' the moon; this thorn-bush my thorn-bush; and this dog my dog.
BOT: Why, all these should be in the lantern, for all these are in the moon. But silence; here comes Thisbe.
USER: This is old Ninny's tomb. Where is my love?
BOT: Oh!
USER: Well roared, Lion.
BOT: Well run, Thisbe.
USER: Well shone, Moon. Truly, the moon shines with a good grace.
BOT: Well moused, Lion.
USER: And then came Pyramus.
BOT: And so the lion vanished.
USER: This passion, and the death of a dear friend, would go near to make a man look sad.
BOT: Beshrew my heart, but I pity the man.
USER: No die, but an ace, for him; for he is but one.
BOT: Less than an ace, man; for he is dead, he is nothing.
USER: With the help of a surgeon he might yet recover and prove an ass.
BOT: How chance Moonshine is gone before Thisbe comes back and finds her lover?
USER: She will find him by starlight. Here she comes, and her passion ends the play.
BOT: Methinks she should not use a long one for such a Pyramus. I hope she will be brief.
USER: A mote will turn the balance, which Pyramus, which Thisbe, is the better: he for a man, God warrant us; she for a woman, God bless us!
BOT: She hath spied him already with those sweet eyes.
USER: And thus she means, _videlicet_ -
BOT: Moonshine and Lion are left to bury the dead.
USER: Ay, and Wall too.
BOT: No, I assure you; the wall is down that parted their fathers. Will it please you to see the epilogue, or to hear a Bergomask dance between two of our company?
USER: Through the house give glimmering light, By the dead and drowsy fire. Every elf and fairy sprite Hop as light as bird from brier, And this ditty after me, Sing and dance it trippingly.
BOT: First rehearse your song by rote, To each word a warbling note; Hand in hand, with fairy grace, Will we sing, and bless this place.

USER: I learn in this letter that Don Pedro of Arragon comes this night to Messina.
BOT: He is very near by this: he was not three leagues off when I left him.
USER: How many gentlemen have you lost in this action?
BOT: But few of any sort, and none of name.
USER: A victory is twice itself when the achiever brings home full numbers. I find here that Don Pedro hath bestowed much honour on a young Florentine called Claudio.
BOT: He hath an uncle here in Messina will be very much glad of it.
USER: I have already delivered him letters, and there appears much joy in him; even so much that joy could not show itself modest enough without a badge of bitterness.
BOT: Did he break out into tears?
USER: In great measure.
BOT: A kind overflow of kindness. There are no faces truer than those that are so washed; how much better is it to weep at joy than to joy at weeping!
USER: I pray you, is Signior Mountanto returned from the wars or no?
BOT: I know none of that name, lady: there was none such in the army of any sort.
USER: What is he that you ask for, niece?
BOT: My cousin means Signior Benedick of Padua.
USER: O! he is returned, and as pleasant as ever he was.
BOT: Faith, niece, you tax Signior Benedick too much; but he'll be meet with you, I doubt it not.
USER: He hath done good service, lady, in these wars.
BOT: You had musty victual, and he hath holp to eat it; he is a very valiant trencher-man; he hath an excellent stomach.
USER: And a good soldier too, lady.
BOT: And a good soldier to a lady; but what is he to a lord?
USER: A lord to a lord, a man to a man; stuffed with all honourable virtues.
BOT: It is so indeed; he is no less than a stuffed man; but for the stuffing, - well, we are all mortal.
USER: You must not, sir, mistake my niece. There is a kind of merry war betwixt Signior Benedick and her; they never meet but there's a skirmish of wit between them.
BOT: Is't possible?
USER: Very easily possible: he wears his faith but as the fashion of his hat; it ever changes with the next block.
BOT: I see, lady, the gentleman is not in your books.
USER: No; and he were, I would burn my study. But I pray you, who is his companion? Is there no young squarer now that will make a voyage with him to the devil?
BOT: He is most in the company of the right noble Claudio.
USER: I will hold friends with you, lady.
BOT: Do, good friend.
USER: You will never run mad, niece.
BOT: No, not till a hot January.
USER: Don Pedro is approached. Balthasar and Others.
BOT: Good Signior Leonato, you are come to meet your trouble: the fashion of the world is to avoid cost, and you encounter it.
USER: Never came trouble to my house in the likeness of your Grace, for trouble being gone, comfort should remain; but when you depart from me, sorrow abides and happiness takes his leave.
BOT: You embrace your charge too willingly. I think this is your daughter.
USER: Her mother hath many times told me so.
BOT: Were you in doubt, sir, that you asked her?
USER: Signior Benedick, no; for then were you a child.
BOT: You have it full, Benedick: we may guess by this what you are, being a man. Truly the lady fathers herself. Be happy, lady, for you are like an honourable father.
USER: If Signior Leonato be her father, she would not have his head on her shoulders for all Messina, as like him as she is.
BOT: I wonder that you will still be talking, Signior Benedick: nobody marks you.
USER: What! my dear Lady Disdain, are you yet living?
BOT: Is it possible Disdain should die while she hath such meet food to feed it as Signior Benedick? Courtesy itself must convert to disdain if you come in her presence.
USER: Then is courtesy a turncoat. But it is certain I am loved of all ladies, only you excepted; and I would I could find in my heart that I had not a hard heart; for, truly, I love none.
BOT: A dear happiness to women: they would else have been troubled with a pernicious suitor. I thank God and my cold blood, I am of your humour for that. I had rather hear my dog bark at a crow than a man swear he loves me.
USER: God keep your Ladyship still in that mind; so some gentleman or other shall scape a predestinate scratched face.
BOT: Scratching could not make it worse, and 'twere such a face as yours were.
USER: Well, you are a rare parrot-teacher.
BOT: A bird of my tongue is better than a beast of yours.
USER: I would my horse had the speed of your tongue, and so good a continuer. But keep your way, i' God's name; I have done.
BOT: You always end with a jade's trick: I know you of old.
USER: If you swear, my lord, you shall not be forsworn. [To Don John] Let me bid you welcome, my lord: being reconciled to the Prince your brother, I owe you all duty.
BOT: I thank you: I am not of many words, but I thank you.
USER: Please it your Grace lead on?
BOT: Your hand, Leonato; we will go together.
USER: Benedick, didst thou note the daughter of Signior Leonato?
BOT: I noted her not; but I looked on her.
USER: Is she not a modest young lady?
BOT: Do you question me, as an honest man should do, for my simple true judgment; or would you have me speak after my custom, as being a professed tyrant to their sex?
USER: No; I pray thee speak in sober judgment.
BOT: Thou thinkest I am in sport: I pray thee tell me truly how thou likest her.
USER: Would you buy her, that you enquire after her?
BOT: Can the world buy such a jewel?
USER: In mine eye she is the sweetest lady that ever I looked on.
BOT: I would scarce trust myself, though I had sworn to the contrary, if Hero would be my wife.
USER: What secret hath held you here, that you followed not to Leonato's?
BOT: I would your Grace would constrain me to tell.
USER: I charge thee on thy allegiance.
BOT: If this were so, so were it uttered.
USER: Like the old tale, my lord: 'it is not so, nor 'twas not so; but indeed, God forbid it should be so.'
BOT: If my passion change not shortly, God forbid it should be otherwise.
USER: Amen, if you love her; for the lady is very well worthy.
BOT: You speak this to fetch me in, my lord.
USER: By my troth, I speak my thought.
BOT: And in faith, my lord, I spoke mine.
USER: And by my two faiths and troths, my lord, I spoke mine.
BOT: That I love her, I feel.
USER: That she is worthy, I know.
BOT: That I neither feel how she should be loved, nor know how she should be worthy, is the opinion that fire cannot melt out of me: I will die in it at the stake.
USER: Thou wast ever an obstinate heretic in the despite of beauty.
BOT: And never could maintain his part but in the force of his will.
USER: I shall see thee, ere I die, look pale with love.
BOT: Well, if ever thou dost fall from this faith, thou wilt prove a notable argument.
USER: If I do, hang me in a bottle like a cat and shoot at me; and he that hits me, let him be clapped on the shoulder and called Adam.
BOT: Well, as time shall try: 'In time the savage bull doth bear the yoke.'
USER: If this should ever happen, thou wouldst be horn-mad.
BOT: Nay, if Cupid have not spent all his quiver in Venice, thou wilt quake for this shortly.
USER: I look for an earthquake too then.
BOT: Well, you will temporize with the hours. In the meantime, good Signior Benedick, repair to Leonato's: commend me to him and tell him I will not fail him at supper; for indeed he hath made great preparation.
USER: I have almost matter enough in me for such an embassage; and so I commit you -
BOT: To the tuition of God: from my house, if I had it, -
USER: The sixth of July: your loving friend, Benedick.
BOT: Nay, mock not, mock not. The body of your discourse is sometime guarded with fragments, and the guards are but slightly basted on neither: ere you flout old ends any further, examine your conscience: and so I leave you.
USER: My liege, your Highness now may do me good.
BOT: My love is thine to teach: teach it but how, And thou shalt see how apt it is to learn Any hard lesson that may do thee good.
USER: Hath Leonato any son, my lord?
BOT: No child but Hero; she's his only heir. Dost thou affect her, Claudio?
USER: How sweetly you do minister to love, That know love's grief by his complexion! But lest my liking might too sudden seem, I would have salv'd it with a longer treatise.

USER: How now, brother? Where is my cousin your son? Hath he provided this music?
BOT: He is very busy about it. But, brother, I can tell you strange news that you yet dreamt not of.
USER: Are they good?
BOT: Hath the fellow any wit that told you this?
USER: A good sharp fellow: I will send for him; and question him yourself.

USER: What the good-year, my lord! why are you thus out of measure sad?
BOT: There is no measure in the occasion that breeds; therefore the sadness is without limit.
USER: You should hear reason.
BOT: And when I have heard it, what blessings brings it?
USER: If not a present remedy, at least a patient sufferance.
BOT: Can you make no use of your discontent?
USER: I make all use of it, for I use it only. Who comes here? What news, Borachio?
BOT: I came yonder from a great supper: the Prince your brother is royally entertained by Leonato; and I can give you intelligence of an intended marriage.
USER: Will it serve for any model to build mischief on? What is he for a fool that betroths himself to unquietness?
BOT: Marry, it is your brother's right hand.
USER: Who? the most exquisite Claudio?
BOT: Even he.
USER: A proper squire! And who, and who? which way looks he?
BOT: Marry, on Hero, the daughter and heir of Leonato.
USER: A very forward March-chick! How came you to this?
BOT: Come, come; let us thither: this may prove food to my displeasure. That young start-up hath all the glory of my overthrow: if I can cross him any way, I bless myself every way. You are both sure, and will assist me?
USER: To the death, my lord.
BOT: Let us to the great supper: their cheer is the greater that I am subdued. Would the cook were of my mind! Shall we go to prove what's to be done?
USER: We'll wait upon your Lordship.

USER: Was not Count John here at supper?
BOT: I saw him not.
USER: How tartly that gentleman looks! I never can see him but I am heart-burned an hour after.
BOT: He is of a very melancholy disposition.
USER: He were an excellent man that were made just in the mid-way between him and Benedick: the one is too like an image, and says nothing; and the other too like my lady's eldest son, evermore tattling.
BOT: Then half Signior Benedick's tongue in Count John's mouth, and half Count John's melancholy in Signior Benedick's face -
USER: With a good leg and a good foot, uncle, and money enough in his purse, such a man would win any woman in the world if a' could get her good will.
BOT: By my troth, niece, thou wilt never get thee a husband, if thou be so shrewd of thy tongue.
USER: In faith, she's too curst.
BOT: Too curst is more than curst: I shall lessen God's sending that way; for it is said, 'God sends a curst cow short horns;' but to a cow too curst he sends none.
USER: So, by being too curst, God will send you no horns?
BOT: Just, if he send me no husband; for the which blessing I am at him upon my knees every morning and evening. Lord! I could not endure a husband with a beard on his face: I had rather lie in the woollen.
USER: You may light on a husband that hath no beard.
BOT: Well then, go you into hell?
USER: Yes, faith; it is my cousin's duty to make curtsy, and say, 'Father, as it please you:' - but yet for all that, cousin, let him be a handsome fellow, or else make another curtsy, and say, 'Father, as it please me.'
BOT: Well, niece, I hope to see you one day fitted with a husband.
USER: Daughter, remember what I told you: if the Prince do solicit you in that kind, you know your answer.
BOT: Cousin, you apprehend passing shrewdly.
USER: I have a good eye, uncle: I can see a church by daylight.
BOT: The revellers are entering, brother: make good room. John, Borachio, Margaret, Ursula and Others, masked.
USER: Lady, will you walk about with your friend?
BOT: So you walk softly and look sweetly and say nothing, I am yours for the walk; and especially when I walk away.
USER: With me in your company?
BOT: I may say so, when I please.
USER: And when please you to say so?
BOT: When I like your favour; for God defend the lute should be like the case!
USER: My visor is Philemon's roof; within the house is Jove.
BOT: Why, then, your visor should be thatch'd.
USER: Speak low, if you speak love.
BOT: Well, I would you did like me.
USER: So would not I, for your own sake; for I have many ill qualities.
BOT: Which is one?
USER: I say my prayers aloud.
BOT: I love you the better; the hearers may cry Amen.
USER: God match me with a good dancer!
BOT: Amen.
USER: And God keep him out of my sight when the dance is done! Answer, clerk.
BOT: No more words: the clerk is answered.
USER: I know you well enough: you are Signior Antonio.
BOT: At a word, I am not.
USER: I know you by the waggling of your head.
BOT: To tell you true, I counterfeit him.
USER: You could never do him so ill-well, unless you were the very man. Here's his dry hand up and down: you are he, you are he.
BOT: At a word, I am not.
USER: Come, come; do you think I do not know you by your excellent wit? Can virtue hide itself? Go to, mum, you are he: graces will appear, and there's an end.
BOT: Will you not tell me who told you so?
USER: No, you shall pardon me.
BOT: Nor will you not tell me who you are?
USER: Not now.
BOT: That I was disdainful, and that I had my good wit out of the 'Hundred Merry Tales.' Well, this was Signior Benedick that said so.
USER: What's he?
BOT: I am sure you know him well enough.
USER: Not I, believe me.
BOT: Did he never make you laugh?
USER: I pray you, what is he?
BOT: When I know the gentleman, I'll tell him what you say.
USER: In every good thing.
BOT: Nay, if they lead to any ill, I will leave them at the next turning. and Claudio.]
USER: Sure my brother is amorous on Hero, and hath withdrawn her father to break with him about it. The ladies follow her and but one visor remains.
BOT: And that is Claudio: I know him by his bearing.
USER: Are you not Signior Benedick?
BOT: You know me well; I am he.
USER: Signior, you are very near my brother in his love: he is enamoured on Hero; I pray you, dissuade him from her; she is no equal for his birth: you may do the part of an honest man in it.
BOT: How know you he loves her?
USER: I heard him swear his affection.
BOT: So did I too; and he swore he would marry her tonight.
USER: Come, let us to the banquet.
BOT: Count Claudio?
USER: Yea, the same.
BOT: Come, will you go with me?
USER: Whither?
BOT: I wish him joy of her.
USER: Why, that's spoken like an honest drovier: so they sell bullocks. But did you think the Prince would have served you thus?
BOT: I pray you, leave me.
USER: Ho! now you strike like the blind man: 'twas the boy that stole your meat, and you'll beat the post.
BOT: If it will not be, I'll leave you.
USER: Now, signior, where's the Count? Did you see him?
BOT: To be whipped! What's his fault?
USER: The flat transgression of a school-boy, who, being overjoy'd with finding a bird's nest, shows it his companion, and he steals it.
BOT: Wilt thou make a trust a transgression? The transgression is in the stealer.
USER: Yet it had not been amiss the rod had been made, and the garland too; for the garland he might have worn himself, and the rod he might have bestowed on you, who, as I take it, have stolen his bird's nest.
BOT: I will but teach them to sing, and restore them to the owner.
USER: If their singing answer your saying, by my faith, you say honestly.
BOT: The Lady Beatrice hath a quarrel to you: the gentleman that danced with her told her she is much wronged by you.
USER: Look! here she comes.
BOT: None, but to desire your good company.
USER: O God, sir, here's a dish I love not: I cannot endure my Lady Tongue.
BOT: Come, lady, come; you have lost the heart of Signior Benedick.
USER: Indeed, my lord, he lent it me awhile; and I gave him use for it, a double heart for a single one: marry, once before he won it of me with false dice, therefore your Grace may well say I have lost it.
BOT: You have put him down, lady, you have put him down.
USER: So I would not he should do me, my lord, lest I should prove the mother of fools. I have brought Count Claudio, whom you sent me to seek.
BOT: Why, how now, Count! wherefore are you sad?
USER: Not sad, my lord.
BOT: How then? Sick?
USER: Neither, my lord.
BOT: The Count is neither sad, nor sick, nor merry, nor well; but civil Count, civil as an orange, and something of that jealous complexion.
USER: Count, take of me my daughter, and with her my fortunes: his Grace hath made the match, and all grace say Amen to it!
BOT: Speak, Count, 'tis your cue.
USER: Silence is the perfectest herald of joy: I were but little happy, if I could say how much. Lady, as you are mine, I am yours: I give away myself for you and dote upon the exchange.
BOT: Speak, cousin; or, if you cannot, stop his mouth with a kiss, and let not him speak neither.
USER: In faith, lady, you have a merry heart.
BOT: Yea, my lord; I thank it, poor fool, it keeps on the windy side of care. My cousin tells him in his ear that he is in her heart.
USER: And so she doth, cousin.
BOT: Good Lord, for alliance! Thus goes everyone to the world but I, and I am sunburnt. I may sit in a corner and cry heigh-ho for a husband!
USER: Lady Beatrice, I will get you one.
BOT: I would rather have one of your father's getting. Hath your Grace ne'er a brother like you? Your father got excellent husbands, if a maid could come by them.
USER: Will you have me, lady?
BOT: No, my lord, unless I might have another for working days: your Grace is too costly to wear every day. But, I beseech your Grace, pardon me; I was born to speak all mirth and no matter.
USER: Your silence most offends me, and to be merry best becomes you; for out of question, you were born in a merry hour.
BOT: No, sure, my lord, my mother cried; but then there was a star danced, and under that was I born. Cousins, God give you joy!
USER: Niece, will you look to those things I told you of?
BOT: I cry you mercy, uncle. By your Grace's pardon.
USER: By my troth, a pleasant spirited lady.
BOT: She cannot endure to hear tell of a husband.
USER: O! by no means: she mocks all her wooers out of suit.
BOT: She were an excellent wife for Benedick.
USER: O Lord! my lord, if they were but a week married, they would talk themselves mad.
BOT: Count Claudio, when mean you to go to church?
USER: Tomorrow, my lord. Time goes on crutches till love have all his rites.
BOT: Not till Monday, my dear son, which is hence a just seven-night; and a time too brief too, to have all things answer my mind.
USER: My lord, I am for you, though it cost me ten nights' watchings.
BOT: And I, my lord.
USER: And you too, gentle Hero?
BOT: I will do any modest office, my lord, to help my cousin to a good husband.

USER: It is so; the Count Claudio shall marry the daughter of Leonato.
BOT: Yea, my lord; but I can cross it.
USER: Any bar, any cross, any impediment will be medicinable to me: I am sick in displeasure to him, and whatsoever comes athwart his affection ranges evenly with mine. How canst thou cross this marriage?
BOT: Not honestly, my lord; but so covertly that no dishonesty shall appear in me.
USER: Show me briefly how.
BOT: I think I told your lordship, a year since, how much I am in the favour of Margaret, the waiting gentlewoman to Hero.
USER: I remember.
BOT: I can, at any unseasonable instant of the night, appoint her to look out at her lady's chamber window.
USER: What life is in that, to be the death of this marriage?
BOT: What proof shall I make of that?
USER: Proof enough to misuse the Prince, to vex Claudio, to undo Hero, and kill Leonato. Look you for any other issue?
BOT: Only to despite them, I will endeavour anything.
USER: Grow this to what adverse issue it can, I will put it in practice. Be cunning in the working this, and thy fee is a thousand ducats.
BOT: Be you constant in the accusation, and my cunning shall not shame me.
USER: I will presently go learn their day of marriage.

USER: Boy!
BOT: Signior?
USER: In my chamber window lies a book; bring it hither to me in the orchard.
BOT: I am here already, sir.
USER: Come, shall we hear this music?
BOT: Yea, my good lord. How still the evening is, As hush'd on purpose to grace harmony!
USER: See you where Benedick hath hid himself?
BOT: O! very well, my lord: the music ended, We'll fit the kid-fox with a penny-worth.
USER: Come, Balthasar, we'll hear that song again.
BOT: O! good my lord, tax not so bad a voice To slander music any more than once.
USER: It is the witness still of excellency, To put a strange face on his own perfection. I pray thee, sing, and let me woo no more.
BOT: Because you talk of wooing, I will sing; Since many a wooer doth commence his suit To her he thinks not worthy; yet he wooes; Yet will he swear he loves.
USER: Nay, pray thee come; Or if thou wilt hold longer argument, Do it in notes.
BOT: Note this before my notes; There's not a note of mine that's worth the noting.
USER: Why these are very crotchets that he speaks; Notes, notes, forsooth, and nothing!
BOT: By my troth, a good song.
USER: And an ill singer, my lord.
BOT: Ha, no, no, faith; thou singest well enough for a shift.
