USER: The best I can, my lord.
BOT: Do so: farewell. Come hither, Leonato: what was it you told me of today, that your niece Beatrice was in love with Signior Benedick?
USER: O! ay: - [Aside to Don Pedro] Stalk on, stalk on; the fowl sits. I did never think that lady would have loved any man.
BOT: No, nor I neither; but most wonderful that she should so dote on Signior Benedick, whom she hath in all outward behaviours seemed ever to abhor.
USER: By my troth, my lord, I cannot tell what to think of it but that she loves him with an enraged affection: it is past the infinite of thought.
BOT: Maybe she doth but counterfeit.
USER: Faith, like enough.
BOT: O God! counterfeit! There was never counterfeit of passion came so near the life of passion as she discovers it.
USER: Why, what effects of passion shows she?
BOT: What effects, my lord? She will sit you; [To Claudio] You heard my daughter tell you how.
USER: She did, indeed.
BOT: How, how, I pray you? You amaze me: I would have thought her spirit had been invincible against all assaults of affection.
USER: I would have sworn it had, my lord; especially against Benedick.
BOT: white-bearded fellow speaks it: knavery cannot, sure, hide itself in such reverence.
USER: up.
BOT: Hath she made her affection known to Benedick?
USER: No; and swears she never will: that's her torment.
BOT: 'Tis true, indeed; so your daughter says: 'Shall I,' says she, 'that have so oft encountered him with scorn, write to him that I love him?'
USER: This says she now when she is beginning to write to him; for she'll be up twenty times a night, and there will she sit in her smock till she have writ a sheet of paper: my daughter tells us all.
BOT: Now you talk of a sheet of paper, I remember a pretty jest your daughter told us of.
USER: O! when she had writ it, and was reading it over, she found Benedick and Beatrice between the sheet?
BOT: That.
USER: Then down upon her knees she falls, weeps, sobs, beats her heart, tears her hair, prays, curses; 'O sweet Benedick! God give me patience!'
BOT: She doth indeed; my daughter says so; and the ecstasy hath so much overborne her, that my daughter is sometimes afeard she will do a desperate outrage to herself. It is very true.
USER: It were good that Benedick knew of it by some other, if she will not discover it.
BOT: To what end? he would make but a sport of it and torment the poor lady worse.
USER: And he should, it were an alms to hang him. She's an excellent sweet lady, and, out of all suspicion, she is virtuous.
BOT: And she is exceeding wise.
USER: In everything but in loving Benedick.
BOT: O! my lord, wisdom and blood combating in so tender a body, we have ten proofs to one that blood hath the victory. I am sorry for her, as I have just cause, being her uncle and her guardian.
USER: I would she had bestowed this dotage on me; I would have daffed all other respects and made her half myself. I pray you, tell Benedick of it, and hear what he will say.
BOT: Were it good, think you?
USER: She doth well: if she should make tender of her love, 'tis very possible he'll scorn it; for the man, - as you know all, - hath a contemptible spirit.
BOT: He is a very proper man.
USER: He hath indeed a good outward happiness.
BOT: 'Fore God, and in my mind, very wise.
USER: He doth indeed show some sparks that are like wit.
BOT: And I take him to be valiant.
USER: As Hector, I assure you: and in the managing of quarrels you may say he is wise; for either he avoids them with great discretion, or undertakes them with a most Christian-like fear.
BOT: If he do fear God, a' must necessarily keep peace: if he break the peace, he ought to enter into a quarrel with fear and trembling.
USER: And so will he do; for the man doth fear God, howsoever it seems not in him by some large jests he will make. Well, I am sorry for your niece. Shall we go seek Benedick and tell him of her love?
BOT: Never tell him, my lord: let her wear it out with good counsel.
USER: Nay, that's impossible: she may wear her heart out first.
BOT: Well, we will hear further of it by your daughter: let it cool the while. I love Benedick well, and I could wish he would modestly examine himself, to see how much he is unworthy so good a lady.
USER: My lord, will you walk? dinner is ready.
BOT: trust my expectation.
USER: Against my will I am sent to bid you come in to dinner.
BOT: Fair Beatrice, I thank you for your pains.
USER: I took no more pains for those thanks than you take pains to thank me: if it had been painful, I would not have come.
BOT: You take pleasure then in the message?
USER: Yea, just so much as you may take upon a knife's point, and choke a daw withal. You have no stomach, signior: fare you well.

USER: I'll make her come, I warrant you, presently.
BOT: Then go we near her, that her ear lose nothing Of the false sweet bait that we lay for it. No, truly, Ursula, she is too disdainful; I know her spirits are as coy and wild As haggards of the rock.
USER: But are you sure That Benedick loves Beatrice so entirely?
BOT: So says the Prince, and my new-trothed lord.
USER: And did they bid you tell her of it, madam?
BOT: They did entreat me to acquaint her of it; But I persuaded them, if they lov'd Benedick, To wish him wrestle with affection, And never to let Beatrice know of it.
USER: Why did you so? Doth not the gentleman Deserve as full as fortunate a bed As ever Beatrice shall couch upon?
BOT: Sure I think so; And therefore certainly it were not good She knew his love, lest she make sport at it.
USER: Sure, sure, such carping is not commendable.
BOT: Yet tell her of it: hear what she will say.
USER: No; rather I will go to Benedick, And counsel him to fight against his passion. And, truly, I'll devise some honest slanders To stain my cousin with. One doth not know How much an ill word may empoison liking.
BOT: O! do not do your cousin such a wrong. She cannot be so much without true judgment, - Having so swift and excellent a wit As she is priz'd to have, - as to refuse So rare a gentleman as Signior Benedick.
USER: He is the only man of Italy, Always excepted my dear Claudio.
BOT: I pray you, be not angry with me, madam, Speaking my fancy: Signior Benedick, For shape, for bearing, argument and valour, Goes foremost in report through Italy.
USER: Indeed, he hath an excellent good name.
BOT: His excellence did earn it, ere he had it. When are you married, madam?
USER: Why, every day, tomorrow. Come, go in: I'll show thee some attires, and have thy counsel Which is the best to furnish me tomorrow.
BOT: She's lim'd, I warrant you, We have caught her, madam.
USER: If it prove so, then loving goes by haps: Some Cupid kills with arrows, some with traps.

USER: I do but stay till your marriage be consummate, and then go I toward Arragon.
BOT: I'll bring you thither, my lord, if you'll vouchsafe me.
USER: Gallants, I am not as I have been.
BOT: So say I: methinks you are sadder.
USER: I hope he be in love.
BOT: Hang him, truant! there's no true drop of blood in him to be truly touched with love. If he be sad, he wants money.
USER: I have the tooth-ache.
BOT: Draw it.
USER: Hang it.
BOT: You must hang it first, and draw it afterwards.
USER: What! sigh for the tooth-ache?
BOT: Where is but a humour or a worm?
USER: Well, everyone can master a grief but he that has it.
BOT: Yet say I, he is in love.
USER: If he be not in love with some woman, there is no believing old signs: a' brushes his hat a mornings; what should that bode?
BOT: Hath any man seen him at the barber's?
USER: No, but the barber's man hath been seen with him; and the old ornament of his cheek hath already stuffed tennis balls.
BOT: Indeed he looks younger than he did, by the loss of a beard.
USER: Nay, a' rubs himself with civet: can you smell him out by that?
BOT: That's as much as to say the sweet youth's in love.
USER: The greatest note of it is his melancholy.
BOT: And when was he wont to wash his face?
USER: Yea, or to paint himself? for the which, I hear what they say of him.
BOT: Nay, but his jesting spirit; which is now crept into a lute-string, and now governed by stops.
USER: Indeed, that tells a heavy tale for him. Conclude, conclude he is in love.
BOT: Nay, but I know who loves him.
USER: That would I know too: I warrant, one that knows him not.
BOT: Yes, and his ill conditions; and in despite of all, dies for him.
USER: She shall be buried with her face upwards.
BOT: Yet is this no charm for the tooth-ache. Old signior, walk aside with me: I have studied eight or nine wise words to speak to you, which these hobby-horses must not hear.
USER: For my life, to break with him about Beatrice.
BOT: 'Tis even so. Hero and Margaret have by this played their parts with Beatrice, and then the two bears will not bite one another when they meet.
USER: My lord and brother, God save you!
BOT: Good den, brother.
USER: If your leisure served, I would speak with you.
BOT: In private?
USER: If it please you; yet Count Claudio may hear, for what I would speak of concerns him.
BOT: What's the matter?
USER: You know he does.
BOT: I know not that, when he knows what I know.
USER: If there be any impediment, I pray you discover it.
BOT: Why, what's the matter?
USER: I came hither to tell you; and circumstances shortened, - for she has been too long a talking of, - the lady is disloyal.
BOT: Who, Hero?
USER: Even she: Leonato's Hero, your Hero, every man's Hero.
BOT: Disloyal?
USER: May this be so?
BOT: I will not think it.
USER: If you dare not trust that you see, confess not that you know. If you will follow me, I will show you enough; and when you have seen more and heard more, proceed accordingly.
BOT: If I see anything tonight why I should not marry her tomorrow, in the congregation, where I should wed, there will I shame her.
USER: And, as I wooed for thee to obtain her, I will join with thee to disgrace her.
BOT: I will disparage her no farther till you are my witnesses: bear it coldly but till midnight, and let the issue show itself.
USER: O day untowardly turned!
BOT: O mischief strangely thwarting!
USER: O plague right well prevented! So will you say when you have seen the sequel.

USER: Are you good men and true?
BOT: Yea, or else it were pity but they should suffer salvation, body and soul.
USER: Nay, that were a punishment too good for them, if they should have any allegiance in them, being chosen for the Prince's watch.
BOT: Well, give them their charge, neighbour Dogberry.
USER: First, who think you the most desartless man to be constable?
BOT: Hugh Oatcake, sir, or George Seacoal; for they can write and read.
USER: Come hither, neighbour Seacoal. God hath blessed you with a good name: to be a well-favoured man is the gift of Fortune; but to write and read comes by Nature.
BOT: Both which, Master Constable, -
USER: How, if a' will not stand?
BOT: Why, then, take no note of him, but let him go; and presently call the rest of the watch together, and thank God you are rid of a knave.
USER: If he will not stand when he is bidden, he is none of the Prince's subjects.
BOT: True, and they are to meddle with none but the Prince's subjects. You shall also make no noise in the streets: for, for the watch to babble and to talk is most tolerable and not to be endured.
USER: We will rather sleep than talk: we know what belongs to a watch.
BOT: How if they will not?
USER: Why then, let them alone till they are sober: if they make you not then the better answer, you may say they are not the men you took them for.
BOT: Well, sir.
USER: If you meet a thief, you may suspect him, by virtue of your office, to be no true man; and, for such kind of men, the less you meddle or make with them, why, the more is for your honesty.
BOT: If we know him to be a thief, shall we not lay hands on him?
USER: Truly, by your office, you may; but I think they that touch pitch will be defiled. The most peaceable way for you, if you do take a thief, is to let him show himself what he is and steal out of your company.
BOT: You have been always called a merciful man, partner.
USER: Truly, I would not hang a dog by my will, much more a man who hath any honesty in him.
BOT: If you hear a child cry in the night, you must call to the nurse and bid her still it.
USER: How if the nurse be asleep and will not hear us?
BOT: Why then, depart in peace, and let the child wake her with crying; for the ewe that will not hear her lamb when it baes, will never answer a calf when he bleats.
USER: 'Tis very true.
BOT: This is the end of the charge. You constable, are to present the Prince's own person: if you meet the Prince in the night, you may stay him.
USER: Nay, by'r lady, that I think, a' cannot.
BOT: By'r lady, I think it be so.
USER: Ha, ah, ha! Well, masters, good night: an there be any matter of weight chances, call up me: keep your fellows' counsels and your own, and good night. Come, neighbour.
BOT: Well, masters, we hear our charge: let us go sit here upon the church bench till two, and then all to bed.
USER: One word more, honest neighbours. I pray you, watch about Signior Leonato's door; for the wedding being there tomorrow, there is a great coil tonight. Adieu; be vigitant, I beseech you.
BOT: What, Conrade!
USER: Conrade, I say!
BOT: Here, man. I am at thy elbow.
USER: Mass, and my elbow itched; I thought there would a scab follow.
BOT: I will owe thee an answer for that; and now forward with thy tale.
USER: Stand thee close then under this penthouse, for it drizzles rain, and I will, like a true drunkard, utter all to thee.
BOT: Therefore know, I have earned of Don John a thousand ducats.
USER: Is it possible that any villainy should be so dear?
BOT: Thou shouldst rather ask if it were possible any villainy should be so rich; for when rich villains have need of poor ones, poor ones may make what price they will.
USER: I wonder at it.
BOT: That shows thou art unconfirmed. Thou knowest that the fashion of a doublet, or a hat, or a cloak, is nothing to a man.
USER: Yes, it is apparel.
BOT: I mean, the fashion.
USER: Yes, the fashion is the fashion.
BOT: Tush! I may as well say the fool's the fool. But seest thou not what a deformed thief this fashion is?
USER: this seven years; a' goes up and down like a gentleman: I remember his name.
BOT: Didst thou not hear somebody?
USER: No: 'twas the vane on the house.
BOT: All this I see, and I see that the fashion wears out more apparel than the man. But art not thou thyself giddy with the fashion too, that thou hast shifted out of thy tale into telling me of the fashion?
USER: And thought they Margaret was Hero?
BOT: We charge you in the Prince's name, stand!
USER: Call up the right Master Constable. We have here recovered the most dangerous piece of lechery that ever was known in the commonwealth.
BOT: And one Deformed is one of them: I know him, a' wears a lock.
USER: Masters, masters!
BOT: You'll be made bring Deformed forth, I warrant you.
USER: Masters, -
BOT: Never speak: we charge you let us obey you to go with us.
USER: We are like to prove a goodly commodity, being taken up of these men's bills.
BOT: A commodity in question, I warrant you. Come, we'll obey you.

USER: Good Ursula, wake my cousin Beatrice, and desire her to rise.
BOT: I will, lady.
USER: And bid her come hither.
BOT: Well.
USER: Troth, I think your other rebato were better.
BOT: No, pray thee, good Meg, I'll wear this.
USER: By my troth's not so good; and I warrant your cousin will say so.
BOT: My cousin's a fool, and thou art another: I'll wear none but this.
USER: I like the new tire within excellently, if the hair were a thought browner; and your gown's a most rare fashion, i' faith. I saw the Duchess of Milan's gown that they praise so.
BOT: O! that exceeds, they say.
USER: God give me joy to wear it! for my heart is exceeding heavy.
BOT: 'Twill be heavier soon by the weight of a man.
USER: Fie upon thee! art not ashamed?
BOT: Good morrow, coz.
USER: Good morrow, sweet Hero.
BOT: Why, how now? do you speak in the sick tune?
USER: I am out of all other tune, methinks.
BOT: Clap's into 'Light o' love'; that goes without a burden: do you sing it, and I'll dance it.
USER: Ye, light o' love with your heels! then, if your husband have stables enough, you'll see he shall lack no barnes.
BOT: O illegitimate construction! I scorn that with my heels.
USER: 'Tis almost five o'clock, cousin; 'tis time you were ready. By my troth, I am exceeding ill. Heigh-ho!
BOT: For a hawk, a horse, or a husband?
USER: For the letter that begins them all, H.
BOT: Well, and you be not turned Turk, there's no more sailing by the star.
USER: What means the fool, trow?
BOT: Nothing I; but God send everyone their heart's desire!
USER: These gloves the Count sent me; they are an excellent perfume.
BOT: I am stuffed, cousin, I cannot smell.
USER: A maid, and stuffed! there's goodly catching of cold.
BOT: O, God help me! God help me! how long have you professed apprehension?
USER: Ever since you left it. Doth not my wit become me rarely!
BOT: It is not seen enough, you should wear it in your cap. By my troth, I am sick.
USER: Get you some of this distilled Carduus benedictus, and lay it to your heart: it is the only thing for a qualm.
BOT: There thou prick'st her with a thistle.
USER: Benedictus! why benedictus? you have some moral in this benedictus.
BOT: What pace is this that thy tongue keeps?
USER: Not a false gallop.
BOT: Madam, withdraw: the Prince, the Count, Signior Benedick, Don John, and all the gallants of the town are come to fetch you to church.
USER: Help to dress me, good coz, good Meg, good Ursula.

USER: What would you with me, honest neighbour?
BOT: Marry, sir, I would have some confidence with you, that decerns you nearly.
USER: Brief, I pray you; for you see it is a busy time with me.
BOT: Marry, this it is, sir.
USER: Yes, in truth it is, sir.
BOT: What is it, my good friends?
USER: Goodman Verges, sir, speaks a little off the matter: an old man, sir, and his wits are not so blunt as, God help, I would desire they were; but, in faith, honest as the skin between his brows.
BOT: Yes, I thank God, I am as honest as any man living, that is an old man and no honester than I.
USER: Comparisons are odorous: palabras, neighbour Verges.
BOT: Neighbours, you are tedious.
USER: It pleases your worship to say so, but we are the poor Duke's officers; but truly, for mine own part, if I were as tedious as a king, I could find in my heart to bestow it all of your worship.
BOT: All thy tediousness on me! ah?
USER: Yea, and 'twere a thousand pound more than 'tis; for I hear as good exclamation on your worship, as of any man in the city, and though I be but a poor man, I am glad to hear it.
BOT: And so am I.
USER: I would fain know what you have to say.
BOT: Marry, sir, our watch tonight, excepting your worship's presence, ha' ta'en a couple of as arrant knaves as any in Messina.
USER: Indeed, neighbour, he comes too short of you.
BOT: Gifts that God gives.
USER: I must leave you.
BOT: One word, sir: our watch, sir, have indeed comprehended two aspicious persons, and we would have them this morning examined before your worship.
USER: Take their examination yourself, and bring it me: I am now in great haste, as may appear unto you.
BOT: It shall be suffigance.
USER: Drink some wine ere you go: fare you well.
BOT: My lord, they stay for you to give your daughter to her husband.
USER: I'll wait upon them: I am ready.
BOT: Go, good partner, go get you to Francis Seacoal; bid him bring his pen and inkhorn to the gaol: we are now to examination these men.
USER: And we must do it wisely.
BOT: We will spare for no wit, I warrant you; here's that shall drive some of them to a non-come: only get the learned writer to set down our excommunication, and meet me at the gaol.

USER: Come, Friar Francis, be brief: only to the plain form of marriage, and you shall recount their particular duties afterwards.
BOT: You come hither, my lord, to marry this lady?
USER: No.
BOT: To be married to her, friar; you come to marry her.
USER: Lady, you come hither to be married to this Count?
BOT: I do.
USER: If either of you know any inward impediment, why you should not be conjoined, I charge you, on your souls, to utter it.
BOT: Know you any, Hero?
USER: None, my lord.
BOT: Know you any, Count?
USER: I dare make his answer; none.
BOT: O! what men dare do! what men may do! what men daily do, not knowing what they do!
USER: How now! Interjections? Why then, some be of laughing, as ah! ha! he!
BOT: Stand thee by, Friar. Father, by your leave: Will you with free and unconstrained soul Give me this maid, your daughter?
USER: As freely, son, as God did give her me.
BOT: And what have I to give you back whose worth May counterpoise this rich and precious gift?
USER: Nothing, unless you render her again.
BOT: What do you mean, my lord?
USER: Not to be married, Not to knit my soul to an approved wanton.
BOT: Dear my lord, if you, in your own proof, Have vanquish'd the resistance of her youth, And made defeat of her virginity, -
USER: And seem'd I ever otherwise to you?
BOT: Is my lord well, that he doth speak so wide?
USER: Sweet Prince, why speak not you?
BOT: What should I speak? I stand dishonour'd, that have gone about To link my dear friend to a common stale.
USER: Are these things spoken, or do I but dream?
BOT: Sir, they are spoken, and these things are true.
USER: This looks not like a nuptial.
BOT: True! O God!
USER: Leonato, stand I here? Is this the Prince? Is this the Prince's brother? Is this face Hero's? Are our eyes our own?
BOT: All this is so; but what of this, my lord?
USER: Let me but move one question to your daughter, And by that fatherly and kindly power That you have in her, bid her answer truly.
BOT: I charge thee do so, as thou art my child.
USER: O, God defend me! how am I beset! What kind of catechizing call you this?
BOT: To make you answer truly to your name.
USER: Is it not Hero? Who can blot that name With any just reproach?
BOT: Marry, that can Hero: Hero itself can blot out Hero's virtue. What man was he talk'd with you yesternight Out at your window, betwixt twelve and one? Now, if you are a maid, answer to this.
USER: I talk'd with no man at that hour, my lord.
BOT: Fie, fie! they are not to be nam'd, my lord, Not to be spoke of; There is not chastity enough in language Without offence to utter them. Thus, pretty lady, I am sorry for thy much misgovernment.
USER: Hath no man's dagger here a point for me?
BOT: Why, how now, cousin! wherefore sink you down?
USER: Come, let us go. These things, come thus to light, Smother her spirits up.
BOT: How doth the lady?
USER: Dead, I think! Help, uncle! Hero! why, Hero! Uncle! Signior Benedick! Friar!
BOT: O Fate! take not away thy heavy hand: Death is the fairest cover for her shame That may be wish'd for.
USER: How now, cousin Hero?
BOT: Have comfort, lady.
USER: Dost thou look up?
BOT: Yea; wherefore should she not?
USER: Sir, sir, be patient. For my part, I am so attir'd in wonder, I know not what to say.
BOT: O! on my soul, my cousin is belied!
USER: Lady, were you her bedfellow last night?
BOT: No, truly, not; although, until last night, I have this twelvemonth been her bedfellow.
USER: Lady, what man is he you are accus'd of?
BOT: There is some strange misprision in the princes.
USER: Two of them have the very bent of honour; And if their wisdoms be misled in this, The practice of it lives in John the bastard, Whose spirits toil in frame of villainies.
BOT: What shall become of this? What will this do?
USER: Being that I flow in grief, The smallest twine may lead me.
BOT: 'Tis well consented: presently away; For to strange sores strangely they strain the cure. Come, lady, die to live: this wedding day Perhaps is but prolong'd: have patience and endure.
USER: Lady Beatrice, have you wept all this while?
BOT: Yea, and I will weep a while longer.
USER: I will not desire that.
BOT: You have no reason; I do it freely.
USER: Surely I do believe your fair cousin is wronged.
BOT: Ah! how much might the man deserve of me that would right her.
USER: Is there any way to show such friendship?
BOT: A very even way, but no such friend.
USER: May a man do it?
BOT: It is a man's office, but not yours.
USER: I do love nothing in the world so well as you: is not that strange?
BOT: As strange as the thing I know not. It were as possible for me to say I loved nothing so well as you; but believe me not, and yet I lie not; I confess nothing, nor I deny nothing. I am sorry for my cousin.
USER: By my sword, Beatrice, thou lovest me.
BOT: Do not swear by it, and eat it.
USER: I will swear by it that you love me; and I will make him eat it that says I love not you.
BOT: Will you not eat your word?
USER: With no sauce that can be devised to it. I protest I love thee.
BOT: Why then, God forgive me!
USER: What offence, sweet Beatrice?
BOT: You have stayed me in a happy hour: I was about to protest I loved you.
USER: And do it with all thy heart.
BOT: I love you with so much of my heart that none is left to protest.
USER: Come, bid me do anything for thee.
BOT: Kill Claudio.
USER: Ha! not for the wide world.
BOT: You kill me to deny it. Farewell.
USER: Tarry, sweet Beatrice.
BOT: I am gone, though I am here: there is no love in you: nay, I pray you, let me go.
USER: Beatrice, -
BOT: In faith, I will go.
USER: We'll be friends first.
BOT: You dare easier be friends with me than fight with mine enemy.
USER: Is Claudio thine enemy?
BOT: Hear me, Beatrice, -
USER: Talk with a man out at a window! a proper saying!
BOT: Nay, but Beatrice, -
USER: Sweet Hero! she is wronged, she is slandered, she is undone.
BOT: Beat -
USER: Tarry, good Beatrice. By this hand, I love thee.
BOT: Use it for my love some other way than swearing by it.
USER: Think you in your soul the Count Claudio hath wronged Hero?
BOT: Yea, as sure is I have a thought or a soul.

USER: Is our whole dissembly appeared?
BOT: O! a stool and a cushion for the sexton.
USER: Which be the malefactors?
BOT: Marry, that am I and my partner.
USER: Nay, that's certain: we have the exhibition to examine.
BOT: But which are the offenders that are to be examined? let them come before Master Constable.
USER: Yea, marry, let them come before me. What is your name, friend?
BOT: Borachio.
USER: Pray write down Borachio. Yours, sirrah?
BOT: I am a gentleman, sir, and my name is Conrade.
USER: Write down Master gentleman Conrade. Masters, do you serve God?
BOT: Yea, sir, we hope.
USER: Marry, sir, we say we are none.
BOT: A marvellous witty fellow, I assure you; but I will go about with him. Come you hither, sirrah; a word in your ear: sir, I say to you, it is thought you are false knaves.
USER: Sir, I say to you we are none.
BOT: Well, stand aside. Fore God, they are both in a tale. Have you writ down, that they are none?
USER: Master Constable, you go not the way to examine: you must call forth the watch that are their accusers.
BOT: Yea, marry, that's the eftest way. Let the watch come forth. Masters, I charge you, in the Prince's name, accuse these men.
USER: This man said, sir, that Don John, the Prince's brother, was a villain.
BOT: Write down Prince John a villain. Why, this is flat perjury, to call a Prince's brother villain.
USER: Master Constable, -
BOT: Pray thee, fellow, peace: I do not like thy look, I promise thee.
USER: What heard you him say else?
BOT: Marry, that he had received a thousand ducats of Don John for accusing the Lady Hero wrongfully.
USER: Flat burglary as ever was committed.
BOT: Yea, by the mass, that it is.
USER: What else, fellow?
BOT: And that Count Claudio did mean, upon his words, to disgrace Hero before the whole assembly, and not marry her.
USER: O villain! thou wilt be condemned into everlasting redemption for this.
BOT: What else?
USER: This is all.
BOT: Come, let them be opinioned.
USER: Let them be in the hands -
BOT: Off, coxcomb!
USER: God's my life! where's the sexton? let him write down the Prince's officer coxcomb. Come, bind them. Thou naughty varlet!
BOT: Away! you are an ass; you are an ass.

USER: If you go on thus, you will kill yourself And 'tis not wisdom thus to second grief Against yourself.
BOT: Therein do men from children nothing differ.
USER: I pray thee peace! I will be flesh and blood; For there was never yet philosopher That could endure the toothache patiently, However they have writ the style of gods And made a push at chance and sufferance.
BOT: Yet bend not all the harm upon yourself; Make those that do offend you suffer too.
USER: There thou speak'st reason: nay, I will do so. My soul doth tell me Hero is belied; And that shall Claudio know; so shall the Prince, And all of them that thus dishonour her.
BOT: Here comes the Prince and Claudio hastily.
USER: Good den, good den.
BOT: Good day to both of you.
USER: Hear you, my lords, -
BOT: We have some haste, Leonato.
USER: Some haste, my lord! well, fare you well, my lord: Are you so hasty now? - well, all is one.
BOT: Nay, do not quarrel with us, good old man.
USER: If he could right himself with quarrelling, Some of us would lie low.
BOT: Who wrongs him?
USER: Marry, thou dost wrong me; thou dissembler, thou. Nay, never lay thy hand upon thy sword; I fear thee not.
BOT: Marry, beshrew my hand, If it should give your age such cause of fear. In faith, my hand meant nothing to my sword.
USER: My villainy?
BOT: Thine, Claudio; thine, I say.
USER: You say not right, old man.
BOT: My lord, my lord, I'll prove it on his body, if he dare, Despite his nice fence and his active practice, His May of youth and bloom of lustihood.
USER: Away! I will not have to do with you.
BOT: Canst thou so daff me? Thou hast kill'd my child; If thou kill'st me, boy, thou shalt kill a man.
USER: Brother, -
BOT: Content yourself. God knows I lov'd my niece; And she is dead, slander'd to death by villains, That dare as well answer a man indeed As I dare take a serpent by the tongue. Boys, apes, braggarts, Jacks, milksops!
USER: Brother Anthony, -
BOT: But, brother Anthony, -
USER: Come, 'tis no matter: Do not you meddle, let me deal in this.
BOT: Gentlemen both, we will not wake your patience. My heart is sorry for your daughter's death; But, on my honour, she was charg'd with nothing But what was true and very full of proof.
USER: My lord, my lord -
BOT: I will not hear you.
USER: No? Come, brother, away. I will be heard. -
BOT: And shall, or some of us will smart for it.
USER: See, see; here comes the man we went to seek.
BOT: Now, signior, what news?
USER: Good day, my lord.
BOT: Welcome, signior: you are almost come to part almost a fray.
USER: We had like to have had our two noses snapped off with two old men without teeth.
BOT: Leonato and his brother. What think'st thou? Had we fought, I doubt we should have been too young for them.
USER: In a false quarrel there is no true valour. I came to seek you both.
BOT: We have been up and down to seek thee; for we are high-proof melancholy, and would fain have it beaten away. Wilt thou use thy wit?
USER: It is in my scabbard; shall I draw it?
BOT: Dost thou wear thy wit by thy side?
USER: Never any did so, though very many have been beside their wit. I will bid thee draw, as we do the minstrels; draw, to pleasure us.
BOT: As I am an honest man, he looks pale. Art thou sick, or angry?
USER: What, courage, man! What though care killed a cat, thou hast mettle enough in thee to kill care.
BOT: Sir, I shall meet your wit in the career, and you charge it against me. I pray you choose another subject.
USER: Nay then, give him another staff: this last was broke cross.
BOT: By this light, he changes more and more: I think he be angry indeed.
USER: If he be, he knows how to turn his girdle.
BOT: Shall I speak a word in your ear?
USER: God bless me from a challenge!
BOT: make it good how you dare, with what you dare, and when you dare. Do me right, or I will protest your cowardice. You have killed a sweet lady, and her death shall fall heavy on you. Let me hear from you.
USER: Well I will meet you, so I may have good cheer.
BOT: What, a feast, a feast?
USER: I' faith, I thank him; he hath bid me to a calf's-head and a capon, the which if I do not carve most curiously, say my knife's naught. Shall I not find a woodcock too?
BOT: Sir, your wit ambles well; it goes easily.
USER: For the which she wept heartily and said she cared not.
BOT: Yea, that she did; but yet, for all that, an if she did not hate him deadly, she would love him dearly. The old man's daughter told us all.
USER: All, all; and moreover, God saw him when he was hid in the garden.
BOT: But when shall we set the savage bull's horns on the sensible Benedick's head?
USER: Yea, and text underneath, 'Here dwells Benedick the married man!'
BOT: He is in earnest.
USER: In most profound earnest; and, I'll warrant you, for the love of Beatrice.
BOT: And hath challenged thee?
USER: Most sincerely.
BOT: What a pretty thing man is when he goes in his doublet and hose and leaves off his wit!
USER: He is then a giant to an ape; but then is an ape a doctor to such a man.
BOT: But, soft you; let me be: pluck up, my heart, and be sad! Did he not say my brother was fled? Conrade and Borachio.
USER: Come you, sir: if justice cannot tame you, she shall ne'er weigh more reasons in her balance. Nay, an you be a cursing hypocrite once, you must be looked to.
BOT: How now! two of my brother's men bound! Borachio, one!
USER: Hearken after their offence, my lord.
BOT: Officers, what offence have these men done?
USER: First, I ask thee what they have done; thirdly, I ask thee what's their offence; sixth and lastly, why they are committed; and, to conclude, what you lay to their charge?
BOT: Rightly reasoned, and in his own division; and, by my troth, there's one meaning well suited.
USER: Who have you offended, masters, that you are thus bound to your answer? This learned constable is too cunning to be understood. What's your offence?
BOT: Runs not this speech like iron through your blood?
USER: I have drunk poison whiles he utter'd it.
BOT: But did my brother set thee on to this?
USER: Yea; and paid me richly for the practice of it.
BOT: He is compos'd and fram'd of treachery: And fled he is upon this villainy.
USER: Sweet Hero! now thy image doth appear In the rare semblance that I lov'd it first.
BOT: Come, bring away the plaintiffs: by this time our sexton hath reformed Signior Leonato of the matter. And masters, do not forget to specify, when time and place shall serve, that I am an ass.
USER: Here, here comes Master Signior Leonato, and the sexton too.
BOT: Which is the villain? Let me see his eyes, That, when I note another man like him, I may avoid him. Which of these is he?
USER: If you would know your wronger, look on me.
BOT: Art thou the slave that with thy breath hast kill'd Mine innocent child?
USER: Yea, even I alone.
BOT: I know not how to pray your patience; Yet I must speak. Choose your revenge yourself; Impose me to what penance your invention Can lay upon my sin: yet sinn'd I not But in mistaking.
USER: By my soul, nor I: And yet, to satisfy this good old man, I would bend under any heavy weight That he'll enjoin me to.
BOT: O noble sir, Your over-kindness doth wring tears from me! I do embrace your offer; and dispose For henceforth of poor Claudio.
USER: Tomorrow then I will expect your coming; Tonight I take my leave. This naughty man Shall face to face be brought to Margaret, Who, I believe, was pack'd in all this wrong, Hir'd to it by your brother.
BOT: No, by my soul she was not; Nor knew not what she did when she spoke to me; But always hath been just and virtuous In anything that I do know by her.
USER: I thank thee for thy care and honest pains.
BOT: Your worship speaks like a most thankful and reverent youth, and I praise God for you.
USER: There's for thy pains.
BOT: God save the foundation!
USER: Go, I discharge thee of thy prisoner, and I thank thee.
BOT: Until tomorrow morning, lords, farewell.
USER: Farewell, my lords: we look for you tomorrow.
BOT: We will not fail.
USER: Tonight I'll mourn with Hero.
BOT: Margaret, How her acquaintance grew with this lewd fellow.

USER: Pray thee, sweet Mistress Margaret, deserve well at my hands by helping me to the speech of Beatrice.
BOT: Will you then write me a sonnet in praise of my beauty?
USER: In so high a style, Margaret, that no man living shall come over it; for, in most comely truth, thou deservest it.
BOT: To have no man come over me! why, shall I always keep below stairs?
USER: Thy wit is as quick as the greyhound's mouth; it catches.
BOT: And yours as blunt as the fencer's foils, which hit, but hurt not.
USER: A most manly wit, Margaret; it will not hurt a woman: and so, I pray thee, call Beatrice. I give thee the bucklers.
BOT: Give us the swords, we have bucklers of our own.
USER: If you use them, Margaret, you must put in the pikes with a vice; and they are dangerous weapons for maids.
BOT: Well, I will call Beatrice to you, who I think hath legs.
USER: Yea, signior; and depart when you bid me.
BOT: O, stay but till then!
USER: 'Then' is spoken; fare you well now: and yet, ere I go, let me go with that I came for; which is, with knowing what hath passed between you and Claudio.
BOT: Only foul words; and thereupon I will kiss thee.
USER: Foul words is but foul wind, and foul wind is but foul breath, and foul breath is noisome; therefore I will depart unkissed.
BOT: For them all together; which maintained so politic a state of evil that they will not admit any good part to intermingle with them. But for which of my good parts did you first suffer love for me?
USER: 'Suffer love,' a good epithet! I do suffer love indeed, for I love thee against my will.
BOT: In spite of your heart, I think. Alas, poor heart! If you spite it for my sake, I will spite it for yours; for I will never love that which my friend hates.
USER: Thou and I are too wise to woo peaceably.
BOT: It appears not in this confession: there's not one wise man among twenty that will praise himself.
USER: An old, an old instance, Beatrice, that lived in the time of good neighbours. If a man do not erect in this age his own tomb ere he dies, he shall live no longer in monument than the bell rings and the widow weeps.
BOT: And how long is that think you?
USER: Very ill.
BOT: And how do you?
USER: Very ill too.
BOT: Serve God, love me, and mend. There will I leave you too, for here comes one in haste.
USER: Will you go hear this news, signior?
BOT: I will live in thy heart, die in thy lap, and be buried in thy eyes; and moreover I will go with thee to thy uncle's.

USER: Is this the monument of Leonato?
BOT: It is, my lord.
USER: Now, unto thy bones good night! Yearly will I do this rite.
BOT: Good morrow, masters: each his several way.
USER: Come, let us hence, and put on other weeds; And then to Leonato's we will go.
BOT: And Hymen now with luckier issue speed's, Than this for whom we rend'red up this woe!

USER: Did I not tell you she was innocent?
BOT: So are the Prince and Claudio, who accus'd her Upon the error that you heard debated: But Margaret was in some fault for this, Although against her will, as it appears In the true course of all the question.
USER: Well, I am glad that all things sort so well.
BOT: And so am I, being else by faith enforc'd To call young Claudio to a reckoning for it.
USER: Which I will do with confirm'd countenance.
BOT: Friar, I must entreat your pains, I think.
USER: To do what, signior?
BOT: To bind me, or undo me; one of them. Signior Leonato, truth it is, good signior, Your niece regards me with an eye of favour.
USER: That eye my daughter lent her. 'Tis most true.
BOT: And I do with an eye of love requite her.
USER: The sight whereof I think, you had from me, From Claudio, and the Prince. But what's your will?
BOT: Your answer, sir, is enigmatical: But, for my will, my will is your good will May stand with ours, this day to be conjoin'd In the state of honourable marriage: In which, good friar, I shall desire your help.
USER: My heart is with your liking.
BOT: And my help. Here comes the Prince and Claudio. with Attendants.
USER: Good morrow to this fair assembly.
BOT: Good morrow, Prince; good morrow, Claudio: We here attend you. Are you yet determin'd Today to marry with my brother's daughter?
USER: I'll hold my mind, were she an Ethiope.
BOT: Call her forth, brother: here's the friar ready.
USER: Good morrow, Benedick. Why, what's the matter, That you have such a February face, So full of frost, of storm and cloudiness?
BOT: I think he thinks upon the savage bull. Tush! fear not, man, we'll tip thy horns with gold, And all Europa shall rejoice at thee, As once Europa did at lusty Jove, When he would play the noble beast in love.
USER: Bull Jove, sir, had an amiable low: And some such strange bull leap'd your father's cow, And got a calf in that same noble feat, Much like to you, for you have just his bleat.
BOT: For this I owe you: here comes other reckonings. Which is the lady I must seize upon?
USER: This same is she, and I do give you her.
BOT: Why then, she's mine. Sweet, let me see your face.
USER: No, that you shall not, till you take her hand Before this friar, and swear to marry her.
BOT: Give me your hand: before this holy friar, I am your husband, if you like of me.
USER: And when I liv'd, I was your other wife:
BOT: Another Hero!
USER: Nothing certainer: One Hero died defil'd, but I do live, And surely as I live, I am a maid.
BOT: The former Hero! Hero that is dead!
USER: She died, my lord, but whiles her slander liv'd.
BOT: All this amazement can I qualify: When after that the holy rites are ended, I'll tell you largely of fair Hero's death: Meantime, let wonder seem familiar, And to the chapel let us presently.
USER: Soft and fair, friar. Which is Beatrice?
BOT: Do not you love me?
USER: Why, no; no more than reason.
BOT: Why, then, your uncle and the Prince and Claudio Have been deceived; for they swore you did.
USER: Do not you love me?
BOT: Troth, no; no more than reason.
USER: Why, then my cousin, Margaret, and Ursula, Are much deceiv'd; for they did swear you did.
BOT: They swore that you were almost sick for me.
USER: They swore that you were well-nigh dead for me.
BOT: 'Tis no such matter. Then you do not love me?
USER: No, truly, but in friendly recompense.
BOT: Come, cousin, I am sure you love the gentleman.
USER: And I'll be sworn upon 't that he loves her; For here's a paper written in his hand, A halting sonnet of his own pure brain, Fashion'd to Beatrice.
BOT: And here's another, Writ in my cousin's hand, stolen from her pocket, Containing her affection unto Benedick.
USER: A miracle! here's our own hands against our hearts. Come, I will have thee; but, by this light, I take thee for pity.
BOT: I would not deny you; but, by this good day, I yield upon great persuasion, and partly to save your life, for I was told you were in a consumption.
USER: Peace! I will stop your mouth. [Kisses her.]
BOT: How dost thou, Benedick, the married man?
USER: Come, come, we are friends. Let's have a dance ere we are married, that we may lighten our own hearts and our wives' heels.
BOT: We'll have dancing afterward.
USER: First, of my word; therefore play, music! Prince, thou art sad; get thee a wife, get thee a wife: there is no staff more reverent than one tipped with horn.
BOT: My lord, your brother John is ta'en in flight, And brought with armed men back to Messina.
USER: Think not on him till tomorrow: I'll devise thee brave punishments for him. Strike up, pipers! THE TRAGEDY OF OTHELLO, THE MOOR OF VENICE Contents

USER: Tush, never tell me, I take it much unkindly That thou, Iago, who hast had my purse, As if the strings were thine, shouldst know of this.
BOT: 'Sblood, but you will not hear me. If ever I did dream of such a matter, Abhor me.
USER: Thou told'st me, thou didst hold him in thy hate.
BOT: By heaven, I rather would have been his hangman.
USER: I would not follow him, then.
BOT: What a full fortune does the thick-lips owe, If he can carry't thus!
USER: Here is her father's house, I'll call aloud.
BOT: Do, with like timorous accent and dire yell As when, by night and negligence, the fire Is spied in populous cities.
USER: What ho, Brabantio! Signior Brabantio, ho!
BOT: Awake! what ho, Brabantio! Thieves, thieves! Look to your house, your daughter, and your bags! Thieves, thieves! Brabantio appears above at a window.
USER: What is the reason of this terrible summons? What is the matter there?
BOT: Signior, is all your family within?
USER: Are your doors locked?
BOT: Why, wherefore ask you this?
USER: What, have you lost your wits?
BOT: Most reverend signior, do you know my voice?
USER: Not I. What are you?
BOT: My name is Roderigo.
USER: Sir, sir, sir, -
BOT: But thou must needs be sure My spirit and my place have in them power To make this bitter to thee.
USER: Patience, good sir.
BOT: What tell'st thou me of robbing? This is Venice. My house is not a grange.
USER: Most grave Brabantio, In simple and pure soul I come to you.
BOT: What profane wretch art thou?
USER: I am one, sir, that comes to tell you your daughter and the Moor are now making the beast with two backs.
BOT: Thou art a villain.
USER: You are a senator.
BOT: This thou shalt answer. I know thee, Roderigo.
USER: Strike on the tinder, ho! Give me a taper! Call up all my people! This accident is not unlike my dream, Belief of it oppresses me already. Light, I say, light!
BOT: Truly I think they are.
USER: Yes, sir, I have indeed.
BOT: Call up my brother. O, would you had had her! Some one way, some another. Do you know Where we may apprehend her and the Moor?
USER: I think I can discover him, if you please To get good guard, and go along with me.
BOT: Pray you lead on. At every house I'll call, I may command at most. Get weapons, ho! And raise some special officers of night. On, good Roderigo. I will deserve your pains.

USER: 'Tis better as it is.
BOT: Those are the raised father and his friends: You were best go in.
USER: Not I; I must be found. My parts, my title, and my perfect soul Shall manifest me rightly. Is it they?
BOT: By Janus, I think no.
USER: The servants of the duke and my lieutenant. The goodness of the night upon you, friends! What is the news?
BOT: The duke does greet you, general, And he requires your haste-post-haste appearance Even on the instant.
USER: What is the matter, think you?
BOT: 'Tis well I am found by you. I will but spend a word here in the house, And go with you.
USER: Ancient, what makes he here?
BOT: Faith, he tonight hath boarded a land carrack: If it prove lawful prize, he's made forever.
USER: I do not understand.
BOT: He's married.
USER: To who?
BOT: Marry to - Come, captain, will you go?
USER: Have with you.
BOT: Here comes another troop to seek for you.
USER: It is Brabantio. General, be advis'd, He comes to bad intent.
BOT: Holla, stand there!
USER: Signior, it is the Moor.
BOT: Down with him, thief!
USER: You, Roderigo! Come, sir, I am for you.
BOT: Keep up your bright swords, for the dew will rust them. Good signior, you shall more command with years Than with your weapons.
USER: Hold your hands, Both you of my inclining and the rest: Were it my cue to fight, I should have known it Without a prompter. Where will you that I go To answer this your charge?
BOT: To prison, till fit time Of law and course of direct session Call thee to answer.
USER: What if I do obey? How may the duke be therewith satisfied, Whose messengers are here about my side, Upon some present business of the state, To bring me to him?
BOT: 'Tis true, most worthy signior, The duke's in council, and your noble self, I am sure is sent for.

USER: There is no composition in these news That gives them credit.
BOT: Indeed, they are disproportion'd; My letters say a hundred and seven galleys.
USER: Nay, it is possible enough to judgement: I do not so secure me in the error, But the main article I do approve In fearful sense.
BOT: A messenger from the galleys.
USER: Now, - what's the business?
BOT: The Turkish preparation makes for Rhodes, So was I bid report here to the state By Signior Angelo.
USER: How say you by this change?
BOT: Nay, in all confidence, he's not for Rhodes.
USER: Here is more news.
BOT: The Ottomites, reverend and gracious, Steering with due course toward the isle of Rhodes, Have there injointed them with an after fleet.
USER: Ay, so I thought. How many, as you guess?
BOT: 'Tis certain, then, for Cyprus. Marcus Luccicos, is not he in town?
USER: He's now in Florence.
BOT: Write from us to him; post-post-haste dispatch.
USER: Here comes Brabantio and the valiant Moor.
BOT: Valiant Othello, we must straight employ you Against the general enemy Ottoman. We lack'd your counsel and your help tonight.
USER: Why, what's the matter?
BOT: My daughter! O, my daughter! DUKE and SENATORS. Dead?
USER: Ay, to me. She is abused, stol'n from me, and corrupted By spells and medicines bought of mountebanks; For nature so preposterously to err, Being not deficient, blind, or lame of sense, Sans witchcraft could not.
BOT: Humbly I thank your grace. Here is the man, this Moor, whom now it seems Your special mandate for the state affairs Hath hither brought.
USER: We are very sorry for 't.
BOT: Nothing, but this is so.
USER: To vouch this is no proof; Without more wider and more overt test Than these thin habits and poor likelihoods Of modern seeming do prefer against him.
BOT: But, Othello, speak: Did you by indirect and forced courses Subdue and poison this young maid's affections? Or came it by request, and such fair question As soul to soul affordeth?
USER: Fetch Desdemona hither.
BOT: Say it, Othello.
USER: I think this tale would win my daughter too. Good Brabantio, Take up this mangled matter at the best. Men do their broken weapons rather use Than their bare hands.
BOT: If you please, Be't at her father's.
USER: I'll not have it so.
BOT: Nor I.
USER: Nor I. I would not there reside, To put my father in impatient thoughts, By being in his eye. Most gracious duke, To my unfolding lend your prosperous ear, And let me find a charter in your voice T' assist my simpleness.
BOT: What would you, Desdemona?
USER: Be it as you shall privately determine, Either for her stay or going. The affair cries haste, And speed must answer it.
BOT: You must away tonight.
USER: With all my heart.
BOT: At nine i' the morning here we'll meet again. Othello, leave some officer behind, And he shall our commission bring to you, With such things else of quality and respect As doth import you.
USER: So please your grace, my ancient, A man he is of honesty and trust, To his conveyance I assign my wife, With what else needful your good grace shall think To be sent after me.
BOT: Let it be so. Good night to everyone. [_To Brabantio._] And, noble signior, If virtue no delighted beauty lack, Your son-in-law is far more fair than black.
USER: Adieu, brave Moor, use Desdemona well.
BOT: Look to her, Moor, if thou hast eyes to see: She has deceiv'd her father, and may thee.
USER: Iago -
BOT: What sayst thou, noble heart?
USER: What will I do, thinkest thou?
BOT: Why, go to bed and sleep.
USER: I will incontinently drown myself.
BOT: If thou dost, I shall never love thee after. Why, thou silly gentleman!
USER: It is silliness to live, when to live is torment; and then have we a prescription to die when death is our physician.
BOT: What should I do? I confess it is my shame to be so fond, but it is not in my virtue to amend it.
USER: It cannot be.
BOT: Wilt thou be fast to my hopes if I depend on the issue?
USER: Where shall we meet i' the morning?
BOT: At my lodging.
USER: I'll be with thee betimes.
BOT: Go to, farewell. Do you hear, Roderigo?
USER: What say you?
BOT: No more of drowning, do you hear?
USER: I am changed. I'll sell all my land.

USER: What from the cape can you discern at sea?
BOT: Nothing at all, it is a high-wrought flood. I cannot 'twixt the heaven and the main Descry a sail.
USER: If that the Turkish fleet Be not enshelter'd, and embay'd, they are drown'd. It is impossible to bear it out.
BOT: News, lads! Our wars are done. The desperate tempest hath so bang'd the Turks That their designment halts. A noble ship of Venice Hath seen a grievous wreck and sufferance On most part of their fleet.
USER: How? Is this true?
BOT: The ship is here put in, A Veronessa; Michael Cassio, Lieutenant to the warlike Moor Othello, Is come on shore; the Moor himself at sea, And is in full commission here for Cyprus.
USER: I am glad on't. 'Tis a worthy governor.
BOT: But this same Cassio, though he speak of comfort Touching the Turkish loss, yet he looks sadly, And prays the Moor be safe; for they were parted With foul and violent tempest.
USER: Come, let's do so; For every minute is expectancy Of more arrivance.
BOT: Thanks you, the valiant of this warlike isle, That so approve the Moor! O, let the heavens Give him defence against the elements, For I have lost him on a dangerous sea.
USER: Is he well shipp'd?
BOT: His bark is stoutly timber'd, and his pilot Of very expert and approv'd allowance; Therefore my hopes, not surfeited to death, Stand in bold cure.
USER: What noise?
BOT: The town is empty; on the brow o' the sea Stand ranks of people, and they cry "A sail!"
USER: My hopes do shape him for the governor.
BOT: They do discharge their shot of courtesy. Our friends at least.
USER: I pray you, sir, go forth, And give us truth who 'tis that is arriv'd.
BOT: I shall.
USER: But, good lieutenant, is your general wiv'd?
BOT: Most fortunately: he hath achiev'd a maid That paragons description and wild fame, One that excels the quirks of blazoning pens, And in the essential vesture of creation Does tire the ingener. How now? Who has put in?
USER: 'Tis one Iago, ancient to the general.
BOT: What is she?
USER: I thank you, valiant Cassio. What tidings can you tell me of my lord?
BOT: He is not yet arrived, nor know I aught But that he's well, and will be shortly here.
USER: O, but I fear - How lost you company?
BOT: The great contention of the sea and skies Parted our fellowship. But, hark! a sail.
USER: They give their greeting to the citadel. This likewise is a friend.
BOT: See for the news. Good ancient, you are welcome. [_To Emilia._] Welcome, mistress. Let it not gall your patience, good Iago, That I extend my manners; 'tis my breeding That gives me this bold show of courtesy.
USER: Sir, would she give you so much of her lips As of her tongue she oft bestows on me, You would have enough.
BOT: Alas, she has no speech.
USER: In faith, too much. I find it still when I have list to sleep. Marry, before your ladyship, I grant, She puts her tongue a little in her heart, And chides with thinking.
BOT: You have little cause to say so.
USER: Come on, come on; you are pictures out of doors, Bells in your parlours, wild-cats in your kitchens, Saints in your injuries, devils being offended, Players in your housewifery, and housewives in your beds.
BOT: O, fie upon thee, slanderer!
USER: Nay, it is true, or else I am a Turk. You rise to play, and go to bed to work.
BOT: You shall not write my praise.
USER: No, let me not.
BOT: What wouldst thou write of me, if thou shouldst praise me?
USER: O gentle lady, do not put me to't, For I am nothing if not critical.
BOT: Come on, assay. - There's one gone to the harbour?
USER: Ay, madam.
BOT: I am not merry, but I do beguile The thing I am, by seeming otherwise. - Come, how wouldst thou praise me?
USER: Well prais'd! How if she be black and witty?
BOT: If she be black, and thereto have a wit, She'll find a white that shall her blackness fit.
USER: Worse and worse.
BOT: How if fair and foolish?
USER: She never yet was foolish that was fair, For even her folly help'd her to an heir.
BOT: These are old fond paradoxes to make fools laugh i' the alehouse. What miserable praise hast thou for her that's foul and foolish?
USER: There's none so foul and foolish thereunto, But does foul pranks which fair and wise ones do.
BOT: O heavy ignorance! Thou praisest the worst best. But what praise couldst thou bestow on a deserving woman indeed, one that in the authority of her merit did justly put on the vouch of very malice itself?
USER: To do what?
BOT: To suckle fools and chronicle small beer.
USER: O most lame and impotent conclusion! - Do not learn of him, Emilia, though he be thy husband. - How say you, Cassio? is he not a most profane and liberal counsellor?
BOT: He speaks home, madam. You may relish him more in the soldier than in the scholar.
USER: 'Tis truly so.
BOT: Let's meet him, and receive him.
USER: Lo, where he comes!
BOT: O my fair warrior!
USER: My dear Othello!
BOT: The heavens forbid But that our loves and comforts should increase Even as our days do grow!
USER: Amen to that, sweet powers! I cannot speak enough of this content. It stops me here; it is too much of joy: And this, and this, the greatest discords be [_They kiss._] That e'er our hearts shall make!
BOT: But I'll set down the pegs that make this music, As honest as I am.
USER: With him? Why, 'tis not possible.
BOT: I cannot believe that in her, she is full of most blessed condition.
USER: Blest fig's end! the wine she drinks is made of grapes: if she had been blessed, she would never have loved the Moor. Blessed pudding! Didst thou not see her paddle with the palm of his hand? Didst not mark that?
BOT: Yes, that I did. But that was but courtesy.
USER: Well.
BOT: I will do this, if I can bring it to any opportunity.
USER: I warrant thee. Meet me by and by at the citadel: I must fetch his necessaries ashore. Farewell.
BOT: Adieu.

USER: Good Michael, look you to the guard tonight. Let's teach ourselves that honourable stop, Not to outsport discretion.
BOT: Iago hath direction what to do. But notwithstanding with my personal eye Will I look to't.
USER: Welcome, Iago. We must to the watch.
BOT: She's a most exquisite lady.
USER: And, I'll warrant her, full of game.
BOT: Indeed, she is a most fresh and delicate creature.
USER: What an eye she has! methinks it sounds a parley to provocation.
BOT: An inviting eye, and yet methinks right modest.
USER: And when she speaks, is it not an alarm to love?
BOT: She is indeed perfection.
USER: Well, happiness to their sheets! Come, lieutenant, I have a stoup of wine; and here without are a brace of Cyprus gallants that would fain have a measure to the health of black Othello.
BOT: Not tonight, good Iago. I have very poor and unhappy brains for drinking. I could well wish courtesy would invent some other custom of entertainment.
USER: O, they are our friends; but one cup: I'll drink for you.
BOT: I have drunk but one cup tonight, and that was craftily qualified too, and behold, what innovation it makes here: I am unfortunate in the infirmity, and dare not task my weakness with any more.
USER: What, man! 'Tis a night of revels. The gallants desire it.
BOT: Where are they?
USER: Here at the door. I pray you, call them in.
BOT: I'll do't; but it dislikes me.
USER: 'Fore God, they have given me a rouse already.
BOT: Good faith, a little one; not past a pint, as I am a soldier.
USER: Some wine, ho! _And let me the cannikin clink, clink, And let me the cannikin clink, clink: A soldier's a man, O, man's life's but a span, Why then let a soldier drink._ Some wine, boys!
BOT: 'Fore God, an excellent song.
USER: I learned it in England, where indeed they are most potent in potting: your Dane, your German, and your swag-bellied Hollander, - drink, ho! - are nothing to your English.
BOT: Is your Englishman so expert in his drinking?
USER: Why, he drinks you, with facility, your Dane dead drunk; he sweats not to overthrow your Almain; he gives your Hollander a vomit ere the next pottle can be filled.
BOT: To the health of our general!
USER: I am for it, lieutenant; and I'll do you justice.
BOT: 'Fore God, this is a more exquisite song than the other.
USER: Will you hear 't again?
BOT: No, for I hold him to be unworthy of his place that does those things. Well, God's above all, and there be souls must be saved, and there be souls must not be saved.
USER: It's true, good lieutenant.
BOT: For mine own part, no offence to the general, nor any man of quality, I hope to be saved.
USER: And so do I too, lieutenant.
BOT: Excellent well.
USER: Why, very well then. You must not think, then, that I am drunk.
BOT: To the platform, masters. Come, let's set the watch.
USER: But is he often thus?
BOT: 'Tis evermore the prologue to his sleep: He'll watch the horologe a double set If drink rock not his cradle.
USER: It were well The general were put in mind of it. Perhaps he sees it not, or his good nature Prizes the virtue that appears in Cassio, And looks not on his evils: is not this true?
BOT: I pray you, after the lieutenant; go.
USER: And 'tis great pity that the noble Moor Should hazard such a place as his own second With one of an ingraft infirmity: It were an honest action to say so To the Moor.
BOT: Not I, for this fair island. I do love Cassio well and would do much To cure him of this evil. But, hark! What noise?
USER: Zounds, you rogue, you rascal!
BOT: What's the matter, lieutenant?
USER: A knave teach me my duty! I'll beat the knave into a twiggen bottle.
BOT: Beat me?
USER: Dost thou prate, rogue?
BOT: Nay, good lieutenant; I pray you, sir, hold your hand.
USER: Let me go, sir, Or I'll knock you o'er the mazard.
BOT: Come, come, you're drunk.
USER: Drunk?
BOT: What is the matter here?
USER: Zounds, I bleed still, I am hurt to the death.
BOT: Hold, for your lives!
USER: Hold, ho! lieutenant, - sir, - Montano, - gentlemen, - Have you forgot all place of sense and duty? Hold! The general speaks to you; hold, hold, for shame!
BOT: How comes it, Michael, you are thus forgot?
USER: I pray you, pardon me; I cannot speak.
BOT: If partially affin'd, or leagu'd in office, Thou dost deliver more or less than truth, Thou art no soldier.
USER: I know, Iago, Thy honesty and love doth mince this matter, Making it light to Cassio. Cassio, I love thee, But never more be officer of mine. Look, if my gentle love be not rais'd up! I'll make thee an example.
BOT: What's the matter?
USER: What, are you hurt, lieutenant?
BOT: Ay, past all surgery.
USER: Marry, Heaven forbid!
BOT: Reputation, reputation, reputation! O, I have lost my reputation! I have lost the immortal part of myself, and what remains is bestial. My reputation, Iago, my reputation!
USER: What was he that you followed with your sword? What had he done to you?
BOT: I know not.
USER: Is't possible?
BOT: Why, but you are now well enough: how came you thus recovered?
USER: It hath pleased the devil drunkenness to give place to the devil wrath. One unperfectness shows me another, to make me frankly despise myself.
BOT: Come, you are too severe a moraler. As the time, the place, and the condition of this country stands, I could heartily wish this had not befallen; but since it is as it is, mend it for your own good.
USER: Come, come, good wine is a good familiar creature, if it be well used. Exclaim no more against it. And, good lieutenant, I think you think I love you.
BOT: I have well approved it, sir. - I drunk!
USER: You advise me well.
BOT: I protest, in the sincerity of love and honest kindness.
USER: I think it freely; and betimes in the morning I will beseech the virtuous Desdemona to undertake for me; I am desperate of my fortunes if they check me here.
BOT: You are in the right. Good night, lieutenant, I must to the watch.
USER: Good night, honest Iago.

USER: Masters, play here, I will content your pains, Something that's brief; and bid "Good morrow, general."
BOT: Why, masters, have your instruments been in Naples, that they speak i' the nose thus?
USER: How, sir, how?
BOT: Are these, I pray you, wind instruments?
USER: Ay, marry, are they, sir.
BOT: O, thereby hangs a tail.
USER: Whereby hangs a tale, sir?
BOT: Marry, sir, by many a wind instrument that I know. But, masters, here's money for you: and the general so likes your music, that he desires you, for love's sake, to make no more noise with it.
USER: Well, sir, we will not.
BOT: If you have any music that may not be heard, to't again. But, as they say, to hear music the general does not greatly care.
USER: We have none such, sir.
BOT: Then put up your pipes in your bag, for I'll away. Go, vanish into air, away!
USER: Dost thou hear, mine honest friend?
BOT: No, I hear not your honest friend. I hear you.
USER: Prithee, keep up thy quillets. There's a poor piece of gold for thee: if the gentlewoman that attends the general's wife be stirring, tell her there's one Cassio entreats her a little favour of speech. Wilt thou do this?
BOT: She is stirring, sir; if she will stir hither, I shall seem to notify unto her.
USER: Do, good my friend. In happy time, Iago.
BOT: You have not been a-bed, then?
USER: Why, no. The day had broke Before we parted. I have made bold, Iago, To send in to your wife. My suit to her Is, that she will to virtuous Desdemona Procure me some access.
BOT: I'll send her to you presently, And I'll devise a mean to draw the Moor Out of the way, that your converse and business May be more free.
USER: I humbly thank you for't. I never knew A Florentine more kind and honest.
BOT: Yet, I beseech you, If you think fit, or that it may be done, Give me advantage of some brief discourse With Desdemona alone.
USER: Pray you, come in. I will bestow you where you shall have time To speak your bosom freely.
BOT: I am much bound to you.

USER: These letters give, Iago, to the pilot, And by him do my duties to the senate. That done, I will be walking on the works, Repair there to me.
BOT: Well, my good lord, I'll do't.
USER: This fortification, gentlemen, shall we see't?
BOT: We'll wait upon your lordship.

USER: Be thou assured, good Cassio, I will do All my abilities in thy behalf.
BOT: Good madam, do. I warrant it grieves my husband As if the cause were his.
USER: O, that's an honest fellow. Do not doubt, Cassio, But I will have my lord and you again As friendly as you were.
BOT: Bounteous madam, Whatever shall become of Michael Cassio, He's never anything but your true servant.
USER: I know't. I thank you. You do love my lord. You have known him long; and be you well assur'd He shall in strangeness stand no farther off Than in a politic distance.
BOT: Madam, here comes my lord.
USER: Madam, I'll take my leave.
BOT: Why, stay, and hear me speak.
USER: Madam, not now. I am very ill at ease, Unfit for mine own purposes.
BOT: Well, do your discretion.
USER: Ha, I like not that.
BOT: What dost thou say?
USER: Nothing, my lord; or if - I know not what.
BOT: Was not that Cassio parted from my wife?
USER: Cassio, my lord? No, sure, I cannot think it, That he would steal away so guilty-like, Seeing you coming.
BOT: I do believe 'twas he.
USER: How now, my lord? I have been talking with a suitor here, A man that languishes in your displeasure.
BOT: Who is't you mean?
USER: Went he hence now?
BOT: Ay, sooth; so humbled That he hath left part of his grief with me To suffer with him. Good love, call him back.
USER: Not now, sweet Desdemon, some other time.
BOT: But shall't be shortly?
USER: The sooner, sweet, for you.
BOT: Shall't be tonight at supper?
USER: No, not tonight.
BOT: Tomorrow dinner then?
USER: I shall not dine at home; I meet the captains at the citadel.
BOT: Prithee no more. Let him come when he will; I will deny thee nothing.
USER: I will deny thee nothing. Whereon, I do beseech thee, grant me this, To leave me but a little to myself.
BOT: Shall I deny you? No, farewell, my lord.
USER: Farewell, my Desdemona. I'll come to thee straight.
BOT: Emilia, come. Be as your fancies teach you. Whate'er you be, I am obedient.
USER: Excellent wretch! Perdition catch my soul, But I do love thee! And when I love thee not, Chaos is come again.
BOT: My noble lord, -
USER: What dost thou say, Iago?
BOT: Did Michael Cassio, when you woo'd my lady, Know of your love?
USER: He did, from first to last. Why dost thou ask?
BOT: But for a satisfaction of my thought. No further harm.
USER: Why of thy thought, Iago?
BOT: I did not think he had been acquainted with her.
USER: O yes, and went between us very oft.
BOT: Indeed?
USER: Indeed? Ay, indeed. Discern'st thou aught in that? Is he not honest?
BOT: Honest, my lord?
USER: Honest? ay, honest.
BOT: My lord, for aught I know.
USER: What dost thou think?
BOT: Think, my lord?
USER: My lord, you know I love you.
BOT: For Michael Cassio, I dare be sworn I think that he is honest.
USER: I think so too.
BOT: Men should be what they seem; Or those that be not, would they might seem none!
USER: Certain, men should be what they seem.
BOT: Why then, I think Cassio's an honest man.
USER: Nay, yet there's more in this: I prithee, speak to me as to thy thinkings, As thou dost ruminate, and give thy worst of thoughts The worst of words.
BOT: Thou dost conspire against thy friend, Iago, If thou but think'st him wrong'd and mak'st his ear A stranger to thy thoughts.
USER: What dost thou mean?
BOT: By heaven, I'll know thy thoughts.
USER: You cannot, if my heart were in your hand, Nor shall not, whilst 'tis in my custody.
BOT: Ha?
USER: O misery!
BOT: Poor and content is rich, and rich enough; But riches fineless is as poor as winter To him that ever fears he shall be poor. Good heaven, the souls of all my tribe defend From jealousy!
USER: Dost thou say so?
BOT: She did deceive her father, marrying you; And when she seem'd to shake and fear your looks, She loved them most.
USER: And so she did.
BOT: I am bound to thee for ever.
USER: I see this hath a little dash'd your spirits.
BOT: Not a jot, not a jot.
USER: Trust me, I fear it has. I hope you will consider what is spoke Comes from my love. But I do see you're mov'd. I am to pray you not to strain my speech To grosser issues nor to larger reach Than to suspicion.
BOT: I will not.
USER: Should you do so, my lord, My speech should fall into such vile success Which my thoughts aim'd not. Cassio's my worthy friend. My lord, I see you're mov'd.
BOT: No, not much mov'd. I do not think but Desdemona's honest.
USER: Long live she so! And long live you to think so!
BOT: And yet, how nature erring from itself -
USER: Farewell, farewell: If more thou dost perceive, let me know more; Set on thy wife to observe. Leave me, Iago.
BOT: Why did I marry? This honest creature doubtless Sees and knows more, much more, than he unfolds.
USER: Fear not my government.
BOT: I once more take my leave.
USER: How now, my dear Othello? Your dinner, and the generous islanders By you invited, do attend your presence.
BOT: I am to blame.
USER: Why do you speak so faintly? Are you not well?
BOT: I have a pain upon my forehead here.
USER: Faith, that's with watching, 'twill away again; Let me but bind it hard, within this hour It will be well.
BOT: Your napkin is too little; Let it alone. Come, I'll go in with you.
USER: I am very sorry that you are not well.
BOT: How now? What do you here alone?
USER: Do not you chide. I have a thing for you.
BOT: A thing for me? It is a common thing -
USER: Ha?
BOT: To have a foolish wife.
USER: O, is that all? What will you give me now For that same handkerchief?
BOT: What handkerchief?
USER: What handkerchief? Why, that the Moor first gave to Desdemona, That which so often you did bid me steal.
BOT: Hast stol'n it from her?
USER: No, faith, she let it drop by negligence, And, to the advantage, I being here, took 't up. Look, here it is.
BOT: A good wench, give it me.
USER: What will you do with't, that you have been so earnest To have me filch it?
BOT: If it be not for some purpose of import, Give 't me again. Poor lady, she'll run mad When she shall lack it.
USER: Ha! ha! false to me?
BOT: Why, how now, general? No more of that.
USER: Avaunt! be gone! Thou hast set me on the rack. I swear 'tis better to be much abus'd Than but to know't a little.
BOT: How now, my lord?
USER: I am sorry to hear this.
BOT: Is't possible, my lord?
USER: Villain, be sure thou prove my love a whore; Be sure of it. Give me the ocular proof, Or, by the worth of man's eternal soul, Thou hadst been better have been born a dog Than answer my wak'd wrath.
BOT: Is't come to this?
USER: Make me to see't, or at the least so prove it, That the probation bear no hinge nor loop To hang a doubt on, or woe upon thy life!
BOT: My noble lord, -
USER: Nay, stay. Thou shouldst be honest.
BOT: I should be wise; for honesty's a fool, And loses that it works for.
USER: I see, sir, you are eaten up with passion. I do repent me that I put it to you. You would be satisfied?
BOT: Would? Nay, I will.
USER: And may; but how? How satisfied, my lord? Would you, the supervisor, grossly gape on, Behold her topp'd?
BOT: Death and damnation! O!
USER: Give me a living reason she's disloyal.
BOT: O monstrous! monstrous!
USER: Nay, this was but his dream.
BOT: But this denoted a foregone conclusion. 'Tis a shrewd doubt, though it be but a dream.
USER: And this may help to thicken other proofs That do demonstrate thinly.
BOT: I'll tear her all to pieces.
USER: Nay, but be wise. Yet we see nothing done, She may be honest yet. Tell me but this, Have you not sometimes seen a handkerchief Spotted with strawberries in your wife's hand?
BOT: I gave her such a one, 'twas my first gift.
USER: I know not that: but such a handkerchief (I am sure it was your wife's) did I today See Cassio wipe his beard with.
BOT: If it be that, -
USER: If it be that, or any that was hers, It speaks against her with the other proofs.
BOT: Yet be content.
USER: O, blood, Iago, blood!
BOT: Patience, I say. Your mind perhaps may change.
USER: I greet thy love, Not with vain thanks, but with acceptance bounteous, And will upon the instant put thee to 't. Within these three days let me hear thee say That Cassio's not alive.
BOT: My friend is dead. 'Tis done at your request. But let her live.
USER: Damn her, lewd minx! O, damn her, damn her! Come, go with me apart, I will withdraw To furnish me with some swift means of death For the fair devil. Now art thou my lieutenant.
BOT: I am your own for ever.

USER: Do you know, sirrah, where Lieutenant Cassio lies?
BOT: I dare not say he lies anywhere.
USER: Why, man?
BOT: He's a soldier; and for one to say a soldier lies is stabbing.
USER: Go to. Where lodges he?
BOT: To tell you where he lodges is to tell you where I lie.
USER: Can anything be made of this?
BOT: I know not where he lodges; and for me to devise a lodging, and say he lies here, or he lies there, were to lie in mine own throat.
USER: Can you inquire him out, and be edified by report?
BOT: I will catechize the world for him, that is, make questions and by them answer.
USER: Seek him, bid him come hither. Tell him I have moved my lord on his behalf, and hope all will be well.
BOT: To do this is within the compass of man's wit, and therefore I will attempt the doing it.
USER: Where should I lose that handkerchief, Emilia?
BOT: I know not, madam.
USER: Believe me, I had rather have lost my purse Full of crusadoes. And but my noble Moor Is true of mind and made of no such baseness As jealous creatures are, it were enough To put him to ill thinking.
BOT: Is he not jealous?
USER: Who, he? I think the sun where he was born Drew all such humours from him.
BOT: Look, where he comes.
USER: I will not leave him now till Cassio Be call'd to him. How is't with you, my lord?
BOT: Well, my good lady. [_Aside._] O, hardness to dissemble! How do you, Desdemona?
USER: Well, my good lord.
BOT: Give me your hand. This hand is moist, my lady.
USER: It yet hath felt no age nor known no sorrow.
BOT: You may indeed say so, For 'twas that hand that gave away my heart.
USER: A liberal hand. The hearts of old gave hands, But our new heraldry is hands, not hearts.
BOT: I cannot speak of this. Come now, your promise.
USER: What promise, chuck?
BOT: I have sent to bid Cassio come speak with you.
USER: I have a salt and sorry rheum offends me. Lend me thy handkerchief.
BOT: Here, my lord.
USER: That which I gave you.
BOT: I have it not about me.
USER: Not?
BOT: No, faith, my lord.
USER: Is't possible?
BOT: Indeed? Is't true?
USER: Most veritable, therefore look to 't well.
BOT: Then would to God that I had never seen 't!
USER: Ha? wherefore?
BOT: Why do you speak so startingly and rash?
USER: Is't lost? is't gone? speak, is it out of the way?
BOT: Heaven bless us!
USER: Say you?
BOT: It is not lost, but what and if it were?
USER: How?
BOT: I say it is not lost.
USER: Fetch't, let me see 't.
BOT: Why, so I can, sir, but I will not now. This is a trick to put me from my suit. Pray you, let Cassio be receiv'd again.
USER: Fetch me the handkerchief! My mind misgives.
BOT: Come, come. You'll never meet a more sufficient man.
USER: The handkerchief!
BOT: I pray, talk me of Cassio.
USER: The handkerchief!
BOT: A man that all his time Hath founded his good fortunes on your love, Shar'd dangers with you, -
USER: The handkerchief!
BOT: In sooth, you are to blame.
USER: Away!
BOT: Is not this man jealous?
USER: I ne'er saw this before. Sure there's some wonder in this handkerchief, I am most unhappy in the loss of it.
BOT: 'Tis not a year or two shows us a man: They are all but stomachs and we all but food; They eat us hungerly, and when they are full, They belch us. Look you, Cassio and my husband.
USER: There is no other way; 'tis she must do 't, And, lo, the happiness! Go and importune her.
BOT: How now, good Cassio, what's the news with you?
USER: Is my lord angry?
BOT: He went hence but now, And certainly in strange unquietness.
USER: Pray heaven it be state matters, as you think, And no conception nor no jealous toy Concerning you.
BOT: Alas the day, I never gave him cause!
USER: But jealous souls will not be answer'd so; They are not ever jealous for the cause, But jealous for they are jealous: 'tis a monster Begot upon itself, born on itself.
BOT: Heaven keep that monster from Othello's mind!
USER: Lady, amen.
BOT: I will go seek him. Cassio, walk hereabout: If I do find him fit, I'll move your suit, And seek to effect it to my uttermost.
USER: I humbly thank your ladyship.
BOT: Save you, friend Cassio!
USER: What make you from home? How is it with you, my most fair Bianca? I' faith, sweet love, I was coming to your house.
BOT: And I was going to your lodging, Cassio. What, keep a week away? Seven days and nights? Eight score eight hours, and lovers' absent hours, More tedious than the dial eight score times? O weary reckoning!
USER: Pardon me, Bianca. I have this while with leaden thoughts been press'd, But I shall in a more continuate time Strike off this score of absence. Sweet Bianca, Take me this work out.
BOT: O Cassio, whence came this? This is some token from a newer friend. To the felt absence now I feel a cause. Is't come to this? Well, well.
USER: Go to, woman! Throw your vile guesses in the devil's teeth, From whence you have them. You are jealous now That this is from some mistress, some remembrance. No, in good troth, Bianca.
BOT: Why, whose is it?
USER: I know not neither. I found it in my chamber. I like the work well. Ere it be demanded, As like enough it will, I'd have it copied. Take it, and do 't, and leave me for this time.
BOT: Leave you, wherefore?
USER: I do attend here on the general, And think it no addition, nor my wish, To have him see me woman'd.
BOT: Why, I pray you?
USER: Not that I love you not.
BOT: But that you do not love me. I pray you bring me on the way a little, And say if I shall see you soon at night.
USER: 'Tis but a little way that I can bring you, For I attend here. But I'll see you soon.
BOT: 'Tis very good; I must be circumstanc'd.

USER: Will you think so?
BOT: Think so, Iago?
USER: What, To kiss in private?
BOT: An unauthoriz'd kiss.
USER: Or to be naked with her friend in bed An hour or more, not meaning any harm?
