BOT: Naked in bed, Iago, and not mean harm? It is hypocrisy against the devil: They that mean virtuously and yet do so, The devil their virtue tempts, and they tempt heaven.
USER: So they do nothing, 'tis a venial slip. But if I give my wife a handkerchief -
BOT: What then?
USER: Why then, 'tis hers, my lord, and being hers, She may, I think, bestow't on any man.
BOT: She is protectress of her honour too. May she give that?
USER: Her honour is an essence that's not seen; They have it very oft that have it not: But for the handkerchief -
BOT: By heaven, I would most gladly have forgot it. Thou said'st (O, it comes o'er my memory, As doth the raven o'er the infected house, Boding to all) he had my handkerchief.
USER: Ay, what of that?
BOT: That's not so good now.
USER: Hath he said anything?
BOT: He hath, my lord, but be you well assur'd, No more than he'll unswear.
USER: What hath he said?
BOT: Faith, that he did - I know not what he did.
USER: What? What?
BOT: Lie.
USER: With her?
BOT: With her, on her, what you will.
USER: Work on, My medicine, work! Thus credulous fools are caught, And many worthy and chaste dames even thus, All guiltless, meet reproach. What, ho! my lord! My lord, I say! Othello! How now, Cassio!
BOT: What's the matter?
USER: My lord is fallen into an epilepsy. This is his second fit. He had one yesterday.
BOT: Rub him about the temples.
USER: Dost thou mock me?
BOT: I mock you? No, by heaven. Would you would bear your fortune like a man!
USER: A horned man's a monster and a beast.
BOT: There's many a beast, then, in a populous city, And many a civil monster.
USER: Did he confess it?
BOT: O, thou art wise, 'tis certain.
USER: Dost thou hear, Iago? I will be found most cunning in my patience; But, - dost thou hear? - most bloody.
BOT: The worser that you give me the addition Whose want even kills me.
USER: Ply Desdemona well, and you are sure on't. How quickly should you speed!
BOT: Alas, poor caitiff!
USER: I never knew a woman love man so.
BOT: Alas, poor rogue! I think, i' faith, she loves me.
USER: Do you hear, Cassio?
BOT: Now he importunes him To tell it o'er. Go to, well said, well said.
USER: She gives it out that you shall marry her. Do you intend it?
BOT: Ha, ha, ha!
USER: Do you triumph, Roman? Do you triumph?
BOT: I marry her? What? A customer? I prithee, bear some charity to my wit, do not think it so unwholesome. Ha, ha, ha!
USER: So, so, so, so. They laugh that wins.
BOT: Faith, the cry goes that you shall marry her.
USER: Prithee say true.
BOT: I am a very villain else.
USER: Have you scored me? Well.
BOT: This is the monkey's own giving out. She is persuaded I will marry her, out of her own love and flattery, not out of my promise.
USER: Iago beckons me. Now he begins the story.
BOT: She was here even now. She haunts me in every place. I was the other day talking on the sea-bank with certain Venetians, and thither comes the bauble, and falls thus about my neck.
USER: Crying, "O dear Cassio!" as it were: his gesture imports it.
BOT: So hangs, and lolls, and weeps upon me; so hales and pulls me. Ha, ha, ha!
USER: Now he tells how she plucked him to my chamber. O, I see that nose of yours, but not that dog I shall throw it to.
BOT: Well, I must leave her company.
USER: Before me! look where she comes.
BOT: 'Tis such another fitchew! Marry, a perfum'd one. What do you mean by this haunting of me?
USER: How now, my sweet Bianca? How now, how now?
BOT: By heaven, that should be my handkerchief!
USER: If you'll come to supper tonight, you may. If you will not, come when you are next prepared for.
BOT: After her, after her.
USER: Faith, I must; she'll rail in the street else.
BOT: Will you sup there?
USER: Faith, I intend so.
BOT: Well, I may chance to see you, for I would very fain speak with you.
USER: Prithee come, will you?
BOT: Go to; say no more.
USER: Did you perceive how he laughed at his vice?
BOT: O Iago!
USER: And did you see the handkerchief?
BOT: Was that mine?
USER: Yours, by this hand: and to see how he prizes the foolish woman your wife! she gave it him, and he hath given it his whore.
BOT: I would have him nine years a-killing. A fine woman, a fair woman, a sweet woman!
USER: Nay, you must forget that.
BOT: Nay, that's not your way.
USER: Hang her, I do but say what she is. So delicate with her needle, an admirable musician! O, she will sing the savageness out of a bear! Of so high and plenteous wit and invention!
BOT: She's the worse for all this.
USER: O, a thousand, a thousand times: and then of so gentle a condition!
BOT: Ay, too gentle.
USER: Nay, that's certain. But yet the pity of it, Iago! O Iago, the pity of it, Iago!
BOT: If you are so fond over her iniquity, give her patent to offend, for if it touch not you, it comes near nobody.
USER: I will chop her into messes. Cuckold me!
BOT: O, 'tis foul in her.
USER: With mine officer!
BOT: That's fouler.
USER: Get me some poison, Iago; this night. I'll not expostulate with her, lest her body and beauty unprovide my mind again. This night, Iago.
BOT: Do it not with poison, strangle her in her bed, even the bed she hath contaminated.
USER: Good, good. The justice of it pleases. Very good.
BOT: And for Cassio, let me be his undertaker. You shall hear more by midnight.
USER: Excellent good. [_A trumpet within._] What trumpet is that same?
BOT: Something from Venice, sure. 'Tis Lodovico Come from the duke. See, your wife is with him.
USER: Save you, worthy general!
BOT: With all my heart, sir.
USER: The duke and senators of Venice greet you.
BOT: I kiss the instrument of their pleasures.
USER: And what's the news, good cousin Lodovico?
BOT: I am very glad to see you, signior. Welcome to Cyprus.
USER: I thank you. How does Lieutenant Cassio?
BOT: Lives, sir.
USER: Cousin, there's fall'n between him and my lord An unkind breach, but you shall make all well.
BOT: Are you sure of that?
USER: My lord?
BOT: He did not call; he's busy in the paper. Is there division 'twixt my lord and Cassio?
USER: A most unhappy one. I would do much To atone them, for the love I bear to Cassio.
BOT: Fire and brimstone!
USER: My lord?
BOT: Are you wise?
USER: What, is he angry?
BOT: May be the letter mov'd him; For, as I think, they do command him home, Deputing Cassio in his government.
USER: Trust me, I am glad on't.
BOT: Indeed!
USER: My lord?
BOT: I am glad to see you mad.
USER: Why, sweet Othello?
BOT: Devil!
USER: I have not deserv'd this.
BOT: My lord, this would not be believ'd in Venice, Though I should swear I saw't: 'tis very much. Make her amends. She weeps.
USER: O devil, devil! If that the earth could teem with woman's tears, Each drop she falls would prove a crocodile. Out of my sight!
BOT: I will not stay to offend you.
USER: Truly, an obedient lady. I do beseech your lordship, call her back.
BOT: Mistress!
USER: My lord?
BOT: What would you with her, sir?
USER: Who, I, my lord?
BOT: Is this the noble Moor, whom our full senate Call all in all sufficient? Is this the nature Whom passion could not shake? whose solid virtue The shot of accident nor dart of chance Could neither graze nor pierce?
USER: He is much chang'd.
BOT: Are his wits safe? Is he not light of brain?
USER: He's that he is. I may not breathe my censure What he might be. If what he might he is not, I would to heaven he were!
BOT: What, strike his wife?
USER: Faith, that was not so well; yet would I knew That stroke would prove the worst!
BOT: Is it his use? Or did the letters work upon his blood, And new-create this fault?
USER: Alas, alas! It is not honesty in me to speak What I have seen and known. You shall observe him, And his own courses will denote him so That I may save my speech: do but go after, And mark how he continues.
BOT: I am sorry that I am deceiv'd in him.

USER: You have seen nothing, then?
BOT: Nor ever heard, nor ever did suspect.
USER: Yes, you have seen Cassio and she together.
BOT: But then I saw no harm, and then I heard Each syllable that breath made up between them.
USER: What, did they never whisper?
BOT: Never, my lord.
USER: Nor send you out o' the way?
BOT: Never.
USER: To fetch her fan, her gloves, her mask, nor nothing?
BOT: Never, my lord.
USER: That's strange.
BOT: Bid her come hither. Go. She says enough. Yet she's a simple bawd That cannot say as much. This is a subtle whore, A closet lock and key of villainous secrets. And yet she'll kneel and pray. I have seen her do 't.
USER: My lord, what is your will?
BOT: Pray, chuck, come hither.
USER: What is your pleasure?
BOT: Let me see your eyes. Look in my face.
USER: What horrible fancy's this?
BOT: Leave procreants alone, and shut the door. Cough, or cry hem, if anybody come. Your mystery, your mystery. Nay, dispatch.
USER: Upon my knees, what doth your speech import? I understand a fury in your words, But not the words.
BOT: Why, what art thou?
USER: Your wife, my lord, your true and loyal wife.
BOT: Come, swear it, damn thyself, Lest, being like one of heaven, the devils themselves Should fear to seize thee. Therefore be double-damn'd. Swear thou art honest.
USER: Heaven doth truly know it.
BOT: Heaven truly knows that thou art false as hell.
USER: To whom, my lord? With whom? How am I false?
BOT: O Desdemona, away! away! away!
USER: I hope my noble lord esteems me honest.
BOT: O, ay, as summer flies are in the shambles, That quicken even with blowing. O thou weed, Who art so lovely fair, and smell'st so sweet, That the sense aches at thee, Would thou hadst ne'er been born!
USER: Alas, what ignorant sin have I committed?
BOT: By heaven, you do me wrong.
USER: Are not you a strumpet?
BOT: No, as I am a Christian: If to preserve this vessel for my lord From any other foul unlawful touch Be not to be a strumpet, I am none.
USER: What, not a whore?
BOT: No, as I shall be sav'd.
USER: Is't possible?
BOT: O, heaven forgive us!
USER: Alas, what does this gentleman conceive? How do you, madam? How do you, my good lady?
BOT: Faith, half asleep.
USER: Good madam, what's the matter with my lord?
BOT: With who?
USER: Why, with my lord, madam.
BOT: Who is thy lord?
USER: He that is yours, sweet lady.
BOT: I have none. Do not talk to me, Emilia, I cannot weep, nor answer have I none But what should go by water. Prithee, tonight Lay on my bed my wedding sheets, remember, And call thy husband hither.
USER: Here's a change indeed!
BOT: 'Tis meet I should be us'd so, very meet. How have I been behav'd, that he might stick The small'st opinion on my least misuse?
USER: What is your pleasure, madam? How is't with you?
BOT: I cannot tell. Those that do teach young babes Do it with gentle means and easy tasks. He might have chid me so, for, in good faith, I am a child to chiding.
USER: What's the matter, lady?
BOT: Alas, Iago, my lord hath so bewhor'd her, Thrown such despite and heavy terms upon her, As true hearts cannot bear.
USER: Am I that name, Iago?
BOT: What name, fair lady?
USER: Such as she says my lord did say I was.
BOT: He call'd her whore: a beggar in his drink Could not have laid such terms upon his callet.
USER: Why did he so?
BOT: I do not know. I am sure I am none such.
USER: Do not weep, do not weep: alas the day!
BOT: Hath she forsook so many noble matches, Her father, and her country, and her friends, To be call'd whore? would it not make one weep?
USER: It is my wretched fortune.
BOT: Beshrew him for't! How comes this trick upon him?
USER: Nay, heaven doth know.
BOT: I will be hang'd, if some eternal villain, Some busy and insinuating rogue, Some cogging, cozening slave, to get some office, Have not devis'd this slander. I'll be hang'd else.
USER: Fie, there is no such man. It is impossible.
BOT: If any such there be, heaven pardon him!
USER: Speak within door.
BOT: O, fie upon them! Some such squire he was That turn'd your wit the seamy side without, And made you to suspect me with the Moor.
USER: You are a fool. Go to.
BOT: I pray you, be content. 'Tis but his humour. The business of the state does him offence, And he does chide with you.
USER: If 'twere no other, -
BOT: 'Tis but so, I warrant. Hark, how these instruments summon to supper. The messengers of Venice stay the meat. Go in, and weep not. All things shall be well. How now, Roderigo?
USER: I do not find that thou dealest justly with me.
BOT: What in the contrary?
USER: Will you hear me, Roderigo?
BOT: Faith, I have heard too much, for your words and performances are no kin together.
USER: You charge me most unjustly.
BOT: Well, go to, very well.
USER: Very well, go to, I cannot go to, man, nor 'tis not very well. Nay, I say 'tis very scurvy, and begin to find myself fopped in it.
BOT: Very well.
USER: You have said now.
BOT: Ay, and said nothing but what I protest intendment of doing.
USER: It hath not appeared.
BOT: Well, what is it? Is it within reason and compass?
USER: Sir, there is especial commission come from Venice to depute Cassio in Othello's place.
BOT: Is that true? Why then Othello and Desdemona return again to Venice.
USER: O, no; he goes into Mauritania, and takes away with him the fair Desdemona, unless his abode be lingered here by some accident: wherein none can be so determinate as the removing of Cassio.
BOT: How do you mean "removing" of him?
USER: Why, by making him uncapable of Othello's place: knocking out his brains.
BOT: And that you would have me to do?
USER: I will hear further reason for this.
BOT: And you shall be satisfied.

USER: I do beseech you, sir, trouble yourself no further.
BOT: O, pardon me; 'twill do me good to walk.
USER: Madam, good night. I humbly thank your ladyship.
BOT: Your honour is most welcome.
USER: Will you walk, sir? - O, Desdemona, -
BOT: My lord?
USER: Get you to bed on th' instant, I will be return'd forthwith. Dismiss your attendant there. Look 't be done.
BOT: I will, my lord.
USER: How goes it now? He looks gentler than he did.
BOT: He says he will return incontinent, He hath commanded me to go to bed, And bade me to dismiss you.
USER: Dismiss me?
BOT: It was his bidding. Therefore, good Emilia, Give me my nightly wearing, and adieu. We must not now displease him.
USER: I would you had never seen him!
BOT: So would not I. My love doth so approve him, That even his stubbornness, his checks, his frowns, - Prithee, unpin me, - have grace and favour in them.
USER: I have laid those sheets you bade me on the bed.
BOT: All's one. Good faith, how foolish are our minds! If I do die before thee, prithee, shroud me In one of those same sheets.
USER: Come, come, you talk.
BOT: Shall I go fetch your night-gown?
USER: No, unpin me here. This Lodovico is a proper man.
BOT: A very handsome man.
USER: He speaks well.
BOT: I know a lady in Venice would have walked barefoot to Palestine for a touch of his nether lip.
USER: It's the wind.
BOT: _I call'd my love false love; but what said he then? Sing willow, willow, willow: If I court mo women, you'll couch with mo men._ So get thee gone; good night. Mine eyes do itch; Doth that bode weeping?
USER: 'Tis neither here nor there.
BOT: I have heard it said so. O, these men, these men! Dost thou in conscience think, - tell me, Emilia, - That there be women do abuse their husbands In such gross kind?
USER: There be some such, no question.
BOT: Wouldst thou do such a deed for all the world?
USER: Why, would not you?
BOT: No, by this heavenly light!
USER: Nor I neither by this heavenly light, I might do't as well i' the dark.
BOT: Wouldst thou do such a deed for all the world?
USER: The world's a huge thing. It is a great price For a small vice.
BOT: In troth, I think thou wouldst not.
USER: Beshrew me, if I would do such a wrong for the whole world.
BOT: Why, the wrong is but a wrong i' the world; and having the world for your labour, 'tis a wrong in your own world, and you might quickly make it right.
USER: I do not think there is any such woman.
BOT: Good night, good night. Heaven me such usage send, Not to pick bad from bad, but by bad mend!

USER: Be near at hand, I may miscarry in 't.
BOT: Here, at thy hand. Be bold, and take thy stand.
USER: I have no great devotion to the deed; And yet he hath given me satisfying reasons. 'Tis but a man gone. Forth, my sword; he dies.
BOT: I know his gait; 'tis he. Villain, thou diest!
USER: That thrust had been mine enemy indeed, But that my coat is better than thou know'st. I will make proof of thine.
BOT: O, I am slain!
USER: I am maim'd forever. Help, ho! murder, murder!
BOT: The voice of Cassio. Iago keeps his word.
USER: O, villain that I am!
BOT: It is even so.
USER: O, help, ho! light! a surgeon!
BOT: What, ho! No watch? No passage? murder, murder!
USER: 'Tis some mischance; the cry is very direful.
BOT: O, help!
USER: Hark!
BOT: O wretched villain!
USER: Two or three groan. It is a heavy night. These may be counterfeits. Let's think't unsafe To come in to the cry without more help.
BOT: Nobody come? Then shall I bleed to death.
USER: Hark!
BOT: Here's one comes in his shirt, with light and weapons.
USER: Who's there? Whose noise is this that cries on murder?
BOT: We do not know.
USER: Did not you hear a cry?
BOT: Here, here! for heaven's sake, help me!
USER: What's the matter?
BOT: This is Othello's ancient, as I take it.
USER: The same indeed, a very valiant fellow.
BOT: What are you here that cry so grievously?
USER: Iago? O, I am spoil'd, undone by villains! Give me some help.
BOT: O me, lieutenant! What villains have done this?
USER: I think that one of them is hereabout, And cannot make away.
BOT: O treacherous villains! Come in and give some help.
USER: O, help me here!
BOT: That's one of them.
USER: O murderous slave! O villain!
BOT: O damn'd Iago! O inhuman dog!
USER: Kill men i' the dark! Where be these bloody thieves? How silent is this town! Ho! murder! murder! What may you be? Are you of good or evil?
BOT: As you shall prove us, praise us.
USER: Signior Lodovico?
BOT: He, sir.
USER: I cry you mercy. Here's Cassio hurt by villains.
BOT: Cassio!
USER: How is't, brother?
BOT: My leg is cut in two.
USER: Marry, heaven forbid! Light, gentlemen, I'll bind it with my shirt.
BOT: What is the matter, ho? Who is't that cried?
USER: Who is't that cried?
BOT: O my dear Cassio, my sweet Cassio! O Cassio, Cassio, Cassio!
USER: O notable strumpet! Cassio, may you suspect Who they should be that have thus mangled you?
BOT: No.
USER: I am sorry to find you thus; I have been to seek you.
BOT: Lend me a garter. So. - O, for a chair, To bear him easily hence!
USER: Alas, he faints! O Cassio, Cassio, Cassio!
BOT: What, of Venice?
USER: Even he, sir. Did you know him?
BOT: Know him? Ay.
USER: Signior Gratiano? I cry you gentle pardon. These bloody accidents must excuse my manners, That so neglected you.
BOT: I am glad to see you.
USER: How do you, Cassio? O, a chair, a chair!
BOT: Roderigo!
USER: None in the world. Nor do I know the man.
BOT: 'Las, what's the matter? What's the matter, husband?
USER: Cassio hath here been set on in the dark By Roderigo, and fellows that are 'scap'd. He's almost slain, and Roderigo dead.
BOT: Alas, good gentleman! alas, good Cassio!
USER: This is the fruit of whoring. Prithee, Emilia, Go know of Cassio where he supp'd tonight. What, do you shake at that?
BOT: He supp'd at my house, but I therefore shake not.
USER: O, did he so? I charge you go with me.
BOT: Fie, fie upon thee, strumpet!
USER: I am no strumpet, but of life as honest As you that thus abuse me.
BOT: As I? Foh! fie upon thee!

USER: Who's there? Othello?
BOT: Ay, Desdemona.
USER: Will you come to bed, my lord?
BOT: Have you pray'd tonight, Desdemona?
USER: Ay, my lord.
BOT: If you bethink yourself of any crime Unreconcil'd as yet to heaven and grace, Solicit for it straight.
USER: Alack, my lord, what may you mean by that?
BOT: Well, do it, and be brief, I will walk by. I would not kill thy unprepared spirit, No, heaven forfend! I would not kill thy soul.
USER: Talk you of killing?
BOT: Ay, I do.
USER: Then heaven have mercy on me!
BOT: Amen, with all my heart!
USER: If you say so, I hope you will not kill me.
BOT: Hum!
USER: And yet I fear you, for you are fatal then When your eyes roll so: why I should fear I know not, Since guiltiness I know not, but yet I feel I fear.
BOT: Think on thy sins.
USER: They are loves I bear to you.
BOT: Ay, and for that thou diest.
USER: That death's unnatural that kills for loving. Alas, why gnaw you so your nether lip? Some bloody passion shakes your very frame. These are portents; but yet I hope, I hope, They do not point on me.
BOT: Peace, and be still!
USER: I will so. What's the matter?
BOT: That handkerchief which I so loved and gave thee, Thou gav'st to Cassio.
USER: No, by my life and soul! Send for the man and ask him.
BOT: Sweet soul, take heed, take heed of perjury, Thou'rt on thy death-bed.
USER: Ay, but not yet to die.
BOT: Yes, presently: Therefore confess thee freely of thy sin. For to deny each article with oath Cannot remove nor choke the strong conception That I do groan withal. Thou art to die.
USER: Then, Lord have mercy on me!
BOT: I say, amen.
USER: And have you mercy too! I never did Offend you in my life, never lov'd Cassio But with such general warranty of heaven As I might love. I never gave him token.
BOT: By heaven, I saw my handkerchief in 's hand. O perjur'd woman, thou dost stone my heart, And mak'st me call what I intend to do A murder, which I thought a sacrifice. I saw the handkerchief.
USER: He found it then. I never gave it him. Send for him hither. Let him confess a truth.
BOT: He hath confess'd.
USER: What, my lord?
BOT: That he hath us'd thee.
USER: How? Unlawfully?
BOT: Ay.
USER: He will not say so.
BOT: No, his mouth is stopp'd. Honest Iago hath ta'en order for 't.
USER: O, my fear interprets! What, is he dead?
BOT: Had all his hairs been lives, my great revenge Had stomach for them all.
USER: Alas, he is betray'd, and I undone!
BOT: Out, strumpet! Weep'st thou for him to my face?
USER: O, banish me, my lord, but kill me not!
BOT: Down, strumpet!
USER: Kill me tomorrow, let me live tonight!
BOT: Nay, if you strive, -
USER: But half an hour!
BOT: Being done, there is no pause.
USER: But while I say one prayer!
BOT: It is too late.
USER: What noise is this? Not dead? Not yet quite dead? I that am cruel am yet merciful, I would not have thee linger in thy pain. So, so.
BOT: Who's there?
USER: That I may speak with you, O, good my lord!
BOT: I had forgot thee: O, come in, Emilia. Soft, by and by; let me the curtains draw. Where art thou? What's the matter with thee now?
USER: O, my good lord, yonder's foul murders done!
BOT: What, now?
USER: But now, my lord.
BOT: It is the very error of the moon, She comes more nearer earth than she was wont And makes men mad.
USER: Cassio, my lord, hath kill'd a young Venetian Call'd Roderigo.
BOT: Roderigo kill'd? And Cassio kill'd?
USER: No, Cassio is not kill'd.
BOT: Not Cassio kill'd! Then murder's out of tune, And sweet revenge grows harsh.
USER: O, falsely, falsely murder'd!
BOT: Alas, what cry is that?
USER: That? What?
BOT: Out, and alas! That was my lady's voice. Help! help, ho! help! O lady, speak again, Sweet Desdemona! O sweet mistress, speak!
USER: A guiltless death I die.
BOT: O, who hath done this deed?
USER: Nobody; I myself. Farewell. Commend me to my kind lord. O, farewell!
BOT: Why, how should she be murder'd?
USER: Alas, who knows?
BOT: You heard her say herself, it was not I.
USER: She said so. I must needs report the truth.
BOT: She's like a liar, gone to burning hell. 'Twas I that kill'd her.
USER: O, the more angel she, And you the blacker devil!
BOT: She turn'd to folly, and she was a whore.
USER: Thou dost belie her, and thou art a devil.
BOT: She was false as water.
USER: Thou art rash as fire, to say That she was false: O, she was heavenly true!
BOT: Cassio did top her; ask thy husband else. O, I were damn'd beneath all depth in hell, But that I did proceed upon just grounds To this extremity. Thy husband knew it all.
USER: My husband?
BOT: Thy husband.
USER: That she was false to wedlock?
BOT: Ay, with Cassio. Nay, had she been true, If heaven would make me such another world Of one entire and perfect chrysolite, I'd not have sold her for it.
USER: My husband?
BOT: Ay, 'twas he that told me first. An honest man he is, and hates the slime That sticks on filthy deeds.
USER: My husband?
BOT: What needs this iterance, woman? I say thy husband.
USER: O mistress, villainy hath made mocks with love! My husband say that she was false?
BOT: He, woman; I say thy husband: dost understand the word? My friend, thy husband, honest, honest Iago.
USER: If he say so, may his pernicious soul Rot half a grain a day! he lies to the heart. She was too fond of her most filthy bargain.
BOT: Ha!
USER: Do thy worst: This deed of thine is no more worthy heaven Than thou wast worthy her.
BOT: Peace, you were best.
USER: What is the matter? How now, general?
BOT: O, are you come, Iago? You have done well, That men must lay their murders on your neck.
USER: What is the matter?
BOT: Disprove this villain, if thou be'st a man. He says thou told'st him that his wife was false. I know thou didst not, thou'rt not such a villain. Speak, for my heart is full.
USER: I told him what I thought, and told no more Than what he found himself was apt and true.
BOT: But did you ever tell him she was false?
USER: I did.
BOT: You told a lie, an odious, damned lie; Upon my soul, a lie; a wicked lie. She false with Cassio? Did you say with Cassio?
USER: With Cassio, mistress. Go to, charm your tongue.
BOT: I will not charm my tongue; I am bound to speak. My mistress here lies murder'd in her bed.
USER: O heavens forfend!
BOT: And your reports have set the murder on.
USER: Nay, stare not, masters, it is true indeed.
BOT: 'Tis a strange truth.
USER: O monstrous act!
BOT: Villany, villany, villany! I think upon't, I think I smell't; O villany! I thought so then. I'll kill myself for grief. O villany, villany!
USER: What, are you mad? I charge you, get you home.
BOT: Good gentlemen, let me have leave to speak. 'Tis proper I obey him, but not now. Perchance, Iago, I will ne'er go home.
USER: O! O! O!
BOT: Nay, lay thee down and roar; For thou hast kill'd the sweetest innocent That e'er did lift up eye.
USER: I scarce did know you, uncle, there lies your niece, Whose breath indeed these hands have newly stopp'd. I know this act shows horrible and grim.
BOT: O heaven! O heavenly powers!
USER: Come, hold your peace.
BOT: 'Twill out, 'twill out. I peace? No, I will speak as liberal as the north. Let heaven and men and devils, let them all, All, all, cry shame against me, yet I'll speak.
USER: Be wise, and get you home.
BOT: I will not.
USER: Fie! Your sword upon a woman?
BOT: O thou dull Moor, that handkerchief thou speak'st of I found by fortune and did give my husband; For often with a solemn earnestness, More than indeed belong'd to such a trifle, He begg'd of me to steal it.
USER: Villanous whore!
BOT: She give it Cassio! no, alas, I found it, And I did give't my husband.
USER: Filth, thou liest!
BOT: By heaven, I do not, I do not, gentlemen. O murderous coxcomb! What should such a fool Do with so good a wife? OTHELLO Are there not stones in heaven But what serve for thunder? Precious villain!
USER: The woman falls. Sure, he hath kill'd his wife.
BOT: Ay, ay. O, lay me by my mistress' side.
USER: He's gone, but his wife's kill'd.
BOT: 'Tis a notorious villain. Take you this weapon, Which I have here recover'd from the Moor. Come, guard the door without, let him not pass, But kill him rather. I'll after that same villain, For 'tis a damned slave.
USER: I am not valiant neither, But every puny whipster gets my sword. But why should honour outlive honesty? Let it go all.
BOT: I have another weapon in this chamber, It is a sword of Spain, the ice-brook's temper. O, here it is. Uncle, I must come forth.
USER: Thou hast no weapon, and perforce must suffer.
BOT: Look in upon me then, and speak with me, Or, naked as I am, I will assault thee.
USER: What is the matter?
BOT: Where is this rash and most unfortunate man?
USER: That's he that was Othello. Here I am.
BOT: Where is that viper? Bring the villain forth.
USER: I look down towards his feet; but that's a fable. If that thou be'st a devil, I cannot kill thee.
BOT: Wrench his sword from him.
USER: I bleed, sir, but not kill'd.
BOT: I am not sorry neither. I'd have thee live, For in my sense 'tis happiness to die.
USER: O thou Othello, that wert once so good, Fall'n in the practice of a damned slave, What shall be said to thee?
BOT: Why, anything; An honourable murderer, if you will. For naught did I in hate, but all in honour.
USER: This wretch hath part confess'd his villany. Did you and he consent in Cassio's death?
BOT: Ay.
USER: Dear general, I never gave you cause.
BOT: I do believe it, and I ask your pardon. Will you, I pray, demand that demi-devil Why he hath thus ensnar'd my soul and body?
USER: Demand me nothing. What you know, you know. From this time forth I never will speak word.
BOT: What, not to pray?
USER: Torments will ope your lips.
BOT: Well, thou dost best.
USER: O villain!
BOT: Most heathenish and most gross!
USER: Now here's another discontented paper, Found in his pocket too; and this, it seems, Roderigo meant to have sent this damned villain, But that, belike, Iago in the interim Came in and satisfied him.
BOT: O the pernicious caitiff! How came you, Cassio, by that handkerchief That was my wife's?
USER: I found it in my chamber. And he himself confess'd but even now, That there he dropp'd it for a special purpose Which wrought to his desire.
BOT: O fool! fool! fool!
USER: There is besides in Roderigo's letter, How he upbraids Iago, that he made him Brave me upon the watch, whereon it came That I was cast. And even but now he spake, After long seeming dead, Iago hurt him, Iago set him on.
BOT: O bloody period!
USER: All that's spoke is marr'd.
BOT: I kiss'd thee ere I kill'd thee. No way but this, Killing myself, to die upon a kiss.
USER: This did I fear, but thought he had no weapon, For he was great of heart.

USER: Young prince of Tyre, you have at large received The danger of the task you undertake.
BOT: I have, Antiochus, and, with a soul Emboldened with the glory of her praise, Think death no hazard in this enterprise.
USER: Prince Pericles, -
BOT: That would be son to great Antiochus.
USER: Scorning advice, read the conclusion, then: Which read and not expounded, 'tis decreed, As these before thee thou thyself shalt bleed.
BOT: Prince Pericles, touch not, upon thy life, For that's an article within our law, As dangerous as the rest. Your time's expired: Either expound now, or receive your sentence.
USER: Doth your highness call?
BOT: My lord, 'tis done.
USER: Enough. Let your breath cool yourself, telling your haste.
BOT: My lord, Prince Pericles is fled.
USER: As thou wilt live, fly after: and like an arrow shot From a well-experienced archer hits the mark His eye doth level at, so thou ne'er return Unless thou say 'Prince Pericles is dead.'
BOT: My lord, if I can get him within my pistol's length, I'll make him sure enough: so, farewell to your highness.
USER: Thaliard! adieu! Till Pericles be dead, My heart can lend no succour to my head.

USER: Joy and all comfort in your sacred breast!
BOT: And keep your mind, till you return to us, Peaceful and comfortable!
USER: All leave us else, but let your cares o'erlook What shipping and what lading's in our haven, And then return to us. Helicanus, thou Hast moved us: what seest thou in our looks?
BOT: An angry brow, dread lord.
USER: If there be such a dart in princes' frowns, How durst thy tongue move anger to our face?
BOT: How dares the plants look up to heaven, from whence They have their nourishment?
USER: Thou know'st I have power To take thy life from thee. HELICANUS. [_Kneeling._] I have ground the axe myself; Do but you strike the blow.
BOT: To bear with patience Such griefs as you yourself do lay upon yourself.
USER: Alas, sir!
BOT: I do not doubt thy faith; But should he wrong my liberties in my absence?
USER: We'll mingle our bloods together in the earth, From whence we had our being and our birth.

USER: You shall not need, my fellow peers of Tyre, Further to question me of your king's departure: His seal'd commission, left in trust with me, Doth speak sufficiently he's gone to travel.
BOT: If further yet you will be satisfied, Why, as it were unlicensed of your loves, He would depart, I'll give some light unto you. Being at Antioch -
USER: I shall not be hang'd now, although I would; But since he's gone, the king's seas must please He 'scaped the land, to perish at the sea. I'll present myself. Peace to the lords of Tyre!
BOT: Lord Thaliard from Antiochus is welcome.
USER: From him I come With message unto princely Pericles; But since my landing I have understood Your lord has betook himself to unknown travels, My message must return from whence it came.
BOT: We have no reason to desire it, Commended to our master, not to us: Yet, ere you shall depart, this we desire, As friends to Antioch, we may feast in Tyre.

USER: My Dionyza, shall we rest us here, And by relating tales of others' griefs, See if 'twill teach us to forget our own?
BOT: I'll do my best, sir.
USER: O, 'tis too true.
BOT: Our cheeks and hollow eyes do witness it.
USER: O, let those cities that of plenty's cup And her prosperities so largely taste, With their superflous riots, hear these tears! The misery of Tarsus may be theirs.
BOT: Where's the lord governor?
USER: Here. Speak out thy sorrows which thou bring'st in haste, For comfort is too far for us to expect.
BOT: We have descried, upon our neighbouring shore, A portly sail of ships make hitherward.
USER: That's the least fear; for, by the semblance Of their white flags display'd, they bring us peace, And come to us as favourers, not as foes.
BOT: I go, my lord.
USER: Welcome is peace, if he on peace consist; If wars, we are unable to resist.
BOT: The gods of Greece protect you! And we'll pray for you.
USER: Arise, I pray you, rise: We do not look for reverence, but for love, And harbourage for ourself, our ships and men.
BOT: Which welcome we'll accept; feast here awhile, Until our stars that frown lend us a smile.

USER: What, ho, Pilch!
BOT: Ha, come and bring away the nets!
USER: What, Patch-breech, I say!
BOT: What say you, master?
USER: Look how thou stirrest now! Come away, or I'll fetch thee with a wanion.
BOT: Faith, master, I am thinking of the poor men that were cast away before us even now.
USER: Alas, poor souls, it grieved my heart to hear what pitiful cries they made to us to help them, when, well-a-day, we could scarce help ourselves.
BOT: But, master, if I had been the sexton, I would have been that day in the belfry.
USER: Why, man?
BOT: We would purge the land of these drones, that rob the bee of her honey.
USER: These fishers tell the infirmities of men; And from their watery empire recollect All that may men approve or men detect! Peace be at your labour, honest fishermen.
BOT: Honest! good fellow, what's that? If it be a day fits you, search out of the calendar, and nobody look after it.
USER: May see the sea hath cast upon your coast.
BOT: What a drunken knave was the sea to cast thee in our way!
USER: A man whom both the waters and the wind, In that vast tennis-court, have made the ball For them to play upon, entreats you pity him; He asks of you, that never used to beg.
BOT: No, friend, cannot you beg? Here's them in our country of Greece gets more with begging than we can do with working.
USER: Canst thou catch any fishes, then?
BOT: I never practised it.
USER: Nay, then thou wilt starve, sure; for here's nothing to be got now-a-days, unless thou canst fish for't.
BOT: I thank you, sir.
USER: Hark you, my friend; you said you could not beg?
BOT: I did but crave.
USER: But crave! Then I'll turn craver too, and so I shall 'scape whipping.
BOT: Why, are your beggars whipped, then?
USER: O, not all, my friend, not all; for if all your beggars were whipped, I would wish no better office than to be beadle. But, master, I'll go draw up the net.
BOT: Hark you, sir, do you know where ye are?
USER: Not well.
BOT: Why, I'll tell you: this is called Pentapolis, and our King, the good Simonides.
USER: The good Simonides, do you call him?
BOT: Ay, sir; and he deserves so to be called for his peaceable reign and good government.
USER: He is a happy king, since he gains from his subjects the name of good government. How far is his court distant from this shore?
BOT: Marry sir, half a day's journey: and I'll tell you, he hath a fair daughter, and tomorrow is her birth-day; and there are princes and knights come from all parts of the world to joust and tourney for her love.
USER: Were my fortunes equal to my desires, I could wish to make one there.
BOT: O, sir, things must be as they may; and what a man cannot get, he may lawfully deal for - his wife's soul.
USER: Help, master, help! here's a fish hangs in the net, like a poor man's right in the law; 'twill hardly come out. Ha! bots on't, 'tis come at last, and 'tis turned to a rusty armour.
BOT: What mean you sir?
USER: Why, wilt thou tourney for the lady?
BOT: I'll show the virtue I have borne in arms.
USER: Why, d'ye take it, and the gods give thee good on't!
BOT: Ay, but hark you, my friend; 'twas we that made up this garment through the rough seams of the waters: there are certain condolements, certain vails. I hope, sir, if you thrive, you'll remember from whence you had them.
USER: We'll sure provide: thou shalt have my best gown to make thee a pair; and I'll bring thee to the court myself.
BOT: Then honour be but a goal to my will, This day I'll rise, or else add ill to ill.

USER: Are the knights ready to begin the triumph?
BOT: They are, my liege; And stay your coming to present themselves.
USER: Return them, we are ready; and our daughter, In honour of whose birth these triumphs are, Sits here, like beauty's child, whom Nature gat For men to see, and seeing wonder at.
BOT: It pleaseth you, my royal father, to express My commendations great, whose merit's less.
USER: Which, to preserve mine honour, I'll perform. The first Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield to Thaisa.
BOT: Who is the first that doth prefer himself?
USER: A knight of Sparta, my renowned father; And the device he bears upon his shield Is a black Ethiope reaching at the sun: The word, _Lux tua vita mihi._
BOT: He loves you well that holds his life of you. The second Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield to Thaisa. Who is the second that presents himself?
USER: And what's the third?
BOT: The third of Antioch; And his device, a wreath of chivalry; The word, _Me pompae provexit apex._ The fourth Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield to Thaisa.
USER: What is the fourth?
BOT: A burning torch that's turned upside down; The word, _Quod me alit me extinguit._
USER: Which shows that beauty hath his power and will, Which can as well inflame as it can kill. The fifth Knight passes by, and his Squire presents his shield to Thaisa.
BOT: And what's the sixth and last, the which the knight himself With such a graceful courtesy deliver'd?
USER: He seems to be a stranger; but his present is A wither'd branch, that's only green at top; The motto, _In hac spe vivo._
BOT: A pretty moral; From the dejected state wherein he is, He hopes by you his fortunes yet may flourish.
USER: He had need mean better than his outward show Can any way speak in his just commend; For by his rusty outside he appears To have practised more the whipstock than the lance.
BOT: He well may be a stranger, for he comes To an honour'd triumph strangely furnished.
USER: And on set purpose let his armour rust Until this day, to scour it in the dust.
BOT: Opinion's but a fool, that makes us scan The outward habit by the inward man. But stay, the knights are coming. We will withdraw into the gallery.

USER: But you, my knight and guest; To whom this wreath of victory I give, And crown you king of this day's happiness.
BOT: 'Tis more by fortune, lady, than by merit.
USER: We are honour'd much by good Simonides.
BOT: Your presence glads our days; honour we love; For who hates honour hates the gods above.
USER: Sir, yonder is your place.
BOT: Some other is more fit.
USER: Contend not, sir; for we are gentlemen Have neither in our hearts nor outward eyes Envied the great, nor shall the low despise.
BOT: You are right courteous knights.
USER: Sit, sir, sit. By Jove, I wonder, that is king of thoughts, These cates resist me, he but thought upon.
BOT: By Juno, that is queen of marriage, All viands that I eat do seem unsavoury, Wishing him my meat. Sure, he's a gallant gentleman.
USER: He's but a country gentleman; Has done no more than other knights have done; Has broken a staff or so; so let it pass.
BOT: To me he seems like diamond to glass.
USER: What, are you merry, knights?
BOT: Who can be other in this royal presence?
USER: Here, with a cup that's stored unto the brim, - As you do love, fill to your mistress' lips, - We drink this health to you.
BOT: We thank your grace.
USER: Yet pause awhile. Yon knight doth sit too melancholy, As if the entertainment in our court Had not a show might countervail his worth. Note it not you, Thaisa?
BOT: What is't to me, my father?
USER: Alas, my father, it befits not me Unto a stranger knight to be so bold: He may my proffer take for an offence, Since men take women's gifts for impudence.
BOT: How? Do as I bid you, or you'll move me else.
USER: And furthermore tell him, we desire to know of him, Of whence he is, his name and parentage.
BOT: The king my father, sir, has drunk to you.
USER: I thank him.
BOT: Wishing it so much blood unto your life.
USER: I thank both him and you, and pledge him freely.
BOT: And further he desires to know of you, Of whence you are, your name and parentage.
USER: A gentleman of Tyre; my name, Pericles; My education been in arts and arms; Who, looking for adventures in the world, Was by the rough seas reft of ships and men, And after shipwreck driven upon this shore.
BOT: He thanks your grace; names himself Pericles, A gentleman of Tyre, Who only by misfortune of the seas Bereft of ships and men, cast on this shore.
USER: In those that practise them they are, my lord.
BOT: O, that's as much as you would be denied Of your fair courtesy. Unclasp, unclasp: Thanks gentlemen, to all; all have done well. These knights unto their several lodgings.
USER: I am at your grace's pleasure.
BOT: Princes, it is too late to talk of love; And that's the mark I know you level at: Therefore each one betake him to his rest; Tomorrow all for speeding do their best.

USER: 'Twas very strange
BOT: And yet but justice; for though this king were great; His greatness was no guard to bar heaven's shaft, But sin had his reward.
USER: 'Tis very true.
BOT: See, not a man in private conference Or council has respect with him but he.
USER: It shall no longer grieve without reproof.
BOT: And cursed be he that will not second it.
USER: Follow me, then. Lord Helicane, a word.
BOT: With me? and welcome: happy day, my lords.
USER: Know that our griefs are risen to the top, And now at length they overflow their banks.
BOT: Your griefs! for what? Wrong not your prince you love.
USER: Live, noble Helicane!
BOT: To wisdom he's a fool that will not yield; And since Lord Helicane enjoineth us, We with our travels will endeavour us.
USER: Then you love us, we you, and we'll clasp hands: When peers thus knit, a kingdom ever stands.

USER: Good morrow to the good Simonides.
BOT: Knights, from my daughter this I let you know, That for this twelvemonth she'll not undertake A married life. Her reason to herself is only known, Which yet from her by no means can I get.
USER: May we not get access to her, my lord?
BOT: Loath to bid farewell, we take our leaves.
USER: All fortune to the good Simonides!
BOT: To you as much. Sir, I am beholding to you For your sweet music this last night: I do Protest my ears were never better fed With such delightful pleasing harmony.
USER: It is your grace's pleasure to commend; Not my desert.
BOT: Sir, you are music's master.
USER: The worst of all her scholars, my good lord.
BOT: Let me ask you one thing: What do you think of my daughter, sir?
USER: A most virtuous princess.
BOT: And she is fair too, is she not?
USER: As a fair day in summer, wondrous fair.
BOT: Sir, my daughter thinks very well of you; Ay, so well, that you must be her master, And she will be your scholar: therefore look to it.
USER: I am unworthy for her schoolmaster.
BOT: She thinks not so; peruse this writing else.
USER: 'Tis the king's subtlety to have my life. O, seek not to entrap me, gracious lord, A stranger and distressed gentleman, That never aim'd so high to love your daughter, But bent all offices to honour her.
BOT: Thou hast bewitch'd my daughter, And thou art a villain.
USER: By the gods, I have not: Never did thought of mine levy offence; Nor never did my actions yet commence A deed might gain her love or your displeasure.
BOT: Traitor, thou liest.
USER: Traitor?
BOT: Ay, traitor.
USER: Even in his throat - unless it be the king - That calls me traitor, I return the lie.
BOT: No? Here comes my daughter, she can witness it.
USER: Then, as you are as virtuous as fair, Resolve your angry father, if my tongue Did e'er solicit, or my hand subscribe To any syllable that made love to you.
BOT: Why, sir, say if you had, Who takes offence at that would make me glad?
USER: Yes, if you love me, sir.
BOT: Even as my life my blood that fosters it.
USER: What, are you both agreed?
BOT: Yes, if't please your majesty.
USER: It pleaseth me so well, that I will see you wed; And then with what haste you can, get you to bed.

USER: Here is a thing too young for such a place, Who, if it had conceit, would die, as I Am like to do: take in your arms this piece Of your dead queen.
BOT: How? how, Lychorida?
USER: Patience, good sir; do not assist the storm. Here's all that is left living of your queen, A little daughter: for the sake of it, Be manly, and take comfort.
BOT: O you gods! Why do you make us love your goodly gifts, And snatch them straight away? We here below Recall not what we give, and therein may Vie honour with you.
USER: Patience, good sir. Even for this charge.
BOT: What courage, sir? God save you!
USER: Courage enough: I do not fear the flaw; It hath done to me the worst. Yet, for the love Of this poor infant, this fresh new sea-farer, I would it would be quiet.
BOT: Slack the bolins there! Thou wilt not, wilt thou? Blow, and split thyself.
USER: But sea-room, and the brine and cloudy billow kiss the moon, I care not.
BOT: Sir, your queen must overboard: the sea works high, the wind is loud and will not lie till the ship be cleared of the dead.
USER: That's your superstition.
BOT: Pardon us, sir; with us at sea it has been still observed; and we are strong in custom. Therefore briefly yield her; for she must overboard straight.
USER: As you think meet. Most wretched queen!
BOT: Here she lies, sir.
USER: Sir, we have a chest beneath the hatches, caulked and bitumed ready.
BOT: I thank thee. Mariner, say what coast is this?
USER: We are near Tarsus.
BOT: Thither, gentle mariner, Alter thy course for Tyre. When canst thou reach it?
USER: By break of day, if the wind cease.
BOT: O, make for Tarsus! There will I visit Cleon, for the babe Cannot hold out to Tyrus. There I'll leave it At careful nursing. Go thy ways, good mariner: I'll bring the body presently.

USER: Philemon, ho!
BOT: Doth my lord call?
USER: Get fire and meat for these poor men: 'T has been a turbulent and stormy night.
BOT: I have been in many; but such a night as this, Till now, I ne'er endured.
USER: Your master will be dead ere you return; There's nothing can be minister'd to nature That can recover him. [_To Philemon._] Give this to the 'pothecary, And tell me how it works.
BOT: Good morrow.
USER: Good morrow to your lordship.
BOT: Gentlemen, why do you stir so early?
USER: Sir, our lodgings, standing bleak upon the sea, Shook as the earth did quake; The very principals did seem to rend, And all to topple: pure surprise and fear Made me to quit the house.
BOT: That is the cause we trouble you so early; 'Tis not our husbandry.
USER: O, you say well.
BOT: So, lift there.
USER: What's that?
BOT: Sir, even now Did the sea toss upon our shore this chest: 'Tis of some wreck.
USER: Set't down, let's look upon't.
BOT: 'Tis like a coffin, sir.
USER: Whate'er it be, 'Tis wondrous heavy. Wrench it open straight: If the sea's stomach be o'ercharged with gold, 'Tis a good constraint of fortune it belches upon us.
BOT: 'Tis so, my lord.
USER: How close 'tis caulk'd and bitumed! Did the sea cast it up?
BOT: I never saw so huge a billow, sir, As toss'd it upon shore.
USER: Wrench it open; Soft! it smells most sweetly in my sense.
BOT: A delicate odour.
USER: As ever hit my nostril. So up with it. O you most potent gods! what's here? a corpse!
BOT: Most strange!
USER: Most likely, sir.
BOT: The heavens, through you, increase our wonder And sets up your fame for ever.
USER: O dear Diana, Where am I? Where's my lord? What world is this?
BOT: Is not this strange?
USER: Most rare.
BOT: Hush, my gentle neighbours! Lend me your hands; to the next chamber bear her. Get linen: now this matter must be look'd to, For her relapse is mortal. Come, come; And Aesculapius guide us!

USER: Most honour'd Cleon, I must needs be gone; My twelve months are expired, and Tyrus stands In a litigious peace. You and your lady, Take from my heart all thankfulness! The gods Make up the rest upon you!
BOT: Your shafts of fortune, though they hurt you mortally, Yet glance full wanderingly on us.
USER: O, your sweet queen! That the strict fates had pleased you had brought her hither, To have bless'd mine eyes with her!
BOT: I have one myself, Who shall not be more dear to my respect Than yours, my lord.
USER: Madam, my thanks and prayers.
BOT: We'll bring your grace e'en to the edge o'the shore, Then give you up to the mask'd Neptune and The gentlest winds of heaven.
USER: I will embrace your offer. Come, dearest madam. O, no tears, Lychorida, no tears. Look to your little mistress, on whose grace You may depend hereafter. Come, my lord.

USER: Madam, this letter, and some certain jewels, Lay with you in your coffer, which are At your command. Know you the character?
BOT: Madam, if this you purpose as ye speak, Diana's temple is not distant far, Where you may abide till your date expire. Moreover, if you please, a niece of mine Shall there attend you.
USER: My recompense is thanks, that's all; Yet my good will is great, though the gift small.

USER: I will do't; but yet she is a goodly creature.
BOT: The fitter, then, the gods should have her. Here she comes weeping for her only mistress' death. Thou art resolved?
USER: I am resolved.
BOT: No, I pray you; I'll not bereave you of your servant.
USER: Well, I will go; But yet I have no desire to it.
BOT: Come, come, I know 'tis good for you. Walk half an hour, Leonine, at the least: Remember what I have said.
USER: I warrant you, madam.
BOT: I'll leave you, my sweet lady, for a while: Pray, walk softly, do not heat your blood: What! I must have a care of you.
USER: My thanks, sweet madam. Is this wind westerly that blows?
BOT: South-west.
USER: When I was born the wind was north.
BOT: Was't so?
USER: My father, as nurse said, did never fear, But cried 'Good seamen!' to the sailors, Galling his kingly hands, haling ropes; And clasping to the mast, endured a sea That almost burst the deck.
BOT: When was this?
USER: Come, say your prayers.
BOT: What mean you?
USER: If you require a little space for prayer, I grant it: pray; but be not tedious, For the gods are quick of ear, and I am sworn To do my work with haste.
BOT: Why will you kill me?
USER: To satisfy my lady.
BOT: My commission Is not to reason of the deed, but do it.
USER: I am sworn, And will dispatch.
BOT: Hold, villain!
USER: A prize! a prize!
BOT: Half part, mates, half part, Come, let's have her aboard suddenly.

USER: Boult!
BOT: Sir?
USER: Search the market narrowly; Mytilene is full of gallants. We lost too much money this mart by being too wenchless.
BOT: We were never so much out of creatures. We have but poor three, and they can do no more than they can do; and they with continual action are even as good as rotten.
USER: Therefore let's have fresh ones, whate'er we pay for them. If there be not a conscience to be used in every trade, we shall never prosper.
BOT: Thou sayest true: 'tis not our bringing up of poor bastards, - as, I think, I have brought up some eleven -
USER: Ay, to eleven; and brought them down again. But shall I search the market?
BOT: What else, man? The stuff we have, a strong wind will blow it to pieces, they are so pitifully sodden.
USER: Thou sayest true; they're too unwholesome, o' conscience. The poor Transylvanian is dead, that lay with the little baggage.
BOT: Ay, she quickly pooped him; she made him roast-meat for worms. But I'll go search the market.
USER: Three or four thousand chequins were as pretty a proportion to live quietly, and so give over.
BOT: Why to give over, I pray you? Is it a shame to get when we are old?
USER: Come, others sorts offend as well as we.
BOT: As well as we! ay, and better too; we offend worse. Neither is our profession any trade; it's no calling. But here comes Boult. BOULT
USER: O sir, we doubt it not.
BOT: Master, I have gone through for this piece, you see: if you like her, so; if not, I have lost my earnest.
USER: Boult, has she any qualities?
BOT: She has a good face, speaks well and has excellent good clothes: there's no farther necessity of qualities can make her be refused.
USER: What is her price, Boult?
BOT: I cannot be baited one doit of a thousand pieces.
USER: Well, follow me, my masters, you shall have your money presently. Wife, take her in; instruct her what she has to do, that she may not be raw in her entertainment.
BOT: Performance shall follow.
USER: Alack that Leonine was so slack, so slow! He should have struck, not spoke; or that these pirates, Not enough barbarous, had not o'erboard thrown me For to seek my mother!
BOT: Why lament you, pretty one?
USER: That I am pretty.
BOT: Come, the gods have done their part in you.
USER: I accuse them not.
BOT: You are light into my hands, where you are like to live.
USER: The more my fault To scape his hands where I was like to die.
BOT: Ay, and you shall live in pleasure.
USER: No.
BOT: Yes, indeed shall you, and taste gentlemen of all fashions: you shall fare well; you shall have the difference of all complexions. What! do you stop your ears?
USER: Are you a woman?
BOT: What would you have me be, an I be not a woman?
USER: An honest woman, or not a woman.
BOT: Marry, whip the gosling: I think I shall have something to do with you. Come, you're a young foolish sapling, and must be bowed as I would have you.
USER: The gods defend me!
BOT: If it please the gods to defend you by men, then men must comfort you, men must feed you, men stir you up. Boult's returned. Now, sir, hast thou cried her through the market?
USER: I have cried her almost to the number of her hairs; I have drawn her picture with my voice.
BOT: And I prithee tell me, how dost thou find the inclination of the people, especially of the younger sort?
USER: Faith, they listened to me as they would have hearkened to their father's testament. There was a Spaniard's mouth so watered, that he went to bed to her very description.
BOT: We shall have him here tomorrow with his best ruff on.
USER: Tonight, tonight. But, mistress, do you know the French knight that cowers i' the hams?
BOT: Who, Monsieur Veroles?
USER: Ay, he: he offered to cut a caper at the proclamation; but he made a groan at it, and swore he would see her tomorrow.
BOT: Well, well, as for him, he brought his disease hither: here he does but repair it. I know he will come in our shadow, to scatter his crowns in the sun.
USER: I understand you not.
BOT: O, take her home, mistress, take her home: these blushes of hers must be quenched with some present practice.
USER: Thou sayest true, i'faith so they must; for your bride goes to that with shame which is her way to go with warrant.
BOT: Faith, some do and some do not. But, mistress, if I have bargained for the joint, -
USER: Thou mayst cut a morsel off the spit.
BOT: I may so. BAWD. Who should deny it? Come young one, I like the manner of your garments well.
USER: Ay, by my faith, they shall not be changed yet.
BOT: I warrant you, mistress, thunder shall not so awake the beds of eels as my giving out her beauty stirs up the lewdly inclined. I'll bring home some tonight.
USER: Come your ways; follow me.
BOT: If fires be hot, knives sharp, or waters deep, Untied I still my virgin knot will keep. Diana, aid my purpose!
USER: What have we to do with Diana? Pray you, will you go with us?

USER: Why, are you foolish? Can it be undone?
BOT: O, Dionyza, such a piece of slaughter The sun and moon ne'er look'd upon!
USER: I think you'll turn a child again.
BOT: O, go to. Well, well, Of all the faults beneath the heavens, the gods Do like this worst.
USER: Be one of those that thinks The petty wrens of Tarsus will fly hence, And open this to Pericles. I do shame To think of what a noble strain you are, And of how coward a spirit.
BOT: To such proceeding Whoever but his approbation added, Though not his prime consent, he did not flow From honourable courses.
USER: Heavens forgive it!
BOT: Thou art like the harpy, Which, to betray, dost, with thine angel's face, Seize with thine eagle's talons.
USER: You are like one that superstitiously Doth swear to the gods that winter kills the flies: But yet I know you'll do as I advise.

USER: Did you ever hear the like?
BOT: No, nor never shall do in such a place as this, she being once gone.
USER: But to have divinity preached there! did you ever dream of such a thing?
BOT: No, no. Come, I am for no more bawdy houses: shall's go hear the vestals sing?
USER: I'll do anything now that is virtuous; but I am out of the road of rutting for ever.

USER: Well, I had rather than twice the worth of her she had ne'er come here.
BOT: Faith, I must ravish her, or she'll disfurnish us of all our cavaliers, and make our swearers priests.
USER: Now, the pox upon her green sickness for me!
BOT: Faith, there's no way to be rid on't but by the way to the pox. Here comes the Lord Lysimachus disguised.
USER: We should have both lord and lown, if the peevish baggage would but give way to customers.
BOT: How now! How a dozen of virginities?
USER: Now, the gods to bless your honour!
BOT: I am glad to see your honour in good health.
USER: You may so; 'tis the better for you that your resorters stand upon sound legs. How now? Wholesome iniquity have you that a man may deal withal, and defy the surgeon?
BOT: We have here one, sir, if she would - but there never came her like in Mytilene.
USER: If she'd do the deed of darkness, thou wouldst say.
BOT: Your honour knows what 'tis to say well enough.
USER: Well, call forth, call forth.
BOT: For flesh and blood, sir, white and red, you shall see a rose; and she were a rose indeed, if she had but -
USER: What, prithee?
BOT: O, sir, I can be modest.
USER: That dignifies the renown of a bawd no less than it gives a good report to a number to be chaste.
BOT: Here comes that which grows to the stalk; never plucked yet, I can assure you. Is she not a fair creature?
USER: Faith, she would serve after a long voyage at sea. Well, there's for you: leave us.
BOT: I beseech your honour, give me leave: a word, and I'll have done presently.
USER: I beseech you, do.
BOT: I desire to find him so, that I may worthily note him.
USER: Next, he's the governor of this country, and a man whom I am bound to.
BOT: If he govern the country, you are bound to him indeed; but how honourable he is in that, I know not. BAWD. Pray you, without any more virginal fencing, will you use him kindly? He will line your apron with gold.
USER: What he will do graciously, I will thankfully receive.
BOT: Ha' you done?
USER: My lord, she's not paced yet: you must take some pains to work her to your manage. Come, we will leave his honour and her together. Go thy ways.
BOT: Now, pretty one, how long have you been at this trade?
USER: What trade, sir?
BOT: Why, I cannot name't but I shall offend.
USER: I cannot be offended with my trade. Please you to name it.
BOT: How long have you been of this profession?
USER: E'er since I can remember. LYSIMACHUS. Did you go to't so young? Were you a gamester at five or at seven?
BOT: Earlier, too, sir, if now I be one.
USER: Why, the house you dwell in proclaims you to be a creature of sale.
BOT: Do you know this house to be a place of such resort, and will come into't? I hear say you are of honourable parts, and are the governor of this place.
USER: Why, hath your principal made known unto you who I am?
BOT: Who is my principal?
USER: If you were born to honour, show it now; If put upon you, make the judgement good That thought you worthy of it.
BOT: How's this? how's this? Some more; be sage.
USER: The good gods preserve you!
BOT: I beseech your honour, one piece for me.
USER: Avaunt, thou damned door-keeper! Your house but for this virgin that doth prop it, Would sink and overwhelm you. Away!
BOT: Whither would you have me?
USER: I must have your maidenhead taken off, or the common hangman shall execute it. Come your ways. We'll have no more gentlemen driven away. Come your ways, I say.
BOT: How now! what's the matter?
USER: Worse and worse, mistress; she has here spoken holy words to the Lord Lysimachus.
BOT: O, abominable!
USER: She makes our profession as it were to stink afore the face of the gods.
BOT: Marry, hang her up for ever!
USER: The nobleman would have dealt with her like a nobleman, and she sent him away as cold as a snowball; saying his prayers too.
BOT: Boult, take her away; use her at thy pleasure: crack the glass of her virginity, and make the rest malleable.
USER: An if she were a thornier piece of ground than she is, she shall be ploughed.
BOT: Hark, hark, you gods!
USER: She conjures: away with her! Would she had never come within my doors! Marry, hang you! She's born to undo us. Will you not go the way of womankind? Marry, come up, my dish of chastity with rosemary and bays!
BOT: Come, mistress; come your way with me.
USER: Whither wilt thou have me?
BOT: To take from you the jewel you hold so dear.
USER: Prithee, tell me one thing first.
BOT: Come now, your one thing?
USER: What canst thou wish thine enemy to be?
BOT: Why, I could wish him to be my master, or rather, my mistress.
USER: What would you have me do? Go to the wars, would you? where a man may serve seven years for the loss of a leg, and have not money enough in

USER: But can you teach all this you speak of?
BOT: Prove that I cannot, take me home again, And prostitute me to the basest groom That doth frequent your house.
USER: Well, I will see what I can do for thee: if I can place thee, I will.
BOT: But amongst honest women.

USER: Where is lord Helicanus? He can resolve you. O, here he is. Sir, there's a barge put off from Mytilene, And in it is Lysimachus the governor, Who craves to come aboard. What is your will?
BOT: That he have his. Call up some gentlemen.
USER: Ho, gentlemen! my lord calls.
BOT: Doth your lordship call?
USER: Gentlemen, there is some of worth would come aboard; I pray ye, greet them fairly. barge._] Enter, from thence, Lysimachus and Lords; with the Gentlemen and the two Sailors.
BOT: Sir, This is the man that can, in aught you would, Resolve you.
USER: Hail, reverend sir! the gods preserve you!
BOT: And you, sir, to outlive the age I am, And die as I would do.
USER: You wish me well. Being on shore, honouring of Neptune's triumphs, Seeing this goodly vessel ride before us, I made to it, to know of whence you are.
BOT: First, what is your place?
USER: I am the governor of this place you lie before.
BOT: Sir, our vessel is of Tyre, in it the king; A man who for this three months hath not spoken To anyone, nor taken sustenance But to prorogue his grief.
USER: Upon what ground is his distemperature?
BOT: 'Twould be too tedious to repeat; But the main grief springs from the loss Of a beloved daughter and a wife.
USER: May we not see him?
BOT: You may; But bootless is your sight: he will not speak To any.
USER: Yet let me obtain my wish.
BOT: Behold him. This was a goodly person. Till the disaster that, one mortal night, Drove him to this.
USER: Sir king, all hail! The gods preserve you! Hail, royal sir!
BOT: It is in vain; he will not speak to you.
USER: Sir, we have a maid in Mytilene, I durst wager, Would win some words of him.
BOT: O, sir, a courtesy Which if we should deny, the most just gods For every graff would send a caterpillar, And so inflict our province. Yet once more Let me entreat to know at large the cause Of your king's sorrow.
USER: Sit, sir, I will recount it to you: But, see, I am prevented.
BOT: O, here is the lady that I sent for. Welcome, fair one! Is't not a goodly presence?
USER: She's a gallant lady.
BOT: Sir, I will use My utmost skill in his recovery, provided That none but I and my companion maid Be suffer'd to come near him.
USER: Come, let us leave her, And the gods make her prosperous!
BOT: Mark'd he your music?
USER: No, nor look'd on us.
BOT: See, she will speak to him.
USER: Hail, sir! My lord, lend ear.
BOT: Hum, ha!
USER: My fortunes - parentage - good parentage - To equal mine! - was it not thus? what say you?
BOT: I said, my lord, if you did know my parentage, You would not do me violence.
USER: I do think so. Pray you, turn your eyes upon me. You are like something that - what country-woman? Here of these shores?
BOT: No, nor of any shores: Yet I was mortally brought forth, and am No other than I appear.
USER: Where I am but a stranger: from the deck You may discern the place.
BOT: Where were you bred? And how achieved you these endowments, which You make more rich to owe?
USER: If I should tell my history, it would seem Like lies disdain'd in the reporting.
BOT: So indeed I did.
USER: Report thy parentage. I think thou said'st Thou hadst been toss'd from wrong to injury, And that thou thought'st thy griefs might equal mine, If both were open'd.
BOT: Some such thing, I said, and said no more but what my thoughts Did warrant me was likely.
USER: My name is Marina.
BOT: O, I am mock'd, And thou by some incensed god sent hither To make the world to laugh at me.
USER: Patience, good sir, Or here I'll cease.
BOT: Nay, I'll be patient. Thou little know'st how thou dost startle me, To call thyself Marina.
USER: The name Was given me by one that had some power, My father, and a king.
BOT: How! a king's daughter? And call'd Marina?
USER: You said you would believe me; But, not to be a troubler of your peace, I will end here.
BOT: But are you flesh and blood? Have you a working pulse? and are no fairy? Motion! Well; speak on. Where were you born? And wherefore call'd Marina?
USER: Call'd Marina For I was born at sea.
BOT: At sea! What mother?
USER: My mother was the daughter of a king; Who died the minute I was born, As my good nurse Lychorida hath oft Deliver'd weeping.
BOT: You scorn: believe me, 'twere best I did give o'er.
USER: I will believe you by the syllable Of what you shall deliver. Yet, give me leave: How came you in these parts? Where were you bred?
BOT: Ho, Helicanus!
USER: Calls my lord?
BOT: Thou art a grave and noble counsellor, Most wise in general: tell me, if thou canst, What this maid is, or what is like to be, That thus hath made me weep.
USER: I know not, But here is the regent, sir, of Mytilene Speaks nobly of her.
BOT: She would never tell Her parentage; being demanded that, She would sit still and weep.
USER: First, sir, I pray, what is your title?
BOT: I am Pericles of Tyre: but tell me now My drown'd queen's name, as in the rest you said Thou hast been godlike perfect, The heir of kingdoms and another life To Pericles thy father.
USER: Is it no more to be your daughter than To say my mother's name was Thaisa? Thaisa was my mother, who did end The minute I began.
BOT: Sir, 'tis the governor of Mytilene, Who, hearing of your melancholy state, Did come to see you.
USER: My lord, I hear none.
BOT: None! The music of the spheres! List, my Marina.
USER: It is not good to cross him; give him way.
BOT: Rarest sounds! Do ye not hear?
USER: Music, my lord? I hear.
BOT: Most heavenly music! It nips me unto listening, and thick slumber Hangs upon mine eyes: let me rest.
USER: A pillow for his head: So, leave him all. Well, my companion friends, If this but answer to my just belief, I'll well remember you. Diana appears to Pericles as in a vision.
BOT: Celestial Dian, goddess argentine, I will obey thee. Helicanus!
USER: Sir?
BOT: Sir, with all my heart, And when you come ashore I have another suit.
USER: You shall prevail, were it to woo my daughter; For it seems you have been noble towards her.
BOT: Sir, lend me your arm.
USER: Come, my Marina.

USER: Voice and favour! You are, you are - O royal Pericles!
BOT: What means the nun? She dies! help, gentlemen!
USER: Noble sir, If you have told Diana's altar true, This is your wife.
BOT: Reverend appearer, no; I threw her overboard with these very arms.
USER: Upon this coast, I warrant you.
BOT: 'Tis most certain.
USER: Look to the lady; O, she's but o'er-joy'd. Early in blustering morn this lady was Thrown upon this shore. I oped the coffin, Found there rich jewels; recover'd her, and placed her Here in Diana's temple.
BOT: May we see them?
USER: Great sir, they shall be brought you to my house, Whither I invite you. Look, Thaisa is Recovered.
BOT: The voice of dead Thaisa!
USER: That Thaisa am I, supposed dead And drown'd.
BOT: Immortal Dian!
USER: Now I know you better, When we with tears parted Pentapolis, The king my father gave you such a ring.
BOT: This, this: no more, you gods! your present kindness Makes my past miseries sports: you shall do well, That on the touching of her lips I may Melt and no more be seen. O, come, be buried A second time within these arms.
USER: My heart Leaps to be gone into my mother's bosom.
BOT: Look, who kneels here! Flesh of thy flesh, Thaisa; Thy burden at the sea, and call'd Marina For she was yielded there.
USER: Blest, and mine own!
BOT: Hail, madam, and my queen!
USER: I know you not.
BOT: You have heard me say, when I did fly from Tyre, I left behind an ancient substitute: Can you remember what I call'd the man? I have named him oft.
USER: 'Twas Helicanus then.
BOT: Still confirmation: Embrace him, dear Thaisa; this is he. Now do I long to hear how you were found: How possibly preserved; and who to thank, Besides the gods, for this great miracle.
USER: Lord Cerimon, my lord; this man, Through whom the gods have shown their power; that can From first to last resolve you.
BOT: Reverend sir, The gods can have no mortal officer More like a god than you. Will you deliver How this dead queen relives?
USER: I will, my lord. Beseech you, first go with me to my house, Where shall be shown you all was found with her; How she came placed here in the temple; No needful thing omitted.
BOT: Lord Cerimon hath letters of good credit, sir, My father's dead.

USER: I have, my liege.
BOT: Tell me, moreover, hast thou sounded him If he appeal the Duke on ancient malice, Or worthily, as a good subject should, On some known ground of treachery in him?
USER: As near as I could sift him on that argument, On some apparent danger seen in him Aimed at your Highness, no inveterate malice.
BOT: Then call them to our presence. Face to face And frowning brow to brow, ourselves will hear The accuser and the accused freely speak. High-stomached are they both and full of ire, In rage, deaf as the sea, hasty as fire.
USER: Many years of happy days befall My gracious sovereign, my most loving liege!
BOT: Each day still better other's happiness Until the heavens, envying earth's good hap, Add an immortal title to your crown!
USER: We thank you both. Yet one but flatters us, As well appeareth by the cause you come, Namely, to appeal each other of high treason. Cousin of Hereford, what dost thou object Against the Duke of Norfolk, Thomas Mowbray?
BOT: What doth our cousin lay to Mowbray's charge? It must be great that can inherit us So much as of a thought of ill in him.
USER: How high a pitch his resolution soars! Thomas of Norfolk, what sayst thou to this?
BOT: O! let my sovereign turn away his face And bid his ears a little while be deaf, Till I have told this slander of his blood How God and good men hate so foul a liar.
USER: To be a make-peace shall become my age. Throw down, my son, the Duke of Norfolk's gage.
BOT: And, Norfolk, throw down his.
USER: When, Harry, when? Obedience bids I should not bid again.
BOT: Norfolk, throw down, we bid; there is no boot.
USER: Rage must be withstood. Give me his gage. Lions make leopards tame.
BOT: Cousin, throw up your gage; do you begin.

USER: God's is the quarrel; for God's substitute, His deputy anointed in His sight, Hath caused his death, the which if wrongfully, Let heaven revenge, for I may never lift An angry arm against His minister.
BOT: Where then, alas! may I complain myself?
USER: To God, the widow's champion and defence.
BOT: Sister, farewell; I must to Coventry. As much good stay with thee as go with me!

USER: My Lord Aumerle, is Harry Hereford armed?
BOT: Yea, at all points, and longs to enter in.
USER: The Duke of Norfolk, sprightfully and bold, Stays but the summons of the appelant's trumpet.
BOT: Marshal, demand of yonder champion The cause of his arrival here in arms. Ask him his name, and orderly proceed To swear him in the justice of his cause.
USER: Marshal, ask yonder knight in arms Both who he is and why he cometh hither Thus plated in habiliments of war, And formally, according to our law, Depose him in the justice of his cause.
BOT: What is thy name? And wherefore com'st thou hither Before King Richard in his royal lists? Against whom comest thou? and what's thy quarrel? Speak like a true knight, so defend thee heaven!
USER: On pain of death, no person be so bold Or daring-hardy as to touch the lists, Except the Marshal and such officers Appointed to direct these fair designs.
BOT: Farewell, my lord. Securely I espy Virtue with valour couched in thine eye. Order the trial, Marshal, and begin.
USER: Harry of Hereford, Lancaster, and Derby, Receive thy lance; and God defend the right. BOLINGBROKE. [_Rising_.] Strong as a tower in hope, I cry "Amen"!
BOT: Sound trumpets, and set forward, combatants. Stay! the King hath thrown his warder down.
USER: Your will be done. This must my comfort be: That sun that warms you here shall shine on me, And those his golden beams to you here lent Shall point on me and gild my banishment.
BOT: It boots thee not to be compassionate. After our sentence plaining comes too late.
USER: Then thus I turn me from my country's light, To dwell in solemn shades of endless night.
BOT: I swear.
USER: And I, to keep all this.
BOT: How long a time lies in one little word! Four lagging winters and four wanton springs End in a word: such is the breath of kings.
USER: Why, uncle, thou hast many years to live.
BOT: Thy son is banished upon good advice, Whereto thy tongue a party-verdict gave. Why at our justice seem'st thou then to lour?
USER: Cousin, farewell, and, uncle, bid him so. Six years we banish him, and he shall go.
BOT: Cousin, farewell. What presence must not know, From where you do remain let paper show.
USER: My lord, no leave take I, for I will ride, As far as land will let me, by your side.
BOT: O, to what purpose dost thou hoard thy words, That thou return'st no greeting to thy friends?
USER: I have too few to take my leave of you, When the tongue's office should be prodigal To breathe the abundant dolour of the heart.
BOT: Thy grief is but thy absence for a time.
USER: Joy absent, grief is present for that time.
BOT: What is six winters? They are quickly gone.
USER: To men in joy; but grief makes one hour ten.
BOT: Call it a travel that thou tak'st for pleasure.
USER: My heart will sigh when I miscall it so, Which finds it an enforced pilgrimage.
BOT: The sullen passage of thy weary steps Esteem as foil wherein thou art to set The precious jewel of thy home return.
USER: Come, come, my son, I'll bring thee on thy way. Had I thy youth and cause, I would not stay.
BOT: Then, England's ground, farewell; sweet soil, adieu, My mother and my nurse that bears me yet! Where'er I wander, boast of this I can, Though banished, yet a true-born Englishman.

USER: We did observe. - Cousin Aumerle, How far brought you high Hereford on his way?
BOT: I brought high Hereford, if you call him so, But to the next highway, and there I left him.
USER: And say, what store of parting tears were shed?
BOT: Faith, none for me, except the northeast wind, Which then blew bitterly against our faces, Awaked the sleeping rheum, and so by chance Did grace our hollow parting with a tear.
USER: What said our cousin when you parted with him?
BOT: Old John of Gaunt is grievous sick, my lord, Suddenly taken, and hath sent posthaste To entreat your Majesty to visit him.
USER: Where lies he?
BOT: At Ely House.
USER: Amen!

USER: Will the King come, that I may breathe my last In wholesome counsel to his unstaid youth?
BOT: Vex not yourself, nor strive not with your breath, For all in vain comes counsel to his ear.
USER: The King is come. Deal mildly with his youth, For young hot colts, being raged, do rage the more.
BOT: How fares our noble uncle, Lancaster?
USER: What comfort, man? How is't with aged Gaunt?
BOT: Can sick men play so nicely with their names?
USER: No, misery makes sport to mock itself. Since thou dost seek to kill my name in me, I mock my name, great king, to flatter thee.
BOT: Should dying men flatter with those that live?
USER: No, no, men living flatter those that die.
BOT: Thou, now a-dying, sayest thou flatterest me.
USER: O, no, thou diest, though I the sicker be.
BOT: I am in health, I breathe, and see thee ill.
USER: And let them die that age and sullens have, For both hast thou, and both become the grave.
BOT: I do beseech your Majesty, impute his words To wayward sickliness and age in him. He loves you, on my life, and holds you dear As Harry, Duke of Hereford, were he here.
USER: Right, you say true: as Hereford's love, so his; As theirs, so mine; and all be as it is.
BOT: My liege, old Gaunt commends him to your Majesty.
USER: What says he?
BOT: Nay, nothing; all is said. His tongue is now a stringless instrument; Words, life, and all, old Lancaster hath spent.
USER: Be York the next that must be bankrupt so! Though death be poor, it ends a mortal woe.
BOT: Why, uncle, what's the matter?
USER: Think what you will, we seize into our hands His plate, his goods, his money, and his lands.
BOT: I'll not be by the while. My liege, farewell. What will ensue hereof there's none can tell; But by bad courses may be understood That their events can never fall out good.
USER: Well, lords, the Duke of Lancaster is dead.
BOT: And living too, for now his son is Duke.
USER: Barely in title, not in revenues.
BOT: Richly in both, if justice had her right.
USER: My heart is great, but it must break with silence Ere't be disburdened with a liberal tongue.
BOT: Nay, speak thy mind, and let him ne'er speak more That speaks thy words again to do thee harm!
USER: Tends that thou wouldst speak to the Duke of Hereford? If it be so, out with it boldly, man. Quick is mine ear to hear of good towards him.
BOT: No good at all that I can do for him, Unless you call it good to pity him, Bereft and gelded of his patrimony.
