USER: The commons hath he pilled with grievous taxes, And quite lost their hearts. The nobles hath he fined For ancient quarrels and quite lost their hearts.
BOT: And daily new exactions are devised, As blanks, benevolences, and I wot not what. But what, i' God's name, doth become of this?
USER: Wars hath not wasted it, for warred he hath not, But basely yielded upon compromise That which his ancestors achieved with blows. More hath he spent in peace than they in wars.
BOT: The Earl of Wiltshire hath the realm in farm.
USER: The King's grown bankrupt like a broken man.
BOT: Reproach and dissolution hangeth over him.
USER: He hath not money for these Irish wars, His burdenous taxations notwithstanding, But by the robbing of the banished Duke.
BOT: His noble kinsman. Most degenerate king! But, lords, we hear this fearful tempest sing, Yet seek no shelter to avoid the storm; We see the wind sit sore upon our sails, And yet we strike not, but securely perish.
USER: We see the very wrack that we must suffer; And unavoided is the danger now For suffering so the causes of our wrack.
BOT: Not so. Even through the hollow eyes of death I spy life peering; but I dare not say How near the tidings of our comfort is.
USER: Nay, let us share thy thoughts as thou dost ours.
BOT: Be confident to speak, Northumberland. We three are but thyself, and, speaking so, Thy words are but as thoughts. Therefore be bold.
USER: To horse, to horse! Urge doubts to them that fear.
BOT: Hold out my horse, and I will first be there.

USER: Madam, your Majesty is too much sad. You promised, when you parted with the King, To lay aside life-harming heaviness And entertain a cheerful disposition.
BOT: It may be so; but yet my inward soul Persuades me it is otherwise. Howe'er it be, I cannot but be sad - so heavy sad As thought, in thinking, on no thought I think, Makes me with heavy nothing faint and shrink.
USER: 'Tis nothing but conceit, my gracious lady.
BOT: God save your majesty! And well met, gentlemen. I hope the King is not yet shipped for Ireland.
USER: Why hop'st thou so? 'Tis better hope he is, For his designs crave haste, his haste good hope. Then wherefore dost thou hope he is not shipped?
BOT: Now God in heaven forbid!
USER: Ah, madam, 'tis too true; and that is worse, The Lord Northumberland, his son young Harry Percy, The Lords of Ross, Beaumond, and Willoughby, With all their powerful friends, are fled to him.
BOT: Why have you not proclaimed Northumberland And all the rest revolted faction traitors?
USER: We have, whereupon the Earl of Worcester Hath broken his staff, resigned his stewardship, And all the household servants fled with him To Bolingbroke.
BOT: So, Green, thou art the midwife to my woe, And Bolingbroke my sorrow's dismal heir. Now hath my soul brought forth her prodigy, And I, a gasping new-delivered mother, Have woe to woe, sorrow to sorrow joined.
USER: Despair not, madam.
BOT: Who shall hinder me? I will despair and be at enmity With cozening hope. He is a flatterer, A parasite, a keeper-back of death, Who gently would dissolve the bands of life, Which false hope lingers in extremity.
USER: Here comes the Duke of York.
BOT: With signs of war about his aged neck. O! full of careful business are his looks! Uncle, for God's sake, speak comfortable words.
USER: My lord, your son was gone before I came.
BOT: My lord, I had forgot to tell your lordship: Today, as I came by, I called there - But I shall grieve you to report the rest.
USER: What is't, knave?
BOT: An hour before I came, the Duchess died.
USER: The wind sits fair for news to go to Ireland, But none returns. For us to levy power Proportionable to the enemy Is all unpossible.
BOT: Besides, our nearness to the King in love Is near the hate of those love not the King.
USER: And that is the wavering commons, for their love Lies in their purses; and whoso empties them, By so much fills their hearts with deadly hate.
BOT: Wherein the King stands generally condemned.
USER: If judgment lie in them, then so do we, Because we ever have been near the King.
BOT: Well, I will for refuge straight to Bristol Castle. The Earl of Wiltshire is already there.
USER: Thither will I with you, for little office Will the hateful commons perform for us, Except like curs to tear us all to pieces. Will you go along with us?
BOT: No, I will to Ireland to his Majesty. Farewell. If heart's presages be not vain, We three here part that ne'er shall meet again.
USER: That's as York thrives to beat back Bolingbroke.
BOT: Alas, poor Duke! The task he undertakes Is numb'ring sands and drinking oceans dry. Where one on his side fights, thousands will fly. Farewell at once, for once, for all, and ever.
USER: Well, we may meet again.
BOT: I fear me, never.

USER: How far is it, my lord, to Berkeley now?
BOT: Of much less value is my company Than your good words. But who comes here?
USER: It is my son, young Harry Percy, Sent from my brother Worcester, whencesoever. Harry, how fares your uncle?
BOT: I had thought, my lord, to have learned his health of you.
USER: Why, is he not with the Queen?
BOT: No, my good lord. He hath forsook the court, Broken his staff of office, and dispersed The household of the King.
USER: What was his reason? He was not so resolved when last we spake together.
BOT: Have you forgot the Duke of Hereford, boy?
USER: No, my good lord; for that is not forgot Which ne'er I did remember. To my knowledge, I never in my life did look on him.
BOT: Then learn to know him now. This is the Duke.
USER: My gracious lord, I tender you my service, Such as it is, being tender, raw, and young, Which elder days shall ripen and confirm To more approved service and desert.
BOT: How far is it to Berkeley, and what stir Keeps good old York there with his men of war?
USER: There stands the castle by yon tuft of trees, Manned with three hundred men, as I have heard. And in it are the Lords of York, Berkeley, and Seymour, None else of name and noble estimate.
BOT: Here come the Lords of Ross and Willoughby, Bloody with spurring, fiery-red with haste.
USER: Welcome, my lords. I wot your love pursues A banished traitor. All my treasury Is yet but unfelt thanks, which, more enriched, Shall be your love and labour's recompense.
BOT: Your presence makes us rich, most noble lord.
USER: And far surmounts our labour to attain it.
BOT: Evermore thanks, the exchequer of the poor; Which, till my infant fortune comes to years, Stands for my bounty. But who comes here?
USER: It is my Lord of Berkeley, as I guess.
BOT: My Lord of Hereford, my message is to you.
USER: My lord, my answer is - to "Lancaster", And I am come to seek that name in England; And I must find that title in your tongue Before I make reply to aught you say.
BOT: I shall not need transport my words by you. Here comes his Grace in person. My noble uncle!
USER: Show me thy humble heart, and not thy knee, Whose duty is deceivable and false.
BOT: My gracious uncle -
USER: My gracious uncle, let me know my fault. On what condition stands it and wherein?
BOT: Even in condition of the worst degree, In gross rebellion and detested treason. Thou art a banished man, and here art come, Before the expiration of thy time, In braving arms against thy sovereign.
USER: The noble Duke hath been too much abused.
BOT: It stands your Grace upon to do him right.
USER: Base men by his endowments are made great.
BOT: The noble Duke hath sworn his coming is But for his own; and for the right of that We all have strongly sworn to give him aid; And let him never see joy that breaks that oath!
USER: It may be I will go with you; but yet I'll pause, For I am loath to break our country's laws. Nor friends nor foes, to me welcome you are. Things past redress are now with me past care.

USER: My Lord of Salisbury, we have stayed ten days And hardly kept our countrymen together, And yet we hear no tidings from the King. Therefore we will disperse ourselves. Farewell.
BOT: Stay yet another day, thou trusty Welshman. The King reposeth all his confidence in thee.

USER: More welcome is the stroke of death to me Than Bolingbroke to England. Lords, farewell.
BOT: My comfort is that heaven will take our souls And plague injustice with the pains of hell.
USER: My Lord Northumberland, see them dispatched. Uncle, you say the Queen is at your house; For God's sake, fairly let her be entreated. Tell her I send to her my kind commends; Take special care my greetings be delivered.
BOT: A gentleman of mine I have dispatched With letters of your love to her at large.
USER: Thanks, gentle uncle. Come, lords, away, To fight with Glendower and his complices. A while to work, and after holiday.

USER: Barkloughly Castle call they this at hand?
BOT: Yea, my lord. How brooks your Grace the air After your late tossing on the breaking seas?
USER: He means, my lord, that we are too remiss, Whilst Bolingbroke, through our security, Grows strong and great in substance and in power.
BOT: Comfort, my liege. Why looks your Grace so pale?
USER: Comfort, my liege. Remember who you are.
BOT: More health and happiness betide my liege Than can my care-tuned tongue deliver him.
USER: Peace have they made with him indeed, my lord.
BOT: Is Bushy, Green, and the Earl of Wiltshire dead?
USER: Ay, all of them at Bristol lost their heads.
BOT: Where is the Duke my father with his power?
USER: My father hath a power. Enquire of him, And learn to make a body of a limb.
BOT: My liege, one word.
USER: He does me double wrong That wounds me with the flatteries of his tongue. Discharge my followers. Let them hence away, From Richard's night to Bolingbroke's fair day.

USER: So that by this intelligence we learn The Welshmen are dispersed, and Salisbury Is gone to meet the King, who lately landed With some few private friends upon this coast.
BOT: The news is very fair and good, my lord: Richard not far from hence hath hid his head.
USER: It would beseem the Lord Northumberland To say "King Richard". Alack the heavy day When such a sacred king should hide his head!
BOT: Your Grace mistakes; only to be brief Left I his title out.
USER: The time hath been, Would you have been so brief with him, he would Have been so brief with you to shorten you, For taking so the head, your whole head's length.
BOT: Mistake not, uncle, further than you should.
USER: Take not, good cousin, further than you should, Lest you mistake. The heavens are o'er our heads.
BOT: I know it, uncle, and oppose not myself Against their will. But who comes here? Welcome, Harry. What, will not this castle yield?
USER: The castle royally is manned, my lord, Against thy entrance.
BOT: Royally! Why, it contains no king?
USER: O, belike it is the Bishop of Carlisle.
BOT: Yet he looks like a king. Behold, his eye, As bright as is the eagle's, lightens forth Controlling majesty. Alack, alack, for woe That any harm should stain so fair a show!
USER: No, good my lord. Let's fight with gentle words Till time lend friends, and friends their helpful swords.
BOT: Northumberland comes back from Bolingbroke.
USER: My lord, in the base court he doth attend To speak with you. May it please you to come down?
BOT: What says his Majesty?
USER: Sorrow and grief of heart Makes him speak fondly like a frantic man. Yet he is come.
BOT: Stand all apart, And show fair duty to his Majesty. [_Kneeling_.] My gracious lord.
USER: My gracious lord, I come but for mine own.
BOT: Your own is yours, and I am yours, and all.
USER: So far be mine, my most redoubted lord, As my true service shall deserve your love.
BOT: Yea, my good lord.
USER: Then I must not say no.

USER: What sport shall we devise here in this garden To drive away the heavy thought of care?
BOT: Madam, we'll play at bowls.
USER: 'Twill make me think the world is full of rubs And that my fortune runs against the bias.
BOT: Madam, we'll dance.
USER: My legs can keep no measure in delight When my poor heart no measure keeps in grief. Therefore no dancing, girl; some other sport.
BOT: Madam, we'll tell tales.
USER: Of sorrow or of joy?
BOT: Of either, madam.
USER: Madam, I'll sing.
BOT: 'Tis well that thou hast cause; But thou shouldst please me better wouldst thou weep.
USER: I could weep, madam, would it do you good.
BOT: What, are they dead?
USER: What, think you the King shall be deposed?
BOT: Depressed he is already, and deposed 'Tis doubt he will be. Letters came last night To a dear friend of the good Duke of York's That tell black tidings.

USER: Call forth Bagot. Now, Bagot, freely speak thy mind, What thou dost know of noble Gloucester's death, Who wrought it with the King, and who performed The bloody office of his timeless end.
BOT: Then set before my face the Lord Aumerle.
USER: Cousin, stand forth, and look upon that man.
BOT: Bagot, forbear. Thou shalt not take it up.
USER: Excepting one, I would he were the best In all this presence that hath moved me so.
BOT: Thou dar'st not, coward, live to see that day.
USER: Now, by my soul, I would it were this hour.
BOT: Fitzwater, thou art damned to hell for this.
USER: Aumerle, thou liest. His honour is as true In this appeal as thou art an unjust; And that thou art so, there I throw my gage, To prove it on thee to the extremest point Of mortal breathing. Seize it if thou dar'st.
BOT: And if I do not, may my hands rot off And never brandish more revengeful steel Over the glittering helmet of my foe!
USER: I task the earth to the like, forsworn Aumerle, And spur thee on with full as many lies As may be holloaed in thy treacherous ear From sun to sun. There is my honour's pawn. Engage it to the trial if thou dar'st.
BOT: Who sets me else? By heaven, I'll throw at all. I have a thousand spirits in one breast To answer twenty thousand such as you.
USER: My Lord Fitzwater, I do remember well The very time Aumerle and you did talk.
BOT: 'Tis very true. You were in presence then, And you can witness with me this is true.
USER: As false, by heaven, as heaven itself is true.
BOT: Surrey, thou liest.
USER: Some honest Christian trust me with a gage. That Norfolk lies, here do I throw down this, If he may be repealed to try his honour.
BOT: Why, Bishop, is Norfolk dead?
USER: As surely as I live, my lord.
BOT: Sweet peace conduct his sweet soul to the bosom Of good old Abraham! Lords appellants, Your differences shall all rest under gage Till we assign you to your days of trial.
USER: In God's name, I'll ascend the regal throne.
BOT: Fetch hither Richard, that in common view He may surrender. So we shall proceed Without suspicion.
USER: I will be his conduct.
BOT: Lords, you that here are under our arrest, Procure your sureties for your days of answer. Little are we beholding to your love, And little looked for at your helping hands.
USER: To do that office of thine own good will Which tired majesty did make thee offer: The resignation of thy state and crown To Henry Bolingbroke.
BOT: I thought you had been willing to resign.
USER: My crown I am, but still my griefs are mine. You may my glories and my state depose, But not my griefs; still am I king of those.
BOT: Part of your cares you give me with your crown.
USER: Are you contented to resign the crown?
BOT: These accusations, and these grievous crimes Committed by your person and your followers Against the state and profit of this land; That, by confessing them, the souls of men May deem that you are worthily deposed.
USER: My lord, dispatch. Read o'er these articles.
BOT: My lord -
USER: Go, some of you, and fetch a looking-glass.
BOT: Read o'er this paper while the glass doth come.
USER: Fiend, thou torments me ere I come to hell!
BOT: Urge it no more, my Lord Northumberland.
USER: The commons will not then be satisfied.
BOT: The shadow of your sorrow hath destroyed The shadow of your face.
USER: Name it, fair cousin.
BOT: "Fair cousin"? I am greater than a king; For when I was a king, my flatterers Were then but subjects. Being now a subject, I have a king here to my flatterer. Being so great, I have no need to beg.
USER: Yet ask.
BOT: And shall I have?
USER: You shall.
BOT: Then give me leave to go.
USER: Whither?
BOT: Whither you will, so I were from your sights.
USER: Go, some of you, convey him to the Tower.
BOT: O, good! "Convey"? Conveyers are you all, That rise thus nimbly by a true king's fall.
USER: On Wednesday next we solemnly set down Our coronation. Lords, prepare yourselves. Aumerle._]
BOT: A woeful pageant have we here beheld.
USER: The woe's to come. The children yet unborn Shall feel this day as sharp to them as thorn.
BOT: You holy clergymen, is there no plot To rid the realm of this pernicious blot?

USER: My lord, the mind of Bolingbroke is changed. You must to Pomfret, not unto the Tower. And, madam, there is order ta'en for you: With all swift speed you must away to France.
BOT: My guilt be on my head, and there an end. Take leave and part, for you must part forthwith.
USER: And must we be divided? Must we part?
BOT: Ay, hand from hand, my love, and heart from heart.
USER: Banish us both, and send the King with me.
BOT: That were some love, but little policy.
USER: Then whither he goes, thither let me go.
BOT: So two, together weeping, make one woe. Weep thou for me in France, I for thee here; Better far off than near, be ne'er the near. Go, count thy way with sighs, I mine with groans.
USER: So longest way shall have the longest moans.
BOT: Give me mine own again; 'twere no good part To take on me to keep and kill thy heart. So, now I have mine own again, be gone, That I may strive to kill it with a groan.
USER: We make woe wanton with this fond delay: Once more, adieu. The rest let sorrow say.

USER: My Lord, you told me you would tell the rest, When weeping made you break the story off Of our two cousins' coming into London.
BOT: Where did I leave?
USER: At that sad stop, my lord, Where rude misgoverned hands from windows' tops Threw dust and rubbish on King Richard's head.
BOT: Alack, poor Richard! Where rode he the whilst?
USER: Here comes my son Aumerle.
BOT: Aumerle that was; But that is lost for being Richard's friend, And, madam, you must call him Rutland now. I am in Parliament pledge for his truth And lasting fealty to the new-made king.
USER: Welcome, my son. Who are the violets now That strew the green lap of the new-come spring?
BOT: Madam, I know not, nor I greatly care not. God knows I had as lief be none as one.
USER: Well, bear you well in this new spring of time, Lest you be cropped before you come to prime. What news from Oxford? Do these jousts and triumphs hold?
BOT: For aught I know, my lord, they do.
USER: You will be there, I know.
BOT: If God prevent not, I purpose so.
USER: What seal is that that hangs without thy bosom? Yea, look'st thou pale? Let me see the writing.
BOT: My lord, 'tis nothing.
USER: No matter, then, who see it. I will be satisfied. Let me see the writing.
BOT: I do beseech your Grace to pardon me. It is a matter of small consequence, Which for some reasons I would not have seen.
USER: Which for some reasons, sir, I mean to see. I fear, I fear -
BOT: What should you fear? 'Tis nothing but some bond that he is entered into For gay apparel 'gainst the triumph day.
USER: Bound to himself? What doth he with a bond That he is bound to? Wife, thou art a fool. Boy, let me see the writing.
BOT: I do beseech you, pardon me. I may not show it.
USER: I will be satisfied. Let me see it, I say. Treason, foul treason! Villain! traitor! slave!
BOT: What is the matter, my lord?
USER: Ho! who is within there? Saddle my horse. God for his mercy, what treachery is here!
BOT: Why, what is it, my lord?
USER: Give me my boots, I say. Saddle my horse. Now, by mine honour, by my life, my troth, I will appeach the villain.
BOT: What is the matter?
USER: Peace, foolish woman.
BOT: I will not peace. What is the matter, Aumerle?
USER: Good mother, be content. It is no more Than my poor life must answer.
BOT: Thy life answer?
USER: Bring me my boots. I will unto the King.
BOT: Strike him, Aumerle! Poor boy, thou art amazed. Hence, villain! Never more come in my sight.
USER: Give me my boots, I say.
BOT: Thou fond mad woman, Wilt thou conceal this dark conspiracy? A dozen of them here have ta'en the sacrament And interchangeably set down their hands To kill the King at Oxford.
USER: He shall be none; We'll keep him here. Then what is that to him?
BOT: Away, fond woman! Were he twenty times my son, I would appeach him.
USER: Make way, unruly woman!

USER: My lord, some two days since I saw the Prince, And told him of those triumphs held at Oxford.
BOT: And what said the gallant?
USER: His answer was he would unto the stews, And from the common'st creature pluck a glove And wear it as a favour, and with that He would unhorse the lustiest challenger.
BOT: As dissolute as desperate! Yet through both I see some sparks of better hope, which elder years May happily bring forth. But who comes here?
USER: Where is the King?
BOT: What means our cousin that he stares and looks so wildly?
USER: God save your Grace! I do beseech your majesty To have some conference with your Grace alone.
BOT: Withdraw yourselves, and leave us here alone. What is the matter with our cousin now?
USER: My tongue cleave to my roof within my mouth, Unless a pardon ere I rise or speak.
BOT: Intended or committed was this fault? If on the first, how heinous e'er it be, To win thy after-love I pardon thee.
USER: Then give me leave that I may turn the key, That no man enter till my tale be done.
BOT: Have thy desire.
USER: Thou hast a traitor in thy presence there.
BOT: Stay thy revengeful hand. Thou hast no cause to fear.
USER: Shall I for love speak treason to thy face? Open the door, or I will break it open.
BOT: What is the matter, uncle? Speak! Recover breath. Tell us how near is danger, That we may arm us to encounter it.
USER: Peruse this writing here, and thou shalt know The treason that my haste forbids me show.
BOT: Remember, as thou read'st, thy promise passed. I do repent me. Read not my name there; My heart is not confederate with my hand.
USER: It was, villain, ere thy hand did set it down. I tore it from the traitor's bosom, king. Fear, and not love, begets his penitence. Forget to pity him, lest thy pity prove A serpent that will sting thee to the heart.
BOT: What shrill-voiced suppliant makes this eager cry?
USER: Speak with me, pity me, open the door! A beggar begs that never begged before.
BOT: Our scene is altered from a serious thing, And now changed to "The Beggar and the King." My dangerous cousin, let your mother in. I know she's come to pray for your foul sin.
USER: If thou do pardon whosoever pray, More sins for this forgiveness prosper may. This festered joint cut off, the rest rest sound; This let alone will all the rest confound.
BOT: O King, believe not this hard-hearted man. Love loving not itself none other can.
USER: Thou frantic woman, what dost thou make here? Shall thy old dugs once more a traitor rear?
BOT: Sweet York, be patient. [_Kneels_.] Hear me, gentle liege.
USER: Rise up, good aunt.
BOT: Not yet, I thee beseech. For ever will I walk upon my knees And never see day that the happy sees, Till thou give joy, until thou bid me joy By pardoning Rutland, my transgressing boy.
USER: Unto my mother's prayers I bend my knee.
BOT: Against them both, my true joints bended be. Ill mayst thou thrive if thou grant any grace!
USER: Good aunt, stand up.
BOT: Speak it in French, King, say "pardonne moy."
USER: Good aunt, stand up.
BOT: I do not sue to stand. Pardon is all the suit I have in hand.
USER: I pardon him, as God shall pardon me.
BOT: O, happy vantage of a kneeling knee! Yet am I sick for fear. Speak it again, Twice saying "pardon" doth not pardon twain, But makes one pardon strong.
USER: With all my heart I pardon him.
BOT: A god on earth thou art.
USER: Come, my old son. I pray God make thee new.

USER: Didst thou not mark the King, what words he spake: "Have I no friend will rid me of this living fear?" Was it not so?
BOT: These were his very words.
USER: "Have I no friend?" quoth he. He spake it twice And urged it twice together, did he not?
BOT: He did.

USER: Hail, royal Prince!
BOT: Thanks, noble peer. The cheapest of us is ten groats too dear. What art thou, and how comest thou hither Where no man never comes but that sad dog That brings me food to make misfortune live?
USER: Rode he on Barbary? Tell me, gentle friend, How went he under him?
BOT: So proudly as if he disdained the ground.
USER: If thou love me, 'tis time thou wert away.
BOT: My tongue dares not, that my heart shall say.
USER: My lord, will't please you to fall to?
BOT: Taste of it first as thou art wont to do.
USER: My lord, I dare not. Sir Pierce of Exton, Who lately came from the King, commands the contrary.
BOT: The devil take Henry of Lancaster and thee! Patience is stale, and I am weary of it.
USER: Help, help, help!

USER: Kind uncle York, the latest news we hear Is that the rebels have consumed with fire Our town of Cicester in Gloucestershire, But whether they be ta'en or slain we hear not. Welcome, my lord. What is the news?
BOT: First, to thy sacred state wish I all happiness. The next news is: I have to London sent The heads of Salisbury, Spencer, Blunt, and Kent. The manner of their taking may appear At large discoursed in this paper here.
USER: We thank thee, gentle Percy, for thy pains, And to thy worth will add right worthy gains.
BOT: My lord, I have from Oxford sent to London The heads of Brocas and Sir Bennet Seely, Two of the dangerous consorted traitors That sought at Oxford thy dire overthrow.
USER: Thy pains, Fitzwater, shall not be forgot. Right noble is thy merit, well I wot.
BOT: The grand conspirator, Abbot of Westminster, With clog of conscience and sour melancholy, Hath yielded up his body to the grave. But here is Carlisle living, to abide Thy kingly doom and sentence of his pride.
USER: Great king, within this coffin I present Thy buried fear. Herein all breathless lies The mightiest of thy greatest enemies, Richard of Bordeaux, by me hither brought.
BOT: Exton, I thank thee not, for thou hast wrought A deed of slander with thy fatal hand Upon my head and all this famous land.
USER: From your own mouth, my lord, did I this deed.

USER: His Majesty, Tend'ring my person's safety, hath appointed This conduct to convey me to the Tower.
BOT: Upon what cause?
USER: Because my name is George.
BOT: I beseech your Graces both to pardon me. His Majesty hath straitly given in charge That no man shall have private conference, Of what degree soever, with your brother.
USER: With this, my lord, myself have naught to do.
BOT: Naught to do with Mistress Shore? I tell thee, fellow, He that doth naught with her, excepting one, Were best to do it secretly alone.
USER: What one, my lord?
BOT: Her husband, knave! Wouldst thou betray me?
USER: I do beseech your Grace to pardon me, and withal Forbear your conference with the noble Duke.
BOT: We know thy charge, Brakenbury, and will obey.
USER: I know it pleaseth neither of us well.
BOT: Well, your imprisonment shall not be long. I will deliver or else lie for you. Meantime, have patience.
USER: I must perforce. Farewell.
BOT: Good time of day unto my gracious lord.
USER: As much unto my good Lord Chamberlain. Well are you welcome to the open air. How hath your lordship brooked imprisonment?
BOT: With patience, noble lord, as prisoners must; But I shall live, my lord, to give them thanks That were the cause of my imprisonment.
USER: No doubt, no doubt; and so shall Clarence too, For they that were your enemies are his, And have prevailed as much on him as you.
BOT: More pity that the eagles should be mewed, Whiles kites and buzzards prey at liberty.
USER: What news abroad?
BOT: No news so bad abroad as this at home: The King is sickly, weak, and melancholy, And his physicians fear him mightily.
USER: Now, by Saint John, that news is bad indeed. O, he hath kept an evil diet long, And overmuch consumed his royal person. 'Tis very grievous to be thought upon. Where is he, in his bed?
BOT: He is.

USER: Stay, you that bear the corse, and set it down.
BOT: What black magician conjures up this fiend To stop devoted charitable deeds?
USER: Villains, set down the corse or, by Saint Paul, I'll make a corse of him that disobeys!
BOT: My lord, stand back, and let the coffin pass.
USER: Unmannered dog, stand thou, when I command! Advance thy halberd higher than my breast, Or by Saint Paul I'll strike thee to my foot And spurn upon thee, beggar, for thy boldness.
BOT: Sweet saint, for charity, be not so curst.
USER: Lady, you know no rules of charity, Which renders good for bad, blessings for curses.
BOT: Villain, thou know'st nor law of God nor man. No beast so fierce but knows some touch of pity.
USER: But I know none, and therefore am no beast.
BOT: O wonderful, when devils tell the truth!
USER: More wonderful when angels are so angry. Vouchsafe, divine perfection of a woman, Of these supposed crimes to give me leave, By circumstance, but to acquit myself.
BOT: Vouchsafe, diffused infection of a man, Of these known evils but to give me leave, By circumstance, to accuse thy cursed self.
USER: Fairer than tongue can name thee, let me have Some patient leisure to excuse myself.
BOT: Fouler than heart can think thee, thou canst make No excuse current but to hang thyself.
USER: By such despair I should accuse myself.
BOT: And by despairing shalt thou stand excused For doing worthy vengeance on thyself That didst unworthy slaughter upon others.
USER: Say that I slew them not?
BOT: Then say they were not slain. But dead they are, and, devilish slave, by thee.
USER: I did not kill your husband.
BOT: Why then he is alive.
USER: Nay, he is dead, and slain by Edward's hand.
BOT: In thy foul throat thou liest. Queen Margaret saw Thy murd'rous falchion smoking in his blood, The which thou once didst bend against her breast, But that thy brothers beat aside the point.
USER: I was provoked by her sland'rous tongue, That laid their guilt upon my guiltless shoulders.
BOT: Thou wast provoked by thy bloody mind, That never dream'st on aught but butcheries. Didst thou not kill this King?
USER: I grant ye.
BOT: Dost grant me, hedgehog? Then, God grant me too Thou mayst be damned for that wicked deed. O, he was gentle, mild, and virtuous.
USER: The better for the King of Heaven that hath him.
BOT: He is in heaven, where thou shalt never come.
USER: Let him thank me that holp to send him thither, For he was fitter for that place than earth.
BOT: And thou unfit for any place but hell.
USER: Yes, one place else, if you will hear me name it.
BOT: Some dungeon.
USER: Your bed-chamber.
BOT: Ill rest betide the chamber where thou liest!
USER: So will it, madam, till I lie with you.
BOT: I hope so.
USER: Thou wast the cause and most accursed effect.
BOT: Your beauty was the cause of that effect: Your beauty, that did haunt me in my sleep To undertake the death of all the world, So I might live one hour in your sweet bosom.
USER: If I thought that, I tell thee, homicide, These nails should rend that beauty from my cheeks.
BOT: These eyes could not endure that beauty's wrack; You should not blemish it if I stood by. As all the world is cheered by the sun, So I by that; it is my day, my life.
USER: Black night o'ershade thy day, and death thy life.
BOT: Curse not thyself, fair creature; thou art both.
USER: I would I were, to be revenged on thee.
BOT: It is a quarrel most unnatural, To be revenged on him that loveth thee.
USER: It is a quarrel just and reasonable, To be revenged on him that killed my husband.
BOT: He that bereft thee, lady, of thy husband, Did it to help thee to a better husband.
USER: His better doth not breathe upon the earth.
BOT: He lives that loves thee better than he could.
USER: Name him.
BOT: Plantagenet.
USER: Why, that was he.
BOT: The selfsame name, but one of better nature.
USER: Where is he?
BOT: Here. Why dost thou spit at me?
USER: Would it were mortal poison, for thy sake.
BOT: Never came poison from so sweet a place.
USER: Never hung poison on a fouler toad. Out of my sight! Thou dost infect mine eyes.
BOT: Thine eyes, sweet lady, have infected mine.
USER: Would they were basilisks to strike thee dead!
BOT: Arise, dissembler. Though I wish thy death, I will not be thy executioner.
USER: Then bid me kill myself, and I will do it.
BOT: I have already.
USER: That was in thy rage. Speak it again, and even with the word, This hand, which for thy love did kill thy love, Shall for thy love kill a far truer love. To both their deaths shalt thou be accessary.
BOT: I would I knew thy heart.
USER: 'Tis figured in my tongue.
BOT: I fear me both are false.
USER: Then never was man true.
BOT: Well, well, put up your sword.
USER: Say then my peace is made.
BOT: That shalt thou know hereafter.
USER: But shall I live in hope?
BOT: All men, I hope, live so.
USER: Vouchsafe to wear this ring.
BOT: To take is not to give.
USER: What is it?
BOT: With all my heart, and much it joys me too To see you are become so penitent. Tressel and Berkeley, go along with me.
USER: Bid me farewell.
BOT: 'Tis more than you deserve; But since you teach me how to flatter you, Imagine I have said farewell already.
USER: Sirs, take up the corse.
BOT: Towards Chertsey, noble lord?

USER: Have patience, madam. There's no doubt his Majesty Will soon recover his accustomed health.
BOT: In that you brook it ill, it makes him worse. Therefore, for God's sake, entertain good comfort, And cheer his Grace with quick and merry eyes.
USER: If he were dead, what would betide on me?
BOT: No other harm but loss of such a lord.
USER: The loss of such a lord includes all harms.
BOT: The heavens have blessed you with a goodly son To be your comforter when he is gone.
USER: Ah, he is young, and his minority Is put unto the trust of Richard Gloucester, A man that loves not me, nor none of you.
BOT: Is it concluded he shall be Protector?
USER: It is determined, not concluded yet; But so it must be, if the King miscarry.
BOT: Here come the Lords of Buckingham and Derby.
USER: Good time of day unto your royal Grace.
BOT: God make your Majesty joyful as you have been.
USER: The Countess Richmond, good my Lord of Derby, To your good prayer will scarcely say amen. Yet, Derby, notwithstanding she's your wife, And loves not me, be you, good lord, assured I hate not you for her proud arrogance.
BOT: I do beseech you, either not believe The envious slanders of her false accusers, Or if she be accused on true report, Bear with her weakness, which I think proceeds From wayward sickness, and no grounded malice.
USER: Saw you the King today, my Lord of Derby?
BOT: But now the Duke of Buckingham and I Are come from visiting his Majesty.
USER: What likelihood of his amendment, lords?
BOT: Madam, good hope; his Grace speaks cheerfully.
USER: God grant him health! Did you confer with him?
BOT: Ay, madam; he desires to make atonement Between the Duke of Gloucester and your brothers, And between them and my Lord Chamberlain; And sent to warn them to his royal presence.
USER: Would all were well - but that will never be. I fear our happiness is at the height.
BOT: To who in all this presence speaks your Grace?
USER: I cannot tell. The world is grown so bad That wrens make prey where eagles dare not perch. Since every Jack became a gentleman, There's many a gentle person made a Jack.
BOT: Come, come, we know your meaning, brother Gloucester. You envy my advancement, and my friends'. God grant we never may have need of you.
USER: You may deny that you were not the mean Of my Lord Hastings' late imprisonment.
BOT: She may, my lord; for -
USER: What, marry, may she?
BOT: What, marry, may she? Marry with a king, A bachelor, and a handsome stripling too. Iwis your grandam had a worser match.
USER: Thy honour, state, and seat, is due to me.
BOT: Thou killed'st my husband Henry in the Tower, And Edward, my poor son, at Tewksbury.
USER: Ere you were queen, ay, or your husband king, I was a pack-horse in his great affairs; A weeder-out of his proud adversaries, A liberal rewarder of his friends. To royalize his blood, I spilt mine own.
BOT: Poor Clarence did forsake his father Warwick, Ay, and forswore himself - which Jesu pardon! -
USER: To fight on Edward's party for the crown; And for his meed, poor lord, he is mewed up. I would to God my heart were flint, like Edward's, Or Edward's soft and pitiful, like mine. I am too childish-foolish for this world.
BOT: Thou cacodemon! There thy kingdom is.
USER: My lord of Gloucester, in those busy days Which here you urge to prove us enemies, We followed then our lord, our sovereign king. So should we you, if you should be our king.
BOT: If I should be! I had rather be a pedler. Far be it from my heart, the thought thereof.
USER: As little joy, my lord, as you suppose You should enjoy, were you this country's king, As little joy you may suppose in me That I enjoy, being the Queen thereof.
BOT: Foul wrinkled witch, what mak'st thou in my sight?
USER: But repetition of what thou hast marred. That will I make before I let thee go.
BOT: Wert thou not banished on pain of death?
USER: So just is God, to right the innocent.
BOT: O, 'twas the foulest deed to slay that babe, And the most merciless that e'er was heard of.
USER: Tyrants themselves wept when it was reported.
BOT: No man but prophesied revenge for it.
USER: Northumberland, then present, wept to see it.
BOT: Have done thy charm, thou hateful withered hag.
USER: Margaret.
BOT: Richard!
USER: Ha?
BOT: I call thee not.
USER: I cry thee mercy then, for I did think That thou hadst called me all these bitter names.
BOT: Why, so I did, but looked for no reply. O, let me make the period to my curse!
USER: 'Tis done by me, and ends in "Margaret".
BOT: Thus have you breathed your curse against yourself.
USER: False-boding woman, end thy frantic curse, Lest to thy harm thou move our patience.
BOT: Foul shame upon you, you have all moved mine.
USER: Were you well served, you would be taught your duty.
BOT: To serve me well, you all should do me duty: Teach me to be your queen, and you my subjects. O, serve me well, and teach yourselves that duty!
USER: Dispute not with her; she is lunatic.
BOT: Good counsel, marry. Learn it, learn it, Marquess.
USER: It touches you, my lord, as much as me.
BOT: Ay, and much more; but I was born so high. Our aery buildeth in the cedar's top, And dallies with the wind, and scorns the sun.
USER: Peace, peace, for shame, if not for charity.
BOT: Urge neither charity nor shame to me. Uncharitably with me have you dealt, And shamefully my hopes by you are butchered. My charity is outrage, life my shame, And in that shame still live my sorrow's rage.
USER: Have done, have done.
BOT: O princely Buckingham, I'll kiss thy hand In sign of league and amity with thee. Now fair befall thee and thy noble house! Thy garments are not spotted with our blood, Nor thou within the compass of my curse.
USER: Nor no one here, for curses never pass The lips of those that breathe them in the air.
BOT: What doth she say, my lord of Buckingham?
USER: Nothing that I respect, my gracious lord.
BOT: My hair doth stand on end to hear her curses.
USER: And so doth mine. I muse why she's at liberty.
BOT: I cannot blame her. By God's holy mother, She hath had too much wrong; and I repent My part thereof that I have done to her.
USER: I never did her any, to my knowledge.
BOT: A virtuous and a Christian-like conclusion, To pray for them that have done scathe to us.
USER: So do I ever - (_Speaks to himself_) being well advised; For had I cursed now, I had cursed myself.
BOT: Madam, his Majesty doth call for you, And for your Grace, and you, my gracious lords.
USER: Catesby, I come. Lords, will you go with me?
BOT: We wait upon your Grace.
USER: We are, my lord, and come to have the warrant, That we may be admitted where he is.
BOT: Tut, tut, my lord, we will not stand to prate. Talkers are no good doers. Be assured We go to use our hands, and not our tongues.
USER: Your eyes drop millstones when fools' eyes fall tears. I like you, lads. About your business straight. Go, go, dispatch.
BOT: We will, my noble lord.

USER: Why looks your Grace so heavily today?
BOT: What was your dream, my lord? I pray you tell me.
USER: Had you such leisure in the time of death To gaze upon these secrets of the deep?
BOT: Awaked you not in this sore agony?
USER: No marvel, lord, though it affrighted you; I am afraid, methinks, to hear you tell it.
BOT: I will, my lord; God give your Grace good rest.
USER: Ho, who's here?
BOT: What wouldst thou, fellow? And how cam'st thou hither?
USER: I would speak with Clarence, and I came hither on my legs.
BOT: What, so brief?
USER: 'Tis better, sir, than to be tedious. Let him see our commission, and talk no more.
BOT: You may, sir; 'tis a point of wisdom. Fare you well.
USER: What, shall I stab him as he sleeps?
BOT: No. He'll say 'twas done cowardly, when he wakes.
USER: Why, he shall never wake until the great Judgement Day.
BOT: Why, then he'll say we stabbed him sleeping.
USER: The urging of that word "judgement" hath bred a kind of remorse in me.
BOT: What, art thou afraid?
USER: Not to kill him, having a warrant, but to be damned for killing him, from the which no warrant can defend me.
BOT: I thought thou hadst been resolute.
USER: So I am - to let him live.
BOT: I'll back to the Duke of Gloucester and tell him so.
USER: Nay, I prithee stay a little. I hope this passionate humour will change. It was wont to hold me but while one tells twenty.
BOT: How dost thou feel thyself now?
USER: Faith, some certain dregs of conscience are yet within me.
BOT: Remember our reward, when the deed's done.
USER: Zounds, he dies! I had forgot the reward.
BOT: Where's thy conscience now?
USER: O, in the Duke of Gloucester's purse.
BOT: So, when he opens his purse to give us our reward, thy conscience flies out.
USER: 'Tis no matter; let it go. There's few or none will entertain it.
BOT: What if it come to thee again?
USER: Zounds, 'tis even now at my elbow, persuading me not to kill the Duke.
BOT: Take the devil in thy mind, and believe him not. He would insinuate with thee but to make thee sigh.
USER: I am strong-framed; he cannot prevail with me.
BOT: Spoke like a tall man that respects thy reputation. Come, shall we fall to work?
USER: Take him on the costard with the hilts of thy sword, and then throw him in the malmsey-butt in the next room.
BOT: O excellent device - and make a sop of him.
USER: Soft, he wakes.
BOT: Strike!
USER: No, we'll reason with him.
BOT: Where art thou, keeper? Give me a cup of wine.
USER: You shall have wine enough, my lord, anon.
BOT: In God's name, what art thou?
USER: A man, as you are.
BOT: But not as I am, royal.
USER: Nor you as we are, loyal.
BOT: Thy voice is thunder, but thy looks are humble.
USER: My voice is now the King's, my looks mine own.
BOT: How darkly and how deadly dost thou speak! Your eyes do menace me; why look you pale? Who sent you hither? Wherefore do you come?
USER: To, to, to -
BOT: To murder me?
USER: Ay, ay.
BOT: You scarcely have the hearts to tell me so, And therefore cannot have the hearts to do it. Wherein, my friends, have I offended you?
USER: Offended us you have not, but the King.
BOT: I shall be reconciled to him again.
USER: Never, my lord; therefore prepare to die.
BOT: What we will do, we do upon command.
USER: And he that hath commanded is our King.
BOT: And that same vengeance doth He hurl on thee For false forswearing, and for murder too. Thou didst receive the sacrament to fight In quarrel of the house of Lancaster.
USER: And like a traitor to the name of God Didst break that vow, and with thy treacherous blade Unrippedst the bowels of thy sovereign's son.
BOT: Whom thou wast sworn to cherish and defend.
USER: How canst thou urge God's dreadful law to us, When thou hast broke it in such dear degree?
BOT: Who made thee then a bloody minister When gallant-springing, brave Plantagenet, That princely novice, was struck dead by thee?
USER: My brother's love, the devil, and my rage.
BOT: Thy brother's love, our duty, and thy faults, Provoke us hither now to slaughter thee.
USER: You are deceived. Your brother Gloucester hates you.
BOT: O no, he loves me, and he holds me dear. Go you to him from me.
USER: Ay, so we will.
BOT: Ay, millstones, as he lessoned us to weep.
USER: O, do not slander him, for he is kind.
BOT: Right, as snow in harvest. Come, you deceive yourself. 'Tis he that sends us to destroy you here.
USER: It cannot be, for he bewept my fortune, And hugged me in his arms, and swore with sobs That he would labour my delivery.
BOT: Why, so he doth, when he delivers you From this earth's thraldom to the joys of heaven.
USER: Make peace with God, for you must die, my lord.
BOT: What shall we do?
USER: Relent, and save your souls.
BOT: Relent? No, 'tis cowardly and womanish.
USER: Look behind you, my lord.
BOT: Take that, and that! [_Stabs him._] If all this will not do, I'll drown you in the malmsey-butt within.
USER: A bloody deed, and desperately dispatched. How fain, like Pilate, would I wash my hands Of this most grievous murder.
BOT: How now? What mean'st thou that thou help'st me not? By heavens, the Duke shall know how slack you have been.
USER: I would he knew that I had saved his brother. Take thou the fee, and tell him what I say, For I repent me that the Duke is slain.
BOT: So do not I. Go, coward as thou art. Well, I'll go hide the body in some hole Till that the Duke give order for his burial. And when I have my meed, I will away, For this will out, and then I must not stay.

USER: By heaven, my soul is purged from grudging hate, And with my hand I seal my true heart's love.
BOT: So thrive I, as I truly swear the like.
USER: Take heed you dally not before your King, Lest He that is the supreme King of kings Confound your hidden falsehood, and award Either of you to be the other's end.
BOT: So prosper I, as I swear perfect love.
USER: And I, as I love Hastings with my heart.
BOT: Madam, yourself is not exempt from this; Nor you, son Dorset; Buckingham, nor you. You have been factious one against the other. Wife, love Lord Hastings, let him kiss your hand, And what you do, do it unfeignedly.
USER: There, Hastings, I will never more remember Our former hatred, so thrive I and mine.
BOT: Dorset, embrace him; Hastings, love lord Marquess.
USER: This interchange of love, I here protest, Upon my part shall be inviolable.
BOT: And so swear I.
USER: Now, princely Buckingham, seal thou this league With thy embracements to my wife's allies, And make me happy in your unity.
BOT: A pleasing cordial, princely Buckingham, Is this thy vow unto my sickly heart. There wanteth now our brother Gloucester here, To make the blessed period of this peace.
USER: And in good time, Here comes Sir Ratcliffe and the Duke.
BOT: Good morrow to my sovereign King and Queen; And, princely peers, a happy time of day.
USER: Happy indeed, as we have spent the day. Gloucester, we have done deeds of charity, Made peace of enmity, fair love of hate, Between these swelling wrong-incensed peers.
BOT: A holy day shall this be kept hereafter. I would to God all strifes were well compounded. My sovereign lord, I do beseech your Highness To take our brother Clarence to your grace.
USER: Why, madam, have I offered love for this, To be so flouted in this royal presence? Who knows not that the gentle Duke is dead? You do him injury to scorn his corse.
BOT: Who knows not he is dead! Who knows he is?
USER: All-seeing heaven, what a world is this!
BOT: Look I so pale, Lord Dorset, as the rest?
USER: Ay, my good lord, and no man in the presence But his red colour hath forsook his cheeks.
BOT: Is Clarence dead? The order was reversed.
USER: A boon, my sovereign, for my service done!
BOT: I prithee, peace. My soul is full of sorrow.
USER: I will not rise unless your Highness hear me.
BOT: Then say at once what is it thou requests.
USER: The forfeit, sovereign, of my servant's life Who slew today a riotous gentleman Lately attendant on the Duke of Norfolk.
BOT: We wait upon your Grace.

USER: Good grandam, tell us, is our father dead?
BOT: No, boy.
USER: Why do you weep so oft, and beat your breast, And cry "O Clarence, my unhappy son"?
BOT: Why do you look on us, and shake your head, And call us orphans, wretches, castaways, If that our noble father were alive?
USER: My pretty cousins, you mistake me both. I do lament the sickness of the King, As loath to lose him, not your father's death. It were lost sorrow to wail one that's lost.
BOT: Then you conclude, my grandam, he is dead. The King mine uncle is to blame for it. God will revenge it, whom I will importune With earnest prayers all to that effect.
USER: And so will I.
BOT: Peace, children, peace. The King doth love you well. Incapable and shallow innocents, You cannot guess who caused your father's death.
USER: Ah, that deceit should steal such gentle shape, And with a virtuous visard hide deep vice! He is my son, ay, and therein my shame; Yet from my dugs he drew not this deceit.
BOT: Think you my uncle did dissemble, grandam?
USER: Ay, boy.
BOT: I cannot think it. Hark, what noise is this? after her.
USER: Ah, who shall hinder me to wail and weep, To chide my fortune, and torment myself? I'll join with black despair against my soul And to myself become an enemy.
BOT: What means this scene of rude impatience?
USER: Ah, aunt, you wept not for our father's death. How can we aid you with our kindred tears?
BOT: Our fatherless distress was left unmoaned. Your widow-dolour likewise be unwept!
USER: Ah for our father, for our dear Lord Clarence!
BOT: Alas for both, both mine, Edward and Clarence!
USER: What stay had I but Edward? And he's gone.
BOT: What stay had we but Clarence? And he's gone.
USER: What stays had I but they? And they are gone.
BOT: Was never widow had so dear a loss.
USER: Were never orphans had so dear a loss.
BOT: God bless thee, and put meekness in thy breast, Love, charity, obedience, and true duty.
USER: Amen. [_Aside_.] And make me die a good old man! That is the butt end of a mother's blessing; I marvel that her Grace did leave it out.
BOT: Why with some little train, my Lord of Buckingham?
USER: I hope the King made peace with all of us; And the compact is firm and true in me.
BOT: And so say I.
USER: Then be it so, and go we to determine Who they shall be that straight shall post to Ludlow. Madam, and you, my sister, will you go To give your censures in this business?
BOT: My lord, whoever journeys to the Prince, For God's sake, let not us two stay at home. For by the way I'll sort occasion, As index to the story we late talked of, To part the Queen's proud kindred from the Prince.
USER: My other self, my counsel's consistory, My oracle, my prophet, my dear cousin, I, as a child, will go by thy direction. Toward Ludlow then, for we'll not stay behind.

USER: Good morrow, neighbour, whither away so fast?
BOT: I promise you, I scarcely know myself. Hear you the news abroad?
USER: Yes, that the King is dead.
BOT: Ill news, by'r Lady; seldom comes the better. I fear, I fear 'twill prove a giddy world.
USER: Neighbours, God speed.
BOT: Give you good morrow, sir.
USER: Doth the news hold of good King Edward's death?
BOT: Ay, sir, it is too true, God help the while.
USER: Then, masters, look to see a troublous world.
BOT: No, no; by God's good grace, his son shall reign.
USER: Woe to that land that's governed by a child.
BOT: In him there is a hope of government, Which, in his nonage, council under him, And, in his full and ripened years, himself, No doubt shall then, and till then, govern well.
USER: So stood the state when Henry the Sixth Was crowned in Paris but at nine months old.
BOT: Stood the state so? No, no, good friends, God wot. For then this land was famously enriched With politic grave counsel; then the King Had virtuous uncles to protect his Grace.
USER: Why, so hath this, both by his father and mother.
BOT: Come, come, we fear the worst; all will be well.
USER: Truly, the hearts of men are full of fear. You cannot reason almost with a man That looks not heavily and full of dread.
BOT: Before the days of change, still is it so. By a divine instinct men's minds mistrust Ensuing danger, as by proof we see The water swell before a boist'rous storm. But leave it all to God. Whither away?
USER: Marry, we were sent for to the Justices.
BOT: And so was I. I'll bear you company.

USER: Last night, I hear, they lay at Stony Stratford, And at Northampton they do rest tonight. Tomorrow or next day they will be here.
BOT: I long with all my heart to see the Prince. I hope he is much grown since last I saw him.
USER: But I hear no; they say my son of York Has almost overta'en him in his growth.
BOT: Ay, mother, but I would not have it so.
USER: Why, my good cousin? It is good to grow.
BOT: And so no doubt he is, my gracious madam.
USER: I hope he is, but yet let mothers doubt.
BOT: Now, by my troth, if I had been remembered, I could have given my uncle's Grace a flout To touch his growth nearer than he touched mine.
USER: How, my young York? I prithee let me hear it.
BOT: Marry, they say my uncle grew so fast That he could gnaw a crust at two hours old. 'Twas full two years ere I could get a tooth. Grandam, this would have been a biting jest.
USER: I prithee, pretty York, who told thee this?
BOT: Grandam, his nurse.
USER: His nurse? Why she was dead ere thou wast born.
BOT: If 'twere not she, I cannot tell who told me.
USER: A parlous boy! Go to, you are too shrewd.
BOT: Good madam, be not angry with the child.
USER: Pitchers have ears.
BOT: Here comes a messenger. What news?
USER: Such news, my lord, as grieves me to report.
BOT: How doth the Prince?
USER: Well, madam, and in health.
BOT: What is thy news?
USER: Lord Rivers and Lord Grey are sent to Pomfret, And, with them Sir Thomas Vaughan, prisoners.
BOT: Who hath committed them?
USER: The mighty Dukes, Gloucester and Buckingham.
BOT: For what offence?
USER: The sum of all I can, I have disclosed. Why or for what the nobles were committed Is all unknown to me, my gracious lord.
BOT: Come, come, my boy. We will to sanctuary. Madam, farewell.
USER: Stay, I will go with you.
BOT: You have no cause.
USER: And thither bear your treasure and your goods. For my part, I'll resign unto your Grace The seal I keep; and so betide to me As well I tender you and all of yours. Go, I'll conduct you to the sanctuary.

USER: Welcome, sweet Prince, to London, to your chamber.
BOT: Welcome, dear cousin, my thoughts' sovereign. The weary way hath made you melancholy.
USER: No, uncle, but our crosses on the way Have made it tedious, wearisome, and heavy. I want more uncles here to welcome me.
BOT: God keep me from false friends, but they were none.
USER: My lord, the Mayor of London comes to greet you.
BOT: God bless your Grace with health and happy days!
USER: I thank you, good my lord, and thank you all. I thought my mother and my brother York Would long ere this have met us on the way. Fie, what a slug is Hastings, that he comes not To tell us whether they will come or no!
BOT: And in good time, here comes the sweating lord.
USER: Welcome, my lord. What, will our mother come?
BOT: On what occasion God He knows, not I, The Queen your mother and your brother York Have taken sanctuary. The tender prince Would fain have come with me to meet your Grace, But by his mother was perforce withheld.
USER: My lord, you shall o'errule my mind for once. Come on, Lord Hastings, will you go with me?
BOT: I go, my lord.
USER: Good lords, make all the speedy haste you may. Say, uncle Gloucester, if our brother come, Where shall we sojourn till our coronation?
BOT: Where it seems best unto your royal self. If I may counsel you, some day or two Your Highness shall repose you at the Tower, Then where you please and shall be thought most fit For your best health and recreation.
USER: I do not like the Tower, of any place. Did Julius Caesar build that place, my lord?
BOT: He did, my gracious lord, begin that place, Which, since, succeeding ages have re-edified.
USER: Is it upon record, or else reported Successively from age to age, he built it?
BOT: Upon record, my gracious lord.
USER: But say, my lord, it were not registered, Methinks the truth should live from age to age, As 'twere retailed to all posterity, Even to the general all-ending day.
BOT: What say you, uncle?
USER: I say, without characters, fame lives long. I moralize two meanings in one word.
BOT: What, my gracious lord?
USER: An if I live until I be a man, I'll win our ancient right in France again, Or die a soldier, as I lived a king.
BOT: Now, in good time here comes the Duke of York.
USER: Richard of York, how fares our loving brother?
BOT: Well, my dread lord - so must I call you now.
USER: Ay brother, to our grief, as it is yours. Too late he died that might have kept that title, Which by his death hath lost much majesty.
BOT: How fares our cousin, noble lord of York?
USER: I thank you, gentle uncle. O, my lord, You said that idle weeds are fast in growth. The Prince my brother hath outgrown me far.
BOT: He hath, my lord.
USER: And therefore is he idle?
BOT: O, my fair cousin, I must not say so.
USER: Then he is more beholding to you than I.
BOT: He may command me as my sovereign, But you have power in me as in a kinsman.
USER: I pray you, uncle, give me this dagger.
BOT: My dagger, little cousin? With all my heart.
USER: A beggar, brother?
BOT: Of my kind uncle, that I know will give, And being but a toy, which is no grief to give.
USER: A greater gift than that I'll give my cousin.
BOT: A greater gift? O, that's the sword to it.
USER: Ay, gentle cousin, were it light enough.
BOT: O, then I see you will part but with light gifts; In weightier things you'll say a beggar nay.
USER: It is too heavy for your Grace to wear.
BOT: I weigh it lightly, were it heavier.
USER: What, would you have my weapon, little lord?
BOT: I would, that I might thank you as you call me.
USER: How?
BOT: Little.
USER: My lord of York will still be cross in talk. Uncle, your Grace knows how to bear with him.
BOT: You mean, to bear me, not to bear with me. Uncle, my brother mocks both you and me. Because that I am little, like an ape, He thinks that you should bear me on your shoulders.
USER: With what a sharp-provided wit he reasons! To mitigate the scorn he gives his uncle, He prettily and aptly taunts himself. So cunning and so young is wonderful.
BOT: My lord, wil't please you pass along? Myself and my good cousin Buckingham Will to your mother, to entreat of her To meet you at the Tower and welcome you.
USER: What, will you go unto the Tower, my lord?
BOT: My Lord Protector needs will have it so.
USER: I shall not sleep in quiet at the Tower.
BOT: Why, what should you fear?
USER: Marry, my uncle Clarence' angry ghost. My grandam told me he was murdered there.
BOT: I fear no uncles dead.
USER: Nor none that live, I hope.
BOT: An if they live, I hope I need not fear. But come, my lord. With a heavy heart, Thinking on them, go I unto the Tower. Richard, Buckingham and Catesby._]
USER: Think you, my lord, this little prating York Was not incensed by his subtle mother To taunt and scorn you thus opprobriously?
BOT: No doubt, no doubt. O, 'tis a parlous boy, Bold, quick, ingenious, forward, capable. He is all the mother's, from the top to toe.
USER: He for his father's sake so loves the Prince That he will not be won to aught against him.
BOT: What think'st thou then of Stanley? Will not he?
USER: He will do all in all as Hastings doth.
BOT: Commend me to Lord William. Tell him, Catesby, His ancient knot of dangerous adversaries Tomorrow are let blood at Pomfret Castle, And bid my lord, for joy of this good news, Give Mistress Shore one gentle kiss the more.
USER: Good Catesby, go effect this business soundly.
BOT: My good lords both, with all the heed I can.
USER: Shall we hear from you, Catesby, ere we sleep?
BOT: You shall, my lord.
USER: At Crosby Place, there shall you find us both.
BOT: Now, my lord, what shall we do if we perceive Lord Hastings will not yield to our complots?
USER: Chop off his head, man; somewhat we will do. And look when I am king, claim thou of me The earldom of Hereford, and all the movables Whereof the King my brother was possessed.
BOT: I'll claim that promise at your Grace's hand.
USER: And look to have it yielded with all kindness. Come, let us sup betimes, that afterwards We may digest our complots in some form.

USER: My lord, my lord!
BOT: One from the Lord Stanley.
USER: Upon the stroke of four.
BOT: Cannot my Lord Stanley sleep these tedious nights?
USER: So it appears by that I have to say. First, he commends him to your noble self.
BOT: What then?
USER: I'll go, my lord, and tell him what you say.
BOT: Many good morrows to my noble lord.
USER: Good morrow, Catesby; you are early stirring. What news, what news in this our tott'ring state?
BOT: It is a reeling world indeed, my lord, And I believe will never stand upright Till Richard wear the garland of the realm.
USER: How, wear the garland? Dost thou mean the crown?
BOT: Ay, my good lord.
USER: I'll have this crown of mine cut from my shoulders Before I'll see the crown so foul misplaced. But canst thou guess that he doth aim at it?
BOT: Ay, on my life, and hopes to find you forward Upon his party for the gain thereof; And thereupon he sends you this good news, That this same very day your enemies, The kindred of the Queen, must die at Pomfret.
USER: Indeed, I am no mourner for that news, Because they have been still my adversaries. But that I'll give my voice on Richard's side To bar my master's heirs in true descent, God knows I will not do it, to the death.
BOT: God keep your lordship in that gracious mind.
USER: 'Tis a vile thing to die, my gracious lord, When men are unprepared and look not for it.
BOT: The Princes both make high account of you -
USER: I know they do, and I have well deserved it. Come on, come on. Where is your boar-spear, man? Fear you the boar, and go so unprovided?
BOT: My lord, good morrow; good morrow, Catesby. You may jest on, but, by the Holy Rood, I do not like these several councils, I.
USER: My lord, I hold my life as dear as you do yours, And never in my days, I do protest, Was it so precious to me as 'tis now. Think you, but that I know our state secure, I would be so triumphant as I am?
BOT: Come, come. Have with you. Wot you what, my lord? Today the lords you talked of are beheaded.
USER: They, for their truth, might better wear their heads Than some that have accused them wear their hats. But come, my lord, let's away.
BOT: Go on before; I'll talk with this good fellow. How now, sirrah? How goes the world with thee?
USER: The better that your lordship please to ask.
BOT: God hold it, to your honour's good content!
USER: Gramercy, fellow. There, drink that for me.
BOT: I thank your honour.
USER: Well met, my lord; I am glad to see your honour.
BOT: I thank thee, good Sir John, with all my heart. I am in your debt for your last exercise. Come the next sabbath, and I will content you.
USER: I'll wait upon your lordship.
BOT: What, talking with a priest, Lord Chamberlain? Your friends at Pomfret, they do need the priest; Your honour hath no shriving work in hand.
USER: Good faith, and when I met this holy man, The men you talk of came into my mind. What, go you toward the Tower?
BOT: I do, my lord, but long I cannot stay there. I shall return before your lordship thence.
USER: Nay, like enough, for I stay dinner there.
BOT: Come, will you go?
USER: I'll wait upon your lordship.

USER: Sir Richard Ratcliffe, let me tell thee this: Today shalt thou behold a subject die For truth, for duty, and for loyalty.
BOT: God bless the Prince from all the pack of you! A knot you are of damned bloodsuckers. VAUGHAN You live that shall cry woe for this hereafter. RATCLIFFE Dispatch. The limit of your lives is out.
USER: Now Margaret's curse is fall'n upon our heads, When she exclaimed on Hastings, you, and I, For standing by when Richard stabbed her son.
BOT: Make haste. The hour of death is expiate.
USER: Come, Grey, come, Vaughan, let us here embrace. Farewell, until we meet again in heaven.

USER: Now, noble peers, the cause why we are met Is to determine of the coronation. In God's name speak. When is the royal day?
BOT: Is all things ready for that royal time?
USER: It is, and wants but nomination.
BOT: Tomorrow, then, I judge a happy day.
USER: Who knows the Lord Protector's mind herein? Who is most inward with the noble Duke?
BOT: Your Grace, we think, should soonest know his mind.
USER: We know each other's faces; for our hearts, He knows no more of mine than I of yours, Or I of his, my lord, than you of mine. Lord Hastings, you and he are near in love.
BOT: In happy time, here comes the Duke himself.
USER: My noble lords and cousins all, good morrow. I have been long a sleeper; but I trust My absence doth neglect no great design Which by my presence might have been concluded.
BOT: Had you not come upon your cue, my lord, William Lord Hastings had pronounced your part - I mean your voice for crowning of the King.
USER: Marry, and will, my lord, with all my heart.
BOT: Withdraw yourself awhile. I'll go with you.
USER: We have not yet set down this day of triumph. Tomorrow, in my judgement, is too sudden, For I myself am not so well provided As else I would be, were the day prolonged.
BOT: Where is my lord the Duke of Gloucester? I have sent for these strawberries.
USER: What of his heart perceive you in his face By any livelihood he showed today?
BOT: Marry, that with no man here he is offended, For were he, he had shown it in his looks.
USER: I pray you all, tell me what they deserve That do conspire my death with devilish plots Of damned witchcraft, and that have prevailed Upon my body with their hellish charms?
BOT: The tender love I bear your Grace, my lord, Makes me most forward in this princely presence To doom th' offenders, whosoe'er they be. I say, my lord, they have deserved death.
USER: If they have done this deed, my noble lord -
BOT: Come, come, dispatch. The Duke would be at dinner: Make a short shrift. He longs to see your head.
USER: Come, come, dispatch. 'Tis bootless to exclaim.
BOT: O bloody Richard! Miserable England, I prophesy the fearfull'st time to thee That ever wretched age hath looked upon. Come, lead me to the block. Bear him my head. They smile at me who shortly shall be dead.

USER: Come, cousin, canst thou quake and change thy colour, Murder thy breath in middle of a word, And then again begin, and stop again, As if thou were distraught and mad with terror?
BOT: He is; and, see, he brings the Mayor along.
USER: Lord Mayor -
BOT: Look to the drawbridge there!
USER: Hark, a drum.
BOT: Catesby, o'erlook the walls.
USER: Lord Mayor, the reason we have sent -
BOT: Look back! Defend thee, here are enemies.
USER: God and our innocence defend and guard us!
BOT: Be patient, they are friends, Ratcliffe and Lovell.
USER: Here is the head of that ignoble traitor, The dangerous and unsuspected Hastings.
BOT: Had he done so?
USER: Now, fair befall you! He deserved his death, And your good Graces both have well proceeded, To warn false traitors from the like attempts.
BOT: But, my good lord, your Grace's word shall serve As well as I had seen and heard him speak; And do not doubt, right noble princes both, But I'll acquaint our duteous citizens With all your just proceedings in this case.
USER: And to that end we wished your lordship here, T' avoid the censures of the carping world.
BOT: But since you come too late of our intent, Yet witness what you hear we did intend. And so, my good Lord Mayor, we bid farewell.
USER: Doubt not, my lord, I'll play the orator As if the golden fee for which I plead Were for myself. And so, my lord, adieu.
BOT: If you thrive well, bring them to Baynard's Castle, Where you shall find me well accompanied With reverend fathers and well-learned bishops.
USER: I go; and towards three or four o'clock Look for the news that the Guildhall affords.

USER: How now, how now? What say the citizens?
BOT: Now, by the holy mother of our Lord, The citizens are mum, say not a word.
USER: Touched you the bastardy of Edward's children?
BOT: And did they so?
USER: What, tongueless blocks were they! Would they not speak? Will not the Mayor then and his brethren, come?
BOT: I go, and if you plead as well for them As I can say nay to thee for myself, No doubt we bring it to a happy issue.
USER: Go, go, up to the leads, the Lord Mayor knocks. Welcome, my lord. I dance attendance here. I think the Duke will not be spoke withal. Now, Catesby, what says your lord to my request?
BOT: Return, good Catesby, to the gracious Duke; Tell him myself, the Mayor and aldermen, In deep designs, in matter of great moment, No less importing than our general good, Are come to have some conference with his Grace.
USER: I'll signify so much unto him straight.
BOT: Marry, God defend his Grace should say us nay!
USER: I fear he will. Here Catesby comes again. Now, Catesby, what says his Grace?
BOT: He wonders to what end you have assembled Such troops of citizens to come to him, His Grace not being warned thereof before. He fears, my lord, you mean no good to him.
USER: See where his Grace stands 'tween two clergymen!
BOT: My lord, there needs no such apology. I do beseech your Grace to pardon me, Who, earnest in the service of my God, Deferred the visitation of my friends. But, leaving this, what is your Grace's pleasure?
USER: Even that, I hope, which pleaseth God above, And all good men of this ungoverned isle.
BOT: I do suspect I have done some offence That seems disgracious in the city's eye, And that you come to reprehend my ignorance.
USER: You have, my lord. Would it might please your Grace, On our entreaties, to amend your fault.
BOT: Else wherefore breathe I in a Christian land?
USER: Do, good my lord. Your citizens entreat you.
BOT: Refuse not, mighty lord, this proffered love.
USER: O, make them joyful; grant their lawful suit.
BOT: Alas, why would you heap those cares on me? I am unfit for state and majesty. I do beseech you, take it not amiss; I cannot, nor I will not, yield to you.
USER: Call him again, sweet Prince; accept their suit. If you deny them, all the land will rue it.
BOT: God bless your Grace! We see it, and will say it.
USER: In saying so, you shall but say the truth.
BOT: Then I salute you with this royal title: Long live King Richard, England's worthy King!
USER: Amen.
BOT: Tomorrow may it please you to be crowned?
USER: Even when you please, for you will have it so.
BOT: Tomorrow, then, we will attend your Grace; And so most joyfully we take our leave.
USER: Farewell, my cousin, farewell, gentle friends.

USER: Who meets us here? My niece Plantagenet Led in the hand of her kind aunt of Gloucester? Now, for my life, she's wandering to the Tower, On pure heart's love, to greet the tender Prince. Daughter, well met.
BOT: God give your Graces both A happy and a joyful time of day.
USER: As much to you, good sister. Whither away?
BOT: No farther than the Tower, and, as I guess, Upon the like devotion as yourselves, To gratulate the gentle Princes there.
USER: Kind sister, thanks; we'll enter all together. And in good time, here the Lieutenant comes. Master Lieutenant, pray you, by your leave, How doth the Prince and my young son of York?
BOT: Right well, dear madam. By your patience, I may not suffer you to visit them. The King hath strictly charged the contrary.
USER: The King? Who's that?
BOT: I mean the Lord Protector.
USER: The Lord protect him from that kingly title! Hath he set bounds between their love and me? I am their mother; who shall bar me from them?
BOT: I am their father's mother. I will see them.
USER: Their aunt I am in law, in love their mother. Then bring me to their sights. I'll bear thy blame, And take thy office from thee, on my peril.
BOT: No, madam, no. I may not leave it so. I am bound by oath, and therefore pardon me.
USER: Let me but meet you, ladies, one hour hence, And I'll salute your Grace of York as mother And reverend looker-on of two fair queens. There to be crowned Richard's royal queen.
BOT: Ah, cut my lace asunder That my pent heart may have some scope to beat, Or else I swoon with this dead-killing news!
USER: Despiteful tidings! O unpleasing news!
BOT: Be of good cheer, mother. How fares your Grace?
USER: Full of wise care is this your counsel, madam. Take all the swift advantage of the hours; You shall have letters from me to my son In your behalf, to meet you on the way. Be not ta'en tardy by unwise delay.
BOT: O ill-dispersing wind of misery! O my accursed womb, the bed of death! A cockatrice hast thou hatched to the world, Whose unavoided eye is murderous.
USER: Come, madam, come. I in all haste was sent.
BOT: Go, go, poor soul; I envy not thy glory. To feed my humour, wish thyself no harm.
USER: Poor heart, adieu; I pity thy complaining.
BOT: No more than with my soul I mourn for yours.
USER: Farewell, thou woeful welcomer of glory.
BOT: Adieu, poor soul, that tak'st thy leave of it.
USER: thee. I to my grave, where peace and rest lie with me. Eighty odd years of sorrow have I seen, And each hour's joy wracked with a week of teen.

USER: Stand all apart. Cousin of Buckingham!
BOT: My gracious sovereign!
USER: Give me thy hand. Thus high, by thy advice And thy assistance is King Richard seated. But shall we wear these glories for a day, Or shall they last, and we rejoice in them?
BOT: Still live they, and for ever let them last!
USER: Ah, Buckingham, now do I play the touch, To try if thou be current gold indeed. Young Edward lives; think now what I would speak.
BOT: Say on, my loving lord.
USER: Why, Buckingham, I say I would be King.
BOT: Why, so you are, my thrice-renowned lord.
USER: Ha! Am I King? 'Tis so - but Edward lives.
BOT: True, noble Prince.
USER: Your Grace may do your pleasure.
BOT: Tut, tut, thou art all ice; thy kindness freezes. Say, have I thy consent that they shall die?
USER: Give me some little breath, some pause, dear lord, Before I positively speak in this. I will resolve you herein presently.
BOT: And unrespective boys; none are for me That look into me with considerate eyes. High-reaching Buckingham grows circumspect. Boy!
USER: My lord?
BOT: Know'st thou not any whom corrupting gold Will tempt unto a close exploit of death?
USER: I know a discontented gentleman Whose humble means match not his haughty spirit. Gold were as good as twenty orators, And will, no doubt, tempt him to anything.
BOT: What is his name?
USER: His name, my lord, is Tyrrel.
BOT: Know, my loving lord, The Marquess Dorset, as I hear, is fled To Richmond, in the parts where he abides.
USER: James Tyrrel, and your most obedient subject.
BOT: Art thou indeed?
USER: Prove me, my gracious lord.
BOT: Dar'st thou resolve to kill a friend of mine?
USER: Please you. But I had rather kill two enemies.
BOT: Why then thou hast it; two deep enemies, Foes to my rest, and my sweet sleep's disturbers, Are they that I would have thee deal upon. Tyrell, I mean those bastards in the Tower.
USER: Let me have open means to come to them, And soon I'll rid you from the fear of them.
BOT: Thou sing'st sweet music. Hark, come hither, Tyrrel. Go, by this token. Rise, and lend thine ear. And I will love thee, and prefer thee for it.
USER: I will dispatch it straight.
BOT: My lord, I have considered in my mind The late request that you did sound me in.
USER: Well, let that rest. Dorset is fled to Richmond.
BOT: I hear the news, my lord.
USER: Stanley, he is your wife's son. Well, look unto it.
BOT: My lord, I claim the gift, my due by promise, For which your honour and your faith is pawned: Th' earldom of Hereford, and the movables Which you have promised I shall possess.
USER: Stanley, look to your wife. If she convey Letters to Richmond, you shall answer it.
BOT: What says your Highness to my just request?
USER: I do remember me, Henry the Sixth Did prophesy that Richmond should be King, When Richmond was a little peevish boy. A king perhaps -
BOT: My lord -
USER: How chance the prophet could not at that time Have told me, I being by, that I should kill him?
BOT: My lord, your promise for the earldom -
USER: Richmond! When last I was at Exeter, The Mayor in courtesy showed me the castle And called it Rougemount, at which name I started, Because a bard of Ireland told me once I should not live long after I saw Richmond.
BOT: My lord -
USER: Ay, what's o'clock?
BOT: I am thus bold to put your Grace in mind Of what you promised me.
USER: Well, but what's o'clock?
BOT: Upon the stroke of ten.
USER: Well, let it strike.
BOT: Why let it strike?
USER: Because that, like a jack, thou keep'st the stroke Betwixt thy begging and my meditation. I am not in the giving vein today.
BOT: Why then, resolve me whether you will or no.
USER: Thou troublest me; I am not in the vein.
BOT: And is it thus? Repays he my deep service With such contempt? Made I him King for this? O, let me think on Hastings, and be gone To Brecknock while my fearful head is on!

USER: Kind Tyrrel, am I happy in thy news?
BOT: If to have done the thing you gave in charge Beget your happiness, be happy then, For it is done.
USER: But didst thou see them dead?
BOT: I did, my lord.
USER: And buried, gentle Tyrrel?
BOT: The chaplain of the Tower hath buried them, But where, to say the truth, I do not know.
USER: Come to me, Tyrrel, soon, at after-supper, When thou shalt tell the process of their death. Meantime, but think how I may do thee good, And be inheritor of thy desire. Farewell till then.
BOT: I humbly take my leave.
USER: My lord!
BOT: Good or bad news, that thou com'st in so bluntly?
USER: Bad news, my lord. Morton is fled to Richmond, And Buckingham, backed with the hardy Welshmen, Is in the field, and still his power increaseth.

USER: Hath dimmed your infant morn to aged night.
BOT: So many miseries have crazed my voice That my woe-wearied tongue is still and mute. Edward Plantagenet, why art thou dead?
USER: Edward for Edward pays a dying debt.
BOT: Wilt thou, O God, fly from such gentle lambs, And throw them in the entrails of the wolf? When didst Thou sleep when such a deed was done?
USER: Dead life, blind sight, poor mortal living ghost, Woe's scene, world's shame, grave's due by life usurped, Brief abstract and record of tedious days, Rest thy unrest on England's lawful earth,
BOT: Ah, that thou wouldst as soon afford a grave As thou canst yield a melancholy seat, Then would I hide my bones, not rest them here.
USER: I had a Richard too, and thou didst kill him; I had a Rutland too; thou holp'st to kill him.
BOT: O Harry's wife, triumph not in my woes! God witness with me, I have wept for thine.
USER: O, thou didst prophesy the time would come That I should wish for thee to help me curse That bottled spider, that foul bunch-backed toad!
BOT: O thou well skilled in curses, stay awhile, And teach me how to curse mine enemies.
USER: My words are dull. O, quicken them with thine!
BOT: Thy woes will make them sharp and pierce like mine.
USER: Why should calamity be full of words?
BOT: Windy attorneys to their clients' woes, Airy succeeders of intestate joys, Poor breathing orators of miseries, Let them have scope, though what they do impart Help nothing else, yet do they ease the heart.
USER: If so, then be not tongue-tied. Go with me, And in the breath of bitter words let's smother My damned son, that thy two sweet sons smothered. The trumpet sounds. Be copious in exclaims.
BOT: Who intercepts me in my expedition?
USER: O, she that might have intercepted thee, By strangling thee in her accursed womb, From all the slaughters, wretch, that thou hast done.
