BOT: Thou toad, thou toad, where is thy brother Clarence, And little Ned Plantagenet his son?
USER: Where is the gentle Rivers, Vaughan, Grey?
BOT: Where is kind Hastings?
USER: Art thou my son?
BOT: Ay, I thank God, my father, and yourself.
USER: Then patiently hear my impatience.
BOT: Madam, I have a touch of your condition, That cannot brook the accent of reproof.
USER: O, let me speak!
BOT: Do then, but I'll not hear.
USER: I will be mild and gentle in my words.
BOT: And brief, good mother, for I am in haste.
USER: Art thou so hasty? I have stayed for thee, God knows, in torment and in agony.
BOT: And came I not at last to comfort you?
USER: Faith, none but Humphrey Hower, that called your Grace To breakfast once, forth of my company. If I be so disgracious in your eye, Let me march on and not offend you, madam. Strike up the drum.
BOT: I prithee, hear me speak.
USER: You speak too bitterly.
BOT: Hear me a word, For I shall never speak to thee again.
USER: So.
BOT: Though far more cause, yet much less spirit to curse Abides in me, I say amen to her.
USER: Stay, madam, I must talk a word with you.
BOT: I have no more sons of the royal blood For thee to slaughter. For my daughters, Richard, They shall be praying nuns, not weeping queens, And therefore level not to hit their lives.
USER: You have a daughter called Elizabeth, Virtuous and fair, royal and gracious.
BOT: Wrong not her birth; she is a royal princess.
USER: To save her life I'll say she is not so.
BOT: Her life is safest only in her birth.
USER: And only in that safety died her brothers.
BOT: Lo, at their births good stars were opposite.
USER: No, to their lives ill friends were contrary.
BOT: All unavoided is the doom of destiny.
USER: True, when avoided grace makes destiny. My babes were destined to a fairer death, If grace had blessed thee with a fairer life.
BOT: You speak as if that I had slain my cousins.
USER: Madam, so thrive I in my enterprise And dangerous success of bloody wars, As I intend more good to you and yours Than ever you or yours by me were harmed!
BOT: What good is covered with the face of heaven, To be discovered, that can do me good?
USER: Th' advancement of your children, gentle lady.
BOT: Up to some scaffold, there to lose their heads.
USER: Unto the dignity and height of fortune, The high imperial type of this earth's glory.
BOT: Flatter my sorrows with report of it. Tell me what state, what dignity, what honour, Canst thou demise to any child of mine?
USER: Even all I have - ay, and myself and all Will I withal endow a child of thine; So in the Lethe of thy angry soul Thou drown the sad remembrance of those wrongs Which thou supposest I have done to thee.
BOT: Be brief, lest that the process of thy kindness Last longer telling than thy kindness' date.
USER: Then know, that from my soul I love thy daughter.
BOT: My daughter's mother thinks it with her soul.
USER: What do you think?
BOT: That thou dost love my daughter from thy soul. So from thy soul's love didst thou love her brothers, And from my heart's love I do thank thee for it.
USER: Be not so hasty to confound my meaning. I mean that with my soul I love thy daughter, And do intend to make her Queen of England.
BOT: Well, then, who dost thou mean shall be her king?
USER: Even he that makes her Queen. Who else should be?
BOT: What, thou?
USER: Even so. How think you of it?
BOT: How canst thou woo her?
USER: That would I learn of you, As one being best acquainted with her humour.
BOT: And wilt thou learn of me?
USER: Madam, with all my heart.
BOT: You mock me, madam; this is not the way To win your daughter.
USER: There is no other way, Unless thou couldst put on some other shape, And not be Richard, that hath done all this.
BOT: Say that I did all this for love of her?
USER: Nay, then indeed she cannot choose but hate thee, Having bought love with such a bloody spoil.
BOT: Infer fair England's peace by this alliance.
USER: Which she shall purchase with still-lasting war.
BOT: Tell her the King, that may command, entreats.
USER: That at her hands, which the King's King forbids.
BOT: Say she shall be a high and mighty queen.
USER: To vail the title, as her mother doth.
BOT: Say I will love her everlastingly.
USER: But how long shall that title "ever" last?
BOT: Sweetly in force unto her fair life's end.
USER: But how long fairly shall her sweet life last?
BOT: As long as heaven and nature lengthens it.
USER: As long as hell and Richard likes of it.
BOT: Say I, her sovereign, am her subject low.
USER: But she, your subject, loathes such sovereignty.
BOT: Be eloquent in my behalf to her.
USER: An honest tale speeds best being plainly told.
BOT: Then plainly to her tell my loving tale.
USER: Plain and not honest is too harsh a style.
BOT: Your reasons are too shallow and too quick.
USER: O no, my reasons are too deep and dead - Too deep and dead, poor infants, in their graves.
BOT: Harp not on that string, madam; that is past.
USER: Harp on it still shall I till heart-strings break.
BOT: Now, by my George, my Garter, and my crown -
USER: Profaned, dishonoured, and the third usurped.
BOT: I swear -
USER: Now, by the world -
BOT: 'Tis full of thy foul wrongs.
USER: My father's death -
BOT: Thy life hath that dishonoured.
USER: Then, by myself -
BOT: Thyself is self-misused.
USER: Why, then, by God -
BOT: The time to come.
USER: Shall I be tempted of the devil thus?
BOT: Ay, if the devil tempt you to do good.
USER: Shall I forget myself to be myself?
BOT: Ay, if your self's remembrance wrong yourself.
USER: Yet thou didst kill my children.
BOT: But in your daughter's womb I bury them, Where, in that nest of spicery, they will breed Selves of themselves, to your recomforture.
USER: Shall I go win my daughter to thy will?
BOT: And be a happy mother by the deed.
USER: I go. Write to me very shortly, And you shall understand from me her mind.
BOT: Bear her my true love's kiss; and so, farewell. Relenting fool, and shallow, changing woman! How now, what news?
USER: Some light-foot friend post to the Duke of Norfolk. Ratcliffe, thyself, or Catesby. Where is he?
BOT: Here, my good lord.
USER: Catesby, fly to the Duke.
BOT: I will my lord, with all convenient haste.
USER: Ratcliffe, come hither. Post to Salisbury. When thou com'st thither - Why stay'st thou here, and go'st not to the Duke?
BOT: First, mighty liege, tell me your Highness' pleasure, What from your Grace I shall deliver to him.
USER: O, true, good Catesby. Bid him levy straight The greatest strength and power that he can make, And meet me suddenly at Salisbury.
BOT: I go.
USER: What, may it please you, shall I do at Salisbury?
BOT: Why, what wouldst thou do there before I go?
USER: Your Highness told me I should post before.
BOT: My mind is changed. Stanley, what news with you?
USER: None good, my liege, to please you with the hearing; Nor none so bad but well may be reported.
BOT: Hoyday, a riddle! Neither good nor bad. What need'st thou run so many miles about When thou mayst tell thy tale the nearest way? Once more, what news?
USER: Richmond is on the seas.
BOT: There let him sink, and be the seas on him! White-livered runagate, what doth he there?
USER: I know not, mighty sovereign, but by guess.
BOT: Well, as you guess?
USER: Stirred up by Dorset, Buckingham, and Morton, He makes for England, here to claim the crown.
BOT: Is the chair empty? Is the sword unswayed? Is the King dead? The empire unpossessed? What heir of York is there alive but we? And who is England's King but great York's heir? Then tell me, what makes he upon the seas?
USER: Unless for that, my liege, I cannot guess.
BOT: Unless for that he comes to be your liege, You cannot guess wherefore the Welshman comes. Thou wilt revolt and fly to him, I fear.
USER: No, my good lord; therefore mistrust me not.
BOT: Where is thy power, then, to beat him back? Where be thy tenants and thy followers? Are they not now upon the western shore, Safe-conducting the rebels from their ships?
USER: No, my good lord, my friends are in the north.
BOT: Cold friends to me. What do they in the north, When they should serve their sovereign in the west?
USER: They have not been commanded, mighty King. Pleaseth your Majesty to give me leave, I'll muster up my friends, and meet your Grace Where and what time your Majesty shall please.
BOT: Ay, ay, thou wouldst be gone to join with Richmond. But I'll not trust thee.
USER: Most mighty sovereign, You have no cause to hold my friendship doubtful. I never was nor never will be false.
BOT: Go then, and muster men, but leave behind Your son George Stanley. Look your heart be firm, Or else his head's assurance is but frail.
USER: So deal with him as I prove true to you.
BOT: My gracious sovereign, now in Devonshire, As I by friends am well advertised, Sir Edward Courtney, and the haughty prelate, Bishop of Exeter, his elder brother, With many more confederates, are in arms.
USER: In Kent, my liege, the Guilfords are in arms, And every hour more competitors Flock to the rebels, and their power grows strong.
BOT: My lord, the army of great Buckingham -
USER: Out on you, owls! Nothing but songs of death? There, take thou that till thou bring better news.
BOT: The news I have to tell your Majesty Is, that by sudden floods and fall of waters, Buckingham's army is dispersed and scattered, And he himself wandered away alone, No man knows whither.
USER: I cry thee mercy. There is my purse to cure that blow of thine. Hath any well-advised friend proclaimed Reward to him that brings the traitor in?
BOT: Such proclamation hath been made, my lord.
USER: March on, march on, since we are up in arms, If not to fight with foreign enemies, Yet to beat down these rebels here at home.
BOT: My liege, the Duke of Buckingham is taken. That is the best news. That the Earl of Richmond Is with a mighty power landed at Milford Is colder tidings, yet they must be told.
USER: Away towards Salisbury! While we reason here A royal battle might be won and lost. Someone take order Buckingham be brought To Salisbury; the rest march on with me.

USER: At Pembroke, or at Ha'rfordwest in Wales.
BOT: What men of name resort to him?
USER: Well, hie thee to thy lord; I kiss his hand. My letter will resolve him of my mind. Farewell.

USER: Will not King Richard let me speak with him?
BOT: No, my good lord; therefore be patient.
USER: It is.

USER: Every man's conscience is a thousand men, To fight against that guilty homicide.
BOT: I doubt not but his friends will turn to us.
USER: He hath no friends but what are friends for fear, Which in his dearest need will fly from him.
BOT: All for our vantage. Then in God's name, march. True hope is swift, and flies with swallow's wings; Kings it makes gods, and meaner creatures kings.

USER: Here pitch our tent, even here in Bosworth field. My Lord of Surrey, why look you so sad?
BOT: My heart is ten times lighter than my looks.
USER: My lord of Norfolk.
BOT: Here, most gracious liege.
USER: Norfolk, we must have knocks, ha, must we not?
BOT: We must both give and take, my loving lord.
USER: Up with my tent! Here will I lie tonight. But where tomorrow? Well, all's one for that. Who hath descried the number of the traitors?
BOT: Six or seven thousand is their utmost power.
USER: Unless I have mista'en his colours much, Which well I am assured I have not done, His regiment lies half a mile at least South from the mighty power of the King.
BOT: If without peril it be possible, Sweet Blunt, make some good means to speak with him, And give him from me this most needful note.
USER: Upon my life, my lord, I'll undertake it; And so God give you quiet rest tonight.
BOT: Good night, good Captain Blunt. Come, gentlemen, Let us consult upon tomorrow's business; Into my tent. The dew is raw and cold. others exeunt._] Soldiers.
USER: What is't o'clock?
BOT: It's supper time, my lord. It's nine o'clock.
USER: I will not sup tonight. Give me some ink and paper. What, is my beaver easier than it was? And all my armour laid into my tent?
BOT: It is, my liege, and all things are in readiness.
USER: Good Norfolk, hie thee to thy charge; Use careful watch; choose trusty sentinels.
BOT: I go, my lord.
USER: Stir with the lark tomorrow, gentle Norfolk.
BOT: I warrant you, my lord.
USER: Catesby!
BOT: My lord?
USER: My lord?
BOT: Saw'st thou the melancholy Lord Northumberland?
USER: Thomas the Earl of Surrey and himself, Much about cockshut time, from troop to troop Went through the army, cheering up the soldiers.
BOT: So, I am satisfied. Give me a bowl of wine. I have not that alacrity of spirit Nor cheer of mind that I was wont to have. Set it down. Is ink and paper ready?
USER: It is, my lord.
BOT: Bid my guard watch; leave me. Ratcliffe, about the mid of night come to my tent And help to arm me. Leave me, I say. guard it_.]
USER: Fortune and victory sit on thy helm!
BOT: All comfort that the dark night can afford Be to thy person, noble father-in-law. Tell me, how fares our loving mother?
USER: Think how thou stabbed'st me in my prime of youth At Tewksbury; despair therefore, and die! Of butchered princes fight in thy behalf. King Henry's issue, Richmond, comforts thee.
BOT: Rivers that died at Pomfret. Despair and die!
USER: Let fall thy lance. Despair and die!
BOT: Will conquer him. Awake, and win the day.
USER: And in a bloody battle end thy days. Think on Lord Hastings. Despair and die! Arm, fight, and conquer for fair England's sake.
BOT: My lord!
USER: Zounds! Who's there?
BOT: Ratcliffe, my lord; 'tis I. The early village cock Hath twice done salutation to the morn; Your friends are up and buckle on their armour.
USER: O Ratcliffe, I have dreamed a fearful dream! What think'st thou, will our friends prove all true?
BOT: No doubt, my lord.
USER: O Ratcliffe, I fear, I fear!
BOT: Nay, good my lord, be not afraid of shadows.
USER: Good morrow, Richmond.
BOT: Cry mercy, lords and watchful gentlemen, That you have ta'en a tardy sluggard here.
USER: How have you slept, my lord?
BOT: Upon the stroke of four.
USER: What said Northumberland as touching Richmond?
BOT: That he was never trained up in arms.
USER: He said the truth. And what said Surrey then?
BOT: He smiled, and said, "The better for our purpose."
USER: He was in the right, and so indeed it is. Tell the clock there. Give me a calendar. Who saw the sun today?
BOT: Not I, my lord.
USER: Then he disdains to shine, for by the book He should have braved the east an hour ago. A black day will it be to somebody. Ratcliffe!
BOT: My lord?
USER: Arm, arm, my lord. The foe vaunts in the field.
BOT: A good direction, warlike sovereign. This found I on my tent this morning.
USER: My lord, he doth deny to come.
BOT: Off with his son George's head!
USER: My lord, the enemy is past the marsh. After the battle let George Stanley die.
BOT: A thousand hearts are great within my bosom. Advance our standards! Set upon our foes! Our ancient word of courage, fair Saint George, Inspire us with the spleen of fiery dragons! Upon them! Victory sits on our helms.

USER: A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!
BOT: Withdraw, my lord; I'll help you to a horse.
USER: Slave, I have set my life upon a cast, And I will stand the hazard of the die. I think there be six Richmonds in the field; Five have I slain today instead of him. A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!

USER: God and your arms be praised, victorious friends! The day is ours, the bloody dog is dead.
BOT: Courageous Richmond, well hast thou acquit thee! Lo, here, this long-usurped royalty From the dead temples of this bloody wretch Have I plucked off, to grace thy brows withal. Wear it, enjoy it, and make much of it.
USER: Great God of heaven, say Amen to all! But tell me, is young George Stanley living?
BOT: He is, my lord, and safe in Leicester town, Whither, if it please you, we may now withdraw us.
USER: What men of name are slain on either side?
BOT: John, Duke of Norfolk, Walter, Lord Ferrers, Sir Robert Brakenbury, and Sir William Brandon.

USER: Gregory, on my word, we'll not carry coals.
BOT: No, for then we should be colliers.
USER: I mean, if we be in choler, we'll draw.
BOT: Ay, while you live, draw your neck out o' the collar.
USER: I strike quickly, being moved.
BOT: But thou art not quickly moved to strike.
USER: A dog of the house of Montague moves me.
BOT: To move is to stir; and to be valiant is to stand: therefore, if thou art moved, thou runn'st away.
USER: A dog of that house shall move me to stand. I will take the wall of any man or maid of Montague's.
BOT: That shows thee a weak slave, for the weakest goes to the wall.
USER: True, and therefore women, being the weaker vessels, are ever thrust to the wall: therefore I will push Montague's men from the wall, and thrust his maids to the wall.
BOT: The quarrel is between our masters and us their men.
USER: 'Tis all one, I will show myself a tyrant: when I have fought with the men I will be civil with the maids, I will cut off their heads.
BOT: The heads of the maids?
USER: Ay, the heads of the maids, or their maidenheads; take it in what sense thou wilt.
BOT: They must take it in sense that feel it.
USER: Me they shall feel while I am able to stand: and 'tis known I am a pretty piece of flesh.
BOT: 'Tis well thou art not fish; if thou hadst, thou hadst been poor John. Draw thy tool; here comes of the house of Montagues.
USER: My naked weapon is out: quarrel, I will back thee.
BOT: How? Turn thy back and run?
USER: Fear me not.
BOT: No, marry; I fear thee!
USER: Let us take the law of our sides; let them begin.
BOT: I will frown as I pass by, and let them take it as they list.
USER: Nay, as they dare. I will bite my thumb at them, which is disgrace to them if they bear it.
BOT: Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?
USER: I do bite my thumb, sir.
BOT: Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?
USER: Is the law of our side if I say ay?
BOT: No.
USER: No sir, I do not bite my thumb at you, sir; but I bite my thumb, sir.
BOT: Do you quarrel, sir?
USER: Quarrel, sir? No, sir.
BOT: But if you do, sir, I am for you. I serve as good a man as you.
USER: No better.
BOT: Well, sir.
USER: Say better; here comes one of my master's kinsmen.
BOT: Yes, better, sir.
USER: You lie.
BOT: Draw, if you be men. Gregory, remember thy washing blow.
USER: Part, fools! put up your swords, you know not what you do.
BOT: What, art thou drawn among these heartless hinds? Turn thee Benvolio, look upon thy death.
USER: I do but keep the peace, put up thy sword, Or manage it to part these men with me.
BOT: What, drawn, and talk of peace? I hate the word As I hate hell, all Montagues, and thee: Have at thee, coward.
USER: Clubs, bills and partisans! Strike! Beat them down! Down with the Capulets! Down with the Montagues!
BOT: What noise is this? Give me my long sword, ho!
USER: A crutch, a crutch! Why call you for a sword?
BOT: My sword, I say! Old Montague is come, And flourishes his blade in spite of me.
USER: Thou villain Capulet! Hold me not, let me go.
BOT: Thou shalt not stir one foot to seek a foe.
USER: Who set this ancient quarrel new abroach? Speak, nephew, were you by when it began?
BOT: O where is Romeo, saw you him today? Right glad I am he was not at this fray.
USER: My noble uncle, do you know the cause?
BOT: I neither know it nor can learn of him.
USER: Have you importun'd him by any means?
BOT: See, where he comes. So please you step aside; I'll know his grievance or be much denied.
USER: I would thou wert so happy by thy stay To hear true shrift. Come, madam, let's away,
BOT: Good morrow, cousin.
USER: Is the day so young?
BOT: But new struck nine.
USER: Ay me, sad hours seem long. Was that my father that went hence so fast?
BOT: It was. What sadness lengthens Romeo's hours?
USER: Not having that which, having, makes them short.
BOT: In love?
USER: Out.
BOT: Of love?
USER: Out of her favour where I am in love.
BOT: Alas that love so gentle in his view, Should be so tyrannous and rough in proof.
USER: No coz, I rather weep.
BOT: Good heart, at what?
USER: At thy good heart's oppression.
BOT: Soft! I will go along: And if you leave me so, you do me wrong.
USER: Tut! I have lost myself; I am not here. This is not Romeo, he's some other where.
BOT: Tell me in sadness who is that you love?
USER: What, shall I groan and tell thee?
BOT: Groan! Why, no; but sadly tell me who.
USER: Bid a sick man in sadness make his will, A word ill urg'd to one that is so ill. In sadness, cousin, I do love a woman.
BOT: I aim'd so near when I suppos'd you lov'd.
USER: A right good markman, and she's fair I love.
BOT: A right fair mark, fair coz, is soonest hit.
USER: Then she hath sworn that she will still live chaste?
BOT: Be rul'd by me, forget to think of her.
USER: O teach me how I should forget to think.
BOT: By giving liberty unto thine eyes; Examine other beauties.
USER: I'll pay that doctrine, or else die in debt.

USER: But Montague is bound as well as I, In penalty alike; and 'tis not hard, I think, For men so old as we to keep the peace.
BOT: Of honourable reckoning are you both, And pity 'tis you liv'd at odds so long. But now my lord, what say you to my suit?
USER: But saying o'er what I have said before. My child is yet a stranger in the world, She hath not seen the change of fourteen years; Let two more summers wither in their pride Ere we may think her ripe to be a bride.
BOT: Younger than she are happy mothers made.
USER: Your plantain leaf is excellent for that.
BOT: For what, I pray thee?
USER: For your broken shin.
BOT: Why, Romeo, art thou mad?
USER: Not mad, but bound more than a madman is: Shut up in prison, kept without my food, Whipp'd and tormented and - God-den, good fellow.
BOT: God gi' go-den. I pray, sir, can you read?
USER: Ay, mine own fortune in my misery.
BOT: Perhaps you have learned it without book. But I pray, can you read anything you see?
USER: Ay, If I know the letters and the language.
BOT: Ye say honestly, rest you merry!
USER: Up.
BOT: Whither to supper?
USER: To our house.
BOT: Whose house?
USER: My master's.
BOT: Indeed I should have ask'd you that before.
USER: Now I'll tell you without asking. My master is the great rich Capulet, and if you be not of the house of Montagues, I pray come and crush a cup of wine. Rest you merry.
BOT: I'll go along, no such sight to be shown, But to rejoice in splendour of my own.

USER: Nurse, where's my daughter? Call her forth to me.
BOT: Now, by my maidenhead, at twelve year old, I bade her come. What, lamb! What ladybird! God forbid! Where's this girl? What, Juliet!
USER: How now, who calls?
BOT: Your mother.
USER: Madam, I am here. What is your will?
BOT: This is the matter. Nurse, give leave awhile, We must talk in secret. Nurse, come back again, I have remember'd me, thou's hear our counsel. Thou knowest my daughter's of a pretty age.
USER: Faith, I can tell her age unto an hour.
BOT: She's not fourteen.
USER: I'll lay fourteen of my teeth, And yet, to my teen be it spoken, I have but four, She is not fourteen. How long is it now To Lammas-tide?
BOT: A fortnight and odd days.
USER: Enough of this; I pray thee hold thy peace.
BOT: And stint thou too, I pray thee, Nurse, say I.
USER: Peace, I have done. God mark thee to his grace Thou wast the prettiest babe that e'er I nurs'd: And I might live to see thee married once, I have my wish.
BOT: Marry, that marry is the very theme I came to talk of. Tell me, daughter Juliet, How stands your disposition to be married?
USER: It is an honour that I dream not of.
BOT: An honour! Were not I thine only nurse, I would say thou hadst suck'd wisdom from thy teat.
USER: A man, young lady! Lady, such a man As all the world - why he's a man of wax.
BOT: Verona's summer hath not such a flower.
USER: Nay, he's a flower, in faith a very flower.
BOT: No less, nay bigger. Women grow by men.
USER: Speak briefly, can you like of Paris' love?
BOT: I'll look to like, if looking liking move: But no more deep will I endart mine eye Than your consent gives strength to make it fly.
USER: Madam, the guests are come, supper served up, you called, my young lady asked for, the Nurse cursed in the pantry, and everything in extremity. I must hence to wait, I beseech you follow straight.
BOT: We follow thee. Juliet, the County stays.
USER: Go, girl, seek happy nights to happy days.

USER: What, shall this speech be spoke for our excuse? Or shall we on without apology?
BOT: Give me a torch, I am not for this ambling; Being but heavy I will bear the light.
USER: Nay, gentle Romeo, we must have you dance.
BOT: Not I, believe me, you have dancing shoes, With nimble soles, I have a soul of lead So stakes me to the ground I cannot move.
USER: You are a lover, borrow Cupid's wings, And soar with them above a common bound.
BOT: I am too sore enpierced with his shaft To soar with his light feathers, and so bound, I cannot bound a pitch above dull woe. Under love's heavy burden do I sink.
USER: And, to sink in it, should you burden love; Too great oppression for a tender thing.
BOT: Is love a tender thing? It is too rough, Too rude, too boisterous; and it pricks like thorn.
USER: Come, knock and enter; and no sooner in But every man betake him to his legs.
BOT: A torch for me: let wantons, light of heart, Tickle the senseless rushes with their heels; For I am proverb'd with a grandsire phrase, I'll be a candle-holder and look on, The game was ne'er so fair, and I am done.
USER: Tut, dun's the mouse, the constable's own word: If thou art dun, we'll draw thee from the mire Or save your reverence love, wherein thou stickest Up to the ears. Come, we burn daylight, ho.
BOT: Nay, that's not so.
USER: I mean sir, in delay We waste our lights in vain, light lights by day. Take our good meaning, for our judgment sits Five times in that ere once in our five wits.
BOT: And we mean well in going to this mask; But 'tis no wit to go.
USER: Why, may one ask?
BOT: I dreamt a dream tonight.
USER: And so did I.
BOT: Well what was yours?
USER: That dreamers often lie.
BOT: In bed asleep, while they do dream things true.
USER: Peace, peace, Mercutio, peace, Thou talk'st of nothing.
BOT: This wind you talk of blows us from ourselves: Supper is done, and we shall come too late.
USER: Strike, drum.

USER: Where's Potpan, that he helps not to take away? He shift a trencher! He scrape a trencher!
BOT: When good manners shall lie all in one or two men's hands, and they unwash'd too, 'tis a foul thing.
USER: Away with the join-stools, remove the court-cupboard, look to the plate. Good thou, save me a piece of marchpane; and as thou loves me, let the porter let in Susan Grindstone and Nell. Antony and Potpan!
BOT: Ay, boy, ready.
USER: You are looked for and called for, asked for and sought for, in the great chamber.
BOT: We cannot be here and there too. Cheerly, boys. Be brisk awhile, and the longer liver take all.
USER: By'r Lady, thirty years.
BOT: What, man, 'tis not so much, 'tis not so much: 'Tis since the nuptial of Lucentio, Come Pentecost as quickly as it will, Some five and twenty years; and then we mask'd.
USER: 'Tis more, 'tis more, his son is elder, sir; His son is thirty.
BOT: Will you tell me that? His son was but a ward two years ago.
USER: What lady is that, which doth enrich the hand Of yonder knight?
BOT: I know not, sir.
USER: Why how now, kinsman! Wherefore storm you so?
BOT: Uncle, this is a Montague, our foe; A villain that is hither come in spite, To scorn at our solemnity this night.
USER: Young Romeo, is it?
BOT: 'Tis he, that villain Romeo.
USER: It fits when such a villain is a guest: I'll not endure him.
BOT: Why, uncle, 'tis a shame.
USER: Patience perforce with wilful choler meeting Makes my flesh tremble in their different greeting. I will withdraw: but this intrusion shall, Now seeming sweet, convert to bitter gall.
BOT: This holy shrine, the gentle sin is this, My lips, two blushing pilgrims, ready stand To smooth that rough touch with a tender kiss.
USER: Good pilgrim, you do wrong your hand too much, Which mannerly devotion shows in this; For saints have hands that pilgrims' hands do touch, And palm to palm is holy palmers' kiss.
BOT: Have not saints lips, and holy palmers too?
USER: Ay, pilgrim, lips that they must use in prayer.
BOT: O, then, dear saint, let lips do what hands do: They pray, grant thou, lest faith turn to despair.
USER: Saints do not move, though grant for prayers' sake.
BOT: Then move not while my prayer's effect I take. Thus from my lips, by thine my sin is purg'd.
USER: Then have my lips the sin that they have took.
BOT: Sin from my lips? O trespass sweetly urg'd! Give me my sin again.
USER: You kiss by the book.
BOT: Madam, your mother craves a word with you.
USER: What is her mother?
BOT: Marry, bachelor, Her mother is the lady of the house, And a good lady, and a wise and virtuous. I nurs'd her daughter that you talk'd withal. I tell you, he that can lay hold of her Shall have the chinks.
USER: Is she a Capulet? O dear account! My life is my foe's debt.
BOT: Away, be gone; the sport is at the best.
USER: Ay, so I fear; the more is my unrest.
BOT: Come hither, Nurse. What is yond gentleman?
USER: The son and heir of old Tiberio.
BOT: What's he that now is going out of door?
USER: Marry, that I think be young Petruchio.
BOT: What's he that follows here, that would not dance?
USER: I know not.
BOT: Go ask his name. If he be married, My grave is like to be my wedding bed.
USER: His name is Romeo, and a Montague, The only son of your great enemy.
BOT: My only love sprung from my only hate! Too early seen unknown, and known too late! Prodigious birth of love it is to me, That I must love a loathed enemy.
USER: What's this? What's this?
BOT: A rhyme I learn'd even now Of one I danc'd withal.
USER: Anon, anon! Come let's away, the strangers all are gone.

USER: Can I go forward when my heart is here? Turn back, dull earth, and find thy centre out.
BOT: Romeo! My cousin Romeo! Romeo!
USER: He is wise, And on my life hath stol'n him home to bed.
BOT: He ran this way, and leap'd this orchard wall: Call, good Mercutio.
USER: An if he hear thee, thou wilt anger him.
BOT: Come, he hath hid himself among these trees To be consorted with the humorous night. Blind is his love, and best befits the dark.
USER: Go then; for 'tis in vain To seek him here that means not to be found.

USER: Ay me.
BOT: O Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo? Deny thy father and refuse thy name. Or if thou wilt not, be but sworn my love, And I'll no longer be a Capulet.
USER: I take thee at thy word. Call me but love, and I'll be new baptis'd; Henceforth I never will be Romeo.
BOT: What man art thou that, thus bescreen'd in night So stumblest on my counsel?
USER: By a name I know not how to tell thee who I am: My name, dear saint, is hateful to myself, Because it is an enemy to thee. Had I it written, I would tear the word.
BOT: My ears have yet not drunk a hundred words Of thy tongue's utterance, yet I know the sound. Art thou not Romeo, and a Montague?
USER: Neither, fair maid, if either thee dislike.
BOT: How cam'st thou hither, tell me, and wherefore? The orchard walls are high and hard to climb, And the place death, considering who thou art, If any of my kinsmen find thee here.
USER: With love's light wings did I o'erperch these walls, For stony limits cannot hold love out, And what love can do, that dares love attempt: Therefore thy kinsmen are no stop to me.
BOT: If they do see thee, they will murder thee.
USER: Alack, there lies more peril in thine eye Than twenty of their swords. Look thou but sweet, And I am proof against their enmity.
BOT: I would not for the world they saw thee here.
USER: I have night's cloak to hide me from their eyes, And but thou love me, let them find me here. My life were better ended by their hate Than death prorogued, wanting of thy love.
BOT: By whose direction found'st thou out this place?
USER: By love, that first did prompt me to enquire; He lent me counsel, and I lent him eyes. I am no pilot; yet wert thou as far As that vast shore wash'd with the farthest sea, I should adventure for such merchandise.
BOT: Lady, by yonder blessed moon I vow, That tips with silver all these fruit-tree tops, -
USER: O swear not by the moon, th'inconstant moon, That monthly changes in her circled orb, Lest that thy love prove likewise variable.
BOT: What shall I swear by?
USER: Do not swear at all. Or if thou wilt, swear by thy gracious self, Which is the god of my idolatry, And I'll believe thee.
BOT: If my heart's dear love, -
USER: O wilt thou leave me so unsatisfied?
BOT: What satisfaction canst thou have tonight?
USER: Th'exchange of thy love's faithful vow for mine.
BOT: I gave thee mine before thou didst request it; And yet I would it were to give again.
USER: Would'st thou withdraw it? For what purpose, love?
BOT: O blessed, blessed night. I am afeard, Being in night, all this is but a dream, Too flattering sweet to be substantial.
USER: I come, anon. - But if thou meanest not well, I do beseech thee, -
BOT: By and by I come - To cease thy strife and leave me to my grief. Tomorrow will I send.
USER: So thrive my soul, -
BOT: A thousand times good night.
USER: A thousand times the worse, to want thy light. Love goes toward love as schoolboys from their books, But love from love, towards school with heavy looks.
BOT: It is my soul that calls upon my name. How silver-sweet sound lovers' tongues by night, Like softest music to attending ears.
USER: Romeo.
BOT: My nyas?
USER: What o'clock tomorrow Shall I send to thee?
BOT: By the hour of nine.
USER: I will not fail. 'Tis twenty years till then. I have forgot why I did call thee back.
BOT: Let me stand here till thou remember it.
USER: I shall forget, to have thee still stand there, Remembering how I love thy company.
BOT: And I'll still stay, to have thee still forget, Forgetting any other home but this.
USER: I would I were thy bird.
BOT: Sweet, so would I: Yet I should kill thee with much cherishing. Good night, good night. Parting is such sweet sorrow That I shall say good night till it be morrow.
USER: Sleep dwell upon thine eyes, peace in thy breast. Would I were sleep and peace, so sweet to rest. Hence will I to my ghostly Sire's cell, His help to crave and my dear hap to tell.

USER: Good morrow, father.
BOT: That last is true; the sweeter rest was mine.
USER: God pardon sin. Wast thou with Rosaline?
BOT: With Rosaline, my ghostly father? No. I have forgot that name, and that name's woe.
USER: That's my good son. But where hast thou been then?
BOT: Be plain, good son, and homely in thy drift; Riddling confession finds but riddling shrift.
USER: Thou chidd'st me oft for loving Rosaline.
BOT: For doting, not for loving, pupil mine.
USER: And bad'st me bury love.
BOT: Not in a grave To lay one in, another out to have.
USER: I pray thee chide me not, her I love now Doth grace for grace and love for love allow. The other did not so.
BOT: O let us hence; I stand on sudden haste.
USER: Wisely and slow; they stumble that run fast.

USER: Where the devil should this Romeo be? Came he not home tonight?
BOT: Not to his father's; I spoke with his man.
USER: Why, that same pale hard-hearted wench, that Rosaline, torments him so that he will sure run mad.
BOT: Tybalt, the kinsman to old Capulet, hath sent a letter to his father's house.
USER: A challenge, on my life.
BOT: Romeo will answer it.
USER: Any man that can write may answer a letter.
BOT: Nay, he will answer the letter's master, how he dares, being dared.
USER: Alas poor Romeo, he is already dead, stabbed with a white wench's black eye; run through the ear with a love song, the very pin of his heart cleft with the blind bow-boy's butt-shaft. And is he a man to encounter Tybalt?
BOT: Why, what is Tybalt?
USER: The what?
BOT: Here comes Romeo, here comes Romeo!
USER: Good morrow to you both. What counterfeit did I give you?
BOT: The slip sir, the slip; can you not conceive?
USER: Pardon, good Mercutio, my business was great, and in such a case as mine a man may strain courtesy.
BOT: That's as much as to say, such a case as yours constrains a man to bow in the hams.
USER: Meaning, to curtsy.
BOT: Thou hast most kindly hit it.
USER: A most courteous exposition.
BOT: Nay, I am the very pink of courtesy.
USER: Pink for flower.
BOT: Right.
USER: Why, then is my pump well flowered.
BOT: Sure wit, follow me this jest now, till thou hast worn out thy pump, that when the single sole of it is worn, the jest may remain after the wearing, solely singular.
USER: O single-soled jest, solely singular for the singleness!
BOT: Come between us, good Benvolio; my wits faint.
USER: Swits and spurs, swits and spurs; or I'll cry a match.
BOT: Nay, if thy wits run the wild-goose chase, I am done. For thou hast more of the wild-goose in one of thy wits, than I am sure, I have in my whole five. Was I with you there for the goose?
USER: Thou wast never with me for anything, when thou wast not there for the goose.
BOT: I will bite thee by the ear for that jest.
USER: Nay, good goose, bite not.
BOT: Thy wit is a very bitter sweeting, it is a most sharp sauce.
USER: And is it not then well served in to a sweet goose?
BOT: O here's a wit of cheveril, that stretches from an inch narrow to an ell broad.
USER: I stretch it out for that word broad, which added to the goose, proves thee far and wide a broad goose.
BOT: Stop there, stop there.
USER: Thou desirest me to stop in my tale against the hair.
BOT: Thou wouldst else have made thy tale large.
USER: O, thou art deceived; I would have made it short, for I was come to the whole depth of my tale, and meant indeed to occupy the argument no longer.
BOT: Here's goodly gear! A sail, a sail!
USER: Two, two; a shirt and a smock.
BOT: Peter!
USER: Anon.
BOT: My fan, Peter.
USER: Good Peter, to hide her face; for her fan's the fairer face.
BOT: God ye good morrow, gentlemen.
USER: God ye good-den, fair gentlewoman.
BOT: Is it good-den?
USER: 'Tis no less, I tell ye; for the bawdy hand of the dial is now upon the prick of noon.
BOT: Out upon you! What a man are you?
USER: One, gentlewoman, that God hath made for himself to mar.
BOT: By my troth, it is well said; for himself to mar, quoth a? Gentlemen, can any of you tell me where I may find the young Romeo?
USER: I can tell you: but young Romeo will be older when you have found him than he was when you sought him. I am the youngest of that name, for fault of a worse.
BOT: You say well.
USER: Yea, is the worst well? Very well took, i'faith; wisely, wisely.
BOT: If you be he, sir, I desire some confidence with you.
USER: She will endite him to some supper.
BOT: A bawd, a bawd, a bawd! So ho!
USER: What hast thou found?
BOT: I will follow you.
USER: Farewell, ancient lady; farewell, lady, lady, lady.
BOT: I pray you, sir, what saucy merchant was this that was so full of his ropery?
USER: A gentleman, Nurse, that loves to hear himself talk, and will speak more in a minute than he will stand to in a month.
BOT: I saw no man use you at his pleasure; if I had, my weapon should quickly have been out. I warrant you, I dare draw as soon as another man, if I see occasion in a good quarrel, and the law on my side.
USER: Good heart, and i'faith I will tell her as much. Lord, Lord, she will be a joyful woman.
BOT: What wilt thou tell her, Nurse? Thou dost not mark me.
USER: I will tell her, sir, that you do protest, which, as I take it, is a gentlemanlike offer.
BOT: Bid her devise Some means to come to shrift this afternoon, And there she shall at Friar Lawrence' cell Be shriv'd and married. Here is for thy pains.
USER: No truly, sir; not a penny.
BOT: Go to; I say you shall.
USER: This afternoon, sir? Well, she shall be there.
BOT: Now God in heaven bless thee. Hark you, sir.
USER: What say'st thou, my dear Nurse?
BOT: Is your man secret? Did you ne'er hear say, Two may keep counsel, putting one away?
USER: I warrant thee my man's as true as steel.
BOT: Ay, Nurse; what of that? Both with an R.
USER: Ah, mocker! That's the dog's name. R is for the - no, I know it begins with some other letter, and she hath the prettiest sententious of it, of you and rosemary, that it would do you good to hear it.
BOT: Commend me to thy lady.
USER: Ay, a thousand times. Peter!
BOT: Anon.
USER: Before and apace.

USER: Peter, stay at the gate.
BOT: Now, good sweet Nurse, - O Lord, why look'st thou sad? Though news be sad, yet tell them merrily; If good, thou sham'st the music of sweet news By playing it to me with so sour a face.
USER: I am aweary, give me leave awhile; Fie, how my bones ache! What a jaunt have I had!
BOT: I would thou hadst my bones, and I thy news: Nay come, I pray thee speak; good, good Nurse, speak.
USER: Jesu, what haste? Can you not stay a while? Do you not see that I am out of breath?
BOT: No, no. But all this did I know before. What says he of our marriage? What of that?
USER: Lord, how my head aches! What a head have I! It beats as it would fall in twenty pieces. My back o' t'other side, - O my back, my back! Beshrew your heart for sending me about To catch my death with jauncing up and down.
BOT: I'faith, I am sorry that thou art not well. Sweet, sweet, sweet Nurse, tell me, what says my love?
USER: Your love says like an honest gentleman, And a courteous, and a kind, and a handsome, And I warrant a virtuous, - Where is your mother?
BOT: Where is my mother? Why, she is within. Where should she be? How oddly thou repliest. 'Your love says, like an honest gentleman, 'Where is your mother?'
USER: O God's lady dear, Are you so hot? Marry, come up, I trow. Is this the poultice for my aching bones? Henceforward do your messages yourself.
BOT: Here's such a coil. Come, what says Romeo?
USER: Have you got leave to go to shrift today?
BOT: I have.
USER: Hie to high fortune! Honest Nurse, farewell.

USER: So smile the heavens upon this holy act That after-hours with sorrow chide us not.
BOT: Good even to my ghostly confessor.
USER: Romeo shall thank thee, daughter, for us both.
BOT: As much to him, else is his thanks too much.
USER: Conceit more rich in matter than in words, Brags of his substance, not of ornament. They are but beggars that can count their worth; But my true love is grown to such excess, I cannot sum up sum of half my wealth.
BOT: Come, come with me, and we will make short work, For, by your leaves, you shall not stay alone Till holy church incorporate two in one.

USER: I pray thee, good Mercutio, let's retire: The day is hot, the Capulets abroad, And if we meet, we shall not scape a brawl, For now these hot days, is the mad blood stirring.
BOT: Am I like such a fellow?
USER: Come, come, thou art as hot a Jack in thy mood as any in Italy; and as soon moved to be moody, and as soon moody to be moved.
BOT: And what to?
USER: And I were so apt to quarrel as thou art, any man should buy the fee simple of my life for an hour and a quarter.
BOT: The fee simple! O simple!
USER: By my head, here comes the Capulets.
BOT: By my heel, I care not.
USER: Follow me close, for I will speak to them. Gentlemen, good-den: a word with one of you.
BOT: And but one word with one of us? Couple it with something; make it a word and a blow.
USER: You shall find me apt enough to that, sir, and you will give me occasion.
BOT: Could you not take some occasion without giving?
USER: Mercutio, thou consortest with Romeo.
BOT: Consort? What, dost thou make us minstrels? And thou make minstrels of us, look to hear nothing but discords. Here's my fiddlestick, here's that shall make you dance. Zounds, consort!
USER: We talk here in the public haunt of men. Either withdraw unto some private place, And reason coldly of your grievances, Or else depart; here all eyes gaze on us.
BOT: Men's eyes were made to look, and let them gaze. I will not budge for no man's pleasure, I.
USER: Well, peace be with you, sir, here comes my man.
BOT: But I'll be hanged, sir, if he wear your livery. Marry, go before to field, he'll be your follower; Your worship in that sense may call him man.
USER: Romeo, the love I bear thee can afford No better term than this: Thou art a villain.
BOT: Tybalt, the reason that I have to love thee Doth much excuse the appertaining rage To such a greeting. Villain am I none; Therefore farewell; I see thou know'st me not.
USER: Boy, this shall not excuse the injuries That thou hast done me, therefore turn and draw.
BOT: I do protest I never injur'd thee, But love thee better than thou canst devise Till thou shalt know the reason of my love. And so good Capulet, which name I tender As dearly as mine own, be satisfied.
USER: O calm, dishonourable, vile submission! Tybalt, you rat-catcher, will you walk?
BOT: What wouldst thou have with me?
USER: Gentle Mercutio, put thy rapier up.
BOT: Come, sir, your passado.
USER: Draw, Benvolio; beat down their weapons. Gentlemen, for shame, forbear this outrage, Tybalt, Mercutio, the Prince expressly hath Forbid this bandying in Verona streets. Hold, Tybalt! Good Mercutio!
BOT: I am hurt. A plague o' both your houses. I am sped. Is he gone, and hath nothing?
USER: What, art thou hurt?
BOT: Ay, ay, a scratch, a scratch. Marry, 'tis enough. Where is my page? Go villain, fetch a surgeon.
USER: Courage, man; the hurt cannot be much.
BOT: I thought all for the best.
USER: Help me into some house, Benvolio, Or I shall faint. A plague o' both your houses. They have made worms' meat of me. I have it, and soundly too. Your houses!
BOT: O Romeo, Romeo, brave Mercutio's dead, That gallant spirit hath aspir'd the clouds, Which too untimely here did scorn the earth.
USER: This day's black fate on mo days doth depend; This but begins the woe others must end.
BOT: Here comes the furious Tybalt back again.
USER: Thou wretched boy, that didst consort him here, Shalt with him hence.
BOT: This shall determine that.
USER: Romeo, away, be gone! The citizens are up, and Tybalt slain. Stand not amaz'd. The Prince will doom thee death If thou art taken. Hence, be gone, away!
BOT: O, I am fortune's fool!
USER: Why dost thou stay?
BOT: Which way ran he that kill'd Mercutio? Tybalt, that murderer, which way ran he?
USER: There lies that Tybalt.
BOT: Up, sir, go with me. I charge thee in the Prince's name obey.
USER: Where are the vile beginners of this fray?
BOT: O noble Prince, I can discover all The unlucky manage of this fatal brawl. There lies the man, slain by young Romeo, That slew thy kinsman, brave Mercutio.
USER: Tybalt, my cousin! O my brother's child! O Prince! O husband! O, the blood is spill'd Of my dear kinsman! Prince, as thou art true, For blood of ours shed blood of Montague. O cousin, cousin.
BOT: Benvolio, who began this bloody fray?
USER: Romeo slew him, he slew Mercutio. Who now the price of his dear blood doth owe?
BOT: Not Romeo, Prince, he was Mercutio's friend; His fault concludes but what the law should end, The life of Tybalt.

USER: Ay, ay, the cords.
BOT: Ay me, what news? Why dost thou wring thy hands?
USER: Ah, well-a-day, he's dead, he's dead, he's dead! We are undone, lady, we are undone. Alack the day, he's gone, he's kill'd, he's dead.
BOT: Can heaven be so envious?
USER: Romeo can, Though heaven cannot. O Romeo, Romeo. Who ever would have thought it? Romeo!
BOT: I saw the wound, I saw it with mine eyes, God save the mark! - here on his manly breast. A piteous corse, a bloody piteous corse; Pale, pale as ashes, all bedaub'd in blood, All in gore-blood. I swounded at the sight.
USER: O, break, my heart. Poor bankrout, break at once. To prison, eyes; ne'er look on liberty. Vile earth to earth resign; end motion here, And thou and Romeo press one heavy bier.
BOT: O Tybalt, Tybalt, the best friend I had. O courteous Tybalt, honest gentleman! That ever I should live to see thee dead.
USER: What storm is this that blows so contrary? Is Romeo slaughter'd and is Tybalt dead? My dearest cousin, and my dearer lord? Then dreadful trumpet sound the general doom, For who is living, if those two are gone?
BOT: Tybalt is gone, and Romeo banished, Romeo that kill'd him, he is banished.
USER: O God! Did Romeo's hand shed Tybalt's blood?
BOT: It did, it did; alas the day, it did.
USER: Will you speak well of him that kill'd your cousin?
BOT: Weeping and wailing over Tybalt's corse. Will you go to them? I will bring you thither.
USER: Hie to your chamber. I'll find Romeo To comfort you. I wot well where he is. Hark ye, your Romeo will be here at night. I'll to him, he is hid at Lawrence' cell.
BOT: O find him, give this ring to my true knight, And bid him come to take his last farewell.

USER: Romeo, come forth; come forth, thou fearful man. Affliction is enanmour'd of thy parts And thou art wedded to calamity.
BOT: Father, what news? What is the Prince's doom? What sorrow craves acquaintance at my hand, That I yet know not?
USER: Too familiar Is my dear son with such sour company. I bring thee tidings of the Prince's doom.
BOT: What less than doomsday is the Prince's doom?
USER: A gentler judgment vanish'd from his lips, Not body's death, but body's banishment.
BOT: Ha, banishment? Be merciful, say death; For exile hath more terror in his look, Much more than death. Do not say banishment.
USER: Hence from Verona art thou banished. Be patient, for the world is broad and wide.
BOT: Thou fond mad man, hear me speak a little,
USER: O, thou wilt speak again of banishment.
BOT: I'll give thee armour to keep off that word, Adversity's sweet milk, philosophy, To comfort thee, though thou art banished.
USER: Yet banished? Hang up philosophy. Unless philosophy can make a Juliet, Displant a town, reverse a Prince's doom, It helps not, it prevails not, talk no more.
BOT: O, then I see that mad men have no ears.
USER: How should they, when that wise men have no eyes?
BOT: Let me dispute with thee of thy estate.
USER: Arise; one knocks. Good Romeo, hide thyself.
BOT: Not I, unless the breath of heartsick groans Mist-like infold me from the search of eyes.
USER: I come from Lady Juliet.
BOT: Welcome then.
USER: O holy Friar, O, tell me, holy Friar, Where is my lady's lord, where's Romeo?
BOT: There on the ground, with his own tears made drunk.
USER: Nurse.
BOT: Ah sir, ah sir, death's the end of all.
USER: O, she says nothing, sir, but weeps and weeps; And now falls on her bed, and then starts up, And Tybalt calls, and then on Romeo cries, And then down falls again.
BOT: O Lord, I could have stay'd here all the night To hear good counsel. O, what learning is! My lord, I'll tell my lady you will come.
USER: Do so, and bid my sweet prepare to chide.
BOT: Here sir, a ring she bid me give you, sir. Hie you, make haste, for it grows very late.
USER: How well my comfort is reviv'd by this.
BOT: But that a joy past joy calls out on me, It were a grief so brief to part with thee. Farewell.

USER: These times of woe afford no tune to woo. Madam, good night. Commend me to your daughter.
BOT: I will, and know her mind early tomorrow; Tonight she's mew'd up to her heaviness.
USER: Monday, my lord.
BOT: My lord, I would that Thursday were tomorrow.

USER: Wilt thou be gone? It is not yet near day. It was the nightingale, and not the lark, That pierc'd the fearful hollow of thine ear; Nightly she sings on yond pomegranate tree. Believe me, love, it was the nightingale.
BOT: Yond light is not daylight, I know it, I. It is some meteor that the sun exhales To be to thee this night a torchbearer And light thee on thy way to Mantua. Therefore stay yet, thou need'st not to be gone.
USER: More light and light, more dark and dark our woes.
BOT: Madam.
USER: Nurse?
BOT: Your lady mother is coming to your chamber. The day is broke, be wary, look about.
USER: Then, window, let day in, and let life out.
BOT: Farewell, farewell, one kiss, and I'll descend.
USER: Art thou gone so? Love, lord, ay husband, friend, I must hear from thee every day in the hour, For in a minute there are many days. O, by this count I shall be much in years Ere I again behold my Romeo.
BOT: Farewell! I will omit no opportunity That may convey my greetings, love, to thee.
USER: O thinkest thou we shall ever meet again?
BOT: I doubt it not, and all these woes shall serve For sweet discourses in our time to come.
USER: O God! I have an ill-divining soul! Methinks I see thee, now thou art so low, As one dead in the bottom of a tomb. Either my eyesight fails, or thou look'st pale.
BOT: And trust me, love, in my eye so do you. Dry sorrow drinks our blood. Adieu, adieu.
USER: O Fortune, Fortune! All men call thee fickle, If thou art fickle, what dost thou with him That is renown'd for faith? Be fickle, Fortune; For then, I hope thou wilt not keep him long But send him back.
BOT: Who is't that calls? Is it my lady mother? Is she not down so late, or up so early? What unaccustom'd cause procures her hither?
USER: Why, how now, Juliet?
BOT: Madam, I am not well.
USER: Yet let me weep for such a feeling loss.
BOT: So shall you feel the loss, but not the friend Which you weep for.
USER: Feeling so the loss, I cannot choose but ever weep the friend.
BOT: Well, girl, thou weep'st not so much for his death As that the villain lives which slaughter'd him.
USER: What villain, madam?
BOT: That same villain Romeo.
USER: Villain and he be many miles asunder. God pardon him. I do, with all my heart. And yet no man like he doth grieve my heart.
BOT: That is because the traitor murderer lives.
USER: Ay madam, from the reach of these my hands. Would none but I might venge my cousin's death.
BOT: Find thou the means, and I'll find such a man. But now I'll tell thee joyful tidings, girl.
USER: And joy comes well in such a needy time. What are they, I beseech your ladyship?
BOT: Well, well, thou hast a careful father, child; One who to put thee from thy heaviness, Hath sorted out a sudden day of joy, That thou expects not, nor I look'd not for.
USER: Madam, in happy time, what day is that?
BOT: Marry, my child, early next Thursday morn The gallant, young, and noble gentleman, The County Paris, at Saint Peter's Church, Shall happily make thee there a joyful bride.
USER: Here comes your father, tell him so yourself, And see how he will take it at your hands.
BOT: Ay, sir; but she will none, she gives you thanks. I would the fool were married to her grave.
USER: Not proud you have, but thankful that you have. Proud can I never be of what I hate; But thankful even for hate that is meant love.
BOT: Fie, fie! What, are you mad?
USER: Good father, I beseech you on my knees, Hear me with patience but to speak a word.
BOT: God in heaven bless her. You are to blame, my lord, to rate her so.
USER: And why, my lady wisdom? Hold your tongue, Good prudence; smatter with your gossips, go.
BOT: I speak no treason.
USER: O God ye good-en!
BOT: May not one speak?
USER: Peace, you mumbling fool! Utter your gravity o'er a gossip's bowl, For here we need it not.
BOT: You are too hot.
USER: Talk not to me, for I'll not speak a word. Do as thou wilt, for I have done with thee.
BOT: Speakest thou from thy heart?
USER: And from my soul too, Or else beshrew them both.
BOT: Amen.
USER: What?
BOT: Well, thou hast comforted me marvellous much. Go in, and tell my lady I am gone, Having displeas'd my father, to Lawrence' cell, To make confession and to be absolv'd.
USER: Marry, I will; and this is wisely done.

USER: On Thursday, sir? The time is very short.
BOT: My father Capulet will have it so; And I am nothing slow to slack his haste.
USER: You say you do not know the lady's mind. Uneven is the course; I like it not.
BOT: Look, sir, here comes the lady toward my cell.
USER: Happily met, my lady and my wife!
BOT: That may be, sir, when I may be a wife.
USER: That may be, must be, love, on Thursday next.
BOT: What must be shall be.
USER: That's a certain text.
BOT: Come you to make confession to this father?
USER: To answer that, I should confess to you.
BOT: Do not deny to him that you love me.
USER: I will confess to you that I love him.
BOT: So will ye, I am sure, that you love me.
USER: If I do so, it will be of more price, Being spoke behind your back than to your face.
BOT: Poor soul, thy face is much abus'd with tears.
USER: The tears have got small victory by that; For it was bad enough before their spite.
BOT: Thou wrong'st it more than tears with that report.
USER: That is no slander, sir, which is a truth, And what I spake, I spake it to my face.
BOT: Thy face is mine, and thou hast slander'd it.
USER: It may be so, for it is not mine own. Are you at leisure, holy father, now, Or shall I come to you at evening mass?
BOT: My leisure serves me, pensive daughter, now. - My lord, we must entreat the time alone.
USER: God shield I should disturb devotion! - Juliet, on Thursday early will I rouse ye, Till then, adieu; and keep this holy kiss.
BOT: O shut the door, and when thou hast done so, Come weep with me, past hope, past cure, past help!
USER: O Juliet, I already know thy grief; It strains me past the compass of my wits. I hear thou must, and nothing may prorogue it, On Thursday next be married to this County.
BOT: Give me, give me! O tell not me of fear!
USER: Hold; get you gone, be strong and prosperous In this resolve. I'll send a friar with speed To Mantua, with my letters to thy lord.
BOT: Love give me strength, and strength shall help afford. Farewell, dear father.

USER: So many guests invite as here are writ. Sirrah, go hire me twenty cunning cooks.
BOT: You shall have none ill, sir; for I'll try if they can lick their fingers.
USER: How canst thou try them so?
BOT: Marry, sir, 'tis an ill cook that cannot lick his own fingers; therefore he that cannot lick his fingers goes not with me.
USER: Go, begone. We shall be much unfurnish'd for this time. What, is my daughter gone to Friar Lawrence?
BOT: Ay, forsooth.
USER: Well, he may chance to do some good on her. A peevish self-will'd harlotry it is.
BOT: See where she comes from shrift with merry look.
USER: How now, my headstrong. Where have you been gadding?
BOT: Send for the County, go tell him of this. I'll have this knot knit up tomorrow morning.
USER: I met the youthful lord at Lawrence' cell, And gave him what becomed love I might, Not stepping o'er the bounds of modesty.
BOT: Why, I am glad on't. This is well. Stand up. This is as't should be. Let me see the County. Ay, marry. Go, I say, and fetch him hither. Now afore God, this reverend holy Friar, All our whole city is much bound to him.
USER: Nurse, will you go with me into my closet, To help me sort such needful ornaments As you think fit to furnish me tomorrow?
BOT: No, not till Thursday. There is time enough.
USER: Go, Nurse, go with her. We'll to church tomorrow.
BOT: We shall be short in our provision, 'Tis now near night.

USER: Ay, those attires are best. But, gentle Nurse, I pray thee leave me to myself tonight; For I have need of many orisons To move the heavens to smile upon my state, Which, well thou know'st, is cross and full of sin.
BOT: What, are you busy, ho? Need you my help?
USER: Good night. Get thee to bed and rest, for thou hast need.

USER: Hold, take these keys and fetch more spices, Nurse.
BOT: They call for dates and quinces in the pastry.
USER: Come, stir, stir, stir! The second cock hath crow'd, The curfew bell hath rung, 'tis three o'clock. Look to the bak'd meats, good Angelica; Spare not for cost.
BOT: Go, you cot-quean, go, Get you to bed; faith, you'll be sick tomorrow For this night's watching.
USER: No, not a whit. What! I have watch'd ere now All night for lesser cause, and ne'er been sick.
BOT: Ay, you have been a mouse-hunt in your time; But I will watch you from such watching now.
USER: A jealous-hood, a jealous-hood! Now, fellow, what's there?
BOT: Things for the cook, sir; but I know not what.
USER: Make haste, make haste. - Sirrah, fetch drier logs. Call Peter, he will show thee where they are.
BOT: I have a head, sir, that will find out logs And never trouble Peter for the matter.

USER: What noise is here?
BOT: O lamentable day!
USER: What is the matter?
BOT: Look, look! O heavy day!
USER: O me, O me! My child, my only life. Revive, look up, or I will die with thee. Help, help! Call help.
BOT: For shame, bring Juliet forth, her lord is come.
USER: She's dead, deceas'd, she's dead; alack the day!
BOT: Alack the day, she's dead, she's dead, she's dead!
USER: Ha! Let me see her. Out alas! She's cold, Her blood is settled and her joints are stiff. Life and these lips have long been separated. Death lies on her like an untimely frost Upon the sweetest flower of all the field.
BOT: O lamentable day!
USER: O woful time!
BOT: Death, that hath ta'en her hence to make me wail, Ties up my tongue and will not let me speak.
USER: Come, is the bride ready to go to church?
BOT: Have I thought long to see this morning's face, And doth it give me such a sight as this?
USER: O woe! O woeful, woeful, woeful day. Most lamentable day, most woeful day That ever, ever, I did yet behold! O day, O day, O day, O hateful day. Never was seen so black a day as this. O woeful day, O woeful day.
BOT: Beguil'd, divorced, wronged, spited, slain. Most detestable death, by thee beguil'd, By cruel, cruel thee quite overthrown. O love! O life! Not life, but love in death!
USER: Sir, go you in, and, madam, go with him, And go, Sir Paris, everyone prepare To follow this fair corse unto her grave. The heavens do lower upon you for some ill; Move them no more by crossing their high will.
BOT: Faith, we may put up our pipes and be gone.
USER: Honest good fellows, ah, put up, put up, For well you know this is a pitiful case.
BOT: Ay, by my troth, the case may be amended.
USER: Musicians, O, musicians, 'Heart's ease,' 'Heart's ease', O, and you will have me live, play 'Heart's ease.'
BOT: Why 'Heart's ease'?
USER: O musicians, because my heart itself plays 'My heart is full'. O play me some merry dump to comfort me.
BOT: Not a dump we, 'tis no time to play now.
USER: You will not then?
BOT: No.
USER: I will then give it you soundly.
BOT: What will you give us?
USER: No money, on my faith, but the gleek! I will give you the minstrel.
BOT: Then will I give you the serving-creature.
USER: Then will I lay the serving-creature's dagger on your pate. I will carry no crotchets. I'll re you, I'll fa you. Do you note me?
BOT: And you re us and fa us, you note us.
USER: Pray you put up your dagger, and put out your wit.
BOT: Marry, sir, because silver hath a sweet sound.
USER: Prates. What say you, Hugh Rebeck?
BOT: I say 'silver sound' because musicians sound for silver.
USER: Prates too! What say you, James Soundpost?
BOT: Faith, I know not what to say.
USER: O, I cry you mercy, you are the singer. I will say for you. It is 'music with her silver sound' because musicians have no gold for sounding. 'Then music with her silver sound With speedy help doth lend redress.'
BOT: What a pestilent knave is this same!
USER: Hang him, Jack. Come, we'll in here, tarry for the mourners, and stay dinner.

USER: Is it even so? Then I defy you, stars! Thou know'st my lodging. Get me ink and paper, And hire post-horses. I will hence tonight.
BOT: I do beseech you sir, have patience. Your looks are pale and wild, and do import Some misadventure.
USER: Tush, thou art deceiv'd. Leave me, and do the thing I bid thee do. Hast thou no letters to me from the Friar?
BOT: No, my good lord.
USER: Who calls so loud?
BOT: Such mortal drugs I have, but Mantua's law Is death to any he that utters them.
USER: My poverty, but not my will consents.
BOT: I pay thy poverty, and not thy will.
USER: Put this in any liquid thing you will And drink it off; and, if you had the strength Of twenty men, it would despatch you straight.

USER: Holy Franciscan Friar! Brother, ho!
BOT: This same should be the voice of Friar John. Welcome from Mantua. What says Romeo? Or, if his mind be writ, give me his letter.
USER: Who bare my letter then to Romeo?
BOT: I could not send it, - here it is again, - Nor get a messenger to bring it thee, So fearful were they of infection.
USER: Unhappy fortune! By my brotherhood, The letter was not nice, but full of charge, Of dear import, and the neglecting it May do much danger. Friar John, go hence, Get me an iron crow and bring it straight Unto my cell.
BOT: Brother, I'll go and bring it thee.

USER: Here in the churchyard; yet I will adventure.
BOT: I will be gone, sir, and not trouble you.
USER: So shalt thou show me friendship. Take thou that. Live, and be prosperous, and farewell, good fellow.
BOT: For all this same, I'll hide me hereabout. His looks I fear, and his intents I doubt.
USER: Thou detestable maw, thou womb of death, Gorg'd with the dearest morsel of the earth, Thus I enforce thy rotten jaws to open, And in despite, I'll cram thee with more food.
BOT: I do defy thy conjuration, And apprehend thee for a felon here.
USER: Wilt thou provoke me? Then have at thee, boy!
BOT: O lord, they fight! I will go call the watch.
USER: O, I am slain! [_Falls._] If thou be merciful, Open the tomb, lay me with Juliet.
BOT: Saint Francis be my speed. How oft tonight Have my old feet stumbled at graves? Who's there? Who is it that consorts, so late, the dead?
USER: Here's one, a friend, and one that knows you well.
BOT: Bliss be upon you. Tell me, good my friend, What torch is yond that vainly lends his light To grubs and eyeless skulls? As I discern, It burneth in the Capels' monument.
USER: It doth so, holy sir, and there's my master, One that you love.
BOT: Who is it?
USER: Romeo.
BOT: How long hath he been there?
USER: Full half an hour.
BOT: Go with me to the vault.
USER: I dare not, sir; My master knows not but I am gone hence, And fearfully did menace me with death If I did stay to look on his intents.
BOT: Stay then, I'll go alone. Fear comes upon me. O, much I fear some ill unlucky thing.
USER: As I did sleep under this yew tree here, I dreamt my master and another fought, And that my master slew him.
BOT: O comfortable Friar, where is my lord? I do remember well where I should be, And there I am. Where is my Romeo?
USER: Yea, noise? Then I'll be brief. O happy dagger. This is thy sheath. [_stabs herself_] There rest, and let me die.
BOT: This is the place. There, where the torch doth burn.
USER: Here's Romeo's man. We found him in the churchyard.
BOT: Hold him in safety till the Prince come hither. THIRD WATCH. Here is a Friar that trembles, sighs, and weeps. We took this mattock and this spade from him As he was coming from this churchyard side.
USER: A great suspicion. Stay the Friar too.
BOT: What misadventure is so early up, That calls our person from our morning's rest?
USER: What should it be that they so shriek abroad?
BOT: O the people in the street cry Romeo, Some Juliet, and some Paris, and all run With open outcry toward our monument.
USER: What fear is this which startles in our ears?
BOT: Sovereign, here lies the County Paris slain, And Romeo dead, and Juliet, dead before, Warm and new kill'd.
USER: Search, seek, and know how this foul murder comes.
BOT: Here is a Friar, and slaughter'd Romeo's man, With instruments upon them fit to open These dead men's tombs.
USER: O heaven! O wife, look how our daughter bleeds! This dagger hath mista'en, for lo, his house Is empty on the back of Montague, And it mis-sheathed in my daughter's bosom.
BOT: O me! This sight of death is as a bell That warns my old age to a sepulchre.
USER: Come, Montague, for thou art early up, To see thy son and heir more early down.
BOT: Alas, my liege, my wife is dead tonight. Grief of my son's exile hath stopp'd her breath. What further woe conspires against mine age?
USER: Look, and thou shalt see.
BOT: O thou untaught! What manners is in this, To press before thy father to a grave?
USER: I am the greatest, able to do least, Yet most suspected, as the time and place Doth make against me, of this direful murder. And here I stand, both to impeach and purge Myself condemned and myself excus'd.
BOT: Then say at once what thou dost know in this.
USER: We still have known thee for a holy man. Where's Romeo's man? What can he say to this?
BOT: Give me the letter, I will look on it. Where is the County's Page that rais'd the watch? Sirrah, what made your master in this place?
USER: He came with flowers to strew his lady's grave, And bid me stand aloof, and so I did. Anon comes one with light to ope the tomb, And by and by my master drew on him, And then I ran away to call the watch.
BOT: O brother Montague, give me thy hand. This is my daughter's jointure, for no more Can I demand.
USER: But I can give thee more, For I will raise her statue in pure gold, That whiles Verona by that name is known, There shall no figure at such rate be set As that of true and faithful Juliet.
BOT: As rich shall Romeo's by his lady's lie, Poor sacrifices of our enmity.

USER: I'll pheeze you, in faith.
BOT: A pair of stocks, you rogue!
USER: Y'are a baggage; the Slys are no rogues; look in the chronicles: we came in with Richard Conqueror. Therefore, _paucas pallabris_; let the world slide. Sessa!
BOT: You will not pay for the glasses you have burst?
USER: No, not a denier. Go by, Saint Jeronimy, go to thy cold bed and warm thee.
BOT: I know my remedy; I must go fetch the third-borough.
USER: Third, or fourth, or fifth borough, I'll answer him by law. I'll not budge an inch, boy: let him come, and kindly. Horns winded. Enter a Lord from hunting, with Huntsmen and Servants.
BOT: Why, Bellman is as good as he, my lord; He cried upon it at the merest loss, And twice today pick'd out the dullest scent; Trust me, I take him for the better dog.
USER: Thou art a fool: if Echo were as fleet, I would esteem him worth a dozen such. But sup them well, and look unto them all; Tomorrow I intend to hunt again.
BOT: I will, my lord.
USER: See, doth he breathe?
BOT: He breathes, my lord. Were he not warm'd with ale, This were a bed but cold to sleep so soundly.
USER: Believe me, lord, I think he cannot choose.
BOT: It would seem strange unto him when he wak'd.
USER: My lord, I warrant you we will play our part, As he shall think by our true diligence, He is no less than what we say he is.
BOT: An it please your honour, players That offer service to your lordship.
USER: Bid them come near. Now, fellows, you are welcome.
BOT: We thank your honour.
USER: Do you intend to stay with me tonight?
BOT: So please your lordship to accept our duty.
USER: With all my heart. This fellow I remember Since once he play'd a farmer's eldest son; 'Twas where you woo'd the gentlewoman so well. I have forgot your name; but, sure, that part Was aptly fitted and naturally perform'd.
BOT: I think 'twas Soto that your honour means.
USER: Fear not, my lord; we can contain ourselves, Were he the veriest antick in the world.

USER: For God's sake! a pot of small ale.
BOT: Will't please your lordship drink a cup of sack?
USER: Will't please your honour taste of these conserves?
BOT: What raiment will your honour wear today?
USER: Heaven cease this idle humour in your honour! O, that a mighty man of such descent, Of such possessions, and so high esteem, Should be infused with so foul a spirit!
BOT: O! this it is that makes your lady mourn.
USER: O! this is it that makes your servants droop.
BOT: Say thou wilt course; thy greyhounds are as swift As breathed stags; ay, fleeter than the roe.
USER: Dost thou love pictures? We will fetch thee straight Adonis painted by a running brook, And Cytherea all in sedges hid, Which seem to move and wanton with her breath Even as the waving sedges play with wind.
BOT: We'll show thee Io as she was a maid And how she was beguiled and surpris'd, As lively painted as the deed was done.
USER: Or Daphne roaming through a thorny wood, Scratching her legs, that one shall swear she bleeds And at that sight shall sad Apollo weep, So workmanly the blood and tears are drawn.
BOT: Thou art a lord, and nothing but a lord: Thou hast a lady far more beautiful Than any woman in this waning age.
USER: And, till the tears that she hath shed for thee Like envious floods o'er-run her lovely face, She was the fairest creature in the world; And yet she is inferior to none.
BOT: These fifteen years! by my fay, a goodly nap. But did I never speak of all that time?
USER: Ay, the woman's maid of the house.
BOT: Now, Lord be thanked for my good amends!
USER: Amen.
BOT: I thank thee; thou shalt not lose by it.
USER: How fares my noble lord?
BOT: Marry, I fare well; for here is cheer enough. Where is my wife?
USER: Here, noble lord: what is thy will with her?
BOT: Are you my wife, and will not call me husband? My men should call me lord: I am your goodman.
USER: My husband and my lord, my lord and husband; I am your wife in all obedience.
BOT: I know it well. What must I call her?
USER: Madam.
BOT: Alice madam, or Joan madam?
USER: Madam, and nothing else; so lords call ladies.
BOT: Madam wife, they say that I have dream'd And slept above some fifteen year or more.
USER: Ay, and the time seems thirty unto me, Being all this time abandon'd from your bed.
BOT: 'Tis much. Servants, leave me and her alone. Madam, undress you, and come now to bed.
USER: Ay, it stands so that I may hardly tarry so long; but I would be loath to fall into my dreams again: I will therefore tarry in despite of the flesh and the blood.
BOT: Marry, I will; let them play it. Is not a commonty a Christmas gambold or a tumbling-trick?
USER: No, my good lord; it is more pleasing stuff.
BOT: What! household stuff?
USER: It is a kind of history.
BOT: Well, we'll see't. Come, madam wife, sit by my side and let the world slip: we shall ne'er be younger.

USER: Master, some show to welcome us to town.
BOT: To cart her rather: she's too rough for me. There, there, Hortensio, will you any wife?
USER: To make a stale of me amongst these mates?
BOT: Mates, maid! How mean you that? No mates for you, Unless you were of gentler, milder mould.
USER: From all such devils, good Lord deliver us!
BOT: And me, too, good Lord!
USER: Husht, master! Here's some good pastime toward: That wench is stark mad or wonderful froward.
BOT: But in the other's silence do I see Maid's mild behaviour and sobriety. Peace, Tranio!
USER: Well said, master; mum! and gaze your fill.
BOT: Gentlemen, that I may soon make good What I have said, - Bianca, get you in: And let it not displease thee, good Bianca, For I will love thee ne'er the less, my girl.
USER: A pretty peat! it is best put finger in the eye, and she knew why.
BOT: Sister, content you in my discontent. Sir, to your pleasure humbly I subscribe: My books and instruments shall be my company, On them to look, and practise by myself.
USER: Hark, Tranio! thou mayst hear Minerva speak.
BOT: Signior Baptista, will you be so strange? Sorry am I that our good will effects Bianca's grief.
USER: Why will you mew her up, Signior Baptista, for this fiend of hell, And make her bear the penance of her tongue?
BOT: Why, and I trust I may go too, may I not? What! shall I be appointed hours, as though, belike, I knew not what to take and what to leave? Ha!
USER: What's that, I pray?
BOT: Marry, sir, to get a husband for her sister.
USER: A husband! a devil.
BOT: I say, a husband.
USER: I say, a devil. Thinkest thou, Hortensio, though her father be very rich, any man is so very a fool to be married to hell?
BOT: Tush, Gremio! Though it pass your patience and mine to endure her loud alarums, why, man, there be good fellows in the world, and a man could light on them, would take her with all faults, and money enough.
USER: I cannot tell; but I had as lief take her dowry with this condition: to be whipp'd at the high cross every morning.
BOT: I am agreed; and would I had given him the best horse in Padua to begin his wooing, that would thoroughly woo her, wed her, and bed her, and rid the house of her. Come on.
USER: I pray, sir, tell me, is it possible That love should of a sudden take such hold?
BOT: Master, it is no time to chide you now; Affection is not rated from the heart: If love have touch'd you, nought remains but so: _Redime te captum quam queas minimo._
USER: Gramercies, lad; go forward; this contents; The rest will comfort, for thy counsel's sound.
BOT: Master, you look'd so longly on the maid. Perhaps you mark'd not what's the pith of all.
USER: O, yes, I saw sweet beauty in her face, Such as the daughter of Agenor had, That made great Jove to humble him to her hand, When with his knees he kiss'd the Cretan strand.
BOT: Saw you no more? mark'd you not how her sister Began to scold and raise up such a storm That mortal ears might hardly endure the din?
USER: Tranio, I saw her coral lips to move, And with her breath she did perfume the air; Sacred and sweet was all I saw in her.
BOT: Ah, Tranio, what a cruel father's he! But art thou not advis'd he took some care To get her cunning schoolmasters to instruct her?
USER: Ay, marry, am I, sir, and now 'tis plotted.
BOT: I have it, Tranio.
USER: Master, for my hand, Both our inventions meet and jump in one.
BOT: Tell me thine first.
USER: You will be schoolmaster, And undertake the teaching of the maid: That's your device.
BOT: It is: may it be done?
USER: Not possible; for who shall bear your part And be in Padua here Vincentio's son; Keep house and ply his book, welcome his friends; Visit his countrymen, and banquet them?
BOT: Tranio, be so, because Lucentio loves; And let me be a slave, to achieve that maid Whose sudden sight hath thrall'd my wounded eye. Here comes the rogue. Sirrah, where have you been?
USER: Where have I been? Nay, how now! where are you? Master, has my fellow Tranio stol'n your clothes? Or you stol'n his? or both? Pray, what's the news?
BOT: I, sir! Ne'er a whit.
USER: And not a jot of Tranio in your mouth: Tranio is changed to Lucentio.
BOT: The better for him: would I were so too!
USER: Tranio, let's go. One thing more rests, that thyself execute, To make one among these wooers: if thou ask me why, Sufficeth my reasons are both good and weighty.
BOT: My lord, you nod; you do not mind the play.
USER: Yes, by Saint Anne, I do. A good matter, surely: comes there any more of it?
BOT: My lord, 'tis but begun.
USER: 'Tis a very excellent piece of work, madam lady: would 'twere done!

USER: Verona, for a while I take my leave, To see my friends in Padua; but of all My best beloved and approved friend, Hortensio; and I trow this is his house. Here, sirrah Grumio, knock, I say.
BOT: Knock, sir? Whom should I knock? Is there any man has rebused your worship?
USER: Villain, I say, knock me here soundly.
BOT: Knock you here, sir? Why, sir, what am I, sir, that I should knock you here, sir?
USER: Villain, I say, knock me at this gate; And rap me well, or I'll knock your knave's pate.
BOT: My master is grown quarrelsome. I should knock you first, And then I know after who comes by the worst.
USER: Will it not be? Faith, sirrah, and you'll not knock, I'll ring it; I'll try how you can sol, fa, and sing it.
BOT: Help, masters, help! my master is mad.
USER: Now, knock when I bid you, sirrah villain!
BOT: How now! what's the matter? My old friend Grumio! and my good friend Petruchio! How do you all at Verona?
USER: Signior Hortensio, come you to part the fray? _Con tutto il cuore ben trovato_, may I say.
BOT: _Alla nostra casa ben venuto; molto honorato signor mio Petruchio._ Rise, Grumio, rise: we will compound this quarrel.
USER: A senseless villain! Good Hortensio, I bade the rascal knock upon your gate, And could not get him for my heart to do it.
BOT: Knock at the gate! O heavens! Spake you not these words plain: 'Sirrah knock me here, rap me here, knock me well, and knock me soundly'? And come you now with 'knocking at the gate'?
USER: Sirrah, be gone, or talk not, I advise you.
BOT: Hortensio, peace! thou know'st not gold's effect: Tell me her father's name, and 'tis enough; For I will board her, though she chide as loud As thunder when the clouds in autumn crack.
