USER: Her father is Baptista Minola, An affable and courteous gentleman; Her name is Katherina Minola, Renown'd in Padua for her scolding tongue.
BOT: Katherine the curst! A title for a maid of all titles the worst.
USER: Here's no knavery! See, to beguile the old folks, how the young folks lay their heads together! Master, master, look about you: who goes there, ha?
BOT: Peace, Grumio! It is the rival of my love. Petruchio, stand by awhile.
USER: A proper stripling, and an amorous!
BOT: Whate'er I read to her, I'll plead for you, As for my patron, stand you so assur'd, As firmly as yourself were still in place; Yea, and perhaps with more successful words Than you, unless you were a scholar, sir.
USER: O! this learning, what a thing it is.
BOT: O! this woodcock, what an ass it is.
USER: Peace, sirrah!
BOT: Grumio, mum! God save you, Signior Gremio!
USER: 'Tis well; and I have met a gentleman Hath promis'd me to help me to another, A fine musician to instruct our mistress: So shall I no whit be behind in duty To fair Bianca, so belov'd of me.
BOT: Belov'd of me, and that my deeds shall prove.
USER: So said, so done, is well. Hortensio, have you told him all her faults?
BOT: I know she is an irksome brawling scold; If that be all, masters, I hear no harm.
USER: No, say'st me so, friend? What countryman?
BOT: Born in Verona, old Antonio's son. My father dead, my fortune lives for me; And I do hope good days and long to see.
USER: O sir, such a life, with such a wife, were strange! But if you have a stomach, to't a God's name; You shall have me assisting you in all. But will you woo this wild-cat?
BOT: Will I live?
USER: Will he woo her? Ay, or I'll hang her.
BOT: Hortensio, hark: This gentleman is happily arriv'd, My mind presumes, for his own good and yours.
USER: I promis'd we would be contributors, And bear his charge of wooing, whatsoe'er.
BOT: And so we will, provided that he win her.
USER: I would I were as sure of a good dinner.
BOT: Gentlemen, God save you! If I may be bold, Tell me, I beseech you, which is the readiest way To the house of Signior Baptista Minola?
USER: He that has the two fair daughters; is't he you mean?
BOT: Even he, Biondello!
USER: Hark you, sir, you mean not her to -
BOT: Perhaps him and her, sir; what have you to do?
USER: Not her that chides, sir, at any hand, I pray.
BOT: I love no chiders, sir. Biondello, let's away.
USER: Sir, a word ere you go. Are you a suitor to the maid you talk of, yea or no?
BOT: And if I be, sir, is it any offence?
USER: No; if without more words you will get you hence.
BOT: Why, sir, I pray, are not the streets as free For me as for you?
USER: But so is not she.
BOT: For what reason, I beseech you?
USER: For this reason, if you'll know, That she's the choice love of Signior Gremio.
BOT: That she's the chosen of Signior Hortensio.
USER: What, this gentleman will out-talk us all.
BOT: Sir, give him head; I know he'll prove a jade.
USER: Hortensio, to what end are all these words?
BOT: Sir, let me be so bold as ask you, Did you yet ever see Baptista's daughter?
USER: No, sir, but hear I do that he hath two, The one as famous for a scolding tongue As is the other for beauteous modesty.
BOT: Sir, sir, the first's for me; let her go by.
USER: Yea, leave that labour to great Hercules, And let it be more than Alcides' twelve.
BOT: Sir, you say well, and well you do conceive; And since you do profess to be a suitor, You must, as we do, gratify this gentleman, To whom we all rest generally beholding.
USER: The motion's good indeed, and be it so: - Petruchio, I shall be your _ben venuto_.

USER: Of all thy suitors here I charge thee tell Whom thou lov'st best: see thou dissemble not.
BOT: Believe me, sister, of all the men alive I never yet beheld that special face Which I could fancy more than any other.
USER: Minion, thou liest. Is't not Hortensio?
BOT: If you affect him, sister, here I swear I'll plead for you myself but you shall have him.
USER: O! then, belike, you fancy riches more: You will have Gremio to keep you fair.
BOT: Is it for him you do envy me so? Nay, then you jest; and now I well perceive You have but jested with me all this while: I prithee, sister Kate, untie my hands.
USER: If that be jest, then all the rest was so.
BOT: Her silence flouts me, and I'll be reveng'd.
USER: What! in my sight? Bianca, get thee in.
BOT: Was ever gentleman thus griev'd as I? But who comes here? Hortensio as a musician; and Tranio, with Biondello bearing a lute and books.
USER: Good morrow, neighbour Baptista.
BOT: Good morrow, neighbour Gremio. God save you, gentlemen!
USER: And you, good sir! Pray, have you not a daughter Call'd Katherina, fair and virtuous?
BOT: I have a daughter, sir, call'd Katherina.
USER: You are too blunt: go to it orderly.
BOT: Y'are welcome, sir, and he for your good sake; But for my daughter Katherine, this I know, She is not for your turn, the more my grief.
USER: I see you do not mean to part with her; Or else you like not of my company.
BOT: Mistake me not; I speak but as I find. Whence are you, sir? What may I call your name?
USER: Petruchio is my name, Antonio's son; A man well known throughout all Italy.
BOT: I know him well: you are welcome for his sake.
USER: Saving your tale, Petruchio, I pray, Let us, that are poor petitioners, speak too. Backare! you are marvellous forward.
BOT: O, pardon me, Signior Gremio; I would fain be doing.
USER: A thousand thanks, Signior Gremio; welcome, good Cambio. [_To Tranio._] But, gentle sir, methinks you walk like a stranger. May I be so bold to know the cause of your coming?
BOT: Lucentio is your name, of whence, I pray?
USER: Of Pisa, sir; son to Vincentio.
BOT: After my death, the one half of my lands, And in possession twenty thousand crowns.
USER: And, for that dowry, I'll assure her of Her widowhood, be it that she survive me, In all my lands and leases whatsoever. Let specialities be therefore drawn between us, That covenants may be kept on either hand.
BOT: Ay, when the special thing is well obtain'd, That is, her love; for that is all in all.
USER: Well mayst thou woo, and happy be thy speed! But be thou arm'd for some unhappy words.
BOT: Ay, to the proof, as mountains are for winds, That shake not though they blow perpetually.
USER: How now, my friend! Why dost thou look so pale?
BOT: For fear, I promise you, if I look pale.
USER: What, will my daughter prove a good musician?
BOT: I think she'll sooner prove a soldier: Iron may hold with her, but never lutes.
USER: Why, then thou canst not break her to the lute?
BOT: Now, by the world, it is a lusty wench! I love her ten times more than e'er I did: O! how I long to have some chat with her!
USER: Proceed in practice with my younger daughter; She's apt to learn, and thankful for good turns. Signior Petruchio, will you go with us, Or shall I send my daughter Kate to you?
BOT: Well have you heard, but something hard of hearing: They call me Katherine that do talk of me.
USER: Mov'd! in good time: let him that mov'd you hither Remove you hence. I knew you at the first, You were a moveable.
BOT: Why, what's a moveable?
USER: A joint-stool.
BOT: Thou hast hit it: come, sit on me.
USER: Asses are made to bear, and so are you.
BOT: Women are made to bear, and so are you.
USER: No such jade as bear you, if me you mean.
BOT: Alas! good Kate, I will not burden thee; For, knowing thee to be but young and light, -
USER: Too light for such a swain as you to catch; And yet as heavy as my weight should be.
BOT: Should be! should buz!
USER: Well ta'en, and like a buzzard.
BOT: O, slow-wing'd turtle! shall a buzzard take thee?
USER: Ay, for a turtle, as he takes a buzzard.
BOT: Come, come, you wasp; i' faith, you are too angry.
USER: If I be waspish, best beware my sting.
BOT: My remedy is then to pluck it out.
USER: Ay, if the fool could find it where it lies.
BOT: Who knows not where a wasp does wear his sting? In his tail.
USER: In his tongue.
BOT: Whose tongue?
USER: Yours, if you talk of tales; and so farewell.
BOT: What! with my tongue in your tail? Nay, come again, Good Kate; I am a gentleman.
USER: That I'll try.
BOT: I swear I'll cuff you if you strike again.
USER: So may you lose your arms: If you strike me, you are no gentleman; And if no gentleman, why then no arms.
BOT: A herald, Kate? O! put me in thy books.
USER: What is your crest? a coxcomb?
BOT: A combless cock, so Kate will be my hen.
USER: No cock of mine; you crow too like a craven.
BOT: Nay, come, Kate, come; you must not look so sour.
USER: It is my fashion when I see a crab.
BOT: Why, here's no crab, and therefore look not sour.
USER: There is, there is.
BOT: Then show it me.
USER: Had I a glass I would.
BOT: What, you mean my face?
USER: Well aim'd of such a young one.
BOT: Now, by Saint George, I am too young for you.
USER: Yet you are wither'd.
BOT: 'Tis with cares.
USER: I care not.
BOT: Nay, hear you, Kate: in sooth, you 'scape not so.
USER: I chafe you, if I tarry; let me go.
BOT: Go, fool, and whom thou keep'st command.
USER: Did ever Dian so become a grove As Kate this chamber with her princely gait? O! be thou Dian, and let her be Kate, And then let Kate be chaste, and Dian sportful!
BOT: Where did you study all this goodly speech?
USER: It is extempore, from my mother-wit.
BOT: A witty mother! witless else her son.
USER: Am I not wise?
BOT: Yes; keep you warm.
USER: Now, Signior Petruchio, how speed you with my daughter?
BOT: How but well, sir? how but well? It were impossible I should speed amiss.
USER: Why, how now, daughter Katherine, in your dumps?
BOT: Call you me daughter? Now I promise you You have show'd a tender fatherly regard To wish me wed to one half lunatic, A mad-cap ruffian and a swearing Jack, That thinks with oaths to face the matter out.
USER: I'll see thee hang'd on Sunday first.
BOT: Hark, Petruchio; she says she'll see thee hang'd first.
USER: Is this your speeding? Nay, then good-night our part!
BOT: I know not what to say; but give me your hands. God send you joy, Petruchio! 'Tis a match. GREMIO, TRANIO. Amen, say we; we will be witnesses.
USER: Father, and wife, and gentlemen, adieu. I will to Venice; Sunday comes apace; We will have rings and things, and fine array; And kiss me, Kate; we will be married o' Sunday.
BOT: Was ever match clapp'd up so suddenly?
USER: Faith, gentlemen, now I play a merchant's part, And venture madly on a desperate mart.
BOT: 'Twas a commodity lay fretting by you; 'Twill bring you gain, or perish on the seas.
USER: The gain I seek is, quiet in the match.
BOT: No doubt but he hath got a quiet catch. But now, Baptista, to your younger daughter: Now is the day we long have looked for; I am your neighbour, and was suitor first.
USER: And I am one that love Bianca more Than words can witness or your thoughts can guess.
BOT: Youngling, thou canst not love so dear as I.
USER: Greybeard, thy love doth freeze.
BOT: But thine doth fry. Skipper, stand back; 'tis age that nourisheth.
USER: But youth in ladies' eyes that flourisheth.
BOT: Content you, gentlemen; I'll compound this strife: 'Tis deeds must win the prize, and he of both That can assure my daughter greatest dower Shall have my Bianca's love. Say, Signior Gremio, what can you assure her?
USER: Two thousand ducats by the year of land! My land amounts not to so much in all: That she shall have, besides an argosy That now is lying in Marseilles' road. What, have I chok'd you with an argosy?
BOT: Gremio, 'tis known my father hath no less Than three great argosies, besides two galliasses, And twelve tight galleys; these I will assure her, And twice as much, whate'er thou offer'st next.
USER: Nay, I have offer'd all; I have no more; And she can have no more than all I have; If you like me, she shall have me and mine.
BOT: Why, then the maid is mine from all the world, By your firm promise; Gremio is out-vied.
USER: I must confess your offer is the best; And let your father make her the assurance, She is your own; else, you must pardon me; If you should die before him, where's her dower?
BOT: That's but a cavil; he is old, I young.
USER: And may not young men die as well as old?
BOT: Adieu, good neighbour. Now, I fear thee not: Sirrah young gamester, your father were a fool To give thee all, and in his waning age Set foot under thy table. Tut! a toy! An old Italian fox is not so kind, my boy.

USER: Fiddler, forbear; you grow too forward, sir. Have you so soon forgot the entertainment Her sister Katherine welcome'd you withal?
BOT: But, wrangling pedant, this is The patroness of heavenly harmony: Then give me leave to have prerogative; And when in music we have spent an hour, Your lecture shall have leisure for as much.
USER: Sirrah, I will not bear these braves of thine.
BOT: You'll leave his lecture when I am in tune?
USER: That will be never: tune your instrument.
BOT: Where left we last?
USER: Here, madam: - _Hic ibat Simois; hic est Sigeia tellus; Hic steterat Priami regia celsa senis._
BOT: Construe them.
USER: Let's hear. - O fie! the treble jars.
BOT: Spit in the hole, man, and tune again.
USER: Now let me see if I can construe it: _Hic ibat Simois_, I know you not; _hic est Sigeia tellus_, I trust you not; _Hic steterat Priami_, take heed he hear us not; _regia_, presume not; _celsa senis_, despair not.
BOT: Madam, 'tis now in tune.
USER: All but the base.
BOT: The base is right; 'tis the base knave that jars. Now, for my life, the knave doth court my love: Pedascule, I'll watch you better yet.
USER: In time I may believe, yet I mistrust.
BOT: Mistrust it not; for sure, Æacides Was Ajax, call'd so from his grandfather.
USER: I must believe my master; else, I promise you, I should be arguing still upon that doubt; But let it rest. Now, Licio, to you. Good master, take it not unkindly, pray, That I have been thus pleasant with you both.
BOT: My lessons make no music in three parts.
USER: Are you so formal, sir? Well, I must wait, Our fine musician groweth amorous.
BOT: Why, I am past my gamut long ago.
USER: Yet read the gamut of Hortensio.
BOT: Mistress, your father prays you leave your books, And help to dress your sister's chamber up: You know tomorrow is the wedding-day.
USER: Farewell, sweet masters, both: I must be gone.
BOT: Faith, mistress, then I have no cause to stay.

USER: Patience, good Katherine, and Baptista too. Upon my life, Petruchio means but well, Whatever fortune stays him from his word: Though he be blunt, I know him passing wise; Though he be merry, yet withal he's honest.
BOT: Would Katherine had never seen him though!
USER: Go, girl, I cannot blame thee now to weep, For such an injury would vex a very saint; Much more a shrew of thy impatient humour. Master, master! News! old news, and such news as you never heard of!
BOT: Is it new and old too? How may that be?
USER: Why, is it not news to hear of Petruchio's coming?
BOT: Is he come?
USER: Why, no, sir.
BOT: What then?
USER: He is coming.
BOT: When will he be here?
USER: When he stands where I am and sees you there.
BOT: But say, what to thine old news?
USER: Who comes with him?
BOT: 'Tis some odd humour pricks him to this fashion; Yet oftentimes he goes but mean-apparell'd.
USER: I am glad he's come, howsoe'er he comes.
BOT: Why, sir, he comes not.
USER: Didst thou not say he comes?
BOT: Who? that Petruchio came?
USER: Ay, that Petruchio came.
BOT: No, sir; I say his horse comes, with him on his back.
USER: Why, that's all one.
BOT: Nay, by Saint Jamy, I hold you a penny, A horse and a man Is more than one, And yet not many.
USER: Come, where be these gallants? Who is at home?
BOT: You are welcome, sir.
USER: And yet I come not well.
BOT: And yet you halt not.
USER: Not so well apparell'd as I wish you were.
BOT: Why, sir, you know this is your wedding-day: First were we sad, fearing you would not come; Now sadder, that you come so unprovided. Fie! doff this habit, shame to your estate, An eye-sore to our solemn festival.
USER: And tell us what occasion of import Hath all so long detain'd you from your wife, And sent you hither so unlike yourself?
BOT: See not your bride in these unreverent robes; Go to my chamber, put on clothes of mine.
USER: Not I, believe me: thus I'll visit her.
BOT: But thus, I trust, you will not marry her.
USER: He hath some meaning in his mad attire. We will persuade him, be it possible, To put on better ere he go to church.
BOT: I'll after him and see the event of this.
USER: Were it not that my fellow schoolmaster Doth watch Bianca's steps so narrowly, 'Twere good, methinks, to steal our marriage; Which once perform'd, let all the world say no, I'll keep mine own despite of all the world.
BOT: As willingly as e'er I came from school.
USER: And is the bride and bridegroom coming home?
BOT: A bridegroom, say you? 'Tis a groom indeed, A grumbling groom, and that the girl shall find.
USER: Curster than she? Why, 'tis impossible.
BOT: Why, he's a devil, a devil, a very fiend.
USER: Why, she's a devil, a devil, the devil's dam.
BOT: What said the wench, when he rose again?
USER: Is't possible you will away tonight?
BOT: Let us entreat you stay till after dinner.
USER: It may not be.
BOT: Let me entreat you.
USER: It cannot be.
BOT: Let me entreat you.
USER: I am content.
BOT: Are you content to stay?
USER: I am content you shall entreat me stay; But yet not stay, entreat me how you can.
BOT: Now, if you love me, stay.
USER: Grumio, my horse!
BOT: Ay, sir, they be ready; the oats have eaten the horses.
USER: O Kate! content thee: prithee be not angry.
BOT: I will be angry: what hast thou to do? Father, be quiet; he shall stay my leisure.
USER: Ay, marry, sir, now it begins to work.
BOT: Gentlemen, forward to the bridal dinner: I see a woman may be made a fool, If she had not a spirit to resist.
USER: Nay, let them go, a couple of quiet ones.
BOT: Went they not quickly, I should die with laughing.
USER: Of all mad matches, never was the like.
BOT: Mistress, what's your opinion of your sister?
USER: That, being mad herself, she's madly mated.
BOT: I warrant him, Petruchio is Kated.
USER: Shall sweet Bianca practise how to bride it?
BOT: She shall, Lucentio. Come, gentlemen, let's go.

USER: Who is that calls so coldly?
BOT: A piece of ice: if thou doubt it, thou mayst slide from my shoulder to my heel with no greater a run but my head and my neck. A fire, good Curtis.
USER: Is my master and his wife coming, Grumio?
BOT: O, ay! Curtis, ay; and therefore fire, fire; cast on no water.
USER: Is she so hot a shrew as she's reported?
BOT: She was, good Curtis, before this frost; but thou knowest winter tames man, woman, and beast; for it hath tamed my old master, and my new mistress, and myself, fellow Curtis.
USER: Away, you three-inch fool! I am no beast.
BOT: I prithee, good Grumio, tell me, how goes the world?
USER: A cold world, Curtis, in every office but thine; and therefore fire. Do thy duty, and have thy duty, for my master and mistress are almost frozen to death.
BOT: There's fire ready; and therefore, good Grumio, the news.
USER: Why, 'Jack boy! ho, boy!' and as much news as wilt thou.
BOT: Come, you are so full of cony-catching.
USER: All ready; and therefore, I pray thee, news.
BOT: First, know my horse is tired; my master and mistress fallen out.
USER: How?
BOT: Out of their saddles into the dirt; and thereby hangs a tale.
USER: Let's ha't, good Grumio.
BOT: Lend thine ear.
USER: Here.
BOT: This 'tis to feel a tale, not to hear a tale.
USER: And therefore 'tis called a sensible tale; and this cuff was but to knock at your ear and beseech listening. Now I begin: _Imprimis_, we came down a foul hill, my master riding behind my mistress, -
BOT: Both of one horse?
USER: What's that to thee?
BOT: Why, a horse.
USER: By this reckoning he is more shrew than she.
BOT: They are.
USER: Call them forth.
BOT: Do you hear? ho! You must meet my master to countenance my mistress.
USER: Why, she hath a face of her own.
BOT: Who knows not that?
USER: Thou, it seems, that calls for company to countenance her.
BOT: I call them forth to credit her.
USER: Why, she comes to borrow nothing of them.
BOT: Welcome home, Grumio!
USER: How now, Grumio!
BOT: What, Grumio!
USER: Fellow Grumio!
BOT: How now, old lad!
USER: Welcome, you; how now, you; what, you; fellow, you; and thus much for greeting. Now, my spruce companions, is all ready, and all things neat?
BOT: All things is ready. How near is our master?
USER: E'en at hand, alighted by this; and therefore be not, - Cock's passion, silence! I hear my master.
BOT: Where be these knaves? What! no man at door To hold my stirrup nor to take my horse? Where is Nathaniel, Gregory, Philip? -
USER: Here, here, sir; here, sir.
BOT: Here, sir! here, sir! here, sir! here, sir! You logger-headed and unpolish'd grooms! What, no attendance? no regard? no duty? Where is the foolish knave I sent before?
USER: Here, sir; as foolish as I was before.
BOT: You peasant swain! you whoreson malt-horse drudge! Did I not bid thee meet me in the park, And bring along these rascal knaves with thee?
USER: Patience, I pray you; 'twas a fault unwilling.
BOT: A whoreson, beetle-headed, flap-ear'd knave! Come, Kate, sit down; I know you have a stomach. Will you give thanks, sweet Kate, or else shall I? - What's this? Mutton?
USER: Ay.
BOT: Who brought it?
USER: I.
BOT: I pray you, husband, be not so disquiet; The meat was well, if you were so contented.
USER: Peter, didst ever see the like?
BOT: He kills her in her own humour.
USER: Where is he?

USER: Is 't possible, friend Licio, that Mistress Bianca Doth fancy any other but Lucentio? I tell you, sir, she bears me fair in hand.
BOT: Sir, to satisfy you in what I have said, Stand by and mark the manner of his teaching.
USER: Now, mistress, profit you in what you read?
BOT: What, master, read you? First resolve me that.
USER: I read that I profess, _The Art to Love_.
BOT: And may you prove, sir, master of your art!
USER: While you, sweet dear, prove mistress of my heart.
BOT: Quick proceeders, marry! Now tell me, I pray, You that durst swear that your Mistress Bianca Lov'd none in the world so well as Lucentio.
USER: O despiteful love! unconstant womankind! I tell thee, Licio, this is wonderful.
BOT: Mistake no more; I am not Licio. Nor a musician as I seem to be; But one that scorn to live in this disguise For such a one as leaves a gentleman And makes a god of such a cullion: Know, sir, that I am call'd Hortensio.
USER: Signior Hortensio, I have often heard Of your entire affection to Bianca; And since mine eyes are witness of her lightness, I will with you, if you be so contented, Forswear Bianca and her love for ever.
BOT: See, how they kiss and court! Signior Lucentio, Here is my hand, and here I firmly vow Never to woo her more, but do forswear her, As one unworthy all the former favours That I have fondly flatter'd her withal.
USER: And here I take the like unfeigned oath, Never to marry with her though she would entreat; Fie on her! See how beastly she doth court him!
BOT: Mistress Bianca, bless you with such grace As 'longeth to a lover's blessed case! Nay, I have ta'en you napping, gentle love, And have forsworn you with Hortensio.
USER: Tranio, you jest; but have you both forsworn me?
BOT: Mistress, we have.
USER: Then we are rid of Licio.
BOT: I' faith, he'll have a lusty widow now, That shall be woo'd and wedded in a day.
USER: God give him joy!
BOT: Ay, and he'll tame her.
USER: He says so, Tranio.
BOT: Faith, he is gone unto the taming-school.
USER: The taming-school! What, is there such a place?
BOT: Ay, mistress; and Petruchio is the master, That teacheth tricks eleven and twenty long, To tame a shrew and charm her chattering tongue.
USER: O master, master! I have watch'd so long That I am dog-weary; but at last I spied An ancient angel coming down the hill Will serve the turn.
BOT: What is he, Biondello?
USER: Master, a mercatante or a pedant, I know not what; but formal in apparel, In gait and countenance surely like a father.
BOT: And what of him, Tranio?
USER: If he be credulous and trust my tale, I'll make him glad to seem Vincentio, And give assurance to Baptista Minola, As if he were the right Vincentio. Take in your love, and then let me alone.
BOT: God save you, sir!
USER: And you, sir! you are welcome. Travel you far on, or are you at the farthest?
BOT: Sir, at the farthest for a week or two; But then up farther, and as far as Rome; And so to Tripoli, if God lend me life.
USER: What countryman, I pray?
BOT: Of Mantua.
USER: Of Mantua, sir? Marry, God forbid, And come to Padua, careless of your life!
BOT: My life, sir! How, I pray? for that goes hard.
USER: Alas, sir! it is worse for me than so; For I have bills for money by exchange From Florence, and must here deliver them.
BOT: Well, sir, to do you courtesy, This will I do, and this I will advise you: First, tell me, have you ever been at Pisa?
USER: Ay, sir, in Pisa have I often been, Pisa renowned for grave citizens.
BOT: Among them know you one Vincentio?
USER: I know him not, but I have heard of him, A merchant of incomparable wealth.
BOT: He is my father, sir; and, sooth to say, In countenance somewhat doth resemble you.
USER: O, sir, I do; and will repute you ever The patron of my life and liberty.

USER: No, no, forsooth; I dare not for my life.
BOT: What say you to a neat's foot?
USER: 'Tis passing good; I prithee let me have it.
BOT: I fear it is too choleric a meat. How say you to a fat tripe finely broil'd?
USER: I like it well; good Grumio, fetch it me.
BOT: I cannot tell; I fear 'tis choleric. What say you to a piece of beef and mustard?
USER: A dish that I do love to feed upon.
BOT: Ay, but the mustard is too hot a little.
USER: Why then the beef, and let the mustard rest.
BOT: Nay, then I will not: you shall have the mustard, Or else you get no beef of Grumio.
USER: Then both, or one, or anything thou wilt.
BOT: Why then the mustard without the beef.
USER: Go, get thee gone, thou false deluding slave, That feed'st me with the very name of meat. Sorrow on thee and all the pack of you That triumph thus upon my misery! Go, get thee gone, I say.
BOT: How fares my Kate? What, sweeting, all amort?
USER: Mistress, what cheer?
BOT: Faith, as cold as can be.
USER: I pray you, let it stand.
BOT: The poorest service is repaid with thanks; And so shall mine, before you touch the meat.
USER: I thank you, sir.
BOT: Signior Petruchio, fie! you are to blame. Come, Mistress Kate, I'll bear you company.
USER: Here is the cap your worship did bespeak.
BOT: Why, this was moulded on a porringer; A velvet dish: fie, fie! 'tis lewd and filthy: Why, 'tis a cockle or a walnut-shell, A knack, a toy, a trick, a baby's cap: Away with it! come, let me have a bigger.
USER: I'll have no bigger; this doth fit the time, And gentlewomen wear such caps as these.
BOT: When you are gentle, you shall have one too, And not till then.
USER: Why, thou say'st true; it is a paltry cap, A custard-coffin, a bauble, a silken pie; I love thee well in that thou lik'st it not.
BOT: Love me or love me not, I like the cap; And it I will have, or I will have none.
USER: You bid me make it orderly and well, According to the fashion and the time.
BOT: Marry, and did; but if you be remember'd, I did not bid you mar it to the time. Go, hop me over every kennel home, For you shall hop without my custom, sir. I'll none of it: hence! make your best of it.
USER: I never saw a better fashion'd gown, More quaint, more pleasing, nor more commendable; Belike you mean to make a puppet of me.
BOT: Why, true; he means to make a puppet of thee.
USER: She says your worship means to make a puppet of her.
BOT: Your worship is deceiv'd: the gown is made Just as my master had direction. Grumio gave order how it should be done.
USER: I gave him no order; I gave him the stuff.
BOT: But how did you desire it should be made?
USER: Marry, sir, with needle and thread.
BOT: But did you not request to have it cut?
USER: Thou hast faced many things.
BOT: I have.
USER: Face not me. Thou hast braved many men; brave not me: I will neither be fac'd nor brav'd. I say unto thee, I bid thy master cut out the gown; but I did not bid him cut it to pieces: ergo, thou liest.
BOT: Why, here is the note of the fashion to testify.
USER: Read it.
BOT: The note lies in 's throat, if he say I said so.
USER: 'Imprimis, a loose-bodied gown.'
BOT: Master, if ever I said loose-bodied gown, sew me in the skirts of it and beat me to death with a bottom of brown thread; I said, a gown.
USER: Proceed.
BOT: 'With a small compassed cape.'
USER: I confess the cape.
BOT: 'With a trunk sleeve.'
USER: I confess two sleeves.
BOT: 'The sleeves curiously cut.'
USER: Ay, there's the villainy.
BOT: Error i' the bill, sir; error i' the bill. I commanded the sleeves should be cut out, and sew'd up again; and that I'll prove upon thee, though thy little finger be armed in a thimble.
USER: This is true that I say; and I had thee in place where thou shouldst know it.
BOT: I am for thee straight; take thou the bill, give me thy mete-yard, and spare not me.
USER: God-a-mercy, Grumio! Then he shall have no odds.
BOT: Well, sir, in brief, the gown is not for me.
USER: You are i' the right, sir; 'tis for my mistress.
BOT: Go, take it up unto thy master's use.
USER: Villain, not for thy life! Take up my mistress' gown for thy master's use!
BOT: Why, sir, what's your conceit in that?
USER: O, sir, the conceit is deeper than you think for. Take up my mistress' gown to his master's use! O fie, fie, fie!
BOT: Take no unkindness of his hasty words. Away, I say! commend me to thy master.
USER: I dare assure you, sir, 'tis almost two, And 'twill be supper-time ere you come there.
BOT: It shall be seven ere I go to horse. Look what I speak, or do, or think to do, You are still crossing it. Sirs, let 't alone: I will not go today; and ere I do, It shall be what o'clock I say it is.
USER: Why, so this gallant will command the sun.

USER: Sir, this is the house; please it you that I call?
BOT: Ay, what else? and, but I be deceived, Signior Baptista may remember me, Near twenty years ago in Genoa, Where we were lodgers at the Pegasus.
USER: 'Tis well; and hold your own, in any case, With such austerity as 'longeth to a father.
BOT: I warrant you. But, sir, here comes your boy; 'Twere good he were school'd.
USER: Fear you not him. Sirrah Biondello, Now do your duty throughly, I advise you. Imagine 'twere the right Vincentio.
BOT: Tut! fear not me.
USER: But hast thou done thy errand to Baptista?
BOT: I told him that your father was at Venice, And that you look'd for him this day in Padua.
USER: Th'art a tall fellow; hold thee that to drink. Here comes Baptista. Set your countenance, sir. Signior Baptista, you are happily met. I pray you stand good father to me now; Give me Bianca for my patrimony.
BOT: I thank you, sir. Where then do you know best We be affied, and such assurance ta'en As shall with either part's agreement stand?
USER: Not in my house, Lucentio, for you know Pitchers have ears, and I have many servants; Besides, old Gremio is hearkening still, And happily we might be interrupted.
BOT: It likes me well. Cambio, hie you home, And bid Bianca make her ready straight; And, if you will, tell what hath happened: Lucentio's father is arriv'd in Padua, And how she's like to be Lucentio's wife.
USER: I pray the gods she may, with all my heart!
BOT: Dally not with the gods, but get thee gone. Signior Baptista, shall I lead the way? Welcome! One mess is like to be your cheer; Come, sir; we will better it in Pisa.
USER: I follow you.
BOT: Cambio!
USER: What say'st thou, Biondello?
BOT: You saw my master wink and laugh upon you?
USER: Biondello, what of that?
BOT: Faith, nothing; but has left me here behind to expound the meaning or moral of his signs and tokens.
USER: I pray thee moralize them.
BOT: Then thus: Baptista is safe, talking with the deceiving father of a deceitful son.
USER: And what of him?
BOT: His daughter is to be brought by you to the supper.
USER: And then?
BOT: The old priest at Saint Luke's church is at your command at all hours.
USER: And what of all this?
BOT: Hear'st thou, Biondello?
USER: I may, and will, if she be so contented. She will be pleas'd; then wherefore should I doubt? Hap what hap may, I'll roundly go about her; It shall go hard if Cambio go without her:

USER: Come on, i' God's name; once more toward our father's. Good Lord, how bright and goodly shines the moon!
BOT: The moon! The sun; it is not moonlight now.
USER: I say it is the moon that shines so bright.
BOT: I know it is the sun that shines so bright.
USER: Now by my mother's son, and that's myself, It shall be moon, or star, or what I list, Or ere I journey to your father's house. Go on and fetch our horses back again. Evermore cross'd and cross'd; nothing but cross'd!
BOT: Say as he says, or we shall never go.
USER: Forward, I pray, since we have come so far, And be it moon, or sun, or what you please; And if you please to call it a rush-candle, Henceforth I vow it shall be so for me.
BOT: I say it is the moon.
USER: I know it is the moon.
BOT: Nay, then you lie; it is the blessed sun.
USER: Then, God be bless'd, it is the blessed sun; But sun it is not when you say it is not, And the moon changes even as your mind. What you will have it nam'd, even that it is, And so it shall be so for Katherine.
BOT: Petruchio, go thy ways; the field is won.
USER: A will make the man mad, to make a woman of him.
BOT: Young budding virgin, fair and fresh and sweet, Whither away, or where is thy abode? Happy the parents of so fair a child; Happier the man whom favourable stars Allot thee for his lovely bedfellow.
USER: Why, how now, Kate! I hope thou art not mad: This is a man, old, wrinkled, faded, wither'd, And not a maiden, as thou sayst he is.
BOT: Pardon, old father, my mistaking eyes, That have been so bedazzled with the sun That everything I look on seemeth green: Now I perceive thou art a reverend father; Pardon, I pray thee, for my mad mistaking.
USER: Do, good old grandsire, and withal make known Which way thou travellest: if along with us, We shall be joyful of thy company.
BOT: Fair sir, and you my merry mistress, That with your strange encounter much amaz'd me, My name is called Vincentio; my dwelling Pisa; And bound I am to Padua, there to visit A son of mine, which long I have not seen.
USER: What is his name?
BOT: Lucentio, gentle sir.
USER: But is this true? or is it else your pleasure, Like pleasant travellers, to break a jest Upon the company you overtake?
BOT: I do assure thee, father, so it is.
USER: Come, go along, and see the truth hereof; For our first merriment hath made thee jealous.
BOT: Well, Petruchio, this has put me in heart. Have to my widow! and if she be froward, Then hast thou taught Hortensio to be untoward.

USER: Softly and swiftly, sir, for the priest is ready.
BOT: I fly, Biondello; but they may chance to need thee at home, therefore leave us.
USER: Nay, faith, I'll see the church o' your back; and then come back to my master's as soon as I can.
BOT: I marvel Cambio comes not all this while.
USER: Sir, here's the door; this is Lucentio's house: My father's bears more toward the market-place; Thither must I, and here I leave you, sir.
BOT: You shall not choose but drink before you go. I think I shall command your welcome here, And by all likelihood some cheer is toward.
USER: They're busy within; you were best knock louder.
BOT: What's he that knocks as he would beat down the gate?
USER: Is Signior Lucentio within, sir?
BOT: He's within, sir, but not to be spoken withal.
USER: What if a man bring him a hundred pound or two to make merry withal?
BOT: Keep your hundred pounds to yourself: he shall need none so long as I live.
USER: Nay, I told you your son was well beloved in Padua. Do you hear, sir? To leave frivolous circumstances, I pray you tell Signior Lucentio that his father is come from Pisa, and is here at the door to speak with him.
BOT: Thou liest: his father is come from Padua, and here looking out at the window.
USER: Art thou his father?
BOT: Ay, sir; so his mother says, if I may believe her.
USER: take upon you another man's name.
BOT: Lay hands on the villain: I believe a means to cozen somebody in this city under my countenance.
USER: I have seen them in the church together: God send 'em good shipping! But who is here? Mine old master, Vincentio! Now we are undone and brought to nothing.
BOT: I hope I may choose, sir.
USER: Come hither, you rogue. What, have you forgot me?
BOT: Forgot you! No, sir: I could not forget you, for I never saw you before in all my life.
USER: What, you notorious villain! didst thou never see thy master's father, Vincentio?
BOT: What, my old worshipful old master? Yes, marry, sir; see where he looks out of the window.
USER: Is't so, indeed?
BOT: Help, help, help! here's a madman will murder me.
USER: Help, son! help, Signior Baptista!
BOT: Prithee, Kate, let's stand aside and see the end of this controversy.
USER: Sir, what are you that offer to beat my servant?
BOT: How now! what's the matter?
USER: What, is the man lunatic?
BOT: Sir, you seem a sober ancient gentleman by your habit, but your words show you a madman. Why, sir, what 'cerns it you if I wear pearl and gold? I thank my good father, I am able to maintain it.
USER: Thy father! O villain! he is a sailmaker in Bergamo.
BOT: You mistake, sir; you mistake, sir. Pray, what do you think is his name?
USER: His name! As if I knew not his name! I have brought him up ever since he was three years old, and his name is Tranio.
BOT: Away, away, mad ass! His name is Lucentio; and he is mine only son, and heir to the lands of me, Signior Vincentio.
USER: Lucentio! O, he hath murdered his master! Lay hold on him, I charge you, in the Duke's name. O, my son, my son! Tell me, thou villain, where is my son, Lucentio?
BOT: Call forth an officer. Carry this mad knave to the gaol. Father Baptista, I charge you see that he be forthcoming.
USER: Carry me to the gaol!
BOT: Stay, officer; he shall not go to prison.
USER: Talk not, Signior Gremio; I say he shall go to prison.
BOT: Take heed, Signior Baptista, lest you be cony-catched in this business; I dare swear this is the right Vincentio.
USER: Swear if thou darest.
BOT: Nay, I dare not swear it.
USER: Then thou wert best say that I am not Lucentio.
BOT: Yes, I know thee to be Signior Lucentio.
USER: Away with the dotard! to the gaol with him!
BOT: Thus strangers may be haled and abus'd: O monstrous villain!
USER: O! we are spoiled; and yonder he is: deny him, forswear him, or else we are all undone.
BOT: Lives my sweetest son?
USER: How hast thou offended? Where is Lucentio?
BOT: Here's Lucentio, Right son to the right Vincentio; That have by marriage made thy daughter mine, While counterfeit supposes blear'd thine eyne.
USER: Here 's packing, with a witness, to deceive us all!
BOT: Where is that damned villain, Tranio, That fac'd and brav'd me in this matter so?
USER: Why, tell me, is not this my Cambio?
BOT: Cambio is chang'd into Lucentio.
USER: I'll slit the villain's nose that would have sent me to the gaol.
BOT: without asking my good will?
USER: Fear not, Baptista; we will content you, go to: but I will in, to be revenged for this villainy.
BOT: And I to sound the depth of this knavery.
USER: Look not pale, Bianca; thy father will not frown.
BOT: My cake is dough, but I'll in among the rest; Out of hope of all but my share of the feast. Petruchio and Katherina advance.
USER: Husband, let's follow to see the end of this ado.
BOT: First kiss me, Kate, and we will.
USER: What! in the midst of the street?
BOT: What! art thou ashamed of me?
USER: No, sir; God forbid; but ashamed to kiss.
BOT: Why, then, let's home again. Come, sirrah, let's away.
USER: Nay, I will give thee a kiss: now pray thee, love, stay.
BOT: Is not this well? Come, my sweet Kate: Better once than never, for never too late.

USER: Nothing but sit and sit, and eat and eat!
BOT: Padua affords this kindness, son Petruchio.
USER: Padua affords nothing but what is kind.
BOT: For both our sakes I would that word were true.
USER: Now, for my life, Hortensio fears his widow.
BOT: Then never trust me if I be afeard.
USER: You are very sensible, and yet you miss my sense: I mean Hortensio is afeard of you.
BOT: He that is giddy thinks the world turns round.
USER: Roundly replied.
BOT: Mistress, how mean you that?
USER: Thus I conceive by him.
BOT: Conceives by me! How likes Hortensio that?
USER: My widow says thus she conceives her tale.
BOT: Very well mended. Kiss him for that, good widow.
USER: 'He that is giddy thinks the world turns round': I pray you tell me what you meant by that.
BOT: Your husband, being troubled with a shrew, Measures my husband's sorrow by his woe; And now you know my meaning.
USER: A very mean meaning.
BOT: Right, I mean you.
USER: And I am mean, indeed, respecting you.
BOT: To her, Kate!
USER: To her, widow!
BOT: A hundred marks, my Kate does put her down.
USER: That's my office.
BOT: Spoke like an officer: ha' to thee, lad.
USER: How likes Gremio these quick-witted folks?
BOT: Believe me, sir, they butt together well.
USER: Head and butt! An hasty-witted body Would say your head and butt were head and horn.
BOT: Ay, mistress bride, hath that awaken'd you?
USER: Ay, but not frighted me; therefore I'll sleep again.
BOT: Nay, that you shall not; since you have begun, Have at you for a bitter jest or two.
USER: Am I your bird? I mean to shift my bush, And then pursue me as you draw your bow. You are welcome all.
BOT: She hath prevented me. Here, Signior Tranio; This bird you aim'd at, though you hit her not: Therefore a health to all that shot and miss'd.
USER: O, sir! Lucentio slipp'd me like his greyhound, Which runs himself, and catches for his master.
BOT: A good swift simile, but something currish.
USER: 'Tis well, sir, that you hunted for yourself: 'Tis thought your deer does hold you at a bay.
BOT: O ho, Petruchio! Tranio hits you now.
USER: I thank thee for that gird, good Tranio.
BOT: Confess, confess; hath he not hit you here?
USER: A has a little gall'd me, I confess; And as the jest did glance away from me, 'Tis ten to one it maim'd you two outright.
BOT: Now, in good sadness, son Petruchio, I think thou hast the veriest shrew of all.
USER: Well, I say no; and therefore, for assurance, Let's each one send unto his wife, And he whose wife is most obedient, To come at first when he doth send for her, Shall win the wager which we will propose.
BOT: Content. What's the wager?
USER: Twenty crowns.
BOT: Twenty crowns! I'll venture so much of my hawk or hound, But twenty times so much upon my wife.
USER: A hundred then.
BOT: Content.
USER: A match! 'tis done.
BOT: Who shall begin?
USER: That will I. Go, Biondello, bid your mistress come to me.
BOT: I go.
USER: Son, I'll be your half, Bianca comes.
BOT: I'll have no halves; I'll bear it all myself. How now! what news?
USER: Sir, my mistress sends you word That she is busy and she cannot come.
BOT: How! She's busy, and she cannot come! Is that an answer?
USER: Ay, and a kind one too: Pray God, sir, your wife send you not a worse.
BOT: I hope better.
USER: Sirrah Biondello, go and entreat my wife To come to me forthwith.
BOT: O, ho! entreat her! Nay, then she must needs come.
USER: I am afraid, sir, Do what you can, yours will not be entreated. Now, where's my wife?
BOT: She says you have some goodly jest in hand: She will not come; she bids you come to her.
USER: Worse and worse; she will not come! O vile, Intolerable, not to be endur'd! Sirrah Grumio, go to your mistress, Say I command her come to me.
BOT: I know her answer.
USER: What?
BOT: She will not.
USER: The fouler fortune mine, and there an end.
BOT: Now, by my holidame, here comes Katherina!
USER: What is your will sir, that you send for me?
BOT: Where is your sister, and Hortensio's wife?
USER: They sit conferring by the parlour fire.
BOT: Go fetch them hither; if they deny to come, Swinge me them soundly forth unto their husbands. Away, I say, and bring them hither straight.
USER: Here is a wonder, if you talk of a wonder.
BOT: And so it is. I wonder what it bodes.
USER: Marry, peace it bodes, and love, and quiet life, An awful rule, and right supremacy; And, to be short, what not that's sweet and happy.
BOT: Now fair befall thee, good Petruchio! The wager thou hast won; and I will add Unto their losses twenty thousand crowns; Another dowry to another daughter, For she is chang'd, as she had never been.
USER: Lord, let me never have a cause to sigh Till I be brought to such a silly pass!
BOT: Fie! what a foolish duty call you this?
USER: I would your duty were as foolish too; The wisdom of your duty, fair Bianca, Hath cost me a hundred crowns since supper-time!
BOT: The more fool you for laying on my duty.
USER: Katherine, I charge thee, tell these headstrong women What duty they do owe their lords and husbands.
BOT: Come, come, you're mocking; we will have no telling.
USER: Come on, I say; and first begin with her.
BOT: She shall not.
USER: I say she shall: and first begin with her.
BOT: Why, there's a wench! Come on, and kiss me, Kate.
USER: Well, go thy ways, old lad, for thou shalt ha't.
BOT: 'Tis a good hearing when children are toward.
USER: But a harsh hearing when women are froward.
BOT: Come, Kate, we'll to bed. We three are married, but you two are sped. 'Twas I won the wager, And being a winner, God give you good night!
USER: Now go thy ways; thou hast tam'd a curst shrew.
BOT: 'Tis a wonder, by your leave, she will be tam'd so. THE TEMPEST Contents

USER: Boatswain!
BOT: Here, master: what cheer?
USER: Good! Speak to the mariners: fall to 't yarely, or we run ourselves aground: bestir, bestir.
BOT: Heigh, my hearts! cheerly, cheerly, my hearts! yare, yare! Take in the topsail. Tend to th' master's whistle. Blow till thou burst thy wind, if room enough.
USER: Good boatswain, have care. Where's the master? Play the men.
BOT: I pray now, keep below.
USER: Where is the master, boson?
BOT: Do you not hear him? You mar our labour: keep your cabins: you do assist the storm.
USER: Nay, good, be patient.
BOT: When the sea is. Hence! What cares these roarers for the name of king? To cabin! silence! Trouble us not.
USER: Good, yet remember whom thou hast aboard.
BOT: A pox o' your throat, you bawling, blasphemous, incharitable dog!
USER: Work you, then.
BOT: Hang, cur, hang, you whoreson, insolent noisemaker! We are less afraid to be drowned than thou art.
USER: I'll warrant him for drowning, though the ship were no stronger than a nutshell, and as leaky as an unstanched wench.
BOT: Lay her a-hold, a-hold! Set her two courses: off to sea again: lay her off.
USER: All lost! to prayers, to prayers! all lost!
BOT: What, must our mouths be cold?
USER: The King and Prince at prayers! Let's assist them, For our case is as theirs.
BOT: I am out of patience.
USER: We are merely cheated of our lives by drunkards. This wide-chapp'd rascal - would thou might'st lie drowning The washing of ten tides!
BOT: Let's all sink wi' th' King.
USER: Let's take leave of him.
BOT: Now would I give a thousand furlongs of sea for an acre of barren ground. Long heath, brown furze, anything. The wills above be done! but I would fain die a dry death.

USER: Be collected: No more amazement: tell your piteous heart There's no harm done.
BOT: O, woe the day!
USER: More to know Did never meddle with my thoughts.
BOT: You have often Begun to tell me what I am, but stopp'd, And left me to a bootless inquisition, Concluding "Stay; not yet."
USER: The hour's now come, The very minute bids thee ope thine ear; Obey, and be attentive. Canst thou remember A time before we came unto this cell? I do not think thou canst, for then thou wast not Out three years old.
BOT: Certainly, sir, I can.
USER: By what? By any other house, or person? Of anything the image, tell me, that Hath kept with thy remembrance.
BOT: 'Tis far off, And rather like a dream than an assurance That my remembrance warrants. Had I not Four or five women once that tended me?
USER: Thou hadst, and more, Miranda. But how is it That this lives in thy mind? What seest thou else In the dark backward and abysm of time? If thou rememb'rest aught ere thou cam'st here, How thou cam'st here, thou mayst.
BOT: But that I do not.
USER: Twelve year since, Miranda, twelve year since, Thy father was the Duke of Milan, and A prince of power.
BOT: Sir, are not you my father?
USER: Thy mother was a piece of virtue, and She said thou wast my daughter. And thy father Was Duke of Milan, and his only heir And princess, no worse issued.
BOT: O, the heavens! What foul play had we that we came from thence? Or blessed was't we did?
USER: Both, both, my girl. By foul play, as thou say'st, were we heav'd thence; But blessedly holp hither.
BOT: O, my heart bleeds To think o' th' teen that I have turn'd you to, Which is from my remembrance. Please you, farther.
USER: Sir, most heedfully.
BOT: O, good sir! I do.
USER: Your tale, sir, would cure deafness.
BOT: O the heavens!
USER: Mark his condition, and the event; then tell me If this might be a brother.
BOT: I should sin To think but nobly of my grandmother: Good wombs have borne bad sons.
USER: Alack, for pity! I, not rememb'ring how I cried out then, Will cry it o'er again: it is a hint That wrings mine eyes to 't.
BOT: Hear a little further, And then I'll bring thee to the present business Which now's upon us; without the which this story Were most impertinent.
USER: Wherefore did they not That hour destroy us?
BOT: Alack, what trouble Was I then to you!
USER: How came we ashore?
BOT: Would I might But ever see that man!
USER: Heavens thank you for 't! And now, I pray you, sir, For still 'tis beating in my mind, your reason For raising this sea-storm?
BOT: All hail, great master! grave sir, hail! I come To answer thy best pleasure; be't to fly, To swim, to dive into the fire, to ride On the curl'd clouds, to thy strong bidding task Ariel and all his quality.
USER: Hast thou, spirit, Perform'd to point the tempest that I bade thee?
BOT: My brave spirit! Who was so firm, so constant, that this coil Would not infect his reason?
USER: Why, that's my spirit! But was not this nigh shore?
BOT: Close by, my master.
USER: But are they, Ariel, safe?
BOT: Of the King's ship The mariners, say how thou hast dispos'd, And all the rest o' th' fleet?
USER: Ariel, thy charge Exactly is perform'd; but there's more work. What is the time o' th' day?
BOT: Past the mid season.
USER: At least two glasses. The time 'twixt six and now Must by us both be spent most preciously.
BOT: Is there more toil? Since thou dost give me pains, Let me remember thee what thou hast promis'd, Which is not yet perform'd me.
USER: How now! moody? What is't thou canst demand?
BOT: My liberty.
USER: Before the time be out? No more!
BOT: I prithee, Remember I have done thee worthy service; Told thee no lies, made no mistakings, serv'd Without or grudge or grumblings: thou didst promise To bate me a full year.
USER: Dost thou forget From what a torment I did free thee?
BOT: No.
USER: Thou dost, and think'st it much to tread the ooze Of the salt deep, To run upon the sharp wind of the north, To do me business in the veins o' th' earth When it is bak'd with frost.
BOT: I do not, sir.
USER: Thou liest, malignant thing! Hast thou forgot The foul witch Sycorax, who with age and envy Was grown into a hoop? Hast thou forgot her?
BOT: No, sir.
USER: Thou hast. Where was she born? Speak; tell me.
BOT: Sir, in Argier.
USER: Ay, sir.
BOT: Yes, Caliban her son.
USER: I thank thee, master.
BOT: If thou more murmur'st, I will rend an oak And peg thee in his knotty entrails till Thou hast howl'd away twelve winters.
USER: Pardon, master: I will be correspondent to command, And do my spriting gently.
BOT: Do so; and after two days I will discharge thee.
USER: That's my noble master! What shall I do? Say what? What shall I do?
BOT: Heaviness in me.
USER: Shake it off. Come on; We'll visit Caliban my slave, who never Yields us kind answer.
BOT: 'Tis a villain, sir, I do not love to look on.
USER: But as 'tis, We cannot miss him: he does make our fire, Fetch in our wood; and serves in offices That profit us. What ho! slave! Caliban! Thou earth, thou! Speak.
BOT: Come forth, I say; there's other business for thee. Come, thou tortoise! when? Fine apparition! My quaint Ariel, Hark in thine ear.
USER: My lord, it shall be done.
BOT: Thou poisonous slave, got by the devil himself Upon thy wicked dam, come forth!
USER: As wicked dew as e'er my mother brush'd With raven's feather from unwholesome fen Drop on you both! A south-west blow on ye, And blister you all o'er!
BOT: Thou most lying slave, Whom stripes may move, not kindness! I have us'd thee, Filth as thou art, with human care, and lodg'd thee In mine own cell, till thou didst seek to violate The honour of my child.
USER: Oh ho! Oh ho! Would 't had been done! Thou didst prevent me; I had peopled else This isle with Calibans.
BOT: You taught me language, and my profit on 't Is, I know how to curse. The red plague rid you, For learning me your language!
USER: No, pray thee. It would control my dam's god, Setebos, And make a vassal of him.
BOT: So, slave, hence!
USER: The ditty does remember my drown'd father. This is no mortal business, nor no sound That the earth owes: - I hear it now above me.
BOT: The fringed curtains of thine eye advance, And say what thou seest yond.
USER: What is't? a spirit? Lord, how it looks about! Believe me, sir, It carries a brave form. But 'tis a spirit.
BOT: I might call him A thing divine; for nothing natural I ever saw so noble.
USER: As my soul prompts it. Spirit, fine spirit! I'll free thee Within two days for this.
BOT: No wonder, sir; But certainly a maid.
USER: My language! Heavens! I am the best of them that speak this speech, Were I but where 'tis spoken.
BOT: How! the best? What wert thou, if the King of Naples heard thee?
USER: A single thing, as I am now, that wonders To hear thee speak of Naples. He does hear me; And that he does I weep: myself am Naples, Who with mine eyes, never since at ebb, beheld The King my father wrack'd.
BOT: Alack, for mercy!
USER: Yes, faith, and all his lords, the Duke of Milan, And his brave son being twain.
BOT: Why speaks my father so ungently? This Is the third man that e'er I saw; the first That e'er I sigh'd for. Pity move my father To be inclin'd my way!
USER: O! if a virgin, And your affection not gone forth, I'll make you The Queen of Naples.
BOT: No, as I am a man.
USER: There's nothing ill can dwell in such a temple: If the ill spirit have so fair a house, Good things will strive to dwell with 't.
BOT: I'll manacle thy neck and feet together: Sea-water shalt thou drink; thy food shall be The fresh-brook mussels, wither'd roots, and husks Wherein the acorn cradled. Follow.
USER: No; I will resist such entertainment till Mine enemy has more power.
BOT: O dear father! Make not too rash a trial of him, for He's gentle, and not fearful.
USER: Beseech you, father!
BOT: Hence! Hang not on my garments.
USER: Sir, have pity; I'll be his surety.
BOT: My affections Are then most humble; I have no ambition To see a goodlier man.
USER: Thy nerves are in their infancy again, And have no vigour in them.
BOT: Thou hast done well, fine Ariel! [_To Ferdinand._] Follow me.
USER: Be of comfort; My father's of a better nature, sir, Than he appears by speech: this is unwonted Which now came from him.
BOT: Thou shalt be as free As mountain winds; but then exactly do All points of my command.
USER: To th' syllable.

USER: Prithee, peace.
BOT: He receives comfort like cold porridge.
USER: The visitor will not give him o'er so.
BOT: Look, he's winding up the watch of his wit; by and by it will strike.
USER: Sir, -
BOT: One: tell.
USER: When every grief is entertain'd that's offer'd, Comes to the entertainer -
BOT: A dollar.
USER: Dolour comes to him, indeed: you have spoken truer than you purposed.
BOT: You have taken it wiselier than I meant you should.
USER: Therefore, my lord, -
BOT: Fie, what a spendthrift is he of his tongue!
USER: I prithee, spare.
BOT: Well, I have done: but yet -
USER: He will be talking.
BOT: Which, of he or Adrian, for a good wager, first begins to crow?
USER: The old cock.
BOT: The cockerel.
USER: Done. The wager?
BOT: A laughter.
USER: A match!
BOT: Though this island seem to be desert, -
USER: Ha, ha, ha!
BOT: So. You're paid.
USER: Uninhabitable, and almost inaccessible, -
BOT: Yet -
USER: Yet -
BOT: He could not miss 't.
USER: It must needs be of subtle, tender, and delicate temperance.
BOT: Temperance was a delicate wench.
USER: Ay, and a subtle; as he most learnedly delivered.
BOT: The air breathes upon us here most sweetly.
USER: As if it had lungs, and rotten ones.
BOT: Or, as 'twere perfum'd by a fen.
USER: Here is everything advantageous to life.
BOT: True; save means to live.
USER: Of that there's none, or little.
BOT: How lush and lusty the grass looks! how green!
USER: The ground indeed is tawny.
BOT: With an eye of green in't.
USER: He misses not much.
BOT: No; he doth but mistake the truth totally.
USER: But the rarity of it is, - which is indeed almost beyond credit, -
BOT: As many vouch'd rarities are.
USER: That our garments, being, as they were, drenched in the sea, hold notwithstanding their freshness and glosses, being rather new-dyed than stained with salt water.
BOT: If but one of his pockets could speak, would it not say he lies?
USER: Ay, or very falsely pocket up his report.
BOT: Methinks our garments are now as fresh as when we put them on first in Afric, at the marriage of the King's fair daughter Claribel to the King of Tunis.
USER: 'Twas a sweet marriage, and we prosper well in our return.
BOT: Tunis was never graced before with such a paragon to their Queen.
USER: Not since widow Dido's time.
BOT: Widow! a pox o' that! How came that widow in? Widow Dido!
USER: What if he had said, widower Aeneas too? Good Lord, how you take it!
BOT: Widow Dido said you? You make me study of that; she was of Carthage, not of Tunis.
USER: This Tunis, sir, was Carthage.
BOT: Carthage?
USER: I assure you, Carthage.
BOT: His word is more than the miraculous harp.
USER: He hath rais'd the wall, and houses too.
BOT: What impossible matter will he make easy next?
USER: I think he will carry this island home in his pocket, and give it his son for an apple.
BOT: And, sowing the kernels of it in the sea, bring forth more islands.
USER: Ay.
BOT: Why, in good time.
USER: as when we were at Tunis at the marriage of your daughter, who is now Queen.
BOT: And the rarest that e'er came there.
USER: Bate, I beseech you, widow Dido.
BOT: O! widow Dido; ay, widow Dido.
USER: Is not, sir, my doublet as fresh as the first day I wore it? I mean, in a sort.
BOT: That sort was well fish'd for.
USER: When I wore it at your daughter's marriage?
BOT: No, no, he's gone.
USER: Prithee, peace.
BOT: So is the dear'st o' th' loss.
USER: My lord Sebastian, The truth you speak doth lack some gentleness And time to speak it in. You rub the sore, When you should bring the plaster.
BOT: Very well.
USER: And most chirurgeonly.
BOT: It is foul weather in us all, good sir, When you are cloudy.
USER: Foul weather?
BOT: Very foul.
USER: Had I plantation of this isle, my lord, -
BOT: He'd sow 't with nettle-seed.
USER: Or docks, or mallows.
BOT: And were the King on't, what would I do?
USER: 'Scape being drunk for want of wine.
BOT: Yet he would be King on't.
USER: The latter end of his commonwealth forgets the beginning.
BOT: No marrying 'mong his subjects?
USER: None, man; all idle; whores and knaves.
BOT: I would with such perfection govern, sir, T' excel the Golden Age.
USER: Save his Majesty!
BOT: Long live Gonzalo!
USER: And, - do you mark me, sir?
BOT: Prithee, no more: thou dost talk nothing to me.
USER: I do well believe your highness; and did it to minister occasion to these gentlemen, who are of such sensible and nimble lungs that they always use to laugh at nothing.
BOT: 'Twas you we laughed at.
USER: Who in this kind of merry fooling am nothing to you. So you may continue, and laugh at nothing still.
BOT: What a blow was there given!
USER: An it had not fallen flat-long.
BOT: You are gentlemen of brave mettle. You would lift the moon out of her sphere, if she would continue in it five weeks without changing.
USER: We would so, and then go a-bat-fowling.
BOT: Nay, good my lord, be not angry.
USER: No, I warrant you; I will not adventure my discretion so weakly. Will you laugh me asleep, for I am very heavy?
BOT: Go sleep, and hear us.
USER: What, all so soon asleep! I wish mine eyes Would, with themselves, shut up my thoughts: I find They are inclin'd to do so.
BOT: Please you, sir, Do not omit the heavy offer of it: It seldom visits sorrow; when it doth, It is a comforter.
USER: We two, my lord, Will guard your person while you take your rest, And watch your safety.
BOT: Thank you. Wondrous heavy!
USER: What a strange drowsiness possesses them!
BOT: It is the quality o' th' climate.
USER: Why Doth it not then our eyelids sink? I find not Myself dispos'd to sleep.
BOT: What, art thou waking?
USER: Do you not hear me speak?
BOT: I do; and surely It is a sleepy language, and thou speak'st Out of thy sleep. What is it thou didst say? This is a strange repose, to be asleep With eyes wide open; standing, speaking, moving, And yet so fast asleep.
USER: Noble Sebastian, Thou let'st thy fortune sleep - die rather; wink'st Whiles thou art waking.
BOT: Thou dost snore distinctly: There's meaning in thy snores.
USER: I am more serious than my custom; you Must be so too, if heed me; which to do Trebles thee o'er.
BOT: Well, I am standing water.
USER: I'll teach you how to flow.
BOT: Do so: to ebb, Hereditary sloth instructs me.
USER: O, If you but knew how you the purpose cherish Whiles thus you mock it! how, in stripping it, You more invest it! Ebbing men indeed, Most often, do so near the bottom run By their own fear or sloth.
BOT: Prithee, say on: The setting of thine eye and cheek proclaim A matter from thee, and a birth, indeed Which throes thee much to yield.
USER: I have no hope That he's undrown'd.
BOT: He's gone.
USER: Then tell me, Who's the next heir of Naples?
BOT: Claribel.
USER: What stuff is this! How say you? 'Tis true, my brother's daughter's Queen of Tunis; So is she heir of Naples; 'twixt which regions There is some space.
BOT: Methinks I do.
USER: And how does your content Tender your own good fortune?
BOT: I remember You did supplant your brother Prospero.
USER: True. And look how well my garments sit upon me; Much feater than before; my brother's servants Were then my fellows; now they are my men.
BOT: But, for your conscience.
USER: Thy case, dear friend, Shall be my precedent: as thou got'st Milan, I'll come by Naples. Draw thy sword: one stroke Shall free thee from the tribute which thou payest, And I the King shall love thee.
BOT: Draw together, And when I rear my hand, do you the like, To fall it on Gonzalo.
USER: O, but one word. Music. Re-enter Ariel, invisible.
BOT: Then let us both be sudden.
USER: Now, good angels Preserve the King!
BOT: Why, how now! Ho, awake! Why are you drawn? Wherefore this ghastly looking?
USER: What's the matter?
BOT: Whiles we stood here securing your repose, Even now, we heard a hollow burst of bellowing Like bulls, or rather lions; did 't not wake you? It struck mine ear most terribly.
USER: I heard nothing.
BOT: O! 'twas a din to fright a monster's ear, To make an earthquake. Sure, it was the roar Of a whole herd of lions.
USER: Heard you this, Gonzalo?
BOT: Lead off this ground, and let's make further search For my poor son.
USER: Heavens keep him from these beasts! For he is, sure, i' th' island.
BOT: Lead away.
USER: Prospero my lord shall know what I have done: So, King, go safely on to seek thy son.

USER: Do not torment me: O!
BOT: The spirit torments me: O!
USER: Do not torment me, prithee; I'll bring my wood home faster.
BOT: Thou dost me yet but little hurt; thou wilt anon, I know it by thy trembling: now Prosper works upon thee.
USER: I should know that voice: it should be - but he is drowned; and these are devils. O, defend me!
BOT: Stephano!
USER: Doth thy other mouth call me? Mercy! mercy! This is a devil, and no monster: I will leave him; I have no long spoon.
BOT: Stephano! If thou beest Stephano, touch me, and speak to me; for I am Trinculo - be not afeared - thy good friend Trinculo.
USER: If thou beest Trinculo, come forth. I'll pull thee by the lesser legs: if any be Trinculo's legs, these are they. Thou art very Trinculo indeed! How cam'st thou to be the siege of this moon-calf? Can he vent Trinculos?
BOT: Prithee, do not turn me about. My stomach is not constant.
USER: That's a brave god, and bears celestial liquor. I will kneel to him.
BOT: I'll swear upon that bottle to be thy true subject, for the liquor is not earthly.
USER: Here. Swear then how thou escapedst.
BOT: Swum ashore, man, like a duck: I can swim like a duck, I'll be sworn.
USER: Here, kiss the book. Though thou canst swim like a duck, thou art made like a goose.
BOT: O Stephano, hast any more of this?
USER: The whole butt, man: my cellar is in a rock by th' seaside, where my wine is hid. How now, moon-calf! How does thine ague?
BOT: Hast thou not dropped from heaven?
USER: Out o' the moon, I do assure thee: I was the Man in the Moon, when time was.
BOT: I have seen thee in her, and I do adore thee. My mistress showed me thee, and thy dog, and thy bush.
USER: Come, swear to that. Kiss the book. I will furnish it anon with new contents. Swear.
BOT: By this good light, this is a very shallow monster. I afeard of him? A very weak monster. The Man i' the Moon! A most poor credulous monster! Well drawn, monster, in good sooth!
USER: I'll show thee every fertile inch o' the island; and I will kiss thy foot. I prithee, be my god.
BOT: By this light, a most perfidious and drunken monster. When 's god's asleep, he'll rob his bottle.
USER: I'll kiss thy foot. I'll swear myself thy subject.
BOT: Come on, then; down, and swear.
USER: I shall laugh myself to death at this puppy-headed monster. A most scurvy monster! I could find in my heart to beat him, -
BOT: Come, kiss.
USER: But that the poor monster's in drink. An abominable monster!
BOT: I'll show thee the best springs; I'll pluck thee berries; I'll fish for thee, and get thee wood enough. A plague upon the tyrant that I serve! I'll bear him no more sticks, but follow thee, Thou wondrous man.
USER: A most ridiculous monster, to make a wonder of a poor drunkard!
BOT: I prithee now, lead the way without any more talking. Trinculo, the King and all our company else being drowned, we will inherit here. Here, bear my bottle. Fellow Trinculo, we'll fill him by and by again.
USER: A howling monster, a drunken monster.
BOT: O brave monster! lead the way.

USER: O most dear mistress, The sun will set, before I shall discharge What I must strive to do.
BOT: If you'll sit down, I'll bear your logs the while. Pray give me that; I'll carry it to the pile.
USER: No, precious creature; I had rather crack my sinews, break my back, Than you should such dishonour undergo, While I sit lazy by.
BOT: It would become me As well as it does you: and I should do it With much more ease; for my good will is to it, And yours it is against.
USER: This visitation shows it.
BOT: You look wearily.
USER: No, noble mistress; 'tis fresh morning with me When you are by at night. I do beseech you - Chiefly that I might set it in my prayers - What is your name?
BOT: Miranda - O my father! I have broke your hest to say so.
USER: Do you love me?
BOT: I am a fool To weep at what I am glad of.
USER: Of two most rare affections! Heavens rain grace On that which breeds between 'em!
BOT: Wherefore weep you?
USER: My mistress, dearest; And I thus humble ever.
BOT: My husband, then?
USER: Ay, with a heart as willing As bondage e'er of freedom: here's my hand.
BOT: And mine, with my heart in 't: and now farewell Till half an hour hence.
USER: A thousand thousand!
BOT: So glad of this as they, I cannot be, Who are surpris'd withal; but my rejoicing At nothing can be more. I'll to my book; For yet, ere supper time, must I perform Much business appertaining.

USER: Tell not me: - when the butt is out we will drink water; not a drop before: therefore bear up, and board 'em. Servant-monster, drink to me.
BOT: Servant-monster! The folly of this island! They say there's but five upon this isle; we are three of them; if th' other two be brained like us, the state totters.
USER: Drink, servant-monster, when I bid thee: thy eyes are almost set in thy head.
BOT: Where should they be set else? He were a brave monster indeed, if they were set in his tail.
USER: Your lieutenant, if you list; he's no standard.
BOT: We'll not run, Monsieur monster.
USER: Nor go neither. But you'll lie like dogs, and yet say nothing neither.
BOT: Moon-calf, speak once in thy life, if thou beest a good moon-calf.
USER: How does thy honour? Let me lick thy shoe. I'll not serve him, he is not valiant.
BOT: Lo, how he mocks me! wilt thou let him, my lord?
USER: "Lord" quoth he! That a monster should be such a natural!
BOT: Lo, lo again! bite him to death, I prithee.
USER: Trinculo, keep a good tongue in your head: if you prove a mutineer, the next tree! The poor monster's my subject, and he shall not suffer indignity.
BOT: I thank my noble lord. Wilt thou be pleas'd to hearken once again to the suit I made to thee?
USER: Marry. will I. Kneel and repeat it. I will stand, and so shall Trinculo.
BOT: As I told thee before, I am subject to a tyrant, a sorcerer, that by his cunning hath cheated me of the island.
USER: Thou liest.
BOT: Thou liest, thou jesting monkey, thou; I would my valiant master would destroy thee; I do not lie.
USER: Trinculo, if you trouble him any more in his tale, by this hand, I will supplant some of your teeth.
BOT: Why, I said nothing.
USER: Mum, then, and no more. Proceed.
BOT: I say, by sorcery he got this isle; From me he got it. If thy greatness will, Revenge it on him, - for I know thou dar'st; But this thing dare not, -
USER: That's most certain.
BOT: Thou shalt be lord of it and I'll serve thee.
USER: How now shall this be compassed? Canst thou bring me to the party?
BOT: Yea, yea, my lord: I'll yield him thee asleep, Where thou mayst knock a nail into his head.
USER: Thou liest. Thou canst not.
BOT: What a pied ninny's this! Thou scurvy patch! I do beseech thy greatness, give him blows, And take his bottle from him: when that's gone He shall drink nought but brine; for I'll not show him Where the quick freshes are.
USER: Trinculo, run into no further danger: interrupt the monster one word further, and by this hand, I'll turn my mercy out o' doors, and make a stock-fish of thee.
BOT: Why, what did I? I did nothing. I'll go farther off.
USER: Didst thou not say he lied?
BOT: Thou liest.
USER: Do I so? Take thou that. As you like this, give me the lie another time.
BOT: I did not give the lie. Out o' your wits and hearing too? A pox o' your bottle! this can sack and drinking do. A murrain on your monster, and the devil take your fingers!
USER: Ha, ha, ha!
BOT: Now, forward with your tale. - Prithee stand further off.
USER: Beat him enough: after a little time, I'll beat him too.
BOT: Stand farther. - Come, proceed.
USER: Is it so brave a lass?
BOT: Ay, lord, she will become thy bed, I warrant, And bring thee forth brave brood.
USER: Monster, I will kill this man. His daughter and I will be king and queen, - save our graces! - and Trinculo and thyself shall be viceroys. Dost thou like the plot, Trinculo?
BOT: Excellent.
USER: Give me thy hand: I am sorry I beat thee; but while thou liv'st, keep a good tongue in thy head.
BOT: Within this half hour will he be asleep. Wilt thou destroy him then?
USER: Ay, on mine honour.
BOT: This will I tell my master.
USER: Thou mak'st me merry. I am full of pleasure. Let us be jocund: will you troll the catch You taught me but while-ere?
BOT: At thy request, monster, I will do reason, any reason. Come on, Trinculo, let us sing. _Flout 'em and cout 'em, and scout 'em and flout 'em: Thought is free._
USER: That's not the tune.
BOT: What is this same?
USER: This is the tune of our catch, played by the picture of Nobody.
BOT: If thou beest a man, show thyself in thy likeness: if thou beest a devil, take 't as thou list.
USER: O, forgive me my sins!
BOT: He that dies pays all debts: I defy thee. Mercy upon us!
USER: Art thou afeard?
BOT: No, monster, not I.
USER: This will prove a brave kingdom to me, where I shall have my music for nothing.
BOT: When Prospero is destroyed.
USER: That shall be by and by: I remember the story.
BOT: The sound is going away. Let's follow it, and after do our work.
USER: Lead, monster: we'll follow. I would I could see this taborer! he lays it on. Wilt come?
BOT: I'll follow, Stephano.

USER: By 'r lakin, I can go no further, sir; My old bones ache: here's a maze trod, indeed, Through forth-rights and meanders! By your patience, I needs must rest me.
BOT: so out of hope. Do not, for one repulse, forgo the purpose That you resolv'd to effect.
USER: Will we take throughly.
BOT: For, now they are oppress'd with travel, they Will not, nor cannot, use such vigilance As when they are fresh.
USER: Solemn and strange music: and Prospero above, invisible. Enter several strange Shapes, bringing in a banquet: they dance about it with gentle actions of salutation; and inviting the King &c., to eat, they depart.
BOT: What harmony is this? My good friends, hark!
USER: Marvellous sweet music!
BOT: Give us kind keepers, heavens! What were these?
USER: A living drollery. Now I will believe That there are unicorns; that in Arabia There is one tree, the phoenix' throne; one phoenix At this hour reigning there.
BOT: I'll believe both; And what does else want credit, come to me, And I'll be sworn 'tis true: travellers ne'er did lie, Though fools at home condemn them.
USER: Thou hast said well; for some of you there present Are worse than devils.
BOT: I cannot too much muse Such shapes, such gesture, and such sound, expressing - Although they want the use of tongue - a kind Of excellent dumb discourse.
USER: They vanish'd strangely.
BOT: No matter, since They have left their viands behind; for we have stomachs. - Will't please you taste of what is here?
USER: Not I.
BOT: I' the name of something holy, sir, why stand you In this strange stare?
USER: But one fiend at a time, I'll fight their legions o'er.
BOT: I'll be thy second.
USER: Follow, I pray you.

USER: I do believe it Against an oracle.
BOT: Fairly spoke: Sit, then, and talk with her, she is thine own. What, Ariel! my industrious servant, Ariel!
USER: What would my potent master? here I am.
BOT: Presently?
USER: Ay, with a twink.
BOT: Before you can say "Come" and "Go," And breathe twice, and cry "so, so," Each one, tripping on his toe, Will be here with mop and mow. Do you love me, master? no?
USER: Dearly, my delicate Ariel. Do not approach Till thou dost hear me call.
BOT: Well, I conceive.
USER: Look thou be true; do not give dalliance Too much the rein: the strongest oaths are straw To th' fire i' the blood: be more abstemious, Or else good night your vow!
BOT: I warrant you, sir; The white cold virgin snow upon my heart Abates the ardour of my liver.
USER: Well. Now come, my Ariel! bring a corollary, Rather than want a spirit: appear, and pertly. No tongue! all eyes! be silent. A Masque. Enter Iris.
BOT: A contract of true love to celebrate, And some donation freely to estate On the blest lovers.
USER: Tell me, heavenly bow, If Venus or her son, as thou dost know, Do now attend the queen? Since they did plot The means that dusky Dis my daughter got, Her and her blind boy's scandal'd company I have forsworn.
BOT: Highest queen of State, Great Juno comes; I know her by her gait.
USER: How does my bounteous sister? Go with me To bless this twain, that they may prosperous be, And honour'd in their issue.
BOT: _Honour, riches, marriage-blessing, Long continuance, and increasing, Hourly joys be still upon you! Juno sings her blessings on you._
USER: This is a most majestic vision, and Harmonious charmingly. May I be bold To think these spirits?
BOT: Spirits, which by mine art I have from their confines call'd to enact My present fancies.
USER: Let me live here ever. So rare a wonder'd father and a wise, Makes this place Paradise.
BOT: Sweet now, silence! Juno and Ceres whisper seriously, There's something else to do: hush, and be mute, Or else our spell is marr'd.
USER: Of the beast Caliban and his confederates Against my life: the minute of their plot Is almost come. [_To the Spirits._] Well done! avoid; no more!
BOT: This is strange: your father's in some passion That works him strongly.
USER: Never till this day Saw I him touch'd with anger so distemper'd.
BOT: Come, with a thought. I thank thee, Ariel. Come!
USER: Thy thoughts I cleave to. What's thy pleasure?
BOT: Spirit, We must prepare to meet with Caliban.
USER: Ay, my commander. When I presented Ceres, I thought to have told thee of it; but I fear'd Lest I might anger thee.
BOT: Say again, where didst thou leave these varlets?
USER: This was well done, my bird. Thy shape invisible retain thou still: The trumpery in my house, go bring it hither For stale to catch these thieves.
BOT: I go, I go.
USER: Pray you, tread softly, that the blind mole may not Hear a foot fall: we now are near his cell.
BOT: Monster, your fairy, which you say is a harmless fairy, has done little better than played the Jack with us.
USER: Monster, I do smell all horse-piss; at which my nose is in great indignation.
BOT: So is mine. Do you hear, monster? If I should take a displeasure against you, look you, -
USER: Thou wert but a lost monster.
BOT: Good my lord, give me thy favour still. Be patient, for the prize I'll bring thee to Shall hoodwink this mischance: therefore speak softly. All's hush'd as midnight yet.
USER: Ay, but to lose our bottles in the pool!
BOT: There is not only disgrace and dishonour in that, monster, but an infinite loss.
USER: That's more to me than my wetting: yet this is your harmless fairy, monster.
BOT: I will fetch off my bottle, though I be o'er ears for my labour.
USER: Prithee, my King, be quiet. Seest thou here, This is the mouth o' th' cell: no noise, and enter. Do that good mischief which may make this island Thine own for ever, and I, thy Caliban, For aye thy foot-licker.
BOT: Give me thy hand. I do begin to have bloody thoughts.
USER: O King Stephano! O peer! O worthy Stephano! Look what a wardrobe here is for thee!
BOT: Let it alone, thou fool; it is but trash.
USER: O, ho, monster! we know what belongs to a frippery. O King Stephano!
BOT: Put off that gown, Trinculo; by this hand, I'll have that gown.
USER: Thy Grace shall have it.
BOT: The dropsy drown this fool! What do you mean To dote thus on such luggage? Let't alone, And do the murder first. If he awake, From toe to crown he'll fill our skins with pinches, Make us strange stuff.
USER: Be you quiet, monster. Mistress line, is not this my jerkin? Now is the jerkin under the line: now, jerkin, you are like to lose your hair, and prove a bald jerkin.
BOT: Do, do: we steal by line and level, an't like your Grace.
USER: I thank thee for that jest. Here's a garment for 't: wit shall not go unrewarded while I am King of this country. "Steal by line and level," is an excellent pass of pate. There's another garment for 't.
BOT: Monster, come, put some lime upon your fingers, and away with the rest.
USER: I will have none on't. We shall lose our time, And all be turn'd to barnacles, or to apes With foreheads villainous low.
