BOT: Monster, lay-to your fingers: help to bear this away where my hogshead of wine is, or I'll turn you out of my kingdom. Go to, carry this.
USER: And this.
BOT: Ay, and this. A noise of hunters heard. Enter divers Spirits, in shape of dogs and hounds, and hunt them about; Prospero and Ariel setting them on.
USER: Hey, Mountain, hey!
BOT: Silver! there it goes, Silver!
USER: Hark, they roar.
BOT: Let them be hunted soundly. At this hour Lies at my mercy all mine enemies. Shortly shall all my labours end, and thou Shalt have the air at freedom. For a little Follow, and do me service.

USER: Now does my project gather to a head: My charms crack not; my spirits obey, and time Goes upright with his carriage. How's the day?
BOT: On the sixth hour; at which time, my lord, You said our work should cease.
USER: I did say so, When first I rais'd the tempest. Say, my spirit, How fares the King and 's followers?
BOT: Dost thou think so, spirit?
USER: Mine would, sir, were I human.
BOT: I'll fetch them, sir.
USER: I drink the air before me, and return Or ere your pulse twice beat.
BOT: All torment, trouble, wonder and amazement Inhabits here. Some heavenly power guide us Out of this fearful country!
USER: Behold, sir King, The wronged Duke of Milan, Prospero. For more assurance that a living prince Does now speak to thee, I embrace thy body; And to thee and thy company I bid A hearty welcome.
BOT: First, noble friend, Let me embrace thine age, whose honour cannot Be measur'd or confin'd.
USER: Whether this be Or be not, I'll not swear.
BOT: No. For you, most wicked sir, whom to call brother Would even infect my mouth, I do forgive Thy rankest fault, all of them; and require My dukedom of thee, which perforce I know Thou must restore.
USER: I am woe for 't, sir.
BOT: Irreparable is the loss, and patience Says it is past her cure.
USER: I rather think You have not sought her help, of whose soft grace, For the like loss I have her sovereign aid, And rest myself content.
BOT: You the like loss!
USER: As great to me, as late; and, supportable To make the dear loss, have I means much weaker Than you may call to comfort you, for I Have lost my daughter.
BOT: A daughter? O heavens, that they were living both in Naples, The King and Queen there! That they were, I wish Myself were mudded in that oozy bed Where my son lies. When did you lose your daughter?
USER: Sweet lord, you play me false.
BOT: No, my dearest love, I would not for the world.
USER: Yes, for a score of kingdoms you should wrangle, And I would call it fair play.
BOT: If this prove A vision of the island, one dear son Shall I twice lose.
USER: A most high miracle!
BOT: Though the seas threaten, they are merciful. I have curs'd them without cause.
USER: Now all the blessings Of a glad father compass thee about! Arise, and say how thou cam'st here.
BOT: O, wonder! How many goodly creatures are there here! How beauteous mankind is! O brave new world That has such people in 't!
USER: 'Tis new to thee.
BOT: What is this maid, with whom thou wast at play? Your eld'st acquaintance cannot be three hours: Is she the goddess that hath sever'd us, And brought us thus together?
USER: I am hers: But, O, how oddly will it sound that I Must ask my child forgiveness!
BOT: There, sir, stop: Let us not burden our remembrances with A heaviness that's gone.
USER: I have inly wept, Or should have spoke ere this. Look down, you gods, And on this couple drop a blessed crown; For it is you that have chalk'd forth the way Which brought us hither.
BOT: I say, Amen, Gonzalo!
USER: Let grief and sorrow still embrace his heart That doth not wish you joy!
BOT: The best news is that we have safely found Our King and company. The next, our ship, - Which but three glasses since, we gave out split, Is tight and yare, and bravely rigg'd as when We first put out to sea.
USER: Have I done since I went.
BOT: These are not natural events; they strengthen From strange to stranger. Say, how came you hither?
USER: This is as strange a maze as e'er men trod; And there is in this business more than nature Was ever conduct of: some oracle Must rectify our knowledge.
BOT: Every man shift for all the rest, and let no man take care for himself, for all is but fortune. - Coragio! bully-monster, coragio!
USER: If these be true spies which I wear in my head, here's a goodly sight.
BOT: O Setebos, these be brave spirits indeed. How fine my master is! I am afraid He will chastise me.
USER: Ha, ha! What things are these, my lord Antonio? Will money buy them?
BOT: Very like; one of them Is a plain fish, and, no doubt, marketable.
USER: I shall be pinch'd to death.
BOT: Is not this Stephano, my drunken butler?
USER: He is drunk now: where had he wine?
BOT: And Trinculo is reeling-ripe: where should they Find this grand liquor that hath gilded 'em? How cam'st thou in this pickle?
USER: I have been in such a pickle since I saw you last that, I fear me, will never out of my bones. I shall not fear fly-blowing.
BOT: Why, how now, Stephano!
USER: O! touch me not. I am not Stephano, but a cramp.
BOT: You'd be King o' the isle, sirrah?
USER: I should have been a sore one, then.
BOT: This is as strange a thing as e'er I look'd on.
USER: He is as disproportioned in his manners As in his shape. Go, sirrah, to my cell; Take with you your companions. As you look To have my pardon, trim it handsomely.
BOT: Ay, that I will; and I'll be wise hereafter, And seek for grace. What a thrice-double ass Was I, to take this drunkard for a god, And worship this dull fool!
USER: Go to; away!
BOT: Hence, and bestow your luggage where you found it.
USER: Or stole it, rather.
BOT: I long To hear the story of your life, which must Take the ear strangely.

USER: Good day, sir.
BOT: I am glad you're well.
USER: I have not seen you long. How goes the world?
BOT: It wears, sir, as it grows.
USER: Ay, that's well known. But what particular rarity? What strange, Which manifold record not matches? See, Magic of bounty, all these spirits thy power Hath conjured to attend! I know the merchant.
BOT: I know them both. Th' other's a jeweller.
USER: O, 'tis a worthy lord!
BOT: Nay, that's most fixed.
USER: A most incomparable man, breathed, as it were, To an untirable and continuate goodness. He passes.
BOT: I have a jewel here -
USER: O, pray let's see't. For the Lord Timon, sir?
BOT: If he will touch the estimate. But for that -
USER: When we for recompense have praised the vile, It stains the glory in that happy verse Which aptly sings the good.
BOT: 'Tis a good form.
USER: And rich. Here is a water, look ye.
BOT: You are rapt, sir, in some work, some dedication To the great lord.
USER: A picture, sir. When comes your book forth?
BOT: Upon the heels of my presentment, sir. Let's see your piece.
USER: 'Tis a good piece.
BOT: So 'tis. This comes off well and excellent.
USER: Indifferent.
BOT: Admirable! How this grace Speaks his own standing! What a mental power This eye shoots forth! How big imagination Moves in this lip! To th' dumbness of the gesture One might interpret.
USER: It is a pretty mocking of the life. Here is a touch. Is't good?
BOT: I'll say of it, It tutors nature. Artificial strife Lives in these touches livelier than life.
USER: How this lord is followed!
BOT: The senators of Athens, happy men!
USER: Look, more!
BOT: How shall I understand you?
USER: I saw them speak together.
BOT: Ay, marry, what of these?
USER: Imprisoned is he, say you?
BOT: Ay, my good lord. Five talents is his debt, His means most short, his creditors most strait. Your honourable letter he desires To those have shut him up, which, failing, Periods his comfort.
USER: Noble Ventidius. Well, I am not of that feather to shake off My friend when he must need me. I do know him A gentleman that well deserves a help, Which he shall have. I'll pay the debt and free him.
BOT: Your lordship ever binds him.
USER: Commend me to him, I will send his ransom; And, being enfranchised, bid him come to me. 'Tis not enough to help the feeble up, But to support him after. Fare you well.
BOT: All happiness to your honour.
USER: Lord Timon, hear me speak.
BOT: Freely, good father.
USER: Thou hast a servant named Lucilius.
BOT: I have so. What of him?
USER: Most noble Timon, call the man before thee.
BOT: Attends he here or no? Lucilius!
USER: Here, at your lordship's service.
BOT: This fellow here, Lord Timon, this thy creature, By night frequents my house. I am a man That from my first have been inclined to thrift, And my estate deserves an heir more raised Than one which holds a trencher.
USER: Well, what further?
BOT: The man is honest.
USER: Therefore he will be, Timon. His honesty rewards him in itself; It must not bear my daughter.
BOT: Does she love him?
USER: She is young and apt. Our own precedent passions do instruct us What levity's in youth.
BOT: Ay, my good lord, and she accepts of it.
USER: If in her marriage my consent be missing, I call the gods to witness, I will choose Mine heir from forth the beggars of the world And dispossess her all.
BOT: How shall she be endowed, If she be mated with an equal husband?
USER: Three talents on the present; in future, all.
BOT: This gentleman of mine hath served me long. To build his fortune I will strain a little, For 'tis a bond in men. Give him thy daughter. What you bestow, in him I'll counterpoise, And make him weigh with her.
USER: Most noble lord, Pawn me to this your honour, she is his.
BOT: My hand to thee; mine honour on my promise.
USER: Humbly I thank your lordship. Never may That state or fortune fall into my keeping Which is not owed to you.
BOT: Vouchsafe my labour, and long live your lordship.
USER: I thank you, you shall hear from me anon. Go not away. - What have you there, my friend?
BOT: A piece of painting, which I do beseech Your lordship to accept.
USER: The gods preserve you.
BOT: Well fare you, gentleman. Give me your hand. We must needs dine together. Sir, your jewel Hath suffered under praise.
USER: What, my lord, dispraise?
BOT: A mere satiety of commendations. If I should pay you for 't as 'tis extolled, It would unclew me quite.
USER: My lord, 'tis rated As those which sell would give. But you well know Things of like value, differing in the owners, Are prized by their masters. Believe't, dear lord, You mend the jewel by the wearing it.
BOT: Well mocked.
USER: No, my good lord, he speaks the common tongue, Which all men speak with him.
BOT: Look who comes here. Will you be chid?
USER: We'll bear, with your lordship.
BOT: He'll spare none.
USER: Good morrow to thee, gentle Apemantus.
BOT: Till I be gentle, stay thou for thy good morrow - When thou art Timon's dog, and these knaves honest.
USER: Why dost thou call them knaves? Thou know'st them not.
BOT: Are they not Athenians?
USER: Yes.
BOT: Then I repent not.
USER: You know me, Apemantus?
BOT: Thou know'st I do, I called thee by thy name.
USER: Thou art proud, Apemantus.
BOT: Of nothing so much as that I am not like Timon.
USER: Whither art going?
BOT: To knock out an honest Athenian's brains.
USER: That's a deed thou'lt die for.
BOT: Right, if doing nothing be death by th' law.
USER: How lik'st thou this picture, Apemantus?
BOT: The best, for the innocence.
USER: Wrought he not well that painted it?
BOT: He wrought better that made the painter, and yet he's but a filthy piece of work.
USER: You're a dog.
BOT: Thy mother's of my generation. What's she, if I be a dog?
USER: Wilt dine with me, Apemantus?
BOT: No, I eat not lords.
USER: An thou shouldst, thou'dst anger ladies.
BOT: O, they eat lords. So they come by great bellies.
USER: That's a lascivious apprehension.
BOT: So thou apprehend'st it, take it for thy labour.
USER: How dost thou like this jewel, Apemantus?
BOT: Not so well as plain-dealing, which will not cost a man a doit.
USER: What dost thou think 'tis worth?
BOT: Not worth my thinking. How now, poet?
USER: How now, philosopher?
BOT: Thou liest.
USER: Art not one?
BOT: Yes.
USER: Then I lie not.
BOT: Art not a poet?
USER: Yes.
BOT: Then thou liest. Look in thy last work, where thou hast feigned him a worthy fellow.
USER: That's not feigned, he is so.
BOT: Yes, he is worthy of thee, and to pay thee for thy labour. He that loves to be flattered is worthy o' th' flatterer. Heavens, that I were a lord!
USER: What wouldst do then, Apemantus?
BOT: E'en as Apemantus does now, hate a lord with my heart.
USER: What, thyself?
BOT: Ay.
USER: Wherefore?
BOT: That I had no angry wit to be a lord. Art not thou a merchant?
USER: Ay, Apemantus.
BOT: Traffic confound thee, if the gods will not.
USER: If traffic do it, the gods do it.
BOT: Traffic's thy god, and thy god confound thee! Trumpet sounds. Enter a Messenger.
USER: What trumpet's that?
BOT: 'Tis Alcibiades, and some twenty horse, All of companionship.
USER: Pray entertain them, give them guide to us. You must needs dine with me. Go not you hence Till I have thanked you; when dinner's done, Show me this piece. I am joyful of your sights. Most welcome, sir.
BOT: Aches contract and starve your supple joints! That there should be small love amongst these sweet knaves, And all this courtesy! The strain of man's bred out Into baboon and monkey.
USER: Sir, you have saved my longing, and I feed Most hungerly on your sight.
BOT: Right welcome, sir! Ere we depart we'll share a bounteous time In different pleasures. Pray you, let us in.
USER: What time o' day is't, Apemantus?
BOT: Time to be honest.
USER: That time serves still.
BOT: The more accursed thou, that still omitt'st it.
USER: Thou art going to Lord Timon's feast?
BOT: Ay, to see meat fill knaves and wine heat fools.
USER: Fare thee well, fare thee well.
BOT: Thou art a fool to bid me farewell twice.
USER: Why, Apemantus?
BOT: Shouldst have kept one to thyself, for I mean to give thee none.
USER: Hang thyself!
BOT: No, I will do nothing at thy bidding. Make thy requests to thy friend.
USER: Away, unpeaceable dog, or I'll spurn thee hence.
BOT: I will fly, like a dog, the heels o' th' ass.
USER: He's opposite to humanity. Come, shall we in And taste Lord Timon's bounty? He outgoes The very heart of kindness.
BOT: He pours it out; Plutus, the god of gold, Is but his steward. No meed but he repays Sevenfold above itself, no gift to him But breeds the giver a return exceeding All use of quittance.
USER: The noblest mind he carries That ever governed man.
BOT: Long may he live in fortunes. Shall we in?
USER: I'll keep you company.

USER: A noble spirit!
BOT: My lord, we always have confessed it.
USER: Ho, ho, confessed it? Hanged it, have you not?
BOT: O Apemantus, you are welcome.
USER: No, You shall not make me welcome. I come to have thee thrust me out of doors.
BOT: Let me stay at thine apperil, Timon. I come to observe; I give thee warning on't.
USER: I take no heed of thee. Thou'rt an Athenian, therefore, welcome. I myself would have no power; prithee; let my meat make thee silent.
BOT: My lord, in heart, and let the health go round.
USER: Let it flow this way, my good lord.
BOT: Captain Alcibiades, your heart's in the field now.
USER: My heart is ever at your service, my lord.
BOT: You had rather be at a breakfast of enemies than a dinner of friends.
USER: So they were bleeding new, my lord, there's no meat like 'em. I could wish my best friend at such a feast.
BOT: Would all those flatterers were thine enemies then, that then thou mightst kill 'em, and bid me to 'em.
USER: Might we but have that happiness, my lord, that you would once use our hearts, whereby we might express some part of our zeals, we should think ourselves for ever perfect.
BOT: Thou weep'st to make them drink, Timon.
USER: Joy had the like conception in our eyes And, at that instant like a babe sprung up.
BOT: Ho, ho! I laugh to think that babe a bastard.
USER: I promise you, my lord, you moved me much.
BOT: Much!
USER: What means that trump? How now?
BOT: Please you, my lord, there are certain ladies most desirous of admittance.
USER: Ladies? What are their wills?
BOT: There comes with them a forerunner, my lord, which bears that office, to signify their pleasures.
USER: I pray, let them be admitted.
BOT: They're welcome all, let 'em have kind admittance. Music, make their welcome!
USER: You see, my lord, how ample you're beloved. Music. Enter a masque of Ladies as Amazons, with lutes in their hands, dancing and playing.
BOT: My lord, you take us even at the best.
USER: Faith, for the worst is filthy and would not hold taking, I doubt me.
BOT: Ladies, there is an idle banquet attends you, Please you to dispose yourselves.
USER: Most thankfully, my lord.
BOT: Flavius!
USER: My lord?
BOT: The little casket bring me hither.
USER: Where be our men?
BOT: Here, my lord, in readiness.
USER: Our horses!
BOT: O, my friends, I have one word To say to you. Look you, my good lord, I must entreat you, honour me so much As to advance this jewel. Accept it and wear it, Kind my lord.
USER: I am so far already in your gifts -
BOT: So are we all.
USER: My lord, there are certain nobles of the Senate Newly alighted and come to visit you.
BOT: They are fairly welcome.
USER: I beseech your honour, Vouchsafe me a word. It does concern you near.
BOT: Near? Why then, another time I'll hear thee. I prithee let's be provided to show them entertainment.
USER: May it please your honour, Lord Lucius, Out of his free love, hath presented to you Four milk-white horses, trapped in silver.
BOT: I shall accept them fairly; let the presents Be worthily entertained. How now? What news?
USER: Please you, my lord, that honourable gentleman, Lord Lucullus, entreats your company tomorrow to hunt with him and has sent your honour two brace of greyhounds.
BOT: I'll hunt with him; and let them be received, Not without fair reward.
USER: You do yourselves much wrong, You bate too much of your own merits. Here, my lord, a trifle of our love.
BOT: With more than common thanks I will receive it.
USER: O, he's the very soul of bounty!
BOT: And now I remember, my lord, you gave good words the other day of a bay courser I rode on. 'Tis yours because you liked it.
USER: O, I beseech you, pardon me, my lord, in that.
BOT: You may take my word, my lord. I know no man Can justly praise but what he does affect. I weigh my friend's affection with mine own. I'll tell you true, I'll call to you.
USER: O, none so welcome!
BOT: Ay, defiled land, my lord.
USER: We are so virtuously bound -
BOT: And so am I to you.
USER: So infinitely endeared -
BOT: All to you. Lights, more lights!
USER: The best of happiness, honour, and fortunes keep with you, Lord Timon.
BOT: Ready for his friends.
USER: Now, Apemantus, if thou wert not sullen, I would be good to thee.
BOT: Nay, an you begin to rail on society once, I am sworn not to give regard to you. Farewell, and come with better music.
USER: So. Thou wilt not hear me now, thou shalt not then. I'll lock thy heaven from thee. O, that men's ears should be To counsel deaf, but not to flattery!

USER: Here, sir, what is your pleasure?
BOT: I go, sir.
USER: Take the bonds along with you, And have the dates in. Come.
BOT: I will, sir.
USER: Go.

USER: Good even, Varro. What, you come for money?
BOT: Is't not your business too?
USER: It is. And yours too, Isidore?
BOT: It is so.
USER: Would we were all discharged!
BOT: I fear it.
USER: Here comes the lord.
BOT: So soon as dinner's done, we'll forth again, My Alcibiades. With me? What is your will?
USER: My lord, here is a note of certain dues.
BOT: Dues? Whence are you?
USER: Of Athens here, my lord.
BOT: Go to my steward.
USER: Mine honest friend, I prithee but repair to me next morning.
BOT: Nay, good my lord -
USER: Contain thyself, good friend.
BOT: One Varro's servant, my good lord -
USER: From Isidore. He humbly prays your speedy payment.
BOT: If you did know, my lord, my master's wants -
USER: 'Twas due on forfeiture, my lord, six weeks and past.
BOT: Your steward puts me off, my lord, and I Am sent expressly to your lordship.
USER: Please you, gentlemen, The time is unagreeable to this business. Your importunacy cease till after dinner, That I may make his lordship understand Wherefore you are not paid.
BOT: Do so, my friends. See them well entertained.
USER: Pray, draw near.
BOT: Stay, stay, here comes the fool with Apemantus. Let's ha' some sport with 'em.
USER: Hang him, he'll abuse us.
BOT: A plague upon him, dog!
USER: How dost, fool?
BOT: Dost dialogue with thy shadow?
USER: I speak not to thee.
BOT: No, 'tis to thyself.
USER: No, thou stand'st single; thou'rt not on him yet.
BOT: Where's the fool now?
USER: He last asked the question. Poor rogues and usurers' men, bawds between gold and want.
BOT: What are we, Apemantus?
USER: Asses.
BOT: Why?
USER: That you ask me what you are, and do not know yourselves. Speak to 'em, fool.
BOT: How do you, gentlemen?
USER: Gramercies, good fool. How does your mistress?
BOT: She's e'en setting on water to scald such chickens as you are. Would we could see you at Corinth!
USER: Good, gramercy.
BOT: Look you, here comes my mistress' page.
USER: company? How dost thou, Apemantus?
BOT: Would I had a rod in my mouth, that I might answer thee profitably.
USER: Prithee, Apemantus, read me the superscription of these letters. I know not which is which.
BOT: Canst not read?
USER: No.
BOT: There will little learning die, then, that day thou art hanged. This is to Lord Timon, this to Alcibiades. Go, thou wast born a bastard, and thou'lt die a bawd.
USER: Thou wast whelped a dog, and thou shalt famish a dog's death. Answer not; I am gone.
BOT: E'en so thou outrunn'st grace. Fool, I will go with you to Lord Timon's.
USER: Will you leave me there?
BOT: If Timon stay at home. - You three serve three usurers?
USER: Ay, would they served us!
BOT: So would I - as good a trick as ever hangman served thief.
USER: Are you three usurers' men?
BOT: Ay, fool.
USER: I could render one.
BOT: Do it then, that we may account thee a whoremaster and a knave, which notwithstanding, thou shalt be no less esteemed.
USER: What is a whoremaster, fool?
BOT: Thou art not altogether a fool.
USER: Nor thou altogether a wise man. As much foolery as I have, so much wit thou lack'st.
BOT: That answer might have become Apemantus.
USER: Aside, aside, here comes Lord Timon.
BOT: Come with me, fool, come.
USER: I do not always follow lover, elder brother, and woman; sometime the philosopher.
BOT: Pray you walk near. I'll speak with you anon.
USER: You make me marvel wherefore ere this time Had you not fully laid my state before me, That I might so have rated my expense As I had leave of means.
BOT: You would not hear me, At many leisures I proposed.
USER: Go to. Perchance some single vantages you took When my indisposition put you back, And that unaptness made your minister Thus to excuse yourself.
BOT: Let all my land be sold.
USER: 'Tis all engaged, some forfeited and gone, And what remains will hardly stop the mouth Of present dues; the future comes apace. What shall defend the interim? And at length How goes our reckoning?
BOT: To Lacedaemon did my land extend.
USER: O my good lord, the world is but a word; Were it all yours to give it in a breath, How quickly were it gone!
BOT: You tell me true.
USER: Prithee, no more.
BOT: Assurance bless your thoughts!
USER: My lord, my lord.
BOT: I will dispatch you severally. [_To Servilius_.] You to Lord Lucius; and I am proud, say, that my occasions have found time to use 'em toward a supply of money. Let the request be fifty talents.
USER: As you have said, my lord.
BOT: Go you, sir, to the senators, Of whom, even to the state's best health, I have Deserved this hearing, Bid 'em send o' th' instant A thousand talents to me.
USER: I have been bold - For that I knew it the most general way - To them to use your signet and your name, But they do shake their heads, and I am here No richer in return.
BOT: Is't true? Can't be?
USER: I would I could not think it. That thought is bounty's foe; Being free itself, it thinks all others so.

USER: I have told my lord of you; he is coming down to you.
BOT: I thank you, sir.
USER: Here's my lord.
BOT: His health is well, sir.
USER: I am right glad that his health is well, sir. And what hast thou there under thy cloak, pretty Flaminius?
BOT: Please your lordship, here is the wine.
USER: Flaminius, I have noted thee always wise. Here's to thee.
BOT: Your lordship speaks your pleasure.
USER: Is't possible the world should so much differ, And we alive that lived? Fly, damned baseness, To him that worships thee.
BOT: Ha! Now I see thou art a fool and fit for thy master.

USER: Who, the Lord Timon? He is my very good friend and an honourable gentleman.
BOT: We know him for no less, though we are but strangers to him. But I can tell you one thing, my lord, and which I hear from common rumours: now Lord Timon's happy hours are done and past, and his estate shrinks from him.
USER: Fie, no, do not believe it; he cannot want for money.
BOT: But believe you this, my lord, that, not long ago one of his men was with the Lord Lucullus to borrow so many talents, nay, urged extremely for't, and showed what necessity belonged to't, and yet was denied.
USER: How?
BOT: I tell you, denied, my lord.
USER: See, by good hap, yonder's my lord; I have sweat to see his honour.
BOT: Servilius? You are kindly met, sir. Fare thee well. Commend me to thy honourable virtuous lord, my very exquisite friend.
USER: May it please your honour, my lord hath sent -
BOT: Ha! What has he sent? I am so much endeared to that lord; he's ever sending. How shall I thank him, thinkest thou? And what has he sent now?
USER: Has only sent his present occasion now, my lord, requesting your lordship to supply his instant use with so many talents.
BOT: I know his lordship is but merry with me; He cannot want fifty-five hundred talents.
USER: But in the meantime he wants less, my lord. If his occasion were not virtuous, I should not urge it half so faithfully.
BOT: Dost thou speak seriously, Servilius?
USER: Upon my soul, 'tis true, sir.
BOT: Yes, sir, I shall.
USER: I'll look you out a good turn, Servilius. True, as you said, Timon is shrunk indeed, And he that's once denied will hardly speed.
BOT: Do you observe this, Hostilius?
USER: Ay, too well.
BOT: Religion groans at it.

USER: Must he needs trouble me in't? Hum! 'Bove all others? He might have tried Lord Lucius or Lucullus; And now Ventidius is wealthy too, Whom he redeemed from prison. All these Owe their estates unto him.
BOT: My lord, They have all been touched and found base metal, For they have all denied him.

USER: Well met, good morrow, Titus and Hortensius.
BOT: The like to you, kind Varro.
USER: Lucius! What, do we meet together?
BOT: Ay, and I think One business does command us all; For mine is money.
USER: So is theirs and ours.
BOT: And, sir, Philotus too!
USER: Good day at once.
BOT: Welcome, good brother. What do you think the hour?
USER: Labouring for nine.
BOT: So much?
USER: Is not my lord seen yet?
BOT: Not yet.
USER: I wonder on't, he was wont to shine at seven.
BOT: I am of your fear for that.
USER: I'll show you how t' observe a strange event. Your lord sends now for money?
BOT: Most true, he does.
USER: And he wears jewels now of Timon's gift, For which I wait for money.
BOT: It is against my heart.
USER: Mark how strange it shows, Timon in this should pay more than he owes, And e'en as if your lord should wear rich jewels And send for money for 'em.
BOT: I'm weary of this charge, the gods can witness. I know my lord hath spent of Timon's wealth, And now ingratitude makes it worse than stealth.
USER: Yes, mine's three thousand crowns. What's yours?
BOT: Five thousand mine.
USER: 'Tis much deep, and it should seem by th' sum Your master's confidence was above mine, Else surely his had equalled.
BOT: One of Lord Timon's men.
USER: Flaminius? Sir, a word. Pray, is my lord ready to come forth?
BOT: No, indeed he is not.
USER: We attend his lordship; pray, signify so much.
BOT: I need not tell him that, he knows you are too diligent.
USER: Ha, is not that his steward muffled so? He goes away in a cloud. Call him, call him.
BOT: Do you hear, sir?
USER: By your leave, sir.
BOT: What do you ask of me, my friend?
USER: We wait for certain money here, sir.
BOT: Ay, but this answer will not serve.
USER: If 'twill not serve, 'tis not so base as you, For you serve knaves.
BOT: How? What does his cashiered worship mutter?
USER: No matter what, he's poor, and that's revenge enough. Who can speak broader than he that has no house to put his head in? Such may rail against great buildings.
BOT: O, here's Servilius; now we shall know some answer.
USER: Many do keep their chambers are not sick. And if it be so far beyond his health, Methinks he should the sooner pay his debts And make a clear way to the gods.
BOT: Good gods!
USER: We cannot take this for answer, sir.
BOT: What, are my doors opposed against my passage? Have I been ever free, and must my house Be my retentive enemy, my jail? The place which I have feasted, does it now, Like all mankind, show me an iron heart?
USER: Put in now, Titus.
BOT: My lord, here is my bill.
USER: Here's mine.
BOT: And mine, my lord.
USER: And ours, my lord.
BOT: All our bills.
USER: Knock me down with 'em! Cleave me to the girdle.
BOT: Alas, my lord -
USER: Cut my heart in sums!
BOT: Mine, fifty talents.
USER: Tell out my blood.
BOT: Five thousand crowns, my lord.
USER: Five thousand drops pays that. What yours, and yours?
BOT: My lord -
USER: My lord -
BOT: Tear me, take me, and the gods fall upon you!
USER: Faith, I perceive our masters may throw their caps at their money. These debts may well be called desperate ones, for a madman owes 'em.
BOT: They have e'en put my breath from me, the slaves. Creditors? Devils!
USER: My dear lord -
BOT: What if it should be so?
USER: My lord -
BOT: I'll have it so. - My steward!
USER: Here, my lord.
BOT: So fitly? Go, bid all my friends again, Lucius, Lucullus, and Sempronius, all. I'll once more feast the rascals.
USER: O my lord, You only speak from your distracted soul; There is not so much left to furnish out A moderate table.
BOT: Be it not in thy care. Go, I charge thee, invite them all. Let in the tide Of knaves once more. My cook and I'll provide.

USER: My lord, you have my voice to 't. The fault's Bloody. 'Tis necessary he should die. Nothing emboldens sin so much as mercy.
BOT: Most true, the law shall bruise 'em.
USER: Honour, health, and compassion to the senate!
BOT: Now, captain?
USER: My lord -
BOT: You cannot make gross sins look clear. To revenge is no valour, but to bear.
USER: You breathe in vain.
BOT: In vain? His service done At Lacedaemon and Byzantium Were a sufficient briber for his life.
USER: What's that?
BOT: Why, I say, my lords, has done fair service And slain in fight many of your enemies. How full of valour did he bear himself In the last conflict, and made plenteous wounds!
USER: He dies.
BOT: We are for law. He dies. Urge it no more, On height of our displeasure. Friend or brother, He forfeits his own blood that spills another.
USER: Must it be so? It must not be. My lords, I do beseech you, know me.
BOT: How?
USER: Call me to your remembrances.
BOT: What?
USER: I cannot think but your age has forgot me, It could not else be I should prove so base To sue and be denied such common grace. My wounds ache at you.
BOT: Do you dare our anger? 'Tis in few words, but spacious in effect: We banish thee for ever.
USER: Banish me? Banish your dotage, banish usury, That makes the Senate ugly.
BOT: If, after two days' shine, Athens contain thee, Attend our weightier judgment. And, not to swell our spirit, He shall be executed presently.

USER: The good time of day to you, sir.
BOT: I also wish it to you. I think this honourable lord did but try us this other day.
USER: Upon that were my thoughts tiring when we encountered. I hope it is not so low with him as he made it seem in the trial of his several friends.
BOT: It should not be, by the persuasion of his new feasting.
USER: I should think so. He hath sent me an earnest inviting, which many my near occasions did urge me to put off; but he hath conjured me beyond them, and I must needs appear.
BOT: In like manner was I in debt to my importunate business, but he would not hear my excuse. I am sorry, when he sent to borrow of me, that my provision was out.
USER: I am sick of that grief too, as I understand how all things go.
BOT: Every man here's so. What would he have borrowed you?
USER: A thousand pieces.
BOT: A thousand pieces!
USER: What of you?
BOT: He sent to me, sir - here he comes.
USER: With all my heart, gentlemen both! And how fare you?
BOT: Ever at the best, hearing well of your lordship.
USER: The swallow follows not summer more willing than we your lordship.
BOT: Gentlemen, our dinner will not recompense this long stay. Feast your ears with the music awhile, if they will fare so harshly o' th' trumpet's sound; we shall to't presently.
USER: I hope it remains not unkindly with your lordship that I returned you an empty messenger.
BOT: O, sir, let it not trouble you.
USER: My noble lord -
BOT: Ah, my good friend, what cheer?
USER: My most honourable lord, I am e'en sick of shame that when your lordship this other day sent to me I was so unfortunate a beggar.
BOT: Think not on't, sir.
USER: If you had sent but two hours before -
BOT: Let it not cumber your better remembrance. Come, bring in all together.
USER: All covered dishes!
BOT: Royal cheer, I warrant you.
USER: Doubt not that, if money and the season can yield it.
BOT: How do you? What's the news?
USER: Alcibiades is banished. Hear you of it?
BOT: Alcibiades banished?
USER: 'Tis so, be sure of it.
BOT: How, how?
USER: I pray you, upon what?
BOT: My worthy friends, will you draw near?
USER: I'll tell you more anon. Here's a noble feast toward.
BOT: This is the old man still.
USER: Will't hold, will't hold?
BOT: It does, but time will - and so -
USER: I do conceive.
BOT: What does his lordship mean?
USER: I know not.
BOT: How now, my lords?
USER: Know you the quality of Lord Timon's fury?
BOT: Push! Did you see my cap?
USER: I have lost my gown.
BOT: He's but a mad lord, and nought but humours sways him. He gave me a jewel th' other day, and now he has beat it out of my hat. Did you see my jewel?
USER: Did you see my cap?
BOT: Here 'tis.
USER: Here lies my gown.
BOT: Let's make no stay.
USER: Lord Timon's mad.
BOT: I feel't upon my bones.
USER: One day he gives us diamonds, next day stones.

USER: Hear you, Master Steward, where's our master? Are we undone, cast off, nothing remaining?
BOT: Alack, my fellows, what should I say to you? Let me be recorded by the righteous gods, I am as poor as you.
USER: Such a house broke? So noble a master fall'n? All gone, and not One friend to take his fortune by the arm And go along with him?
BOT: All broken implements of a ruined house.

USER: What art thou there? Speak.
BOT: A beast, as thou art. The canker gnaw thy heart For showing me again the eyes of man!
USER: What is thy name? Is man so hateful to thee That art thyself a man?
BOT: I am Misanthropos and hate mankind. For thy part, I do wish thou wert a dog, That I might love thee something.
USER: I know thee well, But in thy fortunes am unlearned and strange.
BOT: Thy lips rot off!
USER: I will not kiss thee, then the rot returns To thine own lips again.
BOT: How came the noble Timon to this change?
USER: As the moon does, by wanting light to give. But then renew I could not like the moon; There were no suns to borrow of.
BOT: Noble Timon, What friendship may I do thee?
USER: None, but to maintain my opinion.
BOT: What is it, Timon?
USER: Promise me friendship, but perform none. If thou wilt not promise, the gods plague thee, for thou art a man. If thou dost perform, confound thee, for thou art a man.
BOT: I have heard in some sort of thy miseries.
USER: Thou saw'st them when I had prosperity.
BOT: I see them now; then was a blessed time.
USER: As thine is now, held with a brace of harlots.
BOT: Is this th' Athenian minion whom the world Voiced so regardfully?
USER: Art thou Timandra?
BOT: Yes.
USER: Hang thee, monster!
BOT: I prithee, beat thy drum and get thee gone.
USER: I am thy friend and pity thee, dear Timon.
BOT: How dost thou pity him whom thou dost trouble? I had rather be alone.
USER: Why, fare thee well. Here is some gold for thee.
BOT: Keep it, I cannot eat it.
USER: When I have laid proud Athens on a heap -
BOT: Warr'st thou 'gainst Athens?
USER: Ay, Timon, and have cause.
BOT: The gods confound them all in thy conquest, And thee after, when thou hast conquered!
USER: Why me, Timon?
BOT: Hast thou gold yet? I'll take the gold thou giv'st me, Not all thy counsel.
USER: Dost thou or dost thou not, heaven's curse upon thee!
BOT: Give us some gold, good Timon. Hast thou more?
USER: Well, more gold. What then? Believe't that we'll do anything for gold.
BOT: More counsel with more money, bounteous Timon.
USER: More whore, more mischief first! I have given you earnest.
BOT: Strike up the drum towards Athens. Farewell, Timon. If I thrive well, I'll visit thee again.
USER: If I hope well, I'll never see thee more.
BOT: I never did thee harm.
USER: Yes, thou spok'st well of me.
BOT: Call'st thou that harm?
USER: Men daily find it. Get thee away, and take Thy beagles with thee.
BOT: We but offend him. Strike.
USER: I was directed hither. Men report Thou dost affect my manners and dost use them.
BOT: 'Tis, then, because thou dost not keep a dog Whom I would imitate. Consumption catch thee!
USER: Were I like thee, I'd throw away myself.
BOT: A fool of thee. Depart.
USER: I love thee better now than e'er I did.
BOT: I hate thee worse.
USER: Why?
BOT: Thou flatter'st misery.
USER: I flatter not, but say thou art a caitiff.
BOT: Why dost thou seek me out?
USER: To vex thee.
BOT: Always a villain's office or a fool's. Dost please thyself in't?
USER: Ay.
BOT: What, a knave too?
USER: Art thou proud yet?
BOT: Ay, that I am not thee.
USER: I, that I was no prodigal.
BOT: I, that I am one now. Were all the wealth I have shut up in thee, I'd give thee leave to hang it. Get thee gone. That the whole life of Athens were in this! Thus would I eat it.
USER: Here, I will mend thy feast.
BOT: First mend my company, take away thyself.
USER: So I shall mend mine own, by th' lack of thine.
BOT: 'Tis not well mended so, it is but botched. If not, I would it were.
USER: What wouldst thou have to Athens?
BOT: Thee thither in a whirlwind. If thou wilt, Tell them there I have gold. Look, so I have.
USER: Here is no use for gold.
BOT: The best and truest, For here it sleeps and does no hired harm.
USER: Where liest a-nights, Timon?
BOT: Under that's above me. Where feed'st thou a-days, Apemantus?
USER: Where my stomach finds meat, or rather where I eat it.
BOT: Would poison were obedient and knew my mind!
USER: Where wouldst thou send it?
BOT: To sauce thy dishes.
USER: On what I hate I feed not.
BOT: Dost hate a medlar?
USER: Ay, though it look like thee.
BOT: An thou'dst hated medlars sooner, thou shouldst have loved thyself better now. What man didst thou ever know unthrift that was beloved after his means?
USER: Who, without those means thou talk'st of, didst thou ever know beloved?
BOT: Myself.
USER: I understand thee. Thou hadst some means to keep a dog.
BOT: What things in the world canst thou nearest compare to thy flatterers?
USER: Women nearest; but men - men are the things themselves. What wouldst thou do with the world, Apemantus, if it lay in thy power?
BOT: Give it the beasts, to be rid of the men.
USER: Wouldst thou have thyself fall in the confusion of men and remain a beast with the beasts?
BOT: Ay, Timon.
USER: If thou couldst please me with speaking to me, thou mightst have hit upon it here. The commonwealth of Athens is become a forest of beasts.
BOT: How has the ass broke the wall, that thou art out of the city?
USER: Yonder comes a poet and a painter. The plague of company light upon thee! I will fear to catch it, and give way. When I know not what else to do, I'll see thee again.
BOT: When there is nothing living but thee, thou shalt be welcome. I had rather be a beggar's dog than Apemantus.
USER: Thou art the cap of all the fools alive.
BOT: Would thou wert clean enough to spit upon!
USER: A plague on thee! Thou art too bad to curse.
BOT: All villains that do stand by thee are pure.
USER: There is no leprosy but what thou speak'st.
BOT: If I name thee, I'll beat thee, but I should infect my hands.
USER: I would my tongue could rot them off!
BOT: Away, thou issue of a mangy dog! Choler does kill me that thou art alive. I swoon to see thee.
USER: Would thou wouldst burst!
BOT: Away, thou tedious rogue! I am sorry I shall lose a stone by thee.
USER: Beast!
BOT: Slave!
USER: Toad!
BOT: Would 'twere so! But not till I am dead. I'll say thou'st gold; Thou wilt be thronged to shortly.
USER: Thronged to?
BOT: Ay.
USER: Thy back, I prithee.
BOT: Live and love thy misery.
USER: Long live so, and so die! I am quit.
BOT: More things like men. Eat, Timon, and abhor them.
USER: Where should he have this gold? It is some poor fragment, some slender ort of his remainder. The mere want of gold and the falling-from of his friends drove him into this melancholy.
BOT: It is noised he hath a mass of treasure.
USER: Let us make the assay upon him. If he care not for't, he will supply us easily; if he covetously reserve it, how shall's get it?
BOT: True, for he bears it not about him. 'Tis hid.
USER: Is not this he?
BOT: Where?
USER: 'Tis his description.
BOT: He; I know him.
USER: Save thee, Timon!
BOT: Now, thieves?
USER: Soldiers, not thieves.
BOT: Both too, and women's sons.
USER: We are not thieves, but men that much do want.
BOT: We cannot live on grass, on berries, water, As beasts and birds and fishes.
USER: Has almost charmed me from my profession by persuading me to it.
BOT: 'Tis in the malice of mankind that he thus advises us, not to have us thrive in our mystery.
USER: I'll believe him as an enemy and give over my trade.
BOT: Let us first see peace in Athens. There is no time so miserable but a man may be true.
USER: Away! What art thou?
BOT: Have you forgot me, sir?
USER: Why dost ask that? I have forgot all men. Then, if thou grant'st thou'rt a man, I have forgot thee.
BOT: An honest poor servant of yours.
USER: Then I know thee not. I never had honest man about me. I; all I kept were knaves to serve in meat to villains.
BOT: The gods are witness, Ne'er did poor steward wear a truer grief For his undone lord than mine eyes for you.
USER: I beg of you to know me, good my lord, T' accept my grief, and whilst this poor wealth lasts To entertain me as your steward still.
BOT: O, let me stay And comfort you, my master.
USER: If thou hat'st curses, Stay not. Fly whilst thou'rt blest and free. Ne'er see thou man, and let me ne'er see thee.

USER: As I took note of the place, it cannot be far where he abides.
BOT: What's to be thought of him? Does the rumour hold for true that he is so full of gold?
USER: Certain. Alcibiades reports it; Phrynia and Timandra had gold of him. He likewise enriched poor straggling soldiers with great quantity. 'Tis said he gave unto his steward a mighty sum.
BOT: Then this breaking of his has been but a try for his friends?
USER: What have you now to present unto him?
BOT: Nothing at this time but my visitation; only I will promise him an excellent piece.
USER: I must serve him so too, tell him of an intent that's coming toward him.
BOT: thyself.
USER: I am thinking what I shall say I have provided for him. It must be a personating of himself, a satire against the softness of prosperity, with a discovery of the infinite flatteries that follow youth and opulency.
BOT: thou whip thine own faults in other men? Do so, I have gold for thee.
USER: Nay, let's seek him. Then do we sin against our own estate When we may profit meet and come too late.
BOT: True. When the day serves, before black-cornered night, Find what thou want'st by free and offered light. Come.
USER: Hail, worthy Timon!
BOT: Our late noble master!
USER: Have I once lived to see two honest men?
BOT: Let it go naked. Men may see't the better. You that are honest, by being what you are, Make them best seen and known.
USER: He and myself Have travailed in the great shower of your gifts, And sweetly felt it.
BOT: Ay, you are honest men.
USER: We are hither come to offer you our service.
BOT: Most honest men! Why, how shall I requite you? Can you eat roots and drink cold water? No?
USER: What we can do we'll do, to do you service.
BOT: Ye're honest men. Ye've heard that I have gold, I am sure you have. Speak truth, you're honest men.
USER: So it is said, my noble lord; but therefore Came not my friend nor I.
BOT: Good honest men! [_To Painter_.] Thou draw'st a counterfeit Best in all Athens. Thou'rt indeed the best, Thou counterfeit'st most lively.
USER: So so, my lord.
BOT: Beseech your honour To make it known to us.
USER: You'll take it ill.
BOT: Most thankfully, my lord.
USER: Will you indeed?
BOT: Doubt it not, worthy lord.
USER: There's never a one of you but trusts a knave That mightily deceives you.
BOT: Do we, my lord?
USER: Ay, and you hear him cog, see him dissemble, Know his gross patchery, love him, feed him, Keep in your bosom, yet remain assured That he's a made-up villain.
BOT: I know not such, my lord.
USER: Nor I.
BOT: Look you, I love you well. I'll give you gold. Rid me these villains from your companies, Hang them or stab them, drown them in a draught, Confound them by some course, and come to me, I'll give you gold enough.
USER: Name them, my lord, let's know them.

USER: It is vain that you would speak with Timon. For he is set so only to himself That nothing but himself which looks like man Is friendly with him.
BOT: Bring us to his cave. It is our part and promise to th' Athenians To speak with Timon.
USER: Here is his cave. Peace and content be here! Lord Timon! Timon, Look out and speak to friends. The Athenians By two of their most reverend senate greet thee. Speak to them, noble Timon.
BOT: Thou sun that comforts, burn! Speak and be hanged! For each true word, a blister, and each false Be as a cantherizing to the root o' th' tongue, Consuming it with speaking.
USER: Worthy Timon -
BOT: Of none but such as you, and you of Timon.
USER: The senators of Athens greet thee, Timon.
BOT: Could I but catch it for them.
USER: O, forget What we are sorry for ourselves in thee. The senators with one consent of love Entreat thee back to Athens, who have thought On special dignities, which vacant lie For thy best use and wearing.
BOT: You witch me in it, Surprise me to the very brink of tears. Lend me a fool's heart and a woman's eyes And I'll beweep these comforts, worthy senators.
USER: And shakes his threatening sword Against the walls of Athens.
BOT: Therefore, Timon -
USER: Stay not, all's in vain.
BOT: We speak in vain.
USER: But yet I love my country and am not One that rejoices in the common wrack, As common bruit doth put it.
BOT: That's well spoke.
USER: Commend me to my loving countrymen.
BOT: These words become your lips as they pass through them.
USER: And enter in our ears like great triumphers In their applauding gates.
BOT: Trouble him no further; thus you still shall find him.
USER: His discontents are unremovably Coupled to nature.
BOT: Our hope in him is dead. Let us return And strain what other means is left unto us In our dear peril.
USER: It requires swift foot.

USER: Thou hast painfully discovered. Are his files As full as thy report?
BOT: I have spoke the least. Besides, his expedition promises Present approach.
USER: We stand much hazard if they bring not Timon.
BOT: Here come our brothers.
USER: No talk of Timon, nothing of him expect. The enemy's drum is heard, and fearful scouring Doth choke the air with dust. In, and prepare. Ours is the fall, I fear, our foe's the snare.

USER: Noble and young, When thy first griefs were but a mere conceit, Ere thou hadst power or we had cause of fear, We sent to thee to give thy rages balm, To wipe out our ingratitude with loves Above their quantity.
BOT: So did we woo Transformed Timon to our city's love By humble message and by promised means. We were not all unkind, nor all deserve The common stroke of war.
USER: These walls of ours Were not erected by their hands from whom You have received your griefs; nor are they such That these great towers, trophies, and schools should fall For private faults in them.
BOT: What thou wilt, Thou rather shalt enforce it with thy smile Than hew to 't with thy sword.
USER: Set but thy foot Against our rampired gates and they shall ope, So thou wilt send thy gentle heart before To say thou'lt enter friendly.
BOT: Throw thy glove, Or any token of thine honour else, That thou wilt use the wars as thy redress And not as our confusion, all thy powers Shall make their harbour in our town till we Have sealed thy full desire.
USER: 'Tis most nobly spoken.
BOT: Descend, and keep your words.
USER: My noble general, Timon is dead, Entombed upon the very hem o' th' sea, And on his gravestone this insculpture, which With wax I brought away, whose soft impression Interprets for my poor ignorance.

USER: How fair the tribune speaks to calm my thoughts!
BOT: Tribunes, and me, a poor competitor.
USER: I give him you, the noblest that survives, The eldest son of this distressed queen.
BOT: Away with him, and make a fire straight, And with our swords, upon a pile of wood, Let's hew his limbs till they be clean consumed.
USER: O cruel, irreligious piety!
BOT: Was never Scythia half so barbarous!
USER: Kind Rome, that hast thus lovingly reserved The cordial of mine age to glad my heart! Lavinia, live; outlive thy father's days, And fame's eternal date, for virtue's praise. and others.
BOT: Long live Lord Titus, my beloved brother, Gracious triumpher in the eyes of Rome.
USER: Thanks, gentle tribune, noble brother Marcus.
BOT: Titus, thou shalt obtain and ask the empery.
USER: Proud and ambitious tribune, canst thou tell?
BOT: Patience, Prince Saturninus.
USER: Romans, do me right. Patricians, draw your swords, and sheathe them not Till Saturninus be Rome's emperor. Andronicus, would thou were shipped to hell Rather than rob me of the people's hearts!
BOT: Proud Saturnine, interrupter of the good That noble-minded Titus means to thee!
USER: Content thee, prince; I will restore to thee The people's hearts, and wean them from themselves.
BOT: Andronicus, I do not flatter thee, But honour thee, and will do till I die. My faction if thou strengthen with thy friends, I will most thankful be; and thanks to men Of noble minds is honourable meed.
USER: People of Rome, and people's tribunes here, I ask your voices and your suffrages. Will you bestow them friendly on Andronicus?
BOT: To gratify the good Andronicus, And gratulate his safe return to Rome, The people will accept whom he admits.
USER: With voices and applause of every sort, Patricians and plebeians, we create Lord Saturninus Rome's great emperor, And say "Long live our Emperor Saturnine!"
BOT: Thanks, noble Titus, father of my life. How proud I am of thee and of thy gifts Rome shall record, and when I do forget The least of these unspeakable deserts, Romans, forget your fealty to me.
USER: To him that for your honour and your state Will use you nobly and your followers.
BOT: Not I, my lord, sith true nobility Warrants these words in princely courtesy.
USER: Thanks, sweet Lavinia. Romans, let us go. Ransomless here we set our prisoners free. Proclaim our honours, lords, with trump and drum. Tribunes and Senators exit aloft._]
BOT: Lord Titus, by your leave, this maid is mine.
USER: How, sir? Are you in earnest then, my lord?
BOT: Ay, noble Titus; and resolved withal To do myself this reason and this right.
USER: _Suum cuique_ is our Roman justice. This prince in justice seizeth but his own.
BOT: And that he will and shall, if Lucius live.
USER: Traitors, avaunt! Where is the emperor's guard? Treason, my lord, Lavinia is surprised.
BOT: Surprised? By whom?
USER: By him that justly may Bear his betrothed from all the world away.
BOT: Brothers, help to convey her hence away, And with my sword I'll keep this door safe.
USER: Follow, my lord, and I'll soon bring her back.
BOT: My lord, you pass not here.
USER: What, villain boy, Barr'st me my way in Rome?
BOT: Help, Lucius, help!
USER: My lord, you are unjust, and more than so, In wrongful quarrel you have slain your son.
BOT: Nor thou nor he are any sons of mine; My sons would never so dishonour me. Traitor, restore Lavinia to the Emperor.
USER: Dead, if you will; but not to be his wife, That is another's lawful promised love. Aaron the Moor.
BOT: O monstrous! What reproachful words are these?
USER: But go thy ways; go, give that changing piece To him that flourished for her with his sword. A valiant son-in-law thou shalt enjoy; One fit to bandy with thy lawless sons, To ruffle in the commonwealth of Rome.
BOT: These words are razors to my wounded heart.
USER: And here in sight of heaven to Rome I swear, If Saturnine advance the Queen of Goths, She will a handmaid be to his desires, A loving nurse, a mother to his youth.
BOT: Ascend, fair queen, Pantheon. Lords, accompany Your noble emperor and his lovely bride, Sent by the heavens for Prince Saturnine, Whose wisdom hath her fortune conquered. There shall we consummate our spousal rites.
USER: I am not bid to wait upon this bride. Titus, when wert thou wont to walk alone, Dishonoured thus, and challenged of wrongs?
BOT: O Titus, see, O, see what thou hast done! In a bad quarrel slain a virtuous son.
USER: No, foolish tribune, no; no son of mine, Nor thou, nor these, confederates in the deed That hath dishonoured all our family. Unworthy brother and unworthy sons!
BOT: But let us give him burial, as becomes; Give Mutius burial with our brethren.
USER: My lord, this is impiety in you. My nephew Mutius' deeds do plead for him; He must be buried with his brethren.
BOT: And shall, or him we will accompany.
USER: "And shall"? What villain was it spake that word?
BOT: He that would vouch it in any place but here.
USER: What, would you bury him in my despite?
BOT: No, noble Titus, but entreat of thee To pardon Mutius and to bury him.
USER: Marcus, even thou hast struck upon my crest, And with these boys mine honour thou hast wounded. My foes I do repute you every one; So trouble me no more, but get you gone.
BOT: He is not with himself; let us withdraw.
USER: Not I, till Mutius' bones be buried.
BOT: Brother, for in that name doth nature plead, -
USER: Father, and in that name doth nature speak, -
BOT: Speak thou no more, if all the rest will speed.
USER: Renowned Titus, more than half my soul, -
BOT: Dear father, soul and substance of us all, -
USER: Rise, Marcus, rise. The dismall'st day is this that e'er I saw, To be dishonoured by my sons in Rome! Well, bury him, and bury me the next.
BOT: There lie thy bones, sweet Mutius, with thy friends, Till we with trophies do adorn thy tomb.
USER: He lives in fame that died in virtue's cause.
BOT: My lord, to step out of these dreary dumps, How comes it that the subtle Queen of Goths Is of a sudden thus advanced in Rome?
USER: So, Bassianus, you have played your prize. God give you joy, sir, of your gallant bride.
BOT: And you of yours, my lord. I say no more, Nor wish no less; and so I take my leave.
USER: Traitor, if Rome have law or we have power, Thou and thy faction shall repent this rape.
BOT: Rape call you it, my lord, to seize my own, My true betrothed love, and now my wife? But let the laws of Rome determine all; Meanwhile am I possessed of that is mine.
USER: 'Tis good, sir. You are very short with us; But if we live, we'll be as sharp with you.
BOT: Prince Bassianus, leave to plead my deeds. 'Tis thou, and those, that have dishonoured me. Rome and the righteous heavens be my judge How I have loved and honoured Saturnine.
USER: My worthy lord, if ever Tamora Were gracious in those princely eyes of thine, Then hear me speak indifferently for all; And at my suit, sweet, pardon what is past.
BOT: What, madam, be dishonoured openly, And basely put it up without revenge?
USER: Rise, Titus, rise; my empress hath prevailed.
BOT: I thank your majesty and her, my lord. These words, these looks, infuse new life in me.
USER: We do, and vow to heaven and to his highness That what we did was mildly as we might, Tend'ring our sister's honour and our own.
BOT: That on mine honour here do I protest.
USER: Away, and talk not; trouble us no more.
BOT: Nay, nay, sweet emperor, we must all be friends. The tribune and his nephews kneel for grace; I will not be denied. Sweet heart, look back.
USER: Tomorrow, an it please your majesty To hunt the panther and the hart with me, With horn and hound we'll give your grace _bonjour_.
BOT: Be it so, Titus, and gramercy too.

USER: Chiron, thy years wants wit, thy wit wants edge And manners, to intrude where I am graced, And may, for aught thou knowest, affected be.
BOT: Why, boy, although our mother, unadvised, Gave you a dancing-rapier by your side, Are you so desperate grown to threat your friends? Go to; have your lath glued within your sheath Till you know better how to handle it.
USER: Meanwhile, sir, with the little skill I have, Full well shalt thou perceive how much I dare.
BOT: Ay, boy, grow ye so brave?
USER: Not I, till I have sheathed My rapier in his bosom, and withal Thrust those reproachful speeches down his throat That he hath breathed in my dishonour here.
BOT: For that I am prepared and full resolved, Foul-spoken coward, that thund'rest with thy tongue, And with thy weapon nothing dar'st perform.
USER: I care not, I, knew she and all the world. I love Lavinia more than all the world.
BOT: Youngling, learn thou to make some meaner choice. Lavina is thine elder brother's hope.
USER: Why, are ye mad? Or know ye not in Rome How furious and impatient they be, And cannot brook competitors in love? I tell you, lords, you do but plot your deaths By this device.
BOT: Aaron, a thousand deaths Would I propose to achieve her whom I love.
USER: To achieve her! How?
BOT: Then why should he despair that knows to court it With words, fair looks, and liberality? What, hast not thou full often struck a doe, And borne her cleanly by the keeper's nose?
USER: Why, then, it seems some certain snatch or so Would serve your turns.
BOT: Ay, so the turn were served.
USER: Aaron, thou hast hit it.
BOT: Would you had hit it too! Then should not we be tired with this ado. Why, hark ye, hark ye, and are you such fools To square for this? Would it offend you then That both should speed?
USER: Faith, not me.
BOT: Nor me, so I were one.
USER: Thy counsel, lad, smells of no cowardice.
BOT: _Sit fas aut nefas_, till I find the stream To cool this heat, a charm to calm these fits, _Per Stygia, per manes vehor._

USER: And you have rung it lustily, my lords; Somewhat too early for new-married ladies.
BOT: Lavinia, how say you?
USER: I say no; I have been broad awake two hours and more.
BOT: Come on then; horse and chariots let us have, And to our sport. [_To Tamora_.] Madam, now shall ye see Our Roman hunting.
USER: I have dogs, my lord, Will rouse the proudest panther in the chase, And climb the highest promontory top.
BOT: And I have horse will follow where the game Makes way, and run like swallows o'er the plain.
USER: Chiron, we hunt not, we, with horse nor hound, But hope to pluck a dainty doe to ground.

USER: Ah, my sweet Moor, sweeter to me than life!
BOT: No more, great empress. Bassianus comes. Be cross with him; and I'll go fetch thy sons To back thy quarrels, whatsoe'er they be.
USER: Who have we here? Rome's royal empress, Unfurnished of her well-beseeming troop? Or is it Dian, habited like her, Who hath abandoned her holy groves To see the general hunting in this forest?
BOT: And, being intercepted in your sport, Great reason that my noble lord be rated For sauciness. I pray you, let us hence, And let her joy her raven-coloured love; This valley fits the purpose passing well.
USER: The king my brother shall have notice of this.
BOT: Ay, for these slips have made him noted long. Good king, to be so mightily abused!
USER: Why, I have patience to endure all this.
BOT: How now, dear sovereign, and our gracious mother! Why doth your highness look so pale and wan?
USER: This is a witness that I am thy son.
BOT: And this for me, struck home to show my strength.
USER: Ay, come, Semiramis, nay, barbarous Tamora, For no name fits thy nature but thy own!
BOT: Give me thy poniard; you shall know, my boys, Your mother's hand shall right your mother's wrong.
USER: And if she do, I would I were an eunuch. Drag hence her husband to some secret hole, And make his dead trunk pillow to our lust.
BOT: But when ye have the honey ye desire, Let not this wasp outlive, us both to sting.
USER: I warrant you, madam, we will make that sure. Come, mistress, now perforce we will enjoy That nice-preserved honesty of yours.
BOT: O Tamora, thou bearest a woman's face, -
USER: I will not hear her speak; away with her!
BOT: Sweet lords, entreat her hear me but a word.
USER: Listen, fair madam: let it be your glory To see her tears; but be your heart to them As unrelenting flint to drops of rain.
BOT: What, wouldst thou have me prove myself a bastard?
USER: I know not what it means; away with her!
BOT: O, let me teach thee! For my father's sake, That gave thee life when well he might have slain thee, Be not obdurate, open thy deaf ears.
USER: O Tamora, be called a gentle queen, And with thine own hands kill me in this place! For 'tis not life that I have begged so long; Poor I was slain when Bassianus died.
BOT: What begg'st thou, then? Fond woman, let me go.
USER: So should I rob my sweet sons of their fee. No, let them satisfy their lust on thee.
BOT: Away, for thou hast stayed us here too long.
USER: No grace, no womanhood? Ah, beastly creature, The blot and enemy to our general name! Confusion fall -
BOT: Nay, then I'll stop your mouth. Bring thou her husband. This is the hole where Aaron bid us hide him. Lavinia._]
USER: Farewell, my sons. See that you make her sure. Ne'er let my heart know merry cheer indeed Till all the Andronici be made away. Now will I hence to seek my lovely Moor, And let my spleenful sons this trull deflower.
BOT: Come on, my lords, the better foot before. Straight will I bring you to the loathsome pit Where I espied the panther fast asleep.
USER: My sight is very dull, whate'er it bodes.
BOT: And mine, I promise you. Were it not for shame, Well could I leave our sport to sleep awhile.
USER: O brother, with the dismall'st object hurt That ever eye with sight made heart lament!
BOT: That he thereby may have a likely guess How these were they that made away his brother.
USER: Why dost not comfort me, and help me out From this unhallowed and blood-stained hole?
BOT: I am surprised with an uncouth fear; A chilling sweat o'er-runs my trembling joints. My heart suspects more than mine eye can see.
USER: To prove thou hast a true-divining heart, Aaron and thou look down into this den, And see a fearful sight of blood and death.
BOT: Aaron is gone, and my compassionate heart Will not permit mine eyes once to behold The thing whereat it trembles by surmise. O, tell me who it is; for ne'er till now Was I a child to fear I know not what.
USER: Lord Bassianus lies berayed in blood, All on a heap, like to a slaughtered lamb, In this detested, dark, blood-drinking pit.
BOT: If it be dark, how dost thou know 'tis he?
USER: Reach me thy hand, that I may help thee out, Or, wanting strength to do thee so much good, I may be plucked into the swallowing womb Of this deep pit, poor Bassianus' grave. I have no strength to pluck thee to the brink.
BOT: Nor I no strength to climb without thy help.
USER: Thy hand once more; I will not loose again, Till thou art here aloft, or I below. Thou canst not come to me. I come to thee.
BOT: Along with me! I'll see what hole is here, And what he is that now is leapt into it. Say, who art thou that lately didst descend Into this gaping hollow of the earth?
USER: The unhappy sons of old Andronicus, Brought hither in a most unlucky hour, To find thy brother Bassianus dead.
BOT: My brother dead! I know thou dost but jest. He and his lady both are at the lodge Upon the north side of this pleasant chase; 'Tis not an hour since I left them there.
USER: We know not where you left them all alive; But, out, alas, here have we found him dead.
BOT: Where is my lord the king?
USER: Here, Tamora; though grieved with killing grief.
BOT: Where is thy brother Bassianus?
USER: Now to the bottom dost thou search my wound. Poor Bassianus here lies murdered.
BOT: Then all too late I bring this fatal writ, The complot of this timeless tragedy; And wonder greatly that man's face can fold In pleasing smiles such murderous tyranny.
USER: My gracious lord, here is the bag of gold.
BOT: Have here bereft my brother of his life. Sirs, drag them from the pit unto the prison. There let them bide until we have devised Some never-heard-of torturing pain for them.
USER: What, are they in this pit? O wondrous thing! How easily murder is discovered!
BOT: High emperor, upon my feeble knee I beg this boon, with tears not lightly shed, That this fell fault of my accursed sons, Accursed if the fault be proved in them -
USER: If it be proved! You see it is apparent. Who found this letter? Tamora, was it you?
BOT: Andronicus himself did take it up.
USER: I did, my lord, yet let me be their bail; For by my fathers' reverend tomb I vow They shall be ready at your highness' will To answer their suspicion with their lives.
BOT: Andronicus, I will entreat the king. Fear not thy sons; they shall do well enough.
USER: Come, Lucius, come; stay not to talk with them.

USER: So, now go tell, an if thy tongue can speak, Who 'twas that cut thy tongue and ravished thee.
BOT: Write down thy mind, bewray thy meaning so, An if thy stumps will let thee play the scribe.
USER: See how with signs and tokens she can scrowl.
BOT: Go home, call for sweet water, wash thy hands.
USER: She hath no tongue to call, nor hands to wash; And so let's leave her to her silent walks.
BOT: An 'twere my cause, I should go hang myself.
USER: If thou hadst hands to help thee knit the cord.

USER: O noble father, you lament in vain. The tribunes hear you not, no man is by; And you recount your sorrows to a stone.
BOT: Ah, Lucius, for thy brothers let me plead. Grave tribunes, once more I entreat of you -
USER: My gracious lord, no tribune hears you speak.
BOT: To rescue my two brothers from their death; For which attempt the judges have pronounced My everlasting doom of banishment.
USER: Titus, prepare thy aged eyes to weep; Or if not so, thy noble heart to break. I bring consuming sorrow to thine age.
BOT: Will it consume me? Let me see it then.
USER: This was thy daughter.
BOT: Why, Marcus, so she is.
USER: Ay me, this object kills me!
BOT: Speak, gentle sister, who hath martyred thee?
USER: O, that delightful engine of her thoughts, That blabbed them with such pleasing eloquence, Is torn from forth that pretty hollow cage, Where, like a sweet melodious bird, it sung Sweet varied notes, enchanting every ear.
BOT: O, say thou for her, who hath done this deed?
USER: O, thus I found her straying in the park, Seeking to hide herself, as doth the deer That hath received some unrecuring wound.
BOT: Perchance she weeps because they killed her husband; Perchance because she knows them innocent.
USER: Sweet father, cease your tears; for at your grief See how my wretched sister sobs and weeps.
BOT: Patience, dear niece. Good Titus, dry thine eyes.
USER: Ah, Marcus, Marcus! Brother, well I wot Thy napkin cannot drink a tear of mine, For thou, poor man, hast drowned it with thine own.
BOT: Ah, my Lavinia, I will wipe thy cheeks.
USER: O gracious emperor! O gentle Aaron! Did ever raven sing so like a lark That gives sweet tidings of the sun's uprise? With all my heart I'll send the emperor my hand. Good Aaron, wilt thou help to chop it off?
BOT: Nay, come, agree whose hand shall go along, For fear they die before their pardon come.
USER: My hand shall go.
BOT: By heaven, it shall not go!
USER: Sirs, strive no more. Such withered herbs as these Are meet for plucking up, and therefore mine.
BOT: Sweet father, if I shall be thought thy son, Let me redeem my brothers both from death.
USER: And for our father's sake and mother's care, Now let me show a brother's love to thee.
BOT: Agree between you; I will spare my hand.
USER: Then I'll go fetch an axe.
BOT: But I will use the axe.
USER: Come hither, Aaron; I'll deceive them both. Lend me thy hand, and I will give thee mine.
BOT: And never whilst I live deceive men so. But I'll deceive you in another sort, And that you'll say ere half an hour pass.
USER: I go, Andronicus; and for thy hand Look by and by to have thy sons with thee. Doth fat me with the very thoughts of it! Let fools do good, and fair men call for grace, Aaron will have his soul black like his face.
BOT: O brother, speak with possibility, And do not break into these deep extremes.
USER: Is not my sorrow deep, having no bottom? Then be my passions bottomless with them.
BOT: But yet let reason govern thy lament.
USER: Now let hot Etna cool in Sicily, And be my heart an ever-burning hell! These miseries are more than may be borne. To weep with them that weep doth ease some deal, But sorrow flouted at is double death.
BOT: Ah, that this sight should make so deep a wound, And yet detested life not shrink thereat! That ever death should let life bear his name, Where life hath no more interest but to breathe!
USER: Alas, poor heart, that kiss is comfortless As frozen water to a starved snake.
BOT: When will this fearful slumber have an end?
USER: Ha, ha, ha!
BOT: Why dost thou laugh? It fits not with this hour.

USER: Fie, brother, fie! Teach her not thus to lay Such violent hands upon her tender life.
BOT: Good grandsire, leave these bitter deep laments. Make my aunt merry with some pleasing tale.
USER: Alas, the tender boy, in passion moved, Doth weep to see his grandsire's heaviness.
BOT: Peace, tender sapling; thou art made of tears, And tears will quickly melt thy life away. What dost thou strike at, Marcus, with thy knife?
USER: At that that I have killed, my lord, a fly.
BOT: Out on thee, murderer! Thou kill'st my heart; Mine eyes are cloyed with view of tyranny; A deed of death done on the innocent Becomes not Titus' brother. Get thee gone; I see thou art not for my company.
USER: Alas, my lord, I have but killed a fly.
BOT: Pardon me, sir; 'twas a black ill-favoured fly, Like to the empress' Moor; therefore I killed him.
USER: Alas, poor man, grief has so wrought on him, He takes false shadows for true substances.
BOT: Come, take away. Lavinia, go with me. I'll to thy closet, and go read with thee Sad stories chanced in the times of old. Come, boy, and go with me. Thy sight is young, And thou shalt read when mine begin to dazzle.

USER: Help, grandsire, help! My aunt Lavinia Follows me everywhere, I know not why. Good uncle Marcus, see how swift she comes! Alas, sweet aunt, I know not what you mean.
BOT: Stand by me, Lucius. Do not fear thine aunt.
USER: She loves thee, boy, too well to do thee harm. YOUNG LUCIUS Ay, when my father was in Rome she did.
BOT: What means my niece Lavinia by these signs?
USER: Canst thou not guess wherefore she plies thee thus?
BOT: Lucius, I will. fall._]
USER: I think she means that there were more than one Confederate in the fact. Ay, more there was, Or else to heaven she heaves them for revenge.
BOT: Lucius, what book is that she tosseth so?
USER: Grandsire, 'tis Ovid's _Metamorphosis_. My mother gave it me.
BOT: For love of her that's gone, Perhaps, she culled it from among the rest.
USER: Soft! So busily she turns the leaves! Help her! What would she find? Lavinia, shall I read? This is the tragic tale of Philomel, And treats of Tereus' treason and his rape; And rape, I fear, was root of thy annoy.
BOT: See, brother, see! Note how she quotes the leaves.
USER: O, why should nature build so foul a den, Unless the gods delight in tragedies?
BOT: Give signs, sweet girl, for here are none but friends, What Roman lord it was durst do the deed. Or slunk not Saturnine, as Tarquin erst, That left the camp to sin in Lucrece' bed?
USER: "_Stuprum_. Chiron. Demetrius."
BOT: What, what! The lustful sons of Tamora Performers of this heinous bloody deed?
USER: _Magni Dominator poli, Tam lentus audis scelera, tam lentus vides?_
BOT: I say, my lord, that if I were a man, Their mother's bedchamber should not be safe For these base bondmen to the yoke of Rome.
USER: Ay, that's my boy! Thy father hath full oft For his ungrateful country done the like.
BOT: And, uncle, so will I, an if I live.
USER: Come, go with me into mine armoury. Lucius, I'll fit thee; and withal, my boy, Shall carry from me to the empress' sons Presents that I intend to send them both. Come, come; thou'lt do my message, wilt thou not?
BOT: Ay, with my dagger in their bosoms, grandsire.
USER: No, boy, not so. I'll teach thee another course. Lavinia, come. Marcus, look to my house. Lucius and I'll go brave it at the court; Ay, marry, will we, sir; and we'll be waited on.

USER: Demetrius, here's the son of Lucius; He hath some message to deliver us.
BOT: Ay, some mad message from his mad grandfather.
USER: My lords, with all the humbleness I may, I greet your honours from Andronicus;
BOT: Gramercy, lovely Lucius. What's the news?
USER: What's here? A scroll; and written round about? Let's see: Non eget Mauri iaculis, nec arcu._
BOT: O, 'tis a verse in Horace; I know it well. I read it in the grammar long ago.
USER: But me more good to see so great a lord Basely insinuate and send us gifts.
BOT: Had he not reason, Lord Demetrius? Did you not use his daughter very friendly?
USER: I would we had a thousand Roman dames At such a bay, by turn to serve our lust.
BOT: A charitable wish, and full of love.
USER: Here lacks but your mother for to say amen.
BOT: And that would she for twenty thousand more.
USER: Come, let us go and pray to all the gods For our beloved mother in her pains.
BOT: Why do the emperor's trumpets flourish thus?
USER: Belike for joy the emperor hath a son.
BOT: Soft, who comes here?
USER: Good morrow, lords. O, tell me, did you see Aaron the Moor?
BOT: Well, more or less, or ne'er a whit at all, Here Aaron is; and what with Aaron now?
USER: O gentle Aaron, we are all undone! Now help, or woe betide thee evermore!
BOT: Why, what a caterwauling dost thou keep! What dost thou wrap and fumble in thy arms?
USER: O, that which I would hide from heaven's eye, Our empress' shame and stately Rome's disgrace. She is delivered, lords, she is delivered.
BOT: To whom?
USER: I mean, she's brought a-bed.
BOT: Well, God give her good rest! What hath he sent her?
USER: A devil.
BOT: Why, then she is the devil's dam. A joyful issue.
USER: Zounds, ye whore, is black so base a hue? Sweet blowse, you are a beauteous blossom sure.
BOT: Villain, what hast thou done?
USER: That which thou canst not undo.
BOT: Thou hast undone our mother.
USER: Villain, I have done thy mother.
BOT: And therein, hellish dog, thou hast undone her. Woe to her chance, and damned her loathed choice! Accursed the offspring of so foul a fiend!
USER: It shall not live.
BOT: It shall not die.
USER: Aaron, it must; the mother wills it so.
BOT: What, must it, nurse? Then let no man but I Do execution on my flesh and blood.
USER: I'll broach the tadpole on my rapier's point. Nurse, give it me; my sword shall soon dispatch it.
BOT: Wilt thou betray thy noble mistress thus?
USER: My mistress is my mistress; this my self; The vigour and the picture of my youth. This before all the world do I prefer; This maugre all the world will I keep safe, Or some of you shall smoke for it in Rome.
BOT: By this our mother is for ever shamed.
USER: Rome will despise her for this foul escape.
BOT: The emperor in his rage will doom her death.
USER: I blush to think upon this ignomy.
BOT: Aaron, what shall I say unto the empress?
USER: Advise thee, Aaron, what is to be done, And we will all subscribe to thy advice. Save thou the child, so we may all be safe.
BOT: Then sit we down, and let us all consult. My son and I will have the wind of you. Keep there. Now talk at pleasure of your safety.
USER: How many women saw this child of his?
BOT: Why, so, brave lords! When we join in league, I am a lamb; but if you brave the Moor, The chafed boar, the mountain lioness, The ocean swells not so as Aaron storms. But say again, how many saw the child?
USER: Cornelia the midwife and myself, And no one else but the delivered empress.
BOT: The empress, the midwife, and yourself. Two may keep counsel when the third's away. Go to the empress; tell her this I said. "Wheak, wheak!" So cries a pig prepared to the spit.
USER: What mean'st thou, Aaron? Wherefore didst thou this?
BOT: Aaron, I see thou wilt not trust the air With secrets.
USER: For this care of Tamora, Herself and hers are highly bound to thee.

USER: O Publius, is not this a heavy case, To see thy noble uncle thus distract?
BOT: Therefore, my lords, it highly us concerns By day and night to attend him carefully, And feed his humour kindly as we may, Till time beget some careful remedy.
USER: Kinsmen, his sorrows are past remedy, But . . . . Join with the Goths, and with revengeful war Take wreak on Rome for this ingratitude, And vengeance on the traitor Saturnine.
BOT: Publius, how now? How now, my masters? What, have you met with her?
USER: Kinsmen, shoot all your shafts into the court. We will afflict the emperor in his pride.
BOT: Now, masters, draw. [_They shoot_.] O, well said, Lucius! Good boy, in Virgo's lap! Give it Pallas.
USER: My lord, I aim a mile beyond the moon. Your letter is with Jupiter by this.
BOT: Ha! ha! Publius, Publius, what hast thou done? See, see, thou hast shot off one of Taurus' horns.
USER: Why, there it goes. God give his lordship joy! News, news from heaven! Marcus, the post is come. Sirrah, what tidings? Have you any letters? Shall I have justice? What says Jupiter?
BOT: Ho, the gibbet-maker? He says that he hath taken them down again, for the man must not be hanged till the next week.
USER: But what says Jupiter, I ask thee?
BOT: Alas, sir, I know not Jubiter; I never drank with him in all my life.
USER: Why, villain, art not thou the carrier?
BOT: Ay, of my pigeons, sir; nothing else.
USER: Why, didst thou not come from heaven?
BOT: Why, sir, that is as fit as can be to serve for your oration; and let him deliver the pigeons to the emperor from you.
USER: Tell me, can you deliver an oration to the emperor with a grace?
BOT: Nay, truly, sir, I could never say grace in all my life.
USER: Ay, sir.
BOT: I warrant you, sir; let me alone.
USER: Sirrah, hast thou a knife? Come let me see it. Here, Marcus, fold it in the oration; For thou hast made it like a humble suppliant. And when thou hast given it to the emperor, Knock at my door, and tell me what he says.
BOT: God be with you, sir; I will.
USER: Come, Marcus, let us go. Publius, follow me.

USER: Yes, forsooth, an your mistresship be emperial.
BOT: Empress I am, but yonder sits the emperor.
USER: 'Tis he. God and Saint Stephen give you good e'en. I have brought you a letter and a couple of pigeons here.
BOT: Go take him away, and hang him presently.
USER: How much money must I have?
BOT: Come, sirrah, you must be hanged.
USER: Hanged! by'r Lady, then I have brought up a neck to a fair end.
BOT: Why should you fear? Is not your city strong?
USER: Ay, but the citizens favour Lucius, And will revolt from me to succour him.
BOT: But he will not entreat his son for us.
