USER: Aemilius, do this message honourably, And if he stand on hostage for his safety, Bid him demand what pledge will please him best.
BOT: Your bidding shall I do effectually.
USER: Now will I to that old Andronicus, And temper him with all the art I have, To pluck proud Lucius from the warlike Goths. And now, sweet emperor, be blithe again, And bury all thy fear in my devices.
BOT: Then go successantly, and plead to him.

USER: And as he saith, so say we all with him.
BOT: I humbly thank him, and I thank you all. But who comes here, led by a lusty Goth?
USER: Touch not the boy, he is of royal blood.
BOT: Too like the sire for ever being good. First hang the child, that he may see it sprawl, A sight to vex the father's soul withal. Get me a ladder.
USER: Say on, and if it please me which thou speak'st, Thy child shall live, and I will see it nourished.
BOT: Tell on thy mind; I say thy child shall live.
USER: Swear that he shall, and then I will begin.
BOT: Who should I swear by? Thou believ'st no god. That granted, how canst thou believe an oath?
USER: Even by my god I swear to thee I will.
BOT: First know thou, I begot him on the empress.
USER: O most insatiate and luxurious woman!
BOT: O detestable villain, call'st thou that trimming?
USER: Why, she was washed, and cut, and trimmed; and 'twas Trim sport for them which had the doing of it.
BOT: O barbarous beastly villains, like thyself!
USER: What, canst thou say all this and never blush?
BOT: Ay, like a black dog, as the saying is.
USER: Art thou not sorry for these heinous deeds?
BOT: Bring down the devil, for he must not die So sweet a death as hanging presently.
USER: If there be devils, would I were a devil, To live and burn in everlasting fire, So I might have your company in hell But to torment you with my bitter tongue!
BOT: Sirs, stop his mouth, and let him speak no more.
USER: My lord, there is a messenger from Rome Desires to be admitted to your presence.
BOT: Let him come near. Welcome, Aemilius. What's the news from Rome?
USER: What says our general?
BOT: Aemilius, let the emperor give his pledges Unto my father and my uncle Marcus, And we will come. March away.

USER: Titus, I am come to talk with thee.
BOT: No, not a word; how can I grace my talk, Wanting a hand to give it action? Thou hast the odds of me; therefore no more.
USER: If thou didst know me, thou wouldst talk with me.
BOT: Art thou Revenge? And art thou sent to me To be a torment to mine enemies?
USER: I am; therefore come down and welcome me.
BOT: These are my ministers, and come with me.
USER: Are they thy ministers? What are they called?
BOT: Rapine and Murder; therefore called so 'Cause they take vengeance of such kind of men.
USER: What wouldst thou have us do, Andronicus?
BOT: Show me a murderer, I'll deal with him.
USER: Show me a villain that hath done a rape, And I am sent to be revenged on him.
BOT: Show me a thousand that hath done thee wrong, And I will be revenged on them all.
USER: This will I do, and soon return again.
BOT: Now will I hence about thy business, And take my ministers along with me.
USER: Nay, nay, let Rape and Murder stay with me, Or else I'll call my brother back again And cleave to no revenge but Lucius.
BOT: Whiles I go tell my lord the emperor How I have governed our determined jest? Yield to his humour, smooth and speak him fair, And tarry with him till I come again.
USER: And will o'erreach them in their own devices, A pair of cursed hell-hounds and their dam.
BOT: Madam, depart at pleasure; leave us here.
USER: Farewell, Andronicus. Revenge now goes To lay a complot to betray thy foes.
BOT: I know thou dost; and, sweet Revenge, farewell.
USER: Tell us, old man, how shall we be employed?
BOT: Tut, I have work enough for you to do. Publius, come hither, Caius, and Valentine.
USER: What is your will?
BOT: Know you these two?
USER: The empress' sons, I take them, Chiron, Demetrius.
BOT: Villains, forbear! We are the empress' sons.
USER: And therefore do we what we are commanded. Stop close their mouths, let them not speak a word. Is he sure bound? Look that you bind them fast.

USER: Uncle Marcus, since 'tis my father's mind That I repair to Rome, I am content.
BOT: And ours with thine, befall what fortune will.
USER: Some devil whisper curses in my ear, And prompt me that my tongue may utter forth The venomous malice of my swelling heart!
BOT: Away, inhuman dog, unhallowed slave! Sirs, help our uncle to convey him in. The trumpets show the emperor is at hand. others.
USER: What, hath the firmament more suns than one?
BOT: What boots it thee to call thyself a sun?
USER: Marcus, we will. Trumpets sounding, enter Titus like a cook, placing the dishes, with Young Lucius and others, and Lavinia with a veil over her face.
BOT: Welcome, my lord; welcome, dread queen; Welcome, ye warlike Goths; welcome, Lucius; And welcome all. Although the cheer be poor, 'Twill fill your stomachs; please you eat of it.
USER: Why art thou thus attired, Andronicus?
BOT: Because I would be sure to have all well To entertain your highness and your empress.
USER: We are beholden to you, good Andronicus.
BOT: An if your highness knew my heart, you were. My lord the emperor, resolve me this: Was it well done of rash Virginius To slay his daughter with his own right hand, Because she was enforced, stained, and deflowered?
USER: It was, Andronicus.
BOT: Your reason, mighty lord?
USER: Because the girl should not survive her shame, And by her presence still renew his sorrows.
BOT: A reason mighty, strong, and effectual; A pattern, precedent, and lively warrant For me, most wretched, to perform the like. Die, die, Lavinia, and thy shame with thee; And with thy shame thy father's sorrow die!
USER: What hast thou done, unnatural and unkind?
BOT: Killed her for whom my tears have made me blind. I am as woeful as Virginius was, And have a thousand times more cause than he To do this outrage, and it now is done.
USER: What, was she ravished? Tell who did the deed.
BOT: Will't please you eat? Will't please your highness feed?
USER: Why hast thou slain thine only daughter thus?
BOT: Not I; 'twas Chiron and Demetrius. They ravished her, and cut away her tongue; And they, 'twas they, that did her all this wrong.
USER: Go fetch them hither to us presently.
BOT: Why, there they are, both baked in that pie, Whereof their mother daintily hath fed, Eating the flesh that she herself hath bred. 'Tis true, 'tis true; witness my knife's sharp point.
USER: Die, frantic wretch, for this accursed deed.
BOT: Can the son's eye behold his father bleed? There's meed for meed, death for a deadly deed. stage._]
USER: Come, come, thou reverend man of Rome, And bring our emperor gently in thy hand, Lucius our emperor; for well I know The common voice do cry it shall be so.
BOT: Lucius, all hail, Rome's royal emperor!
USER: Go, go into old Titus' sorrowful house, And hither hale that misbelieving Moor To be adjudged some direful slaught'ring death, As punishment for his most wicked life. stage._]
BOT: Lucius, all hail, Rome's gracious governor!
USER: Tear for tear and loving kiss for kiss Thy brother Marcus tenders on thy lips. O, were the sum of these that I should pay Countless and infinite, yet would I pay them.
BOT: O grandsire, grandsire, e'en with all my heart Would I were dead, so you did live again! O Lord, I cannot speak to him for weeping; My tears will choke me if I ope my mouth.
USER: You sad Andronici, have done with woes. Give sentence on the execrable wretch That hath been breeder of these dire events.
BOT: Set him breast-deep in earth and famish him; There let him stand and rave and cry for food. If anyone relieves or pities him, For the offence he dies. This is our doom. Some stay to see him fastened in the earth.

USER: Call here my varlet; I'll unarm again. Why should I war without the walls of Troy That find such cruel battle here within? Each Trojan that is master of his heart, Let him to field; Troilus, alas! hath none.
BOT: Will this gear ne'er be mended?
USER: Well, I have told you enough of this; for my part, I'll not meddle nor make no farther. He that will have a cake out of the wheat must tarry the grinding.
BOT: Have I not tarried?
USER: Ay, the grinding; but you must tarry the bolting.
BOT: Have I not tarried?
USER: Ay, the bolting; but you must tarry the leavening.
BOT: Still have I tarried.
USER: Ay, to the leavening; but here's yet in the word 'hereafter' the kneading, the making of the cake, the heating of the oven, and the baking; nay, you must stay the cooling too, or you may chance burn your lips.
BOT: Patience herself, what goddess e'er she be, Doth lesser blench at suff'rance than I do. At Priam's royal table do I sit; And when fair Cressid comes into my thoughts, So, traitor! 'when she comes'! when she is thence?
USER: Well, she look'd yesternight fairer than ever I saw her look, or any woman else.
BOT: I speak no more than truth.
USER: Thou dost not speak so much.
BOT: Faith, I'll not meddle in't. Let her be as she is: if she be fair, 'tis the better for her; and she be not, she has the mends in her own hands.
USER: Good Pandarus! How now, Pandarus!
BOT: I have had my labour for my travail, ill thought on of her and ill thought on of you; gone between and between, but small thanks for my labour.
USER: What! art thou angry, Pandarus? What! with me?
BOT: Because she's kin to me, therefore she's not so fair as Helen. And she were not kin to me, she would be as fair on Friday as Helen is on Sunday. But what care I? I care not and she were a blackamoor; 'tis all one to me.
USER: Say I she is not fair?
BOT: I do not care whether you do or no. She's a fool to stay behind her father. Let her to the Greeks; and so I'll tell her the next time I see her. For my part, I'll meddle nor make no more i' the matter.
USER: Pandarus -
BOT: Not I.
USER: Sweet Pandarus -
BOT: Pray you, speak no more to me: I will leave all as I found it, and there an end.
USER: How now, Prince Troilus! Wherefore not afield?
BOT: Because not there. This woman's answer sorts, For womanish it is to be from thence. What news, Aeneas, from the field today?
USER: That Paris is returned home, and hurt.
BOT: By whom, Aeneas?
USER: Troilus, by Menelaus.
BOT: Let Paris bleed: 'tis but a scar to scorn; Paris is gor'd with Menelaus' horn.
USER: Hark what good sport is out of town today!
BOT: Better at home, if 'would I might' were 'may.' But to the sport abroad. Are you bound thither?
USER: In all swift haste.
BOT: Come, go we then together.

USER: Who were those went by?
BOT: Queen Hecuba and Helen.
USER: And whither go they?
BOT: What was his cause of anger?
USER: The noise goes, this: there is among the Greeks A lord of Trojan blood, nephew to Hector; They call him Ajax.
BOT: Good; and what of him?
USER: They say he is a very man _per se_ And stands alone.
BOT: So do all men, unless they are drunk, sick, or have no legs.
USER: But how should this man, that makes me smile, make Hector angry?
BOT: They say he yesterday cop'd Hector in the battle and struck him down, the disdain and shame whereof hath ever since kept Hector fasting and waking.
USER: Who comes here?
BOT: Madam, your uncle Pandarus.
USER: Hector's a gallant man.
BOT: As may be in the world, lady.
USER: What's that? What's that?
BOT: Good morrow, uncle Pandarus.
USER: Good morrow, cousin Cressid. What do you talk of? - Good morrow, Alexander. - How do you, cousin? When were you at Ilium?
BOT: This morning, uncle.
USER: What were you talking of when I came? Was Hector arm'd and gone ere you came to Ilium? Helen was not up, was she?
BOT: Hector was gone; but Helen was not up.
USER: E'en so. Hector was stirring early.
BOT: That were we talking of, and of his anger.
USER: Was he angry?
BOT: So he says here.
USER: True, he was so; I know the cause too; he'll lay about him today, I can tell them that. And there's Troilus will not come far behind him; let them take heed of Troilus, I can tell them that too.
BOT: What, is he angry too?
USER: Who, Troilus? Troilus is the better man of the two.
BOT: O Jupiter! there's no comparison.
USER: What, not between Troilus and Hector? Do you know a man if you see him?
BOT: Ay, if I ever saw him before and knew him.
USER: Well, I say Troilus is Troilus.
BOT: Then you say as I say, for I am sure he is not Hector.
USER: No, nor Hector is not Troilus in some degrees.
BOT: 'Tis just to each of them: he is himself.
USER: Himself! Alas, poor Troilus! I would he were!
BOT: So he is.
USER: Condition I had gone barefoot to India.
BOT: He is not Hector.
USER: Himself! no, he's not himself. Would a' were himself! Well, the gods are above; time must friend or end. Well, Troilus, well! I would my heart were in her body! No, Hector is not a better man than Troilus.
BOT: Excuse me.
USER: He is elder.
BOT: Pardon me, pardon me.
USER: Th'other's not come to't; you shall tell me another tale when th'other's come to't. Hector shall not have his wit this year.
BOT: He shall not need it if he have his own.
USER: Nor his qualities.
BOT: No matter.
USER: Nor his beauty.
BOT: 'Twould not become him: his own's better.
USER: You have no judgement, niece. Helen herself swore th'other day that Troilus, for a brown favour, for so 'tis, I must confess - not brown neither -
BOT: No, but brown.
USER: Faith, to say truth, brown and not brown.
BOT: To say the truth, true and not true.
USER: She prais'd his complexion above Paris.
BOT: Why, Paris hath colour enough.
USER: So he has.
BOT: I swear to you I think Helen loves him better than Paris.
USER: Then she's a merry Greek indeed.
BOT: Nay, I am sure she does. She came to him th'other day into the compass'd window - and you know he has not past three or four hairs on his chin -
USER: Indeed a tapster's arithmetic may soon bring his particulars therein to a total.
BOT: Why, he is very young, and yet will he within three pound lift as much as his brother Hector.
USER: Is he so young a man and so old a lifter?
BOT: But to prove to you that Helen loves him: she came and puts me her white hand to his cloven chin -
USER: Juno have mercy! How came it cloven?
BOT: Why, you know, 'tis dimpled. I think his smiling becomes him better than any man in all Phrygia.
USER: O, he smiles valiantly!
BOT: Does he not?
USER: O yes, an 'twere a cloud in autumn!
BOT: Why, go to, then! But to prove to you that Helen loves Troilus -
USER: Troilus will stand to the proof, if you'll prove it so.
BOT: Troilus! Why, he esteems her no more than I esteem an addle egg.
USER: If you love an addle egg as well as you love an idle head, you would eat chickens i' th' shell.
BOT: I cannot choose but laugh to think how she tickled his chin. Indeed, she has a marvell's white hand, I must needs confess.
USER: Without the rack.
BOT: And she takes upon her to spy a white hair on his chin.
USER: Alas, poor chin! Many a wart is richer.
BOT: But there was such laughing! Queen Hecuba laugh'd that her eyes ran o'er.
USER: With millstones.
BOT: And Cassandra laugh'd.
USER: But there was a more temperate fire under the pot of her eyes. Did her eyes run o'er too?
BOT: And Hector laugh'd.
USER: At what was all this laughing?
BOT: Marry, at the white hair that Helen spied on Troilus' chin.
USER: And't had been a green hair I should have laugh'd too.
BOT: They laugh'd not so much at the hair as at his pretty answer.
USER: What was his answer?
BOT: Quoth she 'Here's but two and fifty hairs on your chin, and one of them is white.'
USER: This is her question.
BOT: So let it now; for it has been a great while going by.
USER: Well, cousin, I told you a thing yesterday; think on't.
BOT: So I do.
USER: I'll be sworn 'tis true; he will weep you, and 'twere a man born in April.
BOT: And I'll spring up in his tears, an 'twere a nettle against May.
USER: Hark! they are coming from the field. Shall we stand up here and see them as they pass toward Ilium? Good niece, do, sweet niece Cressida.
BOT: At your pleasure.
USER: Here, here, here's an excellent place; here we may see most bravely. I'll tell you them all by their names as they pass by; but mark Troilus above the rest.
BOT: Speak not so loud.
USER: That's Aeneas. Is not that a brave man? He's one of the flowers of Troy, I can tell you. But mark Troilus; you shall see anon.
BOT: Who's that?
USER: Will he give you the nod?
BOT: You shall see.
USER: If he do, the rich shall have more.
BOT: That's Hector, that, that, look you, that; there's a fellow! Go thy way, Hector! There's a brave man, niece. O brave Hector! Look how he looks. There's a countenance! Is't not a brave man?
USER: O, a brave man!
BOT: Is a' not? It does a man's heart good. Look you what hacks are on his helmet! Look you yonder, do you see? Look you there. There's no jesting; there's laying on; take't off who will, as they say. There be hacks.
USER: Be those with swords?
BOT: Who's that?
USER: That's Helenus. I marvel where Troilus is. That's Helenus. I think he went not forth today. That's Helenus.
BOT: Can Helenus fight, uncle?
USER: Helenus! no. Yes, he'll fight indifferent well. I marvel where Troilus is. Hark! do you not hear the people cry 'Troilus'? - Helenus is a priest.
BOT: What sneaking fellow comes yonder?
USER: Where? yonder? That's Deiphobus. 'Tis Troilus. There's a man, niece. Hem! Brave Troilus, the prince of chivalry!
BOT: Peace, for shame, peace!
USER: Here comes more.
BOT: There is amongst the Greeks Achilles, a better man than Troilus.
USER: Achilles? A drayman, a porter, a very camel!
BOT: Well, well.
USER: Ay, a minc'd man; and then to be bak'd with no date in the pie, for then the man's date is out.
BOT: You are such a woman! A man knows not at what ward you lie.
USER: Upon my back, to defend my belly; upon my wit, to defend my wiles; upon my secrecy, to defend mine honesty; my mask, to defend my beauty; and you, to defend all these; and at all these wards I lie, at a thousand watches.
BOT: Say one of your watches.
USER: You are such another!
BOT: Sir, my lord would instantly speak with you.
USER: Where?
BOT: At your own house; there he unarms him.
USER: Good boy, tell him I come. [_Exit_ Boy.] I doubt he be hurt. Fare ye well, good niece.
BOT: Adieu, uncle.
USER: I will be with you, niece, by and by.
BOT: To bring, uncle.
USER: Ay, a token from Troilus.

USER: Speak, Prince of Ithaca; and be't of less expect That matter needless, of importless burden, Divide thy lips than we are confident, When rank Thersites opes his mastic jaws, We shall hear music, wit, and oracle.
BOT: Most wisely hath Ulysses here discover'd The fever whereof all our power is sick.
USER: The nature of the sickness found, Ulysses, What is the remedy?
BOT: Let this be granted, and Achilles' horse Makes many Thetis' sons.
USER: What trumpet? Look, Menelaus.
BOT: From Troy.
USER: What would you fore our tent?
BOT: Is this great Agamemnon's tent, I pray you?
USER: Even this.
BOT: May one that is a herald and a prince Do a fair message to his kingly eyes?
USER: With surety stronger than Achilles' arm Fore all the Greekish heads, which with one voice Call Agamemnon head and general.
BOT: Fair leave and large security. How may A stranger to those most imperial looks Know them from eyes of other mortals?
USER: How?
BOT: This Trojan scorns us, or the men of Troy Are ceremonious courtiers.
USER: Sir, you of Troy, call you yourself Aeneas?
BOT: Ay, Greek, that is my name.
USER: What's your affairs, I pray you?
BOT: Sir, pardon; 'tis for Agamemnon's ears. AGAMEMNON He hears naught privately that comes from Troy.
USER: Nor I from Troy come not to whisper with him; I bring a trumpet to awake his ear, To set his sense on the attentive bent, And then to speak.
BOT: Speak frankly as the wind; It is not Agamemnon's sleeping hour. That thou shalt know, Trojan, he is awake, He tells thee so himself.
USER: Now heavens forfend such scarcity of youth!
BOT: Amen.
USER: Nestor!
BOT: What says Ulysses?
USER: I have a young conception in my brain; Be you my time to bring it to some shape.
BOT: What is't?
USER: This 'tis: Blunt wedges rive hard knots. The seeded pride That hath to this maturity blown up In rank Achilles must or now be cropp'd Or, shedding, breed a nursery of like evil To overbulk us all.
BOT: Well, and how?
USER: This challenge that the gallant Hector sends, However it is spread in general name, Relates in purpose only to Achilles.
BOT: And wake him to the answer, think you?
USER: I see them not with my old eyes. What are they?
BOT: Now, Ulysses, I begin to relish thy advice; And I will give a taste thereof forthwith To Agamemnon. Go we to him straight. Two curs shall tame each other: pride alone Must tarre the mastiffs on, as 'twere their bone.

USER: Thersites!
BOT: Agamemnon - how if he had boils, full, all over, generally?
USER: Thersites!
BOT: And those boils did run - say so. Did not the general run then? Were not that a botchy core?
USER: Dog!
BOT: Then there would come some matter from him; I see none now.
USER: Thou bitch-wolf's son, canst thou not hear? Feel, then.
BOT: The plague of Greece upon thee, thou mongrel beef-witted lord!
USER: Speak, then, thou unsalted leaven, speak. I will beat thee into handsomeness.
BOT: I shall sooner rail thee into wit and holiness; but I think thy horse will sooner con an oration than thou learn a prayer without book. Thou canst strike, canst thou? A red murrain o' thy jade's tricks!
USER: Toadstool, learn me the proclamation.
BOT: Dost thou think I have no sense, thou strikest me thus?
USER: The proclamation!
BOT: Thou art proclaim'd fool, I think.
USER: Do not, porpentine, do not; my fingers itch.
BOT: I would thou didst itch from head to foot and I had the scratching of thee; I would make thee the loathsomest scab in Greece. When thou art forth in the incursions, thou strikest as slow as another.
USER: I say, the proclamation.
BOT: Thou grumblest and railest every hour on Achilles; and thou art as full of envy at his greatness as Cerberus is at Proserpina's beauty - ay, that thou bark'st at him.
USER: Mistress Thersites!
BOT: Thou shouldst strike him.
USER: Cobloaf!
BOT: He would pun thee into shivers with his fist, as a sailor breaks a biscuit.
USER: You whoreson cur!
BOT: Do, do.
USER: Thou stool for a witch!
BOT: You dog!
USER: You scurvy lord!
BOT: You cur!
USER: Mars his idiot! Do, rudeness; do, camel; do, do.
BOT: Why, how now, Ajax! Wherefore do ye thus? How now, Thersites! What's the matter, man?
USER: You see him there, do you?
BOT: Ay; what's the matter?
USER: Nay, look upon him.
BOT: So I do. What's the matter?
USER: Nay, but regard him well.
BOT: Well! why, so I do.
USER: But yet you look not well upon him; for whosomever you take him to be, he is Ajax.
BOT: I know that, fool.
USER: Ay, but that fool knows not himself.
BOT: Therefore I beat thee.
USER: What?
BOT: I say this Ajax -
USER: Nay, good Ajax.
BOT: Has not so much wit -
USER: Nay, I must hold you.
BOT: As will stop the eye of Helen's needle, for whom he comes to fight.
USER: Peace, fool.
BOT: I would have peace and quietness, but the fool will not - he there; that he; look you there.
USER: O thou damned cur! I shall -
BOT: Will you set your wit to a fool's?
USER: No, I warrant you, the fool's will shame it.
BOT: Good words, Thersites.
USER: What's the quarrel?
BOT: I bade the vile owl go learn me the tenour of the proclamation, and he rails upon me.
USER: I serve thee not.
BOT: Well, go to, go to.
USER: I serve here voluntary.
BOT: Your last service was suff'rance; 'twas not voluntary. No man is beaten voluntary. Ajax was here the voluntary, and you as under an impress.
USER: E'en so; a great deal of your wit too lies in your sinews, or else there be liars. Hector shall have a great catch and knock out either of your brains: a' were as good crack a fusty nut with no kernel.
BOT: What, with me too, Thersites?
USER: There's Ulysses and old Nestor - whose wit was mouldy ere your grandsires had nails on their toes - yoke you like draught oxen, and make you plough up the wars.
BOT: What, what?
USER: Yes, good sooth. To Achilles, to Ajax, to -
BOT: I shall cut out your tongue.
USER: 'Tis no matter; I shall speak as much as thou afterwards.
BOT: No more words, Thersites; peace!
USER: I will hold my peace when Achilles' brach bids me, shall I?
BOT: There's for you, Patroclus.
USER: I will see you hang'd like clotpoles ere I come any more to your tents. I will keep where there is wit stirring, and leave the faction of fools.
BOT: A good riddance.
USER: Farewell. Who shall answer him?
BOT: I know not; 'tis put to lott'ry, otherwise, He knew his man.
USER: O, meaning you? I will go learn more of it.

USER: No marvel though you bite so sharp of reasons, You are so empty of them. Should not our father Bear the great sway of his affairs with reason, Because your speech hath none that tells him so?
BOT: Brother, she is not worth what she doth cost the keeping.
USER: What's aught but as 'tis valued?
BOT: What noise, what shriek is this?
USER: 'Tis our mad sister; I do know her voice.
BOT: It is Cassandra.
USER: Cry, Trojans, cry. Lend me ten thousand eyes, And I will fill them with prophetic tears.
BOT: Peace, sister, peace.
USER: Paris, you speak Like one besotted on your sweet delights. You have the honey still, but these the gall; So to be valiant is no praise at all.

USER: Who's there? Thersites! Good Thersites, come in and rail.
BOT: What, art thou devout? Wast thou in prayer?
USER: Ay, the heavens hear me!
BOT: Amen.
USER: Who's there?
BOT: Thersites, my lord.
USER: Where, where? O, where? Art thou come? Why, my cheese, my digestion, why hast thou not served thyself in to my table so many meals? Come, what's Agamemnon?
BOT: Thy commander, Achilles. Then tell me, Patroclus, what's Achilles?
USER: Thy lord, Thersites. Then tell me, I pray thee, what's Thersites?
BOT: Thy knower, Patroclus. Then tell me, Patroclus, what art thou?
USER: Thou must tell that knowest.
BOT: O, tell, tell,
USER: I'll decline the whole question. Agamemnon commands Achilles; Achilles is my lord; I am Patroclus' knower; and Patroclus is a fool.
BOT: You rascal!
USER: Peace, fool! I have not done.
BOT: He is a privileg'd man. Proceed, Thersites.
USER: Agamemnon is a fool; Achilles is a fool; Thersites is a fool; and, as aforesaid, Patroclus is a fool.
BOT: Derive this; come.
USER: Agamemnon is a fool to offer to command Achilles; Achilles is a fool to be commanded of Agamemnon; Thersites is a fool to serve such a fool; and this Patroclus is a fool positive.
BOT: Why am I a fool?
USER: Make that demand of the Creator. It suffices me thou art. Look you, who comes here?
BOT: Come, Patroclus, I'll speak with nobody. Come in with me, Thersites.
USER: Where is Achilles?
BOT: Within his tent; but ill-dispos'd, my lord.
USER: I shall say so to him.
BOT: We saw him at the opening of his tent. He is not sick.
USER: Yes, lion-sick, sick of proud heart. You may call it melancholy, if you will favour the man; but, by my head, 'tis pride. But why, why? Let him show us a cause. A word, my lord.
BOT: What moves Ajax thus to bay at him?
USER: Achilles hath inveigled his fool from him.
BOT: Who, Thersites?
USER: He.
BOT: Then will Ajax lack matter, if he have lost his argument.
USER: No; you see he is his argument that has his argument, Achilles.
BOT: All the better; their fraction is more our wish than their faction. But it was a strong composure a fool could disunite!
USER: The amity that wisdom knits not, folly may easily untie. Here comes Patroclus.
BOT: No Achilles with him.
USER: The elephant hath joints, but none for courtesy; his legs are legs for necessity, not for flexure.
BOT: I shall, and bring his answer presently.
USER: In second voice we'll not be satisfied; We come to speak with him. Ulysses, enter you.
BOT: What is he more than another?
USER: No more than what he thinks he is.
BOT: Is he so much? Do you not think he thinks himself a better man than I am?
USER: No question.
BOT: Will you subscribe his thought and say he is?
USER: No, noble Ajax; you are as strong, as valiant, as wise, no less noble, much more gentle, and altogether more tractable.
BOT: Why should a man be proud? How doth pride grow? I know not what pride is.
USER: I do hate a proud man as I do hate the engend'ring of toads.
BOT: Achilles will not to the field tomorrow.
USER: What's his excuse?
BOT: He doth rely on none; But carries on the stream of his dispose, Without observance or respect of any, In will peculiar and in self-admission.
USER: Why will he not, upon our fair request, Untent his person and share th'air with us?
BOT: Let Ajax go to him. Dear lord, go you and greet him in his tent. 'Tis said he holds you well; and will be led At your request a little from himself.
USER: If I go to him, with my armed fist I'll pash him o'er the face.
BOT: O, no, you shall not go.
USER: An a' be proud with me I'll pheeze his pride. Let me go to him.
BOT: Not for the worth that hangs upon our quarrel.
USER: A paltry, insolent fellow!
BOT: Can he not be sociable?
USER: I'll let his humours blood.
BOT: And all men were o' my mind -
USER: A' should not bear it so, a' should eat's words first. Shall pride carry it?
BOT: I will knead him, I'll make him supple.
USER: pour in; his ambition is dry.
BOT: Our noble general, do not do so.
USER: You must prepare to fight without Achilles.
BOT: Why 'tis this naming of him does him harm. Here is a man - but 'tis before his face; I will be silent.
USER: Wherefore should you so? He is not emulous, as Achilles is.
BOT: Know the whole world, he is as valiant.
USER: A whoreson dog, that shall palter with us thus! Would he were a Trojan!
BOT: What a vice were it in Ajax now -
USER: If he were proud.
BOT: Or covetous of praise.
USER: Ay, or surly borne.
BOT: Or strange, or self-affected.
USER: Shall I call you father?
BOT: Ay, my good son.
USER: Be rul'd by him, Lord Ajax.
BOT: Go we to council. Let Achilles sleep. Light boats sail swift, though greater hulks draw deep.

USER: Friend, you - pray you, a word. Do you not follow the young Lord Paris?
BOT: Ay, sir, when he goes before me.
USER: You depend upon him, I mean?
BOT: Sir, I do depend upon the Lord.
USER: You depend upon a notable gentleman; I must needs praise him.
BOT: The Lord be praised!
USER: You know me, do you not?
BOT: Faith, sir, superficially.
USER: Friend, know me better: I am the Lord Pandarus.
BOT: I hope I shall know your honour better.
USER: I do desire it.
BOT: You are in the state of grace?
USER: Grace? Not so, friend; honour and lordship are my titles. What music is this?
BOT: I do but partly know, sir; it is music in parts.
USER: Know you the musicians?
BOT: Wholly, sir.
USER: Who play they to?
BOT: To the hearers, sir.
USER: At whose pleasure, friend?
BOT: At mine, sir, and theirs that love music.
USER: Command, I mean, friend.
BOT: Who shall I command, sir?
USER: Friend, we understand not one another: I am too courtly, and thou art too cunning. At whose request do these men play?
BOT: That's to't, indeed, sir. Marry, sir, at the request of Paris my lord, who is there in person; with him the mortal Venus, the heart-blood of beauty, love's invisible soul -
USER: Who, my cousin, Cressida?
BOT: No, sir, Helen. Could not you find out that by her attributes?
USER: It should seem, fellow, that thou hast not seen the Lady Cressida. I come to speak with Paris from the Prince Troilus; I will make a complimental assault upon him, for my business seethes.
BOT: Sodden business! There's a stew'd phrase indeed!
USER: Fair be to you, my lord, and to all this fair company! Fair desires, in all fair measure, fairly guide them - especially to you, fair queen! Fair thoughts be your fair pillow.
BOT: Dear lord, you are full of fair words.
USER: You speak your fair pleasure, sweet queen. Fair prince, here is good broken music.
BOT: You have broke it, cousin; and by my life, you shall make it whole again; you shall piece it out with a piece of your performance.
USER: He is full of harmony.
BOT: Truly, lady, no.
USER: O, sir -
BOT: Rude, in sooth; in good sooth, very rude.
USER: Well said, my lord. Well, you say so in fits.
BOT: I have business to my lord, dear queen. My lord, will you vouchsafe me a word?
USER: Nay, this shall not hedge us out. We'll hear you sing, certainly -
BOT: Well sweet queen, you are pleasant with me. But, marry, thus, my lord: my dear lord and most esteemed friend, your brother Troilus -
USER: My Lord Pandarus, honey-sweet lord -
BOT: Go to, sweet queen, go to - commends himself most affectionately to you -
USER: You shall not bob us out of our melody. If you do, our melancholy upon your head!
BOT: Sweet queen, sweet queen; that's a sweet queen, i' faith.
USER: And to make a sweet lady sad is a sour offence.
BOT: Nay, that shall not serve your turn; that shall it not, in truth, la. Nay, I care not for such words; no, no. - And, my lord, he desires you that, if the King call for him at supper, you will make his excuse.
USER: My Lord Pandarus!
BOT: What says my sweet queen, my very very sweet queen?
USER: What exploit's in hand? Where sups he tonight?
BOT: Nay, but, my lord -
USER: What says my sweet queen? - My cousin will fall out with you.
BOT: You must not know where he sups.
USER: I'll lay my life, with my disposer Cressida.
BOT: No, no, no such matter; you are wide. Come, your disposer is sick.
USER: Well, I'll make's excuse.
BOT: Ay, good my lord. Why should you say Cressida? No, your poor disposer's sick.
USER: I spy.
BOT: You spy! What do you spy? - Come, give me an instrument. Now, sweet queen.
USER: Why, this is kindly done.
BOT: My niece is horribly in love with a thing you have, sweet queen.
USER: She shall have it, my lord, if it be not my Lord Paris.
BOT: He? No, she'll none of him; they two are twain.
USER: Falling in, after falling out, may make them three.
BOT: Come, come. I'll hear no more of this; I'll sing you a song now.
USER: Ay, ay, prithee now. By my troth, sweet lord, thou hast a fine forehead.
BOT: Ay, you may, you may.
USER: Let thy song be love. This love will undo us all. O Cupid, Cupid, Cupid!
BOT: Love! Ay, that it shall, i' faith.
USER: Ay, good now, love, love, nothing but love.
BOT: In love, i' faith, to the very tip of the nose.
USER: He eats nothing but doves, love; and that breeds hot blood, and hot blood begets hot thoughts, and hot thoughts beget hot deeds, and hot deeds is love.
BOT: Is this the generation of love: hot blood, hot thoughts, and hot deeds? Why, they are vipers. Is love a generation of vipers? Sweet lord, who's a-field today?
USER: Hector, Deiphobus, Helenus, Antenor, and all the gallantry of Troy. I would fain have arm'd today, but my Nell would not have it so. How chance my brother Troilus went not?
BOT: He hangs the lip at something. You know all, Lord Pandarus.
USER: Not I, honey-sweet queen. I long to hear how they spend today. You'll remember your brother's excuse?
BOT: To a hair.
USER: Farewell, sweet queen.
BOT: Commend me to your niece.
USER: I will, sweet queen.
BOT: 'Twill make us proud to be his servant, Paris; Yea, what he shall receive of us in duty Gives us more palm in beauty than we have, Yea, overshines ourself.
USER: Sweet, above thought I love thee.

USER: How now! Where's thy master? At my cousin Cressida's?
BOT: No, sir; he stays for you to conduct him thither.
USER: O, here he comes. How now, how now?
BOT: Sirrah, walk off.
USER: Have you seen my cousin?
BOT: Walk here i' th' orchard, I'll bring her straight.
USER: Even such a passion doth embrace my bosom. My heart beats thicker than a feverous pulse, And all my powers do their bestowing lose, Like vassalage at unawares encount'ring The eye of majesty.
BOT: You have bereft me of all words, lady.
USER: Will you walk in, my lord?
BOT: O Cressid, how often have I wish'd me thus!
USER: Wish'd, my lord! The gods grant - O my lord!
BOT: What should they grant? What makes this pretty abruption? What too curious dreg espies my sweet lady in the fountain of our love?
USER: More dregs than water, if my fears have eyes.
BOT: Fears make devils of cherubins; they never see truly.
USER: Blind fear, that seeing reason leads, finds safer footing than blind reason stumbling without fear. To fear the worst oft cures the worse.
BOT: O, let my lady apprehend no fear! In all Cupid's pageant there is presented no monster.
USER: Nor nothing monstrous neither?
BOT: Will you walk in, my lord?
USER: What, blushing still? Have you not done talking yet?
BOT: Well, uncle, what folly I commit, I dedicate to you.
USER: I thank you for that; if my lord get a boy of you, you'll give him me. Be true to my lord; if he flinch, chide me for it.
BOT: You know now your hostages: your uncle's word and my firm faith.
USER: Nay, I'll give my word for her too: our kindred, though they be long ere they are wooed, they are constant being won; they are burs, I can tell you; they'll stick where they are thrown.
BOT: Boldness comes to me now and brings me heart. Prince Troilus, I have lov'd you night and day For many weary months.
USER: Why was my Cressid then so hard to win?
BOT: And shall, albeit sweet music issues thence.
USER: Pretty, i' faith.
BOT: My lord, I do beseech you, pardon me; 'Twas not my purpose thus to beg a kiss. I am asham'd. O heavens! what have I done? For this time will I take my leave, my lord.
USER: Your leave, sweet Cressid!
BOT: Leave! And you take leave till tomorrow morning -
USER: Pray you, content you.
BOT: What offends you, lady?
USER: Sir, mine own company.
BOT: You cannot shun yourself.
USER: Let me go and try. I have a kind of self resides with you; But an unkind self, that itself will leave To be another's fool. I would be gone. Where is my wit? I know not what I speak.
BOT: Well know they what they speak that speak so wisely.
USER: In that I'll war with you.
BOT: Amen.
USER: Amen.

USER: What wouldst thou of us, Trojan? Make demand.
BOT: This shall I undertake; and 'tis a burden Which I am proud to bear.
USER: We'll execute your purpose, and put on A form of strangeness as we pass along. So do each lord; and either greet him not, Or else disdainfully, which shall shake him more Than if not look'd on. I will lead the way.
BOT: What comes the general to speak with me? You know my mind. I'll fight no more 'gainst Troy.
USER: What says Achilles? Would he aught with us?
BOT: Would you, my lord, aught with the general?
USER: No.
BOT: Nothing, my lord.
USER: The better.
BOT: Good day, good day.
USER: How do you? How do you?
BOT: What, does the cuckold scorn me?
USER: How now, Patroclus?
BOT: Good morrow, Ajax.
USER: Ha?
BOT: Good morrow.
USER: Ay, and good next day too.
BOT: What mean these fellows? Know they not Achilles?
USER: They pass by strangely. They were us'd to bend, To send their smiles before them to Achilles, To come as humbly as they us'd to creep To holy altars.
BOT: Now, great Thetis' son!
USER: What are you reading?
BOT: I do believe it; for they pass'd by me As misers do by beggars, neither gave to me Good word nor look. What, are my deeds forgot?
USER: Of this my privacy I have strong reasons.
BOT: But 'gainst your privacy The reasons are more potent and heroical. 'Tis known, Achilles, that you are in love With one of Priam's daughters.
USER: Ha! known!
BOT: Shall Ajax fight with Hector?
USER: Ay, and perhaps receive much honour by him.
BOT: I see my reputation is at stake; My fame is shrewdly gor'd.
USER: A wonder!
BOT: What?
USER: Ajax goes up and down the field asking for himself.
BOT: How so?
USER: He must fight singly tomorrow with Hector, and is so prophetically proud of an heroical cudgelling that he raves in saying nothing.
BOT: How can that be?
USER: Thou must be my ambassador to him, Thersites.
BOT: Who, I? Why, he'll answer nobody; he professes not answering. Speaking is for beggars: he wears his tongue in's arms. I will put on his presence. Let Patroclus make his demands to me, you shall see the pageant of Ajax.
USER: Jove bless great Ajax!
BOT: Hum!
USER: I come from the worthy Achilles -
BOT: Ha!
USER: Who most humbly desires you to invite Hector to his tent -
BOT: Hum!
USER: And to procure safe conduct from Agamemnon.
BOT: Agamemnon?
USER: Ay, my lord.
BOT: Ha!
USER: What you say to't?
BOT: God buy you, with all my heart.
USER: Your answer, sir.
BOT: If tomorrow be a fair day, by eleven of the clock it will go one way or other. Howsoever, he shall pay for me ere he has me.
USER: Your answer, sir.
BOT: Fare ye well, with all my heart.
USER: Why, but he is not in this tune, is he?
BOT: No, but out of tune thus. What music will be in him when Hector has knock'd out his brains, I know not; but, I am sure, none; unless the fiddler Apollo get his sinews to make catlings on.
USER: Come, thou shalt bear a letter to him straight.
BOT: Let me bear another to his horse; for that's the more capable creature.
USER: My mind is troubled, like a fountain stirr'd; And I myself see not the bottom of it.
BOT: Would the fountain of your mind were clear again, that I might water an ass at it. I had rather be a tick in a sheep than such a valiant ignorance.

USER: See, ho! Who is that there?
BOT: It is the Lord Aeneas.
USER: Is the Prince there in person? Had I so good occasion to lie long As you, Prince Paris, nothing but heavenly business Should rob my bed-mate of my company.
BOT: That's my mind too. Good morrow, Lord Aeneas.
USER: A valiant Greek, Aeneas - take his hand: Witness the process of your speech, wherein You told how Diomed, a whole week by days, Did haunt you in the field.
BOT: Health to you, valiant sir, During all question of the gentle truce; But when I meet you arm'd, as black defiance As heart can think or courage execute.
USER: The one and other Diomed embraces. Our bloods are now in calm; and so long health! But when contention and occasion meet, By Jove, I'll play the hunter for thy life With all my force, pursuit, and policy.
BOT: We sympathise. Jove let Aeneas live, If to my sword his fate be not the glory, A thousand complete courses of the sun! But in mine emulous honour let him die With every joint a wound, and that tomorrow!
USER: We know each other well.
BOT: We do; and long to know each other worse.
USER: This is the most despiteful gentle greeting, The noblest hateful love, that e'er I heard of. What business, lord, so early?
BOT: I was sent for to the King; but why, I know not.
USER: That I assure you: Troilus had rather Troy were borne to Greece Than Cressid borne from Troy.
BOT: There is no help; The bitter disposition of the time Will have it so. On, lord; we'll follow you.
USER: Good morrow, all.
BOT: And tell me, noble Diomed, faith, tell me true, Even in the soul of sound good-fellowship, Who in your thoughts deserves fair Helen best, Myself, or Menelaus?
USER: You are too bitter to your country-woman.
BOT: Fair Diomed, you do as chapmen do, Dispraise the thing that you desire to buy; But we in silence hold this virtue well, We'll not commend what we intend to sell. Here lies our way.

USER: Dear, trouble not yourself; the morn is cold.
BOT: Then, sweet my lord, I'll call mine uncle down; He shall unbolt the gates.
USER: Trouble him not; To bed, to bed! Sleep kill those pretty eyes, And give as soft attachment to thy senses As infants empty of all thought!
BOT: Good morrow, then.
USER: I prithee now, to bed.
BOT: Are you aweary of me?
USER: O Cressida! but that the busy day, Wak'd by the lark, hath rous'd the ribald crows, And dreaming night will hide our joys no longer, I would not from thee.
BOT: Night hath been too brief.
USER: Beshrew the witch! with venomous wights she stays As tediously as hell, but flies the grasps of love With wings more momentary-swift than thought. You will catch cold, and curse me.
BOT: Prithee tarry. You men will never tarry. O foolish Cressid! I might have still held off, And then you would have tarried. Hark! there's one up.
USER: It is your uncle.
BOT: A pestilence on him! Now will he be mocking. I shall have such a life!
USER: How now, how now! How go maidenheads? Here, you maid! Where's my cousin Cressid?
BOT: Go hang yourself, you naughty mocking uncle. You bring me to do, and then you flout me too.
USER: To do what? to do what? Let her say what. What have I brought you to do?
BOT: Come, come, beshrew your heart! You'll ne'er be good, nor suffer others.
USER: Ha, ha! Alas, poor wretch! Ah, poor capocchia! Hast not slept tonight? Would he not, a naughty man, let it sleep? A bugbear take him!
BOT: Did not I tell you? Would he were knock'd i' th' head! Who's that at door? Good uncle, go and see. My lord, come you again into my chamber. You smile and mock me, as if I meant naughtily.
USER: Ha! ha!
BOT: Come, you are deceiv'd, I think of no such thing. How earnestly they knock! Pray you come in: I would not for half Troy have you seen here.
USER: Who's there? What's the matter? Will you beat down the door? How now? What's the matter?
BOT: Good morrow, lord, good morrow.
USER: Who's there? My lord Aeneas? By my troth, I knew you not. What news with you so early?
BOT: Is not Prince Troilus here?
USER: Here! What should he do here?
BOT: Come, he is here, my lord; do not deny him. It doth import him much to speak with me.
USER: Is he here, say you? It's more than I know, I'll be sworn. For my own part, I came in late. What should he do here?
BOT: Who, nay then! Come, come, you'll do him wrong ere you are ware; you'll be so true to him to be false to him. Do not you know of him, but yet go fetch him hither; go.
USER: How now! What's the matter?
BOT: Is it so concluded?
USER: By Priam and the general state of Troy. They are at hand, and ready to effect it.
BOT: How my achievements mock me! I will go meet them; and, my Lord Aeneas, We met by chance; you did not find me here.
USER: Good, good, my lord, the secrets of neighbour Pandar Have not more gift in taciturnity.
BOT: Is't possible? No sooner got but lost? The devil take Antenor! The young prince will go mad. A plague upon Antenor! I would they had broke's neck.
USER: How now! What's the matter? Who was here?
BOT: Ah, ah!
USER: Why sigh you so profoundly? Where's my lord? Gone? Tell me, sweet uncle, what's the matter?
BOT: Would I were as deep under the earth as I am above!
USER: O the gods! What's the matter?
BOT: Pray thee get thee in. Would thou hadst ne'er been born! I knew thou wouldst be his death! O, poor gentleman! A plague upon Antenor!
USER: Good uncle, I beseech you, on my knees I beseech you, what's the matter?
BOT: Thou must be gone, wench, thou must be gone; thou art chang'd for Antenor; thou must to thy father, and be gone from Troilus. 'Twill be his death; 'twill be his bane; he cannot bear it.
USER: O you immortal gods! I will not go.
BOT: Thou must.
USER: Do, do.
BOT: Tear my bright hair, and scratch my praised cheeks, Crack my clear voice with sobs and break my heart, With sounding 'Troilus.' I will not go from Troy.

USER: It is great morning; and the hour prefix'd For her delivery to this valiant Greek Comes fast upon. Good my brother Troilus, Tell you the lady what she is to do And haste her to the purpose.
BOT: Walk into her house. I'll bring her to the Grecian presently; And to his hand when I deliver her, Think it an altar, and thy brother Troilus A priest, there off'ring to it his own heart.
USER: I know what 'tis to love, And would, as I shall pity, I could help! Please you walk in, my lords?

USER: Be moderate, be moderate.
BOT: Here, here, here he comes. Ah, sweet ducks!
USER: Cressid, I love thee in so strain'd a purity That the bless'd gods, as angry with my fancy, More bright in zeal than the devotion which Cold lips blow to their deities, take thee from me.
BOT: Have the gods envy?
USER: Ay, ay, ay, ay; 'tis too plain a case.
BOT: And is it true that I must go from Troy?
USER: A hateful truth.
BOT: What! and from Troilus too?
USER: From Troy and Troilus.
BOT: Is't possible?
USER: Hark! you are call'd. Some say the Genius Cries so to him that instantly must die. Bid them have patience; she shall come anon.
BOT: Where are my tears? Rain, to lay this wind, or my heart will be blown up by my throat!
USER: I must then to the Grecians?
BOT: No remedy.
USER: A woeful Cressid 'mongst the merry Greeks! When shall we see again?
BOT: Hear me, my love. Be thou but true of heart.
USER: I true? How now! What wicked deem is this?
BOT: O! you shall be expos'd, my lord, to dangers As infinite as imminent! But I'll be true.
USER: And I'll grow friend with danger. Wear this sleeve.
BOT: And you this glove. When shall I see you?
USER: I will corrupt the Grecian sentinels To give thee nightly visitation. But yet be true.
BOT: O heavens! 'Be true' again!
USER: O heavens! you love me not!
BOT: Do you think I will?
USER: No. But something may be done that we will not; And sometimes we are devils to ourselves, When we will tempt the frailty of our powers, Presuming on their changeful potency.
BOT: Come, kiss; and let us part.
USER: Good brother, come you hither; And bring Aeneas and the Grecian with you.
BOT: My lord, will you be true?
USER: Fair Lady Cressid, So please you, save the thanks this prince expects. The lustre in your eye, heaven in your cheek, Pleads your fair usage; and to Diomed You shall be mistress, and command him wholly.
BOT: Come, to the port. I'll tell thee, Diomed, This brave shall oft make thee to hide thy head. Lady, give me your hand; and, as we walk, To our own selves bend we our needful talk.
USER: Hark! Hector's trumpet.
BOT: How have we spent this morning! The Prince must think me tardy and remiss, That swore to ride before him to the field.
USER: 'Tis Troilus' fault. Come, come to field with him.
BOT: Let us make ready straight.
USER: Yea, with a bridegroom's fresh alacrity Let us address to tend on Hector's heels. The glory of our Troy doth this day lie On his fair worth and single chivalry.

USER: No trumpet answers.
BOT: 'Tis but early days.
USER: Is not yond Diomed, with Calchas' daughter?
BOT: 'Tis he, I ken the manner of his gait: He rises on the toe. That spirit of his In aspiration lifts him from the earth.
USER: Is this the Lady Cressid?
BOT: Even she.
USER: Most dearly welcome to the Greeks, sweet lady.
BOT: Our general doth salute you with a kiss.
USER: Yet is the kindness but particular; 'Twere better she were kiss'd in general.
BOT: And very courtly counsel: I'll begin. So much for Nestor.
USER: I'll take that winter from your lips, fair lady. Achilles bids you welcome.
BOT: I had good argument for kissing once.
USER: But that's no argument for kissing now; For thus popp'd Paris in his hardiment, And parted thus you and your argument.
BOT: O deadly gall, and theme of all our scorns! For which we lose our heads to gild his horns.
USER: The first was Menelaus' kiss; this, mine: Patroclus kisses you.
BOT: O, this is trim!
USER: Paris and I kiss evermore for him.
BOT: I'll have my kiss, sir. Lady, by your leave.
USER: In kissing, do you render or receive?
BOT: Both take and give.
USER: I'll make my match to live, The kiss you take is better than you give; Therefore no kiss.
BOT: I'll give you boot; I'll give you three for one.
USER: You are an odd man; give even or give none.
BOT: An odd man, lady! Every man is odd.
USER: No, Paris is not; for you know 'tis true That you are odd, and he is even with you.
BOT: You fillip me o' th'head.
USER: No, I'll be sworn.
BOT: It were no match, your nail against his horn. May I, sweet lady, beg a kiss of you?
USER: You may.
BOT: I do desire it.
USER: Why, beg then.
BOT: Why then, for Venus' sake give me a kiss When Helen is a maid again, and his.
USER: I am your debtor; claim it when 'tis due.
BOT: Never's my day, and then a kiss of you.
USER: Lady, a word. I'll bring you to your father.
BOT: A woman of quick sense.
USER: The Trojans' trumpet.
BOT: Yonder comes the troop. Trojans, with attendants.
USER: Which way would Hector have it?
BOT: He cares not; he'll obey conditions.
USER: 'Tis done like Hector.
BOT: But securely done, A little proudly, and great deal misprising The knight oppos'd.
USER: If not Achilles, sir, What is your name?
BOT: If not Achilles, nothing.
USER: A maiden battle then? O! I perceive you.
BOT: They are oppos'd already.
USER: What Trojan is that same that looks so heavy?
BOT: They are in action.
USER: Now, Ajax, hold thine own!
BOT: Hector, thou sleep'st; awake thee!
USER: His blows are well dispos'd. There, Ajax!
BOT: You must no more.
USER: Princes, enough, so please you.
BOT: I am not warm yet; let us fight again.
USER: As Hector pleases.
BOT: I thank thee, Hector. Thou art too gentle and too free a man. I came to kill thee, cousin, and bear hence A great addition earned in thy death.
USER: Not Neoptolemus so mirable, On whose bright crest Fame with her loud'st Oyes Cries 'This is he!' could promise to himself A thought of added honour torn from Hector.
BOT: There is expectance here from both the sides What further you will do.
USER: We'll answer it: The issue is embracement. Ajax, farewell.
BOT: If I might in entreaties find success, As seld' I have the chance, I would desire My famous cousin to our Grecian tents.
USER: 'Tis Agamemnon's wish; and great Achilles Doth long to see unarm'd the valiant Hector.
BOT: Great Agamemnon comes to meet us here.
USER: The worthiest of them tell me name by name; But for Achilles, my own searching eyes Shall find him by his large and portly size.
BOT: I thank thee, most imperious Agamemnon.
USER: Let me confirm my princely brother's greeting. You brace of warlike brothers, welcome hither.
BOT: Who must we answer?
USER: The noble Menelaus.
BOT: O you, my lord? By Mars his gauntlet, thanks! Mock not that I affect the untraded oath; Your quondam wife swears still by Venus' glove. She's well, but bade me not commend her to you.
USER: Name her not now, sir; she's a deadly theme.
BOT: O, pardon; I offend.
USER: 'Tis the old Nestor.
BOT: Let me embrace thee, good old chronicle, That hast so long walk'd hand in hand with time. Most reverend Nestor, I am glad to clasp thee.
USER: I would my arms could match thee in contention As they contend with thee in courtesy.
BOT: I would they could.
USER: Ha! By this white beard, I'd fight with thee tomorrow. Well, welcome, welcome! I have seen the time.
BOT: I wonder now how yonder city stands, When we have here her base and pillar by us.
USER: I know your favour, Lord Ulysses, well. Ah, sir, there's many a Greek and Trojan dead, Since first I saw yourself and Diomed In Ilion on your Greekish embassy.
BOT: Sir, I foretold you then what would ensue. My prophecy is but half his journey yet; For yonder walls, that pertly front your town, Yon towers, whose wanton tops do buss the clouds, Must kiss their own feet.
USER: I must not believe you. There they stand yet; and modestly I think The fall of every Phrygian stone will cost A drop of Grecian blood. The end crowns all; And that old common arbitrator, Time, Will one day end it.
BOT: So to him we leave it. Most gentle and most valiant Hector, welcome. After the General, I beseech you next To feast with me and see me at my tent.
USER: I shall forestall thee, Lord Ulysses, thou! Now, Hector, I have fed mine eyes on thee; I have with exact view perus'd thee, Hector, And quoted joint by joint.
BOT: Is this Achilles?
USER: I am Achilles.
BOT: Stand fair, I pray thee; let me look on thee.
USER: Behold thy fill.
BOT: Nay, I have done already.
USER: Thou art too brief. I will the second time, As I would buy thee, view thee limb by limb.
BOT: O, like a book of sport thou'lt read me o'er; But there's more in me than thou understand'st. Why dost thou so oppress me with thine eye?
USER: It would discredit the blest gods, proud man, To answer such a question. Stand again. Think'st thou to catch my life so pleasantly As to prenominate in nice conjecture Where thou wilt hit me dead?
BOT: I tell thee yea.
USER: I pray you let us see you in the field; We have had pelting wars since you refus'd The Grecians' cause.
BOT: Dost thou entreat me, Hector? Tomorrow do I meet thee, fell as death; Tonight all friends.
USER: Thy hand upon that match.
BOT: My Lord Ulysses, tell me, I beseech you, In what place of the field doth Calchas keep?
USER: At Menelaus' tent, most princely Troilus. There Diomed doth feast with him tonight, Who neither looks upon the heaven nor earth, But gives all gaze and bent of amorous view On the fair Cressid.
BOT: Shall I, sweet lord, be bound to you so much, After we part from Agamemnon's tent, To bring me thither?
USER: You shall command me, sir. As gentle tell me of what honour was This Cressida in Troy? Had she no lover there That wails her absence?
BOT: O, sir, to such as boasting show their scars A mock is due. Will you walk on, my lord? She was belov'd, she lov'd; she is, and doth; But still sweet love is food for fortune's tooth.

USER: I'll heat his blood with Greekish wine tonight, Which with my scimitar I'll cool tomorrow. Patroclus, let us feast him to the height.
BOT: Here comes Thersites.
USER: How now, thou core of envy! Thou crusty batch of nature, what's the news?
BOT: Why, thou picture of what thou seemest, and idol of idiot worshippers, here's a letter for thee.
USER: From whence, fragment?
BOT: Why, thou full dish of fool, from Troy.
USER: Who keeps the tent now?
BOT: The surgeon's box or the patient's wound.
USER: Well said, adversity! And what needs these tricks?
BOT: Prithee, be silent, boy; I profit not by thy talk; thou art said to be Achilles' male varlet.
USER: Male varlet, you rogue! What's that?
BOT: Why, thou damnable box of envy, thou, what meanest thou to curse thus?
USER: Do I curse thee?
BOT: Why, no, you ruinous butt; you whoreson indistinguishable cur, no.
USER: Out, gall!
BOT: Finch egg!
USER: We go wrong, we go wrong.
BOT: No, yonder 'tis; There, where we see the lights.
USER: I trouble you.
BOT: No, not a whit.
USER: Here comes himself to guide you.
BOT: Welcome, brave Hector; welcome, Princes all.
USER: So now, fair Prince of Troy, I bid good night; Ajax commands the guard to tend on you.
BOT: Thanks, and good night to the Greeks' general.
USER: Good night, my lord.
BOT: Good night, sweet Lord Menelaus.
USER: Sweet draught! 'Sweet' quoth a'! Sweet sink, sweet sewer!
BOT: Good night and welcome, both at once, to those That go or tarry.
USER: Good night.
BOT: Old Nestor tarries; and you too, Diomed, Keep Hector company an hour or two.
USER: I cannot, lord; I have important business, The tide whereof is now. Good night, great Hector.
BOT: Give me your hand.
USER: Calchas' tent; I'll keep you company.
BOT: Sweet sir, you honour me.
USER: And so, good night.
BOT: Come, come, enter my tent.

USER: What, are you up here, ho! Speak.
BOT: Diomed. Calchas, I think. Where's your daughter?
USER: Stand where the torch may not discover us.
BOT: Cressid comes forth to him.
USER: How now, my charge!
BOT: Now, my sweet guardian! Hark, a word with you.
USER: Yea, so familiar?
BOT: She will sing any man at first sight.
USER: And any man may sing her, if he can take her cliff; she's noted.
BOT: Will you remember?
USER: Remember! Yes.
BOT: Nay, but do, then; And let your mind be coupled with your words.
USER: What should she remember?
BOT: List!
USER: Sweet honey Greek, tempt me no more to folly.
BOT: Roguery!
USER: Nay, then -
BOT: I'll tell you what -
USER: Fo, fo! come, tell a pin; you are a forsworn.
BOT: In faith, I cannot. What would you have me do?
USER: A juggling trick, to be secretly open.
BOT: What did you swear you would bestow on me?
USER: I prithee, do not hold me to mine oath; Bid me do anything but that, sweet Greek.
BOT: Good night.
USER: Hold, patience!
BOT: How now, Trojan!
USER: Diomed!
BOT: No, no, good night; I'll be your fool no more.
USER: Thy better must.
BOT: Hark! a word in your ear.
USER: O plague and madness!
BOT: You are moved, Prince; let us depart, I pray, Lest your displeasure should enlarge itself To wrathful terms. This place is dangerous; The time right deadly; I beseech you, go.
USER: Behold, I pray you.
BOT: Nay, good my lord, go off; You flow to great distraction; come, my lord.
USER: I pray thee stay.
BOT: You have not patience; come.
USER: I pray you, stay; by hell and all hell's torments, I will not speak a word.
BOT: And so, good night.
USER: Nay, but you part in anger.
BOT: Doth that grieve thee? O withered truth!
USER: How now, my lord?
BOT: By Jove, I will be patient.
USER: Guardian! Why, Greek!
BOT: Fo, fo! adieu! you palter.
USER: In faith, I do not. Come hither once again.
BOT: You shake, my lord, at something; will you go? You will break out.
USER: She strokes his cheek.
BOT: Come, come.
USER: Nay, stay; by Jove, I will not speak a word: There is between my will and all offences A guard of patience. Stay a little while.
BOT: How the devil Luxury, with his fat rump and potato finger, tickles these together! Fry, lechery, fry!
USER: But will you, then?
BOT: In faith, I will, la; never trust me else.
USER: Give me some token for the surety of it.
BOT: I'll fetch you one.
USER: You have sworn patience.
BOT: Fear me not, my lord; I will not be myself, nor have cognition Of what I feel. I am all patience.
USER: Now the pledge; now, now, now!
BOT: Here, Diomed, keep this sleeve.
USER: O beauty! where is thy faith?
BOT: My lord!
USER: I will be patient; outwardly I will.
BOT: You look upon that sleeve; behold it well. He lov'd me - O false wench! - Give't me again.
USER: Whose was't?
BOT: It is no matter, now I have't again. I will not meet with you tomorrow night. I prithee, Diomed, visit me no more.
USER: Now she sharpens. Well said, whetstone.
BOT: I shall have it.
USER: What, this?
BOT: Ay, that.
USER: I had your heart before; this follows it.
BOT: I did swear patience.
USER: You shall not have it, Diomed; faith, you shall not; I'll give you something else.
BOT: I will have this. Whose was it?
USER: It is no matter.
BOT: Come, tell me whose it was.
USER: 'Twas one's that lov'd me better than you will. But, now you have it, take it.
BOT: Whose was it?
USER: By all Diana's waiting women yond, And by herself, I will not tell you whose.
BOT: Tomorrow will I wear it on my helm, And grieve his spirit that dares not challenge it.
USER: Wert thou the devil and wor'st it on thy horn, It should be challeng'd.
BOT: Well, well, 'tis done, 'tis past; and yet it is not; I will not keep my word.
USER: Why, then farewell; Thou never shalt mock Diomed again.
BOT: You shall not go. One cannot speak a word But it straight starts you.
USER: I do not like this fooling.
BOT: Nor I, by Pluto; but that that likes not you Pleases me best.
USER: What, shall I come? The hour?
BOT: Ay, come; O Jove! Do come. I shall be plagu'd.
USER: Farewell till then.
BOT: A proof of strength she could not publish more, Unless she said 'My mind is now turn'd whore.'
USER: All's done, my lord.
BOT: It is.
USER: Why stay we, then?
BOT: I cannot conjure, Trojan.
USER: She was not, sure.
BOT: Most sure she was.
USER: Why, my negation hath no taste of madness.
BOT: Nor mine, my lord. Cressid was here but now.
USER: Let it not be believ'd for womanhood. Think, we had mothers; do not give advantage To stubborn critics, apt, without a theme, For depravation, to square the general sex By Cressid's rule. Rather think this not Cressid.
BOT: What hath she done, Prince, that can soil our mothers?
USER: Nothing at all, unless that this were she.
BOT: Will he swagger himself out on's own eyes?
USER: May worthy Troilus be half attach'd With that which here his passion doth express?
BOT: He'll tickle it for his concupy.
USER: O Cressid! O false Cressid! false, false, false! Let all untruths stand by thy stained name, And they'll seem glorious.
BOT: O, contain yourself; Your passion draws ears hither.
USER: I have been seeking you this hour, my lord. Hector, by this, is arming him in Troy; Ajax, your guard, stays to conduct you home.
BOT: Have with you, Prince. My courteous lord, adieu. Fairwell, revolted fair! and, Diomed, Stand fast, and wear a castle on thy head.
USER: I'll bring you to the gates.

USER: When was my lord so much ungently temper'd To stop his ears against admonishment? Unarm, unarm, and do not fight today.
BOT: You train me to offend you; get you in. By all the everlasting gods, I'll go.
USER: My dreams will, sure, prove ominous to the day.
BOT: No more, I say.
USER: Where is my brother Hector?
BOT: O, 'tis true!
USER: Ho! bid my trumpet sound.
BOT: No notes of sally, for the heavens, sweet brother!
USER: Be gone, I say. The gods have heard me swear.
BOT: The gods are deaf to hot and peevish vows; They are polluted off'rings, more abhorr'd Than spotted livers in the sacrifice.
USER: O, be persuaded! Do not count it holy To hurt by being just. It is as lawful, For we would give much, to use violent thefts And rob in the behalf of charity.
BOT: It is the purpose that makes strong the vow; But vows to every purpose must not hold. Unarm, sweet Hector.
USER: Hold you still, I say. Mine honour keeps the weather of my fate. Life every man holds dear; but the dear man Holds honour far more precious dear than life. How now, young man! Mean'st thou to fight today?
BOT: Cassandra, call my father to persuade.
USER: Brother, you have a vice of mercy in you, Which better fits a lion than a man.
BOT: What vice is that? Good Troilus, chide me for it.
USER: When many times the captive Grecian falls, Even in the fan and wind of your fair sword, You bid them rise and live.
BOT: O, 'tis fair play!
USER: Fool's play, by heaven, Hector.
BOT: How now? how now?
USER: For th' love of all the gods, Let's leave the hermit Pity with our mother; And when we have our armours buckled on, The venom'd vengeance ride upon our swords, Spur them to ruthful work, rein them from ruth!
BOT: Fie, savage, fie!
USER: Hector, then 'tis wars.
BOT: Troilus, I would not have you fight today.
USER: Lay hold upon him, Priam, hold him fast; He is thy crutch; now if thou lose thy stay, Thou on him leaning, and all Troy on thee, Fall all together.
BOT: Come, Hector, come, go back. Thy wife hath dreamt; thy mother hath had visions; Cassandra doth foresee; and I myself Am like a prophet suddenly enrapt To tell thee that this day is ominous. Therefore, come back.
USER: Aeneas is a-field; And I do stand engag'd to many Greeks, Even in the faith of valour, to appear This morning to them.
BOT: Ay, but thou shalt not go.
USER: I must not break my faith. You know me dutiful; therefore, dear sir, Let me not shame respect; but give me leave To take that course by your consent and voice Which you do here forbid me, royal Priam.
BOT: O Priam, yield not to him!
USER: Do not, dear father.
BOT: Andromache, I am offended with you. Upon the love you bear me, get you in.
USER: This foolish, dreaming, superstitious girl Makes all these bodements.
BOT: Away, away!
USER: Farewell! yet, soft! Hector, I take my leave. Thou dost thyself and all our Troy deceive.
BOT: You are amaz'd, my liege, at her exclaim. Go in, and cheer the town; we'll forth, and fight, Do deeds worth praise and tell you them at night.
USER: Farewell. The gods with safety stand about thee!
BOT: They are at it, hark! Proud Diomed, believe, I come to lose my arm or win my sleeve.
USER: Do you hear, my lord? Do you hear?
BOT: What now?
USER: Here's a letter come from yond poor girl.
BOT: Let me read.

USER: Fly not; for shouldst thou take the river Styx, I would swim after.
BOT: Thou dost miscall retire. I do not fly; but advantageous care Withdrew me from the odds of multitude. Have at thee!
USER: Hold thy whore, Grecian; now for thy whore, Trojan! now the sleeve, now the sleeve!
BOT: What art thou, Greek? Art thou for Hector's match? Art thou of blood and honour?
USER: No, no I am a rascal; a scurvy railing knave; a very filthy rogue.
BOT: I do believe thee. Live.

USER: Go, go, my servant, take thou Troilus' horse; Present the fair steed to my lady Cressid. Fellow, commend my service to her beauty; Tell her I have chastis'd the amorous Trojan, And am her knight by proof.
BOT: I go, my lord.
USER: Troilus! thou coward Troilus!
BOT: Ay, there, there.
USER: So, so, we draw together.
BOT: Where is this Hector? Come, come, thou boy-queller, show thy face; Know what it is to meet Achilles angry. Hector! where's Hector? I will none but Hector.

USER: Troilus, thou coward Troilus, show thy head.
BOT: Troilus, I say! Where's Troilus?
USER: What wouldst thou?
BOT: I would correct him.
USER: Were I the general, thou shouldst have my office Ere that correction. Troilus, I say! What, Troilus!
BOT: O traitor Diomed! Turn thy false face, thou traitor, And pay thy life thou owest me for my horse.
USER: Ha! art thou there?
BOT: I'll fight with him alone. Stand, Diomed.
USER: He is my prize. I will not look upon.
BOT: Come, both, you cogging Greeks; have at you both!
USER: Yea, Troilus? O, well fought, my youngest brother!
BOT: Now do I see thee. Ha! have at thee, Hector!
USER: Pause, if thou wilt.
BOT: I do disdain thy courtesy, proud Trojan. Be happy that my arms are out of use; My rest and negligence befriend thee now, But thou anon shalt hear of me again; Till when, go seek thy fortune.
USER: Fare thee well. I would have been much more a fresher man, Had I expected thee. How now, my brother!
BOT: Ajax hath ta'en Aeneas. Shall it be? No, by the flame of yonder glorious heaven, He shall not carry him; I'll be ta'en too, Or bring him off. Fate, hear me what I say: I reck not though thou end my life today.

USER: The cuckold and the cuckold-maker are at it. Now, bull! Now, dog! 'Loo, Paris, 'loo! now my double-hen'd Spartan! 'loo, Paris, 'loo! The bull has the game. 'Ware horns, ho!
BOT: Turn, slave, and fight.
USER: What art thou?
BOT: A bastard son of Priam's.
USER: The devil take thee, coward!

USER: Most putrified core so fair without, Thy goodly armour thus hath cost thy life. Now is my day's work done; I'll take my breath: Rest, sword; thou hast thy fill of blood and death!
BOT: Look, Hector, how the sun begins to set, How ugly night comes breathing at his heels; Even with the vail and dark'ning of the sun, To close the day up, Hector's life is done.
USER: I am unarm'd; forego this vantage, Greek.
BOT: The Trojan trumpets sound the like, my lord.

USER: Hark! hark! what shout is this?
BOT: Peace, drums!
USER: The bruit is, Hector's slain, and by Achilles.
BOT: If it be so, yet bragless let it be; Great Hector was as good a man as he.
USER: March patiently along. Let one be sent To pray Achilles see us at our tent. If in his death the gods have us befriended; Great Troy is ours, and our sharp wars are ended.

USER: Stand, ho! yet are we masters of the field. Never go home; here starve we out the night.
BOT: Hector is slain.
USER: Hector! The gods forbid!
BOT: My lord, you do discomfort all the host.
USER: But hear you, hear you!
BOT: Hence, broker-lackey. Ignominy and shame Pursue thy life, and live aye with thy name!

USER: Will you go hunt, my lord?
BOT: What, Curio?
USER: The hart.

USER: What country, friends, is this?
BOT: This is Illyria, lady.
USER: And what should I do in Illyria? My brother he is in Elysium. Perchance he is not drown'd. What think you, sailors?
BOT: It is perchance that you yourself were sav'd.
USER: O my poor brother! and so perchance may he be.
BOT: For saying so, there's gold! Mine own escape unfoldeth to my hope, Whereto thy speech serves for authority, The like of him. Know'st thou this country?
USER: Ay, madam, well, for I was bred and born Not three hours' travel from this very place.
BOT: Who governs here?
USER: A noble duke, in nature as in name.
BOT: What is his name?
USER: Orsino.
BOT: Orsino! I have heard my father name him. He was a bachelor then.
USER: And so is now, or was so very late; For but a month ago I went from hence, And then 'twas fresh in murmur, (as, you know, What great ones do, the less will prattle of) That he did seek the love of fair Olivia.
BOT: What's she?
USER: O that I served that lady, And might not be delivered to the world, Till I had made mine own occasion mellow, What my estate is.
BOT: That were hard to compass, Because she will admit no kind of suit, No, not the Duke's.
USER: Be you his eunuch and your mute I'll be; When my tongue blabs, then let mine eyes not see.
BOT: I thank thee. Lead me on.

USER: What a plague means my niece to take the death of her brother thus? I am sure care's an enemy to life.
BOT: By my troth, Sir Toby, you must come in earlier o' nights; your cousin, my lady, takes great exceptions to your ill hours.
USER: Why, let her except, before excepted.
BOT: Ay, but you must confine yourself within the modest limits of order.
USER: Confine? I'll confine myself no finer than I am. These clothes are good enough to drink in, and so be these boots too; and they be not, let them hang themselves in their own straps.
BOT: That quaffing and drinking will undo you: I heard my lady talk of it yesterday; and of a foolish knight that you brought in one night here to be her wooer.
USER: Who? Sir Andrew Aguecheek?
BOT: Ay, he.
USER: He's as tall a man as any's in Illyria.
BOT: What's that to th' purpose?
USER: Why, he has three thousand ducats a year.
BOT: Ay, but he'll have but a year in all these ducats. He's a very fool, and a prodigal.
USER: Fie, that you'll say so! he plays o' the viol-de-gamboys, and speaks three or four languages word for word without book, and hath all the good gifts of nature.
BOT: By this hand, they are scoundrels and substractors that say so of him. Who are they?
USER: They that add, moreover, he's drunk nightly in your company.
BOT: Sir Toby Belch! How now, Sir Toby Belch?
USER: Sweet Sir Andrew!
BOT: Bless you, fair shrew.
USER: And you too, sir.
BOT: Accost, Sir Andrew, accost.
USER: What's that?
BOT: My niece's chamber-maid.
USER: Good Mistress Accost, I desire better acquaintance.
BOT: My name is Mary, sir.
USER: Good Mistress Mary Accost, -
BOT: You mistake, knight: accost is front her, board her, woo her, assail her.
USER: By my troth, I would not undertake her in this company. Is that the meaning of accost?
BOT: Fare you well, gentlemen.
USER: And thou let part so, Sir Andrew, would thou mightst never draw sword again.
BOT: And you part so, mistress, I would I might never draw sword again. Fair lady, do you think you have fools in hand?
USER: Sir, I have not you by the hand.
BOT: Marry, but you shall have, and here's my hand.
USER: Now, sir, thought is free. I pray you, bring your hand to th' buttery bar and let it drink.
BOT: Wherefore, sweetheart? What's your metaphor?
USER: It's dry, sir.
BOT: Why, I think so; I am not such an ass but I can keep my hand dry. But what's your jest?
USER: A dry jest, sir.
BOT: Are you full of them?
USER: Ay, sir, I have them at my fingers' ends: marry, now I let go your hand, I am barren.
BOT: O knight, thou lack'st a cup of canary: When did I see thee so put down?
USER: Never in your life, I think, unless you see canary put me down. Methinks sometimes I have no more wit than a Christian or an ordinary man has; but I am a great eater of beef, and I believe that does harm to my wit.
BOT: No question.
USER: And I thought that, I'd forswear it. I'll ride home tomorrow, Sir Toby.
BOT: _Pourquoy_, my dear knight?
USER: What is _pourquoy?_ Do, or not do? I would I had bestowed that time in the tongues that I have in fencing, dancing, and bear-baiting. O, had I but followed the arts!
BOT: Then hadst thou had an excellent head of hair.
USER: Why, would that have mended my hair?
BOT: Past question; for thou seest it will not curl by nature.
USER: But it becomes me well enough, does't not?
BOT: Excellent, it hangs like flax on a distaff; and I hope to see a huswife take thee between her legs, and spin it off.
USER: Faith, I'll home tomorrow, Sir Toby; your niece will not be seen, or if she be, it's four to one she'll none of me; the Count himself here hard by woos her.
BOT: She'll none o' the Count; she'll not match above her degree, neither in estate, years, nor wit; I have heard her swear't. Tut, there's life in't, man.
USER: I'll stay a month longer. I am a fellow o' the strangest mind i' the world; I delight in masques and revels sometimes altogether.
BOT: Art thou good at these kick-shawses, knight?
USER: As any man in Illyria, whatsoever he be, under the degree of my betters; and yet I will not compare with an old man.
BOT: What is thy excellence in a galliard, knight?
USER: Faith, I can cut a caper.
BOT: And I can cut the mutton to't.
USER: And I think I have the back-trick simply as strong as any man in Illyria.
BOT: Ay, 'tis strong, and it does indifferent well in a dam'd-colour'd stock. Shall we set about some revels?
USER: What shall we do else? Were we not born under Taurus?
BOT: Taurus? That's sides and heart.
USER: No, sir, it is legs and thighs. Let me see thee caper. Ha, higher: ha, ha, excellent!

USER: If the duke continue these favours towards you, Cesario, you are like to be much advanced; he hath known you but three days, and already you are no stranger.
BOT: You either fear his humour or my negligence, that you call in question the continuance of his love. Is he inconstant, sir, in his favours?
USER: No, believe me.
BOT: I thank you. Here comes the Count.
USER: Who saw Cesario, ho?
BOT: On your attendance, my lord, here.
USER: Sure, my noble lord, If she be so abandon'd to her sorrow As it is spoke, she never will admit me.
BOT: Be clamorous and leap all civil bounds, Rather than make unprofited return.
USER: Say I do speak with her, my lord, what then?
BOT: O then unfold the passion of my love, Surprise her with discourse of my dear faith; It shall become thee well to act my woes; She will attend it better in thy youth, Than in a nuncio's of more grave aspect.
USER: I think not so, my lord.
BOT: I'll do my best To woo your lady. [_Aside._] Yet, a barful strife! Whoe'er I woo, myself would be his wife.

USER: Nay; either tell me where thou hast been, or I will not open my lips so wide as a bristle may enter, in way of thy excuse: my lady will hang thee for thy absence.
BOT: Let her hang me: he that is well hanged in this world needs to fear no colours.
USER: Make that good.
BOT: He shall see none to fear.
USER: A good lenten answer. I can tell thee where that saying was born, of I fear no colours.
BOT: Where, good Mistress Mary?
USER: In the wars, and that may you be bold to say in your foolery.
BOT: Well, God give them wisdom that have it; and those that are fools, let them use their talents.
USER: Yet you will be hanged for being so long absent; or to be turned away; is not that as good as a hanging to you?
BOT: Many a good hanging prevents a bad marriage; and for turning away, let summer bear it out.
USER: You are resolute then?
BOT: Not so, neither, but I am resolved on two points.
USER: That if one break, the other will hold; or if both break, your gaskins fall.
BOT: Apt, in good faith, very apt! Well, go thy way; if Sir Toby would leave drinking, thou wert as witty a piece of Eve's flesh as any in Illyria.
USER: Peace, you rogue, no more o' that. Here comes my lady: make your excuse wisely, you were best.
BOT: Take the fool away.
USER: Do you not hear, fellows? Take away the lady.
BOT: Go to, y'are a dry fool; I'll no more of you. Besides, you grow dishonest.
USER: Sir, I bade them take away you.
BOT: Misprision in the highest degree! Lady, _cucullus non facit monachum:_ that's as much to say, I wear not motley in my brain. Good madonna, give me leave to prove you a fool.
USER: Can you do it?
BOT: Dexteriously, good madonna.
USER: Make your proof.
BOT: I must catechize you for it, madonna. Good my mouse of virtue, answer me.
USER: Well sir, for want of other idleness, I'll 'bide your proof.
BOT: Good madonna, why mourn'st thou?
USER: Good fool, for my brother's death.
BOT: I think his soul is in hell, madonna.
USER: I know his soul is in heaven, fool.
BOT: The more fool you, madonna, to mourn for your brother's soul being in heaven. Take away the fool, gentlemen.
USER: What think you of this fool, Malvolio? doth he not mend?
