BOT: Yes; and shall do, till the pangs of death shake him. Infirmity, that decays the wise, doth ever make the better fool.
USER: God send you, sir, a speedy infirmity, for the better increasing your folly! Sir Toby will be sworn that I am no fox; but he will not pass his word for twopence that you are no fool.
BOT: How say you to that, Malvolio?
USER: Now Mercury endue thee with leasing, for thou speak'st well of fools!
BOT: Madam, there is at the gate a young gentleman much desires to speak with you.
USER: From the Count Orsino, is it?
BOT: I know not, madam; 'tis a fair young man, and well attended.
USER: Who of my people hold him in delay?
BOT: Sir Toby, madam, your kinsman.
USER: Thou hast spoke for us, madonna, as if thy eldest son should be a fool: whose skull Jove cram with brains, for here he comes, one of thy kin has a most weak _pia mater_.
BOT: By mine honour, half drunk. What is he at the gate, cousin?
USER: A gentleman.
BOT: A gentleman? What gentleman?
USER: 'Tis a gentleman here. A plague o' these pickle-herrings! How now, sot?
BOT: Good Sir Toby.
USER: Cousin, cousin, how have you come so early by this lethargy?
BOT: Lechery! I defy lechery. There's one at the gate.
USER: Ay, marry, what is he?
BOT: Let him be the devil an he will, I care not: give me faith, say I. Well, it's all one.
USER: What's a drunken man like, fool?
BOT: Like a drowned man, a fool, and a madman: one draught above heat makes him a fool, the second mads him, and a third drowns him.
USER: Go thou and seek the coroner, and let him sit o' my coz; for he's in the third degree of drink; he's drowned. Go, look after him.
BOT: He is but mad yet, madonna; and the fool shall look to the madman.
USER: Tell him, he shall not speak with me.
BOT: Has been told so; and he says he'll stand at your door like a sheriff's post, and be the supporter of a bench, but he'll speak with you.
USER: What kind o' man is he?
BOT: Why, of mankind.
USER: What manner of man?
BOT: Of very ill manner; he'll speak with you, will you or no.
USER: Of what personage and years is he?
BOT: Let him approach. Call in my gentlewoman.
USER: Gentlewoman, my lady calls.
BOT: Give me my veil; come, throw it o'er my face. We'll once more hear Orsino's embassy.
USER: The honourable lady of the house, which is she?
BOT: Speak to me; I shall answer for her. Your will?
USER: Whence came you, sir?
BOT: I can say little more than I have studied, and that question's out of my part. Good gentle one, give me modest assurance, if you be the lady of the house, that I may proceed in my speech.
USER: Are you a comedian?
BOT: No, my profound heart: and yet, by the very fangs of malice I swear, I am not that I play. Are you the lady of the house?
USER: If I do not usurp myself, I am.
BOT: Come to what is important in't: I forgive you the praise.
USER: Alas, I took great pains to study it, and 'tis poetical.
BOT: Will you hoist sail, sir? Here lies your way.
USER: No, good swabber, I am to hull here a little longer. Some mollification for your giant, sweet lady. Tell me your mind. I am a messenger.
BOT: Sure, you have some hideous matter to deliver, when the courtesy of it is so fearful. Speak your office.
USER: It alone concerns your ear. I bring no overture of war, no taxation of homage; I hold the olive in my hand: my words are as full of peace as matter.
BOT: Yet you began rudely. What are you? What would you?
USER: The rudeness that hath appeared in me have I learned from my entertainment. What I am and what I would are as secret as maidenhead: to your ears, divinity; to any other's, profanation.
BOT: Give us the place alone: we will hear this divinity. Now, sir, what is your text?
USER: Most sweet lady -
BOT: A comfortable doctrine, and much may be said of it. Where lies your text?
USER: In Orsino's bosom.
BOT: In his bosom? In what chapter of his bosom?
USER: To answer by the method, in the first of his heart.
BOT: O, I have read it; it is heresy. Have you no more to say?
USER: Good madam, let me see your face.
BOT: Excellently done, if God did all.
USER: 'Tis in grain, sir; 'twill endure wind and weather.
BOT: 'Tis beauty truly blent, whose red and white Nature's own sweet and cunning hand laid on. Lady, you are the cruel'st she alive If you will lead these graces to the grave, And leave the world no copy.
USER: I see you what you are, you are too proud; But, if you were the devil, you are fair. My lord and master loves you. O, such love Could be but recompens'd though you were crown'd The nonpareil of beauty!
BOT: How does he love me?
USER: With adorations, fertile tears, With groans that thunder love, with sighs of fire.
BOT: If I did love you in my master's flame, With such a suff'ring, such a deadly life, In your denial I would find no sense, I would not understand it.
USER: Why, what would you?
BOT: You might do much. What is your parentage?
USER: Above my fortunes, yet my state is well: I am a gentleman.
BOT: Get you to your lord; I cannot love him: let him send no more, Unless, perchance, you come to me again, To tell me how he takes it. Fare you well: I thank you for your pains: spend this for me.
USER: I am no fee'd post, lady; keep your purse; My master, not myself, lacks recompense. Love make his heart of flint that you shall love, And let your fervour like my master's be Plac'd in contempt. Farewell, fair cruelty.
BOT: Here, madam, at your service.
USER: Madam, I will.
BOT: I do I know not what, and fear to find Mine eye too great a flatterer for my mind. Fate, show thy force, ourselves we do not owe. What is decreed must be; and be this so!

USER: Will you stay no longer? Nor will you not that I go with you?
BOT: Let me know of you whither you are bound.
USER: Alas the day!
BOT: Pardon me, sir, your bad entertainment.
USER: O good Antonio, forgive me your trouble.
BOT: If you will not murder me for my love, let me be your servant.
USER: The gentleness of all the gods go with thee! I have many enemies in Orsino's court, Else would I very shortly see thee there: But come what may, I do adore thee so, That danger shall seem sport, and I will go.

USER: Were you not even now with the Countess Olivia?
BOT: Even now, sir; on a moderate pace I have since arrived but hither.
USER: She took the ring of me: I'll none of it.
BOT: Come sir, you peevishly threw it to her; and her will is it should be so returned. If it be worth stooping for, there it lies in your eye; if not, be it his that finds it.

USER: Approach, Sir Andrew; not to be abed after midnight, is to be up betimes; and _diluculo surgere_, thou know'st.
BOT: Nay, by my troth, I know not; but I know to be up late is to be up late.
USER: A false conclusion; I hate it as an unfilled can. To be up after midnight, and to go to bed then is early: so that to go to bed after midnight is to go to bed betimes. Does not our lives consist of the four elements?
BOT: Faith, so they say, but I think it rather consists of eating and drinking.
USER: Th'art a scholar; let us therefore eat and drink. Marian, I say! a stoup of wine.
BOT: Here comes the fool, i' faith.
USER: How now, my hearts? Did you never see the picture of "we three"?
BOT: Welcome, ass. Now let's have a catch.
USER: I did impeticos thy gratillity; for Malvolio's nose is no whipstock. My lady has a white hand, and the Myrmidons are no bottle-ale houses.
BOT: Excellent! Why, this is the best fooling, when all is done. Now, a song.
USER: Come on, there is sixpence for you. Let's have a song.
BOT: There's a testril of me too: if one knight give a -
USER: Would you have a love-song, or a song of good life?
BOT: A love-song, a love-song.
USER: Excellent good, i' faith.
BOT: Good, good.
USER: _What is love? 'Tis not hereafter, Present mirth hath present laughter. What's to come is still unsure. In delay there lies no plenty, Then come kiss me, sweet and twenty. Youth's a stuff will not endure._
BOT: A mellifluous voice, as I am true knight.
USER: A contagious breath.
BOT: Very sweet and contagious, i' faith.
USER: To hear by the nose, it is dulcet in contagion. But shall we make the welkin dance indeed? Shall we rouse the night-owl in a catch that will draw three souls out of one weaver? Shall we do that?
BOT: And you love me, let's do't: I am dog at a catch.
USER: By'r lady, sir, and some dogs will catch well.
BOT: Most certain. Let our catch be, "Thou knave."
USER: "Hold thy peace, thou knave" knight? I shall be constrain'd in't to call thee knave, knight.
BOT: 'Tis not the first time I have constrained one to call me knave. Begin, fool; it begins "Hold thy peace."
USER: I shall never begin if I hold my peace.
BOT: Good, i' faith! Come, begin.
USER: What a caterwauling do you keep here! If my lady have not called up her steward Malvolio, and bid him turn you out of doors, never trust me.
BOT: My lady's a Cataian, we are politicians, Malvolio's a Peg-a-Ramsey, and of her blood? Tilly-vally! "Lady"! _There dwelt a man in Babylon, Lady, Lady._
USER: Beshrew me, the knight's in admirable fooling.
BOT: Ay, he does well enough if he be disposed, and so do I too; he does it with a better grace, but I do it more natural.
USER: For the love o' God, peace!
BOT: We did keep time, sir, in our catches. Sneck up!
USER: Nay, good Sir Toby.
BOT: Is't even so?
USER: This is much credit to you.
BOT: Out o' tune? sir, ye lie. Art any more than a steward? Dost thou think, because thou art virtuous, there shall be no more cakes and ale?
USER: Yes, by Saint Anne, and ginger shall be hot i' the mouth too.
BOT: Th'art i' the right. Go, sir, rub your chain with crumbs. A stoup of wine, Maria!
USER: Mistress Mary, if you prized my lady's favour at anything more than contempt, you would not give means for this uncivil rule; she shall know of it, by this hand.
BOT: Go shake your ears.
USER: 'Twere as good a deed as to drink when a man's a-hungry, to challenge him the field, and then to break promise with him and make a fool of him.
BOT: Do't, knight. I'll write thee a challenge; or I'll deliver thy indignation to him by word of mouth.
USER: Possess us, possess us, tell us something of him.
BOT: Marry, sir, sometimes he is a kind of Puritan.
USER: O, if I thought that, I'd beat him like a dog.
BOT: What, for being a Puritan? Thy exquisite reason, dear knight?
USER: I have no exquisite reason for't, but I have reason good enough.
BOT: What wilt thou do?
USER: Excellent! I smell a device.
BOT: I have't in my nose too.
USER: He shall think, by the letters that thou wilt drop, that they come from my niece, and that she is in love with him.
BOT: My purpose is indeed a horse of that colour.
USER: And your horse now would make him an ass.
BOT: Ass, I doubt not.
USER: O 'twill be admirable!
BOT: Good night, Penthesilea.
USER: Before me, she's a good wench.
BOT: She's a beagle true bred, and one that adores me. What o' that?
USER: I was adored once too.
BOT: Let's to bed, knight. Thou hadst need send for more money.
USER: If I cannot recover your niece, I am a foul way out.
BOT: Send for money, knight; if thou hast her not i' th' end, call me cut.
USER: If I do not, never trust me, take it how you will.
BOT: Come, come, I'll go burn some sack, 'tis too late to go to bed now. Come, knight, come, knight.

USER: He is not here, so please your lordship, that should sing it.
BOT: Who was it?
USER: Feste, the jester, my lord, a fool that the Lady Olivia's father took much delight in. He is about the house.
BOT: It gives a very echo to the seat Where love is throned.
USER: Thou dost speak masterly. My life upon't, young though thou art, thine eye Hath stayed upon some favour that it loves. Hath it not, boy?
BOT: A little, by your favour.
USER: What kind of woman is't?
BOT: Of your complexion.
USER: She is not worth thee, then. What years, i' faith?
BOT: About your years, my lord.
USER: I think it well, my lord.
BOT: Then let thy love be younger than thyself, Or thy affection cannot hold the bent: For women are as roses, whose fair flower Being once display'd, doth fall that very hour.
USER: And so they are: alas, that they are so; To die, even when they to perfection grow!
BOT: Are you ready, sir?
USER: There's for thy pains.
BOT: No pains, sir; I take pleasure in singing, sir.
USER: I'll pay thy pleasure, then.
BOT: Truly sir, and pleasure will be paid one time or another.
USER: Give me now leave to leave thee.
BOT: But if she cannot love you, sir?
USER: I cannot be so answer'd.
BOT: Sooth, but you must. Say that some lady, as perhaps there is, Hath for your love as great a pang of heart As you have for Olivia: you cannot love her; You tell her so. Must she not then be answer'd?
USER: Ay, but I know -
BOT: What dost thou know?
USER: Too well what love women to men may owe. In faith, they are as true of heart as we. My father had a daughter loved a man, As it might be perhaps, were I a woman, I should your lordship.
BOT: And what's her history?
USER: But died thy sister of her love, my boy?
BOT: I am all the daughters of my father's house, And all the brothers too: and yet I know not. Sir, shall I to this lady?
USER: Ay, that's the theme. To her in haste. Give her this jewel; say My love can give no place, bide no denay.

USER: Come thy ways, Signior Fabian.
BOT: Nay, I'll come. If I lose a scruple of this sport, let me be boiled to death with melancholy.
USER: Wouldst thou not be glad to have the niggardly rascally sheep-biter come by some notable shame?
BOT: I would exult, man. You know he brought me out o' favour with my lady about a bear-baiting here.
USER: To anger him we'll have the bear again, and we will fool him black and blue, shall we not, Sir Andrew?
BOT: And we do not, it is pity of our lives.
USER: Here comes the little villain. How now, my metal of India?
BOT: Here's an overweening rogue!
USER: O, peace! Contemplation makes a rare turkey-cock of him; how he jets under his advanced plumes!
BOT: 'Slight, I could so beat the rogue!
USER: Peace, I say.
BOT: To be Count Malvolio.
USER: Ah, rogue!
BOT: Pistol him, pistol him.
USER: Peace, peace.
BOT: There is example for't. The lady of the Strachy married the yeoman of the wardrobe.
USER: Fie on him, Jezebel!
BOT: O, peace! now he's deeply in; look how imagination blows him.
USER: Having been three months married to her, sitting in my state -
BOT: O for a stone-bow to hit him in the eye!
USER: Calling my officers about me, in my branched velvet gown; having come from a day-bed, where I have left Olivia sleeping.
BOT: Fire and brimstone!
USER: O, peace, peace.
BOT: And then to have the humour of state; and after a demure travel of regard, telling them I know my place as I would they should do theirs, to ask for my kinsman Toby.
USER: Bolts and shackles!
BOT: O, peace, peace, peace! Now, now.
USER: Seven of my people, with an obedient start, make out for him. I frown the while, and perchance wind up my watch, or play with some rich jewel. Toby approaches; curtsies there to me -
BOT: Shall this fellow live?
USER: Though our silence be drawn from us with cars, yet peace!
BOT: I extend my hand to him thus, quenching my familiar smile with an austere regard of control -
USER: And does not Toby take you a blow o' the lips then?
BOT: Saying 'Cousin Toby, my fortunes having cast me on your niece, give me this prerogative of speech - '
USER: What, what?
BOT: 'You must amend your drunkenness.'
USER: Out, scab!
BOT: Nay, patience, or we break the sinews of our plot.
USER: 'Besides, you waste the treasure of your time with a foolish knight - '
BOT: That's me, I warrant you.
USER: 'One Sir Andrew.'
BOT: I knew 'twas I, for many do call me fool.
USER: Now is the woodcock near the gin.
BOT: O, peace! And the spirit of humours intimate reading aloud to him!
USER: By my life, this is my lady's hand: these be her very C's, her U's, and her T's, and thus makes she her great P's. It is in contempt of question, her hand.
BOT: Her C's, her U's, and her T's. Why that?
USER: phrases! By your leave, wax. Soft! and the impressure her Lucrece, with which she uses to seal: 'tis my lady. To whom should this be?
BOT: This wins him, liver and all.
USER: _ Jove knows I love, But who? Lips, do not move, No man must know._ 'No man must know.' What follows? The numbers alter'd! 'No man must know.' - If this should be thee, Malvolio?
BOT: Marry, hang thee, brock!
USER: _ I may command where I adore, But silence, like a Lucrece knife, With bloodless stroke my heart doth gore; M.O.A.I. doth sway my life._
BOT: A fustian riddle!
USER: Excellent wench, say I.
BOT: 'M.O.A.I. doth sway my life.' - Nay, but first let me see, let me see, let me see.
USER: What dish o' poison has she dressed him!
BOT: And with what wing the staniel checks at it!
USER: O, ay, make up that: - he is now at a cold scent.
BOT: Sowter will cry upon't for all this, though it be as rank as a fox.
USER: 'M' - Malvolio; 'M!' Why, that begins my name!
BOT: Did not I say he would work it out? The cur is excellent at faults.
USER: 'M' - But then there is no consonancy in the sequel; that suffers under probation: 'A' should follow, but 'O' does.
BOT: And 'O' shall end, I hope.
USER: Ay, or I'll cudgel him, and make him cry 'O!'
BOT: And then 'I' comes behind.
USER: Ay, and you had any eye behind you, you might see more detraction at your heels than fortunes before you.
BOT: I will not give my part of this sport for a pension of thousands to be paid from the Sophy.
USER: I could marry this wench for this device.
BOT: So could I too.
USER: And ask no other dowry with her but such another jest.
BOT: Nor I neither.
USER: Here comes my noble gull-catcher.
BOT: Wilt thou set thy foot o' my neck?
USER: Or o' mine either?
BOT: Shall I play my freedom at tray-trip, and become thy bond-slave?
USER: I' faith, or I either?
BOT: Why, thou hast put him in such a dream, that when the image of it leaves him he must run mad.
USER: Nay, but say true, does it work upon him?
BOT: Like aqua-vitae with a midwife.
USER: To the gates of Tartar, thou most excellent devil of wit!
BOT: I'll make one too.

USER: Save thee, friend, and thy music. Dost thou live by thy tabor?
BOT: No, sir, I live by the church.
USER: Art thou a churchman?
BOT: No such matter, sir. I do live by the church, for I do live at my house, and my house doth stand by the church.
USER: So thou mayst say the king lies by a beggar, if a beggar dwell near him; or the church stands by thy tabor, if thy tabor stand by the church.
BOT: You have said, sir. To see this age! A sentence is but a chev'ril glove to a good wit. How quickly the wrong side may be turned outward!
USER: Nay, that's certain; they that dally nicely with words may quickly make them wanton.
BOT: I would, therefore, my sister had had no name, sir.
USER: Why, man?
BOT: Why, sir, her name's a word; and to dally with that word might make my sister wanton. But indeed, words are very rascals, since bonds disgraced them.
USER: Thy reason, man?
BOT: Troth, sir, I can yield you none without words, and words are grown so false, I am loath to prove reason with them.
USER: I warrant thou art a merry fellow, and car'st for nothing.
BOT: Not so, sir, I do care for something. But in my conscience, sir, I do not care for you. If that be to care for nothing, sir, I would it would make you invisible.
USER: Art not thou the Lady Olivia's fool?
BOT: I saw thee late at the Count Orsino's.
USER: Foolery, sir, does walk about the orb like the sun; it shines everywhere. I would be sorry, sir, but the fool should be as oft with your master as with my mistress. I think I saw your wisdom there.
BOT: Nay, and thou pass upon me, I'll no more with thee. Hold, there's expenses for thee.
USER: Now Jove, in his next commodity of hair, send thee a beard!
BOT: By my troth, I'll tell thee, I am almost sick for one, though I would not have it grow on my chin. Is thy lady within?
USER: Would not a pair of these have bred, sir?
BOT: Yes, being kept together, and put to use.
USER: I would play Lord Pandarus of Phrygia, sir, to bring a Cressida to this Troilus.
BOT: I understand you, sir; 'tis well begged.
USER: Save you, gentleman.
BOT: And you, sir.
USER: _Dieu vous garde, monsieur._
BOT: _Et vous aussi; votre serviteur._
USER: I hope, sir, you are, and I am yours.
BOT: Will you encounter the house? My niece is desirous you should enter, if your trade be to her.
USER: I am bound to your niece, sir, I mean, she is the list of my voyage.
BOT: Taste your legs, sir, put them to motion.
USER: My legs do better understand me, sir, than I understand what you mean by bidding me taste my legs.
BOT: I mean, to go, sir, to enter.
USER: I will answer you with gait and entrance: but we are prevented. Most excellent accomplished lady, the heavens rain odours on you!
BOT: That youth's a rare courtier. 'Rain odours,' well.
USER: My matter hath no voice, lady, but to your own most pregnant and vouchsafed ear.
BOT: 'Odours,' 'pregnant,' and 'vouchsafed.' - I'll get 'em all three ready.
USER: Let the garden door be shut, and leave me to my hearing. Give me your hand, sir.
BOT: My duty, madam, and most humble service.
USER: What is your name?
BOT: Cesario is your servant's name, fair princess.
USER: My servant, sir! 'Twas never merry world, Since lowly feigning was call'd compliment: Y'are servant to the Count Orsino, youth.
BOT: And he is yours, and his must needs be yours. Your servant's servant is your servant, madam.
USER: For him, I think not on him: for his thoughts, Would they were blanks rather than fill'd with me!
BOT: Madam, I come to whet your gentle thoughts On his behalf.
USER: O, by your leave, I pray you. I bade you never speak again of him. But would you undertake another suit, I had rather hear you to solicit that Than music from the spheres.
BOT: Dear lady -
USER: I pity you.
BOT: That's a degree to love.
USER: No, not a grize; for 'tis a vulgar proof That very oft we pity enemies.
BOT: Then westward ho! Grace and good disposition attend your ladyship! You'll nothing, madam, to my lord by me?
USER: Stay: I prithee tell me what thou think'st of me.
BOT: That you do think you are not what you are.
USER: If I think so, I think the same of you.
BOT: Then think you right; I am not what I am.
USER: I would you were as I would have you be.
BOT: Would it be better, madam, than I am? I wish it might, for now I am your fool.
USER: Yet come again: for thou perhaps mayst move That heart, which now abhors, to like his love.

USER: No, faith, I'll not stay a jot longer.
BOT: Thy reason, dear venom, give thy reason.
USER: You must needs yield your reason, Sir Andrew.
BOT: Marry, I saw your niece do more favours to the Count's servingman than ever she bestowed upon me; I saw't i' th' orchard.
USER: Did she see thee the while, old boy? Tell me that.
BOT: As plain as I see you now.
USER: This was a great argument of love in her toward you.
BOT: 'Slight! will you make an ass o' me?
USER: I will prove it legitimate, sir, upon the oaths of judgment and reason.
BOT: And they have been grand-jurymen since before Noah was a sailor.
USER: And't be any way, it must be with valour, for policy I hate; I had as lief be a Brownist as a politician.
BOT: There is no way but this, Sir Andrew.
USER: Will either of you bear me a challenge to him?
BOT: Where shall I find you?
USER: We'll call thee at the cubiculo. Go.
BOT: This is a dear manikin to you, Sir Toby.
USER: I have been dear to him, lad, some two thousand strong, or so.
BOT: We shall have a rare letter from him; but you'll not deliver it.
USER: And his opposite, the youth, bears in his visage no great presage of cruelty.
BOT: Look where the youngest wren of nine comes.
USER: And cross-gartered?
BOT: Come, bring us, bring us where he is.

USER: I would not by my will have troubled you, But since you make your pleasure of your pains, I will no further chide you.
BOT: Tomorrow, sir; best first go see your lodging.
USER: I am not weary, and 'tis long to night; I pray you, let us satisfy our eyes With the memorials and the things of fame That do renown this city.
BOT: Would you'd pardon me. I do not without danger walk these streets. Once in a sea-fight, 'gainst the Count his galleys, I did some service, of such note indeed, That were I ta'en here, it would scarce be answer'd.
USER: Belike you slew great number of his people.
BOT: Do not then walk too open.
USER: Why I your purse?
BOT: Haply your eye shall light upon some toy You have desire to purchase; and your store, I think, is not for idle markets, sir.
USER: I'll be your purse-bearer, and leave you for an hour.
BOT: To th' Elephant.
USER: I do remember.

USER: He's coming, madam: But in very strange manner. He is sure possessed, madam.
BOT: Why, what's the matter? Does he rave?
USER: No, madam, he does nothing but smile: your ladyship were best to have some guard about you if he come, for sure the man is tainted in 's wits.
BOT: Go call him hither. I'm as mad as he, If sad and merry madness equal be. How now, Malvolio?
USER: Sweet lady, ho, ho!
BOT: Smil'st thou? I sent for thee upon a sad occasion.
USER: Sad, lady? I could be sad: this does make some obstruction in the blood, this cross-gartering. But what of that? If it please the eye of one, it is with me as the very true sonnet is: 'Please one and please all.'
BOT: Why, how dost thou, man? What is the matter with thee?
USER: Not black in my mind, though yellow in my legs. It did come to his hands, and commands shall be executed. I think we do know the sweet Roman hand.
BOT: Wilt thou go to bed, Malvolio?
USER: To bed? Ay, sweetheart, and I'll come to thee.
BOT: God comfort thee! Why dost thou smile so, and kiss thy hand so oft?
USER: How do you, Malvolio?
BOT: At your request? Yes, nightingales answer daws!
USER: Why appear you with this ridiculous boldness before my lady?
BOT: 'Be not afraid of greatness.' 'Twas well writ.
USER: What mean'st thou by that, Malvolio?
BOT: 'Some are born great' -
USER: Ha?
BOT: 'Some achieve greatness' -
USER: What say'st thou?
BOT: 'And some have greatness thrust upon them.'
USER: Heaven restore thee!
BOT: 'Remember who commended thy yellow stockings' -
USER: Thy yellow stockings?
BOT: 'And wished to see thee cross-gartered.'
USER: Cross-gartered?
BOT: 'Go to: thou art made, if thou desir'st to be so:' -
USER: Am I made?
BOT: 'If not, let me see thee a servant still.'
USER: Why, this is very midsummer madness.
BOT: Madam, the young gentleman of the Count Orsino's is returned; I could hardly entreat him back. He attends your ladyship's pleasure.
USER: I'll come to him. Good Maria, let this fellow be looked to. Where's my cousin Toby? Let some of my people have a special care of him; I would not have him miscarry for the half of my dowry.
BOT: Which way is he, in the name of sanctity? If all the devils of hell be drawn in little, and Legion himself possessed him, yet I'll speak to him.
USER: Here he is, here he is. How is't with you, sir? How is't with you, man?
BOT: Go off, I discard you. Let me enjoy my private. Go off.
USER: Lo, how hollow the fiend speaks within him! Did not I tell you? Sir Toby, my lady prays you to have a care of him.
BOT: Ah, ha! does she so?
USER: Go to, go to; peace, peace, we must deal gently with him. Let me alone. How do you, Malvolio? How is't with you? What, man! defy the devil! Consider, he's an enemy to mankind.
BOT: Do you know what you say?
USER: La you, an you speak ill of the devil, how he takes it at heart! Pray God he be not bewitched.
BOT: Carry his water to th' wise woman.
USER: Marry, and it shall be done tomorrow morning, if I live. My lady would not lose him for more than I'll say.
BOT: How now, mistress!
USER: O Lord!
BOT: Prithee hold thy peace, this is not the way. Do you not see you move him? Let me alone with him.
USER: No way but gentleness, gently, gently. The fiend is rough, and will not be roughly used.
BOT: Why, how now, my bawcock? How dost thou, chuck?
USER: Sir!
BOT: Ay, biddy, come with me. What, man, 'tis not for gravity to play at cherry-pit with Satan. Hang him, foul collier!
USER: Get him to say his prayers, good Sir Toby, get him to pray.
BOT: My prayers, minx?
USER: No, I warrant you, he will not hear of godliness.
BOT: Go, hang yourselves all! You are idle, shallow things. I am not of your element. You shall know more hereafter.
USER: Is't possible?
BOT: If this were played upon a stage now, I could condemn it as an improbable fiction.
USER: His very genius hath taken the infection of the device, man.
BOT: Nay, pursue him now, lest the device take air and taint.
USER: Why, we shall make him mad indeed.
BOT: The house will be the quieter.
USER: More matter for a May morning.
BOT: Here's the challenge, read it. I warrant there's vinegar and pepper in't.
USER: Is't so saucy?
BOT: Ay, is't, I warrant him. Do but read.
USER: Give me. [_Reads._] _Youth, whatsoever thou art, thou art but a scurvy fellow._
BOT: Good, and valiant.
USER: _Wonder not, nor admire not in thy mind, why I do call thee so, for I will show thee no reason for't._
BOT: A good note, that keeps you from the blow of the law.
USER: _Thou comest to the Lady Olivia, and in my sight she uses thee kindly: but thou liest in thy throat; that is not the matter I challenge thee for._
BOT: Very brief, and to exceeding good sense - less.
USER: _I will waylay thee going home; where if it be thy chance to kill me - _
BOT: Good.
USER: _Thou kill'st me like a rogue and a villain._
BOT: Still you keep o' th' windy side of the law. Good.
USER: You may have very fit occasion for't. He is now in some commerce with my lady, and will by and by depart.
BOT: Nay, let me alone for swearing.
USER: Here he comes with your niece; give them way till he take leave, and presently after him.
BOT: I will meditate the while upon some horrid message for a challenge.
USER: I have said too much unto a heart of stone, And laid mine honour too unchary on't: There's something in me that reproves my fault: But such a headstrong potent fault it is, That it but mocks reproof.
BOT: With the same 'haviour that your passion bears Goes on my master's griefs.
USER: Here, wear this jewel for me, 'tis my picture. Refuse it not, it hath no tongue to vex you. And I beseech you come again tomorrow. What shall you ask of me that I'll deny, That honour sav'd, may upon asking give?
BOT: Nothing but this, your true love for my master.
USER: How with mine honour may I give him that Which I have given to you?
BOT: I will acquit you.
USER: Well, come again tomorrow. Fare thee well; A fiend like thee might bear my soul to hell.
BOT: Gentleman, God save thee.
USER: And you, sir.
BOT: You mistake, sir; I am sure no man hath any quarrel to me. My remembrance is very free and clear from any image of offence done to any man.
USER: You'll find it otherwise, I assure you. Therefore, if you hold your life at any price, betake you to your guard, for your opposite hath in him what youth, strength, skill, and wrath, can furnish man withal.
BOT: I pray you, sir, what is he?
USER: I will return again into the house and desire some conduct of the lady. I am no fighter. I have heard of some kind of men that put quarrels purposely on others to taste their valour: belike this is a man of that quirk.
BOT: This is as uncivil as strange. I beseech you, do me this courteous office, as to know of the knight what my offence to him is. It is something of my negligence, nothing of my purpose.
USER: I will do so. Signior Fabian, stay you by this gentleman till my return.
BOT: Pray you, sir, do you know of this matter?
USER: I know the knight is incensed against you, even to a mortal arbitrement, but nothing of the circumstance more.
BOT: I beseech you, what manner of man is he?
USER: I shall be much bound to you for't. I am one that had rather go with sir priest than sir knight: I care not who knows so much of my mettle.
BOT: Pox on't, I'll not meddle with him.
USER: Ay, but he will not now be pacified: Fabian can scarce hold him yonder.
BOT: Plague on't, an I thought he had been valiant, and so cunning in fence, I'd have seen him damned ere I'd have challenged him. Let him let the matter slip, and I'll give him my horse, grey Capilet.
USER: I'll make the motion. Stand here, make a good show on't. This shall end without the perdition of souls. [_Aside._] Marry, I'll ride your horse as well as I ride you. persuaded him the youth's a devil.
BOT: He is as horribly conceited of him, and pants and looks pale, as if a bear were at his heels.
USER: how much I lack of a man.
BOT: Give ground if you see him furious.
USER: Put up your sword. If this young gentleman Have done offence, I take the fault on me. If you offend him, I for him defy you.
BOT: You, sir? Why, what are you?
USER: Than you have heard him brag to you he will.
BOT: O good Sir Toby, hold! Here come the officers.
USER: Marry, will I, sir; and for that I promised you, I'll be as good as my word. He will bear you easily, and reins well.
BOT: This is the man; do thy office.
USER: Antonio, I arrest thee at the suit Of Count Orsino.
BOT: You do mistake me, sir.
USER: No, sir, no jot. I know your favour well, Though now you have no sea-cap on your head. - Take him away, he knows I know him well.
BOT: Come, sir, away.
USER: I must entreat of you some of that money.
BOT: Will you deny me now? Is't possible that my deserts to you Can lack persuasion? Do not tempt my misery, Lest that it make me so unsound a man As to upbraid you with those kindnesses That I have done for you.
USER: I know of none, Nor know I you by voice or any feature. I hate ingratitude more in a man Than lying, vainness, babbling, drunkenness, Or any taint of vice whose strong corruption Inhabits our frail blood.
BOT: O heavens themselves!
USER: Come, sir, I pray you go.
BOT: What's that to us? The time goes by. Away!
USER: The man grows mad, away with him. Come, come, sir.
BOT: Lead me on.
USER: Methinks his words do from such passion fly That he believes himself; so do not I. Prove true, imagination, O prove true, That I, dear brother, be now ta'en for you!
BOT: Come hither, knight; come hither, Fabian. We'll whisper o'er a couplet or two of most sage saws.
USER: A very dishonest paltry boy, and more a coward than a hare. His dishonesty appears in leaving his friend here in necessity, and denying him; and for his cowardship, ask Fabian.
BOT: A coward, a most devout coward, religious in it.
USER: 'Slid, I'll after him again and beat him.
BOT: Do, cuff him soundly, but never draw thy sword.
USER: And I do not -
BOT: Come, let's see the event.
USER: I dare lay any money 'twill be nothing yet.

USER: Will you make me believe that I am not sent for you?
BOT: Go to, go to, thou art a foolish fellow. Let me be clear of thee.
USER: Well held out, i' faith! No, I do not know you, nor I am not sent to you by my lady, to bid you come speak with her; nor your name is not Master Cesario; nor this is not my nose neither. Nothing that is so, is so.
BOT: I prithee vent thy folly somewhere else, Thou know'st not me.
USER: I prithee, foolish Greek, depart from me. There's money for thee; if you tarry longer I shall give worse payment.
BOT: By my troth, thou hast an open hand. These wise men that give fools money get themselves a good report - after fourteen years' purchase.
USER: Now sir, have I met you again? There's for you.
BOT: Why, there's for thee, and there, and there. Are all the people mad?
USER: Hold, sir, or I'll throw your dagger o'er the house.
BOT: This will I tell my lady straight. I would not be in some of your coats for twopence.
USER: Come on, sir, hold!
BOT: Nay, let him alone, I'll go another way to work with him. I'll have an action of battery against him, if there be any law in Illyria. Though I struck him first, yet it's no matter for that.
USER: Let go thy hand!
BOT: Come, sir, I will not let you go. Come, my young soldier, put up your iron: you are well fleshed. Come on.
USER: I will be free from thee. What wouldst thou now? If thou dar'st tempt me further, draw thy sword.
BOT: What, what? Nay, then, I must have an ounce or two of this malapert blood from you.
USER: Hold, Toby! On thy life I charge thee hold!
BOT: Madam.
USER: What relish is in this? How runs the stream? Or I am mad, or else this is a dream. Let fancy still my sense in Lethe steep; If it be thus to dream, still let me sleep!
BOT: Nay, come, I prithee. Would thou'dst be ruled by me!
USER: Madam, I will.
BOT: O, say so, and so be!

USER: Nay, I prithee, put on this gown and this beard; make him believe thou art Sir Topas the curate. Do it quickly. I'll call Sir Toby the whilst.
BOT: Jove bless thee, Master Parson.
USER: To him, Sir Topas.
BOT: What ho, I say! Peace in this prison!
USER: The knave counterfeits well. A good knave. Malvolio within.
BOT: Who calls there?
USER: Sir Topas the curate, who comes to visit Malvolio the lunatic.
BOT: Sir Topas, Sir Topas, good Sir Topas, go to my lady.
USER: Out, hyperbolical fiend! how vexest thou this man? Talkest thou nothing but of ladies?
BOT: Well said, Master Parson.
USER: Sir Topas, never was man thus wronged. Good Sir Topas, do not think I am mad. They have laid me here in hideous darkness.
BOT: Fie, thou dishonest Satan! I call thee by the most modest terms, for I am one of those gentle ones that will use the devil himself with courtesy. Say'st thou that house is dark?
USER: As hell, Sir Topas.
BOT: Why, it hath bay windows transparent as barricadoes, and the clerestories toward the south-north are as lustrous as ebony; and yet complainest thou of obstruction?
USER: I am not mad, Sir Topas. I say to you this house is dark.
BOT: Madman, thou errest. I say there is no darkness but ignorance, in which thou art more puzzled than the Egyptians in their fog.
USER: I say this house is as dark as ignorance, though ignorance were as dark as hell; and I say there was never man thus abused. I am no more mad than you are. Make the trial of it in any constant question.
BOT: What is the opinion of Pythagoras concerning wildfowl?
USER: That the soul of our grandam might haply inhabit a bird.
BOT: What think'st thou of his opinion?
USER: I think nobly of the soul, and no way approve his opinion.
BOT: Fare thee well. Remain thou still in darkness. Thou shalt hold the opinion of Pythagoras ere I will allow of thy wits, and fear to kill a woodcock, lest thou dispossess the soul of thy grandam. Fare thee well.
USER: Sir Topas, Sir Topas!
BOT: My most exquisite Sir Topas!
USER: Nay, I am for all waters.
BOT: Thou mightst have done this without thy beard and gown. He sees thee not.
USER: _Hey, Robin, jolly Robin, Tell me how thy lady does._
BOT: Fool!
USER: _My lady is unkind, perdy._
BOT: Fool!
USER: _Alas, why is she so?_
BOT: Fool, I say!
USER: _She loves another_ - Who calls, ha?
BOT: Good fool, as ever thou wilt deserve well at my hand, help me to a candle, and pen, ink, and paper. As I am a gentleman, I will live to be thankful to thee for't.
USER: Master Malvolio?
BOT: Ay, good fool.
USER: Alas, sir, how fell you besides your five wits?
BOT: Fool, there was never man so notoriously abused. I am as well in my wits, fool, as thou art.
USER: But as well? Then you are mad indeed, if you be no better in your wits than a fool.
BOT: They have here propertied me; keep me in darkness, send ministers to me, asses, and do all they can to face me out of my wits.
USER: Advise you what you say: the minister is here. [_As Sir Topas_] Malvolio, Malvolio, thy wits the heavens restore. Endeavour thyself to sleep, and leave thy vain bibble-babble.
BOT: Sir Topas!
USER: himself_] Who, I, sir? not I, sir. God buy you, good Sir Topas. [_As Sir Topas_] Marry, amen. [_As himself_] I will sir, I will.
BOT: Fool, fool, fool, I say!
USER: Alas, sir, be patient. What say you, sir? I am shent for speaking to you.
BOT: Good fool, help me to some light and some paper. I tell thee I am as well in my wits as any man in Illyria.
USER: Well-a-day that you were, sir!
BOT: By this hand, I am. Good fool, some ink, paper, and light, and convey what I will set down to my lady. It shall advantage thee more than ever the bearing of letter did.
USER: I will help you to't. But tell me true, are you not mad indeed? or do you but counterfeit?
BOT: Believe me, I am not. I tell thee true.
USER: Nay, I'll ne'er believe a madman till I see his brains. I will fetch you light, and paper, and ink.
BOT: Fool, I'll requite it in the highest degree: I prithee be gone.

USER: I'll follow this good man, and go with you, And having sworn truth, ever will be true.
BOT: Then lead the way, good father, and heavens so shine, That they may fairly note this act of mine!

USER: Now, as thou lov'st me, let me see his letter.
BOT: Good Master Fabian, grant me another request.
USER: Anything.
BOT: Do not desire to see this letter.
USER: This is to give a dog, and in recompense desire my dog again.
BOT: Belong you to the Lady Olivia, friends?
USER: Ay, sir, we are some of her trappings.
BOT: I know thee well. How dost thou, my good fellow?
USER: Truly, sir, the better for my foes, and the worse for my friends.
BOT: Just the contrary; the better for thy friends.
USER: No, sir, the worse.
BOT: How can that be?
USER: Why, this is excellent.
BOT: By my troth, sir, no; though it please you to be one of my friends.
USER: Thou shalt not be the worse for me; there's gold.
BOT: But that it would be double-dealing, sir, I would you could make it another.
USER: O, you give me ill counsel.
BOT: Put your grace in your pocket, sir, for this once, and let your flesh and blood obey it.
USER: Well, I will be so much a sinner to be a double-dealer: there's another.
BOT: _Primo, secundo, tertio_, is a good play, and the old saying is, the third pays for all; the triplex, sir, is a good tripping measure; or the bells of Saint Bennet, sir, may put you in mind - one, two, three.
USER: You can fool no more money out of me at this throw. If you will let your lady know I am here to speak with her, and bring her along with you, it may awake my bounty further.
BOT: Here comes the man, sir, that did rescue me.
USER: He did me kindness, sir; drew on my side, But in conclusion, put strange speech upon me. I know not what 'twas, but distraction.
BOT: Notable pirate, thou salt-water thief, What foolish boldness brought thee to their mercies, Whom thou, in terms so bloody and so dear, Hast made thine enemies?
USER: How can this be?
BOT: When came he to this town?
USER: Today, my lord; and for three months before, No int'rim, not a minute's vacancy, Both day and night did we keep company.
BOT: Here comes the Countess, now heaven walks on earth. But for thee, fellow, fellow, thy words are madness. Three months this youth hath tended upon me; But more of that anon. Take him aside.
USER: What would my lord, but that he may not have, Wherein Olivia may seem serviceable? Cesario, you do not keep promise with me.
BOT: Madam?
USER: Gracious Olivia -
BOT: What do you say, Cesario? Good my lord -
USER: My lord would speak, my duty hushes me.
BOT: If it be aught to the old tune, my lord, It is as fat and fulsome to mine ear As howling after music.
USER: Still so cruel?
BOT: Still so constant, lord.
USER: What, to perverseness? You uncivil lady, To whose ingrate and unauspicious altars My soul the faithfull'st off'rings hath breathed out That e'er devotion tender'd! What shall I do?
BOT: Even what it please my lord that shall become him.
USER: And I, most jocund, apt, and willingly, To do you rest, a thousand deaths would die.
BOT: Where goes Cesario?
USER: After him I love More than I love these eyes, more than my life, More, by all mores, than e'er I shall love wife. If I do feign, you witnesses above Punish my life for tainting of my love.
BOT: Ah me, detested! how am I beguil'd!
USER: Who does beguile you? Who does do you wrong?
BOT: Hast thou forgot thyself? Is it so long? Call forth the holy father.
USER: Whither, my lord? Cesario, husband, stay.
BOT: Husband?
USER: Ay, husband. Can he that deny?
BOT: Her husband, sirrah?
USER: No, my lord, not I.
BOT: My lord, I do protest -
USER: O, do not swear. Hold little faith, though thou has too much fear.
BOT: For the love of God, a surgeon! Send one presently to Sir Toby.
USER: What's the matter?
BOT: 'Has broke my head across, and has given Sir Toby a bloody coxcomb too. For the love of God, your help! I had rather than forty pound I were at home.
USER: Who has done this, Sir Andrew?
BOT: The Count's gentleman, one Cesario. We took him for a coward, but he's the very devil incardinate.
USER: My gentleman, Cesario?
BOT: 'Od's lifelings, here he is! - You broke my head for nothing; and that that I did, I was set on to do't by Sir Toby.
USER: Why do you speak to me? I never hurt you: You drew your sword upon me without cause, But I bespake you fair and hurt you not.
BOT: How now, gentleman? How is't with you?
USER: That's all one; 'has hurt me, and there's th' end on't. Sot, didst see Dick Surgeon, sot?
BOT: O, he's drunk, Sir Toby, an hour agone; his eyes were set at eight i' th' morning.
USER: Then he's a rogue, and a passy measures pavin. I hate a drunken rogue.
BOT: Away with him. Who hath made this havoc with them?
USER: I'll help you, Sir Toby, because we'll be dressed together.
BOT: Will you help? An ass-head, and a coxcomb, and a knave, a thin-faced knave, a gull?
USER: Get him to bed, and let his hurt be looked to.
BOT: One face, one voice, one habit, and two persons! A natural perspective, that is, and is not!
USER: Antonio, O my dear Antonio! How have the hours rack'd and tortur'd me Since I have lost thee.
BOT: Sebastian are you?
USER: Fear'st thou that, Antonio?
BOT: How have you made division of yourself? An apple cleft in two is not more twin Than these two creatures. Which is Sebastian?
USER: Most wonderful!
BOT: Of Messaline: Sebastian was my father; Such a Sebastian was my brother too: So went he suited to his watery tomb. If spirits can assume both form and suit, You come to fright us.
USER: My father had a mole upon his brow.
BOT: And so had mine.
USER: And died that day when Viola from her birth Had numbered thirteen years.
BOT: O, that record is lively in my soul! He finished indeed his mortal act That day that made my sister thirteen years.
USER: But nature to her bias drew in that. You would have been contracted to a maid; Nor are you therein, by my life, deceived: You are betroth'd both to a maid and man.
BOT: Be not amazed; right noble is his blood. If this be so, as yet the glass seems true, I shall have share in this most happy wreck. Thou never shouldst love woman like to me.
USER: And all those sayings will I over-swear, And all those swearings keep as true in soul As doth that orbed continent the fire That severs day from night.
BOT: Give me thy hand, And let me see thee in thy woman's weeds.
USER: The captain that did bring me first on shore Hath my maid's garments. He, upon some action, Is now in durance, at Malvolio's suit, A gentleman and follower of my lady's.
BOT: Open 't, and read it.
USER: Look then to be well edified, when the fool delivers the madman. _By the Lord, madam, - _
BOT: How now, art thou mad?
USER: No, madam, I do but read madness: an your ladyship will have it as it ought to be, you must allow _vox_.
BOT: Prithee, read i' thy right wits.
USER: So I do, madonna. But to read his right wits is to read thus; therefore perpend, my princess, and give ear.
BOT: Did he write this?
USER: Ay, madam.
BOT: This savours not much of distraction.
USER: A sister? You are she.
BOT: Is this the madman?
USER: Ay, my lord, this same. How now, Malvolio?
BOT: Madam, you have done me wrong, Notorious wrong.
USER: Have I, Malvolio? No.
BOT: Alas, poor fool, how have they baffled thee!
USER: I'll be revenged on the whole pack of you.
BOT: He hath been most notoriously abus'd.

USER: And on a love-book pray for my success?
BOT: Upon some book I love I'll pray for thee.
USER: That's on some shallow story of deep love, How young Leander crossed the Hellespont.
BOT: That's a deep story of a deeper love, For he was more than over shoes in love.
USER: 'Tis true; for you are over boots in love, And yet you never swam the Hellespont.
BOT: Over the boots? Nay, give me not the boots.
USER: No, I will not, for it boots thee not.
BOT: What?
USER: So, by your circumstance, you call me fool.
BOT: So, by your circumstance, I fear you'll prove.
USER: 'Tis love you cavil at. I am not Love.
BOT: Love is your master, for he masters you; And he that is so yoked by a fool Methinks should not be chronicled for wise.
USER: Yet writers say, as in the sweetest bud The eating canker dwells, so eating love Inhabits in the finest wits of all.
BOT: And thither will I bring thee, Valentine.
USER: Sweet Proteus, no. Now let us take our leave. To Milan let me hear from thee by letters Of thy success in love, and what news else Betideth here in absence of thy friend; And I likewise will visit thee with mine.
BOT: All happiness bechance to thee in Milan.
USER: As much to you at home, and so farewell.
BOT: Sir Proteus, 'save you. Saw you my master?
USER: But now he parted hence to embark for Milan.
BOT: Twenty to one, then, he is shipped already, And I have played the sheep in losing him.
USER: Indeed a sheep doth very often stray, An if the shepherd be a while away.
BOT: You conclude that my master is a shepherd then, and I a sheep?
USER: I do.
BOT: Why then, my horns are his horns, whether I wake or sleep.
USER: A silly answer, and fitting well a sheep.
BOT: This proves me still a sheep.
USER: True, and thy master a shepherd.
BOT: Nay, that I can deny by a circumstance.
USER: It shall go hard but I'll prove it by another.
BOT: The shepherd seeks the sheep, and not the sheep the shepherd; but I seek my master, and my master seeks not me. Therefore I am no sheep.
USER: The sheep for fodder follow the shepherd; the shepherd for food follows not the sheep. Thou for wages followest thy master; thy master for wages follows not thee. Therefore thou art a sheep.
BOT: Such another proof will make me cry "baa".
USER: But dost thou hear? Gav'st thou my letter to Julia?
BOT: Ay, sir. I, a lost mutton, gave your letter to her, a laced mutton, and she, a laced mutton, gave me, a lost mutton, nothing for my labour.
USER: Here's too small a pasture for such store of muttons.
BOT: If the ground be overcharged, you were best stick her.
USER: Nay, in that you are astray; 'twere best pound you.
BOT: Nay, sir, less than a pound shall serve me for carrying your letter.
USER: You mistake; I mean the pound, a pinfold.
BOT: From a pound to a pin? Fold it over and over, 'Tis threefold too little for carrying a letter to your lover.
USER: But what said she?
BOT: Nod - "Ay". Why, that's "noddy".
USER: You mistook, sir. I say she did nod, and you ask me if she did nod; and I say "Ay".
BOT: And that set together is "noddy".
USER: Now you have taken the pains to set it together, take it for your pains.
BOT: No, no, you shall have it for bearing the letter.
USER: Well, I perceive I must be fain to bear with you.
BOT: Why, sir, how do you bear with me?
USER: Marry, sir, the letter, very orderly, having nothing but the word "noddy" for my pains.
BOT: Beshrew me, but you have a quick wit.
USER: And yet it cannot overtake your slow purse.
BOT: Come, come, open the matter; in brief, what said she?
USER: Open your purse, that the money and the matter may be both at once delivered.
BOT: she?
USER: Truly, sir, I think you'll hardly win her.
BOT: Why? Couldst thou perceive so much from her?
USER: What said she, nothing?
BOT: No, not so much as "Take this for thy pains." To testify your bounty, I thank you, you have testerned me; in requital whereof, henceforth carry your letters yourself. And so, sir, I'll commend you to my master.

USER: But say, Lucetta, now we are alone, Wouldst thou then counsel me to fall in love?
BOT: Ay, madam, so you stumble not unheedfully.
USER: Of all the fair resort of gentlemen That every day with parle encounter me, In thy opinion which is worthiest love?
BOT: Please you, repeat their names, I'll show my mind According to my shallow simple skill.
USER: What think'st thou of the fair Sir Eglamour?
BOT: As of a knight well-spoken, neat, and fine; But, were I you, he never should be mine.
USER: What think'st thou of the rich Mercatio?
BOT: Well of his wealth; but of himself, so-so.
USER: What think'st thou of the gentle Proteus?
BOT: Lord, Lord, to see what folly reigns in us!
USER: How now? What means this passion at his name?
BOT: Pardon, dear madam, 'tis a passing shame That I, unworthy body as I am, Should censure thus on lovely gentlemen.
USER: Why not on Proteus, as of all the rest?
BOT: Then thus: of many good I think him best.
USER: Your reason?
BOT: I have no other but a woman's reason: I think him so because I think him so.
USER: And wouldst thou have me cast my love on him?
BOT: Ay, if you thought your love not cast away.
USER: Why, he of all the rest hath never moved me.
BOT: Yet he of all the rest I think best loves ye.
USER: His little speaking shows his love but small.
BOT: Fire that's closest kept burns most of all.
USER: They do not love that do not show their love.
BOT: O, they love least that let men know their love.
USER: I would I knew his mind.
BOT: Peruse this paper, madam.
USER: _To Julia_ - Say, from whom?
BOT: That the contents will show.
USER: Say, say, who gave it thee?
BOT: Sir Valentine's page, and sent, I think, from Proteus. He would have given it you, but I, being in the way, Did in your name receive it. Pardon the fault, I pray.
USER: To plead for love deserves more fee than hate.
BOT: Will ye be gone?
USER: That you may ruminate.
BOT: What would your ladyship?
USER: Is 't near dinner time?
BOT: I would it were, That you might kill your stomach on your meat And not upon your maid.
USER: What is't that you took up so gingerly?
BOT: Nothing.
USER: Why didst thou stoop, then?
BOT: To take a paper up that I let fall.
USER: And is that paper nothing?
BOT: Nothing concerning me.
USER: Then let it lie for those that it concerns.
BOT: Madam, it will not lie where it concerns, Unless it have a false interpreter.
USER: Some love of yours hath writ to you in rhyme.
BOT: That I might sing it, madam, to a tune. Give me a note. Your ladyship can set -
USER: As little by such toys as may be possible. Best sing it to the tune of "Light o' Love".
BOT: It is too heavy for so light a tune.
USER: Heavy? Belike it hath some burden then?
BOT: Ay, and melodious were it, would you sing it.
USER: And why not you?
BOT: I cannot reach so high.
USER: Let's see your song. [_Taking the letter_.] How now, minion!
BOT: Keep tune there still, so you will sing it out. And yet methinks I do not like this tune.
USER: You do not?
BOT: No, madam, it is too sharp.
USER: You, minion, are too saucy.
BOT: Nay, now you are too flat And mar the concord with too harsh a descant. There wanteth but a mean to fill your song.
USER: The mean is drowned with your unruly bass.
BOT: Indeed, I bid the base for Proteus.
USER: This babble shall not henceforth trouble me. Here is a coil with protestation! [_Tears the letter_.] Go, get you gone, and let the papers lie. You would be fingering them to anger me.
BOT: She makes it strange, but she would be best pleased To be so angered with another letter.
USER: Madam, dinner is ready, and your father stays.
BOT: Well, let us go.
USER: What, shall these papers lie like tell-tales here?
BOT: If you respect them, best to take them up.
USER: Nay, I was taken up for laying them down. Yet here they shall not lie, for catching cold.
BOT: I see you have a month's mind to them.
USER: Ay, madam, you may say what sights you see; I see things too, although you judge I wink.
BOT: Come, come, will't please you go?

USER: Tell me, Pantino, what sad talk was that Wherewith my brother held you in the cloister?
BOT: 'Twas of his nephew Proteus, your son.
USER: Why, what of him?
BOT: I think your lordship is not ignorant How his companion, youthful Valentine, Attends the Emperor in his royal court.
USER: I know it well.
BOT: 'Twere good, I think, your lordship sent him thither. There shall he practise tilts and tournaments, Hear sweet discourse, converse with noblemen, And be in eye of every exercise Worthy his youth and nobleness of birth.
USER: I like thy counsel; well hast thou advised, And that thou mayst perceive how well I like it, The execution of it shall make known. Even with the speediest expedition I will dispatch him to the Emperor's court.
BOT: Tomorrow, may it please you, Don Alphonso With other gentlemen of good esteem Are journeying to salute the Emperor And to commend their service to his will.
USER: Good company. With them shall Proteus go. And in good time! Now will we break with him.
BOT: How now? What letter are you reading there?
USER: May't please your lordship, 'tis a word or two Of commendations sent from Valentine, Delivered by a friend that came from him.
BOT: Lend me the letter. Let me see what news.
USER: There is no news, my lord, but that he writes How happily he lives, how well beloved And daily graced by the Emperor, Wishing me with him, partner of his fortune.
BOT: And how stand you affected to his wish?
USER: As one relying on your lordship's will, And not depending on his friendly wish.
BOT: My lord, I cannot be so soon provided; Please you deliberate a day or two.
USER: Look what thou want'st shall be sent after thee. No more of stay. Tomorrow thou must go. Come on, Pantino, you shall be employed To hasten on his expedition.
BOT: Sir Proteus, your father calls for you. He is in haste. Therefore, I pray you, go.
USER: Why, this it is: my heart accords thereto, And yet a thousand times it answers "No".

USER: Sir, your glove.
BOT: Not mine. My gloves are on.
USER: Why, then, this may be yours, for this is but one.
BOT: Ha? Let me see. Ay, give it me, it's mine. Sweet ornament that decks a thing divine! Ah, Silvia, Silvia!
USER: How now, sirrah?
BOT: She is not within hearing, sir.
USER: Why, sir, who bade you call her?
BOT: Your worship, sir, or else I mistook.
USER: Well, you'll still be too forward.
BOT: And yet I was last chidden for being too slow.
USER: Go to, sir. Tell me, do you know Madam Silvia?
BOT: She that your worship loves?
USER: Why, how know you that I am in love?
BOT: Are all these things perceived in me?
USER: They are all perceived without ye.
BOT: Without me? They cannot.
USER: But tell me, dost thou know my lady Silvia?
BOT: She that you gaze on so as she sits at supper?
USER: Hast thou observed that? Even she I mean.
BOT: Why, sir, I know her not.
USER: Dost thou know her by my gazing on her, and yet know'st her not?
BOT: Is she not hard-favoured, sir?
USER: Not so fair, boy, as well-favoured.
BOT: Sir, I know that well enough.
USER: What dost thou know?
BOT: That she is not so fair as, of you, well-favoured.
USER: I mean that her beauty is exquisite but her favour infinite.
BOT: That's because the one is painted, and the other out of all count.
USER: How painted? And how out of count?
BOT: Marry, sir, so painted to make her fair, that no man counts of her beauty.
USER: How esteem'st thou me? I account of her beauty.
BOT: You never saw her since she was deformed.
USER: How long hath she been deformed?
BOT: Ever since you loved her.
USER: I have loved her ever since I saw her, and still I see her beautiful.
BOT: If you love her, you cannot see her.
USER: Why?
BOT: Because Love is blind. O, that you had mine eyes, or your own eyes had the lights they were wont to have when you chid at Sir Proteus for going ungartered!
USER: What should I see then?
BOT: Your own present folly and her passing deformity; for he, being in love, could not see to garter his hose; and you, being in love, cannot see to put on your hose.
USER: Belike, boy, then you are in love, for last morning you could not see to wipe my shoes.
BOT: True, sir, I was in love with my bed. I thank you, you swinged me for my love, which makes me the bolder to chide you for yours.
USER: In conclusion, I stand affected to her.
BOT: I would you were set, so your affection would cease.
USER: Last night she enjoined me to write some lines to one she loves.
BOT: And have you?
USER: I have.
BOT: Are they not lamely writ?
USER: No, boy, but as well as I can do them. Peace, here she comes.
BOT: Now will he interpret to her.
USER: Madam and mistress, a thousand good-morrows.
BOT: Sir Valentine and servant, to you two thousand.
USER: As you enjoined me, I have writ your letter Unto the secret nameless friend of yours, Which I was much unwilling to proceed in But for my duty to your ladyship.
BOT: I thank you, gentle servant, 'tis very clerkly done.
USER: Now trust me, madam, it came hardly off, For, being ignorant to whom it goes, I writ at random, very doubtfully.
BOT: Perchance you think too much of so much pains?
USER: No, madam; so it stead you, I will write, Please you command, a thousand times as much. And yet -
BOT: A pretty period. Well, I guess the sequel; And yet I will not name it. And yet I care not. And yet take this again. And yet I thank you, Meaning henceforth to trouble you no more.
USER: What means your ladyship? Do you not like it?
BOT: Yes, yes, the lines are very quaintly writ, But, since unwillingly, take them again. Nay, take them.
USER: Madam, they are for you.
BOT: Ay, ay, you writ them, sir, at my request, But I will none of them. They are for you. I would have had them writ more movingly.
USER: Please you, I'll write your ladyship another.
BOT: And when it's writ, for my sake read it over, And if it please you, so; if not, why, so.
USER: If it please me, madam? What then?
BOT: Why, if it please you, take it for your labour. And so good morrow, servant.
USER: How now, sir? What are you reasoning with yourself?
BOT: Nay, I was rhyming. 'Tis you that have the reason.
USER: To do what?
BOT: To be a spokesman from Madam Silvia.
USER: To whom?
BOT: To yourself. Why, she woos you by a figure.
USER: What figure?
BOT: By a letter, I should say.
USER: Why, she hath not writ to me.
BOT: What need she, when she hath made you write to yourself? Why, do you not perceive the jest?
USER: No, believe me.
BOT: No believing you indeed, sir. But did you perceive her earnest?
USER: She gave me none, except an angry word.
BOT: Why, she hath given you a letter.
USER: That's the letter I writ to her friend.
BOT: And that letter hath she delivered, and there an end.
USER: I would it were no worse.
BOT: I have dined.
USER: Ay, but hearken, sir, though the chameleon Love can feed on the air, I am one that am nourished by my victuals, and would fain have meat. O, be not like your mistress! Be moved, be moved.

USER: Have patience, gentle Julia.
BOT: I must, where is no remedy.
USER: When possibly I can, I will return.
BOT: If you turn not, you will return the sooner. Keep this remembrance for thy Julia's sake.
USER: Why, then we'll make exchange. Here, take you this.
BOT: And seal the bargain with a holy kiss.
USER: Sir Proteus, you are stayed for.
BOT: Go, I come, I come. Alas, this parting strikes poor lovers dumb.

USER: Lance, away, away! Aboard! Thy master is shipped, and thou art to post after with oars. What's the matter? Why weep'st thou, man? Away, ass. You'll lose the tide if you tarry any longer.
BOT: It is no matter if the tied were lost, for it is the unkindest tied that ever any man tied.
USER: What's the unkindest tide?
BOT: Why, he that's tied here, Crab, my dog.
USER: For fear thou shouldst lose thy tongue.
BOT: Where should I lose my tongue?
USER: In thy tale.
BOT: In thy tail!
USER: Lose the tide, and the voyage, and the master, and the service, and the tied? Why, man, if the river were dry, I am able to fill it with my tears; if the wind were down, I could drive the boat with my sighs.
BOT: Come, come away, man. I was sent to call thee.
USER: Sir, call me what thou dar'st.
BOT: Will thou go?
USER: Well, I will go.

USER: Servant!
BOT: Mistress?
USER: Master, Sir Thurio frowns on you.
BOT: Ay, boy, it's for love.
USER: Not of you.
BOT: Of my mistress, then.
USER: 'Twere good you knocked him.
BOT: Servant, you are sad.
USER: Indeed, madam, I seem so.
BOT: Seem you that you are not?
USER: Haply I do.
BOT: So do counterfeits.
USER: So do you.
BOT: What seem I that I am not?
USER: Wise.
BOT: What instance of the contrary?
USER: Your folly.
BOT: And how quote you my folly?
USER: I quote it in your jerkin.
BOT: My jerkin is a doublet.
USER: Well, then, I'll double your folly.
BOT: How!
USER: What, angry, Sir Thurio? Do you change colour?
BOT: Give him leave, madam, he is a kind of chameleon.
USER: That hath more mind to feed on your blood than live in your air.
BOT: You have said, sir.
USER: Ay, sir, and done too, for this time.
BOT: I know it well, sir. You always end ere you begin.
USER: A fine volley of words, gentlemen, and quickly shot off.
BOT: 'Tis indeed, madam, we thank the giver.
USER: Who is that, servant?
BOT: Yourself, sweet lady, for you gave the fire. Sir Thurio borrows his wit from your ladyship's looks, and spends what he borrows kindly in your company.
USER: Sir, if you spend word for word with me, I shall make your wit bankrupt.
BOT: I know it well, sir. You have an exchequer of words and, I think, no other treasure to give your followers, for it appears by their bare liveries that they live by your bare words.
USER: No more, gentlemen, no more. Here comes my father.
BOT: Now, daughter Silvia, you are hard beset. Sir Valentine, your father is in good health. What say you to a letter from your friends Of much good news?
USER: My lord, I will be thankful To any happy messenger from thence.
BOT: Know ye Don Antonio, your countryman?
USER: Ay, my good lord, I know the gentleman To be of worth and worthy estimation, And not without desert so well reputed.
BOT: Hath he not a son?
USER: Ay, my good lord, a son that well deserves The honour and regard of such a father.
BOT: You know him well?
USER: Should I have wished a thing, it had been he.
BOT: Welcome him then according to his worth. Silvia, I speak to you, and you, Sir Thurio. For Valentine, I need not cite him to it. I will send him hither to you presently.
USER: This is the gentleman I told your ladyship Had come along with me but that his mistresss Did hold his eyes locked in her crystal looks.
BOT: Belike that now she hath enfranchised them Upon some other pawn for fealty.
USER: Nay, sure, I think she holds them prisoners still.
BOT: Nay, then, he should be blind, and being blind How could he see his way to seek out you?
USER: Why, lady, Love hath twenty pair of eyes.
BOT: They say that Love hath not an eye at all.
USER: To see such lovers, Thurio, as yourself. Upon a homely object, Love can wink.
BOT: Have done, have done. Here comes the gentleman.
USER: Welcome, dear Proteus! Mistress, I beseech you Confirm his welcome with some special favour.
BOT: His worth is warrant for his welcome hither, If this be he you oft have wished to hear from.
USER: Mistress, it is. Sweet lady, entertain him To be my fellow-servant to your ladyship.
BOT: Too low a mistress for so high a servant.
USER: Not so, sweet lady, but too mean a servant To have a look of such a worthy mistress.
BOT: Leave off discourse of disability. Sweet lady, entertain him for your servant.
USER: My duty will I boast of, nothing else.
BOT: And duty never yet did want his meed. Servant, you are welcome to a worthless mistress.
USER: I'll die on him that says so but yourself.
BOT: That you are welcome?
USER: That you are worthless.
BOT: Madam, my lord your father would speak with you.
USER: I wait upon his pleasure. Come, Sir Thurio, Go with me. - Once more, new servant, welcome. I'll leave you to confer of home affairs; When you have done, we look to hear from you.
BOT: We'll both attend upon your ladyship.
USER: Now, tell me, how do all from whence you came?
BOT: Your friends are well and have them much commended.
USER: And how do yours?
BOT: I left them all in health.
USER: How does your lady? And how thrives your love?
BOT: My tales of love were wont to weary you; I know you joy not in a love-discourse.
USER: Enough; I read your fortune in your eye. Was this the idol that you worship so?
BOT: Even she; and is she not a heavenly saint?
USER: No, but she is an earthly paragon.
BOT: Call her divine.
USER: I will not flatter her.
BOT: O, flatter me, for love delights in praises.
USER: When I was sick, you gave me bitter pills, And I must minister the like to you.
BOT: Then speak the truth by her; if not divine, Yet let her be a principality, Sovereign to all the creatures on the earth.
USER: Except my mistress.
BOT: Sweet, except not any, Except thou wilt except against my love.
USER: Have I not reason to prefer mine own?
BOT: Why, Valentine, what braggartism is this?
USER: Pardon me, Proteus, all I can is nothing To her, whose worth makes other worthies nothing; She is alone.
BOT: Then let her alone.
USER: But she loves you?
BOT: Go on before; I shall enquire you forth. I must unto the road to disembark Some necessaries that I needs must use, And then I'll presently attend you.
USER: Will you make haste?

USER: Lance, by mine honesty, welcome to Milan!
BOT: Forswear not thyself, sweet youth, for I am not welcome. I reckon this always, that a man is never undone till he be hanged, nor never welcome to a place till some certain shot be paid and the hostess say "Welcome".
USER: Come on, you madcap. I'll to the alehouse with you presently, where, for one shot of five pence, thou shalt have five thousand welcomes. But, sirrah, how did thy master part with Madam Julia?
BOT: Marry, after they closed in earnest, they parted very fairly in jest.
USER: But shall she marry him?
BOT: No.
USER: How then? Shall he marry her?
BOT: No, neither.
USER: What, are they broken?
BOT: No, they are both as whole as a fish.
USER: Why then, how stands the matter with them?
BOT: Marry, thus: when it stands well with him, it stands well with her.
USER: What an ass art thou! I understand thee not.
BOT: What a block art thou that thou canst not! My staff understands me.
USER: What thou sayst?
BOT: Ay, and what I do too. Look thee, I'll but lean, and my staff understands me.
USER: It stands under thee indeed.
BOT: Why, stand-under and under-stand is all one.
USER: But tell me true, will't be a match?
BOT: Ask my dog. If he say "Ay", it will; if he say "No", it will; if he shake his tail and say nothing, it will.
USER: The conclusion is, then, that it will.
BOT: Thou shalt never get such a secret from me but by a parable.
USER: 'Tis well that I get it so. But, Lance, how sayst thou that my master is become a notable lover?
BOT: I never knew him otherwise.
USER: Than how?
BOT: A notable lubber, as thou reportest him to be.
USER: Why, thou whoreson ass, thou mistak'st me.
BOT: Why, fool, I meant not thee, I meant thy master.
USER: I tell thee, my master is become a hot lover.
BOT: Why, I tell thee, I care not though he burn himself in love. If thou wilt, go with me to the alehouse; if not, thou art an Hebrew, a Jew, and not worth the name of a Christian.
USER: Why?
BOT: Because thou hast not so much charity in thee as to go to the ale with a Christian. Wilt thou go?
USER: At thy service.

USER: Alas, the way is wearisome and long.
BOT: A true-devoted pilgrim is not weary To measure kingdoms with his feeble steps; Much less shall she that hath Love's wings to fly, And when the flight is made to one so dear, Of such divine perfection, as Sir Proteus.
USER: Better forbear till Proteus make return.
BOT: I do not seek to quench your love's hot fire, But qualify the fire's extreme rage, Lest it should burn above the bounds of reason.
USER: But in what habit will you go along?
BOT: Not like a woman, for I would prevent The loose encounters of lascivious men. Gentle Lucetta, fit me with such weeds As may beseem some well-reputed page.
USER: Why then, your ladyship must cut your hair.
BOT: No, girl, I'll knit it up in silken strings With twenty odd-conceited true-love knots. To be fantastic may become a youth Of greater time than I shall show to be.
USER: What fashion, madam, shall I make your breeches?
BOT: That fits as well as "Tell me, good my lord, What compass will you wear your farthingale?" Why e'en what fashion thou best likes, Lucetta.
USER: You must needs have them with a codpiece, madam.
BOT: Out, out, Lucetta, that will be ill-favoured.
USER: A round hose, madam, now's not worth a pin Unless you have a codpiece to stick pins on.
BOT: Lucetta, as thou lov'st me, let me have What thou think'st meet and is most mannerly. But tell me, wench, how will the world repute me For undertaking so unstaid a journey? I fear me it will make me scandalized.
USER: If you think so, then stay at home and go not.
BOT: Nay, that I will not.
USER: Then never dream on infamy, but go. If Proteus like your journey when you come, No matter who's displeased when you are gone. I fear me he will scarce be pleased withal.
BOT: That is the least, Lucetta, of my fear. A thousand oaths, an ocean of his tears, And instances of infinite of love, Warrant me welcome to my Proteus.
USER: All these are servants to deceitful men.
BOT: Pray heav'n he prove so when you come to him.

USER: Sir Thurio, give us leave, I pray, awhile; We have some secrets to confer about. Now tell me, Proteus, what's your will with me?
BOT: Upon mine honour, he shall never know That I had any light from thee of this.
USER: Adieu, my lord, Sir Valentine is coming.
BOT: Sir Valentine, whither away so fast?
USER: Please it your Grace, there is a messenger That stays to bear my letters to my friends, And I am going to deliver them.
BOT: Be they of much import?
USER: The tenor of them doth but signify My health and happy being at your court.
BOT: Nay then, no matter. Stay with me awhile; I am to break with thee of some affairs That touch me near, wherein thou must be secret. 'Tis not unknown to thee that I have sought To match my friend Sir Thurio to my daughter.
USER: What would your Grace have me to do in this?
BOT: Win her with gifts if she respect not words; Dumb jewels often in their silent kind More than quick words do move a woman's mind.
USER: But she did scorn a present that I sent her.
BOT: But she I mean is promised by her friends Unto a youthful gentleman of worth, And kept severely from resort of men, That no man hath access by day to her.
USER: Why then, I would resort to her by night.
BOT: Ay, but the doors be locked and keys kept safe, That no man hath recourse to her by night.
USER: What lets but one may enter at her window?
BOT: Her chamber is aloft, far from the ground, And built so shelving that one cannot climb it Without apparent hazard of his life.
USER: Why, then a ladder quaintly made of cords To cast up with a pair of anchoring hooks, Would serve to scale another Hero's tower, So bold Leander would adventure it.
BOT: Now, as thou art a gentleman of blood, Advise me where I may have such a ladder.
USER: When would you use it? Pray, sir, tell me that.
BOT: This very night; for Love is like a child That longs for everything that he can come by.
USER: By seven o'clock I'll get you such a ladder.
BOT: But, hark thee: I will go to her alone; How shall I best convey the ladder thither?
USER: It will be light, my lord, that you may bear it Under a cloak that is of any length.
BOT: A cloak as long as thine will serve the turn?
USER: Ay, my good lord.
BOT: Then let me see thy cloak; I'll get me one of such another length.
USER: Why, any cloak will serve the turn, my lord.
BOT: Run, boy, run, run, seek him out.
USER: So-ho, so-ho!
BOT: What seest thou?
USER: Him we go to find. There's not a hair on 's head but 'tis a Valentine.
BOT: Valentine?
USER: No.
BOT: Who then? His spirit?
USER: Neither.
BOT: What then?
USER: Nothing.
BOT: Can nothing speak? Master, shall I strike?
USER: Who wouldst thou strike?
BOT: Nothing.
USER: Villain, forbear.
BOT: Why, sir, I'll strike nothing. I pray you -
USER: Sirrah, I say, forbear. - Friend Valentine, a word.
BOT: My ears are stopped and cannot hear good news, So much of bad already hath possessed them.
USER: Then in dumb silence will I bury mine, For they are harsh, untuneable, and bad.
BOT: Is Silvia dead?
USER: No, Valentine.
BOT: No Valentine indeed for sacred Silvia. Hath she forsworn me?
USER: No, Valentine.
BOT: No Valentine, if Silvia have forsworn me. What is your news?
USER: Sir, there is a proclamation that you are vanished.
BOT: That thou art banished - O, that's the news - From hence, from Silvia, and from me thy friend.
USER: O, I have fed upon this woe already, And now excess of it will make me surfeit. Doth Silvia know that I am banished?
BOT: No more, unless the next word that thou speak'st Have some malignant power upon my life. If so, I pray thee breathe it in mine ear, As ending anthem of my endless dolour.
USER: I pray thee, Lance, an if thou seest my boy, Bid him make haste and meet me at the North Gate.
BOT: Go, sirrah, find him out. Come, Valentine.
USER: O, my dear Silvia! Hapless Valentine!
BOT: How now, Signior Lance? What news with your mastership?
USER: With my master's ship? Why, it is at sea.
BOT: Well, your old vice still: mistake the word. What news, then, in your paper?
USER: The blackest news that ever thou heard'st.
BOT: Why, man? How black?
USER: Why, as black as ink.
BOT: Let me read them.
USER: Fie on thee, jolt-head, thou canst not read.
BOT: Thou liest. I can.
USER: I will try thee. Tell me this, who begot thee?
BOT: Marry, the son of my grandfather.
USER: O, illiterate loiterer! It was the son of thy grandmother. This proves that thou canst not read.
BOT: Come, fool, come; try me in thy paper.
USER: _Imprimis, She can milk._
BOT: Ay, that she can.
USER: _Item, She brews good ale._
BOT: And thereof comes the proverb, "Blessing of your heart, you brew good ale."
USER: _Item, She can sew._
BOT: That's as much as to say, "Can she so?"
USER: _Item, She can knit._
BOT: What need a man care for a stock with a wench, when she can knit him a stock?
USER: _Item, She can wash and scour._
BOT: A special virtue, for then she need not be washed and scoured.
USER: _Item, She can spin._
BOT: Then may I set the world on wheels, when she can spin for her living.
USER: _Item, She hath many nameless virtues._
BOT: That's as much as to say, "bastard virtues", that indeed know not their fathers, and therefore have no names.
USER: Here follow her vices.
BOT: Close at the heels of her virtues.
USER: _Item, She is not to be kissed fasting in respect of her breath._
BOT: Well, that fault may be mended with a breakfast. Read on.
USER: _Item, She hath a sweet mouth._
BOT: That makes amends for her sour breath.
USER: _Item, She doth talk in her sleep._
BOT: It's no matter for that, so she sleep not in her talk.
USER: _Item, She is slow in words._
BOT: O villain, that set this down among her vices! To be slow in words is a woman's only virtue. I pray thee, out with't, and place it for her chief virtue.
USER: _Item, She is proud._
BOT: Out with that too; it was Eve's legacy and cannot be ta'en from her.
USER: _Item, She hath no teeth._
BOT: I care not for that neither, because I love crusts.
USER: _Item, She is curst._
BOT: Well, the best is, she hath no teeth to bite.
USER: _Item, She will often praise her liquor._
BOT: If her liquor be good, she shall; if she will not, I will, for good things should be praised.
USER: _Item, She is too liberal._
BOT: Of her tongue she cannot, for that's writ down she is slow of; of her purse she shall not, for that I'll keep shut. Now, of another thing she may, and that cannot I help. Well, proceed.
USER: _Item, She hath more hair than wit, and more faults than hairs, and more wealth than faults._
BOT: Stop there; I'll have her. She was mine and not mine twice or thrice in that last article. Rehearse that once more.
USER: _Item, She hath more hair than wit_ -
