BOT: More hair than wit. It may be; I'll prove it: the cover of the salt hides the salt, and therefore it is more than the salt; the hair that covers the wit is more than the wit, for the greater hides the less. What's next?
USER: _And more faults than hairs._
BOT: That's monstrous! O, that that were out!
USER: _And more wealth than faults._
BOT: Why, that word makes the faults gracious. Well, I'll have her; an if it be a match, as nothing is impossible -
USER: What then?
BOT: Why, then will I tell thee that thy master stays for thee at the North Gate.
USER: For me?
BOT: For thee? Ay, who art thou? He hath stayed for a better man than thee.
USER: And must I go to him?
BOT: Thou must run to him, for thou hast stayed so long that going will scarce serve the turn.
USER: Why didst not tell me sooner? Pox of your love letters!
BOT: Now will he be swinged for reading my letter; an unmannerly slave, that will thrust himself into secrets. I'll after, to rejoice in the boy's correction.

USER: Sir Thurio, fear not but that she will love you Now Valentine is banished from her sight.
BOT: Since his exile she hath despised me most, Forsworn my company and railed at me, That I am desperate of obtaining her.
USER: Gone, my good lord.
BOT: My daughter takes his going grievously.
USER: A little time, my lord, will kill that grief.
BOT: So I believe, but Thurio thinks not so. Proteus, the good conceit I hold of thee, For thou hast shown some sign of good desert, Makes me the better to confer with thee.
USER: Longer than I prove loyal to your Grace Let me not live to look upon your Grace.
BOT: Thou know'st how willingly I would effect The match between Sir Thurio and my daughter?
USER: I do, my lord.
BOT: And also, I think, thou art not ignorant How she opposes her against my will?
USER: She did, my lord, when Valentine was here.
BOT: Ay, and perversely she persevers so. What might we do to make the girl forget The love of Valentine, and love Sir Thurio?
USER: The best way is to slander Valentine With falsehood, cowardice, and poor descent, Three things that women highly hold in hate.
BOT: Ay, but she'll think that it is spoke in hate.
USER: Ay, if his enemy deliver it; Therefore it must with circumstance be spoken By one whom she esteemeth as his friend.
BOT: Then you must undertake to slander him.
USER: And that, my lord, I shall be loath to do. 'Tis an ill office for a gentleman, Especially against his very friend.
BOT: Where your good word cannot advantage him, Your slander never can endamage him; Therefore the office is indifferent, Being entreated to it by your friend.
USER: You have prevailed, my lord. If I can do it By aught that I can speak in his dispraise, She shall not long continue love to him. But say this weed her love from Valentine, It follows not that she will love Sir Thurio.
BOT: Therefore, as you unwind her love from him, Lest it should ravel and be good to none, You must provide to bottom it on me, Which must be done by praising me as much As you in worth dispraise Sir Valentine.
USER: As much as I can do I will effect. But you, Sir Thurio, are not sharp enough. You must lay lime to tangle her desires By wailful sonnets, whose composed rhymes Should be full-fraught with serviceable vows.
BOT: Ay, much is the force of heaven-bred poesy.
USER: This discipline shows thou hast been in love.
BOT: About it, gentlemen!
USER: We'll wait upon your Grace till after supper, And afterward determine our proceedings.
BOT: Even now about it! I will pardon you.

USER: Fellows, stand fast. I see a passenger.
BOT: If there be ten, shrink not, but down with 'em.
USER: Stand, sir, and throw us that you have about ye. If not, we'll make you sit, and rifle you.
BOT: Sir, we are undone: these are the villains That all the travellers do fear so much.
USER: My friends -
BOT: That's not so, sir. We are your enemies.
USER: Peace! We'll hear him.
BOT: Ay, by my beard, will we, for he is a proper man.
USER: Then know that I have little wealth to lose. A man I am crossed with adversity; My riches are these poor habiliments, Of which if you should here disfurnish me, You take the sum and substance that I have.
BOT: Whither travel you?
USER: To Verona.
BOT: Whence came you?
USER: From Milan.
BOT: Have you long sojourned there?
USER: Some sixteen months, and longer might have stayed If crooked fortune had not thwarted me.
BOT: What, were you banished thence?
USER: I was.
BOT: For what offence?
USER: For that which now torments me to rehearse; I killed a man, whose death I much repent, But yet I slew him manfully in fight, Without false vantage or base treachery.
BOT: Why, ne'er repent it, if it were done so. But were you banished for so small a fault?
USER: I was, and held me glad of such a doom.
BOT: Have you the tongues?
USER: My youthful travel therein made me happy, Or else I often had been miserable.
BOT: By the bare scalp of Robin Hood's fat friar, This fellow were a king for our wild faction.
USER: We'll have him. Sirs, a word.
BOT: Master, be one of them. It's an honourable kind of thievery.
USER: Peace, villain.
BOT: Tell us this: have you anything to take to?
USER: Nothing but my fortune.
BOT: And I from Mantua, for a gentleman Who, in my mood, I stabbed unto the heart.
USER: Indeed because you are a banished man, Therefore, above the rest, we parley to you. Are you content to be our general? To make a virtue of necessity And live as we do in this wilderness?
BOT: What sayst thou? Wilt thou be of our consort? Say "Ay", and be the captain of us all, We'll do thee homage and be ruled by thee, Love thee as our commander and our king.
USER: But if thou scorn our courtesy, thou diest.
BOT: Thou shalt not live to brag what we have offered.
USER: I take your offer and will live with you, Provided that you do no outrages On silly women or poor passengers.
BOT: No, we detest such vile base practices. Come, go with us; we'll bring thee to our crews And show thee all the treasure we have got, Which, with ourselves, all rest at thy dispose.

USER: How now, Sir Proteus, are you crept before us?
BOT: Ay, gentle Thurio, for you know that love Will creep in service where it cannot go.
USER: Ay, but I hope, sir, that you love not here.
BOT: Sir, but I do, or else I would be hence.
USER: Who? Silvia?
BOT: Ay, Silvia, for your sake.
USER: I thank you for your own. Now, gentlemen, Let's tune, and to it lustily awhile.
BOT: Now, my young guest, methinks you're allycholly. I pray you, why is it?
USER: Marry, mine host, because I cannot be merry.
BOT: Come, we'll have you merry. I'll bring you where you shall hear music, and see the gentleman that you asked for.
USER: But shall I hear him speak?
BOT: Ay, that you shall.
USER: That will be music.
BOT: Hark, hark!
USER: Is he among these?
BOT: Ay; but peace, let's hear 'em. SONG
USER: How now, are you sadder than you were before? How do you, man? The music likes you not.
BOT: You mistake; the musician likes me not.
USER: Why, my pretty youth?
BOT: He plays false, father.
USER: How, out of tune on the strings?
BOT: Not so; but yet so false that he grieves my very heart-strings.
USER: You have a quick ear.
BOT: Ay, I would I were deaf; it makes me have a slow heart.
USER: I perceive you delight not in music.
BOT: Not a whit, when it jars so.
USER: Hark, what fine change is in the music!
BOT: Ay, that change is the spite.
USER: You would have them always play but one thing?
BOT: I would always have one play but one thing. But, host, doth this Sir Proteus, that we talk on, Often resort unto this gentlewoman?
USER: I tell you what Lance, his man, told me: he loved her out of all nick.
BOT: Where is Lance?
USER: Gone to seek his dog, which tomorrow, by his master's command, he must carry for a present to his lady.
BOT: Peace, stand aside. The company parts.
USER: Sir Thurio, fear not you; I will so plead That you shall say my cunning drift excels.
BOT: Where meet we?
USER: At Saint Gregory's well.
BOT: Farewell.
USER: Madam, good even to your ladyship.
BOT: I thank you for your music, gentlemen. Who is that that spake?
USER: One, lady, if you knew his pure heart's truth, You would quickly learn to know him by his voice.
BOT: Sir Proteus, as I take it.
USER: Sir Proteus, gentle lady, and your servant.
BOT: What's your will?
USER: That I may compass yours.
BOT: I grant, sweet love, that I did love a lady, But she is dead.
USER: For I am sure she is not buried.
BOT: Say that she be; yet Valentine thy friend Survives, to whom, thyself art witness, I am betrothed. And art thou not ashamed To wrong him with thy importunacy?
USER: I likewise hear that Valentine is dead.
BOT: And so suppose am I, for in his grave, Assure thyself, my love is buried.
USER: Sweet lady, let me rake it from the earth.
BOT: Go to thy lady's grave and call hers thence, Or, at the least, in hers sepulchre thine.
USER: And make it but a shadow, as I am.
BOT: I am very loath to be your idol, sir; But since your falsehood shall become you well To worship shadows and adore false shapes, Send to me in the morning, and I'll send it. And so, good rest.
USER: As wretches have o'ernight That wait for execution in the morn.
BOT: Host, will you go?
USER: By my halidom, I was fast asleep.
BOT: Pray you, where lies Sir Proteus?
USER: Marry, at my house. Trust me, I think 'tis almost day.
BOT: Not so; but it hath been the longest night That e'er I watched, and the most heaviest.

USER: This is the hour that Madam Silvia Entreated me to call and know her mind; There's some great matter she'd employ me in. Madam, madam!
BOT: Who calls?
USER: Your servant and your friend; One that attends your ladyship's command.
BOT: Sir Eglamour, a thousand times good morrow.
USER: As many, worthy lady, to yourself. According to your ladyship's impose, I am thus early come to know what service It is your pleasure to command me in.
BOT: Madam, I pity much your grievances, Which, since I know they virtuously are placed, I give consent to go along with you, Recking as little what betideth me As much I wish all good befortune you. When will you go?
USER: This evening coming.
BOT: Where shall I meet you?
USER: At Friar Patrick's cell, Where I intend holy confession.
BOT: I will not fail your ladyship. Good morrow, gentle lady.
USER: Good morrow, kind Sir Eglamour.

USER: Sebastian is thy name? I like thee well, And will employ thee in some service presently.
BOT: In what you please; I'll do what I can.
USER: I hope thou wilt. [_To Lance_.] How now, you whoreson peasant, Where have you been these two days loitering?
BOT: Marry, sir, I carried Mistress Silvia the dog you bade me.
USER: And what says she to my little jewel?
BOT: Marry, she says your dog was a cur, and tells you currish thanks is good enough for such a present.
USER: But she received my dog?
BOT: No, indeed, did she not. Here have I brought him back again.
USER: What, didst thou offer her this from me?
BOT: Ay, sir, the other squirrel was stolen from me by the hangman's boys in the market-place, and then I offered her mine own, who is a dog as big as ten of yours, and therefore the gift the greater.
USER: It seems you loved not her, to leave her token. She's dead belike?
BOT: Not so; I think she lives.
USER: Alas!
BOT: Why dost thou cry "Alas"?
USER: I cannot choose But pity her.
BOT: Wherefore shouldst thou pity her?
USER: What would you with her, if that I be she?
BOT: If you be she, I do entreat your patience To hear me speak the message I am sent on.
USER: From whom?
BOT: From my master, Sir Proteus, madam.
USER: O, he sends you for a picture?
BOT: Ay, madam.
USER: Ursula, bring my picture there. Go, give your master this. Tell him from me, One Julia, that his changing thoughts forget, Would better fit his chamber than this shadow.
BOT: Madam, please you peruse this letter. Pardon me, madam, I have unadvised Delivered you a paper that I should not. This is the letter to your ladyship.
USER: I pray thee, let me look on that again.
BOT: It may not be. Good madam, pardon me.
USER: There, hold. I will not look upon your master's lines. I know they are stuffed with protestations And full of new-found oaths, which he will break As easily as I do tear his paper.
BOT: Madam, he sends your ladyship this ring.
USER: The more shame for him that he sends it me; For I have heard him say a thousand times His Julia gave it him at his departure. Though his false finger have profaned the ring, Mine shall not do his Julia so much wrong.
BOT: She thanks you.
USER: What sayst thou?
BOT: I thank you, madam, that you tender her. Poor gentlewoman, my master wrongs her much.
USER: Dost thou know her?
BOT: Almost as well as I do know myself. To think upon her woes, I do protest That I have wept a hundred several times.
USER: Belike she thinks that Proteus hath forsook her?
BOT: I think she doth, and that's her cause of sorrow.
USER: Is she not passing fair?
BOT: How tall was she?

USER: Amen, amen. Go on, good Eglamour, Out at the postern by the abbey wall. I fear I am attended by some spies.
BOT: Fear not. The forest is not three leagues off; If we recover that, we are sure enough.

USER: Sir Proteus, what says Silvia to my suit?
BOT: O, sir, I find her milder than she was, And yet she takes exceptions at your person.
USER: What? That my leg is too long?
BOT: No, that it is too little.
USER: I'll wear a boot to make it somewhat rounder.
BOT: What says she to my face?
USER: She says it is a fair one.
BOT: Nay, then, the wanton lies; my face is black.
USER: But pearls are fair; and the old saying is, "Black men are pearls in beauteous ladies' eyes."
BOT: For I had rather wink than look on them.
USER: How likes she my discourse?
BOT: Ill, when you talk of war.
USER: But well when I discourse of love and peace.
BOT: What says she to my valour?
USER: O, sir, she makes no doubt of that.
BOT: What says she to my birth?
USER: That you are well derived.
BOT: Considers she my possessions?
USER: O, ay, and pities them.
BOT: Wherefore?
USER: That they are out by lease.
BOT: Here comes the Duke.
USER: How now, Sir Proteus! How now, Thurio! Which of you saw Sir Eglamour of late?
BOT: Not I.
USER: Nor I.
BOT: Saw you my daughter?
USER: Neither.
BOT: Why, this it is to be a peevish girl That flies her fortune when it follows her. I'll after, more to be revenged on Eglamour Than for the love of reckless Silvia.
USER: And I will follow, more for Silvia's love Than hate of Eglamour that goes with her.
BOT: And I will follow, more to cross that love Than hate for Silvia, that is gone for love.

USER: Come, come, be patient. We must bring you to our captain.
BOT: A thousand more mischances than this one Have learned me how to brook this patiently.
USER: Come, bring her away.
BOT: Where is the gentleman that was with her?
USER: Being nimble-footed, he hath outrun us. But Moyses and Valerius follow him. Go thou with her to the west end of the wood; There is our captain. We'll follow him that's fled. The thicket is beset; he cannot 'scape.
BOT: Come, I must bring you to our captain's cave. Fear not; he bears an honourable mind And will not use a woman lawlessly.
USER: O Valentine, this I endure for thee!

USER: Love, lend me patience to forbear awhile.
BOT: O miserable, unhappy that I am!
USER: Unhappy were you, madam, ere I came; But by my coming I have made you happy.
BOT: By thy approach thou mak'st me most unhappy.
USER: What dangerous action, stood it next to death, Would I not undergo for one calm look! O, 'tis the curse in love, and still approved, When women cannot love where they're beloved.
BOT: In love Who respects friend?
USER: All men but Proteus.
BOT: Nay, if the gentle spirit of moving words Can no way change you to a milder form, I'll woo you like a soldier, at arms' end, And love you 'gainst the nature of love - force ye.
USER: O heaven!
BOT: I'll force thee yield to my desire.
USER: Thou friend of an ill fashion!
BOT: Valentine!
USER: My shame and guilt confounds me. Forgive me, Valentine; if hearty sorrow Be a sufficient ransom for offence, I tender 't here. I do as truly suffer As e'er I did commit.
BOT: O me unhappy!
USER: Look to the boy.
BOT: Why, boy! Why, wag! How now? What's the matter? Look up; speak.
USER: O good sir, my master charged me to deliver a ring to Madam Silvia, which out of my neglect was never done.
BOT: Where is that ring, boy?
USER: Here 'tis; this is it.
BOT: How, let me see. Why, this is the ring I gave to Julia.
USER: O, cry you mercy, sir, I have mistook. This is the ring you sent to Silvia.
BOT: But how cam'st thou by this ring? At my depart I gave this unto Julia.
USER: And Julia herself did give it me, And Julia herself have brought it hither.
BOT: How? Julia?
USER: Come, come, a hand from either. Let me be blest to make this happy close. 'Twere pity two such friends should be long foes.
BOT: Bear witness, heaven, I have my wish for ever.
USER: And I mine.
BOT: A prize, a prize, a prize!
USER: Forbear, forbear, I say! It is my lord the Duke. Your Grace is welcome to a man disgraced, Banished Valentine.
BOT: Sir Valentine!
USER: Yonder is Silvia, and Silvia's mine.
BOT: Sir Valentine, I care not for her, I. I hold him but a fool that will endanger His body for a girl that loves him not. I claim her not, and therefore she is thine.
USER: I thank your Grace; the gift hath made me happy. I now beseech you, for your daughter's sake, To grant one boon that I shall ask of you.
BOT: I grant it for thine own, whate'er it be.
USER: Thou hast prevailed; I pardon them and thee. Dispose of them as thou know'st their deserts. Come, let us go; we will include all jars With triumphs, mirth, and rare solemnity.
BOT: And as we walk along, I dare be bold With our discourse to make your Grace to smile. What think you of this page, my lord?
USER: I think the boy hath grace in him; he blushes.
BOT: I warrant you, my lord, more grace than boy.
USER: What mean you by that saying?

USER: For pity's sake and true gentility's, Hear and respect me.
BOT: For your mother's sake, And as you wish your womb may thrive with fair ones, Hear and respect me.
USER: Sad lady, rise.
BOT: Stand up.
USER: No knees to me. What woman I may stead that is distressed, Does bind me to her.
BOT: What's your request? Deliver you for all.
USER: O, I hope some god, Some god hath put his mercy in your manhood, Whereto he'll infuse power, and press you forth Our undertaker.
BOT: O, no knees, none, widow! Unto the helmeted Bellona use them, And pray for me, your soldier. Troubled I am.
USER: Poor lady, say no more. I had as lief trace this good action with you As that whereto I am going, and never yet Went I so willing way. My lord is taken Heart-deep with your distress. Let him consider; I'll speak anon.
BOT: O, my petition was Set down in ice, which by hot grief uncandied Melts into drops; so sorrow, wanting form, Is pressed with deeper matter.
USER: Pray, stand up; Your grief is written in your cheek.
BOT: Forward to th' temple! Leave not out a jot O' th' sacred ceremony.
USER: What griefs our beds, That our dear lords have none!
BOT: None fit for th' dead. Those that with cords, knives, drams, precipitance, Weary of this world's light, have to themselves Been death's most horrid agents, human grace Affords them dust and shadow.
USER: But our lords Lie blist'ring 'fore the visitating sun, And were good kings when living.
BOT: It is true, and I will give you comfort To give your dead lords graves; The which to do must make some work with Creon.
USER: Now you may take him, drunk with his victory.
BOT: And his army full of bread and sloth.
USER: Dowagers, take hands. Let us be widows to our woes; delay Commends us to a famishing hope.
BOT: Farewell!
USER: We come unseasonably; but when could grief Cull forth, as unpanged judgement can, fitt'st time For best solicitation?
BOT: Why, good ladies, This is a service, whereto I am going, Greater than any war; it more imports me Than all the actions that I have foregone, Or futurely can cope.
USER: Our cause cries for your knee.
BOT: My sister her petition in that force, With that celerity and nature, which She makes it in, from henceforth I'll not dare To ask you anything, nor be so hardy Ever to take a husband.
USER: Sir, I'll follow you at heels. The feast's solemnity Shall want till your return.
BOT: Cousin, I charge you, Budge not from Athens. We shall be returning Ere you can end this feast, of which I pray you Make no abatement. Once more, farewell all.
USER: Thus dost thou still make good the tongue o' th' world.
BOT: And earn'st a deity equal with Mars.
USER: If not above him, for Thou, being but mortal, mak'st affections bend To godlike honours; they themselves, some say, Groan under such a mast'ry.
BOT: As we are men, Thus should we do; being sensually subdued, We lose our human title. Good cheer, ladies. Now turn we towards your comforts.

USER: Are you not out? Meet you no ruin but the soldier in The cranks and turns of Thebes? You did begin As if you met decays of many kinds. Perceive you none that do arouse your pity But th' unconsidered soldier?
BOT: Yes, I pity Decays where'er I find them, but such most That, sweating in an honourable toil, Are paid with ice to cool 'em.
USER: Our uncle Creon.
BOT: Clear-spirited cousin, Let's leave his court, that we may nothing share Of his loud infamy; for our milk Will relish of the pasture, and we must Be vile or disobedient; not his kinsmen In blood unless in quality.
USER: Nothing truer. I think the echoes of his shames have deafed The ears of heavenly justice. Widows' cries Descend again into their throats and have not Due audience of the gods. Valerius!
BOT: The King calls for you; yet be leaden-footed Till his great rage be off him. Phœbus, when He broke his whipstock and exclaimed against The horses of the sun, but whispered to The loudness of his fury.
USER: Small winds shake him. But what's the matter?
BOT: Theseus, who where he threats appalls, hath sent Deadly defiance to him and pronounces Ruin to Thebes, who is at hand to seal The promise of his wrath.
USER: So we must. On fail of some condition?
BOT: 'Tis in motion; The intelligence of state came in the instant With the defier.
USER: Let th' event, That never-erring arbitrator, tell us When we know all ourselves; and let us follow The becking of our chance.

USER: No further.
BOT: Though I know His ocean needs not my poor drops, yet they Must yield their tribute there. My precious maid, Those best affections that the heavens infuse In their best-tempered pieces keep enthroned In your dear heart!
USER: Peace be to you As I pursue this war, which shall be then Beyond further requiring.
BOT: 'Twas Flavina.
USER: You're out of breath; And this high-speeded pace is but to say That you shall never, like the maid Flavina, Love any that's called man.
BOT: I am sure I shall not.
USER: I am not Against your faith, yet I continue mine.

USER: To thee no star be dark!
BOT: Both heaven and earth Friend thee for ever!
USER: All the good that may Be wished upon thy head, I cry "Amen" to 't!
BOT: Men of great quality, as may be judged By their appointment. Some of Thebes have told 's They are sisters' children, nephews to the King.
USER: Wi' leave, they're called Arcite and Palamon.
BOT: 'Tis right; those, those. They are not dead?
USER: Nor in a state of life. Had they been taken When their last hurts were given, 'twas possible They might have been recovered; yet they breathe And have the name of men.

USER: This funeral path brings to your household's grave. Joy seize on you again; peace sleep with him.
BOT: And this to yours.
USER: Yours this way. Heavens lend A thousand differing ways to one sure end.
BOT: This world's a city full of straying streets, And death's the market-place where each one meets.

USER: Sir, I demand no more than your own offer, and I will estate your daughter in what I have promised.
BOT: Well, we will talk more of this when the solemnity is past. But have you a full promise of her? When that shall be seen, I tender my consent.
USER: I have sir. Here she comes.
BOT: They are famed to be a pair of absolute men.
USER: By my troth, I think fame but stammers 'em; they stand a grise above the reach of report.
BOT: I heard them reported in the battle to be the only doers.
USER: Do they so?
BOT: I never saw 'em.
USER: The Duke himself came privately in the night, and so did they. What the reason of it is, I know not. Look, yonder they are; that's Arcite looks out.
BOT: No, sir, no, that's Palamon. Arcite is the lower of the twain; you may perceive a part of him.
USER: Go to, leave your pointing; they would not make us their object. Out of their sight.
BOT: It is a holiday to look on them. Lord, the difference of men!

USER: How do you, noble cousin?
BOT: How do you, sir?
USER: Why, strong enough to laugh at misery And bear the chance of war; yet we are prisoners I fear for ever, cousin.
BOT: I believe it, And to that destiny have patiently Laid up my hour to come.
USER: Shall we make worthy uses of this place That all men hate so much?
BOT: How, gentle cousin?
USER: I would hear you still.
BOT: Ye shall. Is there record of any two that loved Better than we do, Arcite?
USER: Sure, there cannot.
BOT: I do not think it possible our friendship Should ever leave us.
USER: Till our deaths it cannot; And after death our spirits shall be led To those that love eternally. Speak on, sir.
BOT: This garden has a world of pleasures in't. What flower is this?
USER: 'Tis called narcissus, madam.
BOT: That was a fair boy, certain, but a fool, To love himself. Were there not maids enough?
USER: Pray, forward.
BOT: Yes.
USER: Or were they all hard-hearted?
BOT: They could not be to one so fair.
USER: Thou wouldst not.
BOT: I think I should not, madam.
USER: That's a good wench. But take heed to your kindness, though.
BOT: Why, madam?
USER: Men are mad things.
BOT: Will ye go forward, cousin?
USER: Canst not thou work such flowers in silk, wench?
BOT: Yes.
USER: I'll have a gown full of 'em, and of these. This is a pretty colour; will 't not do Rarely upon a skirt, wench?
BOT: Dainty, madam.
USER: Cousin, cousin! How do you, sir? Why, Palamon!
BOT: Never till now I was in prison, Arcite.
USER: Why, what's the matter, man?
BOT: Behold, and wonder! By heaven, she is a goddess.
USER: Ha!
BOT: Do reverence. She is a goddess, Arcite.
USER: Of all flowers, Methinks a rose is best.
BOT: Why, gentle madam?
USER: Yet, good madam, Sometimes her modesty will blow so far She falls for 't. A maid, If she have any honour, would be loath To take example by her.
BOT: Thou art wanton.
USER: She is wondrous fair.
BOT: She is all the beauty extant.
USER: The sun grows high; let's walk in. Keep these flowers. We'll see how near art can come near their colours. I am wondrous merry-hearted. I could laugh now.
BOT: I could lie down, I am sure.
USER: And take one with you?
BOT: That's as we bargain, madam.
USER: Well, agree then.
BOT: What think you of this beauty?
USER: 'Tis a rare one.
BOT: Is't but a rare one?
USER: Yes, a matchless beauty.
BOT: Might not a man well lose himself, and love her?
USER: I cannot tell what you have done; I have, Beshrew mine eyes for't! Now I feel my shackles.
BOT: You love her, then?
USER: Who would not?
BOT: And desire her?
USER: Before my liberty.
BOT: I saw her first.
USER: That's nothing.
BOT: But it shall be.
USER: I saw her too.
BOT: Yes, but you must not love her.
USER: I will not, as you do, to worship her As she is heavenly and a blessed goddess. I love her as a woman, to enjoy her. So both may love.
BOT: You shall not love at all.
USER: Not love at all! Who shall deny me?
BOT: Have I called thee friend?
USER: Yes, and have found me so. Why are you moved thus? Let me deal coldly with you: am not I Part of your blood, part of your soul? You have told me That I was Palamon and you were Arcite.
BOT: Yes.
USER: Am not I liable to those affections, Those joys, griefs, angers, fears, my friend shall suffer?
BOT: Ye may be.
USER: Why then would you deal so cunningly, So strangely, so unlike a noble kinsman, To love alone? Speak truly; do you think me Unworthy of her sight?
BOT: No; but unjust, If thou pursue that sight.
USER: Because another First sees the enemy, shall I stand still And let mine honour down, and never charge?
BOT: Yes, if he be but one.
USER: But say that one Had rather combat me?
BOT: Let that one say so, And use thy freedom. Else, if thou pursuest her, Be as that cursed man that hates his country, A branded villain.
USER: You are mad.
BOT: I must be, Till thou art worthy, Arcite; it concerns me; And in this madness, if I hazard thee And take thy life, I deal but truely.
USER: Fie, sir! You play the child extremely. I will love her; I must, I ought to do so, and I dare, And all this justly.
BOT: Thou dar'st not, fool, thou canst not, thou art feeble. Put my head out? I'll throw my body out And leap the garden, when I see her next And pitch between her arms, to anger thee.
USER: No more; the keeper's coming. I shall live To knock thy brains out with my shackles.
BOT: Do!
USER: By your leave, gentlemen.
BOT: Now, honest keeper?
USER: Lord Arcite, you must presently to th' Duke; The cause I know not yet.
BOT: I am ready, keeper.
USER: Prince Palamon, I must awhile bereave you Of your fair cousin's company.
BOT: Banished. Prince Pirithous Obtained his liberty, but never more Upon his oath and life must he set foot Upon this kingdom.
USER: My lord for you I have this charge to -
BOT: To discharge my life?
USER: No, but from this place to remove your lordship; The windows are too open.
BOT: Devils take 'em, That are so envious to me! Prithee, kill me.
USER: And hang for't afterward!
BOT: By this good light, Had I a sword I would kill thee.
USER: Why, my Lord?
BOT: Thou bringst such pelting, scurvy news continually, Thou art not worthy life. I will not go.
USER: Indeed, you must, my lord.
BOT: May I see the garden?
USER: No.
BOT: Then I am resolved, I will not go.
USER: I must constrain you then; and, for you are dangerous, I'll clap more irons on you.
BOT: Do, good keeper. I'll shake 'em so, ye shall not sleep; I'll make you a new morris. Must I go?
USER: There is no remedy.
BOT: Farewell, kind window. May rude wind never hurt thee! - O, my lady, If ever thou hast felt what sorrow was, Dream how I suffer. - Come, now bury me.

USER: My masters, I'll be there, that's certain.
BOT: And I'll be there.
USER: And I.
BOT: Why, then, have with you, boys. 'Tis but a chiding. Let the plough play today; I'll tickle 't out Of the jades' tails tomorrow.
USER: I am sure To have my wife as jealous as a turkey, But that's all one. I'll go through; let her mumble.
BOT: Clap her aboard tomorrow night, and stow her, And all's made up again.
USER: Ay, do but put A fescue in her fist and you shall see her Take a new lesson out and be a good wench. Do we all hold against the Maying?
BOT: Hold? What should ail us?
USER: Arcas will be there.
BOT: And Sennois. And Rycas; and three better lads ne'er danced Under green tree. And ye know what wenches, ha? But will the dainty domine, the schoolmaster, Keep touch, do you think? For he does all, ye know.
USER: He'll eat a hornbook ere he fail. Go to; The matter's too far driven between him And the tanner's daughter to let slip now; And she must see the Duke, and she must dance too.
BOT: Shall we be lusty?
USER: All the boys in Athens Blow wind i' th' breech on 's. And here I'll be, And there I'll be, for our town, and here again, And there again. Ha, boys, hey for the weavers!
BOT: This must be done i' th' woods.
USER: O, pardon me.
BOT: By any means; our thing of learning says so - Where he himself will edify the Duke Most parlously in our behalfs. He's excellent i' th' woods; Bring him to th' plains, his learning makes no cry.
USER: We'll see the sports, then every man to 's tackle; And, sweet companions, let's rehearse, by any means, Before the ladies see us, and do sweetly, And God knows what may come on 't.
BOT: Content; the sports once ended, we'll perform. Away, boys, and hold.
USER: By your leaves, honest friends: pray you, whither go you?
BOT: Whither? Why, what a question's that?
USER: Yes, 'tis a question To me that know not.
BOT: To the games, my friend.
USER: Where were you bred, you know it not?
BOT: Not far, sir; Are there such games today?
USER: Yes, marry, are there, And such as you never saw; the Duke himself Will be in person there.
BOT: What pastimes are they?
USER: Wrestling, and running. - 'Tis a pretty fellow.
BOT: Thou wilt not go along?
USER: Not yet, sir.
BOT: Well, sir, Take your own time. Come, boys.
USER: My mind misgives me, This fellow has a vengeance trick o' th' hip; Mark how his body's made for 't.
BOT: I'll be hanged, though, If he dare venture. Hang him, plum porridge! He wrestle? He roast eggs! Come, let's be gone, lads.

USER: You have done worthily. I have not seen, Since Hercules, a man of tougher sinews. Whate'er you are, you run the best and wrestle, That these times can allow.
BOT: I am proud to please you.
USER: What country bred you?
BOT: This; but far off, Prince.
USER: Are you a gentleman?
BOT: My father said so; And to those gentle uses gave me life.
USER: Are you his heir?
BOT: His youngest, sir.
USER: Your father Sure is a happy sire then. What profess you?
BOT: You are perfect.
USER: Upon my soul, a proper man.
BOT: He is so.
USER: How do you like him, lady?
BOT: I admire him. I have not seen so young a man so noble, If he say true, of his sort.
USER: Believe, His mother was a wondrous handsome woman; His face, methinks, goes that way.
BOT: But his body And fiery mind illustrate a brave father.
USER: Mark how his virtue, like a hidden sun, Breaks through his baser garments.
BOT: He's well got, sure.
USER: What made you seek this place, sir?
BOT: Noble Theseus, To purchase name and do my ablest service To such a well-found wonder as thy worth; For only in thy court, of all the world, Dwells fair-eyed Honour.
USER: All his words are worthy.
BOT: Sir, we are much indebted to your travel, Nor shall you lose your wish. - Pirithous, Dispose of this fair gentleman.
USER: Sir, you're a noble giver. - Dearest beauty, Thus let me seal my vowed faith. When your servant, Your most unworthy creature, but offends you, Command him die, he shall.
BOT: That were too cruel. If you deserve well, sir, I shall soon see 't. You're mine, and somewhat better than your rank I'll use you.
USER: I'll see you furnished, and because you say You are a horseman, I must needs entreat you This afternoon to ride, but 'tis a rough one.
BOT: I like him better, Prince; I shall not then Freeze in my saddle.
USER: That were a shame, sir, While I have horses. - Take your choice, and what You want at any time, let me but know it. If you serve faithfully, I dare assure you You'll find a loving mistress.
BOT: If I do not, Let me find that my father ever hated, Disgrace and blows.
USER: I hope too wise for that, sir.

USER: Dear cousin Palamon -
BOT: Cozener Arcite, give me language such As thou hast showed me feat.
USER: That thou durst, Arcite!
BOT: Kinsman, you might as well Speak this and act it in your glass as to His ear which now disdains you.
USER: Oh you heavens, dares any So noble bear a guilty business? None But only Arcite, therefore none but Arcite In this kind is so bold.
BOT: Sweet Palamon.
USER: I do embrace you and your offer; for Your offer do 't I only, sir; your person, Without hypocrisy I may not wish More than my sword's edge on 't.
BOT: You hear the horns. Be crossed ere met. Give me your hand; farewell. I'll bring you every needful thing. I pray you, Take comfort and be strong.
USER: Plainly spoken. Yet pardon me hard language. When I spur My horse, I chide him not; content and anger In me have but one face. Hark, sir, they call The scattered to the banquet. You must guess I have an office there.
BOT: Sir, your attendance Cannot please heaven, and I know your office Unjustly is achieved.
USER: 'Tis a good title. I am persuaded, this question, sick between 's, By bleeding must be cured. I am a suitor That to your sword you will bequeath this plea, And talk of it no more.
BOT: But this one word: You are going now to gaze upon my mistress, For, note you, mine she is -
USER: Nay, then -
BOT: Nay, pray you, You talk of feeding me to breed me strength. You are going now to look upon a sun That strengthens what it looks on; there You have a vantage o'er me. But enjoy 't till I may enforce my remedy. Farewell.

USER: I should be near the place. - Ho! Cousin Palamon!
BOT: The same. I have brought you food and files. Come forth and fear not; here's no Theseus.
USER: Nor none so honest, Arcite.
BOT: That's no matter. We'll argue that hereafter. Come, take courage; You shall not die thus beastly. Here, sir, drink - I know you are faint - then I'll talk further with you.
USER: Arcite, thou mightst now poison me.
BOT: I might; But I must fear you first. Sit down and, good now, No more of these vain parleys; let us not, Having our ancient reputation with us, Make talk for fools and cowards. To your health.
USER: Do.
BOT: Pray sit down, then, and let me entreat you, By all the honesty and honour in you, No mention of this woman; 'twill disturb us. We shall have time enough.
USER: Well, sir, I'll pledge you.
BOT: Drink a good hearty draught; it breeds good blood, man. Do not you feel it thaw you?
USER: Stay, I'll tell you After a draught or two more.
BOT: Spare it not; the Duke has more, coz. Eat now.
USER: Yes.
BOT: I am glad you have so good a stomach.
USER: I am gladder I have so good meat to 't.
BOT: Is't not mad lodging, Here in the wild woods, cousin?
USER: Yes, for them That have wild consciences.
BOT: How tastes your victuals? Your hunger needs no sauce, I see.
USER: Not much. But if it did, yours is too tart, sweet cousin. What is this?
BOT: Venison.
USER: 'Tis a lusty meat. Give me more wine. Here, Arcite, to the wenches We have known in our days! The Lord Steward's daughter, Do you remember her?
BOT: After you, coz.
USER: She loved a black-haired man.
BOT: She did so; well, sir?
USER: And I have heard some call him Arcite, and -
BOT: Out with't, faith.
USER: She met him in an arbour. What did she there, coz? Play o' th' virginals?
BOT: Something she did, sir.
USER: Made her groan a month for 't, Or two, or three, or ten.
BOT: The Marshal's sister Had her share too, as I remember, cousin, Else there be tales abroad. You'll pledge her?
USER: Yes.
BOT: A pretty brown wench 'tis. There was a time When young men went a-hunting, and a wood, And a broad beech; and thereby hangs a tale. Heigh ho!
USER: For Emily, upon my life! Fool, Away with this strained mirth! I say again That sigh was breathed for Emily. Base cousin, Dar'st thou break first?
BOT: You are wide.
USER: By heaven and earth, There's nothing in thee honest.
BOT: Then I'll leave you. You are a beast now.
USER: As thou mak'st me, traitor.
BOT: There's all things needful: files and shirts and perfumes. I'll come again some two hours hence, and bring That that shall quiet all.
USER: A sword and armour?
BOT: Fear me not. You are now too foul. Farewell. Get off your trinkets; you shall want naught.
USER: Sirrah -
BOT: I'll hear no more.
USER: If he keep touch, he dies for 't.

USER: And sweetly we will do it, Master Gerald.
BOT: Draw up the company. Where's the taborer?
USER: Why, Timothy!
BOT: Here, my mad boys, have at ye.
USER: But I say, where's their women?
BOT: Here's Friz and Maudlin.
USER: And little Luce with the white legs, and bouncing Barbary.
BOT: And freckled Nel, that never failed her master.
USER: Where be your ribbons, maids? Swim with your bodies, And carry it sweetly and deliverly, And now and then a favour and a frisk.
BOT: Let us alone, sir.
USER: Where's the rest o' th' music?
BOT: Dispersed, as you commanded.
USER: Couple, then, And see what's wanting. Where's the Bavian? My friend, carry your tail without offence Or scandal to the ladies; and be sure You tumble with audacity and manhood; And when you bark, do it with judgement.
BOT: Yes, sir.
USER: _Quo usque tandem?_ Here is a woman wanting.
BOT: We may go whistle; all the fat's i' th' fire.
USER: We have, as learned authors utter, washed a tile. we have been _fatuus_ and laboured vainly.
BOT: An eel and woman, A learned poet says, unless by th' tail And with thy teeth thou hold, will either fail. In manners this was false position.
USER: A fire ill take her; does she flinch now?
BOT: What Shall we determine, sir?
USER: Nothing. Our business is become a nullity, Yea, and a woeful and a piteous nullity.
BOT: Now, when the credit of our town lay on it, Now to be frampul, now to piss o' th' nettle! Go thy ways; I'll remember thee. I'll fit thee.
USER: There's a dainty mad woman, Master, Comes i' th' nick, as mad as a March hare. If we can get her dance, we are made again; I warrant her, she'll do the rarest gambols.
BOT: A madwoman? We are made, boys.
USER: And are you mad, good woman?
BOT: I would be sorry else. Give me your hand.
USER: Why?
BOT: _Dii boni!_ A tinker, damsel?
USER: Or a conjurer. Raise me a devil now, and let him play _Qui passa_ o' th' bells and bones.
BOT: Go, take her, And fluently persuade her to a peace. _Et opus exegi, quod nec Jovis ira, nec ignis - _ Strike up, and lead her in.
USER: Come, lass, let's trip it.
BOT: I'll lead.
USER: Do, do!
BOT: Persuasively, and cunningly. Away, boys; I hear the horns. Give me some meditation, And mark your cue. Pallas inspire me.
USER: This way the stag took.
BOT: Stay, and edify!
USER: What have we here?
BOT: Some country sport, upon my life, sir.
USER: Well, sir, go forward; we will "edify." Ladies, sit down. We'll stay it.
BOT: Thou doughty Duke, all hail! All hail, sweet ladies!
USER: This is a cold beginning.
BOT: Ay, ay, by any means, dear _Domine_.
USER: Produce.
BOT: Take twenty, _Domine_. - How does my sweetheart?
USER: Never so pleased, sir.
BOT: 'Twas an excellent dance, And, for a preface, I never heard a better.
USER: Schoolmaster, I thank you. - One see'em all rewarded.
BOT: And here's something to paint your pole withal.
USER: Now to our sports again.

USER: Good morrow, noble kinsman.
BOT: I have put you To too much pains, sir.
USER: That too much, fair cousin, Is but a debt to honour, and my duty.
BOT: Would you were so in all, sir; I could wish ye As kind a kinsman as you force me find A beneficial foe, that my embraces Might thank ye, not my blows.
USER: I shall think either, Well done, a noble recompence.
BOT: Then I shall quit you.
USER: Arcite, thou art so brave an enemy, That no man but thy cousin's fit to kill thee. I am well and lusty; choose your arms.
BOT: Choose you, sir.
USER: Wilt thou exceed in all, or dost thou do it To make me spare thee?
BOT: If you think so, cousin, You are deceived, for as I am a soldier, I will not spare you.
USER: That's well said.
BOT: You'll find it.
USER: Then, as I am an honest man and love With all the justice of affection, I'll pay thee soundly. This I'll take.
BOT: That's mine, then. I'll arm you first.
USER: Do. Pray thee, tell me, cousin, Where got'st thou this good armour?
BOT: 'Tis the Duke's, And, to say true, I stole it. Do I pinch you?
USER: No.
BOT: Is't not too heavy?
USER: I have worn a lighter, But I shall make it serve.
BOT: I'll buckle 't close.
USER: By any means.
BOT: You care not for a grand guard?
USER: No, no; we'll use no horses: I perceive You would fain be at that fight.
BOT: I am indifferent.
USER: Faith, so am I. Good cousin, thrust the buckle Through far enough.
BOT: I warrant you.
USER: My casque now.
BOT: Will you fight bare-armed?
USER: We shall be the nimbler.
BOT: But use your gauntlets though. Those are o' th' least; Prithee take mine, good cousin.
USER: Thank you, Arcite. How do I look? Am I fall'n much away?
BOT: Faith, very little; love has used you kindly.
USER: I'll warrant thee, I'll strike home.
BOT: Do, and spare not. I'll give you cause, sweet cousin.
USER: Now to you, sir. Methinks this armour's very like that, Arcite, Thou wor'st that day the three kings fell, but lighter.
BOT: That was a very good one; and that day, I well remember, you outdid me, cousin; I never saw such valour. When you charged Upon the left wing of the enemy, I spurred hard to come up, and under me I had a right good horse.
USER: You had indeed; A bright bay, I remember.
BOT: Yes, but all Was vainly laboured in me; you outwent me, Nor could my wishes reach you. Yet a little I did by imitation.
USER: More by virtue; You are modest, cousin.
BOT: When I saw you charge first, Me thought I heard a dreadful clap of thunder Break from the troop.
USER: But still before that flew The lightning of your valour. Stay a little; Is not this piece too strait?
BOT: No, no, 'tis well.
USER: I would have nothing hurt thee but my sword. A bruise would be dishonour.
BOT: Now I am perfect.
USER: Stand off, then.
BOT: Take my sword; I hold it better.
USER: I thank ye, no; keep it; your life lies on it. Here's one; if it but hold, I ask no more For all my hopes. My cause and honour guard me!
BOT: And me my love! Is there aught else to say?
USER: Here, Palamon. This hand shall never more Come near thee with such friendship.
BOT: I commend thee.
USER: If I fall, curse me, and say I was a coward, For none but such dare die in these just trials. Once more farewell, my cousin.
BOT: Farewell, Arcite.
USER: Lo, cousin, lo, our folly has undone us.
BOT: Why?
USER: No, no, cousin, I will no more be hidden, nor put off This great adventure to a second trial; I know your cunning and I know your cause. He that faints now, shame take him! Put thyself Upon thy present guard -
BOT: You are not mad?
USER: Or I will make th'advantage of this hour Mine own, and what to come shall threaten me I fear less than my fortune. Know, weak cousin, I love Emilia, and in that I'll bury Thee, and all crosses else.
BOT: Then, come what can come, Thou shalt know, Palamon, I dare as well Die, as discourse, or sleep. Only this fears me, The law will have the honour of our ends. Have at thy life!
USER: Look to thine own well, Arcite.
BOT: What ignorant and mad malicious traitors Are you, that 'gainst the tenor of my laws Are making battle, thus like knights appointed, Without my leave, and officers of arms? By Castor, both shall die.
USER: O heaven, What more than man is this!
BOT: I have sworn.
USER: Alas the pity! Now or never, sister, Speak, not to be denied. That face of yours Will bear the curses else of after ages For these lost cousins.
BOT: By your own spotless honour -
USER: By that faith, That fair hand, and that honest heart you gave me -
BOT: By that you would have pity in another, By your own virtues infinite -
USER: By valour, By all the chaste nights I have ever pleased you -
BOT: These are strange conjurings.
USER: Nay, then, I'll in too. By all our friendship, sir, by all our dangers, By all you love most: wars and this sweet lady -
BOT: By that you would have trembled to deny A blushing maid -
USER: By your own eyes, by strength, In which you swore I went beyond all women, Almost all men, and yet I yielded, Theseus -
BOT: To crown all this, by your most noble soul, Which cannot want due mercy, I beg first.
USER: Next, hear my prayers.
BOT: Last, let me entreat, sir.
USER: For mercy.
BOT: Mercy.
USER: Mercy on these princes.
BOT: Ye make my faith reel. Say I felt Compassion to'em both, how would you place it?
USER: Upon their lives. But with their banishments.
BOT: What is it, sister?
USER: Urge it home, brave lady.
BOT: On what conditions?
USER: Swear 'em never more To make me their contention, or to know me, To tread upon thy dukedom, and to be, Wherever they shall travel, ever strangers To one another.
BOT: Will you, Arcite, Take these conditions?
USER: He's a villain, then.
BOT: These are men!
USER: No, never, Duke. 'Tis worse to me than begging To take my life so basely. Though I think I never shall enjoy her, yet I'll preserve The honour of affection, and die for her, Make death a devil.
BOT: What may be done? For now I feel compassion.
USER: Let it not fall again, sir.
BOT: With all our souls.
USER: He that she refuses Must die, then.
BOT: Any death thou canst invent, Duke.
USER: If I fall from that mouth, I fall with favour, And lovers yet unborn shall bless my ashes.
BOT: If she refuse me, yet my grave will wed me, And soldiers sing my epitaph.
USER: Make choice, then.
BOT: I cannot, sir, they are both too excellent; For me, a hair shall never fall of these men.
USER: What will become of 'em?
BOT: Yes. Here, cousin Arcite, I am friends again, till that hour.
USER: I embrace ye.
BOT: Are you content, sister?
USER: Yes, I must, sir, Else both miscarry.
BOT: Come, shake hands again, then; And take heed, as you are gentlemen, this quarrel Sleep till the hour prefixed, and hold your course.
USER: We dare not fail thee, Theseus.
BOT: Come, I'll give ye Now usage like to princes, and to friends. When ye return, who wins, I'll settle here; Who loses, yet I'll weep upon his bier.

USER: Hear you no more? Was nothing said of me Concerning the escape of Palamon? Good sir, remember.
BOT: Pray heaven it hold so.
USER: Be of good comfort, man; I bring you news, Good news.
BOT: They are welcome.
USER: You are a good man And ever bring good news.
BOT: How was it ended?
USER: Why, as it should be; they that never begged But they prevailed had their suits fairly granted; The prisoners have their lives.
BOT: I knew 'twould be so.
USER: But there be new conditions, which you'll hear of At better time.
BOT: I hope they are good.
USER: They are honourable; How good they'll prove, I know not.
BOT: 'Twill be known.
USER: Alas, sir, where's your daughter?
BOT: Why do you ask?
USER: O, sir, when did you see her?
BOT: How he looks?
USER: This morning.
BOT: Was she well? Was she in health, sir? When did she sleep?
USER: These are strange questions.
BOT: Nothing but my pity. But you must know it, and as good by me As by another that less loves her.
USER: Well, sir?
BOT: Not right?
USER: Not well?
BOT: No, sir, not well: 'Tis too true, she is mad.
USER: It cannot be.
BOT: Believe, you'll find it so.
USER: I half suspected What you have told me. The gods comfort her! Either this was her love to Palamon, Or fear of my miscarrying on his 'scape, Or both.
BOT: 'Tis likely.
USER: But why all this haste, sir?
BOT: Pray, go on, sir.
USER: She sung much, but no sense; only I heard her Repeat this often: "Palamon is gone, Is gone to th' wood to gather mulberries; I'll find him out tomorrow."
BOT: Pretty soul!
USER: Alas, what pity it is!
BOT: _May you never more enjoy the light, &c._ Is not this a fine song?
USER: O, a very fine one.
BOT: I can sing twenty more.
USER: I think you can.
BOT: Yes, truly can I. I can sing "The Broom" and "Bonny Robin." Are not you a tailor?
USER: Yes.
BOT: Where's my wedding gown?
USER: I'll bring it tomorrow.
BOT: Do, very rarely, I must be abroad else To call the maids and pay the minstrels, For I must lose my maidenhead by cocklight. 'Twill never thrive else.
USER: 'Tis true.
BOT: Good ev'n, good men; pray, did you ever hear Of one young Palamon?
USER: Yes, wench, we know him.
BOT: Is't not a fine young gentleman?
USER: 'Tis, love.
BOT: By no means cross her; she is then distempered Far worse than now she shows.
USER: Yes, he's a fine man.
BOT: O, is he so? You have a sister?
USER: Yes.
BOT: Yes.
USER: This is strange.
BOT: As ever you heard, but say nothing.
USER: No.
BOT: They come from all parts of the dukedom to him. I'll warrant ye, he had not so few last night As twenty to dispatch. He'll tickle 't up In two hours, if his hand be in.
USER: She's lost Past all cure.
BOT: Heaven forbid, man!
USER: Come hither, you are a wise man.
BOT: You are master of a ship?
USER: Yes.
BOT: Where's your compass?
USER: Here.
BOT: Set it to th' north. And now direct your course to th' wood, where Palamon Lies longing for me. For the tackling, Let me alone. Come, weigh, my hearts, cheerly.
USER: Owgh, owgh, owgh! 'Tis up, the wind's fair! Top the bowline; out with the mainsail; Where's your whistle, master?
BOT: Let's get her in.
USER: Up to the top, boy.
BOT: Where's the pilot?
USER: Here.
BOT: What kenn'st thou?
USER: A fair wood.
BOT: Bear for it, master. Tack about! _When Cinthia with her borrowed light, &c._

USER: How now, sir?
BOT: From the noble Duke your brother, Madam, I bring you news. The knights are come.
USER: To end the quarrel?
BOT: Yes.
USER: Bring 'em in Quickly, by any means; I long to see 'em. Your two contending lovers are returned, And with them their fair knights. Now, my fair sister, You must love one of them.
BOT: I had rather both, So neither for my sake should fall untimely.
USER: Who saw 'em?
BOT: I a while.
USER: And I.
BOT: From whence come you, sir?
USER: From the knights.
BOT: Pray, speak, You that have seen them, what they are.
USER: Thou hast well described him.
BOT: Yet a great deal short, Methinks, of him that's first with Palamon.
USER: Pray, speak him, friend.
BOT: Must these men die too?
USER: When he speaks, his tongue Sounds like a trumpet. All his lineaments Are as a man would wish 'em, strong and clean. He wears a well-steeled axe, the staff of gold; His age some five-and-twenty.
BOT: There's another, A little man, but of a tough soul, seeming As great as any; fairer promises In such a body yet I never looked on.
USER: O, he that's freckle-faced?
BOT: The same, my lord; Are they not sweet ones?
USER: Yes, they are well.
BOT: Are they all thus?
USER: They are all the sons of honour.
BOT: Now, as I have a soul, I long to see'em. Lady, you shall see men fight now.
USER: You have steeled 'em with your beauty. Honoured friend, To you I give the field; pray order it Fitting the persons that must use it.
BOT: Yes, sir.
USER: Come, I'll go visit 'em. I cannot stay, Their fame has fired me so; till they appear. Good friend, be royal.
BOT: There shall want no bravery.
USER: Poor wench, go weep, for whosoever wins, Loses a noble cousin for thy sins.

USER: Her distraction is more at some time of the moon, than at other some, is it not?
BOT: What stuff's here? Poor soul!
USER: Even thus all day long.
BOT: How prettily she's amiss! Note her a little further.
USER: How her brain coins!
BOT: How she continues this fancy! 'Tis not an engraffed madness, but a most thick, and profound melancholy.
USER: What think you of her, sir?
BOT: I think she has a perturbed mind, which I cannot minister to.
USER: Alas, what then?
BOT: Understand you she ever affected any man ere she beheld Palamon?
USER: I was once, sir, in great hope she had fixed her liking on this gentleman, my friend.
BOT: I did think so too, and would account I had a great penn'orth on't, to give half my state, that both she and I at this present stood unfeignedly on the same terms.

USER: Sir, they enter.
BOT: Honour crown the worthiest.
USER: You speak well. Before I turn, let me embrace thee, cousin. This I shall never do again.
BOT: One farewell.
USER: Why, let it be so. Farewell, coz.

USER: Has this advice I told you, done any good upon her?
BOT: 'Twas well done. Twenty times had been far better, For there the cure lies mainly.
USER: Then she told me She would watch with me tonight, for well she knew What hour my fit would take me.
BOT: Let her do so, And when your fit comes, fit her home, and presently.
USER: She would have me sing.
BOT: You did so?
USER: No.
BOT: 'Twas very ill done, then; You should observe her every way.
USER: Alas, I have no voice, sir, to confirm her that way.
BOT: That's all one, if ye make a noise. If she entreat again, do anything. Lie with her, if she ask you.
USER: Hoa, there, doctor!
BOT: Yes, in the way of cure.
USER: But first, by your leave, I' th' way of honesty.
BOT: That's but a niceness, Ne'er cast your child away for honesty. Cure her first this way; then if she will be honest, She has the path before her.
USER: Thank ye, Doctor.
BOT: Pray, bring her in, And let's see how she is.
USER: I will, and tell her Her Palamon stays for her. But, Doctor, Methinks you are i' th' wrong still.
BOT: Go, go; You fathers are fine fools. Her honesty? An we should give her physic till we find that!
USER: Why, do you think she is not honest, sir?
BOT: How old is she?
USER: She's eighteen.
BOT: She may be, But that's all one; 'tis nothing to our purpose. Whate'er her father says, if you perceive Her mood inclining that way that I spoke of, _Videlicet_, the way of flesh - you have me?
USER: Yes, very well, sir.
BOT: Please her appetite, And do it home; it cures her, _ipso facto_, The melancholy humour that infects her.
USER: I am of your mind, Doctor.
BOT: You'll find it so. She comes, pray, humour her.
USER: Come, your love Palamon stays for you, child, And has done this long hour, to visit you.
BOT: I thank him for his gentle patience; He's a kind gentleman, and I am much bound to him. Did you ne'er see the horse he gave me?
USER: Yes.
BOT: How do you like him?
USER: He's a very fair one.
BOT: You never saw him dance?
USER: No.
BOT: I have often. He dances very finely, very comely, And for a jig, come cut and long tail to him, He turns ye like a top.
USER: That's fine, indeed.
BOT: He'll dance the morris twenty mile an hour, And that will founder the best hobby-horse If I have any skill in all the parish, And gallops to the tune of "Light o' love." What think you of this horse?
USER: Having these virtues, I think he might be brought to play at tennis.
BOT: Alas, that's nothing.
USER: Can he write and read too?
BOT: A very fair hand, and casts himself th' accounts Of all his hay and provender. That hostler Must rise betime that cozens him. You know The chestnut mare the Duke has?
USER: Very well.
BOT: She is horribly in love with him, poor beast; But he is like his master, coy and scornful.
USER: What dowry has she?
BOT: Some two hundred bottles, And twenty strike of oates; but he'll ne'er have her. He lisps in's neighing, able to entice A miller's mare. He'll be the death of her.
USER: What stuff she utters!
BOT: Make curtsy; here your love comes.
USER: Pretty soul, How do ye? That's a fine maid; there's a curtsy!
BOT: Yours to command i' th' way of honesty. How far is't now to' th' end o' th' world, my masters?
USER: Why, a day's journey, wench.
BOT: Will you go with me?
USER: What shall we do there, wench?
BOT: Why, play at stool-ball; What is there else to do?
USER: I am content, If we shall keep our wedding there.
BOT: Do not you know me?
USER: Yes, but you care not for me. I have nothing But this poor petticoat, and two coarse smocks.
BOT: That's all one; I will have you.
USER: Will you surely?
BOT: We'll to bed, then.
USER: E'en when you will.
BOT: Why do you rub my kiss off?
USER: 'Tis a sweet one, And will perfume me finely against the wedding. Is not this your cousin Arcite?
BOT: Yes, sweetheart, And I am glad my cousin Palamon Has made so fair a choice.
USER: Do you think he'll have me?
BOT: Yes, without doubt.
USER: Do you think so too?
BOT: Yes.
USER: What do you here? You'll lose the noblest sight That e'er was seen.
BOT: Are they i' th' field?
USER: They are. You bear a charge there too.
BOT: I'll away straight. I must e'en leave you here.
USER: Nay, we'll go with you; I will not lose the sight.
BOT: How did you like her?
USER: I'll warrant you, within these three or four days I'll make her right again. You must not from her, But still preserve her in this way.
BOT: I will.
USER: Let's get her in.
BOT: Come, sweet, we'll go to dinner; And then we'll play at cards.
USER: And shall we kiss too?
BOT: A hundred times.
USER: And twenty.
BOT: Ay, and twenty.
USER: And then we'll sleep together.
BOT: Take her offer.
USER: Yes, marry, will we.
BOT: But you shall not hurt me.
USER: I will not, sweet.
BOT: If you do, love, I'll cry.

USER: I'll no step further.
BOT: Will you lose this sight?
USER: Sir, my good lord, Your sister will no further.
BOT: Pardon me; If I were there, I'd wink.
USER: You must be there; This trial is as 'twere i' th' night, and you The only star to shine.
BOT: You must go.
USER: In faith, I will not.
BOT: Why, the knights must kindle Their valour at your eye. Know, of this war You are the treasure, and must needs be by To give the service pay.
USER: Sir, pardon me; The title of a kingdom may be tried Out of itself.
BOT: Well, well, then, at your pleasure. Those that remain with you could wish their office To any of their enemies.
USER: Farewell, sister. I am like to know your husband 'fore yourself By some small start of time. He whom the gods Do of the two know best, I pray them he Be made your lot.
BOT: The cry's "À Palamon."
USER: Then he has won. 'Twas ever likely. He looked all grace and success, and he is Doubtless the prim'st of men. I prithee run And tell me how it goes.
BOT: Still "Palamon."
USER: Nay, now the sound is "Arcite."
BOT: I prithee, lay attention to the cry; Set both thine ears to th' business.
USER: The cry is "Arcite", and "Victory!" Hark, "Arcite, victory!" The combat's consummation is proclaimed By the wind instruments.
BOT: Emily, To buy you, I have lost what's dearest to me, Save what is bought; and yet I purchase cheaply, As I do rate your value.
USER: Infinite pity That four such eyes should be so fixed on one That two must needs be blind for 't.
BOT: So it is.

USER: What ending could be Of more content? O'er us the victors have Fortune, whose title is as momentary, As to us death is certain. A grain of honour They not o'erweigh us.
BOT: Let us bid farewell; And with our patience anger tottering Fortune, Who at her certain'st reels.
USER: Come; who begins?
BOT: Sir, she's well restored, And to be married shortly.
USER: By my short life, I am most glad on't. 'Tis the latest thing I shall be glad of; prithee, tell her so. Commend me to her, and, to piece her portion, Tender her this.
BOT: Nay let's be offerers all.
USER: Is it a maid?
BOT: Verily, I think so. A right good creature, more to me deserving Then I can 'quite or speak of.
USER: Commend us to her.
BOT: The gods requite you all, and make her thankful.
USER: Adieu; and let my life be now as short As my leave-taking.
BOT: Lead, courageous cousin.
USER: We'll follow cheerfully.
BOT: Hold, hold! O hold, hold, hold!
USER: Hold, ho! It is a cursed haste you made If you have done so quickly! - Noble Palamon, The gods will show their glory in a life That thou art yet to lead.
BOT: Can that be, When Venus, I have said, is false? How do things fare?
USER: Arise, great sir, and give the tidings ear That are most dearly sweet and bitter.
BOT: What Hath waked us from our dream?
USER: O miserable end of our alliance! The gods are mighty. Arcite, if thy heart, Thy worthy, manly heart, be yet unbroken, Give me thy last words. I am Palamon, One that yet loves thee dying.
BOT: Take Emilia And with her all the world's joy. Reach thy hand; Farewell. I have told my last hour. I was false, Yet never treacherous. Forgive me, cousin. One kiss from fair Emilia. 'Tis done. Take her. I die.
USER: Thy brave soul seek Elysium!
BOT: I'll close thine eyes, Prince; blessed souls be with thee! Thou art a right good man, and, while I live, This day I give to tears.
USER: And I to honour.
BOT: O cousin, That we should things desire, which do cost us The loss of our desire! That naught could buy Dear love, but loss of dear love!

USER: If you shall chance, Camillo, to visit Bohemia, on the like occasion whereon my services are now on foot, you shall see, as I have said, great difference betwixt our Bohemia and your Sicilia.
BOT: I think this coming summer the King of Sicilia means to pay Bohemia the visitation which he justly owes him.
USER: Wherein our entertainment shall shame us; we will be justified in our loves. For indeed, -
BOT: Beseech you -
USER: You pay a great deal too dear for what's given freely.
BOT: Believe me, I speak as my understanding instructs me and as mine honesty puts it to utterance.
USER: I think there is not in the world either malice or matter to alter it. You have an unspeakable comfort of your young Prince Mamillius. It is a gentleman of the greatest promise that ever came into my note.
BOT: I very well agree with you in the hopes of him. It is a gallant child; one that indeed physics the subject, makes old hearts fresh. They that went on crutches ere he was born desire yet their life to see him a man.
USER: Would they else be content to die?
BOT: Yes, if there were no other excuse why they should desire to live.
USER: If the king had no son, they would desire to live on crutches till he had one.

USER: Stay your thanks a while, And pay them when you part.
BOT: We are tougher, brother, Than you can put us to 't.
USER: No longer stay.
BOT: One seve'night longer.
USER: Very sooth, tomorrow.
BOT: We'll part the time between 's then: and in that I'll no gainsaying.
USER: Tongue-tied, our queen? Speak you.
BOT: Well said, Hermione.
USER: No, madam.
BOT: Nay, but you will?
USER: I may not, verily.
BOT: Your guest, then, madam. To be your prisoner should import offending; Which is for me less easy to commit Than you to punish.
USER: Not your gaoler then, But your kind hostess. Come, I'll question you Of my lord's tricks and yours when you were boys. You were pretty lordings then.
BOT: We were, fair queen, Two lads that thought there was no more behind But such a day tomorrow as today, And to be boy eternal.
USER: Was not my lord The verier wag o' th' two?
BOT: By this we gather You have tripp'd since.
USER: O my most sacred lady, Temptations have since then been born to 's! for In those unfledg'd days was my wife a girl; Your precious self had then not cross'd the eyes Of my young play-fellow.
BOT: Is he won yet?
USER: He'll stay, my lord.
BOT: At my request he would not. Hermione, my dearest, thou never spok'st To better purpose.
USER: Never?
BOT: Never but once.
USER: Why, that was when Three crabbed months had sour'd themselves to death, Ere I could make thee open thy white hand And clap thyself my love; then didst thou utter "I am yours for ever."
BOT: 'Tis Grace indeed. Why, lo you now, I have spoke to th' purpose twice. The one for ever earn'd a royal husband; Th' other for some while a friend.
USER: Ay, my good lord.
BOT: Yes, if you will, my lord.
USER: What means Sicilia?
BOT: He something seems unsettled.
USER: How, my lord? What cheer? How is't with you, best brother?
BOT: You look As if you held a brow of much distraction: Are you mov'd, my lord?
USER: No, my lord, I'll fight.
BOT: You will? Why, happy man be 's dole! My brother, Are you so fond of your young prince as we Do seem to be of ours?
USER: If you would seek us, We are yours i' the garden. Shall 's attend you there?
BOT: I am like you, they say.
USER: Why, that's some comfort. What! Camillo there?
BOT: Ay, my good lord.
USER: Go play, Mamillius; thou'rt an honest man. Camillo, this great sir will yet stay longer.
BOT: You had much ado to make his anchor hold: When you cast out, it still came home.
USER: Didst note it?
BOT: He would not stay at your petitions; made His business more material.
USER: Didst perceive it? "Sicilia is a so-forth." 'Tis far gone When I shall gust it last. - How came't, Camillo, That he did stay?
BOT: At the good queen's entreaty.
USER: Business, my lord? I think most understand Bohemia stays here longer.
BOT: Ha?
USER: Stays here longer.
BOT: Ay, but why?
USER: To satisfy your highness, and the entreaties Of our most gracious mistress.
BOT: Be it forbid, my lord!
USER: Good my lord, be cur'd Of this diseas'd opinion, and betimes, For 'tis most dangerous.
BOT: Say it be, 'tis true.
USER: No, no, my lord.
BOT: Who does infect her?
USER: Thou dost advise me Even so as I mine own course have set down: I'll give no blemish to her honour, none.
BOT: My lord, Go then; and with a countenance as clear As friendship wears at feasts, keep with Bohemia And with your queen. I am his cupbearer. If from me he have wholesome beverage, Account me not your servant.
USER: This is all: Do't, and thou hast the one half of my heart; Do't not, thou splitt'st thine own.
BOT: I'll do't, my lord.
USER: I will seem friendly, as thou hast advis'd me.
BOT: This is strange. Methinks My favour here begins to warp. Not speak? Good day, Camillo.
USER: Hail, most royal sir!
BOT: What is the news i' th' court?
USER: None rare, my lord.
BOT: I dare not know, my lord.
USER: There is a sickness Which puts some of us in distemper, but I cannot name the disease, and it is caught Of you that yet are well.
BOT: I may not answer.
USER: On, good Camillo.
BOT: I am appointed him to murder you.
USER: By whom, Camillo?
BOT: By the king.
USER: For what?
BOT: He thinks, nay, with all confidence he swears, As he had seen't or been an instrument To vice you to't, that you have touch'd his queen Forbiddenly.
USER: How should this grow?
BOT: It is in mine authority to command The keys of all the posterns: please your highness To take the urgent hour. Come, sir, away.

USER: Take the boy to you: he so troubles me, 'Tis past enduring.
BOT: Come, my gracious lord, Shall I be your playfellow?
USER: No, I'll none of you.
BOT: Why, my sweet lord?
USER: You'll kiss me hard, and speak to me as if I were a baby still. I love you better.
BOT: And why so, my lord?
USER: Not for because Your brows are blacker; yet black brows, they say, Become some women best, so that there be not Too much hair there, but in a semicircle Or a half-moon made with a pen.
BOT: Who taught this?
USER: I learn'd it out of women's faces. Pray now, What colour are your eyebrows?
BOT: Blue, my lord.
USER: Nay, that's a mock. I have seen a lady's nose That has been blue, but not her eyebrows.
BOT: Hark ye, The queen your mother rounds apace. We shall Present our services to a fine new prince One of these days, and then you'd wanton with us, If we would have you.
USER: She is spread of late Into a goodly bulk: good time encounter her!
BOT: What wisdom stirs amongst you? Come, sir, now I am for you again. Pray you sit by us, And tell 's a tale.
USER: Merry or sad shall't be?
BOT: As merry as you will.
USER: A sad tale's best for winter. I have one Of sprites and goblins.
BOT: Let's have that, good sir. Come on, sit down. Come on, and do your best To fright me with your sprites: you're powerful at it.
USER: There was a man, -
BOT: Nay, come, sit down, then on.
USER: Dwelt by a churchyard. I will tell it softly, Yond crickets shall not hear it.
BOT: Come on then, And give't me in mine ear.
USER: Was he met there? his train? Camillo with him?
BOT: Behind the tuft of pines I met them, never Saw I men scour so on their way: I ey'd them Even to their ships.
USER: By his great authority, Which often hath no less prevail'd than so On your command.
BOT: I know't too well. Give me the boy. I am glad you did not nurse him. Though he does bear some signs of me, yet you Have too much blood in him.
USER: What is this? sport?
BOT: Bear the boy hence, he shall not come about her, Away with him, and let her sport herself With that she's big with; for 'tis Polixenes Has made thee swell thus.
USER: But I'd say he had not, And I'll be sworn you would believe my saying, Howe'er you learn th' nayward.
BOT: Should a villain say so, The most replenish'd villain in the world, He were as much more villain: you, my lord, Do but mistake.
USER: No, by my life, Privy to none of this. How will this grieve you, When you shall come to clearer knowledge, that You thus have publish'd me! Gentle my lord, You scarce can right me throughly then, to say You did mistake.
BOT: No. If I mistake In those foundations which I build upon, The centre is not big enough to bear A school-boy's top. Away with her to prison! He who shall speak for her is afar off guilty But that he speaks.
USER: Shall I be heard?
BOT: Go, do our bidding. Hence!
USER: Beseech your highness, call the queen again.
BOT: Be certain what you do, sir, lest your justice Prove violence, in the which three great ones suffer, Yourself, your queen, your son.
USER: For her, my lord, I dare my life lay down, and will do't, sir, Please you to accept it, that the queen is spotless I' th' eyes of heaven and to you - I mean In this which you accuse her.
BOT: Hold your peaces.
USER: Good my lord, -
BOT: Cease; no more. You smell this business with a sense as cold As is a dead man's nose: but I do see't and feel't, As you feel doing thus; and see withal The instruments that feel.
USER: If it be so, We need no grave to bury honesty. There's not a grain of it the face to sweeten Of the whole dungy earth.
BOT: What! Lack I credit?
USER: I had rather you did lack than I, my lord, Upon this ground: and more it would content me To have her honour true than your suspicion, Be blam'd for't how you might.
BOT: And I wish, my liege, You had only in your silent judgement tried it, Without more overture.
USER: Well done, my lord.
BOT: If the good truth were known.

USER: The keeper of the prison, call to him; Let him have knowledge who I am. Good lady! No court in Europe is too good for thee; What dost thou then in prison? Now, good sir, You know me, do you not?
BOT: For a worthy lady And one who much I honour.
USER: Pray you then, Conduct me to the queen.
BOT: I may not, madam. To the contrary I have express commandment.
USER: Here's ado, to lock up honesty and honour from Th' access of gentle visitors! Is't lawful, pray you, To see her women? any of them? Emilia?
BOT: So please you, madam, To put apart these your attendants, I Shall bring Emilia forth.
USER: I pray now, call her. Withdraw yourselves.
BOT: And, madam, I must be present at your conference.
USER: Well, be't so, prithee. Here's such ado to make no stain a stain As passes colouring. Dear gentlewoman, How fares our gracious lady?
BOT: As well as one so great and so forlorn May hold together: on her frights and griefs, (Which never tender lady hath borne greater) She is, something before her time, deliver'd.
USER: A boy?
BOT: A daughter; and a goodly babe, Lusty, and like to live: the queen receives Much comfort in 't; says "My poor prisoner, I am as innocent as you."
USER: Tell her, Emilia, I'll use that tongue I have: if wit flow from 't As boldness from my bosom, let't not be doubted I shall do good.
BOT: Now be you blest for it! I'll to the queen: please you come something nearer.
USER: Madam, if 't please the queen to send the babe, I know not what I shall incur to pass it, Having no warrant.
BOT: I do believe it.
USER: Do not you fear: upon mine honour, I Will stand betwixt you and danger.

USER: My lord.
BOT: How does the boy?
USER: He took good rest tonight; 'Tis hop'd his sickness is discharg'd.
BOT: You must not enter.
USER: Nay, rather, good my lords, be second to me: Fear you his tyrannous passion more, alas, Than the queen's life? a gracious innocent soul, More free than he is jealous.
BOT: That's enough.
USER: Madam, he hath not slept tonight; commanded None should come at him.
BOT: What noise there, ho?
USER: No noise, my lord; but needful conference About some gossips for your highness.
BOT: How! Away with that audacious lady! Antigonus, I charg'd thee that she should not come about me. I knew she would.
USER: I told her so, my lord, On your displeasure's peril and on mine, She should not visit you.
BOT: What, canst not rule her?
USER: From all dishonesty he can. In this, Unless he take the course that you have done, Commit me for committing honour - trust it, He shall not rule me.
BOT: La you now, you hear. When she will take the rein I let her run; But she'll not stumble.
USER: Good queen!
BOT: Good queen, my lord, good queen: I say, good queen, And would by combat make her good, so were I A man, the worst about you.
USER: Force her hence.
BOT: Out! A mankind witch! Hence with her, out o' door: A most intelligencing bawd!
USER: Not so. I am as ignorant in that as you In so entitling me; and no less honest Than you are mad; which is enough, I'll warrant, As this world goes, to pass for honest.
BOT: Traitors! Will you not push her out? [_To Antigonus._] Give her the bastard, Thou dotard! Thou art woman-tir'd, unroosted By thy Dame Partlet here. Take up the bastard, Take't up, I say; give't to thy crone.
USER: For ever Unvenerable be thy hands, if thou Tak'st up the princess by that forced baseness Which he has put upon 't!
BOT: He dreads his wife.
USER: So I would you did; then 'twere past all doubt You'd call your children yours.
BOT: A nest of traitors!
USER: I am none, by this good light.
BOT: A callat Of boundless tongue, who late hath beat her husband, And now baits me! This brat is none of mine; It is the issue of Polixenes. Hence with it, and together with the dam Commit them to the fire.
USER: A gross hag! And, losel, thou art worthy to be hang'd That wilt not stay her tongue.
BOT: Hang all the husbands That cannot do that feat, you'll leave yourself Hardly one subject.
USER: Once more, take her hence.
BOT: A most unworthy and unnatural lord Can do no more.
USER: I'll have thee burnt.
BOT: On your allegiance, Out of the chamber with her! Were I a tyrant, Where were her life? She durst not call me so, If she did know me one. Away with her!
USER: I did not, sir: These lords, my noble fellows, if they please, Can clear me in 't. LORDS We can: my royal liege, He is not guilty of her coming hither.
BOT: You're liars all.
USER: Anything, my lord, That my ability may undergo, And nobleness impose: at least thus much: I'll pawn the little blood which I have left To save the innocent. Anything possible.
BOT: It shall be possible. Swear by this sword Thou wilt perform my bidding.
USER: I will, my lord.
BOT: No, I'll not rear Another's issue.
USER: Please your highness, posts From those you sent to th' oracle are come An hour since: Cleomenes and Dion, Being well arriv'd from Delphos, are both landed, Hasting to th' court.
BOT: So please you, sir, their speed Hath been beyond account.

USER: It is his highness' pleasure that the queen Appear in person here in court. Silence! Hermione is brought in guarded; Paulina and Ladies attending.
BOT: Read the indictment.
USER: I ne'er heard yet That any of these bolder vices wanted Less impudence to gainsay what they did Than to perform it first.
BOT: That's true enough; Though 'tis a saying, sir, not due to me.
USER: You will not own it.
BOT: You knew of his departure, as you know What you have underta'en to do in 's absence.
USER: Sir, You speak a language that I understand not: My life stands in the level of your dreams, Which I'll lay down.
BOT: This your request Is altogether just: therefore bring forth, And in Apollo's name, his oracle:
USER: The Emperor of Russia was my father. O that he were alive, and here beholding His daughter's trial! that he did but see The flatness of my misery; yet with eyes Of pity, not revenge!
BOT: Break up the seals and read.
USER: subject; Leontes a jealous tyrant; his innocent babe truly begotten; and the king shall live without an heir, if that which is lost be not found." LORDS Now blessed be the great Apollo!
BOT: Praised!
USER: Hast thou read truth?
BOT: Ay, my lord, even so As it is here set down.
USER: There is no truth at all i' th' oracle: The sessions shall proceed: this is mere falsehood.
BOT: My lord the king, the king!
USER: What is the business?
BOT: O sir, I shall be hated to report it. The prince your son, with mere conceit and fear Of the queen's speed, is gone.
USER: How! gone?
BOT: Is dead.
USER: Apollo's angry, and the heavens themselves Do strike at my injustice. How now there?
BOT: This news is mortal to the queen. Look down And see what death is doing.
USER: Woe the while! O, cut my lace, lest my heart, cracking it, Break too!
BOT: What fit is this, good lady?
USER: The higher powers forbid!
BOT: Go on, go on: Thou canst not speak too much; I have deserv'd All tongues to talk their bitterest.
USER: Say no more: Howe'er the business goes, you have made fault I' th' boldness of your speech.

USER: Thou art perfect, then, our ship hath touch'd upon The deserts of Bohemia?
