BOT: Ay, my lord, and fear We have landed in ill time: the skies look grimly, And threaten present blusters. In my conscience, The heavens with that we have in hand are angry, And frown upon 's.
USER: Their sacred wills be done! Go, get aboard; Look to thy bark: I'll not be long before I call upon thee.
BOT: Make your best haste, and go not Too far i' th' land: 'tis like to be loud weather; Besides, this place is famous for the creatures Of prey that keep upon 't.
USER: Go thou away: I'll follow instantly.
BOT: I am glad at heart To be so rid o' th' business.
USER: Hilloa, loa!
BOT: What, art so near? If thou'lt see a thing to talk on when thou art dead and rotten, come hither. What ail'st thou, man?
USER: I have seen two such sights, by sea and by land! But I am not to say it is a sea, for it is now the sky: betwixt the firmament and it, you cannot thrust a bodkin's point.
BOT: Why, boy, how is it?
USER: Name of mercy, when was this, boy?
BOT: Now, now. I have not winked since I saw these sights: the men are not yet cold under water, nor the bear half dined on the gentleman. He's at it now.
USER: Would I had been by to have helped the old man!
BOT: I would you had been by the ship side, to have helped her: there your charity would have lacked footing.
USER: You're a made old man. If the sins of your youth are forgiven you, you're well to live. Gold! all gold!
BOT: This is fairy gold, boy, and 'twill prove so. Up with it, keep it close: home, home, the next way. We are lucky, boy, and to be so still requires nothing but secrecy. Let my sheep go: come, good boy, the next way home.
USER: Go you the next way with your findings. I'll go see if the bear be gone from the gentleman, and how much he hath eaten. They are never curst but when they are hungry: if there be any of him left, I'll bury it.
BOT: That's a good deed. If thou mayest discern by that which is left of him what he is, fetch me to th' sight of him.
USER: Marry, will I; and you shall help to put him i' th' ground.
BOT: 'Tis a lucky day, boy, and we'll do good deeds on 't.

USER: I pray thee, good Camillo, be no more importunate: 'tis a sickness denying thee anything; a death to grant this.
BOT: I have heard, sir, of such a man, who hath a daughter of most rare note: the report of her is extended more than can be thought to begin from such a cottage.
USER: I willingly obey your command.
BOT: My best Camillo! We must disguise ourselves.

USER: Let me see: every 'leven wether tods; every tod yields pound and odd shilling; fifteen hundred shorn, what comes the wool to?
BOT: I' th' name of me!
USER: O, help me, help me! Pluck but off these rags; and then, death, death!
BOT: Alack, poor soul! thou hast need of more rags to lay on thee, rather than have these off.
USER: O sir, the loathsomeness of them offends me more than the stripes I have received, which are mighty ones and millions.
BOT: Alas, poor man! a million of beating may come to a great matter.
USER: I am robbed, sir, and beaten; my money and apparel ta'en from me, and these detestable things put upon me.
BOT: What, by a horseman or a footman?
USER: A footman, sweet sir, a footman.
BOT: Indeed, he should be a footman by the garments he has left with thee: if this be a horseman's coat, it hath seen very hot service. Lend me thy hand, I'll help thee: come, lend me thy hand.
USER: O, good sir, tenderly, O!
BOT: Alas, poor soul!
USER: O, good sir, softly, good sir. I fear, sir, my shoulder blade is out.
BOT: How now! canst stand?
USER: Softly, dear sir! [_Picks his pocket._] good sir, softly. You ha' done me a charitable office.
BOT: Dost lack any money? I have a little money for thee.
USER: What manner of fellow was he that robbed you?
BOT: A fellow, sir, that I have known to go about with troll-my-dames. I knew him once a servant of the prince; I cannot tell, good sir, for which of his virtues it was, but he was certainly whipped out of the court.
USER: His vices, you would say; there's no virtue whipped out of the court. They cherish it to make it stay there; and yet it will no more but abide.
BOT: Out upon him! prig, for my life, prig: he haunts wakes, fairs, and bear-baitings.
USER: Very true, sir; he, sir, he; that's the rogue that put me into this apparel.
BOT: Not a more cowardly rogue in all Bohemia. If you had but looked big and spit at him, he'd have run.
USER: I must confess to you, sir, I am no fighter. I am false of heart that way; and that he knew, I warrant him.
BOT: How do you now?
USER: Sweet sir, much better than I was. I can stand and walk: I will even take my leave of you and pace softly towards my kinsman's.
BOT: Shall I bring thee on the way?
USER: No, good-faced sir; no, sweet sir.
BOT: Then fare thee well. I must go buy spices for our sheep-shearing.

USER: These your unusual weeds to each part of you Do give a life, no shepherdess, but Flora Peering in April's front. This your sheep-shearing Is as a meeting of the petty gods, And you the queen on 't.
BOT: I bless the time When my good falcon made her flight across Thy father's ground.
USER: O, but, sir, Your resolution cannot hold when 'tis Oppos'd, as it must be, by the power of the king: One of these two must be necessities, Which then will speak, that you must change this purpose, Or I my life.
BOT: O lady Fortune, Stand you auspicious!
USER: See, your guests approach: Address yourself to entertain them sprightly, And let's be red with mirth. Dorcas with others.
BOT: Shepherdess - A fair one are you - well you fit our ages With flowers of winter.
USER: Wherefore, gentle maiden, Do you neglect them?
BOT: For I have heard it said There is an art which, in their piedness, shares With great creating nature.
USER: So it is.
BOT: Then make your garden rich in gillyvors, And do not call them bastards.
USER: I should leave grazing, were I of your flock, And only live by gazing.
BOT: What, like a corse?
USER: I think you have As little skill to fear as I have purpose To put you to 't. But, come; our dance, I pray. Your hand, my Perdita. So turtles pair That never mean to part.
BOT: I'll swear for 'em.
USER: This is the prettiest low-born lass that ever Ran on the green-sward. Nothing she does or seems But smacks of something greater than herself, Too noble for this place.
BOT: He tells her something That makes her blood look out. Good sooth, she is The queen of curds and cream.
USER: Come on, strike up.
BOT: Mopsa must be your mistress: marry, garlic, to mend her kissing with!
USER: Now, in good time!
BOT: Not a word, a word; we stand upon our manners. Come, strike up.
USER: Pray, good shepherd, what fair swain is this Which dances with your daughter?
BOT: She dances featly.
USER: So she does anything, though I report it That should be silent. If young Doricles Do light upon her, she shall bring him that Which he not dreams of.
BOT: He could never come better: he shall come in. I love a ballad but even too well, if it be doleful matter merrily set down, or a very pleasant thing indeed and sung lamentably.
USER: This is a brave fellow.
BOT: Believe me, thou talkest of an admirable conceited fellow. Has he any unbraided wares?
USER: Prithee bring him in; and let him approach singing.
BOT: Forewarn him that he use no scurrilous words in 's tunes.
USER: You have of these pedlars that have more in them than you'd think, sister.
BOT: Ay, good brother, or go about to think.
USER: If I were not in love with Mopsa, thou shouldst take no money of me; but being enthralled as I am, it will also be the bondage of certain ribbons and gloves.
BOT: I was promised them against the feast; but they come not too late now.
USER: He hath promised you more than that, or there be liars.
BOT: He hath paid you all he promised you. Maybe he has paid you more, which will shame you to give him again.
USER: I have done. Come, you promised me a tawdry lace and a pair of sweet gloves.
BOT: Have I not told thee how I was cozened by the way and lost all my money?
USER: And indeed, sir, there are cozeners abroad; therefore it behoves men to be wary.
BOT: Fear not thou, man. Thou shalt lose nothing here.
USER: I hope so, sir; for I have about me many parcels of charge.
BOT: What hast here? Ballads?
USER: Pray now, buy some. I love a ballad in print alife, for then we are sure they are true.
BOT: Here's one to a very doleful tune. How a usurer's wife was brought to bed of twenty money-bags at a burden, and how she longed to eat adders' heads and toads carbonadoed.
USER: Is it true, think you?
BOT: Very true, and but a month old.
USER: Bless me from marrying a usurer!
BOT: Here's the midwife's name to't, one Mistress Taleporter, and five or six honest wives that were present. Why should I carry lies abroad?
USER: Pray you now, buy it.
BOT: Come on, lay it by; and let's first see more ballads. We'll buy the other things anon.
USER: Is it true too, think you?
BOT: Five justices' hands at it, and witnesses more than my pack will hold.
USER: Lay it by too: another.
BOT: This is a merry ballad; but a very pretty one.
USER: Let's have some merry ones.
BOT: Why, this is a passing merry one and goes to the tune of "Two maids wooing a man." There's scarce a maid westward but she sings it. 'Tis in request, I can tell you.
USER: We can both sing it: if thou'lt bear a part, thou shalt hear; 'tis in three parts.
BOT: We had the tune on 't a month ago.
USER: I can bear my part; you must know 'tis my occupation: have at it with you.
BOT: _Get you hence, for I must go Where it fits not you to know._
USER: _Whither?_
BOT: _O, whither?_
USER: _Whither?_
BOT: _It becomes thy oath full well Thou to me thy secrets tell._
USER: _Me too! Let me go thither._
BOT: Or thou goest to th' grange or mill.
USER: _If to either, thou dost ill._
BOT: _Neither._
USER: _What, neither?_
BOT: _Neither._
USER: _Thou hast sworn my love to be._
BOT: _Thou hast sworn it more to me. Then whither goest? Say, whither?_
USER: Away! we'll none on 't. Here has been too much homely foolery already. I know, sir, we weary you.
BOT: You weary those that refresh us: pray, let's see these four threes of herdsmen.
USER: One three of them, by their own report, sir, hath danced before the king; and not the worst of the three but jumps twelve foot and a half by th' square.
BOT: Leave your prating: since these good men are pleased, let them come in; but quickly now.
USER: Why, they stay at door, sir. exeunt.
BOT: What follows this? How prettily the young swain seems to wash The hand was fair before! I have put you out. But to your protestation. Let me hear What you profess.
USER: Do, and be witness to 't.
BOT: And this my neighbour, too?
USER: Fairly offer'd.
BOT: This shows a sound affection.
USER: But my daughter, Say you the like to him?
BOT: I cannot speak So well, nothing so well; no, nor mean better: By th' pattern of mine own thoughts I cut out The purity of his.
USER: Take hands, a bargain! And, friends unknown, you shall bear witness to't. I give my daughter to him, and will make Her portion equal his.
BOT: O, that must be I' th' virtue of your daughter: one being dead, I shall have more than you can dream of yet; Enough then for your wonder. But come on, Contract us 'fore these witnesses.
USER: Come, your hand; And, daughter, yours.
BOT: Soft, swain, awhile, beseech you; Have you a father?
USER: I have; but what of him?
BOT: Knows he of this?
USER: He neither does nor shall.
BOT: No, good sir; He has his health, and ampler strength indeed Than most have of his age.
USER: I yield all this; But for some other reasons, my grave sir, Which 'tis not fit you know, I not acquaint My father of this business.
BOT: Let him know 't.
USER: He shall not.
BOT: Prithee let him.
USER: No, he must not.
BOT: Let him, my son: he shall not need to grieve At knowing of thy choice.
USER: Come, come, he must not. Mark our contract.
BOT: O, my heart!
USER: Why, how now, father! Speak ere thou diest.
BOT: Why look you so upon me? I am but sorry, not afeard; delay'd, But nothing alt'red: what I was, I am: More straining on for plucking back; not following My leash unwillingly.
USER: I not purpose it. I think Camillo?
BOT: Even he, my lord.
USER: How often have I told you 'twould be thus! How often said my dignity would last But till 'twere known!
BOT: It cannot fail but by The violation of my faith; and then Let nature crush the sides o' th' earth together And mar the seeds within! Lift up thy looks. From my succession wipe me, father; I Am heir to my affection.
USER: Be advis'd.
BOT: I am, and by my fancy. If my reason Will thereto be obedient, I have reason; If not, my senses, better pleas'd with madness, Do bid it welcome.
USER: This is desperate, sir.
BOT: O my lord, I would your spirit were easier for advice, Or stronger for your need.
USER: Hark, Perdita. [_Takes her aside._]
BOT: Now, good Camillo, I am so fraught with curious business that I leave out ceremony.
USER: Sir, I think You have heard of my poor services, i' th' love That I have borne your father?
BOT: Very nobly Have you deserv'd: it is my father's music To speak your deeds, not little of his care To have them recompens'd as thought on.
USER: How, Camillo, May this, almost a miracle, be done? That I may call thee something more than man, And after that trust to thee.
BOT: Have you thought on A place whereto you'll go?
USER: Not any yet. But as th' unthought-on accident is guilty To what we wildly do, so we profess Ourselves to be the slaves of chance, and flies Of every wind that blows.
BOT: Worthy Camillo, What colour for my visitation shall I Hold up before him?
USER: I am bound to you: There is some sap in this.
BOT: One of these is true: I think affliction may subdue the cheek, But not take in the mind.
USER: Yea, say you so? There shall not at your father's house, these seven years Be born another such.
BOT: My good Camillo, She is as forward of her breeding as She is i' th' rear our birth.
USER: I cannot say 'tis pity She lacks instructions, for she seems a mistress To most that teach.
BOT: Your pardon, sir; for this I'll blush you thanks.
USER: My prettiest Perdita! But, O, the thorns we stand upon! Camillo, Preserver of my father, now of me, The medicine of our house, how shall we do? We are not furnish'd like Bohemia's son, Nor shall appear in Sicilia.
BOT: Nay, but my letters, by this means being there So soon as you arrive, shall clear that doubt.
USER: And those that you'll procure from king Leontes?
BOT: Shall satisfy your father.
USER: Happy be you! All that you speak shows fair.
BOT: We'll make an instrument of this; omit Nothing may give us aid.
USER: How now, good fellow! why shakest thou so? Fear not, man; here's no harm intended to thee.
BOT: I am a poor fellow, sir.
USER: I am a poor fellow, sir: [_Aside._] I know ye well enough.
BOT: Nay, prithee dispatch: the gentleman is half flayed already.
USER: Are you in earnest, sir? [_Aside._] I smell the trick on't.
BOT: Dispatch, I prithee.
USER: Indeed, I have had earnest; but I cannot with conscience take it.
BOT: I see the play so lies That I must bear a part.
USER: No remedy. Have you done there?
BOT: Should I now meet my father, He would not call me son.
USER: Nay, you shall have no hat. [_Giving it to Perdita._] Come, lady, come. Farewell, my friend.
BOT: Adieu, sir.
USER: O Perdita, what have we twain forgot? Pray you a word.
BOT: Of this escape, and whither they are bound; Wherein my hope is I shall so prevail To force him after: in whose company I shall re-view Sicilia; for whose sight I have a woman's longing.
USER: Fortune speed us! Thus we set on, Camillo, to the sea-side.
BOT: The swifter speed the better.
USER: See, see; what a man you are now! There is no other way but to tell the king she's a changeling, and none of your flesh and blood.
BOT: Nay, but hear me.
USER: Nay, but hear me.
BOT: Go to, then.
USER: I will tell the king all, every word, yea, and his son's pranks too; who, I may say, is no honest man neither to his father nor to me, to go about to make me the king's brother-in-law.
BOT: Indeed, brother-in-law was the farthest off you could have been to him, and then your blood had been the dearer by I know how much an ounce.
USER: Well, let us to the king: there is that in this fardel will make him scratch his beard.
BOT: flight of my master.
USER: Pray heartily he be at' palace.
BOT: chance. Let me pocket up my pedlar's excrement. [_Takes off his false beard._] How now, rustics! whither are you bound?
USER: To the palace, an it like your worship.
BOT: Your affairs there, what, with whom, the condition of that fardel, the place of your dwelling, your names, your ages, of what having, breeding, and anything that is fitting to be known? discover!
USER: We are but plain fellows, sir.
BOT: Your worship had like to have given us one, if you had not taken yourself with the manner.
USER: Are you a courtier, an 't like you, sir?
BOT: My business, sir, is to the king.
USER: What advocate hast thou to him?
BOT: I know not, an 't like you.
USER: Advocate's the court-word for a pheasant. Say you have none.
BOT: None, sir; I have no pheasant, cock nor hen.
USER: How bless'd are we that are not simple men! Yet nature might have made me as these are, Therefore I will not disdain.
BOT: This cannot be but a great courtier.
USER: His garments are rich, but he wears them not handsomely.
BOT: He seems to be the more noble in being fantastical: a great man, I'll warrant; I know by the picking on's teeth.
USER: The fardel there? What's i' th' fardel? Wherefore that box?
BOT: Sir, there lies such secrets in this fardel and box which none must know but the king; and which he shall know within this hour, if I may come to th' speech of him.
USER: Age, thou hast lost thy labour.
BOT: Why, sir?
USER: The king is not at the palace; he is gone aboard a new ship to purge melancholy and air himself: for, if thou beest capable of things serious, thou must know the king is full of grief.
BOT: So 'tis said, sir; about his son, that should have married a shepherd's daughter.
USER: If that shepherd be not in hand-fast, let him fly. The curses he shall have, the tortures he shall feel, will break the back of man, the heart of monster.
BOT: Think you so, sir?
USER: Has the old man e'er a son, sir, do you hear, an 't like you, sir?
BOT: An 't please you, sir, to undertake the business for us, here is that gold I have. I'll make it as much more, and leave this young man in pawn till I bring it you.
USER: After I have done what I promised?
BOT: Ay, sir.
USER: Well, give me the moiety. Are you a party in this business?
BOT: In some sort, sir: but though my case be a pitiful one, I hope I shall not be flayed out of it.
USER: O, that's the case of the shepherd's son. Hang him, he'll be made an example.
BOT: I will trust you. Walk before toward the sea-side; go on the right-hand. I will but look upon the hedge, and follow you.
USER: We are blessed in this man, as I may say, even blessed.
BOT: Let's before, as he bids us. He was provided to do us good.

USER: True, too true, my lord. If, one by one, you wedded all the world, Or from the all that are took something good, To make a perfect woman, she you kill'd Would be unparallel'd.
BOT: You are one of those Would have him wed again.
USER: Good Paulina, Who hast the memory of Hermione, I know, in honour, O that ever I Had squar'd me to thy counsel! Then, even now, I might have look'd upon my queen's full eyes, Have taken treasure from her lips, -
BOT: And left them More rich for what they yielded.
USER: Had she such power, She had just cause.
BOT: She had; and would incense me To murder her I married.
USER: Stars, stars, And all eyes else dead coals! Fear thou no wife; I'll have no wife, Paulina.
BOT: Will you swear Never to marry but by my free leave?
USER: Never, Paulina; so be bless'd my spirit!
BOT: Then, good my lords, bear witness to his oath. CLEOMENES You tempt him over-much.
USER: Unless another, As like Hermione as is her picture, Affront his eye. CLEOMENES Good madam, -
BOT: My true Paulina, We shall not marry till thou bid'st us.
USER: That Shall be when your first queen's again in breath; Never till then.
BOT: One that gives out himself Prince Florizel, Son of Polixenes, with his princess (she The fairest I have yet beheld) desires access To your high presence.
USER: What with him? he comes not Like to his father's greatness: his approach, So out of circumstance and sudden, tells us 'Tis not a visitation fram'd, but forc'd By need and accident. What train?
BOT: But few, And those but mean.
USER: His princess, say you, with him?
BOT: Ay, the most peerless piece of earth, I think, That e'er the sun shone bright on.
USER: How! not women?
BOT: Women will love her that she is a woman More worth than any man; men, that she is The rarest of all women.
USER: Go, Cleomenes; Yourself, assisted with your honour'd friends, Bring them to our embracement. Still, 'tis strange He thus should steal upon us.
BOT: Had our prince, Jewel of children, seen this hour, he had pair'd Well with this lord. There was not full a month Between their births.
USER: Good, my lord, She came from Libya.
BOT: Where the warlike Smalus, That noble honour'd lord, is fear'd and lov'd?
USER: Where's Bohemia? speak.
BOT: Camillo has betray'd me; Whose honour and whose honesty till now, Endur'd all weathers.
USER: Lay 't so to his charge. He's with the king your father.
BOT: Who? Camillo?
USER: O my poor father! The heaven sets spies upon us, will not have Our contract celebrated.
BOT: You are married?
USER: We are not, sir, nor are we like to be. The stars, I see, will kiss the valleys first. The odds for high and low's alike.
BOT: My lord, Is this the daughter of a king?
USER: She is, When once she is my wife.
BOT: Would he do so, I'd beg your precious mistress, Which he counts but a trifle.
USER: Sir, my liege, Your eye hath too much youth in 't: not a month 'Fore your queen died, she was more worth such gazes Than what you look on now.

USER: Beseech you, sir, were you present at this relation?
BOT: I would most gladly know the issue of it.
USER: No.
BOT: What, pray you, became of Antigonus, that carried hence the child?
USER: What became of his bark and his followers?
BOT: The dignity of this act was worth the audience of kings and princes; for by such was it acted.
USER: Are they returned to the court?
BOT: I thought she had some great matter there in hand; for she hath privately twice or thrice a day, ever since the death of Hermione, visited that removed house. Shall we thither, and with our company piece the rejoicing?
USER: Who would be thence that has the benefit of access? Every wink of an eye some new grace will be born. Our absence makes us unthrifty to our knowledge. Let's along.
BOT: Come, boy; I am past more children, but thy sons and daughters will be all gentlemen born.
USER: I know you are now, sir, a gentleman born.
BOT: Ay, and have been so any time these four hours.
USER: And so have I, boy!
BOT: We may live, son, to shed many more.
USER: Ay; or else 'twere hard luck, being in so preposterous estate as we are.
BOT: I humbly beseech you, sir, to pardon me all the faults I have committed to your worship, and to give me your good report to the prince my master.
USER: Prithee, son, do; for we must be gentle, now we are gentlemen.
BOT: Thou wilt amend thy life?
USER: Ay, an it like your good worship.
BOT: Give me thy hand: I will swear to the prince thou art as honest a true fellow as any is in Bohemia.
USER: You may say it, but not swear it.
BOT: Not swear it, now I am a gentleman? Let boors and franklins say it, I'll swear it.
USER: How if it be false, son?
BOT: I will prove so, sir, to my power.

USER: O grave and good Paulina, the great comfort That I have had of thee!
BOT: O, not by much!
USER: So much the more our carver's excellence, Which lets go by some sixteen years and makes her As she liv'd now.
BOT: And give me leave, And do not say 'tis superstition, that I kneel, and then implore her blessing. Lady, Dear queen, that ended when I but began, Give me that hand of yours to kiss.
USER: O, patience! The statue is but newly fix'd, the colour's Not dry.
BOT: My lord, your sorrow was too sore laid on, Which sixteen winters cannot blow away, So many summers dry. Scarce any joy Did ever so long live; no sorrow But kill'd itself much sooner.
USER: Dear my brother, Let him that was the cause of this have power To take off so much grief from you as he Will piece up in himself.
BOT: Indeed, my lord, If I had thought the sight of my poor image Would thus have wrought you - for the stone is mine - I'd not have show'd it.
USER: Do not draw the curtain.
BOT: No longer shall you gaze on't, lest your fancy May think anon it moves.
USER: Let be, let be. Would I were dead, but that methinks already - What was he that did make it? See, my lord, Would you not deem it breath'd? And that those veins Did verily bear blood?
BOT: Masterly done: The very life seems warm upon her lip.
USER: The fixture of her eye has motion in 't, As we are mock'd with art.
BOT: I'll draw the curtain: My lord's almost so far transported that He'll think anon it lives.
USER: O sweet Paulina, Make me to think so twenty years together! No settled senses of the world can match The pleasure of that madness. Let 't alone.
BOT: I am sorry, sir, I have thus far stirr'd you: but I could afflict you further.
USER: Do, Paulina; For this affliction has a taste as sweet As any cordial comfort. Still methinks There is an air comes from her. What fine chisel Could ever yet cut breath? Let no man mock me, For I will kiss her!
BOT: Good my lord, forbear: The ruddiness upon her lip is wet; You'll mar it if you kiss it, stain your own With oily painting. Shall I draw the curtain?
USER: No, not these twenty years.
BOT: So long could I Stand by, a looker on.
USER: What you can make her do I am content to look on: what to speak, I am content to hear; for 'tis as easy To make her speak as move.
BOT: It is requir'd You do awake your faith. Then all stand still; Or those that think it is unlawful business I am about, let them depart.
USER: Proceed: No foot shall stir.
BOT: If this be magic, let it be an art Lawful as eating.
USER: She embraces him.
BOT: She hangs about his neck. If she pertain to life, let her speak too.
